symptoms: Palpitations, syncope, AF, ventricular arrhythmias, SCD risk
treatment_goal: Reduce arrhythmia burden, prevent SCD and AF-related stroke
first_line_soc: Beta-blockers or verapamil for symptom control. Amiodarone or disopyramide for AF/VT. Anticoagulation (DOACs) for AF. ICD for high SCD risk.
treatment_algorithm: AF management: rate control (beta-blockers, verapamil) + rhythm control (amiodarone, disopyramide) + anticoagulation (DOACs preferred). SCD prevention: ICD for high-risk patients. Cardiac myosin inhibitor mavacamten (Camzyos, ~$89K/year WAC) reduces LVOT gradient and may reduce arrhythmic substrate. Catheter ablation of AF and VT circuits. Septal reduction therapy (LVOT obstruction). Genetic testing for SCD risk factors.
us_prevalence: HCM total ~700,000–750,000 US patients (1 in 500 prevalence); with arrhythmias: ~200,000–300,000.
patients_needing_tx: ~200,000–250,000 HCM patients with clinically significant arrhythmias requiring pharmacologic management.
price: $80,000–$100,000/year for cardiac myosin inhibitors + antiarrhythmic combination (mavacamten benchmark $89.5K WAC).
registrational_endpoint: AF burden reduction (% time in AF by implantable loop recorder); composite of arrhythmic events (AF, VT, appropriate ICD shock) over 12 months.
btd_threshold_vs_soc: ≥40% reduction in AF burden vs. standard antiarrhythmics; ≥50% reduction in composite arrhythmic events vs. amiodarone.

symptoms: Prolonged QTc on ECG, syncope, torsades de pointes, sudden cardiac death
treatment_goal: Shorten QTc, prevent torsades de pointes and sudden cardiac death
first_line_soc: Beta-blockers (nadolol or propranolol) are first-line for LQT1 and LQT2. Mexiletine is first-line adjunct for LQT3. Avoid QT-prolonging drugs.
treatment_algorithm: LQT1: beta-blockers (nadolol preferred), avoid competitive sports. LQT2: beta-blockers + avoid hypokalemia/sudden sounds; mexiletine/flecainide add-on. LQT3 (SCN5A gain-of-function): mexiletine (sodium channel blocker) — markedly effective; beta-blockers less effective. ICD for survivors of cardiac arrest; consider for breakthrough events on beta-blockers. LCSD for patients intolerant of ICD or refractory. Gene-specific therapy: late sodium current inhibitors (ranolazine, GS-458967) for LQT3 in trials. Gene silencing (siRNA) for LQT2 (KCNH2 dominant-negative) investigational.
us_prevalence: ~500,000–1,000,000 US patients (prevalence ~1 in 2,000; many undiagnosed). Diagnosed/symptomatic: ~100,000–200,000.
patients_needing_tx: ~150,000–200,000 requiring pharmacologic management.
price: $40,000–$80,000/year for a genotype-specific ion channel modulator.
registrational_endpoint: QTc shortening from baseline (ms) at 4–8 weeks; reduction in syncope rate; VT/VF-free survival.
btd_threshold_vs_soc: QTc shortening ≥30 ms at steady state (vs. ~10–15 ms with beta-blockers in LQT2/3); or ≥60% reduction in syncope over 12 months.

symptoms: Progressive heart block, bradycardia, syncope, pacemaker dependency
treatment_goal: Restore conduction, prevent complete heart block and sudden death
first_line_soc: Pacemaker implantation is the definitive treatment for symptomatic high-degree AV block. No approved pharmacologic SoC for the underlying progressive conduction disease.
treatment_algorithm: Pacemaker (PM) for complete/high-degree AV block. Identify and treat reversible causes (Lyme, sarcoidosis, amyloidosis). LMNA mutation carriers: earlier ICD consideration (risk of VT/VF beyond just conduction disease). Genetic testing (SCN5A, LMNA, GJA5 mutations) guides prognosis and family screening. Investigational: SCN5A gene therapy, HCN4-targeted agents. No approved pharmacologic disease-modifying therapy.
us_prevalence: ~100,000–300,000 US patients with progressive conduction disease requiring pacemakers annually; genetic isolated PCCD (Lev-Lenègre): ~50,000–100,000.
patients_needing_tx: ~80,000–100,000 genetic PCCD patients needing pharmacologic or device management.
price: $80,000–$150,000/year for gene therapy or SCN5A modulator.
registrational_endpoint: Time to pacemaker implantation; PR interval and QRS duration progression at 24 months.
btd_threshold_vs_soc: ≥30% delay in time to pacemaker implantation vs. placebo; PR interval stabilization vs. progressive prolongation in control.

symptoms: Syncope, sudden cardiac death (often nocturnal), abnormal ECG (Type 1 pattern)
treatment_goal: Prevent VF and sudden cardiac death
first_line_soc: ICD implantation is first-line for patients with prior VF or spontaneous Type 1 ECG with symptoms. For fever-triggered events: antipyretics. Quinidine (class IA) or isoproterenol for electrical storm.
treatment_algorithm: Asymptomatic/drug-induced Type 1: observation, avoid sodium channel blockers, manage fever aggressively. Symptomatic: ICD (gold standard for SCD prevention). Pharmacologic: quinidine (only drug with evidence for VF reduction; often unavailable). Radiofrequency catheter ablation of epicardial RV substrate (emerging). Avoid: class IC/IA Na+ channel blockers, TCAs, lithium, cocaine. Pipeline: SCN5A gene therapy; late sodium current modulators in research.
us_prevalence: ~100,000–200,000 US patients with Brugada ECG pattern; symptomatic (clinically significant) ~20,000–50,000.
patients_needing_tx: ~30,000–50,000 requiring active management (ICD and/or pharmacologic).
price: $50,000–$120,000/year for a novel SCN5A-targeted therapy or sodium channel modulator.
registrational_endpoint: VF-free survival / appropriate ICD therapy-free survival over 2–3 years.
btd_threshold_vs_soc: ≥50% reduction in VF or appropriate ICD events vs. quinidine or observation in high-risk patients.

symptoms: Heart block, ventricular arrhythmias, heart failure, sudden cardiac death
treatment_goal: Suppress inflammation, prevent arrhythmias and heart block progression
first_line_soc: Corticosteroids (prednisone 30–40 mg/day tapered) are first-line to suppress granulomatous inflammation and improve conduction.
treatment_algorithm: Corticosteroids (first-line); add steroid-sparing agents for long-term (methotrexate, azathioprine, hydroxychloroquine) to reduce steroid toxicity. ICD for significant arrhythmias or low EF. Pacemaker for complete heart block. Antiarrhythmics (amiodarone, sotalol) for VT. Catheter ablation for refractory VT. Advanced disease: cardiac transplantation. Investigational: JAK inhibitors (ruxolitinib) for refractory cardiac sarcoidosis (case series 2024–2025).
us_prevalence: ~25,000–50,000 US patients with cardiac sarcoidosis (cardiac involvement in ~5–10% of systemic sarcoidosis; US sarcoidosis prevalence ~200,000).
patients_needing_tx: ~20,000–40,000 requiring pharmacologic therapy.
price: $50,000–$150,000/year for targeted anti-inflammatory (JAK inhibitor, anti-TNF in cardiac sarcoidosis).
registrational_endpoint: PET-CT cardiac FDG uptake (standardized uptake value) reduction at 6 months; LVEF improvement; conduction disturbance resolution rate.
btd_threshold_vs_soc: ≥50% reduction in FDG-PET cardiac inflammation; LVEF improvement ≥5% vs. prednisone alone; AV block resolution in ≥50% vs. ~40% with steroids.

symptoms: Palpitations, syncope, ventricular arrhythmias, right ventricular failure
treatment_goal: Prevent sudden cardiac death, control arrhythmias, slow progression
first_line_soc: ICD implantation for SCD prevention in high-risk patients. Beta-blockers (sotalol, metoprolol) as first-line antiarrhythmic therapy.
treatment_algorithm: Lifestyle: competitive sports restriction (critical). Antiarrhythmics: beta-blockers (sotalol preferred for VT suppression), amiodarone for refractory VT. ICD for secondary prevention (post-VF/sustained VT) and high-risk primary prevention. Catheter ablation for recurrent VT refractory to medications. Advanced disease: cardiac transplantation. 2024 pipeline: flecainide for ARVC-related PVC burden; no approved disease-modifying agents. Anti-desmosomal gene therapies in preclinical development.
us_prevalence: ~25,000–50,000 US patients (prevalence ~1 in 5,000–10,000; higher with genetic screening).
patients_needing_tx: ~20,000–30,000 patients requiring pharmacologic treatment and/or ICD. ~15,000 on antiarrhythmic therapy.
price: $50,000–$100,000/year for a novel desmosomal stabilizer or anti-arrhythmic agent.
registrational_endpoint: Composite of sustained VT, VF, appropriate ICD shock, or SCD over 18–24 months.
btd_threshold_vs_soc: ≥40% reduction in composite arrhythmic events vs. standard beta-blocker therapy.

symptoms: Heart failure, arrhythmias, thromboemboli, prominent trabeculations on echo
treatment_goal: Prevent HF progression, arrhythmias, and thromboembolic events
first_line_soc: Standard HFrEF therapy (ACEi/ARB/ARNI + beta-blocker + MRA + SGLT2i) for those with reduced ejection fraction. Anticoagulation for LV thrombus or severe dysfunction.
treatment_algorithm: HFrEF SoC: ARNI (sacubitril/valsartan), beta-blocker, ACEi/ARB, MRA, SGLT2i (quadruple therapy). Anticoagulation (warfarin or DOAC) for EF <35% or documented thrombus. ICD for EF <35% or symptomatic VT. Cardiac resynchronization (LBBB + EF <35%). For preserved/normal EF: symptom management + arrhythmia treatment. Cardiac transplantation for end-stage. No LVNC-specific approved therapies. Genetic testing (MYH7, MYBPC3, TTN) guides family screening.
us_prevalence: ~20,000–50,000 US patients (population prevalence ~0.01–0.05%).
patients_needing_tx: ~15,000–30,000 requiring pharmacologic management.
price: $50,000–$100,000/year for a gene/sarcomere-targeted therapy.
registrational_endpoint: Composite of HF hospitalization, VT/VF, systemic embolism, or death at 24 months; LVEF change.
btd_threshold_vs_soc: ≥30% reduction in composite events vs. standard HF therapy; LVEF improvement ≥8% in ≥40% of patients.

symptoms: Arrhythmias, heart block, dilated cardiomyopathy, skeletal muscle weakness
treatment_goal: Prevent sudden cardiac death, manage HF, slow cardiomyopathy progression
first_line_soc: ICD implantation is strongly recommended for LMNA mutation carriers with arrhythmias or EF <45%. Beta-blockers and ACE inhibitors/ARBs for HF management.
treatment_algorithm: ICD for SCD prevention (LMNA carriers have high VT/VF risk; lower threshold than other DCM). HF therapy: ACEi/ARB, beta-blocker, MRA, SGLT2i (guideline-directed medical therapy). Antiarrhythmics: amiodarone as bridge. Pacemaker for complete heart block. Cardiac resynchronization therapy (CRT) for LBBB + reduced EF. LVAD/transplant for end-stage. Research: LMNA gene correction with base editing (preclinical 2024–2025). No approved disease-modifying agent.
us_prevalence: ~2,000–5,000 US patients with confirmed LMNA-related cardiomyopathy (LMNA mutations in 5–10% of familial DCM; DCM US prevalence ~250,000).
patients_needing_tx: ~3,000–5,000 patients requiring pharmacologic/device management.
price: $200,000–$500,000/year for LMNA-targeted gene therapy or splicing correction approach.
registrational_endpoint: Composite of VT, VF, appropriate ICD shock, HF hospitalization, or death at 24 months; LVEF change from baseline.
btd_threshold_vs_soc: ≥40% reduction in composite endpoint vs. standard HF therapy; LVEF improvement ≥5% in ≥40% of patients.

symptoms: Exercise/emotion-triggered syncope, bidirectional VT, sudden cardiac death
treatment_goal: Prevent exercise-induced VT/VF and sudden cardiac death
first_line_soc: Beta-blockers (nadolol preferred over metoprolol for RyR2 mutation carriers) are first-line pharmacologic therapy; flecainide as add-on for breakthrough VT.
treatment_algorithm: Beta-blockers (nadolol at maximally tolerated dose — superior to atenolol/metoprolol for CPVT). Add flecainide for breakthrough events (sodium channel stabilization of RyR2 — Class IIa). ICD for survivors of cardiac arrest, breakthrough VT on therapy. Left cardiac sympathetic denervation (LCSD) for refractory VT. Strict exercise restriction. RYR2 and CASQ2 gene therapy in preclinical development.
us_prevalence: ~5,000–15,000 US patients (prevalence ~1 in 10,000–20,000).
patients_needing_tx: ~8,000–12,000 requiring pharmacologic therapy.
price: $50,000–$150,000/year for a RyR2-targeted stabilizer (flecainide-successor class).
registrational_endpoint: Exercise stress test: suppression of ≥2 consecutive VPBs or bidirectional VT on maximum exercise at endpoint.
btd_threshold_vs_soc: ≥70% reduction in exercise-induced VT burden vs. beta-blocker alone; VT-free exercise test in ≥60% vs. ~30% with nadolol monotherapy.

symptoms: Often asymptomatic; VF, sudden cardiac death in young, idiopathic
treatment_goal: Prevent VF and sudden cardiac death in high-risk patients
first_line_soc: ICD implantation for survivors of VF. Quinidine for recurrent VF or ICD shocks. No approved drug-specific SoC beyond ICD.
treatment_algorithm: Observation for asymptomatic ERP. Survivors of VF: ICD mandatory. Quinidine (Ito current blocker) for VF storm. Isoproterenol IV for acute VF storm. Catheter ablation of triggering PVCs emerging as adjunct. Research: identify high-risk genotypes (KCNJ8, CACNA1C, ABCC9 mutations) — genetic testing-guided risk stratification. Avoid sodium channel blockers.
us_prevalence: ~500,000–1,000,000 US adults with early repolarization ECG pattern; symptomatic/high-risk ERS with VF risk: ~5,000–15,000.
patients_needing_tx: High-risk ERS: ~5,000–10,000 requiring pharmacologic/device management.
price: $50,000–$100,000/year for genotype-specific SCD prevention agent.
registrational_endpoint: VF-free survival / appropriate ICD therapy over 24–36 months.
btd_threshold_vs_soc: ≥60% reduction in VF events vs. observation; or VF-free survival >90% at 2 years vs. ~70% with ICD alone.

symptoms: Shortened QTc on ECG, palpitations, AF, sudden cardiac death
treatment_goal: Prevent VF and sudden cardiac death; lengthen QT interval
first_line_soc: ICD implantation for survivors of VF or high-risk patients. Quinidine (increases QT interval) is the primary pharmacologic option for SQT1.
treatment_algorithm: ICD for all symptomatic patients and SCA survivors. Quinidine for SQT1 (KCNH2 gain-of-function) — effective but limited availability. Sotalol, propafenone considered for SQT2/3. Avoid AF triggers. Genetic testing for KCNH2, KCNQ1, KCNJ2 mutations. Research: specific KCNH2 gain-of-function blockers; extremely limited pipeline given rarity.
us_prevalence: Extremely rare: <5,000 US patients. Estimated 1,000–3,000.
patients_needing_tx: ~2,000 requiring pharmacologic management.
price: $100,000–$200,000/year for a specific KCNH2 blocker.
registrational_endpoint: QTc prolongation (ms) from baseline; VF-free survival over 24 months.
btd_threshold_vs_soc: QTc prolongation ≥40 ms at steady state vs. quinidine (~30 ms) without quinidine's adverse effects; VF-free rate ≥90% at 2 years.

symptoms: Chest pain, leg pain with walking, stroke, heart attack, silent arterial narrowing
treatment_goal: Reduce LDL, prevent MACE (MI, stroke, CV death)
first_line_soc: High-intensity statins (atorvastatin 40–80mg, rosuvastatin 20–40mg) are first-line for LDL reduction and CV risk reduction.
treatment_algorithm: Step 1: High-intensity statins. Step 2: Add ezetimibe (Zetia) if LDL >70 mg/dL on max statin. Step 3: PCSK9 inhibitors (evolocumab/Repatha, alirocumab/Praluent) for very high-risk or FH patients. Step 4: Inclisiran (Leqvio, siRNA PCSK9 inhibitor, 2× yearly injection). Newer agents: bempedoic acid (Nexletol) for statin-intolerant. Icosapentaenoic acid (Vascepa) for elevated TG + statin-treated patients. Antiplatelet therapy (aspirin for secondary prevention, P2Y12 inhibitors for dual antiplatelet). BP control (ACEi/ARB, CCB). Pipeline 2024–2025: zilebesiran (siRNA angiotensinogen), pelacarsen (Lp(a) lowering, Phase 3).
us_prevalence: ~18–20 million US adults with clinically significant atherosclerotic CVD (ASCVD). Very high CV risk population: ~40–50 million requiring intensive LDL management.
patients_needing_tx: ~20M with established ASCVD needing secondary prevention. ~40M high-risk for primary prevention. Novel agent (e.g., Lp(a) inhibitor) addressable population: ~10–20M with elevated Lp(a).
price: $6,000–$12,000/year for siRNA agents (inclisiran: ~$6,500/year); $15,000–$25,000/year for next-gen PCSK9 antibodies; Lp(a) reducers $50,000–$80,000/year.
tam_gross: Lp(a) inhibitor: 15M × $60K = $900B (theoretical); realistic: 5M patients on novel agent × $60K = $300B
peak_sales: Lp(a) inhibitor (pelacarsen/olpasiran): 3M patients × $60K × 10% share = $18B; siRNA PCSK9: 3M × $6.5K × 30% = $5.85B
registrational_endpoint: MACE composite (CV death, non-fatal MI, non-fatal stroke) over 3–5 year RCT (time-to-event).
btd_threshold_vs_soc: ≥20% MACE reduction on top of maximally tolerated statin therapy (HR ≤0.80); Lp(a) reducer: ≥25% MACE reduction vs. placebo.

symptoms: Stiff arteries, elevated pulse pressure, heart failure with preserved EF, kidney disease
treatment_goal: Reduce arterial stiffness, control hypertension, prevent organ damage
first_line_soc: Antihypertensive therapy (ACEi/ARB, dihydropyridine CCBs) to control blood pressure and reduce arterial wall stress.
treatment_algorithm: Antihypertensives: ACEi/ARB (RAAS blockade for renal protection and arterial remodeling), CCBs (amlodipine). Phosphodiesterase inhibitors (investigational for arterial stiffness). Lifestyle modification. No specific approved pharmacologic agent targeting arterial stiffness directly. Research: AGE-crosslink breakers (alagebrium, failed; newer compounds in development), extracellular matrix modulators. SGLT2 inhibitors show modest arterial stiffness reduction.
us_prevalence: ~50–70 million US adults with clinically significant arterial stiffness/medial calcification (subset of hypertension population).
patients_needing_tx: ~30–40M requiring antihypertensive pharmacotherapy; novel arterial stiffness-specific agent: ~5–10M addressable.
price: $3,000–$10,000/year for antihypertensives; novel AGE-crosslink breaker: $15,000–$30,000/year.
peak_sales: 5M × $20K × 10% share = $10B (if distinct arterial stiffness indication approved)
registrational_endpoint: Change in aortic pulse wave velocity (aPWV, m/s) at 12–24 months; SBP reduction.
btd_threshold_vs_soc: ≥1.5 m/s reduction in aPWV vs. antihypertensive control; SBP reduction ≥5 mmHg beyond SoC.

symptoms: Emboli, obstruction, arrhythmias, dyspnea, constitutional symptoms (variable by type)
treatment_goal: Remove tumor surgically; prevent embolic complications; palliative control for malignant
first_line_soc: Surgical resection is first-line for benign primary cardiac tumors (myxoma, fibroelastoma). No pharmacologic SoC for primary cardiac tumors. Systemic chemotherapy (anthracycline-based) for primary cardiac sarcomas.
treatment_algorithm: Benign primary (myxoma, lipoma): urgent surgical resection + anticoagulation until surgery. Malignant primary sarcomas (angiosarcoma, rhabdomyosarcoma): anthracycline + ifosfamide ± surgery ± radiation. Metastatic/secondary cardiac tumors: systemic therapy per primary malignancy. Carcinoid-related cardiac disease: octreotide/lanreotide for symptom control; valve surgery. Tuberous sclerosis rhabdomyomas: everolimus (mTOR inhibitor) causes regression — FDA-approved for TSC.
us_prevalence: ~200,000–500,000 with incidental cardiac masses (most benign); primary cardiac tumors: ~2,000–5,000 cases/year. Cardiac metastases: ~50,000+.
patients_needing_tx: ~5,000–10,000/year requiring pharmacologic or surgical intervention; malignant cardiac tumors: ~1,000–2,000/year.
price: $100,000–$300,000/year for targeted therapy in cardiac sarcoma or TSC/rhabdomyoma.
registrational_endpoint: Overall survival and progression-free survival at 12–24 months for malignant cardiac tumors; cardiac tumor volume reduction (% change) for TSC.
btd_threshold_vs_soc: mOS >18 months for cardiac angiosarcoma vs. ~12 months historical; tumor regression ≥50% at 3 months for TSC (everolimus achieves this).

symptoms: Severe dyspnea, edema, ascites, elevated JVP, HF with preserved EF
treatment_goal: Manage fluid overload, treat underlying cause, bridge to transplant
first_line_soc: Diuretics (loop diuretics: furosemide, torsemide) for fluid management. Treatment of underlying cause (amyloidosis, sarcoidosis, hemochromatosis) is paramount.
treatment_algorithm: Etiology-directed: ATTR amyloid: tafamidis (Vyndaqel/Vyndamax, $225–268K/year), acoramidis (Attruby, FDA approved 2024), patisiran/vutrisiran for hATTR. AL amyloid: chemotherapy (bortezomib, daratumumab regimens). Sarcoidosis: corticosteroids. Hemochromatosis: phlebotomy/chelation. Eosinophilic (Löffler/endomyocardial fibrosis): corticosteroids. General HFpEF: diuretics, SGLT2 inhibitors (modest benefit). Advanced: heart transplantation. Avoid digoxin/CCBs in ATTR amyloid.
us_prevalence: ~50,000–100,000 US patients with restrictive cardiomyopathy pattern; ATTR amyloid (main cause): ~250,000–500,000 US patients.
patients_needing_tx: ~50,000 primary RCM + ~250,000 ATTR amyloid on pharmacologic treatment.
price: $200,000–$270,000/year for ATTR stabilizers (tafamidis $225–268K; acoramidis ~$225K).
registrational_endpoint: All-cause mortality and CV hospitalization composite (time-to-event); 6-minute walk test; KCCQ score at 30 months.
btd_threshold_vs_soc: ≥30% relative risk reduction in all-cause mortality vs. placebo (tafamidis: 30% reduction at 30 months in ATTR-ACT); KCCQ-OS improvement ≥5 points.

symptoms: Exertional dyspnea, chest pain, syncope, palpitations, heart failure
treatment_goal: Reduce LVOT obstruction, improve symptoms, prevent SCD
first_line_soc: Beta-blockers (metoprolol, atenolol) or non-dihydropyridine CCBs (verapamil) for obstructive symptoms. Mavacamten (Camzyos, cardiac myosin inhibitor) FDA-approved 2022 for obstructive HCM (oHCM) — now first-line option per 2024 AHA/ACC guidelines.
treatment_algorithm: oHCM: Step 1: Beta-blockers or verapamil (symptom relief). Step 2: Mavacamten (Camzyos, $89.5K/year WAC) — first-in-class cardiac myosin inhibitor; aficamten (Nuvation Bio/Cytokinetics, NDA submitted 2025) as competitor. Disopyramide add-on. Step 3: Septal reduction therapy (alcohol septal ablation or surgical myectomy) for drug-refractory. SCD prevention: ICD per 2024 AHA/ACC risk calculator (HCM Risk-SCD). Non-obstructive HCM: treat HF symptoms, ICD for high-risk. AF: anticoagulation + rhythm control.
us_prevalence: ~700,000–750,000 US patients (1 in 500 prevalence per 2024 AHA/ACC guidelines); oHCM: ~350,000.
patients_needing_tx: ~350,000 oHCM patients; ~150,000–200,000 candidates for myosin inhibitor therapy.
price: $89,000–$100,000/year (mavacamten $89.5K WAC; aficamten expected similar or slightly lower).
registrational_endpoint: pVO2 improvement ≥1.5 mL/kg/min + NYHA class improvement by ≥1, or pVO2 improvement ≥3.0 mL/kg/min without worsening at 30 weeks (EXPLORER-HCM design).
btd_threshold_vs_soc: ≥35% of patients achieving composite pVO2/NYHA endpoint vs. ~17% placebo (mavacamten benchmark); LVOT gradient <30 mmHg in ≥50% of patients.

symptoms: Progressive breathlessness, edema, fatigue, reduced exercise tolerance
treatment_goal: Improve EF, reduce hospitalizations and mortality, prevent SCD
first_line_soc: RAAS inhibitors (ACEi or ARNI: sacubitril/valsartan/Entresto) + beta-blockers (carvedilol, metoprolol succinate) as cornerstone first-line therapy.
treatment_algorithm: Guideline-directed medical therapy (GDMT) quadruple combination: (1) ARNI (sacubitril/valsartan, Entresto) or ACEi; (2) Beta-blocker; (3) MRA (spironolactone, eplerenone); (4) SGLT2 inhibitor (dapagliflozin/Farxiga, empagliflozin/Jardiance). ICD for EF <35%. CRT for EF <35% + LBBB. Ivabradine for elevated HR on max beta-blocker. Vericiguat (soluble guanylate cyclase stimulator) for high-risk HFrEF. Cardiac transplantation/LVAD for end-stage. Emerging: mavacamten for HCM-related DCM; ACM gene therapy trials for TTN/LMNA variants.
us_prevalence: ~250,000–400,000 US patients with primary DCM (broader HFrEF: ~3.5M).
patients_needing_tx: ~250,000–300,000 primary DCM patients; ~3M HFrEF patients overall on GDMT.
price: $6,000–$12,000/year for SGLT2i (existing; dapagliflozin ~$5,500/year); novel gene therapy: $2,000,000–$5,000,000 one-time.
registrational_endpoint: Composite of CV death and HF hospitalization (time-to-first event); LVEF change from baseline at 12 months.
btd_threshold_vs_soc: ≥25% reduction in HF hospitalization + CV death vs. maximally optimized GDMT; LVEF improvement ≥8% in ≥40% of patients.

symptoms: Chest tightness, pressure, or burning with exertion, relieved by rest/nitroglycerin
treatment_goal: Eliminate angina, improve exercise tolerance, prevent MI
first_line_soc: Short-acting nitroglycerin for acute relief + beta-blockers as first-line for chronic stable angina prevention. CCBs (amlodipine, diltiazem) as alternatives.
treatment_algorithm: Step 1: Beta-blockers (metoprolol, atenolol, carvedilol). Alternatively, CCBs (amlodipine, diltiazem). Step 2: Long-acting nitrates (isosorbide mono/dinitrate) as add-on; ranolazine (Ranexa, late sodium current inhibitor). Step 3: Ivabradine (rate reduction), trimetazidine (metabolic, EU). Revascularization (PCI/CABG) for refractory or anatomically appropriate cases. Risk factor modification: statins, antihypertensives, antiplatelet. Pipeline: coronary sinus reducer for refractory angina; gene therapy for angiogenesis (VEGF).
us_prevalence: ~9–10M US adults with stable angina (AHA 2025 Heart/Stroke stats).
patients_needing_tx: ~8M requiring pharmacologic management.
price: $15,000–$30,000/year for a novel anti-anginal (ranolazine ~$4,000/year; premium novel agent).
registrational_endpoint: Change in total exercise duration (treadmill ETT, Bruce protocol) from baseline; angina frequency (episodes/week).
btd_threshold_vs_soc: ≥60s improvement in exercise duration vs. beta-blocker + CCB; ≥50% reduction in weekly angina episodes vs. SoC.

symptoms: Chest pain at rest or minimal exertion, diaphoresis, dyspnea, ST changes
treatment_goal: Restore coronary perfusion, prevent reinfarction, reduce mortality
first_line_soc: Dual antiplatelet therapy (aspirin + P2Y12 inhibitor: ticagrelor/Brilinta or prasugrel/Effient preferred over clopidogrel) + anticoagulation (heparin/enoxaparin) + high-intensity statin initiated immediately.
treatment_algorithm: Acute phase: aspirin + P2Y12 inhibitor (ticagrelor or prasugrel) + anticoagulant (UFH, LMWH, bivalirudin, or fondaparinux). Primary PCI preferred for STEMI. Fibrinolysis if PCI not available within 120 min. Secondary prevention: DAPT 12 months, high-intensity statin (atorvastatin 80mg), ACEi/ARB, beta-blocker. PCSK9 inhibitors if LDL ≥70 on max statin. Colchicine (LoDoCo2) for residual inflammatory risk. Icosapentaenoic acid (Vascepa) for elevated TG. New 2024: ziltivekimab (IL-6 ligand inhibitor, RESCUE-ACS positive signal); colchicine for ACS inflammatory risk.
us_prevalence: ~800,000 ACS events/year in US (800K MI + UA). Prevalence of patients with prior ACS history: ~8–10M.
patients_needing_tx: ~800,000 acute events/year requiring immediate pharmacologic treatment; ~8M secondary prevention patients on long-term therapy.
price: $5,000–$15,000/year for antiinflammatory add-ons (colchicine ~$1,500/year); $10,000–$25,000/year for novel anti-thrombotic.
registrational_endpoint: MACE composite (CV death, MI, stroke, urgent revascularization) at 12 months (time-to-first event).
btd_threshold_vs_soc: ≥15% MACE reduction on top of DAPT + statin (HR ≤0.85); or ≥25% for high-sensitivity CRP >2 mg/L subgroup.

symptoms: Severe chest pain, diaphoresis, nausea, ST elevation, troponin rise
treatment_goal: Restore perfusion, limit infarct size, prevent HF and recurrent events
first_line_soc: Primary PCI + aspirin + P2Y12 inhibitor (ticagrelor/prasugrel) + anticoagulation + high-intensity statin. Beta-blockers and ACEi initiated post-MI.
treatment_algorithm: See ACS SoC above (STEMI-specific). Primary PCI within 90 min of first medical contact. Pharmacologic: aspirin + ticagrelor (or prasugrel for PCI), + UFH or bivalirudin during procedure. Post-MI: beta-blockers (carvedilol/metoprolol), ACEi/ARB, statin (atorvastatin 80mg), DAPT 12 months, MRA (eplerenone for EF <40% + DM or HF — EPHESUS). SGLT2i if EF <40% developing (DAPA-HF, EMPEROR-Reduced). ICD if EF persists <35% at 40 days. Novel post-MI: inclisiran, PCSK9 inhibitors to intensify LDL control; omega-3 FA (Vascepa).
us_prevalence: ~800,000 acute MI events/year (CDC/AHA 2025 stats); ~8–10M prevalent post-MI patients.
patients_needing_tx: ~800,000 acute + ~8M chronic post-MI secondary prevention.
price: $8,000–$20,000/year for novel add-on therapy (post-MI inflammatory target, lipid-lowering).
registrational_endpoint: Infarct size (% LV mass by CMR at 30 days) for acute agents; MACE over 12–24 months for secondary prevention.
btd_threshold_vs_soc: ≥25% reduction in infarct size by CMR vs. PCI alone; or ≥20% reduction in MACE over 12 months on top of optimal therapy.

symptoms: Chest pain without obstructive CAD, exertional/rest symptoms, abnormal perfusion
treatment_goal: Reduce ischemia, relieve chest pain, improve quality of life
first_line_soc: Beta-blockers and long-acting nitrates are typically first-line. ACEi/ARB for endothelial dysfunction. Ranolazine for refractory symptoms.
treatment_algorithm: Step 1: Lifestyle (exercise, smoking cessation). Step 2: Beta-blockers (reduce demand ischemia); CCBs (reduce vasospasm component). Step 3: Ranolazine (Ranexa). Step 4: ACEi/ARB for endothelial protection. Step 5: Imipramine (reduces pain perception, limited evidence). Emerging: PDE5 inhibitors (sildenafil for microvascular dysfunction), nicorandil (EU), fasudil (Rho-kinase inhibitor, Japan). Coronary sinus reducer (device) for refractory.
us_prevalence: ~4–6M US patients with INOCA (ischemia with no obstructive CAD), of which microvascular angina is the predominant mechanism in ~50–60%: ~2–3M.
patients_needing_tx: ~2.5M requiring pharmacologic management.
price: $20,000–$40,000/year for a targeted microvascular/endothelial function agent.
registrational_endpoint: Coronary flow reserve (CFR by PET or CMR) at 6–12 months; angina frequency (SAQ angina frequency domain); ETT duration.
btd_threshold_vs_soc: CFR improvement ≥0.5 units (absolute) vs. placebo; ≥40% reduction in weekly angina frequency vs. beta-blocker.

symptoms: New or worsening chest pain at rest or minimal activity, without troponin rise
treatment_goal: Prevent MI, stabilize plaque, relieve ischemia
first_line_soc: Aspirin + P2Y12 inhibitor (clopidogrel or ticagrelor) + anticoagulation (heparin/enoxaparin) + high-intensity statin + nitrates for symptom relief.
treatment_algorithm: Same acute pathway as NSTE-ACS. Anti-ischemic: IV nitrates, beta-blockers. Anticoagulation: UFH or enoxaparin. DAPT: aspirin + ticagrelor (preferred by PLATO data). Coronary angiography ± PCI guided by TIMI/GRACE risk score. Post-discharge: same secondary prevention as stable CAD/ACS. Risk stratification key to distinguish from NSTEMI. Declining incidence with improved troponin sensitivity — most UA reclassified as NSTEMI-1.
us_prevalence: ~200,000–400,000 annual UA hospitalizations (declining as troponin sensitivity improves); ~5–6M prevalent unstable/high-risk CAD patients.
patients_needing_tx: ~400,000 acute/year + ~5M chronic secondary prevention.
price: $5,000–$15,000/year for long-term add-on antiplatelet or anti-inflammatory agent.
registrational_endpoint: MACE at 30 days and 12 months (CV death, MI, urgent revascularization).
btd_threshold_vs_soc: ≥15% MACE reduction vs. DAPT + statin at 12 months (HR ≤0.85).

symptoms: Chest pain at rest (often nocturnal), ST elevation with normal coronaries
treatment_goal: Prevent coronary artery spasm, relieve angina, prevent MI and sudden death
first_line_soc: Calcium channel blockers (diltiazem or amlodipine) are first-line for vasospastic/Prinzmetal angina. Long-acting nitrates as add-on.
treatment_algorithm: CCBs (diltiazem, amlodipine, nifedipine) at high doses — most effective. Long-acting nitrates (isosorbide mononitrate). Avoid beta-blockers (can worsen spasm). Avoid vasoconstrictors (ergot alkaloids, triptans, cocaine). Fasudil (Rho-kinase inhibitor, Japan) for refractory. Statins (pleiotropic anti-spastic effect). ACE inhibitors. In refractory: stellate ganglion block, LVEF monitoring.
us_prevalence: ~300,000–500,000 US patients with vasospastic angina (subset of INOCA).
patients_needing_tx: ~400,000 requiring pharmacologic management.
price: $25,000–$50,000/year for a novel Rho-kinase or endothelin antagonist.
registrational_endpoint: Frequency of vasospastic angina episodes/month; holter ST changes; acetylcholine provocation test normalization.
btd_threshold_vs_soc: ≥60% reduction in monthly angina episodes vs. CCB; provocation test normalization in ≥50% vs. ~20% with CCB.

symptoms: Usually asymptomatic; back/chest/abdominal pain; risk of rupture
treatment_goal: Prevent rupture through size monitoring and surgery; reduce growth rate
first_line_soc: Statins + antihypertensive therapy (ACEi/ARB or beta-blockers) + smoking cessation for medical management. Surgical/endovascular repair based on size criteria.
treatment_algorithm: See AAA and TAA entries for specific management. General aortic: intensive statin therapy (atorvastatin 40–80mg), blood pressure control (target <130/80). Beta-blockers for Marfan syndrome aortic dilation. Losartan for Marfan/LDS. No approved growth-reducing pharmacologic agent for sporadic aneurysms. Connective tissue disorder (Marfan, Loeys-Dietz): beta-blocker + ARB (TGF-β pathway modification).
us_prevalence: ~3–4M US patients with all aortic aneurysms (AAA + TAA combined).
patients_needing_tx: ~2–3M requiring pharmacologic risk factor management.
price: $15,000–$35,000/year for novel aortic wall stabilizer.
registrational_endpoint: Aortic diameter growth rate (mm/year) at 2–3 years; time to elective repair.
btd_threshold_vs_soc: ≥35% reduction in growth rate vs. statin + antihypertensive control.

symptoms: Usually asymptomatic; pulsatile abdominal mass, back/abdominal pain (symptomatic)
treatment_goal: Prevent rupture; reduce AAA growth rate; lower operative mortality
first_line_soc: Surveillance ultrasound for small AAA (<5.5 cm). Risk factor modification: smoking cessation, statin therapy, antihypertensive agents (beta-blockers, ACEi). Surgical/endovascular repair for AAA ≥5.5 cm or rapid growth.
treatment_algorithm: Medical: statins (reduce growth rate modestly), beta-blockers (controversial), ACEi/ARB (RAAS blockade may slow AAA growth). Smoking cessation paramount. No approved pharmacologic agent to reduce AAA growth. Repair: EVAR (endovascular) or open surgical for large/symptomatic AAA. Investigational: doxycycline (MMP inhibitor), losartan, roxithromycin, metformin (aortic protective effect) — all failed in large RCTs. Pipeline: SCD-10 peptide, biologic MMP inhibitors.
us_prevalence: ~1.5–2M US patients with AAA (prevalence ~1.7% in screened men 65–75 years; AHA 2025 stats).
patients_needing_tx: ~1.5M with diagnosed AAA needing monitoring/pharmacologic risk reduction; ~75,000/year undergo repair.
price: $15,000–$40,000/year for a novel AAA growth-reducing agent.
registrational_endpoint: AAA diameter growth rate (mm/year) at 2–3 years; time to intervention/rupture.
btd_threshold_vs_soc: ≥40% reduction in AAA growth rate (e.g., <2 mm/year vs. ~3–4 mm/year control); significant delay in time to repair vs. placebo.

symptoms: Usually asymptomatic; hoarseness, dysphagia (compression symptoms); rupture risk
treatment_goal: Prevent rupture; control growth with BP/HR management; timely surgery
first_line_soc: Beta-blockers to reduce aortic wall stress (HR target <60). ACEi/ARB for additional RAAS blockade. Statins for co-existing atherosclerosis. Surgical repair for ≥5.5 cm (bicuspid AV: ≥5.0 cm).
treatment_algorithm: Medical: beta-blocker (atenolol or metoprolol) for all TAA. Losartan for Marfan/LDS (inhibits TGF-β via RAAS blockade). Statins for atherosclerotic TAA. Intensive BP control (<130/80 mmHg). Open surgical or TEVAR repair for large/symptomatic aneurysms or rapid growth >5 mm/year. Genetic testing (FBN1, TGFBR1/2, ACTA2, MYH11, SMAD3) for inherited aortopathy guides thresholds and medication selection.
us_prevalence: ~500,000–1,000,000 US patients with TAA; incidence ~10,000 new cases/year.
patients_needing_tx: ~500,000–700,000 on pharmacologic management.
price: $15,000–$30,000/year for a novel aortic wall-stabilizing agent.
registrational_endpoint: Aortic diameter growth rate (mm/year); time to surgery; composite of aortic events (dissection, rupture, repair).
btd_threshold_vs_soc: ≥35% reduction in aortic growth rate vs. beta-blocker + ARB; time-to-surgery delay ≥2 years vs. medical therapy alone.

symptoms: Usually asymptomatic; localized pulsatile mass; rupture risk varies by location
treatment_goal: Prevent rupture; preserve branch vessel perfusion
first_line_soc: Statin + antihypertensive therapy for risk reduction. Surveillance imaging. Endovascular or surgical repair for large or symptomatic aneurysms.
treatment_algorithm: Medical management same as aortic aneurysms (statins, BP control, antiplatelet). Endovascular repair (covered stent, coil embolization) for visceral/renal/iliac branch aneurysms. Open repair for complex anatomy. No specific pharmacologic disease-modifying treatment.
us_prevalence: ~200,000–500,000 US patients (visceral, renal, iliac branch aneurysms combined).
patients_needing_tx: ~200,000–300,000 requiring pharmacologic management.
registrational_endpoint: Aneurysm growth rate; time to intervention.
btd_threshold_vs_soc: ≥35% reduction in annual aneurysm growth vs. observation + statin.

symptoms: Sudden severe 'tearing' chest/back pain radiating to abdomen, BP differential between arms
treatment_goal: Control BP/heart rate acutely; prevent propagation and aortic rupture
first_line_soc: IV beta-blocker (esmolol, labetalol) to target HR <60 and SBP 100–120 mmHg is first-line acute pharmacologic treatment. Type A: emergency surgery. Type B: medical management ± TEVAR.
treatment_algorithm: Acute: IV beta-blocker (esmolol) + vasodilator (sodium nitroprusside) if needed. Type A: urgent surgical repair. Type B uncomplicated: intensive medical therapy (IV to oral beta-blocker transition; ACEi for long-term). Type B complicated (malperfusion, expansion): TEVAR. Chronic: beta-blocker + ACEi/ARB for all. Marfan/genetic: losartan + beta-blocker. No approved pharmacologic agent specifically for dissection healing/aortic remodeling. Post-TEVAR: imaging surveillance, BP targets <130/80.
us_prevalence: ~10,000–15,000 acute aortic dissections/year in US. ~30,000–50,000 surviving chronic aortic dissection patients.
patients_needing_tx: ~50,000 chronic dissection patients on long-term pharmacologic therapy.
price: $30,000–$80,000/year for a novel aortic wall healing or remodeling agent.
registrational_endpoint: Aortic false lumen thrombosis/remodeling rate at 12 months (CT aortic volume); re-intervention rate; survival.
btd_threshold_vs_soc: Complete false lumen thrombosis in ≥40% vs. ~15% with medical therapy alone; ≥30% reduction in aortic diameter at 12 months.

symptoms: Fever, weight loss, elevated ESR/CRP, aortic wall thickening/enhancement on imaging
treatment_goal: Suppress vascular inflammation, prevent aortic complications
first_line_soc: High-dose corticosteroids (prednisone 40–60 mg/day) are first-line for inflammatory aortitis (Takayasu, giant cell). Antibiotics (for infectious syphilitic/bacterial aortitis).
treatment_algorithm: Takayasu arteritis/GCA aortitis: corticosteroids + tocilizumab (Actemra, IL-6 receptor inhibitor — FDA approved for GCA). Steroid-sparing DMARDs: methotrexate, azathioprine, mycophenolate. Anti-TNF (infliximab) for refractory. Avacopan (C5a inhibitor) investigational for vasculitis. Infectious aortitis: targeted antibiotic therapy. PET/CT or MRI for monitoring. Surgical/endovascular repair for complications.
us_prevalence: ~25,000–50,000 US patients with inflammatory aortitis; GCA (~200,000 US patients) with aortic involvement in ~20%; Takayasu (~20,000 US patients).
patients_needing_tx: ~30,000–50,000 requiring pharmacologic anti-inflammatory treatment.
price: $25,000–$80,000/year for biologic (tocilizumab ~$50K/year; avacopan ~$46K/year).
registrational_endpoint: Sustained remission rate at 52 weeks (GCA definition: absence of signs/symptoms + CRP normalization); relapse-free survival.
btd_threshold_vs_soc: Sustained remission at 52 weeks ≥50% vs. ~25–30% with prednisone alone (tocilizumab benchmark: 56% vs. 26%).

symptoms: Leg weakness, buttock/hip pain, erectile dysfunction (Leriche syndrome), absent femoral pulses
treatment_goal: Restore perfusion, prevent limb loss and ischemic complications
first_line_soc: Antiplatelet therapy (aspirin ± clopidogrel), statins, and antihypertensives for risk factor modification. Revascularization (aortobifemoral bypass or endovascular stenting) for symptomatic complete occlusion.
treatment_algorithm: Medical: antiplatelet (aspirin + clopidogrel), statin, ACEi/ARB, smoking cessation. Supervised exercise therapy. Revascularization for Leriche syndrome (aortobifemoral bypass or EVAR). Thrombolysis for acute thrombosis. Rivaroxaban 2.5mg twice daily + aspirin (COMPASS regimen) for high-risk PAD patients. No specific pharmacologic agent for aortic branch occlusion.
us_prevalence: ~50,000–150,000 US patients (subset of aortoiliac occlusive disease within PAD population of ~8M).
patients_needing_tx: ~100,000 requiring pharmacologic management.
price: $10,000–$25,000/year for novel antiplatelet or endothelial protective agent.
registrational_endpoint: Ankle-brachial index (ABI) improvement; limb salvage rate at 12 months; MACE.
btd_threshold_vs_soc: ABI improvement ≥0.15 vs. baseline; ≥30% MACE reduction vs. aspirin alone.

symptoms: Fever, new heart murmur, emboli, fatigue, positive blood cultures
treatment_goal: Eradicate infection, prevent emboli and valve destruction, repair valves
first_line_soc: High-dose bactericidal IV antibiotics guided by organism and susceptibility (penicillin/nafcillin/vancomycin for streptococcal/staphylococcal IE); typically 4–6 weeks of IV therapy.
treatment_algorithm: Blood culture-guided IV antibiotics: Strep viridans/bovis: penicillin G ± gentamicin 4 weeks or ceftriaxone 4 weeks. MRSA/Staph aureus: vancomycin 6 weeks; daptomycin alternative. HACEK: ceftriaxone 4 weeks. Enterococcus: ampicillin + ceftriaxone 6 weeks. Valve surgery for: severe valvular regurgitation, heart failure, abscess, persistent infection, large mobile vegetations (>10mm). No approved pharmacologic add-on (biofilm dispersals in trials). Bacteriophage therapy investigational.
us_prevalence: ~15,000–25,000 US cases/year (rising with IVDU epidemic). ~50,000–80,000 prevalent patients with sequelae.
patients_needing_tx: ~20,000–25,000 requiring acute pharmacologic treatment annually.
price: $50,000–$200,000/year for novel anti-biofilm, bacteriophage, or adjunctive agent.
registrational_endpoint: Clinical cure rate (absence of fever, bacteremia, vegetation resolution) at 6 weeks; 90-day all-cause mortality.
btd_threshold_vs_soc: Clinical cure ≥85% and 90-day mortality ≤10% vs. ~70% cure rate and ~15–20% mortality with current antibiotics.

symptoms: Embolic stroke/TIA, fever absent, sterile vegetations, associated with malignancy or autoimmune disease
treatment_goal: Prevent emboli, treat underlying cause (malignancy/SLE/APS)
first_line_soc: Anticoagulation (UFH or LMWH — warfarin less effective for NBTE) to prevent emboli. Treat underlying cause (cancer, SLE, APS).
treatment_algorithm: Anticoagulation: IV/LMWH or warfarin for NBTE. Warfarin for Libman-Sacks endocarditis (SLE-APS): hydroxychloroquine + anticoagulation. Antibiotics NOT indicated (no infection). Corticosteroids for SLE-associated. Valve surgery rarely needed. Cancer-associated NBTE: treat malignancy (cancer SoC per tumor type). No specific approved pharmacologic agent for NBTE itself.
us_prevalence: ~5,000–15,000 US cases/year clinically recognized (highly underdiagnosed).
patients_needing_tx: ~10,000 requiring active anticoagulation and treatment.
price: $15,000–$30,000/year for a novel anti-thrombotic/vegetatinn-dissolution agent.
registrational_endpoint: Vegetation resolution rate on TTE/TEE at 3 months; embolic event rate at 6 months.
btd_threshold_vs_soc: ≥50% vegetation resolution at 3 months and ≥50% reduction in embolic events vs. anticoagulation alone.

symptoms: Persistent dyspnea, fatigue, ankle swelling, reduced exercise tolerance
treatment_goal: Reduce mortality and hospitalizations, improve symptoms and QoL
first_line_soc: Guideline-directed quadruple medical therapy: ARNI (sacubitril/valsartan/Entresto) + beta-blocker (carvedilol/metoprolol succinate) + MRA (spironolactone/eplerenone) + SGLT2 inhibitor (dapagliflozin or empagliflozin).
treatment_algorithm: HFrEF (EF <40%): GDMT quadruple therapy. Ivabradine (Corlanor) if HR ≥70 on max beta-blocker. Vericiguat (Verquvo) for high-risk worsening HF. Diuretics for congestion. ICD for EF <35%. CRT. LVAD as bridge/destination. Cardiac transplant. HFpEF (EF >50%): SGLT2i (empagliflozin EMPEROR-Preserved, dapagliflozin HFpEF trial) — first disease-modifying HFpEF approvals. Finerenone (Kerendia) for HFpEF + CKD + DM. HFmrEF: SGLT2i effective. Emerging: zilebesiran, sotatercept (for pulmonary hypertension component), mavacamten in obstructive HCM-HF.
us_prevalence: ~7–8M US patients with HF (HFSA HF Stats 2025). HFrEF: ~3.5M; HFpEF: ~3–4M.
patients_needing_tx: ~6–7M requiring pharmacologic management. GDMT-eligible: ~3.5M HFrEF; SGLT2i for HFpEF: ~3M.
price: $5,000–$10,000/year for SGLT2i (dapagliflozin ~$5,500; empagliflozin ~$5,200); novel HFpEF agent: $15,000–$30,000/year.
peak_sales: Novel HFpEF agent: 3M × $20K × 20% share = $12B; SGLT2i in HF: 4M × $6K × 40% = $9.6B
registrational_endpoint: HFrEF: composite of CV death and HF hospitalization (primary; PARADIGM-HF template). HFpEF: composite of CV death and HF hospitalization/urgent visit.
btd_threshold_vs_soc: HFrEF: ≥20% reduction in CV death + HF hospitalization vs. ACEi (PARADIGM-HF: 20% reduction for Entresto); HFpEF: ≥20% reduction in composite events vs. placebo.

symptoms: Sudden severe dyspnea, orthopnea, frothy sputum, diaphoresis, low saturation
treatment_goal: Rapidly relieve congestion, restore hemodynamic stability, prevent end-organ damage
first_line_soc: IV loop diuretics (furosemide/bumetanide) are first-line for decongestive acute HF. IV vasodilators (nitroglycerin, nesiritide) for hypertensive acute HF. Inotropes (dobutamine, milrinone) for cardiogenic shock.
treatment_algorithm: IV furosemide (2–2.5× oral dose). IV nitroglycerin for hypertensive AHF. Oxygen/NIV (BiPAP) for respiratory distress. Inotropes for cardiogenic shock: dobutamine, milrinone, or levosimendan (EU). Vasopressors (norepinephrine, vasopressin) for profound hypotension. Serelaxin (failed Phase III). Novel: vericiguat (Verquvo) approved 2021 for worsening HF. Omecamtiv mecarbil (positive GALACTIC-HF). Mechanical support: IABP, Impella, ECMO for cardiogenic shock. 2024: acetazolamide (ADVOR trial) + loop diuretic for decongestion.
us_prevalence: ~1M acute HF hospitalizations/year in US (AHA 2025 stats).
patients_needing_tx: ~1M patients/year requiring acute pharmacologic management.
price: $15,000–$50,000/per hospitalization course for a novel acute agent; $5,000–$10,000/year for outpatient preventive escalation.
registrational_endpoint: Dyspnea relief VAS score at 6–24 hours (primary); 30-day all-cause mortality + HF rehospitalization (composite).
btd_threshold_vs_soc: Clinically meaningful dyspnea improvement in ≥50% vs. ~35% diuretic alone at 12 hours; ≥20% reduction in 30-day death/rehospitalization vs. diuretic SoC.

disease: Right Heart Failure and Cor Pulmonale
symptoms: Leg edema, ascites, raised JVP, dyspnea, reduced exercise tolerance
treatment_goal: Reduce pulmonary vascular resistance, relieve RV pressure overload, treat underlying cause
first_line_soc: Treat underlying cause (pulmonary hypertension, COPD, PE). Diuretics for fluid overload. PAH-specific drugs for Group 1 PH: PDE5 inhibitors (sildenafil, tadalafil) or endothelin receptor antagonists (ambrisentan, macitentan) as first-line combination.
treatment_algorithm: Group 1 PAH (most pharmacologically active): combination therapy — ambrisentan (ERA) + tadalafil (PDE5i) as first-line upfront combination (AMBITION trial). Prostacyclin pathway: IV epoprostenol (gold standard, severe PAH), inhaled iloprost/treprostinil, oral treprostinil (Orenitram), selexipag (Uptravi, oral prostacyclin receptor agonist). Sotatercept (Winrevair, activin receptor fusion — FDA approved 2024) — new class for PAH, highly effective. For COPD-related cor pulmonale: optimize COPD therapy (LABA/ICS/LAMA). Diuretics (loop) for all RHF. Digoxin (limited role). Cardiac transplantation/lung transplant for end-stage.
us_prevalence: Group 1 PAH: ~40,000–100,000 US patients. Broader RHF/cor pulmonale: ~1–2M (COPD, PE, etc.).
patients_needing_tx: PAH-specific: ~50,000–80,000 patients. Broader RHF: ~500,000.
price: $100,000–$200,000/year for PAH-specific agents (sotatercept Winrevair WAC ~$140K/year; selexipag ~$150K/year; macitentan ~$100K).
registrational_endpoint: 6-minute walk distance (6MWD) improvement from baseline; composite of clinical worsening events (death, hospitalization, disease progression); PVR reduction.
btd_threshold_vs_soc: ≥30m 6MWD improvement vs. ERA + PDE5i combination; PVR reduction ≥30% (sotatercept: -68% 6MWD improvement vs. +15m placebo in STELLAR).

symptoms: Acute severe dyspnea, frothy pink sputum, crackles, desaturation, orthopnea
treatment_goal: Rapidly reduce pulmonary venous pressure, restore oxygenation
first_line_soc: IV furosemide (immediate diuresis) + oxygen/NIV (CPAP/BiPAP) + IV nitroglycerin (vasodilation, reduces preload) are the acute first-line triad.
treatment_algorithm: Same as acute HF management above. IV morphine historically used but evidence weak (may worsen outcomes). IV nitroprusside for hypertensive pulmonary edema. Intubation and mechanical ventilation for severe respiratory failure. Inotropes for cardiogenic shock etiology. Treat precipitating cause (ACS, arrhythmia, hypertensive crisis, valve dysfunction). Novel: see Acute HF above.
us_prevalence: ~200,000–400,000 acute cardiogenic pulmonary edema events/year (subset of ~1M AHF hospitalizations).
patients_needing_tx: ~300,000/year requiring acute pharmacologic treatment.
price: $20,000–$40,000 per acute episode for a novel agent.
registrational_endpoint: Time to dyspnea relief (VAS); oxygenation (SpO2) normalization at 30–60 min; 30-day mortality.
btd_threshold_vs_soc: SpO2 normalization ≥95% in ≥70% of patients at 30 min; ≥25% reduction in 30-day mortality vs. IV furosemide + nitrates.

symptoms: Usually asymptomatic; headache, blurred vision in severe cases; end-organ damage
treatment_goal: Lower BP to <130/80 mmHg, prevent MACE and end-organ damage
first_line_soc: Thiazide diuretics, dihydropyridine CCBs (amlodipine), or ACEi/ARBs are each acceptable first-line agents; combination recommended for most patients with BP ≥20/10 mmHg above goal.
treatment_algorithm: First-line: ACEi (lisinopril, ramipril) or ARB (losartan, valsartan) + CCB (amlodipine) + thiazide/thiazide-like diuretic (HCTZ, chlorthalidone, indapamide). Resistant HTN (≥3 drugs): add MRA (spironolactone) or amiloride. Renal denervation (Recor) FDA-approved 2023 for device-based therapy. Novel 2024–2025: zilebesiran (siRNA angiotensinogen, Phase 2/3) — bi-annual injection maintains BP; aprocitentan (dual endothelin antagonist, FDA approved 2023 for resistant HTN); baxdrostat (aldosterone synthase inhibitor, Phase 3).
us_prevalence: ~122 million US adults (nearly 50% of all US adults) have hypertension per AHA 2025 guideline update (new threshold ≥130/80 mmHg).
patients_needing_tx: ~100 million requiring pharmacologic treatment (adjusted for treatment-eligible threshold).
price: $1,000–$3,000/year for generic SoC; novel branded agents: $5,000–$15,000/year; RNA-based (zilebesiran 2×/year): $10,000–$20,000/year.
registrational_endpoint: Change in mean 24-hour ambulatory SBP from baseline at 3–6 months.
btd_threshold_vs_soc: ≥10 mmHg additional SBP reduction vs. maximally optimized SoC; or ≥15 mmHg reduction in resistant HTN population.

symptoms: Refractory hypertension, flash pulmonary edema, acute renal failure on ACEi, bruits
treatment_goal: Control BP, preserve renal function, revascularize if functionally significant stenosis
first_line_soc: ACEi/ARBs (caution: monitor renal function) + other antihypertensives. Percutaneous renal angioplasty/stenting for FMD; medical management preferred for atherosclerotic RAS per CORAL trial.
treatment_algorithm: Medical: ACEi/ARB (use cautiously with bilateral or unilateral RAS); add CCB, thiazide diuretic. Revascularization: balloon angioplasty (FMD — excellent outcomes); stenting (atherosclerotic RAS — CORAL trial showed no benefit over medical therapy for most patients). Renal artery surgery rarely needed. Antiplatelets, statins for atherosclerotic RAS. Monitor renal function closely.
us_prevalence: ~5–10% of all hypertension may be renovascular (~5–10M patients); clinically significant diagnosed: ~500,000–1,000,000.
patients_needing_tx: ~500,000–750,000 requiring pharmacologic management.
price: $10,000–$25,000/year for novel mineralocorticoid receptor antagonist or targeted renal protective agent.
registrational_endpoint: SBP reduction from baseline at 6 months; GFR preservation (eGFR change); time to dialysis.
btd_threshold_vs_soc: ≥10 mmHg additional SBP reduction vs. standard antihypertensives; eGFR stabilization vs. progressive decline in control arm.

symptoms: Severe BP elevation (>180/120) with acute end-organ damage: encephalopathy, MI, aortic dissection, acute HF
treatment_goal: Rapidly lower BP by 20–25% in first hour, prevent further end-organ damage
first_line_soc: IV labetalol or nicardipine are first-line IV antihypertensives for most hypertensive emergencies. IV nitroprusside for aortic dissection. IV nitroglycerin for ACS-associated HTN.
treatment_algorithm: Hypertensive encephalopathy/urgency: IV labetalol, nicardipine, or clevidipine (titratable CCB). Hypertensive emergency with aortic dissection: IV esmolol + nitroprusside. Preeclampsia/eclampsia: IV labetalol or hydralazine + magnesium for seizures. Pheochromocytoma crisis: alpha-blockade (phentolamine) before beta-blocker. Acute heart failure: IV nitroglycerin + diuretics. Clonidine for hypertensive urgency (outpatient). Novel: clevidipine (Cleviprex) as titratable IV CCB. Transition to oral therapy once BP controlled.
us_prevalence: ~500,000–1,000,000 hypertensive emergency episodes/year requiring IV therapy.
patients_needing_tx: ~700,000/year requiring acute IV pharmacologic management.
price: $5,000–$20,000 per acute hospitalization course.
registrational_endpoint: Time to target BP range; proportion achieving BP <160/100 at 30 minutes without hypotension.
btd_threshold_vs_soc: ≥80% achieving target BP range at 30 min without overshoot (hypotension <10% cases) vs. ~60–70% with IV labetalol.

symptoms: Persistent limb swelling, heaviness, skin changes, recurrent cellulitis
treatment_goal: Reduce limb volume, prevent fibrosis, improve function and QoL
first_line_soc: Complete decongestive therapy (CDT): manual lymphatic drainage + multilayer compression bandaging + exercise + skin care. No FDA-approved pharmacologic disease-modifying treatment as of 2025.
treatment_algorithm: CDT (non-pharmacologic gold standard). Diuretics: NOT recommended (increase protein deposition). Benzopyrones (coumarin): limited evidence, withdrawn in US. Ketoprofen DMSO (topical, investigational). Surgical: lymphovenous anastomosis, lymph node transfer, debulking surgery for severe cases. Emerging pharmacologic: inhibitors of TGF-β (vascular fibrosis), VEGF-C (lymphangiogenesis stimulators — Phase 2 trials). Ketoprofen for inflammatory component (off-label). Pipeline: ubenimex (LPA1 antagonist — failed); AM0010 (IL-10); integrin inhibitors.
us_prevalence: ~3–5M US patients with lymphedema; cancer-related: ~1.5–2M (breast cancer 20–30% post-mastectomy); primary lymphedema ~100,000.
patients_needing_tx: ~3M requiring active management; pharmacologic opportunity: ~1M needing disease-modifying therapy.
price: $15,000–$40,000/year for a novel anti-fibrotic/lymphangiogenic agent.
registrational_endpoint: Limb volume reduction (% change from baseline via water displacement or circumference) at 6–12 months; cellulitis episode rate; QoL (LYMQOL).
btd_threshold_vs_soc: ≥15% limb volume reduction vs. CDT alone; ≥50% reduction in cellulitis episodes at 12 months.

symptoms: Enlarged lymph nodes (localized or generalized), tenderness, fever, night sweats (depending on cause)
treatment_goal: Identify and treat underlying cause; reduce node enlargement if malignant or infectious
first_line_soc: Treat underlying cause: antibiotics for infectious (bacterial lymphadenitis); antiviral for viral; chemotherapy/immunotherapy for malignant causes. No specific pharmacologic SoC for reactive lymphadenopathy.
treatment_algorithm: Reactive/infectious: treat underlying infection (antibiotics, antivirals). Malignant: biopsy-guided chemotherapy, immunotherapy, radiation. Autoimmune: corticosteroids, immunosuppressants. Sarcoid: corticosteroids. Rosai-Dorfman, Kimura: corticosteroids. Persistent unexplained: serial monitoring. No stand-alone pharmacologic treatment for lymphadenopathy per se.
us_prevalence: Lymphadenopathy is a symptom/sign, not a standalone disease. ~10–15 million US visits/year for lymphadenopathy evaluation; significant subset (~500,000) have malignant etiology.
patients_needing_tx: As a symptom: all require evaluation; pharmacologic treatment determined by etiology.
price: N/A as standalone indication; incorporated into treatment of underlying disease.
tam_gross: Not applicable as standalone drug target.
peak_sales: Not applicable; treated under underlying disease indication.
registrational_endpoint: N/A as standalone; endpoint depends on underlying disease (e.g., complete response for lymphoma).
btd_threshold_vs_soc: N/A as standalone indication.

symptoms: Chest pain, dyspnea, palpitations, fever, acute heart failure, arrhythmias
treatment_goal: Reduce cardiac inflammation, preserve LV function, prevent arrhythmic death
first_line_soc: Supportive care with HF therapy (ACEi, beta-blockers) for LV dysfunction. NSAIDs/colchicine for pericarditic chest pain component. Antiviral therapy if specific virus identified (rarely applicable).
treatment_algorithm: Acute fulminant: hemodynamic support (inotropes, IABP, Impella, ECMO). Non-specific: avoid NSAIDs (may worsen in viral myocarditis). Immunosuppression: IVIG (high-dose, limited evidence) for fulminant; steroids + azathioprine for autoimmune/giant cell myocarditis (GCM) + cyclosporine for GCM. Cardiac MRI for diagnosis. ICD/pacemaker for arrhythmias. Heart transplantation for end-stage. Immune checkpoint inhibitor (ICI)-induced myocarditis: high-dose steroids + abatacept or ruxolitinib (emerging 2024–2025). No FDA-approved specific treatment as of 2025.
us_prevalence: ~50,000–100,000 US cases/year; predominantly viral/idiopathic. ICI-induced myocarditis increasing (~1% of ICI patients).
patients_needing_tx: ~75,000/year requiring pharmacologic management.
price: $50,000–$150,000/year for anti-inflammatory targeted biologic.
registrational_endpoint: LVEF recovery at 3 months; myocardial edema reduction on cardiac MRI (T2 ratio, LGE); composite of death, transplant, hemodynamic compromise at 6 months.
btd_threshold_vs_soc: ≥10% LVEF improvement vs. supportive care; myocardial inflammation resolution (MRI T2 normalization) in ≥50% vs. ~30%; 6-month composite mortality reduction ≥30%.

symptoms: Sharp pleuritic chest pain relieved by sitting forward, pericardial rub, ST elevation
treatment_goal: Resolve inflammation, prevent recurrence and constrictive pericarditis
first_line_soc: Aspirin + colchicine (anti-IL-1β mechanism) is first-line combination for acute and recurrent pericarditis.
treatment_algorithm: First episode: aspirin (750–1000mg TID ×2 weeks) + colchicine (0.5mg BID ×3 months) — ICAP trial evidence. Avoid NSAIDs (indomethacin alternative). Corticosteroids only for specific indications (connective tissue disease, pregnancy, NSAID/colchicine contraindication — associated with recurrence). Recurrent pericarditis: colchicine 6–12 months. For IL-1β driven idiopathic recurrent pericarditis: rilonacept (Arcalyst, IL-1α/β trap) FDA approved 2021 — first and only approved treatment for recurrent pericarditis. Anakinra (IL-1RA) for rilonacept refractory cases. Pericardiectomy for constrictive pericarditis.
us_prevalence: ~70,000–100,000 cases/year; recurrent pericarditis (~30% of initial cases): ~20,000–30,000/year.
patients_needing_tx: ~80,000 acute + ~20,000–30,000 recurrent requiring pharmacologic management.
price: $110,000–$120,000/year for rilonacept (Arcalyst WAC ~$110K/year for pericarditis indication).
registrational_endpoint: Time to recurrence after achieving pericarditis remission; proportion recurrence-free at 18 months (RHAPSODY trial template).
btd_threshold_vs_soc: Recurrence-free at 18 months ≥80% vs. ~40% with colchicine alone (rilonacept: 74% vs. 21% at 16 weeks).

symptoms: Calf or thigh pain with walking (claudication), leg wounds, rest pain, limb loss
treatment_goal: Improve walking distance, prevent limb loss and cardiovascular events
first_line_soc: Supervised exercise therapy + antiplatelet therapy (aspirin ± clopidogrel; or rivaroxaban 2.5mg BID + aspirin per COMPASS for high-risk PAD) + statin + ACEi/ARB.
treatment_algorithm: Step 1: Supervised exercise therapy. Step 2: Antiplatelet (aspirin; clopidogrel preferred for symptomatic PAD per CAPRIE; add rivaroxaban 2.5mg for high-risk per COMPASS). Statin therapy. ACEi (ramipril — HOPE trial). Step 3: Cilostazol (PDE3 inhibitor) for claudication — significantly improves walking distance. Vorapaxar (PAR-1 antagonist) add-on for high-risk. Step 4: Revascularization (endovascular or surgical bypass) for severe symptoms or CLI. Pipeline: PCSK9 inhibitors for PAD LDL management; gene therapy (VEGF/HIF-1α) for angiogenesis in no-option CLI.
us_prevalence: ~8–12M US patients with PAD (AHA 2025 stats; PMC 2025 forecasting study); ~2M with critical limb ischemia.
patients_needing_tx: ~8M requiring pharmacologic management; ~3M candidates for novel advanced therapies.
price: $10,000–$25,000/year for novel antiplatelet/antithrombotic or vascular regeneration agent.
registrational_endpoint: Change in peak walking distance on standardized treadmill test (Gardener protocol) at 12 weeks; ABI; 6-minute walk distance; MACE at 36 months.
btd_threshold_vs_soc: ≥60% improvement in peak walking distance vs. exercise + aspirin; ≥20% MACE reduction vs. aspirin alone (rivaroxaban + aspirin: 15% MACE reduction vs. aspirin in COMPASS).

symptoms: Color changes in fingers/toes with cold exposure (white→blue→red), pain, numbness
treatment_goal: Reduce frequency and severity of vasospastic attacks, prevent digital ulcers
first_line_soc: Dihydropyridine calcium channel blockers (nifedipine ER, amlodipine) are first-line pharmacologic treatment.
treatment_algorithm: Step 1: Behavioral (avoid cold, gloves, smoking cessation). Step 2: CCBs (nifedipine 30–90mg/day — most effective, reduces attack frequency ~30%). Step 3: PDE5 inhibitors (sildenafil, tadalafil) — particularly effective for secondary Raynaud/SSc. Prostacyclin IV infusions (iloprost) for severe digital ischemia/ulcers. Bosentan (ERA) FDA-approved for prevention of new digital ulcers in SSc-Raynaud. Alpha-1 blockers (prazosin). Topical nitrates. Sympathectomy for refractory severe cases.
us_prevalence: ~15–20M US patients with Raynaud phenomenon (primary ~75%; secondary ~25% e.g., SSc). ~5M with secondary RP requiring more aggressive treatment.
patients_needing_tx: ~5–8M requiring pharmacologic management; ~2M candidates for advanced therapies.
price: $15,000–$30,000/year for advanced vasodilator or endothelin-targeted agent.
registrational_endpoint: Raynaud's Condition Score (RCS) or daily digital color change diary; attack frequency/duration at 12–16 weeks.
btd_threshold_vs_soc: ≥40% reduction in attack frequency vs. nifedipine; ≥1.5-point RCS improvement vs. CCB monotherapy.

symptoms: Burning pain, redness and warmth in extremities, triggered by heat or exercise
treatment_goal: Reduce frequency and severity of flares, improve pain control
first_line_soc: Aspirin is first-line for secondary erythromelalgia associated with myeloproliferative disorders. For primary/genetic (SCN9A mutation): cooling measures and sodium channel blockers (mexiletine).
treatment_algorithm: Primary (hereditary, SCN9A Nav1.7 gain-of-function): sodium channel blockers (mexiletine, lidocaine, carbamazepine). Cooling (wet socks, fans). Behavioral: avoid heat, exercise. Secondary (MPN): aspirin (rapidly effective), treat underlying MPN. CCBs (nifedipine). Serotonin reuptake inhibitors. Misoprostol (prostaglandin). Intravenous lidocaine for severe refractory. Nav1.7-specific inhibitors in clinical development (2024–2025 trials).
us_prevalence: ~100,000–200,000 US patients (prevalence ~1.3–3/100,000; possibly higher with MPN-associated).
patients_needing_tx: ~150,000 requiring pharmacologic management.
price: $40,000–$100,000/year for a Nav1.7-selective inhibitor.
registrational_endpoint: Average pain NRS score at 12 weeks; erythromelalgia flare frequency/duration (daily diary).
btd_threshold_vs_soc: ≥2-point NRS pain reduction vs. placebo; ≥50% reduction in flare frequency vs. placebo or mexiletine.

symptoms: Hypertension (renal FMD), headache, pulsatile tinnitus, cervical bruit, spontaneous arterial dissection
treatment_goal: Control hypertension, prevent arterial dissection and stroke
first_line_soc: Antiplatelet therapy (aspirin 100mg/day) for FMD with cerebrovascular involvement. Antihypertensives (ACEi/ARB or CCB) for renal FMD. Percutaneous transluminal angioplasty (PTA) for symptomatic stenoses.
treatment_algorithm: Medical: antiplatelet (aspirin) + antihypertensives. PTA (angioplasty without stent) is preferred revascularization for renal FMD — excellent outcomes, largely curative. Carotid/vertebral FMD: PTA or medical management. Stenting avoided (poor long-term results). ACEi/ARB for BP control in renal FMD. No pharmacologic agent modifying FMD natural history. Monitoring for intracranial aneurysms (co-occurring in ~7%).
us_prevalence: ~200,000–600,000 US patients (predominantly women ~90%); likely underdiagnosed.
patients_needing_tx: ~200,000 requiring pharmacologic management.
price: $20,000–$40,000/year for a novel arterial remodeling/connective tissue agent.
registrational_endpoint: BP control (SBP <130 mmHg) at 12 months; arterial dissection-free survival; EUROFIBRO registry-based composite.
btd_threshold_vs_soc: ≥80% achieving BP <130/80 vs. ~60% with antihypertensives alone; zero arterial dissection recurrence vs. 3–5% annual rate.

symptoms: Foot/hand pain, digital ulcers, rest pain, claudication in young smokers
treatment_goal: Complete smoking cessation, preserve limbs, prevent amputations
first_line_soc: Complete tobacco cessation is the only proven treatment to halt disease progression. Pharmacologic: prostanoids (IV iloprost or oral beraprost) for digital ischemia.
treatment_algorithm: Mandatory: complete smoking cessation (including electronic cigarettes/smokeless tobacco). IV iloprost (prostacyclin analog) for critical ischemia — improves pain, promotes ulcer healing. Oral prostanoids (beraprost — limited availability in US). Spinal cord stimulation for pain. Anticoagulation (limited evidence). No disease-modifying pharmacologic agent. Sympathectomy for severe vasospasm. Limb amputation as last resort. Gene therapy (VEGF angiogenesis) in trials.
us_prevalence: ~100,000–300,000 US patients (0.5–1% of PAD cases; lower in US than Asia due to smoking trends).
patients_needing_tx: ~150,000 requiring pharmacologic management.
price: $20,000–$60,000/year for oral prostanoid or VEGF-targeted agent.
registrational_endpoint: Major amputation rate at 12 months; rest pain improvement (VAS); digital ulcer healing rate.
btd_threshold_vs_soc: ≥70% ulcer healing at 6 months vs. ~50% with IV iloprost; amputation rate <5% vs. ~10–15% historical.

symptoms: Sudden severe limb pain, pallor, pulselessness, paresthesias, paralysis (6 Ps)
treatment_goal: Urgent restoration of arterial flow to prevent amputation and death
first_line_soc: IV unfractionated heparin immediately upon diagnosis. Emergent revascularization: catheter-directed thrombolysis (CDT) or surgical embolectomy/thrombectomy.
treatment_algorithm: IV heparin anticoagulation immediately. Revascularization: CDT (tPA, urokinase) for Rutherford IIa/IIb; surgical embolectomy/bypass for Rutherford IIb/III. Mechanical thrombectomy (e.g., AngioJet) as adjunct. Post-revascularization: transition to systemic anticoagulation (DOAC or warfarin depending on etiology). Fasciotomy if compartment syndrome post-reperfusion. Identify and treat underlying cause (AF, hypercoagulable state). Vorapaxar, ticagrelor for recurrence prevention.
us_prevalence: ~30,000–50,000 acute limb ischemia events/year in US.
patients_needing_tx: ~40,000/year requiring acute pharmacologic + revascularization treatment.
price: $30,000–$60,000 per acute episode for a novel thrombolytic or cytoprotective.
registrational_endpoint: Amputation-free survival at 30 days; complete lysis rate on angiography; limb salvage rate.
btd_threshold_vs_soc: Limb salvage rate ≥90% at 30 days vs. ~75–80% with CDT; complete lysis ≥60% vs. ~40% with standard tPA.

symptoms: Pulsatile mass (popliteal), distal ischemia from thromboembolism, leg pain
treatment_goal: Prevent thrombotic/embolic complications, preserve limb perfusion
first_line_soc: Anticoagulation (antiplatelet or systemic anticoagulation) for thromboembolism risk reduction. Surgical or endovascular repair for large/symptomatic peripheral aneurysms.
treatment_algorithm: Medical: antiplatelet therapy (aspirin ± clopidogrel). Anticoagulation (warfarin or DOAC) for mural thrombus. Statin therapy. Repair: open surgical bypass (popliteal aneurysm — gold standard) or endovascular (covered stent) for visceral/renal/iliac. No specific pharmacologic disease-modifying agent.
us_prevalence: ~50,000–150,000 US patients with peripheral artery aneurysms (popliteal most common peripheral arterial aneurysm).
patients_needing_tx: ~80,000–100,000 requiring pharmacologic management.
registrational_endpoint: Limb salvage rate; freedom from thromboembolism at 12 months; aneurysm growth.
btd_threshold_vs_soc: Freedom from thromboembolism ≥95% at 12 months vs. ~75–80% antiplatelet alone.

symptoms: Persistent bluish discoloration of hands/feet, cold extremities, non-painful
treatment_goal: Identify secondary cause; reassure; manage vasospasm if symptomatic
first_line_soc: Primarily lifestyle measures (warmth, avoidance of cold). No specific pharmacologic first-line SoC for primary acrocyanosis. CCBs for symptomatic secondary causes.
treatment_algorithm: Primary acrocyanosis: reassurance, behavioral measures (warm clothing, cold avoidance). Secondary: treat underlying cause (connective tissue disease, eating disorder, medications). CCBs (nifedipine) for vasospastic component. Alpha-blockers (prazosin) for refractory. No specific approved treatment.
us_prevalence: ~200,000–500,000 US patients (largely benign primary form); clinically significant secondary acrocyanosis in smaller subset.
patients_needing_tx: ~100,000 requiring pharmacologic management.
price: $10,000–$20,000/year for a novel vasodilator.
registrational_endpoint: Digital skin temperature and color assessment at 3 months; Raynaud/vasospasm frequency (secondary acrocyanosis).
btd_threshold_vs_soc: ≥2°C improvement in digital skin temperature vs. placebo; ≥40% reduction in vasospastic episodes.

disease: Chronic Venous Insufficiency and Post-Thrombotic Syndrome
symptoms: Leg swelling, varicose veins, skin discoloration, venous ulcers, heaviness
treatment_goal: Reduce edema, heal venous ulcers, prevent recurrence and progression
first_line_soc: Compression therapy (graduated compression stockings or wraps) is first-line. Wound care for venous ulcers. Pharmacologic: rutosides or micronized purified flavonoid fraction (MPFF/Daflon) — widely used in Europe.
treatment_algorithm: Compression stockings (20–30 or 30–40 mmHg). MPFF (Daflon 500/1000mg) — improves venous tone, reduces symptoms; widely used but not FDA-approved. Pentoxifylline for venous ulcers (FDA-approved as adjunct). Anticoagulation (DOAC or LMWH) for post-thrombotic syndrome related DVT. Interventional: endovenous ablation (laser, radiofrequency) for saphenous reflux. Venous stenting for iliac occlusion (May-Thurner). Wound care for ulcers.
us_prevalence: ~25–30M US patients with CVI; ~2–3M with venous ulcers; post-thrombotic syndrome: ~1–2M.
patients_needing_tx: ~10M requiring pharmacologic management (compression + Rx agents).
price: $10,000–$25,000/year for a novel venous toning/anti-fibrotic agent.
registrational_endpoint: Venous Clinical Severity Score (VCSS) change; venous ulcer healing rate at 24 weeks; recurrence rate.
btd_threshold_vs_soc: ≥3-point VCSS improvement vs. compression alone; venous ulcer healing ≥50% at 24 weeks vs. ~30% with compression.

symptoms: Unilateral leg swelling, pain, redness, warmth; PE risk if propagates
treatment_goal: Prevent PE, resolve thrombus, prevent post-thrombotic syndrome, avoid recurrence
first_line_soc: Direct oral anticoagulants (DOACs) — rivaroxaban (Xarelto) or apixaban (Eliquis) are first-line anticoagulants for acute DVT treatment (preferred over LMWH/warfarin).
treatment_algorithm: Acute: DOAC (rivaroxaban 15mg BID ×21 days then 20mg daily; or apixaban 10mg BID ×7 days then 5mg BID) — first-line per AHA/ACC/CHEST guidelines. Alternative: LMWH → warfarin (INR 2–3). Extended anticoagulation for recurrent/unprovoked: DOAC at prophylactic dose (apixaban 2.5mg BID). CDT for iliofemoral DVT with high clot burden (reduce PTS risk). Compression stockings for PTS prevention. IVC filter only if anticoagulation contraindicated. Novel 2025: tenecteplase-modified protocols; factor XI inhibitors (abelacimab — AZALEA, positive results; osocimab) for DVT prevention with less bleeding.
us_prevalence: ~900,000–1M VTE events/year (DVT ~600,000; PE ~300,000). Prevalent patients on extended anticoagulation: ~3–5M.
patients_needing_tx: ~900,000 acute + ~3–5M long-term anticoagulation.
price: $3,000–$5,000/year for DOAC (generic/brand); novel Factor XI inhibitor: $10,000–$20,000/year (expected).
registrational_endpoint: Symptomatic recurrent VTE at 6–12 months (non-inferiority vs. DOAC for new agents); major bleeding rate (key safety).
btd_threshold_vs_soc: Non-inferiority: recurrent VTE ≤2.3% (non-inferior to apixaban ~2.3%) + major bleeding ≤0.5% (superior to DOAC ~1.0%); FXI inhibitor achieving ~75% bleeding reduction vs. DOAC.

symptoms: Twisted, bulging leg veins; aching, heaviness, swelling, itching
treatment_goal: Relieve symptoms, prevent chronic venous insufficiency progression
first_line_soc: Compression stockings (20–30 mmHg) are first-line conservative management. Sclerotherapy (foam or liquid) or endovenous ablation (laser, radiofrequency) for symptomatic disease requiring intervention.
treatment_algorithm: Conservative: compression stockings, leg elevation, weight loss. Pharmacologic: venoactive drugs (diosmin/hesperidin — Daflon, widely used outside US; horse chestnut seed extract) for symptom relief — not FDA-approved but used OTC. Procedural: sclerotherapy, endovenous thermal ablation (EVLA, RFA), ambulatory phlebectomy, VenaSeal (cyanoacrylate closure). No disease-modifying pharmacologic SoC in US. NSAIDs for acute pain.
us_prevalence: ~23–25 million Americans have varicose veins; ~6 million have chronic venous insufficiency with advanced skin changes. Annual new symptomatic cases ~2 million.
patients_needing_tx: ~6–8 million seek treatment annually; ~2–3 million require procedural or pharmacologic intervention. Pharmacologic candidates: ~1–2 million (venoactive agents, novel drugs).
price: $3,000–$8,000/year for a novel venoactive/anti-inflammatory oral agent for symptomatic relief.
registrational_endpoint: Venous Clinical Severity Score (VCSS) change from baseline; patient-reported symptom score (heaviness, pain, edema VAS) at 6 months.
btd_threshold_vs_soc: ≥4-point reduction in VCSS vs. placebo (vs. ~1–2 for compression alone); superior to sham compression on symptom VAS with p<0.01.

symptoms: Small dilated blood vessels on skin/mucous membranes; usually cosmetic; bleeding if mucosal
treatment_goal: Cosmetic improvement; prevent recurrent bleeding in HHT-associated telangiectasias
first_line_soc: Laser therapy or sclerotherapy for cosmetic skin telangiectasias. For hereditary hemorrhagic telangiectasia (HHT)-related: bevacizumab (anti-VEGF) is emerging standard; iron supplementation for chronic bleeding-related anemia.
treatment_algorithm: Isolated cosmetic telangiectasias: laser/sclerotherapy — no pharmacologic SoC. HHT (if underlying): bevacizumab (anti-VEGF, IV or intranasal) for epistaxis — PAVMs treated by embolization. Thalidomide/lenalidomide (anti-angiogenic) for severe GI bleeding. Pazopanib (Phase 2 trials). Intranasal bevacizumab spray for epistaxis prevention. Hormone therapy (estrogen-progesterone) historically used.
us_prevalence: Idiopathic telangiectasias: ~10–20M US patients (largely cosmetic). HHT: ~100,000 US patients (1 in 5,000–8,000).
patients_needing_tx: HHT: ~50,000 requiring pharmacologic management; idiopathic: minimal pharmacologic need.
price: $40,000–$80,000/year for anti-VEGF/anti-angiogenic biologic (bevacizumab for HHT: off-label ~$50K/year).
registrational_endpoint: Epistaxis severity score (ESS) change from baseline; hemoglobin stabilization; transfusion requirement reduction at 6 months.
btd_threshold_vs_soc: ≥50% reduction in ESS vs. best supportive care; hemoglobin improvement ≥1.5g/dL vs. iron supplementation alone.

symptoms: Pulsatile swelling, bruit/thrill over lesion, high-output heart failure (if large)
treatment_goal: Eliminate AV shunting, prevent high-output HF and ischemia
first_line_soc: Endovascular embolization or surgical ligation is first-line treatment. No pharmacologic SoC for the AV fistula itself.
treatment_algorithm: Acquired (traumatic/iatrogenic): endovascular or surgical closure. Congenital AVM: embolization ± surgery. HF management for high-output HF: standard HF therapy. Anticoagulation/antiplatelet for thrombotic complications. Compression for venous hypertension. No specific pharmacologic disease-modifying treatment.
us_prevalence: ~100,000–200,000 US patients with pathologic AV fistulas/AVMs (varying etiology).
patients_needing_tx: ~50,000–80,000 requiring active management; pharmacologic for symptoms/complications.
price: $20,000–$50,000/year for novel anti-angiogenic or vascular remodeling agent.
registrational_endpoint: AV fistula flow reduction; closure rate; recurrence rate at 12 months.
btd_threshold_vs_soc: Non-surgical closure rate ≥60% vs. surgical/embolization standard.

symptoms: Painful, red, cord-like vein on skin; local warmth, tenderness
treatment_goal: Relieve pain, prevent DVT/PE extension
first_line_soc: Fondaparinux 2.5 mg SC once daily for 45 days for large/proximal SVT (CALISTO trial). Topical or oral NSAIDs for symptomatic relief of small distal SVT.
treatment_algorithm: Significant SVT (≥5 cm, within 3 cm of saphenofemoral junction): fondaparinux 2.5 mg SC × 45 days (CALISTO: 85% reduction in VTE complications).
