Alternative: prophylactic-dose LMWH. DOACs (rivaroxaban) increasingly used off-label. Topical diclofenac gel or oral NSAIDs for pain/inflammation. Compression stockings. Small distal SVT: topical NSAIDs + observation. Anticoagulation reserved for high-risk anatomy.
us_prevalence: ~125,000–150,000 new cases/year in the US. Lifetime incidence ~1 in 60 adults. Often under-reported; ~600,000–800,000 prevalent symptomatic cases.
patients_needing_tx: ~100,000–125,000 per year need pharmacologic therapy (fondaparinux or anticoagulation); remainder managed with NSAIDs/compression. Annual treatment population: ~100K.
price: $8,000–$15,000/year equivalent course for novel anticoagulant; short treatment duration limits annual revenue per episode (~$500–$1,500/course). Novel agent priced at $3,000–$5,000/45-day course.
registrational_endpoint: Composite of symptomatic DVT, PE, symptomatic SVT extension, or SVT-related death at day 47 (CALISTO-design); non-inferiority vs. fondaparinux.
btd_threshold_vs_soc: ≥80% reduction in composite thromboembolic endpoint vs. placebo; non-inferiority to fondaparinux with ≤2% absolute margin.

symptoms: Palpitations, fatigue, shortness of breath, irregular heartbeat, dizziness
treatment_goal: Prevent stroke, control rate/rhythm, improve quality of life
first_line_soc: Anticoagulation (direct oral anticoagulants — apixaban, rivaroxaban preferred over warfarin) for stroke prevention in CHA₂DS₂-VASc ≥2. Rate control: beta-blockers or non-dihydropyridine CCBs (diltiazem, verapamil) as first-line.
treatment_algorithm: Stroke prevention: DOACs (apixaban/Eliquis, rivaroxaban/Xarelto, dabigatran/Pradaxa, edoxaban/Savaysa) preferred over warfarin. Rate control: beta-blockers (metoprolol, carvedilol), diltiazem, verapamil; digoxin add-on. Rhythm control: antiarrhythmic drugs (flecainide, propafenone for paroxysmal AF without structural disease; amiodarone, sotalol, dofetilide for persistent/structural disease). Catheter ablation (pulmonary vein isolation) for drug-refractory or patient-preferred rhythm control. Novel agents: vernakalant (IV, acute cardioversion). 2024-approved: etripamide (MAb-based), flecainide acetate inhalation powder (Staccato). Electrophysiology: His-bundle pacing, AV node ablation + pacing.
us_prevalence: ~6–7 million US adults with AF in 2025 (AHA 2025 statistics). Projected to reach 12.1 million by 2030. Annual incidence ~750,000 new cases.
patients_needing_tx: ~6M need anticoagulation + rate/rhythm control. ~2–3M are candidates for rhythm control drugs or ablation. All 6M need pharmacotherapy. Novel rhythm control drug addressable population: ~2M.
price: $15,000–$25,000/year for novel antiarrhythmic (vs. generic amiodarone ~$600/year). Oral novel agent for AF rhythm control: $18K–$22K/year.
registrational_endpoint: Time to first documented AF recurrence after cardioversion; proportion in sinus rhythm at 12 months (AFFIRM-style). For stroke prevention: non-inferiority on stroke/systemic embolism rate vs. warfarin with superior bleeding profile.
btd_threshold_vs_soc: ≥30% reduction in AF recurrence vs. best antiarrhythmic drug at 12 months; or non-inferior stroke prevention + ≥30% reduction in major bleeding vs. warfarin.

symptoms: Extra heartbeats, palpitations, thumping sensation; usually benign; may cause cardiomyopathy if frequent
treatment_goal: Reduce symptom burden; prevent VPB-induced cardiomyopathy if high burden (>10%)
first_line_soc: Beta-blockers (metoprolol, bisoprolol) or CCBs (verapamil) are first-line for symptomatic VPBs. Reassurance for asymptomatic/low-burden VPBs.
treatment_algorithm: Asymptomatic: reassurance, lifestyle modification (reduce caffeine, alcohol, stress). Symptomatic: beta-blockers (preferred if structural heart disease); verapamil/diltiazem for idiopathic RVOT VPBs. High-burden VPBs causing cardiomyopathy (>15–20%): catheter ablation (curative in ~80% of RVOT-origin VPBs). Antiarrhythmics (flecainide, propafenone) if no structural disease; mexiletine for LV/ischemic origin. Amiodarone: last resort given toxicity. Beta-blocker + antiarrhythmic combination for refractory cases. Ablation preferred over long-term antiarrhythmics when applicable.
us_prevalence: VPBs detectable in nearly all adults on prolonged monitoring. Clinically significant (symptomatic or high burden >500/day): ~5–10M US adults. VPB-induced cardiomyopathy: ~100,000–200,000.
patients_needing_tx: ~2–3M Americans need pharmacologic treatment for symptomatic VPBs; ~150,000–200,000 with VPB-cardiomyopathy needing ablation or antiarrhythmic.
price: $10,000–$18,000/year for novel targeted antiarrhythmic for VPB suppression.
registrational_endpoint: ≥50% reduction in VPB burden (24h Holter count) from baseline; LVEF improvement ≥5% in VPB-cardiomyopathy subgroup at 6 months.
btd_threshold_vs_soc: ≥80% VPB burden reduction + LVEF improvement ≥10% vs. beta-blocker at 6 months; superior to placebo on symptom score with p<0.001.

disease: Bundle Branch Block and Fascicular Block
symptoms: Usually asymptomatic; may cause dizziness, syncope, or heart failure if complete
treatment_goal: Identify and treat underlying cause; prevent progression to complete AV block
first_line_soc: No specific pharmacologic SoC for isolated BBB/fascicular block. Underlying disease treatment (ischemia, HF) is primary. CRT (cardiac resynchronization therapy) pacemaker for LBBB with heart failure and reduced EF (≤35%).
treatment_algorithm: Isolated RBBB: observation; no treatment needed unless symptoms. LBBB with heart failure (EF ≤35%, QRS ≥150 ms): CRT-D (defibrillator-resynchronizer) — Class I indication. His bundle pacing or left bundle branch area pacing (LBBAP) as emerging alternative to CRT. Underlying etiology treatment: revascularization for ischemic LBBB, HF guideline-directed medical therapy (beta-blockers, ACEi/ARNi, SGLT2i, MRA). No pharmacologic agent specifically for BBB reversal.
us_prevalence: LBBB prevalence: ~0.5–1% of adults (~1.6–3.3M US patients). RBBB: ~0.8–2.5% (~2.6–8.3M US patients). Clinically significant (with symptoms/HF): ~500,000–1M.
patients_needing_tx: ~300,000–500,000 with symptomatic LBBB or LBBB+HF requiring CRT or His-bundle pacing. Pharmacologic treatment largely via underlying HF management.
price: $30,000–$50,000/year for novel cardiac conduction-restoring gene therapy or biologic.
tam_gross: 500K × $40K = $20B (primarily device market; pharmacologic TAM much smaller)
registrational_endpoint: QRS duration normalization (≤120 ms) or LVEF improvement ≥5% at 6 months; 6-minute walk distance change from baseline.
btd_threshold_vs_soc: ≥10% absolute LVEF improvement + QRS narrowing ≥30 ms vs. CRT at 6 months; or ≥50% reduction in HF hospitalization vs. optimal medical therapy alone.

symptoms: Slow heartbeat, dizziness, syncope, fatigue, alternating fast/slow rate (tachy-brady)
treatment_goal: Maintain adequate heart rate; manage tachy-brady syndrome; prevent syncope
first_line_soc: Permanent pacemaker implantation is definitive therapy for symptomatic SSS. No pharmacologic agent reliably and safely corrects the underlying SA node dysfunction.
treatment_algorithm: Symptomatic bradycardia/syncope: permanent pacemaker (AAI for isolated SA disease; DDD for combined SA and AV node disease). Tachy-brady: pacemaker + antiarrhythmic drugs (flecainide, propafenone, amiodarone, or beta-blocker) to suppress tachyarrhythmia after rate support from pacemaker — caution as drugs worsen bradycardia. Acute reversible causes: treat hypothyroidism, drug toxicity (beta-blockers, digoxin, CCBs, amiodarone), sleep apnea. No pharmacologic SoC for chronic SSS per se. HCN4 channel modulators (e.g., If current enhancers) under research.
us_prevalence: ~1 in 600 adults over 65 (~300,000–400,000 US patients); pacemaker implants for SSS: ~60,000–80,000/year.
patients_needing_tx: ~300,000–400,000 with device therapy; pharmacologic adjuncts: ~100,000–150,000 requiring antiarrhythmic on top of pacing.
price: $40,000–$80,000/year for a novel SA node modulator/biological pacemaker substitute.
registrational_endpoint: Proportion free of symptomatic bradycardia/syncope at 12 months; minimum daytime heart rate ≥50 bpm; chronotropic incompetence improvement on exercise testing.
btd_threshold_vs_soc: ≥70% freedom from symptomatic bradycardia vs. placebo; minimum HR ≥50 bpm maintained in ≥80% of patients without pacing.

symptoms: Extra heartbeats, palpitations, irregular pulse; usually benign but distressing
treatment_goal: Reduce symptom burden; treat only if causing cardiomyopathy or severe symptoms
first_line_soc: Beta-blockers (metoprolol, atenolol) or non-dihydropyridine CCBs (diltiazem, verapamil) as first-line for symptomatic PACs/ectopic SVT. Reassurance and lifestyle modification for asymptomatic/mildly symptomatic patients.
treatment_algorithm: Reassurance + lifestyle: reduce caffeine, alcohol, stress. Symptomatic PACs/junctional ectopics: beta-blockers or diltiazem. If PAC burden >10,000/day causing tachycardia-induced cardiomyopathy: catheter ablation (focal trigger elimination). Anti-arrhythmics (flecainide, propafenone) for refractory ectopy. Treatment of underlying causes (thyroid, sleep apnea, electrolyte imbalance). Low pharmacologic market need — most managed with beta-blockers.
us_prevalence: PACs detectable in >99% of adults on prolonged monitoring; frequent PACs (>100/day): ~5–10% of adults (~16–33M). Clinically significant/symptomatic: ~2–5M.
patients_needing_tx: ~1–2M require pharmacologic treatment (beta-blockers for symptom control). Novel drugs: ~500,000 with drug-refractory ectopy or ectopy-induced cardiomyopathy.
price: $10,000–$18,000/year for novel antiarrhythmic targeting focal ectopy.
registrational_endpoint: ≥50% reduction in PAC burden (Holter-assessed over 24h) and improvement in symptom score (palpitation diary) at 12 weeks.
btd_threshold_vs_soc: ≥70% reduction in PAC burden + ≥40% improvement in symptom score vs. best beta-blocker at 12 weeks.

symptoms: Persistent elevated resting HR (>100 bpm), palpitations, fatigue, exercise intolerance
treatment_goal: Lower resting heart rate; reduce palpitations and fatigue
first_line_soc: Ivabradine (Corlanor) — selective If current inhibitor — is first-line pharmacologic therapy for IST when sinus rate reduction is needed without AV node blockade. Beta-blockers also used first-line.
treatment_algorithm: Non-pharmacologic: exercise training, compression stockings (if POTS component), avoidance of triggers (caffeine, alcohol, dehydration). Pharmacologic Step 1: beta-blockers (metoprolol, atenolol). Step 2: Ivabradine (Corlanor) — reduces HR specifically; approved for heart failure but used off-label in IST and POTS — very effective. Step 3: Combination beta-blocker + ivabradine. Step 4: CCBs (diltiazem, verapamil). Refractory: catheter ablation of sinus node modification (limited efficacy; risks). Low-dose propranolol or clonidine for autonomic dysregulation component.
us_prevalence: ~0.2–1.6% of adults; estimated ~500,000–1M US patients with IST diagnosis. Overlap with POTS (postural orthostatic tachycardia syndrome): ~3M.
patients_needing_tx: ~300,000–500,000 with moderate-severe IST requiring pharmacologic treatment.
price: $12,000–$18,000/year for novel If inhibitor/autonomic modulator (ivabradine generic emerging; branded ~$3,600/year; novel agent premium).
registrational_endpoint: Mean 24-hour heart rate reduction ≥8 bpm from baseline (Holter); proportion achieving mean HR <90 bpm at rest at 12 weeks; symptom score improvement.
btd_threshold_vs_soc: ≥15 bpm mean HR reduction + ≥40% improvement in symptom score vs. placebo at 12 weeks; superior to metoprolol on combined HR + symptom endpoint.

symptoms: Rapid pounding heartbeat (≥100 bpm), dizziness, syncope; structural heart disease common
treatment_goal: Terminate acute VT; prevent recurrence; reduce ICD shocks and sudden death
first_line_soc: Hemodynamically unstable VT: synchronized DC cardioversion immediately. Stable sustained VT: IV amiodarone (150 mg over 10 min then 1 mg/min infusion) or procainamide IV as first-line.
treatment_algorithm: Acute: synchronized cardioversion for unstable VT. Stable monomorphic VT: IV amiodarone (first-line) or IV procainamide; lidocaine for ischemic VT. Post-stabilization: oral amiodarone, sotalol, mexiletine as adjuncts. ICD implantation for secondary prevention in structural heart disease (Class I). Catheter ablation of VT (endo/epicardial) reduces ICD shocks and VT storm; Class IIa for recurrent VT post-ICD. Beta-blockers reduce SCD and ICD shocks. Cardiac sympathetic denervation for electrical storm refractory to ablation. Novel: celivarone, dofetilide-derived agents in trials. 2025: subcutaneous ICD + VT ablation combination therapy.
us_prevalence: ~300,000–400,000 US patients with recurrent sustained VT; ~200,000 VT storm episodes/year. ICD recipients for VT: ~500,000.
patients_needing_tx: ~350,000 need chronic antiarrhythmic pharmacotherapy + ICD. Novel antiarrhythmic addressable: ~200,000 with inadequate control on amiodarone/sotalol.
price: $20,000–$40,000/year for novel VT-specific antiarrhythmic (vs. generic amiodarone ~$500–$1,500/year).
registrational_endpoint: Time to first appropriate ICD therapy or VT recurrence at 24 months (secondary prevention); VT storm reduction (ICD shocks/6 months) in adjunct therapy trial.
btd_threshold_vs_soc: ≥50% reduction in appropriate ICD shocks vs. amiodarone at 12 months; VT storm: ≥70% reduction in ICD therapies/episode vs. best antiarrhythmic.

symptoms: Bradycardia, dizziness, syncope, fatigue; complete block causes very slow pulse
treatment_goal: Restore adequate heart rate; prevent sudden death, syncope
first_line_soc: Permanent pacemaker implantation is definitive treatment for symptomatic high-degree (Mobitz II, complete) AV block. Acute: IV atropine or temporary pacing for hemodynamically compromised patients.
treatment_algorithm: Acute/reversible: IV atropine (0.5–1 mg) for Mobitz I or high-degree block with hemodynamic compromise; isoproterenol IV infusion or transcutaneous/transvenous pacing if atropine fails. Treat reversible causes: hyperkalemia, drug toxicity (beta-blocker, digoxin, CCB), Lyme carditis (ceftriaxone), acute MI (especially inferior MI causing AV block). Permanent: pacemaker implantation (VVI, DDD) for symptomatic Mobitz II or complete AV block. No pharmacologic SoC for chronic AV block — no drug replaces pacemaker.
us_prevalence: ~3–5% of adults have some degree of AV conduction disease; complete AV block: ~0.04% (~130,000 US patients). Annual pacemaker implants for AV block: ~100,000.
patients_needing_tx: ~100,000–130,000 with high-degree AV block needing intervention; ~50,000–60,000 per year for pharmacologic bridging (acute) or novel therapies. Chronic pharmacologic market is small — device is SoC.
price: $30,000–$60,000/year for novel biological pacemaker or gene therapy (HCN4 gene therapy in development) in select patients.
registrational_endpoint: Freedom from symptomatic high-degree AV block requiring device upgrade or rescue pacing at 12 months; heart rate response during exercise stress testing.
btd_threshold_vs_soc: Biological/gene therapy: ≥70% of patients achieving intrinsic heart rate ≥50 bpm at rest and ≥100 bpm at peak exercise without device backup, vs. 0% for no-treatment control.

symptoms: Sudden rapid palpitations (150–250 bpm), dizziness, chest tightness, abrupt onset/offset
treatment_goal: Terminate acute episodes; prevent recurrence
first_line_soc: Acute termination: vagal maneuvers first; if unsuccessful, IV adenosine (Adenocard, 6–12 mg rapid push) is first-line pharmacologic treatment with ~90% success.
treatment_algorithm: Acute: Vagal maneuvers (Valsalva, carotid massage, ice water immersion). If unsuccessful: adenosine IV 6 mg (then 12 mg). Alternative: diltiazem or verapamil IV; esmolol IV. Hemodynamically unstable: synchronized cardioversion. Chronic prevention: beta-blockers, CCBs (diltiazem, verapamil) as first-line. If refractory: catheter ablation (curative ~95% for AVNRT, ~90% for AVRT/WPW). Antiarrhythmics (flecainide, propafenone) for patients refusing ablation. 2024-2025: etripamide (OTC sublingual formulation in development for self-termination by patients).
us_prevalence: ~570,000–1 million US patients with PSVT. Annual incidence ~89,000 new cases. Point prevalence ~2.25 per 1,000 (~750,000 affected Americans).
patients_needing_tx: ~500,000–700,000 require chronic pharmacologic management or ablation. Pharmacologic candidates: ~250,000–350,000 (ablation-ineligible or preference for medication).
price: $12,000–$20,000/year for oral novel antiarrhythmic for PSVT prevention; ~$500–$1,000 per acute-use self-administered formulation.
registrational_endpoint: Time to first PSVT recurrence (EKG-documented) over 12 months in chronic prevention trial; or proportion with successful self-termination of acute episode within 15 minutes.
btd_threshold_vs_soc: ≥50% reduction in PSVT episodes/month vs. diltiazem/beta-blocker over 6 months; or ≥80% acute termination rate vs. placebo within 15 minutes.

symptoms: Regular rapid palpitations (~150 bpm), fatigue, shortness of breath, chest flutter
treatment_goal: Restore sinus rhythm, control ventricular rate, prevent thromboembolism
first_line_soc: Rate control: IV beta-blockers or diltiazem for acute rate control. Rhythm conversion: electrical cardioversion (highly effective, ~95%) or ibutilide IV. Anticoagulation: same stroke risk stratification as AF (DOACs).
treatment_algorithm: Acute rate control: IV diltiazem, metoprolol, or digoxin. Cardioversion: DC cardioversion (highly effective) or ibutilide/dofetilide IV pharmacologic conversion. Anticoagulation: DOACs per CHA₂DS₂-VASc score. Long-term: catheter ablation of cavotricuspid isthmus (CTI ablation) — first-line definitive treatment, ~90–95% success. Antiarrhythmics (amiodarone, sotalol, dofetilide) for recurrence. Note: type I flutter is highly amenable to ablation; type II flutter more complex.
us_prevalence: ~200,000–250,000 annual new cases in US; prevalent cases ~500,000–800,000. Often co-exists with AF.
patients_needing_tx: ~400,000–500,000 need pharmacologic and/or ablation therapy. Pharmacologic (pre-ablation, recurrent, ablation-ineligible): ~150,000.
price: $15,000–$22,000/year for novel antiarrhythmic for flutter/AF spectrum.
registrational_endpoint: Proportion maintaining sinus rhythm at 12 months post-cardioversion; time to first recurrence of atrial flutter/AF.
btd_threshold_vs_soc: ≥40% reduction in flutter recurrence vs. antiarrhythmic SOC at 12 months; or non-inferiority to CTI ablation with improved safety.

symptoms: Palpitations, delta wave on ECG; risk of rapid AF and sudden cardiac death
treatment_goal: Eliminate accessory pathway; prevent dangerous arrhythmias and sudden death
first_line_soc: Catheter ablation (radiofrequency) of accessory pathway is definitive first-line treatment in symptomatic patients or asymptomatic high-risk patients (~95% cure rate). Pharmacologically: flecainide or propafenone for symptom suppression in those awaiting ablation.
treatment_algorithm: Definitive: catheter ablation of accessory pathway (RFA or cryo) — ~95% curative. Acute tachycardia (AVRT): vagal maneuvers → adenosine IV → DC cardioversion. AF with pre-excitation (see WPW+AF entry): IV procainamide, avoid AV nodal blockers. Chronic pharmacologic (ablation-ineligible): flecainide, propafenone, sotalol, or amiodarone. Asymptomatic WPW: ablation recommended if high-risk pathway (short refractory period); otherwise observation. Risk stratification with EPS (electrophysiology study).
us_prevalence: ~1–3 per 1,000 population; ~330,000–990,000 US patients with WPW pattern; symptomatic WPW: ~100,000–300,000.
patients_needing_tx: ~200,000–250,000 with symptomatic or high-risk WPW requiring ablation or pharmacologic therapy. Pharmacologic (chronic): ~50,000–75,000 (post-ablation failure, ablation-ineligible).
price: $20,000–$35,000/year for novel accessory pathway-targeted pharmacotherapy.
registrational_endpoint: Freedom from symptomatic arrhythmia (AVRT, pre-excited AF) at 12 months; proportion with accessory pathway conduction suppression (loss of delta wave) on ECG.
btd_threshold_vs_soc: ≥60% delta wave suppression rate + ≥70% reduction in arrhythmia episodes vs. placebo; non-inferior to ablation in low-risk WPW at 12 months.

disease: Atrial Fibrillation and Wolff-Parkinson-White Syndrome (WPW Syndrome)
symptoms: Rapid irregular palpitations, pre-excitation on ECG, risk of sudden death
treatment_goal: Prevent life-threatening rapid ventricular response; eliminate accessory pathway
first_line_soc: Catheter ablation of accessory pathway is definitive treatment. Acute AF with WPW: IV procainamide or ibutilide preferred; avoid AV nodal blockers (digoxin, verapamil, adenosine — may accelerate conduction via accessory pathway).
treatment_algorithm: Acute: IV procainamide (preferred), ibutilide, or electrical cardioversion for hemodynamically unstable patients. AVOID: adenosine, beta-blockers, CCBs (may accelerate accessory pathway conduction → VF). Long-term: catheter ablation (radiofrequency or cryoablation) of accessory pathway — curative ~95%. For patients refusing ablation: flecainide, propafenone, or sotalol as chronic suppression. Asymptomatic pre-excitation with low-risk pathway: observation vs. ablation (shared decision).
us_prevalence: WPW syndrome: ~1–3 per 1,000 (~330,000–990,000 US patients with pre-excitation pattern); symptomatic WPW with AF: ~100,000–200,000.
patients_needing_tx: ~150,000–200,000 with symptomatic WPW-AF requiring ablation or chronic antiarrhythmics. Pharmacologic candidates: ~50,000–75,000 (ablation-ineligible or recurrent).
price: $20,000–$35,000/year for novel antiarrhythmic specifically validated in WPW-AF context.
registrational_endpoint: Proportion free of AF recurrence with rapid pre-excited response at 12 months; time to first symptomatic AF episode in WPW patients post-ablation failure.
btd_threshold_vs_soc: ≥50% reduction in AF episodes with pre-excited conduction vs. best antiarrhythmic comparator at 12 months.

symptoms: Syncope, palpitations, sudden twisting QRS pattern on ECG; risk of VF and sudden death
treatment_goal: Terminate TdP, correct QTc, prevent recurrence and VF
first_line_soc: IV magnesium sulfate (1–2 g IV over 5–15 min) is first-line for acute TdP, effective even without documented hypomagnesemia. Remove offending QT-prolonging drugs. Correct electrolyte imbalances (K+, Mg2+).
treatment_algorithm: Acute: IV magnesium sulfate (first-line). Correct hypokalemia (K+ ≥4.5 mEq/L), hypomagnesemia. Overdrive pacing (temporary transvenous pacing at 90–100 bpm) shortens QTc and suppresses TdP. Isoproterenol IV to increase HR and shorten QT (for pause-dependent TdP). Remove/stop QT-prolonging drugs. Long-term: identify and treat underlying LQTS (see LQTS entry). ICD implantation for high-risk LQTS. Mexiletine (late Na+ channel blocker) specifically for LQTS3. Avoid Class Ia, Ic, III antiarrhythmics.
us_prevalence: ~100,000–200,000 episodes of drug-induced TdP annually in US. Congenital LQTS-related TdP: ~200,000 patients at risk. Total patients with clinically significant recurrent TdP: ~50,000–100,000.
patients_needing_tx: ~50,000–100,000 with recurrent or high-risk TdP requiring ongoing pharmacologic prevention (mexiletine, potassium supplementation, magnesium maintenance).
price: $15,000–$30,000/year for novel late Na+ channel blocker or targeted LQTS pharmacotherapy.
registrational_endpoint: Change in QTc interval from baseline; TdP recurrence rate; VF/cardiac arrest-free survival at 12 months.
btd_threshold_vs_soc: ≥50 ms QTc reduction + 0% TdP recurrence vs. placebo (vs. ~30–40% recurrence without treatment) at 12 months; superior to mexiletine on QTc + safety composite.

symptoms: Sudden cardiac arrest, loss of consciousness, absent pulse; fatal without immediate defibrillation
treatment_goal: Immediate defibrillation; prevent recurrence with ICD; treat underlying cause
first_line_soc: Immediate unsynchronized DC defibrillation (200–360 J biphasic) is the only effective acute treatment. For VF refractory to defibrillation: IV amiodarone (300 mg) or lidocaine (1.5 mg/kg) plus continued CPR.
treatment_algorithm: Acute ACLS: CPR + immediate defibrillation (200 J biphasic). Vasopressors: epinephrine 1 mg IV q3–5 min. Refractory VF: amiodarone 300 mg IV (then 150 mg), or lidocaine 1–1.5 mg/kg. Correct reversible causes (5 H's and 5 T's). Post-resuscitation: targeted temperature management (32–36°C), coronary angiography/PCI. Long-term secondary prevention: ICD implantation (Class I for VF survivors without reversible cause). Beta-blockers (carvedilol, metoprolol) reduce recurrence. Amiodarone as adjunct to ICD to reduce shocks. Treatment of underlying disease (HCM, ARVC, channelopathy-specific therapies).
us_prevalence: ~350,000 sudden cardiac arrests/year in US; ~80–85% due to VF. Post-VF survivors needing secondary prevention: ~30,000–50,000/year (with ICD + drugs). Prevalent ICD recipients for VF history: ~500,000.
patients_needing_tx: ~300,000–400,000 annual acute VF episodes requiring pharmacologic resuscitation; ~50,000/year new ICD + adjunct drug candidates.
price: $15,000–$25,000/year for novel anti-VF drug adjunct to ICD (acute IV formulation valued per-episode; chronic prevention: $15–25K/year).
peak_sales: 500K prevalent ICD patients × $3K adjunct drug supplement = $1.5B; acute use novel agent: 50K/year × $5K per episode = $250M. Total: ~$750M
registrational_endpoint: Time to first appropriate ICD shock (VF recurrence) at 24 months (secondary prevention trial); VF termination rate in acute (ACLS) setting.
btd_threshold_vs_soc: ≥40% reduction in appropriate ICD shocks vs. placebo+ICD at 24 months; or acute VF: ≥80% single-shock termination rate vs. standard defibrillation+amiodarone.

symptoms: Sudden cardiac arrest during or after exercise; typically in young athlete; often first symptom is collapse
treatment_goal: Identify high-risk athletes; prevent SCD via screening and targeted treatment
first_line_soc: Immediate treatment: CPR + AED defibrillation. Prevention: treat underlying cause (HCM — mavacamten; ARVC — antiarrhythmics + ICD; CPVT — nadolol + flecainide). Beta-blockers are first-line for CPVT/LQTS-related SCD risk.
treatment_algorithm: Acute: CPR + AED; ACLS (epinephrine, amiodarone). Prevention: pre-participation cardiovascular screening (ECG, echo). Underlying HCM: ICD + beta-blockers; obstructive HCM: mavacamten (Camzyos). Underlying ARVC: ICD + sotalol or nadolol; restrict from competitive sports. Underlying LQTS/CPVT: beta-blockers (nadolol preferred) + ICD in high-risk; flecainide add-on for CPVT. Commotio cordis: preventive equipment. Anomalous coronary artery: surgical correction. Congenital structural CHD: repair. No single pharmacologic agent for 'SCD prevention in athletes' broadly — disease-specific.
us_prevalence: ~100–200 athletic SCD events/year in competitive athletes (NCAA: ~1:80,000 athlete-years). Overall SCD in active adults <35: ~1,000–3,000/year. Total young competitive athletes at risk: ~9 million.
patients_needing_tx: ~10,000–50,000 high-risk athletes with identifiable conditions (HCM, ARVC, CPVT, LQTS) requiring disease-specific pharmacotherapy + ICD.
price: $50,000–$90,000/year for novel targeted therapy (e.g., gene therapy for CPVT, RyR2-targeted agents) in high-risk athletes.
peak_sales: Single novel agent for CPVT/SCD prevention in athletes: 15K × $70K × 30% = $315M
registrational_endpoint: Rate of appropriate ICD shocks (VF/VT) or resuscitated SCD over 24 months in high-risk athletes; VT inducibility on EPS.
btd_threshold_vs_soc: ≥70% reduction in VT/VF events vs. standard beta-blocker therapy at 24 months; 0% SCD rate in treated group vs. expected ~1–2%/year in high-risk patients.

symptoms: Enlarged heart, low resting heart rate, benign ECG changes; asymptomatic physiologic adaptation
treatment_goal: Distinguish from pathologic cardiomyopathy; no treatment needed for true athlete's heart
first_line_soc: None — athlete's heart is a normal physiologic adaptation. Management is diagnostic: cardiac MRI to distinguish from HCM/ARVC; genetic testing if unclear. No pharmacologic SoC.
treatment_algorithm: No pharmacologic treatment required. Workup: 12-lead ECG (trained athlete criteria), echocardiography, cardiac MRI with gadolinium (distinguishes HCM from athlete's heart by fibrosis/strain patterns). Genetic testing if HCM/ARVC cannot be excluded. Detraining trial if still uncertain (athlete's heart regresses with detraining; HCM does not). Return to sport clearance once cardiomyopathy excluded.
us_prevalence: ~35–45 million Americans engage in regular intense exercise; ~1–5% (350,000–2.25M) meet criteria for athlete's heart on imaging. Not a disease — no treatment market.
patients_needing_tx: ~0 requiring pharmacologic treatment for athlete's heart per se. Diagnostic workup is the intervention. Hypothetical novel drug market: negligible.
price: N/A — not a disease indication. Hypothetical diagnostic biomarker test: $500–$2,000 per patient.
peak_sales: N/A — no drug treatment; diagnostic market ~$200M for imaging/biomarker differentiation
registrational_endpoint: N/A — no drug treatment endpoint applicable. Diagnostic: sensitivity/specificity of biomarker to distinguish athlete's heart from HCM (≥95% sensitivity, ≥90% specificity).

symptoms: Dyspnea on exertion, fatigue, palpitations, heart murmur, reduced exercise capacity
treatment_goal: Preserve LV function; prevent heart failure; timely repair or replacement
first_line_soc: Primary MR: surgical mitral valve repair (preferred) or replacement is definitive. Secondary MR (due to HF): GDMT for HF (ACEi/ARB, beta-blocker, SGLT2i, MRA) plus transcatheter MitraClip (TEER) for eligible high-surgical-risk patients. No drug reverses MR.
treatment_algorithm: Primary (degenerative) MR: Watchful waiting until criteria met (EF <60%, LVESD ≥40mm, or symptoms). Surgical repair preferred over replacement. MitraClip TEER for inoperable primary MR. Secondary (functional) MR: HF GDMT (quadruple therapy: ARNi, beta-blocker, MRA, SGLT2i). MitraClip (COAPT trial: improved outcomes in properly selected secondary MR). Novel pharmacologic: omecamtiv mecarbil (cardiac myosin activator) and mavacamten studied in secondary MR of HCM. Terlipressin and vasopressin analogues for acute decompensated MR. Anticoagulation for AF comorbidity.
us_prevalence: ~10 million US adults with MR; clinically significant (moderate-severe): ~2–3M; severe MR: ~800,000–1M; ~100,000 interventions/year.
patients_needing_tx: ~500,000–1M with symptomatic/moderate-severe MR needing pharmacologic management or procedural therapy. Novel pharmacologic (anti-remodeling) addressable: ~300,000.
price: $25,000–$45,000/year for novel anti-remodeling biologic or small molecule for secondary MR.
registrational_endpoint: LVEF preservation (EF ≥50%) at 24 months; or composite of HF hospitalization, MR progression to severe, or death. EROA (effective regurgitant orifice area) reduction by echo.
btd_threshold_vs_soc: ≥30% reduction in EROA + ≥40% reduction in HF hospitalization/death composite vs. GDMT alone at 24 months; superior to MitraClip alone.

symptoms: Exertional dyspnea, angina, syncope, systolic murmur, exercise intolerance
treatment_goal: Prevent heart failure, syncope, sudden death; timely valve replacement
first_line_soc: No proven pharmacologic SoC to reverse or halt AS progression. Transcatheter aortic valve implantation (TAVI/TAVR) or surgical AVR is definitive treatment. Medical: treat comorbidities (hypertension, HF). Statins do not halt AS progression (SEAS, SALTIRE trials failed).
treatment_algorithm: Medical management (no disease-modifying drugs proven): Control hypertension (avoid vasodilators in severe AS — risk of hypotension); treat HF if present (diuretics, cautious RAAS). TAVR: now standard for severe symptomatic AS across all risk groups (low, intermediate, high surgical risk) — 2024: PARTNER 3 5-year data. Balloon aortic valvuloplasty: bridge to TAVR. Novel pharmacologic targets in development: lipoprotein(a)-lowering (aortic valve calcification prevention — olpasiran, pelacarsen trials); PCSK9 inhibitors not proven effective yet. 2025: no approved drug halts AS. Biotin/vitamin K2 in early trials for calcification.
us_prevalence: ~5 million US adults with some degree of AS; severe AS: ~500,000–800,000; ~250,000–300,000 TAVR/SAVR procedures/year.
patients_needing_tx: ~500,000 with clinically significant AS needing medical management or procedure. Disease-modifying drug addressable (Lp(a) trials): ~200,000 moderate AS patients.
price: $30,000–$50,000/year for novel anti-calcification agent (anti-Lp(a) biologic) that slows AS progression.
registrational_endpoint: Rate of change of aortic valve area (cm²/year) by Doppler echocardiography; or time to TAVR/SAVR/death composite over 3–5 years.
btd_threshold_vs_soc: ≥50% reduction in rate of AV area decline (cm²/year) + ≥30% reduction in time-to-intervention composite vs. placebo over 3 years; statistically significant p<0.01.

symptoms: Breathlessness, fatigue, water-hammer pulse, heart murmur, exercise intolerance
treatment_goal: Prevent LV dysfunction and heart failure; timely surgical intervention
first_line_soc: Vasodilators (ACE inhibitors or ARBs — nifedipine historically used) to reduce afterload in chronic severe AR with LV enlargement in patients not yet meeting surgical criteria. Definitive: surgical aortic valve replacement (SAVR) or TAVI.
treatment_algorithm: Medical: Vasodilators (dihydropyridine CCBs: nifedipine; ACEi/ARBs) for hypertensive patients with AR or to temporize before surgery. Beta-blockers avoided (may worsen AR by reducing HR and increasing diastole time). Definitive: surgical AVR (SAVR) for eligible patients; TAVI increasingly for high-risk/inoperable. No pharmacologic agent alters the natural history of AR independent of surgery. Post-valve replacement: anticoagulation (mechanical valves: warfarin), antibiotic prophylaxis.
us_prevalence: ~5 million US adults with AR; moderate-severe AR: ~800,000–1M; symptomatic/severe requiring intervention: ~200,000–300,000 annually.
patients_needing_tx: ~300,000 with symptomatic severe AR requiring pharmacologic bridge or definitive therapy. Novel pharmacologic (LV remodeling prevention) addressable: ~200,000.
price: $20,000–$40,000/year for novel anti-remodeling agent (RAAS modulator, sacubitril/valsartan equivalent for AR).
registrational_endpoint: Change in LVESV (LV end-systolic volume index) from baseline by echocardiography at 12 months; reduction in time to AVR or LVEF deterioration composite.
btd_threshold_vs_soc: ≥15% reduction in LVESVI vs. placebo + ≥30% reduction in composite of AVR need/LVEF decline <50%/HF hospitalization at 24 months.

symptoms: Leg edema, abdominal distension, fatigue, jugular venous distension, pulsatile liver
treatment_goal: Reduce RV volume overload, prevent progressive RV failure
first_line_soc: Diuretics (furosemide, spironolactone) to manage systemic congestion in symptomatic TR. Treat underlying cause (AF: rate control + anticoagulation; pulmonary hypertension: specific therapies). Tricuspid TEER (TriClip, CLASP TR) emerging as transcatheter option.
treatment_algorithm: Medical: Diuretics (furosemide, metolazone, spironolactone) for volume overload. Treat underlying HF with GDMT. Treat AF (rate control + anticoagulation). Treat pulmonary hypertension (reduces functional TR). Transcatheter: TriClip (Abbott, FDA-approved 2023) for inoperable symptomatic severe TR. CLASP TR (Edwards). TRILUMINATE Pivotal data showed improved quality of life. Surgical tricuspid valve repair/replacement: for severe TR at time of left-heart surgery. No pharmacologic agent directly reduces TR severity.
us_prevalence: ~1.6 million US adults with moderate-severe TR; ~3–5 million with any TR on echo. Clinically significant requiring treatment: ~500,000–800,000.
patients_needing_tx: ~600,000 require pharmacologic management (diuretics, HF therapy). Transcatheter + adjunct pharmacologic candidates: ~200,000.
price: $20,000–$35,000/year for novel pulmonary vasodilator or RV-unloading agent adjunct to TR management.
registrational_endpoint: TR severity grade (EROA reduction by echo) at 12 months; quality of life (KCCQ) improvement; 6-minute walk test change; HF hospitalization rate.
btd_threshold_vs_soc: ≥1 grade TR reduction in ≥70% of patients + ≥15-point KCCQ improvement vs. medical therapy alone; superior to best diuretic management on HF hospitalization composite.

symptoms: Usually asymptomatic; may cause palpitations, atypical chest pain, anxiety, click on exam
treatment_goal: Monitor for MR progression; prevent arrhythmias; treat symptoms
first_line_soc: Beta-blockers (atenolol, metoprolol) for palpitations, chest pain, and anxiety in MVP with arrhythmias. Most patients require no pharmacologic treatment.
treatment_algorithm: Reassurance for most MVP. Symptomatic (palpitations, chest pain): beta-blockers. MR complication: see Mitral Regurgitation entry. MVP + arrhythmias (VPBs, NSVT — 'malignant MVP' or MVPS): beta-blockers + flecainide; ICD if syncope/VF history. Antibiotic prophylaxis: no longer routinely recommended. Anticoagulation: aspirin for MVP + cerebrovascular events. Serial echo surveillance. Avoid stimulants (caffeine, decongestants). Surgical repair for MVP causing severe MR (Class I indication for symptomatic severe MR).
us_prevalence: ~5–7% of US adults have MVP on echocardiography; ~16–23 million. Clinically significant (moderate+ MR, arrhythmias): ~1–2M. Malignant MVP: ~200,000.
patients_needing_tx: ~1–2M need pharmacologic treatment (beta-blockers, antiarrhythmics). Novel arrhythmia/MR prevention drugs: ~200,000–300,000.
price: $12,000–$20,000/year for novel anti-arrhythmic or valve-stabilizing agent for malignant MVP.
registrational_endpoint: Freedom from VT/VF/arrhythmia-related syncope at 24 months; VPB burden reduction (≥50%) on Holter; change in MR severity by echo.
btd_threshold_vs_soc: ≥60% VPB burden reduction + 0% VF events vs. beta-blocker at 12 months in malignant MVP; superior to flecainide on composite arrhythmia endpoint.

symptoms: RV volume overload, exercise intolerance, palpitations; often post-tetralogy repair
treatment_goal: Prevent RV failure; optimal timing of pulmonary valve replacement
first_line_soc: Medical management of RV failure: diuretics (furosemide), ACE inhibitors/ARBs for symptom relief. No pharmacologic agent reverses PR. Pulmonary valve replacement (surgical or transcatheter — Melody, Sapien) is definitive.
treatment_algorithm: Medical: Diuretics for RV volume overload. Beta-blockers for arrhythmias. ACEi/ARBs for symptomatic RV dilation. No drugs halt PR progression. Monitoring: serial CMR for RV size/function. Timing of PVR: RV enlargement above CMR thresholds (RVEDVI ≥160 mL/m²). Transcatheter PVR (Melody, Sapien valves) for conduit-related PR; growing use. Antiarrhythmic therapy (amiodarone, beta-blocker) for VT in repaired tetralogy. ICD for high-risk patients.
us_prevalence: ~500,000–1M US patients with hemodynamically significant PR; majority from repaired congenital heart disease (tetralogy of Fallot: ~60,000–80,000 adults in US). Isolated PR: ~100,000.
patients_needing_tx: ~200,000–300,000 require pharmacologic management (diuretics, antiarrhythmics + monitoring). Novel pharmacologic: ~100,000.
price: $25,000–$50,000/year for novel RV-protective agent (sacubitril/valsartan equivalent for RV failure, or anti-fibrotic for RV).
registrational_endpoint: Change in RVEDVI (RV end-diastolic volume index by CMR) at 12 months; time to pulmonary valve replacement; exercise capacity (VO₂ max).
btd_threshold_vs_soc: ≥15% reduction in RVEDVI + ≥30% reduction in time-to-PVR vs. best supportive care at 24 months.

symptoms: Dyspnea, fatigue, atrial fibrillation, hemoptysis, diastolic murmur
treatment_goal: Control heart rate, prevent AF complications, relieve obstruction via intervention
first_line_soc: Rate control with beta-blockers or diltiazem for AF/tachycardia in MS. Anticoagulation (warfarin or DOAC) for AF comorbidity. Diuretics for pulmonary congestion.
treatment_algorithm: Medical: Rate control: beta-blockers (metoprolol), non-DHP CCBs (diltiazem, verapamil) or digoxin. Diuretics for symptom relief. Anticoagulation: warfarin (target INR 2–3) for AF; DOACs increasingly used. No drug modifies MS natural history or relieves mechanical obstruction. Rheumatic MS prevention: penicillin prophylaxis for recurrent strep/rheumatic fever. Interventional: percutaneous balloon mitral valvuloplasty (PBMV, first-line for pliable non-calcified valve). Surgical: open commissurotomy or mitral valve replacement for unsuitable anatomy or post-PBMV failure.
us_prevalence: MS prevalence declining in US (rheumatic fever decline); ~800,000–1M US patients with MS; mostly rheumatic (immigrant population) or degenerative (elderly). Severe MS: ~100,000–150,000.
patients_needing_tx: ~400,000–600,000 require pharmacologic management (rate control, diuretics, anticoagulation). Novel valve-specific pharmacologic addressable: ~150,000.
price: $15,000–$25,000/year for novel anti-rheumatic/anti-inflammatory agent to slow rheumatic MS progression; anti-fibrotic targeting mitral valve.
registrational_endpoint: Change in mitral valve area (MVA, cm²) by echocardiography over 24 months; time to balloon valvuloplasty or valve replacement.
btd_threshold_vs_soc: Halt/reverse MVA decline (≥0.2 cm² improvement or arrest of progression) vs. placebo over 24 months; ≥40% reduction in time-to-intervention.

symptoms: Dyspnea on exertion, fatigue, RV hypertrophy, systolic murmur; often congenital
treatment_goal: Relieve RV outflow obstruction; prevent RV failure
first_line_soc: Balloon pulmonary valvuloplasty (BPV) is first-line for moderate-severe valvular PS (gradient ≥40 mmHg). No pharmacologic SoC modifies PS. Symptom management: diuretics for RV failure.
treatment_algorithm: Mild PS: observation. Moderate-severe PS: balloon pulmonary valvuloplasty (excellent results, curative in most). Subvalvular/supravalvular PS: surgery. Associated with Noonan syndrome: treat underlying condition. Post-valvuloplasty PR: manage with monitoring (see PR entry). Medical: diuretics, beta-blockers for RV failure symptoms. No pharmacologic agent reverses PS. Concomitant conditions (Noonan: MEK inhibitor trametinib in trials for PTPN11-mutant tumors — not for cardiac PS per se).
us_prevalence: ~200,000–300,000 US adults with PS (congenital: 7–10% of all CHD; prevalence ~120,000 adults); includes Noonan syndrome-related and acquired cases.
patients_needing_tx: ~100,000–150,000 require intervention or pharmacologic support. Novel pharmacologic: limited — ~50,000.
price: $20,000–$40,000/year for novel anti-stenotic/RV protective agent (e.g., MEK inhibitor for Noonan-associated PS).
registrational_endpoint: Reduction in peak RV-to-PA gradient (mmHg) by catheterization/Doppler echo at 6 months; proportion with gradient reduction ≥20 mmHg.
btd_threshold_vs_soc: ≥40 mmHg reduction in peak gradient + avoidance of BPV/surgery in ≥50% vs. placebo at 12 months.

symptoms: Fatigue, peripheral edema, ascites, diastolic murmur; almost always rheumatic etiology
treatment_goal: Relieve RA-to-RV obstruction; manage systemic venous congestion
first_line_soc: Diuretics for systemic congestion. Definitive: balloon valvuloplasty or surgical repair/replacement for severe TS (gradient >5 mmHg). No pharmacologic SoC modifies TS.
treatment_algorithm: Medical: Diuretics, salt restriction, fluid management for systemic venous congestion. Treat AF with rate control (diltiazem/beta-blocker) + anticoagulation. TS almost always co-exists with MS (rheumatic). Rheumatic fever prevention: penicillin prophylaxis. Interventional: balloon valvuloplasty (limited experience vs. MS; often done simultaneously with PMV). Surgical: tricuspid valve repair (commissurotomy) or replacement — typically at time of mitral surgery. No pharmacologic agent reverses TS.
us_prevalence: Very rare in US; ~30,000–50,000 US patients (mostly immigrant populations with rheumatic heart disease). Severe isolated TS: <10,000.
patients_needing_tx: ~20,000–30,000 require pharmacologic management; definitive intervention: ~5,000–10,000.
price: $20,000–$35,000/year hypothetical anti-rheumatic/anti-fibrotic agent for rheumatic TS.
registrational_endpoint: Mean tricuspid gradient reduction (mmHg) by echo; change in RA area by CMR; HF symptom score (NYHA class) at 12 months.
btd_threshold_vs_soc: ≥3 mmHg mean gradient reduction + NYHA improvement ≥1 class in ≥50% vs. placebo at 12 months; reduction in RA area ≥15% by CMR.

symptoms: Tooth pain, sensitivity, swelling, possible fever or abscess
treatment_goal: Relieve pain, eliminate infection, preserve tooth
first_line_soc: NSAIDs (ibuprofen 400–600 mg QID) ± acetaminophen for pain; antibiotics (amoxicillin 500 mg TID) only if infection with systemic signs present.
treatment_algorithm: Step 1: OTC analgesics (ibuprofen + acetaminophen alternating); Step 2: Amoxicillin 500 mg TID if infection confirmed (ADA guidelines); penicillin allergy: azithromycin/clindamycin; Step 3: Definitive dental treatment (filling, root canal, extraction) to address source; Step 4: Local anesthetic (lidocaine injection) for acute pain management; Step 5: Opioids ONLY for severe refractory pain short-term. No novel pharmacologic approvals.
us_prevalence: ~22 million Americans experience toothache annually; one of the most common reasons for dental/ED visits in the US (CDC).
patients_needing_tx: ~22 million patients requiring pain management; ~4–6 million with infections requiring antibiotics. Estimated ~22M for analgesics, ~5M for antibiotics.
price: $300–$1,500 for a targeted non-opioid dental analgesic; a pulpal pain-specific Nav1.7 blocker could command $2,000–$5,000/course.
registrational_endpoint: Pain NRS reduction and time to meaningful pain relief at 4 hours and 24 hours.
btd_threshold_vs_soc: ≥2-point NRS reduction at 1 hour AND ≥75% responders at 4 hours vs. ibuprofen/acetaminophen.

symptoms: Severe toothache, prolonged pain to temperature, spontaneous pain
treatment_goal: Relieve pulp pain, preserve or remove affected pulp
first_line_soc: NSAIDs (ibuprofen 600 mg QID) for pain relief; definitive treatment is root canal therapy (irreversible pulpitis) or indirect pulp cap with calcium hydroxide/MTA (reversible pulpitis).
treatment_algorithm: Step 1: Pain management: NSAIDs (ibuprofen) ± acetaminophen; Step 2: Reversible pulpitis: indirect pulp cap with calcium hydroxide or MTA; Step 3: Irreversible pulpitis: root canal treatment (endodontic therapy); Step 4: Antibiotics ONLY if systemic infection/abscess present (amoxicillin 500 mg TID x 7 days). Antibiotics do NOT treat pulpitis directly (per ADA/AAE guidelines). No pharmacologic cure.
us_prevalence: ~15–20 million Americans experience acute pulpitis annually; ~16 million root canals performed/year in the US (AAE data).
patients_needing_tx: ~15 million patients requiring pain management; ~5–7 million with irreversible pulpitis needing root canal + pharmacologic support. Estimated ~15M for pain treatment.
price: $500–$1,500 for a novel pulp-capping biological (e.g., BMP-containing material) to avoid root canal.
registrational_endpoint: Pulp vitality preservation rate and pain NRS at Week 4 for pulp-capping agents.
btd_threshold_vs_soc: ≥70% pulp vitality preservation at 12 months vs. ~55% with calcium hydroxide alone.

symptoms: Tooth pain, visible cavities, sensitivity to hot/cold/sweet
treatment_goal: Halt decay, prevent progression, remineralize enamel
first_line_soc: Fluoride (topical fluoride varnish 5%, sodium fluoride toothpaste 5,000 ppm for high-risk patients); silver diamine fluoride (SDF 38%) for arrest of active caries.
treatment_algorithm: Step 1: Fluoride varnish applications (2–4×/year); Step 2: High-concentration fluoride toothpaste (PreviDent 5000); Step 3: Silver diamine fluoride (SDF, Advantage Arrest) – FDA-cleared for caries arrest; Step 4: Xylitol-containing products; Step 5: Antimicrobial chlorhexidine varnish or rinse; Step 6: Restorative dentistry for cavitated lesions. No novel biologic approvals 2024–2025.
us_prevalence: ~92% of US adults aged 20–64 have had caries; ~26% have untreated cavities (~65 million Americans with active untreated caries per CDC).
patients_needing_tx: ~65 million with active untreated caries requiring pharmacologic/professional treatment; millions more use preventive fluoride. Focused pharmacologic market: ~20M moderate-high risk patients.
price: $300–$600/year for a novel remineralizing agent or microbiome-targeting antimicrobial; OTC-adjacent pricing constrains premium.
registrational_endpoint: Caries lesion arrest rate (ICDAS score progression halt or reversal) and new caries increment over 24 months.
btd_threshold_vs_soc: ≥40% reduction in new caries lesion development vs. standard fluoride alone at 24 months.

symptoms: Severe throbbing toothache, swelling, fever, pus, bad taste
treatment_goal: Drain abscess, eliminate infection, relieve pain
first_line_soc: Incision and drainage (I&D) or root canal therapy for source control; amoxicillin 500 mg TID x 5–7 days (or amoxicillin-clavulanate for spreading infection) for bacteremia prevention.
treatment_algorithm: Step 1: Definitive dental procedure (root canal, extraction, I&D); Step 2: Amoxicillin 500 mg PO TID x 5–7 days (first-line per ADA); penicillin allergy: clindamycin 300 mg TID or azithromycin; Step 3: NSAIDs (ibuprofen 600 mg QID) for pain; Step 4: Hospitalization + IV antibiotics (ampicillin-sulbactam or clindamycin IV) for spreading odontogenic infection with airway compromise risk; Step 5: Metronidazole + beta-lactam for polymicrobial infections.
us_prevalence: ~3–5 million dental abscesses treated annually in US EDs/dental offices; ~200,000–300,000 ED visits for dental abscess/year (HCUP data).
patients_needing_tx: ~3–5 million requiring antibiotics annually. Estimated ~4M.
price: $500–$2,000/course for a novel targeted dental antimicrobial; market constrained by generics.
registrational_endpoint: Clinical cure rate (pain resolution, absence of swelling/fever, no re-treatment) at Day 7.
btd_threshold_vs_soc: ≥90% clinical cure at Day 7 vs. ~75–80% with amoxicillin + drainage.

symptoms: Acute tooth pain, bleeding, visible fracture or missing tooth
treatment_goal: Preserve tooth, prevent infection, restore function
first_line_soc: None (surgical/dental procedure is primary treatment); NSAIDs for pain, antibiotics (amoxicillin) if infection present post-trauma.
treatment_algorithm: Primarily procedural: splinting, root canal, reimplantation (avulsed teeth), crown restoration. Pharmacologic adjuncts: NSAIDs (ibuprofen) for pain; amoxicillin 500 mg TID x 7 days if periapical infection; tetracycline-soaked gauze for avulsed tooth storage (reduces external root resorption – off-label). No pharmacologic SoC.
us_prevalence: ~5 million teeth avulsed annually in the US; dental fractures affect ~20–30 million Americans/year (trauma, sports, accidents).
patients_needing_tx: ~5M avulsed tooth cases + ~2M significant fractures requiring antibiotic prophylaxis or pain management annually.
price: $500–$1,500/course for a novel biological tooth-preservation or regeneration product.
registrational_endpoint: Tooth survival rate and absence of periapical pathology at 12 months post-intervention.
btd_threshold_vs_soc: ≥75% tooth survival at 12 months vs. ~50% with standard reimplantation/splinting.

symptoms: Exposed jaw bone, pain, infection, non-healing oral wound
treatment_goal: Promote bone healing, prevent infection, preserve jaw function
first_line_soc: Antibiotics (amoxicillin-clavulanate or penicillin + metronidazole) for infection; chlorhexidine 0.12% rinse; conservative debridement. Drug holiday from bisphosphonate/denosumab if oncologically feasible.
treatment_algorithm: Stage-based approach: Stage 1 (asymptomatic): antibacterial rinse + monitoring; Stage 2 (infected): systemic antibiotics (amoxicillin-clavulanate 875 mg BID) + chlorhexidine; Stage 3 (severe): surgical debridement/sequestrectomy + antibiotics; Experimental: teriparatide (PTH analog) to promote bone healing – off-label; hyperbaric oxygen therapy adjunct; pentoxifylline + tocopherol – some evidence. No FDA-approved specific therapy for MRONJ.
us_prevalence: ~26,000–30,000 US patients with MRONJ annually; prevalence rising with bisphosphonate/denosumab/anti-angiogenic drug use (AAOMS estimates).
patients_needing_tx: Essentially all ~26,000–30,000 patients require pharmacologic treatment. Estimated ~28,000.
price: $15,000–$30,000/treatment course for a targeted bone-healing biologic or PTH-pathway agent.
registrational_endpoint: Complete mucosal healing and cessation of bone exposure at Week 24 (MRONJ stage improvement).
btd_threshold_vs_soc: ≥60% complete healing rate at Week 24 vs. ~30% with standard antibiotics + debridement.

symptoms: Dry socket pain, bleeding, infection, delayed healing after tooth removal
treatment_goal: Relieve pain, prevent and treat infection, promote healing
first_line_soc: Dry socket: zinc oxide-eugenol dressing (Alvogyl) placed in socket; NSAIDs for pain; antibiotics (amoxicillin) only if infection confirmed.
treatment_algorithm: Dry socket (alveolar osteitis): Step 1: Irrigation + Alvogyl medicated dressing; Step 2: NSAIDs (ibuprofen 600 mg QID); Step 3: Systemic antibiotics only if systemic signs of infection; Bleeding: topical tranexamic acid or gel foam; Infection: amoxicillin-clavulanate 875 mg BID x 7 days; Chlorhexidine 0.12% rinse post-extraction prophylaxis. Alvogyl is the primary pharmacologic product.
us_prevalence: ~5 million wisdom tooth extractions annually in the US; dry socket occurs in ~3–4% of extractions (~150,000–200,000 cases/year). Post-extraction infections ~1–2%.
patients_needing_tx: ~300,000–400,000 patients annually requiring pharmacologic management for post-extraction complications. Estimated ~350K.
price: $500–$1,500/treatment course for a novel socket-healing product or antibiotic/anti-inflammatory combination.
registrational_endpoint: Pain NRS and complete socket healing rate at Day 7.
btd_threshold_vs_soc: ≥50% pain reduction vs. standard Alvogyl dressing at Day 7.

symptoms: Painful lip ulcers, cracking, redness, burning sensation
treatment_goal: Heal ulcers, reduce inflammation, prevent recurrence
first_line_soc: Topical corticosteroids (triamcinolone acetonide 0.1% dental paste) for inflammatory lesions; antiviral (acyclovir/valacyclovir) for HSV-related labial ulcers.
treatment_algorithm: Step 1: Identify etiology (HSV, aphthous, angular cheilitis, autoimmune); Step 2: HSV labialis: valacyclovir 2 g BID x 1 day or topical acyclovir 5%; Step 3: Aphthous-type: triamcinolone paste 0.1% (Kenalog) or amlexanox 5% paste (Aphthasol); Step 4: Systemic corticosteroids for severe recurrent cases; Step 5: Angular cheilitis: topical antifungal + antibacterial. No specific 2024–2025 approvals.
us_prevalence: ~50–70 million Americans experience recurrent lip/oral ulcers; HSV-1 labialis affects ~50–80% of adults (seroprevalence); recurrent aphthous ~5–20 million.
patients_needing_tx: ~15–20 million seeking pharmacologic treatment for recurrent or chronic lip ulcers/inflammation. Estimated ~17M.
price: $2,000–$6,000/year for a novel antiviral or topical immunomodulator.
registrational_endpoint: Time to healing (complete epithelialization) and pain NRS at Day 4 for acute episodes; recurrence rate at 6 months.
btd_threshold_vs_soc: ≥30% reduction in healing time vs. standard topical treatment at Day 4.

symptoms: Burning, pain, soreness, or tingling of tongue
treatment_goal: Relieve tongue pain and address underlying etiology
first_line_soc: Identify and treat underlying cause (nutritional deficiency, candidiasis, BMS, geographic tongue); topical analgesics (viscous lidocaine) for symptom relief.
treatment_algorithm: Step 1: B12/folate/iron supplementation if deficient; Step 2: Antifungals (nystatin/fluconazole) if candidal; Step 3: Clonazepam or low-dose TCAs for neuropathic component (BMS-related); Step 4: Viscous lidocaine 2% for acute relief; Step 5: Corticosteroid topical for inflammatory causes. No specific FDA-approved treatment.
us_prevalence: ~5–7 million Americans with chronic tongue discomfort of various etiologies.
patients_needing_tx: ~3–4 million requiring pharmacologic treatment. Estimated ~3.5M.
price: $3,000–$8,000/year for a novel neuropathic oral pain agent.
registrational_endpoint: Pain NRS and quality-of-life (OHIP-14) score improvement at Week 8.
btd_threshold_vs_soc: ≥2-point NRS reduction AND ≥30% OHIP-14 improvement vs. placebo at Week 8.

symptoms: Swollen lips, discomfort, possible itching or warmth
treatment_goal: Reduce swelling, identify and treat underlying cause
first_line_soc: Antihistamines (cetirizine, diphenhydramine) for allergic etiology; corticosteroids (prednisone) for angioedema; epinephrine IM for anaphylaxis.
treatment_algorithm: Step 1: Identify cause (allergic, infectious, traumatic, hereditary angioedema, Crohn-related cheilitis); Step 2: Antihistamines (cetirizine 10 mg/day) for allergic; Step 3: Systemic corticosteroids (prednisone 40 mg/day taper) for inflammatory/immune; Step 4: Epinephrine 0.3 mg IM for anaphylaxis; Step 5: For hereditary angioedema: icatibant (Firazyr), C1-inhibitor concentrate, berotralstat (Orladeyo) for prophylaxis. No SoC specific to lip swelling as a diagnosis.
us_prevalence: Lip swelling is a symptom, not a standalone disease; episodic allergic angioedema affects ~1–3 million Americans/year.
patients_needing_tx: ~1–2 million patients per year with recurrent lip swelling requiring pharmacologic management. Estimated ~1.5M.
price: $3,000–$8,000/year for a novel angioedema prophylactic.
registrational_endpoint: Time to resolution of swelling episode and recurrence rate at 6 months.
btd_threshold_vs_soc: ≥50% reduction in monthly angioedema attack rate vs. placebo at 6 months.

symptoms: Black/white/discolored tongue, altered surface texture
treatment_goal: Identify and treat underlying cause, restore normal tongue appearance
first_line_soc: None (address underlying cause: discontinue causative medications, antifungals for candida, antibiotics if bacterial). Oral hygiene measures.
treatment_algorithm: Black hairy tongue: discontinue bismuth/antibiotics, improve oral hygiene; White tongue (geographic tongue): no pharmacologic treatment needed; Oral hairy leukoplakia (EBV in HIV): acyclovir/valacyclovir or antiretroviral therapy; Oral candidiasis: nystatin suspension 500,000 units QID or clotrimazole troche 10 mg 5×/day. Treatment is etiology-directed.
us_prevalence: ~5–10 million Americans with notable tongue changes annually; most are benign and self-limiting.
patients_needing_tx: ~2–3 million requiring pharmacologic treatment (primarily antifungal). Estimated ~2.5M.
price: $500–$1,500/treatment course for an improved topical antifungal or microbiome-targeting rinse.
registrational_endpoint: Complete resolution of discoloration/lesion at Day 14 (clinical photography-based).
btd_threshold_vs_soc: ≥80% complete resolution at Day 14 vs. ~65% with standard nystatin/hygiene.

symptoms: Pain, swelling, bleeding, laceration of tongue after injury
treatment_goal: Control bleeding, promote healing, prevent infection
first_line_soc: None (wound care and observation primary; suturing for lacerations >1 cm); NSAIDs for pain; antibiotics only if infected.
treatment_algorithm: Most tongue lacerations heal spontaneously without intervention. Management: pressure hemostasis; suturing under local anesthesia for large or gaping wounds; amoxicillin-clavulanate 875 mg BID x 5 days if bite wound infection risk; magic mouthwash for comfort; soft diet. No pharmacologic SoC.
us_prevalence: ~1–2 million tongue trauma cases annually (sports, biting accidents, seizure-related).
patients_needing_tx: ~500,000–800,000 requiring pharmacologic management (antibiotics, pain control). Estimated ~600K.
price: $500–$1,500 for a novel tissue adhesive or growth factor oral gel.
registrational_endpoint: Time to complete wound healing (closure and epithelialization) at Day 7–14.
btd_threshold_vs_soc: ≥30% faster healing vs. standard suturing/conservative care.

symptoms: Painless or painful oral mass, ulceration, bleeding, difficulty eating
treatment_goal: Remove or reduce growth, rule out malignancy
first_line_soc: None (surgical excision is definitive; biopsy required); no pharmacologic first-line SoC for benign oral growths.
treatment_algorithm: Surgical excision under local anesthesia for most benign growths (fibromas, papillomas, pyogenic granulomas, mucoceles). For recurrent/aggressive benign lesions: intralesional corticosteroid injection; For oral papillomas (HPV-related): no pharmacologic treatment beyond excision; For potentially malignant: refer oncology. No pharmacologic SoC for general 'oral growths'.
us_prevalence: ~5–8 million Americans have benign oral lesions requiring evaluation annually; ~50,000 oral cavity cancers diagnosed/year.
patients_needing_tx: ~5M with benign growths needing professional management; limited pharmacologic market. ~200K with potentially malignant lesions needing medical management.
price: $1,000–$3,000 for a topical immunomodulator or biological for recurrent benign oral lesions.
registrational_endpoint: Lesion size reduction ≥50% by clinical measurement at Week 12 and recurrence rate at 6 months.
btd_threshold_vs_soc: ≥70% lesion resolution rate at Week 12 vs. excision alone at 6-month recurrence.

symptoms: Oral manifestations of systemic disease: ulcers, dryness, pigmentation, infections
treatment_goal: Manage underlying systemic disease to resolve oral manifestations
first_line_soc: Treat the underlying systemic condition (e.g., immunosuppressants for autoimmune disease, antiretrovirals for HIV); topical supportive care for oral lesions.
treatment_algorithm: Oral manifestations reflect diverse systemic conditions (HIV/AIDS, Crohn's, Sjögren's, diabetes, hematologic malignancies, autoimmune). Treatment is directed at the underlying disease. Pharmacologic management for oral manifestations: topical corticosteroids for inflammatory lesions; antifungals for immunocompromised patients; pilocarpine/cevimeline for xerostomia in Sjögren's. No unified pharmacologic SoC.
us_prevalence: ~20–30 million Americans with systemic diseases that affect the oral cavity (autoimmune, HIV, diabetes, hematologic conditions).
patients_needing_tx: ~5–10 million with clinically significant oral manifestations of systemic disease requiring pharmacologic management. Estimated ~7M.
price: $5,000–$20,000/year depending on underlying disease category.
registrational_endpoint: Disease-specific; typically oral manifestation severity score improvement at Month 6.
btd_threshold_vs_soc: N/A (heterogeneous category; endpoint varies by underlying systemic disease).

symptoms: Widespread oral pain, ulcers, redness, swallowing difficulty
treatment_goal: Reduce mucosal inflammation, treat underlying cause, relieve pain
first_line_soc: Magic mouthwash (compounded lidocaine/diphenhydramine/antacid) for symptomatic relief; treat underlying cause (antifungals for thrush, antivirals for HSV).
treatment_algorithm: Step 1: Treat underlying cause: nystatin suspension/fluconazole for candidal stomatitis; acyclovir/valacyclovir for herpes; Step 2: Supportive: magic mouthwash, viscous lidocaine 2% for pain; Step 3: For chemotherapy-induced mucositis: palifermin (Kepivance – keratinocyte growth factor, FDA-approved); glutamine supplements; Step 4: Low-level laser therapy; Step 5: For autoimmune stomatitis: systemic corticosteroids. Palifermin approved for chemo-induced oral mucositis.
us_prevalence: ~5–8 million Americans with stomatitis annually; chemotherapy-induced oral mucositis affects ~400,000 cancer patients/year; denture-related stomatitis ~25% of denture wearers (~10M).
patients_needing_tx: ~5M with stomatitis requiring pharmacologic treatment annually. Estimated ~5M.
price: $3,000–$10,000/course for a novel mucosal healing biologic (Kepivance costs ~$12,000/course as reference).
registrational_endpoint: WHO oral mucositis scale or OMAS score at peak (Day 14–21 for chemo-induced); proportion with grade ≥3 mucositis.
btd_threshold_vs_soc: ≥30% absolute reduction in grade ≥3 oral mucositis incidence vs. standard supportive care.

symptoms: Painful recurring mouth ulcers, eating difficulty, oral discomfort
treatment_goal: Shorten ulcer duration, reduce pain, decrease recurrence
first_line_soc: Topical corticosteroids (triamcinolone acetonide 0.1% dental paste; clobetasol gel) or amlexanox 5% paste (Aphthasol) for pain and healing; colchicine or thalidomide for severe recurrent cases.
treatment_algorithm: Step 1: Topical corticosteroids (triamcinolone paste, fluocinonide gel, dexamethasone rinse); Step 2: Amlexanox 5% paste (Aphthasol – FDA-approved); Step 3: Tetracycline mouthwash (250 mg/5 mL QID); Step 4: For major/herpetiform aphthous: colchicine 0.5 mg BID–TID; Step 5: Thalidomide 50–200 mg/day for severe refractory cases (FDA-approved for oral aphthous in HIV patients – thalomid); Step 6: Pentoxifylline, vitamin B12 supplementation. No 2024–2025 specific FDA approvals for RAS.
us_prevalence: ~20–25% of Americans experience aphthous ulcers; ~5–6 million with recurrent aphthous stomatitis requiring regular treatment (NIDCR).
patients_needing_tx: ~4–5 million Americans with frequent recurrence requiring pharmacologic treatment. Estimated ~4.5M.
price: $3,000–$8,000/year for a novel biologic or targeted immunomodulator for RAS.
registrational_endpoint: Ulcer healing time (days to complete epithelialization) and 6-month recurrence rate.
btd_threshold_vs_soc: ≥40% reduction in ulcer healing time AND ≥40% reduction in recurrence frequency vs. placebo at 6 months.

symptoms: Chronic oral burning, altered taste, dry mouth sensation
treatment_goal: Reduce pain intensity, improve quality of life
first_line_soc: Clonazepam 0.5–1 mg (topical oral rinse or systemic), low-dose tricyclic antidepressants (amitriptyline 10–25 mg QHS), or alpha-lipoic acid 600 mg/day (off-label).
treatment_algorithm: Step 1: Rule out secondary causes (nutritional deficiencies, candidiasis, medications); treat underlying cause; Step 2: Clonazepam topical (dissolve and spit) or systemic low-dose; Step 3: Low-dose TCAs (amitriptyline) or SNRIs (duloxetine); Step 4: Alpha-lipoic acid 600 mg/day (evidence modest); Step 5: Capsaicin topical 0.025%; Step 6: Cognitive behavioral therapy. No FDA-approved drug specifically for BMS as of 2025.
us_prevalence: ~700,000–1.3 million Americans affected; predominantly postmenopausal women (NIDCR data).
patients_needing_tx: ~500,000–800,000 seeking pharmacologic treatment; many go undiagnosed or untreated. Estimated ~600K.
price: $8,000–$15,000/year for a novel neuropathic pain agent targeting oral TRPV1/Nav channels.
registrational_endpoint: Change from baseline in oral pain NRS (0–10) at Week 12 using daily electronic diary.
btd_threshold_vs_soc: ≥2-point reduction in mean pain NRS vs. placebo at Week 12 with ≥50% responder rate.

symptoms: Dry mouth, difficulty swallowing/speaking, increased cavities, burning
treatment_goal: Increase salivary flow, relieve dryness, prevent dental complications
first_line_soc: Pilocarpine 5 mg TID (cholinergic agonist) or cevimeline 30 mg TID (Evoxac, FDA-approved for Sjögren's); saliva substitutes (Biotène, OralBalance) for symptom relief.
treatment_algorithm: Step 1: Saliva substitutes and oral moisturizers; Step 2: Pilocarpine HCl 5 mg TID (Salagen – FDA-approved for radiation-induced and Sjögren's xerostomia); Step 3: Cevimeline 30 mg TID (Evoxac – FDA-approved for Sjögren's); Step 4: Identify and modify causative medications; Step 5: High-fluoride toothpaste to prevent caries; Step 6: For radiation-induced: amifostine (Ethyol) IV pre-radiotherapy to protect salivary glands (FDA-approved). Novel: submandibular gland transfer, acupuncture adjuncts.
us_prevalence: ~25–30 million Americans affected; medication-induced xerostomia (800+ drugs) is most common cause; Sjögren's-related ~4 million (Sjögren's Foundation).
patients_needing_tx: ~8–12 million with moderate-severe xerostomia requiring pharmacologic treatment. Estimated ~10M.
price: $2,000–$6,000/year for a novel salivary gland stimulant or biological spray.
registrational_endpoint: Unstimulated whole salivary flow rate (mL/min) and xerostomia questionnaire (XQ) score at Week 12.
btd_threshold_vs_soc: ≥0.1 mL/min increase in salivary flow AND ≥30% XQ improvement vs. placebo at Week 12.

symptoms: Persistent bad breath, unpleasant oral taste
treatment_goal: Eliminate malodor, address underlying oral/systemic cause
first_line_soc: Zinc-containing mouthwash/toothpaste; chlorhexidine 0.2% rinse; tongue scraping (non-pharmacologic); treat underlying periodontal disease or GERD.
treatment_algorithm: Step 1: Oral hygiene optimization + tongue scraping; Step 2: Zinc chloride or zinc acetate mouthwash (neutralizes VSCs); Step 3: Chlorhexidine 0.12–0.2% rinse; Step 4: Treat underlying cause: antibiotics for tonsillar pathology, PPIs for GERD-related halitosis; Step 5: Probiotics (Lactobacillus salivarius) – emerging evidence. No FDA-approved drug specifically for halitosis.
us_prevalence: ~30–50 million Americans experience chronic halitosis; true pathologic halitosis ~15–20 million (AADR estimates).
patients_needing_tx: ~15–20 million Americans seeking active pharmacologic management for persistent halitosis. Estimated ~17M.
price: $300–$800/year for an OTC-adjacent product; prescription-grade novel microbiome product could reach $1,500–$3,000/year.
registrational_endpoint: Change in organoleptic score (Rosenberg scale 0–5) and volatile sulfur compound (VSC) levels at Week 8.
btd_threshold_vs_soc: ≥2-point reduction in organoleptic score AND ≥50% reduction in VSC vs. placebo at Week 8.

symptoms: Misaligned bite, crowded or protruding teeth, jaw pain
treatment_goal: Correct bite alignment, prevent dental and joint complications
first_line_soc: None (orthodontic/surgical treatment; no pharmacologic SoC).
treatment_algorithm: Primarily orthodontic (braces, aligners, Invisalign) or orthognathic surgery for severe skeletal malocclusion. Pharmacologic adjuncts are minimal: NSAIDs for discomfort, possibly prostaglandin analogs being studied to accelerate tooth movement (experimental). No approved pharmacologic SoC.
us_prevalence: ~200 million Americans have some degree of malocclusion; clinically significant malocclusion requiring treatment affects ~45–50 million (ADA/AAO data).
patients_needing_tx: ~4–5 million new patients per year begin orthodontic treatment; pharmacologic acceleration market very early stage. ~4.5M annually.
price: $500–$2,000 as adjunct to accelerate orthodontic tooth movement.
registrational_endpoint: Rate of tooth movement (mm/month) and total treatment duration reduction vs. standard orthodontics alone.
btd_threshold_vs_soc: ≥25% reduction in total orthodontic treatment duration vs. standard orthodontics alone.

symptoms: Device-related discomfort, ulcers, irritation, fit problems
treatment_goal: Relieve appliance-related tissue irritation and inflammation
first_line_soc: None (primarily mechanical adjustment); topical analgesics (benzocaine 20% gel) and chlorhexidine rinse for mucosal inflammation.
treatment_algorithm: Management is primarily mechanical: appliance adjustment, relining, or replacement. Pharmacologic adjuncts: topical benzocaine/lidocaine gel for pain; triamcinolone acetonide 0.1% dental paste (Kenalog in Orabase) for mucosal ulcers; chlorhexidine 0.12% rinse for infection prevention. No pharmacologic SoC per se.
us_prevalence: ~40 million Americans wear dental appliances (dentures, braces, retainers, night guards); appliance-related complications affect ~30–40% of wearers.
patients_needing_tx: ~5–10 million patients with symptomatic appliance-related tissue problems requiring pharmacologic management annually. Estimated ~7M.
price: $200–$500/course for a novel mucosal healing product; market is OTC-dominated.
registrational_endpoint: Complete mucosal healing rate (ulcer/lesion resolution) at Day 14.
btd_threshold_vs_soc: ≥70% complete healing at Day 14 vs. ~45% for standard benzocaine/rinse alone.

symptoms: Bleeding gums, loose teeth, bone loss, bad breath, gum recession
treatment_goal: Halt bone loss, resolve infection, prevent tooth loss
first_line_soc: Scaling and root planing (SRP) + local drug delivery: minocycline microspheres (Arestin) or doxycycline gel (Atridox) as adjuncts to SRP.
treatment_algorithm: Step 1: SRP (non-surgical periodontal therapy); Step 2: Local antimicrobial adjuncts: Arestin (minocycline microspheres), Atridox (doxycycline 10% gel), PerioChip (chlorhexidine chip); Step 3: Systemic antibiotics: metronidazole 500 mg + amoxicillin 500 mg TID x 7–10 days for generalized aggressive periodontitis; Step 4: Sub-antimicrobial doxycycline (Periostat 20 mg BID) as host modulation; Step 5: Periodontal surgery for refractory pocketing; Step 6: Novel: Ublituximab/other biologics in trials; FDA-cleared PerioRx kit. No major 2024–2025 FDA approvals.
us_prevalence: ~47% of US adults aged ≥30 have some periodontitis; ~8% have severe periodontitis (~20 million Americans with moderate-severe disease per CDC).
patients_needing_tx: ~20 million with moderate-severe periodontitis requiring pharmacologic treatment. Estimated ~20M.
price: $3,000–$8,000/year for a novel biologic or sustained-release antimicrobial adjunct.
registrational_endpoint: Change in probing pocket depth (PPD) and clinical attachment level (CAL) at Week 24–36 compared to SRP alone.
btd_threshold_vs_soc: ≥1.5 mm additional PPD reduction vs. SRP + placebo at Week 24.

symptoms: Red swollen bleeding gums, bad breath, gum tenderness
treatment_goal: Resolve gingival inflammation, prevent progression to periodontitis
first_line_soc: Professional scaling and polishing + improved oral hygiene; chlorhexidine 0.12% rinse BID as adjunct pharmacologic agent.
treatment_algorithm: Step 1: Professional teeth cleaning (scaling/polishing); Step 2: Patient education on oral hygiene (brushing 2×/day, flossing); Step 3: Chlorhexidine gluconate 0.12% rinse (Peridex) BID x 2 weeks; Step 4: For necrotizing gingivitis: add metronidazole. Triclosan-containing toothpastes removed from market (FDA 2017). No novel 2024–2025 FDA approvals.
us_prevalence: ~64–77 million Americans have some form of gum disease; gingivitis alone affects ~47% of adults (CDC NHANES data).
patients_needing_tx: ~30–40 million Americans with clinically significant gingivitis requiring professional intervention + pharmacologic adjunct. Estimated ~35M.
price: $500–$1,500/year for a novel gingival antimicrobial/anti-inflammatory (e.g., targeted oral microbiome modulator).
registrational_endpoint: Change in Gingival Index (GI) and bleeding on probing (BOP) score at Week 12.
btd_threshold_vs_soc: ≥35% reduction in BOP vs. standard oral hygiene + placebo rinse at Week 12.

symptoms: Gum inflammation around implant, bone loss, implant mobility
treatment_goal: Halt bone loss, resolve infection, preserve implant
first_line_soc: Mechanical debridement of implant surface (professional cleaning) + local antiseptic (chlorhexidine chip or gel); systemic antibiotics (azithromycin or metronidazole) for peri-implantitis.
treatment_algorithm: Step 1: Non-surgical: debridement + chlorhexidine chip (PerioChip) or topical tetracycline; Step 2: Local drug delivery: minocycline microspheres (Arestin) – off-label for peri-implantitis; Step 3: Systemic antibiotics (metronidazole 500 mg + amoxicillin 500 mg TID x 7–10 days) for peri-implantitis; Step 4: Surgical: resective or regenerative surgery with bone grafts; Step 5: Implant removal if salvage fails. No FDA-approved drug specifically for peri-implantitis.
us_prevalence: ~5–6 million dental implants placed annually; peri-implantitis affects ~20% of implant patients – ~10–12 million cumulative cases in US (AAP/JADA data).
patients_needing_tx: ~3–5 million with active peri-implant disease requiring pharmacologic treatment. Estimated ~4M.
price: $2,000–$5,000/treatment course for a novel sustained-release antimicrobial or anti-inflammatory.
registrational_endpoint: Probing pocket depth reduction and bone level change on radiograph at Week 24.
btd_threshold_vs_soc: ≥2 mm probing depth reduction AND radiographic bone gain vs. placebo/standard debridement at Week 24.

symptoms: Overgrown swollen gums, difficulty chewing, cosmetic concern
treatment_goal: Reduce gingival overgrowth, restore normal gum architecture
first_line_soc: None for pharmacologic; primary management is removal of causative drug (if drug-induced: phenytoin, cyclosporine, calcium channel blockers) + professional oral hygiene.
treatment_algorithm: Step 1: Identify and eliminate causative factor (drug substitution if possible); Step 2: Professional scaling/root planing; Step 3: Improved oral hygiene; Step 4: Surgical gingivectomy for persistent/severe cases; Step 5: Topical or systemic antibiotics (metronidazole, azithromycin) for inflammatory component – off-label. No pharmacologic SoC for drug-induced gingival hyperplasia.
us_prevalence: ~3–5 million Americans with drug-induced gingival hyperplasia (particularly phenytoin, cyclosporine, nifedipine users); ~0.5–1M have clinically significant disease.
patients_needing_tx: ~500,000–800,000 patients with moderate-severe hyperplasia requiring active management. Estimated ~600K.
price: $3,000–$6,000/year for a topical anti-fibrotic or biologic to reduce gingival overgrowth.
registrational_endpoint: Reduction in gingival overgrowth index (GOI) score at Week 24 vs. baseline.
btd_threshold_vs_soc: ≥40% reduction in GOI vs. standard oral hygiene/professional cleaning alone at Week 24.

symptoms: Painful bleeding gums, gray pseudomembrane, fever, foul breath
treatment_goal: Eradicate infection, restore healthy gingival tissue
first_line_soc: Metronidazole (nitroimidazole antibiotic) 250–500 mg TID x 7 days, combined with professional debridement and oral rinse (chlorhexidine 0.12%).
treatment_algorithm: Step 1: Urgent dental debridement under local anesthesia; Step 2: Metronidazole 250–500 mg PO TID x 7 days (or amoxicillin if metronidazole contraindicated); Step 3: Chlorhexidine 0.12% rinse BID; Step 4: Pain control with NSAIDs; Step 5: Address predisposing factors (smoking cessation, HIV testing, nutritional support). No novel 2024–2025 approvals.
us_prevalence: ~150,000–200,000 cases annually in the US; predominantly young adults and immunocompromised patients (CDC/ADA estimates).
patients_needing_tx: ~150,000–200,000 active cases per year requiring pharmacologic treatment; virtually all acute cases need antibiotics. Estimated ~175K.
price: $800–$1,500/course; dental antibiotic market is price-constrained by generics; a targeted novel antimicrobial oral rinse or biologic could command $1,500–$3,000/course.
registrational_endpoint: Clinical resolution rate (composite: absence of pseudomembrane, pain score ≤1/10, cessation of gingival bleeding) at Day 7.
btd_threshold_vs_soc: ≥80% clinical resolution at Day 7 vs. ~65% for standard metronidazole + debridement alone.

symptoms: Jaw pain, clicking, headache, limited mouth opening, muscle tenderness
treatment_goal: Reduce pain, restore jaw function, prevent chronification
first_line_soc: NSAIDs (ibuprofen 400–800 mg TID) or naproxen + occlusal splint (non-pharmacologic); muscle relaxants (cyclobenzaprine 5–10 mg QHS) for myofascial component.
