treatment_algorithm: Step 1: Patient education, soft diet, moist heat/ice; Step 2: NSAIDs + cyclobenzaprine for muscle-predominant TMD; Step 3: Occlusal stabilization splint; Step 4: Physical therapy; Step 5: Intra-articular corticosteroid or hyaluronic acid injection; Step 6: Botulinum toxin A injections for masticatory muscle pain; Step 7: Tricyclic antidepressants (amitriptyline) for chronic pain; Step 8: For inflammatory arthritis subset: DMARDs. No FDA-approved drug specifically for TMD as of 2025.
us_prevalence: ~10–15 million Americans have clinically significant TMDs; TMD affects ~5–12% of the US population (NIH/NIDCR estimates).
patients_needing_tx: ~5–8 million Americans with moderate-severe TMD requiring active pharmacologic management. Estimated ~6.5M.
price: $5,000–$15,000/year for a novel targeted TMJ biologic or neuromodulatory agent.
registrational_endpoint: Change from baseline in jaw pain NRS (0–10) and jaw function limitation (JFLS-8) at Week 12.
btd_threshold_vs_soc: ≥2-point reduction in pain NRS AND ≥30% improvement in JFLS-8 vs. placebo at Week 12.

symptoms: Jaw muscle pain, trigger points, referred headache, stiffness
treatment_goal: Reduce muscle pain and spasm, restore normal jaw function
first_line_soc: Cyclobenzaprine 5–10 mg QHS (muscle relaxant) + NSAIDs; trigger point injections with lidocaine 1% or bupivacaine 0.5%.
treatment_algorithm: Step 1: NSAIDs + muscle relaxants (cyclobenzaprine, diazepam 2.5–5 mg QHS); Step 2: Trigger point injections; Step 3: Botulinum toxin A injections (Botox) into masseter/temporalis/medial pterygoid – off-label but evidence-supported; Step 4: Low-dose TCA (amitriptyline 10–25 mg) for chronic myofascial pain; Step 5: Physical therapy, TENS, dry needling. No FDA-approved specific therapy for TMJ myofascial pain.
us_prevalence: ~8–12 million Americans affected; masticatory muscle pain is the most common form of TMD (NIDCR).
patients_needing_tx: ~4–6 million requiring regular pharmacologic management. Estimated ~5M.
price: $5,000–$12,000/year for a novel CGRP antagonist or selective muscle relaxant targeting jaw muscles.
registrational_endpoint: Change from baseline in jaw pain NRS and pressure pain threshold (algometry) at Week 8.
btd_threshold_vs_soc: ≥2-point NRS pain reduction AND ≥25% improvement in pressure pain threshold vs. placebo at Week 8.

symptoms: Jaw clicking, locking, pain with opening, limited jaw movement
treatment_goal: Restore disc position, relieve pain, improve jaw opening
first_line_soc: NSAIDs (ibuprofen, naproxen) for pain/inflammation + occlusal splint therapy (non-pharmacologic).
treatment_algorithm: Step 1: Soft diet + NSAIDs; Step 2: Stabilization splint (non-pharmacologic); Step 3: Physical therapy; Step 4: Intra-articular corticosteroid or hyaluronic acid injection (Hylan G-F 20 – off-label for TMJ); Step 5: Arthrocentesis with or without lysis and lavage; Step 6: Arthroscopic surgery for refractory disc displacement. No FDA-approved drug for TMJ disc derangement.
us_prevalence: ~10 million Americans with TMJ internal derangements; disc displacement most common, predominantly women 20–40 years (NIH NIDCR).
patients_needing_tx: ~3–5 million with moderate-severe disc derangement requiring active pharmacologic or interventional treatment. Estimated ~4M.
price: $5,000–$12,000/course for an intra-articular biologic (e.g., PRP, hyaluronic acid derivative, IL-1 inhibitor).
registrational_endpoint: Pain NRS (0–10) and maximum interincisal opening at Week 12.
btd_threshold_vs_soc: ≥2-point pain NRS reduction AND ≥5 mm MIO improvement vs. splint/NSAID alone at Week 12.

symptoms: Teeth grinding or clenching, jaw pain, headache, worn teeth
treatment_goal: Reduce grinding frequency, protect teeth, relieve pain
first_line_soc: Occlusal splint/night guard (non-pharmacologic); for pharmacologic adjunct: botulinum toxin A (Botox) injections into masseter/temporalis muscles for severe sleep bruxism.
treatment_algorithm: Step 1: Occlusal splint (dental appliance); Step 2: Botulinum toxin A injections (onabotulinumtoxinA; Botox – used off-label) into masseter/temporalis for refractory cases; Step 3: Cognitive behavioral therapy; Step 4: For secondary bruxism from SSRIs/antipsychotics: consider dose reduction or clonazepam 0.5–1 mg QHS; Step 5: Buspirone has been used off-label. No dedicated FDA-approved drug for bruxism as of 2025.
us_prevalence: ~30–40 million Americans affected by bruxism (sleep and awake); ~8–31% prevalence (AASM/ADA data).
patients_needing_tx: ~5–8 million patients with moderate-severe bruxism seeking pharmacologic treatment (majority managed with dental appliances). Estimated ~6M.
price: $2,500–$5,000/year for an oral medication or $1,500/treatment cycle for botulinum toxin; a novel oral agent could command $4,000–$6,000/year.
registrational_endpoint: Change from baseline in grinding episodes per night (measured by polysomnography or bruxism recording device) at Week 12.
btd_threshold_vs_soc: ≥50% reduction in grinding episodes/night vs. placebo at Week 12.

symptoms: Jaw joint pain, clicking, limited mouth opening, facial pain
treatment_goal: Reduce inflammation, restore pain-free jaw function
first_line_soc: NSAIDs (e.g., ibuprofen 400–800 mg TID or naproxen 500 mg BID) combined with splint therapy; short-course corticosteroid intra-articular injection for flares.
treatment_algorithm: Step 1: NSAIDs (ibuprofen, naproxen) + occlusal splint; Step 2: Intra-articular corticosteroid injection (triamcinolone) for refractory cases; Step 3: For RA-related TMJ: disease-modifying antirheumatic drugs (DMARDs) per rheumatology guidelines (methotrexate, TNF inhibitors such as adalimumab/Humira); Step 4: Surgical intervention (arthroscopy, arthroplasty) for structural damage. No specific 2024–2025 FDA approvals for TMJ arthritis.
us_prevalence: ~10 million Americans affected by TMJ disorders broadly; inflammatory TMJ arthritis (RA, PsA, OA-specific) estimated ~1–2 million patients.
patients_needing_tx: ~600,000–1,000,000 patients with moderate-severe TMJ arthritis requiring pharmacologic therapy beyond OTC analgesics. Estimated ~750K.
price: $18,000–$30,000/year for a targeted biologic (e.g., IL-6 or TNF inhibitor formulated for intra-articular delivery); comparable to RA biologics.
registrational_endpoint: Change from baseline in jaw pain NRS (0–10) and maximum interincisal opening (MIO) at Week 24.
btd_threshold_vs_soc: ≥50% reduction in jaw pain NRS AND ≥5 mm MIO improvement vs. placebo/SoC at Week 24.

symptoms: Progressive facial asymmetry, bite abnormality, jaw pain
treatment_goal: Arrest condylar growth, correct facial deformity
first_line_soc: None (condylectomy is primary treatment when active growth confirmed by bone scan; no pharmacologic SoC).
treatment_algorithm: Step 1: High condylectomy to remove hyperactive cartilage; Step 2: Orthognathic surgery for correction of established deformity; Step 3: Orthodontic treatment pre/post-surgery. Pharmacologic role is limited to post-surgical pain management and bisphosphonates have been studied experimentally to arrest condylar growth (not standard). No approved drug.
us_prevalence: ~20,000–40,000 US cases; rare condition primarily affecting adolescents and young adults.
patients_needing_tx: ~15,000–30,000 patients requiring active treatment (surgical or pharmacologic). Estimated ~20,000.
price: $10,000–$20,000 for a pharmacologic agent to arrest condylar growth (e.g., bisphosphonate or IGF-1 pathway inhibitor).
registrational_endpoint: Cessation of condylar growth measured by SPECT bone scan and facial asymmetry index at 12 months.
btd_threshold_vs_soc: ≥80% rate of growth arrest (SPECT normalization) at 12 months vs. observation/surgery.

symptoms: Facial asymmetry, small jaw, bite problems, limited mouth opening
treatment_goal: Restore jaw symmetry and function through surgical/device intervention
first_line_soc: None (surgical/prosthetic correction; no pharmacologic SoC).
treatment_algorithm: Treatment is primarily surgical: distraction osteogenesis, costochondral rib graft, or total TMJ prosthesis (Biomet TMJ replacement system). Post-surgical orthodontics. Growth hormone supplementation explored for congenital hypoplasia in children but not standard. No pharmacologic SoC.
us_prevalence: ~15,000–30,000 US patients; often congenital (Treacher Collins, hemifacial microsomia) or acquired (trauma, radiation in childhood).
patients_needing_tx: ~10,000–20,000 patients requiring treatment. Estimated ~15,000.
price: $15,000–$25,000 for a biological scaffold or growth factor therapy to promote condylar regeneration.
registrational_endpoint: Mandibular condylar volume change by 3D CT and MIO at 24 months.
btd_threshold_vs_soc: ≥30% improvement in condylar volume and ≥10 mm MIO gain vs. no treatment/standard surgery alone.

symptoms: Inability to open mouth, jaw stiffness, facial asymmetry, chewing difficulty
treatment_goal: Restore jaw mobility and function surgically
first_line_soc: None (surgical release is definitive treatment; no pharmacologic first-line SoC).
treatment_algorithm: Treatment is primarily surgical: gap arthroplasty, interpositional arthroplasty, or total joint replacement. Post-op physiotherapy is mandatory. Pharmacologic adjuncts include post-surgical NSAIDs/corticosteroids to reduce recurrence. No approved pharmacologic SoC.
us_prevalence: ~10,000–20,000 prevalent cases in the US; rare, mostly post-traumatic or post-infectious in origin.
patients_needing_tx: ~10,000–15,000 patients requiring surgical intervention; virtually all cases require treatment. ~12,500 estimated.
price: $15,000–$25,000/treatment course (biologic or small molecule to prevent re-ankylosis post-surgery); orphan-adjacent pricing.
registrational_endpoint: Maximum interincisal opening (MIO) improvement ≥15 mm from baseline at 6 months post-treatment.
btd_threshold_vs_soc: MIO improvement ≥20 mm vs. standard surgical care alone at 6 months.

symptoms: Jaw locked open, inability to close mouth, pain, facial deformity
treatment_goal: Manual reduction of dislocated jaw, prevent recurrence
first_line_soc: None (manual closed reduction is primary treatment); muscle relaxants (diazepam 5–10 mg IV) to facilitate reduction for acute dislocation.
treatment_algorithm: Step 1: Manual reduction (Hippocratic method) under sedation/analgesia; Step 2: Diazepam or midazolam IV for muscle relaxation; Step 3: Elastic bandage/Barton bandage post-reduction for 2–4 weeks; Step 4: For recurrent dislocation: injection of sclerosing agents (sodium tetradecyl sulfate) or autologous blood injection into joint – off-label; Step 5: Surgical eminectomy for chronic recurrent cases. No specific pharmacologic SoC.
us_prevalence: ~50,000–100,000 episodes of acute TMJ dislocation annually in the US; recurrent cases ~10,000–20,000.
patients_needing_tx: ~20,000–30,000 patients with recurrent dislocation needing pharmacologic/procedural prevention. Estimated ~25,000.
price: $2,000–$5,000/procedure for a novel intra-articular sclerosing or stabilizing injection.
registrational_endpoint: Recurrent dislocation rate at 12 months post-treatment.
btd_threshold_vs_soc: ≤10% recurrence rate at 12 months vs. ~50% with conservative management alone.

symptoms: Pimples, blackheads, whiteheads, cysts, oily skin, scarring
treatment_goal: Clear lesions, prevent scarring, maintain remission
first_line_soc: Topical retinoids (tretinoin 0.025–0.1%, adapalene 0.1–0.3%) + topical benzoyl peroxide (BPO 2.5–5%) for mild-moderate acne; oral doxycycline 100 mg BID for moderate-severe inflammatory acne.
treatment_algorithm: Mild: Topical retinoid + BPO; Moderate: add oral doxycycline/minocycline; Severe/nodular: isotretinoin (Accutane/Absorica/Zenatane) 0.5–1 mg/kg/day x 5–6 months (FDA-approved); Women: combined OCP (Estrostep, Ortho Tri-Cyclen, Yaz) or spironolactone 50–150 mg/day (anti-androgen); 2024 approvals: Clascoterone 1% cream (Winlevi – androgen receptor inhibitor, FDA-approved 2020); Trifarotene 0.005% cream (Aklief – RAR-γ agonist); Sarecycline (Seysara – narrow-spectrum tetracycline); Tazarotene foam 0.1% (Fabior). Biologics in trials (IL-17, sebum-targeting).
us_prevalence: ~50 million Americans affected annually; most prevalent skin disease in the US (AAD).
patients_needing_tx: ~20–25 million actively seeking pharmacologic treatment (moderate-severe or scarring acne). Estimated ~22M.
price: $3,000–$8,000/year for a novel biologic or sebum-targeting agent; topical retinoids are generic; novel branded topicals ~$2,000–$4,000/year.
registrational_endpoint: IGA (Investigator Global Assessment) success rate (IGA 0 or 1 with ≥2-grade improvement) AND percent reduction in inflammatory and non-inflammatory lesion count at Week 12.
btd_threshold_vs_soc: ≥45% IGA success at Week 12 vs. ~30% for standard topical retinoid/BPO.

symptoms: Facial redness, visible blood vessels, pimples, flushing, eye irritation
treatment_goal: Control flushing and papulopustules, prevent progression
first_line_soc: Metronidazole 0.75–1% cream/gel BID (MetroGel, MetroCream) or azelaic acid 15% gel (Finacea) for papulopustular rosacea; brimonidine 0.33% gel (Mirvaso) for erythematotelangiectatic rosacea.
treatment_algorithm: ETR: Brimonidine (Mirvaso), oxymetazoline 1% cream (Rhofade – FDA-approved); PPR: Metronidazole, azelaic acid, ivermectin 1% cream (Soolantra – FDA-approved); Oral: doxycycline 40 mg modified-release (Oracea – FDA-approved), low-dose isotretinoin; Ocular rosacea: topical cyclosporine (Restasis), oral doxycycline; 2024–2025: Encarna (topical oxymetazoline formulation updates). Novel: microbiome targeting, TRPV1 antagonists in development.
us_prevalence: ~16 million Americans with rosacea (NRS estimates); ~5–6 million with moderate-severe disease requiring prescription treatment.
patients_needing_tx: ~6–8 million seeking active pharmacologic management. Estimated ~7M.
price: $3,000–$7,000/year for novel topical/systemic agents.
registrational_endpoint: IGA success rate (0 clear/1 almost clear with ≥2-grade improvement) at Week 12; inflammatory lesion count reduction.
btd_threshold_vs_soc: ≥50% IGA success at Week 12 vs. ~35–40% for standard metronidazole/ivermectin.

symptoms: Painful nodules, abscesses, tunnels in armpits/groin, scarring
treatment_goal: Reduce flares, heal nodules, prevent tunneling and scarring
first_line_soc: Topical clindamycin 1% lotion for mild HS (Hurley Stage I); oral doxycycline 100 mg BID for moderate HS (Hurley Stage II–III combination therapy).
treatment_algorithm: Mild: topical clindamycin; Moderate-severe: oral doxycycline or rifampicin 300 mg BID + clindamycin 300 mg BID x 10 weeks; Biologics: adalimumab (Humira – FDA-approved 2015 for HS), secukinumab (Cosentyx – FDA-approved 2023 for HS), bimekizumab (Bimzelx – FDA-approved 2023 for HS); Adjunct: zinc gluconate; intralesional triamcinolone for acute nodules; surgical: wide local excision for Hurley III. 2024: izokibep (IL-17A inhibitor) in Phase III trials; imsidolimab in trials.
us_prevalence: ~1–4% prevalence; ~1–3 million Americans with HS; moderate-severe: ~500,000 (AAD/HS Foundation estimates).
patients_needing_tx: ~500,000–800,000 with moderate-severe HS requiring biologic therapy. Estimated ~600K.
price: $35,000–$55,000/year (comparable to adalimumab/secukinumab WAC pricing).
registrational_endpoint: HiSCR50 (≥50% reduction in abscess + inflammatory nodule count with no increase in abscess/draining tunnel count) at Week 16.
btd_threshold_vs_soc: ≥60% HiSCR50 responders at Week 16 vs. ~45% for adalimumab.

symptoms: Small red bumps around mouth and nose, mild burning, flaking
treatment_goal: Resolve perioral eruption, prevent steroid rebound
first_line_soc: Discontinue topical corticosteroids (key step); topical metronidazole 0.75–1% gel BID or azelaic acid 15% gel BID as first-line pharmacologic therapy.
treatment_algorithm: Step 1: Stop topical corticosteroids (may worsen transiently); Step 2: Topical metronidazole 0.75% or 1% gel; topical erythromycin 2%; Step 3: Azelaic acid 15% gel; Step 4: Oral doxycycline 50–100 mg/day for 6–12 weeks for moderate-severe; Step 5: Oral isotretinoin for refractory cases (off-label). No specific FDA approval for perioral dermatitis.
us_prevalence: ~1–2% prevalence; ~3–5 million Americans affected, predominantly women aged 16–45 (AAD).
patients_needing_tx: ~2–3 million seeking pharmacologic treatment. Estimated ~2.5M.
price: $1,500–$4,000/year for a novel topical agent.
registrational_endpoint: IGA success (0 or 1 with ≥2-grade improvement) at Week 12.
btd_threshold_vs_soc: ≥50% IGA success at Week 12 vs. ~35% for metronidazole gel.

symptoms: Red, warm, swollen, tender spreading skin area, possible fever
treatment_goal: Eradicate bacterial infection, prevent spread and complications
first_line_soc: Cephalexin 500 mg QID x 5–7 days for non-purulent cellulitis (streptococcal); TMP-SMX DS BID or doxycycline 100 mg BID if MRSA suspected (purulent).
treatment_algorithm: Mild (outpatient): cephalexin 500 mg QID or dicloxacillin 500 mg QID; MRSA coverage: TMP-SMX DS BID or doxycycline 100 mg BID; Moderate-severe (IV): cefazolin 1 g TID IV, or vancomycin if MRSA; Penicillin allergy: clindamycin; Recurrent: prophylactic penicillin V 250 mg BID; Novel: omadacycline (Nuzyra), delafloxacin (Baxdela) – newer approvals. Mark borders to track spreading.
us_prevalence: ~14 million cases of cellulitis annually in US; leading skin infection diagnosis (~700,000 hospitalizations/year per HCUP).
patients_needing_tx: ~14 million requiring antibiotic treatment; ~700K requiring IV therapy. Estimated ~14M.
price: $1,500–$4,000/course for a novel oral antibiotic with MRSA/broad-spectrum activity.
registrational_endpoint: Clinical success (cessation of spread + reduction in erythema area ≥20%) at Day 3 and clinical cure at Day 14.
btd_threshold_vs_soc: ≥80% clinical success at Day 3 with ≥85% clinical cure at Day 14 vs. ~70% for cephalexin.

symptoms: Red pus-filled hair follicle bumps, itching, tenderness
treatment_goal: Eliminate causative organism, prevent recurrence
first_line_soc: Topical clindamycin 1% or mupirocin 2% for limited bacterial folliculitis; antifungal (ketoconazole 2% shampoo) for Malassezia folliculitis.
treatment_algorithm: Superficial bacterial: topical mupirocin or clindamycin; Extensive: oral cephalexin 500 mg QID or dicloxacillin; MRSA: TMP-SMX or doxycycline; Malassezia: ketoconazole 2%/selenium sulfide 2.5% shampoo ± oral itraconazole; Pseudomonal (hot tub): ciprofloxacin 500 mg BID if persistent; Eosinophilic folliculitis (HIV): topical permethrin, isotretinoin, or HAART optimization. No novel 2024–2025 approvals.
us_prevalence: ~10–15 million cases annually; very common, often self-limiting.
patients_needing_tx: ~5–8 million requiring pharmacologic treatment. Estimated ~6M.
price: $1,000–$3,000/course for a novel topical antimicrobial.
registrational_endpoint: Lesion count reduction ≥90% and IGA success at Week 4.
btd_threshold_vs_soc: ≥75% lesion clearance at Week 4 vs. ~55% for topical antibiotics alone.

symptoms: Painful pus-filled boils, swelling, surrounding redness, fever
treatment_goal: Drain and eradicate S. aureus infection, prevent spread
first_line_soc: I&D is definitive for furuncles/carbuncles; TMP-SMX DS BID x 5–7 days as adjunct for large lesions, carbuncles, or immunocompromised patients.
treatment_algorithm: Step 1: I&D; Step 2: TMP-SMX DS BID x 5–7 days (improves cure, reduces recurrence per NEJM data); clindamycin 300 mg TID alternative; Step 3: Nasal mupirocin + chlorhexidine decolonization for recurrent furunculosis; Step 4: IV vancomycin for systemic MRSA. No novel approvals.
us_prevalence: ~3–4 million cases annually; MRSA-predominant (60–80% community-acquired MRSA).
patients_needing_tx: ~2–3 million requiring antibiotic adjuvant therapy. Estimated ~2.5M.
price: $1,500–$3,500/course for a novel decolonization regimen or anti-MRSA agent.
registrational_endpoint: Clinical cure and recurrence rate at Day 30.
btd_threshold_vs_soc: ≤5% recurrence at 90 days vs. ~20% with standard I&D + TMP-SMX.

symptoms: Honey-crusted sores, blisters, shallow skin ulcers, itching
treatment_goal: Eradicate bacterial infection, prevent spread and complications
first_line_soc: Mupirocin 2% ointment (Bactroban) TID x 5 days for localized impetigo; topical retapamulin (Altabax) 1% BID x 5 days as alternative.
treatment_algorithm: Localized: mupirocin 2% or retapamulin 1%; ozenoxacin 1% cream (Xepi – FDA-approved 2017); Extensive/systemic: oral cephalexin 250–500 mg QID x 7 days; amoxicillin-clavulanate; MRSA: TMP-SMX or doxycycline; Ecthyma (deeper): oral antibiotics preferred. 2024: No major new approvals.
us_prevalence: ~3–4 million cases annually; most common in children, highly contagious.
patients_needing_tx: ~3–4 million requiring topical or oral antibiotic treatment. Estimated ~3.5M.
price: $1,000–$2,500/course for a novel topical antibacterial.
registrational_endpoint: Clinical success (≥90% lesion clearance) at Day 5–7 for topical; Day 7–10 for oral.
btd_threshold_vs_soc: ≥90% clinical success at Day 5 vs. ~80% for mupirocin.

symptoms: Painful swollen skin lump with pus, warmth, redness, fever
treatment_goal: Drain abscess, eradicate infection, prevent recurrence
first_line_soc: Incision and drainage (I&D) is primary treatment; TMP-SMX DS BID x 5–7 days as adjunct to I&D for moderate-severe abscesses (per NEJM 2017 RCT).
treatment_algorithm: Step 1: I&D is definitive; Step 2: TMP-SMX DS BID x 5–7 days as adjunct (reduces treatment failure and recurrence); Step 3: Clindamycin 300 mg TID as alternative; Step 4: For recurrent furunculosis: decolonization with mupirocin nasal ointment + chlorhexidine body wash; Step 5: IV antibiotics (vancomycin) for severe or systemic infection. No novel 2024–2025 specific approvals.
us_prevalence: ~3–5 million cutaneous abscesses treated annually in US outpatient settings and EDs.
patients_needing_tx: ~2–3 million requiring I&D + antibiotic therapy. Estimated ~2.5M.
price: $1,000–$3,000/course for a novel anti-MRSA topical or targeted bacteriocin.
registrational_endpoint: Clinical cure rate (healed wound, no need for re-drainage, no recurrence) at Day 7 and Day 30.
btd_threshold_vs_soc: ≥85% clinical cure at Day 30 vs. ~75% with I&D + TMP-SMX.

symptoms: Enlarged tender lymph nodes, fever, skin warmth, pain
treatment_goal: Eradicate infection, reduce lymph node swelling
first_line_soc: Antibiotics targeting likely pathogen: amoxicillin-clavulanate 875 mg BID x 10–14 days for acute bacterial lymphadenitis; directed therapy for atypical mycobacteria or specific pathogens.
treatment_algorithm: Acute: amoxicillin-clavulanate or cephalexin; MRSA: TMP-SMX + rifampin; Cat-scratch disease (Bartonella): azithromycin 500 mg Day 1 then 250 mg x 4 days; Atypical mycobacteria: clarithromycin + ethambutol ± rifabutin x 3–6 months; Suppurative: I&D + antibiotics; Necrotizing: IV broad-spectrum antibiotics + surgical debridement. No novel 2024–2025 approvals.
us_prevalence: ~1–2 million acute bacterial lymphadenitis cases annually; cervical lymphadenitis most common in children.
patients_needing_tx: ~1–2 million requiring antibiotic treatment. Estimated ~1.5M.
registrational_endpoint: Lymph node size reduction ≥50% and clinical cure at Day 14.
btd_threshold_vs_soc: ≥85% clinical cure at Day 14 vs. ~70% for standard antibiotic therapy.

symptoms: Reddish-brown scaly patches in skin folds, mild itching
treatment_goal: Clear Corynebacterium minutissimum infection
first_line_soc: Topical erythromycin 2% solution or topical clindamycin 1%; oral erythromycin 250 mg QID x 14 days for extensive disease.
treatment_algorithm: Topical: erythromycin 2% solution, clindamycin 1%, fusidic acid; Systemic: erythromycin 250 mg QID x 14 days or clarithromycin 1 g single dose (high efficacy); Benzoyl peroxide wash 10% as adjunct. No novel 2024–2025 approvals.
us_prevalence: ~3–5 million Americans affected; common in diabetics and obese patients.
patients_needing_tx: ~2–3 million requiring treatment. Estimated ~2.5M.
price: $500–$1,500/course; market is generics-dominated.
registrational_endpoint: Wood's lamp and clinical cure (resolution of coral-red fluorescence and scale) at Day 14.
btd_threshold_vs_soc: ≥90% mycological and clinical cure at Day 14 vs. ~75% for topical erythromycin.

symptoms: Sharply demarcated red raised skin plaque, fever, pain, warmth
treatment_goal: Eradicate streptococcal infection, prevent recurrence and complications
first_line_soc: Penicillin V 500 mg QID x 5–10 days (or IV penicillin G for severe cases); amoxicillin 500 mg TID x 10 days is an alternative.
treatment_algorithm: Step 1: Penicillin V or amoxicillin (Group A Strep is primary etiology); Step 2: Penicillin allergy: clarithromycin 500 mg BID or clindamycin 300 mg QID; Step 3: Severe/systemic: IV penicillin G or ceftriaxone; Step 4: Recurrent erysipelas prophylaxis: penicillin V 250 mg BID or benzathine penicillin G IM q4 weeks; Step 5: Compression therapy for lymphedema underlying factor. No novel 2024–2025 approvals.
us_prevalence: ~1–2 million cases annually in the US; often overlap classified with cellulitis.
patients_needing_tx: ~1–2 million requiring antibiotic treatment annually. Estimated ~1.5M.
price: $1,000–$2,500/course for a novel targeted anti-streptococcal agent.
registrational_endpoint: Clinical cure (complete resolution of erythema, induration, systemic symptoms) at Day 7–10.
btd_threshold_vs_soc: ≥90% clinical cure at Day 7 vs. ~80% with penicillin.

symptoms: Red streaks from wound toward lymph nodes, fever, pain
treatment_goal: Eradicate bacterial infection, prevent systemic spread
first_line_soc: Penicillinase-resistant penicillin (dicloxacillin 500 mg QID x 10 days) or cephalexin 500 mg QID; IV cefazolin for severe cases.
treatment_algorithm: Mild-moderate: cephalexin or dicloxacillin; MRSA: TMP-SMX or vancomycin; Severe/systemic: IV cefazolin, nafcillin, or vancomycin (MRSA); Treat underlying wound/infection source; Elevation and warm compresses. No novel 2024–2025 approvals.
us_prevalence: ~500,000–1,000,000 cases annually; often complicates cellulitis or wound infections.
patients_needing_tx: ~500K–1M requiring antibiotic treatment. Estimated ~750K.
registrational_endpoint: Resolution of lymphangitic streaking and systemic signs at Day 7.
btd_threshold_vs_soc: ≥90% resolution at Day 7 vs. ~80% with standard penicillin/cephalosporin.

symptoms: Rapidly spreading pain, skin necrosis, systemic toxicity, crepitus
treatment_goal: Emergency surgical debridement, broad-spectrum antibiotics, survival
first_line_soc: Broad-spectrum IV antibiotics: vancomycin + piperacillin-tazobactam + clindamycin (to suppress toxin production); emergency surgical debridement is mandatory.
treatment_algorithm: STAT: IV vancomycin + piperacillin-tazobactam (or meropenem) + clindamycin; Surgical: serial debridement within hours; Hyperbaric oxygen therapy (adjunctive, evidence limited); IVIG for streptococcal toxic shock; Hemodynamic support (ICU). No dedicated FDA-approved drug for NSTI.
us_prevalence: ~500–2,000 cases per million; ~9,000–15,000 NSTI cases annually in US (mortality 20–40%).
patients_needing_tx: ~9,000–15,000 requiring aggressive antimicrobial treatment. Estimated ~12,000.
price: $20,000–$50,000/treatment course for a novel toxin-neutralizing biologic (e.g., anti-streptolysin O/S monoclonal antibody).
registrational_endpoint: 28-day mortality and number of debridement surgeries required (composite endpoint).
btd_threshold_vs_soc: ≥30% relative reduction in 28-day mortality vs. standard antibiotics + surgery (from ~25% to ≤17.5%).

symptoms: Widespread blistering, skin peeling, fever, in neonates/infants
treatment_goal: Eradicate S. aureus infection, manage fluid loss, prevent sepsis
first_line_soc: IV nafcillin or oxacillin (adults/older children); IV cloxacillin; MRSA: IV vancomycin; supportive care with wound management and fluid replacement.
treatment_algorithm: Step 1: IV anti-staphylococcal penicillin (nafcillin/oxacillin) or cefazolin for MSSA; Step 2: Vancomycin for MRSA; Step 3: Supportive: wound care, fluid management, temperature regulation; Step 4: No role for systemic corticosteroids (may worsen); Step 5: Transition to oral when improving. No novel 2024–2025 approvals.
us_prevalence: ~300–600 cases annually; predominantly neonates and young children; rare in adults (immunocompromised).
patients_needing_tx: ~300–600 cases all requiring treatment. Estimated ~450.
price: $50,000–$100,000/treatment course for a novel anti-staphylococcal toxin-targeted agent; orphan pricing.
registrational_endpoint: Time to skin re-epithelialization and fever resolution.
btd_threshold_vs_soc: ≥30% faster skin healing vs. standard IV antibiotics + supportive care.

symptoms: Visible blood vessels, redness, purple or red skin discoloration
treatment_goal: Reduce vascular visibility, prevent complications
first_line_soc: Pulsed dye laser (PDL) for most superficial vascular lesions (standard of care, non-pharmacologic); topical timolol or brimonidine for certain vascular conditions.
treatment_algorithm: Depends on lesion type: Spider veins/telangiectasias: PDL, Nd:YAG laser, sclerotherapy; Port-wine stains: PDL series; Venous malformations: sclerotherapy ± sirolimus; Hemangiomas: propranolol; Angiomas: laser. Pharmacologic options limited and type-specific. No universal pharmacologic SoC.
us_prevalence: Extremely common; ~50–80 million Americans have various vascular skin lesions (telangiectasias, spider veins, hemangiomas combined).
patients_needing_tx: ~10–20 million seeking treatment annually (cosmetic + medical). Pharmacologic share modest. Estimated ~15M.
price: $2,000–$6,000/year for a novel topical or systemic anti-angiogenic.
registrational_endpoint: Colorimetric reduction in lesion intensity (ΔE CIELab ≥10) at Month 3.
btd_threshold_vs_soc: ≥50% colorimetric improvement at Month 3 vs. laser alone.

symptoms: Raised itchy painful scar overgrowth beyond wound margin
treatment_goal: Flatten keloid, reduce symptoms, prevent recurrence
first_line_soc: Intralesional triamcinolone acetonide 10–40 mg/mL injections monthly (corticosteroid) as first-line; compression therapy as adjunct.
treatment_algorithm: Step 1: Intralesional corticosteroid (triamcinolone) injections; Step 2: Silicone gel sheets/pressure garments; Step 3: Surgical excision + immediate post-op corticosteroid injection (recurrence risk without adjuvant); Step 4: Radiation therapy post-excision; Step 5: Intralesional fluorouracil 50 mg/mL ± triamcinolone; Step 6: Intralesional cryotherapy; Step 7: Emerging: anti-TGF-β (pirfenidone), IL-13 inhibitors (dupilumab) studied off-label. 2024: FAB117-HC (intralesional anti-inflammatory) in Phase 2.
us_prevalence: ~10 million Americans with keloids; 5–15% of all wounds form keloids in predisposed individuals (AAD); higher prevalence in darker skin types.
patients_needing_tx: ~3–5 million actively seeking treatment for symptomatic keloids. Estimated ~4M.
price: $3,000–$10,000/year for a novel targeted anti-fibrotic agent.
registrational_endpoint: Keloid volume reduction (3D imaging) ≥50% and Patient/Observer Scar Assessment Scale (POSAS) improvement at Month 6.
btd_threshold_vs_soc: ≥60% keloid volume reduction at Month 6 vs. ~40% for triamcinolone injections alone.

symptoms: Rough waxy stuck-on tan-brown skin growths, itching, cosmetic concern
treatment_goal: Remove cosmetically bothersome or symptomatic lesions
first_line_soc: Cryotherapy (liquid nitrogen) or electrodessication for removal; topical hydrogen peroxide 40% solution (Eskata – FDA-approved 2017 for seborrheic keratoses) is only FDA-approved pharmacologic option.
treatment_algorithm: Cryotherapy; curettage; electrodessication; shave excision; Topical: hydrogen peroxide 40% (Eskata – FDA-approved, but removed from market commercially); Topical trichloroacetic acid (off-label). Eskata showed ~24% clearance in trials but was withdrawn commercially.
us_prevalence: ~83 million Americans over age 50 have seborrheic keratoses; nearly universal with aging.
patients_needing_tx: ~15–20 million seeking active removal for cosmetic/symptomatic reasons annually. Estimated ~17M.
price: $500–$2,000/course for a novel topical lesion-clearing agent.
registrational_endpoint: Proportion of target lesions achieving complete clearance at Day 106 (15 weeks).
btd_threshold_vs_soc: ≥45% complete clearance vs. ~4% for vehicle at Day 106.

symptoms: Benign pigmented skin spots, variable size, may be raised
treatment_goal: Monitor for change; remove cosmetically bothersome or suspicious lesions
first_line_soc: None (observation and periodic dermoscopic monitoring; excision for suspicious lesions); no pharmacologic SoC.
treatment_algorithm: Observation with dermoscopy; excision (shave, punch, or elliptical) for cosmetic or suspicious lesions; no pharmacologic treatment indicated for benign nevi.
us_prevalence: Nearly all Americans have moles; ~100 million Americans have >5 moles; clinically significant surveillance ~15–20 million.
patients_needing_tx: ~5–10 million seeking professional evaluation/removal annually; pharmacologic market minimal.
price: $2,000–$5,000 for a topical chemoprevention/depigmentation agent.
registrational_endpoint: Rate of atypical transformation at 3 years vs. observation.
btd_threshold_vs_soc: ≥40% reduction in atypical conversion rate vs. observation.

symptoms: Painless skin lump, slow growth, possible infection/rupture
treatment_goal: Remove cyst, treat infection if present, prevent recurrence
first_line_soc: None (surgical excision is definitive); antibiotics (cephalexin or TMP-SMX) only if secondarily infected.
treatment_algorithm: Surgical excision (complete capsule removal) for epidermal inclusion cysts, pilar cysts; intralesional triamcinolone for inflamed non-ruptured cysts; I&D + antibiotics for infected cysts; no pharmacologic SoC for non-infected cysts.
us_prevalence: ~5–10 million Americans with symptomatic cutaneous cysts; epidermoid cysts most common.
patients_needing_tx: ~3–5 million seeking treatment (excision or pharmacologic) annually. Estimated ~4M.
price: $1,000–$3,000 for intralesional biologic to dissolve cyst.
registrational_endpoint: Complete cyst resolution (clinical + ultrasound confirmation) at Week 12 without surgery.
btd_threshold_vs_soc: ≥50% complete non-surgical resolution at Week 12 vs. ~20% for intralesional triamcinolone.

symptoms: Soft painless fat-filled lump under skin, slow growing
treatment_goal: Remove cosmetically unacceptable or symptomatic lipomas
first_line_soc: None (surgical excision or liposuction is definitive; no pharmacologic SoC); deoxycholic acid (Kybella – FDA-approved for submental fat) used off-label for lipomas by some practitioners.
treatment_algorithm: Primarily surgical: excision under local anesthesia; liposuction for larger lesions. Phosphatidylcholine/deoxycholic acid injections (Kybella off-label) – limited evidence. No FDA-approved pharmacologic treatment for lipomas.
us_prevalence: ~1 in 1000 adults; ~20–30 million Americans with at least one lipoma.
patients_needing_tx: ~2–3 million seeking treatment annually. Estimated ~2.5M.
price: $1,500–$4,000/treatment course for an injectable lipolytic agent.
registrational_endpoint: Lipoma volume reduction ≥75% at Week 12 (ultrasound-measured).
btd_threshold_vs_soc: ≥60% complete resolution at Week 12 vs. ~0% for sham injection.

symptoms: Soft tissue swelling, cysts, recurrent infection, functional impairment
treatment_goal: Reduce lesion size, prevent infection, restore function
first_line_soc: Sirolimus (mTOR inhibitor) – off-label for macrocystic/microcystic LM; sclerotherapy (doxycycline, bleomycin, OK-432) for macrocystic lesions.
treatment_algorithm: Macrocystic: sclerotherapy (doxycycline 10 mg/mL or bleomycin); Sirolimus (rapamycin) off-label – significant evidence in refractory cases; Surgery for localized lesions; Antibiotics for recurrent infections; 2024: Alpelisib (BYLvigo – PIK3CA inhibitor) approved for PIK3CA-related LM (PROS spectrum); sirolimus (Rapamune) off-label use increasing with registry data.
us_prevalence: ~1–2 per 1000 births; ~300,000–600,000 Americans with lymphatic malformations.
patients_needing_tx: ~100,000–200,000 with symptomatic LM requiring treatment. Estimated ~150K.
price: $20,000–$50,000/year for targeted PIK3CA/mTOR inhibitor.
registrational_endpoint: Lesion volume reduction ≥50% by MRI volumetry at Month 6.
btd_threshold_vs_soc: ≥50% volumetric reduction in ≥50% of patients at Month 6 vs. sclerotherapy alone.

symptoms: Small bright red benign skin dots, mostly cosmetic, occasionally bleed
treatment_goal: Remove cosmetically bothersome lesions; monitoring only
first_line_soc: None (electrodessication, laser, or cryotherapy for removal; purely procedural); no pharmacologic SoC.
treatment_algorithm: Elective removal via laser (pulsed dye laser, Nd:YAG), electrodessication, cryotherapy, or shave excision. Extremely common and benign; no pharmacologic intervention needed or approved.
us_prevalence: ~75% of adults over 30 have cherry angiomas (~180 million Americans); predominantly cosmetic concern.
patients_needing_tx: ~5–10 million seeking professional removal treatment annually; cosmetic market only.
price: $500–$1,500/course for a novel topical anti-angiogenic cream.
registrational_endpoint: Complete clearance rate of target cherry angiomas at Week 8 (clinical photography).
btd_threshold_vs_soc: ≥70% complete clearance at Week 8 vs. ~0% for vehicle.

symptoms: Small flesh-colored soft outgrowths on skin, may catch on clothing
treatment_goal: Cosmetic removal; no medical treatment required
first_line_soc: None (snip excision, electrodessication, cryotherapy, ligation for removal; no pharmacologic SoC).
treatment_algorithm: Primarily procedural: cryotherapy, electrodessication, snip excision, ligation. No pharmacologic SoC. OTC products (Compound W Skin Tag Remover, TagAway) have limited evidence.
us_prevalence: ~100 million Americans have skin tags; extremely common, particularly with obesity and older age.
patients_needing_tx: ~10–15 million seeking professional removal annually; primarily cosmetic.
price: $200–$800 for a topical chemical removal agent.
registrational_endpoint: Complete skin tag removal rate at Week 4 (clinical photography).
btd_threshold_vs_soc: ≥80% complete removal at Week 4 vs. ~0% for vehicle.

symptoms: Permanent pink-red skin stain (port-wine stain), cosmetic concern, possible thickening
treatment_goal: Reduce vascular lesion visibility, prevent progressive thickening
first_line_soc: Pulsed dye laser (PDL) is standard treatment (non-pharmacologic); topical rapamycin (sirolimus) 1% under investigation.
treatment_algorithm: Step 1: Pulsed dye laser (585/595 nm) – multiple sessions required; Step 2: Topical sirolimus (mTOR inhibitor) emerging for vascular malformations – off-label; Step 3: Systemic sirolimus for Sturge-Weber syndrome-related extensive lesions; Step 4: Oral propranolol for Sturge-Weber (off-label). 2024: Milademetan and other PIK3CA inhibitors in trials for PIK3CA-related capillary malformations.
us_prevalence: ~300,000–500,000 Americans with capillary malformations (port-wine stains); 3/1000 births.
patients_needing_tx: ~200,000–400,000 patients seeking treatment. Estimated ~300K.
price: $5,000–$15,000/year for topical sirolimus formulation or novel PIK3CA inhibitor.
registrational_endpoint: Percent reduction in colorimetric lesion intensity (L* CIELab score) at Month 6.
btd_threshold_vs_soc: ≥40% reduction in colorimetric intensity vs. laser alone or vehicle at Month 6.

symptoms: Red spider-like vascular mark with central dot on skin
treatment_goal: Cosmetic removal; address underlying cause if present
first_line_soc: None (electrodessication or laser for cosmetic removal; treat underlying liver disease or estrogen excess if present); no pharmacologic SoC.
treatment_algorithm: Spider angiomas (nevus araneus) are benign vascular lesions; treatment is elective. Options: electrodessication, pulsed dye laser, Nd:YAG laser. If due to liver disease: treat underlying hepatic pathology. If due to OCP/pregnancy: typically resolve after hormone normalization. No pharmacologic SoC.
us_prevalence: ~20–30 million Americans with spider angiomas; common in children and adults; higher in liver disease.
patients_needing_tx: ~3–5 million seeking cosmetic removal annually. Pharmacologic market minimal.
price: $500–$1,500 for a topical vascular-targeting agent.
registrational_endpoint: Complete clearance rate at Week 8 by clinical photography and colorimetry.
btd_threshold_vs_soc: ≥70% complete clearance at Week 8 vs. ~0% for vehicle.

symptoms: Firm brown skin bump, dimpling on pinch, mild tenderness, itching
treatment_goal: Relieve symptoms; cosmetic removal if desired
first_line_soc: None (observation or surgical excision if symptomatic/cosmetically bothersome); no pharmacologic SoC.
treatment_algorithm: Observation (spontaneous regression rare but possible); surgical excision (shave or punch excision); liquid nitrogen cryotherapy for superficial lesions; intralesional corticosteroid for pruritic lesions. No pharmacologic SoC.
us_prevalence: ~5–10 million Americans with at least one dermatofibroma; common in young-middle-aged women.
patients_needing_tx: ~1–2 million seeking treatment annually. Estimated ~1.5M.
price: $1,000–$2,500 for a novel intralesional softening agent.
registrational_endpoint: Lesion volume reduction (ultrasound) ≥50% at Week 12.
btd_threshold_vs_soc: ≥60% lesion resolution rate at Week 12 vs. ~20% for intralesional corticosteroid.

symptoms: Irregular large moles, asymmetry, variable color, raised surface
treatment_goal: Monitor for malignant transformation, excise suspicious lesions
first_line_soc: None (surgical excision for suspicious lesions; close monitoring protocol); no pharmacologic SoC.
treatment_algorithm: Management is surveillance-based (dermoscopy, photography, total body skin exam); excision of lesions meeting ABCDE criteria. Topical agents (imiquimod) used off-label for lentigo maligna-type but not standard for atypical nevi. No pharmacologic SoC.
us_prevalence: ~1 in 10 Americans (~33 million) have at least one atypical nevus; dysplastic nevus syndrome: ~2–8 million (SEER/NCI).
patients_needing_tx: ~2–3 million with multiple atypical nevi under surveillance; limited pharmacologic market. Chemoprevention niche ~500K.
price: $2,000–$5,000/year for topical chemoprevention agent.
registrational_endpoint: Rate of conversion to melanoma over 3 years vs. surveillance alone.
btd_threshold_vs_soc: ≥50% relative reduction in malignant transformation rate vs. observation.

symptoms: Rapidly growing red vascular bump, bleeds easily on contact
treatment_goal: Remove lesion, prevent bleeding and recurrence
first_line_soc: Surgical shave excision + electrodessication of base; topical timolol 0.5% gel (off-label) for small superficial lesions.
treatment_algorithm: Small: topical timolol 0.5% gel (significant evidence – off-label); Larger: shave excision + cauterization; curettage + electrodessication; Laser (PDL, Nd:YAG); Recurrent: complete excision with margin. Topical imiquimod also studied. No FDA-approved pharmacologic agent specifically for pyogenic granulomas.
us_prevalence: ~1 million cases annually; common in children and pregnant women.
patients_needing_tx: ~500K–1M requiring treatment. Estimated ~750K.
price: $500–$1,500 for a novel topical anti-angiogenic gel.
registrational_endpoint: Complete clinical resolution at Week 8.
btd_threshold_vs_soc: ≥75% complete resolution at Week 8 vs. ~60% for topical timolol.

symptoms: Rapidly growing bright red skin tumor in infants, ulceration risk
treatment_goal: Accelerate involution, prevent functional impairment or scarring
first_line_soc: Oral propranolol 1–3 mg/kg/day (Hemangeol – FDA-approved 2014 for infantile hemangioma) is first-line for high-risk IH.
treatment_algorithm: First-line: oral propranolol (Hemangeol) 1–3 mg/kg/day x 6 months (FDA-approved); Topical: timolol 0.5% solution (off-label) for superficial lesions; Refractory/steroid era: systemic corticosteroids (now largely replaced by propranolol); Laser (pulsed dye) for residual telangiectasias; Vincristine for refractory cases. 2024: acebutolol studies ongoing.
us_prevalence: ~60,000–100,000 new cases per year in US (4–5% of infants); high-risk (requiring treatment) ~20,000–30,000/year.
patients_needing_tx: ~20,000–30,000 infants/year requiring pharmacologic treatment. Estimated ~25,000.
price: $8,000–$15,000/treatment course; Hemangeol WAC ~$12,000–$15,000/course.
registrational_endpoint: Complete or near-complete hemangioma resolution (IGA 0/1) at Week 24.
btd_threshold_vs_soc: ≥60% complete resolution at Week 24 vs. ~44% for propranolol 3 mg/kg/day.

symptoms: Severe skin blistering from minor trauma, wounds, scarring, pain
treatment_goal: Reduce blister formation, promote wound healing, prevent complications
first_line_soc: Wound care management (non-pharmacologic); systemic retinoids (acitretin) for palmoplantar EB simplex; no pharmacologic cure for underlying genetic defect.
treatment_algorithm: Gene-mutation specific approach: Wound care (Mepitel, Mepilex); Retinoids for EB simplex; Gentamicin 0.1% topical or IV for nonsense-mutation EB (aminoglycoside readthrough); Systemic corticosteroids or colchicine for junctional EB flares; 2023 FDA approval: Beremagene geperpavec (Vyjuvek – COL7A1-encoding HSV-1 gel, first gene therapy for EB, approved 2023 for DEB); Birch bark extract (Filsuvez – oleogel-S10) FDA-approved 2023 for EB wound healing; 2024: ABCD1 and type VII collagen protein replacement in trials.
us_prevalence: ~30,000–50,000 Americans with EB; ~1 in 50,000 births; rare disease.
patients_needing_tx: ~25,000–40,000 requiring active pharmacologic/wound management. Estimated ~35K.
price: $100,000–$500,000/year for gene therapy (Vyjuvek ~$630,000/year as reference); wound biologics ~$20,000–$50,000.
registrational_endpoint: Proportion of target wounds achieving complete closure at Week 12 (VALIDATE/EASE trial design).
btd_threshold_vs_soc: ≥50% complete wound closure at Week 12 vs. ~16% for vehicle (as demonstrated by Vyjuvek).

symptoms: Large tense blisters on skin, intense itching, redness, elderly patients
treatment_goal: Suppress blister formation, achieve disease control
first_line_soc: Topical clobetasol propionate 0.05% applied to entire body surface (Joly 2002 regimen) for localized disease; systemic prednisone 0.5–1 mg/kg/day for widespread disease.
treatment_algorithm: Mild-moderate: topical clobetasol; Moderate-severe: systemic prednisone ± steroid-sparing agents (azathioprine, mycophenolate mofetil, doxycycline + niacinamide); Refractory: rituximab (Rituxan – off-label but evidence-supported), IVIg, omalizumab (off-label); 2024: dupilumab (IL-4Rα) - positive Phase 3 trial data for BP; bertilimumab (anti-IL-5) in trials; bimekizumab studied. Dupilumab FDA approval for BP anticipated 2025.
us_prevalence: ~40,000–60,000 patients in the US; incidence rising with aging population (~7/100,000/year); ~45K prevalent cases.
patients_needing_tx: ~40,000–50,000 requiring pharmacologic treatment. Estimated ~45K.
price: $35,000–$55,000/year (comparable to dupilumab WAC ~$37,000/year).
registrational_endpoint: Disease control rate (no new lesions for ≥2 weeks) at Week 16 and proportion with complete remission off therapy at Week 52.
btd_threshold_vs_soc: ≥75% disease control at Week 16 vs. ~56% for prednisone alone.

symptoms: Painful oral blisters progressing to skin erosions, flaccid blisters
treatment_goal: Achieve complete remission, minimize corticosteroid exposure
first_line_soc: Rituximab (Rituxan – FDA-approved 2018 for moderate-severe PV) 1000 mg IV x 2 doses (Days 1 and 15) as first-line biologic; prednisone 1–1.5 mg/kg/day as initial bridging therapy.
treatment_algorithm: First-line: rituximab + short-course prednisone; Steroid-sparing: mycophenolate mofetil 2–3 g/day, azathioprine; Refractory: IVIG, immunoadsorption; 2023–2024: efgartigimod alfa (Argenx's anti-FcRn) in Phase 3 BALLAD trial; rozanolixizumab; nipocalimab. Rituximab approved 2018; expanding use in all moderate-severe cases.
us_prevalence: ~25,000–30,000 US patients; incidence ~1–5/100,000/year.
patients_needing_tx: ~25,000–30,000 requiring pharmacologic treatment. Estimated ~27K.
price: $40,000–$80,000/year for a novel anti-FcRn or anti-CD20 agent.
registrational_endpoint: Complete remission rate (≥2 months blister-free off corticosteroids) at Week 52 (Pemphigus Disease Area Index = 0).
btd_threshold_vs_soc: ≥45% complete remission at Week 52 vs. ~28% for mycophenolate + prednisone.

symptoms: Intensely itchy blistering rash on elbows/knees/buttocks, celiac disease-associated
treatment_goal: Suppress rash, maintain gluten-free diet adherence
first_line_soc: Dapsone 25–100 mg/day (first-line pharmacologic) + strict gluten-free diet (GFD); GFD is the only disease-modifying treatment.
treatment_algorithm: Step 1: Dapsone 25–100 mg/day (rapid symptom suppression); Step 2: Strict GFD (reduces dapsone requirements and eventually can allow discontinuation); Step 3: Sulfapyridine 1–1.5 g/day if dapsone intolerant; Step 4: Colchicine for dapsone-intolerant cases; Step 5: Novel: nexvax2 (gluten-specific T-cell therapy) in trials for celiac/DH. No FDA-approved agent other than dapsone/GFD.
us_prevalence: ~11–12 per 100,000 US prevalence; ~36,000–40,000 patients in the US.
patients_needing_tx: ~35,000–40,000 requiring pharmacologic treatment. Estimated ~38K.
price: $15,000–$30,000/year for a novel targeted biologic or gluten-neutralizing therapy.
registrational_endpoint: Pruritus NRS and blister count reduction at Week 12; proportion achieving complete skin clearance.
btd_threshold_vs_soc: ≥70% complete clearance at Week 12 vs. ~65% for dapsone.

symptoms: Blistering of oral/conjunctival/genital mucosa, scarring, potential blindness
treatment_goal: Prevent scarring and blindness, suppress mucosal blistering
first_line_soc: Dapsone 50–200 mg/day for mild disease; systemic prednisone 1 mg/kg/day for moderate-severe or ocular involvement.
treatment_algorithm: Mild: dapsone or tetracycline + niacinamide; Moderate-severe: prednisone + cyclophosphamide or mycophenolate mofetil; Ocular: rituximab has shown strong efficacy; IVIG for refractory; 2024: rozanolixizumab (anti-FcRn, neonatal Fc receptor) in trials; nipocalimab in trials for pemphigoid spectrum. No FDA-approved specific therapy beyond systemic corticosteroids.
us_prevalence: ~2–5 per million; ~5,000–15,000 US patients.
patients_needing_tx: ~5,000–12,000 requiring pharmacologic treatment. Estimated ~9,000.
price: $50,000–$120,000/year for a novel anti-FcRn or complement inhibitor.
registrational_endpoint: Complete mucosal remission (no new lesions for ≥8 weeks off steroids) at Week 52.
btd_threshold_vs_soc: ≥50% complete remission off steroids at Week 52 vs. ~25% for standard immunosuppression.

symptoms: Crusted superficial blisters on face/scalp/chest, burning sensation
treatment_goal: Achieve blister-free remission off systemic corticosteroids
first_line_soc: Prednisone 1 mg/kg/day as initial treatment; rituximab (Rituxan) as steroid-sparing agent (off-label but evidence-supported).
treatment_algorithm: Step 1: Systemic prednisone; Step 2: Steroid-sparing: azathioprine 1–3 mg/kg/day, mycophenolate mofetil 2–3 g/day; Step 3: Rituximab (375 mg/m² or 1000 mg x2 doses) – highly effective; Step 4: IVIG for refractory cases; Step 5: Novel: dupilumab (positive case reports); efgartigimod (anti-FcRn) in trials. 2023–2024: rozanolixizumab Phase 3 trials ongoing.
us_prevalence: ~10,000–15,000 US patients (less common than pemphigus vulgaris); ~0.5–1/100,000.
patients_needing_tx: ~10,000–15,000 requiring pharmacologic treatment. Estimated ~12K.
price: $40,000–$80,000/year for a novel biologic (comparable to rituximab).
registrational_endpoint: Complete remission rate (no lesions off systemic treatment for ≥2 months) at Week 52.
btd_threshold_vs_soc: ≥40% complete remission off treatment at Week 52 vs. ~20% for prednisone alone.

symptoms: Trauma-induced blisters, scarring, mucosal involvement, nail loss
treatment_goal: Suppress anti-COL7 autoimmunity, achieve blister-free state
first_line_soc: Dapsone 1–2 mg/kg/day as first-line for the inflammatory variant; colchicine 0.5–1.5 mg/day as alternative.
treatment_algorithm: Inflammatory variant: dapsone or colchicine; Mechanobullous (classic) variant: immunosuppression with cyclosporine, mycophenolate mofetil + prednisone; Refractory: IVIG, rituximab; 2024: dapirolizumab pegol (anti-CD40L) in trials; IVIg combination regimens. Very rare disease; treatment largely extrapolated from pemphigoid.
us_prevalence: ~3,000–5,000 patients in the US; ultra-rare autoimmune blistering disease.
patients_needing_tx: ~3,000–5,000 requiring pharmacologic treatment. Estimated ~4,000.
price: $50,000–$150,000/year for a targeted novel biologic (orphan drug pricing).
registrational_endpoint: Disease control rate (cessation of new blister formation) at Week 16.
btd_threshold_vs_soc: ≥70% disease control at Week 16 vs. ~40% for dapsone/colchicine.

symptoms: Clustered blisters with IgA deposit at dermo-epidermal junction, mucosal lesions
treatment_goal: Suppress IgA-mediated blister formation, achieve remission
first_line_soc: Dapsone 50–200 mg/day (first-line); sulphamethoxypyridazine as alternative.
treatment_algorithm: Step 1: Dapsone; Step 2: Sulphamethoxypyridazine or sulfapyridine; Step 3: Colchicine; Step 4: For refractory: systemic corticosteroids; cyclosporine; IVIg; rituximab; dicloxacillin (for antibiotic-induced cases: stop offending drug). No specific FDA-approved agent for linear IgA disease.
us_prevalence: ~1–2 per million; ~400–700 patients in the US; ultra-rare.
patients_needing_tx: ~400–700 requiring pharmacologic treatment. Estimated ~550.
price: $50,000–$150,000/year (orphan pricing).
registrational_endpoint: Complete blister resolution and IgA deposition clearance at Week 12.
btd_threshold_vs_soc: ≥70% complete clinical remission at Week 12 vs. ~50% for dapsone.

symptoms: Small rough bumps on upper arms/thighs/cheeks, skin redness, sandpaper texture
treatment_goal: Smooth skin texture, reduce redness, cosmetic improvement
first_line_soc: Topical keratolytics: ammonium lactate 12% (Lac-Hydrin) or urea 20–40% lotion; topical retinoids (tretinoin 0.025–0.05%) for textural improvement.
treatment_algorithm: Step 1: Moisturizers with lactic acid or urea; Step 2: Ammonium lactate 12% cream; Step 3: Topical tretinoin or tazarotene; Step 4: Topical salicylic acid; Step 5: Laser therapy (PDL or Nd:YAG for erythema component). No FDA-approved specific therapy for KP.
us_prevalence: ~40% of adults (~100 million Americans) have KP; extremely common, considered a normal variant by many.
patients_needing_tx: ~20–30 million seeking active treatment. Estimated ~25M.
price: $1,000–$3,000/year for a novel topical agent targeting follicular keratinization.
registrational_endpoint: Global assessment score and follicular keratosis count reduction at Week 12.
btd_threshold_vs_soc: ≥50% reduction in follicular keratosis count AND IGA 0/1 in ≥35% at Week 12 vs. vehicle.

symptoms: Dry rough scaly skin, itching, tightness, cracking
treatment_goal: Restore skin barrier, reduce dryness, relieve itching
first_line_soc: Emollients (petroleum jelly, ceramide-containing moisturizers such as CeraVe) and urea-containing lotions (10–20%); ammonium lactate 12% cream (Lac-Hydrin).
treatment_algorithm: Step 1: Moisturizers applied immediately after bathing; Step 2: Ammonium lactate 12% or lactic acid lotion; Step 3: Urea 20–40% cream for thickened areas; Step 4: Mild topical corticosteroids for secondary eczematization; Step 5: Identify underlying cause (hypothyroidism, atopic predisposition, medication). No novel pharmacologic approvals.
us_prevalence: ~40–50 million Americans with medically significant xeroderma (beyond simple dry skin); highest in elderly.
patients_needing_tx: ~15–20 million seeking prescription or professional-grade pharmacologic treatment. Estimated ~17M.
price: $1,000–$3,000/year for a novel skin barrier repair biologic or ceramide formulation.
registrational_endpoint: Transepidermal water loss (TEWL), IGA dryness score, and visual analog scale itch score at Week 4.
btd_threshold_vs_soc: ≥40% reduction in TEWL AND ≥50% itch VAS improvement vs. vehicle at Week 4.

symptoms: Thickened hard skin on feet/hands, pain on pressure, tenderness
treatment_goal: Reduce hyperkeratosis, relieve pain, prevent recurrence
first_line_soc: Salicylic acid 17–40% (topical keratolytic) applied to lesion; mechanical paring/debridement (non-pharmacologic).
treatment_algorithm: Step 1: Offloading (padded insoles) + paring by podiatrist; Step 2: Topical salicylic acid (Compound W, Dr. Scholl's); Step 3: Urea 10–40% cream for hydration/keratolysis; Step 4: Injection of triamcinolone or collagen for deep corns; Step 5: Surgical correction of underlying bony deformity if recurrent. No novel pharmacologic approvals.
us_prevalence: ~50–70 million Americans with calluses/corns; extremely common with aging and ill-fitting footwear.
patients_needing_tx: ~20–30 million seeking active treatment. Estimated ~25M.
price: $200–$800/course; OTC-dominated market.
registrational_endpoint: Lesion thickness reduction (OCT or caliper measurement) and pain NRS at Week 4.
btd_threshold_vs_soc: ≥70% lesion thickness reduction at Week 4 vs. ~50% for salicylic acid 40%.

symptoms: Dry fish-scale skin, itching, skin cracking, painful fissures
treatment_goal: Improve skin hydration, reduce scaling, prevent complications
first_line_soc: Emollients (ammonium lactate 12% cream, Lac-Hydrin) and keratolytics (urea 10–40% cream, salicylic acid) for most forms; retinoids (acitretin 25–50 mg/day) for severe congenital ichthyoses.
treatment_algorithm: Ichthyosis vulgaris: emollients + ammonium lactate; Lamellar ichthyosis/non-bullous CRIE: acitretin or isotretinoin; Epidermolytic ichthyosis: gentle cleansing + emollients ± retinoids; X-linked ichthyosis: topical statins (lovastatin) emerging; 2024: TMB-001 (isotretinoin 0.1% liposomal cream, FDA-approved Oct 2022 for XLOC/LI) – first topical drug specifically for congenital ichthyosis. Gene therapy approaches in early trials.
us_prevalence: ~16,000 patients with severe congenital ichthyosis; common ichthyosis vulgaris ~1 in 250 (~1.3 million Americans).
patients_needing_tx: ~300,000 with forms requiring prescription pharmacologic treatment; ~16K with severe forms. Estimated ~300K.
price: $10,000–$50,000/year for mutation-specific therapies; TMB-001 reference ~$15,000/year.
registrational_endpoint: IGA score improvement and Ichthyosis Area and Severity Index (IASI) change at Week 12.
btd_threshold_vs_soc: ≥2-grade IGA improvement in ≥40% of patients at Week 12 vs. ~20% for vehicle.

symptoms: Thick hard skin on palms/soles, fissuring, pain, disability
treatment_goal: Reduce hyperkeratosis, relieve pain, improve hand/foot function
first_line_soc: Emollients (urea 20–40% cream), mechanical debridement; systemic retinoids (acitretin 10–25 mg/day) for severe hereditary PPK.
treatment_algorithm: Mild-moderate: urea 20–40% cream, salicylic acid 10–20% ointment; Moderate-severe hereditary: acitretin 10–25 mg/day; Epidermolytic PPK: acitretin + emollients; Mutation-directed: emerging gene therapy for ABCA12 and other mutations; 2024: research into topical sirolimus for PIK3CA-related keratodermas. No novel FDA approvals for PPK specifically.
us_prevalence: ~50,000–100,000 Americans with hereditary PPK forms; acquired (callus-related) much more common.
patients_needing_tx: ~30,000–80,000 with hereditary PPK requiring pharmacologic treatment. Estimated ~50K.
price: $15,000–$40,000/year for a targeted mutation-specific therapy.
registrational_endpoint: PPK severity scoring (thickness by OCT, pain NRS) at Week 12.
btd_threshold_vs_soc: ≥50% reduction in keratoderma thickness AND pain NRS ≤3 in ≥40% of patients at Week 12.

symptoms: Intense itching, dry inflamed red patches, skin thickening, sleep disturbance
treatment_goal: Achieve clear/almost clear skin, control itch, prevent flares
first_line_soc: Topical corticosteroids (e.g., triamcinolone 0.1% for moderate AD) as first-line for flares; topical calcineurin inhibitors (tacrolimus 0.03–0.1%, pimecrolimus 1%) as steroid-sparing agents.
treatment_algorithm: Step 1: Emollients (barrier repair) + bathing routines; Step 2: Topical corticosteroids (TCS); Step 3: Topical calcineurin inhibitors (tacrolimus/pimecrolimus); Step 4: Crisaborole 2% ointment (Eucrisa – PDE4 inhibitor, FDA-approved 2016); Tapinarof 1% cream (Vtama – AhR agonist, FDA-approved 2023); Step 5: Dupilumab (Dupixent – IL-4/IL-13 inhibitor, FDA-approved 2017, expanded to ≥6 months 2023) for moderate-severe; Tralokinumab (Adbry – IL-13 inhibitor, FDA-approved 2021); Abrocitinib (Cibinqo – JAK1 inhibitor, FDA-approved 2022); Upadacitinib (Rinvoq – JAK1 inhibitor, FDA-approved 2022); Lebrikizumab (Ebglyss – IL-13 inhibitor, FDA-approved 2023); 2024: amlitelimab (anti-OX40L) in Phase 3; nemolizumab (anti-IL-31Rα, FDA review 2024).
us_prevalence: ~31 million Americans with atopic dermatitis; ~7 million with moderate-severe AD (AAD/NEA).
patients_needing_tx: ~7 million with moderate-severe AD requiring systemic or advanced topical therapy. Estimated ~7M.
price: $35,000–$55,000/year for novel biologics (dupilumab WAC ~$37,000/year).
registrational_endpoint: IGA 0/1 (clear/almost clear) AND EASI-75 (≥75% improvement in Eczema Area and Severity Index) at Week 16.
btd_threshold_vs_soc: ≥50% IGA 0/1 at Week 16 AND EASI-75 in ≥65% vs. ~18% IGA 0/1 for dupilumab placebo.

symptoms: Skin inflammation: redness, itching, swelling, oozing or scaling
treatment_goal: Classify dermatitis type, apply appropriate targeted therapy
first_line_soc: Topical corticosteroids as general first-line across dermatitis subtypes; treatment is subtype-specific.
treatment_algorithm: Dermatitis is a general category encompassing atopic, contact, seborrheic, nummular, stasis dermatitis. Standard approach: identify subtype → apply subtype-specific therapy. General: topical corticosteroids for inflammation; emollients for barrier repair; antihistamines for pruritus. No unified pharmacologic SoC for 'dermatitis' as a single entity.
us_prevalence: ~50–70 million Americans experience dermatitis of any type annually (all subtypes combined).
patients_needing_tx: ~25–35 million requiring pharmacologic treatment across all dermatitis subtypes. Estimated ~30M.
price: $3,000–$10,000/year depending on subtype severity.
registrational_endpoint: IGA 0/1 and ≥4-point DLQI improvement at Week 12 (specific endpoint varies by subtype).
btd_threshold_vs_soc: ≥50% IGA 0/1 at Week 12 vs. ~30% for standard TCS/emollient regimen.

symptoms: Blistering, scaling, cracking, itching of hands and feet
treatment_goal: Clear skin, prevent fissuring, maintain hand function
first_line_soc: Potent topical corticosteroids (clobetasol 0.05% ointment BID) for acute flares; Alitretinoin 10–30 mg/day (Toctino – oral retinoid, approved in Europe/Canada for chronic hand eczema, not FDA-approved in US).
treatment_algorithm: Step 1: Potent topical corticosteroids + emollients; Step 2: Topical tacrolimus 0.1% or pimecrolimus; Step 3: Alitretinoin (Europe/Canada approved for chronic hand eczema – FDA application pending); Step 4: Dupilumab for refractory hand eczema; Step 5: Phototherapy (PUVA, NB-UVB); Step 6: Systemic immunosuppressants (cyclosporine, methotrexate) for severe. 2024: delgocitinib 20 mg/g cream (Corectim/LEO 130088 – JAK inhibitor, approved Japan/EU 2020/2021) – FDA review expected 2025.
us_prevalence: ~10–15 million Americans with chronic hand/foot dermatitis (CHE); ~1–5% adult prevalence (ACAAI/AAD).
patients_needing_tx: ~3–5 million with moderate-severe CHE needing systemic or advanced treatment. Estimated ~4M.
price: $20,000–$40,000/year for a novel JAK inhibitor or biologic specifically for CHE.
registrational_endpoint: IGA-CHE 0/1 at Week 16 (as used in Phase 3 delgocitinib cream trials).
btd_threshold_vs_soc: ≥35% IGA-CHE 0/1 at Week 16 vs. ~5% for vehicle.

symptoms: Thick leathery itchy skin patches from chronic scratching, intense pruritus
treatment_goal: Break itch-scratch cycle, restore normal skin texture
first_line_soc: High-potency topical corticosteroids (clobetasol 0.05% BID under occlusion) + topical tacrolimus 0.1% for maintenance; sedating antihistamines (hydroxyzine 25 mg QHS) for nocturnal itch.
treatment_algorithm: Step 1: High-potency TCS under occlusion; Step 2: Topical tacrolimus/pimecrolimus; Step 3: Hydroxyzine or doxepin for pruritus; Step 4: Intralesional triamcinolone for localized resistant lesions; Step 5: Gabapentin or pregabalin for neuropathic itch component; Step 6: Behavioral therapy; Step 7: Novel: nemolizumab (anti-IL-31Rα) and dupilumab for refractory cases. No specific FDA approval.
us_prevalence: ~5–10 million Americans with lichen simplex chronicus.
patients_needing_tx: ~3–5 million requiring pharmacologic treatment. Estimated ~4M.
price: $10,000–$30,000/year for a novel anti-itch biologic.
registrational_endpoint: Peak pruritus NRS and IGA at Week 12.
btd_threshold_vs_soc: ≥4-point peak pruritus NRS reduction AND IGA 0/1 in ≥40% at Week 12 vs. TCS/antihistamine alone.

symptoms: Itchy red rash at site of allergen/irritant exposure, blistering, weeping
treatment_goal: Identify and avoid trigger, suppress allergic inflammation
first_line_soc: Topical corticosteroids (mid-potency: triamcinolone 0.1% for body; high-potency: clobetasol 0.05% for hands/feet) for acute flare; systemic prednisone 40–60 mg/day taper for severe/widespread contact dermatitis.
treatment_algorithm: Step 1: Identify and eliminate allergen/irritant (patch testing for allergic CD); Step 2: Topical corticosteroids; Step 3: Systemic prednisone 40 mg/day x 5–7 days taper for severe cases; Step 4: Antihistamines for sedation/itch; Step 5: Protopic/Elidel for maintenance; Step 6: Novel biologics (dupilumab) for chronic refractory ACD. No specific FDA approval for CD but dupilumab covers allergic/atopic overlap.
us_prevalence: ~20–25 million Americans annually with contact dermatitis (allergic + irritant); ~15% of general population annually (ACAAI).
patients_needing_tx: ~10–15 million seeking pharmacologic treatment annually. Estimated ~12M.
price: $3,000–$10,000/year for a novel topical barrier-targeted or immune-modulating agent.
registrational_endpoint: IGA score improvement and EASI change at Week 4 for topical agents.
btd_threshold_vs_soc: ≥65% IGA 0/1 at Week 4 vs. ~40% for TCS alone.

symptoms: Greasy yellow-white scales on scalp/face, redness, itching
treatment_goal: Control flaking and inflammation, maintain remission
first_line_soc: Ketoconazole 2% shampoo/cream (Nizoral) or zinc pyrithione shampoos for scalp; ketoconazole 2% cream for face/body.
treatment_algorithm: Scalp: ketoconazole 2% shampoo, selenium sulfide 2.5%, zinc pyrithione, ciclopirox 1% shampoo; Face/body: ketoconazole 2% cream, desonide 0.05% (mild TCS), tacrolimus/pimecrolimus for maintenance; Severe: itraconazole 200 mg/day x 1–2 weeks; 2023–2024: tapinarof (Vtama) being studied for seborrheic dermatitis; roflumilast foam 0.3% (Zoryve foam – FDA-approved 2023 for seborrheic dermatitis in adults ≥9 years old, specific indication).
us_prevalence: ~11% prevalence (~36 million Americans); ubiquitous in immunocompromised (HIV: 36–83%).
patients_needing_tx: ~15–20 million seeking active pharmacologic treatment. Estimated ~17M.
price: $2,000–$5,000/year for novel prescription-strength agent.
registrational_endpoint: Scaliness, erythema, and pruritus composite score (Seborrheic Dermatitis Area and Severity Index – SDASI) at Week 8; IGA success.
btd_threshold_vs_soc: ≥50% IGA success at Week 8 vs. ~38% for roflumilast foam (Zoryve trial data).

symptoms: Leg swelling, itchy brown discoloration, weeping skin, varicose veins
treatment_goal: Reduce venous hypertension, heal skin, prevent ulceration
first_line_soc: Compression therapy (30–40 mmHg stockings) is cornerstone; topical corticosteroids (triamcinolone 0.1% or betamethasone) for acute dermatitis component.
treatment_algorithm: Step 1: Compression stockings 20–40 mmHg (cornerstone, non-pharmacologic); Step 2: Topical corticosteroids for flares; Step 3: Emollients; Step 4: Wound care for ulceration; Step 5: Treat underlying venous insufficiency (endovenous laser ablation, sclerotherapy); Step 6: Pentoxifylline 400 mg TID for venous leg ulcers (off-label, evidence-supported). No FDA-approved pharmacologic SoC for stasis dermatitis specifically.
us_prevalence: ~7–10 million Americans with chronic venous insufficiency with stasis dermatitis.
patients_needing_tx: ~3–5 million with active stasis dermatitis requiring pharmacologic treatment. Estimated ~4M.
price: $5,000–$15,000/year for a novel barrier/venous-targeting agent.
registrational_endpoint: Venous Clinical Severity Score (VCSS) and IGA improvement at Week 12.
btd_threshold_vs_soc: ≥40% VCSS improvement AND IGA 0/1 in ≥40% at Week 12 vs. compression alone.

symptoms: Coin-shaped itchy skin plaques, oozing, crusting, widespread distribution
treatment_goal: Clear coin-shaped lesions, reduce itch, prevent recurrence
first_line_soc: Mid-to-high potency topical corticosteroids (betamethasone valerate 0.1% or triamcinolone 0.1%) BID + emollients.
treatment_algorithm: Step 1: High-potency topical corticosteroids; Step 2: Oral antibiotics if bacterial superinfection suspected; Step 3: Antihistamines for pruritus; Step 4: Dupilumab for refractory widespread disease; Step 5: Phototherapy (NB-UVB) for extensive disease. No specific FDA approval for nummular dermatitis.
us_prevalence: ~2–3 million Americans; bimodal age distribution (young adults and elderly).
patients_needing_tx: ~1–2 million requiring pharmacologic treatment. Estimated ~1.5M.
price: $5,000–$20,000/year for a novel topical or systemic agent.
registrational_endpoint: IGA 0/1 and lesion count reduction at Week 12.
btd_threshold_vs_soc: ≥50% IGA 0/1 AND ≥70% lesion clearance at Week 12 vs. ~30% for TCS alone.

symptoms: Widespread red scaling skin, intense itching, fever, chills
treatment_goal: Identify cause, control inflammation, prevent complications
first_line_soc: Hospitalization with supportive care; treat underlying cause (psoriasis, eczema, drug reaction, lymphoma); topical emollients and mild corticosteroids; systemic retinoids (acitretin) for psoriatic erythroderma.
treatment_algorithm: Step 1: Hospitalization; fluid/electrolyte management; temperature control; Step 2: Identify etiology: psoriatic erythroderma: cyclosporine 3–5 mg/kg/day (rapid onset) or biologics (infliximab); Eczematous: systemic corticosteroids + dupilumab; Drug-induced: discontinue offending drug; CTCL-related: PUVA, IFN, retinoids; Step 3: Topical emollients; Step 4: IV antibiotics if superinfection. No single FDA-approved therapy.
us_prevalence: ~3–10 per 100,000; ~10,000–30,000 hospitalizations annually for erythroderma in US.
patients_needing_tx: ~10,000–30,000 cases requiring pharmacologic treatment annually. Estimated ~20K.
price: $20,000–$50,000/year for a novel immunomodulator effective across erythroderma etiologies.
registrational_endpoint: BSA involvement reduction ≥50% and IGA improvement at Week 12.
btd_threshold_vs_soc: ≥70% BSA reduction at Week 12 vs. ~50% for cyclosporine/acitretin.

symptoms: White oral patches, red skin rash in body folds, burning
treatment_goal: Eradicate Candida infection, prevent recurrence
first_line_soc: Topical nystatin suspension 100,000 units/mL QID x 7–14 days or clotrimazole troches 10 mg 5×/day for oral candidiasis; topical miconazole/clotrimazole for cutaneous candidiasis.
treatment_algorithm: Oral/oropharyngeal: clotrimazole troches or nystatin suspension first-line; systemic: fluconazole 150–200 mg/day for resistant/immunocompromised; Skin folds: topical clotrimazole, miconazole 2%, ketoconazole 2%; Recurrent vulvovaginal: fluconazole 150 mg PO every 3 days x 3 doses, then weekly x 6 months; Invasive/immunocompromised: echinocandins (caspofungin, micafungin) IV; Ibrexafungerp (Brexafemme – FDA-approved 2021 for vulvovaginal candidiasis); Oteseconazole (Vivjoa – FDA-approved 2022 for RVVC). 2024: Ibrexafungerp tablet approved for invasive candidiasis extension.
us_prevalence: ~75% of women have at least 1 episode of VVC; ~10 million cases annually; recurrent VVC ~5% of women (~7 million).
patients_needing_tx: ~15–20 million requiring active pharmacologic treatment annually. Estimated ~17M.
price: $500–$3,000/year for a novel antifungal; Oteseconazole ~$4,500/year as reference.
registrational_endpoint: Clinical cure (resolution of signs and symptoms) and mycological cure at Day 14 for acute episode; recurrence rate at 6 months for RVVC.
btd_threshold_vs_soc: ≤5% recurrence at 6 months vs. ~50% for fluconazole 150 mg single dose.

symptoms: Redness and rawness in skin folds, burning, itching, moisture
treatment_goal: Eliminate fungal/bacterial colonization, restore skin barrier
first_line_soc: Topical miconazole 2% or clotrimazole 1% for Candida-predominant intertrigo; absorptive powder (zinc oxide or talc) and moisture control.
treatment_algorithm: Step 1: Keep skin dry (absorbent cloths, moisture-wicking garments); Step 2: Topical antifungal (miconazole 2% or clotrimazole 1%) if Candida; Step 3: Topical antibacterial if bacteria predominant (mupirocin or gentamicin); Step 4: Mild topical corticosteroid for inflammation; Step 5: Zinc oxide paste barrier; Step 6: Oral fluconazole for recurrent/resistant Candida intertrigo. No specific FDA-approved treatment.
us_prevalence: ~15–20 million Americans with intertrigo; very common in obese, diabetic, and elderly patients.
patients_needing_tx: ~8–12 million seeking pharmacologic treatment. Estimated ~10M.
price: $500–$2,000/course for a novel antifungal-antibacterial combination.
registrational_endpoint: IGA success and Candida/bacterial clearance at Day 14.
btd_threshold_vs_soc: ≥70% IGA 0/1 AND mycological cure at Day 14 vs. ~50% for miconazole alone.

symptoms: Itchy, scaly, cracked skin between toes and on soles
treatment_goal: Eradicate foot dermatophyte, prevent recurrence
first_line_soc: Topical terbinafine 1% cream or spray (Lamisil AT) BID x 1–2 weeks; or topical azoles (clotrimazole, miconazole) BID x 4 weeks.
treatment_algorithm: Topical: terbinafine 1% (preferred), butenafine, naftifine 2% gel (Naftin – approved for tinea pedis); Azoles: clotrimazole, miconazole, econazole, luliconazole; Severe/moccasin type: oral terbinafine 250 mg/day x 2–6 weeks or itraconazole 200 mg/day x 1 week; Preventive: antifungal powder, dry footwear. No novel 2024–2025 approvals.
us_prevalence: ~15–26 million active cases annually; up to 70 million Americans affected at some point per year.
patients_needing_tx: ~10–15 million requiring pharmacologic treatment annually. Estimated ~12M.
registrational_endpoint: Complete cure (mycological cure + effective treatment defined as IGA 0 or 1) at Day 42.
btd_threshold_vs_soc: ≥50% complete cure at Day 42 vs. ~35–40% for terbinafine cream.

symptoms: Itchy red ring-shaped rash in groin and inner thighs
treatment_goal: Eradicate dermatophyte, resolve groin rash
first_line_soc: Topical terbinafine 1% cream (Lamisil AT) or clotrimazole 1% BID x 2–4 weeks; keep area dry.
treatment_algorithm: Topical: terbinafine 1%, clotrimazole 1%, miconazole 2%, econazole 1%, luliconazole 1% (Luzu – FDA approved for tinea cruris); Extensive/resistant cases: oral terbinafine 250 mg/day x 2 weeks or itraconazole 200 mg/day x 1 week; absorbent powder adjunct; loose clothing. No major new 2024–2025 approvals.
us_prevalence: ~10–20 million cases annually; predominantly adult males.
patients_needing_tx: ~8–15 million requiring topical antifungal treatment; most mild cases self-treat OTC. Estimated ~10M pharmacologically treated.
price: $500–$1,500/course for a novel topical antifungal.
registrational_endpoint: Complete cure (KOH negative + IGA 0) at Day 28.
btd_threshold_vs_soc: ≥55% complete cure at Day 28 vs. ~40% for standard topical terbinafine.

symptoms: Ring-shaped scaly red skin patches, central clearing, itching
treatment_goal: Clear dermatophyte infection, eliminate itching
first_line_soc: Topical terbinafine 1% cream (Lamisil AT) or topical azoles (clotrimazole 1%, miconazole 2%) BID x 2–4 weeks.
treatment_algorithm: Localized: topical terbinafine 1% or azoles; Extensive/resistant: oral terbinafine 250 mg/day x 2–4 weeks; itraconazole 200–400 mg/day x 1 week; fluconazole 150 mg weekly x 4 weeks; 2024: efinaconazole 10% solution (Jublia – onychomycosis) and luliconazole 1% cream (Luzu – approved for tinea corporis/pedis/cruris). No major new 2024–2025 approvals specifically.
us_prevalence: ~3–5 million cases annually.
patients_needing_tx: ~2–4 million requiring pharmacologic treatment. Estimated ~3M.
registrational_endpoint: Complete cure (KOH negative + IGA 0) at Day 28.
btd_threshold_vs_soc: ≥60% complete cure at Day 28 vs. ~45% for topical terbinafine.

symptoms: Discolored scaly patches on trunk, shoulders; itch in heat
treatment_goal: Eradicate Malassezia overgrowth, restore even skin tone
first_line_soc: Topical selenium sulfide 2.5% shampoo or ketoconazole 2% shampoo applied as body wash x 5–10 days; or topical azoles (clotrimazole, miconazole) BID x 2–4 weeks.
treatment_algorithm: Topical: selenium sulfide 2.5%, ketoconazole 2% shampoo/foam, zinc pyrithione; Azoles: clotrimazole, econazole, miconazole; Systemic (extensive/recurrent): single-dose fluconazole 400 mg or itraconazole 200 mg/day x 5–7 days; prophylaxis: monthly fluconazole 300 mg. No novel 2024–2025 approvals.
us_prevalence: ~5–8 million cases annually in the US; more common in warm climates and teens/young adults.
patients_needing_tx: ~4–6 million requiring pharmacologic treatment. Estimated ~5M.
registrational_endpoint: Mycological cure rate (KOH negative) and IGA improvement at Week 4.
btd_threshold_vs_soc: ≥80% mycological cure at Week 4 vs. ~65–70% for selenium sulfide.

symptoms: Itchy vesicular rash distant from primary fungal infection site
treatment_goal: Treat primary dermatophyte infection; id reaction resolves spontaneously
first_line_soc: Treat the primary fungal infection (oral terbinafine or itraconazole); antihistamines for pruritic id reaction.
treatment_algorithm: Treat underlying fungal infection (tinea pedis, tinea capitis, etc.) with systemic antifungals (terbinafine 250 mg/day or itraconazole 100 mg BID); topical corticosteroids for pruritic vesicular reaction; antihistamines; id reaction resolves once primary infection is treated. No specific therapy for id reaction itself.
us_prevalence: ~500,000–1,000,000 cases annually; occurs in ~5–20% of patients with tinea infections.
patients_needing_tx: ~500K–1M requiring treatment (systemic antifungal for primary infection). Estimated ~750K.
registrational_endpoint: Clearance of primary fungal infection AND resolution of id reaction at Week 4.
btd_threshold_vs_soc: ≥90% id reaction resolution at Week 4 coincident with primary fungal clearance.

symptoms: Patchy scalp hair loss, scaly dandruff-like patches, inflamed boggy kerion
treatment_goal: Eradicate scalp dermatophyte, prevent permanent alopecia
first_line_soc: Oral griseofulvin (microsize 20–25 mg/kg/day x 6–12 weeks) or terbinafine 250 mg/day x 2–4 weeks (preferred for Trichophyton infections); adjunct: selenium sulfide or ketoconazole shampoo to reduce shedding.
