treatment_algorithm: Terbinafine (drug of choice for Trichophyton spp.); Griseofulvin (still preferred for Microsporum in children); Itraconazole 5 mg/kg/day x 2–6 weeks as alternative; Systemic corticosteroids for kerion (prednisolone 1 mg/kg/day x 2–3 weeks); Antifungal shampoo adjunct. No novel 2024–2025 FDA approvals.
us_prevalence: ~300,000–500,000 cases annually; predominantly children, urban African-American communities.
patients_needing_tx: ~300K–500K requiring systemic antifungal. Estimated ~400K.
registrational_endpoint: Complete cure (mycological + clinical) at Week 8.
btd_threshold_vs_soc: ≥70% complete cure at Week 8 vs. ~55–65% for terbinafine.

symptoms: Fungal infection of beard area, pustular folliculitis, inflammation, crust
treatment_goal: Eradicate dermatophyte, resolve follicular infection
first_line_soc: Oral terbinafine 250 mg/day x 4 weeks (systemic therapy required for deep fungal folliculitis).
treatment_algorithm: Oral terbinafine 250 mg/day x 4 weeks; itraconazole 100 mg BID x 2–4 weeks as alternative; topical antifungals (clotrimazole) insufficient for deep follicular involvement but used as adjunct; gentle cleansing. No novel 2024–2025 approvals.
us_prevalence: ~100,000–300,000 cases annually; predominantly affecting farmers and manual workers.
patients_needing_tx: ~100K–300K requiring systemic antifungal. Estimated ~200K.
registrational_endpoint: Mycological cure and clinical cure at Week 4.
btd_threshold_vs_soc: ≥90% mycological cure at Week 4 vs. ~80% for terbinafine.

symptoms: Diffuse or patterned hair loss, scalp visibility, thinning
treatment_goal: Halt hair loss progression, stimulate regrowth
first_line_soc: Minoxidil 2% or 5% topical solution/foam (OTC; Rogaine) applied daily; for androgenetic alopecia in men: finasteride 1 mg/day oral (Propecia).
treatment_algorithm: Androgenetic alopecia (AGA): topical minoxidil (2–5%), oral finasteride 1 mg/day (men), dutasteride 0.5 mg/day (off-label); for women: spironolactone 100–200 mg/day (off-label), low-dose oral minoxidil 0.25–1.25 mg/day; 2022–2023: oral finasteride/minoxidil combo approved; For diffuse effluvium: treat underlying cause; JAK inhibitors (ruxolitinib topical) emerging. No major 2024–2025 AGA-specific FDA approvals.
us_prevalence: ~80 million Americans affected by androgenetic alopecia (50M men, 30M women); all-cause alopecia ~100M.
patients_needing_tx: ~25–40 million seeking pharmacologic treatment. Estimated ~30M.
price: $1,200–$3,000/year for a novel oral/topical agent.
registrational_endpoint: Change from baseline in hair count (target area hair count/TAHC) at Month 6; or Global Photographic Assessment score.
btd_threshold_vs_soc: ≥15% increase in TAHC at 6 months vs. placebo or established comparator.

symptoms: Sudden patchy coin-sized hair loss on scalp or body
treatment_goal: Restore hair growth, prevent new patches
first_line_soc: Intralesional corticosteroids (triamcinolone acetonide 5–10 mg/mL) injected into affected patches every 4–6 weeks; topical corticosteroids for children or extensive disease.
treatment_algorithm: Step 1: Intralesional triamcinolone acetonide (most patches); Step 2: Topical super-potent corticosteroids (clobetasol 0.05%) or topical minoxidil adjunct; Step 3: Systemic corticosteroids for rapidly progressive disease; Step 4: JAK inhibitors – baricitinib 2–4 mg/day (Olumiant – FDA approved June 2022, first-ever systemic for alopecia areata), ritlecitinib 50 mg/day (Litfulo – FDA approved June 2023); deuruxolitinib (FDA approved 2024, ages ≥12); Step 5: Contact immunotherapy (DPCP) in refractory cases.
us_prevalence: ~700,000–1 million active moderate-to-severe cases; lifetime prevalence ~2% (~6.8 million ever affected).
patients_needing_tx: ~300,000–500,000 with moderate-to-severe alopecia areata (≥50% scalp loss) potentially eligible for systemic therapy. Estimated ~400K.
price: $45,000–$60,000/year (JAK inhibitors currently priced ~$50K/year).
registrational_endpoint: Severity of Alopecia Tool (SALT) score ≤20 (≥80% scalp coverage) at Week 36.
btd_threshold_vs_soc: ≥40% of patients achieving SALT ≤20 at Week 36 vs. ~5–10% for placebo.

symptoms: Excess coarse facial and body hair in women, male-pattern distribution
treatment_goal: Reduce unwanted hair, treat androgen excess if present
first_line_soc: Eflornithine 13.9% cream (Vaniqa) topical BID to face; combined with hormonal therapy – oral contraceptives (estrogen-progestin) for PCOS-related hirsutism.
treatment_algorithm: Step 1: Identify and treat underlying cause (PCOS, adrenal hyperplasia, medication); Step 2: Hormonal: OCP (ethinyl estradiol + norgestimate or drospirenone), spironolactone 100–200 mg/day (anti-androgen), finasteride 5 mg/day (off-label); Step 3: Eflornithine 13.9% cream (Vaniqa) for facial hair; Step 4: Physical methods: laser hair removal, electrolysis; No major 2024–2025 approvals.
us_prevalence: ~5–7 million women in the US with clinically significant hirsutism (mFG score ≥8).
patients_needing_tx: ~3–5 million seeking pharmacologic treatment. Estimated ~4M.
price: $1,000–$2,500/year for a novel androgen receptor antagonist or topical agent.
registrational_endpoint: Change from baseline in modified Ferriman-Gallwey (mFG) score at Month 6.
btd_threshold_vs_soc: ≥30% reduction in mFG score at Month 6 vs. ~15–20% for OCP monotherapy.

symptoms: Ingrown beard hairs, papules, pustules, dark spots on shaved skin
treatment_goal: Prevent ingrown hairs, clear papules, reduce scarring
first_line_soc: Topical retinoids (tretinoin 0.025–0.05% cream) and/or topical glycolic acid; low-potency topical corticosteroids for inflammation.
treatment_algorithm: Step 1: Shaving technique modification (no close shave, use single-blade razor, grow beard); Step 2: Topical retinoids (tretinoin, adapalene) to prevent follicular plugging; Step 3: Topical benzoyl peroxide or clindamycin for secondary infection; Step 4: Chemical depilatories (barium sulfide); Step 5: Laser hair removal (Nd:YAG 1064 nm preferred for darker skin); Topical eflornithine (Vaniqa) to reduce growth rate. No novel 2024–2025 FDA approvals.
us_prevalence: ~3–5 million Americans affected, predominantly Black men (45–85% of regularly shaving Black men).
patients_needing_tx: ~1–3 million seeking pharmacologic treatment. Estimated ~2M.
price: $500–$1,500/year for a topical agent.
registrational_endpoint: Change from baseline in papule/pustule count and IGA at Week 12.
btd_threshold_vs_soc: ≥50% reduction in lesion count and ≥2-grade IGA improvement at Week 12 vs. vehicle.

symptoms: Itchy widespread rash, urticaria, or blistering after medication exposure
treatment_goal: Identify offending drug, suppress immune reaction, prevent recurrence
first_line_soc: Discontinue causative drug; systemic antihistamines (cetirizine, diphenhydramine) for urticarial reactions; oral corticosteroids (prednisone 0.5–1 mg/kg) for severe morbilliform eruptions.
treatment_algorithm: Step 1: Discontinue offending drug immediately; Step 2: Urticarial/morbilliform: antihistamines + topical corticosteroids; Step 3: Moderate-severe: systemic corticosteroids (prednisone); Step 4: DRESS syndrome: systemic corticosteroids, cyclosporine, IVIG; Step 5: SJS/TEN: see separate entry; Supportive wound care for erosive reactions. No novel 2024–2025 approvals (area treated supportively).
us_prevalence: ~2–3 million drug eruption events annually; all-cause drug reactions affect ~7% of hospitalized patients.
patients_needing_tx: ~1–2 million requiring active pharmacologic treatment beyond drug withdrawal. Estimated ~1.5M.
price: $5,000–$15,000/course for a novel immune-targeted agent for DRESS or severe reactions.
registrational_endpoint: Skin clearance rate (EASI or IGA 0/1) and time to resolution at Day 14–28.
btd_threshold_vs_soc: ≥60% skin clearance at Day 14 vs. ~40% for systemic corticosteroids.

symptoms: Ring-shaped smooth bumps on hands, feet, elbows; usually painless
treatment_goal: Resolve skin lesions, address cosmetic concern
first_line_soc: Potent topical corticosteroids (clobetasol 0.05%) under occlusion; intralesional triamcinolone acetonide injections (5–10 mg/mL) for localized disease.
treatment_algorithm: Step 1: Observation (many self-resolve); Step 2: Topical superpotent corticosteroids ± occlusion; Step 3: Intralesional triamcinolone; Step 4: For generalized: hydroxychloroquine 200–400 mg/day, dapsone 100 mg/day, isotretinoin, or PUVA phototherapy; Step 5: Biologics (off-label): anti-TNF (adalimumab), dupilumab, IL-17 inhibitors reported in case series. No FDA-approved therapy; no 2024–2025 approvals.
us_prevalence: ~300,000–500,000 cases; predominantly women; associated with diabetes.
patients_needing_tx: ~100,000–200,000 seeking active pharmacologic treatment. Estimated ~150K.
price: $15,000–$30,000/year for a targeted biologic.
registrational_endpoint: Investigator Global Assessment (IGA) clear or almost clear (0/1) at Week 16.
btd_threshold_vs_soc: ≥40% IGA 0/1 at Week 16 vs. ~15% for placebo/vehicle.

symptoms: Rapidly expanding painful ulcer with purple undermined borders
treatment_goal: Halt ulcer progression, promote wound healing, prevent recurrence
first_line_soc: Systemic corticosteroids (prednisone 0.5–1 mg/kg/day) for moderate-severe; topical superpotent corticosteroids or tacrolimus 0.1% ointment for peristomal/limited disease.
treatment_algorithm: Step 1: Wound care (avoid debridement/surgery – pathergy risk); Step 2: Prednisone (mainstay first-line); Step 3: Cyclosporine 3–5 mg/kg/day (equivalent efficacy to prednisone per RCT); Step 4: Biologics: infliximab (Remicade – most evidence), adalimumab, ustekinumab; 2024: IL-1 inhibitors (anakinra, canakinumab) in trials; Step 5: Dapsone, mycophenolate mofetil, azathioprine as steroid-sparing. No FDA-approved therapy for PG.
us_prevalence: ~10,000–20,000 cases annually; often associated with IBD, arthritis, hematologic malignancy.
patients_needing_tx: ~8,000–18,000 requiring systemic treatment. Estimated ~15K.
price: $40,000–$70,000/year for a targeted biologic.
registrational_endpoint: Wound surface area reduction ≥30% and PGA 0/1 at Week 26.
btd_threshold_vs_soc: ≥50% wound area reduction at Week 16 vs. ~30% for systemic corticosteroids.

symptoms: Painful red nodules deep in skin fat, mostly lower legs
treatment_goal: Reduce inflammation, treat underlying cause
first_line_soc: NSAIDs (ibuprofen, indomethacin) for symptomatic relief; systemic corticosteroids (prednisone 0.5–1 mg/kg) for moderate-severe cases.
treatment_algorithm: Step 1: Identify and treat underlying cause (alpha-1 antitrypsin deficiency, pancreatic disease, infection, drugs); Step 2: NSAIDs; Step 3: Hydroxychloroquine 200–400 mg/day; Step 4: Systemic corticosteroids; Step 5: Alpha-1 antitrypsin concentrate (Prolastin, Aralast NP) for alpha-1 AT deficiency-related panniculitis; Colchicine, doxycycline for specific subtypes.
us_prevalence: ~50,000–150,000 cases annually; diverse etiologies.
patients_needing_tx: ~40,000–100,000 requiring pharmacologic treatment. Estimated ~70K.
registrational_endpoint: Change from baseline in affected nodule area, erythema, and pain VAS at Week 12.
btd_threshold_vs_soc: ≥50% reduction in lesion area and pain VAS at Week 12 vs. placebo.

symptoms: Target-shaped skin lesions, mainly palms, soles, and mucous membranes
treatment_goal: Suppress immune reaction, treat underlying trigger (HSV)
first_line_soc: Acyclovir 400 mg BID (for HSV-associated EM) as prophylaxis to prevent recurrence; symptomatic: topical corticosteroids and antihistamines.
treatment_algorithm: Step 1: Identify trigger (HSV most common, drugs, Mycoplasma); Step 2: Acyclovir 400 mg PO BID for HSV-associated EM (suppressive therapy); Step 3: Topical corticosteroids for skin lesions; Step 4: Oral corticosteroids (prednisone) for severe/recurrent; Step 5: Valacyclovir 500–1000 mg/day for refractory HSV-associated; Dapsone, thalidomide, azathioprine for truly refractory cases. No novel 2024–2025 approvals.
us_prevalence: ~100,000–300,000 episodes annually; predominantly young adults.
patients_needing_tx: ~80,000–200,000 requiring pharmacologic treatment. Estimated ~150K.
registrational_endpoint: Time to complete lesion resolution and recurrence-free rate at 12 months.
btd_threshold_vs_soc: ≥60% recurrence-free at 12 months vs. ~40% for acyclovir monotherapy.

symptoms: Sudden painful red skin plaques, fever, raised neutrophil count
treatment_goal: Rapidly suppress neutrophilic inflammation, prevent recurrence
first_line_soc: Systemic corticosteroids: prednisone 0.5–1 mg/kg/day orally for 4–6 weeks with gradual taper.
treatment_algorithm: Step 1: Rule out underlying malignancy (AML, IBD); treat underlying cause; Step 2: Prednisone 0.5–1 mg/kg/day (mainstay); Step 3: Alternatives: colchicine 0.5 mg TID, dapsone 100–200 mg/day, potassium iodide; Step 4: Indomethacin for mild cases; Step 5: Cyclosporine or anakinra (IL-1 inhibitor) for refractory cases. No 2024–2025 FDA approvals specific to Sweet syndrome.
us_prevalence: ~5,000–15,000 cases annually; rare condition; ~50–70% associated with underlying malignancy or infection.
patients_needing_tx: ~5,000–15,000 requiring systemic treatment. Estimated ~10K.
price: $20,000–$50,000/year for a targeted IL-1 or IL-8 pathway inhibitor.
registrational_endpoint: Complete skin lesion resolution (IGA 0) and absence of fever at Week 4.
btd_threshold_vs_soc: ≥70% complete resolution at Week 4 vs. ~50% for systemic corticosteroids alone.

disease: Stevens-Johnson Syndrome (SJS) and Toxic Epidermal Necrolysis (TEN)
symptoms: Widespread blistering, skin peeling, painful mucous membrane erosions, fever
treatment_goal: Halt skin detachment, prevent sepsis, support wound healing
first_line_soc: Immediate discontinuation of causative drug; intensive supportive care in ICU/burn unit; IVIG (1 g/kg/day x 3 days) or cyclosporine 3 mg/kg/day to halt progression.
treatment_algorithm: Step 1: Drug withdrawal (critical); Step 2: ICU/burn unit care – fluid resuscitation, wound care, nutritional support, eye care; Step 3: Immunomodulation: cyclosporine A 3–5 mg/kg/day (most evidence in TEN); IVIG 1 g/kg/day x 3–4 days; TNF-alpha inhibitors (etanercept 50 mg SC x 2 doses – strong evidence); Step 4: Systemic corticosteroids controversial; Step 5: Supportive: ophthalmology consultation (ocular sequelae major), prophylactic antibiotics only if infection confirmed. Etanercept 2-dose regimen emerging as preferred immunotherapy.
us_prevalence: ~5,000–8,000 cases/year (SJS ~3,500; TEN ~1,500); mortality 10% SJS, 30–50% TEN.
patients_needing_tx: ~5,000–8,000 requiring active immunotherapy beyond supportive care. Estimated ~6K.
price: $25,000–$80,000/acute course for a targeted immunotherapy.
registrational_endpoint: Arrest of skin detachment within 72 hours and 30-day mortality reduction vs. historical standard of care.
btd_threshold_vs_soc: 30-day mortality ≤15% (TEN) vs. historical ~30–40%; or skin progression arrest rate ≥75% at 72 hours.

symptoms: Tender red nodules on shins, fever, joint aches
treatment_goal: Reduce painful nodules, identify and treat underlying cause
first_line_soc: NSAIDs (ibuprofen 400–800 mg TID or indomethacin 50 mg TID) for symptomatic relief; rest and leg elevation.
treatment_algorithm: Step 1: Identify underlying cause (strep, sarcoidosis, IBD, TB, drugs, pregnancy); treat cause; Step 2: NSAIDs for pain and inflammation; Step 3: Potassium iodide (SSKI) 300–1500 mg/day for persistent cases; Step 4: Systemic corticosteroids if refractory and infectious cause excluded; Colchicine for recurrent EN. Most resolve in 3–6 weeks without specific therapy.
us_prevalence: ~50,000–100,000 cases annually; predominantly women aged 20–40.
patients_needing_tx: ~40,000–80,000 requiring pharmacologic treatment. Estimated ~60K.
price: $2,000–$8,000/course for a targeted anti-inflammatory.
registrational_endpoint: Change from baseline in nodule count and pain VAS at Week 4.
btd_threshold_vs_soc: ≥60% reduction in nodule count at Week 4 vs. ~40% for NSAID alone.

symptoms: Thickened, yellow-brown, crumbly toenails; nail separation
treatment_goal: Eradicate nail fungus, restore clear healthy nails
first_line_soc: Oral terbinafine 250 mg/day x 12 weeks (fingernails) or 12–16 weeks (toenails) – most effective systemic; topical efinaconazole 10% solution (Jublia) or tavaborole 5% solution (Kerydin) for mild-moderate disease.
treatment_algorithm: Step 1: Oral terbinafine (preferred, highest cure rates ~50–70%); Step 2: Oral itraconazole pulse therapy (200 mg BID x 1 week/month x 3–4 months); fluconazole 150–300 mg weekly x 9–12 months; Step 3: Topical: efinaconazole 10% (Jublia – 2014), tavaborole 5% (Kerydin – 2014), ciclopirox 8% lacquer; Step 4: Laser therapy (adjunct, limited evidence); 2024: Oteseconazole (Vivjoa) – new triazole approved for recurrent vulvovaginal candidiasis, Phase 2 data in onychomycosis ongoing.
us_prevalence: ~35–40 million Americans with onychomycosis (toenail most common); prevalence increases with age.
patients_needing_tx: ~15–25 million seeking pharmacologic treatment. Estimated ~20M.
price: $2,000–$5,000/course for novel oral or topical antifungal.
registrational_endpoint: Complete cure (mycological cure + clinical cure: ≤5% nail involvement + negative KOH and culture) at Week 52.
btd_threshold_vs_soc: ≥35% complete cure at Week 52 vs. ~20–25% for oral terbinafine.

symptoms: Thickened, discolored, brittle, misshapen nails affecting multiple digits
treatment_goal: Restore nail appearance and integrity, identify systemic cause
first_line_soc: Treat underlying cause (psoriasis: topical calcipotriol/corticosteroid; lichen planus: intralesional/systemic corticosteroids); topical tazarotene for nail psoriasis; biotin supplements (weak evidence).
treatment_algorithm: Depends on etiology: Psoriatic nail: topical calcipotriol + betamethasone (Enstilar, Taclonex), tazarotene; systemic biologics (TNF, IL-17, IL-23 inhibitors) for psoriatic nail with PsA; Lichen planus nail: intralesional triamcinolone, systemic retinoids; Twenty-nail dystrophy: spontaneous resolution in children; Trauma/habit tic: protective measures. No specific nail dystrophy approval in 2024–2025.
us_prevalence: ~5–10 million Americans with nail dystrophies of various causes.
patients_needing_tx: ~2–4 million seeking pharmacologic treatment. Estimated ~3M.
registrational_endpoint: Nail Psoriasis Severity Index (NAPSI) reduction ≥50% from baseline at Week 16.
btd_threshold_vs_soc: ≥60% NAPSI50 at Week 16 vs. ~30% for topical calcipotriol/betamethasone.

symptoms: Painful nail edge piercing skin, redness, swelling, drainage
treatment_goal: Relieve pain, prevent infection, correct nail growth
first_line_soc: Warm water soaks, cotton or waxed dental floss under nail edge; topical antibiotics (neomycin/polymyxin B) if infected; definitive: partial nail avulsion with phenol ablation of matrix.
treatment_algorithm: Conservative: warm soaks, nail bracing/splinting; Pharmacologic: topical antibiotics; oral antibiotics (cephalexin) if paronychia; Step 3: Minor surgery: partial nail avulsion (most effective); phenol ablation of lateral matrix; Step 4: Recurrent: total nail avulsion. No pharmacologic SoC; procedure-based.
us_prevalence: ~5–6 million cases annually; extremely common.
patients_needing_tx: ~3–5 million requiring medical/procedural intervention. Estimated ~4M.
price: $200–$1,000/course for a topical novel compound preventing recurrence.
registrational_endpoint: Resolution rate (no pain, no erythema, no recurrence) at 3 months post-treatment.
btd_threshold_vs_soc: ≥70% recurrence-free at 12 months vs. ~50% for phenol ablation alone.

symptoms: Persistent swollen nail fold, nail discoloration, recurrent flares
treatment_goal: Restore nail fold integrity, eradicate yeast colonization
first_line_soc: Topical antifungals (clotrimazole, miconazole) with or without topical corticosteroids (combined nystatin-triamcinolone or clotrimazole-betamethasone); keep hands dry.
treatment_algorithm: Step 1: Avoid wet work; protective gloves; Step 2: Topical azole antifungals (clotrimazole, miconazole) BID for Candida; topical corticosteroids for inflammation; Step 3: For resistant/recurrent: oral itraconazole 200 mg/day x 2–3 months; Step 4: Intralesional triamcinolone for persistent inflammation; Step 5: Surgical: eponychial marsupialization for chronic cases unresponsive to medical therapy.
us_prevalence: ~1–3 million cases; predominantly housewives, food handlers, healthcare workers.
patients_needing_tx: ~800,000–2,000,000 requiring treatment. Estimated ~1.5M.
registrational_endpoint: Complete clinical cure and mycological cure at Week 12.
btd_threshold_vs_soc: ≥50% complete cure at Week 12 vs. ~35% for topical azole monotherapy.

symptoms: Subungual hematoma, nail avulsion, nail bed laceration, pain
treatment_goal: Relieve pain, prevent infection, allow nail regrowth
first_line_soc: None (primarily procedural: trephination for subungual hematoma, wound closure for nail bed laceration); topical antibiotics post-procedure if infection risk.
treatment_algorithm: Subungual hematoma: trephination (needle or cautery); Nail avulsion: replacement of avulsed nail or stent; Nail bed laceration: repair with absorbable sutures; Topical antibiotics (bacitracin, mupirocin) post-repair; tetanus prophylaxis if needed. Pain: NSAIDs. No pharmacologic SoC.
us_prevalence: ~2–4 million nail trauma events annually.
patients_needing_tx: ~1–3 million requiring active treatment (procedural + topical). Estimated ~2M.
price: $200–$800/course for wound care/anti-infective.
registrational_endpoint: Normal nail regrowth rate and infection incidence at 3 months.
btd_threshold_vs_soc: ≥85% normal regrowth without infection at 3 months vs. ~70% for standard care.

symptoms: Swollen, red, pus-filled nail fold, pain around fingernail
treatment_goal: Drain infection, eradicate bacteria, reduce inflammation
first_line_soc: Warm soaks 3–4x/day; oral antibiotics (amoxicillin-clavulanate 875 mg BID or cephalexin 500 mg QID x 5–7 days) if cellulitis present; incision and drainage for fluctuant abscess.
treatment_algorithm: Step 1: Warm soaks; Step 2: I&D for fluctuant abscess; Step 3: Oral antibiotics if cellulitis (amoxicillin-clavulanate, TMP-SMX if MRSA risk); Topical antibiotics (mupirocin) for mild. Most cases resolve with I&D alone. No novel 2024–2025 approvals.
us_prevalence: ~2–3 million cases annually; most common nail problem seen in primary care.
patients_needing_tx: ~1–2 million requiring pharmacologic treatment. Estimated ~1.5M.
registrational_endpoint: Clinical cure (resolution of erythema, swelling, and pain) at Day 7.
btd_threshold_vs_soc: ≥90% clinical cure at Day 7 vs. ~75% for standard antibiotics + I&D.

symptoms: Ingrowth of nail back into proximal nail fold, painful swelling
treatment_goal: Remove embedded nail plate, allow healthy regrowth
first_line_soc: None (surgical proximal nail avulsion is the definitive treatment; no pharmacologic SoC).
treatment_algorithm: Proximal nail avulsion (surgical removal of embedded nail segments) is curative; adjunct: topical antibiotics for secondary infection; NSAIDs for pain. Rare condition; no pharmacologic SoC approved.
us_prevalence: ~10,000–50,000 cases annually; rare, under-recognized.
patients_needing_tx: ~10,000–40,000 requiring treatment. Estimated ~25K.
price: $500–$2,000/course for a hypothetical wound-healing agent.
registrational_endpoint: Complete nail regrowth without re-embedding at 6 months post-procedure.
btd_threshold_vs_soc: ≥80% successful regrowth at 6 months vs. ~60% for surgical avulsion alone.

symptoms: Intense scalp itch, visible nits on hair, skin excoriations
treatment_goal: Eradicate lice and nits, prevent reinfestation
first_line_soc: Topical permethrin 1% cream rinse (Nix) for head lice – first-line OTC; spinosad 0.9% suspension (Natroba) or ivermectin 0.5% lotion (Sklice) for permethrin-resistant lice.
treatment_algorithm: Head lice: permethrin 1% (Nix), malathion 0.5% lotion (Ovide), benzyl alcohol 5% lotion (Ulesfia), spinosad 0.9% (Natroba), ivermectin 0.5% lotion (Sklice); Oral: ivermectin 200–400 mcg/kg x 2 doses 7 days apart for resistant; Body lice: laundering + malathion; Pubic lice: permethrin 1% or malathion. Fine-toothed comb (nit removal). No novel 2024–2025 approvals.
us_prevalence: ~6–12 million head lice infestations annually, predominantly children aged 3–12.
patients_needing_tx: ~6–12 million requiring pharmacologic treatment. Estimated ~9M.
registrational_endpoint: Lice-free rate (no live lice + no viable nits) at Day 14.
btd_threshold_vs_soc: ≥95% lice-free at Day 14 vs. ~70–80% for permethrin 1%.

symptoms: Intensely itchy burrows between fingers, wrists, genitalia; worse at night
treatment_goal: Eradicate Sarcoptes scabiei mite, relieve pruritus
first_line_soc: Topical permethrin 5% cream (Elimite) applied head-to-toe x 8–14 hours, repeated in 1 week; oral ivermectin 200 mcg/kg x 2 doses 1–2 weeks apart as equally effective alternative.
treatment_algorithm: Step 1: Permethrin 5% cream (gold standard topical); Step 2: Oral ivermectin (preferred in crusted/institutional outbreaks); Step 3: Topical benzyl benzoate 25% (alternative where permethrin unavailable); sulfur 10% ointment; Step 4: Adjunct: oral antihistamines, topical corticosteroids for post-scabetic itch (may persist 4–6 weeks after cure); antibiotics for secondary infection; Treat all household contacts simultaneously. No novel 2024–2025 approvals.
us_prevalence: ~1–2 million cases annually in the US; higher in institutional settings (nursing homes, prisons).
patients_needing_tx: ~1–2 million requiring pharmacologic treatment. Estimated ~1.5M.
registrational_endpoint: Mite eradication rate (skin scraping negative) and pruritus NRS reduction at Week 4.
btd_threshold_vs_soc: ≥95% mite eradication at Week 4 with a single-dose regimen vs. ~85% for standard permethrin.

symptoms: Itchy linear or grouped bites, red papules, sleep disturbance
treatment_goal: Relieve itch, treat secondary infection, eliminate infestation
first_line_soc: Topical corticosteroids (hydrocortisone 1%) for itch; oral antihistamines (diphenhydramine, cetirizine) for severe pruritus; topical/oral antibiotics for secondary infection. Environmental pest control is the primary intervention.
treatment_algorithm: Step 1: Environmental extermination of bedbugs (insecticides, heat treatment – not pharmacologic); Step 2: Topical corticosteroids for bite reactions; Step 3: Oral antihistamines for severe pruritus; Step 4: Topical/oral antibiotics for secondary impetiginization; Step 5: For psychological distress: counseling. No FDA-approved treatment for bedbug bites per se.
us_prevalence: ~1–2 million American households infested annually; bites affect millions more.
patients_needing_tx: ~1–2 million seeking pharmacologic relief for skin reactions. Estimated ~1.5M.
price: $200–$1,000/course for a topical anti-pruritic or anti-inflammatory.
registrational_endpoint: Pruritus severity score (NRS) reduction ≥50% at Day 7 vs. baseline.
btd_threshold_vs_soc: ≥70% NRS reduction at Day 7 vs. ~50% for topical hydrocortisone.

symptoms: False belief of parasite infestation, skin picking, excoriations
treatment_goal: Treat psychosis, improve skin and quality of life
first_line_soc: Low-dose antipsychotic: risperidone 0.5–2 mg/day or olanzapine 2.5–5 mg/day; or pimozide 1–2 mg/day (historically used but QTc concerns).
treatment_algorithm: Step 1: Establish therapeutic alliance; avoid confrontation; Step 2: Antipsychotic: risperidone or olanzapine (preferred over pimozide due to better safety profile); aripiprazole as alternative; Step 3: SSRIs as adjunct if comorbid OCD/anxiety; Step 4: Wound care for excoriations; Step 5: Psychiatric consultation for severe/refractory. No FDA-approved drug specifically for Ekbom syndrome.
us_prevalence: ~150,000–300,000 cases in the US; predominantly women aged 50–70.
patients_needing_tx: ~100,000–200,000 requiring pharmacologic treatment. Estimated ~150K.
registrational_endpoint: Clinical Global Impression-Improvement (CGI-I) score ≤2 and Delusional Intensity Scale reduction at Week 12.
btd_threshold_vs_soc: ≥60% CGI-I ≤2 (much or very much improved) at Week 12 vs. ~35% for standard antipsychotics.

symptoms: Intensely itchy red serpentine creeping track on skin
treatment_goal: Kill hookworm larvae, relieve severe itching
first_line_soc: Oral ivermectin 200 mcg/kg single dose (drug of choice) or oral albendazole 400 mg/day x 3–7 days.
treatment_algorithm: Step 1: Oral ivermectin 200 mcg/kg x 1–2 doses (cure rate >97%); Step 2: Alternative: oral albendazole 400 mg/day x 3–7 days; Step 3: Topical thiabendazole 15% cream (compounded) for localized disease; Antihistamines for pruritus relief. No novel 2024–2025 approvals.
us_prevalence: ~50,000–200,000 cases/year in the US (mostly travelers returning from tropics); rising with increased travel.
patients_needing_tx: ~50K–200K requiring anthelmintic treatment. Estimated ~100K.
price: $100–$500/course (generics exist; novel agent could command $1,000–$2,000).
registrational_endpoint: Clinical cure (complete resolution of track and pruritus) at Day 14.
btd_threshold_vs_soc: ≥98% cure rate at Day 14 vs. ~90–95% for oral ivermectin.

symptoms: Painful boil-like skin lesion, sensation of movement underneath
treatment_goal: Remove larvae safely, prevent secondary infection
first_line_soc: None (mechanical extraction of larvae under occlusion or surgically; topical ivermectin or oral ivermectin 200 mcg/kg used adjunctively for some species).
treatment_algorithm: Furuncular myiasis: occlusion technique (petrolatum/bacon to suffocate larva) then extraction; surgical excision; Wound myiasis: irrigation, debridement; ivermectin (oral or topical) 1–2% solution adjunct; Oral ivermectin 200 mcg/kg for extensive infestation; Antibiotics for secondary infection. No pharmacologic SoC.
us_prevalence: ~5,000–20,000 cases annually (mostly travelers/immigrants); rare in US.
patients_needing_tx: ~5,000–15,000 requiring treatment. Estimated ~10K.
registrational_endpoint: Complete larval extraction and wound healing rate at Day 14.
btd_threshold_vs_soc: ≥95% extraction success and wound healing at Day 14 vs. ~80% for standard occlusion.

symptoms: White depigmented patches on skin, progressive loss of skin color
treatment_goal: Repigment lesions, halt disease progression
first_line_soc: Topical calcineurin inhibitors (tacrolimus 0.1% ointment or pimecrolimus 1% cream) for face/sensitive areas; topical potent corticosteroids for body lesions.
treatment_algorithm: Step 1: Topical calcineurin inhibitors (face) or potent corticosteroids (body); Step 2: Narrowband UVB phototherapy 2–3x/week (most effective for widespread); Step 3: Excimer laser (targeted phototherapy) for localized; Step 4: JAK inhibitors: ruxolitinib 1.5% cream (Opzelura – FDA approved July 2022, first topical JAK inhibitor for non-segmental vitiligo); Step 5: Oral minipulse methylprednisolone for actively spreading disease; Surgical: autologous melanocyte transplantation for stable segmental.
us_prevalence: ~1.5–2 million Americans; worldwide ~0.5–2% prevalence.
patients_needing_tx: ~1–1.5 million seeking pharmacologic or light treatment. Estimated ~1.2M.
price: $15,000–$30,000/year (ruxolitinib cream ~$15,000–$20,000/year at WAC).
registrational_endpoint: F-VASI50 (≥50% improvement in facial Vitiligo Area Scoring Index) at Week 24.
btd_threshold_vs_soc: ≥30% F-VASI50 at Week 24 vs. ~7% for vehicle (as seen in ruxolitinib trials).

symptoms: Dark patches or spots on skin; melasma, post-inflammatory, solar lentigines
treatment_goal: Lighten hyperpigmented areas, even skin tone
first_line_soc: Topical hydroquinone 2–4% (Tri-Luma, EpiQuin Micro) ± tretinoin 0.05–0.1% ± topical corticosteroid (Kligman's formula); broad-spectrum SPF 50+ sunscreen.
treatment_algorithm: Step 1: Sun protection (mandatory); Step 2: Hydroquinone 2–4% topical (bleaching agent); Step 3: Triple combination: hydroquinone 4% + tretinoin 0.05% + fluocinolone 0.01% (Tri-Luma – FDA approved for melasma); Step 4: Alternatives/adjuncts: azelaic acid 15–20%, kojic acid, niacinamide, tranexamic acid topical 2–5%; Step 5: Systemic tranexamic acid 250 mg BID for refractory melasma; Chemical peels; Laser (IPL, Q-switched Nd:YAG); 2024–2025: Sofpironium, improved tranexamic acid formulations in trials.
us_prevalence: ~5–7 million Americans with melasma; ~30–40 million with any form of significant hyperpigmentation.
patients_needing_tx: ~10–15 million seeking pharmacologic treatment. Estimated ~12M.
price: $500–$3,000/year for a novel topical or oral agent.
registrational_endpoint: mMASI (modified Melasma Area and Severity Index) reduction ≥50% from baseline at Week 24.
btd_threshold_vs_soc: ≥50% mMASI reduction at Week 24 vs. ~40% for Tri-Luma.

symptoms: Absent or reduced skin/hair/eye pigment, photosensitivity, vision impairment
treatment_goal: Protect from UV damage, prevent skin cancer, support vision
first_line_soc: None (no pharmacologic treatment changes underlying pigmentation); sun protection (broad-spectrum SPF ≥50 sunscreen daily), UV-protective clothing, regular dermatology and ophthalmology follow-up.
treatment_algorithm: Sun protection: daily broad-spectrum SPF 50+ sunscreen, UV-protective clothing; ophthalmologic management: corrective lenses, patching for nystagmus; regular skin cancer surveillance; Nitisinone (Orfadin – 2P-OA type) investigated for increasing melanin in OCA1B (Phase 2 trials); No FDA-approved pharmacologic therapy for albinism pigmentation.
us_prevalence: ~100,000 Americans with oculocutaneous albinism (OCA); 1 in 17,000–20,000 live births.
patients_needing_tx: ~100,000 all requiring preventive care; ~10,000–20,000 with complications requiring pharmacologic attention. Estimated ~100K for preventive regimen.
price: $5,000–$30,000/year for a pigmentation-promoting small molecule or enzyme replacement.
registrational_endpoint: Increase in melanin content (melanin index by reflectance spectrophotometry) at 6 months; skin cancer incidence reduction.
btd_threshold_vs_soc: ≥20% increase in melanin index at 6 months vs. placebo.

symptoms: Skin and tissue breakdown over bony prominences, pain, open sores
treatment_goal: Promote wound healing, prevent infection, relieve pressure
first_line_soc: Pressure offloading (repositioning, specialized mattresses); wound debridement; moisture-retentive dressings (hydrocolloid, foam); topical antimicrobials (silver-based dressings) for infected wounds.
treatment_algorithm: Step 1: Pressure redistribution (turning schedule, pressure-relief mattress); Step 2: Wound debridement (enzymatic – collagenase [Santyl], autolytic, mechanical); Stage I–II: moisture barrier creams, protective dressings; Stage III–IV: advanced wound dressings, negative pressure wound therapy (NPWT/VAC); Infected wounds: systemic antibiotics per culture; Topical growth factors: becaplermin 0.01% gel (Regranex – PDGF-BB for diabetic foot ulcers, used off-label); 2024: Cerament (synthetic bone filler) for underlying osteomyelitis.
us_prevalence: ~2.5 million Americans develop pressure injuries each year; ~100,000 die partially due to complications; higher in nursing homes (~1 million residents affected).
patients_needing_tx: ~1–2 million requiring active pharmacologic/advanced wound care. Estimated ~1.5M.
price: $3,000–$15,000/course for a novel topical healing agent or biologic.
registrational_endpoint: Wound closure rate (complete epithelialization) at Week 12 vs. standard care.
btd_threshold_vs_soc: ≥50% complete wound closure at Week 12 vs. ~30% for standard wound care.

symptoms: Red scaly plaques on elbows, knees, scalp; itch, joint pain
treatment_goal: Achieve clear/near-clear skin (PASI 90/100), sustain remission
first_line_soc: Topical: calcipotriene-betamethasone dipropionate foam (Enstilar, Wynzora cream – 2024) or gel (Taclonex) for mild-moderate; for moderate-severe: escalate to systemic therapy.
treatment_algorithm: Mild: topical calcipotriene/betamethasone (Enstilar, Taclonex, Wynzora), topical corticosteroids, vitamin D analogs, tazarotene, roflumilast foam 0.3% (Zoryve – FDA approved 2022); Moderate-severe: NB-UVB phototherapy; Systemic: methotrexate, cyclosporine, acitretin; Biologics: TNF inhibitors (adalimumab/Humira, etanercept, infliximab), IL-12/23 (ustekinumab/Stelara), IL-17A (secukinumab/Cosentyx, ixekizumab/Taltz, bimekizumab/Bimzelx – 2023 FDA approved), IL-23 (risankizumab/Skyrizi, guselkumab/Tremfya, tildrakizumab/Ilumya); Oral: apremilast (Otezla); 2024: bimekizumab expanding indications.
us_prevalence: ~7.5–8 million Americans with psoriasis; ~2.5–3 million with moderate-to-severe requiring systemic therapy.
patients_needing_tx: ~2.5–3 million with moderate-to-severe requiring biologics or systemic. Estimated ~2.8M.
price: $40,000–$80,000/year for a biologic (established pricing range).
registrational_endpoint: PASI 90 response (≥90% improvement in PASI score) at Week 16.
btd_threshold_vs_soc: ≥75% PASI 90 at Week 16 vs. ~50–60% for leading IL-23 inhibitors.

symptoms: Itchy purple flat-topped papules on wrists, ankles, mucous membranes
treatment_goal: Reduce itch, clear skin and mucosal lesions
first_line_soc: Topical superpotent corticosteroids (clobetasol 0.05%) for skin lesions; topical tacrolimus 0.1% for oral lichen planus; systemic corticosteroids (prednisone 30–60 mg/day) for widespread/severe.
treatment_algorithm: Step 1: Topical corticosteroids (mainstay for skin LP); tacrolimus 0.1% or pimecrolimus for oral/mucosal; Step 2: Intralesional triamcinolone for hypertrophic; Step 3: Systemic: hydroxychloroquine 200–400 mg/day, acitretin, cyclosporine, methotrexate; Step 4: Phototherapy (PUVA, NB-UVB) for widespread; Step 5: Biologics emerging (dupilumab, baricitinib in case series); No FDA-approved drug specifically for lichen planus.
us_prevalence: ~1–2 million Americans with lichen planus; prevalence ~0.5–1% of population.
patients_needing_tx: ~500,000–1,000,000 requiring pharmacologic treatment. Estimated ~700K.
price: $15,000–$35,000/year for a targeted biologic.
registrational_endpoint: IGA 0/1 (clear or almost clear) and reduction in pruritus NRS at Week 16.
btd_threshold_vs_soc: ≥40% IGA 0/1 at Week 16 vs. ~15–20% for topical corticosteroids.

symptoms: White atrophic itchy patches on genitalia, scarring, sexual pain
treatment_goal: Relieve itch, prevent scarring and squamous cell carcinoma risk
first_line_soc: Topical ultrapotent corticosteroids: clobetasol propionate 0.05% ointment applied daily x 3 months, then maintenance.
treatment_algorithm: Step 1: Clobetasol 0.05% ointment (standard first-line – reduces itch and prevents scarring); Step 2: Topical calcineurin inhibitors (tacrolimus 0.1%) as maintenance or steroid-sparing; Step 3: Topical testosterone (not recommended per current guidelines); Step 4: Systemic: hydroxychloroquine, acitretin for refractory; Step 5: Platelet-rich plasma injections; surgical release for phimosis; 2024: clobetasol butyrate propionate cream in development; dupilumab in case reports.
us_prevalence: ~300,000–500,000 Americans diagnosed; estimated 1 in 1,000 women; significantly underdiagnosed.
patients_needing_tx: ~250,000–400,000 requiring pharmacologic treatment. Estimated ~350K.
price: $8,000–$20,000/year for a novel topical anti-fibrotic or IL-4/13 inhibitor.
registrational_endpoint: IGA 0/1 and physician-assessed scarring score at Week 24.
btd_threshold_vs_soc: ≥50% IGA 0/1 at Week 24 vs. ~40% for clobetasol 0.05%.

symptoms: Orange-red scaling plaques, follicular keratosis, palmoplantar keratoderma
treatment_goal: Achieve remission of extensive erythrokeratoderma
first_line_soc: Oral retinoids: acitretin 25–50 mg/day or isotretinoin 0.5–1 mg/kg/day; these are most effective systemic agents.
treatment_algorithm: Step 1: Oral retinoids (acitretin – first-line systemic); Step 2: Methotrexate 10–25 mg/week; Step 3: Biologics (TNF inhibitors – infliximab, adalimumab; IL-12/23 inhibitors – ustekinumab most evidence for PRP); Step 4: Cyclosporine for acute management; Step 5: Topical retinoids + emollients as adjunct; 2024: IL-17 and IL-23 inhibitors in case series with promising results.
us_prevalence: ~10,000–30,000 Americans; rare condition (1–2 per 400,000).
patients_needing_tx: ~8,000–25,000 requiring systemic treatment. Estimated ~15K.
price: $30,000–$60,000/year for a targeted biologic.
registrational_endpoint: BSA reduction ≥75% and PGA 0/1 at Week 24.
btd_threshold_vs_soc: ≥50% PGA 0/1 at Week 24 vs. ~25% for acitretin monotherapy.

symptoms: Chronic scaly pink-tan patches, mostly asymptomatic, possible lymphoma risk
treatment_goal: Clear skin lesions, monitor for cutaneous T-cell lymphoma
first_line_soc: Topical corticosteroids (moderate-potent) and/or phototherapy (NB-UVB 2–3x/week) for symptomatic or large-plaque parapsoriasis.
treatment_algorithm: Step 1: Observation for small-plaque (typically benign course); Step 2: Topical corticosteroids; NB-UVB phototherapy; Step 3: PUVA for large-plaque (higher malignant potential); Step 4: Emollients for symptomatic itch; Step 5: Monitor with biopsy for evolution to mycosis fungoides (CTCL). No approved pharmacologic SoC.
us_prevalence: ~50,000–150,000 cases; often underdiagnosed or misclassified.
patients_needing_tx: ~30,000–100,000 requiring pharmacologic/phototherapy treatment. Estimated ~60K.
registrational_endpoint: IGA 0/1 and body surface area (BSA) reduction ≥50% at Week 16.
btd_threshold_vs_soc: ≥40% IGA 0/1 at Week 16 vs. ~20% for topical corticosteroids alone.

symptoms: Recurrent crops of papules that ulcerate, crust, and scar
treatment_goal: Suppress disease activity, prevent scarring
first_line_soc: Topical corticosteroids for mild disease; oral erythromycin 250 mg QID or tetracycline for anti-inflammatory effects; phototherapy (NB-UVB) for widespread.
treatment_algorithm: Step 1: Topical corticosteroids; Step 2: Oral antibiotics (erythromycin, doxycycline – anti-inflammatory mechanism); Step 3: NB-UVB phototherapy; Step 4: Methotrexate for PLEVA (severe febrile Mucha-Habermann); Step 5: Cyclosporine for severe/refractory; Acitretin for PLC. No FDA-approved therapy.
us_prevalence: ~20,000–50,000 cases; rare condition predominantly in children and young adults.
patients_needing_tx: ~15,000–40,000 requiring pharmacologic treatment. Estimated ~25K.
registrational_endpoint: IGA 0/1 and lesion count reduction at Week 16.
btd_threshold_vs_soc: ≥50% IGA 0/1 at Week 16 vs. ~25% for NB-UVB.

symptoms: Herald patch followed by Christmas tree-pattern oval pink scaly rash
treatment_goal: Relieve itch, accelerate rash resolution
first_line_soc: Self-limiting (resolves in 6–8 weeks); symptomatic: oral antihistamines and topical corticosteroids; oral acyclovir 800 mg 5x/day x 1 week may accelerate resolution.
treatment_algorithm: Observation (usually self-limiting); Acyclovir 800 mg 5x/day x 1–2 weeks for early symptomatic resolution (HHV-6/7 association); Topical corticosteroids for itch; Oral antihistamines; NB-UVB phototherapy for resistant/itchy cases; Erythromycin 250 mg QID x 2 weeks (limited evidence). No FDA-approved therapy.
us_prevalence: ~500,000–1,000,000 cases annually; typically self-limiting.
patients_needing_tx: ~200,000–500,000 seeking pharmacologic treatment. Estimated ~350K.
registrational_endpoint: Complete skin clearance and time to resolution at Week 4.
btd_threshold_vs_soc: ≥70% complete clearance at Week 4 vs. ~30% for placebo.

symptoms: Rough scaly pink-red patches on sun-exposed skin, pre-cancerous
treatment_goal: Eradicate AKs to prevent progression to squamous cell carcinoma
first_line_soc: Field therapy: 5-fluorouracil (5-FU) 0.5–5% cream (Carac, Efudex) x 2–4 weeks; or imiquimod 5% cream (Aldara) 3x/week x 4–16 weeks; or photodynamic therapy (PDT) with aminolevulinic acid (ALA/Levulan) single treatment.
treatment_algorithm: Lesion-directed: cryotherapy (liquid nitrogen), curettage; Field therapy: 5-FU (Carac, Efudex – most cost-effective), imiquimod 5% (Aldara), diclofenac 3% gel (Solaraze), ingenol mebutate (Picato – withdrawn 2020), tirbanibulin 1% ointment (Klisyri – FDA approved Dec 2020); PDT: ALA (Levulan Kerastick) or methyl ALA (BF-200 ALA/Ameluz); 2024: Tirbanibulin gaining use; BF-200 ALA daylight PDT.
us_prevalence: ~58 million Americans with actinic keratoses; most common pre-malignant skin condition.
patients_needing_tx: ~30–40 million requiring active pharmacologic treatment or procedural intervention. Estimated ~35M.
registrational_endpoint: Complete lesion clearance rate (all AKs in treatment area) at Day 57–84.
btd_threshold_vs_soc: ≥70% complete clearance at Day 57 vs. ~55% for 5-FU or imiquimod.

symptoms: Leathery wrinkled skin, solar lentigines, deep wrinkles, telangiectasias
treatment_goal: Reverse photoaging, improve skin texture and tone
first_line_soc: Topical tretinoin 0.05–0.1% cream (Retin-A, Renova – FDA approved for photoaging); broad-spectrum SPF 50+ sunscreen.
treatment_algorithm: Step 1: Sun protection (SPF 50+, UV-protective clothing); Step 2: Topical retinoids (tretinoin 0.05–0.1% – only FDA-approved for fine wrinkles; or retinol OTC); Step 3: Topical antioxidants (vitamin C, niacinamide); AHAs (glycolic, lactic acid); Step 4: In-office procedures: laser resurfacing (CO2, Er:YAG), intense pulsed light (IPL), chemical peels, fillers; Step 5: Topical tazarotene for more pronounced photoaging. No novel 2024–2025 approvals for photoaging specifically.
us_prevalence: ~90 million Americans with clinically significant photoaging (Glogau grade II–IV).
patients_needing_tx: ~30–50 million seeking pharmacologic treatment for photoaging. Estimated ~40M.
registrational_endpoint: Investigator assessment of fine wrinkles, mottled hyperpigmentation, and overall photoaging score at Month 6.
btd_threshold_vs_soc: ≥2-grade improvement in global photoaging score at Month 6 vs. ~1-grade for tretinoin 0.05%.

symptoms: Abnormal skin rash after sun exposure: urticaria, blistering, eczema
treatment_goal: Prevent sun-induced reactions, reduce photosensitivity
first_line_soc: Strict sun avoidance; broad-spectrum SPF 50+ sunscreen; topical corticosteroids for acute reactions; oral antihistamines for polymorphous light eruption (PMLE).
treatment_algorithm: PMLE (most common): antihistamines, topical corticosteroids, hardening phototherapy (gradual NB-UVB exposure); Chronic actinic dermatitis: immunosuppressants (cyclosporine, azathioprine), tacrolimus topical; Solar urticaria: antihistamines, PUVA, omalizumab (Xolair) for refractory; Lupus photosensitivity: hydroxychloroquine 200–400 mg/day; Drug-induced: discontinue offending agent. No single FDA approval for photosensitivity.
us_prevalence: ~5–10 million Americans with clinically significant photosensitivity disorders.
patients_needing_tx: ~3–6 million requiring pharmacologic treatment. Estimated ~4M.
registrational_endpoint: Minimal erythema dose (MED) improvement and provocation test score at Week 16.
btd_threshold_vs_soc: ≥2-fold increase in MED at Week 16 vs. placebo.

symptoms: Red painful hot skin after UV exposure, blistering if severe
treatment_goal: Relieve pain, reduce inflammation, promote healing
first_line_soc: Cool compresses, topical aloe vera; oral NSAIDs (ibuprofen 400 mg TID) for pain and inflammation; avoid further UV exposure.
treatment_algorithm: Step 1: Cool water baths/compresses; topical aloe vera or moisturizers; Step 2: Oral NSAIDs (ibuprofen, naproxen) within first 6–24 hours to reduce inflammation; Step 3: Topical lidocaine for pain relief; topical corticosteroids (hydrocortisone 1%) – benefit limited; Step 4: Blistered/severe: wound care, avoid rupturing blisters; systemic corticosteroids not recommended; Step 5: Hydration for extensive burns. No FDA-approved drug specifically for sunburn.
us_prevalence: ~33% of US adults report at least one sunburn/year; ~100–150 million sunburn events annually.
patients_needing_tx: ~20–30 million seeking pharmacologic relief. Estimated ~25M.
registrational_endpoint: Pain NRS reduction and erythema resolution (chromameter measurement) at 24–48 hours.
btd_threshold_vs_soc: ≥50% pain reduction at 24 hours and ≥30% erythema reduction vs. placebo.

symptoms: Excessive sweating of palms, armpits, feet beyond thermoregulatory need
treatment_goal: Reduce sweating to normal levels, improve quality of life
first_line_soc: Topical aluminum chloride hexahydrate 10–20% (Drysol, Certain Dri) applied to affected area at bedtime; or topical glycopyrronium tosylate 2.4% cloth (Qbrexza – FDA approved 2018 for primary axillary hyperhidrosis).
treatment_algorithm: Step 1: Topical aluminum chloride or Qbrexza (glycopyrronium); Step 2: Iontophoresis for palmar/plantar; Step 3: Oral anticholinergics (glycopyrrolate 1–2 mg BID, oxybutynin 5–10 mg/day – off-label but widely used); Step 4: Botulinum toxin injections (onabotulinumtoxinA/Botox – FDA approved for axillary; off-label for palmar, plantar, craniofacial); Step 5: Microwave thermolysis (miraDry – FDA cleared for axillary); Surgery: endoscopic thoracic sympathectomy (ETS) for palmar (last resort); 2024: sofpironium bromide 15% gel (Ecclock – Japan, Phase 3 US trials).
us_prevalence: ~15 million Americans with primary hyperhidrosis (~4.8% of US population).
patients_needing_tx: ~8–10 million seeking pharmacologic treatment. Estimated ~9M.
registrational_endpoint: HDSS (Hyperhidrosis Disease Severity Scale) response (≥2-grade improvement from score ≥3) at Week 4.
btd_threshold_vs_soc: ≥60% HDSS responders at Week 4 vs. ~50% for Qbrexza.

symptoms: Excessive unpleasant body odor from apocrine or eccrine sweat
treatment_goal: Eliminate offensive body odor, reduce bacterial colonization
first_line_soc: Topical aluminum chloride hexahydrate 20% (Drysol) antiperspirant; regular hygiene; topical antibacterial agents (clindamycin, erythromycin).
treatment_algorithm: Step 1: Hygiene optimization; topical aluminum chloride antiperspirant; antibacterial soap; Step 2: Topical antibiotics (clindamycin solution) to reduce odor-causing bacteria; Step 3: Botulinum toxin injections (Botox, Dysport) for axillary bromhidrosis; Step 4: Surgical: subcutaneous curettage of apocrine glands; laser (Nd:YAG, microwave) for permanent reduction (miraDry for axillary); No specific FDA pharmacologic approval for bromhidrosis.
us_prevalence: ~10–20 million Americans with clinically significant bromhidrosis.
patients_needing_tx: ~5–10 million seeking pharmacologic treatment. Estimated ~7M.
registrational_endpoint: Patient-reported odor severity VAS reduction and panelist odor rating at Week 8.
btd_threshold_vs_soc: ≥50% patient-reported odor VAS reduction at Week 8 vs. ~25% for standard antiperspirant.

symptoms: Reduced sweating, heat intolerance, overheating, dry skin
treatment_goal: Prevent heat-related illness, identify and treat underlying cause
first_line_soc: None pharmacologic (no approved drug restores sweat gland function); cooling strategies and heat avoidance are cornerstone.
treatment_algorithm: Treat underlying cause (anhidrosis from anticholinergic drugs: discontinue; Sjögren's: pilocarpine 5 mg TID for dry glands; ectodermal dysplasia: no pharmacologic cure); Supportive: cooling vests, wet skin evaporation, avoid heat; Pilocarpine for cholinergic stimulation in some forms; No FDA-approved treatment for primary hypohidrosis.
us_prevalence: ~200,000–500,000 Americans with clinically significant hypohidrosis/anhidrosis.
patients_needing_tx: ~100,000–300,000 requiring medical management. Estimated ~200K.
price: $5,000–$20,000/year for a novel sweat gland activator.
registrational_endpoint: Sweat rate (mg/cm²/min by gravimetry) improvement and heat tolerance test score at Week 12.
btd_threshold_vs_soc: ≥50% improvement in sweat rate vs. baseline and vs. placebo.

symptoms: Tiny itchy heat-rash bumps after sweating; prickly heat sensation
treatment_goal: Relieve itch, open blocked sweat ducts, prevent recurrence
first_line_soc: None pharmacologic; cool environment and light clothing (primary); topical calamine lotion or mild topical corticosteroids (hydrocortisone 1%) for itch.
treatment_algorithm: Step 1: Cool environment, remove occlusive clothing, air conditioning; Step 2: Calamine lotion or light topical corticosteroid (hydrocortisone 1%); Step 3: Oral antihistamines for pruritus; Step 4: For miliaria profunda: vitamin C supplementation (experimental evidence); Avoid topical creams/ointments that worsen occlusion. No approved pharmacologic SoC.
us_prevalence: ~5–10 million cases annually (seasonal, hot climate); more common in neonates and military personnel.
patients_needing_tx: ~2–5 million seeking pharmacologic treatment. Estimated ~3M.
registrational_endpoint: Pruritus NRS reduction and IGA improvement at Day 14.
btd_threshold_vs_soc: ≥60% NRS reduction at Day 14 vs. ~40% for calamine lotion.

symptoms: Rough skin-colored growths on hands, feet, genitalia; HPV-caused
treatment_goal: Eradicate warts, prevent recurrence and spread
first_line_soc: Topical salicylic acid 17–40% (Compound W, Duofilm) daily for common/plantar warts; cryotherapy (liquid nitrogen) every 2–3 weeks for persistent or genital warts.
treatment_algorithm: Common/plantar: salicylic acid preparations (OTC, Rx); cryotherapy; Genital warts (condylomata): imiquimod 5% cream (Aldara) 3x/week x 16 weeks, sinecatechins 15% ointment (Veregen – FDA approved), podophyllin resin, podofilox 0.5% solution (Condylox); trichloroacetic acid (TCA); cryotherapy; CO2 laser; HPV vaccination (Gardasil 9 – prevention); 2024: systemic treatments for recalcitrant warts remain limited; intralesional immunotherapy (Candida antigen, bleomycin) for resistant.
us_prevalence: ~10–13 million Americans with common/plantar warts; ~1 million new genital wart cases/year.
patients_needing_tx: ~8–12 million seeking pharmacologic treatment. Estimated ~10M.
registrational_endpoint: Complete clearance of all target warts at Day 84 (12 weeks).
btd_threshold_vs_soc: ≥60% complete clearance at Day 84 vs. ~50% for imiquimod or cryotherapy.

symptoms: Small flesh-colored umbilicated skin bumps; itchy if inflamed
treatment_goal: Eradicate viral lesions, prevent autoinoculation and spread
first_line_soc: Topical cantharidin (Ycanth – FDA approved July 2023, first FDA-approved treatment for molluscum in children and adults) applied by clinician every 3 weeks.
treatment_algorithm: Observation (self-limited in immunocompetent); Step 1: Cantharidin topical solution (Ycanth – FDA approved 2023 – major advance); Step 2: Curettage or cryotherapy (liquid nitrogen); Step 3: Imiquimod 5% cream (off-label, especially immunocompromised); Step 4: Topical tretinoin or salicylic acid; Step 5: Topical cidofovir or IV cidofovir for severe immunocompromised; Sinecatechins 10% (Veregen) off-label. Ycanth approval 2023 is landmark.
us_prevalence: ~6 million Americans with molluscum contagiosum annually; predominantly children aged 2–12 and sexually active adults.
patients_needing_tx: ~3–5 million seeking active treatment. Estimated ~4M.
registrational_endpoint: Complete lesion clearance at Week 12.
btd_threshold_vs_soc: ≥90% complete clearance at Week 12 vs. ~60% for cantharidin.

symptoms: Variable: rash, nodules, ulcers, or vesicles from animal-transmitted pathogens
treatment_goal: Eradicate zoonotic pathogen, prevent complications
first_line_soc: Depends on pathogen: Ringworm (tinea): topical/oral antifungals; Orf virus: self-limiting (no treatment); Cowpox/monkeypox: supportive ± tecovirimat (TPOXX) for severe mpox; Cat-scratch disease: azithromycin 500 mg x 1 then 250 mg x 4 days.
treatment_algorithm: Cat-scratch disease (Bartonella): azithromycin or doxycycline; Orf/parapoxvirus: self-limiting; Monkeypox (now mpox): tecovirimat (TPOXX – FDA approved for smallpox, EUA for mpox) + brincidofovir; Milker's nodule: self-limiting; Tularemia: doxycycline 100 mg BID x 14–21 days; Anthrax: ciprofloxacin; Sporotrichosis: itraconazole 200 mg/day x 3–6 months; Tinea capitis from animals: oral terbinafine.
us_prevalence: ~200,000–500,000 zoonotic skin infection cases annually of various types.
patients_needing_tx: ~150,000–400,000 requiring pharmacologic treatment. Estimated ~250K.
registrational_endpoint: Pathogen-specific: clinical cure at Day 14–28 per relevant guidelines.
btd_threshold_vs_soc: ≥90% clinical cure at end of treatment vs. ~75–85% for SoC antibiotics.

symptoms: High blood pressure, leg swelling, low potassium, renin-driven aldosterone excess
treatment_goal: Treat underlying cause (heart failure, cirrhosis, renovascular HTN)
first_line_soc: Treat underlying cause; ACE inhibitors/ARBs for renovascular/heart failure etiology; mineralocorticoid receptor antagonists (spironolactone, eplerenone) for symptom control.
treatment_algorithm: Step 1: Address primary cause (heart failure: ACE/ARB/ARNI; cirrhosis: treat liver disease; nephrotic syndrome: treat underlying renal disease); Step 2: MRAs: spironolactone 25–100 mg/day (heart failure with reduced ejection fraction – mortality benefit); eplerenone (Inspra); Step 3: Loop diuretics for edema; Step 4: SGLT2i (empagliflozin, dapagliflozin) reduce secondary aldosteronism in HFrEF; No targeted pharmacologic SoC specifically for secondary aldosteronism beyond treating primary cause.
us_prevalence: ~5–10 million Americans with secondary aldosteronism related to heart failure, cirrhosis, or renovascular disease.
patients_needing_tx: ~3–6 million requiring pharmacologic management. Estimated ~5M.
registrational_endpoint: Systolic BP reduction and normalization of plasma renin/aldosterone at Week 12 in heart failure patients.
btd_threshold_vs_soc: ≥10 mmHg SBP reduction and ≥30% aldosterone reduction vs. placebo.

symptoms: Hypertension resistant to treatment, low potassium, muscle weakness, fatigue
treatment_goal: Normalize aldosterone excess, control blood pressure and potassium
first_line_soc: Unilateral adenoma: laparoscopic adrenalectomy (curative). Bilateral hyperplasia: mineralocorticoid receptor antagonist (MRA): spironolactone 25–400 mg/day or eplerenone 50–200 mg/day.
treatment_algorithm: Step 1: Subtype localization (adrenal CT + adrenal vein sampling); Step 2: Unilateral: adrenalectomy; Step 3: Bilateral: spironolactone (most potent, first-line) or eplerenone (fewer side effects); Step 4: Potassium supplementation; antihypertensive add-on if needed; 2024: lorundrostat (aldosterone synthase inhibitor) Phase 3 positive results (Corcept); baxdrostat (CYP11B2 inhibitor – AstraZeneca, Phase 3 CLARION-CKD published 2024 – significant BP reduction).
us_prevalence: ~3–6 million Americans with primary aldosteronism (5–10% of hypertensive patients); significantly underdiagnosed.
patients_needing_tx: ~1–3 million requiring pharmacologic treatment (bilateral form + pre/post-op). Estimated ~2M.
price: $8,000–$20,000/year for a novel CYP11B2/aldosterone synthase inhibitor.
registrational_endpoint: Systolic BP reduction from baseline at Week 12 and normalization of plasma aldosterone-to-renin ratio (ARR).
btd_threshold_vs_soc: ≥10 mmHg additional SBP reduction AND ARR normalization vs. placebo at Week 12.

symptoms: Excess body/facial hair, acne, clitoral enlargement, deepened voice in women
treatment_goal: Suppress excess androgen production, normalize virilization
first_line_soc: Glucocorticoid replacement: hydrocortisone 10–20 mg/day (in divided doses) to suppress ACTH and adrenal androgen excess in congenital adrenal hyperplasia (most common cause).
treatment_algorithm: CAH-related: hydrocortisone (children), prednisone or dexamethasone (adults); Tumor-related: adrenalectomy; Adjuncts: anti-androgen therapy (spironolactone, flutamide) for virilization symptoms; eflornithine topical for facial hair; 2023: crinecerfont (NVK-002) – Phase 3 data positive for non-classical CAH, FDA NDA filed 2024; Tildacerfont – CRF1 receptor antagonist for CAH in Phase 3.
us_prevalence: ~50,000–75,000 Americans with classic CAH-related virilism; ~300,000 with non-classic forms.
patients_needing_tx: ~50,000–100,000 requiring pharmacologic management. Estimated ~75K.
price: $15,000–$50,000/year for a novel CRF1 antagonist.
registrational_endpoint: Normalization of androstenedione and 17-hydroxyprogesterone (17-OHP) levels at 24 weeks plus glucocorticoid dose reduction.
btd_threshold_vs_soc: ≥50% of patients achieving androstenedione in normal range at Week 24 with ≥20% GC dose reduction vs. placebo.

symptoms: Central obesity, moon face, purple striae, easy bruising, hypertension
treatment_goal: Normalize cortisol levels, reverse metabolic complications
first_line_soc: Surgical: transsphenoidal surgery (pituitary adenoma – Cushing disease) is definitive first-line. Pharmacologic bridging: metyrapone 500 mg–6 g/day or ketoconazole 400–1200 mg/day (adrenal steroidogenesis inhibitors).
treatment_algorithm: Step 1: Surgery (transsphenoidal, adrenalectomy per etiology); Step 2: Radiation for persistent Cushing disease; Step 3: Pharmacologic: metyrapone (Metopirone), ketoconazole (FDA approved for Cushing), osilodrostat (Isturisa – FDA approved 2020), levoketoconazole (Recorlev – FDA approved 2021), pasireotide (Signifor – FDA approved 2012 for Cushing disease), mifepristone (Korlym – FDA approved 2012 for Cushing-related hyperglycemia); Cabergoline adjunct; Mitotane for adrenal carcinoma.
us_prevalence: ~15,000–20,000 Americans with active Cushing syndrome (~10–15 per million prevalence).
patients_needing_tx: ~10,000–18,000 requiring pharmacologic management. Estimated ~15K.
price: $80,000–$200,000/year (osilodrostat ~$150K/year WAC; Korlym ~$170K/year).
registrational_endpoint: Normalization of 24-hour urinary free cortisol (UFC) to ≤ULN at Week 24 without dose uptitration (complete response).
btd_threshold_vs_soc: ≥40% complete UFC normalization at Week 24 vs. ~25% for placebo.

symptoms: Fatigue, nausea, low blood pressure, weight loss, no hyperpigmentation
treatment_goal: Replace cortisol physiologically, prevent adrenal crisis
first_line_soc: Hydrocortisone 15–25 mg/day in divided doses (same as primary AI); no mineralocorticoid needed (RAAS preserved). Identify and treat underlying pituitary/hypothalamic cause.
treatment_algorithm: Same glucocorticoid replacement as Addison's: hydrocortisone, prednisone, dexamethasone; Modified-release hydrocortisone (Efmody/Plenadren) for better circadian rhythm mimicry; Address underlying cause (pituitary tumor – surgery; steroid withdrawal – taper slowly; pituitary irradiation – GC replacement); DHEA replacement (off-label for women); Adrenal crisis: IV hydrocortisone 100 mg. No novel 2024–2025 specific approvals.
us_prevalence: ~150,000–200,000 Americans with secondary AI (more common than primary); most due to exogenous glucocorticoid suppression or pituitary disease.
patients_needing_tx: ~150,000–200,000 requiring pharmacologic management. Estimated ~175K.
registrational_endpoint: Cortisol AUC normalization and AddiQoL or similar QoL score at 6 months.
btd_threshold_vs_soc: ≥30% QoL improvement and physiologic cortisol AUC in ≥75% vs. standard hydrocortisone TID.

symptoms: Usually asymptomatic; incidentally found on imaging; mild back discomfort
treatment_goal: Rule out malignancy and hormonal hypersecretion; monitor stable lesions
first_line_soc: None pharmacologic (surveillance with repeat imaging at 6–12 months; biochemical testing to exclude pheochromocytoma and subclinical Cushing's); surgical adrenalectomy for masses >4 cm or growing.
treatment_algorithm: Step 1: Biochemical workup (24h urine catecholamines, 1mg DST, aldosterone:renin); Step 2: CT characteristics (Hounsfield units <10 – adenoma); Step 3: Observation for small (<4 cm) stable lipid-rich adenomas; Step 4: Adrenalectomy for growing, >4 cm, suspicious, or hormonally active; Adjunctive: metyrapone/ketoconazole if autonomous cortisol secretion causing metabolic comorbidities; No pharmacologic SoC for incidentaloma per se.
us_prevalence: ~3–5% of CT scans reveal adrenal incidentalomas; ~600,000–1,000,000 new diagnoses annually.
patients_needing_tx: ~50,000–200,000 requiring active pharmacologic treatment (autonomous cortisol secretion). Estimated ~100K.
price: $5,000–$20,000/year for a novel adrenal-targeted agent.
registrational_endpoint: Normalization of 1mg overnight DST and metabolic parameters at 12 months.
btd_threshold_vs_soc: ≥60% DST normalization at 12 months vs. ~30% for adrenalectomy avoidance with watchful waiting.

symptoms: Fatigue, weight loss, salt craving, hyperpigmentation, low blood pressure, vomiting
treatment_goal: Replace cortisol and aldosterone lifelong, prevent adrenal crises
first_line_soc: Glucocorticoid: hydrocortisone 15–25 mg/day in divided doses (most physiologic); fludrocortisone 0.05–0.2 mg/day for mineralocorticoid replacement.
treatment_algorithm: Glucocorticoid: hydrocortisone (most physiologic), prednisone, or cortisone acetate; Sick day rules: double/triple dose; Adrenal crisis: IV hydrocortisone 100 mg bolus + continuous drip; Mineralocorticoid: fludrocortisone (Florinef); DHEA supplementation for women with Addison's (off-label); 2024: Modified-release hydrocortisone (Plenadren – once-daily) available in Europe, NDA filed in US; Efmody (multi-compartment MR hydrocortisone) FDA approved 2022.
us_prevalence: ~100,000–120,000 Americans with primary adrenal insufficiency.
patients_needing_tx: ~100,000–120,000 requiring lifelong replacement. Estimated ~110K.
price: $3,000–$15,000/year for a novel physiologic replacement formulation.
registrational_endpoint: Quality of life (AddiQoL score) improvement and cortisol exposure profile (AUC 24hr) within physiologic range at 6 months.
btd_threshold_vs_soc: ≥30% AddiQoL improvement AND cortisol AUC in physiologic range in ≥80% vs. standard thrice-daily hydrocortisone.

symptoms: Episodic hypertensive crises, palpitations, sweating, severe headache, pallor
treatment_goal: Control hypertension, surgically remove tumor safely
first_line_soc: Alpha-blockade: phenoxybenzamine (Dibenzyline) 10–40 mg/day or selective alpha-1 blocker (doxazosin 8–16 mg/day) for 10–14 days pre-operatively; then beta-blocker added after alpha-blockade established.
treatment_algorithm: Pre-op (mandatory): alpha-blockade (phenoxybenzamine preferred; doxazosin alternative) x 10–14 days; beta-blocker ONLY after alpha; calcium channel blocker (nicardipine) adjunct; metyrosine (DEMSER – tyrosine hydroxylase inhibitor, reduces catecholamine synthesis) for refractory; Step 2: Laparoscopic adrenalectomy (curative); Malignant/metastatic pheo: iodine-131 MIBG (Azedra – FDA approved 2018); sunitinib, temozolomide/dacarbazine; 2024: 177Lu-DOTATATE (Lutathera) for SSTR-expressing tumors.
us_prevalence: ~500–1,000 new pheochromocytomas/year in US; ~5,000–10,000 prevalent cases.
patients_needing_tx: ~5,000–10,000 requiring pharmacologic management (perioperative + medical control). Estimated ~7K.
price: $20,000–$80,000/year for malignant pheo systemic therapy.
registrational_endpoint: For malignant pheo: overall response rate (RECIST) and biochemical response (catecholamine normalization) at 6 months.
btd_threshold_vs_soc: ≥30% ORR at 6 months vs. ~20% for MIBG therapy.

symptoms: Multiple endocrine gland failures (thyroid, adrenal, pancreas, gonads), autoimmune
treatment_goal: Replace all deficient hormones, treat autoimmune complications
first_line_soc: Hormone replacement for each deficient axis: hydrocortisone (Addison's), levothyroxine (hypothyroidism), insulin (T1DM), testosterone/estrogen (gonadal failure).
treatment_algorithm: APS-1 (AIRE mutation): calcium + vitamin D (hypoparathyroidism), antifungals (candidiasis), cortisol, thyroid; APS-2 (most common): hydrocortisone + levothyroxine + insulin; Immunomodulation: emerging CTLA-4 Ig (abatacept) for APS-1 autoimmune component trials; No approved immunomodulatory therapy for APS syndromes; 2024: teplizumab (Tzield – anti-CD3, approved Nov 2022) for T1DM delay in APS-2.
us_prevalence: ~100,000–150,000 Americans with APS-2 (most common); APS-1 ~1:100,000 (very rare).
patients_needing_tx: ~100,000–150,000 requiring lifelong multi-hormone replacement. Estimated ~125K.
price: $10,000–$30,000/year for comprehensive replacement + immunotherapy.
registrational_endpoint: All hormonal axes normalized and autoimmune disease activity score at 12 months.
btd_threshold_vs_soc: ≥30% reduction in new gland involvement at 5 years with immunomodulatory therapy vs. standard hormone replacement only.

symptoms: Drug-triggered lupus-like illness: joint pain, rash, fever, positive ANA
treatment_goal: Identify and discontinue offending drug, suppress autoimmune manifestations
first_line_soc: Discontinue causative drug; NSAIDs for mild symptoms; hydroxychloroquine 200–400 mg/day for persistent features.
treatment_algorithm: Step 1: Drug withdrawal (most cases resolve spontaneously); Step 2: NSAIDs for arthralgias; Step 3: Hydroxychloroquine for persistent skin/joint manifestations; Step 4: Systemic corticosteroids (prednisone 20–60 mg/day) for severe manifestations (serositis, nephritis); Step 5: Immunosuppressants (azathioprine, mycophenolate) if prolonged course or overlap syndrome; Monitor ANA/anti-histone antibodies. No FDA-approved specific treatment.
us_prevalence: ~15,000–30,000 cases annually; hydralazine, procainamide, isoniazid, biologics (TNF inhibitors) most common triggers.
patients_needing_tx: ~10,000–25,000 requiring pharmacologic treatment. Estimated ~20K.
registrational_endpoint: SLEDAI score normalization and ANA titer reduction at Month 6.
btd_threshold_vs_soc: ≥60% SLEDAI normalization at Month 6 vs. ~40% for hydroxychloroquine alone.

symptoms: Severe immune dysregulation, enteropathy, neonatal Type 1 diabetes, eczema
treatment_goal: Achieve curative hematopoietic stem cell transplant, immunosuppression as bridge
first_line_soc: Immunosuppression as bridge to HSCT: sirolimus (rapamycin) 0.3–6 mg/m²/day (most effective medical therapy); tacrolimus for refractory.
treatment_algorithm: Step 1: Sirolimus (most evidence, targets mTOR pathway, consistent with FOXP3 defect); Step 2: Cyclosporine or tacrolimus; Step 3: Nutritional support (parenteral nutrition for enteropathy); Step 4: HSCT – only curative treatment (performed in early childhood); Gene therapy in development (Phase 1/2 trials 2024 for FOXP3 gene correction); No FDA-approved drug specifically for IPEX.
us_prevalence: ~200–500 living patients in the US; extremely rare (1–2 per million live births).
patients_needing_tx: ~200–500 active patients requiring treatment. Estimated ~350.
price: $200,000–$500,000/year for gene therapy or novel biologic; HSCT ~$300,000–$500,000 one-time.
registrational_endpoint: Resolution of enteropathy and immune dysregulation (IPEX score composite) and freedom from HSCT or severe immunosuppression at 12 months.
btd_threshold_vs_soc: ≥50% patients achieving clinical remission (no enteropathy, no diabetes exacerbation) at 12 months vs. ~20–30% for sirolimus alone.

symptoms: Peripheral neuropathy, retinopathy, nephropathy, foot ulcers, cardiovascular disease
treatment_goal: Prevent progression of organ damage, protect kidneys and heart
first_line_soc: Optimal glycemic control (A1C <7%) with standard diabetes agents; ACE inhibitor/ARB for diabetic nephropathy; statin for dyslipidemia; aggressive BP control.
treatment_algorithm: Nephropathy: ACE inhibitor/ARB first-line; SGLT2 inhibitors (dapagliflozin/Farxiga, empagliflozin/Jardiance) – FDA approved for diabetic kidney disease + CKD; finerenone (Kerendia – nonsteroidal MRA, FDA approved 2021) for CKD + T2DM; Neuropathy: duloxetine (Cymbalta), pregabalin (Lyrica), gabapentin; Retinopathy: anti-VEGF injections (ranibizumab/Lucentis, aflibercept/Eylea, faricimab/Vabysmo) for DME; Foot ulcers: becaplermin, NPWT; CV: GLP-1 RAs and SGLT2i with proven CV benefits; 2024: sparsentan for diabetic nephropathy Phase 3 results.
us_prevalence: ~37 million diabetics; ~40–50% with some complication; diabetic nephropathy ~11M, neuropathy ~15M, retinopathy ~9M.
patients_needing_tx: ~20–30 million requiring pharmacologic treatment for diabetic complications. Estimated ~25M.
price: $8,000–$25,000/year for a novel organ-protective agent.
registrational_endpoint: For diabetic kidney disease: composite kidney endpoint (sustained 40% decline in eGFR, ESKD, or renal/CV death) over 2–4 years.
btd_threshold_vs_soc: ≥25% relative risk reduction in kidney composite endpoint vs. placebo on top of RAS blockade.

symptoms: Excessive thirst, frequent urination, weight loss, fatigue, blurred vision
treatment_goal: Maintain near-normal glucose, prevent DKA and long-term complications
first_line_soc: Insulin replacement therapy (basal-bolus regimen): long-acting insulin (glargine U-100/U-300 [Lantus/Toujeo], detemir [Levemir], degludec [Tresiba]) plus rapid-acting insulin (lispro [Humalog], aspart [NovoLog/Fiasp], glulisine [Apidra]) with continuous glucose monitoring (CGM).
treatment_algorithm: Step 1: Lifelong insulin replacement — basal-bolus MDI or continuous subcutaneous insulin infusion (CSII/insulin pump); Step 2: CGM (Dexterity G7, Libre 3) + closed-loop automated insulin delivery (AID/hybrid closed-loop: Tandem Control-IQ, Omnipod 5); Step 3: Adjuncts to reduce insulin dose/hypoglycemia: pramlintide (Symlin — amylin analog); SGLT-2 inhibitors (sotagliflozin [Inpefa] — FDA approved 2023 for heart failure; dapagliflozin off-label); Step 4: Immunotherapy to preserve beta-cell function at diagnosis: teplizumab (Tzield — anti-CD3 mAb) — FDA approved Nov 2022, first disease-modifying therapy for T1D, delays clinical T1D onset by median 2+ years in stage 2; Step 5: Emerging: stem cell/islet transplantation (Lantidra — donislecel, FDA approved 2023 for severe hypoglycemia unawareness); Step 6: Ultra-rapid insulin (URLi — lispro-aabc [Lyumjev], BioChaperone combo formulations). 2024–2025: CPCB-RPE1 encapsulated islets, VX-880/VX-264 (Vertex — stem cell-derived islet therapy) in Phase 3.
us_prevalence: ~1.9–2.1 million Americans with T1D (ADA/JDRF estimates 2025); incidence ~64,000 new cases/year including ~18,000 in youth. Prevalence rising ~2–3%/year.
patients_needing_tx: ~2.0 million — virtually all T1D patients require lifelong insulin. ~1.7–1.9 million are actively treated in the US healthcare system. Estimated 1.85M.
price: Teplizumab (Tzield): ~$193,900/14-day course (stage 2); Novel disease-modifying biologics: $150,000–$250,000/course. Adjunct SGLT-2i: $8,000–$12,000/year. Insulin analogs (branded): $3,000–$8,000/year. A novel curative cell therapy (Vertex VX-880 type): $500,000–$1,000,000 one-time.
tam_gross: 1.85M × $200,000 (disease-modifying/curative therapy blended) = $370B theoretical; realistic annual addressable market for novel adjunct/biologic: 500K × $25,000 = $12.5B
registrational_endpoint: Time to clinical T1D diagnosis (two consecutive abnormal glucose tests) in at-risk stage 2 patients (teplizumab model); or HbA1c change from baseline at 26 weeks plus time-in-range (TIR ≥70%) for adjunct therapies; or insulin independence rate at 1 year for cell therapies.
btd_threshold_vs_soc: For disease-modifying therapy: ≥50% reduction in rate of progression to clinical T1D vs. placebo at 2 years (teplizumab achieved HR ~0.41). For adjunct: HbA1c reduction ≥0.4% AND TIR increase ≥10 percentage points vs. placebo. For cell therapy: ≥50% insulin independence at 12 months.

symptoms: Increased thirst and urination, fatigue, blurred vision, slow wound healing
treatment_goal: Lower HbA1c, prevent cardiovascular and renal complications
first_line_soc: Metformin (biguanide) 500–2000 mg/day as foundational first-line therapy combined with lifestyle modification (weight loss, diet, exercise); updated 2022–2024 ADA/EASD guidelines now recommend GLP-1 receptor agonists or SGLT-2 inhibitors as first-line or early add-on in patients with established ASCVD, heart failure, or CKD regardless of HbA1c.
treatment_algorithm: Step 1: Lifestyle modification + metformin (if tolerated/not contraindicated); Step 2: Add GLP-1 RA (semaglutide [Ozempic SC, Rybelsus PO], dulaglutide [Trulicity], liraglutide [Victoza]) — preferred in ASCVD/obesity/CKD; Step 3: Add SGLT-2 inhibitor (empagliflozin [Jardiance], dapagliflozin [Farxiga], canagliflozin [Invokana]) — preferred in HF/CKD; Step 4: DPP-4 inhibitors (sitagliptin [Januvia], alogliptin, linagliptin [Tradjenta]) — weight-neutral, low hypoglycemia risk; Step 5: Sulfonylureas (glipizide, glimepiride, glyburide) — low cost, hypoglycemia risk; Step 6: Thiazolidinediones (pioglitazone [Actos]) — weight gain, fluid retention; Step 7: Insulin (basal ± prandial) — progressive disease; Step 8: GIP/GLP-1 dual agonist: tirzepatide (Mounjaro — FDA approved 2022 for T2D; Zepbound 2023 for obesity) — superior HbA1c reduction vs. semaglutide; Step 9: 2024: retatrutide (GLP-1/GIP/glucagon triple agonist) in Phase 3; oral semaglutide 25/50 mg (Rybelsus higher doses) trials; survodutide, orforglipron (oral GLP-1 small molecule, Phase 3 2024–2025); Step 10: Finerenone (Kerendia — mineralocorticoid receptor antagonist) for T2D + CKD; Step 11: Bariatric surgery for BMI ≥35 + T2D.
us_prevalence: ~38–40 million Americans with diagnosed T2D (CDC 2025); additional ~8–9 million undiagnosed; ~96 million with prediabetes. T2D accounts for ~90–95% of all diabetes cases. Annual incidence ~1.4–1.5 million new diagnoses.
patients_needing_tx: ~35–38 million with diagnosed T2D require pharmacologic management. ~28–30 million are actively on glucose-lowering medications. For novel premium agents (GLP-1 RAs, SGLT-2i, tirzepatide class): ~10–15 million currently on these agents with significant room for uptake expansion. Estimated ~12M on novel agents.
price: GLP-1 RAs (semaglutide/Ozempic): ~$10,000–$12,000/year WAC; Tirzepatide (Mounjaro): ~$13,000/year WAC; Novel triple agonists/next-gen: expected $12,000–$18,000/year; oral GLP-1 small molecules: potentially $8,000–$12,000/year. Blended novel drug price estimate: ~$12,000/year.
peak_sales: 12M × $12,000 × 20% = $28.8B (leading agent in a crowded market; semaglutide/tirzepatide already approach this scale individually)
registrational_endpoint: Change from baseline in HbA1c at 26 weeks (primary); key secondary: body weight change, fasting plasma glucose, proportion achieving HbA1c <7.0%; for cardiovascular indication: MACE (major adverse cardiovascular events) reduction in dedicated CVOT.
btd_threshold_vs_soc: HbA1c reduction ≥1.5% from baseline AND body weight reduction ≥10% at 26 weeks vs. placebo (or active comparator); for CVOT: ≥15% relative risk reduction in MACE vs. placebo. Tirzepatide demonstrated ~2.1% HbA1c reduction and ~12 kg weight loss — this sets the new benchmark.

symptoms: Shakiness, sweating, confusion, palpitations, seizure from low blood sugar
treatment_goal: Rapidly correct blood glucose, prevent recurrence
first_line_soc: Conscious patients: 15–20 g fast-acting carbohydrate (glucose tablets, juice) – '15-15 rule'; Unconscious/severe: glucagon injection (Glucagon Emergency Kit), intranasal glucagon (Baqsimi – FDA approved 2019), or IV dextrose 50% (D50).
treatment_algorithm: Step 1: Mild-moderate: oral glucose 15–20g; Step 2: Severe: IM/SC glucagon kit; intranasal glucagon (Baqsimi 3mg); dasiglucagon auto-injector (Zegalogue – FDA approved 2021); Step 3: IV dextrose (D50) for clinical settings; Step 4: For recurrent hypoglycemia: review insulin regimen, dietary modification, CGM; For insulinoma: diazoxide 3–8 mg/kg/day (suppresses insulin secretion); everolimus for malignant insulinoma; Step 5: Surgery for insulinoma. 2024: PCSK9i impact on hypoglycemia studied.
us_prevalence: ~2–4 million severe hypoglycemic episodes requiring treatment annually; ~100 million diabetic patients experience mild episodes.
patients_needing_tx: ~2–4 million severe events requiring emergency treatment. Estimated ~3M severe events/year.
