price: $200–$800/kit for emergency glucagon products.
registrational_endpoint: Time to blood glucose recovery ≥70 mg/dL within 15 minutes; treatment success rate.
btd_threshold_vs_soc: ≥95% glucose recovery within 10 minutes vs. ~85% for IM glucagon kit.

symptoms: Extreme thirst, confusion, rapid breathing, high/low blood sugar emergencies
treatment_goal: Correct hyperglycemia/ketoacidosis, restore metabolic balance
first_line_soc: IV insulin (regular insulin infusion) for DKA; 0.9% saline IV rehydration; potassium replacement; glucose for hypoglycemia: IV dextrose 50% or oral glucose.
treatment_algorithm: DKA: IV regular insulin 0.1 U/kg/hr, IV 0.9% NaCl, potassium replacement, bicarbonate for pH <6.9; Hyperglycemic hyperosmolar state (HHS): aggressive IV fluids, low-dose insulin; Hypoglycemia: glucose tablets, IV dextrose, glucagon (intramuscular or intranasal Baqsimi/Gvoke auto-injector – FDA approved); Euglycemic DKA (SGLT2 inhibitor-induced): hold SGLT2i, IV fluids, carbohydrates; 2023: dasiglucagon auto-injector (Zegalogue) for severe hypoglycemia.
us_prevalence: ~500,000–800,000 hospitalizations/year for DKA; ~300,000 for HHS; hypoglycemic events: millions/year.
patients_needing_tx: ~1 million acute hospital treatment events annually. Estimated ~1M.
price: $500–$2,000/acute episode (hospitalization costs much higher).
registrational_endpoint: Time to resolution of DKA (pH >7.3, bicarbonate ≥15, glucose <200 mg/dL) or hypoglycemia correction to glucose ≥70 mg/dL within 15 minutes.
btd_threshold_vs_soc: DKA: ≥90% resolution within 12 hours vs. ~75–80% for standard protocol; Hypoglycemia: ≥95% correction within 15 minutes vs. ~85% for IM glucagon.

symptoms: Low libido, erectile dysfunction, fatigue, muscle loss, infertility, low testosterone
treatment_goal: Restore normal testosterone levels, improve sexual function and QoL
first_line_soc: Testosterone replacement therapy (TRT): testosterone gel 1% (AndroGel), transdermal patch (Androderm), IM injection (testosterone cypionate or enanthate 100–200 mg q2 weeks).
treatment_algorithm: Androgen replacement: testosterone gel (AndroGel, Testim, Vogelxo), transdermal patch (Androderm), IM injections (cypionate, enanthate), SC pellets (Testopel), intranasal (Natesto – qTID); 2022: testosterone undecanoate oral (Jatenzo, Tlando, Kyzatrex); Clomiphene citrate/hCG for secondary hypogonadism wanting to preserve fertility; 2024: enclomiphene Phase 3 data; No major 2024–2025 TRT approvals.
us_prevalence: ~4–5 million American men with clinically diagnosed hypogonadism; millions more with borderline testosterone.
patients_needing_tx: ~2–4 million requiring pharmacologic TRT. Estimated ~3M.
registrational_endpoint: Achievement of total testosterone in normal range (300–1000 ng/dL) and IIEF or ADAM questionnaire improvement at Week 12.
btd_threshold_vs_soc: ≥90% testosterone normalization at Week 12 with convenient dosing (once-daily oral) vs. current gel/injection standards.

symptoms: Enlarged glandular breast tissue in males, tenderness, embarrassment
treatment_goal: Reduce breast tissue, identify hormonal cause
first_line_soc: Tamoxifen 10–20 mg/day (SERM – most evidence) for symptomatic gynecomastia; or raloxifene 60 mg/day as alternative; treat underlying cause.
treatment_algorithm: Step 1: Identify and remove cause (drug-induced most common: spironolactone, cimetidine, antipsychotics, anabolic steroids, estrogens); Step 2: Tamoxifen 10–20 mg/day (most effective medical therapy) for pubertal or idiopathic; Step 3: Raloxifene 60 mg/day (may be superior in some studies); Step 4: Aromatase inhibitor (anastrozole) for hypogonadism-related; Step 5: Surgery (reduction mammoplasty) for persistent/fibrotic disease not responding to medical therapy. No FDA-approved drug specifically for gynecomastia.
us_prevalence: ~30–40% of men may have some degree; ~3–5 million with clinically significant symptomatic gynecomastia seeking treatment.
patients_needing_tx: ~1–2 million seeking pharmacologic treatment. Estimated ~1.5M.
registrational_endpoint: Breast volume reduction ≥50% (MRI/ultrasound-based) and pain score improvement at Month 6.
btd_threshold_vs_soc: ≥50% volume reduction at Month 6 vs. ~35% for tamoxifen 20 mg/day.

symptoms: Muscle dysmorphia, dependency, withdrawal fatigue, hypogonadism, aggression
treatment_goal: Restore HPG axis, treat withdrawal, prevent relapse
first_line_soc: Testosterone replacement during withdrawal phase; clomiphene citrate 25–50 mg/day or hCG to restart HPG axis; supportive counseling.
treatment_algorithm: Step 1: Gradual AAS tapering or cessation; Step 2: hCG (human chorionic gonadotropin) 500–1000 IU 3x/week to stimulate testicular function; Step 3: Clomiphene citrate or tamoxifen to restore LH/FSH; Step 4: Testosterone replacement if hypogonadism persists; Step 5: Antidepressants (SSRIs) for mood/depression during withdrawal; behavioral therapy; No FDA-approved treatment specifically for AAS use disorder.
us_prevalence: ~3–4 million Americans have used AAS; ~1 million with current or recent use disorder.
patients_needing_tx: ~500,000–1,000,000 requiring pharmacologic treatment for withdrawal or hypogonadism. Estimated ~750K.
registrational_endpoint: Recovery of testosterone to normal range (>300 ng/dL) and FSH/LH normalization at 6 months.
btd_threshold_vs_soc: ≥70% testosterone normalization at 6 months vs. ~50% for clomiphene monotherapy.

symptoms: Elevated LDL/triglycerides or low HDL; usually asymptomatic until ASCVD events
treatment_goal: Reduce LDL-C to target, lower ASCVD event risk
first_line_soc: High-intensity statins: atorvastatin 40–80 mg/day or rosuvastatin 20–40 mg/day for high-risk patients.
treatment_algorithm: Step 1: Statins (atorvastatin, rosuvastatin – first-line per ACC/AHA); Step 2: Add ezetimibe 10 mg/day for LDL not at goal; Step 3: PCSK9 inhibitors: evolocumab (Repatha) or alirocumab (Praluent) for ASCVD or FH; inclisiran (Leqvio – siRNA, twice-yearly injection) 2021 approval; Step 4: Bempedoic acid (Nexletol/Nexlizet – 2020) for statin-intolerant; fibrates (fenofibrate) for hypertriglyceridemia; omega-3 fatty acids (icosapentaenoic acid/Vascepa – EPA only, 25% MACE reduction); Step 5: Lomitapide (Juxtapid) and mipomersen for homozygous FH; 2024: obicetrapib (CETP inhibitor) Phase 3 positive results.
us_prevalence: ~94 million US adults with total cholesterol ≥200 mg/dL; ~71 million with LDL ≥130 mg/dL; ~29 million with very high LDL or documented ASCVD needing intensive treatment.
patients_needing_tx: ~25–40 million requiring pharmacologic LDL-lowering treatment. Estimated ~35M.
price: $5,000–$15,000/year for a novel non-statin agent (PCSK9i ~$5,500–$14,000/year).
registrational_endpoint: Percent reduction from baseline LDL-C at Week 12 or 24.
btd_threshold_vs_soc: ≥60% LDL-C reduction at Week 12 vs. ~50% for maximally dosed rosuvastatin; or vs. ~55–60% for PCSK9i add-on.

disease: Isolated Elevated High-Density Lipoprotein Cholesterol (HDL-C) Levels
symptoms: Asymptomatic; incidentally high HDL on lipid panel
treatment_goal: Evaluate for secondary cause; no drug treatment needed
first_line_soc: None (isolated elevated HDL-C generally considered favorable or non-pathological; no pharmacologic intervention recommended).
treatment_algorithm: Step 1: Rule out secondary causes (vigorous exercise, alcohol, estrogens, cholesterol ester transfer protein [CETP] deficiency); Step 2: Evaluate overall cardiovascular risk; Step 3: No pharmacologic treatment indicated; lifestyle modification; Step 4: Extremely rare homozygous CETP deficiency: HDL >150 mg/dL may paradoxically increase ASCVD – obicetrapib not applicable here; No pharmacologic SoC.
us_prevalence: ~3–5 million Americans with HDL-C >80 mg/dL incidentally; very small fraction pathologically elevated.
patients_needing_tx: Minimal pharmacologic treatment needed. Estimated ~10,000–50,000 with CETP deficiency or paradoxically risky HDL. ~30K.
price: $5,000–$15,000/year for a hypothetical CETP modulator or HDL functionality enhancer.
registrational_endpoint: Reduction in ASCVD events (MACE) at 3–5 years in patients with dysfunctional HDL.
btd_threshold_vs_soc: ≥15% relative risk reduction in MACE vs. placebo in high-risk dysfunctional HDL population.

symptoms: Abnormally low cholesterol/triglycerides; often asymptomatic; fat malabsorption, neuropathy
treatment_goal: Identify cause, supplement fat-soluble vitamins, prevent neurological complications
first_line_soc: Fat-soluble vitamin supplementation (vitamins A, D, E, K) for hypobetalipoproteinemia or abetalipoproteinemia; high-fat diet restriction.
treatment_algorithm: Abetalipoproteinemia: very high-dose vitamin E (100 mg/kg/day) to prevent ataxia; vitamins A, D, K supplementation; low-fat diet; medium-chain triglyceride supplements; Lomitapide-induced hypolipidemia: dose reduction; Familial hypobetalipoproteinemia: monitoring + vitamins; No specific pharmacologic approval for primary hypolipidemia.
us_prevalence: ~50,000–100,000 Americans with clinically significant primary hypolipidemia.
patients_needing_tx: ~30,000–60,000 requiring vitamin supplementation and monitoring. Estimated ~45K.
registrational_endpoint: Prevention of neurological deterioration (SARA ataxia scale stability) and normalization of fat-soluble vitamin levels at 12 months.
btd_threshold_vs_soc: ≥80% neurological stability at 12 months vs. ~60% for standard supplementation.

symptoms: Excess body fat (BMI ≥30), dyspnea, joint pain, metabolic comorbidities
treatment_goal: Achieve ≥15% body weight reduction, sustain weight loss long-term
first_line_soc: Lifestyle modification plus pharmacotherapy: semaglutide SC 2.4 mg/week (Wegovy – FDA approved 2021, ~15% weight loss) or tirzepatide SC 15 mg/week (Zepbound – FDA approved 2023, ~22% weight loss) as leading first-line agents.
treatment_algorithm: Step 1: Diet + exercise (caloric deficit); Step 2: Pharmacotherapy: semaglutide 2.4 mg SC weekly (Wegovy); tirzepatide 5–15 mg SC weekly (Zepbound); orlistat 120 mg TID (Xenical – oldest, modest efficacy); phentermine-topiramate ER (Qsymia); naltrexone-bupropion ER (Contrave); liraglutide 3 mg SC daily (Saxenda); Step 3: Bariatric surgery for BMI ≥40 or ≥35 + comorbidities; 2024: retatrutide Phase 3 (GLP/GIP/glucagon triple agonist – ~24% weight loss); cagrilintide+sema Phase 3; oral semaglutide (Rybelsus) for obesity trials; orforglipron (oral GLP-1) Phase 3 positive 2025.
us_prevalence: ~130 million US adults with obesity (BMI ≥30); ~60 million with BMI ≥35.
patients_needing_tx: ~50–70 million potentially eligible for pharmacotherapy; ~10–15 million currently using obesity drugs. Estimated addressable with novel agent: ~60M.
price: $12,000–$20,000/year (Wegovy ~$15,060/year; Zepbound ~$13,320/year WAC).
registrational_endpoint: ≥5% body weight loss responder rate AND mean % weight loss from baseline at Week 68.
btd_threshold_vs_soc: ≥20% mean weight loss AND ≥80% ≥5% responder rate at Week 68 vs. ~15% and ~69% for semaglutide 2.4 mg.

symptoms: Central obesity, high blood pressure, high blood sugar, abnormal cholesterol
treatment_goal: Reduce ASCVD and T2DM risk through metabolic parameter improvement
first_line_soc: Lifestyle: intensive diet and exercise (first-line); pharmacologic: treat individual components (statins for LDL, ACE/ARB for BP, metformin if pre-diabetes); GLP-1 RAs or SGLT2i increasingly used.
treatment_algorithm: Lifestyle modification (weight loss ≥5–7%); Treat each component: BP (RAAS inhibitors), LDL (statins), glucose (metformin, GLP-1 RAs); Emerging: GLP-1/GIP dual agonist tirzepatide (Mounjaro/Zepbound) – FDA approved for obesity/T2DM; semaglutide (Wegovy/Ozempic); These address multiple components simultaneously; SGLT2i for cardiometabolic risk; 2024: retatrutide (triple GLP/GIP/glucagon) Phase 3 positive.
us_prevalence: ~103 million US adults with metabolic syndrome (31.7% prevalence); majority overweight/obese.
patients_needing_tx: ~30–50 million seeking pharmacologic treatment. Estimated ~40M.
price: $5,000–$15,000/year for a comprehensive metabolic agent.
registrational_endpoint: Resolution of metabolic syndrome (no longer meeting ≥3 of 5 criteria) at 6–12 months.
btd_threshold_vs_soc: ≥40% syndrome resolution rate at 12 months vs. ~15–20% for metformin alone.

symptoms: Peripheral neuropathy, enlarged organs, endocrine dysfunction, M-protein, skin changes
treatment_goal: Suppress underlying plasma cell dyscrasia, improve neurological function
first_line_soc: High-dose melphalan + autologous stem cell transplant (ASCT) for eligible patients; for non-eligible: lenalidomide (Revlimid) 10–25 mg/day cyclically + dexamethasone.
treatment_algorithm: Step 1: Assess ASCT eligibility; Step 2: Induction then ASCT (best long-term outcomes); Step 3: Non-ASCT: lenalidomide + dexamethasone; thalidomide + dexamethasone; bortezomib-based; Step 4: RT for solitary plasmacytoma; Step 5: Anti-VEGF: bevacizumab (off-label, targets elevated VEGF central to POEMS pathology); 2024: isatuximab + carfilzomib under investigation.
us_prevalence: ~2,000–5,000 Americans with POEMS syndrome; extremely rare.
patients_needing_tx: ~2,000–5,000 requiring pharmacologic treatment. Estimated ~3,500.
price: $100,000–$200,000/year for anti-VEGF or novel plasma cell-targeting agent.
registrational_endpoint: Hematologic response rate (PR or better per uniform response criteria) and neurological improvement (mNIS+7 change) at 12 months.
btd_threshold_vs_soc: ≥60% overall hematologic response AND ≥30% neurological improvement at 12 months vs. ~40% for lenalidomide+dex.

symptoms: Bones pain, kidney stones, fatigue, nausea, confusion, high calcium
treatment_goal: Normalize calcium and PTH, prevent organ damage
first_line_soc: Surgical: parathyroidectomy is curative and first-line for primary HPT. Medical: cinacalcet (Sensipar/calcimimetic) 30–180 mg/day for patients not surgical candidates.
treatment_algorithm: Primary HPT: parathyroidectomy (curative); Cinacalcet (Sensipar) – lowers calcium and PTH for inoperable; for secondary/tertiary HPT (CKD): cinacalcet, etelcalcetide (Parsabiv – IV for dialysis patients; 2017 approval), paricalcitol (Zemplar), doxercalciferol; 2024: upacicalcet (KP-2326) in trials for SHPT; Bisphosphonates (alendronate) for HPT-related osteoporosis.
us_prevalence: ~100,000–200,000 new cases of primary HPT/year; ~2 million prevalent US patients.
patients_needing_tx: ~500,000–1,000,000 requiring pharmacologic treatment (those not surgical candidates + secondary HPT in CKD). Estimated ~700K.
registrational_endpoint: Normalization of serum calcium (<10.2 mg/dL) and >30% reduction in PTH from baseline at Week 26.
btd_threshold_vs_soc: ≥60% calcium normalization at Week 26 vs. ~40–50% for cinacalcet.

symptoms: Muscle cramps, tingling around mouth, seizures from low calcium
treatment_goal: Maintain safe calcium levels, prevent hypocalcemic crises
first_line_soc: Calcium carbonate 1–3 g elemental calcium/day + active vitamin D (calcitriol 0.25–2 mcg/day) orally.
treatment_algorithm: Step 1: Calcium supplements (calcium carbonate, calcium citrate) + calcitriol (active vitamin D); Step 2: Magnesium supplementation if deficient; Step 3: PTH replacement: parathyroid hormone (1-84) – natpara (Shire/AbbVie – FDA approved 2015 for persistent hypoparathyroidism); Palopegteriparatide (TransCon PTH – Ascendis, FDA approved July 2024, once-weekly SC long-acting PTH 1-34 analog – major 2024 approval); Step 4: IV calcium gluconate for acute hypocalcemia; thiazide diuretics to reduce urinary calcium loss.
us_prevalence: ~100,000–120,000 Americans with chronic hypoparathyroidism; most common cause: post-thyroidectomy.
patients_needing_tx: ~100,000–120,000 requiring ongoing pharmacologic management. Estimated ~110K.
price: $30,000–$80,000/year (palopegteriparatide/TransCon PTH projected ~$60,000/year).
registrational_endpoint: Normalization of serum calcium (8.3–10.6 mg/dL) and albumin-corrected calcium without conventional therapy at Week 26.
btd_threshold_vs_soc: ≥70% albumin-corrected calcium normalization with 50% reduction in conventional therapy at 26 weeks.

symptoms: Elevated calcium, bone pain, kidney stones, muscle weakness, fatigue
treatment_goal: Resect parathyroid adenoma/carcinoma, normalize PTH and calcium
first_line_soc: Surgical parathyroidectomy is the definitive treatment. Medical management for inoperable: cinacalcet (Sensipar) 30–180 mg/day to lower serum calcium.
treatment_algorithm: Parathyroid adenoma: minimally invasive parathyroidectomy (curative >95%); Parathyroid carcinoma: en bloc resection; Post-op: calcium + vitamin D supplementation for hungry bone syndrome; Medical (inoperable/recurrent carcinoma): cinacalcet, bisphosphonates (pamidronate, zoledronic acid) for hypercalcemia; Denosumab for hypercalcemia in metastatic; 2024: No specific new pharmacologic approvals for parathyroid carcinoma.
us_prevalence: ~100,000–200,000 new parathyroid adenomas diagnosed annually; parathyroid carcinoma ~1,000/year (rare).
patients_needing_tx: ~50,000–100,000 requiring pharmacologic management pre/post-op or for inoperable. Estimated ~75K.
registrational_endpoint: Normalization of serum calcium and PTH at 6 months; for carcinoma: progression-free survival.
btd_threshold_vs_soc: ≥70% calcium normalization at 6 months vs. ~50% for cinacalcet; or ≥50% PFS improvement at 12 months for carcinoma.

symptoms: Fatigue, cold intolerance, low libido, short stature, adrenal crisis risk
treatment_goal: Replace all deficient pituitary hormones lifelong
first_line_soc: Hormone replacement therapy: hydrocortisone 15–25 mg/day (cortisol replacement – most critical); levothyroxine for TSH deficiency; testosterone/estrogen for gonadal axis.
treatment_algorithm: Cortisol: hydrocortisone 15–25 mg/day in divided doses; Thyroid: levothyroxine dosed to free T4 in upper half of normal range; Gonadal: testosterone (males), estrogen-progesterone (females); GH deficiency: somatropin (GH – Genotropin, Norditropin, Omnitrope) SC injection; ADH: desmopressin if posterior pituitary involved; 2024: lonapegsomatropin (Skytrofa – once-weekly GH – approved 2021 for pediatric GHD); somatrogon (Ngenla – once-weekly, approved 2022); No novel 2024–2025 panhypopituitarism approvals.
us_prevalence: ~80,000–120,000 Americans with generalized hypopituitarism (all axes).
patients_needing_tx: ~80,000–120,000 requiring lifelong multiple hormone replacement. Estimated ~100K.
price: $15,000–$40,000/year for comprehensive replacement including GH.
registrational_endpoint: Quality of life (AHQ-QoL score) and hormonal adequacy (all axes within normal range) at 12 months.
btd_threshold_vs_soc: ≥30% improvement in AHQ-QoL and all hormonal axes normalized in ≥80% vs. standard multi-drug replacement.

symptoms: Headache, visual field defects (bitemporal hemianopsia), hormone excess or deficiency
treatment_goal: Decompress optic chiasm, correct hormone dysfunction
first_line_soc: Depends on lesion type: non-functioning adenoma: surgical resection (transsphenoidal); prolactinoma: dopamine agonist (see Prolactinoma). Medical: cabergoline for prolactinoma, octreotide for TSH-oma.
treatment_algorithm: Non-functioning pituitary adenoma (most common): transsphenoidal surgery; radiation for residual; pharmacologic (no standard); Prolactinoma: cabergoline/bromocriptine; GH-secreting: see Acromegaly; TSH-oma: somatostatin analogs; ACTH-secreting: Cushing disease therapy; Rathke's cleft cyst: drainage; Craniopharyngioma: surgery + radiation; Hormone deficiency replacement per each axis. No novel 2024–2025 pharmacologic approvals for pituitary lesions per se.
us_prevalence: ~10,000–15,000 new pituitary adenomas/year; prevalent ~100,000–200,000.
patients_needing_tx: ~50,000–100,000 requiring pharmacologic management. Estimated ~75K.
registrational_endpoint: Tumor volume reduction ≥20% and restoration of hormone function at 12 months.
btd_threshold_vs_soc: ≥40% tumor volume reduction at 12 months vs. standard surgery alone.

symptoms: Symptoms depend on missing hormone: GH, LH/FSH, TSH, ACTH deficiency
treatment_goal: Replace the specific deficient hormone, monitor for evolution to panhypopituitarism
first_line_soc: Isolated GH deficiency: somatropin SC daily (or once-weekly: lonapegsomatropin/Skytrofa 2021, somatrogon/Ngenla 2022); Isolated hypogonadotropic hypogonadism: pulsatile GnRH or gonadotropins; Isolated ACTH: hydrocortisone; Isolated TSH: levothyroxine; Isolated ADH: desmopressin.
treatment_algorithm: GH deficiency: daily somatropin (Genotropin, Norditropin) or lonapegsomatropin (once-weekly, children); somatrogon (once-weekly, Pfizer); adults: somatropin SC; Isolated hypogonadotropism: pulsatile GnRH pump (Lutrepulse for women) or hCG + FSH; Isolated TSH: levothyroxine; Isolated ACTH: hydrocortisone; Isolated ADH: desmopressin; 2024: pituitary-targeted gene therapy in preclinical.
us_prevalence: ~50,000–100,000 Americans with isolated pituitary hormone deficiencies (GHD most common, ~50,000 adults).
patients_needing_tx: ~50,000–100,000 requiring pharmacologic replacement. Estimated ~75K.
price: $15,000–$50,000/year (adult GHD: somatropin ~$25–50K/year).
registrational_endpoint: For GHD: normalization of IGF-1 and improvement in body composition (lean mass/fat mass) at 12 months; disease-specific endpoints per deficiency.
btd_threshold_vs_soc: ≥80% IGF-1 normalization and ≥10% lean mass increase at 12 months with once-weekly dosing vs. ~70% and ~7% for daily somatropin.

symptoms: Enlarged hands, feet, jaw; headache, joint pain, soft tissue swelling
treatment_goal: Normalize IGF-1 and GH, reduce tumor size, relieve symptoms
first_line_soc: Surgical: transsphenoidal adenomectomy is definitive first-line. Medical: somatostatin receptor ligands (SRLs): octreotide LAR (Sandostatin LAR) 20–30 mg IM q4 weeks, or lanreotide (Somatuline Depot) 90–120 mg SC q4 weeks – first-line medical therapy.
treatment_algorithm: Step 1: Surgery (first-line, achieves remission in ~55%); Step 2: SRLs (octreotide LAR, lanreotide) for residual/recurrent; Step 3: Pegvisomant (Somavert – GH receptor antagonist) SC daily – most effective for normalizing IGF-1 (~90%); Step 4: Dopamine agonist (cabergoline) for modestly elevated IGF-1; Step 5: Pasireotide LAR (Signifor LAR) for incomplete SRL response; Combination SRL + pegvisomant; Radiation for persistent adenoma; 2024: paltusotine (oral SRL) Phase 3 positive.
us_prevalence: ~25,000–35,000 Americans with acromegaly; ~60 new gigantism cases/year.
patients_needing_tx: ~15,000–25,000 requiring pharmacologic treatment. Estimated ~20K.
price: $30,000–$120,000/year (pegvisomant ~$100K/year; SRLs ~$30–60K/year).
registrational_endpoint: Normalization of IGF-1 to age-/sex-adjusted normal range and GH <1 ng/mL at Week 24.
btd_threshold_vs_soc: ≥60% IGF-1 normalization at Week 24 with once-weekly or oral administration vs. ~55% for octreotide LAR.

symptoms: Extreme thirst, massive urine output (>3L/day), nocturia, dehydration
treatment_goal: Replace ADH/AVP, normalize urine output and osmolality
first_line_soc: Desmopressin acetate (DDAVP/dDAVP – ADH analog): intranasal, oral, or subcutaneous; standard dose 0.1–0.4 mg/day orally in divided doses.
treatment_algorithm: Desmopressin (DDAVP) via intranasal spray, oral tablet, subcutaneous injection, or sublingual lyophilisate (SL formulation approved in some countries); 2022: desmopressin acetate SL (Nocdurna) already approved for nocturia in the US; address underlying cause (pituitary tumor: surgery/radiation); Carbamazepine and chlorpropamide (enhance renal AVP sensitivity) for partial CDI; 2024: arginine vasopressin (Vasostrict) primarily for septic shock; no new CDI approvals.
us_prevalence: ~30,000–60,000 Americans with central DI; prevalence ~1 in 25,000.
patients_needing_tx: ~30,000–60,000 requiring lifelong desmopressin. Estimated ~45K.
registrational_endpoint: 24-hour urine volume reduction to ≤2L and normalization of urine osmolality (>300 mOsm/kg) at steady state.
btd_threshold_vs_soc: ≥80% urine volume reduction and normalization in ≥90% of patients vs. ~70–80% for intranasal DDAVP.

symptoms: Milky nipple discharge, irregular periods, infertility, reduced libido, headache
treatment_goal: Normalize prolactin levels, shrink tumor, restore gonadal function
first_line_soc: Dopamine agonists: cabergoline (Dostinex) 0.25–2 mg twice weekly (preferred – higher efficacy and tolerability) or bromocriptine 2.5–10 mg/day.
treatment_algorithm: Step 1: Cabergoline (first-line; ~80% normalize prolactin, >90% tumor shrinkage for microprolactinoma); Step 2: Bromocriptine (alternative, especially in pregnancy); Step 3: For drug-resistant/refractory: quinagolide (Europe); Step 4: Surgery (transsphenoidal) for drug-resistant macroadenoma or vision-threatening; Radiation for unresectable; 2024: temozolomide for aggressive/malignant prolactinoma; No new FDA approvals.
us_prevalence: ~60,000–100,000 Americans with prolactinoma; most common pituitary tumor (30–40% of all).
patients_needing_tx: ~60,000–100,000 requiring pharmacologic treatment. Estimated ~80K.
registrational_endpoint: Prolactin normalization rate (<20 mcg/L in women, <15 mcg/L in men) and tumor volume reduction ≥25% at 6 months.
btd_threshold_vs_soc: ≥90% prolactin normalization at 6 months vs. ~80% for cabergoline, especially in resistant cases.

symptoms: Fatigue, weight gain, cold intolerance, constipation, dry skin
treatment_goal: Normalize TSH and T4, relieve all hypothyroid symptoms
first_line_soc: Levothyroxine (synthetic T4): Synthroid, Levoxyl, Tirosint; dosed at 1.6–1.8 mcg/kg/day; target TSH 0.5–4.5 mIU/L.
treatment_algorithm: Levothyroxine (standard of care; generic and brand available); Combination T4+T3 (liothyronine/Cytomel 5–25 mcg/day) for persistent symptoms on T4 alone; Desiccated thyroid extract (Armour Thyroid – T4+T3 natural); Tirosint (liquid gel-cap levothyroxine for malabsorption); Myxedema coma: IV levothyroxine 200–500 mcg + IV liothyronine + hydrocortisone; 2024: long-acting T3/T4 formulations in development.
us_prevalence: ~20 million Americans with hypothyroidism; subclinical ~15 million additional.
patients_needing_tx: ~15–20 million requiring levothyroxine therapy. Estimated ~18M.
price: $1,000–$5,000/year for a novel formulation or combination.
registrational_endpoint: TSH normalization (0.5–4.5 mIU/L) and symptom relief score at 6 months vs. comparator.
btd_threshold_vs_soc: ≥90% TSH normalization AND ≥40% symptom score improvement at 6 months vs. standard levothyroxine.

symptoms: Palpitations, weight loss, heat intolerance, tremor, anxiety, exophthalmos
treatment_goal: Achieve euthyroid state, prevent cardiac and bone complications
first_line_soc: Thionamides: methimazole 10–40 mg/day (preferred) or propylthiouracil 100–300 mg TID (preferred in first trimester, thyroid storm); beta-blockers (propranolol 40–120 mg/day) for symptom control.
treatment_algorithm: Step 1: Thionamides (methimazole preferred; PTU for thyroid storm/pregnancy); Step 2: Beta-blockers (propranolol) for palpitations/tremor; Step 3: Radioactive iodine (RAI/I-131) – definitive; Step 4: Thyroidectomy; For Graves' ophthalmopathy: selenium, teprotumumab (Tepezza – IGF-1R monoclonal antibody, FDA approved Jan 2020 – landmark); 2024: iscalimab (anti-CD40L) Phase 3 for Graves' disease; 2025: oral olutasidenib for thyroid cancer (different indication).
us_prevalence: ~1.2% of US population (~4 million Americans) with hyperthyroidism; Graves' disease ~2–3 million.
patients_needing_tx: ~3–4 million requiring pharmacologic treatment. Estimated ~3.5M.
price: $5,000–$20,000/year for a novel immune-targeting agent.
registrational_endpoint: Euthyroid state at 12 months (TSH 0.5–4.5 mIU/L) and TRAb negativity rate for Graves' disease.
btd_threshold_vs_soc: ≥70% TRAb-negative remission at 12 months vs. ~45–50% for methimazole alone.

symptoms: Fatigue, weight gain, cold intolerance, depression, constipation
treatment_goal: Restore euthyroid state, normalize TSH
first_line_soc: Levothyroxine (T4 replacement – Synthroid, Levoxyl, Tirosint) dosed to maintain TSH 0.5–2.5 mIU/L; initiated when TSH >10 mIU/L or symptomatic hypothyroidism.
treatment_algorithm: Levothyroxine (gold standard): once-daily synthetic T4; target TSH 0.5–2.5 mIU/L; Combination T4+T3 (liothyronine) for persistent symptoms on T4 alone (controversial); Selenium supplementation 200 mcg/day may reduce TPO antibodies (supplemental); No immunologic therapy to arrest Hashimoto's approved; 2024: no major new approvals.
us_prevalence: ~14 million Americans with Hashimoto thyroiditis; most common cause of hypothyroidism.
patients_needing_tx: ~7–10 million requiring levothyroxine therapy. Estimated ~8M.
price: $2,000–$8,000/year for a novel immune-modulating or T4+T3 formulation.
registrational_endpoint: TSH normalization (0.5–2.5 mIU/L) and symptom score improvement at 6 months.
btd_threshold_vs_soc: ≥90% TSH normalization AND ≥30% symptom improvement vs. standard levothyroxine monotherapy.

symptoms: Usually asymptomatic; neck lump, rare dysphagia or hoarseness
treatment_goal: Exclude malignancy, manage symptomatic or toxic nodules
first_line_soc: Watchful waiting with ultrasound surveillance for benign nodules; fine-needle aspiration (FNA) for suspicious nodules. TSH suppression with levothyroxine rarely used. Radioactive iodine (RAI) or surgery for autonomous/toxic nodules.
treatment_algorithm: Step 1: Thyroid ultrasound + TSH measurement; Step 2: FNA biopsy for nodules ≥1 cm with suspicious features (ATA/ACR TI-RADS criteria); Step 3: Benign cytology → serial ultrasound surveillance; Step 4: Indeterminate (Bethesda III/IV) → molecular testing (ThyroSeq v3, Afirma GEC) to guide surgery; Step 5: Malignant or surgery-indicated → thyroidectomy ± RAI; Step 6: Toxic adenoma → RAI I-131 or surgery; Step 7: Ablation alternatives: ethanol ablation, RFA (radiofrequency ablation) for benign symptomatic nodules — growing adoption 2024–2025. No novel pharmacologic FDA approvals specific to benign thyroid nodule 2024–2025.
us_prevalence: ~300–600 million nodules worldwide; in the US, ~50–70% of adults have detectable thyroid nodules on ultrasound; clinically significant nodules requiring evaluation estimated at ~10–15 million prevalent cases. ~5% are malignant (thyroid cancer).
patients_needing_tx: ~2–4 million require active pharmacologic or interventional management (toxic, symptomatic, or malignant nodules). The vast majority of benign nodules are surveilled only. Estimated ~3M needing tx.
price: $8,000–$15,000/year for a novel targeted agent (e.g., RET/NTRK inhibitor repurposed for high-risk indeterminate nodules); molecular diagnostic tests command $3,000–$5,000 per test. A novel neoadjuvant pharmacologic agent is niche.
registrational_endpoint: Reduction in nodule volume ≥50% at 12 months (for pharmacologic/ablation treatment of benign nodules); or reduction in indeterminate FNA rate for molecular diagnostics.
btd_threshold_vs_soc: ≥50% nodule volume reduction at 12 months vs. <5% for watchful waiting; or molecular test NPV ≥97% avoiding unnecessary surgery.

symptoms: Painless neck mass, enlarged lymph nodes, hoarseness, dysphagia
treatment_goal: Achieve complete remission or durable disease control with minimized recurrence
first_line_soc: Surgical: total thyroidectomy ± neck dissection; then radioiodine (RAI/I-131) for differentiated thyroid cancer (DTC); levothyroxine TSH suppression therapy post-surgery.
treatment_algorithm: DTC (papillary/follicular): surgery + RAI + TSH suppression (levothyroxine); Lenvatinib (Lenvima – FDA approved 2015) and sorafenib (Nexavar – 2013) for RAI-refractory DTC; Cabozantinib (Cabometyx) for DTC 2021; RET fusion/mutation: selpercatinib (Retevmo – 2020), pralsetinib (Gavreto – 2020) for RET-mutant PTC and MTC; MTC: vandetanib (Caprelsa), cabozantinib (Cometriq/Cabometyx); ATC (anaplastic): BRAF V600E: dabrafenib + trametinib (Tafinlar+Mekinist – FDA approved 2018); 2024: RET/ALK/NTRK inhibitors expanding; lenvatinib + pembrolizumab combo for ATC.
us_prevalence: ~900,000 prevalent thyroid cancer survivors; ~43,000 new cases/year (2024 estimate); papillary most common (>85%).
patients_needing_tx: ~50,000–100,000 requiring pharmacologic treatment beyond RAI (advanced/metastatic/RAI-refractory). Estimated ~75K.
price: $120,000–$250,000/year for a targeted kinase inhibitor or combination.
registrational_endpoint: Progression-free survival (PFS) per RECIST 1.1 in RAI-refractory DTC or mutation-specific cohorts.
btd_threshold_vs_soc: ≥12 months median PFS in RAI-refractory DTC vs. ~12.6 months for lenvatinib; ≥50% ORR for BRAF V600E ATC combination.

symptoms: Visible neck swelling, pressure in throat, mostly asymptomatic
treatment_goal: Reduce goiter size, relieve compressive symptoms
first_line_soc: Levothyroxine (TSH suppression therapy) – controversial; observation for small goiters. Iodine supplementation where deficient (not relevant in iodine-sufficient US).
treatment_algorithm: Step 1: Observation for small asymptomatic goiter; Step 2: Levothyroxine (TSH suppression to 0.1–0.5 mIU/L – reduces goiter ~20%; cardiovascular/bone side effects limit use); Step 3: Radioiodine (I-131) for significant reduction in goiter volume (reduces 40–60%); Step 4: Surgery (thyroidectomy) for large compressive goiter; Iodine supplementation: no benefit in iodine-sufficient US.
us_prevalence: ~10–15 million Americans with nontoxic goiter (most subclinical/mild).
patients_needing_tx: ~500,000–1,500,000 requiring pharmacologic treatment. Estimated ~1M.
registrational_endpoint: Goiter volume reduction ≥30% (ultrasound) and symptom score improvement at 6 months.
btd_threshold_vs_soc: ≥40% goiter volume reduction at 6 months vs. ~20% for levothyroxine TSH suppression.

symptoms: Brief hyperthyroid phase then hypothyroidism; painless; postpartum form common
treatment_goal: Manage transient thyroid phases, prevent permanent hypothyroidism
first_line_soc: Beta-blockers (propranolol 10–40 mg TID) for symptomatic hyperthyroid phase (not anti-thyroid drugs – thyroiditis, not overproduction); levothyroxine for hypothyroid phase if symptomatic or TSH >10.
treatment_algorithm: Hyperthyroid phase: propranolol for symptoms (weeks to months, self-resolving); NSAIDs for mild discomfort; Hypothyroid phase: levothyroxine if symptomatic or TSH >10 mIU/L; usually temporary – try discontinuing levothyroxine after 12 months; Monitor TSH q4–6 weeks; Selenium supplementation for postpartum thyroiditis (trial data). No FDA-approved specific therapy.
us_prevalence: ~200,000–500,000 cases annually (postpartum thyroiditis in ~5% of postpartum women; ~2–3 million women/year deliver).
patients_needing_tx: ~100,000–300,000 requiring pharmacologic management. Estimated ~200K.
registrational_endpoint: Rate of permanent hypothyroidism at 12 months and thyroid function normalization.
btd_threshold_vs_soc: ≤5% permanent hypothyroidism at 12 months vs. ~25–30% historical rate without intervention.

symptoms: Painful tender thyroid, fever, jaw pain, transient hyperthyroid then hypothyroid
treatment_goal: Relieve thyroid pain, manage thyroid dysfunction phases
first_line_soc: NSAIDs (aspirin 2–4 g/day or ibuprofen 1600–3200 mg/day) for mild cases; prednisone 30–40 mg/day for severe pain/inflammation.
treatment_algorithm: Step 1: NSAIDs (mild cases); Step 2: Prednisone 40–60 mg/day x 2–4 weeks then taper (moderate-severe); Step 3: Beta-blockers (propranolol) for hyperthyroid symptoms; Step 4: Levothyroxine during hypothyroid phase if symptomatic; usually temporary; Step 5: Relapses (20–40%): repeat prednisone. IL-1 inhibitors (anakinra) in case reports for recurrent. No specific FDA-approved treatment.
us_prevalence: ~100,000–300,000 cases/year; preceding viral illness (often COVID-19-associated post-pandemic surge).
patients_needing_tx: ~80,000–250,000 requiring pharmacologic treatment. Estimated ~150K.
registrational_endpoint: Complete pain resolution (NRS ≤1) and normalization of thyroid function at Week 8.
btd_threshold_vs_soc: ≥80% pain resolution at Week 4 vs. ~60% for prednisone; recurrence rate ≤10% at 12 months.

symptoms: Abnormal thyroid labs during severe illness, no true thyroid disease
treatment_goal: Treat underlying illness; thyroid replacement generally not needed
first_line_soc: None (treat underlying critical illness – surgery, infection, trauma); thyroid hormone replacement is not indicated and may be harmful; supportive care.
treatment_algorithm: Step 1: Identify and treat underlying cause of systemic illness; Step 2: No thyroid hormone supplementation (replacement has not improved outcomes in clinical trials); Step 3: Nutritional support; Step 4: Monitor thyroid function as patient recovers; No pharmacologic SoC specific to ESS.
us_prevalence: ~75% of ICU patients have euthyroid sick syndrome; ~750,000–1,000,000 ICU patients affected annually.
patients_needing_tx: Minimal pharmacologic treatment; ~50,000–100,000 may receive investigational thyroid hormone. Not a significant drug market.
price: $1,000–$5,000/course for IV thyroid hormone analog if future evidence supports.
registrational_endpoint: ICU length of stay reduction, 30-day mortality, and restoration of normal T3 levels vs. placebo.
btd_threshold_vs_soc: ≥20% reduction in ICU mortality vs. placebo in critically ill patients with euthyroid sick syndrome.

symptoms: Severe weight loss, muscle wasting, edema, impaired immunity
treatment_goal: Restore weight and muscle mass; correct immune deficiency
first_line_soc: Oral nutritional supplements (ONS) such as Ensure, Boost providing 300–600 kcal/day with 15–25g protein. Medical nutrition therapy with registered dietitian. Enteral tube feeding for severe cases.
treatment_algorithm: Mild-moderate: ONS, dietary counseling, treat underlying disease. Severe (marasmus/kwashiorkor in US): gradual refeeding (avoid refeeding syndrome), high-calorie high-protein diet, EN/PN if needed. Vitamin/mineral supplementation. Phosphorus, potassium, magnesium monitoring during refeeding. Anabolic agents (oxandrolone, megestrol) for cancer-related/chronic disease wasting.
us_prevalence: ~3–5 million US patients with clinically significant PEU (hospitalized patients, elderly, cancer patients, chronic illness). ~30–50% of hospitalized patients are malnourished. Source: ASPEN, CDC.
patients_needing_tx: ~2–4 million patients requiring pharmacologic nutritional support or anabolic agents; significant commercial pool for ONS and appetite stimulants.
price: $5,000–$20,000/year for a novel anabolic/anti-cachexia agent.
peak_sales: $500M–$2B at 5–10% share for a novel anti-sarcopenia/cachexia agent; anamorelin (ghrelin agonist) achieved ~$200M in Japan.
registrational_endpoint: Increase in lean body mass ≥1.5 kg from baseline and improvement in handgrip strength at 12 weeks vs. placebo.
btd_threshold_vs_soc: ≥1.5 kg increase in LBM at 12 weeks vs. <0.3 kg for placebo in cancer cachexia.

symptoms: Scaly dermatitis, hair loss, impaired wound healing, growth failure
treatment_goal: Restore omega-3/omega-6 balance; resolve dermatologic symptoms
first_line_soc: IV lipid emulsion (e.g., Intralipid 20%, providing linoleic and linolenic acid) for TPN patients; oral fish oil or vegetable oil supplementation for dietary deficiency.
treatment_algorithm: TPN-related EFAD: add IV lipid emulsion (1–2 g/kg/day) to PN. Dietary EFAD: oral supplementation with linoleic acid (safflower oil) and alpha-linolenic acid; increase dietary fat intake. Topical linoleic acid cream for isolated skin manifestations. Correct underlying malabsorption.
us_prevalence: EFAD primarily in long-term TPN patients (~20,000–40,000 at risk) and fat malabsorption syndromes. True EFAD in general population is rare (<5,000). Source: ASPEN, NIH.
patients_needing_tx: ~15,000–25,000 patients with clinically significant EFAD requiring IV lipid or Rx supplementation.
price: $5,000–$20,000/year for a structured lipid emulsion or novel omega-3 formulation.
peak_sales: $30–60M at 20% share in the TPN/hospital segment.
registrational_endpoint: Normalization of triene-to-tetraene ratio (<0.2) at 4 weeks with IV lipid supplementation vs. fat-free PN.

symptoms: Fatigue, pallor, shortness of breath, pica, weakness
treatment_goal: Replenish iron stores; resolve anemia and symptoms
first_line_soc: Oral ferrous sulfate/ferrous gluconate 150–200 mg elemental iron/day in divided doses. IV iron (ferric carboxymaltose, ferumoxytol, low-molecular-weight iron dextran) for intolerance, malabsorption, or severe IDA.
treatment_algorithm: First-line: oral iron 3–6 months. Second-line: IV iron (ferric carboxymaltose [Injectafer], ferumoxytol [Feraheme], ferric derisomaltose [Monoferric]) for failure/intolerance. Identify and treat bleeding source. Red cell transfusion for severe symptomatic anemia. Erythropoietin-stimulating agents adjunctively in CKD.
us_prevalence: ~10 million US patients with iron deficiency anemia; ~20–30 million with iron deficiency without anemia. Source: CDC NHANES, ASH.
patients_needing_tx: ~8–12 million patients needing pharmacologic iron therapy (Rx oral or IV iron); ~3–5 million with IV iron indication.
price: $15,000–$25,000/year for IV iron course (multiple infusions); oral Rx iron $1,000–$3,000/year.
peak_sales: $800M–$2B at 5–10% share (novel IV iron formulations); Feraheme/Injectafer comps at $400–500M/year.
registrational_endpoint: Increase in hemoglobin ≥1 g/dL from baseline and normalization of ferritin at 8 weeks vs. oral iron/IV iron comparator.
btd_threshold_vs_soc: ≥50% achievement of hemoglobin normalization at 8 weeks vs. ~30% for oral iron in moderate-severe IDA.

symptoms: Dermatitis, diarrhea, impaired immunity, poor wound healing, hypogonadism
treatment_goal: Restore zinc; improve immunity and skin health
first_line_soc: Oral zinc sulfate or zinc gluconate 25–50 mg elemental zinc/day (25–50 mg 3× daily for severe deficiency). IV zinc chloride for TPN patients.
treatment_algorithm: Mild: OTC zinc 8–15 mg/day. Moderate: zinc sulfate 220 mg TID × 6 weeks. Acrodermatitis enteropathica (genetic SLC39A4 mutation): lifelong zinc sulfate 30–150 mg/day. TPN: IV zinc 2.5–5 mg/day. Monitor copper levels (zinc inhibits copper absorption). Treat underlying cause.
us_prevalence: ~12% of US population mildly zinc deficient; clinical deficiency: ~1–3 million patients (elderly, alcoholics, malabsorption). Acrodermatitis enteropathica: ~100–200 US patients. Source: NHANES, NIH ODS.
patients_needing_tx: ~500,000–1,500,000 patients requiring pharmacologic zinc supplementation.
price: $1,000–$5,000/year for a novel highly bioavailable zinc formulation.
peak_sales: $50–100M at 5% share; dominated by cheap OTC generics.
registrational_endpoint: Normalization of plasma zinc (>70 mcg/dL) and resolution of skin lesions at 8 weeks vs. placebo.

symptoms: Dental caries, weakened tooth enamel, increased fracture risk
treatment_goal: Restore fluoride; prevent dental caries and bone loss
first_line_soc: Sodium fluoride supplements (0.25–1 mg/day orally, age-appropriate) or fluoride-containing toothpaste/mouthwash. Prescription fluoride for high-risk dental caries.
treatment_algorithm: Dietary fluoride supplementation based on community water fluoride levels. Rx sodium fluoride drops/tablets for children in low-fluoride areas. Fluoride varnish applied by dentist. No systemic pharmacologic Rx for fluoride deficiency per se.
us_prevalence: ~20–25% of US children in non-fluoridated water areas at risk for suboptimal intake; clinically significant deficiency causing disease: several million children. Source: CDC, AAP.
patients_needing_tx: ~2–5 million children/year receiving Rx fluoride supplements from pediatricians/dentists; ongoing need in non-fluoridated communities.
price: $100–$500/year for Rx fluoride supplement (low-cost generic).
peak_sales: $50–100M at 10% share; highly commoditized OTC/Rx generic market.
registrational_endpoint: Reduction in DMFT (decayed, missing, filled teeth) score vs. placebo at 2 years in high-caries-risk children.

symptoms: Goiter, hypothyroidism, fatigue, cretinism in infants
treatment_goal: Restore iodine; normalize thyroid function; prevent goiter
first_line_soc: Iodine supplementation (potassium iodide, iodized salt) for deficiency; levothyroxine if hypothyroidism established. Potassium iodide 150–300 mcg/day orally.
treatment_algorithm: Mild deficiency: iodized salt, OTC potassium iodide. Moderate-severe deficiency: Lugol's iodine or potassium iodide solution. Established hypothyroidism: levothyroxine. Goiter: iodine ± thyroid hormone suppression; surgery/RAI if obstructive. Pregnancy: 220 mcg/day iodine.
us_prevalence: US iodine deficiency relatively uncommon (~10–15% of population mildly deficient); clinical goiter/hypothyroidism from iodine deficiency: ~100,000–300,000. Source: CDC NHANES, ATA.
patients_needing_tx: ~150,000–250,000 patients with clinical disease requiring pharmacologic supplementation or levothyroxine.
price: $1,000–$5,000/year for a targeted iodine-thyroid axis therapy.
peak_sales: $30–50M at 10% share; dominated by cheap generics (levothyroxine).
registrational_endpoint: Normalization of TSH and free T4, reduction in goiter volume at 24 weeks.

symptoms: Anemia, neutropenia, neurologic deficits, bone abnormalities
treatment_goal: Restore copper; reverse hematologic and neurologic deficits
first_line_soc: Oral copper supplementation (copper gluconate or copper sulfate, 2–8 mg/day). IV copper for malabsorption. Copper chloride added to TPN.
treatment_algorithm: Mild-moderate deficiency: oral copper 2–8 mg/day × weeks. Severe/malabsorptive: IV copper 1–2 mg/day. Identify and treat underlying cause (bariatric surgery, zinc excess, celiac disease). No FDA-approved Rx drug specifically for copper deficiency.
us_prevalence: ~50,000–100,000 US patients with clinical copper deficiency, mostly post-bariatric surgery (>250,000 bariatric procedures/year) and GI malabsorption. Source: ASMBS, NIH ODS.
patients_needing_tx: ~30,000–60,000 patients requiring supplementation beyond OTC; ~10,000–15,000 with severe deficiency needing IV or Rx-grade copper.
price: $3,000–$10,000/year for a novel bioavailable copper formulation.
peak_sales: $30–50M at 15–20% share of addressable Rx segment.
registrational_endpoint: Normalization of serum ceruloplasmin and copper levels + improvement in neurologic/hematologic parameters at 12 weeks.

symptoms: Neurologic deterioration, seizures, lens dislocation, sulfite sensitivity
treatment_goal: Restore molybdenum cofactor; prevent neurologic damage
first_line_soc: Cyclic pyranopterin monophosphate (cPMP; fosdenopterin [Nulibry]) for molybdenum cofactor deficiency type A — FDA-approved in 2021 as first Rx for this condition. IV fosdenopterin 0.9 mg/kg/day.
treatment_algorithm: Fosdenopterin (Nulibry) IV daily: only approved treatment for MoCD type A. Dietary molybdenum supplementation for isolated dietary deficiency (extremely rare). Supportive seizure management with antiepileptics. Low-sulfite diet.
us_prevalence: Molybdenum cofactor deficiency (MoCD): ~100–200 affected individuals in the US, ~200 worldwide. Dietary molybdenum deficiency: virtually nonexistent in US. Source: NORD, FDA.
patients_needing_tx: ~100–200 US patients with MoCD type A eligible for fosdenopterin.
price: $750,000–$2,000,000/year (ultra-orphan pricing); Nulibry list price ~$1.7M/year.
peak_sales: $100–200M at 60–80% penetration (Nulibry is approved and only option).
registrational_endpoint: Reduction in urinary S-sulfocysteine levels and improved event-free survival vs. natural history at 12 months.
btd_threshold_vs_soc: Event-free survival improvement of ≥3 months median vs. natural history (typically <18 months median without treatment).

symptoms: Cardiomyopathy (Keshan disease), hypothyroidism, muscle weakness, cognitive impairment
treatment_goal: Restore selenium; prevent cardiac and thyroid complications
first_line_soc: Oral selenium supplementation (sodium selenite or selenomethionine, 50–200 mcg/day). IV selenium chloride for TPN patients.
treatment_algorithm: Dietary deficiency: OTC selenium 100–200 mcg/day. TPN-associated deficiency: IV selenium 20–60 mcg/day. Keshan disease (cardiomyopathy): selenium replacement plus cardiac support. ICU critical illness: IV selenium 500–1000 mcg/day (high-dose; evidence mixed). Autoimmune thyroiditis: selenium 200 mcg/day reduces thyroid antibodies.
us_prevalence: Clinically significant selenium deficiency: ~20,000–50,000 US patients (TPN, malabsorption, severe GI disease). Suboptimal selenium common in dialysis patients. Source: NIH ODS, NHANES.
patients_needing_tx: ~15,000–40,000 patients requiring pharmacologic selenium replacement.
price: $3,000–$10,000/year for a novel IV/oral selenium formulation.
registrational_endpoint: Normalization of plasma selenium (>100 mcg/L) and improvement in cardiac function (EF) at 12 weeks vs. placebo.

symptoms: Impaired glucose tolerance, weight loss, peripheral neuropathy
treatment_goal: Restore chromium levels; normalize glucose metabolism
first_line_soc: Chromium supplementation (chromium picolinate or chromium chloride, 200–1000 mcg/day orally). Typically OTC; IV chromium for TPN patients.
treatment_algorithm: OTC chromium picolinate 200–1000 mcg/day. For TPN-dependent patients: IV chromium chloride added to PN formulation. Dietary improvement with chromium-rich foods. No FDA-approved Rx product.
us_prevalence: True clinical chromium deficiency is rare; primarily seen in long-term TPN patients (~20,000–40,000 at risk). General population suboptimal intake is common but not clinically deficient. Source: NIH ODS.
patients_needing_tx: ~5,000–15,000 patients with clinically significant deficiency (TPN patients, severe malabsorption). Most others managed with OTC supplements.
price: $2,000–$8,000/year for a novel pharmaceutical-grade IV/oral formulation.
peak_sales: $5–10M at 20% share; very limited commercial opportunity given OTC alternatives.
registrational_endpoint: Improvement in fasting glucose and HbA1c vs. placebo at 24 weeks in TPN-dependent chromium-deficient patients.

symptoms: Growth retardation, skeletal abnormalities, impaired glucose tolerance, dermatitis
treatment_goal: Replenish manganese; correct metabolic abnormalities
first_line_soc: Manganese supplementation (manganese chloride or manganese sulfate, 2–5 mg/day orally). Added to TPN for deficient patients. No FDA-approved Rx product.
treatment_algorithm: Oral manganese supplementation or addition to PN formulation. Dietary correction with nuts, legumes, whole grains. Manganese deficiency is extremely rare in clinical practice.
us_prevalence: Clinically significant manganese deficiency: extremely rare (<1,000 US cases); mainly in long-term TPN patients or metabolic disorders. Source: NIH ODS.
patients_needing_tx: ~500–1,000 patients with documented clinical deficiency.
price: $2,000–$8,000/year for a novel bioavailable manganese formulation.
registrational_endpoint: Normalization of whole blood manganese levels and clinical symptom resolution at 8 weeks.

symptoms: Vomiting, abdominal pain, GI bleeding, liver failure, shock
treatment_goal: Remove excess iron; prevent multi-organ failure
first_line_soc: Deferoxamine (IV/IM chelation) for serum iron >500 mcg/dL or symptomatic toxicity. Oral deferiprone or deferasirox for chronic iron overload (thalassemia, hemochromatosis).
treatment_algorithm: Acute poisoning: gastric decontamination (whole bowel irrigation), IV deferoxamine until serum iron normal. Chronic iron overload (hereditary hemochromatosis): therapeutic phlebotomy weekly until ferritin <50; deferoxamine/deferasirox for patients unable to tolerate phlebotomy. Transfusion-related iron overload: oral deferasirox (Exjade) or deferiprone (Ferriprox). Genetic testing, family screening.
us_prevalence: Hereditary hemochromatosis: ~1 million US patients with HFE mutations causing iron overload. Transfusion-related iron overload (thalassemia, MDS, SCD): ~150,000–200,000. Acute iron poisoning: ~15,000–20,000 cases/year (mostly pediatric). Source: NIH, CDC.
patients_needing_tx: ~200,000–400,000 patients with clinically significant iron overload requiring chelation therapy; phlebotomy handles many hemochromatosis cases.
price: $20,000–$50,000/year for novel oral chelators; Exjade branded ~$40,000/year.
peak_sales: $500M–$1B at 5–10% share (highly competitive market with Exjade, Ferriprox generics).
registrational_endpoint: Reduction in serum ferritin by ≥50% from baseline at 52 weeks vs. comparator chelator.
btd_threshold_vs_soc: Ferritin <1,000 ng/mL in >60% of patients at 52 weeks vs. ~40% with deferasirox.

symptoms: Liver disease, Kayser-Fleischer rings, neuropsychiatric symptoms, hemolysis
treatment_goal: Reduce copper accumulation; prevent hepatic and neurologic damage
first_line_soc: D-penicillamine (500–1500 mg/day) or trientine (750–1500 mg/day) as first-line chelation. Zinc acetate (150 mg/day) as maintenance or first-line in pre-symptomatic patients.
treatment_algorithm: Symptomatic patients: D-penicillamine or trientine for decoppering. Penicillamine intolerance: switch to trientine. Maintenance after decoppering: zinc acetate or low-dose chelator. Hepatic failure: liver transplant (curative). Monitoring: serum copper, ceruloplasmin, 24h urine copper, LFTs. Tetrathiomolybdate (ammonium tetrathiomolybdate/ALXN1840) in Phase 3 trials as novel first-line. Dietary copper restriction.
us_prevalence: ~30,000 US patients with Wilson disease; ~3,000–4,000 new diagnoses/year. Source: Wilson Disease Association, NORD.
patients_needing_tx: ~25,000–30,000 patients on lifelong chelation or zinc therapy.
price: $15,000–$50,000/year for novel chelators (e.g., ALXN1840 tetrathiomolybdate); generic trientine ~$5,000–$10,000/year.
peak_sales: $150–300M at 20–40% share for ALXN1840 or next-gen chelator (vs. D-penicillamine/trientine generics).
registrational_endpoint: Normalization of serum non-ceruloplasmin-bound copper (<15 mcg/dL) at 24 weeks vs. SoC chelator.
btd_threshold_vs_soc: Non-ceruloplasmin copper <15 mcg/dL in ≥70% of patients vs. ~40% with D-penicillamine at 24 weeks.

symptoms: Thyroid dysfunction, hyperthyroidism or hypothyroidism, goiter, metallic taste
treatment_goal: Reduce iodine load; normalize thyroid function
first_line_soc: Discontinue iodine source. Antithyroid drugs (methimazole) if hyperthyroidism develops; levothyroxine if hypothyroidism. Supportive care.
treatment_algorithm: Remove iodine exposure. Iodine-induced hyperthyroidism (Jod-Basedow): methimazole 10–40 mg/day; beta-blockers for symptom control. Iodine-induced hypothyroidism: levothyroxine. Monitor thyroid function every 4–6 weeks. No specific antidote.
us_prevalence: Iodine excess relatively common via supplements/amiodarone; clinically significant thyroid dysfunction from iodine toxicity: ~50,000–150,000. Amiodarone-induced thyroid disease alone: ~150,000–300,000. Source: NHANES, ATA.
patients_needing_tx: ~100,000–200,000 patients with amiodarone/iodine-induced thyroid dysfunction needing pharmacologic management.
price: $3,000–$10,000/year for a targeted thyroid-protective agent.
peak_sales: $30–60M at 5–10% share; dominated by methimazole and levothyroxine generics.
registrational_endpoint: Normalization of TSH within reference range at 24 weeks vs. standard antithyroid therapy.

symptoms: Nausea, liver failure, hemolytic anemia, neuropsychiatric symptoms
treatment_goal: Remove excess copper; prevent organ damage
first_line_soc: D-penicillamine (copper chelator, 750–1500 mg/day orally) or trientine (triethylenetetramine, 750–1250 mg/day) as first-line for Wilson disease-related copper overload; tetrathiomolybdate investigational.
treatment_algorithm: Acute poisoning: supportive care, gastric lavage, chelation with D-penicillamine or dimercaprol. Wilson disease (genetic copper overload): D-penicillamine or trientine as first-line; zinc acetate for maintenance or intolerance. Liver transplant for fulminant hepatic failure.
us_prevalence: Wilson disease: ~30,000 US patients. Non-Wilson copper toxicity (dietary, occupational): rare, <5,000 cases/year. Source: NORD, Wilson Disease Association.
patients_needing_tx: ~25,000–30,000 patients with Wilson disease needing ongoing chelation/zinc therapy; ~3,000–5,000 with acute toxic exposures.
price: $15,000–$40,000/year (branded chelator, e.g., TETA/trientine analogues).
peak_sales: $100–200M at 15–30% share (Wilson disease is the main driver).
registrational_endpoint: Improvement in serum free copper (<15 mcg/dL) and hepatic function vs. SoC chelator at 52 weeks in Wilson disease.
btd_threshold_vs_soc: Serum free copper normalized to <15 mcg/dL in >50% of patients vs. ~30% with D-penicillamine.

symptoms: Manganism: Parkinson-like tremor, psychiatric disturbances, cognitive decline
treatment_goal: Remove manganese; manage neurological symptoms
first_line_soc: Remove manganese exposure. Chelation with CaNa2EDTA or para-aminosalicylic acid (PAS) has some evidence. Levodopa for Parkinson-like symptoms (partial response).
treatment_algorithm: Eliminate exposure (occupational welding, contaminated water). CaNa2EDTA chelation: 1g IV/day × 5 days per cycle. PAS: 4g TID for 4-week cycles. Levodopa/carbidopa for motor symptoms. Supportive neurologic care. No FDA-approved treatment.
us_prevalence: ~500,000–1,000,000 workers exposed to manganese (welders); clinically significant manganism: ~10,000–50,000. Source: OSHA, ATSDR.
patients_needing_tx: ~10,000–30,000 workers with symptomatic manganism requiring medical intervention.
price: $15,000–$40,000/year for a targeted chelation or neuroprotective agent.
peak_sales: $40–80M at 10–15% share of the addressable market.
registrational_endpoint: Improvement in UPDRS motor score ≥30% from baseline vs. placebo/chelation at 24 weeks.
btd_threshold_vs_soc: ≥30% improvement in UPDRS at 24 weeks vs. ~10% with standard chelation.

symptoms: Liver/kidney damage, dermatitis, lung cancer risk (hexavalent Cr)
treatment_goal: Remove chromium exposure; support organ function
first_line_soc: None (no FDA-approved pharmacologic antidote). Supportive care: remove exposure, dialysis for renal failure, N-acetylcysteine for oxidative stress. Chelation therapy has limited evidence.
treatment_algorithm: Decontamination, supportive care, hemodialysis for severe renal toxicity. Ascorbic acid may reduce Cr(VI) to Cr(III). No approved pharmacologic treatment.
us_prevalence: Occupational hexavalent chromium toxicity: ~100,000–200,000 workers exposed; clinically significant toxicity requiring treatment: ~5,000–10,000/year. Source: OSHA, CDC ATSDR.
patients_needing_tx: ~5,000–10,000 patients/year with acute/subacute toxicity requiring medical intervention.
price: $10,000–$30,000/course for a targeted chelation agent or antioxidant therapy.
peak_sales: $15–25M at 20% share; niche occupational/environmental toxicology market.
registrational_endpoint: Reduction in urinary chromium levels and improvement in renal/hepatic function vs. supportive care at 4 weeks.

symptoms: Dental/skeletal fluorosis, joint pain, mottled teeth, bone fragility
treatment_goal: Remove fluoride source; manage skeletal complications
first_line_soc: None (no approved pharmacologic antidote). Remove fluoride exposure. Calcium and magnesium supplementation may reduce GI absorption. Supportive care for skeletal fluorosis.
treatment_algorithm: Eliminate fluoride source (water, food, toothpaste overingestion). Calcium supplements to reduce fluoride absorption. Vitamin C and antioxidants may help. Surgical intervention for severe skeletal deformity. No FDA-approved Rx treatment.
us_prevalence: Dental fluorosis: ~40% of US adolescents have some form (mostly mild/cosmetic); skeletal fluorosis requiring treatment: <10,000 US patients. Source: CDC NHANES.
patients_needing_tx: ~5,000–10,000 patients with moderate-severe fluorosis needing medical management.
price: $10,000–$30,000/year for a fluoride chelating or bone-modifying agent.
peak_sales: $10–20M at 15% share; very small niche market.
registrational_endpoint: Reduction in urinary fluoride excretion and improvement in bone density (DXA) vs. supportive care at 52 weeks.

symptoms: Nausea, vomiting, abdominal pain, copper deficiency, anemia
treatment_goal: Reduce zinc; manage copper deficiency and GI symptoms
first_line_soc: None (no antidote). Stop zinc source. Copper supplementation for secondary copper deficiency. Supportive GI care.
treatment_algorithm: Discontinue excess zinc intake. Treat secondary copper deficiency with copper supplementation (2–4 mg/day). Monitor CBC, ceruloplasmin. Supportive care for GI symptoms. EDTA chelation rarely used.
us_prevalence: Zinc toxicity: ~5,000–15,000 US cases/year from denture cream (Poligrip), excess supplementation, industrial exposure. Source: AAPCC, NIH ODS.
patients_needing_tx: ~5,000–10,000 patients/year requiring medical management.
price: $3,000–$10,000/year for a targeted copper-rescue/zinc-chelating formulation.
registrational_endpoint: Normalization of serum zinc and copper, and improvement in neurologic/hematologic parameters at 12 weeks.

symptoms: Selenosis: hair/nail loss, garlic breath, GI distress, neuropathy
treatment_goal: Remove selenium source; manage toxicity symptoms
first_line_soc: None (no antidote). Remove selenium source. Supportive care. No FDA-approved treatment.
treatment_algorithm: Discontinue selenium supplementation or remove exposure. Supportive care for GI symptoms, neuropathy. N-acetylcysteine may reduce oxidative stress. Monitor organ function. Dialysis for severe renal toxicity.
us_prevalence: Selenium toxicity: rare in US (<5,000 clinical cases/year), mostly from supplement overdose or industrial exposure. Source: ATSDR, CDC.
patients_needing_tx: ~2,000–5,000 patients/year requiring medical management.
price: $5,000–$15,000/course for an antioxidant/supportive agent.
registrational_endpoint: Reduction in serum selenium and resolution of symptoms at 4 weeks.

symptoms: Gout-like syndrome, joint pain, elevated uric acid
treatment_goal: Reduce molybdenum intake; manage uric acid/gout symptoms
first_line_soc: None specific. Discontinue excess molybdenum source. Treat gout symptoms with NSAIDs, colchicine, or allopurinol.
treatment_algorithm: Remove source of excess molybdenum. Standard gout management: NSAIDs, colchicine, allopurinol for hyperuricemia. No FDA-approved antidote or specific treatment.
us_prevalence: Clinically significant molybdenum toxicity: extremely rare (<500 US cases). Source: ATSDR, NIH ODS.
patients_needing_tx: ~200–500 patients with symptomatic toxicity.
price: $5,000–$15,000/course for a targeted antidote.
registrational_endpoint: Reduction in serum molybdenum and uric acid to normal range at 8 weeks.

symptoms: Inability to eat, malnutrition, weight loss, impaired healing
treatment_goal: Deliver nutrients enterally; maintain nutritional status
first_line_soc: Standard polymeric enteral formulas (e.g., Jevity, Osmolite) via nasogastric/nasojejunal tube or gastrostomy (PEG/PEJ). Disease-specific formulas for renal, hepatic, diabetic patients.
treatment_algorithm: Initiate enteral feeding within 24–48 hours of ICU admission when oral intake is inadequate. Use standard polymeric formula as first-line; switch to peptide-based (e.g., Peptamen) for malabsorption, semi-elemental for severe GI compromise, or immune-modulating formulas (glutamine, arginine, omega-3) per ASPEN/ESPEN guidelines. Prokinetics for feeding intolerance.
us_prevalence: ~500,000–600,000 US patients on home enteral nutrition per year; ~2 million hospital inpatients receiving enteral nutrition. Source: ASPEN, CMS.
patients_needing_tx: ~500,000 home EN patients + ~2M inpatients; TAM for novel formulas or adjunctive pharmacologic agents is the home EN population (~500,000).
price: $8,000–$20,000/year for a novel disease-specific formula with Rx designation.
peak_sales: $500M–$1B at 10–15% share in disease-specific/novel EN segment.
registrational_endpoint: Body weight maintenance or gain ≥2% and improvement in nutritional markers (albumin, prealbumin) vs. standard formula at 12 weeks.

symptoms: Inability to absorb nutrients enterally; malnutrition; weight loss
treatment_goal: Deliver complete nutrition IV; maintain nitrogen balance
first_line_soc: Central or peripheral parenteral nutrition providing dextrose, amino acids, lipid emulsion, electrolytes, vitamins, trace elements. Standardized 3-in-1 admixtures (e.g., KABIVEN, Clinimix) or custom compounded PN.
treatment_algorithm: Initiate PN when enteral route unavailable >7 days (outpatients) or >3–7 days (critically ill). Central PN via PICC/Hickman for high-osmolarity formulations. Peripheral PN (PPN) for short-term. Monitor glucose, electrolytes, LFTs, triglycerides. Transition to enteral/oral as soon as feasible. Manage PN-associated liver disease (PNALD) with lipid minimization, fish-oil lipid emulsions (Omegaven).
us_prevalence: ~30,000–40,000 US patients on home PN; ~800,000–1,200,000 hospital inpatients receive PN/year. Source: ASPEN, CMS.
patients_needing_tx: ~30,000 home PN patients; hospital PN is standard hospital care not a drug market. Home PN is the commercial TAM.
price: $100,000–$200,000/year for home PN services; novel additives/formulations: $20,000–$50,000/year.
peak_sales: $200–500M at 5–10% share for novel PN formulation/additive innovations.
registrational_endpoint: Maintenance of lean body mass and serum albumin >3.5 g/dL at 12 weeks vs. standard PN.

disease: Nutritional Support for Patients Who Are Dying or Have Severe Dementia
symptoms: Inability to eat, weight loss, aspiration risk, progressive cognitive decline
treatment_goal: Comfort feeding; prevent aspiration; maintain dignity
first_line_soc: Careful hand feeding (oral assisted feeding) is preferred over tube feeding for patients with severe dementia or at end of life. No pharmacologic SoC for nutritional support in this context.
treatment_algorithm: Careful assisted oral feeding × best available comfort. PEG tube placement generally not recommended for advanced dementia (does not improve survival, QoL, or reduce aspiration). Parenteral nutrition is not indicated. Appetite stimulants (megestrol, dronabinol) have limited evidence. Goals-of-care discussion, hospice referral.
us_prevalence: ~1.6 million US nursing home residents with severe dementia; ~1.5–2 million US patients/year on hospice or end-of-life care. Source: CDC, NHPCO.
patients_needing_tx: ~500,000–800,000 patients where nutritional support decisions are actively being made; pharmacologic appetite stimulants used in ~100,000–200,000.
price: $3,000–$10,000/year for a novel appetite stimulant or anti-sarcopenia agent.
peak_sales: $50–100M at 10% share for a novel evidence-based appetite stimulant or anti-cachexia agent in this setting.
registrational_endpoint: Maintained body weight (≤5% loss) and improved caloric intake vs. placebo at 12 weeks in severe dementia patients.

symptoms: Bone pain, muscle weakness, rickets in children, osteomalacia in adults
treatment_goal: Restore vitamin D; normalize calcium, PTH; improve bone density
first_line_soc: Oral cholecalciferol (vitamin D3) 50,000 IU/week × 8–12 weeks, then 1,500–2,000 IU/day maintenance. Ergocalciferol (D2) is alternative. Calcitriol for renal failure. Paricalcitol for secondary hyperparathyroidism in CKD.
treatment_algorithm: Deficiency (<20 ng/mL): vitamin D3 50,000 IU/week × 8–12 weeks, then maintenance. Insufficiency (20–30 ng/mL): 1,500–2,000 IU/day. Vitamin D-dependent rickets type I: calcitriol. Type II (VDR mutation): very high-dose calcitriol ± calcium infusion. CKD-related: active vitamin D analogues (calcitriol, paricalcitol, doxercalciferol). Monitor serum calcium, phosphorus, PTH.
us_prevalence: ~41% of US adults vitamin D deficient (<20 ng/mL) = ~100 million; clinically significant deficiency with symptoms: ~10–20 million. Source: NHANES, Endocrine Society.
patients_needing_tx: ~10–20 million patients needing Rx-grade vitamin D supplementation or active vitamin D analogues.
price: $2,000–$10,000/year for a novel active vitamin D analogue or VDBP antagonist.
peak_sales: $500M–$2B at 3–5% share for a novel differentiated vitamin D product; paricalcitol (Zemplar) ~$600M peak as benchmark.
registrational_endpoint: Normalization of 25-OH-D (>30 ng/mL) and PTH, improvement in BMD by DXA at 52 weeks vs. standard vitamin D3.

symptoms: Macrocytic anemia, peripheral neuropathy, glossitis, cognitive impairment
treatment_goal: Replenish B12; reverse anemia and neurologic damage
first_line_soc: IM cyanocobalamin 1000 mcg/day × 7 days, then weekly × 4 weeks, then monthly (for pernicious anemia/malabsorption). High-dose oral cyanocobalamin 1000–2000 mcg/day for dietary deficiency.
treatment_algorithm: Pernicious anemia/malabsorption: IM cyanocobalamin (traditional) or high-dose oral B12 (equally effective per trials). Dietary deficiency: oral B12 supplements. Intranasal cyanocobalamin (Nascobal) for patients preferring non-injection route. Methylcobalamin for neuropathy (some preference). Monitor serum B12, MMA, homocysteine, CBC. Treat underlying cause (PA, metformin use, bariatric surgery, veganism).
us_prevalence: ~5–6 million US adults with B12 deficiency; ~1–2 million with clinically significant disease (anemia, neuropathy). Source: NHANES, NIH ODS.
patients_needing_tx: ~2–3 million patients requiring pharmacologic B12 replacement (Rx IM or high-dose oral).
price: $1,000–$5,000/year for a novel bioavailable B12 formulation (oral methylcobalamin, sublingual).
peak_sales: $100–250M at 5% share for a novel premium B12 formulation; intranasal Nascobal ~$60M/year as benchmark.
registrational_endpoint: Normalization of serum B12 (>300 pg/mL), MMA, and hemoglobin at 12 weeks vs. IM cyanocobalamin.

symptoms: Easy bruising, prolonged bleeding, elevated PT/INR, newborn hemorrhage
treatment_goal: Restore vitamin K; normalize coagulation factors
first_line_soc: Oral or IV phytonadione (vitamin K1, Mephyton) 2.5–25 mg. IV/IM vitamin K for severe bleeding or INR >10. Vitamin K1 injection for newborns.
treatment_algorithm: Mild: oral vitamin K1 1–10 mg/day. Warfarin reversal: vitamin K1 2.5–10 mg IV slowly. Hemorrhagic disease of newborn: prophylactic vitamin K1 1 mg IM at birth. Malabsorption/cholestasis: water-soluble vitamin K (K2, menaquinone) or parenteral K1. Monitor PT/INR.
us_prevalence: Vitamin K deficiency: ~500,000–1,000,000 US patients (warfarin users with subtherapeutic K, malabsorption, liver disease, antibiotics). Newborn hemorrhage: ~1,500–2,000/year without prophylaxis. Source: NHANES, AAP.
patients_needing_tx: ~500,000–800,000 patients/year requiring pharmacologic vitamin K for clinical deficiency or reversal.
price: $2,000–$10,000/year for a novel K2 formulation with cardiovascular benefits.
peak_sales: $50–150M at 5% share for a novel K2/K3 formulation; most vitamin K is generic and cheap.
registrational_endpoint: Normalization of PT/INR (<1.5) at 48 hours vs. placebo in vitamin K deficient patients.

symptoms: Peripheral neuropathy, seborrheic dermatitis, glossitis, depression, seizures
treatment_goal: Replenish pyridoxine; control seizures in B6-dependent epilepsy
first_line_soc: Oral pyridoxine (vitamin B6) 50–200 mg/day for deficiency. High-dose pyridoxine 10–30 mg/kg/day for pyridoxine-dependent epilepsy (PDE). IV pyridoxine for acute seizures.
treatment_algorithm: Dietary deficiency: OTC pyridoxine 50–200 mg/day. Pyridoxine-dependent epilepsy (ALDH7A1 mutation): high-dose pyridoxine lifelong ± arginine lysine restriction. Isoniazid-induced deficiency: prophylactic B6 25–50 mg/day. Monitor neuropathy with high doses. Antiquitin deficiency: pyridoxal 5-phosphate (P5P) may be superior to pyridoxine in some mutations.
us_prevalence: B6 dietary deficiency: ~10–20 million US adults mildly deficient; clinical deficiency: ~100,000–300,000. PDE (genetic): ~200–400 US patients. Source: NHANES, NORD.
patients_needing_tx: ~200,000–400,000 patients needing pharmacologic B6; ~300 with PDE needing high-dose prescription therapy.
price: $500–$3,000/year dietary; $50,000–$200,000/year for PDE (orphan indication).
peak_sales: $30–60M at 10% share for PDE + dietary B6 combined.
registrational_endpoint: Seizure-free rate ≥50% at 6 months with high-dose pyridoxine vs. baseline in PDE patients.
btd_threshold_vs_soc: Seizure-free rate ≥50% vs. historical ~20% with standard-dose pyridoxine in PDE.

symptoms: Megaloblastic anemia, fatigue, neural tube defects in pregnancy
treatment_goal: Restore folate; prevent anemia and neural tube defects
first_line_soc: Oral folic acid 1–5 mg/day. Folinic acid (leucovorin) for methotrexate-induced deficiency or malabsorption. IV/IM folate for severe cases.
treatment_algorithm: Oral folic acid 1–5 mg/day for dietary deficiency or malabsorption; 400–800 mcg/day for pregnancy prevention of NTDs. Leucovorin for methotrexate rescue or MTHFR mutation. Treat underlying cause (celiac, Crohn's, alcohol use). B12 must be ruled out before high-dose folate.
us_prevalence: ~1–2 million US adults with low folate levels; clinically significant deficiency (anemia): ~200,000–400,000. Pregnant women at risk: ~4 million/year. Source: CDC NHANES, NIH ODS.
patients_needing_tx: ~300,000–500,000 patients needing Rx-grade folate therapy; millions more benefit from OTC supplementation.
price: $500–$2,000/year for a novel bioavailable methylfolate formulation.
peak_sales: $30–60M at 10% share; dominated by cheap generics.
registrational_endpoint: Normalization of serum folate (>7 nmol/L) and hemoglobin at 8 weeks vs. placebo.

symptoms: Hair loss, dermatitis, fatigue, neurologic symptoms
treatment_goal: Replenish biotin/B5; resolve deficiency symptoms
first_line_soc: Oral biotin supplementation (5–20 mg/day) for biotin deficiency; oral pantothenic acid (calcium pantothenate, 5–10 mg/day) for B5 deficiency. Both are over-the-counter vitamins.
treatment_algorithm: Biotin deficiency: oral biotin 5–20 mg/day; for biotinidase deficiency (genetic), lifelong high-dose biotin (5–20 mg/day). Pantothenic acid deficiency: oral pantothenic acid or multivitamin. Diet correction is cornerstone. Parenteral administration for patients unable to take orally.
us_prevalence: Isolated biotin deficiency: ~20,000–50,000 US patients; pantothenic acid deficiency is extremely rare in isolation (~1,000–5,000). Biotinidase deficiency (genetic): ~1 in 60,000 births (~5,500 affected). Source: NIH ODS, NORD.
patients_needing_tx: ~25,000–55,000 patients needing pharmacologic-grade supplementation (primarily biotinidase deficiency and TPN-associated deficiency). Most dietary cases self-correct without Rx products.
price: $5,000–$15,000/year. Rationale: OTC vitamins dominate; a novel pharmaceutical-grade formulation for genetic biotinidase deficiency could command orphan pricing of $10,000–$50,000/yr, but mass-market would be low.
peak_sales: $30–60M at 20–30% share of the ~$300M TAM; limited by OTC competition and small genetic population.
registrational_endpoint: Normalization of serum biotin levels + resolution of clinical symptoms at 12 weeks (Phase 2/3 open-label for biotinidase deficiency).

symptoms: Beriberi: peripheral neuropathy, cardiac failure; Wernicke encephalopathy: confusion, ataxia
treatment_goal: Replenish thiamin urgently; reverse encephalopathy and neuropathy
first_line_soc: IV/IM thiamine (vitamin B1) 100–500 mg/day for Wernicke encephalopathy (high-dose IV thiamine before any glucose). Oral thiamine 100 mg TID for beriberi or chronic deficiency.
treatment_algorithm: Wernicke encephalopathy: IV thiamine 500 mg TID × 3 days, then 250 mg/day × 5 days before switching to oral. Wet beriberi (cardiac): IV thiamine. Dry beriberi (neuropathy): high-dose oral thiamine. Alcohol use disorder prophylaxis: oral thiamine 100 mg/day. TPN patients: thiamine added to formulation. Treat underlying cause.
us_prevalence: ~20,000–50,000 US patients with clinical thiamin deficiency annually; Wernicke encephalopathy: ~1–2% of autopsies, clinically ~12,000–25,000/year. Source: NHANES, UpToDate.
patients_needing_tx: ~30,000–50,000 patients requiring IV/IM thiamine or high-dose oral therapy annually.
price: $2,000–$10,000/course for a novel CNS-penetrant thiamine derivative.
peak_sales: $20–40M at 15% share; largely generic IV thiamine market.
registrational_endpoint: Improvement in Wernicke score (mental status, ataxia, eye movement) at 7 days vs. standard dose IV thiamine.

symptoms: Peripheral neuropathy, ataxia, muscle weakness, hemolytic anemia
treatment_goal: Replenish vitamin E; halt neurologic deterioration
first_line_soc: Oral vitamin E (alpha-tocopherol) 400–1200 IU/day. Water-soluble vitamin E (TPGS) for fat malabsorption. High-dose 100 mg/kg/day for abetalipoproteinemia.
treatment_algorithm: Dietary deficiency: OTC vitamin E 400–800 IU/day. Malabsorption (abetalipoproteinemia, cholestasis): water-soluble TPGS 25–200 mg/kg/day or high-dose oral alpha-tocopherol. Monitor plasma tocopherol levels and neurologic status. Ataxia with vitamin E deficiency (AVED): alpha-tocopherol 800–1200 mg/day lifelong.
us_prevalence: Severe vitamin E deficiency: ~5,000–20,000 US patients (fat malabsorption, abetalipoproteinemia, AVED). AVED: ~100–200 US patients. Source: NHANES, NORD.
patients_needing_tx: ~10,000–20,000 patients requiring Rx-grade vitamin E or TPGS formulations.
price: $3,000–$15,000/year for a novel bioavailable tocopherol formulation.
registrational_endpoint: Normalization of plasma alpha-tocopherol and improvement in ataxia rating scale at 12 months vs. standard vitamin E.

symptoms: Cheilosis, glossitis, corneal vascularization, seborrheic dermatitis, photophobia
treatment_goal: Replenish riboflavin; resolve mucocutaneous symptoms
first_line_soc: Oral riboflavin (vitamin B2) 5–30 mg/day for deficiency. OTC riboflavin 400 mg/day for migraine prophylaxis (separate indication).
treatment_algorithm: Oral riboflavin 5–30 mg/day × 2–6 weeks. Dietary improvement with dairy, meat, leafy vegetables. IM/IV riboflavin for malabsorption. Treat underlying cause. Complete resolution expected with supplementation.
us_prevalence: Clinical riboflavin deficiency: ~50,000–200,000 US patients, primarily in alcohol use disorder, malabsorption, elderly. Source: NHANES, NIH ODS.
patients_needing_tx: ~50,000–150,000 patients requiring Rx-level riboflavin supplementation.
price: $500–$2,000/year for a novel high-bioavailability riboflavin formulation.
peak_sales: $5–15M at 10% share; dominated by cheap generics.
registrational_endpoint: Normalization of erythrocyte glutathione reductase activity coefficient at 8 weeks vs. placebo.

symptoms: Scurvy: bleeding gums, petechiae, joint pain, impaired wound healing
treatment_goal: Replenish vitamin C; reverse scurvy symptoms quickly
first_line_soc: Oral ascorbic acid (vitamin C) 300–1000 mg/day × 1 month. Resolution of clinical symptoms expected within days–weeks.
treatment_algorithm: Oral vitamin C 300–1000 mg/day; IV ascorbic acid for severe cases or malabsorption. Dietary improvement with citrus fruits, vegetables. Treat underlying cause (alcoholism, dietary restriction, malabsorption). Resolution of perifollicular hemorrhages within 1–2 weeks, gum bleeding within days.
us_prevalence: ~5–7% of US adults are vitamin C deficient; clinical scurvy: ~50,000–150,000 cases/year, increasing in low-income and homeless populations. Source: NHANES, CDC.
patients_needing_tx: ~50,000–150,000 patients/year with clinical scurvy requiring therapeutic doses.
price: $300–$1,500/course for Rx ascorbic acid formulation.
registrational_endpoint: Resolution of scorbutic signs and normalization of plasma ascorbate (>23 mcmol/L) at 4 weeks vs. placebo.

symptoms: Night blindness, xerophthalmia, increased infection risk, dry skin
treatment_goal: Restore vitamin A; prevent blindness and immune impairment
