first_line_soc: Oral vitamin A (retinol) 200,000 IU × 2 days then 200,000 IU at day 14 for xerophthalmia (WHO protocol). Vitamin A palmitate IM for malabsorption.
treatment_algorithm: WHO high-dose supplementation protocol for children with xerophthalmia. Dietary improvement. For malabsorption: water-soluble retinol (AQUASOL A) IM or oral. Cystic fibrosis, celiac, Crohn's patients: regular monitoring and supplementation. Retinol 10,000–25,000 IU/day for adults with deficiency.
us_prevalence: US clinical deficiency: rare (~20,000–50,000 adults with malabsorption-related VAD). Subclinical deficiency in ~1% of children. Source: CDC NHANES, NIH ODS.
patients_needing_tx: ~15,000–30,000 US patients requiring pharmacologic Rx vitamin A supplementation.
price: $1,000–$5,000/year for a novel retinoid delivery system.
registrational_endpoint: Improvement in dark-adaptation threshold and serum retinol ≥0.7 mcmol/L at 12 weeks.

symptoms: Pellagra: dermatitis, diarrhea, dementia (3 Ds), mouth sores
treatment_goal: Replenish niacin; resolve pellagra symptoms rapidly
first_line_soc: Oral niacinamide (nicotinamide) 100–300 mg/day in divided doses (preferred over nicotinic acid to avoid flushing). Or niacin 100 mg TID for 2–4 weeks.
treatment_algorithm: Niacinamide 100 mg TID orally × 3–4 weeks. Tryptophan-rich diet. Address underlying cause (alcohol use disorder, carcinoid syndrome, isoniazid use). IV niacin for severe cases. Multivitamin supplementation. Resolution expected within days to weeks.
us_prevalence: Clinical pellagra is rare in the US (<5,000 cases/year); mainly in alcohol use disorder, poverty, carcinoid syndrome, or malabsorption. Source: CDC, UpToDate.
patients_needing_tx: ~3,000–5,000 patients/year with clinical pellagra requiring niacin therapy.
price: $500–$2,000/course for a novel extended-release niacinamide formulation.
registrational_endpoint: Resolution of dermatitis and GI symptoms at 4 weeks vs. placebo in pellagra patients.

symptoms: Flushing, hepatotoxicity, hyperglycemia, hyperuricemia, GI upset
treatment_goal: Manage niacin-related adverse effects; discontinue excess niacin
first_line_soc: None (stop niacin use). Symptomatic: aspirin 325 mg 30 min before dose reduces flushing; antihistamines. Hepatotoxicity: discontinue niacin, supportive care.
treatment_algorithm: Discontinue excess niacin supplementation. Extended-release forms cause less flushing than immediate-release. Symptomatic flushing: pre-treatment with aspirin or laropiprant (withdrawn in EU). Monitor LFTs, glucose, uric acid. No FDA-approved treatment for niacin toxicity per se.
us_prevalence: Niacin-induced toxicity: common in patients taking high-dose niacin for dyslipidemia (~1–3 million Americans take high-dose niacin). Significant hepatotoxicity: ~10,000–50,000/year. Source: AHA, NHANES.
patients_needing_tx: ~50,000–100,000 patients/year with clinically significant niacin-related adverse effects requiring medical management.
price: $5,000–$15,000/year for a flush-reducing niacin formulation (e.g., laropiprant analogue).
peak_sales: $30–80M at 5–15% share; market lost interest after CORONARY/AIM-HIGH trials showed limited CV benefit.
registrational_endpoint: Reduction in flushing episodes ≥50% vs. placebo at 8 weeks in patients requiring high-dose niacin.

symptoms: Sensory neuropathy, ataxia, dermatologic lesions, photosensitivity
treatment_goal: Discontinue excess B6; allow neuropathy to recover
first_line_soc: None (no antidote). Discontinue pyridoxine supplements >200 mg/day. Supportive care for neuropathy.
treatment_algorithm: Discontinue high-dose pyridoxine. Most sensory neuropathy slowly recovers over months after cessation. Physical therapy. Monitor nerve conduction studies. Educate on supplement safety limits.
us_prevalence: B6 toxicity: ~1–3 million US adults take excess B6 supplements; clinically significant neuropathy: ~50,000–150,000. Source: FDA, NIH ODS.
patients_needing_tx: ~50,000–100,000 patients with symptomatic sensory neuropathy from B6 toxicity.
price: $2,000–$8,000/year for a neuroprotective agent.
peak_sales: $15–30M at 5–10% share; no established drug for this indication.
registrational_endpoint: Improvement in nerve conduction studies and neuropathy symptom score at 24 weeks after B6 cessation vs. observation only.

symptoms: Hypercalcemia, nausea, polyuria, kidney stones, soft tissue calcification
treatment_goal: Reduce vitamin D; normalize calcium; prevent renal damage
first_line_soc: Stop vitamin D supplementation. IV fluids (normal saline) for hypercalcemia. Furosemide diuresis. Corticosteroids (prednisone 20–60 mg/day) to reduce intestinal calcium absorption.
treatment_algorithm: Discontinue vitamin D. IV saline hydration. Furosemide 40–80 mg IV for volume-replete patients. Prednisone 40–60 mg/day × weeks. Bisphosphonates (pamidronate) for severe hypercalcemia. Denosumab for refractory cases. Calcitonin for rapid calcium reduction. Monitor calcium, creatinine, EKG.
us_prevalence: Vitamin D toxicity from supplements: ~30,000–80,000 US cases/year (substantial rise with widespread supplementation). Source: AAPCC, NHANES.
patients_needing_tx: ~20,000–50,000 patients/year with symptomatic hypercalcemia from vitamin D toxicity.
price: $3,000–$15,000/course for a targeted CYP24A1-activating agent or vitamin D catabolism enhancer.
registrational_endpoint: Normalization of serum calcium and 25-OH-D at 4 weeks vs. standard care.

symptoms: GI upset, diarrhea, kidney stones (oxalate), hemolysis in G6PD deficiency
treatment_goal: Reduce vitamin C intake; prevent nephrolithiasis
first_line_soc: None (no antidote). Reduce vitamin C intake. Hydration for oxalate nephrolithiasis. Treat kidney stones per urologic guidelines.
treatment_algorithm: Discontinue high-dose vitamin C (>2g/day). Increase hydration. Urine alkalinization may reduce oxalate stone risk. Stone management: ESWL or ureteroscopy for symptomatic stones. No FDA-approved treatment specifically for vitamin C toxicity.
us_prevalence: Vitamin C-related kidney stones: ~50,000–100,000 cases/year (high-dose supplement users). Source: NHANES, urology literature.
patients_needing_tx: ~30,000–60,000 patients/year with symptomatic vitamin C-related complications requiring medical care.
price: $2,000–$8,000/year for a calcium oxalate crystal inhibitor.
registrational_endpoint: Reduction in urinary oxalate excretion and stone recurrence rate at 12 months vs. placebo.

symptoms: Headache, dizziness, nausea, liver damage, teratogenicity, bone pain
treatment_goal: Discontinue vitamin A; manage liver injury and ICP
first_line_soc: None specific. Discontinue vitamin A supplementation. Treat intracranial hypertension with acetazolamide or CSF drainage if pseudotumor cerebri.
treatment_algorithm: Discontinue all vitamin A. Treat symptoms: analgesics for bone pain, acetazolamide for pseudotumor. Monitor liver enzymes; hepatic fibrosis may require long-term monitoring. Teratogenesis: strict avoidance in pregnancy (isotretinoin, vitamin A >10,000 IU/day). Supportive care.
us_prevalence: Vitamin A toxicity: ~50,000–100,000 reported cases/year (mostly supplement overdose); clinically significant with organ damage: ~5,000–15,000/year. Source: AAPCC, NHANES.
patients_needing_tx: ~5,000–15,000 patients/year with symptomatic toxicity.
price: $3,000–$10,000/course for a targeted hepatoprotective agent.
registrational_endpoint: Normalization of serum retinol, liver enzymes, and resolution of intracranial hypertension at 4 weeks.

symptoms: Bleeding tendency, impaired wound healing, fatigue, GI upset
treatment_goal: Reduce vitamin E; manage anticoagulant interactions
first_line_soc: None (no antidote). Discontinue vitamin E supplements. Manage anticoagulation carefully (vitamin E inhibits vitamin K metabolism). No FDA-approved treatment.
treatment_algorithm: Discontinue high-dose vitamin E (>1,000 mg/day). Monitor INR in anticoagulated patients. Supportive care for bleeding; vitamin K reversal if needed.
us_prevalence: Clinical vitamin E toxicity: rare; ~5,000–20,000 patients/year with bleeding complications from high-dose E in anticoagulated patients. Source: NIH ODS.
patients_needing_tx: ~5,000–15,000 patients requiring medical attention for vitamin E-related complications.
price: $2,000–$8,000/year for a targeted vitamin K reversal or anticoagulant management agent.

symptoms: Vitamin K3 (menadione) toxicity: hemolytic anemia, jaundice, hyperbilirubinemia
treatment_goal: Remove vitamin K3 exposure; manage hemolysis
first_line_soc: None (no antidote). Discontinue vitamin K3 (menadione). Supportive care. Note: K1 and K2 are essentially non-toxic at physiologic doses.
treatment_algorithm: Discontinue menadione. Transfusion for severe hemolytic anemia. Exchange transfusion in neonates with hyperbilirubinemia. No FDA-approved treatment for vitamin K toxicity.
us_prevalence: Vitamin K toxicity: very rare in US since K3 (menadione) banned for human use. <500 cases/year from supplement overuse or K3 exposure. Source: NIH ODS.
patients_needing_tx: ~200–500 patients/year.
price: $3,000–$10,000/course for supportive hemolysis management.

symptoms: Sudden severe abdominal pain, nausea, vomiting, tenderness
treatment_goal: Identify cause, relieve pain, prevent complications
first_line_soc: IV opioid analgesics (morphine, hydromorphone) for pain control; NSAIDs as adjuncts. Antiemetics (ondansetron) for nausea.
treatment_algorithm: Initial: IV morphine or hydromorphone (opioid analgesics) for analgesia; ketorolac (NSAID) as adjunct; ondansetron for nausea. Antibiotics (piperacillin-tazobactam or cefoxitin) if infectious etiology suspected. Definitive treatment is typically surgical for causes such as appendicitis, bowel obstruction, or perforation. No single chronic pharmacologic SoC—treatment depends entirely on underlying etiology.
us_prevalence: ~8 million ED visits/year in the US present with acute abdominal pain as chief complaint (CDC/NCHS data); not a standalone disease entity.
patients_needing_tx: Virtually all ~8M ED visits require some pharmacologic symptom management (analgesics, antiemetics); ~1–2M require surgery. For novel drug purposes, the pharmacologically addressable population is ~2M moderate-to-severe non-surgical cases annually.
price: $500–$2,000 per episode (acute episodic use); novel analgesic/anti-inflammatory in acute setting would price as a hospital injectable at ~$1,500/course.
registrational_endpoint: NRS (Numeric Rating Scale) pain score reduction ≥30% at 60 minutes post-dose vs. placebo; responder rate as primary endpoint in acute pain trial.
btd_threshold_vs_soc: ≥40% reduction in NRS pain score vs. SoC (opioid) at 60 min, or non-inferiority with superior safety profile.

symptoms: Visible bulge, local pain, discomfort with exertion, nausea if incarcerated
treatment_goal: Repair defect, prevent incarceration and strangulation
first_line_soc: None (surgical repair is definitive; no pharmacologic SoC for the hernia itself). Pain management with NSAIDs/acetaminophen post-operatively.
treatment_algorithm: None for hernia repair—surgery (open or laparoscopic mesh repair) is the only definitive treatment. Post-op: NSAIDs (ibuprofen), acetaminophen for pain; opioids sparingly. No pharmacologic agents modify hernia biology. A novel drug targeting connective tissue/collagen repair is speculative.
us_prevalence: ~5 million Americans have abdominal wall hernias (ventral/incisional/umbilical); ~700,000 hernia repairs performed annually in the US.
patients_needing_tx: ~700,000/year undergo repair. A post-surgical recurrence-prevention drug (novel biologics/anti-adhesion) could target ~200,000–300,000/year at highest risk.
price: $3,000–$8,000 per course (adjunct to surgical repair; recurrence-prevention biologic).
registrational_endpoint: Hernia recurrence rate at 2 years post-repair (imaging-confirmed); superiority over surgical repair alone.
btd_threshold_vs_soc: ≥50% relative reduction in 2-year recurrence rate vs. mesh repair alone (e.g., 5% vs. 10%).

symptoms: Groin bulge, pain with lifting, dragging sensation, may incarcerate
treatment_goal: Repair defect, prevent incarceration, relieve pain
first_line_soc: None (surgical repair—open Lichtenstein mesh or laparoscopic TEP/TAPP—is definitive SoC). Post-op NSAIDs/acetaminophen for pain.
treatment_algorithm: Surgery is definitive (open or laparoscopic mesh repair). Pre-op: watchful waiting acceptable for asymptomatic males. Post-op pain management: NSAIDs (naproxen, ibuprofen), acetaminophen, local anesthetics (bupivacaine). A hypothetical pharmacologic agent could target recurrence prevention or collagen remodeling.
us_prevalence: ~700,000–800,000 inguinal hernia repairs performed annually in the US; lifetime prevalence ~27% in men, 3% in women. ~3–4 million Americans have diagnosed inguinal hernia.
patients_needing_tx: ~800,000/year require surgical repair. Novel recurrence-prevention adjunct addressable in ~400,000 high-risk patients.
price: $2,000–$5,000 per course (surgical adjunct).
registrational_endpoint: 2-year recurrence rate post-repair (clinical + imaging confirmation); superiority over mesh alone.
btd_threshold_vs_soc: ≥50% relative risk reduction in 2-year recurrence vs. standard mesh repair (e.g., 3% vs. 6%).

symptoms: Severe abdominal pain, vomiting, distension, inability to pass stool or gas
treatment_goal: Relieve obstruction, restore bowel function, prevent ischemia
first_line_soc: For small bowel obstruction (adhesive): IV fluids, NG decompression, bowel rest; water-soluble contrast (Gastrografin) both diagnostic and therapeutic. Surgical intervention for complete or strangulated obstruction.
treatment_algorithm: Step 1: IV fluids, electrolyte correction, NG decompression. Step 2: Gastrografin challenge (water-soluble contrast)—therapeutic for adhesive SBO (reduces need for surgery). Step 3: Surgery (lysis of adhesions, bowel resection) for failure of conservative management or strangulation. Colonic obstruction: endoscopic colonic stent as bridge or palliative. Alvimopan for post-operative management.
us_prevalence: ~300,000–350,000 hospitalizations annually for intestinal obstruction in the US (HCUP data). Adhesions account for ~60–70% of SBO cases.
patients_needing_tx: ~300,000/year require active pharmacologic + surgical management. A novel anti-adhesion or motility agent addressable in ~200,000/year.
price: $3,000–$8,000 per episode (acute inpatient).
registrational_endpoint: Resolution of obstruction without surgery at 72 hours (in adhesive SBO); time to resolution vs. standard decompression alone.
btd_threshold_vs_soc: ≥20% absolute improvement in non-operative resolution rate vs. NG decompression alone (e.g., 75% vs. 55%).

symptoms: Fever, localized abdominal pain, tenderness, leukocytosis
treatment_goal: Drain abscess, eradicate infection, prevent sepsis
first_line_soc: Percutaneous CT-guided drainage plus IV antibiotics: piperacillin-tazobactam (Zosyn) or cefoxitin + metronidazole (Flagyl) covering gram-negative and anaerobic bacteria.
treatment_algorithm: Step 1: Percutaneous image-guided drainage (interventional radiology). Step 2: IV antibiotics—piperacillin-tazobactam or cefepime + metronidazole; carbapenems (meropenem, ertapenem) for resistant organisms. Step 3: Culture-directed therapy de-escalation. Step 4: Surgical drainage if percutaneous fails. Duration: 4–7 days IV then oral step-down (amoxicillin-clavulanate, ciprofloxacin + metronidazole).
us_prevalence: ~100,000–150,000 cases/year in the US (post-surgical, diverticular, appendiceal, Crohn's-related); acute condition.
patients_needing_tx: Nearly all cases (~120,000/year) require drainage + antibiotics. Novel anti-infective addressable population: ~120,000/year.
price: $5,000–$15,000 per course (IV antibiotics, acute inpatient).
registrational_endpoint: Clinical cure (abscess resolution on imaging + clinical improvement) at day 28; response rate superiority vs. SoC antibiotics.
btd_threshold_vs_soc: Clinical cure rate ≥85% vs. ~70% with SoC; or 15% absolute improvement in cure rate.

symptoms: Sudden severe abdominal pain, nausea, vomiting, bloody stool
treatment_goal: Restore mesenteric blood flow, prevent bowel necrosis
first_line_soc: IV heparin (anticoagulant) immediately upon diagnosis; papaverine (vasodilator) via intra-arterial catheter for arterial occlusion. Surgical embolectomy or bowel resection is definitive.
treatment_algorithm: Step 1: Anticoagulation with IV unfractionated heparin. Step 2: For arterial occlusion—intra-arterial papaverine (vasodilator) or catheter-directed thrombolysis (tPA). Step 3: Surgical embolectomy/thrombectomy or percutaneous intervention. Step 4: Bowel resection if necrosis. For venous thrombosis: anticoagulation with heparin then warfarin/DOACs (rivaroxaban, apixaban). Post-op: anticoagulation long-term.
us_prevalence: ~30,000–40,000 cases/year in the US (estimated); rare, ~1–2 per 100,000 hospitalized patients (UpToDate/literature). Acute condition, not chronic prevalence.
patients_needing_tx: Nearly all diagnosed cases (~35,000/year) require urgent pharmacologic and/or surgical treatment. Pharmacologic-only addressable: ~10,000–15,000 (early-stage, catheter-directed).
price: $15,000–$30,000 per episode (acute inpatient setting; small market, severe disease).
registrational_endpoint: 30-day bowel viability/survival without major bowel resection; composite of mortality + major bowel resection at 30 days.
btd_threshold_vs_soc: ≥25% absolute reduction in 30-day composite mortality + major bowel resection vs. standard heparin + surgery.

symptoms: Absent bowel sounds, abdominal distension, nausea, inability to pass gas
treatment_goal: Restore bowel motility, relieve distension, prevent complications
first_line_soc: Supportive care (NPO, IV fluids, nasogastric decompression); alvimopan (Entereg; peripherally acting mu-opioid receptor antagonist) for post-operative ileus.
treatment_algorithm: Supportive: NPO, IV hydration, NG decompression, electrolyte correction. Step 1 pharmacologic: Alvimopan (Entereg)—mu-opioid receptor antagonist; FDA-approved for post-operative GI recovery acceleration; given 2–5 days in hospital setting. Neostigmine (acetylcholinesterase inhibitor) for acute colonic pseudo-obstruction (Ogilvie syndrome). Erythromycin (motilin agonist) as prokinetic. Metoclopramide (dopamine antagonist/prokinetic) for symptomatic relief.
us_prevalence: Post-operative ileus occurs in ~15–25% of abdominal surgeries; ~750,000 cases annually in the US.
patients_needing_tx: ~500,000–750,000 post-op cases annually; ~300,000 moderate-to-severe requiring pharmacologic acceleration. Alvimopan currently used in ~15–20% of eligible patients.
price: $500–$2,000 per course (inpatient 5-day course).
registrational_endpoint: Time to GI-2 recovery (first toleration of solid food AND first bowel movement post-surgery) vs. placebo.
btd_threshold_vs_soc: ≥12-hour reduction in GI-2 recovery time vs. alvimopan; or superiority in patients where alvimopan is contraindicated.

symptoms: Sudden left-sided abdominal cramping, bloody diarrhea, urgency
treatment_goal: Restore colonic blood flow, prevent necrosis and perforation
first_line_soc: Supportive: bowel rest, IV fluids, and broad-spectrum antibiotics (ciprofloxacin + metronidazole or piperacillin-tazobactam) to prevent bacterial translocation.
treatment_algorithm: Step 1: Bowel rest, IV hydration, NG decompression if ileus. Step 2: Broad-spectrum antibiotics (ciprofloxacin + metronidazole or amoxicillin-clavulanate) for moderate-severe cases. Step 3: Discontinue offending drugs (vasoconstrictors, NSAIDs, oral contraceptives). Step 4: Surgery (segmental colectomy) for gangrenous bowel or perforation. No specific approved pharmacologic agent—novel vasodilator or cytoprotective drug is speculative.
us_prevalence: ~17–20 per 100,000 hospitalizations; ~50,000–70,000 cases/year in the US (UpToDate estimates).
patients_needing_tx: ~60,000/year; ~40,000 moderate-to-severe requiring active pharmacologic management.
price: $3,000–$10,000 per episode (acute inpatient).
registrational_endpoint: Clinical resolution (cessation of rectal bleeding + pain) at 7 days without surgery; endoscopic mucosal healing at 4 weeks.
btd_threshold_vs_soc: Clinical resolution in ≥80% vs. ~65% with supportive care + antibiotics; or ≥30% reduction in surgical conversion rate.

symptoms: Right lower abdominal pain, nausea, vomiting, fever, rebound tenderness
treatment_goal: Eliminate infection, prevent perforation or recurrence
first_line_soc: Surgical appendectomy (standard); IV antibiotics (cefoxitin or piperacillin-tazobactam) perioperatively. Non-operative antibiotic management (amoxicillin-clavulanate or IV ertapenem) increasingly used for uncomplicated cases.
treatment_algorithm: Uncomplicated: Non-operative antibiotic therapy—IV ceftriaxone + metronidazole then oral amoxicillin-clavulanate (Augmentin) for 7–10 days (APPAC trial validated). Complicated/perforated: emergent appendectomy + broad-spectrum IV antibiotics (piperacillin-tazobactam, meropenem for resistant organisms). 2024: Non-operative management now guideline-endorsed for select uncomplicated cases.
us_prevalence: ~300,000 appendectomies/year in the US; ~350,000 new cases annually. Non-operative management applicable to ~70,000–100,000 uncomplicated cases.
patients_needing_tx: ~350,000 annual cases all require either surgery or antibiotics. Non-operative antibiotic-only candidates: ~80,000–100,000/year.
price: $2,000–$5,000 per course (acute antibiotic course; novel antimicrobial or anti-inflammatory).
registrational_endpoint: Treatment success (no appendectomy at 90 days + resolution of symptoms) in non-operative management arm.
btd_threshold_vs_soc: Non-operative success rate ≥80% at 1 year vs. ~70% with current antibiotic SoC.

disease: Acute Perforation of the Gastrointestinal Tract
symptoms: Sudden severe abdominal pain, board-like rigidity, fever, shock
treatment_goal: Control peritoneal contamination, prevent sepsis, restore GI integrity
first_line_soc: Broad-spectrum IV antibiotics (piperacillin-tazobactam or imipenem; carbapenems for severe cases) plus emergent surgical repair.
treatment_algorithm: Immediate: NPO, IV fluids, nasogastric decompression. Step 1: IV antibiotics covering gram-negatives and anaerobes—piperacillin-tazobactam (Zosyn) or cefoxitin + metronidazole (Flagyl). Step 2: Surgical intervention (primary repair, resection, or diversion). Step 3: Post-op broad-spectrum antibiotics continued 4–7 days. Antifungals (fluconazole) if Candida peritonitis suspected.
us_prevalence: ~15,000–25,000 annual cases in the US (peptic ulcer perforation most common); acute condition.
patients_needing_tx: All diagnosed cases (~20,000/year) require IV antibiotics; novel anti-infective or anti-inflammatory agent addressable in ~20,000/year.
price: $5,000–$15,000 per episode (acute inpatient).
registrational_endpoint: Clinical cure rate at day 28 (resolution of peritonitis, no re-operation); time to clinical resolution.
btd_threshold_vs_soc: Clinical cure rate ≥75% vs. ~55% with current SoC antibiotics; or 20% absolute improvement.

symptoms: Inability to control bowel movements, leakage of stool, urgency
treatment_goal: Restore continence, improve quality of life, reduce leakage episodes
first_line_soc: Dietary fiber supplementation + anti-diarrheal agents (loperamide; opioid receptor agonist) to firm stool consistency; pelvic floor physical therapy.
treatment_algorithm: Step 1: Dietary modification, fiber, loperamide (Imodium; mu-opioid receptor agonist) or diphenoxylate-atropine (Lomotil). Step 2: Pelvic floor rehabilitation (biofeedback). Step 3: Bulking agents (injectable dextranomer/hyaluronic acid—Solesta; FDA-approved). Step 4: Sacral nerve stimulation (InterStim device). Step 5: Surgical sphincteroplasty or graciloplasty. Clostridium difficile toxin-related incontinence: treat underlying cause.
us_prevalence: ~15–18 million US adults have fecal incontinence; ~1 in 3 patients presenting to gastroenterologists (NIDDK). Underreported.
patients_needing_tx: ~5–7 million seek medical treatment; ~2–3 million require pharmacologic intervention beyond lifestyle. Novel drug addressable: ~2–3M.
price: $3,000–$8,000/year (oral agent or local injectable).
registrational_endpoint: Mean weekly FI episodes reduction from baseline at 12 weeks vs. placebo; proportion achieving ≥50% reduction in FI episodes.
btd_threshold_vs_soc: ≥50% responder rate (≥50% reduction in FI episodes/week) ≥ 65% vs. ~35–40% placebo; or ≥30% improvement over loperamide.

symptoms: Chronic deep rectal/pelvic pain, pressure, spasm in sitting position
treatment_goal: Relieve pelvic floor muscle spasm, reduce chronic pain
first_line_soc: Pelvic floor physical therapy (biofeedback) is first-line; cyclobenzaprine or diazepam (muscle relaxants) as adjuncts.
treatment_algorithm: Step 1: Pelvic floor biofeedback and physiotherapy (most effective). Step 2: Muscle relaxants—cyclobenzaprine (Flexeril; tricyclic muscle relaxant) or diazepam (Valium) rectal suppositories. Step 3: Low-dose tricyclic antidepressants (amitriptyline; neuromodulator) for chronic pain. Step 4: Botulinum toxin A injection into levator ani muscle. Gabapentin/pregabalin (calcium channel alpha-2-delta ligands) for neuropathic component. No FDA-approved drug specifically for levator syndrome.
us_prevalence: ~7–8% of the US population experience chronic functional anorectal pain; ~10–15 million US adults. Levator syndrome specifically: ~5–6 million.
patients_needing_tx: ~2–3 million actively seek treatment; ~1–1.5M require pharmacologic therapy.
price: $2,000–$5,000/year (novel pelvic floor agent).
registrational_endpoint: Mean pelvic pain score (VAS/NRS) reduction from baseline at 12 weeks vs. placebo; responder rate (≥30% pain reduction).
btd_threshold_vs_soc: ≥50% responder rate at 12 weeks vs. ~25–30% with biofeedback alone; or ≥25% absolute improvement over muscle relaxants.

symptoms: Rectal pain, urgency, tenesmus, rectal bleeding, mucus discharge
treatment_goal: Achieve mucosal healing, eliminate infection, relieve symptoms
first_line_soc: For inflammatory (ulcerative proctitis): topical mesalamine suppositories 1g/day (5-ASA; anti-inflammatory). For infectious: pathogen-specific antibiotics (e.g., ceftriaxone + doxycycline for gonococcal; acyclovir for herpetic).
treatment_algorithm: Ulcerative proctitis: Topical mesalamine (Canasa suppositories) 1g/day; topical corticosteroids (hydrocortisone foam/suppositories) for acute flares; oral mesalamine added for refractory cases; systemic corticosteroids for severe proctitis. Infectious: Gonorrhea—ceftriaxone 500mg IM; Chlamydia—doxycycline 100mg BID × 7d; HSV—acyclovir 400mg TID × 7–10d; C. difficile—vancomycin PO or fidaxomicin. Radiation proctitis: sucralfate enemas, formalin instillation.
us_prevalence: Ulcerative proctitis: ~350,000–450,000 US patients (subset of UC). Infectious proctitis: ~200,000–300,000/year (STI-related, rising with MSM population).
patients_needing_tx: ~400,000 ulcerative proctitis patients need pharmacologic therapy; ~250,000 infectious proctitis cases need antibiotics annually.
price: $5,000–$15,000/year (novel topical biologic or small molecule for UC proctitis).
registrational_endpoint: Rectal mucosal healing (endoscopic score ≤1) at 8 weeks; combined clinical + endoscopic remission rate.
btd_threshold_vs_soc: Mucosal healing rate ≥60% at 8 weeks vs. ~35–45% with topical mesalamine alone.

symptoms: Chronic perianal discharge, pain, perianal skin irritation, recurrent abscesses
treatment_goal: Close fistula tract, preserve continence, prevent recurrence
first_line_soc: Surgical fistulotomy for simple low fistulas. Seton placement for complex fistulas. Infliximab (Remicade; anti-TNF biologic) for fistulizing Crohn's disease-associated fistulas.
treatment_algorithm: Simple fistulas: Surgical fistulotomy. Complex/high fistulas: Seton drainage, advancement flap, LIFT procedure. Crohn's fistulas: Step 1—infliximab (Remicade) IV induction/maintenance; adalimumab (Humira) as alternative. Step 2—vedolizumab (Entyvio) or ustekinumab (Stelara) for anti-TNF failures. 2023–2024: Darvadstrocel (Alofisel; adipose-derived mesenchymal stem cells)—EMA-approved for complex perianal fistulas in Crohn's; not yet FDA-approved. Filgotinib (Jyseleca) in trials.
us_prevalence: ~100,000–150,000 US patients with anorectal fistulas; ~35,000–50,000 Crohn's-related perianal fistulas (pharmacologically addressable with biologics).
patients_needing_tx: ~50,000 Crohn's-related fistula patients need biologics; ~100,000 non-Crohn's fistula patients managed surgically. Novel drug addressable: ~50,000–60,000.
price: $40,000–$80,000/year (biologic for Crohn's fistulas) or $30,000–$50,000 one-time cell therapy.
registrational_endpoint: Combined remission (clinical closure of all external fistula openings + absence of collections >2 cm on MRI) at 24 weeks.
btd_threshold_vs_soc: Combined remission rate ≥50% at 24 weeks vs. ~30–35% with infliximab alone.

symptoms: Intense anal itching, burning, perianal skin irritation, excoriation
treatment_goal: Eliminate causative factor, relieve itch, restore skin barrier
first_line_soc: Topical low-potency corticosteroid (hydrocortisone 1% cream) + skin barrier protection; elimination of irritants (soaps, foods). Capsaicin 0.006% cream for idiopathic pruritus ani.
treatment_algorithm: Step 1: Hygiene modification, eliminate irritating foods (coffee, spicy foods, citrus). Step 2: Topical hydrocortisone 1% cream (short-term; anti-inflammatory). Step 3: Topical capsaicin 0.006% (TRPV1 agonist; reduces itch signaling) for idiopathic. Step 4: Calcineurin inhibitors (tacrolimus 0.1% ointment; immunomodulator) for refractory cases. Treat underlying causes (pinworms: mebendazole; tinea: antifungals; hemorrhoids, fissures).
us_prevalence: ~5–10% of US adults experience pruritus ani; ~15–20 million people at some point. Clinically significant chronic cases: ~2–3 million.
patients_needing_tx: ~2–3 million require medical evaluation and pharmacologic treatment; most managed with OTC agents.
price: $500–$2,000/year (novel topical IL-31 antagonist or TRPV1 inhibitor).
registrational_endpoint: NRS itch score reduction ≥4 points from baseline at 4 weeks; proportion achieving itch-free status.
btd_threshold_vs_soc: ≥50% reduction in NRS itch score at 4 weeks vs. ~30% with topical hydrocortisone.

symptoms: Rectal bleeding, anal itching, pain, prolapsing tissue, discomfort
treatment_goal: Reduce symptoms, shrink hemorrhoids, prevent prolapse
first_line_soc: Dietary fiber (psyllium; bulking agent) + sitz baths + topical hydrocortisone/lidocaine (corticosteroid/anesthetic) for symptom relief.
treatment_algorithm: Grade I–II: Dietary fiber, increased fluid, sitz baths, topical agents (Preparation H: phenylephrine + witch hazel; hydrocortisone suppositories). Office procedures: rubber band ligation (most effective for internal hemorrhoids), sclerotherapy, infrared coagulation. Grade III–IV: Surgical hemorrhoidectomy. Novel: Micronized purified flavonoid fraction (Daflon; venotonics)—approved in Europe, widely used off-label in US for symptomatic relief.
us_prevalence: ~10–15 million Americans see a physician for hemorrhoids annually; ~75% of all adults will have hemorrhoids at some point. Active symptomatic prevalence: ~10 million.
patients_needing_tx: ~5–6 million require active pharmacologic management; most are managed with OTC products. Novel prescription drug addressable: ~2–3M symptomatic patients.
price: $300–$1,000/year (prescription oral or topical agent).
registrational_endpoint: Symptom score improvement (bleeding, pain, prolapse composite score) at 8 weeks; superiority vs. placebo or SoC.
btd_threshold_vs_soc: ≥40% improvement in composite hemorrhoid symptom score at 8 weeks vs. ~20% with topical SoC.

symptoms: Sharp anal pain during/after bowel movements, bright red rectal bleeding, skin tear
treatment_goal: Heal fissure, reduce internal sphincter spasm, prevent recurrence
first_line_soc: Topical nitroglycerin 0.2% (nitrate; relaxes internal anal sphincter) or topical diltiazem 2% (calcium channel blocker) applied to anus; sitz baths, fiber, stool softeners.
treatment_algorithm: Step 1: Fiber supplementation, stool softeners (docusate), sitz baths, topical anesthetics (lidocaine). Step 2: Topical nitroglycerin 0.2–0.4% (nitrate; ~50% heal rate) or topical diltiazem 2% (CCB; similar efficacy, fewer headaches). Step 3: Botulinum toxin A injection (Botox) into internal anal sphincter (~60–80% heal rate). Step 4: Surgical lateral internal sphincterotomy for refractory cases. 2024: Topical nifedipine cream also used.
us_prevalence: ~500,000–700,000 US adults have active anal fissures at any given time; annual incidence ~300,000 new cases.
patients_needing_tx: ~400,000–500,000 require pharmacologic therapy (topical agents or Botox); many resolve spontaneously. ~150,000 require Botox or surgery.
price: $200–$800 per course (topical); $1,500–$3,000 for novel injectable agent.
registrational_endpoint: Fissure healing rate at 8 weeks (clinical examination confirmation); pain NRS reduction as secondary endpoint.
btd_threshold_vs_soc: Healing rate ≥75% at 8 weeks vs. ~50% with topical nitroglycerin; or superiority in recurrence rate at 6 months.

symptoms: Protrusion of rectal tissue through anus, mucus discharge, incontinence
treatment_goal: Restore anatomy, preserve continence, prevent recurrence
first_line_soc: None pharmacologic—surgical repair (rectopexy or Altemeier procedure) is definitive. Manual reduction with osmotic agents (sugar/hypertonic solution) for acute prolapse reduction.
treatment_algorithm: Acute reduction: Manual reduction, hypertonic solution (sugar) to reduce edema. Definitive: Surgical rectopexy (laparoscopic or robotic) or perineal rectosigmoidectomy (Altemeier). Associated fecal incontinence: loperamide, biofeedback, sacral nerve stimulation. No pharmacologic agent approved for the prolapse itself.
us_prevalence: ~250,000–350,000 US adults with rectal prolapse; ~30,000–40,000 surgeries/year. More common in elderly women.
patients_needing_tx: ~30,000–40,000/year require surgical intervention; ~100,000 patients managed conservatively with pharmacologic support for associated symptoms.
price: $2,000–$5,000/year (hypothetical agent targeting pelvic floor support or continence).
registrational_endpoint: Prolapse recurrence rate at 1 year post-surgical repair; continence score improvement at 6 months.
btd_threshold_vs_soc: Recurrence-free rate ≥90% at 1 year vs. ~75–80% with surgical repair alone.

symptoms: Severe anal pain, swelling, erythema, fever, fluctuant perianal mass
treatment_goal: Drain abscess, eradicate infection, prevent fistula formation
first_line_soc: Surgical incision and drainage (I&D) is the definitive treatment. Antibiotics (amoxicillin-clavulanate, ciprofloxacin + metronidazole) indicated for cellulitis, immunocompromised patients, or systemic infection.
treatment_algorithm: Step 1: Surgical I&D (definitive; antibiotics alone insufficient). Step 2: Antibiotics for high-risk patients—amoxicillin-clavulanate (Augmentin) orally, or ciprofloxacin + metronidazole for penicillin allergy. IV piperacillin-tazobactam for severe/systemic cases. Step 3: Sitz baths, stool softeners post-drainage. Step 4: Monitor for fistula development (occurs in ~30–50%).
us_prevalence: ~70,000–100,000 cases/year in the US (estimated annual incidence); predominantly acute condition.
patients_needing_tx: ~80,000/year require I&D + possible antibiotics. A novel anti-fistula preventive drug could target ~30,000–40,000/year at high risk of fistula formation.
price: $2,000–$5,000 per course (anti-fistula prevention or enhanced wound healing).
registrational_endpoint: Fistula-free rate at 6 months post-I&D; superiority vs. I&D alone.
btd_threshold_vs_soc: Fistula-free rate ≥85% at 6 months vs. ~60% with I&D alone.

symptoms: Painful cyst/abscess at tailbone, chronic drainage, recurrent infection
treatment_goal: Drain acute abscess, heal chronically, prevent recurrence
first_line_soc: Surgical drainage for acute abscess; wide excision or Karydakis flap for chronic disease. Antibiotics (amoxicillin-clavulanate) only as adjunct in acute infection with cellulitis.
treatment_algorithm: Acute: Incision and drainage. Chronic: Wide local excision (open technique) or off-midline flap closure (Karydakis/Bascom). Antibiotics: amoxicillin-clavulanate or clindamycin for surrounding cellulitis. Fibrin glue (Lord-Millar technique) in some protocols. Hair removal (laser epilation) reduces recurrence. No specific pharmacologic agent for pilonidal disease.
us_prevalence: ~70,000–90,000 pilonidal surgeries/year in the US; ~150,000–200,000 active cases annually. Predominantly young adults aged 15–30.
patients_needing_tx: ~150,000 require medical/surgical management annually; pharmacologic adjunct (antibiotics, wound healing) addressable: ~100,000.
price: $1,000–$3,000 per course (wound-healing biologic adjunct).
registrational_endpoint: Wound closure rate at 6 weeks post-surgery; recurrence-free rate at 1 year.
btd_threshold_vs_soc: Recurrence-free rate ≥85% at 1 year vs. ~65–70% with standard surgery alone.

symptoms: Rectal bleeding, straining, mucus discharge, incomplete evacuation, rectal pain
treatment_goal: Heal ulcer, reduce straining, improve defecation, prevent progression
first_line_soc: Behavioral therapy (defecation retraining) + fiber supplementation; topical sucralfate enemas (mucosal protectant) or mesalamine enemas (5-ASA) as first-line pharmacologic.
treatment_algorithm: Step 1: High-fiber diet, biofeedback for dyssynergic defecation. Step 2: Topical mesalamine (5-ASA) enemas or suppositories; sucralfate enemas (mucosal protectant). Step 3: Topical corticosteroid enemas for refractory inflammation. Step 4: Surgery (rectopexy) for prolapse-associated cases. Botulinum toxin for paradoxical puborectalis contraction. No FDA-approved drug specifically for SRUS.
us_prevalence: Rare condition; estimated prevalence ~1–3.6 per 100,000 US adults; ~3,000–12,000 US patients total.
patients_needing_tx: ~5,000–8,000 patients require active pharmacologic management in the US.
price: $5,000–$15,000/year (rare disease premium pricing).
registrational_endpoint: Endoscopic healing (ulcer closure) at 12 weeks; combined symptomatic improvement (bleeding + straining score).
btd_threshold_vs_soc: Endoscopic healing in ≥60% vs. ~30–40% with topical mesalamine at 12 weeks.

symptoms: Dysphagia, drooling, chest pain, sensation of something stuck in throat
treatment_goal: Remove foreign body safely, prevent esophageal injury
first_line_soc: Endoscopic removal (flexible esophagoscopy) is standard. Glucagon IV (relaxes lower esophageal sphincter; smooth muscle relaxant) used as first-line for food impaction to facilitate spontaneous passage.
treatment_algorithm: Food impaction: IV glucagon 1mg (smooth muscle relaxant)—allows ~50% to pass spontaneously; benzodiazepines as adjunct. True foreign bodies: endoscopic removal with forceps/basket. Sharp objects: urgent endoscopy. Disk batteries: emergency endoscopy within 2 hours. No pharmacologic substitute for endoscopy in most cases.
us_prevalence: ~1,500–2,000 cases requiring endoscopic removal/year in adults in the US; ~100,000+ cases annually in children.
patients_needing_tx: ~80,000–100,000 pediatric + adult cases/year require pharmacologic or procedural management.
price: $100–$500 per episode (acute use in ED).
registrational_endpoint: Rate of successful spontaneous passage without endoscopy within 2 hours of administration vs. placebo.
btd_threshold_vs_soc: Spontaneous passage in ≥70% vs. ~40–50% with IV glucagon at 2 hours.

symptoms: Usually asymptomatic; may cause abdominal pain, obstruction, perforation
treatment_goal: Monitor for natural passage or retrieve to prevent complications
first_line_soc: Watchful waiting with serial X-rays for blunt objects in stomach. Endoscopic or surgical retrieval for sharp objects, large objects, or disk batteries.
treatment_algorithm: Blunt, small objects: Watchful waiting—95% pass spontaneously. Large/sharp objects in stomach: Endoscopic retrieval. Small bowel foreign bodies: Observation; surgery if obstruction or perforation. Prokinetics (metoclopramide) may facilitate passage. Disk batteries: emergency endoscopy. No specific pharmacologic SoC beyond prokinetic use.
us_prevalence: ~5,000–10,000 cases/year requiring medical intervention in adults; higher incidence in children and mentally ill populations.
patients_needing_tx: ~8,000/year require active management; most children's cases resolve spontaneously.
price: $200–$1,000 per course (enhanced prokinetic or mucosal protection agent).
registrational_endpoint: Rate of spontaneous passage within 48 hours without endoscopic or surgical intervention.
btd_threshold_vs_soc: ≥85% spontaneous passage in 48h vs. ~70% with observation + standard prokinetics.

symptoms: Nausea, vomiting, abdominal pain, early satiety, palpable mass
treatment_goal: Dissolve or remove bezoar, prevent recurrence
first_line_soc: Coca-Cola lavage (phytobezoars) or cellulase/acetylcysteine as enzymatic dissolving agents; endoscopic fragmentation for large bezoars.
treatment_algorithm: Phytobezoar: Coca-Cola (commercially available; acidic dissolution), cellulase, acetylcysteine (mucolytic). Trichobezoar: Endoscopic/surgical removal (dissolution ineffective). Pharmacobezoar: Stop offending drug, endoscopic removal. After removal: prokinetics (metoclopramide, cisapride where available) to prevent recurrence. Lactobezoar in neonates: stop feeding, NG suction.
us_prevalence: Uncommon; estimated 1,000–3,000 cases/year in the US requiring medical intervention. Mostly seen post-gastric surgery.
patients_needing_tx: ~2,000 cases/year require active pharmacologic or endoscopic treatment.
price: $500–$2,000 per course (oral enzymatic agent).
registrational_endpoint: Complete bezoar dissolution or fragmentation (imaging-confirmed) at 72 hours; superiority vs. Coca-Cola lavage.
btd_threshold_vs_soc: Complete dissolution in ≥80% within 48 hours vs. ~50–60% with Coca-Cola lavage.

symptoms: Rectal pain, fullness, inability to defecate, perianal discomfort
treatment_goal: Remove foreign body safely, prevent rectal injury or perforation
first_line_soc: None pharmacologic—manual extraction under sedation (IV benzodiazepines, propofol) or endoscopic/surgical removal is standard.
treatment_algorithm: Sedation/analgesia (IV midazolam + propofol or ketamine) for patient relaxation during manual extraction. Smooth muscle relaxants (glucagon, amyl nitrite) to relax internal sphincter. Endoscopic or transanal extraction; laparotomy for high-lying objects or perforation. No pharmacologic agent specifically for rectal foreign body removal.
us_prevalence: ~2,000–3,000 cases/year requiring hospital-based extraction in the US (Emergency Medicine literature).
patients_needing_tx: ~2,500 cases/year requiring procedural management with pharmacologic sedation.
price: $200–$1,000 per episode (sedation adjunct or smooth muscle relaxant).
registrational_endpoint: Successful extraction without general anesthesia or surgical intervention; time to extraction.
btd_threshold_vs_soc: Successful extraction without OR in ≥85% vs. ~65% with standard IV sedation.

symptoms: Usually asymptomatic; may cause bloating, cramping, altered bowel habits
treatment_goal: Prevent progression to diverticulitis, relieve symptoms
first_line_soc: High-fiber diet (bulk-forming agent: psyllium/Metamucil); avoidance of seeds/nuts (limited evidence). No pharmacologic SoC for asymptomatic diverticulosis.
treatment_algorithm: Asymptomatic diverticulosis: Dietary fiber (psyllium 25–38g/day), adequate hydration. Symptomatic uncomplicated diverticular disease (SUDD): Rifaximin 400mg BID × 7 days/month (non-absorbable antibiotic; cyclic regimen)—widely used in Europe, limited in US; mesalamine (5-ASA) studied but not approved for this indication. NSAIDs avoided (worsen risk). Antispasmodics (hyoscine, dicyclomine) for pain.
us_prevalence: ~35–50 million Americans have colonic diverticulosis (prevalence increases with age: >60% over age 60). SUDD affects ~20–25% of those with diverticulosis.
patients_needing_tx: ~7–10 million SUDD patients could benefit from pharmacologic therapy; ~35 million asymptomatic diverticulosis patients for risk reduction.
price: $1,000–$4,000/year (preventive oral agent).
registrational_endpoint: Rate of acute diverticulitis episodes over 2 years; mean symptom score in SUDD patients at 6 months.
btd_threshold_vs_soc: ≥35% relative risk reduction in diverticulitis episodes vs. placebo; or ≥30% improvement in SUDD symptom score.

symptoms: Left lower abdominal pain, fever, nausea, altered bowel habits, tenderness
treatment_goal: Resolve acute infection, prevent perforation and recurrence
first_line_soc: Uncomplicated: Oral antibiotics—ciprofloxacin (fluoroquinolone) + metronidazole (nitroimidazole) for 7–10 days (recent guidelines question need for antibiotics in mild uncomplicated cases). Complicated: IV antibiotics (piperacillin-tazobactam or cefoxitin + metronidazole).
treatment_algorithm: Uncomplicated (Hinchey Ia/Ib): High-fiber diet, NSAIDs for pain; antibiotics for moderate-severe (ciprofloxacin + metronidazole or amoxicillin-clavulanate 7–10 days). 2020+ guidelines support selective antibiotic use for uncomplicated. Complicated (abscess, fistula, perforation): IV antibiotics + CT-guided drainage for abscess ≥3cm. After resolution: Mesalamine (5-ASA) and rifaximin (non-absorbable antibiotic) studied for prevention of recurrence—modest evidence. Probiotics under investigation.
us_prevalence: ~300,000 hospitalizations/year for diverticulitis; ~200,000–250,000 acute episodes requiring antibiotics. Chronic diverticular disease prevalence: ~35 million Americans have diverticulosis.
patients_needing_tx: ~300,000/year acute episodes requiring antibiotics; ~1–2 million recurrent/chronic patients who might benefit from preventive pharmacotherapy.
price: $5,000–$15,000/year (novel preventive anti-inflammatory or microbiome-based agent).
registrational_endpoint: Time to first diverticulitis recurrence at 2 years; proportion recurrence-free at 2 years vs. placebo.
btd_threshold_vs_soc: ≥40% relative risk reduction in 2-year recurrence vs. no prophylaxis (e.g., 20% vs. 35% recurrence rate).

disease: Diverticular Disease of the Stomach and Small Bowel
symptoms: Abdominal pain, bloating, nausea; may cause bleeding or perforation
treatment_goal: Manage complications (bleeding, perforation, infection); prevent recurrence
first_line_soc: For duodenal diverticular complications: antibiotics (amoxicillin-clavulanate); endoscopic or surgical management for bleeding/perforation. No pharmacologic SoC for uncomplicated disease.
treatment_algorithm: Uncomplicated: Observation + dietary modification. Jejunal/duodenal diverticulosis with bacterial overgrowth: Cyclic antibiotics (rifaximin, ciprofloxacin, or tetracycline) for SIBO management. Complications (bleeding, perforation, diverticulitis): Surgical resection + antibiotics (piperacillin-tazobactam). Very rare condition—limited controlled trial data.
us_prevalence: Duodenal diverticula in ~15–22% of adults (usually incidental); ~1–5% symptomatic. Small bowel diverticula: ~0.06–1.9%. Symptomatic cases requiring treatment: ~50,000–100,000/year.
patients_needing_tx: ~50,000 patients with symptomatic disease requiring management annually in the US.
registrational_endpoint: Symptom score improvement (bloating, pain) at 12 weeks; recurrence rate of SIBO-related symptoms.
btd_threshold_vs_soc: ≥50% symptom responder rate at 12 weeks vs. ~30% with rifaximin alone.

symptoms: Painless rectal bleeding (children), abdominal pain, intussusception, obstruction
treatment_goal: Surgical resection of symptomatic diverticulum to prevent complications
first_line_soc: None pharmacologic—surgical resection (laparoscopic Meckel's diverticulectomy) is the only effective treatment.
treatment_algorithm: Symptomatic Meckel's: Surgical diverticulectomy or segmental small bowel resection. Bleeding: supportive transfusion + technetium-99m scan for localization before surgery. Peptic ulceration-like symptoms: PPI (omeprazole; proton pump inhibitor) may temporarily reduce acid-related bleeding from ectopic gastric mucosa as bridge to surgery. No pharmacologic SoC.
us_prevalence: ~2% of the population has Meckel's diverticulum (~6.5 million Americans); ~4% become symptomatic (~260,000 cumulative). Annual symptomatic cases requiring surgery: ~15,000–20,000.
patients_needing_tx: ~15,000–20,000/year symptomatic cases requiring surgical treatment.
price: $1,000–$3,000 per course (bridging pharmacotherapy pre-surgery).
registrational_endpoint: N/A (surgical condition; primary endpoint would be reduction in surgical urgency/complications if bridging therapy used).

symptoms: Heartburn, acid regurgitation, chest pain, belching, dysphagia
treatment_goal: Achieve symptom control, heal esophagitis, prevent complications
first_line_soc: Proton pump inhibitors (PPIs): omeprazole (Prilosec), esomeprazole (Nexium), pantoprazole (Protonix) 20–40mg daily. PPIs are the most potent acid suppressors available.
treatment_algorithm: Step 1: Lifestyle modification (head elevation, weight loss, dietary changes) + OTC antacids/H2RAs. Step 2: PPIs (omeprazole, esomeprazole, pantoprazole, lansoprazole, rabeprazole, dexlansoprazole)—once/twice daily. Step 3: For refractory/nocturnal: add H2RA at bedtime (famotidine; ranitidine discontinued). Step 4: Novel: Vonoprazan (Voquezna; potassium-competitive acid blocker [P-CAB])—FDA-approved 2023 for erosive GERD, superior acid control. Step 5: Anti-reflux surgery (Nissen fundoplication, TIF, LINX device). 2024–2025: Lesogaberan (GABA-B agonist) targeting transient LES relaxations in trials.
us_prevalence: ~70–80 million Americans have GERD (~20–25% prevalence); ~20 million with erosive esophagitis. One of the most common GI conditions in the US (ACG/AGA).
patients_needing_tx: ~60 million require pharmacologic therapy; ~20 million regular PPI users; ~5–8 million refractory/severe candidates for novel agents (vonoprazan or biologics).
price: $3,000–$6,000/year (branded P-CAB like vonoprazan; generic PPIs $100–300/year).
registrational_endpoint: Erosive esophagitis healing rate at 8 weeks (endoscopic confirmation, Los Angeles grade C/D); heartburn-free days at 4 weeks.
btd_threshold_vs_soc: ≥90% healing of LA grade C/D erosive esophagitis at 8 weeks vs. ~80–85% with esomeprazole 40mg; or superior nocturnal heartburn control.

symptoms: Difficulty swallowing solids and/or liquids, choking, coughing, regurgitation
treatment_goal: Restore safe swallowing, prevent aspiration, maintain nutrition
first_line_soc: Depends on etiology: structural (dilation), neurologic (speech therapy), GERD-related (PPIs), eosinophilic (steroids). No universal pharmacologic first-line. PPIs (omeprazole/pantoprazole) for GERD-related dysphagia.
treatment_algorithm: Oropharyngeal dysphagia: Speech therapy, dietary texture modification, neuromuscular electrical stimulation. GERD-related: PPIs (omeprazole, pantoprazole). Achalasia: See achalasia entry. Stricture/ring: Esophageal dilation. Oropharyngeal neurogenic: Botulinum toxin for UES hypertonicity; carbachol or bethanechol (cholinergic) investigated. Medication-induced: Discontinue offending agent. Parkinson's/stroke-related: Levodopa/carbidopa for dysphagia in Parkinson's.
us_prevalence: ~15–22% of the general US adult population has some degree of dysphagia (~50 million); severe/symptomatic: ~5–8 million requiring medical management.
patients_needing_tx: ~5–8 million with clinically significant dysphagia needing pharmacologic or procedural treatment.
price: $5,000–$15,000/year (novel neurologically-targeted or motility-enhancing agent).
registrational_endpoint: Swallowing function score (Sydney Swallowing Questionnaire or DSWAL-QOL) improvement at 12 weeks; time to aspiration pneumonia.
btd_threshold_vs_soc: ≥40% improvement in dysphagia severity score at 12 weeks vs. SoC (speech therapy + dietary modification) or ≥30% reduction in aspiration events.

symptoms: Heartburn, regurgitation, chest pain, dysphagia, belching
treatment_goal: Control reflux symptoms, prevent complications, reduce hernia if symptomatic
first_line_soc: PPIs (omeprazole, pantoprazole) for symptomatic management of associated GERD. Surgical repair (laparoscopic Nissen fundoplication) for large/symptomatic hernias.
treatment_algorithm: Type I (sliding): PPIs for GERD symptoms; lifestyle modification. Large/paraesophageal hernias (Type II–IV): Surgical repair. PPIs indefinitely post-repair for GERD. H2RAs (famotidine) as adjuncts. No pharmacologic agent repairs the hernia; all drug therapy targets acid suppression.
us_prevalence: ~15–20 million Americans have hiatal hernias; most are incidental. Symptomatic: ~7–10 million requiring treatment (often overlap with GERD prevalence).
patients_needing_tx: ~7–10 million symptomatic patients requiring PPIs; ~250,000–300,000/year require surgical repair.
price: $3,000–$6,000/year (novel acid blocker or reflux-modifying agent).
registrational_endpoint: Same as GERD: EE healing rate at 8 weeks; heartburn-free days at 4 weeks.
btd_threshold_vs_soc: Same as GERD entry: ≥90% EE healing at 8 weeks vs. ~80–85% with omeprazole.

symptoms: Dysphagia, food impaction, chest pain, heartburn, abdominal pain
treatment_goal: Achieve histologic remission, prevent stricture, relieve dysphagia
first_line_soc: Swallowed topical corticosteroids: fluticasone propionate (MDI swallowed, not inhaled) or budesonide oral suspension (Eohilia; FDA-approved Feb 2024) or budesonide orally disintegrating tablets (Jorveza; EMA-approved). PPIs also used as initial therapy.
treatment_algorithm: Step 1: PPIs (omeprazole 40mg BID × 8 weeks)—response in ~40%. Step 2: Swallowed topical corticosteroids—budesonide oral suspension (Eohilia, FDA-approved 2024) 2mg BID or fluticasone MDI swallowed 440–880mcg BID. Step 3: Dietary elimination (6-food elimination diet). Step 4: Dupilumab (Dupixent; anti-IL-4Rα biologic)—FDA-approved July 2022 for EoE in adults/adolescents ≥12 years. Step 5: Esophageal dilation for strictures. 2024–2025: Cendakimab (anti-IL-33) and other biologics in Phase 3 trials.
us_prevalence: ~1.5–2 million US patients with EoE (prevalence growing ~15%/year); ~160,000 new diagnoses/year (ACG/AGA data).
patients_needing_tx: ~1.5 million require active pharmacologic treatment; ~300,000–400,000 candidates for biologics (dupilumab-eligible moderate-to-severe).
price: $35,000–$50,000/year (biologic); $8,000–$15,000/year (topical steroid).
registrational_endpoint: Histologic remission (peak eosinophil count ≤6/hpf on esophageal biopsy) at 24 weeks; combined histologic + dysphagia symptom improvement.
btd_threshold_vs_soc: Histologic remission in ≥60% at 24 weeks vs. ~40% with budesonide oral suspension; or significant superiority over dupilumab in head-to-head.

symptoms: Dysphagia to solids and liquids, regurgitation, chest pain, weight loss
treatment_goal: Reduce LES pressure, restore esophageal emptying, relieve dysphagia
first_line_soc: Pneumatic balloon dilation (endoscopic) or laparoscopic Heller myotomy are first-line interventional options. Botulinum toxin A injection into LES (temporary bridge). Calcium channel blockers (nifedipine; diltiazem) or nitrates (isosorbide dinitrate) for pharmacologic palliation.
treatment_algorithm: Step 1 (pharmacologic bridge/mild disease): Calcium channel blockers (nifedipine 10–30mg SL before meals) or isosorbide dinitrate 5mg SL (nitrate); temporary, ~50% improvement. Step 2: Botulinum toxin A injection (Botox) into LES—effective 65–85%, wanes at 1–2 years. Step 3: Pneumatic balloon dilation (definitive, 85–90% efficacy). Step 4: Laparoscopic Heller myotomy ± fundoplication. Step 5: Peroral endoscopic myotomy (POEM)—equivalent to surgery. 2024: Vonoprazan studied for GERD post-POEM.
us_prevalence: ~100,000–150,000 US patients with achalasia; annual incidence ~1.6 per 100,000 (AGA data).
patients_needing_tx: ~100,000–120,000 patients require active management; ~30,000–40,000/year newly diagnosed and needing treatment.
price: $20,000–$50,000/year (novel LES-relaxing biologic or gene therapy).
registrational_endpoint: Eckardt score reduction ≤3 at 6 months (clinical success); timed barium esophagram column height at 1 minute.
btd_threshold_vs_soc: Clinical success (Eckardt ≤3) in ≥80% at 1 year vs. ~65% with pneumatic dilation alone.

symptoms: Dysphagia, chest pain, regurgitation, heartburn, difficulty swallowing
treatment_goal: Normalize esophageal motility, relieve dysphagia and chest pain
first_line_soc: Depends on subtype: CCBs (diltiazem) or nitrates (isosorbide dinitrate) for hypercontractile disorders; PPIs for GERD overlap; TCAs for functional chest pain.
treatment_algorithm: Achalasia: See separate entry. Hypercontractile esophagus (Jackhammer): CCBs (diltiazem), nitrates, Botox injection, POEM. Ineffective esophageal motility: PPIs for GERD, dietary modification. Absent contractility: PPIs, elevation of head of bed. TCAs (imipramine) and SSRIs for visceral hypersensitivity/pain. Buspirone (5-HT1A agonist) studied in ineffective motility.
us_prevalence: ~500,000–1 million US patients with clinically significant non-achalasia esophageal motility disorders per high-resolution manometry studies.
patients_needing_tx: ~300,000–500,000 require pharmacologic management.
registrational_endpoint: DSQ score or global symptom score reduction at 8 weeks; HRM-documented motility improvement as exploratory.
btd_threshold_vs_soc: ≥40% improvement in dysphagia/chest pain composite score vs. ~20% with CCB + PPI combination.

symptoms: Dysphagia, regurgitation, weight loss, food impaction, chest pain
treatment_goal: Restore esophageal patency, relieve obstruction, treat underlying cause
first_line_soc: Endoscopic dilation for benign strictures; PPIs for GERD-related strictures; endoscopic stenting or surgery for malignant/complex obstruction.
treatment_algorithm: Peptic stricture: Esophageal dilation + long-term PPI (esomeprazole 40mg daily). Malignant stricture: Self-expanding metal stent (SEMS) for palliation; chemo/radiation ± surgery for curative intent. EoE-related: Topical steroids + dilation. Schatzki ring: Dilation + PPIs. Vascular compression: Surgical vascular reconstruction.
us_prevalence: ~400,000–600,000 US patients with symptomatic esophageal strictures of any cause requiring intervention.
patients_needing_tx: ~400,000 requiring pharmacologic + procedural management annually.
price: $3,000–$8,000/year (novel anti-fibrotic or biologic).
registrational_endpoint: Stricture recurrence rate at 1 year post-dilation; dysphagia symptom score at 12 weeks.
btd_threshold_vs_soc: Stricture recurrence <20% at 1 year vs. ~40–50% with dilation + PPI alone.

symptoms: Chest pain, dysphagia, non-cardiac chest pain, regurgitation
treatment_goal: Relieve esophageal spasm, reduce chest pain, improve swallowing
first_line_soc: Calcium channel blockers (diltiazem 60–90mg TID; nifedipine; CCBs reduce esophageal smooth muscle spasm) or nitrates (isosorbide dinitrate 5–10mg SL; smooth muscle relaxant).
treatment_algorithm: Step 1: TCAs (imipramine, trazodone; pain modulators) for pain-predominant cases. Step 2: CCBs—diltiazem (Cardizem) 60mg TID or nifedipine 10–30mg. Step 3: Nitrates—isosorbide dinitrate (smooth muscle relaxant). Step 4: Sildenafil (PDE5 inhibitor; studied in small trials for esophageal spasm). Step 5: Botulinum toxin A injection into distal esophagus endoscopically. Step 6: POEM (peroral endoscopic myotomy) for refractory severe spasm. SSRIs (sertraline) for psychological component.
us_prevalence: ~100,000–200,000 US patients with manometrically-confirmed distal esophageal spasm (rare in general; often overlap with hypercontractile esophagus).
patients_needing_tx: ~100,000–150,000 require pharmacologic management.
registrational_endpoint: Dysphagia Symptom Questionnaire (DSQ) score or chest pain episodes/week reduction from baseline at 8 weeks vs. placebo.
btd_threshold_vs_soc: ≥50% reduction in weekly chest pain or dysphagia episodes vs. ~20–30% with diltiazem alone.

symptoms: Intermittent dysphagia to solids, 'steakhouse syndrome', food impaction
treatment_goal: Relieve dysphagia, prevent food impaction episodes
first_line_soc: Endoscopic dilation (bougie or balloon dilation) is first-line. PPIs for associated GERD.
treatment_algorithm: Schatzki ring: Endoscopic dilation (single-dilation effective in ~90%). PPIs post-dilation to prevent recurrence (GERD-related rings). Recurrent rings: Repeat dilation; electrocautery incision endoscopically. No pharmacologic agent dissolves or prevents rings; PPIs reduce recurrence risk.
us_prevalence: ~6–14% of dysphagia patients have Schatzki rings; ~500,000–1 million US patients. Prevalence in general population: ~5–10%.
patients_needing_tx: ~300,000–500,000 symptomatic patients requiring dilation + PPIs.
price: $1,000–$3,000/year (PPI + novel anti-fibrotic to prevent recurrence).
registrational_endpoint: Recurrence-free rate at 1 year post-dilation; dysphagia episode frequency reduction.
btd_threshold_vs_soc: Recurrence-free rate ≥80% at 1 year vs. ~60% with dilation + PPI alone.

symptoms: Dysphagia, regurgitation of undigested food, halitosis, aspiration
treatment_goal: Relieve obstruction, prevent aspiration, eliminate diverticulum
first_line_soc: None pharmacologic—surgical or endoscopic myotomy/diverticulectomy is definitive for Zenker's or epiphrenic diverticula. PPIs for associated GERD.
treatment_algorithm: Zenker's diverticulum: Endoscopic diverticulotomy (flexible or rigid) or open surgical resection + cricopharyngeal myotomy. Epiphrenic diverticulum: Laparoscopic resection + myotomy. PPIs (omeprazole) for associated GERD. No pharmacologic SoC for the diverticulum itself. Antispasmodics may provide temporary symptom relief.
us_prevalence: Zenker's diverticulum: ~2 per 100,000/year incidence; ~50,000–70,000 active US cases. Epiphrenic diverticula: rarer, ~10,000–20,000.
patients_needing_tx: ~50,000 symptomatic Zenker's + other esophageal diverticula patients in the US; most require procedural intervention.
price: $5,000–$15,000/year (novel sphincter-relaxing biologic as bridge).
registrational_endpoint: Dysphagia symptom score improvement at 12 weeks in non-surgical candidates; reduction in regurgitation episodes.
btd_threshold_vs_soc: ≥50% responder rate at 12 weeks vs. ~25% with supportive care in non-surgical candidates.

symptoms: Odynophagia, dysphagia, chest pain, fever, oral thrush (candidal)
treatment_goal: Eradicate esophageal infection, relieve dysphagia
first_line_soc: Candidal esophagitis: fluconazole (Diflucan; azole antifungal) 200–400mg/day × 14–21 days. Herpetic esophagitis: acyclovir (antiviral) 400mg 5×/day × 14–21 days. CMV esophagitis: ganciclovir or valganciclovir (antiviral).
treatment_algorithm: Candida: Fluconazole 200–400mg PO × 14–21 days; amphotericin B IV for fluconazole-resistant or severe immunocompromised cases; caspofungin (echinocandin) for azole-resistant Candida. HSV: Acyclovir 400mg 5×/day × 14–21 days; valacyclovir or famciclovir as alternatives. CMV: Ganciclovir 5mg/kg IV BID × 3–6 weeks, then valganciclovir for maintenance. Treat underlying immunosuppression (HIV: ART; transplant: adjust immunosuppressants).
us_prevalence: ~50,000–100,000 cases/year in immunocompromised patients (HIV, transplant, chemotherapy). Rising with immunosuppressive therapy use.
patients_needing_tx: ~80,000/year require antifungal/antiviral pharmacologic treatment.
price: $3,000–$10,000 per course (novel antifungal or antiviral).
registrational_endpoint: Endoscopic cure (complete healing of esophageal lesions) at day 21; clinical symptom resolution (odynophagia-free) rate.
btd_threshold_vs_soc: Endoscopic cure in ≥90% at day 14 vs. ~75–80% with fluconazole standard course.

symptoms: Dysphagia to solids, intermittent, pulsatile chest sensation from aberrant artery
treatment_goal: Relieve vascular compression of esophagus; surgery if symptomatic
first_line_soc: None pharmacologic—asymptomatic cases observed. Symptomatic: surgical correction (division of aberrant right subclavian artery + vascular reconstruction) is definitive.
treatment_algorithm: Asymptomatic (most cases): Observation. Symptomatic: Surgical repair (division of right subclavian artery + bypass). Antihypertensives (beta-blockers, CCBs) for associated aneurysm management. PPIs if GERD contributes to symptoms. No pharmacologic SoC for the vascular anomaly itself.
us_prevalence: Aberrant right subclavian artery occurs in ~0.5–2% of the population (~1.5–6 million Americans), but only ~10% are symptomatic (~150,000–600,000 symptomatic cases).
patients_needing_tx: ~50,000–100,000 symptomatic cases requiring intervention; majority managed surgically.
price: $3,000–$8,000/year (anti-vascular remodeling adjunct).
registrational_endpoint: Dysphagia symptom score improvement (Dysphagia Handicap Index) at 12 weeks; reduction in dysphagia episode frequency.
btd_threshold_vs_soc: ≥50% reduction in dysphagia episodes vs. placebo at 12 weeks in non-surgical candidates.

symptoms: Bright red hematemesis after repeated vomiting, nausea, retching
treatment_goal: Achieve hemostasis, prevent rebleeding
first_line_soc: Supportive care (IV fluids, antiemetics) in most cases—~90% stop spontaneously. Proton pump inhibitors (IV esomeprazole or pantoprazole) to promote mucosal healing. Endoscopic hemostasis (epinephrine injection, cauterization) for active bleeding.
treatment_algorithm: Step 1: IV fluids, antiemetics (ondansetron), NPO. Step 2: IV PPI (esomeprazole 40mg BID or pantoprazole 80mg bolus then infusion). Step 3: Endoscopic hemostasis for active arterial bleeding (epinephrine injection, thermal coagulation, hemoclip). Step 4: Interventional radiology (transcatheter arterial embolization) for refractory bleeding. Transfusion for significant blood loss.
us_prevalence: ~5–10% of all upper GI bleeding cases; ~40,000–60,000 cases/year in the US requiring hospitalization.
patients_needing_tx: ~50,000 cases/year requiring active pharmacologic (PPI) + potential endoscopic management.
price: $2,000–$5,000 per episode (novel hemostatic or mucosal healing agent).
registrational_endpoint: Rebleeding rate at 30 days; time to hemostasis (endoscopic); 30-day re-hospitalization rate.
btd_threshold_vs_soc: Rebleeding rate <5% vs. ~10% with IV PPI + endoscopic SoC.

symptoms: Severe chest/epigastric pain after vomiting, subcutaneous emphysema, shock
treatment_goal: Urgent surgical or endoscopic repair, control mediastinal contamination
first_line_soc: Broad-spectrum IV antibiotics (piperacillin-tazobactam or carbapenems + antifungals) + emergent surgical repair or covered esophageal stent placement.
treatment_algorithm: Immediate: IV antibiotics (piperacillin-tazobactam or meropenem + fluconazole), NPO, IV fluids. Definitive: Primary surgical repair within 24h (Boerhaave syndrome); covered self-expanding metal stent (CSEMS) endoscopically for contained perforation. Drainage of mediastinum/pleural space. Long-term antibiotics per culture data. Very high mortality (~30–40%) without prompt treatment.
us_prevalence: ~3,000–5,000 cases/year in the US (both spontaneous and iatrogenic); acute emergency.
patients_needing_tx: ~4,000 cases/year requiring urgent pharmacologic + surgical management.
price: $10,000–$30,000 per episode (novel antimicrobial + cytoprotective combination).
registrational_endpoint: 30-day survival without re-operation; time to mediastinal infection resolution.
btd_threshold_vs_soc: 30-day survival ≥80% vs. ~60–70% with current surgical + antibiotic SoC.

symptoms: Dysphagia to solids, intermittent, cervical dysphagia, sometimes iron deficiency
treatment_goal: Restore esophageal lumen patency, treat associated iron deficiency
first_line_soc: Endoscopic dilation (bougie or balloon) is first-line. Iron supplementation for associated iron deficiency anemia (Plummer-Vinson syndrome).
treatment_algorithm: Plummer-Vinson syndrome: Iron supplementation (oral ferrous sulfate 325mg TID) + endoscopic dilation. Post-dilation: maintenance of iron levels. Other webs: Endoscopic rupture/dilation. Recurrence: Repeat dilation. No pharmacologic agent for esophageal web itself; iron replacement for underlying cause.
us_prevalence: Uncommon; ~10,000–30,000 cases in the US. Plummer-Vinson syndrome predominantly in middle-aged women with iron deficiency.
patients_needing_tx: ~15,000–20,000 requiring dilation + pharmacologic management (iron therapy).
price: $500–$2,000/year (iron therapy or novel mucosal healing agent).
registrational_endpoint: Dysphagia symptom resolution (Eckardt subscore for dysphagia ≤1) at 12 weeks; esophageal lumen diameter on imaging.
btd_threshold_vs_soc: Symptom resolution in ≥90% at 12 weeks with pharmacologic + dilation vs. ~70–80% with dilation + iron alone.

symptoms: Epigastric pain, nausea, bloating, indigestion, early satiety
treatment_goal: Relieve symptoms, heal gastric mucosa, treat underlying cause
first_line_soc: PPIs (omeprazole, pantoprazole) for symptom relief and mucosal protection; H. pylori eradication therapy if H. pylori-positive.
treatment_algorithm: H. pylori gastritis: Triple therapy (see H. pylori entry). NSAID-induced: PPIs + discontinue NSAID or switch to COX-2 inhibitor + PPI. Idiopathic: PPIs (omeprazole 20mg daily × 4–8 weeks) + antacids/H2RAs as adjuncts. Autoimmune: B12 replacement (see AMAG entry). Eosinophilic gastritis: Swallowed topical steroids (budesonide), dietary elimination. 2024: Dexpramipexole (eosinophil-reducing agent) in Phase 3 for eosinophilic gastritis.
us_prevalence: ~50–70 million Americans experience symptoms of gastritis; ~10–15 million with documented gastritis on endoscopy.
patients_needing_tx: ~20–30 million require pharmacologic management for symptom relief; ~8–10 million with confirmed H. pylori gastritis need eradication.
registrational_endpoint: Symptom score improvement (SODA questionnaire or Gastrointestinal Symptom Rating Scale) at 4 weeks; endoscopic improvement at 8 weeks.
btd_threshold_vs_soc: ≥50% symptom responder rate at 4 weeks vs. ~35% with PPI alone.

symptoms: Epigastric discomfort, nausea, early satiety, bloating without endoscopic erosions
treatment_goal: Relieve dyspeptic symptoms, treat H. pylori if present
first_line_soc: PPIs (omeprazole 20mg daily) or H2RAs (famotidine 20mg BID) for symptom control. H. pylori eradication if positive.
treatment_algorithm: H. pylori-positive: Eradication therapy (see H. pylori entry)—improves symptoms in ~10–15% beyond placebo. Functional overlap: PPIs or H2RAs; prokinetics (metoclopramide, domperidone where available) for gastroparesis-like symptoms. TCAs (amitriptyline 10–25mg) for visceral hypersensitivity. Buspirone for fundic relaxation. Sucralfate as mucosal protectant.
us_prevalence: ~20–30 million US adults; overlap with functional dyspepsia significant.
patients_needing_tx: ~15–20 million requiring pharmacologic symptom management.
registrational_endpoint: Global dyspepsia symptom score improvement at 4 weeks; proportion with adequate symptom relief.
btd_threshold_vs_soc: ≥50% global symptom responder rate at 4 weeks vs. ~35% with PPI alone.

symptoms: Epigastric pain, nausea, vomiting, GI bleeding, dyspepsia
treatment_goal: Heal mucosal erosions, prevent GI bleeding, remove causative agent
first_line_soc: PPIs (omeprazole 20–40mg daily; pantoprazole) for acid suppression + mucosal healing. Remove offending agent (NSAIDs, alcohol, steroids).
treatment_algorithm: Step 1: Discontinue offending agents (NSAIDs, aspirin, steroids, alcohol). Step 2: PPIs (omeprazole, esomeprazole, pantoprazole) at standard or double dose × 4–8 weeks. Step 3: Sucralfate (mucosal protectant) as adjunct. Step 4: H2RAs (famotidine) if PPIs not tolerated. Acute stress gastritis in ICU: IV pantoprazole or famotidine for prophylaxis. Misoprostol (prostaglandin analog) for NSAID-induced gastritis prophylaxis.
us_prevalence: ~10–15 million US adults have erosive gastritis annually; prevalence driven largely by NSAID use and H. pylori.
patients_needing_tx: ~8–12 million patients require pharmacologic therapy; ~15 million US adults are on chronic NSAIDs requiring gastroprotective PPIs.
price: $2,000–$5,000/year (novel gastroprotective agent).
registrational_endpoint: Endoscopic healing of erosions at 4 weeks; GI bleeding rate reduction vs. placebo in NSAID users.
btd_threshold_vs_soc: ≥95% endoscopic healing at 4 weeks vs. ~85% with omeprazole 20mg; or ≥50% reduction in NSAID-related GI events.

symptoms: Often asymptomatic; pernicious anemia, B12 deficiency, neurologic symptoms
treatment_goal: Correct B12 deficiency, monitor for gastric neoplasia
first_line_soc: Vitamin B12 replacement (IM cyanocobalamin 1000mcg monthly or high-dose oral B12 1000–2000mcg daily). Iron supplementation if iron deficiency present.
treatment_algorithm: Step 1: IM or high-dose oral vitamin B12 (cyanocobalamin or hydroxocobalamin) lifelong. Step 2: Iron supplementation (ferrous sulfate) for iron deficiency. Step 3: Endoscopic surveillance for gastric neoplasia (carcinoid, adenocarcinoma) every 3–5 years. H. pylori treatment if co-infected. No immunomodulatory therapy approved for the autoimmune process. PPIs avoided (not needed; intrinsic achlorhydria).
us_prevalence: ~2–5% of the US population; ~4–10 million Americans. Underdiagnosed. Incidence increases with age.
patients_needing_tx: ~4–6 million patients require lifelong B12 supplementation; ~1–2 million need active GI monitoring.
price: $3,000–$10,000/year (novel immunomodulator targeting anti-parietal cell antibodies).
registrational_endpoint: Normalization of gastric acid secretion + anti-parietal cell antibody titer reduction at 24 weeks; maintenance of B12 levels without supplementation.
btd_threshold_vs_soc: ≥50% reduction in anti-parietal cell antibody titers at 24 weeks + restoration of acid secretion in ≥30% vs. placebo.

symptoms: Asymptomatic or epigastric pain, nausea, bloating, ulcer symptoms
treatment_goal: Eradicate H. pylori, heal mucosa, prevent ulcer and cancer complications
first_line_soc: Clarithromycin-based triple therapy (PPI + clarithromycin + amoxicillin × 14 days) or bismuth quadruple therapy (PPI + bismuth subcitrate + tetracycline + metronidazole × 10–14 days) per ACG 2022 guidelines. Vonoprazan-based dual/triple therapy now first-line preferred in US.
treatment_algorithm: First-line options per ACG 2022: (1) Vonoprazan (Voquezna; P-CAB) + amoxicillin dual therapy × 14 days—FDA-approved 2022. (2) Vonoprazan + clarithromycin + amoxicillin triple × 14 days. (3) Bismuth quadruple (Pylera: bismuth + tetracycline + metronidazole + PPI) × 10–14 days. (4) Clarithromycin triple (if local resistance <15%). (5) Levofloxacin-based triple/sequential. Rescue: rifabutin-based triple for refractory cases. Test of cure: UBT at 4 weeks post-treatment.
us_prevalence: ~35–40% of US adults are H. pylori-positive (~90–100 million); active infection requiring eradication: ~10–15 million annually (symptomatic/newly diagnosed).
patients_needing_tx: ~10–15 million patients/year need eradication therapy in the US. ~3–5 million have refractory/recurrent infection needing second-line regimens.
price: $500–$3,000 per course (10–14 day regimen; vonoprazan-based regimens ~$1,000–2,500).
registrational_endpoint: H. pylori eradication rate (UBT or stool antigen at 4 weeks post-treatment) per-protocol analysis.
btd_threshold_vs_soc: Eradication rate ≥90% PP vs. ~75–80% with standard clarithromycin triple therapy (reflecting increasing antibiotic resistance).

symptoms: Epigastric burning/pain, hunger-like pain, nausea, vomiting, GI bleeding
treatment_goal: Heal ulcer, eradicate H. pylori, prevent recurrence and bleeding
first_line_soc: PPIs (omeprazole 20–40mg BID × 4–8 weeks) for gastric/duodenal ulcers + H. pylori eradication if positive. Misoprostol or PPI for NSAID-induced ulcers.
treatment_algorithm: H. pylori-positive PUD: PPI + H. pylori eradication regimen (4–8 weeks PPI + antibiotics). H. pylori-negative/NSAID-induced: PPIs (omeprazole, esomeprazole, pantoprazole) double-dose × 4–8 weeks; misoprostol (Cytotec; prostaglandin analog) for NSAID prophylaxis. Bleeding ulcer: IV PPI (esomeprazole 80mg bolus → 8mg/h infusion) + endoscopic hemostasis. Refractory: Double-dose PPI or sucralfate added. Zollinger-Ellison: High-dose IV/oral PPI; somatostatin analogs for gastrinoma. 2023–2024: Vonoprazan P-CAB increasingly used.
us_prevalence: ~4–5 million Americans have active PUD; ~500,000 new cases/year. ~100,000 hospitalizations/year for bleeding PUD (AHRQ).
patients_needing_tx: ~4 million active cases requiring pharmacologic therapy; ~500,000 new cases annually.
registrational_endpoint: Ulcer healing rate at 4 weeks (endoscopic confirmation); H. pylori eradication rate; rebleeding rate at 30 days.
btd_threshold_vs_soc: Ulcer healing rate ≥95% at 4 weeks vs. ~85–90% with omeprazole 40mg BID; or superior rebleeding prevention vs. current IV PPI.

symptoms: Abdominal pain, nausea, vomiting, diarrhea, weight loss (varies by type)
treatment_goal: Treat underlying cause, relieve mucosal inflammation, prevent complications
first_line_soc: Depends on subtype: Eosinophilic—budesonide (swallowed); Menetrier's—cetuximab (anti-EGFR biologic) or PPI; Collagenous—budesonide; Lymphocytic—stop offending drug + PPI.
treatment_algorithm: Eosinophilic gastritis: Dietary elimination + swallowed budesonide (topical steroid); dexpramipexole (eosinophil-reducing; Phase 3 2024); dupilumab investigated. Menetrier's disease (giant hypertrophic gastritis): Cetuximab (Erbitux; anti-EGFR antibody) in clinical series; PPI; high-protein diet; gastrectomy for refractory. Lymphocytic: Stop celiac trigger + PPI. Collagenous: Budesonide. Phlegmonous gastritis: IV antibiotics + surgical drainage.
us_prevalence: Rare collectively; eosinophilic gastritis ~2–8/100,000 (~6,000–25,000 US); Menetrier's ~500–1,000 US patients; lymphocytic gastritis ~1–5% of gastroscopies.
patients_needing_tx: ~50,000–100,000 patients across all uncommon gastritis subtypes in the US.
price: $30,000–$80,000/year (for biologic targeting eosinophilic or Menetrier's).
registrational_endpoint: Endoscopic/histologic remission (peak eos ≤30/hpf for EoG, protein normalization for Menetrier's) at 12 weeks.
btd_threshold_vs_soc: Histologic remission in ≥60% at 12 weeks vs. ~20–30% with topical steroids alone.

symptoms: Epigastric pain, nausea, bilious vomiting, early satiety, weight loss
treatment_goal: Manage bile reflux, relieve symptoms, prevent mucosal injury
first_line_soc: Cholestyramine (bile acid sequestrant) to bind refluxed bile; prokinetics (metoclopramide, domperidone where available) to improve gastric emptying; sucralfate (mucosal protectant) as barrier.
treatment_algorithm: Step 1: Prokinetics—metoclopramide (10mg QID) or domperidone (where available; dopamine antagonist) for motility promotion. Step 2: Bile acid binders—cholestyramine (Questran; bile acid sequestrant) 4g TID. Step 3: Sucralfate (Carafate; mucosal barrier) 1g QID between meals. Step 4: Ursodeoxycholic acid (UDCA; bile acid modifier) to reduce bile toxicity. Step 5: Surgical revision (Roux-en-Y reconstruction) for refractory cases.
us_prevalence: ~300,000–500,000 post-gastrectomy patients in the US; ~25–30% develop symptomatic bile reflux gastritis (~75,000–150,000).
patients_needing_tx: ~100,000 patients require active pharmacologic management.
registrational_endpoint: Composite symptom score improvement (nausea, pain, vomiting) at 8 weeks; endoscopic gastritis grade improvement.
