btd_threshold_vs_soc: ≥50% symptom responder rate at 8 weeks vs. ~30% with metoclopramide + sucralfate.

symptoms: Sudden vomiting, watery diarrhea, cramping, low-grade fever, body aches
treatment_goal: Maintain hydration, relieve symptoms, prevent outbreak spread
first_line_soc: Supportive: Oral rehydration therapy (ORS) or IV fluids; antiemetics (ondansetron); zinc supplementation. No specific antiviral approved.
treatment_algorithm: ORS (WHO-ORS or Pedialyte) for mild-moderate dehydration; IV lactated Ringer's or normal saline for severe cases. Ondansetron (4–8mg PO/IV) for vomiting. Nitazoxanide (Alinia; antiparasitic with antiviral properties) studied but not FDA-approved for norovirus. No vaccine or antiviral approved. Rigorous hand hygiene and isolation to prevent spread.
us_prevalence: ~19–21 million norovirus infections/year in the US (CDC); leading cause of acute gastroenteritis. ~56,000–71,000 hospitalizations/year.
patients_needing_tx: ~19 million cases/year but most self-treat; ~1–2 million requiring medical intervention.
price: $500–$2,000 per course (novel antiviral or capsid inhibitor).
registrational_endpoint: Time to symptom resolution (no vomiting + <3 stools/day for 24h); proportion symptom-free at 48h vs. placebo.
btd_threshold_vs_soc: ≥12h reduction in time to symptom resolution vs. placebo (ORS supportive care).

symptoms: Watery or bloody diarrhea, abdominal cramping, fever, nausea
treatment_goal: Resolve infection, prevent complications (HUS in STEC), maintain hydration
first_line_soc: Supportive: ORS/IV hydration. Antibiotics (azithromycin or ciprofloxacin) for ETEC traveler's diarrhea. STEC/EHEC (O157:H7): Supportive only—antibiotics contraindicated (increase HUS risk).
treatment_algorithm: ETEC: Azithromycin 1g single dose or ciprofloxacin 500mg BID × 3 days + ORS; rifaximin (Xifaxan) 200mg TID × 3 days for mild traveler's diarrhea. EPEC/EIEC: Ciprofloxacin or azithromycin. STEC (O157:H7, O104): Supportive care only (IV fluids); antibiotics and antidiarrheals avoided. HUS: Dialysis, plasma exchange; eculizumab (complement inhibitor) studied for STEC-HUS. EAEC: Ciprofloxacin for immunocompromised.
us_prevalence: ~265,000 STEC infections/year (CDC FoodNet 2024); ~1–3 million total E. coli gastroenteritis cases annually. ~3,000–4,000 HUS cases/year.
patients_needing_tx: ~1 million antibiotic-eligible cases/year (ETEC, EPEC, EIEC); ~265,000 STEC requiring supportive care.
price: $500–$3,000 per course.
registrational_endpoint: Time to last unformed stool; stool frequency reduction at 48h; prevention of HUS in STEC (for novel anti-Stx agents).
btd_threshold_vs_soc: ≥24h reduction in diarrhea duration vs. ciprofloxacin + ORS; or HUS prevention in STEC ≥50% relative risk reduction.

symptoms: Sudden watery diarrhea, cramping, nausea, urgency while traveling
treatment_goal: Shorten illness, restore normal stool, enable continued travel
first_line_soc: Rifaximin (Xifaxan 200mg TID × 3 days; non-absorbable antibiotic) for non-dysenteric traveler's diarrhea. Azithromycin (1g single dose) for dysenteric or fluoroquinolone-resistant areas (South/SE Asia).
treatment_algorithm: Self-treatment: ORS + loperamide (Imodium; antidiarrheal) for mild-moderate. Antibiotic: Rifaximin (Xifaxan) 200mg TID × 3 days (best evidence, FDA-approved); Azithromycin 1g × 1 dose or 500mg × 3 days for invasive/bloody diarrhea. Ciprofloxacin (fallback—increasing resistance in Asia). Bismuth subsalicylate prophylaxis in some protocols. Loperamide + antibiotic combination shortens illness duration. Rifamycin (Aemcolo; local antibiotic) FDA-approved 2018 as alternative.
us_prevalence: ~10 million US travelers develop traveler's diarrhea annually; ~20–50% of international travelers.
patients_needing_tx: ~10 million cases/year; ~5 million seeking or self-treating with antibiotics.
price: $100–$500 per course.
registrational_endpoint: Time to last unformed stool (TLUS); proportion with ≤1 unformed stool in 24h and no other symptoms at 24h.
btd_threshold_vs_soc: TLUS ≤24h vs. ~33–36h with rifaximin alone; or ≥20% superiority in proportion symptom-free at 24h.

symptoms: Nausea, vomiting, diarrhea, abdominal cramping after drug/chemical exposure
treatment_goal: Stop offending agent, provide supportive care, prevent dehydration
first_line_soc: Discontinue offending drug/chemical; oral rehydration therapy (ORS); antiemetics (ondansetron); antidiarrheals (loperamide) as appropriate.
treatment_algorithm: Stop causative agent. ORS or IV fluids for dehydration. Ondansetron (5-HT3 antagonist) for nausea/vomiting. Loperamide (opioid receptor agonist) for diarrhea if no bloody stool. Charcoal for toxic ingestion (within 1 hour). Bismuth subsalicylate (Pepto-Bismol) for mild cases. No specific pharmacologic SoC beyond supportive care.
us_prevalence: ~500,000–1 million cases/year where drug/chemical reactions cause gastroenteritis; not a standalone epidemic.
patients_needing_tx: ~500,000/year requiring active management.
price: $500–$2,000 per episode.
registrational_endpoint: Resolution of symptoms (nausea, vomiting, diarrhea-free) at 48 hours; reduction in fluid requirement.
btd_threshold_vs_soc: Symptom resolution in ≥80% at 24h vs. ~60% with ondansetron + ORS.

symptoms: Watery diarrhea, vomiting, fever in children; severe dehydration risk
treatment_goal: Prevent dehydration, shorten illness duration; vaccination for prevention
first_line_soc: ORS (oral rehydration solution) for hydration. Rotavirus vaccines (RotaTeq, Rotarix) prevent ~85–95% of severe rotavirus disease in children—most important intervention.
treatment_algorithm: Supportive: ORS (Pedialyte, WHO-ORS); IV fluids for severe dehydration. Ondansetron for vomiting to facilitate ORS uptake. Zinc supplementation (10–20mg/day × 10–14 days) for children in developing countries. Nitazoxanide (Alinia) studied but not approved for rotavirus. Prevention: Rotarix (2-dose oral vaccine) or RotaTeq (3-dose oral vaccine)—dramatically reduces severe disease. No antiviral SoC.
us_prevalence: ~400,000–700,000 rotavirus cases/year in US children (dramatically reduced post-vaccination program from 2.7M/year pre-2006); ~55,000–70,000 hospitalizations/year pre-vaccine; now much lower.
patients_needing_tx: ~300,000 cases/year requiring medical management (post-vaccination era); ~200,000 requiring ORS and supportive care.
price: $200–$1,000 per course (antiviral; post-exposure prophylaxis).
registrational_endpoint: Time to last watery stool; duration of hospitalization; proportion with severe dehydration at 48h.
btd_threshold_vs_soc: ≥24h reduction in diarrhea duration vs. supportive care; or ≥30% reduction in hospitalization rate.

symptoms: Rapid-onset vomiting, nausea, cramping within 1–6 hours of eating
treatment_goal: Control vomiting, maintain hydration, prevent dehydration
first_line_soc: Supportive only: ORS or IV fluids; antiemetics (ondansetron). Antibiotics not indicated (pre-formed toxin, not active infection).
treatment_algorithm: ORS for mild dehydration; IV fluids (normal saline or LR) for severe vomiting. Ondansetron 4–8mg PO/IV for antiemetic. Antiemetics (promethazine, prochlorperazine as alternatives). No antibiotics needed. Self-limited: resolves in 24–48 hours. Antidiarrheals generally not needed.
us_prevalence: ~241,000 Staph foodborne illness cases/year in the US (CDC estimates); seasonal peaks. Rarely requires hospitalization.
patients_needing_tx: ~241,000/year but <5% require hospital-level care; ~12,000 medical interventions needed.
price: $100–$500 per episode (anti-toxin or enhanced antiemetic).
registrational_endpoint: Time to cessation of vomiting; proportion symptom-free at 24h.
btd_threshold_vs_soc: Time to vomiting cessation ≤4h vs. ~8h with ondansetron alone.

symptoms: Sudden large-volume hematemesis, melena, dizziness, hypotension in cirrhosis
treatment_goal: Achieve hemostasis, prevent rebleeding, treat portal hypertension
first_line_soc: Acute variceal hemorrhage: IV octreotide (somatostatin analog; reduces portal pressure) + emergency endoscopic band ligation (EVL). Non-selective beta-blockers (propranolol, nadolol, carvedilol) for primary and secondary prophylaxis.
treatment_algorithm: Acute: IV octreotide 50mcg bolus → 50mcg/h infusion × 3–5 days + endoscopic EVL + antibiotics (ceftriaxone) for SBP prophylaxis. TIPS procedure for refractory bleeding. Primary prophylaxis: NSBB (propranolol, nadolol, carvedilol) or EVL for high-risk varices. Secondary prophylaxis: NSBB + EVL (superior to either alone). 2024: Sorafenib/antifibrotic agents under investigation for portal hypertension reduction. Simtuzumab, stellate cell-targeted therapies in trials.
us_prevalence: ~600,000–700,000 US cirrhosis patients with varices; ~30,000–40,000 variceal bleeds/year. Cirrhosis affects ~3–4 million Americans.
patients_needing_tx: ~600,000 patients with esophageal varices requiring prophylaxis; ~35,000/year acute bleed requiring emergency management.
price: $15,000–$40,000/year (novel portal pressure-reducing agent).
registrational_endpoint: Primary: Prevention of first variceal bleed at 2 years (primary prophylaxis); rebleeding rate at 1 year (secondary). HVPG reduction ≥20% or to <12 mmHg.
btd_threshold_vs_soc: ≥40% relative risk reduction in variceal bleeding at 1 year vs. carvedilol monotherapy alone; or HVPG responder rate ≥70% vs. ~55% with NSBB.

symptoms: Intermittent or chronic GI bleeding, iron deficiency anemia, melena or hematochezia
treatment_goal: Achieve hemostasis, prevent recurrent bleeding, treat iron deficiency
first_line_soc: Endoscopic thermal coagulation (APC—argon plasma coagulation) for angioectasias/AVMs; IV iron supplementation for associated iron deficiency anemia. Thalidomide (immunomodulator) or octreotide for refractory angiodysplasia bleeding.
treatment_algorithm: Angiodysplasia/AVMs: Endoscopic APC or bipolar electrocoagulation. Iron supplementation (IV ferric carboxymaltose or oral ferrous sulfate). Refractory GI angiodysplasia: Thalidomide (50–100mg/day; antiangiogenic)—shown effective in small trials; pomalidomide (lenalidomide analog) under investigation. Octreotide LAR (somatostatin analog) 20–40mg IM monthly reduces bleeding. GAVE (gastric antral vascular ectasia): APC, TIPS for refractory. Hereditary hemorrhagic telangiectasia (HHT): Bevacizumab (anti-VEGF) IV for systemic angiodysplasia.
us_prevalence: ~1–2% of all GI bleeding attributable to vascular lesions; ~100,000–150,000 cases/year requiring intervention. HHT: ~100,000 US patients.
patients_needing_tx: ~100,000–150,000 requiring pharmacologic + endoscopic management; ~30,000 refractory cases needing systemic therapy.
price: $20,000–$60,000/year (anti-angiogenic biologic for refractory angiodysplasia).
registrational_endpoint: Overt GI rebleeding rate at 1 year; transfusion requirement reduction (units/patient/year) vs. placebo.
btd_threshold_vs_soc: ≥50% reduction in transfusion requirements or rebleeding events at 1 year vs. endoscopic SoC alone.

symptoms: Bloody diarrhea, urgency, abdominal cramping, rectal bleeding, tenesmus
treatment_goal: Achieve and maintain mucosal healing, eliminate rectal bleeding
first_line_soc: Mild-moderate: Oral and/or rectal mesalamine (5-ASA; Asacol HD, Lialda, Apriso). Moderate-severe: Anti-TNF (infliximab, adalimumab) or vedolizumab (Entyvio).
treatment_algorithm: Mild-moderate: Mesalamine (5-ASA) 2.4–4.8g/day oral ± topical; rectal mesalamine suppositories for proctitis; topical corticosteroids for flares. Moderate: Oral prednisone for bridging; azathioprine/6-MP as maintenance immunomodulators. Moderate-severe biologic: Anti-TNF—infliximab (Remicade), adalimumab (Humira), golimumab (Simponi); vedolizumab (Entyvio; anti-α4β7); ustekinumab (Stelara; anti-IL-12/23). JAK inhibitors: tofacitinib (Xeljanz; FDA-approved 2018), upadacitinib (Rinvoq; FDA-approved 2022), filgotinib (Jyseleca; EMA-approved). Anti-IL-23 p19: mirikizumab (Omvoh; FDA-approved 2023); risankizumab (off-label). S1P: ozanimod (Zeposia; FDA-approved 2021), etrasimod (Velsipity; FDA-approved 2023). Severe/hospitalized: IV methylprednisolone → cyclosporine or infliximab rescue. 2024–2025: Icotrokinra (IL-2 mutein), obefazimod (microRNA-124) in late-stage trials.
us_prevalence: ~900,000–1.1 million US patients with UC (CCFA/AGA 2024); ~70,000 new diagnoses/year. Incidence stable-to-rising.
patients_needing_tx: ~800,000 require pharmacologic therapy; ~300,000 on biologics/JAK inhibitors; ~150,000 biologic-refractory.
price: $40,000–$70,000/year (biologic or JAK inhibitor).
registrational_endpoint: Clinical remission (Mayo score ≤2, no subscore >1, rectal bleeding subscore=0) at week 8 (induction) and week 52 (maintenance); endoscopic improvement (Mayo endoscopic subscore ≤1).
btd_threshold_vs_soc: Induction remission ≥25% at week 8 in biologic-refractory patients vs. ~5–10% placebo; or induction remission ≥40% in biologic-naive vs. ~15% placebo.

symptoms: Chronic diarrhea, abdominal pain, weight loss, fatigue, rectal bleeding
treatment_goal: Achieve and maintain deep remission, prevent complications and surgery
first_line_soc: Moderate-severe: Induction with anti-TNF biologic—adalimumab (Humira; anti-TNF) or infliximab (Remicade; anti-TNF) ± immunomodulator (azathioprine/6-MP). Mild-moderate: Budesonide (topical steroid) for ileocolonic Crohn's.
treatment_algorithm: Mild-moderate: Budesonide (Entocort 9mg/day; topical steroid); sulfasalazine for colonic disease. Moderate-severe induction: Anti-TNF (infliximab, adalimumab, certolizumab pegol/Cimzia) ± immunomodulator (azathioprine, 6-mercaptopurine, methotrexate). Anti-integrins: vedolizumab (Entyvio; anti-α4β7). Anti-IL-12/23: ustekinumab (Stelara). Anti-IL-23 (p19): risankizumab (Skyrizi; FDA-approved 2022), mirikizumab (Omvoh; FDA-approved 2023). JAK inhibitors: upadacitinib (Rinvoq; FDA-approved 2023 for moderate-severe CD). Combination therapy (anti-TNF + immunomodulator) for high-risk patients. 2024: Ozanimod (S1P modulator) approved. Biosimilars: infliximab-abda (Renflexis), adalimumab-atto (Amjevita) widely used.
us_prevalence: ~780,000–900,000 US patients with Crohn's disease (CCFA/CDC data); ~70,000 new diagnoses/year. Incidence rising.
patients_needing_tx: ~600,000–700,000 require moderate-to-advanced pharmacologic therapy; ~200,000 on biologics; ~100,000 biologic-refractory needing novel agents.
price: $45,000–$80,000/year (biologic or JAK inhibitor).
registrational_endpoint: Clinical remission (CDAI <150 or PRO-2 score) at week 12 (induction) and week 52 (maintenance); endoscopic response (SES-CD ≥50% reduction) at week 12.
btd_threshold_vs_soc: Clinical remission rate ≥45% at week 12 and ≥35% at week 52 vs. ~20–30% placebo; or head-to-head non-inferiority/superiority vs. anti-TNF.

symptoms: Abdominal pain/cramping, bloating, altered bowel habits (diarrhea/constipation)
treatment_goal: Relieve abdominal pain, normalize stool, improve quality of life
first_line_soc: IBS-D: Rifaximin (Xifaxan 550mg TID × 14 days; non-absorbable antibiotic) or alosetron (Lotronex; 5-HT3 antagonist; women with severe IBS-D). IBS-C: Linaclotide (Linzess; guanylate cyclase agonist) or lubiprostone (Amitiza; chloride channel activator).
treatment_algorithm: First-line: Dietary modification (low-FODMAP diet), fiber supplementation. IBS-C: Linaclotide (Linzess) 290mcg/day, plecanatide (Trulance) 3mg/day, lubiprostone (Amitiza) 8mcg BID—all FDA-approved. Tenapanor (Ibsrela; NHE3 inhibitor)—FDA-approved 2019 for IBS-C. IBS-D: Rifaximin 550mg TID × 14 days (FDA-approved); alosetron 0.5–1mg BID (REMS program); eluxadoline (Viberzi; mu-opioid agonist/delta antagonist)—FDA-approved 2015. Global: Antispasmodics (dicyclomine, hyoscyamine); TCAs (amitriptyline 10–25mg) for pain; SNRIs (duloxetine). IBS-M: Case-by-case.
us_prevalence: ~25–30 million Americans with IBS (10–15% US prevalence; Rome IV criteria); ~10–15 million with moderate-severe IBS seeking medical care.
patients_needing_tx: ~10–15 million require pharmacologic therapy; ~5 million on prescription medications.
registrational_endpoint: FDA-preferred: Composite responder—≥30% reduction in abdominal pain NRS + improvement in stool frequency/consistency for ≥50% of weeks during 12-week treatment period.
btd_threshold_vs_soc: ≥35% composite responder rate vs. ~10–15% placebo; or significant superiority over rifaximin (IBS-D) or linaclotide (IBS-C) in head-to-head.

symptoms: Bloating, gas, diarrhea, abdominal cramping, malabsorption, belching
treatment_goal: Eradicate bacterial overgrowth, relieve symptoms, address underlying cause
first_line_soc: Rifaximin (Xifaxan 550mg TID × 14 days; non-absorbable antibiotic)—most widely used and best-evidence antibiotic for SIBO. Alternatively, metronidazole or neomycin for methane-predominant SIBO.
treatment_algorithm: Step 1: Treat underlying cause (scleroderma, strictures, prokinetic trial). Step 2: Rifaximin (Xifaxan) 550mg TID × 14 days—most data; normalizes breath hydrogen test in ~70%. Step 3: Rotating antibiotics: ciprofloxacin 500mg BID × 7–14 days, or tetracycline, metronidazole, neomycin 500mg BID (especially for methane/constipation-predominant SIBO). Step 4: Prokinetics (low-dose naltrexone, erythromycin, prucalopride) to prevent recurrence. Elemental diet (2 weeks) as non-antibiotic approach. 2024: SYN-010 (modified-release lovastatin; reduces methane) in trials.
us_prevalence: ~30–80 million Americans may have SIBO (wide prevalence estimates based on breath testing); clinically significant SIBO: ~5–15 million. Rising awareness.
patients_needing_tx: ~5–10 million US patients with symptomatic SIBO requiring pharmacologic treatment.
price: $2,000–$6,000 per course or $10,000–$20,000/year (recurrent SIBO).
registrational_endpoint: Normalization of lactulose or glucose breath hydrogen test + global symptom score improvement at 4 weeks post-treatment.
btd_threshold_vs_soc: Breath test normalization in ≥75% and ≥50% symptom responder rate vs. ~50–60% breath test normalization with rifaximin alone.

symptoms: Chronic diarrhea, bloating, weight loss, fatigue, iron deficiency anemia
treatment_goal: Achieve mucosal healing via strict gluten-free diet; manage complications
first_line_soc: Strict gluten-free diet (GFD) is the only currently approved treatment. No pharmacologic first-line SoC exists.
treatment_algorithm: Standard: Strict GFD (all wheat, barley, rye eliminated) lifelong—reverses mucosal changes in ~70–80%. Vitamin/mineral supplementation (B12, folic acid, iron, calcium, vitamin D) for nutritional deficiencies. Refractory celiac disease (RCD): Budesonide or prednisone for symptom control; azathioprine/6-MP. RCD Type II: Cladribine, stem cell transplant. 2023–2025 Novel pipeline: (1) Larazotide acetate (tight junction regulator)—Phase 3; (2) Nexvax2 (vaccine)—trials paused; (3) TAK-101 (nanoparticle tolerance induction)—Phase 2; (4) Latiglutenase (ALV003; gluten-degrading enzyme cocktail)—Phase 3; (5) IgA anti-TG2 in clinical development.
us_prevalence: ~3 million Americans have celiac disease (~1% prevalence; AGA/Celiac Disease Foundation); ~80% undiagnosed.
patients_needing_tx: ~3 million with celiac disease; ~1 million diagnosed and on GFD; ~250,000 with non-responsive celiac disease needing adjunctive pharmacotherapy.
price: $20,000–$50,000/year (biologic or enzyme therapy adjunct to GFD).
registrational_endpoint: Villous atrophy recovery: Vh:Cd ratio improvement on duodenal biopsy at 52 weeks + symptomatic improvement (CSI-total score). Or, for contamination protection: mucosal injury prevention after 6-week gluten challenge.
btd_threshold_vs_soc: ≥50% of patients achieve Vh:Cd ≥3.0 at week 52 vs. ~40% GFD alone; or prevention of mucosal injury (Vh:Cd drop) in ≥80% during gluten challenge vs. ~20% placebo.

symptoms: Chronic diarrhea, malabsorption, malnutrition, weight loss, parenteral nutrition dependence
treatment_goal: Achieve intestinal autonomy, reduce parenteral nutrition dependence
first_line_soc: Teduglutide (Gattex/Revestive; GLP-2 analog) SC injection—FDA-approved 2012; promotes intestinal adaptation, reduces PN requirements. Loperamide for diarrhea control.
treatment_algorithm: Fluid/electrolyte management + parenteral nutrition (PN) in acute phase. Oral rehydration (WHO-ORS). Antidiarrheals: loperamide (high-dose), codeine, tincture of opium. Antisecretories: PPIs + H2RAs; octreotide for high-output stoma. Step 2: Teduglutide (Gattex) 0.05mg/kg/day SC—FDA-approved; reduces PN by ~20–40%. Step 3: Human growth hormone (somatropin; Zorbtive) ± glutamine + specialized rehabilitation diet (FDA-approved for SBS). 2024: Glepaglutide (long-acting GLP-2 analog) Phase 3 completed; apraglutide (GLP-2; once-weekly) Phase 3. Surgical intestinal lengthening (STEP procedure) for select patients.
us_prevalence: ~15,000–25,000 US patients on long-term parenteral nutrition for SBS; ~40,000–50,000 total SBS patients.
patients_needing_tx: ~20,000–25,000 PN-dependent SBS patients requiring pharmacologic therapy; ~15,000 eligible for teduglutide.
price: $200,000–$400,000/year (GLP-2 analogs; teduglutide WAC ~$295,000/year).
registrational_endpoint: Reduction in PN volume ≥20% from baseline at week 24; proportion achieving PN reduction ≥20% (responder rate).
btd_threshold_vs_soc: PN independence achieved in ≥30% at week 52 vs. ~10–15% with teduglutide alone; or PN reduction ≥50% in ≥60% vs. ~35% with teduglutide.

symptoms: Bloating, flatulence, diarrhea, abdominal cramping after carbohydrate ingestion
treatment_goal: Eliminate symptoms, prevent malnutrition from dietary restriction
first_line_soc: Lactase supplementation (Lactaid; exogenous lactase enzyme) for lactose intolerance. Dietary restriction of offending carbohydrate (lactose, fructose, sucrose).
treatment_algorithm: Lactose intolerance: Lactase enzyme supplements (Lactaid; beta-galactosidase) before meals; low-lactose diet; calcium supplementation. Fructose malabsorption: Low-fructose diet (FODMAP restriction). Sucrase-isomaltase deficiency: Sacrosidase (Sucraid; oral enzyme) FDA-approved for congenital sucrase-isomaltase deficiency. GLUT5 fructose transporter deficiency: Dietary restriction. Trehalase deficiency (rare): Avoidance of mushrooms.
us_prevalence: Lactose intolerance: ~30–50 million Americans (10–15% of Caucasians, ~70–90% of African Americans). Congenital sucrase-isomaltase deficiency: ~0.2% (~660,000 Americans).
patients_needing_tx: ~30 million lactose-intolerant seeking medical management; ~660,000 congenital SI deficiency (sacrosidase-eligible). Novel enzyme replacement: ~500,000–1M.
price: $2,000–$10,000/year (enzyme replacement for congenital deficiencies); $200–$500/year OTC for lactose.
registrational_endpoint: Symptom severity score (bloating, pain, stool consistency) at 4 weeks post-treatment vs. placebo; breath hydrogen test normalization.
btd_threshold_vs_soc: ≥50% symptom score improvement vs. ~25% placebo + dietary restriction.

symptoms: Protein-losing enteropathy, bilateral leg edema, diarrhea, low albumin
treatment_goal: Reduce lymph pressure, replace lost proteins, prevent malnutrition
first_line_soc: Low-fat diet with medium-chain triglyceride (MCT) supplementation (MCT oils bypass lymphatics); high-protein diet supplementation.
treatment_algorithm: Dietary: MCT-based diet + high-protein supplementation. Secondary causes: Treat underlying cause (heart disease, retroperitoneal fibrosis, lymphoma). Octreotide (somatostatin analog; reduces intestinal lymph flow) 100–200mcg SC TID—effective in case reports/small series. Sirolimus (mTOR inhibitor) for Gorham-Stout or lymphatic malformation-related cases. Antiplasmin therapy for congenital cases. Corticosteroids for inflammatory causes. No FDA-approved drug.
us_prevalence: Rare; ~1–5 per 100,000; ~3,000–15,000 US patients. Primary (congenital) and secondary forms.
patients_needing_tx: ~5,000–10,000 US patients requiring active management.
price: $30,000–$80,000/year (rare disease; novel lymphangiogenesis modulator or enzyme).
registrational_endpoint: Serum albumin normalization at 12 weeks; fecal α1-antitrypsin clearance (protein loss) reduction; edema resolution.
btd_threshold_vs_soc: Serum albumin ≥3.5 g/dL maintained in ≥60% at 24 weeks vs. ~30% with MCT diet alone.

symptoms: Diarrhea, weight loss, arthralgias, lymphadenopathy, neurologic symptoms
treatment_goal: Eradicate Tropheryma whipplei, achieve clinical and histologic remission
first_line_soc: IV ceftriaxone 2g/day × 2 weeks (induction), then oral trimethoprim-sulfamethoxazole (TMP-SMX; Bactrim) 1 DS tablet BID × 1–2 years (maintenance).
treatment_algorithm: Induction: IV ceftriaxone 2g/day × 2 weeks (or IV penicillin G + streptomycin). Maintenance: TMP-SMX DS BID × minimum 1 year (many recommend 2 years or indefinitely for CNS involvement). Alternative maintenance: Doxycycline + hydroxychloroquine × 12+ months (for intracellular bactericidal activity). Relapse management: repeat IV induction. Nutritional supplementation: vitamins A, D, E, K, B12, folic acid, iron. No novel drug development—extremely rare disease.
us_prevalence: Extremely rare; ~30–40 new cases/year in the US; estimated ~1 per million. Total US prevalence: ~200–500 active cases.
patients_needing_tx: ~300–400 US patients require antibiotic therapy at any given time.
price: $5,000–$20,000 per course (rare disease premium; novel intracellular antibiotic).
registrational_endpoint: PCR negativity for T. whipplei in duodenal biopsy and/or blood at 12 months; clinical remission (no diarrhea, weight gain, arthralgias-free).
btd_threshold_vs_soc: PCR negativity at 6 months in ≥90% vs. ~80% with TMP-SMX; or shorter induction period (1 vs. 2 weeks) with equivalent outcomes.

symptoms: Chronic diarrhea, malabsorption, weight loss, megaloblastic anemia, nutritional deficiencies
treatment_goal: Eradicate tropical sprue, restore mucosal function, correct nutritional deficiencies
first_line_soc: Tetracycline (250mg QID × 3–6 months) + folic acid (5mg/day) supplementation—highly effective combination.
treatment_algorithm: Tetracycline or doxycycline (200mg/day × 3–6 months) + folic acid 5mg/day (usually 1 month of folic acid sufficient for hematologic recovery); Vitamin B12 injections (IM cyanocobalamin) if B12-deficient; Iron supplementation if iron-deficient. Most cases resolve completely with this regimen. No novel pharmacologic development given rare/travel-related nature.
us_prevalence: Rare in the US; seen in travelers/immigrants from tropical regions. ~1,000–5,000 cases/year in the US.
patients_needing_tx: ~3,000 cases/year requiring antibiotic + nutritional supplementation.
price: $200–$1,000 per course (antibiotic + vitamins).
registrational_endpoint: Complete symptom resolution + normalization of D-xylose absorption test at 3 months.
btd_threshold_vs_soc: Remission in ≥95% at 12 weeks vs. ~85–90% with doxycycline + folic acid (shorter course superiority).

symptoms: Chronic epigastric pain, steatorrhea, weight loss, diabetes, malabsorption
treatment_goal: Control pain, treat exocrine/endocrine insufficiency, prevent progression
first_line_soc: Pancreatic enzyme replacement therapy (PERT: pancrelipase [Creon, Pancreaze, Zenpep]; serine protease cocktail) for exocrine pancreatic insufficiency. Analgesics (non-opioids preferred) for pain.
treatment_algorithm: Exocrine insufficiency: PERT—pancrelipase (Creon 72,000 lipase units with main meals + 36,000 with snacks). Endocrine insufficiency: Insulin therapy. Pain: NSAIDs/acetaminophen → TCAs (amitriptyline) → low-dose naltrexone → opioids (last resort). Antioxidants (selenium, ascorbic acid, β-carotene, methionine, vitamin E). Celiac plexus block for refractory pain. Pancreatic enzyme supplementation may reduce pain (feedback inhibition of CCK). 2024: Dihydroergotamine and somatostatin analogs studied for pain. Endoscopic or surgical drainage for ductal obstruction.
us_prevalence: ~280,000–400,000 US patients with chronic pancreatitis; ~50,000 new cases/year. Prevalence rising with alcohol use and pancreatogenic diabetes recognition.
patients_needing_tx: ~200,000–300,000 require PERT; ~100,000 require opioid or interventional pain management; ~150,000 have pancreatogenic diabetes.
price: $20,000–$50,000/year (novel anti-fibrotic or disease-modifying agent).
registrational_endpoint: Pain responder rate (≥30% reduction in NRS weekly pain scores) at 12 weeks; coefficient of fat absorption (CFA) improvement for EPI.
btd_threshold_vs_soc: ≥40% pain responder rate at 12 weeks vs. ~20–25% with non-opioid analgesics + PERT; or CFA ≥85% in ≥80% of EPI patients vs. ~60% with PERT alone.

symptoms: Severe epigastric pain radiating to back, nausea, vomiting, fever, elevated lipase
treatment_goal: Resolve inflammation, prevent necrosis and systemic complications
first_line_soc: Aggressive IV fluid resuscitation (lactated Ringer's solution preferred); early enteral nutrition; analgesics (opioids: hydromorphone, morphine IV). No specific anti-inflammatory drug approved.
treatment_algorithm: Step 1: IV LR at 250–500mL/hr for resuscitation. Step 2: Analgesics—IV hydromorphone or fentanyl; NSAIDs (ketorolac) as adjuncts. Step 3: Early enteral nutrition (nasogastric/nasojejunal) within 24–48h (superior to TPN). Step 4: Antibiotics (imipenem, meropenem) only for infected pancreatic necrosis (not routine). Step 5: ERCP for gallstone pancreatitis with cholangitis. Step 6: Interventional drainage of walled-off necrosis. 2024 pipeline: TLC599 (dexamethasone phospholipid complex); recombinant secretory leukocyte protease inhibitor (rSLPI); IL-6 inhibitors investigated.
us_prevalence: ~270,000–300,000 hospitalizations/year in the US for acute pancreatitis; second most common GI hospitalization. Annual incidence ~30–40/100,000.
patients_needing_tx: ~270,000/year all require active pharmacologic management (analgesics, fluids); ~30,000–50,000 severe cases with necrosis needing advanced therapy.
price: $5,000–$25,000 per episode (novel anti-inflammatory or protease inhibitor inpatient drug).
registrational_endpoint: Modified Marshall score for organ failure at 72 hours; proportion with organ failure or local complications at 7 days; 30-day all-cause mortality.
btd_threshold_vs_soc: ≥30% relative reduction in organ failure rate or 30-day mortality vs. aggressive LR hydration + supportive care.

symptoms: Usually asymptomatic; large cysts cause abdominal fullness, pain, early satiety
treatment_goal: Treat symptomatic or complicated cysts; rule out malignancy
first_line_soc: Observation for asymptomatic simple cysts. Aspiration-sclerotherapy (ethanol injection) or fenestration for large symptomatic cysts. No pharmacologic SoC.
treatment_algorithm: Simple cysts: observation. Large symptomatic: laparoscopic fenestration or percutaneous aspiration + ethanol sclerotherapy. Polycystic liver disease (PLD): mTOR inhibitors (everolimus, sirolimus) or somatostatin analogues (lanreotide) shown to reduce liver volume in PLD (FDA approved lanreotide for PLD 2022). Hydatid (echinococcal) cysts: albendazole 400 mg BID ± PAIR procedure.
us_prevalence: ~3–5% of population with hepatic cysts; PLD: ~50,000–80,000 US patients.
patients_needing_tx: ~100,000 patients with PLD or symptomatic cysts requiring pharmacologic therapy.
registrational_endpoint: Total liver volume reduction ≥20% at 12 months; symptom score improvement; quality of life
btd_threshold_vs_soc: Total liver volume reduction ≥30% vs. ~15% placebo; sustained ≥24 months

symptoms: Usually asymptomatic; RUQ discomfort, incidental finding on imaging
treatment_goal: Monitor for growth/malignant transformation; treat if symptomatic or risk of rupture
first_line_soc: Observation/imaging surveillance for most (hemangiomas, FNH). Cessation of OCP for hepatocellular adenoma. Surgery for symptomatic or large (>5 cm) adenomas.
treatment_algorithm: Hemangioma (most common): observation; surgery/ablation only if symptomatic. FNH: no treatment. Hepatocellular adenoma: stop OCPs/androgens; serial MRI; resection if >5 cm (rupture/malignancy risk). Beta-catenin mutated adenoma: resection preferred. No pharmacologic SoC; OCP cessation leads to regression in many adenomas.
us_prevalence: ~1–5 million US patients with benign liver tumors (majority hemangiomas); FNH ~200,000; HCA ~50,000.
patients_needing_tx: ~30,000 patients with symptomatic or high-risk benign tumors requiring intervention.
price: $30,000–$80,000/year for a novel antiangiogenic/regression-inducing agent. $50,000.
registrational_endpoint: Tumor volume reduction ≥30% at 12 months; regression rate; complication prevention
btd_threshold_vs_soc: Tumor size reduction ≥30% in HCA vs. observation; reduced surgical intervention rate

symptoms: Usually asymptomatic; rarely RUQ pain or Kasabach-Merritt syndrome (giant hemangioma)
treatment_goal: Monitor; treat only symptomatic or giant (>10 cm) hemangiomas
first_line_soc: Observation for most (<5 cm) hepatic hemangiomas. Surgical resection or ablation for giant symptomatic hemangiomas. Propranolol (beta-blocker) for infantile hepatic hemangiomas.
treatment_algorithm: Infantile hemangiomas: oral propranolol 2 mg/kg/day (FDA approved for infantile hemangioma 2014, Hemangeol). Adult hepatic hemangiomas: observation. Giant/symptomatic: surgery, TACE, or liver transplant (rare). Kasabach-Merritt (thrombocytopenia + hemolysis): vincristine + sirolimus (off-label). No pharmacologic SoC for routine adult hepatic hemangioma.
us_prevalence: ~5–10% of adults have liver hemangiomas = ~16–33 million. Infantile hepatic: ~1% of infants requiring treatment (~40,000/year).
patients_needing_tx: ~100,000 patients/year requiring pharmacologic treatment (mostly infantile).
price: $3,000–$8,000/course (propranolol ~$3K/course for infantile). $5,000.
peak_sales: 20% novel agent share vs. propranolol: ~$100M peak
registrational_endpoint: Lesion volume reduction ≥50%; complete involution rate; Kasabach-Merritt: platelet normalization
btd_threshold_vs_soc: Complete involution ≥85% at 12 months vs. ~75% propranolol in infantile hemangioma

symptoms: Right upper quadrant pain, incidental liver mass, rarely bleeding/rupture
treatment_goal: Prevent rupture/malignant transformation; regress or resect lesion
first_line_soc: Cessation of oral contraceptives/anabolic steroids (leads to regression in >50%). Surgical resection for >5 cm lesions or men with adenoma (higher malignant risk).
treatment_algorithm: Discontinue OCP or androgens — monitor for regression at 6 months. Size <5 cm in women: watchful waiting post-OCP cessation. Size >5 cm or men: laparoscopic resection or radiofrequency ablation. Pregnancy: avoid; risk of hemorrhage. Specific subtypes: HNF1α-mutant (low malignant risk), β-catenin mutated (high malignant risk — resect all). No approved pharmacologic agent; GLP-1 agonists (semaglutide) under investigation for metabolic adenoma regression.
us_prevalence: ~30,000–50,000 US patients with HCA, predominantly women of reproductive age on OCPs.
patients_needing_tx: ~15,000 patients requiring pharmacologic or surgical management (OCP cessation alone inadequate or high-risk features).
price: $20,000–$40,000/year for novel β-catenin pathway inhibitor or anti-proliferative agent. $30,000.
registrational_endpoint: Lesion regression ≥50% by volumetric MRI at 12 months; prevention of malignant transformation
btd_threshold_vs_soc: ≥50% volumetric regression in ≥60% of β-catenin-mutant HCA vs. ~10% with OCP cessation alone

symptoms: Usually asymptomatic; rarely RUQ pain or palpable mass on imaging
treatment_goal: Confirm benign diagnosis; reassure patient; no pharmacologic treatment
first_line_soc: Observation with MRI surveillance. No pharmacologic treatment needed.
treatment_algorithm: FNH is benign, non-progressive, and does not require treatment. Contraceptive pill does not cause FNH (unlike adenoma) but OCP may increase lesion vascularity. Surgery only if diagnosis uncertain (exclude fibrolamellar HCC) or symptomatic (rare). No pharmacologic intervention indicated.
us_prevalence: ~300,000–500,000 US patients with FNH (common incidental finding).
patients_needing_tx: ~5,000 patients warranting active workup; essentially no pharmacologic treatment needed.
price: N/A. Hypothetical: $5,000–$15,000 for a regression-inducing agent. $10,000.
registrational_endpoint: Lesion size reduction ≥30%; time to radiographic resolution
btd_threshold_vs_soc: N/A — benign condition with no pharmacologic SoC

symptoms: Recurrent biliary colic, nausea, RUQ pain after meals, dyspepsia
treatment_goal: Relieve symptoms; prevent acute exacerbations; definitive surgical cure
first_line_soc: Laparoscopic cholecystectomy. Symptomatic management pre-surgery: antispasmodics, NSAIDs, low-fat diet.
treatment_algorithm: Surgery (definitive). Pre-operatively: hyoscine butylbromide (antispasmodic) or dicyclomine for pain. Ursodiol for cholesterol stone dissolution in high-surgical-risk patients. H2-blockers/PPIs for dyspeptic symptoms. NSAIDs for pain control. No novel pharmacologic SoC beyond cholecystectomy.
us_prevalence: ~5–10 million US patients with chronic cholecystitis symptoms.
patients_needing_tx: ~1 million patients/year requiring pharmacologic symptom management pre-surgery.
price: $3,000–$8,000/year for a novel anti-inflammatory biliary agent. $5,500.
registrational_endpoint: Pain frequency reduction (VAS score); time to cholecystectomy; biliary colic episode rate
btd_threshold_vs_soc: ≥50% reduction in biliary colic frequency vs. placebo over 12 weeks

symptoms: Jaundice, pruritus, fatigue, cholangitis attacks, elevated ALP/GGT
treatment_goal: Relieve biliary obstruction, prevent cholangiocarcinoma, delay transplant need
first_line_soc: ERCP-guided balloon dilation and short-term stenting for dominant strictures. Antibiotics for cholangitis. UDCA controversial for PSC (avoid high-dose); used for IgG4-SC as 2nd-line after steroids.
treatment_algorithm: Secondary (IgG4-SC): corticosteroids (prednisone 40 mg/day, taper). Primary (PSC): no approved therapy — see PSC entry. Dominant strictures: ERCP every 6–12 months. Cholangitis prophylaxis: rotating antibiotics or continuous trimethoprim. CCA surveillance: MRCP + CA19-9 annually. Liver transplant (PSC): 5-year survival ~85%. 2024-25 pipeline: same as PSC (norUDCA, OAT inhibitors, FXR agonists).
patients_needing_tx: ~45,000 patients requiring pharmacologic + procedural management.
price: $80,000–$120,000/year for first approved PSC-modifying agent. $100,000.
registrational_endpoint: ALP reduction ≥40% AND/OR liver stiffness improvement ≥15% by MRE at 24 weeks; transplant-free survival
btd_threshold_vs_soc: ALP composite ≥40% reduction in ≥35% patients AND liver stiffness HR <0.70 for transplant/death

symptoms: Pruritus, fatigue, jaundice, dry eyes/mouth (Sicca), elevated ALP
treatment_goal: Normalize ALP, slow fibrosis progression, relieve pruritus
first_line_soc: Ursodeoxycholic acid (UDCA) 13–15 mg/kg/day — normalizes ALP, delays fibrosis in ~50–60% biochemical responders.
treatment_algorithm: First-line: UDCA 13–15 mg/kg/day (decades of evidence). Inadequate responder (Paris-II criteria failure): add obeticholic acid (Ocaliva, FXR agonist) 5–10 mg QD (FDA-approved 2016; black box warning cirrhosis). Seladelpar (PPARδ agonist, FDA-approved 2024 — Livdelzi) for patients intolerant/inadequate to UDCA. Elafibranor (PPARα/δ agonist, FDA-approved 2024 — Iqirvo) — another 2L option. Bezafibrate + UDCA (Japan, off-label US). Pruritus: cholestyramine → rifampicin → naltrexone → sertraline → maralixibat (IBAT inhibitor). Liver transplant for end-stage PBC (excellent outcomes).
us_prevalence: ~130,000–150,000 US patients with PBC; predominantly women 40–60 years (F:M ratio 9:1).
patients_needing_tx: ~130,000 patients need UDCA ± add-on therapy; ~40,000 are inadequate UDCA responders.
price: $70,000–$120,000/year for new 2nd-line agent (seladelpar WAC ~$96K; elafibranor ~$104K).
peak_sales: 35% market share for leading 2L agent: ~$1.4B peak
registrational_endpoint: ALP reduction ≥40% from baseline AND ALP <1.67× ULN AND total bilirubin ≤ULN AND not worsened at 12 months (FDA-accepted composite); pruritus NRS for anti-pruritic agents
btd_threshold_vs_soc: ALP composite response ≥50% vs. ~20% with UDCA alone at 12 months; pruritus NRS ≥3-point improvement vs. cholestyramine

symptoms: Pruritus, fatigue, jaundice, cholangitis episodes, IBD co-occurrence
treatment_goal: Slow fibrosis, prevent cholangiocarcinoma, manage complications
first_line_soc: No FDA-approved pharmacologic therapy for PSC fibrosis. UDCA no longer recommended at high dose (UDCA >28 mg/kg associated with harm in UCSF trial). Antibiotics for dominant stricture-associated cholangitis.
treatment_algorithm: Dominant strictures: ERCP balloon dilation ± stenting. Acute bacterial cholangitis: IV ciprofloxacin or piperacillin-tazobactam. Pruritus: cholestyramine → rifampicin. IBD co-management (5-ASA, biologics). High-dose UDCA no longer recommended (PHUSE study showed harm). 2024-25 investigational: norUDCA (Phase 3 — Nor-UDCA trial — modest ALP reduction); NGM282 (FGF19 analog); Vidofludimus calcium (OAT4 agonist, Phase 2); GSK2330811 (oncostatin-M antibody, Phase 2). Liver transplant for end-stage PSC; ~50% recurrence risk post-transplant.
us_prevalence: ~35,000–50,000 US patients with PSC; 70% have co-existing IBD.
patients_needing_tx: ~35,000 patients needing pharmacologic management; large unmet need with no approved therapy.
price: $80,000–$150,000/year for novel anti-fibrotic or immunomodulatory PSC agent. $100,000.
registrational_endpoint: ALP reduction ≥40% from baseline (surrogate for reduced transplant-free survival); liver stiffness reduction by MRE; PSC-specific symptom score improvement
btd_threshold_vs_soc: ALP reduction ≥40% in ≥30% of patients vs. <5% placebo at 24 weeks, AND MRE liver stiffness reduction ≥15%

symptoms: RUQ pain, jaundice, fever/chills (Charcot's triad); Reynold's pentad in severe
treatment_goal: Biliary drainage (ERCP stone removal); eradicate biliary infection; prevent recurrence
first_line_soc: IV antibiotics (ceftriaxone + metronidazole or piperacillin-tazobactam) + urgent ERCP with sphincterotomy and stone extraction.
treatment_algorithm: ERCP with bile duct clearance (gold standard). Antibiotics covering gram-negatives/enterococci/anaerobes (piperacillin-tazobactam, imipenem for severe/septic cholangitis). Percutaneous transhepatic cholangiography (PTC) if ERCP fails. Laparoscopic CBD exploration as alternative. Ursodiol: prevention of stone recurrence in selected patients. No novel targeted pharmacotherapy specifically for choledocholithiasis.
us_prevalence: ~600,000 choledocholithiasis episodes/year; ~200,000 acute cholangitis episodes/year.
patients_needing_tx: ~800,000 patients/year receiving pharmacologic antibiotic treatment.
price: $2,000–$5,000/episode for improved antibiotic or bile acid dissolution agent. $4,000.
registrational_endpoint: Clinical cure rate (fever/pain resolution, bilirubin normalization) at 72 hours; bile duct clearance rate
btd_threshold_vs_soc: Clinical cure ≥90% vs. ~80% standard; recurrence rate <15% at 1 year

symptoms: Severe right upper quadrant pain, fever, nausea, Murphy's sign positive
treatment_goal: Resolve acute inflammation; prevent complications; early cholecystectomy
first_line_soc: IV antibiotics (cefazolin or ampicillin-sulbactam for mild-moderate; piperacillin-tazobactam for severe) + analgesics + early laparoscopic cholecystectomy (<72 hours).
treatment_algorithm: Antibiotics: beta-lactam/BLI or cephalosporins covering gram-negatives and anaerobes. Early laporoscopic cholecystectomy (Tokyo Guidelines TG18). Percutaneous cholecystostomy for critically ill/non-surgical candidates. NSAID analgesia. Short antibiotic course (24 hours post-op for mild). No novel pharmacologic agents specifically for acute cholecystitis.
us_prevalence: ~700,000–1,000,000 US acute cholecystitis episodes/year.
patients_needing_tx: ~1,000,000 patients/year receiving antibiotic pharmacotherapy.
price: $1,000–$5,000/episode for improved antibiotic or anti-inflammatory. $3,000.
registrational_endpoint: Clinical cure rate at 72 hours; surgical complication rate; hospital LOS
btd_threshold_vs_soc: Clinical cure ≥90% vs. ~80% standard antibiotics; reduced surgical complication rate

symptoms: Biliary colic (RUQ pain after fatty meals), nausea, bloating, intolerance of fatty food
treatment_goal: Resolve symptoms; prevent complications (cholecystitis, pancreatitis, choledocholithiasis)
first_line_soc: Laparoscopic cholecystectomy (definitive). Ursodiol (ursodeoxycholic acid) for non-surgical candidates with cholesterol stones (dissolves small stones slowly).
treatment_algorithm: Surgery: laparoscopic cholecystectomy (gold standard). Medical dissolution: ursodiol 8–10 mg/kg/day × 6–24 months for cholesterol stones <5 mm in non-surgical patients. Contact dissolution: MTBE (methyl tert-butyl ether) percutaneous (historical, rarely used). Prevention: ursodiol 300–600 mg/day during rapid weight loss. Ezetimibe investigational for cholesterol stone prevention.
us_prevalence: ~25 million US patients with gallstones; ~1 million symptomatic requiring intervention/year.
patients_needing_tx: ~500,000 patients/year on ursodiol or awaiting surgery needing pharmacologic management.
price: $2,000–$10,000/year for a novel oral dissolution agent. $6,000.
registrational_endpoint: Stone dissolution rate (complete or ≥50% reduction) at 12 months by ultrasound
btd_threshold_vs_soc: Complete stone dissolution ≥50% at 12 months vs. ~30% ursodiol alone

disease: Tumors of the Gallbladder and Bile Ducts
symptoms: Obstructive jaundice, right upper quadrant pain, weight loss, pruritus
treatment_goal: Achieve R0 resection if possible; palliate biliary obstruction; extend survival
first_line_soc: Resectable: surgical resection (cholecystectomy ± liver resection for gallbladder cancer; hepatectomy with bile duct excision for hilar CCA). Unresectable/metastatic: gemcitabine + cisplatin (GEMCIS) chemotherapy — first-line since 2010.
treatment_algorithm: Resectable: R0 surgery + adjuvant capecitabine (BILCAP trial). Unresectable/metastatic first-line: gemcitabine + cisplatin + durvalumab (anti-PD-L1, TOPAZ-1 trial, FDA-approved 2022) — OS 12.8 vs. 11.5 months. IDH1-mutant intrahepatic CCA: ivosidenib (Tibsovo, FDA-approved 2021, 2L). FGFR2 fusion BTC: pemigatinib (Pemazyre, FDA-approved 2020) or infigratinib (Truseltiq). NTRK fusion: entrectinib/larotrectinib. HER2-amplified GBC: trastuzumab (off-label). 2024-25: zanidatamab (HER2 bispecific) Phase 3; KRAS G12C inhibitors in trial for biliary tract; futibatinib (FGFR1-4 inhibitor) approved 2022 for FGFR2+ CCA.
us_prevalence: ~12,000 new biliary tract cancers/year (gallbladder cancer ~4,000; CCA ~8,000); prevalence ~25,000. 5-year survival <20% for unresectable.
patients_needing_tx: ~10,000 patients/year need systemic pharmacologic therapy (unresectable or adjuvant).
price: $200,000–$350,000/year for novel targeted agent (FGFR inhibitors WAC ~$160K; futibatinib ~$200K). $250,000.
registrational_endpoint: Overall survival (OS) as primary; ORR (RECIST v1.1) for accelerated approval in biomarker-selected populations; PFS for 2L studies
btd_threshold_vs_soc: OS HR <0.75 vs. GEMCIS in unselected OR ORR ≥30% AND DOR >6 months in biomarker-selected 2L patients

symptoms: Persistent right upper quadrant pain, dyspepsia, bloating after gallbladder removal
treatment_goal: Identify and treat residual/new biliary pathology; relieve symptoms
first_line_soc: None specific. Symptomatic: antispasmodics (hyoscine/dicyclomine), PPIs for dyspepsia, cholestyramine for bile salt diarrhea. ERCP if biliary cause (retained stone, sphincter of Oddi dysfunction).
treatment_algorithm: Workup to exclude: retained CBD stones (ERCP), sphincter of Oddi dysfunction (manometry), bile salt malabsorption, IBS post-cholecystectomy. Bile salt diarrhea: cholestyramine 4g TID or colesevelam. Sphincter of Oddi type I: biliary sphincterotomy via ERCP. UDCA may help bile salt homeostasis. Tricyclic antidepressants (amitriptyline) for functional pain component. Rifaximin for SIBO contributing to bloating.
us_prevalence: ~10–15% of ~800,000 annual cholecystectomies develop PCS = ~80,000–120,000 new cases/year; ~500,000 prevalent.
patients_needing_tx: ~150,000 patients require ongoing pharmacologic management.
price: $10,000–$20,000/year for novel bile acid modulator or motility agent. $15,000.
registrational_endpoint: Abdominal pain NRS reduction ≥3 points at 12 weeks; quality of life (SF-36 or PGIS) improvement
btd_threshold_vs_soc: ≥50% pain responders (NRS ≥3 reduction) vs. <20% with antispasmodics/placebo at 12 weeks

symptoms: Right upper quadrant pain, nausea, fatty food intolerance, no gallstones
treatment_goal: Relieve biliary pain; correct gallbladder dysmotility
first_line_soc: Cholecystectomy (laparoscopic) for documented gallbladder dysfunction (low ejection fraction on HIDA scan). No pharmacologic SoC for acalculous biliary pain.
treatment_algorithm: Cholecystectomy is definitive. Adjunct: antispasmodics (hyoscine); proton pump inhibitors if GERD component. Ursodiol (ursodeoxycholic acid) occasionally tried (limited evidence for acalculous). Low-fat diet. Antidepressants (amitriptyline) for functional biliary pain without anatomic abnormality. No approved pharmacologic SoC.
us_prevalence: ~200,000–400,000 US patients annually with acalculous biliary pain.
patients_needing_tx: ~100,000 patients not suitable for immediate cholecystectomy requiring pharmacologic management.
price: $5,000–$20,000/year for a novel prokinetic/biliary motility agent. $12,000.
registrational_endpoint: Reduction in biliary pain episodes (PRO measure); gallbladder ejection fraction improvement
btd_threshold_vs_soc: ≥50% reduction in pain frequency vs. placebo; GBEF ≥35% in ≥60% of patients

symptoms: Obstructive jaundice, weight loss, abdominal pain, elevated IgG4 levels
treatment_goal: Achieve remission, reverse biliary strictures, prevent relapse and organ damage
first_line_soc: Oral prednisone 30–40 mg/day × 4–8 weeks, then taper over 3–6 months; highly effective (>90% remission rate).
treatment_algorithm: Induction: prednisone 0.6 mg/kg/day × 4 weeks, taper. Maintenance (frequent relapsers): low-dose prednisone 2.5–5 mg/day or azathioprine 50–100 mg/day. Refractory: rituximab (anti-CD20, off-label, ~75% response) — 1g IV × 2 doses. Biliary stenting for initial management of obstruction until steroids work. 2024-25: inebilizumab (anti-CD19, Phase 2/3 trials for IgG4-RD). Monitoring: IgG4 levels, imaging. Distinguish from cholangiocarcinoma (critical).
us_prevalence: ~5,000–8,000 US patients with IgG4-SC (part of broader IgG4-RD, ~25,000 US total).
patients_needing_tx: ~6,000 patients needing pharmacologic treatment.
price: $80,000–$150,000/year for novel B-cell depleting or targeted IgG4-pathway agent. $100,000.
registrational_endpoint: IgG4-SC Responder Index (composite of IgG4 levels, biliary stricture improvement, ALK-P normalization) at 26 weeks
btd_threshold_vs_soc: Relapse-free remission at 52 weeks ≥70% vs. ~40% with steroids alone

symptoms: RUQ pain, cholestatic liver enzymes (ALP elevation), biliary strictures, cryptosporidium/CMV
treatment_goal: Treat opportunistic biliary infection; optimize antiretroviral therapy
first_line_soc: Combination antiretroviral therapy (cART) — restoring CD4 count resolves cholangiopathy in many. Treat specific opportunistic infection (nitazoxanide for cryptosporidium; ganciclovir/valganciclovir for CMV).
treatment_algorithm: cART (cornerstone): immunological recovery prevents progression. Papillary stenosis: ERCP with sphincterotomy for symptomatic relief. Cryptosporidium: nitazoxanide 500 mg BID × 3 days (limited efficacy in AIDS without immune recovery). CMV: ganciclovir IV or valganciclovir oral. Microsporidium: albendazole. Ursodiol for cholestasis symptom relief.
us_prevalence: ~5,000–10,000 US patients with HIV cholangiopathy (now rare with effective cART).
patients_needing_tx: ~5,000 patients requiring combined cART + biliary pharmacologic management.
price: $30,000–$60,000/year (primarily cART costs). $45,000.
registrational_endpoint: ALP normalization; biliary pain resolution; CD4 count recovery ≥200/μL
btd_threshold_vs_soc: ALP normalization ≥60% at 6 months with dual cART + anti-infective vs. cART alone

symptoms: Jaundice, biliary strictures, elevated LFTs, bile duct injury post-transplant/critical illness
treatment_goal: Restore biliary drainage; prevent secondary biliary cirrhosis
first_line_soc: None (procedural): ERCP with biliary stenting, percutaneous transhepatic cholangiography (PTC) for dominant strictures. Ursodeoxycholic acid (UDCA) to improve bile flow.
treatment_algorithm: ERCP-guided balloon dilation + stenting of dominant strictures. UDCA 13–15 mg/kg/day for cholestasis management. Cholangitis episodes: IV antibiotics (piperacillin-tazobactam or ciprofloxacin). Secondary sclerosing cholangitis: consider liver transplant if progressive. Investigational: biliary stem cells, hepatocyte growth factor. Post-transplant ischemic cholangiopathy: retransplantation may be required.
us_prevalence: ~3,000–5,000 US cases annually (predominantly post-liver transplant or post-ARDS/critical illness).
patients_needing_tx: ~3,000 patients needing active pharmacologic + procedural management.
price: $50,000–$100,000/year for novel biliary epithelial regenerative agent. $70,000.
registrational_endpoint: Cholangiopathy severity score improvement; reduction in repeat ERCP procedures at 12 months; liver transplant-free survival
btd_threshold_vs_soc: ≥50% reduction in biliary stricture recurrence at 12 months vs. ERCP alone; LTx-free survival HR <0.65

symptoms: Fatigue, right upper quadrant discomfort, elevated liver enzymes, jaundice (advanced)
treatment_goal: Achieve viral suppression (HBV) or cure (HCV); prevent cirrhosis and HCC
first_line_soc: Chronic HBV: tenofovir alafenamide (Vemlidy, TAF) or entecavir (Baraclude) — long-term therapy. Chronic HCV: pangenotypic DAA (sofosbuvir + velpatasvir/Epclusa, or glecaprevir + pibrentasvir/Mavyret).
treatment_algorithm: HBV: TAF 25 mg/day (FDA 2016) or entecavir 0.5 mg/day; pegylated interferon alfa-2a (pegIFN) for selected immune-active patients (48 weeks). HCV: 8–12 week DAA course (SVR >95%). Autoimmune hepatitis: prednisone + azathioprine (first-line); mycophenolate mofetil 2L. 2024-25: HBV functional cure agents: oligonucleotide (bepirovirsen), PAPD5/7 inhibitor (imdusiran); HBsAg seroconversion goal.
us_prevalence: Chronic HBV: ~2.4 million; chronic HCV: ~2.4 million; AIH: ~100,000. Total: ~5 million.
patients_needing_tx: ~4 million patients requiring pharmacologic therapy.
price: $20,000–$50,000/year (entecavir generic ~$3K; novel HBV cure: $25,000–$100,000). $50,000.
peak_sales: For a novel HBV cure agent: 20% of 2.4M patients × $50,000 = ~$24B peak (market pioneer)
registrational_endpoint: HBsAg seroconversion (functional cure) for HBV; SVR12 for HCV; ALT normalization for AIH
btd_threshold_vs_soc: HBsAg loss ≥20% at 48 weeks vs. <1% TAF/entecavir; SVR12 ≥95% for HCV

symptoms: Often asymptomatic; fatigue, right upper quadrant discomfort, metabolic syndrome features
treatment_goal: Reduce hepatic steatosis/inflammation, reverse fibrosis, prevent cirrhosis and HCC
first_line_soc: Weight loss (7–10% body weight via diet/exercise) and metabolic risk factor optimization. Semaglutide (Ozempic/Wegovy, GLP-1 RA) now endorsed as first pharmacologic option (2024 guidelines); resmetirom (Rezdiffra) FDA-approved March 2024 for MASH with ≥F2 fibrosis.
treatment_algorithm: Lifestyle: 7–10% weight loss reduces steatosis; 10%+ can reverse fibrosis. Pharmacologic: resmetirom (thyroid hormone receptor-β agonist, Rezdiffra) 80–100 mg QD — FDA approved March 2024 for MASH F2/F3. Semaglutide (GLP-1 RA) shows NASH resolution in Phase 3 (ESSENCE trial 2024: 63% MASH resolution, 34% fibrosis improvement). FGF21 analogues: efruxifermin (Phase 3 SYNCHRONY), pegbelfermin. Obeticholic acid (FXR agonist, Ocaliva) rejected for NASH by FDA 2024 due to safety. Lanifibranor (PPAR agonist, Phase 3). 2025: semaglutide NDA filing expected. HCC surveillance in cirrhotic MASLD.
us_prevalence: ~80–100 million US adults with MASLD (steatosis stage); ~15–17 million with MASH; ~3–5 million with advanced fibrosis (F3–F4).
patients_needing_tx: ~5 million patients with MASH + significant fibrosis (F2–F4) are candidates for pharmacologic treatment.
peak_sales: Leading MASH drug at 10% penetration: 500,000 patients × $35,000 = ~$17.5B peak (blockbuster)
registrational_endpoint: MASH resolution without worsening fibrosis (NAS histopathology ≥2-point reduction) AND/OR ≥1-stage fibrosis improvement; both FDA-accepted co-primary endpoints
btd_threshold_vs_soc: MASH resolution ≥40% AND fibrosis improvement ≥35% at 52 weeks vs. ~10%/15% placebo (as in ESSENCE trial)

symptoms: Jaundice, ascites, encephalopathy, hepatomegaly, spider angiomas, RUQ pain
treatment_goal: Achieve and maintain alcohol abstinence; reduce hepatic inflammation; prevent progression
first_line_soc: Abstinence (cornerstone). Corticosteroids (prednisolone 40 mg/day × 28 days) for severe alcoholic hepatitis (Maddrey DF ≥32). Nutritional support.
treatment_algorithm: Abstinence + nutritional support. Severe alcoholic hepatitis: prednisolone 40 mg × 28 days (response assessed by Lille model at day 7). Pentoxifylline (abandoned—no mortality benefit, STOPAH). NAC as adjunct to steroids (limited evidence). Liver transplant for selected severe non-responding. Anti-fibrotic: cenicriviroc (investigational). 2024: IL-1 inhibitors (canakinumab) and IL-17 inhibitors in phase 2 for alcoholic hepatitis. Alcohol use disorder: acamprosate, naltrexone, disulfiram for abstinence maintenance.
us_prevalence: ~4 million US patients with ARLD spectrum; ~170,000 with severe alcoholic hepatitis/year.
patients_needing_tx: ~500,000 patients requiring pharmacologic intervention (AH corticosteroids + AUD medications).
price: $50,000–$150,000/year for novel hepatitis-modifying or anti-fibrotic agent. $100,000.
peak_sales: 10% novel agent share in severe AH: ~$5B peak
registrational_endpoint: 28-day all-cause mortality (severe AH); Lille model response (day 7); MELD score improvement
btd_threshold_vs_soc: 28-day mortality ≤20% vs. ~35% with prednisolone alone; Lille model responder rate ≥70%

symptoms: Fatigue, jaundice, ascites, spider angiomas, gynecomastia, esophageal varices bleeding
treatment_goal: Prevent decompensation, manage complications, await transplant
first_line_soc: Treat underlying cause (antivirals for HBV/HCV, abstinence for ARLD). Non-selective beta-blockers (propranolol/nadolol or carvedilol) for portal hypertension/variceal prevention.
treatment_algorithm: Treat etiology. Variceal prophylaxis: non-selective beta-blockers (carvedilol preferred) or EVL. Ascites: spironolactone + furosemide. SBP prophylaxis: norfloxacin/trimethoprim-SMX. HE prevention: lactulose + rifaximin (Xifaxan). HRS prevention: albumin IV with SBP treatment. OLTx in Child-Pugh C. Anti-fibrotics: lanifibranor, cilofexor, FXR agonists (obeticholic acid) — advanced trials. 2024: resmetirom for MASH-cirrhosis (FDA 2024).
us_prevalence: ~630,000 US patients with cirrhosis (ACS/AASLD estimates); incidence ~100,000/year.
patients_needing_tx: ~400,000 patients on active pharmacologic management.
price: $80,000–$150,000/year for novel anti-fibrotic. $120,000.
registrational_endpoint: Decompensation rate (first clinical event); MELD score change; histological fibrosis regression
btd_threshold_vs_soc: Decompensation rate reduction ≥25% vs. SoC; fibrosis stage improvement ≥1 in ≥40%

symptoms: Often asymptomatic early; fatigue, RUQ discomfort, signs of portal hypertension late
treatment_goal: Halt or reverse fibrosis progression; prevent cirrhosis and HCC
first_line_soc: Treat underlying cause (HBV/HCV antivirals, alcohol abstinence, weight loss for MASLD). No approved anti-fibrotic pharmacotherapy beyond addressing etiology.
treatment_algorithm: Etiology-directed therapy (antivirals for viral hepatitis, abstinence for alcohol, weight loss/SGLT2i for MASLD). Anti-fibrotic pipeline: obeticholic acid (OCA, FXR agonist) — FDA rejected for NASH 2024 for cirrhosis; lanifibranor (PPAR agonist, Phase 3); cenicriviroc (CCR2/5 antagonist, stopped development). Resmetirom (THRβ agonist, FDA 2024 for MASH F2-F3) — first approved anti-fibrotic for MASLD. 2025: simtuprevir (TGF-β inhibitor), etc.
us_prevalence: ~20–30 million US patients with varying degrees of hepatic fibrosis (F1–F3) from MASLD alone.
patients_needing_tx: ~5 million US patients with significant fibrosis (F2–F4) are pharmacologic treatment candidates; ~1 million with advanced fibrosis (F3–F4) are highest priority.
price: $20,000–$50,000/year for novel anti-fibrotic agent. Resmetirom WAC ~$47,400/yr; projected next-gen ~$40,000.
peak_sales: Leading anti-fibrotic at 8% penetration of total eligible: 400,000 × $40,000 = ~$16B peak (mega-blockbuster)
registrational_endpoint: ≥1-stage fibrosis improvement (Metavir) without worsening of MASH (NAS); or MASH resolution without fibrosis worsening — FDA co-primary histopathological endpoints at 52 weeks
btd_threshold_vs_soc: Fibrosis improvement ≥35% AND MASH resolution ≥40% at 52 weeks vs. placebo (~15%/10%); HR <0.70 for liver-related events in outcomes trial

symptoms: Abdominal distension, weight gain, dyspnea, umbilical hernia, peripheral edema
treatment_goal: Remove fluid, prevent recurrence, prevent SBP, extend survival
first_line_soc: Sodium restriction + diuretics: spironolactone 100 mg/day (first-line) + furosemide 40 mg/day, titrated. Therapeutic paracentesis for large-volume ascites with IV albumin.
treatment_algorithm: Step 1: low-sodium diet + spironolactone ± furosemide. Step 2: increase diuretics to 400 mg spiro + 160 mg furosemide. Refractory: TIPS + albumin infusion; repeated large-volume paracentesis + albumin. Midodrine (alpha-1 agonist) + octreotide for hepatorenal syndrome prevention. ALFA pump (automated peritoneal drainage system). Terlipressin (FDA 2022, Terlivaz) for Type 1 HRS-AKI. 2024: novel vasoconstrictors (norepinephrine vs. terlipressin trials).
us_prevalence: ~500,000–700,000 US patients with cirrhotic ascites; ~100,000 with refractory ascites.
patients_needing_tx: ~400,000 patients on active pharmacologic diuretic management.
price: $50,000–$100,000/year for novel aquaretic, vasopressin receptor, or albumin-like agent. $75,000.
registrational_endpoint: Ascites recurrence rate; paracentesis-free survival; all-cause mortality at 3 months; HRS prevention
btd_threshold_vs_soc: ≥40% reduction in paracentesis frequency vs. spironolactone/furosemide alone; HRS prevention ≥30%

symptoms: Variceal bleeding, ascites, splenomegaly, hepatic encephalopathy
treatment_goal: Prevent variceal hemorrhage, manage ascites, slow progression
first_line_soc: Non-selective beta-blockers (NSBBs): propranolol or carvedilol for primary prophylaxis of variceal bleeding. Acute variceal bleed: terlipressin (Lucassin, FDA-approved 2022) + endoscopic band ligation (EBL).
treatment_algorithm: Primary prophylaxis: carvedilol (preferred) or propranolol → endoscopic band ligation (EBL) for high-risk varices. Acute variceal bleed: terlipressin 2 mg q4h × 72h + EBL; antibiotics (ceftriaxone 1g IV) for SBP prophylaxis. Secondary prophylaxis: NSBB + EBL until eradication. Ascites: spironolactone ± furosemide → TIPS for refractory. Hepatic encephalopathy: lactulose + rifaximin (Xifaxan). 2024-25: statins (simvastatin) for portal pressure reduction in Phase 3; canakinumab for NASH-related portal hypertension.
us_prevalence: ~300,000–500,000 US patients with clinically significant portal hypertension (HVPG ≥10 mmHg), predominantly from cirrhosis.
patients_needing_tx: ~300,000 patients require ongoing pharmacologic management.
price: $30,000–$60,000/year for novel portal pressure-reducing agent. $45,000.
registrational_endpoint: HVPG reduction ≥20% from baseline or to <12 mmHg; first variceal bleeding event (event-driven RCT); decompensation-free survival
btd_threshold_vs_soc: HVPG responder rate ≥55% (vs. ~35% with carvedilol alone) AND first-bleeding event HR <0.65 at 12 months

symptoms: Abdominal pain/tenderness, fever, confusion (hepatic encephalopathy), new or worsening ascites
treatment_goal: Eradicate peritoneal infection; prevent AKI, HE, and in-hospital mortality
first_line_soc: IV cefotaxime 2g q8h × 5 days (or ceftriaxone 1g q24h). IV albumin 1.5 g/kg at diagnosis and 1.0 g/kg on day 3 — reduces AKI and improves 90-day survival.
treatment_algorithm: Treatment: cefotaxime 2g IV q8h × 5 days (first choice); alternatives: amoxicillin-clavulanate IV, oral ofloxacin if uncomplicated. Adjunct IV albumin: mandatory (landmark NEJM trial — reduces 30-day mortality from 29% to 10%). Secondary prophylaxis: norfloxacin 400 mg/day OR ciprofloxacin 500 mg QD (long-term). Primary prophylaxis in high-risk ascites (protein <1.5 g/dL + renal/hepatic impairment): norfloxacin 400 mg QD. Rifaximin alternative to quinolone prophylaxis. Treat underlying cirrhosis aggressively (TIPS, transplant referral).
us_prevalence: ~30,000–40,000 US SBP episodes/year in hospitalized cirrhotic patients; ~15–20% of cirrhotic patients with ascites develop SBP annually.
patients_needing_tx: ~35,000 episodes/year needing IV antibiotics + albumin; ~80,000 patients on long-term prophylaxis.
price: $15,000–$25,000/episode for novel anti-infective or albumin alternative; prophylaxis: $5,000–$10,000/year.
registrational_endpoint: SBP resolution rate at 5 days (SBP treatment); SBP recurrence rate at 12 months (secondary prophylaxis); 90-day transplant-free survival
btd_threshold_vs_soc: SBP recurrence ≤10% at 12 months vs. ~20–40% with norfloxacin; 90-day survival >75% with adjunctive novel agent vs. ~65% with albumin alone

symptoms: Confusion, disorientation, asterixis (hand flap), altered consciousness, coma
treatment_goal: Reverse acute episodes; prevent recurrence; preserve cognitive function
first_line_soc: Lactulose (non-absorbable disaccharide) 30–60 mL BID–TID, titrated to 2–3 soft stools/day. Rifaximin (Xifaxan) 550 mg BID for secondary prophylaxis after ≥1 episode.
treatment_algorithm: Acute HE: lactulose enemas if unable to take orally; treat precipitants (infection, GI bleed, constipation, electrolyte imbalance, sedatives). Rifaximin (Xifaxan) add-on: reduces recurrence by 58% vs. placebo; widely used. Branched-chain amino acids (BCAA) supplementation for refractory HE. LOLA (L-ornithine L-aspartate) IV for acute episodes (off-label US). Polyethylene glycol (PEG) for rapid bowel prep in acute HE. TIPS adjustment for post-TIPS HE. Liver transplant for recurrent severe HE. 2024-25: ornithine phenylacetate (OCR-002) Phase 3; FMT trials.
us_prevalence: ~100,000–150,000 US patients with cirrhosis experience at least one HE episode/year; ~30,000 hospitalizations for overt HE/year.
patients_needing_tx: ~80,000 patients require ongoing pharmacologic prevention and treatment.
price: $15,000–$30,000/year for novel ammonia-lowering agent. $22,000 (rifaximin WAC ~$18,000/yr).
peak_sales: 20% novel agent share vs. rifaximin: ~$352M peak
registrational_endpoint: Time to first overt HE episode (OHE) in secondary prophylaxis trial (event-driven); HE recurrence rate at 6 months
btd_threshold_vs_soc: ≥50% reduction in OHE recurrence at 6 months vs. rifaximin alone (add-on study) or vs. lactulose (monotherapy)

symptoms: Yellow skin/eyes, dark urine, pale stools, itching, fatigue
treatment_goal: Identify and treat underlying cause; relieve cholestasis
first_line_soc: None (treat underlying cause). Cholestyramine or rifampicin for pruritus from cholestatic jaundice. UDCA for cholestatic liver disease.
treatment_algorithm: Hemolytic: treat underlying cause. Hepatocellular: disease-specific therapy. Obstructive (choledocholithiasis): ERCP stone extraction. Obstructive (malignancy): biliary stenting ± surgical bypass. Cholestatic pruritus: cholestyramine 4g TID → rifampicin 150–300 mg/day → naltrexone → sertraline. UDCA for chronic cholestatic disease. 2024-25 novel: maralixibat (IBAT inhibitor) for cholestatic pruritus; linerixibat (GSK) in Phase 3 for PBC pruritus.
us_prevalence: Jaundice is a symptom, not a disease; affects millions/year across etiologies — ~5 million encounters/year in US.
patients_needing_tx: ~500,000 patients/year with chronic cholestatic jaundice (PBC, PSC, malignant obstruction) needing ongoing pharmacologic treatment.
price: $40,000–$80,000/year for novel anti-cholestatic/anti-pruritic agent. $60,000.
registrational_endpoint: Serum bilirubin normalization; pruritus NRS reduction ≥3 points from baseline at 12 weeks
btd_threshold_vs_soc: ≥3-point NRS pruritus reduction in ≥50% of patients vs. <20% with cholestyramine at 12 weeks

symptoms: Severe neonatal jaundice, unconjugated hyperbilirubinemia, kernicterus risk (Type 1)
treatment_goal: Prevent kernicterus; control bilirubin levels; enable curative transplant or gene therapy
first_line_soc: Type 1: phototherapy 10–16 hours/day (lifelong without transplant). Liver transplant (curative). Type 2 (Arias): phenobarbital 60–180 mg/day (induces residual UGT1A1 activity).
treatment_algorithm: Type 1: phototherapy + OLTx (curative, before neurological damage). Type 2: phenobarbital ± phototherapy. Gene therapy: mRNA-based UGT1A1 replacement (Arctus Bio/Sanofi, Phase 2/3 2023–2025). AAV5-UGT1A1 (ACMVEC101) trials. Tin-protoporphyrin (SnPP, heme oxygenase inhibitor, off-label) reduces bilirubin production.
us_prevalence: Extremely rare: <500 US patients total (Type 1: ~100–150; Type 2: ~300–400).
patients_needing_tx: ~400 patients requiring pharmacologic/gene therapy intervention.
price: $500,000–$2,000,000 one-time for gene therapy. Annual pharmacotherapy: $20,000–$50,000. $30,000/year.
registrational_endpoint: Total serum bilirubin normalization (<34 µmol/L); phototherapy hours/day reduction; neurological outcomes
btd_threshold_vs_soc: Bilirubin <34 µmol/L without phototherapy in ≥70% (Type 1 gene therapy)

symptoms: Mild jaundice, splenomegaly, elevated unconjugated bilirubin, mild anemia
treatment_goal: Monitor; no pharmacologic intervention typically needed
first_line_soc: None (benign condition). Dietary management if related to ineffective erythropoiesis; no pharmacologic SoC.
treatment_algorithm: None required for most. Treat underlying cause (dyserythropoiesis, thalassemia). Phlebotomy if iron overload develops. Supportive monitoring. Folic acid supplementation for associated dyserythropoiesis. Differential from Gilbert syndrome and hemolytic anemia essential.
us_prevalence: Very rare; <500 identified US cases. Part of the spectrum of bilirubin metabolism disorders.
patients_needing_tx: Minimal — essentially no pharmacologic intervention needed; ~50–100 patients needing monitoring for complications.
price: $50,000–$100,000/year for a hypothetical gene therapy targeting UGT1A1 pathway. $75,000.
registrational_endpoint: Bilirubin normalization to <1.5× ULN; reduction in hospitalizations for jaundice at 12 months
btd_threshold_vs_soc: Bilirubin normalization in >80% vs. natural history at 6 months (very low bar given rarity)

symptoms: Mild chronic jaundice, conjugated hyperbilirubinemia; otherwise well
treatment_goal: Reassurance; no pharmacologic treatment required
first_line_soc: None. Benign conditions with excellent prognosis; no drug therapy indicated.
treatment_algorithm: No pharmacologic SoC. Avoid hepatotoxic drugs. Genetic counseling. Dubin-Johnson: black/brown liver pigment; benign except potential OCP-related cholestasis (avoid OCPs). Rotor: bile salt retention but benign. Differentiate from more serious conjugated hyperbilirubinemias. Patient education and reassurance.
us_prevalence: Rare: Dubin-Johnson ~1/1,300 Sephardic Jews and <1/1,000,000 in general; total US: ~5,000–10,000.
patients_needing_tx: ~1,000 patients requiring genetic evaluation; minimal pharmacologic need.
price: $10,000–$30,000/year for a hypothetical bilirubin-reducing agent. $20,000.
registrational_endpoint: Serum bilirubin reduction to normal range; jaundice symptom improvement
btd_threshold_vs_soc: N/A — benign condition; any drug >$0 bilirubin reduction clinically irrelevant

symptoms: Mild episodic jaundice (unconjugated), triggered by stress/fasting/illness; otherwise healthy
treatment_goal: Reassure patient; no pharmacologic treatment required
first_line_soc: None. Benign condition; patient education and reassurance.
treatment_algorithm: No pharmacologic SoC. Education on triggers (fasting, stress, illness). Mild unconjugated hyperbilirubinemia (generally <3 mg/dL). Bilirubin may actually be hepatoprotective (antioxidant). Phenobarbital can normalize bilirubin (not indicated for benign GS). Pharmacogenomic implications: UGT1A1*28 → irinotecan toxicity (reduce irinotecan dose).
us_prevalence: Very common: ~5–7% of population = 16–23 million US individuals.
patients_needing_tx: ~100,000 patients seeking diagnosis/reassurance; essentially no pharmacologic treatment.
price: N/A. No pharmacologic intervention warranted.
registrational_endpoint: N/A — benign condition
btd_threshold_vs_soc: N/A — benign condition; bilirubin normalization (<1.0 mg/dL) in ≥80% at 4 weeks for any hypothetical UGT1A1-enhancing agent

symptoms: Elevated LFTs, jaundice, fatigue, nausea, rarely fulminant liver failure
treatment_goal: Identify and stop offending drug; prevent fulminant failure
first_line_soc: Discontinue offending drug (primary intervention). N-acetylcysteine (NAC) for acetaminophen DILI. Oral corticosteroids for immune-allergic DILI (autoimmune features).
treatment_algorithm: Step 1: Drug cessation — most cases resolve. Acetaminophen toxicity: oral/IV NAC within 8 hours is standard of care. DILI with autoimmune features: prednisone 40–60 mg/day × 4 weeks, taper. Cholestatic DILI: UDCA 13–15 mg/day. Cholestyramine for pruritus. Liver transplant for fulminant DILI failure. Investigational: FXR agonists (obeticholic acid), anti-PCSK9 combinations for metabolic DILI. DILI Network (DILIN) registry provides phenotyping data.
us_prevalence: ~20,000 significant DILI cases/year (hospitalizations); acetaminophen overdose causes ~50,000 ER visits/year.
patients_needing_tx: ~15,000 patients/year with significant non-acetaminophen DILI needing specialized intervention; ~50,000 acetaminophen cases needing NAC.
price: $30,000–$60,000/course for novel hepatoprotective agent beyond NAC. $45,000.
registrational_endpoint: ALT normalization at 8 weeks; prevention of progression to acute liver failure (MELD ≥15) at 30 days
btd_threshold_vs_soc: ALT normalization in ≥70% at 4 weeks AND acute liver failure rate <5% vs. ~15% with supportive care in severe idiosyncratic DILI

symptoms: Fever, fatigue, right upper quadrant discomfort, hepatomegaly
treatment_goal: Treat underlying cause; prevent fibrosis progression
first_line_soc: Treat underlying etiology: anti-TB therapy (isoniazid/rifampin/pyrazinamide/ethambutol) for TB granulomas; systemic corticosteroids for sarcoidosis-related hepatic granulomas.
treatment_algorithm: TB: standard 4-drug RHEZ × 2 months then 2-drug continuation. Sarcoidosis: prednisone 20–40 mg/day, taper over 12–24 months. Histoplasmosis: itraconazole or amphotericin B. Drug-induced: cessation of offending agent. Idiopathic: observation vs. corticosteroids if symptomatic/progressing. Ursodeoxycholic acid if cholestatic component.
us_prevalence: ~50,000–80,000 US patients with clinically significant hepatic granulomas; sarcoidosis accounts for ~30%.
patients_needing_tx: ~40,000 patients require pharmacologic treatment targeting underlying etiology.
price: $30,000–$60,000/year for novel anti-fibrotic/anti-granulomatous agent. $45,000.
registrational_endpoint: Reduction in hepatic granuloma burden by imaging/biopsy and ALT normalization at 52 weeks
btd_threshold_vs_soc: ≥50% reduction in granuloma volume AND LFT normalization in ≥60% vs. <30% with SoC

symptoms: Jaundice, elevated LFTs, coagulopathy post-surgery, confusion (HE)
treatment_goal: Preserve liver function; prevent progression to acute liver failure
first_line_soc: None (supportive). IV NAC for hepatoprotection post-hepatectomy. Avoid hepatotoxins; optimize hemodynamics.
treatment_algorithm: Prevention: liver volumetry pre-resection; portal vein embolization to hypertrophy future liver remnant. Post-hepatectomy liver failure (PHLF): MARS (molecular adsorbent recirculating system) for temporary liver support. IV NAC infusion. Lactulose/rifaximin for encephalopathy. Nutritional support (BCAA). Steroids for ischemia-reperfusion injury (controversial). Liver transplant for irreversible failure. 2024-25: hepatocyte growth factor (HGF), FGF4 to promote liver regeneration — in trials.
us_prevalence: ~10,000–15,000 cases of clinically significant postoperative liver dysfunction/year after hepatic or cardiac surgery.
patients_needing_tx: ~8,000 patients/year needing active pharmacologic liver-support intervention.
price: $30,000–$60,000/course for novel liver regeneration-stimulating agent. $45,000.
registrational_endpoint: 50-50 criterion reversal (bilirubin <50 μmol/L AND INR <50 at day 5 post-resection); 90-day mortality reduction
btd_threshold_vs_soc: PHLF grade B/C incidence ≤10% vs. ~25% control; 90-day mortality HR <0.65 in major hepatectomy patients

symptoms: Jaundice, coagulopathy (INR >1.5), encephalopathy, rapid onset <26 weeks
treatment_goal: Support liver function while awaiting recovery or transplant; treat etiology
first_line_soc: N-acetylcysteine (NAC) IV for acetaminophen-induced ALF (and possibly non-APAP). Urgent liver transplant evaluation. ICU management.
treatment_algorithm: APAP toxicity: NAC infusion (Acetadote) 21-hour protocol. Viral HAV/HBV: antiviral (tenofovir/entecavir for HBV). Autoimmune: IV methylprednisolone (selected cases). Wilson's disease: D-penicillamine or trientine. Drug-induced: stop offending drug. Budd-Chiari: anticoagulation ± TIPS. Liver support: MARS (molecular adsorbent recirculating system) or PROMETHEUS. OLTx for refractory. 2024: fecal microbiota transplant + LC-MSI (investigational).
us_prevalence: ~2,000–3,000 US cases/year; ~50% from APAP overdose.
patients_needing_tx: ~2,500 patients/year requiring IV pharmacologic treatment.
price: $50,000–$200,000/episode for a novel hepatoprotective or liver-regenerating agent. $100,000.
registrational_endpoint: Overall survival without transplantation at 21 days; liver function normalization (INR, bilirubin, encephalopathy)
btd_threshold_vs_soc: Transplant-free survival ≥65% vs. ~50% with NAC; INR normalization ≥2 days faster

symptoms: Rapid-onset jaundice, coagulopathy, hepatic encephalopathy within 8 weeks
treatment_goal: Prevent death; support hepatic regeneration; evaluate for urgent liver transplant
first_line_soc: NAC (N-acetylcysteine) for all causes of ALF (strongest evidence for APAP, benefit in non-APAP). Treat etiology. ICU management.
treatment_algorithm: Identical to Acute Liver Failure: NAC IV, treat viral etiology (antivirals), stop hepatotoxic drugs, autoimmune: corticosteroids. Extracorporeal support: MARS or PROMETHEUS. OLTx for King's College Criteria-positive. Rifaximin + lactulose for encephalopathy. Emerging: fecal microbiota transplant (FMT) — Phase 2 trials showing benefit in acute-on-chronic liver failure.
us_prevalence: ~2,000–3,000 US cases/year (overlaps with ALF; fulminant = ALF with encephalopathy within 2 weeks of jaundice).
patients_needing_tx: ~2,000 patients/year requiring urgent pharmacologic + transplant evaluation.
registrational_endpoint: 21-day transplant-free survival; INR normalization; encephalopathy grade improvement
btd_threshold_vs_soc: TFS ≥65% vs. ~50% NAC; complete encephalopathy resolution in ≥70% at 7 days
