symptoms: Hepatomegaly, right upper quadrant pain, abnormal LFTs, rare intraperitoneal hemorrhage
treatment_goal: Remove causative agent; prevent life-threatening hemorrhage
first_line_soc: Discontinue causative drug (anabolic steroids, OCP, tamoxifen) or treat underlying infection (Bartonella: doxycycline + rifampicin for bacillary peliosis in HIV).
treatment_algorithm: Drug-induced: cease offending agent (OCP, androgens, thiopurines, azathioprine); lesions typically regress. Bartonella henselae infection: doxycycline 100 mg BID + rifampicin 300 mg BID × 3–6 months (HIV patients). Surgical resection for symptomatic or large lesions at hemorrhage risk. Angio-embolization for acute hemorrhage. Liver transplant for severe peliosis hepatis with liver failure.
us_prevalence: ~1,000–3,000 US cases/year (rare); predominantly in HIV/AIDS and organ transplant recipients on immunosuppressants.
patients_needing_tx: ~2,000 patients/year needing pharmacologic treatment.
price: $20,000–$40,000/course for novel Bartonella-targeting or vascular normalization agent. $30,000.
registrational_endpoint: Lesion regression (≥50% volumetric reduction by MRI) at 6 months; prevention of hemorrhage
btd_threshold_vs_soc: ≥60% lesion regression at 6 months AND hemorrhage-free rate >90% vs. ~30% spontaneous regression

symptoms: Hepatomegaly, RUQ fullness/pain, jaundice, ascites (cardiac hepatomegaly), pulsatile liver
treatment_goal: Treat underlying cardiac failure; decompress hepatic venous congestion
first_line_soc: Optimize heart failure management (diuretics: furosemide, spironolactone; ACE inhibitors/ARBs; beta-blockers; SGLT2i). Anticoagulation for associated thromboembolic disease.
treatment_algorithm: HF optimization: GDMT (guideline-directed medical therapy). Diuresis: loop diuretics + MRA. LVAD or heart transplant for refractory. Anticoagulation (warfarin) for Fontan patients (common cause). TIPS occasionally for portal hypertension in severe CHF. Hepatic decompression via surgical correction of cardiac defect. No liver-specific pharmacotherapy beyond treating cardiac disease.
us_prevalence: ~200,000–400,000 US patients with significant congestive hepatopathy.
patients_needing_tx: ~150,000 patients requiring pharmacologic management (primarily cardiac drugs).
price: $30,000–$80,000/year for novel cardiac/hepatic combination agent. $50,000.
registrational_endpoint: Hepatic congestion index; ALT/AST normalization; decompensation events; MELD score
btd_threshold_vs_soc: ≥30% reduction in hepatic congestion index vs. standard HF therapy alone

symptoms: Abdominal pain, ascites, variceal bleeding, splenomegaly, portal hypertension
treatment_goal: Recanalize portal vein, prevent extension, manage portal hypertension
first_line_soc: Anticoagulation: low molecular weight heparin (LMWH) bridging to vitamin K antagonist (warfarin) or direct oral anticoagulants (DOACs — rivaroxaban or apixaban) in non-cirrhotic PVT.
treatment_algorithm: Acute non-cirrhotic PVT: LMWH → warfarin (INR 2–3) × 3–6 months (recanalization 70% if early). DOACs (rivaroxaban, apixaban) increasingly used; non-inferior data from small RCTs. Cirrhotic PVT: anticoagulation if thrombosis ≥50% portal vein, rivaroxaban 15mg BID emerging. TIPS for refractory variceal bleeding. Thrombolytics (tPA) via transhepatic route for acute occlusive PVT. MPNs (polycythemia vera/ET): treat underlying MPN + anticoagulate. JAK inhibitors (ruxolitinib) for MPN-associated PVT.
us_prevalence: ~30,000–50,000 US patients with chronic PVT; ~10,000 new cases/year.
patients_needing_tx: ~40,000 patients requiring anticoagulation or novel pharmacologic treatment.
price: $30,000–$60,000/year for novel FXI/FXIa inhibitor or direct thrombin inhibitor for portal circulation. $45,000.
registrational_endpoint: Portal vein recanalization rate (>50% lumen restoration by Doppler ultrasound) at 6 months
btd_threshold_vs_soc: Complete recanalization ≥55% at 6 months vs. ~30% with LMWH/warfarin

symptoms: Painful hepatomegaly, ascites, jaundice, weight gain post-SCT or chemotherapy
treatment_goal: Resolve sinusoidal obstruction; prevent multi-organ failure
first_line_soc: Defibrotide (Defitelio) — oligonucleotide targeting endothelial protection; FDA-approved 2016 for severe/very severe hepatic VOD post-SCT. 6.25 mg/kg IV q6h × ≥21 days.
treatment_algorithm: Defibrotide (Defitelio) for severe/very severe SOS-VOD: 25 mg/kg/day IV in 4 divided doses × 21–60 days (complete response ~47%, 100-day survival ~38% vs. ~25% historical). Prevention (high-risk SCT): defibrotide prophylaxis 25 mg/kg/day pre-/peri-conditioning. UDCA prophylaxis. Low-dose heparin (insufficient evidence alone). Supportive: diuretics for fluid management, avoid nephrotoxins, TIPS for refractory cases. Rituximab-based regimens increase SOS risk — pre-treatment risk stratification. 2024: selinexor (XPO1 inhibitor) as potential SOS prevention in clinical trials.
us_prevalence: ~1,500–2,000 severe SOS cases/year post-SCT (~5–15% of allo-SCT patients; ~10,000 allo-SCTs/year in US).
patients_needing_tx: ~1,500 patients/year needing defibrotide; ~3,000 high-risk patients needing prophylaxis.
price: $150,000–$250,000/course (defibrotide WAC ~$200K/course). Novel agent: $175,000/course.
registrational_endpoint: Day-100 complete response (total bilirubin <2 mg/dL + resolution of ascites/weight gain) post-treatment; day-100 overall survival
btd_threshold_vs_soc: Day-100 CR ≥55% AND day-100 OS ≥55% vs. defibrotide historical ~47% CR and ~38% OS in severe VOD

symptoms: Massive ALT/AST elevation (>10× ULN), jaundice, acute liver dysfunction following hypotension
treatment_goal: Restore hemodynamic stability; prevent acute liver failure
first_line_soc: None (treat underlying cause: cardiogenic shock, sepsis, respiratory failure). Vasopressors (norepinephrine) for hemodynamic support; IV NAC for liver protection.
treatment_algorithm: Treat underlying hemodynamic instability (fluid resuscitation, vasopressors, inotropes for cardiogenic shock). IV N-acetylcysteine (NAC) 150 mg/kg × 15 min loading, then continuous infusion for hepatoprotection (off-label but widely used). Avoid nephrotoxins. ICU monitoring for acute liver failure. Liver transplant for rare fulminant cases. Underlying cardiac resynchronization or revascularization for structural cardiac causes.
us_prevalence: ~35,000–50,000 US cases/year (hospital-acquired in critically ill patients); 2nd most common cause of ALT >1000 U/L in hospitalized patients.
patients_needing_tx: ~35,000 patients/year needing active hepatoprotective intervention in ICU.
price: $15,000–$30,000/course for novel liver-protective agent. $20,000.
registrational_endpoint: ALT normalization (< 2× ULN) within 7 days; 30-day liver failure-free survival
btd_threshold_vs_soc: 30-day liver failure-free survival >85% vs. ~65% with supportive care alone in AKIN-defined ischemic hepatitis

symptoms: Ascites, hepatomegaly, abdominal pain, lower limb edema, hepatic vein occlusion
treatment_goal: Restore hepatic venous drainage; prevent hepatic failure; anticoagulate
first_line_soc: Anticoagulation (LMWH bridge to warfarin or DOAC). Treat underlying thrombophilia (myeloproliferative neoplasm: JAK2 V617F). TIPS for anticoagulation failure.
treatment_algorithm: Anticoagulation: LMWH → warfarin (INR 2–3) or rivaroxaban/apixaban (DOACs increasingly used). JAK2+: hydroxyurea or ruxolitinib for MPN. Thrombolysis for acute/subacute thrombosis (streptokinase, tPA). TIPS (transjugular intrahepatic portosystemic shunt) for persistent portal hypertension. OLTx for fulminant/TIPS-refractory. 2024: thrombectomy catheter procedures.
us_prevalence: ~5,000–8,000 US patients with Budd-Chiari syndrome.
patients_needing_tx: ~5,000 patients requiring pharmacologic anticoagulation ± MPN therapy.
price: $30,000–$100,000/year for novel anticoagulant + MPN agent combination. $75,000.
registrational_endpoint: Hepatic vein recanalization rate; ascites resolution; transplant-free survival at 1 year
btd_threshold_vs_soc: Transplant-free survival ≥80% vs. ~70% anticoagulation alone; recanalization ≥50%

symptoms: RUQ pain, fever, jaundice, biliary ischemia; post-transplant hepatic artery thrombosis
treatment_goal: Restore hepatic arterial flow; prevent biliary necrosis and graft loss (post-transplant)
first_line_soc: Emergent surgical revascularization or endovascular thrombolysis/angioplasty. Anticoagulation (heparin) for thrombotic etiology.
treatment_algorithm: Post-transplant HAT: urgent re-exploration ± thrombectomy; thrombolysis with tPA or urokinase. Anticoagulation: IV heparin → warfarin. Aspirin + anticoagulation for non-surgical cases. Prophylaxis in transplant: aspirin 81 mg/day postoperatively. Biliary ischemia management: ERCP ± stenting. Retransplant if graft failure. No specific approved pharmacotherapy for HAO per se.
us_prevalence: ~3,000–5,000 US patients with hepatic artery thrombosis/occlusion (mostly post-transplant).
patients_needing_tx: ~3,000 patients/year requiring anticoagulation + revascularization.
registrational_endpoint: Graft survival at 1 year; hepatic artery patency rate at 30 days; biliary complication rate
btd_threshold_vs_soc: Graft survival ≥85% vs. ~60% with standard thrombectomy; patency rate ≥80% at 30 days

symptoms: Often asymptomatic; epigastric pain, hemobilia, rupture (life-threatening)
treatment_goal: Prevent rupture; endovascular or surgical treatment; perioperative pharmacologic support
first_line_soc: Endovascular therapy (coil embolization or stent-graft) for aneurysms >2 cm or symptomatic. Perioperative beta-blockers to reduce arterial wall stress.
treatment_algorithm: Endovascular coil embolization or covered stent placement (preferred). Open surgical repair for failed endovascular. Perioperative: beta-blockers (metoprolol), antihypertensives to control BP. Anticoagulation: not routinely indicated. No specific pharmacologic SoC to prevent growth; blood pressure control is paramount. Aspirin for atherosclerotic etiology.
us_prevalence: Very rare: ~5,000–8,000 US patients with hepatic artery aneurysms diagnosed.
patients_needing_tx: ~3,000 patients/year requiring pharmacologic management ± intervention.
price: $30,000–$80,000/year for a novel vascular remodeling inhibitor. $50,000.
registrational_endpoint: Aneurysm growth rate; rupture incidence; blood pressure control metrics
btd_threshold_vs_soc: ≥50% reduction in aneurysm expansion rate vs. BP control alone

symptoms: Fatigue, right upper quadrant discomfort, often asymptomatic; cirrhosis symptoms late
treatment_goal: Achieve SVR12 (functional cure); prevent cirrhosis and HCC
first_line_soc: Glecaprevir/pibrentasvir (Mavyret) 8 weeks OR sofosbuvir/velpatasvir (Epclusa) 12 weeks — pan-genotypic, >97% SVR12.
treatment_algorithm: Pan-genotypic preferred: G/P (NS3/4A + NS5A, 8 wks) or SOF/VEL (NS5B + NS5A, 12 wks). Compensated cirrhosis: G/P 12 wks or SOF/VEL 12 wks. Decompensated cirrhosis: SOF/VEL + ribavirin 12 wks or SOF/VEL/VOX 12 wks. Prior NS5A failures: SOF/VEL/voxilaprevir (Vosevi) 12 wks. HCV/HIV co-infection: same DAA ± attention to drug interactions. Post-SVR: HCC surveillance q6 months if advanced fibrosis. 2024 update: capsid inhibitors in trials for resistant genotypes.
us_prevalence: ~2.4 million chronic HCV infections in US (CDC); ~1.5 million undiagnosed.
patients_needing_tx: ~2.0 million patients needing treatment; only ~600,000 have received DAAs; significant treatment gap in PWID/underserved populations.
price: $50,000–$100,000/course for next-gen pan-genotypic shorter-course agent. $75,000. Rationale: curative intent, payer willingness for elimination.
peak_sales: 5% annual treatment share × $75K = ~$7.5B peak (high-volume elimination scenario)
registrational_endpoint: SVR12 (HCV RNA <LLOQ at 12 weeks post-EOT); non-inferiority or superiority vs. SOF/VEL or G/P
btd_threshold_vs_soc: SVR12 ≥95% in a 4-week pangenotypic regimen, or ≥97% SVR12 in NS5A-experienced cirrhotics (currently <90%)

symptoms: Fatigue, right upper quadrant discomfort, often asymptomatic until cirrhosis
treatment_goal: Achieve HBsAg loss, suppress HBV DNA, prevent cirrhosis/HCC
first_line_soc: Tenofovir alafenamide (TAF/Vemlidy) or tenofovir disoproxil fumarate (TDF/Viread) — nucleotide analogs; entecavir (Baraclude) alternative. Peg-interferon alfa-2a for selected patients (HBeAg+, high ALT).
treatment_algorithm: Preferred: TAF 25 mg QD (superior bone/renal safety vs. TDF). Entecavir 0.5 mg QD (drug-naive). Peg-IFN alfa-2a (Pegasys) ×48 wks for finite treatment goal (HBsAg loss ~5%). 2024-25: abelacimab (anti-FXI), bepirovirsen (siRNA, GSK), VTP-300 (therapeutic vaccine) in Phase 3. Combination siRNA + capsid inhibitor trials ongoing. Long-term monitoring for HCC: AFP + ultrasound q6 months.
us_prevalence: ~2.4 million US patients with chronic HBV (CDC); ~1.2 million diagnosed.
patients_needing_tx: ~800,000 patients meet treatment criteria (HBV DNA > threshold + ALT elevation or significant fibrosis); ~400,000 currently on therapy.
price: $25,000–$50,000/year for novel HBsAg-clearing agent (functional cure). $40,000 (current antivirals $8K–$15K/yr).
peak_sales: 15% novel functional cure agent share: ~$4.8B peak
registrational_endpoint: HBsAg loss (functional cure) rate at 48 or 96 weeks; sustained virologic response (HBV DNA <20 IU/mL) off therapy
btd_threshold_vs_soc: HBsAg loss ≥20% at 48 weeks vs. <5% with peg-IFN, or ≥30% HBsAg loss off-therapy at 24 weeks post-treatment

symptoms: Fatigue, jaundice, nausea, dark urine (often asymptomatic)
treatment_goal: Achieve SVR12; prevent chronicity in >80% who would otherwise progress
first_line_soc: Direct-acting antivirals (DAAs): sofosbuvir/velpatasvir (Epclusa) or glecaprevir/pibrentasvir (Mavyret) × 8 weeks; same as chronic HCV regimens.
treatment_algorithm: Treat immediately with same pan-genotypic DAA regimens as chronic HCV (no need to wait for spontaneous clearance). Glecaprevir/pibrentasvir (G/P, Mavyret) 8 wks — SVR >97%. Sofosbuvir/velpatasvir (SOF/VEL, Epclusa) 12 wks. Monitoring for drug interactions, especially in co-infected HIV patients. Spontaneous clearance occurs in ~20–30% but treatment prevents liver damage during acute phase.
us_prevalence: ~40,000 new acute HCV infections/year (CDC 2024); underreported 10× due to asymptomatic nature; true incidence ~400,000/year.
patients_needing_tx: ~350,000 newly infected per year needing DAA therapy to prevent chronicity; most go undiagnosed acutely.
price: $25,000–$45,000/course; current DAAs ~$20K–$35K. Novel pan-genotypic: $35,000.
peak_sales: 10% incremental share over existing DAAs: ~$1.2B peak
registrational_endpoint: SVR12 (undetectable HCV RNA 12 weeks post-treatment); prevention of chronicity at 6 months
btd_threshold_vs_soc: SVR12 ≥95% with 4-week pangenotypic course (shorter than current 8 wks)

symptoms: Severe fatigue, jaundice, rapid liver disease progression in HBV co-infected
treatment_goal: Achieve HDV RNA suppression; prevent cirrhosis progression
first_line_soc: Bulevirtide (Hepcludex, EMA approved 2020, FDA investigational) — HBV/HDV entry inhibitor 2 mg SC QD. Peg-IFN alfa for 48–72 weeks (only option pre-bulevirtide).
treatment_algorithm: Bulevirtide 2 mg SC QD (now standard in Europe; FDA Breakthrough designation) combined with TAF. Peg-IFN alfa-2a 180 mcg/wk × 48 wks — SVR-off only 25–30%. Lonafarnib + ritonavir (farnesyl transferase inhibitor, Phase 3) — viral clearance benefit. Ocaliva (OCA) adjunct for cholestatic component. Bulevirtide monotherapy or + peg-IFN ongoing Phase 3. HDV requires HBV co-infection — HBV suppression alone insufficient for HDV.
us_prevalence: ~70,000–100,000 US patients with chronic HDV (co-infected with HBV); globally 12–15 million.
patients_needing_tx: ~70,000 US patients with chronic HDV needing therapy.
price: $80,000–$150,000/year (bulevirtide WAC in Europe ~€14K/month = ~$180K/yr). US launch estimated $120,000/yr.
registrational_endpoint: HDV RNA undetectability and ALT normalization at 48 weeks (co-primary); virologic response off-therapy at 24 weeks post-EOT
btd_threshold_vs_soc: HDV RNA undetectable + ALT normal ≥25% at week 48 vs. <5% with peg-IFN alone

symptoms: Jaundice, fatigue, right upper quadrant pain, dark urine, nausea, fever
treatment_goal: Support recovery; prevent progression to fulminant hepatitis or chronic infection
first_line_soc: Supportive care (rest, hydration, avoid hepatotoxins). HAV/HEV: supportive. HBV acute: tenofovir or entecavir if severe/fulminant or immunosuppressed. HCV acute: monitor 12 weeks; treat with DAAs if spontaneous clearance doesn't occur.
treatment_algorithm: HAV: vaccine prevention; supportive treatment. Acute HBV: antiviral (tenofovir disoproxil fumarate or entecavir) if fulminant or severe. HCV acute: genotype-directed DAA (sofosbuvir-based); monitoring 12 weeks before treating. HDV: refer to HDV section. HEV: supportive; ribavirin for immunocompromised. Steroids contraindicated in viral hepatitis.
us_prevalence: ~25,000–50,000 US acute viral hepatitis episodes/year (HAV ~14K, HBV ~21K acute symptomatic, SEER-like estimates).
patients_needing_tx: ~30,000 patients/year requiring pharmacologic antiviral treatment.
price: $20,000–$50,000/course for acute viral treatment. $35,000.
registrational_endpoint: Viral clearance by week 12; normalization of ALT/bilirubin; prevention of chronicity
btd_threshold_vs_soc: SVR12 ≥90% for acute HCV; HBsAg clearance ≥20% at 6 months for acute HBV

symptoms: Jaundice, nausea, fatigue, fever; severe in pregnant women
treatment_goal: Supportive recovery; prevent fulminant failure in pregnant and immunosuppressed
first_line_soc: None approved (supportive). Ribavirin used off-label for chronic HEV in immunocompromised.
treatment_algorithm: Acute HEV genotype 1/2 (travelers): supportive. Pregnant women: close monitoring, ICU for fulminant failure. Chronic HEV genotype 3/4 (immunosuppressed transplant recipients): reduce immunosuppression first; ribavirin 600–1000 mg/day × 3 months if persistent viremia. Sofosbuvir-based regimens under investigation. Liver transplant for ALF. Chinese HEV vaccine (Hecolin, not FDA-approved) available in China.
us_prevalence: ~50,000 acute HEV infections/year estimated in US (mostly asymptomatic travelers/sporadic); chronic HEV in immunosuppressed: ~5,000–8,000.
patients_needing_tx: ~5,000 immunosuppressed chronic HEV patients needing antiviral; ~500 fulminant acute cases needing ICU/antiviral.
price: $30,000–$50,000/course for novel HEV-specific antiviral. $40,000.
registrational_endpoint: HEV RNA clearance at 12 weeks post-EOT; sustained virologic response (SVR12)
btd_threshold_vs_soc: SVR12 ≥80% vs. ~50% with ribavirin in immunosuppressed chronic HEV

symptoms: Nausea, jaundice, fatigue, abdominal pain, dark urine
treatment_goal: Supportive care; prevent dehydration and rare fulminant failure
first_line_soc: None (supportive care only: IV fluids, anti-emetics). Vaccination for prevention.
treatment_algorithm: Supportive: rest, hydration, avoid hepatotoxic drugs/alcohol. Anti-emetics for nausea. No antiviral therapy approved. Liver transplant for fulminant hepatic failure. Prevention: HAV vaccine (Havrix, Vaqta) — 2-dose series, ~97% effective. IVIG post-exposure prophylaxis within 2 weeks.
us_prevalence: ~25,000 acute cases/year (CDC 2024 est.); no chronic carrier state. Increasing due to homelessness/opioid crisis outbreaks.
patients_needing_tx: ~25,000 symptomatic patients/year; most self-limiting, ~1,500 hospitalized, <50 need transplant.
price: $5,000–$10,000/course for a hypothetical antiviral; $7,000. Rationale: self-limiting disease limits price.
registrational_endpoint: Time to ALT normalization and clinical recovery; reduction in hospitalization rate
btd_threshold_vs_soc: ≥2-week reduction in time to ALT normalization vs. SoC in moderate-severe HAV

symptoms: Jaundice, fatigue, nausea, vomiting, right upper quadrant pain
treatment_goal: Prevent progression to chronicity or fulminant failure; support recovery
first_line_soc: None for mild-moderate acute HBV (supportive). Tenofovir or entecavir for fulminant/severe acute HBV to prevent progression.
treatment_algorithm: Mild-moderate: supportive (rest, hydration, avoid alcohol/hepatotoxins). Severe acute HBV (elevated INR, encephalopathy): tenofovir alafenamide (Vemlidy) or entecavir (Baraclude) to reduce viral load and liver injury. Fulminant HBV: list for transplant; antiviral bridging. Monitor for seroconversion; HBsAg clearance expected in 90–95% of immune-competent adults.
us_prevalence: ~20,000 new acute HBV infections/year (CDC); most resolve, ~5–10% progress to chronic.
patients_needing_tx: ~2,000 patients/year with severe acute HBV requiring antiviral intervention; ~500 fulminant cases.
price: $15,000–$30,000/course for novel entry inhibitor or immune modulator. $20,000.
registrational_endpoint: HBsAg clearance rate at 24 weeks; prevention of progression to chronic HBV at 6 months
btd_threshold_vs_soc: HBsAg clearance >70% at 24 weeks vs. ~50% historical with standard antiviral support

symptoms: Unintended loss of ≥5% body weight, weakness, anorexia
treatment_goal: Reverse nutritional deficits, preserve muscle mass, treat underlying cause
first_line_soc: Treat underlying etiology. If anorexia/cachexia is the target: megestrol acetate (progestational agent, appetite stimulant) is the most evidence-backed pharmacologic option for cancer/HIV-related weight loss.
treatment_algorithm: Stepwise approach: (1) Identify and treat underlying cause (cancer, infection, depression, thyroid disease, malabsorption, medication side effects); (2) Nutritional support: high-calorie supplemental feeds, dietitian involvement; (3) Pharmacologic appetite stimulation: megestrol acetate 400–800 mg/day (best evidence for cancer/HIV cachexia); dronabinol 2.5 mg BID (modest evidence); cyproheptadine (antihistamine, appetite stimulant — limited evidence); mirtazapine (SSRI/NaSSA — off-label, modest evidence); (4) Anabolic: testosterone/oxandrolone for HIV/cancer wasting (off-label); anamorelin (ghrelin agonist) — approved in Japan for cancer cachexia, under FDA review; (5) GLP-1 agonists only exacerbate weight loss — avoid; (6) Enteral/parenteral nutrition if GI function adequate/inadequate respectively.
us_prevalence: ~5% annual incidence in US adults; up to 20% in those ≥65, 60% in nursing homes (Merck Manuals 2025). Approximately 10–15 million Americans have clinically significant involuntary weight loss in 2025; cancer cachexia alone affects ~2 million.
patients_needing_tx: ~2–3 million with cachexia/anorexia-cachexia syndrome requiring pharmacotherapy (cancer cachexia, HIV/AIDS wasting, COPD, end-stage renal disease). Megestrol treated ~500,000–800,000/year.
price: $20,000–$50,000/year. Anamorelin (ghrelin agonist) in Japan priced ~$30,000/year; a novel muscle-sparing anabolic/anti-cachexia biologic would price $25K–$50K/year.
peak_sales: ~$2–4 billion for an FDA-approved cachexia agent at 3–6% market penetration. (2,500,000 × 4% × $35,000 = $3.5B). Multiple drugs in development (anamorelin, anti-myostatin antibodies) suggest competitive market.
registrational_endpoint: Change from baseline in lean body mass (LBM, DXA-assessed) at 12 weeks for cancer cachexia; hand grip strength and 6-minute walk test as co-primary or key secondary endpoints. FDA co-primary: LBM + functional measure.
btd_threshold_vs_soc: ≥1.5 kg improvement in LBM vs. placebo at Week 12 + ≥15% improvement in grip strength; patient-reported fatigue/function improvement of ≥10 points on FAACT (Functional Assessment of Anorexia/Cachexia Treatment).

symptoms: Persistent exhaustion unrelieved by rest, cognitive impairment, reduced function
treatment_goal: Identify and treat underlying cause; improve energy and function
first_line_soc: No universal pharmacologic first-line; treat underlying etiology. For ME/CFS (no approved drug): symptomatic management with low-dose tricyclics (amitriptyline) for sleep/pain. For cancer-related fatigue: methylphenidate or modafinil (off-label).
treatment_algorithm: Fatigue is a symptom, not a standalone pharmacologic target. Approach: (1) Identify and treat etiology (anemia → iron/EPO; hypothyroidism → levothyroxine; depression → SSRI; sleep apnea → CPAP); (2) ME/CFS: no FDA-approved drug; NIH report 2025 confirms no approved treatments; symptomatic: low-dose naltrexone (LDN, off-label, emerging evidence), rintatolimod (investigated, not approved), stimulants in selected patients; (3) Cancer fatigue: methylphenidate 5–10 mg BID (moderate evidence); (4) COPD/cardiac fatigue: disease-specific treatment. Energy conservation, pacing, and graded activity titration are essential non-pharmacologic pillars.
us_prevalence: Fatigue is among the most common complaints; ~1.5–3.5 million have ME/CFS (NIH 2025 report: 836,000–2.5 million). Up to 70–80% of cancer patients experience fatigue. Tens of millions have fatigue as a component of chronic disease.
patients_needing_tx: ~3–5 million with ME/CFS or idiopathic chronic fatigue warranting pharmacotherapy; ~2 million with cancer-related fatigue needing pharmacologic intervention. Total: ~5–7 million with fatigue as primary treatment target.
price: $15,000–$50,000/year for an ME/CFS-specific agent. Rationale: ME/CFS has no approved drug, significant disability burden, and represents a large unmet need; first approved drug would command specialty pricing.
peak_sales: ~$2–3 billion for an ME/CFS drug at 5–8% share. (3,000,000 × 6% × $25,000 = $4.5B ceiling; realistic ~$2–3B given emerging competitive landscape).
registrational_endpoint: Fatigue Severity Scale (FSS) or SF-36 Vitality subscale improvement at 24 weeks for ME/CFS; response rate (≥7-point reduction in FSS) as primary endpoint.
btd_threshold_vs_soc: ≥7-point reduction in FSS (0–63 scale) vs. placebo at Week 24 in ME/CFS patients; response rate ≥30% vs. ≤15% placebo.

symptoms: Multi-system symptoms triggered by low-level chemical exposure
treatment_goal: Reduce symptom burden, improve functional capacity and quality of life
first_line_soc: None. No FDA-approved pharmacologic therapy exists; management is avoidance of triggers and symptomatic relief.
treatment_algorithm: None. No specific pharmacotherapy is proven. Approach: (1) Identify and avoid trigger exposures; (2) Treat comorbid anxiety/depression with SSRIs or CBT; (3) Desensitization/EMDR for specific triggers; (4) Symptomatic treatment of headache, GI symptoms, or fatigue as warranted. Cleveland Clinic and major medical organizations do not recognize a validated pharmacologic protocol.
us_prevalence: Estimated 12–16 million Americans report chemical intolerance or MCS symptoms (~4–5% of US population); diagnosed IEI cases are far fewer (~1–2 million) given lack of diagnostic consensus (WHO/AAAAI data).
patients_needing_tx: ~500,000–1 million patients with moderate-to-severe IEI seeking ongoing treatment; pharmacotherapy uptake is low due to lack of approved agents. Realistic drug-treatable pool: ~300,000–500,000.
price: $15,000–$25,000/year. Rationale: No approved competitor; a validated small-molecule or biologic targeting neuroinflammation or mast cell activation would command orphan-adjacent pricing given small addressable market.
peak_sales: ~$300–500 million at 3–5% penetration of symptomatic patients. (500,000 × 4% × $20,000 = $400M). Niche market, high unmet need.
registrational_endpoint: Validated composite symptom score (e.g., Quick Environmental Exposure and Sensitivity Inventory, QEESI) total score reduction vs. placebo at 12–24 weeks.
btd_threshold_vs_soc: ≥30% reduction in QEESI symptom score vs. placebo at Week 12 in patients with moderate-to-severe IEI (QEESI ≥50 at baseline); N/A under current regulatory frameworks as no established benchmark exists.

symptoms: Weakness, exhaustion, slow gait, low activity, unintentional weight loss
treatment_goal: Reverse or slow frailty progression, improve muscle mass, restore function
first_line_soc: Exercise (resistance training + aerobic) combined with protein/caloric supplementation is the most evidence-based intervention. No FDA-approved pharmacologic SoC for frailty.
treatment_algorithm: Multicomponent interventions: Physical rehabilitation + nutrition (high-protein diet ≥1.2g/kg/day, leucine-enriched essential amino acids). Pharmacologic agents under investigation: (1) Testosterone—improves muscle mass in hypogonadal frail men but adverse effects limit use. (2) Growth hormone/IGF-1 analogs. (3) Selective androgen receptor modulators (SARMs; e.g., enobosarm/ostarine)—Phase 3 for cancer cachexia. (4) Myostatin inhibitors—trevogrumab, bimagrumab (anti-activin RII; Phase 2 completed for sarcopenia, 2024 positive data). (5) Ghrelin mimetics—anamorelin (FDA-rejected but in pipeline). (6) Senolytic agents (dasatinib + quercetin)—early trials.
us_prevalence: ~10–25% of US adults ≥65 have frailty; ~7–10 million Americans. Pre-frailty: additional ~20 million.
patients_needing_tx: ~7–10 million frail older adults; ~5 million with clinically significant frailty requiring pharmacologic and non-pharmacologic intervention.
price: $15,000–$50,000/year (novel myostatin inhibitor or SARM).
registrational_endpoint: Change in appendicular lean mass (kg; DEXA) at 6 months; Fried frailty criteria status; grip strength or gait speed improvement.
btd_threshold_vs_soc: ≥1.5 kg increase in appendicular lean mass at 6 months + ≥0.05 m/s gait speed improvement vs. placebo in randomized trial of frail older adults.

symptoms: Unexplained fall, unsteadiness, fear of falling, injury, reduced mobility
treatment_goal: Prevent falls, reduce injury severity, identify modifiable risk factors
first_line_soc: Multifactorial fall prevention: Deprescribing high-risk drugs (sedatives, antihypertensives) + vitamin D supplementation (1000–2000 IU/day; reduces fall risk in deficient patients) + exercise programs (balance/strength).
treatment_algorithm: Step 1: Comprehensive geriatric assessment—medication review (deprescribe benzodiazepines, anticholinergics, psychotropics, antihypertensives). Step 2: Vitamin D supplementation 800–2000 IU/day for patients with deficiency. Step 3: Exercise (Tai chi, resistance training). Step 4: Environmental modifications. Step 5: Vision correction. Pharmacologic: Zoledronic acid (Reclast; bisphosphonate) or other osteoporosis agents for fracture prevention. Testosterone for sarcopenia in men. Teriparatide (Forteo; anabolic bone agent) for high-fracture-risk patients. No drug approved specifically for fall prevention.
us_prevalence: ~1 in 4 adults ≥65 falls each year; ~36 million falls/year in the US; ~3 million ER visits/year for falls; ~800,000 hospitalizations (CDC Fall Data 2024).
patients_needing_tx: ~10–15 million high-risk older adults (recurrent falls, osteoporosis, polypharmacy) potentially benefit from pharmacologic optimization. ~3 million/year with falls needing preventive drug changes.
price: $5,000–$20,000/year (novel sarcopenia-reversing agent or neuromuscular coordinator).
registrational_endpoint: Rate of falls per person-year over 12 months; proportion of patients with ≥1 fall; time to first fall.
btd_threshold_vs_soc: ≥40% relative reduction in fall rate vs. usual care + vitamin D at 12 months.

symptoms: Slow, shuffling, or unsteady walking; reduced stride length; increased fall risk
treatment_goal: Identify underlying cause, improve gait stability, prevent falls
first_line_soc: Treat underlying causes: Levodopa/carbidopa (Sinemet; dopamine precursor) for Parkinson's-related gait; baclofen (muscle relaxant) for spasticity; orthopedic or neurologic surgical referral for structural causes.
treatment_algorithm: Parkinson's gait: Levodopa-carbidopa (standard) + dopamine agonists (pramipexole, ropinirole). Normal pressure hydrocephalus: VP shunting. Peripheral neuropathy: Vitamins B1/B6/B12; treat underlying diabetes. Cervical myelopathy: Surgical decompression. Orthostatic hypotension: Midodrine (alpha-1 agonist), fludrocortisone (mineralocorticoid), droxidopa (norepinephrine precursor; FDA-approved). Cerebrovascular-related: Antiplatelet therapy (aspirin, clopidogrel). Physical therapy (PT) fundamental for all subtypes.
us_prevalence: ~35% of community-dwelling adults ≥70 have gait abnormalities; ~12–14 million US adults ≥65 with significant gait disorder.
patients_needing_tx: ~5–8 million require pharmacologic or procedural treatment for underlying causes.
price: $10,000–$30,000/year (novel neuroregenerative or neurostimulatory agent).
registrational_endpoint: Timed Up and Go (TUG) test improvement at 12 weeks; gait speed (m/s) at 6 months; fall rate reduction at 1 year.
btd_threshold_vs_soc: ≥20% improvement in TUG test time + ≥0.1 m/s gait speed improvement vs. physical therapy alone.

symptoms: Fractures, head injury, lacerations, burns from falls or accidents; delayed healing
treatment_goal: Prevent injury, optimize healing, prevent functional decline post-injury
first_line_soc: Fracture prevention: Bisphosphonates (alendronate, zoledronic acid; bone antiresorptives); denosumab (Prolia; anti-RANKL) for osteoporosis preventing fragility fractures. Acute fracture: Analgesia + vitamin D + calcium + early rehabilitation.
treatment_algorithm: Osteoporosis treatment to prevent fragility fractures: Alendronate (Fosamax) 70mg/week or zoledronic acid (Reclast) 5mg IV annually; denosumab (Prolia) 60mg SC q6mo; teriparatide (Forteo) or abaloparatide (Tymlos; anabolic) 20mcg/day SC × 2 years; romosozumab (Evenity; anti-sclerostin) 210mg SC monthly × 12 months. Wound healing: Growth factors, collagen dressings. Hip fracture: Surgical fixation + aggressive rehabilitation + pharmacologic osteoporosis therapy.
us_prevalence: ~3 million ER visits for fall-related injuries in adults ≥65/year; ~300,000 hip fractures/year (CDC/NCHS). Overall elderly injury: ~800,000 hospitalizations/year.
patients_needing_tx: ~3 million with fragility fractures + osteoporosis needing pharmacologic prevention; ~300,000 acute hip fractures needing analgesics + rehabilitation.
price: $10,000–$30,000/year (anabolic bone agent or novel fracture prevention biologic).
registrational_endpoint: Vertebral fracture rate reduction at 18–24 months vs. placebo; hip fracture rate at 3 years; BMD T-score change at 12 months.
btd_threshold_vs_soc: ≥70% relative reduction in vertebral fractures at 18 months vs. placebo; or ≥40% reduction in hip fractures vs. alendronate.

symptoms: Drug side effects, hospital-acquired delirium, falls, pressure ulcers from medical care
treatment_goal: Prevent drug-related harm, reduce delirium, optimize medications in elderly
first_line_soc: Deprescribing using Beers Criteria (AGS) and STOPP/START criteria. No pharmacologic SoC; best practice involves systematic medication review and reduction of inappropriate prescribing.
treatment_algorithm: Medication optimization: Comprehensive geriatric medication review; discontinue high-risk drugs (PIMs per Beers 2023—benzodiazepines, first-generation antihistamines, NSAIDs, PPIs long-term, antipsychotics). Delirium prevention: Haloperidol (antipsychotic) for delirium management (evidence-limited); dexmedetomidine in ICU setting. Pressure ulcer prevention: Prophylactic dressings, repositioning. Functional decline: Protein/caloric supplementation; early mobilization protocols. VTE prevention: Low-molecular-weight heparin.
us_prevalence: ~800,000+ hospitalized elderly patients experience iatrogenic complications annually; ~10–15% of elderly inpatients (NEJM/VA data).
patients_needing_tx: ~800,000 inpatient episodes + ~3–5 million ambulatory elderly with inappropriate polypharmacy.
price: $5,000–$15,000 per intervention course (novel delirium-prevention or deprescribing decision-support with pharmacologic protocol).
registrational_endpoint: Delirium incidence during hospitalization; number of inappropriate medications discontinued; adverse drug event rate at 90 days.
btd_threshold_vs_soc: ≥40% relative reduction in hospital-acquired delirium vs. usual care; or ≥30% reduction in adverse drug events at 90 days.

symptoms: Adverse drug reactions, drug interactions, under-prescribing, non-adherence, falls
treatment_goal: Optimize pharmacotherapy, eliminate harmful drugs, improve adherence
first_line_soc: Systematic medication review using validated tools (Beers Criteria, STOPP/START); pharmacist-led medication reconciliation. No specific pharmacologic SoC—the intervention is about optimizing existing medications.
treatment_algorithm: Polypharmacy management: Comprehensive geriatric assessment + pharmacist-led drug review; deprescribing protocols (GoodPalliativeCareRegistry, Medstopper tool). High-risk drug classes to deprescribe per Beers 2023: Anticholinergics (diphenhydramine, oxybutynin), sedative-hypnotics (benzodiazepines, Z-drugs), first-generation antihistamines, chronic NSAIDs, antipsychotics, long-acting sulfonylureas (glibenclamide). Under-prescribing of evidence-based medications (statins, ACEi, anticoagulants for AF) also targeted. Digital health and clinical decision support tools increasingly used.
us_prevalence: ~40–50% of community-dwelling elderly ≥65 take ≥5 medications (polypharmacy); ~20–30% take ≥10 medications (hyperpolypharmacy). ~10 million US elderly have medication-related problems.
patients_needing_tx: ~10 million elderly patients with clinically significant medication-related problems requiring intervention.
price: $2,000–$5,000/year (value of pharmacist-led or AI-driven deprescribing program).
registrational_endpoint: Number of inappropriate medications reduced at 3 months; adverse drug events per patient-year; hospitalization rate at 6 months.
btd_threshold_vs_soc: ≥40% relative reduction in adverse drug events + ≥2 inappropriate medications reduced per patient vs. usual care at 6 months.

symptoms: Fatigue, pallor, weakness, shortness of breath on exertion
treatment_goal: Raise hemoglobin to symptom-relief threshold, treat underlying disease
first_line_soc: Treat underlying disease. ESA (epoetin alfa/darbepoetin alfa) if Hgb <10 g/dL with symptoms and non-cancer setting; IV iron if iron-restricted.
treatment_algorithm: ESAs: epoetin alfa (Epogen/Procrit), darbepoetin alfa (Aranesp), for renal/chemo-related. IV iron: ferric carboxymaltose (Injectafer), ferumoxytol (Feraheme) for iron restriction. HIF-PHI: roxadustat (approved ex-US; FDA-rejected in US for ACD outside CKD). Novel: anti-hepcidin (LJPC-401), activin receptor ligand trap (luspatercept for MDS-related ACD). Transfusion for severe acute.
us_prevalence: ~1.5–2 million US patients with clinically significant ACD requiring intervention (subset of broader ACD population of 10M+).
patients_needing_tx: ~1 million patients where pharmacologic tx is warranted (persistent Hgb <10, symptomatic, non-malignant or malignancy not actively bleeding).
price: $15,000–$25,000/year for ESA; novel HIF-PHI or anti-hepcidin might price at $30,000–$50,000/year. $40,000 est.
peak_sales: 15% novel share of addressable market: ~$6.0B peak
registrational_endpoint: Proportion achieving Hgb rise ≥1.5 g/dL or transfusion independence over 24 weeks
btd_threshold_vs_soc: ≥50% of patients achieving Hgb rise ≥1.5 g/dL vs. <25% placebo, or transfusion independence rate >40% vs. <15%

symptoms: Fatigue, pallor, shortness of breath, poor exercise tolerance
treatment_goal: Maintain Hgb 10–11.5 g/dL, reduce transfusion dependence
first_line_soc: Erythropoiesis-stimulating agents (ESAs): darbepoetin alfa (Aranesp) or epoetin alfa (Epogen); supplemental IV iron.
treatment_algorithm: ESAs: epoetin alfa, darbepoetin alfa, methoxy PEG-epoetin beta (Mircera, EU). IV iron: ferric carboxymaltose, ferumoxytol, sucrose. HIF-PHI (hypoxia-inducible factor prolyl hydroxylase inhibitors): daprodustat (Jesduvroq, FDA 2023 for dialysis-dependent CKD), roxadustat (US FDA rejected for non-dialysis but approved dialysis 2023 for Evrenzo). Vadadustat approved in Japan. Transfusion for acute/refractory.
us_prevalence: ~40 million CKD patients; ~500,000 on dialysis (ESRD). ~3 million CKD patients with clinically significant anemia requiring pharmacologic treatment.
patients_needing_tx: ~800,000 ESRD+pre-dialysis patients actively on ESA or HIF-PHI annually.
price: $18,000–$30,000/year ESA; HIF-PHI (daprodustat WAC ~$20,000/year). Novel next-gen: $35,000/year.
registrational_endpoint: Non-inferiority in mean Hgb change from baseline vs. ESA; MACE safety comparator
btd_threshold_vs_soc: Non-inferiority margin Δ≤0.75 g/dL; superiority if achieving Hgb target in >70% vs. <55% with ESA in specific subgroup

symptoms: Fatigue, pallor, shortness of breath, pica, brittle nails
treatment_goal: Correct iron deficiency, normalize hemoglobin, replenish iron stores
first_line_soc: Oral iron supplementation: ferrous sulfate 325 mg (65 mg elemental Fe) TID. IV iron (ferric carboxymaltose, ferumoxytol, iron sucrose) when oral fails or not tolerated.
treatment_algorithm: Oral: ferrous sulfate (cheap, generic). IV iron: ferric carboxymaltose (Injectafer) 750–1,500 mg in 1–2 infusions; ferumoxytol (Feraheme); low-molecular-weight iron dextran (INFeD). ESAs added only if concurrent renal/inflammatory cause. Novel: sucroferric oxyhydroxide (Velphoro, for CKD). Treat underlying cause (GI bleeding, menorrhagia). Blood transfusion for life-threatening IDA.
us_prevalence: ~8 million US patients with IDA; ~3 million with moderate-severe requiring IV iron.
patients_needing_tx: ~3 million requiring IV iron or sustained pharmacologic management annually.
price: $500–$2,000/infusion for IV iron (Injectafer $450/vial). Novel oral: $5,000–$10,000/year. $8,000 est.
registrational_endpoint: Hgb increase ≥2 g/dL from baseline or Hgb normalization at 8 weeks
btd_threshold_vs_soc: Hgb normalization (≥12 g/dL women, ≥13 g/dL men) in ≥70% at 8 weeks; ≥2 g/dL rise in ≥80%

symptoms: Fatigue, pallor, neurologic symptoms (B12 deficiency), glossitis, macrocytosis
treatment_goal: Replace deficient vitamin, reverse anemia and neurologic damage
first_line_soc: Vitamin B12 deficiency: cyanocobalamin IM injections or high-dose oral (1,000 mcg/day). Folate deficiency: folic acid 1–5 mg/day oral.
treatment_algorithm: B12 deficiency: cyanocobalamin 1,000 mcg IM/week × 4 weeks → monthly (or oral high-dose). Methylcobalamin sublingual for some patients. Folate deficiency: folic acid 1–5 mg PO QD × 4 months. Hereditary folate malabsorption: folinic acid (leucovorin) IV. Congenital intrinsic factor deficiency: lifelong IM B12. Drug-induced (methotrexate): leucovorin rescue. No novel pharmacologic drugs routinely needed—standard vitamins are curative.
us_prevalence: ~2 million US patients with megaloblastic anemia; B12 deficiency most common (especially elderly).
patients_needing_tx: ~1.5 million requiring regular pharmacologic vitamin replacement.
price: $500–$2,000/year (generic vitamins extremely cheap). Novel oral B12 bioavailability: $5,000.
registrational_endpoint: Hgb normalization and serum B12/folate normalization at 12 weeks
btd_threshold_vs_soc: Hgb normalization ≥12 g/dL in ≥90% at 12 weeks; neurologic symptom improvement ≥50%

symptoms: Fatigue, jaundice, dark urine, pallor, rapid heart rate
treatment_goal: Stop hemolysis, normalize hemoglobin, achieve durable remission
first_line_soc: Corticosteroids: prednisone 1 mg/kg/day (warm AIHA, first-line).
treatment_algorithm: Warm AIHA: prednisone → rituximab (Rituxan) for steroid-refractory/dependent. Second-line: azathioprine, mycophenolate mofetil, danazol. Third-line: splenectomy. Cold agglutinin disease: rituximab (first-line, per 2024 ASH guidelines) ± bendamustine; sutimlimab (Enjaymo, C1s inhibitor, FDA 2022 for CAD). Novel: iptacopan, rilzabrutinib. Transfusion with washed RBCs for life-threatening.
us_prevalence: ~35,000 US patients; warm AIHA ~25,000; cold agglutinin disease ~6,000–8,000.
patients_needing_tx: ~20,000 patients requiring chronic pharmacologic therapy beyond steroids.
price: $200,000–$350,000/year (sutimlimab WAC ~$270K/year). Novel C1/complement inhibitor: $300,000.
registrational_endpoint: Hgb response (≥2 g/dL rise to ≥10 g/dL) without rescue therapy at week 26
btd_threshold_vs_soc: ≥50% response rate vs. <20% historical placebo for cold agglutinin disease

symptoms: Fatigue, pallor, frequent infections, easy bruising
treatment_goal: Correct anemia, reduce transfusion dependence
first_line_soc: Supportive care (RBC transfusions, G-CSF). Luspatercept (Reblozyl) for transfusion-dependent lower-risk MDS-RS.
treatment_algorithm: Lower-risk MDS: ESAs (epoetin alfa) if EPO <500; lenalidomide for del(5q); luspatercept for ring sideroblasts. Iron chelation (deferasirox/Exjade) for transfusion iron overload. Higher-risk: azacitidine (Vidaza) or decitabine. Allogeneic SCT curative in eligible. 2024: imetelstat (Rytelo, telomerase inhibitor) approved for low/intermediate-risk MDS after ESA failure.
us_prevalence: Overlaps with MDS; ring sideroblast subtype ~20,000 US patients. Iron-transport deficiency anemia (DMT1 mutations): <500 patients.
patients_needing_tx: ~18,000 patients with MDS-associated sideroblastic anemia needing pharmacologic therapy.
price: $150,000–$200,000/year (luspatercept WAC ~$160K/year). $175,000.
registrational_endpoint: Transfusion independence (TI) rate at 24 weeks; duration of TI
btd_threshold_vs_soc: TI rate ≥40% vs. <15% placebo/ESA at 24 weeks

symptoms: Fatigue, pallor, iron overload signs; ring sideroblasts on bone marrow biopsy
treatment_goal: Reduce transfusion dependence; correct anemia; prevent iron overload
first_line_soc: Pyridoxine (vitamin B6) 50–200 mg/day for pyridoxine-responsive (hereditary X-linked) sideroblastic anemia. Supportive transfusions + iron chelation for others.
treatment_algorithm: Hereditary X-linked (ALAS2 mutation): pyridoxine trial (30–50% respond). Acquired (MDS-RS): luspatercept (Reblozyl, FDA 2020), imetelstat (Rytelo, FDA 2024). Mitochondrial sideroblastic: supportive; coenzyme Q10 investigational. Iron chelation: deferasirox if ferritin >1000. Allo-SCT for young patients with severe disease.
us_prevalence: Hereditary: ~5,000–10,000 patients; acquired (MDS-RS): ~20,000–25,000 patients.
patients_needing_tx: ~20,000 patients requiring pharmacologic therapy.
registrational_endpoint: Transfusion independence (TI) rate at ≥8 weeks; Hgb rise ≥1.5 g/dL from baseline
btd_threshold_vs_soc: TI rate ≥45% at 24 weeks vs. ~25% with supportive care/ESA

symptoms: Anemia with helmet cells (schistocytes), thrombocytopenia, end-organ damage
treatment_goal: Treat underlying cause (TTP, HUS, DIC); stop microangiopathy
first_line_soc: Plasma exchange (PEX) + caplacizumab (Cablivi, anti-vWF, FDA 2019) + corticosteroids + rituximab for TTP. Eculizumab for aHUS.
treatment_algorithm: TTP: PEX daily + caplacizumab (reduces exacerbations and TTP-related death, HERCULES trial) + corticosteroids + rituximab for immune TTP. Congenital TTP (Upshaw-Schulman): FFP q3w or recombinant ADAMTS13 (SHP647, emerging). aHUS: eculizumab/ravulizumab. Drug-induced MAHA: stop offending drug. Cancer-associated: treat underlying malignancy.
us_prevalence: TTP: ~10,000–12,000 US prevalent; ~2,000 new episodes/year. aHUS: ~1,500 prevalent.
patients_needing_tx: ~3,500/year requiring acute pharmacologic TMA-directed therapy.
price: $500,000–$700,000/year (caplacizumab WAC ~$540K/episode; eculizumab ~$700K/year). $600,000.
registrational_endpoint: Recurrence of TTP-related events; remission duration; platelet count normalization at 30 days
btd_threshold_vs_soc: ≥50% reduction in TTP exacerbation rate vs. PEX alone; time to platelet normalization <3 days

symptoms: Severe fatigue, frequent infections, easy bruising, bleeding
treatment_goal: Restore bone marrow function, achieve trilineage hematologic response
first_line_soc: Allogeneic bone marrow transplant (BMT) for young patients with matched donor. Immunosuppression (IST): anti-thymocyte globulin (ATG, equine—Atgam) + cyclosporine + eltrombopag (Promacta) for transplant-ineligible.
treatment_algorithm: First-line: allo-BMT (curative, preferred <40 y with sibling donor). IST: horse ATG + cyclosporine A ± eltrombopag (3-drug regimen, NEJM 2017). Second-line: eltrombo­pag monotherapy; rabbit ATG; sirolimus. Refractory: ixazomib, alemtuzumab. Danazol for refractory. 2024: avatrombopag investigational. Supportive: G-CSF, transfusions, iron chelation.
us_prevalence: ~15,000–20,000 US patients; ~2,000–4,000 new severe AA cases/year (SEER/NHLBI).
patients_needing_tx: ~3,000/year newly diagnosed need immediate IST or transplant; ~10,000 prevalent patients need ongoing therapy.
price: $100,000–$150,000/year (eltrombopag WAC ~$90K; novel TPO-RA ~$150K). $125,000/year.
registrational_endpoint: Hematologic response rate (tri-lineage) at 6 months; transfusion independence
btd_threshold_vs_soc: Complete response rate >35% vs. IST-alone historical <20%; overall hematologic response >70% vs. <50%

symptoms: Fatigue, pallor, weight loss, leukoerythroblastic blood smear
treatment_goal: Treat underlying marrow-infiltrating disease
first_line_soc: Treat underlying cause (malignancy, myelofibrosis, granulomatous disease). Transfusion support for symptomatic anemia.
treatment_algorithm: No specific pharmacotherapy for myelophthisis itself. Address underlying: myelofibrosis → ruxolitinib; metastatic cancer → disease-specific chemotherapy/targeted therapy; granulomatous disease → corticosteroids/antimicrobials. RBC transfusions for Hgb <7–8 g/dL. EPO agents generally ineffective due to marrow infiltration.
us_prevalence: No isolated registry; associated with ~20,000–30,000 patients with advanced marrow-infiltrating diseases.
patients_needing_tx: ~10,000 patients where pharmacologic management of anemia is actively pursued beyond transfusion.
price: $80,000–$120,000/year for novel erythropoiesis stimulator. $100,000.
registrational_endpoint: Hgb rise ≥1.5 g/dL from baseline or transfusion independence at 12 weeks
btd_threshold_vs_soc: TI rate ≥30% vs. <5% best supportive care

symptoms: Severe anemia (fatigue, pallor, dyspnea); no reticulocytes; normal WBC/platelets
treatment_goal: Restore erythropoiesis; achieve transfusion independence
first_line_soc: Immunosuppression: cyclosporine A ± corticosteroids (first-line for idiopathic/autoimmune PRCA). Remove offending cause (parvovirus B19: IVIG; anti-EPO antibodies: stop EPO).
treatment_algorithm: Idiopathic: cyclosporine A ± prednisone → rituximab (for CD20+ B-cell mediated) → cyclophosphamide. Thymoma-associated: thymectomy + cyclosporine. Parvovirus: IVIG 0.4 g/kg × 5 days. EPO-antibody: discontinue ESA → cyclosporine. Investigational: eltrombopag (Promacta) promoting progenitor expansion. Allo-SCT for refractory.
us_prevalence: ~5,000–8,000 US patients with PRCA at any given time.
patients_needing_tx: ~5,000 patients on pharmacologic immunosuppression.
registrational_endpoint: Transfusion independence rate (≥8 weeks TI); reticulocyte and Hgb normalization
btd_threshold_vs_soc: TI rate ≥60% at 26 weeks vs. ~40% cyclosporine; Hgb >10 g/dL without transfusion

disease: Stomatocytosis and Anemia Caused by Hypophosphatemia
symptoms: Hemolytic anemia, jaundice, splenomegaly; muscle weakness in hypophosphatemia
treatment_goal: Correct underlying cause; treat hemolysis; normalize phosphate levels
first_line_soc: Phosphate replacement for hypophosphatemia-induced anemia. Treat underlying stomatocytosis with folic acid and supportive care; avoid splenectomy (increased thrombotic risk in hereditary stomatocytosis).
treatment_algorithm: Overhydrated stomatocytosis: no proven pharmacotherapy; supportive. Dehydrated stomatocytosis (DHSt): observe if compensated; folic acid 1 mg/day; avoid iron overload. Hypophosphatemia: oral/IV phosphate supplementation; treat underlying cause (malnutrition, refeeding syndrome, Fanconi syndrome). No targeted pharmacotherapy for stomatocytosis per se.
us_prevalence: Rare; hereditary stomatocytosis: ~5,000–10,000 US patients. Hypophosphatemia-anemia: common transient.
patients_needing_tx: ~3,000 patients with hereditary stomatocytosis needing pharmacologic follow-up.
price: $50,000–$100,000/year for a novel hemolysis-targeting agent. $75,000.
registrational_endpoint: Reduction in hemolysis markers (LDH, bilirubin); Hgb rise ≥1 g/dL; transfusion avoidance
btd_threshold_vs_soc: TI rate ≥50% and Hgb ≥10 g/dL vs. supportive care alone

symptoms: Easy bruising, prolonged bleeding from cuts, heavy periods, nosebleeds
treatment_goal: Control bleeding; prevent perioperative hemorrhage
first_line_soc: Type 1 VWD: desmopressin (DDAVP, 0.3 mcg/kg IV/IN) for mild/moderate bleeding. Types 2/3: VWF concentrate (Humate-P, Wilate) or recombinant VWF (Vonvendi).
treatment_algorithm: Type 1 (most common, 70%): desmopressin for mild bleeding; antifibrinolytics (tranexamic acid) as adjunct. Types 2A/2B/2M: VWF/FVIII concentrate. Type 3 (severe): Vonvendi (recombinant VWF, FDA 2015) or Humate-P. Prophylaxis in Type 3: regular VWF infusions. Fitusiran (RNA interference, FDA 2023) for inhibitor-complicated VWD. 2024-25: fitusiran expanding to VWD without inhibitors (trial).
us_prevalence: ~3 million US patients with VWD (1% of population); clinically significant: ~200,000.
patients_needing_tx: ~150,000 patients requiring pharmacologic therapy.
price: $40,000–$200,000/year (VWF concentrates ~$100K; fitusiran ~$300K). $150,000.
registrational_endpoint: Annualized bleeding rate (ABR) reduction; FVIII:C and VWF:RCo levels; surgical hemostatic success
btd_threshold_vs_soc: ABR ≤2/year vs. ~4–6 on-demand; VWF:RCo ≥50% in ≥80%

symptoms: Spontaneous joint bleeds, muscle bleeds, prolonged bleeding after injury
treatment_goal: Prevent bleeding, achieve zero-bleed status, preserve joint function
first_line_soc: Hemophilia A: emicizumab (Hemlibra, bispecific FVIIIa-mimicking antibody) SC prophylaxis (first-line in many patients). Factor VIII concentrate (recombinant) for on-demand/perioperative. Hemophilia B: factor IX concentrate.
treatment_algorithm: HA without inhibitor: emicizumab prophylaxis (HAVEN trials); recombinant FVIII (octocog alfa, turoctocog alfa, rurioctacog alfa pegol). HA with inhibitor: emicizumab (FDA 2018); bypassing agents (aPCC/rFVIIa) for bleakthroughs. Fitusiran (AT RNAi, Phase 3) for both HA/HB. HB: eptacog beta (Mim8, bispecific); gene therapy (fidanacogene elaparvovec/Beqvez, FDA 2024; etranacogene dezaparvovec/Hemgenix, FDA 2022). HA gene therapy: valrox (still in trials). 2024: fitusiran FDA approved (Alhemo).
us_prevalence: ~30,000 HA patients; ~6,000 HB patients; ~2/3 are on prophylaxis.
patients_needing_tx: ~25,000 HA + 5,000 HB = ~30,000 patients on chronic pharmacologic prophylaxis.
price: $500,000–$700,000/year (emicizumab ~$500K; gene therapy $3.5M one-time). Annual: $600,000.
peak_sales: 30% novel prophylaxis (emicizumab + fitusiran + gene therapy) share: ~$5.4B peak (consistent with Hemlibra ~$4B+ actual)
registrational_endpoint: Annualized bleeding rate (ABR) and annualized joint bleeding rate (AJBR) vs. prior prophylaxis or on-demand
btd_threshold_vs_soc: ABR <3 with zero-bleed in ≥50% of patients; ≥80% reduction in ABR vs. on-demand

symptoms: Easy bruising, petechiae, mucosal bleeding with normal platelet count and coagulation
treatment_goal: Identify and treat underlying cause; minimize bleeding risk
first_line_soc: Treat underlying cause (scurvy: vitamin C; Henoch-Schönlein: corticosteroids; senile purpura: vitamin C/tretinoin). Desmopressin for mild VWD-like vascular disorders.
treatment_algorithm: Scurvy: ascorbic acid 500–1000 mg/day. HSP/IgA vasculitis: corticosteroids for abdominal pain/renal involvement. Hereditary hemorrhagic telangiectasia: bevacizumab IV (FDA compassionate use, anti-VEGF) or thalidomide for epistaxis. Ehlers-Danlos: no specific pharmacotherapy; desmopressin for procedures. Purpura simplex: reassurance.
us_prevalence: Broad category; millions affected by various causes. Clinically significant vascular bleeding disorders: ~500,000 US patients.
patients_needing_tx: ~200,000 patients requiring some pharmacologic intervention.
price: $30,000–$100,000/year for a novel vascular integrity agent. $60,000.
registrational_endpoint: Annualized bleeding rate (ABR) reduction; quality of life PRO score
btd_threshold_vs_soc: ≥40% ABR reduction vs. placebo; epistaxis episodes <3/month in HHT

symptoms: Prolonged bleeding from cuts, heavy menstruation, bruising, nosebleeds
treatment_goal: Identify underlying cause, stop bleeding, prevent recurrence
first_line_soc: Treat underlying cause. Acute: apply pressure, topical thrombin (Recothrom), fibrin sealants. Tranexamic acid IV/oral for various bleeding disorders.
treatment_algorithm: Depends on etiology: hemophilia (factor concentrates, emicizumab); VWD (desmopressin, VWF concentrate); platelet disorders (desmopressin, transfusion); anticoagulant reversal (andexanet alfa/Andexxa for FXa inhibitors; idarucizumab/Praxbind for dabigatran). Tranexamic acid broadly applicable. Recombinant FVIIa for refractory.
us_prevalence: Broad syndromic category; millions with some excessive bleeding. Clinically significant requiring pharmacologic Rx: ~500,000–1M.
patients_needing_tx: ~300,000 patients/year with significant bleeding disorders requiring pharmacologic intervention.
price: $20,000–$50,000/year for broadly applicable hemostatic agent. $35,000.
registrational_endpoint: Annualized bleeding rate (ABR) reduction vs. SoC in randomized trial
btd_threshold_vs_soc: ≥30% ABR reduction vs. SoC with comparable safety profile

symptoms: Varying bleeding: mucosal, joint, post-surgical; depends on specific factor deficiency
treatment_goal: Prevent and treat bleeding with factor replacement
first_line_soc: Factor replacement concentrates: factor VII concentrate (NovoSeven rFVIIa) for FVII deficiency; fibrinogen concentrate (RiaSTAP) for fibrinogenemia; factor XIII concentrate (Tretten) for FXIII deficiency.
treatment_algorithm: FVII deficiency: rFVIIa (NovoSeven) on-demand or prophylaxis. Factor X deficiency: factor X/V complex concentrates or PCCs. Factor XIII: prophylaxis with FXIII-A concentrate (Tretten, FDA 2013). Fibrinogen deficiency: fibrinogen concentrate (RiaSTAP) or cryoprecipitate. FXII deficiency: usually no treatment needed. Novel: fitusiran (RNA interference, antithrombin-lowering, FDA 2023) for rare bleeding disorders. Gene therapy trials ongoing.
us_prevalence: Total rare coagulation disorder patients: ~30,000–50,000 US (FVII ~1 in 500K; FXIII ~1 in 2M; etc.).
patients_needing_tx: ~20,000 patients with symptomatic rare coagulation disorders on pharmacologic therapy.
price: $200,000–$600,000/year for novel factor concentrates. $400,000.
peak_sales: 25% novel agent share across disorders: ~$2B peak
registrational_endpoint: Annualized bleeding rate (ABR) reduction vs. on-demand; factor activity level achieved
btd_threshold_vs_soc: ABR ≤2 bleeds/year vs. ~6–10 on-demand; factor activity ≥50% in ≥75% of patients

symptoms: Recurrent nosebleeds, telangiectasias, GI bleeding, iron deficiency anemia
treatment_goal: Reduce bleeding frequency, treat AVMs, maintain hemoglobin
first_line_soc: Bevacizumab (Avastin, anti-VEGF) IV for severe epistaxis/GI bleeding (off-label, increasingly used). Systemic iron supplementation. Laser therapy for telangiectasias.
treatment_algorithm: Bevacizumab IV (8 mg/kg q2w × 6 infusions then maintenance) for HHT-related epistaxis and GI bleeding (ELLIPSE trial, benefit shown). Thalidomide/lenalidomide (anti-angiogenic) for GI-predominant. Pazopanib oral for moderate HHT (ongoing trials). Intranasal bevacizumab for epistaxis (trial evidence). AVM embolization/laser. Iron therapy (IV iron). Tranexamic acid for epistaxis.
us_prevalence: ~75,000–100,000 US patients with HHT (1/5,000 prevalence).
patients_needing_tx: ~30,000 patients with moderate-severe HHT requiring pharmacologic therapy.
price: $100,000–$200,000/year for anti-angiogenic agent/novel approach. $150,000.
registrational_endpoint: Epistaxis severity score (ESS) reduction and Hgb maintenance at 6 months vs. placebo
btd_threshold_vs_soc: ≥30% reduction in ESS AND Hgb increase ≥1 g/dL vs. placebo

symptoms: Simultaneous bleeding and clotting, organ failure, petechiae, shock
treatment_goal: Treat underlying cause, correct coagulopathy, support organ function
first_line_soc: Treat underlying cause (antibiotics for sepsis, deliver fetus for obstetric DIC). Replace clotting factors: FFP, cryoprecipitate, platelets as clinically needed.
treatment_algorithm: Primary: reverse trigger. Replacement: FFP for prolonged PT/aPTT; cryoprecipitate for fibrinogen <100 mg/dL; platelet transfusion for <50K. Heparin for thrombotic DIC (purpura fulminans, arterial thrombosis). Antithrombin concentrate for AT-deficient DIC (Japan approval). TFPI, recombinant thrombomodulin (approved Japan) investigational. No FDA-approved drug specifically for DIC.
us_prevalence: ~18,000–25,000 cases/year of clinically significant DIC in ICU settings.
patients_needing_tx: ~20,000/year requiring active pharmacologic coagulopathy management.
price: $30,000–$80,000/course for novel DIC-specific agent (e.g., recombinant thrombomodulin). $50,000.
registrational_endpoint: 28-day all-cause mortality and DIC score resolution vs. placebo + SoC
btd_threshold_vs_soc: ≥15% absolute mortality reduction vs. SoC; DIC score normalization >60% vs. <40%

disease: Coagulation Disorders Caused by Circulating Anticoagulants
symptoms: Unexplained severe bleeding, prolonged PTT, hemarthroses
treatment_goal: Eliminate inhibitor, control bleeding, restore normal coagulation
first_line_soc: Acquired hemophilia A (most common): immunosuppression (prednisone ± cyclophosphamide) to eradicate inhibitor; bypassing agents (aPCC/FEIBA or rFVIIa/NovoSeven) for acute bleeding.
treatment_algorithm: Inhibitor eradication: prednisone alone or + cyclophosphamide; rituximab for refractory. Acute bleeding: activated prothrombin complex concentrate (aPCC/FEIBA) or recombinant FVIIa (NovoSeven). Emicizumab (Hemlibra) off-label for acquired hemophilia A with inhibitor. IVIG for some autoimmune forms.
us_prevalence: ~1,500–2,000 new cases acquired hemophilia A/year; total prevalent ~5,000.
patients_needing_tx: ~3,000 patients requiring pharmacologic inhibitor eradication or bypass therapy.
price: $150,000–$300,000/year for novel hemostatic or immunosuppressive agent. $200,000.
registrational_endpoint: Inhibitor eradication rate (negative Bethesda assay) at 24 weeks
btd_threshold_vs_soc: Inhibitor eradication rate >70% at 24 weeks vs. <50% with prednisone alone

symptoms: Headache, ruddy complexion, fatigue, blurred vision, thrombosis risk
treatment_goal: Treat underlying cause; normalize hematocrit to reduce thrombosis risk
first_line_soc: Treat underlying cause (hypoxia, EPO-secreting tumor, sleep apnea). Therapeutic phlebotomy to maintain Hct <50% in symptomatic cases.
treatment_algorithm: Address etiology: CPAP for sleep apnea; supplemental O2 for COPD; treat EPO-secreting tumor. Phlebotomy to Hct <50%. Avoid JAK inhibitors (unlike PV — no JAK2 V617F). Low-dose aspirin for cardiovascular risk. No cytoreductive drug indicated.
us_prevalence: ~200,000 US patients with secondary erythrocytosis from various causes.
patients_needing_tx: ~50,000 patients requiring active treatment (phlebotomy ± pharmacologic).
price: $30,000–$70,000/year for a novel erythrocytosis-modulating agent. $50,000.
registrational_endpoint: Hematocrit normalization (<50%) rate; phlebotomy frequency reduction; thrombotic event rate
btd_threshold_vs_soc: Hct <50% without phlebotomy in ≥60% vs. phlebotomy-only; VTE reduction ≥30%

symptoms: Painful vaso-occlusive crises, anemia, stroke, acute chest syndrome, organ damage
treatment_goal: Reduce vaso-occlusive crises, prevent end-organ damage, increase fetal Hgb
first_line_soc: Hydroxyurea (increases HbF, reduces VOC frequency) — first-line pharmacotherapy for all patients with moderate-severe SCD.
treatment_algorithm: All patients: hydroxyurea 15–35 mg/kg/day. Pain management: NSAIDs/opioids for acute crisis. Anti-sickling: L-glutamine (Endari, FDA 2017); voxelotor (Oxbryta, FDA 2019, withdrawn 2024 per FDA; pfizer discontinued). Crizanlizumab (Adakveo, P-selectin inhibitor, FDA 2019 — withdrawn from market 2023). 2024: mitapivat (PKR activator) showing benefit. Gene therapy: lovotibeglogene autoleucel (Lyfgenia, FDA 2023); exagamglogene autotemcel (Casgevy, CRISPR-based, FDA 2023). Allo-SCT curative. Transfusion for stroke prevention.
us_prevalence: ~100,000 US patients with SCD (predominantly Black/African American).
patients_needing_tx: ~90,000 patients on or needing pharmacologic therapy.
price: $200,000–$3,100,000 (gene therapies one-time ~$2.2M). For ongoing pharmacotherapy: $100,000–$200,000/year. $150,000.
peak_sales: 30% novel non-gene therapy agent share: ~$4B peak; gene therapy: ~$2.5B peak
registrational_endpoint: Annual VOC rate reduction; acute chest syndrome rate; Hgb rise ≥1 g/dL
btd_threshold_vs_soc: ≥40% VOC reduction vs. hydroxyurea; Hgb rise ≥1.5 g/dL; gene therapy: HbA ≥10 g/dL

symptoms: Severe anemia, bone deformities, splenomegaly, growth retardation, iron overload
treatment_goal: Achieve transfusion independence; prevent iron overload; enable curative transplant
first_line_soc: Transfusion-dependent beta-thal major: regular RBC transfusions + iron chelation (deferasirox/Jadenu). Luspatercept (Reblozyl) for non-transfusion-dependent or reducing transfusion burden (FDA 2020).
treatment_algorithm: TDT: RBC transfusions q3–4 weeks. Iron chelation: deferasirox (oral) or deferoxamine (SC). Luspatercept (Reblozyl) reduces transfusion burden in non-transfusion-dependent (BEYOND trial) and TDT (BELIEVE trial). Splenectomy in selected. Allo-SCT: curative in eligible. Gene therapy: betibeglogene spartoflocel (Zynteglo, bluebird bio, FDA 2022) for β-thal TDT; exagamglogene autotemcel (Casgevy, CRISPR, FDA 2023). HbF inducers (hydroxyurea) for HbS/β-thal. 2024: mitapivat for non-TDT.
us_prevalence: ~20,000 US patients with beta-thalassemia (major + intermedia); ~60,000 with sickle-β-thal.
patients_needing_tx: ~15,000 patients requiring active pharmacologic therapy.
price: $200,000–$2,800,000 (gene therapy one-time ~$2.8M). Regular pharmacotherapy: $100,000–$200,000/year. $150,000.
peak_sales: 30% novel non-gene therapy agent share: ~$675M peak; gene therapy: ~$2B total market
registrational_endpoint: Transfusion independence (TI) rate ≥12 weeks without transfusion; Hgb ≥9 g/dL
btd_threshold_vs_soc: TI rate ≥50% vs. ~20% supportive; Hgb ≥10 g/dL without transfusion in ≥50%

symptoms: Intermittent pain crises, retinopathy, avascular necrosis, mild anemia
treatment_goal: Prevent vaso-occlusive crises, retinopathy, and organ damage
first_line_soc: Hydroxyurea to increase HbF (used off-label; less robust benefit vs. HbSS). Folic acid. Ophthalmologic surveillance for proliferative retinopathy.
treatment_algorithm: Hydroxyurea (less effective than in HbSS; used for severe disease). Crizanlizumab (Adakveo, anti-P-selectin) — approved for SCD including HbSC. Voxelotor (Oxbryta, HbS polymerization inhibitor; withdrawn from US market 2024). L-glutamine (Endari). Transfusion for stroke. Laser photocoagulation for retinopathy. Emerging: gene therapy (LentiGlobin/betibeglogene, CRISPR—primarily HbSS/HbS-beta-thal).
us_prevalence: ~25,000–30,000 US patients with HbSC disease.
patients_needing_tx: ~15,000 patients with symptomatic HbSC requiring pharmacologic intervention.
price: $100,000–$180,000/year (crizanlizumab WAC ~$100K; gene therapy not for HbSC mainly). $140,000.
registrational_endpoint: Annualized VOC rate reduction vs. placebo; stroke-free survival
btd_threshold_vs_soc: ≥45% reduction in VOC rate vs. placebo; no stroke recurrence in ≥90% at 2 years

symptoms: Mild anemia, usually asymptomatic; thalassemia-like if compound heterozygous
treatment_goal: Manage anemia in severe compound heterozygous (HbE/beta-thal); supportive for milder forms
first_line_soc: HbE trait/HbEE: no pharmacologic treatment needed usually. HbE/beta-thal: hydroxyurea to increase HbF; transfusion for severe. Luspatercept (Reblozyl) for transfusion-dependent beta-thal including HbE/beta-thal.
treatment_algorithm: HbEE: folic acid, supportive. HbE/beta-thal (moderate-severe): transfusions, iron chelation (deferasirox/Exjade). Hydroxyurea (HbF inducer). Luspatercept (Reblozyl, TGF-β ligand trap, FDA 2020 for non-TD thal; 2024 expanded). Gene therapy/stem cell transplant for severe. Mitapivat investigational.
us_prevalence: ~30,000–50,000 US patients; most Southeast Asian-American ancestry. HbE/beta-thal: ~5,000 with significant disease.
patients_needing_tx: ~8,000 patients with clinically significant HbE disease requiring pharmacologic treatment.
price: $200,000–$300,000/year (luspatercept WAC ~$250K). Gene therapy: $2–3M one-time. $250,000 chronic.
registrational_endpoint: Transfusion burden reduction (≥33% reduction in RBC units transfused); Hgb increase ≥1 g/dL
btd_threshold_vs_soc: ≥50% of patients achieving transfusion independence ≥12 weeks; Hgb rise ≥1.5 g/dL sustained

symptoms: Sickle-like pain crises, anemia severity varies, splenomegaly
treatment_goal: Reduce pain crises, transfusion burden, prevent organ damage
first_line_soc: Hydroxyurea (HbF inducer); transfusion support for severe cases. Management largely as sickle cell disease.
treatment_algorithm: Hydroxyurea (first-line for moderate-severe). L-glutamine (Endari, FDA 2017). Crizanlizumab (Adakveo). Luspatercept for transfusion-dependent component. Gene therapy: betibeglogene (Zynteglo, FDA 2022 for beta-thal including HbS/beta-thal). Allo-SCT if matched donor. Mitapivat (RISE UP trial ongoing for SCD/HbS-beta-thal).
us_prevalence: ~10,000–15,000 US patients with HbS/beta-thal; included in broader SCD statistics.
patients_needing_tx: ~10,000 patients requiring pharmacologic management.
price: $150,000–$250,000/year (gene therapy one-time ~$2.8M). Annual disease-modifying drug: $200,000.
registrational_endpoint: Reduction in annualized VOC rate; transfusion independence (for gene therapy); Hgb increase
btd_threshold_vs_soc: ≥50% VOC reduction vs. placebo; transfusion independence ≥12 months post gene therapy in ≥70%

symptoms: Mild anemia, splenomegaly, fatigue (often mild/asymptomatic)
treatment_goal: Manage anemia complications; no specific treatment usually needed
first_line_soc: Supportive care (folic acid supplementation). No pharmacologic first-line for homozygous HbCC. Management similar to other hemoglobinopathies.
treatment_algorithm: Homozygous HbCC: mild-moderate hemolytic anemia; manage with folic acid, hydration. Splenectomy rarely needed. No disease-modifying drug approved. HbSC (see Hemoglobin S-C): managed as sickle cell variant. Supportive: avoid dehydration, hypoxia.
us_prevalence: ~70,000–90,000 US patients with HbCC; more common in West African ancestry populations.
patients_needing_tx: ~5,000 patients with clinically significant anemia requiring pharmacologic intervention.
price: $100,000–$200,000/year for novel hemoglobin modifier. $150,000.
registrational_endpoint: Hgb level increase and reduction in vaso-occlusive events
btd_threshold_vs_soc: ≥1.5 g/dL Hgb increase; ≥30% VOC reduction vs. placebo

symptoms: Hemolytic anemia, jaundice, splenomegaly, gallstones, fatigue
treatment_goal: Reduce hemolysis, manage anemia, prevent gallstone complications
first_line_soc: Folic acid supplementation. Splenectomy for moderate-severe HS (definitive). No approved pharmacologic disease-modifying drug.
treatment_algorithm: Supportive: folic acid 1 mg QD. Splenectomy for severe HS (reduces hemolysis). Cholecystectomy if gallstones. Transfusion during aplastic crises. Novel: mitapivat (ACTIVATE-T trial for HS, Phase 2 2023—positive results). IACS-9571 (autophagy inhibitor) in preclinical. Iron chelation if transfusion-dependent.
us_prevalence: HS: ~200,000–300,000 US patients (1/2,000–5,000). HE: less severe, ~50,000. Total moderate-severe: ~50,000.
patients_needing_tx: ~20,000 moderate-severe HS patients suitable for pharmacologic disease modification.
price: $300,000–$400,000/year for mitapivat or successor (orphan pricing). $350,000.
registrational_endpoint: Hgb increase ≥1.5 g/dL without transfusion increase at 24 weeks
btd_threshold_vs_soc: ≥50% achieving Hgb ≥1.5 g/dL rise; reduction in hemolytic biomarkers (LDH, bilirubin) ≥30%

symptoms: Dark morning urine, fatigue, thrombosis, abdominal pain, anemia
treatment_goal: Prevent hemolysis and thrombosis; achieve transfusion independence
first_line_soc: Eculizumab (Soliris, C5 complement inhibitor) or ravulizumab (Ultomiris, long-acting C5 inhibitor) — both FDA approved.
treatment_algorithm: C5 inhibitors: eculizumab q2w or ravulizumab q8w (both require meningococcal vaccination). Proximal complement inhibitors (2022–2025): iptacopan (Fabhalta, Factor B inhibitor, FDA 2023) for residual anemia on C5 inhibition; danicopan (Factor D inhibitor, FDA 2024, add-on); crovalimab (Piasky, C5 mAb, FDA 2023) SC formulation. Bone marrow failure: supportive with eltrombopag ± immunosuppression. Allo-SCT for refractory with marrow failure.
us_prevalence: ~10,000–12,000 US patients with clinically significant PNH.
patients_needing_tx: ~8,000 patients requiring complement inhibitor therapy.
registrational_endpoint: Hemoglobin rise ≥2 g/dL; transfusion avoidance rate; lactate dehydrogenase normalization
btd_threshold_vs_soc: TI rate ≥50% vs. <20% on C5 inhibitor alone; Hgb rise ≥2 g/dL in ≥60%

symptoms: Chronic non-spherocytic hemolytic anemia, jaundice, gallstones, fatigue
treatment_goal: Reduce hemolysis, manage anemia, prevent complications
first_line_soc: Supportive: folic acid supplementation, red cell transfusion as needed. Splenectomy for severe PK deficiency. No first-line pharmacologic drug for most.
treatment_algorithm: Pyruvate kinase deficiency (most common): mitapivat (Pyrukynd, PKA activator, FDA 2022) for non-transfusion-dependent adults. Transfusion support. Folic acid. Iron chelation if transfusion-dependent. Splenectomy. Hematopoietic SCT for severe pediatric cases. Gene therapy clinical trials (RUBY trial, AAV-pyruvate kinase).
us_prevalence: PK deficiency ~3,000–5,000 US patients; other glycolytic defects rare (<1,000 combined).
patients_needing_tx: ~2,000–3,000 patients with clinically significant PK deficiency suitable for mitapivat.
price: $350,000–$400,000/year (mitapivat WAC ~$375K). Orphan pricing.
registrational_endpoint: Hgb increase ≥1.5 g/dL from baseline (sustained) without transfusion increase
btd_threshold_vs_soc: ≥40% of patients achieving Hgb rise ≥1.5 g/dL vs. <5% placebo (ACTIVATE trial)

symptoms: Episodic hemolytic anemia triggered by oxidant stress, jaundice, dark urine
treatment_goal: Prevent hemolytic crises by avoiding triggers; support during crises
first_line_soc: Avoid oxidant triggers (primaquine, dapsone, fava beans). Supportive: hydration, transfusion during severe hemolysis. No approved pharmacologic prevention.
treatment_algorithm: Acute crisis: red cell transfusion if Hgb <7 g/dL; IV fluids. Trigger avoidance education. Novel: SBI-183 (G6PD enzyme stabilizer) and tafenoquine interaction avoidance strategies. Investigational: small molecule G6PD enhancers. No FDA-approved pharmacologic preventive therapy.
us_prevalence: ~400,000 diagnosed G6PD-deficient individuals in US (true prevalence ~1 million; predominantly African-American males).
patients_needing_tx: ~50,000 patients/year with significant hemolytic episodes; chronic treatment candidates: ~20,000.
price: $30,000–$60,000/year for novel G6PD stabilizer/activator. $45,000.
registrational_endpoint: Rate of hemolytic episodes and degree of Hgb reduction during oxidant challenge vs. placebo
btd_threshold_vs_soc: ≥50% reduction in hemolytic episode rate; no Hgb drop >3 g/dL during trigger exposure in ≥70%

symptoms: Depends on organ involvement: pulmonary (hemoptysis, dyspnea) or systemic iron deposition
treatment_goal: Remove excess iron, prevent organ damage from iron overload
first_line_soc: Iron chelation: deferoxamine (Desferal, IV/SC), deferasirox (Exjade/Jadenu, oral), or deferiprone (Ferriprox, oral) based on etiology.
treatment_algorithm: Chelation: deferoxamine (parenteral) or deferasirox (oral, preferred for convenience, FDA 2005). Deferiprone (Ferriprox, FDA 2011) particularly for cardiac iron. Combination chelation for refractory. Phlebotomy for hemochromatosis-related. Pulmonary hemosiderosis: corticosteroids, azathioprine for autoimmune cause. Treat underlying cause.
us_prevalence: ~50,000–100,000 US patients with clinically significant hemosiderosis requiring chelation (includes transfusion-dependent anemias).
patients_needing_tx: ~60,000 patients requiring pharmacologic iron chelation.
price: $20,000–$40,000/year (deferasirox ~$35K; deferiprone ~$25K). Novel once-monthly injectable: $50,000.
registrational_endpoint: Serum ferritin reduction ≥30% from baseline and liver iron concentration (LIC) normalization at 52 weeks
btd_threshold_vs_soc: LIC reduction to <5 mg Fe/g dw in ≥60% vs. <30% with standard oral chelation

symptoms: Liver disease, skin bronzing, joint pain, fatigue, cardiac arrhythmias
treatment_goal: Reduce iron burden; prevent end-organ damage (liver, heart, endocrine)
first_line_soc: Iron chelation: deferasirox (Exjade/Jadenu, oral) or deferoxamine (IV/SC). Deferiprone (Ferriprox) as alternative, especially for cardiac iron.
treatment_algorithm: Oral deferasirox 14–28 mg/kg/day (FDA approved for transfusion-dependent iron overload). Deferoxamine SC infusion for patients intolerant of deferasirox. Deferiprone for cardiac iron preferential distribution (chelates cardiac iron best). Combination chelation (deferoxamine + deferiprone) for severe overload. Monitor ferritin, LIC (liver iron concentration by MRI). 2024: luspatercept reduces transfusion need (thereby reducing iron intake).
us_prevalence: ~50,000–80,000 US patients with clinically significant secondary iron overload (from transfusion-dependent disorders).
patients_needing_tx: ~40,000 patients on active iron chelation therapy.
price: $30,000–$60,000/year (deferasirox ~$40K; deferiprone ~$35K). $45,000.
registrational_endpoint: Liver iron concentration (LIC) reduction ≥30%; serum ferritin normalization (<1000 ng/mL)
btd_threshold_vs_soc: LIC reduction ≥50% at 12 months vs. ≥30% deferasirox; cardiac T2* improvement

symptoms: Fatigue, joint pain, liver disease, bronze skin, diabetes, heart failure
treatment_goal: Normalize ferritin/transferrin saturation, prevent organ damage via iron removal
first_line_soc: Therapeutic phlebotomy (weekly 450–500 mL until ferritin <50 ng/mL); maintenance phlebotomy 2–4× yearly. Iron chelation if phlebotomy contraindicated.
treatment_algorithm: Phlebotomy (definitive, standard of care—inexpensive, effective). Chelation: deferasirox or deferoxamine only if phlebotomy contraindicated (severe anemia, cardiovascular disease). Diet: limit iron-rich foods, avoid vitamin C supplementation. Novel: minihepcidins (hepcidin agonists) in development. No approved pharmacologic drug for HH specifically.
us_prevalence: ~1 million US patients with HFE mutations; ~200,000–300,000 with iron overload requiring treatment.
patients_needing_tx: ~150,000 patients with significant iron overload requiring intervention (mostly phlebotomy); ~5,000–10,000 needing pharmacologic chelation.
price: $30,000–$60,000/year for novel minihepcidin/hepcidin analog. $45,000.
registrational_endpoint: Ferritin reduction to <50 ng/mL without phlebotomy over 52 weeks
btd_threshold_vs_soc: Ferritin normalization <50 ng/mL in ≥70% vs. <20% with placebo

symptoms: Cytopenias, enlarged spleen, abdominal fullness, fatigue
treatment_goal: Treat underlying cause, manage cytopenias, avoid life-threatening infections
