first_line_soc: Treat underlying cause (e.g., cirrhosis, hairy cell leukemia). Splenectomy for severe symptomatic hypersplenism. No universal pharmacologic first-line.
treatment_algorithm: Treat underlying disease (portal hypertension: TIPS procedure, diuretics; hairy cell leukemia: cladribine; malaria: antimalarials). Splenic artery embolization as splenectomy alternative. TPO receptor agonists (eltrombopag) for severe thrombocytopenia from hypersplenism (pre-procedure). Splenectomy definitive for intractable cases.
us_prevalence: Secondary to liver disease: ~200,000 US patients with clinically significant hypersplenism.
patients_needing_tx: ~50,000 patients requiring pharmacologic management of cytopenias.
price: $50,000–$100,000/year for novel splenic-targeting or TPO-RA. $75,000.
registrational_endpoint: Platelet count normalization (>100K/μL) and reduction in bleeding events at 24 weeks
btd_threshold_vs_soc: ≥60% achieving platelet >100K vs. <20% with supportive care only

symptoms: Fever, chills, hemolysis, hypotension, respiratory distress after transfusion
treatment_goal: Stop reaction, provide supportive care, prevent recurrence
first_line_soc: Stop transfusion immediately; supportive care (IV fluids, antihistamines, corticosteroids for allergic; diuretics for TACO). No standard pharmacologic prevention beyond leukoreduction.
treatment_algorithm: Hemolytic reactions: IV fluids, forced diuresis, treat DIC. TRALI: supportive ventilation. TACO: diuretics. Febrile non-hemolytic: antipyretics, leukoreduced blood. Allergic: antihistamines, epinephrine for anaphylaxis. Prevention: pre-medication with steroids/diphenhydramine. No FDA-approved specific pharmacologic prevention drug.
us_prevalence: ~30 million transfusions/year; ~1–2% have some reaction (~300,000–600,000 reactions/year). Serious TRALI/hemolytic: ~10,000/year.
patients_needing_tx: ~50,000–100,000 patients/year with clinically significant reactions requiring active pharmacologic management.
price: $5,000–$15,000/course for acute management drugs or prevention strategy. $10,000.
registrational_endpoint: Incidence of transfusion reactions (TRALI, hemolysis) in randomized prevention trial
btd_threshold_vs_soc: ≥50% reduction in serious transfusion reaction rate vs. standard leukoreduction

symptoms: Fatigue, edema, heart failure symptoms, neuropathy, enlarged tongue
treatment_goal: Reduce amyloid production, reverse organ damage, improve survival
first_line_soc: AL amyloidosis: bortezomib (Velcade) + cyclophosphamide + dexamethasone (VCd); daratumumab (Darzalex) added upfront. ATTR: tafamidis (Vyndamax/Vyndaqel) for ATTR-CM.
treatment_algorithm: AL: VCd ± daratumumab (Dara-VCd, preferred 2024); autologous SCT for eligible. ATTR-CM: tafamidis 61 mg QD (FDA 2019); acoramidis (Attruby) FDA approved Nov 2024. ATTR-PN: patisiran (Onpattro, siRNA), inotersen (Tegsedi, ASO), vutrisiran (Amvuttra). SAP depletion: doxycycline+TUDCA experimental. Emerging: NTLA-2001 (CRISPR), small molecules (AG10/acoramidis).
us_prevalence: ATTR-CM: ~100,000–120,000 diagnosed (markedly underdiagnosed, true est. 300,000+); AL amyloid ~4,000 new cases/year, ~20,000 prevalent. Total treated ~150,000.
patients_needing_tx: ATTR-CM: ~80,000 on tafamidis/acoramidis or eligible. AL: ~12,000 active therapy. Total: ~92,000.
price: $225,000–$300,000/year (tafamidis WAC ~$225K; siRNA ~$450K). Range $275,000/year.
peak_sales: 30% share of ATTR-CM market: ~$7.6B peak (consistent with tafamidis ~$5B+ actual)
registrational_endpoint: Hierarchical composite of all-cause mortality and CV hospitalizations (ATTR-CM); neuropathy impairment score (ATTR-PN)
btd_threshold_vs_soc: Win ratio >1.5 on hierarchical composite vs. placebo, or NIS ≥5-point improvement vs. worsening SoC

symptoms: Easy bruising, petechiae, nosebleeds, gum bleeding, low platelet count
treatment_goal: Maintain safe platelet count (>30K), prevent serious bleeding, achieve remission
first_line_soc: Corticosteroids: prednisone 1 mg/kg/day or dexamethasone 40 mg/day ×4 days. IVIG for rapid response. Newly approved 1L: rilzabrutinib (BTKi, FDA 2024), fostamatinib (Tavalisse, SYK inhibitor).
treatment_algorithm: Acute/newly diagnosed: corticosteroids ± IVIG. Chronic/refractory: TPO receptor agonists (eltrombopag/Promacta, romiplostim/Nplate, avatrombopag/Doptelet). Rituximab for corticosteroid-refractory. Fostamatinib (Tavalisse, SYK-i, FDA 2018). Rilzabrutinib (Gazyva, BTKi, FDA 2024). Splenectomy (declining). Danazol, dapsone for refractory. 2024-25: sutimlimab trials in ITP.
us_prevalence: ~200,000 US patients; ~25,000–30,000 new diagnoses/year.
patients_needing_tx: ~120,000 patients on chronic pharmacologic therapy.
price: $80,000–$150,000/year (eltrombopag ~$100K; romiplostim ~$120K; rilzabrutinib ~$130K). $120,000.
registrational_endpoint: Platelet response (≥50K/μL on ≥2 of last 4 visits) at 24 weeks; durable platelet response without rescue
btd_threshold_vs_soc: Durable platelet response >50% at 24 weeks vs. <20% placebo; bleeding event reduction ≥40%

symptoms: Low platelets, splenomegaly, mild bleeding tendency, fatigue
treatment_goal: Treat underlying splenomegaly cause; splenectomy if severe/refractory
first_line_soc: Treat underlying cause (portal hypertension, myelofibrosis, lymphoma). Splenectomy or splenic embolization for severe, symptom-causing thrombocytopenia. TPO-receptor agonists (eltrombopag/avatrombopag) in selected cases.
treatment_algorithm: Address etiology: ruxolitinib for myelofibrosis-related; treat portal hypertension with TIPS or non-selective beta-blockers. Splenectomy if platelet <50K and symptomatic. TPO-RAs: avatrombopag (Doptelet) or eltrombopag (Promacta) for cirrhosis-associated pre-procedurally. HVPG-lowering drugs (propranolol) reduce splenic flow.
us_prevalence: ~100,000 US patients with clinically significant splenic sequestration thrombocytopenia.
patients_needing_tx: ~30,000 patients requiring pharmacologic therapy (TPO-RAs + underlying disease treatment).
registrational_endpoint: Platelet count increase to ≥50×10⁹/L; reduction in bleeding events; spleen volume reduction
btd_threshold_vs_soc: Platelet ≥50K in ≥60% vs. <30% with supportive care; sustained ≥8 weeks

symptoms: Easy bruising, prolonged bleeding, nosebleeds, heavy periods
treatment_goal: Correct underlying cause, stop bleeding episodes
first_line_soc: None (treat underlying cause; avoid offending drugs). Desmopressin (DDAVP) or platelet transfusion for acute bleeding.
treatment_algorithm: Remove causative drugs (NSAIDs, aspirin, clopidogrel). For bleeding: desmopressin 0.3 mcg/kg IV; platelet transfusion in severe/surgical cases. Antifibrinolytics (tranexamic acid) as adjunct. Underlying disease (uremia) treated with dialysis or EPO.
us_prevalence: Extremely common; millions affected transiently (drug-induced). Uremic platelet dysfunction affects ~500,000 dialysis patients. No precise registry count.
patients_needing_tx: ~200,000 patients/year need active pharmacologic intervention for clinically significant bleeding beyond drug cessation.
price: $8,000–$15,000/year; bleeding disorder specialty niche, limited chronicity.
peak_sales: 30% share of treatable subset: $690M peak annual sales
registrational_endpoint: Time to hemostasis (surgical model) or reduction in bleeding events (annualized bleeding rate, ABR) vs. placebo
btd_threshold_vs_soc: ≥30% reduction in ABR or ≥40% reduction in time to hemostasis vs. SoC

symptoms: Thrombocytopenia, microangiopathic hemolytic anemia, neurological symptoms, fever
treatment_goal: Rapidly restore ADAMTS13 activity; prevent death and relapse
first_line_soc: Therapeutic plasma exchange (TPE) + corticosteroids (prednisone). Caplacizumab (Cablivi, anti-VWF nanobody, FDA 2019) added to TPE + immunosuppression as standard.
treatment_algorithm: Acute iTTP: TPE daily + prednisone 1 mg/kg/day + caplacizumab 11 mg SC daily. Rituximab 375 mg/m² weekly × 4 (reduces relapse). Refractory/relapsed: rituximab + cyclosporine or cyclophosphamide. Recombinant ADAMTS13 (SHP655) in clinical trials 2024. Congenital TTP: FFP infusions or ADAMTS13 replacement therapy. Splenectomy historical (rarely used now).
us_prevalence: ~5,000–7,000 US episodes/year; ~3,000 patients with recurrent iTTP on long-term management.
patients_needing_tx: ~5,000 acute episodes/year requiring pharmacologic therapy.
price: $200,000–$400,000/episode for acute management (caplacizumab ~$250K/episode). $300,000 acute; $150,000/year maintenance.
registrational_endpoint: Time to platelet count normalization (≥150×10⁹/L); exacerbation rate; recurrence rate at 1 year
btd_threshold_vs_soc: Platelet normalization ≥2 days faster than TPE alone; recurrence rate <10% vs. ~30% TPE+immunosuppression at 1 year

symptoms: Severe bleeding from cuts, mucosal bleeding, heavy periods, bruising
treatment_goal: Prevent and treat bleeding episodes, manage perioperative hemostasis
first_line_soc: Desmopressin (DDAVP) for milder disorders. Platelet transfusion for severe bleeding (Glanzmann thrombasthenia). Antifibrinolytics (tranexamic acid).
treatment_algorithm: DDAVP for VWD-related and some storage pool disorders. Platelet transfusion (risk of alloimmunization in GT). Recombinant FVIIa (NovoSeven) off-label for GT with antibodies. Antifibrinolytics (tranexamic acid, aminocaproic acid) for mucosal bleeding. Hormonal management for menorrhagia. Gene therapy in clinical trials for GT. No FDA-approved disease-specific pharmacologic agent.
us_prevalence: ~50,000 US patients with inherited platelet function disorders; Glanzmann: ~500–1,000.
patients_needing_tx: ~10,000 patients requiring regular pharmacologic hemostatic management.
price: $100,000–$200,000/year for novel gene therapy or bispecific hemostatic. $150,000.
registrational_endpoint: Annualized bleeding rate (ABR) reduction and surgical hemostasis success rate
btd_threshold_vs_soc: ABR ≤2 in ≥60% of patients; surgical hemostasis success >90% vs. <50% historical

symptoms: Microangiopathic anemia, thrombocytopenia, acute kidney injury, bloody diarrhea
treatment_goal: Prevent end-stage renal disease; resolve thrombotic microangiopathy
first_line_soc: Atypical HUS (aHUS): eculizumab (Soliris, C5 complement inhibitor) or ravulizumab (Ultomiris) first-line for aHUS. Typical STEC-HUS: supportive only (no antibiotics, no steroids).
treatment_algorithm: aHUS: eculizumab IV (FDA 2011) or ravulizumab (FDA 2019, longer dosing interval). Genetic complement testing to guide duration. Typical STEC-HUS: IV fluids, dialysis, transfusions—no specific drug. Plasma exchange for TTP-like presentations. Iptacopan (factor B inhibitor) emerging for aHUS. Prophylactic vaccination (meningococcal) required with eculizumab.
us_prevalence: STEC-HUS: ~3,000 cases/year (mostly children). aHUS: ~1,500–2,000 prevalent US patients.
patients_needing_tx: ~1,500 aHUS patients on complement inhibitor therapy; ~3,000/year STEC-HUS need supportive drug management.
price: $700,000–$900,000/year (eculizumab WAC ~$700K; ravulizumab ~$750K). Novel oral: $200,000.
peak_sales: 30% novel oral factor B share for aHUS: ~$338M peak
registrational_endpoint: TMA response (platelet normalization, LDH normalization, creatinine improvement) at 26 weeks
btd_threshold_vs_soc: TMA response rate >60% at 26 weeks; eGFR preservation vs. decline in placebo

symptoms: Often asymptomatic; underlying condition symptoms (infection, inflammation, iron deficiency)
treatment_goal: Treat underlying cause; no direct thrombocytosis treatment required
first_line_soc: Treat underlying cause (infection, inflammation, iron deficiency). No cytoreductive therapy needed as thrombotic risk is low.
treatment_algorithm: No cytoreductive therapy for reactive thrombocytosis (unlike ET). Address underlying: antibiotics for infection, anti-inflammatory for IBD, iron for deficiency, etc. Aspirin only if symptomatic (vasomotor symptoms). Differentiate from ET/PMF via JAK2/CALR/MPL testing.
us_prevalence: Very common; ~2–5 million US patients annually with reactive thrombocytosis (platelet >450K secondary to other cause).
patients_needing_tx: ~50,000 patients misdiagnosed or requiring treatment evaluation; minimal direct pharmacotherapy.
price: N/A — treat underlying cause. Hypothetical diagnostic tool: $1,000–$3,000.
tam_gross: N/A for direct treatment. Diagnostic market: $50M
peak_sales: Minimal direct drug sales; indirect value in differential diagnosis tools: $10M
registrational_endpoint: N/A (treat underlying cause; platelet normalization is secondary outcome)
btd_threshold_vs_soc: N/A — reactive; treat underlying cause. For hypothetical agent: platelet normalization (<450K) in ≥70% at 8 weeks vs. observation alone

symptoms: Severe sunlight-induced skin burning, pain, swelling without blistering
treatment_goal: Reduce phototoxic attacks, prevent liver disease progression
first_line_soc: Afamelanotide (Scenesse, α-MSH analog, subcutaneous implant) 16 mg every 60 days; FDA approved 2019. Oral beta-carotene (partial, limited efficacy).
treatment_algorithm: Afamelanotide (MC1R agonist): increases melanin, reduces EPP attacks. Supportive: light avoidance, barrier creams, oral beta-carotene. Cholestyramine for liver disease. Liver transplant for EPP-related cirrhosis. XLP: same afamelanotide approach. Gene therapy (AAV-FECH) in clinical trials.
us_prevalence: ~5,000–7,000 US patients with EPP/XLP combined.
patients_needing_tx: ~3,000–4,000 patients with moderate-severe symptoms eligible for afamelanotide.
price: $300,000–$400,000/year (afamelanotide WAC ~$340K/year). Novel/gene therapy: $500,000+.
registrational_endpoint: Annualized phototoxic attack rate and direct sunlight exposure time vs. placebo
btd_threshold_vs_soc: ≥50% reduction in phototoxic attacks; ≥60-minute increase in sunlight tolerance

symptoms: Severe abdominal pain, nausea, confusion, weakness, dark urine
treatment_goal: Abort acute attacks, prevent recurrent attacks
first_line_soc: Hemin (Panhematin) IV infusion 3–4 mg/kg/day × 4 days for acute attacks; IV glucose loading for mild attacks.
treatment_algorithm: Acute attack: IV hemin (Panhematin/Normosang) reduces ALA/PBG. Preventive for recurrent AHP: givosiran (Givlaari), RNA interference, reduces hepatic ALAS1; approved 2019. High-carbohydrate diet, avoid triggers. Liver transplant for refractory AHP.
us_prevalence: ~3,000–5,000 symptomatic AHP patients in US (NORD/EPNET data); AIP most common subtype.
patients_needing_tx: ~1,500–2,500 patients with recurrent attacks need chronic preventive therapy (givosiran-eligible).
price: $575,000/year (givosiran WAC ~$575K); ultra-rare orphan. Rationale: severe attack prevention.
peak_sales: 60% givosiran/novel share in recurrent patients: ~$690M peak
registrational_endpoint: Annualized attack rate (AAR) reduction vs. placebo
btd_threshold_vs_soc: ≥70% reduction in AAR vs. placebo (givosiran achieved ~74%)

symptoms: Skin blistering on sun-exposed areas, fragile skin, hyperpigmentation, hypertrichosis
treatment_goal: Reduce porphyrin levels; heal skin lesions; achieve remission
first_line_soc: Phlebotomy (repeated to reduce iron stores). Low-dose hydroxychloroquine or chloroquine (4-aminoquinolines) as alternative when phlebotomy not tolerated.
treatment_algorithm: Identify and remove triggers (alcohol, estrogens, iron, HCV, HFE mutations). Phlebotomy 450 mL every 1–2 weeks to reduce ferritin <20 ng/mL. If phlebotomy not feasible: hydroxychloroquine 100 mg twice weekly or chloroquine 125 mg twice weekly. Treat HCV if coexistent (SOF-based DAAs lead to remission). Iron chelation (deferasirox) investigational.
us_prevalence: ~15,000–20,000 US patients with PCT (commonest porphyria).
patients_needing_tx: ~10,000 patients requiring pharmacologic or phlebotomy-based treatment.
registrational_endpoint: Normalization of plasma/urine porphyrin levels; complete skin lesion healing rate
btd_threshold_vs_soc: Urinary porphyrin normalization ≥70% at 6 months vs. ~50% with hydroxychloroquine

symptoms: Venous thromboembolism (DVT, PE), recurrent miscarriage, cerebral sinus thrombosis
treatment_goal: Prevent first/recurrent VTE; anticoagulate during high-risk periods
first_line_soc: Long-term anticoagulation (rivaroxaban/apixaban or warfarin) after first VTE event. Prophylactic LMWH during high-risk periods (surgery, pregnancy, immobility) for known carriers.
treatment_algorithm: Asymptomatic heterozygous: no therapy unless provoked. First VTE: 3–6 months DOAC. Recurrent VTE/homozygous: indefinite anticoagulation. Pregnancy: LMWH throughout. Combined defects (with FVL): higher thrombotic risk → indefinite anticoagulation. RNAi therapies targeting prothrombin mRNA (fitusiran, inclisiran-class) in preclinical study.
us_prevalence: ~2 million US carriers; ~200,000 with symptomatic VTE attributable to FII 20210.
patients_needing_tx: ~150,000 patients on active pharmacologic anticoagulation.
price: $8,000–$15,000/year (DOACs). Novel gene-silencing: $50,000–$100,000/year. $75,000.
registrational_endpoint: Recurrent VTE rate over 12–24 months; prothrombin activity level at target
btd_threshold_vs_soc: Annual VTE recurrence <2% vs. ~5% on standard anticoagulation; superior safety profile

disease: Elevated Factor VIII, IX, and XI Levels
symptoms: Recurrent DVT, pulmonary embolism (often asymptomatic until thrombosis)
treatment_goal: Prevent thrombosis with anticoagulation in high-risk settings
first_line_soc: Anticoagulation for acute thrombosis: LMWH bridging → warfarin or DOAC (rivaroxaban, apixaban) long-term.
treatment_algorithm: No specific pharmacologic treatment for elevated factor levels per se. Management = anticoagulation when thrombosis occurs. DOACs preferred over warfarin for most. Duration determined by risk stratification. No approved drug for prevention in factor-elevated carriers without thrombosis.
us_prevalence: Elevated FVIII: ~10% of population; clinically significant thrombotic risk: ~500,000. FXI elevated: ~1% with thrombosis risk.
patients_needing_tx: ~100,000 patients with established thrombosis and elevated coagulation factor levels requiring ongoing anticoagulation.
price: $15,000–$30,000/year for DOAC. Novel factor-specific inhibitor: $50,000.
registrational_endpoint: Recurrent VTE rate at 12 months vs. standard anticoagulation
btd_threshold_vs_soc: ≥40% reduction in recurrent VTE vs. DOAC alone in high-FVIII carriers

symptoms: Recurrent DVT, pulmonary embolism, purpura fulminans in newborns
treatment_goal: Prevent thrombotic events; anticoagulate acutely; manage purpura fulminans
first_line_soc: Long-term anticoagulation: warfarin or DOACs (rivaroxaban/apixaban). Acute thrombosis: LMWH bridge to warfarin. Purpura fulminans: fresh frozen plasma or Protein C concentrate (Ceprotin).
treatment_algorithm: Heterozygous deficiency: anticoagulation for thrombotic events (warfarin INR 2–3 or DOAC). Homozygous/severe: Protein C concentrate (Ceprotin, FDA approved) for acute episodes. Long-term warfarin at high INR (3–4) or recombinant Protein C. Gene therapy in development. Pregnancy: LMWH throughout.
us_prevalence: ~100,000–200,000 US patients with heterozygous Protein C deficiency; severe homozygous: ~100–200 patients.
patients_needing_tx: ~50,000 patients with symptomatic thrombophilia requiring pharmacotherapy.
price: $50,000–$150,000/year (Ceprotin ~$300K/year for severe; DOACs ~$8K). Moderate case: $80,000.
peak_sales: 20% novel gene therapy share in severe: ~$800M peak
registrational_endpoint: VTE recurrence rate; time to first recurrent thrombotic event; Protein C activity level normalization
btd_threshold_vs_soc: VTE recurrence rate <5%/year vs. ~15%/year off anticoagulation; Protein C activity >50% in ≥80%

symptoms: Recurrent venous thromboembolism, DVT, pulmonary embolism
treatment_goal: Prevent recurrent VTE with long-term anticoagulation
first_line_soc: Long-term anticoagulation: warfarin (target INR 2–3) or DOACs. Acute VTE: LMWH or unfractionated heparin bridge.
treatment_algorithm: Similar to Protein C deficiency. Warfarin or DOAC (rivaroxaban, apixaban) for recurrent VTE prevention. No Protein S replacement product commercially available. No specific pharmacologic therapy beyond anticoagulation. Pregnancy: LMWH throughout. Gene therapy in preclinical development.
us_prevalence: ~200,000 US patients with Protein S deficiency; ~50,000 with symptomatic VTE episodes.
patients_needing_tx: ~40,000 patients on long-term pharmacologic anticoagulation.
price: $50,000–$150,000/year for novel anticoagulant or replacement therapy. $100,000.
registrational_endpoint: Recurrent VTE rate; time to first recurrent event over 12–24 months
btd_threshold_vs_soc: Annual VTE recurrence rate <3% vs. ~10–15% without anticoagulation

disease: Factor V Resistance to Activated Protein C (APC)
symptoms: Recurrent DVT, pulmonary embolism, pregnancy complications (often asymptomatic)
treatment_goal: Prevent thrombosis; anticoagulate during high-risk periods
first_line_soc: Anticoagulation for thrombotic events: apixaban (Eliquis) or rivaroxaban (Xarelto) preferred; warfarin alternative.
treatment_algorithm: No drug targeting Factor V Leiden mutation directly. Anticoagulation: DOAC (apixaban, rivaroxaban) or warfarin for established VTE. Prophylactic LMWH during high-risk periods (surgery, pregnancy). Heterozygous FVL: treat as standard VTE; homozygous: indefinite anticoagulation. Genetic counseling.
us_prevalence: Most common thrombophilia: ~5% Caucasian population (~16 million carriers); ~100,000 with symptomatic thrombosis attributable to FVL.
patients_needing_tx: ~80,000 FVL patients on long-term anticoagulation after first VTE.
price: $15,000–$25,000/year DOAC. Novel FVL-specific agent: $40,000.
registrational_endpoint: Recurrent VTE rate at 12 months; net clinical benefit (VTE prevention minus major bleeding)
btd_threshold_vs_soc: HR <0.60 for recurrent VTE vs. DOAC alone in homozygous FVL patients

symptoms: Blood clots, recurrent pregnancy loss, livedo reticularis, headaches
treatment_goal: Prevent recurrent thrombosis and pregnancy loss with anticoagulation
first_line_soc: Warfarin (target INR 2–3 for venous; 3–4 for arterial thrombosis); low-molecular-weight heparin + low-dose aspirin for obstetric APS.
treatment_algorithm: Thrombotic APS: warfarin long-term (VKA preferred; DOACs inferior per TRAPS/EULAR). Obstetric APS: LMWH + aspirin. Hydroxychloroquine added for SLE-APS. Catastrophic APS: anticoagulation + corticosteroids + IVIG/plasma exchange. Novel: rituximab, belimumab for refractory; sirolimus trial. Rivaroxaban failed in high-risk (TRAPS trial).
us_prevalence: ~100,000 diagnosed APS patients in US; broader antiphospholipid antibody positivity ~5 million but asymptomatic majority.
patients_needing_tx: ~80,000 patients with diagnosed thrombotic APS requiring chronic anticoagulation; ~20,000 obstetric APS.
price: $15,000–$30,000/year for novel targeted agent (e.g., anti-complement, BTK inhibitor). $22,000/year est.
registrational_endpoint: Recurrent thrombosis rate (time to first recurrent event) vs. warfarin
btd_threshold_vs_soc: HR <0.60 for recurrent thrombosis vs. warfarin, with non-inferior bleeding

symptoms: Recurrent DVT, pulmonary embolism, clotting in unusual sites
treatment_goal: Prevent life-threatening thrombosis with anticoagulation
first_line_soc: Long-term anticoagulation: warfarin (INR 2–3) or LMWH. Antithrombin concentrate (ATryn, recombinant; Thrombate III) for high-risk periods (surgery, childbirth).
treatment_algorithm: Chronic: warfarin, DOACs (data limited). Perioperative/delivery: AT concentrate (ATryn 250 IU/mL IV; Thrombate III). Gene therapy (fitusiran—anti-AT RNAi, for hemophilia co-use). Monitoring: anti-Xa levels.
us_prevalence: ~30,000–50,000 US patients with hereditary AT deficiency (prevalence 1/2,000–5,000).
patients_needing_tx: ~20,000 symptomatic patients needing chronic anticoagulation; ~5,000/year needing AT concentrate.
price: $50,000–$100,000/year for AT concentrate or gene therapy. $75,000/year est.
registrational_endpoint: Recurrent VTE rate at 6–12 months; AT activity levels as surrogate
btd_threshold_vs_soc: ≥60% reduction in recurrent VTE vs. anticoagulation alone; AT levels normalized >80%

symptoms: Elevated homocysteine, thrombosis risk, cardiovascular events, neuropathy
treatment_goal: Normalize homocysteine levels, reduce thrombosis and cardiovascular risk
first_line_soc: B-vitamin supplementation: folic acid (0.4–5 mg/day) + B6 (pyridoxine) + B12 (cyanocobalamin).
treatment_algorithm: Primary: folic acid + B6 + B12 supplementation (reduces homocysteine levels). CBS-deficient (homocystinuria): pyridoxine-responsive subset gets high-dose B6. Betaine anhydrous (Cystadane, FDA-approved for homocystinuria). MTHFR mutation: methylfolate supplementation. No proven cardiovascular event reduction in large RCTs despite Hcy lowering.
us_prevalence: ~15–20% US population has mild-moderate hyperhomocysteinemia; classical homocystinuria: ~10,000.
patients_needing_tx: ~30,000–50,000 patients with moderate-severe requiring active pharmacologic management (beyond OTC vitamins).
price: $20,000–$40,000/year for novel Hcy-lowering/homocystinuria drug. $30,000.
registrational_endpoint: Plasma homocysteine level normalization (<12 μmol/L) and reduction in thrombotic events
btd_threshold_vs_soc: Homocysteine normalization in ≥70% AND thrombotic event HR <0.70 vs. B-vitamins alone

symptoms: Increased thrombotic risk; associated with stroke and pregnancy complications
treatment_goal: Prevent thrombotic events during high-risk periods; anticoagulate as needed
first_line_soc: Anticoagulation (LMWH or warfarin) in symptomatic patients or during high-risk periods (surgery, pregnancy). No FDA-approved specific therapy.
treatment_algorithm: Clinical significance debated; associated with stroke, VTE, recurrent pregnancy loss. Treat with anticoagulation (LMWH, warfarin, DOACs) per thrombotic event. LMWH during pregnancy for those with recurrent pregnancy loss. No replacement protein available.
us_prevalence: Prevalence uncertain; estimated ~500,000 US carriers of low Protein Z; clinically significant deficiency: ~50,000.
patients_needing_tx: ~10,000 patients with symptomatic Protein Z-related thrombophilia needing pharmacologic therapy.
registrational_endpoint: VTE recurrence rate over 24 months; Protein Z activity normalization
btd_threshold_vs_soc: Annual VTE rate <5% vs. ~10% placebo in high-risk Protein Z-deficient cohort

symptoms: Easy bruising on forearms and hands in elderly, thin fragile skin, petechiae
treatment_goal: Reassure; minimize trauma; no pharmacologic treatment typically needed
first_line_soc: None. Benign cosmetic condition; sun protection, moisturizers, vitamin C.
treatment_algorithm: No pharmacologic SoC. Topical tretinoin (off-label) may improve skin integrity by reducing dermal atrophy. Vitamin C supplementation theoretically beneficial. Avoidance of aspirin/NSAIDs when possible. No established drug therapy — condition is self-limited to quality-of-life impact.
us_prevalence: Extremely common in elderly (>70); affects ~50–70% of elderly adults = ~30–40 million US individuals.
patients_needing_tx: ~500,000 patients seeking medical evaluation; minimal pharmacologic intervention.
price: $5,000–$15,000/year for a topical vascular stabilizing agent. $10,000.
registrational_endpoint: Bruise incidence score reduction; skin fragility PRO at 12 weeks
btd_threshold_vs_soc: ≥40% reduction in bruise incidence vs. placebo over 12 weeks

symptoms: Palpable purpura, skin lesions, hyperviscosity symptoms, fatigue
treatment_goal: Reduce abnormal protein levels, resolve vascular inflammation
first_line_soc: Treat underlying plasma cell or lymphoid disorder (myeloma therapy, rituximab). Plasmapheresis for hyperviscosity.
treatment_algorithm: Address underlying dysproteinemia (myeloma: bortezomib-based; WM: rituximab+BTKi; cryoglobulinemia: rituximab ± antivirals for HCV). Plasmapheresis for acute hyperviscosity. Colchicine for hypersensitivity vasculitis. IVIG for refractory.
us_prevalence: ~5,000–15,000 US patients with clinically significant purpura from dysproteinemia.
patients_needing_tx: ~8,000 patients requiring targeted pharmacologic treatment.
price: $100,000–$150,000/year for novel targeted approach. $125,000.
registrational_endpoint: Purpura clearance rate and M-protein reduction at 6 months
btd_threshold_vs_soc: ≥50% purpura clearance AND ≥25% M-protein reduction vs. SoC

symptoms: Easy bruising, especially lower limbs; normal platelet count and coagulation tests
treatment_goal: Reassure patient; no pharmacologic treatment needed in most cases
first_line_soc: None. Benign condition; reassurance and avoidance of aspirin/NSAIDs sufficient.
treatment_algorithm: No pharmacologic SoC. Patient education regarding trigger avoidance (NSAIDs, alcohol). Vitamin C supplementation if deficiency suspected. Evaluation to exclude other causes of easy bruising. No drug intervention indicated in uncomplicated purpura simplex.
us_prevalence: Very common; affects ~10–15% of women; estimated 10–20 million US individuals.
patients_needing_tx: ~50,000 patients referred to hematology requiring diagnostic workup; minimal pharmacologic intervention.
price: $5,000–$15,000/year for a novel vascular stabilizing agent. $10,000.
registrational_endpoint: Bruising frequency score reduction; patient-reported outcome (PRO) measures
btd_threshold_vs_soc: ≥50% reduction in bruise count per month vs. placebo over 12 weeks

symptoms: Recurrent infections, lymph node swelling (or absence), fatigue, oral ulcers
treatment_goal: Treat underlying cause; restore immune function
first_line_soc: Treat underlying etiology (stop offending drug, treat HIV with ART, treat malnutrition). Prophylactic antibiotics/antivirals for CD4 <200/μL. No general pharmacologic treatment for lymphocytopenia per se.
treatment_algorithm: HIV: ART to restore CD4 count. Post-chemo lymphopenia: G-CSF (limited effect on lymphocytes), IVIG if severe hypogammaglobulinemia. Idiopathic CD4 lymphocytopenia: investigational immunomodulators. IL-7 therapy (in trials for post-chemo). No approved specific pharmacologic agent.
us_prevalence: ~500,000 US patients with clinically significant lymphocytopenia requiring management.
patients_needing_tx: ~100,000 patients needing pharmacologic support beyond treating underlying cause.
price: $20,000–$50,000/year for novel lymphocyte-restoring agent (e.g., IL-7, thymosin). $35,000.
registrational_endpoint: Absolute lymphocyte count normalization (>1,000/μL) and reduction in infectious complications
btd_threshold_vs_soc: ALC >1,000/μL in ≥60% at 12 weeks; ≥40% reduction in serious infections vs. placebo

symptoms: Recurrent bacterial infections, fever, mouth sores, fatigue
treatment_goal: Raise neutrophil count; prevent life-threatening infections
first_line_soc: G-CSF (filgrastim/Neupogen or pegfilgrastim/Neulasta) for severe/symptomatic neutropenia. Treat underlying cause.
treatment_algorithm: Chemo-induced: pegfilgrastim (Neulasta) or lipegfilgrastim prophylaxis. Severe congenital neutropenia (Kostmann): G-CSF (filgrastim) long-term; allo-SCT if refractory. Cyclic neutropenia: G-CSF. Drug-induced: discontinue offending agent + G-CSF if severe. Autoimmune: G-CSF ± corticosteroids ± rituximab. GM-CSF (sargramostim) as alternative. 2024: eflapegrastim (Rolvedon) biosimilar-class.
us_prevalence: Chemo-induced neutropenia: ~800,000 episodes/year. Severe congenital/chronic neutropenia: ~5,000 patients.
patients_needing_tx: ~500,000 patients/year receiving G-CSF; ~5,000 chronic patients on long-term G-CSF.
price: $3,000–$8,000/cycle for prophylaxis (chemo-induced). Chronic setting: $40,000–$80,000/year. $60,000/year chronic.
peak_sales: 20% novel agent share in chronic setting: ~$60M peak
registrational_endpoint: Duration of severe neutropenia (ANC <500/μL); febrile neutropenia rate; time to ANC recovery
btd_threshold_vs_soc: ≥1-day reduction in severe neutropenia duration vs. pegfilgrastim, or FN rate <5% vs. ~15% placebo

symptoms: Varies by cause: asthma, skin rashes, GI symptoms, fatigue
treatment_goal: Treat underlying cause, prevent end-organ damage from eosinophil infiltration
first_line_soc: Treat underlying etiology (antiparasitics for parasitic; steroids for idiopathic or EGPA). Corticosteroids for symptomatic idiopathic eosinophilia.
treatment_algorithm: Parasitic: albendazole, ivermectin, or diethylcarbamazine depending on pathogen. EGPA/CSS: methylprednisolone → azathioprine/cyclophosphamide; mepolizumab (Nucala, anti-IL-5) FDA approved EGPA. Idiopathic hypereosinophilia: imatinib if FIP1L1-PDGFRA+; mepolizumab, reslizumab, benralizumab for non-myeloid eosinophilia.
us_prevalence: ~500,000 with clinically significant eosinophilia requiring treatment; EGPA ~6,000–8,000 prevalent.
patients_needing_tx: ~50,000 patients needing pharmacologic treatment for persistent/damaging eosinophilia.
price: $40,000–$80,000/year for anti-IL-5 or anti-IL-5Rα. $60,000.
registrational_endpoint: Annualized relapse rate and eosinophil count normalization for EGPA
btd_threshold_vs_soc: Annualized relapse rate reduction ≥50% vs. placebo/SoC; eosinophil count <500/μL in ≥80%

symptoms: Elevated eosinophils, organ damage (heart, lung, skin, nervous system)
treatment_goal: Normalize eosinophil count, prevent end-organ damage
first_line_soc: Corticosteroids (prednisone 1 mg/kg/day) for non-myeloid HES. FIP1L1-PDGFRA+: imatinib 100 mg/day (highly effective, FDA-recognized).
treatment_algorithm: Myeloid HES (FIP1L1-PDGFRA+): imatinib (complete and durable remission in majority). Lymphocytic/idiopathic HES: prednisone → hydroxyurea, interferon-α. Mepolizumab (anti-IL-5, FDA 2020 for HES without PDGFRA rearrangement). Benralizumab (anti-IL-5Rα) emerging. Second-line: cyclosporine, vincristine (for cardiac HES emergency). End-organ damage: anticoagulation (endomyocardial).
us_prevalence: ~10,000–15,000 US patients; PDGFRA-rearranged subset: ~1,500.
patients_needing_tx: ~8,000 patients requiring pharmacologic therapy.
price: $50,000–$100,000/year (mepolizumab HES WAC ~$70K/year). Novel anti-IL-5/pathway: $80,000.
registrational_endpoint: Reduction in HES flare rate; eosinophil count <1,000/μL sustained at 32 weeks
btd_threshold_vs_soc: ≥50% flare reduction AND eosinophil normalization in ≥70% vs. placebo

symptoms: Susceptibility to infections (especially mycobacterial/fungal), fatigue
treatment_goal: Treat underlying cause; prevent and treat opportunistic infections
first_line_soc: Treat underlying cause (stop offending drug; treat malignancy). Prophylactic antibiotics/antifungals. No specific pharmacologic first-line for monocytopenia per se.
treatment_algorithm: Hairy cell leukemia (common cause): cladribine (highly effective). GATA2 deficiency: SCT potentially curative. Prophylaxis: trimethoprim-sulfamethoxazole (PCP), fluconazole, azithromycin (MAC). G-CSF (limited monocyte effect). GM-CSF (sargramostim) may partially restore monocyte counts. IFN-γ for specific mycobacterial infections.
us_prevalence: Varies by etiology; significant monocytopenia: ~20,000–30,000 patients annually.
patients_needing_tx: ~10,000 patients requiring active pharmacologic management.
price: $30,000–$60,000/year for novel myeloid growth factor or immunomodulator. $45,000.
registrational_endpoint: Absolute monocyte count normalization and reduction in opportunistic infections at 24 weeks
btd_threshold_vs_soc: AMC >200/μL in ≥70% AND ≥50% reduction in serious infection rate vs. control

symptoms: Sneezing, runny nose, nasal congestion, itchy eyes
treatment_goal: Control symptoms, improve quality of life
first_line_soc: Intranasal corticosteroids (e.g., fluticasone, mometasone) are first-line for persistent allergic rhinitis; oral second-generation antihistamines (cetirizine, loratadine) for mild/intermittent symptoms.
treatment_algorithm: Step 1: Avoidance of allergens + oral non-sedating antihistamines (cetirizine, fexofenadine) or intranasal antihistamines. Step 2: Intranasal corticosteroids (fluticasone, budesonide, mometasone) — gold standard for persistent disease. Step 3: Add leukotriene receptor antagonists (montelukast) if ocular symptoms predominate. Step 4: Allergen immunotherapy (subcutaneous AIT or sublingual SLIT tablets, e.g., Grastek, Odactra, Ragwitek) for long-term disease modification. Biologic option: dupilumab (Dupixent) approved 2024 for moderate-to-severe CRSwNP and increasingly used off-label; omalizumab (Xolair) for severe allergic rhinitis with comorbid asthma.
us_prevalence: ~82 million US patients diagnosed with seasonal allergic rhinitis as of 2024-2025 (AAFA 2026 update). Broader estimates including perennial AR: 40–60 million.
patients_needing_tx: ~35–40 million need pharmacologic treatment (moderate-to-severe symptomatic patients). ~15–20 million use prescription medications; remainder managed OTC. Estimate: 35M.
price: $18,000–$25,000/year; biologic pricing precedent (dupilumab ~$38K WAC, but AR is a large/competitive market requiring below-asthma pricing); novel intranasal biologic or small molecule could command $18–25K.
tam_gross: 35M × $20K = $700B (biologic/novel agent; effectively constrained by generic competition — realistic addressable subset ~5M severe/biologic-eligible patients × $20K = $100B)
registrational_endpoint: Total Nasal Symptom Score (TNSS) change from baseline over 2-week peak pollen season; co-primary endpoint with Total Ocular Symptom Score (TOSS).
btd_threshold_vs_soc: ≥30% reduction in TNSS vs. placebo (vs. ~20–22% for current intranasal steroids); statistically significant with p<0.001.

symptoms: Hives, vomiting, throat swelling, anaphylaxis after food exposure
treatment_goal: Prevent accidental reactions, achieve desensitization/tolerance
first_line_soc: Strict allergen avoidance + epinephrine auto-injector (EpiPen) for emergency use. For peanut allergy (ages 4–17): Palforzia (AR101) OIT FDA-approved. Omalizumab (Xolair) FDA-approved Feb 2024 for multi-food IgE-mediated allergy ≥1 year.
treatment_algorithm: Step 1: Allergen avoidance + epinephrine auto-injector. Step 2: Omalizumab (Xolair) — FDA approved 2024 for reduction of allergic reactions in IgE-mediated food allergy (all ages ≥1 year, multi-allergen). Step 3: Oral immunotherapy — Palforzia (peanut OIT, FDA 2020), investigational for other allergens (milk, egg, tree nuts). Step 4: Epicutaneous immunotherapy (Viaskin Peanut — DBV Technologies). Adjuncts: antihistamines for mild breakthrough reactions. Emerging: next-gen OIT combinations with omalizumab (OUTMATCH trial data 2025 showing superior outcomes).
us_prevalence: ~22 million US patients with food allergies as of 2024 (AAFA). ~18M adults, ~4M children. Peanut allergy: ~4.6M.
patients_needing_tx: ~5–8M moderate-to-severe food allergy patients who are candidates for active treatment (beyond epinephrine): ~3M for omalizumab-eligible, ~2M for OIT candidates.
price: $14,000–$18,000/year for Xolair in food allergy; $10,000–$15,000/year for OIT programs; novel multi-allergen biologic $20,000–$35,000/year.
registrational_endpoint: Proportion of patients tolerating ≥2,000 mg cumulative protein dose (exit food challenge) at 12–24 months.
btd_threshold_vs_soc: ≥40% of patients tolerating 2,000 mg at challenge vs. ≤5% placebo; superiority to current OIT: ≥60% vs. ~40% for Palforzia.

symptoms: Sudden hives, throat swelling, low blood pressure, breathing difficulty
treatment_goal: Prevent fatal reactions, reduce episode frequency and severity
first_line_soc: Epinephrine auto-injector (EpiPen, Auvi-Q) is first-line acute treatment; given IM at onset of anaphylaxis.
treatment_algorithm: Acute: Epinephrine IM (first-line), then adjuncts: diphenhydramine, H2-blockers, corticosteroids, bronchodilators, IV fluids. Prevention: strict allergen avoidance + carry epinephrine auto-injector. For food allergy-triggered anaphylaxis: omalizumab (Xolair) FDA-approved Feb 2024 for IgE-mediated food allergy in patients ≥1 year to reduce reaction severity. OIT (oral immunotherapy, e.g., Palforzia for peanut) desensitizes for specific food triggers. Venom immunotherapy for Hymenoptera anaphylaxis.
us_prevalence: ~45,000–50,000 ER visits/year for anaphylaxis (AAFA). Lifetime prevalence: ~1.6–5.1% of US population (~5–17M episodes total). Drug/food/venom anaphylaxis patients at chronic risk: ~3–5M.
patients_needing_tx: ~3–5M Americans at risk for recurrent anaphylaxis requiring prophylactic therapy (omalizumab, immunotherapy). ~2M are candidates for ongoing preventive pharmacologic treatment.
price: $15,000–$20,000/year; reference: Xolair for food allergy priced ~$14K–$17K/year for typical dosing.
registrational_endpoint: Proportion of patients tolerating ≥2,000 mg of allergen (tolerated dose) without allergic reaction at challenge; or reduction in anaphylaxis episodes per year.
btd_threshold_vs_soc: ≥40% of treated patients tolerating threshold dose vs. ≤10% placebo; or ≥50% reduction in anaphylaxis episodes/year vs. SoC (avoidance only).

symptoms: Rashes, hives, fever, organ inflammation following drug exposure
treatment_goal: Identify causative drug, prevent severe reactions, enable safe alternatives
first_line_soc: Discontinuation of offending drug. Antihistamines (diphenhydramine) and corticosteroids for mild-moderate reactions. Epinephrine for anaphylaxis. No specific preventive pharmacologic SoC beyond avoidance.
treatment_algorithm: Mild reactions: antihistamines, topical corticosteroids. Moderate: systemic corticosteroids (prednisone). Severe (SJS/TEN, DRESS): ICU care, cyclosporine or IVIG for SJS/TEN, systemic corticosteroids for DRESS. Drug desensitization protocols for essential drugs (aspirin, penicillin, checkpoint inhibitors). Preventive: pharmacogenomic testing (HLA-B*5701 for abacavir, HLA-B*1502 for carbamazepine) to prevent reactions before drug initiation.
us_prevalence: ~10–15% of hospitalized patients experience drug hypersensitivity. ~2M ER visits/year. SJS/TEN: ~1,000–2,000 cases/year. DRESS: ~5,000–10,000 cases/year.
patients_needing_tx: Acute: ~1.5–2M needing treatment annually. Chronic management (drug allergy evaluation/desensitization): ~500K–1M.
price: $20,000–$50,000/year for preventive biomarker-guided therapy; $150,000–$250,000/year for severe SJS/TEN novel agent.
peak_sales: SJS/TEN novel agent: 2K × $200K × 40% = $160M; Broad desensitization platform: 200K × $35K × 10% = $700M
registrational_endpoint: For SJS/TEN: Proportion achieving re-epithelialization at 14 days; mortality at 30 days.
btd_threshold_vs_soc: ≥30% improvement in re-epithelialization rate at 14 days vs. supportive care; mortality reduction ≥40% vs. historical ~20–30%.

disease: Hereditary and Acquired C1 Inhibitor Deficiency or Dysfunction
symptoms: Recurrent swelling of face, limbs, abdomen, airway; abdominal pain
treatment_goal: Prevent attacks, rapidly treat acute episodes, normalize C1-INH levels
first_line_soc: For HAE type I/II (C1-INH deficiency): subcutaneous C1-INH (Haegarda) or lanadelumab (Takhzyro, anti-kallikrein MAb) for long-term prophylaxis. For acute attacks: plasma-derived C1-INH (Berinert), icatibant (Firazyr), or ecallantide (Kalbitor).
treatment_algorithm: Long-term prophylaxis (LTP): SC C1-INH (Haegarda 60U/kg twice weekly), lanadelumab (Takhzyro 300mg q2weeks or monthly), or oral kallikrein inhibitor berotralstat (Orladeyo 150mg/day). Acute attack: C1-INH (Berinert IV or Ruconest), icatibant SC (Firazyr), ecallantide SC (Kalbitor). On-demand self-administration available for all acute agents. Pipeline 2024–2025: garadacimab (Phase 3 positive, anti-FXIIa), donidalorsen, marzeptacog. Acquired C1-INH deficiency (AAE): treat underlying lymphoproliferative/autoimmune disorder + C1-INH for attacks.
us_prevalence: HAE C1-INH types I/II: ~7,000–8,000 US patients. HAE all types (including nC1INH): ~10,000 (HAEA 2025). Acquired AAE: ~500–1,000.
patients_needing_tx: ~8,000–9,000 patients on active prophylactic or on-demand pharmacologic therapy.
price: $400,000–$600,000/year (peer: lanadelumab WAC ~$450K; Haegarda ~$500K).
registrational_endpoint: Monthly attack rate (MAR) over 6 months; proportion of patients with ≥50% reduction in attacks.
btd_threshold_vs_soc: MAR ≤0.3 attacks/month vs. ~2–3 attacks/month untreated; ≥80% attack reduction vs. placebo (lanadelumab benchmark: 87% reduction).

symptoms: Deep skin/mucous membrane swelling, abdominal cramping, airway swelling
treatment_goal: Prevent attacks, rapidly abort breakthrough episodes
first_line_soc: Histaminergic angioedema (most common): high-dose non-sedating antihistamines (cetirizine 40mg/day) first-line. For HAE (C1-INH deficiency): C1 esterase inhibitor concentrates (Berinert, Haegarda) or icatibant (Firazyr) for acute attacks; C1-INH or lanadelumab (Takhzyro) for prophylaxis.
treatment_algorithm: Histaminergic: Step 1: 2nd-gen antihistamines up to 4× standard dose. Step 2: Add omalizumab (Xolair) for chronic spontaneous urticaria with angioedema. HAE (C1-INH deficiency): Acute: icatibant (bradykinin B2 receptor antagonist), ecallantide (Kalbitor), or plasma-derived C1-INH (Berinert). Prophylaxis: subcutaneous C1-INH (Haegarda), lanadelumab (Takhzyro, anti-kallikrein MAb), berotralstat (Orladeyo, oral plasma kallikrein inhibitor). HAE-nC1INH: antihistamines, progestin manipulation; icatibant may help. Acquired angioedema (AAE): treat underlying cause + C1-INH replacement.
us_prevalence: Angioedema broadly: ~7.4% lifetime prevalence. HAE (all types): ~10,000 diagnosed US patients (HAEA 2025 study). ACE-inhibitor induced: ~100,000–200,000 episodes/year. Total angioedema patients requiring pharmacologic management: ~500K–1M.
patients_needing_tx: HAE pharmacologic therapy: ~7,000–8,000 US patients. Broader recurrent angioedema needing prescription therapy (omalizumab, antihistamines): ~300K–500K.
price: $250,000–$450,000/year for HAE novel agent (peer: lanadelumab ~$450K WAC; berotralstat ~$250K); $15K–$25K/year for non-HAE angioedema biologic.
tam_gross: HAE: 8K × $350K = $2.8B; Broader angioedema: 400K × $20K = $8B. Combined addressable: ~$3B (HAE focus).
registrational_endpoint: For HAE prophylaxis: Monthly attack rate (MAR) over 6–12 months vs. placebo. For acute treatment: Time to onset of relief and proportion attack-free at 4 hours.
btd_threshold_vs_soc: HAE prophylaxis: ≥50% reduction in MAR vs. placebo (lanadelumab achieved ~87% reduction); acute: ≥70% attack resolution at 4 hours vs. 30% placebo.

symptoms: Fatigue, joint pain, rashes, organ inflammation, variable by subtype
treatment_goal: Achieve remission, prevent organ damage
first_line_soc: Corticosteroids (prednisone) are first-line for acute flares across most autoimmune conditions. Disease-specific hydroxychloroquine for SLE; methotrexate for RA, dermatomyositis.
treatment_algorithm: Broad category. General framework: Step 1: Corticosteroids for acute disease control. Step 2: Conventional DMARDs (hydroxychloroquine, methotrexate, azathioprine, mycophenolate). Step 3: Biologic DMARDs by target: TNF inhibitors (adalimumab, etanercept), IL-6 inhibitors (tocilizumab), anti-CD20 (rituximab), anti-BLyS (belimumab for SLE), JAK inhibitors (tofacitinib, baricitinib, upadacitinib). Step 4: Investigational: CAR-T (CD19 CAR-T for SLE showing early dramatic responses in 2024–2025 trials).
us_prevalence: Autoimmune diseases collectively affect ~50 million Americans (AARDA estimate). Top conditions: RA ~1.5M, SLE ~1.5M, MS ~1M, T1D ~1.7M, IBD ~3.1M, psoriasis ~7.5M.
patients_needing_tx: As a category: ~30–40M need pharmacologic treatment. Moderate-to-severe requiring biologics/advanced therapy: ~5–8M.
price: $30,000–$80,000/year for oral small molecules; $25,000–$60,000/year for biosimilars; novel selective biologics $50,000–$150,000/year depending on indication.
tam_gross: ~8M biologic-eligible × $60K = $480B (theoretical maximum across all autoimmune subtypes)
peak_sales: Disease-specific; for a novel mechanism across key autoimmune indications: 500K patients × $60K × 15% share = $4.5B
registrational_endpoint: Disease-specific: ACR50 for RA; SRI-4 for SLE; CDAI remission for IBD; etc.
btd_threshold_vs_soc: ≥50% ACR response (ACR50) in RA vs. 20–25% with DMARD background; or disease-specific composite remission rate ≥2× SoC.

disease: Mastocytosis and Mast Cell Activation Syndrome
symptoms: Flushing, hives, anaphylaxis, bone pain, abdominal cramping
treatment_goal: Control mast cell mediator symptoms, prevent organ damage in systemic disease
first_line_soc: H1 antihistamines (cetirizine, loratadine) + H2 antihistamines (ranitidine/famotidine) + proton pump inhibitors are first-line for symptom control. Epinephrine auto-injector for anaphylaxis.
treatment_algorithm: Cutaneous/indolent SM: H1+H2 antihistamines, PPI, cromolyn sodium (oral, for GI symptoms), aspirin for flushing (caution in ASA-sensitive). Advanced SM (ASM, MCL, SM-AHN): midostaurin (Rydapt, KIT D816V inhibitor) FDA-approved 2017 for ASM/SM-AHN/MCL; avapritinib (Ayvakit) FDA-approved 2021 for ISM (indolent SM) — first approved for symptomatic ISM 2023. Cladribine for aggressive SM. Imatinib if KIT D816V negative. Omalizumab (Xolair) for mast cell activation syndrome and anaphylaxis.
us_prevalence: Cutaneous mastocytosis: ~10,000–15,000 US patients. Systemic mastocytosis: ~15,000–25,000 US patients. MCAS: poorly defined, estimated 50,000–200,000+.
patients_needing_tx: SM patients needing advanced pharmacologic therapy: ~20,000–25,000. MCAS: ~50,000–100,000 needing antihistamine/mast cell-stabilizing therapy.
price: $200,000–$350,000/year for KIT/mast cell-targeting agents (avapritinib WAC ~$250K/year for ISM indication).
registrational_endpoint: Total Symptom Score (TSS) change from baseline at 24 weeks; tryptase level normalization; bone marrow mast cell burden reduction.
btd_threshold_vs_soc: ≥30% TSS reduction vs. placebo; ≥50% reduction in serum tryptase; response rate (≥35% TSS improvement) ≥50% vs. ~15% placebo.

symptoms: Often asymptomatic; recurrent sinopulmonary infections, GI infections, allergy risk
treatment_goal: Manage recurrent infections; avoid IgA-containing blood products
first_line_soc: Prophylactic antibiotics (amoxicillin, azithromycin) for patients with recurrent infections. IgA-deficient IVIG only if IgG subclass deficiency co-exists (avoid IgA-containing products due to anaphylaxis risk).
treatment_algorithm: Mild/asymptomatic: no pharmacologic treatment. Symptomatic recurrent infections: prophylactic antibiotics. IgG subclass deficiency coexistence: IVIG (IgA-depleted formulations). Allergy management: standard antihistamines, avoidance. No approved disease-modifying therapy. Investigational: approaches targeting APRIL/BAFF pathways to restore IgA production. Monitor for autoimmune disease (celiac, SLE, RA risk elevated).
us_prevalence: Most common primary immunodeficiency: ~1 in 300–700. ~500,000–1,000,000 US patients; most asymptomatic. Symptomatic subset: ~100,000–200,000.
patients_needing_tx: ~100,000–150,000 symptomatic patients needing pharmacologic management.
price: $100,000–$200,000/year for a novel IgA restoration agent; $5,000–$15,000/year for antibiotic prophylaxis (existing SoC).
registrational_endpoint: Serum IgA level normalization; annualized respiratory infection rate reduction.
btd_threshold_vs_soc: Serum IgA ≥70 mg/dL in ≥50% of patients at 12 months (vs. 0 without treatment); ≥50% reduction in symptomatic infection episodes.

symptoms: Recurrent respiratory infections, low antibody levels, fatigue, diarrhea
treatment_goal: Prevent infections, normalize IgG levels, reduce complications
first_line_soc: Immunoglobulin replacement therapy (IVIG or SCIG) — lifelong first-line treatment to maintain serum IgG ≥700–800 mg/dL.
treatment_algorithm: IVIG (Gamunex-C, Privigen, Octagam) or SCIG (Hizentra, Cuvitru, Gammanorm) every 3–4 weeks or weekly subcutaneously. Dose individualized to trough IgG levels and clinical response. Prophylactic antibiotics (azithromycin) for chronic lung disease. Immunomodulation for CVID-related inflammatory complications (autoimmunity, granulomatous disease, lymphoproliferation): corticosteroids, rituximab, sirolimus, abatacept. Monitor for lymphoma (risk ~5–10× general population). Hyper-IgM overlap: supplement CD40L pathway investigation.
us_prevalence: ~25,000–50,000 US patients (prevalence ~1 in 25,000–50,000). Most common serious primary antibody deficiency.
patients_needing_tx: ~20,000–40,000 on lifelong Ig replacement. ~15,000–20,000 are actively on IV/SCIG therapy.
price: $30,000–$80,000/year for Ig replacement (existing therapy); a novel disease-modifying agent targeting the underlying B-cell defect: $200,000–$350,000/year.
registrational_endpoint: Annualized rate of serious bacterial infections (ASBI) over 12 months; co-endpoint: trough IgG levels.
btd_threshold_vs_soc: ASBI ≤0.15/year vs. SoC IVIG (~0.2–0.4/year); or ≥50% reduction in infectious complications; or IgG normalization in ≥70% of patients.

disease: Selective Antibody Deficiency With Normal Immunoglobulins (SADNI)
symptoms: Recurrent ear, sinus, and lung infections with normal IgG/IgA/IgM levels
treatment_goal: Prevent recurrent infections, normalize polysaccharide antibody response
first_line_soc: Prophylactic antibiotics (amoxicillin, azithromycin) first-line. Pneumococcal vaccination (Prevnar 20, Pneumovax 23) with post-vaccination titer assessment to confirm diagnosis/guide treatment.
treatment_algorithm: Prophylactic antibiotics. IVIG as a therapeutic trial if severe and poorly controlled — some patients normalize responses after IVIG. Repeat vaccination trials. For pediatric patients: watchful waiting as some develop normal responses with age. ENT interventions for chronic sinusitis/OM. Annual reassessment of immunoglobulin levels and vaccine responses.
us_prevalence: ~10,000–20,000 US patients (estimated; underdiagnosed). Prevalence ~1 in 15,000–30,000.
patients_needing_tx: ~10,000–15,000 patients requiring pharmacologic management (prophylactic antibiotics ± IVIG).
price: $150,000–$250,000/year for a novel immunomodulatory agent targeting B-cell polysaccharide response.
registrational_endpoint: Reduction in annualized serious infection rate; proportion with ≥2-fold post-vaccine antibody titers for ≥70% of serotypes.
btd_threshold_vs_soc: ≥50% reduction in annualized infection rate vs. prophylactic antibiotics alone; ≥60% of patients achieving protective titers to ≥70% of vaccine serotypes.

symptoms: Absent antibodies, recurrent bacterial infections, absent B cells, enteroviral risk
treatment_goal: Replace absent IgG, prevent bacterial infections and enteroviral encephalitis
first_line_soc: Lifelong IVIG or SCIG replacement therapy to maintain IgG trough ≥500–800 mg/dL.
treatment_algorithm: IVIG (monthly) or SCIG (weekly or biweekly) — cornerstone of management. Dose individualized by trough IgG and clinical response. Prophylactic antibiotics (azithromycin or amoxicillin) for chronic lung disease. Avoid live vaccines. Monitor for enterovirus CNS infection (polio, Echo, Coxsackie) — no effective treatment; prevention by high-dose IVIG. Monitor pulmonary function (chronic lung disease in adulthood). BTK inhibitors (ibrutinib, off-label): improve B-cell counts in some cases of late-onset; BTK is the gene mutated in XLA. Gene therapy: early preclinical/Phase I for XLA.
us_prevalence: ~2,000–5,000 US patients (incidence ~1 in 200,000–250,000 male births). ~3,000–4,000 estimated.
patients_needing_tx: ~3,500 patients; essentially all require lifelong Ig replacement.
price: $250,000–$400,000/year for novel BTK-targeted or gene therapy approach; IVIG currently $30,000–$60,000/year.
registrational_endpoint: Annualized serious bacterial infection rate; serum IgG trough maintenance ≥500 mg/dL.
btd_threshold_vs_soc: ASBI ≤0.1/year vs. IVIG ~0.15–0.3/year; or BTK-targeted approach achieving B-cell reconstitution ≥100/µL in ≥50% of patients.

symptoms: Low antibody levels in infants, recurrent mild respiratory infections, usually self-resolving
treatment_goal: Monitor for resolution; support with prophylactic antibiotics if symptomatic
first_line_soc: Watchful waiting is the primary approach; prophylactic antibiotics (amoxicillin) for infants with recurrent infections. IVIG reserved for severe symptomatic cases.
treatment_algorithm: Observation with regular immunoglobulin monitoring (every 3–6 months). Prophylactic antibiotics for recurrent sinopulmonary infections. IVIG only if IgG <200 mg/dL with recurrent serious infections. Avoid live vaccines until immunoglobulin levels normalize. Most resolve by age 2–4 years. Differentiate from CVID (which does not resolve).
us_prevalence: ~10,000–15,000 US infants at any one time (prevalence ~1 in 250–500 infants during first 2 years of life).
patients_needing_tx: ~5,000–8,000 needing active pharmacologic intervention (antibiotics ± IVIG).
price: $100,000–$200,000/year (novel agent to accelerate immunoglobulin reconstitution); very limited commercial potential given transient/self-resolving nature.
tam_gross: 8,000 × $150K = $1.2B (theoretical; extremely low commercial interest given self-resolving nature)
registrational_endpoint: Time to IgG normalization; annualized serious infection rate in first 2 years of life.
btd_threshold_vs_soc: IgG normalization by 12 months in ≥70% vs. natural history (~30% by 12 months); ≥50% reduction in infection episodes.

symptoms: Recurrent bacterial/opportunistic infections, elevated IgM, absent IgG/IgA/IgE
treatment_goal: Replace deficient immunoglobulins, prevent Pneumocystis and cryptosporidial infections
first_line_soc: IVIG replacement (to supply IgG) + TMP-SMX prophylaxis for Pneumocystis jirovecii are first-line standards for CD40L-deficient (HIGM1) patients.
treatment_algorithm: IVIG + TMP-SMX prophylaxis. Avoid live vaccines. For CD40L deficiency: consider early HSCT (curative, best outcomes <5 years). G-CSF for neutropenia (HIGM type 1/3). Cryptosporidium prevention (use filtered water). For autoimmune cytopenias: IVIG, corticosteroids. Sclerosing cholangitis from cryptosporidium: biliary management. AID deficiency (HIGM type 2): IVIG + antimicrobials. Gene therapy in investigational phase.
us_prevalence: ~300–500 US patients with CD40L deficiency (X-linked, most common form). All types combined: ~600–1,000.
patients_needing_tx: ~800 patients; essentially all require IVIG and prophylactic antibiotics.
price: $500,000–$1,500,000 for gene therapy (one-time curative); $200,000–$350,000/year for targeted biologic.
tam_gross: 800 × $1M (gene therapy one-time) = $800M cumulative; ~100 new cases/year × $1M = $100M/year at steady state
peak_sales: $100M/year for gene therapy at full uptake
registrational_endpoint: Immune reconstitution (normal T-cell counts, IgG normalization), serious infection-free survival at 2 years post-gene therapy.
btd_threshold_vs_soc: ≥70% infection-free survival at 2 years vs. ~40–50% with IVIG + prophylaxis; class I evidence for OS improvement.

symptoms: Progressive loss of coordination, dilated blood vessels, recurrent infections, cancer risk
treatment_goal: Manage infections, delay neurodegeneration, prevent cancer
first_line_soc: No disease-modifying pharmacologic SoC. Supportive care: IVIG for humoral immunodeficiency component; prophylactic antibiotics for recurrent infections; physical/occupational therapy.
treatment_algorithm: IVIG replacement therapy for IgG deficiency. Prophylactic antibiotics (azithromycin or TMP-SMX) for chronic pulmonary disease. Surveillance for malignancy (lymphoma/leukemia risk ~30%). Avoid ionizing radiation (extreme radiosensitivity). Investigational: mTOR inhibitors, antioxidants, stem cell transplant. No approved disease-modifying therapy as of 2025.
us_prevalence: ~1,000–3,000 US patients (incidence ~1 in 40,000–100,000 live births). Estimated 1,500–2,000 living patients in US.
patients_needing_tx: ~1,500 patients; essentially all require some pharmacologic support (IVIG, antibiotics). ~1,200 actively managed.
price: $300,000–$500,000/year; ultra-rare orphan disease pricing precedent.
registrational_endpoint: Neurological function scale (ICARS or SARA score) change from baseline at 12–24 months; pulmonary function (FVC).
btd_threshold_vs_soc: ≥3-point improvement on SARA (Scale for Assessment and Rating of Ataxia) vs. natural history (expected 2–3 point annual decline); or stabilization of FVC decline.

symptoms: Cardiac defects, low calcium, immune deficiency, developmental delay
treatment_goal: Correct thymic deficiency, manage hypocalcemia and cardiac defects
first_line_soc: Calcium and vitamin D supplementation for hypocalcemia. For complete DiGeorge (absent T cells): thymus transplant (RVT-802/Lantidra approach) — only FDA-approved treatment for complete DiGeorge.
treatment_algorithm: Mild/partial (most cases): observation, calcium/vitamin D replacement, prophylactic antimicrobials if T cell count low. Complete DiGeorge (~1%): Thymus transplant (RVT-802, FDA approved 2023) — cultured thymus tissue transplant from deceased donor. IVIG for humoral deficiency. Cardiac surgery for structural defects (not pharmacologic). HSCT in some refractory cases. Gene therapy investigational for 22q11.2 deletion restoration (early research).
us_prevalence: ~150,000–200,000 US patients (1 in 4,000 births — most common chromosomal microdeletion). Complete DiGeorge: ~1,500–2,000 patients.
patients_needing_tx: Complete DiGeorge (~1,500): all need immune reconstitution. Partial (~150K): ~30–40K need pharmacologic management for immune/endocrine complications.
price: $250,000–$500,000/year for thymus-derived cell therapy; $100,000–$200,000/year for gene therapy.
tam_gross: Complete: 1,500 × $400K = $600M; Partial complications: 35K × $50K = $1.75B. Focus novel Rx on complete: $600M.
registrational_endpoint: Naive T cell count (≥50 CD4+CD45RA+ T cells/µL) and immune reconstitution at 12 months; proportion free of serious infections.
btd_threshold_vs_soc: ≥50% of patients achieving naive T cell count ≥50/µL at 12 months vs. near-zero without treatment; or survival advantage.

symptoms: Fulminant EBV-induced hemophagocytic lymphohistiocytosis (HLH), lymphoma, low immunoglobulins
treatment_goal: Prevent EBV-triggered HLH, achieve immune reconstitution via HSCT
first_line_soc: Allogeneic HSCT before EBV exposure is the only curative approach. IVIG to prevent/manage hypogammaglobulinemia. HLH protocol (dexamethasone + etoposide) for acute EBV-HLH.
treatment_algorithm: Pre-EBV: IVIG prophylaxis, early HSCT. EBV-triggered HLH (type 1, SAP deficiency): HLH-94/2004 protocol (dexamethasone, etoposide, cyclosporine) + rituximab to reduce EBV-infected B-cell burden. Post-HLH: HSCT for survivors. Type 2 (XIAP deficiency): higher HLH recurrence risk; HSCT essential. Lymphoma: rituximab + chemotherapy. Emapalumab (anti-IFN-γ) emerging for refractory HLH.
us_prevalence: Extremely rare: <500 US patients. ~200–400 estimated (1 in 1,000,000 males).
patients_needing_tx: ~300–400 patients; all require active management and HSCT preparation.
price: $1,000,000–$2,000,000 for gene therapy or advanced HLH biologics.
registrational_endpoint: EBV-HLH-free survival at 2 years; OS at 5 years post-gene therapy/HSCT.
btd_threshold_vs_soc: ≥80% HLH-free survival at 2 years vs. ~40–50% without HSCT; OS ≥85% at 5 years.

symptoms: Recurrent severe bacterial/fungal infections, abscesses, granuloma formation
treatment_goal: Prevent life-threatening infections, reduce inflammation
first_line_soc: Prophylactic trimethoprim-sulfamethoxazole (TMP-SMX) and itraconazole are standard first-line antimicrobial prophylaxis. Interferon-gamma (Actimmune) reduces infection frequency.
treatment_algorithm: Daily TMP-SMX prophylaxis (antibacterial) + daily itraconazole (antifungal). Interferon-gamma-1b (Actimmune) 3×/week subcutaneously — only approved immunomodulator. Aggressive treatment of acute infections with IV antibiotics/antifungals. Corticosteroids for granulomatous/inflammatory complications (colitis, bladder). Definitive: allogeneic hematopoietic stem cell transplant (HSCT) — curative but high risk. Gene therapy (investigational): lentiviral gene therapy in trials showing promising results (2024–2025).
us_prevalence: ~800–1,200 US patients (incidence ~1 in 200,000–250,000). Estimated 1,000 living US patients.
patients_needing_tx: ~900 patients; essentially all require prophylactic antimicrobials and interferon-gamma. ~500–600 on Actimmune.
price: $400,000–$700,000/year for gene therapy (one-time or annual); $200,000–$350,000/year for novel biologic.
peak_sales: Gene therapy: 200 patients/year × $1.5M one-time = $300M peak annually
registrational_endpoint: Serious infection rate (annualized serious bacterial/fungal infection rate) over 12–24 months vs. historical control or placebo.
btd_threshold_vs_soc: ≥50% reduction in annualized serious infection rate vs. SoC (TMP-SMX + itraconazole); or proportion infection-free at 2 years ≥60% vs. ~20% historical.

symptoms: Recurrent skin abscesses, pneumonia, eczema, elevated IgE, skeletal abnormalities
treatment_goal: Prevent infections, manage eczema, reduce IgE-mediated complications
first_line_soc: Prophylactic TMP-SMX for bacterial infections; prophylactic antifungals (itraconazole) for fungal infections. Skin care/antibiotics for eczema management.
treatment_algorithm: TMP-SMX prophylaxis (primary). Antifungal prophylaxis (itraconazole). Aggressive treatment of acute infections (S. aureus, Aspergillus). For STAT3 LOF-HIES: IgG levels monitored; IVIG if low. For DOCK8 deficiency (AR-HIES): HSCT is curative and recommended. Skin disease: topical corticosteroids, topical calcineurin inhibitors. JAK inhibitors (tofacitinib) investigated for STAT3 GOF overlap. Dupilumab for eczema component (off-label, case series).
us_prevalence: ~1,000–2,000 US patients across all genotypes (STAT3 LOF dominant; DOCK8 recessive; others).
patients_needing_tx: ~1,500 patients; all require some pharmacologic intervention.
price: $200,000–$400,000/year for targeted novel agent; $50,000/year for dupilumab in eczema component.
registrational_endpoint: Annualized serious infection rate over 12 months; IgE reduction; EASI score for eczema component.
btd_threshold_vs_soc: ≥50% reduction in serious infections and ≥50% IgE reduction from baseline; EASI-50 in ≥60% of patients.

symptoms: Bleeding (thrombocytopenia), eczema, recurrent infections, autoimmunity, cancer risk
treatment_goal: Curative HSCT or gene therapy; prevent infections, bleeding, malignancy
first_line_soc: Allogeneic HSCT is curative first-line treatment. IVIG for humoral immunodeficiency; prophylactic TMP-SMX; platelet transfusions for severe bleeding.
treatment_algorithm: IVIG + prophylactic TMP-SMX + antifungal prophylaxis. Platelet transfusions for significant bleeding episodes. Splenectomy contraindicated in severe WAS (immunodeficiency worsens). HSCT recommended for all patients with HLA-matched donor — best outcomes <5 years. Gene therapy (OTL-103, lentiviral WASp gene): FDA Breakthrough Therapy; approved in EU (Lenmeldy for related condition); Phase II/III showing high efficacy. Autoimmune complications: corticosteroids, rituximab. Malignancy (EBV-lymphoma): rituximab + chemotherapy.
us_prevalence: ~800–1,500 US patients (incidence ~1 in 100,000–250,000 male births). ~1,000 estimated living US patients.
patients_needing_tx: ~800–1,000 patients; all require active management. ~100–150 new cases/year eligible for gene therapy.
price: $2,000,000–$3,500,000 one-time gene therapy.
peak_sales: $350M/year at full gene therapy uptake (competitive with HSCT)
registrational_endpoint: Event-free survival at 2 years (freedom from HSCT, death, or serious infection/bleeding); WAS protein expression ≥25% normal in ≥80% of lymphocytes.
btd_threshold_vs_soc: EFS ≥80% at 2 years post-gene therapy vs. ~50–65% EFS with haplo-identical HSCT; WASp normalization in majority of T/B cells.

symptoms: Recurrent infections, partial albinism, bruising, neurological deterioration
treatment_goal: Prevent accelerated phase (HLH), curative HSCT
first_line_soc: Prophylactic antibiotics and antifungals. The only curative treatment is allogeneic HSCT, performed early before accelerated phase. No approved pharmacologic disease-modifying agent.
treatment_algorithm: Prophylactic TMP-SMX + antifungals. HSCT at diagnosis (corrects immunological defect but not neurological). For accelerated phase (HLH): HLH-94/2004 protocol (dexamethasone + etoposide, cyclosporine). Supportive: vitamin C historically used (immunomodulatory, limited evidence). Neurological complications: symptom management. Gene therapy in early investigational stages.
us_prevalence: Extremely rare: <500 living US patients. ~200–300 estimated.
patients_needing_tx: ~250 patients; all require intensive management. ~100 undergo HSCT annually (cumulative).
price: $1,000,000–$2,000,000 one-time gene therapy; ~$500,000/year biologic.
peak_sales: 50 new patients/year × $1.5M = $75M peak annual gene therapy revenue
registrational_endpoint: Proportion achieving engraftment and accelerated phase-free survival at 2 years post-HSCT/gene therapy; or OS at 5 years.
btd_threshold_vs_soc: ≥80% accelerated phase-free survival at 2 years vs. ~50–60% with HSCT.

symptoms: Persistent Candida infections of skin, nails, mucous membranes
treatment_goal: Control Candida infections, manage autoimmune complications
first_line_soc: Long-term oral antifungal azoles (fluconazole, itraconazole, voriconazole) are first-line maintenance therapy.
treatment_algorithm: Continuous azole antifungal therapy (fluconazole as primary, rotate to itraconazole/voriconazole if resistance). Treat underlying cause: if STAT3 GOF, anti-IL-6 (tocilizumab) for autoimmune complications. If DOCK8/IL-17 pathway defect, consider JAK inhibitors or IL-17 targeted therapy (investigational). HSCT for severe/refractory cases. Monitor for Candida resistance; echinocandins for azole-resistant infections.
us_prevalence: Rare; ~1,000–2,000 US patients with primary CMC (STAT1 GOF, DOCK8 deficiency, IL-17 pathway defects).
patients_needing_tx: ~1,500 patients; virtually all require ongoing antifungal therapy.
price: $150,000–$300,000/year for targeted biologic (IL-17 or JAK inhibitor in this indication); IL-17 inhibitors are established but priced at $25–50K for other indications.
registrational_endpoint: Proportion of patients with clinical cure/mycological eradication of Candida at 6 months; or time-to-relapse.
btd_threshold_vs_soc: ≥60% sustained mycological eradication at 12 months vs. ~20–30% with azoles alone.

symptoms: Recurrent severe bacterial infections, delayed wound healing, absent pus formation
treatment_goal: Prevent life-threatening infections; curative HSCT
first_line_soc: Prophylactic TMP-SMX + antifungals as first-line antimicrobial prophylaxis. Definitive: allogeneic HSCT.
treatment_algorithm: Prophylactic TMP-SMX + itraconazole. Aggressive IV antibiotic treatment of acute infections. HSCT recommended for severe LAD I (CD18 expression <1%): curative with good outcomes if done early. LAD II: fucose supplementation (corrects GDP-fucose transporter defect). LAD III (kindlin-3 defect): HSCT. Gene therapy investigational (LAD I). IVIG for humoral component. G-CSF may worsen some features — use cautiously.
us_prevalence: Extremely rare: <200 US patients. LAD I most common; LAD II and III even rarer.
patients_needing_tx: ~150–200 patients; all require active management.
price: $1,000,000–$2,500,000 one-time gene therapy.
registrational_endpoint: CD18 expression restoration (>10% normal), serious infection-free survival at 2 years post-treatment.
btd_threshold_vs_soc: ≥80% serious infection-free survival at 2 years vs. ~20–30% without HSCT.

symptoms: Life-threatening infections from birth, failure to thrive, absent T/B/NK cells
treatment_goal: Curative immune reconstitution via HSCT or gene therapy
first_line_soc: Allogeneic hematopoietic stem cell transplant (HSCT) — definitive first-line curative treatment. Gene therapy is approved for ADA-SCID (Strimvelis in EU; OTL-101/Orchard Therapeutics in US trials). Immediate supportive: IVIG, prophylactic antimicrobials, reverse isolation.
treatment_algorithm: Immediate: reverse isolation, IVIG, TMP-SMX + antifungal + antiviral prophylaxis; avoid live vaccines. Definitive: HLA-matched HSCT (90%+ survival if matched sibling), or haplo-identical HSCT with αβ T cell/CD19 B cell depletion. ADA-SCID: PEGylated ADA enzyme replacement (pegademase, Adagen/Revcovi) as bridge or for non-HSCT candidates; gene therapy (OTL-101 approved EU 2016 as Strimvelis). X-SCID (IL2Rγ): retroviral gene therapy (EFS-γc; Phase II/III trials). RAG1/2, JAK3: HSCT. Post-HSCT: IVIG until B-cell reconstitution.
us_prevalence: ~60–100 new cases per year in US (1 in 50,000–100,000 live births with newborn screening). ~500–1,000 living US patients including older unscreened survivors.
patients_needing_tx: ~100 new patients/year + ~500 existing patients needing maintenance/follow-up. Gene therapy market: ~80–100 new patients/year eligible.
price: $2,000,000–$4,000,000 one-time gene therapy (Strimvelis in EU: ~€600K; ADA-SCID gene therapies targeting $2–4M in US).
peak_sales: $250M/year at ~80% gene therapy adoption for newly diagnosed
registrational_endpoint: Immune reconstitution (naive T cell count ≥50/µL, sustained at 12+ months), event-free survival (EFS) without HSCT or retreatment at 24 months.
btd_threshold_vs_soc: EFS ≥80% at 24 months vs. haplo-identical HSCT (~50–70% EFS without matched donor); naive T cell reconstitution ≥50/µL in ≥90% of patients.

symptoms: Selective CD8+ T cell deficiency, recurrent infections, autoimmunity
treatment_goal: Restore CD8 T cell function, prevent infections, curative HSCT
first_line_soc: Prophylactic antibiotics and antifungals. Allogeneic HSCT — curative for this rare combined immunodeficiency.
treatment_algorithm: Prophylactic TMP-SMX + antifungals. IVIG for humoral support. HSCT recommended: restores ZAP-70 expression and CD8+ T cell counts. Immunosuppression for autoimmune cytopenias. Gene therapy investigational but not in clinical trials as of 2025.
us_prevalence: Extremely rare: <100 US patients globally (fewer than 50 families described in literature).
patients_needing_tx: ~50–100 patients; all require management.
price: $1,500,000–$3,000,000 one-time gene therapy.
peak_sales: $10M/year (extreme orphan; minimal commercial viability without platform strategy)
registrational_endpoint: CD8+ T cell count normalization, EFS at 2 years, serious infection-free survival.
btd_threshold_vs_soc: CD8+ T cells ≥200/µL at 12 months and EFS ≥80% at 2 years vs. natural history (essentially 0% without HSCT).

symptoms: Abdominal pain, fever, gas in tissues, peritonitis, septic shock
treatment_goal: Source control, eradicate anaerobes, prevent peritonitis spread
first_line_soc: Surgical/interventional source control + IV metronidazole (Flagyl, nitroimidazole) combined with a cephalosporin (e.g., cefazolin) or piperacillin-tazobactam for mixed aerobic-anaerobic coverage.
treatment_algorithm: Community-acquired intra-abdominal infection: piperacillin-tazobactam or cefoxitin or ertapenem. Hospital-acquired: imipenem or meropenem. Clostridial pelvic: metronidazole + ceftriaxone or ampicillin-sulbactam. Source control (surgery, percutaneous drainage) is essential adjunct. Duration: 4–7 days after source control per IDSA 2010 (reaffirmed 2024). 2024: Shorter course (4 days) non-inferior per STOP-IT trial data.
us_prevalence: ~300,000 complicated intra-abdominal infections/yr (broad category); clostridial-specific: ~20,000–50,000. (IDSA/NHSN)
patients_needing_tx: ~300,000 complicated intra-abdominal infections require IV antibiotics; clostridial-specific ~30,000.
price: $5,000–$15,000 per course IV antibiotics for complicated infection; per-course price.
peak_sales: $200–500M for novel agent with improved coverage of anaerobes/ESBL in complicated intra-abdominal infections.
registrational_endpoint: Clinical cure at Test of Cure (Day 28) for complicated intra-abdominal infection; composite microbiological + clinical cure per FDA 2018 cIAI guidance.
btd_threshold_vs_soc: Clinical cure ≥80% at Day 28 for ESBL/MDR anaerobic intra-abdominal infections vs. ~60% for carbapenem SoC.

symptoms: Watery diarrhea (≥3/day), cramping, fever, pseudomembranous colitis
treatment_goal: Eradicate C. difficile, prevent recurrence, restore gut microbiome
first_line_soc: Fidaxomicin (Dificid, macrocyclic antibiotic) 200 mg BID × 10 days preferred over vancomycin (glycopeptide) 125 mg QID × 10 days for initial and recurrent CDI per IDSA/SHEA 2021 guidelines.
treatment_algorithm: First episode (non-severe): fidaxomicin 200 mg BID × 10 days (fewer recurrences vs. vancomycin). Alternative: vancomycin 125 mg QID × 10 days. Severe CDI: vancomycin 125 mg QID (oral); fulminant: IV metronidazole + high-dose vancomycin ± fidaxomicin. Recurrent CDI: fidaxomicin extended-pulsed OR bezlotoxumab (Zinplava, monoclonal anti-toxin B mAb) adjunct during antibiotics to prevent further recurrence. Fecal microbiota transplant (FMT): REBYOTA (RBX2660, FDA-approved Nov 2022) or VOWST (SER-109, FDA-approved Apr 2023) for recurrent CDI. Surgery (colectomy) for fulminant/toxic megacolon. 2024: Vowst and Rebyota expanding use; rifaximin chaser regimen.
us_prevalence: ~500,000–700,000 CDI cases/yr in US; ~29,000 deaths. (CDC 2024 threat report update)
patients_needing_tx: ~600,000 patients/yr require pharmacologic treatment; ~150,000/yr have recurrent CDI needing microbiome therapy.
price: Fidaxomicin: ~$3,000/course. Bezlotoxumab: ~$4,500/dose. FMT products: $5,000–$10,000 per treatment. Novel antibiotic: $3,000–$6,000/course.
peak_sales: Fidaxomicin (Dificid) ~$200M peak; VOWST/REBYOTA projected $300–500M combined; novel CDI antibiotic: $300–600M at 20% share.
registrational_endpoint: Sustained cure at 30 days post-treatment end (no recurrence); clinical cure at Day 10 as primary per FDA CDI guidance.
btd_threshold_vs_soc: Sustained cure ≥65% at Day 30 vs. ~55% for fidaxomicin (standard); or recurrence rate <10% vs. ~20% for vancomycin.

symptoms: Foul-smelling abscess, necrotizing tissue, fever, abdominal pain, septic shock
treatment_goal: Eradicate polymicrobial anaerobic bacteria, source control, prevent sepsis
first_line_soc: Metronidazole (Flagyl, nitroimidazole) 500 mg IV q8h combined with beta-lactam (ampicillin or piperacillin-tazobactam Zosyn) for coverage of aerobes + anaerobes. Or meropenem/ertapenem as monotherapy.
treatment_algorithm: Step 1: Piperacillin-tazobactam (Zosyn) 3.375–4.5g IV q6h covering Bacteroides, Prevotella, and gram-negatives. Step 2: Metronidazole + ceftriaxone combination (ertapenem preferred). Step 3: Source control: drainage, debridement, resection of necrotic tissue — essential. Step 4: Carbapenems (meropenem, ertapenem) for severe/high-resistance anaerobic infections. Step 5: Oral step-down: amoxicillin-clavulanate + metronidazole. Clindamycin historically used but Bacteroides resistance >30% in many US areas. 2024: IDSA IAI guidelines updated; ertapenem increasingly used.
us_prevalence: ~300,000–500,000 US cases/yr of significant anaerobic infections (intraabdominal, lung abscess, necrotizing fasciitis components). (IDSA/SCCM estimates)
patients_needing_tx: ~300,000 patients/yr need IV antibiotics; all require source control.
price: $5,000–$15,000 per course (novel anti-anaerobic agent with activity vs. metronidazole-resistant strains; per-course).
peak_sales: $150–300M for novel anaerobic-spectrum antibiotic at 10–15% share of complicated IAI market.
registrational_endpoint: Clinical cure at Test-of-Cure Day 28 for complicated intraabdominal infection (cIAI); 30-day mortality for necrotizing infections.
