btd_threshold_vs_soc: Clinical cure ≥85% for metronidazole-resistant Bacteroides/Prevotella infections vs. ~60% for carbapenem; or necrotizing fasciitis 30-day mortality <10% vs. ~25% for standard regimens.

symptoms: Double vision, difficulty swallowing, descending paralysis, respiratory failure
treatment_goal: Neutralize toxin, support respiration, prevent respiratory death
first_line_soc: Heptavalent botulinum antitoxin (HBAT, BAT — equine-derived) IV as early as possible; mechanical ventilation for respiratory failure. No antibiotic (toxin-mediated); antibiotics contraindicated in wound botulism except penicillin for wound debridement.
treatment_algorithm: Step 1: Heptavalent botulinum antitoxin (HBAT, BAT) — CDC Strategic National Stockpile — neutralizes toxins A–G. Contact CDC immediately. Step 2: ICU admission, mechanical ventilation. Step 3: For wound botulism: wound debridement + penicillin G IV (avoid aminoglycosides — worsen neuromuscular blockade). Step 4: Infant botulism: BabyBIG (botulism immune globulin IV, Human, BIG-IV) — licensed product, California Dept of Health. Supportive care only; no antitoxin role for infant. 2024: Novel antitoxin formats under BARDA development.
us_prevalence: ~120–150 US cases/yr (65% infant, 25% wound [injection drug users], 10% foodborne). (CDC Botulism Annual Summary 2024)
patients_needing_tx: ~120 cases/yr; all require immediate antitoxin + ICU support.
price: $50,000–$200,000 per treatment course (antitoxin/immune globulin); BabyBIG ~$45,300/vial historically. Novel small-molecule botulinum toxin inhibitor could be $10,000–$50,000/course.
tam_gross: 150 patients × $50,000 = $7.5M; biodefense context for mass casualty ~$500M government procurement.
peak_sales: ~$20–50M at commercial level; primarily government procurement/BARDA contracts.
registrational_endpoint: Time to mechanical ventilation weaning; functional recovery (modified Rankin Scale or activities of daily living) at Day 30. FDA Animal Rule applies for mass-casualty scenarios.
btd_threshold_vs_soc: Mechanical ventilation duration reduced by ≥30% vs. supportive care; or ICU length of stay <14 days vs. historical ~5–6 weeks without HBAT.

symptoms: Rapidly spreading tissue necrosis, intense pain, gas in tissue, shock
treatment_goal: Debride necrotic tissue, kill Clostridium, prevent fatal toxemia
first_line_soc: Emergent surgical debridement + IV penicillin G + clindamycin (dual antibiotic therapy to both kill bacteria and inhibit toxin production).
treatment_algorithm: Same principles as clostridial myonecrosis: emergent surgery mandatory, IV penicillin G (10–24M units/day) + clindamycin 900 mg q8h. Hyperbaric oxygen (HBO) when available. IVIG may be considered for streptococcal toxic shock but evidence limited for clostridial. Early initiation of antibiotics pre-operatively. Mortality ~25–70% even with treatment. 2024: No new agents; IDSA SSTI guidelines 2014 updated review 2024.
us_prevalence: ~500–1,000 clostridial necrotizing soft-tissue infections/yr; broader NSTI ~15,000/yr (including Group A Streptococcus). (NHSN/literature)
patients_needing_tx: ~1,000 clostridial NSTI patients/yr; all require immediate IV antibiotics + surgery.
price: $5,000–$20,000 per course (antitoxin/IVIG + IV antibiotics); per-course acute price.
peak_sales: $5–15M for novel anti-clostridial toxin antibody; limited commercial opportunity.
registrational_endpoint: 28-day mortality + limb/tissue salvage rate; SOFA score improvement at 72 hours.
btd_threshold_vs_soc: 28-day mortality ≤15% vs. ~30–40% historical for severe clostridial NSTI + antitoxin adjunct.

symptoms: Gas gangrene, severe tissue pain, crepitus, systemic toxemia
treatment_goal: Debride infected tissue, kill Clostridium, prevent toxin spread
first_line_soc: Surgical debridement/amputation + high-dose IV penicillin G (10–24 million units/day) + clindamycin (protein synthesis inhibitor to halt toxin production); hyperbaric oxygen as adjunct.
treatment_algorithm: Gas gangrene (myonecrosis): emergent surgical debridement mandatory + IV penicillin G + clindamycin (clindamycin inhibits toxin synthesis beyond killing). Penicillin-allergic: metronidazole or clindamycin monotherapy. Hyperbaric oxygen (HBO) controversial but used in refractory cases. Antitoxin available in some European countries but not FDA-approved. Mild soft-tissue infections: amoxicillin-clavulanate oral. 2024: No new agents for clostridial gas gangrene.
us_prevalence: ~1,000–2,000 gas gangrene cases/yr; broader clostridial infections ~50,000 including C. difficile (counted separately). (Literature estimate)
patients_needing_tx: ~1,500 non-CDI clostridial infections/yr requiring IV antibiotics; all need treatment (high mortality without).
price: $3,000–$10,000 per course IV antibiotics for severe gas gangrene; antitoxin if available $20,000–$50,000.
peak_sales: $5–15M for novel anticlostridial agent or antitoxin; limited market.
registrational_endpoint: 28-day mortality + limb salvage rate; resolution of crepitus and imaging evidence of gas gangrene.
btd_threshold_vs_soc: Limb salvage ≥70% at 28 days vs. ~50% historical for standard debridement + antibiotics; or 28-day mortality <15% vs. ~30% historically.

symptoms: Constipation, weak cry, floppy muscle tone, feeding difficulty, respiratory failure in infants
treatment_goal: Neutralize botulinum toxin, support respiration, restore muscle function
first_line_soc: BabyBIG (Botulism Immune Globulin Intravenous [Human], BIG-IV) — the FDA-approved heptavalent treatment for infant botulism; single IV dose administered early.
treatment_algorithm: Step 1: BabyBIG (BIG-IV) single IV infusion as early as possible after diagnosis — reduces hospital stay by ~3.5 weeks. Step 2: Supportive care: mechanical ventilation if respiratory failure, NG/nasogastric tube feeding, stool management. Step 3: Do NOT give antibiotics (aminoglycosides potentiate neuromuscular blockade; antibiotics lyse C. botulinum causing toxin release). Avoid cathartics. Step 4: CDC Strategic National Stockpile provides heptavalent botulinum antitoxin (HBAT) for wound/foodborne botulism in adults. 2024: No new agents for infant botulism; BabyBIG remains sole approved product.
us_prevalence: ~100–170 US infant botulism cases/yr (most common form of botulism in the US); California highest incidence. (CDC botulism surveillance)
patients_needing_tx: ~110–170 infants/yr; all require BabyBIG and supportive care.
price: BabyBIG: ~$45,300 per vial (current price, California Department of Health); novel monoclonal cocktail: $50,000–$100,000 per infusion.
peak_sales: $10–20M for improved next-generation antitoxin at 60–70% market share (replacing BabyBIG with recombinant product).
registrational_endpoint: Mean hospital length of stay reduction (days); time to full oral feeding; time to discontinuation of mechanical ventilation vs. historical control.
btd_threshold_vs_soc: Mean hospital stay <2 weeks vs. ~5.5 weeks for untreated historical; or ≥50% reduction in ICU LOS vs. BabyBIG historical (3.5 week reduction).

symptoms: Severe abdominal pain, bloody diarrhea, small bowel necrosis
treatment_goal: Resect necrotic bowel, eradicate C. perfringens, prevent toxemia death
first_line_soc: Surgical resection of necrotic bowel + IV penicillin G + metronidazole; supportive ICU care.
treatment_algorithm: Surgical bowel resection (often extensive) is mainstay. Antibiotic: IV penicillin G + metronidazole or ampicillin-sulbactam. C. perfringens enterotoxin antitoxin: experimental in some countries. Nutritional support post-resection critical. Very high mortality (40–100%) without surgery. Rare in US (pig-bel in Papua New Guinea; sporadic US cases in diabetics/immunocompromised). 2024: No new drugs.
us_prevalence: <100 US cases/yr (rare, primarily in diabetics, immunocompromised, or malnourished populations). (Literature/case series)
patients_needing_tx: <100 US patients/yr; all require surgical + antibiotic treatment.
price: $5,000–$20,000 per course (C. perfringens antitoxin + IV antibiotics); orphan-level pricing.
registrational_endpoint: 30-day survival + avoidance of massive bowel resection; clinical cure at hospital discharge.
btd_threshold_vs_soc: 30-day survival ≥70% vs. ~40–50% historical without antitoxin; avoidance of bowel resection in mild-moderate cases.

symptoms: Crampy abdominal pain, watery diarrhea (no fever, no vomiting)
treatment_goal: Supportive hydration; self-limiting illness (no antibiotics needed)
first_line_soc: None — self-limiting gastroenteritis; supportive care (oral hydration) only. Antibiotics not indicated.
treatment_algorithm: Supportive care only: oral fluids, electrolytes. Illness typically resolves in 24–48 hours. Antibiotics not recommended (risk of worsening by disrupting microbiome or causing antibiotic-associated diarrhea). In rare severe cases or immunocompromised patients with invasive disease: metronidazole or penicillin G. 2024: No pharmacologic treatment advances.
us_prevalence: ~965,000 US cases/yr (one of most common foodborne illnesses; 2nd after norovirus). (CDC foodborne estimates 2024)
patients_needing_tx: No pharmacologic treatment needed for typical cases; <5,000/yr may need IV hydration for severe dehydration. Novel drug market negligible.
price: N/A — no pharmacologic treatment needed for typical cases.
peak_sales: N/A — no viable pharmacologic market for self-limiting foodborne illness.
registrational_endpoint: N/A — no pharmacologic registration endpoint needed.

symptoms: Muscle stiffness and spasms, lockjaw, rigid abdomen, autonomic instability
treatment_goal: Neutralize tetanus toxin, control spasms, support airway, eradicate Clostridium tetani
first_line_soc: Human tetanus immune globulin (TIG, HTIG — Hypertet S/D) 3,000–6,000 units IM (to neutralize unbound toxin); metronidazole 500 mg IV q8h × 10 days (antibiotic to eliminate C. tetani); benzodiazepines (diazepam IV) for spasm control.
treatment_algorithm: Step 1: HTIG 3,000–6,000 units IM (intrathecal TIG controversial). Step 2: Wound debridement/cleaning. Step 3: Metronidazole 500 mg IV q8h × 7–10 days (superior to penicillin G). Step 4: Benzodiazepines (diazepam, lorazepam) for muscle spasms; propofol or midazolam infusion for severe spasms. Step 5: Magnesium sulfate IV for autonomic instability; labetalol for hypertension. Step 6: Mechanical ventilation (tracheostomy often needed). Tetanus toxoid vaccination series to prevent recurrence. ICU admission essentially universal for generalized tetanus.
us_prevalence: ~30–40 tetanus cases/yr in US; rare due to high vaccination rates. (CDC NNDSS 2024)
patients_needing_tx: ~35 US patients/yr; all require ICU-level supportive care and TIG.
price: Novel tetanus toxin antidote/inhibitor: $10,000–$50,000 per course (biological product analogous to TIG but with broader activity); TIG current price ~$1,000–3,000.
peak_sales: <$2M; micro-niche. Biodefense/stockpile contracts may provide limited upside.
registrational_endpoint: All-cause mortality at Day 30; time to full muscle relaxation; ventilator-free days at Day 28.
btd_threshold_vs_soc: 30-day mortality <5% vs. ~10–20% for standard HTIG/metronidazole; or reduction in ICU duration by ≥5 days.

symptoms: Ear infection, sinus congestion, cough, purulent sputum, COPD exacerbation
treatment_goal: Eradicate Moraxella catarrhalis, resolve respiratory infection, prevent COPD worsening
first_line_soc: Amoxicillin-clavulanate (Augmentin, aminopenicillin + BLI) orally; or TMP-SMX, azithromycin, or 2nd/3rd-gen cephalosporins. M. catarrhalis produces beta-lactamase in >90% of strains — amoxicillin monotherapy ineffective.
treatment_algorithm: Acute otitis media: amoxicillin-clavulanate 875/125 mg BID × 5–7 days. COPD exacerbation: azithromycin 500 mg/day × 3 days or doxycycline 100 mg BID × 5–7 days or amoxicillin-clavulanate. Sinusitis: amoxicillin-clavulanate × 5 days. 2nd-gen cephalosporins (cefuroxime, cefprozil) effective. Fluoroquinolones (levofloxacin, moxifloxacin) for severe disease. 2024: Increasing prevalence as AOM and COPD pathogen; no new dedicated agents.
us_prevalence: ~10–15 million US M. catarrhalis infections/yr (primarily otitis media in children + COPD exacerbations in adults). (CDC RTI data)
patients_needing_tx: ~8–10 million patients/yr need antibiotic treatment.
price: $200–$500 per course (novel oral beta-lactamase-stable agent).
peak_sales: $100–300M for novel oral RTI antibiotic with M. catarrhalis activity at 5% market share (large market dominated by generics).
registrational_endpoint: Clinical cure at Day 10–14 for acute otitis media (primary endpoint per FDA AOM guidance); COPD exacerbation resolution at Day 7.
btd_threshold_vs_soc: Clinical cure ≥88% at Day 14 for AOM with M. catarrhalis vs. ~82% for amoxicillin-clavulanate; or COPD exacerbation resolution ≥80% vs. ~70% for azithromycin.

symptoms: Pneumonia, wound infection, fever, sepsis, organ failure
treatment_goal: Eliminate carbapenem-resistant pathogen, prevent mortality
first_line_soc: Colistin (polymyxin E) or polymyxin B combined with a carbapenem or sulbactam; for carbapenem-susceptible strains, imipenem or meropenem alone. Sulbactam-durlobactam (Xacduro, FDA-approved 2023) is now preferred for carbapenem-resistant Acinetobacter baumannii (CRAB) pneumonia.
treatment_algorithm: Step 1: Determine susceptibility — if carbapenem-susceptible, use imipenem or meropenem. Step 2: For CRAB, sulbactam-durlobactam (Xacduro) IV is first choice for hospital-acquired/ventilator-associated pneumonia per 2023 IDSA guidance. Step 3: Cefiderocol (Fetroja) is an alternative for CRAB if sulbactam-durlobactam unavailable. Step 4: Colistin + meropenem or colistin + rifampin historically used but higher toxicity and inferior to newer agents. Tigecycline (Tygacil) monotherapy inferior; used only in combination. Minocycline IV active against some strains. 2024–2025: Combination regimens being refined; new agents aztreonam-avibactam under evaluation for some MDR gram-negatives.
us_prevalence: ~7,000–12,000 CRAB hospital infections/yr in the US; ~500,000 total Acinetobacter hospital-associated infections (CDC NHSN data); mortality 20–40% for CRAB.
patients_needing_tx: All ~12,000 CRAB cases require IV antimicrobial treatment; ~100,000 total Acinetobacter infections require pharmacologic therapy.
price: $15,000–$25,000 per course (10-day IV); rationale: ultra-premium pricing for CRAB active agent given XDR pathogen, limited alternatives, and ICU setting (comparable to Xacduro ~$17K/course).
peak_sales: ~$200–$400M at 40–50% CRAB market share (6,000 patients × $20,000 = $120M; with pricing leverage and expanded indication ~$300M peak)
registrational_endpoint: 28-day all-cause mortality or clinical cure (composite) in CRAB hospital-acquired/ventilator-associated pneumonia; FDA has accepted 28-day mortality as primary endpoint.
btd_threshold_vs_soc: 28-day all-cause mortality <25% vs. ~40% historical for colistin-based regimens; or clinical cure ≥65% vs. ~45% for comparator.

symptoms: Pneumonia, wound infection, UTI, sepsis, ear infection
treatment_goal: Eradicate MDR Pseudomonas, prevent septic death, preserve lung function
first_line_soc: Piperacillin-tazobactam (Zosyn, antipseudomonal beta-lactam/BLI) 4.5g IV q6h extended infusion for susceptible strains; ceftolozane-tazobactam (Zerbaxa) or ceftazidime-avibactam (Avycaz) for MDR Pseudomonas aeruginosa.
treatment_algorithm: Non-MDR: piperacillin-tazobactam, cefepime, meropenem, or ciprofloxacin (combination often used for severe infection). MDR Pseudomonas: ceftolozane-tazobactam (Zerbaxa) 1.5g IV q8h extended infusion — preferred for MDR-PA VAP/HAP and cUTI. Ceftazidime-avibactam (Avycaz) for KPC/OXA-48 co-producers. Carbapenem-resistant (CRPA): ceftolozane-tazobactam first choice; imipenem-cilastatin-relebactam (Recarbrio) active against some CRPA. Cystic fibrosis chronic Pseudomonas: inhaled tobramycin (TOBI), aztreonam lysine (Cayston), inhaled colistin. 2024–2025: aztreonam-avibactam (Aztreonam-avibactam, Pfizer/AstraZeneca) Phase 3 complete for metallo-beta-lactamase (MBL) producers; cefepime-zidebactam in Phase 3.
us_prevalence: ~51,000 MDR Pseudomonas healthcare-associated infections/yr; ~32,000 deaths attributable to all resistant gram-negatives including Pseudomonas. Total Pseudomonas infections ~400,000/yr. (CDC 2024 Antibiotic Resistance Threats Report)
patients_needing_tx: ~400,000 patients/yr require antipseudomonal antibiotics; ~51,000 MDR cases need novel BL/BLI combinations; ~10,000 CF patients on chronic antipseudomonal therapy.
price: $15,000–$30,000 per course (10–14-day IV for MDR-PA pneumonia/bacteremia); rationale: comparable to Zerbaxa/Avycaz pricing (~$20K/course) in ICU/hospital setting.
peak_sales: ~$400–700M at 30–40% MDR-PA share (20,000 patients × $22,000 = $440M); CF chronic market adds ~$100M
registrational_endpoint: Clinical cure at Day 7–14 (composite clinical + microbiological) for HAP/VAP in MDR-PA; FDA HAP/VAP guidance requires 28-day all-cause mortality or Day 14 clinical cure as primary endpoint.
btd_threshold_vs_soc: Clinical cure ≥65% at Day 14 for CRPA VAP vs. ~40% for colistin-based regimens; or 28-day mortality <20% vs. ~35% for CRPA historical control.

symptoms: Pneumonia, UTI, sepsis, wound infection, fever, multi-organ failure
treatment_goal: Eradicate carbapenem-resistant gram-negative pathogen, prevent septic death
first_line_soc: Carbapenem-susceptible: meropenem or imipenem-cilastatin (beta-lactam carbapenems). KPC-producing CRKP: ceftazidime-avibactam (Avycaz, cephalosporin + BLI) or meropenem-vaborbactam (Vabomere) or imipenem-cilastatin-relebactam (Recarbrio).
treatment_algorithm: Carbapenem-susceptible: meropenem 1–2g IV q8h or imipenem 500 mg IV q6h. KPC-producing CRKP: ceftazidime-avibactam (Avycaz) 2.5g IV q8h × 7–14 days (preferred per IDSA 2023 CRKP guidance) or meropenem-vaborbactam (Vabomere) or imipenem-relebactam (Recarbrio). MBL-producing (NDM, VIM): aztreonam-avibactam (Emblaveo, FDA-approved 2024 for gram-negative complicated UTI/IAI) or ceftazidime-avibactam + aztreonam combination. Aminoglycosides (amikacin, gentamicin) for susceptible strains. Tigecycline adjunct in combo only. Colistin: reserved for pan-resistant strains. Oral step-down options limited; fosfomycin for UTI. 2024–2025: Aztreonam-avibactam (Emblaveo) FDA-approved January 2024 for MBL-producing Enterobacterales.
us_prevalence: ~60,000–80,000 CRKP/carbapenem-resistant Enterobacterales (CRE) hospital infections/yr; total Klebsiella/Enterobacter infections >1 million/yr. (CDC NHSN/AR threats)
patients_needing_tx: ~70,000 CRE patients/yr need novel beta-lactam/BLI combinations; ~1M total Enterobacterales infections need antibiotics.
price: $15,000–$35,000 per course for novel beta-lactam/BLI (Avycaz ~$20K/course; Emblaveo ~$25K/course).
peak_sales: $500M–$1.5B for next-gen CRE-active agent (pan-BLI coverage including MBL) at 30–40% CRE market share.
registrational_endpoint: Clinical cure at Test-of-Cure Day 7–14 for cUTI/IAI (FDA complicated UTI or IAI endpoints); 28-day clinical success for bacteremia/pneumonia.
btd_threshold_vs_soc: Clinical cure ≥70% in CRE/MBL-producing Enterobacterales vs. ~40–50% for polymyxin-based regimens; or mortality <15% vs. ~40% for colistin-treated CRE.

symptoms: Diarrhea (watery or bloody), UTI, bacteremia, hemolytic uremic syndrome
treatment_goal: Eradicate E. coli, prevent HUS, treat sepsis
first_line_soc: Varies by infection type. UTI: TMP-SMX or nitrofurantoin (see Bacterial UTI). Bacteremia: piperacillin-tazobactam or cefepime; ESBL: ertapenem/meropenem. EHEC (O157:H7): supportive — avoid antibiotics (may trigger HUS toxin release per AAP guidelines).
treatment_algorithm: UTI: TMP-SMX or nitrofurantoin empirically; ESBL: oral fosfomycin, IV ertapenem. ETEC diarrhea (traveler's): azithromycin or rifaximin (Xifaxan) × 3 days. EHEC O157:H7: NO antibiotics — increased HUS risk; supportive only (fluids, dialysis for HUS). EPEC/EAEC/EIEC: azithromycin × 3 days. Bacteremia/sepsis: carbapenem for ESBL-producing E. coli. Ceftolozane-tazobactam, ceftazidime-avibactam for MDR-ESBL not carbapenem-susceptible. 2024–2025: Growing ESBL prevalence; novel ESBL-active oral agents under development.
us_prevalence: ~26 million foodborne E. coli episodes/yr; ~500,000 ESBL-producing E. coli infections; ~70,000 EHEC/STEC infections; ~2,300 HUS cases. (CDC FoodNet/NHSN 2024)
patients_needing_tx: ~500,000 patients/yr need pharmacologic treatment (excluding self-limiting EHEC); ESBL-E infections are key target.
price: $500–$5,000 per course (UTI oral agent); $10,000–$25,000 for ESBL bacteremia IV treatment. Per-course price.
peak_sales: $200–500M for novel ESBL-active oral agent for E. coli UTI/uncomplicated bacteremia (e.g., tebipenem pivoxil - withdrawn FDA 2022; fosmanogepix in development).
registrational_endpoint: Composite microbiological + clinical cure at Test of Cure (Day 5–9 post-treatment) for UTI/complicated UTI per FDA guidance.
btd_threshold_vs_soc: Composite cure ≥75% for ESBL-E. coli UTI vs. ~50–60% for IV ertapenem (oral agent not inferior to IV).

symptoms: Meningitis, epiglottitis, pneumonia, otitis media, bacteremia, fever
treatment_goal: Eradicate H. influenzae, prevent meningitis complications, cure pneumonia
first_line_soc: Ceftriaxone (3rd-gen cephalosporin) 2g IV daily for serious infections (meningitis, pneumonia, bacteremia); amoxicillin-clavulanate (Augmentin) for otitis media and mild respiratory infections.
treatment_algorithm: Meningitis: ceftriaxone 2g IV q12h × 7–10 days + dexamethasone 0.15 mg/kg q6h × 4 days. Epiglottitis: ceftriaxone IV + airway management. Pneumonia: amoxicillin-clavulanate or ceftriaxone depending on severity and beta-lactamase status. Otitis media: amoxicillin 80–90 mg/kg/day or amoxicillin-clavulanate. Beta-lactamase-negative ampicillin-sensitive strains (BLNAS): ampicillin. Chloramphenicol for severe penicillin allergy (rarely used). Hib vaccine prevents invasive Hib disease. 2024: Non-typeable H. influenzae (NTHi) rising as cause of COPD exacerbations and otitis; no new drug approvals.
us_prevalence: ~6,000 invasive Hib/NTHi cases/yr post-vaccination era; ~5 million otitis media episodes partly due to NTHi. (CDC Active Bacterial Core surveillance)
patients_needing_tx: ~6,000 invasive cases need IV antibiotics; ~1–2 million NTHi otitis/sinusitis need oral antibiotics.
price: $500–$2,000 per course for oral agent active against beta-lactamase-positive strains; $5,000–$10,000 for novel IV agent.
peak_sales: $50–100M for novel NTHi-active oral agent (5–10% share of respiratory indication) + invasive disease.
registrational_endpoint: Clinical cure at Test-of-Cure Day 10–14 for acute otitis media; microbiological eradication + clinical cure for invasive disease.
btd_threshold_vs_soc: Clinical cure ≥85% for NTHi AOM vs. ~78% for amoxicillin; or invasive disease cure ≥95% for novel IV agent.

symptoms: Sudden fever, stiff neck, petechial/purpuric rash, severe headache, photophobia
treatment_goal: Eradicate Neisseria meningitidis, prevent septic shock and death within hours
first_line_soc: IV penicillin G 4 million units q4h × 7 days; or ceftriaxone (3rd-gen cephalosporin) 2g IV q12h × 7 days (preferred for empiric therapy). Dexamethasone 0.15 mg/kg q6h × 4 days adjunct.
treatment_algorithm: Empiric: ceftriaxone 2g IV q12h immediately. Once confirmed meningococcal: penicillin G (if susceptible) or ceftriaxone. Penicillin allergy: chloramphenicol or meropenem. Adjunct: dexamethasone to reduce neurological sequelae. Close contacts: rifampin 600 mg q12h × 2 days or ciprofloxacin 500 mg single dose or ceftriaxone 250 mg IM. Prevention: MenACWY vaccines (Menactra, Menveo, MenQuadfi), MenB vaccines (Bexsero, Trumenba) — recommended for adolescents and college students. Novel mAb-based treatments in early pipeline. 2024 outbreak: MenB clonal complex 11 outbreak in college students; Trumenba use expanded.
us_prevalence: ~300–500 US invasive meningococcal cases/yr; case fatality rate ~10–15%; 20% with serious sequelae. (CDC ABCs data 2024)
patients_needing_tx: ~400 patients/yr; all require emergency IV antibiotics.
price: $5,000–$20,000 per course (novel bactericidal agent or adjunctive mAb targeting meningococcal toxin).
tam_gross: 400 patients × $12,000 = $4.8M (antibiotics); vaccine market $500M–$1B for MenB vaccines.
peak_sales: MenB vaccine (Bexsero/Trumenba): $400–600M combined annual US sales; novel therapeutic agent: <$10M.
registrational_endpoint: Clinical cure and 30-day survival; Glasgow Outcome Score ≥4 at Day 30; neurological sequelae rate.
btd_threshold_vs_soc: 30-day mortality <3% vs. ~10–15% for ceftriaxone; or adjunctive anti-meningococcal mAb reducing sequelae rate from 20% to <10%.

symptoms: UTI, wound infection, pneumonia, bacteremia, fever, foul-smelling urine
treatment_goal: Eradicate Proteus/Providencia/Morganella, treat UTI/bacteremia, clear ESBL/MDR strains
first_line_soc: TMP-SMX DS BID × 7 days or ciprofloxacin 500 mg BID × 7 days for uncomplicated UTI. IV ceftriaxone or cefepime for bacteremia. ESBL-producing Proteus: meropenem or ertapenem (carbapenem).
treatment_algorithm: Uncomplicated UTI: TMP-SMX or ciprofloxacin × 7 days. Complicated UTI/pyelonephritis: ceftriaxone or ciprofloxacin IV × 10–14 days. Bacteremia: ceftriaxone 2g IV q24h × 14 days. ESBL Proteus: ertapenem 1g IV q24h (preferred, narrower spectrum). MDR Morganella/Providencia: imipenem or meropenem. Urease inhibitors (acetohydroxamic acid) for struvite stones caused by Proteus UTI. Novel: aztreonam-avibactam for ESBL Proteeae. 2024: ESBL-producing Proteus increasing.
us_prevalence: ~200,000 significant Proteeae (Proteus mirabilis, Providencia, Morganella) infections/yr in US (primarily hospital UTI and bacteremia). (CDC NHSN)
patients_needing_tx: ~200,000 patients/yr; all diagnosed cases require antibiotics.
price: $500–$3,000 per course (oral agent for ESBL Proteeae UTI); $5,000–$15,000 IV for systemic ESBL infection.
tam_gross: 200,000 patients × $2,000 avg = $400M (part of broader gram-negative antibiotic market)
peak_sales: $50–100M for novel oral ESBL-Proteus UTI agent at 10–15% market share.
registrational_endpoint: Microbiological cure (urine culture <10³ CFU/mL) at Test-of-Cure Day 5–7 for cUTI; clinical cure composite at Day 14.
btd_threshold_vs_soc: Microbiological cure ≥75% for ESBL Proteeae cUTI vs. ~55% for ertapenem (via oral route); or novel oral agent non-inferior to IV ertapenem (95% CI lower bound >-12.5%).

symptoms: Nausea, vomiting, diarrhea, abdominal cramps, fever
treatment_goal: Reduce severity and duration, prevent bacteremia in high-risk patients
first_line_soc: Most non-typhoidal Salmonella gastroenteritis is self-limiting — antibiotics not routinely recommended for immunocompetent patients. For severe disease, bacteremia, or high-risk hosts: azithromycin (macrolide) or ciprofloxacin (fluoroquinolone); resistance rising, so susceptibility testing essential.
treatment_algorithm: Uncomplicated non-typhoidal gastroenteritis: supportive care only (no antibiotics in immunocompetent). High-risk (immunocompromised, infants <3 months, sickle cell, hemolytic conditions, bacteremia): azithromycin 1g day 1 then 500 mg × 6 days, or ciprofloxacin 500 mg BID × 5–7 days pending sensitivity. MDR Salmonella (rising): ceftriaxone IV as empiric for bacteremic/severe cases. Typhoid fever (S. Typhi): separate entity — azithromycin or ceftriaxone first-line. 2024–2025: MDR non-typhoidal Salmonella increasing; IDSA guidance update on azithromycin as preferred oral agent.
us_prevalence: ~1.35 million non-typhoidal Salmonella infections/yr in US; ~26,500 hospitalizations, ~420 deaths. (CDC FoodNet 2024)
patients_needing_tx: ~200,000–300,000 patients/yr warrant antibiotic treatment (bacteremic, severe, immunocompromised); majority self-resolve without antibiotics.
price: $500–$1,500 per course (novel oral agent for MDR Salmonella); per-course pricing for acute infection.
peak_sales: ~$100–200M at 20–30% share for MDR Salmonella indication; limited by generic azithromycin/ciprofloxacin competition.
registrational_endpoint: Stool culture clearance (microbiological cure) at Day 7 for bacteremic Salmonella; clinical cure (resolution of fever, diarrhea) at Day 5 for gastroenteritis.
btd_threshold_vs_soc: Microbiological cure ≥80% at Day 7 for MDR Salmonella bacteremia vs. ~55% for ceftriaxone; or 30-day recurrence rate <5% vs. ~15%.

symptoms: Bloody diarrhea, tenesmus, fever, abdominal cramps
treatment_goal: Shorten illness, prevent HUS, reduce transmission especially in high-risk settings
first_line_soc: Azithromycin 500 mg daily × 3 days (preferred in US due to fluoroquinolone resistance) or ciprofloxacin 500 mg BID × 3 days (if susceptible). Ceftriaxone 1g IV daily × 3–5 days for severe disease or XDR strains.
treatment_algorithm: Susceptible Shigella: ciprofloxacin 500 mg BID × 3 days or azithromycin 500 mg × 3 days. Pediatric: azithromycin 12 mg/kg/day × 5 days. MDR Shigella: ceftriaxone IV 1g × 3–5 days; XDR Shigella (resistant to ceftriaxone + azithromycin + fluoroquinolones — epidemic among MSM in US): fosfomycin (limited data), carbapenems for bacteremia, or experimental regimens. Oral rehydration therapy essential. Do NOT use antidiarrheal agents (risk of toxic megacolon). 2024–2025: CDC alert on XDR Shigella sonnei among MSM; outbreak management challenge.
us_prevalence: ~450,000 cases/yr; ~77,000 hospitalizations. MDR Shigella among MSM rising sharply. (CDC FoodNet/STI Surveillance 2024)
patients_needing_tx: ~400,000 patients/yr needing antibiotics (excluding self-limiting mild cases); ~10,000–20,000 with MDR/XDR needing alternative agents.
price: $1,000–$2,500 per course for XDR Shigella indication; per-course acute infection pricing.
tam_gross: 400,000 × $500 avg (generics for susceptible; novel for MDR) = $200M; MDR/XDR premium segment ~$1,500 × 20,000 = $30M
peak_sales: ~$50–100M for novel agent with XDR Shigella activity + broader enteric pathogen coverage; limited by small XDR population currently.
registrational_endpoint: Microbiological cure (stool culture negative) at Day 7; clinical cure (resolution of dysentery) at Day 5.
btd_threshold_vs_soc: Cure rate ≥90% for XDR Shigella vs. ~40–50% for azithromycin (resistant strains); or superiority over ceftriaxone with oral route.

disease: H7 and Other Enterohemorrhagic E. coli (EHEC)
symptoms: Bloody diarrhea, severe abdominal cramps, hemolytic uremic syndrome (HUS)
treatment_goal: Supportive care; prevent HUS progression, manage renal failure
first_line_soc: No antibiotics recommended — antibiotics increase Shiga toxin release and HUS risk. Treatment is supportive: IV fluid resuscitation, avoid antidiarrheal agents, monitor for HUS (platelets, creatinine, CBC). For established HUS: dialysis if needed.
treatment_algorithm: Step 1: Avoid antibiotics (fluoroquinolones, TMP-SMX, beta-lactams all associated with increased Shiga toxin release and 2–5× higher HUS risk in observational studies). Step 2: IV fluid resuscitation (isotonic saline) — reduces HUS severity. Step 3: Monitor CBC, creatinine, LDH daily. Step 4: HUS develops in ~5–15% of STEC O157:H7 infections: dialysis (peritoneal or hemodialysis), blood transfusion for hemolytic anemia, platelet transfusion generally avoided. Step 5: Eculizumab (complement C5 inhibitor, Soliris) used off-label for severe HUS — controversial evidence; case series positive, RCT data limited. Step 6: Plasma exchange for atypical HUS differentiation. Prophylactic: vaccine (bovine O157 vaccination reduces human exposure); anti-Shiga toxin mAb (urtoxazumab — Phase 3 trials failed to reduce HUS). 2024: IDSA maintains no antibiotic recommendation; anti-Shiga toxin antibody development ongoing.
us_prevalence: ~265,000 STEC infections/yr in US; ~73,000 STEC O157:H7 specifically; ~3,600 hospitalizations, ~30 deaths/yr. HUS: ~300–500 cases/yr. (CDC FoodNet 2024)
patients_needing_tx: ~265,000 STEC patients require medical management; ~300–500 HUS patients require dialysis/eculizumab annually.
price: $5,000–$50,000 per course for anti-Shiga toxin mAb or eculizumab-like complement inhibitor (per-course for acute HUS); eculizumab ~$500,000/yr annual but used acutely ~$15,000–$50,000 per episode.
tam_gross: 500 HUS patients × $25,000 avg = $12.5M; anti-Shiga toxin preventive approach in 73,000 at-risk × $3,000 = $219M
peak_sales: ~$50–100M for anti-Shiga toxin monoclonal antibody preventing HUS in STEC O157:H7 at 20–30% at-risk patient utilization; complement inhibitor for established HUS ~$20–40M.
registrational_endpoint: Prevention of HUS development at Day 14 in STEC O157:H7-confirmed children; or dialysis-free survival in established HUS at Day 28.
btd_threshold_vs_soc: HUS incidence ≤3% in STEC O157:H7-infected children treated with anti-Stx mAb vs. ~10–15% placebo; or dialysis-free survival ≥75% for established HUS vs. ~60% for supportive care.

symptoms: Bloody diarrhea, cramping, fever, nausea after poultry exposure
treatment_goal: Eradicate pathogen, prevent bacteremia, shorten illness
first_line_soc: Most cases self-limiting; for moderate-severe disease: azithromycin (macrolide) 500 mg/day × 3–5 days first-line. Fluoroquinolones (ciprofloxacin) widely used but resistance >25% globally; ciprofloxacin still acceptable if susceptible.
treatment_algorithm: Mild-moderate: supportive (ORS). Severe/bloody: azithromycin 500 mg/day × 3–5 days (preferred over fluoroquinolones due to resistance). Fluoroquinolone-resistant strains: azithromycin or erythromycin. Bacteremia: gentamicin IV or meropenem. Immunocompromised: longer therapy (7–14 days). Guillain-Barré prevention not achieved by antibiotic treatment. 2024: Azithromycin resistance emerging (~10%); gepotidacin (GSK) active vs. Campylobacter.
us_prevalence: ~1.5 million US cases/yr (second most common foodborne bacterial illness); ~9,000 hospitalizations. (CDC FoodNet 2024)
patients_needing_tx: ~200,000–300,000 patients/yr require antibiotic treatment (severe, immunocompromised, or persistent disease); majority self-resolve.
price: $300–$1,000 per course (oral agent vs. fluoroquinolone-resistant strains); per-course acute price.
registrational_endpoint: Clinical cure (cessation of diarrhea, resolution of fever) at Day 5; microbiological eradication at end of treatment.
btd_threshold_vs_soc: Clinical cure ≥85% by Day 3 vs. ~70% for azithromycin; or active against azithromycin/fluoroquinolone dual-resistant Campylobacter.

symptoms: Diarrhea, fever, abdominal cramps, nausea, vomiting, bloody stool
treatment_goal: Supportive care; antibiotics for high-risk/severe cases; prevent bacteremia
first_line_soc: Most uncomplicated cases: supportive care only (self-limiting 4–7 days). Antibiotics for severe/invasive/high-risk patients: ciprofloxacin (fluoroquinolone) 500 mg BID × 5–7 days or azithromycin 500 mg/day × 3 days; IV ceftriaxone for bacteremia.
treatment_algorithm: Uncomplicated gastroenteritis: fluid replacement, avoid routine antibiotics (prolong shedding, select resistance). High-risk (infants <12 months, elderly >65, immunocompromised, vascular grafts, valvular disease): ciprofloxacin 500 mg BID × 7 days or azithromycin 500 mg × 3 days. Bacteremia/invasive: IV ceftriaxone 2g/day × 7–14 days. Ciprofloxacin-resistant (S. Typhimurium DT104): azithromycin or ceftriaxone. MDR Salmonella (AmpC + ESBL): carbapenems. 2024: XDR S. Typhi (ceftriaxone + cipro resistant) emerging; Salmonella NARMS showing rising resistance.
us_prevalence: ~1.35 million US nontyphoidal Salmonella cases/yr; ~26,500 hospitalizations; ~420 deaths/yr. (CDC FoodNet 2024)
patients_needing_tx: ~100,000–150,000 high-risk/severe US patients/yr need antibiotic treatment; majority self-manage without antibiotics.
price: $300–$1,000 per course (novel oral agent for drug-resistant invasive Salmonella; per-course acute).
peak_sales: $30–80M for novel Salmonella-active agent (part of broader enteric gram-negative antibiotic market) at 15–20% high-risk share.
registrational_endpoint: Clinical cure at Day 7 (stool formed, afebrile, no bloody stools); blood culture negativity for bacteremia at Day 3.
btd_threshold_vs_soc: Clinical cure ≥90% at Day 7 for MDR/XDR Salmonella invasive disease vs. ~70% for ceftriaxone; or single-dose oral agent equivalent to 5-day ciprofloxacin.

symptoms: Severe uncontrollable coughing fits, whooping sound on inspiration, post-cough vomiting
treatment_goal: Eradicate Bordetella pertussis, reduce cough duration, prevent transmission and infant death
first_line_soc: Azithromycin (macrolide) 500 mg × 1 dose then 250 mg/day × 4 days (adults); or erythromycin × 14 days; or clarithromycin × 7 days. TMP-SMX for macrolide-allergic patients.
treatment_algorithm: Azithromycin 500 mg day 1, 250 mg days 2–5 (adult and children >6 months) or erythromycin 2g/day × 14 days or clarithromycin 500 mg BID × 7 days. Infants <1 month: azithromycin (erythromycin associated with pyloric stenosis). TMP-SMX DS BID × 14 days for macrolide allergy. Note: antibiotics primarily reduce transmission and prevent spread; minimal clinical benefit if started after paroxysmal phase (>3 weeks). Hospitalized infants: ICU with respiratory support. Prevention: Tdap/DTaP vaccines. Chemoprophylaxis of household contacts. 2024: Pertussis resurgence with waning immunity post-Tdap.
us_prevalence: ~10,000–20,000 reported US cases/yr; estimated true incidence ~800,000–1.2 million (massive underreporting). (CDC pertussis surveillance 2024)
patients_needing_tx: ~200,000 US patients/yr who seek care for pertussis and are treated; all infants with suspected pertussis treated immediately.
price: $200–$600 per course (novel pertussis-active agent providing faster symptom relief; per-course).
peak_sales: $30–80M for novel pertussis antitoxin or antibiotic with proven symptom reduction (beyond current macrolide benefit); $300–600M for next-gen pertussis vaccine.
registrational_endpoint: Reduction in number of paroxysmal cough episodes (cough count diary) at Week 2; reduction in household transmission rate; PCR negativity at Day 5.
btd_threshold_vs_soc: 50% reduction in paroxysmal cough episodes at Week 2 vs. no proven effect for macrolides in paroxysmal phase; or novel vaccine ≥85% efficacy vs. ~71–89% for acellular pertussis component.

symptoms: Profuse watery diarrhea (rice-water stools), vomiting, severe dehydration
treatment_goal: Restore fluid/electrolytes, shorten illness with antibiotics
first_line_soc: Oral rehydration solution (ORS) is primary; doxycycline 300 mg single dose (adults) or azithromycin 1g single dose (children, pregnant women) for moderate-severe disease.
treatment_algorithm: Step 1: ORS — cornerstone (can reduce mortality from 50% to <1%). Step 2: Antibiotics for moderate-severe: doxycycline 300 mg single PO dose OR azithromycin 1g (pediatric 20 mg/kg) single dose. Step 3: IV Ringer's lactate for severe dehydration. Step 4: Ciprofloxacin 1g single dose (alternative; resistance increasing globally). Step 5: Zinc supplementation (children). Vaccine: Shanchol/ORC-Vax/Vaxchora (oral live-attenuated, approved US) for prevention. 2024: WHO emergency stockpile deployment active in Haiti, Africa.
us_prevalence: <100 US cases/yr (imported travelers/immigrants); globally ~3 million cases/yr. (CDC cholera surveillance 2024)
patients_needing_tx: <100 US patients/yr require treatment; global burden enormous but NTD/global health context.
price: $200–$500 per course (single-dose oral antibiotic active vs. resistant strains; per-course price); global market primarily ORS-driven.
tam_gross: 100 US × $300 = $30K. Global: 3M × $200 = $600M. NTD pricing limits commercial opportunity.
registrational_endpoint: Duration of diarrhea and total stool output; stool vibrio culture negativity at 24–48 hours.
btd_threshold_vs_soc: Diarrhea cessation by 24 hrs vs. ~48 hrs for doxycycline/azithromycin; 100% microbiological clearance at 48 hrs vs. ~90% for azithromycin.

symptoms: Wound infection, necrotizing fasciitis, septicemia, gastroenteritis after raw seafood
treatment_goal: Eradicate Vibrio vulnificus/parahaemolyticus, prevent necrotizing wound infection, prevent death
first_line_soc: Doxycycline 100 mg BID + ceftriaxone 2g IV q12h (combination for V. vulnificus septicemia/wound infection, IDSA guidelines); doxycycline monotherapy for mild gastroenteritis.
treatment_algorithm: V. vulnificus (most lethal, liver disease predisposed): doxycycline 100 mg q12h + ceftriaxone 2g q12h IV × 7–14 days; fluoroquinolone (ciprofloxacin) as alternative. Necrotizing wound infection: urgent surgical debridement ± amputation + antibiotics. V. parahaemolyticus gastroenteritis: usually self-limiting; severe disease: doxycycline or fluoroquinolone. V. cholerae non-O1/O139: doxycycline or azithromycin. 2024: Gulf Coast and Pacific Northwest V. vulnificus cases rising with warming ocean temperatures.
us_prevalence: ~80,000 V. parahaemolyticus gastroenteritis cases/yr; ~200 V. vulnificus infections/yr (high mortality ~20–30%). (CDC Cholera/Vibrio surveillance)
patients_needing_tx: ~200 V. vulnificus patients/yr need IV combination therapy; ~5,000 severe V. parahaemolyticus cases need antibiotics.
price: $3,000–$10,000 per course (novel agent for V. vulnificus necrotizing infection).
peak_sales: $5–15M; small market. V. vulnificus adjunctive monoclonal approach (anti-VvhA toxin) in early research.
registrational_endpoint: 28-day survival for V. vulnificus septicemia; limb salvage rate at Day 28; clinical cure for wound infections at Day 14.
btd_threshold_vs_soc: 28-day survival ≥80% for V. vulnificus vs. ~70–80% for doxycycline/ceftriaxone (already good if early treatment); limb salvage ≥70% vs. ~50% for early combination + surgical.

symptoms: Severe pneumonia, high fever, cough, shortness of breath, confusion
treatment_goal: Eradicate Legionella pneumophila, prevent respiratory failure, reduce mortality
first_line_soc: Azithromycin (Zithromax, macrolide) IV/oral 500 mg/day × 7–10 days; or levofloxacin (Levaquin, fluoroquinolone) 750 mg/day × 7–10 days — both equivalent and preferred over older tetracyclines.
treatment_algorithm: Mild-moderate Legionnaires' disease (outpatient): azithromycin 500 mg/day × 5–7 days or levofloxacin 750 mg/day × 5 days. Severe/ICU: levofloxacin or azithromycin IV, sometimes in combination; consider adding rifampin for severe immunocompromised. Pontiac fever (non-pneumonic): supportive, antibiotics often not needed. Urine antigen test enables rapid diagnosis. Duration: 7–10 days (immunocompetent) to 14–21 days (immunocompromised). 2024: IDSA CAP guidelines updated; levofloxacin and azithromycin both recommended; doxycycline for mild disease.
us_prevalence: ~10,000–20,000 US cases/yr (Legionnaires' disease); increasing trend. ~5–10% case fatality rate. (CDC Legionella surveillance 2024)
patients_needing_tx: ~15,000 patients/yr require antibiotic treatment; all diagnosed cases warrant therapy.
price: $500–$2,000 per course (novel fluoroquinolone/azalide for Legionella-optimized dosing).
peak_sales: $10–25M for novel Legionella-specific agent; dominated by generics.
registrational_endpoint: Clinical cure at Day 7 and 30-day mortality for severe disease; symptom resolution score (CAP-SX) at Day 5.
btd_threshold_vs_soc: 30-day mortality <2% vs. ~5–10% for standard levofloxacin/azithromycin; or shorter treatment duration (3 days) with equivalent cure rate.

symptoms: Subacute endocarditis, fever, malaise, heart murmur, embolic events
treatment_goal: Eradicate HACEK organisms, cure endocarditis, prevent emboli
first_line_soc: Ceftriaxone (3rd-gen cephalosporin) 2g IV daily × 4 weeks for endocarditis (AHA/IDSA endocarditis guidelines 2023).
treatment_algorithm: Step 1: Ceftriaxone 2g IV/IM daily × 4 weeks (native valve) or 6 weeks (prosthetic valve). Step 2: Ampicillin-sulbactam 3g IV q6h as alternative. Step 3: For beta-lactam allergy: fluoroquinolone (ciprofloxacin or levofloxacin) — check susceptibility. Step 4: Surgical valve replacement/repair per endocarditis indications (heart failure, large vegetations, persistent bacteremia). 2024: No new agents; ceftriaxone remains standard.
us_prevalence: ~700–1,000 HACEK endocarditis cases/yr in the US (3–5% of all IE). (AHA endocarditis data)
patients_needing_tx: ~700–1,000 patients/yr; all require IV antibiotics.
price: $5,000–$15,000 per course (novel oral beta-lactam enabling oral step-down from IV ceftriaxone).
peak_sales: <$10M; very small niche market within broader endocarditis.
registrational_endpoint: Microbiological cure (blood culture negativity) and clinical cure (resolution of fever/vegetation by echo) at end of therapy and 3-month follow-up.
btd_threshold_vs_soc: Clinical + microbiological cure ≥95% for oral regimen vs. ~90% for IV ceftriaxone; or oral regimen enabling 100% outpatient therapy.

symptoms: Septic arthritis, osteomyelitis, endocarditis in children, fever, joint swelling
treatment_goal: Eradicate Kingella kingae, cure septic arthritis/osteomyelitis/endocarditis
first_line_soc: Penicillin G or ampicillin (aminopenicillin) IV; amoxicillin-clavulanate for oral step-down. Kingella is highly susceptible to most beta-lactams.
treatment_algorithm: Septic arthritis/osteomyelitis: ampicillin-sulbactam or amoxicillin-clavulanate IV × 2–4 weeks + surgical drainage/debridement. Endocarditis: penicillin G 12–18 million U/day or ceftriaxone 2g/day × 4 weeks. Beta-lactam allergy: fluoroquinolones (ciprofloxacin). Excellent prognosis with appropriate therapy. 2024: No new agents; Kingella is characteristically penicillin-susceptible.
us_prevalence: ~500–1,000 US Kingella cases/yr (mainly pediatric septic arthritis); underdiagnosed due to fastidious culture requirements.
patients_needing_tx: ~500–1,000 patients/yr; all require IV antibiotics.
peak_sales: <$5M; niche pediatric infection, no dedicated drug development.
registrational_endpoint: Clinical cure (resolution of joint swelling, fever, CRP normalization) at Day 14 and 6-week follow-up.
btd_threshold_vs_soc: Clinical cure ≥95% at 6 weeks for oral regimen vs. ~90% for IV therapy (enabling outpatient step-down).

symptoms: Prolonged fever, headache, rose spots rash, splenomegaly, relative bradycardia
treatment_goal: Eradicate Salmonella Typhi, prevent intestinal perforation and relapse
first_line_soc: Azithromycin 1g on Day 1 then 500 mg × 6 days (preferred oral agent for uncomplicated typhoid in most regions); ceftriaxone 2g IV daily × 7–14 days for severe/hospitalized or MDR S. Typhi.
treatment_algorithm: Uncomplicated typhoid: azithromycin 1g Day 1 then 500 mg/day × 6 days (preferred; effective against fluoroquinolone-resistant strains). Fluoroquinolone-susceptible: ciprofloxacin 500 mg BID × 7 days (older regimen; resistance high in South Asia). Severe/complicated: ceftriaxone 2g IV × 10–14 days; switch to azithromycin oral step-down when feasible. XDR Typhoid (H58 lineage, ceftriaxone + fluoroquinolone-resistant): azithromycin (if susceptible) or meropenem IV. Intestinal perforation: surgical repair + broad-spectrum antibiotics. Prevention: Vi polysaccharide vaccine (Typhim Vi) or Ty21a oral vaccine; Vi-TT conjugate vaccine (Typbar TCV) superior immunogenicity. 2024: XDR Typhoid spreading from Pakistan; US imported cases rising.
us_prevalence: ~350–500 US cases/yr (mostly imported travelers); XDR strains growing proportion. (CDC NNDSS 2024)
patients_needing_tx: ~450 US patients/yr; all require antibiotic treatment.
price: $1,000–$3,000 per course for novel oral agent active against XDR Typhoid (per-course pricing).
peak_sales: ~$50–150M globally for novel oral agent active against XDR Typhoid; US-only market negligible.
registrational_endpoint: Fever clearance by Day 5 and culture negativity at Day 7 (blood/stool); treatment failure/relapse rate at 28 days.
btd_threshold_vs_soc: Fever clearance by Day 4 in ≥85% vs. ~75% for azithromycin; cure of XDR Typhoid ≥90% vs. ~80% for meropenem/azithromycin in resistant strains.

symptoms: Pneumonia, septicemia, fever, skin abscesses, disseminated infection
treatment_goal: Eradicate Burkholderia pseudomallei, prevent septic death, prevent relapse
first_line_soc: Intensive phase: IV ceftazidime (3rd-gen cephalosporin) 50 mg/kg q6h × 10–14 days OR IV meropenem 1g q8h × 10–14 days. Eradication phase: TMP-SMX DS BID × 3–6 months.
treatment_algorithm: Intensive (IV) phase: ceftazidime 2g IV q6h × 10–14 days or meropenem 1g IV q8h × 10–14 days (preferred for CNS/severe disease). Eradication (oral) phase: TMP-SMX 320/1600 mg BID × 3–6 months ± doxycycline; amoxicillin-clavulanate as alternative. Critical: without eradication phase, relapse rate ~40%. Ceftazidime-resistant strains: meropenem. Novel: no new agents in pipeline. 2024: WHO classified melioidosis as NTD; rising climate-related cases.
us_prevalence: ~30–50 US cases/yr (travelers from SE Asia/Australia, domestic Hawaii/Texas cases emerging). Global: ~165,000 cases/yr. (CDC/WHO melioidosis burden 2024)
patients_needing_tx: ~50 US patients/yr; all require intensive IV + prolonged oral eradication.
price: $5,000–$20,000 per course (novel oral agent with anti-Burkholderia activity enabling shorter eradication phase).
peak_sales: <$5M US; global market potential if novel agent reduces relapse; orphan drug designation possible.
registrational_endpoint: Clinical cure at Day 28 (intensive completion) and 12-month relapse-free survival; B. pseudomallei blood/sputum culture negativity.
btd_threshold_vs_soc: 12-month relapse rate <5% vs. ~10–20% for TMP-SMX eradication; or 3-month eradication non-inferior to 6-month for reducing relapse.

symptoms: Urinary tract infection, bacteremia, peritonitis (usually in catheterized/compromised patients)
treatment_goal: Eradicate Oligella ureolytica/urethralis, clear UTI/bacteremia
first_line_soc: Beta-lactams (ampicillin, amoxicillin-clavulanate) or fluoroquinolones (ciprofloxacin) for susceptible strains. Susceptibility testing essential (limited data).
treatment_algorithm: Oligella ureolytica/urethralis: usually susceptible to beta-lactams and fluoroquinolones. Mild UTI: amoxicillin or ciprofloxacin × 7 days. Bacteremia: IV ampicillin or ceftriaxone × 14 days. Urease inhibitors (acetohydroxamic acid) sometimes used adjunctively. Very rare pathogen with minimal clinical literature. 2024: No dedicated drug development.
us_prevalence: <500 US cases/yr (rare opportunistic gram-negative pathogen; underreported). Primarily catheter-associated UTI.
patients_needing_tx: <500 US patients/yr; all diagnosed cases require antibiotics.
price: $500–$2,000 per course.
registrational_endpoint: Microbiological cure (urine culture <10³ CFU/mL) at Day 7; clinical resolution of UTI symptoms at Day 5.
btd_threshold_vs_soc: N/A — no dedicated development; clinical cure standard for UTI: ≥90% for any guideline-recommended regimen.

symptoms: Skin infections, boils, wound infections, pneumonia, sepsis, toxic shock
treatment_goal: Eradicate MSSA/MRSA, prevent bacteremia, endocarditis, and metastatic spread
first_line_soc: MSSA: nafcillin or oxacillin (antistaphylococcal penicillins) IV, or cefazolin for surgical prophylaxis. MRSA skin/soft tissue: TMP-SMX or clindamycin. MRSA bacteremia/endocarditis: vancomycin (glycopeptide) IV with AUC/MIC monitoring, or daptomycin (Cubicin, lipopeptide) 6–10 mg/kg IV.
treatment_algorithm: MSSA bloodstream: nafcillin/oxacillin IV 4–6 weeks (endocarditis) or cefazolin IV equivalent. MRSA skin: TMP-SMX (Bactrim) or clindamycin × 5–7 days. MRSA bloodstream/endocarditis: vancomycin IV (AUC-guided, target 400–600) or daptomycin 6–10 mg/kg IV. MRSA persistent bacteremia/failure: addition of ceftaroline (Teflaro, 5th-gen cephalosporin) + daptomycin. Linezolid (Zyvox) for MRSA pneumonia. Ceftaroline (Teflaro) for MRSA ABSSSI. Tedizolid (Sivextro) alternative oxazolidinone. Oritavancin (Orbactiv), dalbavancin (Dalvance): long-acting lipoglycopeptides for ABSSSI (single dose). 2024–2025: S. aureus vaccines in Phase 3 (SA4Ag failed, NDV-3A in development); new β-lactamase-resistant anti-MRSA agents in early pipeline.
us_prevalence: ~500,000 MRSA infections/yr; ~12,000 MRSA deaths/yr. MSSA infections ~2–3 million/yr. (CDC 2024)
patients_needing_tx: ~3–4 million US patients/yr with Staphylococcal infections requiring antibiotics; ~500,000 MRSA cases require specialized treatment.
price: $3,000–$15,000 per course for novel anti-MRSA agents (IV); single-dose lipoglycopeptides $1,500–$2,500 per dose.
peak_sales: ~$500M–1.5B for novel MRSA agent (e.g., new beta-lactam active against MRSA, anti-MRSA vaccine ~$1B if efficacious).
registrational_endpoint: Clinical cure (ABSSSI: ≥20% lesion area reduction by 48–72 hrs; bacteremia: sustained blood culture clearance at Day 7); Day 42 clinical success for endocarditis.
btd_threshold_vs_soc: Clinical cure >85% for MRSA ABSSSI vs. ~75% for vancomycin; or reduction in MRSA bacteremia mortality to <10% vs. ~15–25% current; or MRSA vaccine efficacy ≥50% in high-risk populations.

symptoms: Sore throat, skin infections, fever, scarlet fever rash, necrotizing fasciitis
treatment_goal: Eradicate Group A/B Streptococcus, prevent rheumatic fever, sepsis, and GBS neonatal disease
first_line_soc: Penicillin V (beta-lactam) 500 mg BID-TID × 10 days for Group A Strep pharyngitis; amoxicillin 1g daily × 10 days (equivalent, better compliance). For GAS bacteremia/severe invasive: IV penicillin G + clindamycin (to suppress toxin production).
treatment_algorithm: GAS pharyngitis: penicillin V or amoxicillin × 10 days; amoxicillin 1g/day × 10 days comparable. Penicillin allergy: azithromycin, cephalexin. GAS invasive disease (bacteremia, NF, streptococcal TSS): IV penicillin G 4 million units q4h + clindamycin 900 mg q8h (dual therapy to suppress toxin); surgical debridement for NF. IVIG for streptococcal TSS adjunct. GBS neonatal: ampicillin IV; intrapartum penicillin prophylaxis. Strep pneumoniae: see Pneumococcal Infections. 2024: GAS vaccine in Phase 2 (M protein-based, Pfizer); GBS vaccine in Phase 3 (Pfizer Trumenba-adjacent construct).
us_prevalence: ~10–11 million GAS pharyngitis cases/yr; ~10,000–20,000 invasive GAS cases/yr; ~1,700 GBS neonatal sepsis cases/yr. (CDC Active Bacterial Core Surveillance 2024)
patients_needing_tx: ~12 million patients/yr requiring antibiotics; nearly all GAS pharyngitis and 100% invasive cases require treatment.
price: $500–$2,000 per course for novel oral agent with improved compliance and reduced GAS recurrence; GAS vaccine $100–$200 per dose.
tam_gross: 12M × $500 avg = $6B; novel agent targeting invasive/recurrent segment ~1M × $1,500 = $1.5B
peak_sales: ~$200–500M for novel oral GAS agent with superiority to penicillin; GAS vaccine $500M–$1.5B peak potential.
registrational_endpoint: Microbiological eradication of GAS at Day 5–7 (throat culture negative); clinical cure at Day 10 for pharyngitis. For invasive GAS: 30-day survival + source control.
btd_threshold_vs_soc: Bacteriological cure >95% at Day 5 vs. ~85% for amoxicillin × 10 days; recurrence rate <5% at 30 days vs. ~15%; GAS vaccine efficacy ≥50% against invasive disease.

symptoms: Pneumonia, meningitis, ear infection, septicemia, fever, chest pain
treatment_goal: Eradicate Streptococcus pneumoniae, prevent meningitis/sepsis, treat resistant strains
first_line_soc: Community-acquired pneumonia (outpatient): amoxicillin (aminopenicillin) 1g TID × 5 days or amoxicillin-clavulanate. Inpatient: ceftriaxone 1–2g IV daily. Meningitis: ceftriaxone 2g IV q12h + vancomycin (until susceptibility known) + dexamethasone. High-level PCN resistance: vancomycin or linezolid.
treatment_algorithm: Outpatient CAP (mild): amoxicillin 1g TID × 5 days or doxycycline × 5 days. Hospitalized non-ICU: ceftriaxone IV. ICU: ceftriaxone + azithromycin or ceftriaxone + levofloxacin. Meningitis: ceftriaxone + vancomycin + dexamethasone (pending susceptibility). DRSP (drug-resistant S. pneumoniae): levofloxacin or moxifloxacin; linezolid salvage. Prevention: PCV20 (Prevnar 20, Pfizer) or PCV15 + PPSV23 (Vaxneuvance, Merck) for adults; PCV15/PCV20 for children. Novel: cefiderocol, omadacycline for MDR strains. 2024: PCV20 adult vaccine uptake growing; NiRAN inhibitors in early pipeline targeting DNA polymerase.
us_prevalence: ~150,000 US hospitalizations/yr for pneumococcal pneumonia; ~3,000 meningitis/yr; ~4 million otitis media cases attributable to S. pneumoniae/yr. (CDC ABCs)
patients_needing_tx: ~150,000 hospitalized pneumonia patients/yr + 3,000 meningitis patients need IV antibiotics; ~1 million outpatient pneumonia cases need oral antibiotics.
price: $500–$2,000 per course (oral novel agent for DRSP); $5,000–$15,000 per course (IV agent for DRSP meningitis).
tam_gross: 1,150,000 patients × $1,500 avg = $1.725B; PCV20 adult vaccine market $1B+/yr.
peak_sales: PCV20 (Prevnar 20): ~$1.5–2B US peak annual sales; novel DRSP antibiotic: $100–300M.
registrational_endpoint: Clinical cure at Day 7–14 (non-inferiority for CAP); CSF sterilization and neurological outcome at Day 14 for meningitis. FDA CAP Guidance applies.
btd_threshold_vs_soc: Clinical cure ≥90% for DRSP CAP vs. ~75% for amoxicillin alone in penicillin-resistant strains; meningitis cure ≥85% at Day 14 vs. ~80% for ceftriaxone + vancomycin.

symptoms: UTI, wound infection, bacteremia, endocarditis (hospital-acquired)
treatment_goal: Eradicate VRE/ampicillin-resistant Enterococcus, prevent endocarditis
first_line_soc: Ampicillin-susceptible Enterococcus: ampicillin (beta-lactam) ± gentamicin synergy for endocarditis. Vancomycin-resistant Enterococcus (VRE): linezolid (Zyvox, oxazolidinone) or daptomycin (Cubicin, lipopeptide).
treatment_algorithm: E. faecalis endocarditis: ampicillin + gentamicin OR ampicillin + ceftriaxone (alternative with less renal toxicity). VRE (E. faecium): linezolid 600 mg q12h or daptomycin 6–10 mg/kg/day. Tigecycline combination for pan-resistant VRE. Oritavancin (Orbactiv) and dalbavancin (Dalvance) for VRE susceptible strains. 2024: Tedizolid (Sivextro, oxazolidinone) — VRE activity; limited endocarditis data. Novel agents: BL-9B1 anti-VRE bacteriophage in early development. Resistance to daptomycin and linezolid emerging.
us_prevalence: ~20,000–25,000 VRE infections/yr in US hospitals; ~66,000 total enterococcal infections/yr. (CDC 2019 AR Threats Report updated 2024)
patients_needing_tx: ~25,000 VRE patients + ~40,000 ampicillin-resistant enterococcal infections = ~65,000 patients needing pharmacologic treatment.
price: $15,000–$30,000 per course for VRE-active agent (IV/oral); comparator linezolid ~$3,000/course branded, generic cheaper.
peak_sales: $200–400M for novel VRE-active agent with reduced toxicity vs. linezolid/daptomycin at 10–15% market share.
registrational_endpoint: Clinical cure + microbiological eradication at Day 5–9 post-treatment for complicated UTI; 90-day all-cause mortality for endocarditis; ABSSSI composite at Day 10.
btd_threshold_vs_soc: Clinical cure ≥80% for VRE complicated UTI vs. ~65% for linezolid; or endocarditis 90-day mortality <20% vs. ~30% for daptomycin-based regimens.

symptoms: High fever, diffuse rash, hypotension, multi-organ failure
treatment_goal: Eliminate toxin-producing bacteria, reverse shock, suppress toxin production
first_line_soc: For staphylococcal TSS: IV antistaphylococcal agent (nafcillin for MSSA, vancomycin for MRSA) + clindamycin (to suppress toxin synthesis). For streptococcal TSS: IV penicillin G + clindamycin + IVIG.
treatment_algorithm: Staphylococcal TSS (MRSA): vancomycin IV (AUC/MIC monitoring) + clindamycin 900 mg q8h (toxin inhibition); linezolid as alternative. Staphylococcal TSS (MSSA): nafcillin/oxacillin IV + clindamycin. Streptococcal TSS: penicillin G 4M units q4h IV + clindamycin 900 mg q8h + IVIG 2g/kg × 1 dose. Aggressive fluid resuscitation, vasopressors (norepinephrine), mechanical ventilation, source control (remove tampon, drain abscess, debride NF). IVIG adjunct reduces mortality in streptococcal TSS. 2024: IDSA guidelines emphasize combination therapy with toxin suppressants.
us_prevalence: ~700–1,200 menstrual staphylococcal TSS cases/yr; ~1,000–2,000 non-menstrual staph + strep TSS cases/yr. Total ~2,000–3,000/yr. (CDC TSS surveillance)
patients_needing_tx: ~2,500 patients/yr requiring ICU admission and IV antibiotics; all cases are medical emergencies.
price: $5,000–$20,000 per course for novel anti-toxin therapy (monoclonal antibody against staphylococcal superantigens or streptococcal pyrogenic exotoxins).
peak_sales: ~$30–60M for novel anti-toxin antibody at 40–50% TSS market share; limited by small population.
registrational_endpoint: All-cause mortality at Day 28; time to hemodynamic stability; reversal of organ failure (SOFA score) at Day 7.
btd_threshold_vs_soc: 28-day mortality <5% vs. ~10–15% for current best practice; or time to hemodynamic stability <24 hrs vs. ~48 hrs with standard care.

symptoms: Fever, muscle aches, nausea, stiff neck, confusion, miscarriage in pregnancy
treatment_goal: Eradicate Listeria monocytogenes, prevent neonatal/CNS death
first_line_soc: IV ampicillin (aminopenicillin) 2g q4h × 14–21 days; addition of gentamicin synergistic for severe disease. CNS/neonatal listeriosis: IV ampicillin + gentamicin; meningitis requires 21–42 days.
treatment_algorithm: Non-CNS/bacteremia: IV ampicillin 2g q4h × 14 days ± gentamicin for synergy. Meningitis/encephalitis: IV ampicillin 2g q4h + gentamicin × 21 days. Neonatal: IV ampicillin 200–400 mg/kg/day × 21 days. Penicillin-allergic: TMP-SMX IV. Cephalosporins (including 3rd gen) intrinsically resistant — do NOT use. Pregnant women: IV ampicillin × 14 days. Prevention: refrigeration, avoid high-risk foods. 2024: No new agents; ampicillin remains only proven treatment.
us_prevalence: ~1,600 US cases/yr; ~260 deaths/yr. Predominantly affects elderly, pregnant women, immunocompromised. (CDC FoodNet)
patients_needing_tx: ~1,600 patients/yr; all require IV antibiotics (Listeria is always pathogenic when isolated from blood/CSF).
price: $5,000–$15,000 per course (novel oral agent enabling step-down from IV ampicillin).
peak_sales: $5–10M for novel oral Listeria-active agent; small market.
registrational_endpoint: Clinical cure (afebrile, CSF normalization) at Day 21; microbiological cure (blood/CSF culture negative) by Day 7.
btd_threshold_vs_soc: Clinical cure ≥90% at Day 21 with oral step-down vs. ~85% for all-IV ampicillin regimen; or reduced recurrence in immunocompromised patients.

symptoms: Sore throat, gray pseudomembrane on tonsils, hoarseness, neck swelling
treatment_goal: Neutralize diphtheria toxin, eradicate Corynebacterium, clear membrane
first_line_soc: Diphtheria antitoxin (equine, DAT) IV/IM as soon as suspected (CDC Strategic National Stockpile); plus penicillin G IV or erythromycin × 14 days to eliminate organism.
treatment_algorithm: Step 1: Antitoxin (DAT) as emergency 20,000–100,000 units depending on severity — neutralizes circulating toxin. Step 2: Penicillin G IV 100,000–150,000 units/kg/day × 14 days OR erythromycin × 14 days to eradicate C. diphtheriae. Step 3: Airway management (tracheostomy if membrane obstructs). Step 4: Cardiac monitoring (myocarditis in 10–25%). Vaccine-preventable disease; DTaP/Tdap primary prevention. 2024: No new pharmacologic treatments.
us_prevalence: <5 US cases/yr (rare; mostly unvaccinated travelers). Global resurgence in undervaccinated countries. (CDC VPD surveillance 2024)
patients_needing_tx: <5 US patients/yr; all require emergency antitoxin + antibiotics.
price: $5,000–$50,000 per course (recombinant human antitoxin or toxin-neutralizing antibody vs. equine DAT); improved safety/efficacy over horse serum.
tam_gross: 5 US × $25,000 = $125K. Government stockpile procurement drives market.
peak_sales: Government procurement; <$5M US. Significant global resurgence concern.
registrational_endpoint: Clinical cure by Day 7 (membrane resolution + negative throat culture); 14-day mortality with vs. without antitoxin.
btd_threshold_vs_soc: Mortality <5% vs. ~10% historical for severe diphtheria; membrane resolution by Day 3 vs. Day 5 with equine DAT.

symptoms: Skin redness, painful swollen fingers (fisherman's/butcher's disease)
treatment_goal: Eradicate Erysipelothrix rhusiopathiae, heal skin lesions
first_line_soc: Penicillin G (beta-lactam) 500 mg PO QID × 5–7 days (benzylpenicillin); amoxicillin alternative. Or ampicillin for systemic forms.
treatment_algorithm: Cutaneous (mild): oral penicillin or amoxicillin × 7 days; cephalosporins or clindamycin for penicillin-allergic. Diffuse/systemic erysipeloid: IV penicillin G. Endocarditis (rare): IV penicillin G × 4–6 weeks ± gentamicin. Naturally penicillin-susceptible; vancomycin intrinsically resistant. 2024: No new agents.
us_prevalence: <500 US cases/yr (occupational — fish handlers, farmers); frequently underreported. (Literature/case series)
patients_needing_tx: ~300–400 US patients/yr require antibiotic treatment.
price: $200–$500 per course; no commercial opportunity.
registrational_endpoint: Skin lesion resolution at Day 7 + culture negativity.
btd_threshold_vs_soc: N/A.

symptoms: Chronic cough, fatigue, weight loss, cavitary lung disease, lymphadenopathy, skin lesions
treatment_goal: Eradicate NTM, halt lung disease progression, achieve culture conversion
first_line_soc: MAC (Mycobacterium avium complex, most common US NTM): azithromycin or clarithromycin (macrolide) + ethambutol (Myambutol) + rifampin (Rifadin) 3-drug regimen × 12 months post-culture conversion (IDSA/ATS 2020 guidelines).
treatment_algorithm: MAC pulmonary: azithromycin 250 mg 3×/wk + ethambutol 25 mg/kg 3×/wk + rifampin 600 mg 3×/wk (intermittent for nodular-bronchiectatic) or daily dosing (fibrocavitary). Amikacin liposome inhalation suspension (ALIS, Arikayce) 590 mg/day inhalation added for refractory MAC — FDA-approved 2018. M. abscessus: IV amikacin + IV imipenem or cefoxitin × 4 weeks induction, then macrolide-based maintenance; very difficult to treat. M. kansasii: isoniazid + rifampin + ethambutol. Omadacycline (Nuzyra) and clofazimine used off-label for refractory MAC. 2024: FDA approval of bedaquiline (Sirturo) for MAC complex pulmonary disease under review; new IDSA guidelines on M. abscessus.
us_prevalence: ~100,000–200,000 US patients with NTM pulmonary disease; incidence rising 8%/yr. MAC accounts for ~80%. (ATS NTM Registry; Health Council of Netherlands extrapolated to US)
patients_needing_tx: ~50,000–80,000 US patients/yr require pharmacologic treatment (those meeting ATS treatment criteria); M. abscessus ~3,000–5,000 patients/yr.
price: ALIS (Arikayce): ~$30,000/yr. Novel MAC oral agent: $20,000–$60,000/yr. M. abscessus novel drug: $40,000–$80,000/yr.
peak_sales: $500M–$1.5B for novel MAC/NTM oral agent (improving on ALIS or replacing rifampin-based 3-drug); ALIS (Arikayce) ~$350M peak sales potential; combined NTM market approaching $2B.
registrational_endpoint: Culture conversion (3 consecutive monthly sputum cultures negative for MAC) by Month 12 of treatment (ATS/IDSA primary endpoint); sustained culture conversion at Month 24.
btd_threshold_vs_soc: Culture conversion ≥65% at Month 12 vs. ~50% for guideline-based 3-drug MAC regimen; or M. abscessus culture conversion ≥50% vs. ~30–40% for IV-based induction.

symptoms: Persistent cough, night sweats, weight loss, hemoptysis, fatigue
treatment_goal: Sterilize Mycobacterium tuberculosis, prevent relapse and drug resistance
first_line_soc: RIPE regimen: rifampin + isoniazid + pyrazinamide + ethambutol × 2 months (intensive), then rifampin + isoniazid × 4 months (continuation) — 6-month total for drug-susceptible TB.
treatment_algorithm: Drug-susceptible TB (DS-TB): RIPE × 2 months then RI × 4 months. Short-course regimen: 4-month regimen with rifapentine + moxifloxacin + isoniazid + pyrazinamide (STREAM trial; FDA reviewing). MDR-TB: 6-month BPaL(Z) regimen — bedaquiline (Sirturo) + pretomanid (Dossier) + linezolid ± pyrazinamide (BPaLM/ZeNix trial; WHO-endorsed 2022). Pre-XDR/XDR-TB: bedaquiline + pretomanid + linezolid (BPaL) × 6–9 months. TB meningitis: longer intensive phase, steroids. LTBI: isoniazid × 6–9 months or rifapentine + isoniazid weekly × 12 doses (3HP). TB preventive therapy: dolutegravir-based ART + 3HP in HIV+ patients. 2024–2025: BPaLM (bedaquiline + pretomanid + linezolid + moxifloxacin) 6-month regimen for MDR-TB endorsed by WHO.
us_prevalence: ~8,300 active TB cases/yr in US; ~11–13 million LTBI. (CDC TB Surveillance 2024)
patients_needing_tx: ~8,300 active TB cases requiring RIPE or MDR regimens; ~500,000–1 million LTBI treated/yr preventively.
price: $5,000–$30,000 per course for novel anti-TB agents (MDR/XDR); bedaquiline ~$30,000 per course US price. DS-TB RIPE generics: ~$100–300 course.
peak_sales: ~$300–600M for novel MDR-TB shortening regimen (US + global ex-US); bedaquiline (J&J) ~$500M peak global; pretomanid global contract ~$100–200M.
registrational_endpoint: Culture negativity at 8 weeks (early bactericidal activity); disease-free status at 24 months post-treatment (primary efficacy); relapse-free survival at 24 months.
btd_threshold_vs_soc: Relapse-free success ≥85% at 24 months for MDR-TB vs. ~52% for WHO standard longer MDR-TB regimen; or 4-month DS-TB regimen non-inferior to 6-month RIPE (success rate lower bound of 95% CI > -6%).

symptoms: Lymph node swelling, back pain (spinal TB), meningitis, kidney involvement
treatment_goal: Sterilize M. tuberculosis foci, prevent disability and relapse
first_line_soc: 2HRZE/4HR: Isoniazid (H) + rifampin (R) + pyrazinamide (Z) + ethambutol (E) × 2 months, then INH + rifampin × 4–7 months (total 6–9 months; TB meningitis 9–12 months).
treatment_algorithm: Same core regimen as pulmonary TB (2HRZE/4HR). Modifications: Tuberculous meningitis: 9–12 months, with dexamethasone adjunct (reduces mortality in HIV-negative). Spinal TB: 6–9 months; surgery if cord compression. Pericardial TB: 6 months + prednisolone taper. Renal TB: 6 months. Drug-resistant extrapulmonary TB: MDR regimens per WHO 2022 (bedaquiline + pretomanid + linezolid for XDR-TB). 2024: New WHO consolidated guidelines emphasize shorter BPaL regimen (bedaquiline-pretomanid-linezolid 6 months) for XDR extrapulmonary TB.
us_prevalence: ~900–1,100 US extrapulmonary TB cases/yr (~20–25% of all TB). (CDC TB surveillance 2024)
patients_needing_tx: ~1,000 patients/yr in US require extended multi-drug therapy.
price: $10,000–$50,000 per course (novel shorter-course regimen or MDR-active agent; per-course price).
peak_sales: Included in broader TB drug market (~$1B global for MDR-TB drugs: bedaquiline, delamanid, pretomanid). US alone: $20–50M.
registrational_endpoint: Culture conversion at 8 weeks (sputum/tissue) + relapse-free cure at 24 months; FDA accepts composite endpoint of treatment success (culture conversion + no relapse) for TB drug approval.
btd_threshold_vs_soc: Treatment success ≥85% for extrapulmonary MDR-TB at 24 months vs. ~60–70% for conventional MDR regimens.

symptoms: Skin patches, numbness, muscle weakness, nerve damage, disfigurement
treatment_goal: Eliminate Mycobacterium leprae, prevent nerve damage and disability
first_line_soc: WHO multidrug therapy (MDT): rifampin (rifampicin) + dapsone + clofazimine (Lamprene). PB leprosy (1–5 lesions): rifampin + dapsone × 6 months. MB leprosy (>5 lesions): rifampin + dapsone + clofazimine × 12 months.
treatment_algorithm: WHO MDT regimens: PB — rifampin 600 mg monthly (supervised) + dapsone 100 mg/day × 6 months. MB — rifampin 600 mg/month + clofazimine 300 mg/month + dapsone 100 mg/day + clofazimine 50 mg/day × 12 months. Reactions: lepra type 1 — prednisone; type 2 (ENL) — thalidomide (Thalomid) or prednisone. Novel: no new anti-leprosy drugs in pipeline; drug-resistant leprosy (rifampin-resistant) treated with ofloxacin + minocycline. 2024 WHO strategy: global elimination target; single-dose rifampin PEP for contacts.
us_prevalence: ~150–250 new US cases/yr (Hawaii, Texas, Florida, Louisiana — armadillo exposure); ~3,000 active US cases. (HRSA NHDP data 2024)
patients_needing_tx: ~200 new US patients/yr need MDT; ~3,000 active cases. All diagnosed cases require treatment.
price: $1,000–$5,000 per course (novel anti-leprosy agent enabling shorter therapy; WHO generics available free globally).
peak_sales: <$5M; primarily WHO/public health funded globally. No commercial development interest in US.
registrational_endpoint: Bacterial Index (BI) reduction ≥2+ on slit-skin smear at 12 months; clinical cure (inactive lesions, no new nerve damage) at 24 months.
btd_threshold_vs_soc: BI reduction to 0+ (clearance) in 6 months vs. 12 months for WHO MDT; or single-exposure drug achieving 95% cure (eliminating 6–12 month courses).

symptoms: Urethral discharge, pelvic pain, eye inflammation, pneumonia
treatment_goal: Eradicate Chlamydia, prevent PID, infertility, and transmission
first_line_soc: Doxycycline 100 mg BID × 7 days (first-line for urogenital chlamydia per 2021 CDC STI guidelines, replacing azithromycin single dose due to cure rate superiority in rectal chlamydia).
treatment_algorithm: Genital chlamydia: doxycycline 100 mg BID × 7 days (preferred for genital and rectal infection). Alternative: azithromycin 1g single dose (acceptable for urogenital; inferior for rectal). Pregnant patients: azithromycin 1g PO single dose or amoxicillin 500 mg TID × 7 days. Chlamydia pneumoniae: azithromycin or doxycycline × 10–14 days. LGV: doxycycline × 21 days. Psittacosis: doxycycline × 10–14 days. Ophthalmia neonatorum (trachoma): oral erythromycin × 14 days. 2024: Test-and-treat programs expanding; NAAT-based diagnosis.
us_prevalence: ~1.6 million chlamydia cases reported to CDC/yr; true incidence ~4 million including unreported. (CDC STI surveillance 2024)
patients_needing_tx: ~4 million patients/yr in the US require antibiotic treatment.
price: $200–$600 per course (novel single-dose oral agent for both genital and rectal; per-course price); differentiation from doxycycline adherence issues.
peak_sales: $200–400M for novel single-dose oral agent with improved cure rate for rectal chlamydia and better tolerability at 10–15% market share.
registrational_endpoint: Microbiological cure (NAAT negativity) at Test of Cure (Day 14–21); composite symptom resolution + microbiological cure.
btd_threshold_vs_soc: NAAT cure rate ≥95% for rectal chlamydia vs. ~80% for azithromycin single dose (established inferiority gap); or single-dose equivalent to doxycycline 7-day for rectal.

symptoms: Walking pneumonia, dry cough, fever, headache, sore throat, ear pain
treatment_goal: Eradicate Mycoplasma pneumoniae/genitalium, cure atypical pneumonia, clear STI
first_line_soc: M. pneumoniae (CAP): azithromycin (macrolide) 500 mg/day × 5 days or doxycycline 100 mg BID × 5–7 days. M. genitalium (STI): doxycycline 100 mg BID × 7 days followed by azithromycin 1g or moxifloxacin (fluoroquinolone) × 7–14 days.
treatment_algorithm: M. pneumoniae CAP: azithromycin or doxycycline × 5–7 days; fluoroquinolones (levofloxacin, moxifloxacin) for macrolide-resistant strains (increasing in Asia and US). M. genitalium (STI): doxycycline × 7 days (reduces load) followed by azithromycin 1g + moxifloxacin 400 mg/day × 7 days for macrolide-resistant strains. Pristinamycin (not US-available) effective for M. genitalium. Rezafungin not relevant; lefamulin (Xenleta, pleuromutilin antibiotic) approved for CAP including M. pneumoniae. 2024: Macrolide-resistant M. pneumoniae increasing in US children; tetracyclines remain effective.
us_prevalence: M. pneumoniae: ~1–4 million US CAP cases/yr with Mycoplasma etiology. M. genitalium STI: ~1% of sexually active adults (~1.8M US). (CDC/IDSA)
patients_needing_tx: ~1.5–2 million US patients/yr with atypical pneumonia/M. genitalium need antibiotic treatment.
price: $500–$1,500 per course (novel pleuromutilin/tetracycline-class for macrolide-resistant M. pneumoniae; per course).
peak_sales: $100–300M for novel macrolide-resistant M. pneumoniae/genitalium-active agent (lefamulin market ~$100M peak) at 10–15% market share.
registrational_endpoint: Clinical cure at Day 10 (CAP); microbiological cure (M. genitalium NAAT negative at Day 28) for STI indication.
btd_threshold_vs_soc: M. genitalium NAAT cure ≥90% vs. ~70–80% for moxifloxacin; M. pneumoniae CAP clinical cure ≥90% including macrolide-resistant strains vs. ~75% for azithromycin alone.

symptoms: Skin ulcer with black eschar, chest pain, shock, meningitis
treatment_goal: Kill Bacillus anthracis, neutralize toxin, prevent death
first_line_soc: Ciprofloxacin (fluoroquinolone) 500 mg PO BID × 60 days (post-exposure prophylaxis and cutaneous); IV ciprofloxacin or doxycycline + antitoxin (raxibacumab/obiltoxaximab) for inhalation anthrax.
treatment_algorithm: Cutaneous (mild): ciprofloxacin or doxycycline × 7–10 days. Inhalation/systemic: IV ciprofloxacin 400 mg q8h + doxycycline/clindamycin combination + antitoxin therapy: obiltoxaximab (Anthim, anti-protective antigen mAb) or raxibacumab (Raxibacumab, mAb) IV. Anthrax antitoxin (Anthrasil, equine IgG) for confirmed cases. Meningitis: triple antibiotic including fluoroquinolone + beta-lactam + protein synthesis inhibitor. Anthrax vaccine (BioThrax) for PEP. 2024–2025: Strategic National Stockpile maintains raxibacumab and obiltoxaximab.
us_prevalence: Natural: <5 US cases/yr (sporadic). Bioterrorism concern: mass casualty preparedness scenario. (CDC)
patients_needing_tx: <5 natural cases/yr + biodefense preparedness for mass casualty. All symptomatic cases require immediate treatment.
price: $50,000–$200,000 per course (antitoxin mAb, biodefense product); government procurement driven. Antibiotic component ~$200/course.
tam_gross: Biodefense: government stockpile contracts dominate; annual US biodefense market ~$500M–$1B (BARDA procurement-driven).
peak_sales: Primarily BARDA/SNS procurement; peak commercial ~$50–100M/yr for novel antitoxin in government contracts.
registrational_endpoint: Survival at Day 28 in confirmed inhalation anthrax (FDA animal rule applies); animal efficacy data in rabbit/monkey inhalation model.
btd_threshold_vs_soc: Survival rate ≥75% in non-human primate inhalation model vs. ~15–30% survival without treatment (FDA Animal Rule endpoint).

symptoms: Sudden fever, skin ulcer at bite site, swollen lymph nodes, pneumonia (inhalational)
treatment_goal: Eradicate Francisella tularensis, prevent pneumonic tularemia mortality
first_line_soc: Streptomycin (aminoglycoside) 1g IM BID × 10 days is historical gold standard; gentamicin 5 mg/kg/day IV × 10 days is practical first-line (less toxic, more available); doxycycline 100 mg BID × 14–21 days for mild/moderate disease.
treatment_algorithm: Severe/typhoidal/pneumonic: streptomycin 1g IM BID × 10 days or gentamicin 5 mg/kg IV daily × 10 days. Mild/moderate: doxycycline 100 mg BID × 14–21 days (higher relapse rate vs. aminoglycosides) or ciprofloxacin 400–750 mg BID × 14 days. Bioterrorism mass exposure: ciprofloxacin 500 mg BID × 14 days (oral, scalable). Post-exposure prophylaxis: doxycycline or ciprofloxacin × 14 days. 2024: Category A bioterrorism agent; BARDA stockpile of gentamicin and doxycycline maintained.
us_prevalence: ~125–250 US cases/yr (tick/rabbit exposure); endemic in South-Central US. (CDC NNDSS 2024)
patients_needing_tx: ~200 US patients/yr; all require antibiotic treatment (fatal if untreated ~30%).
price: $2,000–$10,000 per course for novel oral agent effective against inhalational tularemia; biodefense premium pricing.
peak_sales: ~$50–100M for biodefense-focused approval with government procurement; minimal commercial market beyond stockpile.
registrational_endpoint: Clinical cure (resolution of fever, lymphadenopathy, pneumonia) at Day 14; microbiological clearance (culture/PCR negative) at Day 10.
