btd_threshold_vs_soc: Cure rate ≥95% at Day 14 for pneumonic tularemia vs. ~85% for gentamicin; or oral agent non-inferior to IV gentamicin for inhalational/severe disease.

symptoms: Cat-scratch swelling, fever, lymphadenopathy, bacteremia in homeless
treatment_goal: Eliminate Bartonella species, prevent relapse
first_line_soc: Azithromycin (macrolide) for cat-scratch disease; doxycycline ± rifampin for severe/systemic disease (B. henselae, B. quintana, B. bacilliformis); erythromycin for bacillary angiomatosis.
treatment_algorithm: Cat-scratch disease (lymphadenopathy): azithromycin 500 mg × 1 then 250 mg/day × 4 days. Bacteremia/trench fever: doxycycline 200 mg × 1 then 100 mg/day × 4 weeks. Neuroretinitis or severe CNS: doxycycline + rifampin × 4–6 weeks. Bartonella endocarditis: gentamicin 3 mg/kg/day × 14 days + doxycycline × 6 weeks. Bacillary angiomatosis/peliosis (HIV): doxycycline or erythromycin × 3–4 months. 2024: No new specific Bartonella-targeted drugs.
us_prevalence: ~12,000–22,000 cat-scratch disease cases/yr (B. henselae); ~5,000 other Bartonella infections; B. quintana in homeless populations. (CDC estimate 2024)
patients_needing_tx: ~15,000 patients/yr need antibiotic treatment (most cat-scratch disease is self-limiting; treatment benefits lymphadenopathy >3 months, systemic disease, immunocompromised).
price: $500–$1,500 per course (novel macrolide or anti-Wolbachia-like agent); modest premium.
registrational_endpoint: Lymph node resolution at Week 4 + PCR negativity; for systemic/severe disease: clinical cure at end of treatment.
btd_threshold_vs_soc: Lymph node resolution ≥80% at Week 4 vs. ~50–60% for azithromycin; or single-course cure without relapse at 6 months in immunocompromised.

symptoms: Lung abscess, cavitary pneumonia, brain abscess, skin nodules, chronic cough
treatment_goal: Eradicate Nocardia species, drain abscesses, prevent CNS dissemination
first_line_soc: TMP-SMX (Bactrim, sulfonamide) DS BID–QID × 6–12 months (mild/moderate pulmonary); higher doses + IV for CNS/disseminated. For severe/CNS: imipenem + amikacin + TMP-SMX triple therapy initially.
treatment_algorithm: Mild pulmonary: TMP-SMX DS BID × 6 months. Severe/disseminated/CNS: imipenem 500 mg IV q6h + amikacin 7.5 mg/kg q12h + TMP-SMX; treat × 12 months. Step-down: linezolid (600 mg BID) or amoxicillin-clavulanate + minocycline. Species-directed: N. nova susceptible to amoxicillin-clavulanate; N. farcinica often resistant to 3rd-gen cephalosporins — use TMP-SMX + imipenem. Duration: immunocompromised needs 12+ months, sometimes lifelong suppression with TMP-SMX. 2024: No new agents for Nocardia.
us_prevalence: ~1,000–2,000 US cases/yr (immunocompromised: transplant, TNF inhibitor, steroid users, HIV). (IDSA case series)
patients_needing_tx: ~1,500 US patients/yr; all diagnosed cases require prolonged antimicrobial treatment.
price: $5,000–$15,000 per course (novel oral agent enabling shorter Nocardia treatment; per full course).
peak_sales: $5–10M; very niche market; no dedicated drug development.
registrational_endpoint: Clinical cure (resolution of pulmonary/CNS lesions on imaging) at Month 6; microbiological cure (culture negative from affected sites).
btd_threshold_vs_soc: CNS nocardiosis cure ≥70% at 6 months vs. ~55% historical; or shorter treatment duration (3 months) non-inferior to 6-month TMP-SMX for pulmonary nocardiosis.

symptoms: Swollen lymph nodes, skin papule at scratch site, fever, fatigue
treatment_goal: Accelerate lymph node resolution, prevent systemic spread
first_line_soc: Most self-limiting; azithromycin (macrolide) 500 mg × 1 day then 250 mg/day × 4 days for symptomatic lymphadenopathy (accelerates resolution per RCT data).
treatment_algorithm: Typical: azithromycin 5-day course shortens lymphadenopathy by ~50%. Alternatives: ciprofloxacin, trimethoprim-sulfamethoxazole, rifampin (used in retrospective series). Immunocompromised: doxycycline + rifampin × 3 months (prevent relapse). Neuroretinitis or CNS: doxycycline + rifampin. No FDA-approved indication-specific drug; azithromycin off-label. 2024: No new therapies.
us_prevalence: ~12,000–22,000 US cases/yr (CDC estimate ~22,000 ambulatory visits); second most common Bartonella infection. (CDC estimate 2024)
patients_needing_tx: ~10,000–15,000 patients/yr benefit from antibiotics (severe lymphadenopathy, immunocompromised, systemic disease); mild cases managed expectantly.
price: $300–$800 per course (novel macrolide or short-course oral agent); limited differentiation opportunity over generic azithromycin.
registrational_endpoint: Lymph node volume reduction ≥50% at Week 4 by ultrasound + symptom score improvement.
btd_threshold_vs_soc: Lymph node resolution ≥80% at Week 3 vs. ~50% for azithromycin (historical RCT baseline).

symptoms: Slow-growing abscess, sinus tracts, jaw/chest/abdomen swelling
treatment_goal: Eradicate Actinomyces, drain foci, prevent recurrence
first_line_soc: High-dose IV penicillin G (beta-lactam) for 2–6 weeks followed by oral amoxicillin (or penicillin V) for 6–12 months.
treatment_algorithm: Step 1: IV penicillin G 10–20 million units/day × 2–6 weeks (severe/extensive disease). Step 2: Step-down to oral amoxicillin 500 mg TID or penicillin V for total 6–12 months. Step 3: Penicillin-allergic patients: doxycycline, tetracycline, clindamycin, or erythromycin. Step 4: Surgical drainage of large abscesses often required as adjunct. 2024: No new agents; long-course penicillin remains standard.
us_prevalence: ~700–1,000 new US cases/yr (rare, often underdiagnosed); estimated 2,000–3,000 active cases. (IDSA; case series literature)
patients_needing_tx: ~800 patients/yr need long-course antibiotics; all diagnosed cases require treatment given risk of progression.
price: $5,000–$10,000 per course (novel oral agent enabling shorter course); rationale: orphan-level pricing for rare, difficult-to-treat infection with market size constraint.
registrational_endpoint: Clinical cure at 12 weeks (resolution of draining sinus tracts and radiographic improvement); composite endpoint of clinical + microbiologic cure.
btd_threshold_vs_soc: Clinical cure ≥70% at 12 weeks vs. historical ~50% for standard penicillin; or shorter treatment duration achieving equivalent cure.

symptoms: Sudden fever, painful swollen lymph nodes (buboes), pneumonia, septic shock, black skin necrosis
treatment_goal: Rapidly eradicate Yersinia pestis, prevent pneumonic spread, prevent death
first_line_soc: Streptomycin (aminoglycoside) IM 15 mg/kg q12h × 10 days (historical first-line) or gentamicin IV 5 mg/kg/day × 10 days. Alternatives: doxycycline 200 mg IV/oral × 10 days; ciprofloxacin × 10 days. Pneumonic plague: doxycycline or fluoroquinolone prophylaxis for contacts.
treatment_algorithm: Bubonic/septicemic/pneumonic plague: gentamicin 5 mg/kg/day IV × 10 days (preferred over streptomycin due to availability) or doxycycline 200 mg/day IV/oral × 10 days or ciprofloxacin 400 mg IV q12h × 10 days. Meningitic: chloramphenicol IV crosses BBB. Y. enterocolitica (non-plague): TMP-SMX or fluoroquinolone for severe disease; usually self-limiting gastroenteritis. Y. pseudotuberculosis: self-limiting or fluoroquinolone. Post-exposure prophylaxis (plague): doxycycline 100 mg BID × 7 days. 2024: No new agents; plague rare in US (~7 cases/yr).
us_prevalence: ~7 US plague cases/yr (endemic Southwest US); Y. enterocolitica ~97,000 US cases/yr (mostly self-limited). (CDC plague/yersiniosis data)
patients_needing_tx: ~7 plague patients/yr (all need IV antibiotics; 100% fatal without treatment); ~5,000 severe Y. enterocolitica cases/yr.
price: $5,000–$20,000 per course for plague (biodefense orphan); $200–$500 for Y. enterocolitica.
tam_gross: 7 plague × $15,000 + 5,000 yersiniosis × $300 = $1.6M (negligible commercial; BARDA biodefense context)
peak_sales: <$5M commercial; bioterrorism/biodefense stockpile contracts: $20–50M for plague antibiotic/vaccine combo.
registrational_endpoint: 7-day survival for pneumonic/septicemic plague; microbiological cure (blood/bubo culture negative) at Day 7.
btd_threshold_vs_soc: Survival ≥95% for plague treated within 24 hours vs. ~50–80% for gentamicin (depending on form/speed of treatment); or single-dose oral regimen achieving equivalent cure.

symptoms: Fever, rash, joint pain after rat bite or contact
treatment_goal: Eradicate Streptobacillus moniliformis or Spirillum minus, prevent endocarditis
first_line_soc: Penicillin G IV 1.2 million units q6h × 7–10 days for severe/systemic disease; oral penicillin V or amoxicillin × 14 days for mild disease.
treatment_algorithm: Mild disease: amoxicillin 500 mg TID × 14 days or penicillin V 500 mg QID × 14 days. Severe/systemic: IV penicillin G × 7–14 days, step-down to oral. Penicillin allergy: doxycycline 100 mg BID × 14 days or azithromycin. Endocarditis: high-dose IV penicillin G × 4–6 weeks. 2024: No new agents; very rare disease.
us_prevalence: ~200 cases/yr reported; true incidence likely 1,000–2,000 given underreporting. (CDC Rat-Bite Fever data)
patients_needing_tx: ~1,000–2,000 patients/yr; all require antibiotic treatment (fatal if untreated — historically 10–13% mortality untreated).
price: $300–$1,000 per course; negligible novel drug opportunity.
registrational_endpoint: Clinical cure (resolution of fever, rash, arthritis) at Day 14; bacteriological clearance by blood culture.
btd_threshold_vs_soc: Clinical cure ≥90% at Day 14 vs. ~75% historical for oral amoxicillin; or single-dose therapy with equivalent efficacy.

symptoms: Recurrent fever, severe shin bone pain (tibia), headache, rash
treatment_goal: Eradicate Bartonella quintana, prevent bacteremia recurrence in homeless/immunocompromised
first_line_soc: Doxycycline 200 mg daily × 4 weeks + gentamicin 3 mg/kg/day IV × 2 weeks for bacteremia/severe cases; doxycycline monotherapy × 4 weeks for mild disease.
treatment_algorithm: Bacteremia/severe: doxycycline 200 mg/day × 4 weeks + gentamicin 3 mg/kg/day IV × 2 weeks (Bartonella bacteremia regimen per IDSA). Endocarditis: gentamicin 3 mg/kg/day IV × 14 days + doxycycline 200 mg/day × 6 weeks, or gentamicin + rifampin. Mild: doxycycline × 4 weeks or azithromycin × 4 weeks. Rifampin combination for severe/refractory. Body louse (Pediculus humanus) eradication essential to break transmission. 2024: Primarily affects homeless, refugee populations in urban US.
us_prevalence: ~200–400 US cases/yr (urban homeless populations); estimated underreporting. (CDC Bartonella surveillance)
patients_needing_tx: ~300 patients/yr; all require antibiotic treatment.
price: $500–$2,000 per course; niche indication.
registrational_endpoint: Blood culture negativity for Bartonella quintana at Day 28; fever clearance and resolution of bone pain at Day 14.
btd_threshold_vs_soc: Bacteremic clearance ≥90% at 4 weeks vs. ~75% for doxycycline + gentamicin; recurrence-free rate ≥85% at 6 months.

symptoms: Vascular skin lesions, fever, lymphadenopathy in HIV patients
treatment_goal: Eliminate Bartonella, prevent dissemination in immunocompromised
first_line_soc: Erythromycin (macrolide) 500 mg QID × 3 months OR doxycycline 100 mg BID × 3 months; both equally effective for Bartonella henselae/quintana.
treatment_algorithm: Step 1: Erythromycin or doxycycline × 3 months (minimum); some experts recommend 4–6 months in AIDS patients. Step 2: Clarithromycin or azithromycin alternatives in macrolide class. Step 3: Rifampin added for CNS involvement or severe systemic disease. Step 4: Lifetime prophylaxis (azithromycin or doxycycline) for recurrent cases with severe immunosuppression. HIV antiretroviral therapy essential for immune reconstitution. 2024: No new agents.
us_prevalence: ~200–500 US cases/yr (predominantly HIV-positive with CD4 <100); incidence declining with effective ART. (CDC/literature estimate)
patients_needing_tx: All diagnosed cases (~300–400/yr) require antibiotic treatment; untreated is progressive.
price: $500–$1,500 per course (shorter-course novel macrolide); limited commercial opportunity.
peak_sales: <$1M; exclusively treated as part of broader Bartonella/HIV opportunistic infection management.
registrational_endpoint: Complete resolution of vascular lesions at Week 12 + negative PCR for Bartonella.
btd_threshold_vs_soc: N/A — too small a US population for formal BTD threshold; would require global HIV-positive population.

symptoms: Undulant fever, night sweats, joint pain, fatigue, hepatosplenomegaly
treatment_goal: Eradicate Brucella, prevent relapse and focal complications
first_line_soc: Doxycycline 100 mg BID × 6 weeks + gentamicin 5 mg/kg/day × 7–14 days (WHO-preferred); or doxycycline + rifampin 600–900 mg/day × 6 weeks (alternative).
treatment_algorithm: Standard uncomplicated: doxycycline + rifampin × 6 weeks (oral, practical but higher relapse rate than doxycycline + aminoglycoside). Severe/complicated (spondylitis, neurobrucellosis, endocarditis): doxycycline + gentamicin × 4 weeks then doxycycline + rifampin × 8–12 more weeks; TMP-SMX added for CNS disease. Pediatric: TMP-SMX + gentamicin. 2024: Triple therapy for neurobrucellosis (doxycycline + rifampin + TMP-SMX). No new agents.
us_prevalence: ~150–200 US cases/yr; mostly Hispanic adults from endemic countries, veterinarians, abattoir workers. (CDC brucellosis surveillance 2024)
patients_needing_tx: ~180 patients/yr in the US; all require dual antibiotic therapy for 6+ weeks.
price: $1,500–$5,000 per course (novel short-course oral regimen reducing relapse); modest commercial opportunity.
tam_gross: 180 patients × $3,000 = $540K US. Global: ~500,000 cases/yr but mainly LMICs.
peak_sales: <$5M US; global NTD/zoonosis context with limited commercial model.
registrational_endpoint: Cure at 6 months (no relapse; serology declining; asymptomatic); time to symptomatic improvement as secondary.
btd_threshold_vs_soc: Relapse rate <5% at 6 months vs. ~10–15% for doxycycline + rifampin; or shorter 3-week course equivalent in efficacy.

symptoms: Oroya: high fever, severe hemolytic anemia; Verruga Peruana: chronic bleeding skin nodules
treatment_goal: Eradicate Bartonella bacilliformis, prevent fatal hemolytic anemia, resolve skin lesions
first_line_soc: Ciprofloxacin (fluoroquinolone) 500 mg BID × 14 days for Oroya fever (first-line per PAHO/WHO). Azithromycin or rifampin alternatives. Verruga Peruana: azithromycin or rifampin × 14–21 days.
treatment_algorithm: Oroya fever: ciprofloxacin 500 mg BID × 14 days or chloramphenicol 500 mg QID × 14 days (if concurrent Salmonella bacteremia risk). Blood transfusion for severe hemolytic anemia. Verruga Peruana: azithromycin 500 mg/day × 14 days or rifampin 10 mg/kg/day × 14–21 days. 2024: Endemic Peru/Ecuador; rare imported US cases.
us_prevalence: <50 US cases/yr (returned travelers from Peru/Ecuador); endemic Andean regions.
patients_needing_tx: <50 US patients/yr; all require antibiotic treatment (Oroya fever 40–85% mortality without treatment).
price: $500–$2,000 per course.
registrational_endpoint: Clinical cure (afebrile + hemoglobin recovery to ≥10 g/dL) at Day 21; blood culture/PCR negativity.
btd_threshold_vs_soc: Mortality <5% with treatment vs. ~40–85% untreated; single-dose oral regimen achieving equivalent cure to 14-day ciprofloxacin.

symptoms: Bull's-eye rash (EM), fever, joint pain, fatigue, cardiac conduction abnormalities
treatment_goal: Eradicate Borrelia burgdorferi, prevent Lyme arthritis, cardiac, neurological complications
first_line_soc: Doxycycline (tetracycline) 100 mg BID × 10–21 days (first-line for early Lyme disease, early disseminated, Lyme carditis); amoxicillin or cefuroxime as alternatives for those who cannot take doxycycline.
treatment_algorithm: Early localized/disseminated Lyme (EM rash): doxycycline 100 mg BID × 10–21 days. Lyme carditis (1st/2nd degree AVB): doxycycline oral; complete heart block: IV ceftriaxone 2g/day × 14–21 days. Lyme arthritis: doxycycline 100 mg BID × 28 days or amoxicillin 500 mg TID × 28 days. Lyme neuroborreliosis: IV ceftriaxone 2g/day × 14–28 days. Refractory Lyme arthritis: repeat oral antibiotics or IV ceftriaxone × 28 days. PTLDS (post-treatment Lyme disease syndrome): no antibiotic benefit proven; symptomatic management. Novel: VLA15 (Valneva) and mRNA-based (Moderna/Pfizer) Lyme vaccines in late-stage Phase 3 (2024–2025). 2024: VLA15 Lyme vaccine Phase 3 (VALOR trial) completed enrollment; results expected 2025–2026.
us_prevalence: ~476,000 new Lyme disease cases/yr (CDC 2024 estimate using surveillance multiplier); ~100,000 treated early Lyme cases confirmed. Increasing geographic range.
patients_needing_tx: ~400,000 patients/yr receive antibiotic treatment; ~10,000 with late/disseminated Lyme need IV ceftriaxone.
price: $500–$2,000 per course (novel oral agent for late Lyme); Lyme vaccine $200–$400 per dose series.
tam_gross: 400,000 treated patients × $1,000 avg = $400M; Lyme vaccine 10M target population × $300 = $3B.
peak_sales: $200–400M for novel Lyme antibiotic (oral treatment for PTLDS, late Lyme); Lyme vaccine $800M–$2B peak (VLA15 or equivalent).
registrational_endpoint: Resolution of EM rash and constitutional symptoms at Day 14; for Lyme arthritis: ≥50% reduction in joint swelling at Week 4; serological negativity at 6 months (post-treatment).
btd_threshold_vs_soc: EM resolution ≥98% at Day 14 vs. ~93% for doxycycline; Lyme vaccine efficacy ≥80% against confirmed Lyme disease (VLA15 target); or complete cure of late Lyme arthritis in 95% within 28 days (vs. 90% for 2-course oral therapy).

symptoms: Recurrent high fever episodes, chills, headache, myalgias, rash
treatment_goal: Eradicate Borrelia spp., prevent Jarisch-Herxheimer reaction complications
first_line_soc: Doxycycline (tetracycline) 100 mg BID × 7–10 days for louse-borne relapsing fever (single dose 200 mg also effective); doxycycline × 14 days for tick-borne relapsing fever.
treatment_algorithm: Louse-borne (B. recurrentis): single dose doxycycline 200 mg or tetracycline 500 mg (highly effective); erythromycin if doxycycline contraindicated. Tick-borne (B. hermsii, B. turicatae): doxycycline 100 mg BID × 14 days or amoxicillin (pregnancy). Jarisch-Herxheimer reaction (fever spike 2–6 hrs post-treatment): manage with acetaminophen, hydration; prednisolone may attenuate. Prophylactic meptazinol (opioid) studied in Africa for JHR prevention. CNS involvement: IV penicillin G × 14 days.
us_prevalence: ~500–800 tick-borne relapsing fever cases/yr in Western US (CDC); louse-borne essentially absent in US (epidemic disease in Africa/war zones).
patients_needing_tx: ~700 US patients/yr; all require antibiotic treatment.
price: $500–$1,000 per course; small niche market.
registrational_endpoint: Fever clearance by Day 3 and parasitological cure (negative blood smear/PCR) at Day 14; relapse-free at 30 days.
btd_threshold_vs_soc: Relapse-free rate ≥95% at 30 days vs. ~85% for doxycycline monotherapy; prevention of Jarisch-Herxheimer reaction ≥50% reduction.

symptoms: Skin ulcers, bone pain, joint swelling, skin discoloration
treatment_goal: Eliminate Treponema species, heal lesions, prevent bone destruction
first_line_soc: Benzathine penicillin G 1.2 million units IM single dose (gold standard for all three endemic treponematoses); azithromycin single oral dose (30 mg/kg, max 2g) as alternative per WHO.
treatment_algorithm: Single-dose benzathine penicillin G 1.2M units IM (adults) or 600K units IM (children <10 kg). WHO now recommends single oral azithromycin 30 mg/kg (max 2g) for mass treatment campaigns. Penicillin-allergic: tetracycline or doxycycline × 15 days. Follow-up for late bone/joint disease requiring extended therapy. 2024: WHO yaws eradication using MDA azithromycin (Morges Strategy).
us_prevalence: <50 US cases/yr (imported travelers/immigrants). Global: ~200,000 yaws cases, mainly Pacific/Africa/Americas. (WHO 2024)
patients_needing_tx: <50 US patients/yr; global effort through WHO MDA programs.
price: $500–$1,000 per course (improved oral single-dose formulation); primarily global health context.
tam_gross: 50 US patients × $700 = $35K. NTD/global health — no viable commercial market.
registrational_endpoint: Serological cure (RPR/VDRL titer fall ≥2 dilutions) at 6 months + lesion healing at 4 weeks.
btd_threshold_vs_soc: N/A — single-dose benzathine penicillin achieves >95% cure rate; no meaningful BTD threshold gap to exploit.

symptoms: Fever, severe muscle aches, jaundice, red eyes, rash, meningitis signs
treatment_goal: Eradicate Leptospira, prevent Weil disease (renal/hepatic failure), prevent death
first_line_soc: Mild disease: doxycycline (tetracycline) 100 mg BID × 7 days. Severe disease: IV penicillin G 1.5 million U q6h × 7 days or ceftriaxone 1g/day × 7 days.
treatment_algorithm: Mild: doxycycline 100 mg BID × 7 days or ampicillin 500 mg QID × 7 days. Moderate: IV penicillin G or ceftriaxone × 7 days. Severe (Weil disease with renal failure, hemorrhage, myocarditis): IV penicillin G 1.5M units q6h or ceftriaxone 1g q24h × 7 days + supportive care (dialysis, pressors). Prophylaxis (occupational/military): doxycycline 200 mg/week. Leptospira vaccines exist for veterinary use; human vaccine available in Cuba/Europe (Vax-SPIRAL). 2024: No new US-approved agents.
us_prevalence: ~100–200 US cases/yr (mostly Hawaii and Puerto Rico); global ~1 million severe cases/yr. (CDC leptospirosis data)
patients_needing_tx: ~150 US patients/yr need antibiotics; all cases should be treated.
price: $500–$2,000 per course.
peak_sales: <$5M US; global endemic market driven by government procurement.
registrational_endpoint: Clinical cure (afebrile, renal function normalized) at Day 14; microbiological cure (blood/urine culture negative or PCR negative).
btd_threshold_vs_soc: Mortality <2% vs. ~5–10% for severe untreated Weil disease; or 3-day course non-inferior to 7-day course (simplifying treatment in resource-limited settings).

symptoms: Skin rash, nail discoloration, oral thrush, respiratory symptoms, bloodstream infection
treatment_goal: Eradicate fungal pathogen, prevent dissemination, restore immunity
first_line_soc: Depends on species/site. Superficial: topical azoles (clotrimazole, miconazole) or terbinafine. Invasive: systemic azoles (fluconazole, voriconazole, isavuconazole), echinocandins (caspofungin, micafungin), or amphotericin B for severe cases.
treatment_algorithm: Broad category encompassing dermatophytosis (topical azoles/terbinafine), candidiasis (azoles/echinocandins), aspergillosis (voriconazole/isavuconazole), cryptococcosis (AmB + 5-FC), endemic mycoses (itraconazole/amphotericin B), and rare molds. 2024 novel agents: ibrexafungerp (Brexafemme), rezafungin (Rezzayo), olorofim (orotomide, Phase 3), fosmanogepix (Pfizer, glycosylphosphatidylinositol inhibitor, Phase 3). Growing C. auris threat.
us_prevalence: ~1 million invasive fungal infections/yr in immunocompromised; ~75,000 life-threatening cases/yr; 1+ billion superficial fungal infections globally. (CDC 2024)
patients_needing_tx: ~1 million US patients with invasive/systemic fungal infections require systemic antifungals; much larger population for superficial.
price: $30,000–$100,000/course for invasive fungal disease (IV novel agents); $500–$3,000/course for oral novel agents.
tam_gross: 1M invasive × $50,000 = $50B broad; practically addressable by novel agents ~$5–8B.
peak_sales: Total antifungal market ~$10B globally; novel agents (olorofim, fosmanogepix, ibrexafungerp combined) $500M–$2B peak potential.
registrational_endpoint: Overall treatment success (clinical + mycological) at end of treatment; 12-week all-cause mortality for invasive mold infections.
btd_threshold_vs_soc: Overall response ≥45% for mold infections resistant to standard triazoles vs. ~25% historical salvage; or C. auris eradication ≥70% vs. ~40% for echinocandins.

symptoms: White oral patches, vaginal discharge/itching, esophageal pain, bloodstream infection
treatment_goal: Eliminate Candida, prevent dissemination, restore mucosal integrity
first_line_soc: Mucocutaneous: fluconazole (Diflucan, azole) 150–400 mg × 1–7 days. Invasive candidiasis/candidemia: echinocandin (caspofungin/Cancidas, micafungin/Mycamine, anidulafungin/Eraxis) as first-line per IDSA 2016/2024 updated guidance.
treatment_algorithm: Oropharyngeal: fluconazole 200 mg × 1 then 100 mg/day × 7–14 days or clotrimazole troches. Esophageal: fluconazole 200–400 mg/day × 14–21 days. Vulvovaginal: fluconazole 150 mg single dose. Invasive/candidemia: echinocandin first-line (caspofungin, micafungin, anidulafungin) × 14 days minimum after last positive culture; step-down to fluconazole if susceptible. Azole-resistant (C. auris, C. glabrata FKS mutation): echinocandin preferred; amphotericin B for pan-resistant strains. 2024 novel agents: ibrexafungerp (Brexafemme, triterpenoid/glucan synthase inhibitor) — approved for VVC 2021, expanded indications; rezafungin (Rezzayo, long-acting echinocandin) FDA-approved 2023 for candidemia; fosmanogepix (Pfizer) in Phase 3 for C. auris; olorofim (orotomide) for Candida. Oteseconazole (Vivjoa) approved 2022 for recurrent VVC.
us_prevalence: ~25 million VVC cases/yr; ~25,000–50,000 invasive candidiasis cases/yr. C. auris: ~2,400+ US cases (2024, CDC); expanding. (CDC Candida surveillance 2024)
patients_needing_tx: ~25 million VVC patients (most self-treat with OTC); ~50,000 invasive candidiasis patients requiring prescription/IV antifungals.
price: $50–$500 for VVC (per course OTC/Rx); $30,000–$80,000 per course for invasive/C. auris (IV novel echinocandin/novel class). Noting per-course price for acute infections.
peak_sales: ~$300–600M for C. auris/MDR novel antifungal (rezafungin ~$200M projected; ibrexafungerp ~$400M peak). VVC market: oteseconazole ~$100M projected.
registrational_endpoint: VVC: mycological cure at test of cure (Day 14). Invasive: global cure (clinical + mycological) at Day 14; 30-day all-cause mortality for candidemia. C. auris: microbiological eradication as primary.
btd_threshold_vs_soc: C. auris all-cause mortality at Day 30 ≤20% vs. ~40–50% historical; or VVC recurrence prevention ≥70% at 12 months vs. ~50% for fluconazole maintenance.

symptoms: Cough, fever, chest pain, hemoptysis, breathlessness in immunocompromised
treatment_goal: Eliminate Aspergillus, preserve lung function, prevent spread
first_line_soc: Voriconazole (Vfend, triazole antifungal) oral/IV is standard first-line for invasive aspergillosis (IA); isavuconazonium sulfate (Cresemba) is non-inferior alternative.
treatment_algorithm: Step 1: Voriconazole 6 mg/kg IV q12h × 2 doses (load), then 4 mg/kg q12h maintenance; switch to PO when tolerated. Therapeutic drug monitoring essential. Step 2: Isavuconazonium (Cresemba) — equivalent efficacy, better tolerability, approved 2015. Step 3: Liposomal amphotericin B (AmBisome) for voriconazole-refractory or azole-resistant (cyp51A mutation) disease. Step 4: Caspofungin (Cancidas, echinocandin) or micafungin salvage. Step 5: Ibrexafungerp (Brexafemme) active vs. Aspergillus in vitro; limited clinical data. Olorofim (first-in-class orotomide, Ohana Biosciences) completed Phase 3 for refractory IA — FDA submission anticipated 2024–2025. Combination voriconazole + anidulafungin tested (TARGET trial — no significant benefit overall). Step 6: Surgical resection for localized disease or massive hemoptysis. Chronic/allergic aspergillosis (ABPA): oral itraconazole or voriconazole 6 months.
us_prevalence: ~15,000–25,000 invasive aspergillosis cases/yr in US; ~50,000 chronic pulmonary aspergillosis. (CDC/ISHAM data)
patients_needing_tx: ~25,000 IA patients + 50,000 chronic pulmonary aspergillosis = ~75,000 patients needing antifungal treatment.
price: $40,000–$80,000 per course (IV induction + oral maintenance for invasive disease); voriconazole generic ~$3,000; novel agent commands premium for azole-resistant/refractory cases.
peak_sales: ~$400–700M at 10–15% market penetration for novel agent targeting refractory/resistant disease segment (olorofim positioned here); $60,000 × 10,000 patients = $600M.
registrational_endpoint: Global response rate (complete + partial response) at Week 6 in invasive aspergillosis; FDA guidance on invasive fungal disease uses composite clinical/radiographic/mycologic response.
btd_threshold_vs_soc: Overall response rate ≥45% in proven/probable IA refractory to or intolerant of voriconazole vs. ~25–30% historical for salvage therapy.

symptoms: Sinusitis, pneumonia, skin/nail lesions, disseminated infection in immunocompromised
treatment_goal: Eradicate rare mold/yeast pathogen, restore immune function, prevent death
first_line_soc: Voriconazole (Vfend, 2nd-gen triazole) or isavuconazole (Cresemba) for most rare molds; liposomal amphotericin B for highly resistant fungi. Tailored to specific genus/species susceptibility.
treatment_algorithm: Fusarium: voriconazole IV/oral or AmBisome; combination often required due to high mortality. Scedosporium: voriconazole. Trichosporon: voriconazole. Basidiobolomycosis: itraconazole or AmBisome. Pythiosis: itraconazole + AmBisome (difficult to treat). C. auris (also see Candidiasis): echinocandins (caspofungin, micafungin) first-line. Novel: olorofim (orotomide, Phase 3 data 2024) — first-in-class DHODH inhibitor with activity vs. Fusarium, Lomentospora, Scedosporium. Fosmanogepix (GPI inhibitor) active vs. Candida, Aspergillus, Fusarium. 2024–2025: Olorofim NDA filing anticipated; ibrexafungerp (Brexafemme) for selected rare yeasts.
us_prevalence: ~20,000 US cases/yr of rare opportunistic fungal infections (Fusarium, Scedosporium, Trichosporon, others) in immunocompromised. (CDC/ISHAM estimate)
patients_needing_tx: ~20,000 patients/yr need antifungal therapy; mortality >50% for disseminated rare mold infections.
price: $40,000–$120,000/yr for novel antifungal (olorofim ~$50K/yr anticipated; fosmanogepix IV pricing TBD).
peak_sales: $200–500M for olorofim or fosmanogepix at 25–35% share of refractory rare mold market.
registrational_endpoint: Overall treatment success (complete or partial response) at Week 12 for invasive fungal disease; 42-day all-cause mortality for disseminated infections.
btd_threshold_vs_soc: Overall success ≥40% for Fusarium/Scedosporium infections vs. ~25% historical for voriconazole; or 42-day survival >50% vs. ~30% for salvage therapy.

symptoms: Fever, cough, fatigue, shortness of breath, weight loss, lymphadenopathy
treatment_goal: Eradicate Histoplasma capsulatum, prevent dissemination and relapse
first_line_soc: Itraconazole (Sporanox, triazole) 200 mg TID × 3 days then 200 mg BID × 6–12 months for moderate disease. Amphotericin B (liposomal, AmBisome) for severe/disseminated histoplasmosis (induction), then step-down to itraconazole.
treatment_algorithm: Mild/self-limiting: observation only. Moderate pulmonary: itraconazole 200 mg TID × 3 days loading, then 200 mg BID × 6–12 months. Severe/disseminated (AIDS, transplant): liposomal amphotericin B 3–5 mg/kg/day × 1–2 weeks induction, then itraconazole 200 mg BID × 12 months (lifelong in AIDS with CD4 <150). Progressive disseminated histoplasmosis: IDSA 2024 guidelines support isavuconazole as alternative to itraconazole. Drug levels monitoring critical for itraconazole (TDM). Posaconazole and voriconazole alternatives. 2024: Olorofim (Phase 3) showing activity vs. endemic mycoses.
us_prevalence: ~50,000 symptomatic US cases/yr (Ohio/Mississippi Valley endemic); ~3,000–5,000 disseminated/severe cases/yr. (CDC estimates)
patients_needing_tx: ~20,000–25,000 US patients/yr need antifungal treatment (moderate-severe disease, immunocompromised, progressive disease).
price: $15,000–$40,000/yr (oral novel triazole with better PK/safety vs. itraconazole); $25,000–$50,000 IV induction course.
peak_sales: $150–250M for novel triazole (ibrexafungerp, olorofim, or isavuconazole gaining histoplasma indication) at 20–30% market share.
registrational_endpoint: Clinical success (resolution of symptoms + antigenuria) at Week 12 (moderate disease) or Week 48 (disseminated); Histoplasma urine antigen clearance.
btd_threshold_vs_soc: Clinical success ≥85% at Week 12 vs. ~70% for itraconazole; or superior drug-drug interaction profile enabling use in HIV/transplant patients on complex regimens.

symptoms: Fever, cough, fatigue, chest pain, endemic valley region (Arizona/California)
treatment_goal: Eradicate Coccidioides, prevent dissemination, clear lung infiltrates
first_line_soc: Mild-moderate: fluconazole (Diflucan, azole) 400–800 mg/day × 3–6 months; itraconazole equivalent. Severe/disseminated: liposomal amphotericin B × 2–4 weeks then fluconazole step-down.
treatment_algorithm: Many mild pulmonary cases (healthy immunocompetent): observation only. Moderate-severe: fluconazole 400–800 mg/day or itraconazole 200 mg BID × 3–12 months. CNS disease: fluconazole 400–800 mg/day lifelong (cannot clear CNS infection). Diffuse pulmonary/disseminated: liposomal AmB 3–5 mg/kg/day × 2–4 weeks, then azole maintenance. Isavuconazonium and voriconazole for azole-resistant cases. Nikkomycin Z (University of Arizona) — Phase 2 for mild-moderate coccidioidomycosis (limited data). 2024–2025: Olorofim active vs. Coccidioides; clinical data awaited.
us_prevalence: ~20,000–25,000 US cases/yr (90% in AZ/CA); ~200 disseminated cases. Incidence rising due to climate change and migration. (CDC MMWR 2024)
patients_needing_tx: ~10,000–15,000 patients/yr require antifungal treatment (50–60% of cases; rest are mild self-limiting).
price: $25,000–$60,000 per course/year (novel antifungal vs. generic fluconazole ~$500/yr); premium for improved CNS penetration or oral agent eliminating IV requirement.
peak_sales: $100–200M for novel triazole at 15–20% share (olorofim if approved; or isavuconazole brand).
registrational_endpoint: Global clinical success (radiographic improvement + symptomatic resolution) at Week 12; cessation of progressive spread at 4 weeks.
btd_threshold_vs_soc: Clinical success ≥70% at Week 12 vs. ~55–60% for fluconazole; or CNS cure rate improvement over lifelong fluconazole (addressing unmet need).

symptoms: Black nasal discharge, facial swelling, visual loss, orbital cellulitis, rapidly spreading necrosis
treatment_goal: Eradicate Mucor/Rhizopus, prevent fatal tissue invasion, reverse immunosuppression
first_line_soc: Liposomal amphotericin B (AmBisome) 5–10 mg/kg/day IV combined with urgent surgical debridement. Isavuconazole (Cresemba) 200 mg TID × 2 days loading then 200 mg/day oral/IV as step-down or alternative.
treatment_algorithm: Step 1: Liposomal AmBisome 5–10 mg/kg/day IV (maximum doses for CNS/sino-orbital) + immediate surgical debridement — cannot cure without surgery. Step 2: Isavuconazole (Cresemba) step-down oral once stable or as primary treatment for non-CNS cases; comparable to AmBisome in retrospective data. Step 3: Posaconazole IV/oral as salvage or step-down. Step 4: Reverse predisposing factors: control DM, reduce immunosuppression, treat neutropenia. Step 5: Hyperbaric oxygen: limited supporting data; adjunctive in some centers. Novel: olorofim (DHODH inhibitor) lacks Mucor activity. No new agents with Mucor activity in advanced pipeline. 2024–2025: COVID-associated mucormycosis (CAM) surge in India (2021) subsided; DM remains #1 risk factor in US.
us_prevalence: ~5,000–7,000 US mucormycosis cases/yr (rising with DM, hematologic malignancy, post-transplant); mortality 40–80% for disseminated disease. (CDC/ISHAM estimates)
patients_needing_tx: ~5,000–6,000 patients/yr need systemic antifungal + surgical management.
price: $50,000–$150,000 per course (AmBisome equivalent or novel agent; AmBisome ~$2,000/vial; 10 mg/kg for 70kg = ~6 vials/day; 4-week course ~$300,000 drug cost alone).
peak_sales: $150–300M for novel mucormycosis-active oral/IV agent (high unmet need; AmBisome toxicity + no oral option) at 30–40% market share.
registrational_endpoint: 12-week all-cause survival; complete or partial clinical/radiographic response at Week 12; requirement for additional antifungal therapy as secondary.
btd_threshold_vs_soc: 12-week survival ≥65% vs. ~40–50% historical for AmBisome; or oral agent achieving 12-week response ≥55% vs. ~50% for IV AmBisome (enabling outpatient care).

symptoms: Headache, fever, neck stiffness (meningitis), altered mental status
treatment_goal: Eradicate Cryptococcus from CSF and blood, prevent intracranial hypertension
first_line_soc: Induction: amphotericin B deoxycholate (or liposomal AmB, 3–4 mg/kg/day) + flucytosine (5-FC) × 2 weeks. Consolidation: fluconazole 400 mg/day × 8 weeks. Maintenance: fluconazole 200 mg/day × ≥12 months or lifelong if HIV+ with low CD4.
treatment_algorithm: Induction (HIV-associated meningitis): liposomal AmB 3 mg/kg/day + flucytosine 100 mg/kg/day × 2 weeks (2022 WHO single-week induction accepted in resource-limited settings). Fluconazole 1200 mg/day × 2 weeks as alternative in resource-limited. Consolidation: fluconazole 400 mg/day × 8 weeks. Maintenance: fluconazole 200 mg/day. Elevated ICP: serial LP or lumbar drain. Transplant recipients: slightly different regimens. 2024: Ibrexafungerp shows activity vs. Cryptococcus; fosmanogepix (APX001) in clinical development. WHO 2022 cryptococcal antigen screening guidelines.
us_prevalence: ~3,000–5,000 US cases/yr (mostly HIV+ with CD4 <100 + organ transplants); ~20,000 global deaths. (CDC mycotic disease surveillance 2024)
patients_needing_tx: ~4,000 US patients/yr require immediate antifungal treatment; all cases mandate therapy (fatal if untreated).
price: $30,000–$80,000 per course (induction + consolidation + maintenance ×12 months); novel oral induction agent would command premium.
peak_sales: $100–200M at 30–40% share for novel oral induction agent (replacing IV amphotericin B).
registrational_endpoint: CSF culture negativity at 2 weeks (early fungicidal activity, EFA); 10-week all-cause mortality; sustained mycological clearance at 6 months.
btd_threshold_vs_soc: 10-week mortality ≤15% vs. ~25% for AmB + 5-FC; or EFA (rate of CSF clearance) significantly faster than amphotericin B (-0.4 log10 CFU/mL/day).

symptoms: Pneumonia, skin lesions, bone pain, fever in endemic regions
treatment_goal: Eradicate Blastomyces, prevent dissemination, preserve lung function
first_line_soc: Mild–moderate: itraconazole (Sporanox, triazole) 200 mg TID × 3 days load then 200 mg BID × 6–12 months. Severe/CNS/immunocompromised: liposomal amphotericin B (AmBisome) × 1–2 weeks, then itraconazole step-down.
treatment_algorithm: Mild–moderate pulmonary: itraconazole 200 mg BID × 6–12 months (monitor blood levels; target trough 1–2 mcg/mL). Severe (ARDS, CNS, disseminated): liposomal AmB 3–5 mg/kg/day × 1–2 weeks followed by itraconazole × 12 months. Voriconazole or fluconazole (lower activity) for itraconazole-intolerant. Isavuconazonium increasing in use. 2024: Ibrexafungerp (triterpenoid) with in vitro blastomyces activity; no specific trial. Oteseconazole (Vivjoa) approved 2022 for recurrent vaginal candidiasis, being explored off-label for other dimorphic fungi.
us_prevalence: ~3,000–5,000 US cases/yr; endemic Mississippi/Ohio valleys and Great Lakes. (CDC MMWR 2024)
patients_needing_tx: ~4,000 US patients/yr require treatment; all symptomatic cases need antifungals (untreated disseminates).
price: $25,000–$60,000 per course (novel triazole vs. generic itraconazole ~$3,000); premium for improved PK, drug interactions, or refractory disease.
peak_sales: $50–100M at 15–20% share for novel triazole over itraconazole.
registrational_endpoint: Global success rate (clinical + radiographic + mycologic) at 12 months; resolution of pulmonary infiltrates and skin lesions.
btd_threshold_vs_soc: Global success ≥75% vs. ~65% for itraconazole at 12 months; or significantly shorter treatment duration (3–4 months equivalent to 12-month itraconazole).

symptoms: Oral ulcers, hoarseness, skin lesions, respiratory symptoms, lymphadenopathy
treatment_goal: Eradicate Paracoccidioides, prevent dissemination and sequelae
first_line_soc: Itraconazole (Sporanox, triazole) 200 mg/day × 6–12 months (mild-moderate). Severe/disseminated: IV amphotericin B (AmBisome) induction × 2–4 weeks, then itraconazole step-down × 12–18 months.
treatment_algorithm: Mild: itraconazole 200 mg/day × 6 months (cure >90%). Severe: IV AmBisome 1–3 mg/kg/day × 2–4 weeks then itraconazole × 12–18 months. Alternatives: TMP-SMX DS BID × 12 months (cheap, widely used in Latin America). Voriconazole alternative. Long-term azole suppression in immunocompromised. 2024: Mostly affects Latin American immigrants in US; isavuconazole being evaluated.
us_prevalence: <500 US cases/yr (primarily Brazilian/Latin American immigrants; endemic in Brazil with ~10,000 cases/yr). (CDC)
patients_needing_tx: <500 US patients/yr; all diagnosed cases require treatment (progressive without therapy).
price: $10,000–$30,000 per course/yr (novel triazole shortening treatment duration).
peak_sales: <$10M US; orphan designation possible; global market in Brazil/Latin America.
registrational_endpoint: Clinical cure (resolution of oral/skin lesions, radiographic improvement) at Month 6; Paracoccidioides antibody titer reduction as surrogate at Month 12.
btd_threshold_vs_soc: Clinical cure ≥90% at Month 6 vs. ~85% for itraconazole × 6 months; or shorter treatment duration (3 months) with equivalent cure rate.

symptoms: Subcutaneous cyst, skin abscess, sinusitis, CNS infection (rare), fatigue in immunocompromised
treatment_goal: Eradicate dematiaceous (dark) mold, surgically remove cyst, prevent dissemination
first_line_soc: Voriconazole (Vfend, triazole) with surgical excision for CNS/disseminated disease. Itraconazole for superficial/subcutaneous infection. Posaconazole as alternative.
treatment_algorithm: Subcutaneous phaeohyphomycosis: itraconazole 200 mg BID × 6 months + surgical excision (preferred). CNS (Exserohilum, Cladophialophora bantiana): voriconazole IV 6 mg/kg q12h × 2 doses loading then 4 mg/kg q12h + surgical resection. Posaconazole 400 mg BID or isavuconazole as alternatives. Duration: 6–12+ months. Very rare; 2012 US meningitis outbreak from contaminated methylprednisolone (Exserohilum). 2024: No new agents; voriconazole standard for CNS phaeohyphomycosis.
us_prevalence: ~200–500 US cases/yr (subcutaneous primarily in immunocompetent tropical exposure; CNS in immunocompromised). (ISHAM/CDC rare mold data)
patients_needing_tx: ~300 US patients/yr need antifungal treatment ± surgery.
peak_sales: <$10M standalone; part of broader rare/refractory mold antifungal market.
registrational_endpoint: Clinical and radiological cure at Month 6 (CNS); complete excision and culture negative at Month 3 (subcutaneous).
btd_threshold_vs_soc: CNS cure ≥60% at Month 6 vs. ~40% historical for voriconazole + surgery; or shorter treatment duration (3 months) non-inferior to 6 months.

symptoms: Chronic skin/subcutaneous nodules, sinus tracts, bone destruction, foot/limb swelling
treatment_goal: Eliminate eumycetoma or actinomycetoma, prevent limb amputation
first_line_soc: Actinomycetoma (bacterial): TMP-SMX DS BID + amikacin × 6 months as initial intensive phase, then TMP-SMX alone × months–years. Eumycetoma (fungal, e.g., Madurella): itraconazole 400 mg/day × years; very poor response without surgery.
treatment_algorithm: Actinomycetoma: TMP-SMX 320/1600 mg BID + streptomycin/amikacin IM × 4–6 weeks, then TMP-SMX alone × 6–12 months. Alternative: imipenem + amikacin for severe cases. Eumycetoma: itraconazole 400 mg/day for 12–24 months ± surgical excision; voriconazole alternative. Surgical debridement/excision crucial for eumycetoma; amputation in late stage. Novel: fosravuconazole (E1224, Eisai/DNDi) Phase 3 for eumycetoma — results 2024 showing modest activity. 2024: WHO NTD list; DNDi pipeline for Madurella eumycetoma.
us_prevalence: <200 US cases/yr (travelers from endemic regions: sub-Saharan Africa, Indian subcontinent, Mexico). (CDC)
patients_needing_tx: <200 US patients/yr; all diagnosed cases require prolonged therapy.
price: $5,000–$20,000/yr for novel antifungal shortening eumycetoma treatment duration.
tam_gross: 200 US patients × $10,000/yr = $2M (negligible US; global NTD context)
peak_sales: <$5M US; primary impact global endemic setting through orphan pricing.
registrational_endpoint: Radiological response (grain size reduction ≥50%) at Month 12; clinical cure (grain-free tissue + healed sinuses) at Month 24.
btd_threshold_vs_soc: 12-month radiological response ≥60% for eumycetoma vs. ~30–40% for itraconazole; or treatment duration ≤12 months achieving equivalent grain clearance.

symptoms: Skin nodules progressing up lymph channels, lung nodules, joint pain
treatment_goal: Eradicate Sporothrix schenckii, prevent dissemination in immunocompromised
first_line_soc: Itraconazole (Sporanox, azole antifungal) 200 mg daily × 3–6 months for lymphocutaneous sporotrichosis. Amphotericin B IV for disseminated or CNS disease.
treatment_algorithm: Lymphocutaneous: itraconazole 200 mg/day × 3–6 months (IDSA guideline). Pulmonary: itraconazole 200 mg BID × 12 months or longer. Disseminated/severe: liposomal amphotericin B 3–5 mg/kg/day until improvement, then step-down to itraconazole. Osteoarticular: itraconazole × 12 months minimum. Saturated solution of potassium iodide (SSKI) historical treatment; no longer preferred. Terbinafine: alternative oral agent. CNS: amphotericin B + 5-fluorocytosine. Immunocompromised (HIV): amphotericin B induction + itraconazole maintenance. 2024: Azole-resistant Sporothrix brasiliensis emerging in South America.
us_prevalence: ~150–300 US cases/yr; most are cutaneous/lymphocutaneous. (CDC; dermatology case series)
patients_needing_tx: ~250 US patients/yr; all require antifungal treatment.
price: $5,000–$15,000 per course (novel oral azole active against azole-resistant strains).
peak_sales: <$5M; very small market. Global zoonotic outbreak (cat-transmitted S. brasiliensis in Brazil) larger population.
registrational_endpoint: Clinical cure (resolution of all skin lesions) at 12 weeks and mycological cure (negative culture) at 3 months.
btd_threshold_vs_soc: Clinical cure ≥85% at 12 weeks vs. ~70% for itraconazole; activity against azole-resistant S. brasiliensis (cure rate ≥70%).

symptoms: Slow-growing warty skin lesions, tissue fibrosis, painful nodules
treatment_goal: Eradicate dematiaceous fungi, achieve skin lesion clearance
first_line_soc: Itraconazole (Sporanox, triazole) 200–400 mg/day × 6–24 months; combination with terbinafine (Lamisil, allylamine) 250–500 mg/day used for refractory disease.
treatment_algorithm: Mild: local excision + itraconazole. Moderate-severe: itraconazole 200–400 mg/day × 12–24 months; add terbinafine 250 mg BID for refractory. Voriconazole or posaconazole (Noxafil) for itraconazole-resistant/refractory. Cryotherapy or thermotherapy adjunct for plaque-type. Very limited clinical trial data; management largely based on case series. 2024: No new agents.
us_prevalence: <100 US cases/yr (mostly immigrants from tropical regions — Brazil, India). Globally ~1,000–2,000 cases/yr. (Literature estimate)
patients_needing_tx: <100 US patients/yr; all require long-course antifungal treatment.
price: $10,000–$25,000 per year (novel triazole/topical formulation for shorter-course therapy); orphan pricing.
registrational_endpoint: Mycological cure (culture-negative skin lesions) + clinical cure (lesion clearance) at 24 months.
btd_threshold_vs_soc: Cure rate ≥70% at 12 months vs. ~40–50% for itraconazole monotherapy at 12 months.

symptoms: High fever or hypothermia, rapid heart rate, chills, shock
treatment_goal: Eradicate bloodstream pathogen, prevent septic shock, organ failure
first_line_soc: Empiric broad-spectrum antibiotics within 1 hour of sepsis recognition: piperacillin-tazobactam (Zosyn, beta-lactam/beta-lactamase inhibitor) or cefepime + vancomycin; de-escalate once culture/susceptibility results available.
treatment_algorithm: Step 1: Blood cultures × 2 sets, then immediate empiric IV antibiotics (Surviving Sepsis Campaign). Step 2: Empiric MRSA coverage: vancomycin IV (target AUC/MIC 400–600) or daptomycin for non-pulmonary. Step 3: Gram-negative coverage: piperacillin-tazobactam, cefepime, or meropenem (if ESBL/MDR risk). Step 4: De-escalation after 48–72 hrs based on culture data. Step 5: Source control (removal of infected lines/devices). Step 6: For persistent MRSA bacteremia: daptomycin + ceftaroline or oritavancin (single-dose). 2024–2025: Novel beta-lactam/BLI combinations (ceftazidime-avibactam, ceftolozane-tazobactam, imipenem-cilastatin-relebactam) for MDR GNB. Gepotidacin (fluoroquinolone alternative) under evaluation.
us_prevalence: ~1.5–2 million US bacteremia episodes/yr; ~750,000 sepsis cases. (CDC/NHSN 2023)
patients_needing_tx: All ~1.5 million bacteremia patients require IV antibiotics; this is a universal treatment indication.
price: $8,000–$25,000 per course (IV antibiotic course for MDR bacteremia); novel agents command premium over generics in hospital setting.
tam_gross: 1.5M patients × $15,000 avg (blended across all-comer + MDR premium) = $22.5B addressable (actual market for novel agents: ~300K MDR patients × $20,000 = $6B)
peak_sales: ~$500M–$1.5B for novel broad-spectrum IV antibiotic with MDR bacteremia indication (precedent: ceftazidime-avibactam ~$800M peak sales).
registrational_endpoint: 28-day all-cause mortality + clinical cure composite; FDA complex bacteremia/endocarditis guidance accepts Day 28 mortality as primary.
btd_threshold_vs_soc: 28-day mortality <20% vs. ~30% for current SoC in MDR gram-negative bacteremia; or clinical cure ≥60% vs. ~45% for comparator.

symptoms: Painful swollen lump, warmth, redness, pus
treatment_goal: Drain abscess, eradicate bacterial infection
first_line_soc: Incision and drainage (I&D) is first-line; trimethoprim-sulfamethoxazole (TMP-SMX, Bactrim) or clindamycin added for MRSA-associated or systemic abscesses.
treatment_algorithm: Step 1: Incision and drainage for most skin/soft-tissue abscesses. Step 2: Adjunct antibiotics for abscesses >2 cm, systemic signs, immunocompromise, or MRSA risk — TMP-SMX (Bactrim) or clindamycin 5–7 days; doxycycline is alternative. Step 3: For MRSA bacteremia or deep-organ abscesses (liver, brain, lung): IV vancomycin (Vancocin) or daptomycin (Cubicin); linezolid (Zyvox) for oral step-down. Drainage + source control paramount. 2024 IDSA update emphasizes TMP-SMX superiority for MRSA skin abscesses.
us_prevalence: ~3.5 million ED visits annually for skin/soft-tissue abscesses (SSTI); MRSA-related SSTIs ~14 million US cases/yr. (CDC SSTI surveillance data)
patients_needing_tx: ~2.5 million patients/yr need pharmacologic treatment beyond I&D alone (those with systemic signs, MRSA, or deep abscesses); the remainder are managed with I&D only.
price: $1,500–$3,000 per course (acute SSTI indication); rationale: premium over generic TMP-SMX for MRSA-active oral agent with reduced recurrence.
peak_sales: ~$800M–$1.2B at 15–20% share in MRSA/complicated-abscess segment (~500K patients × $2,250 = $1.1B × 80% realizable = ~$880M)
registrational_endpoint: Clinical cure rate at test-of-cure (Day 10–14) in acute bacterial skin and skin structure infections (ABSSSI); FDA ABSSSI guidance requires ≥20% lesion area reduction at 48–72 hrs as early responder endpoint.
btd_threshold_vs_soc: Clinical cure rate >85% at Day 10 vs. ~70–75% for comparator (TMP-SMX/clindamycin), with non-inferiority margin of 10% plus superiority signal.

symptoms: Elevated body temperature, chills, sweating, malaise
treatment_goal: Identify and treat underlying cause; reduce fever discomfort
first_line_soc: Symptomatic: acetaminophen (Tylenol) 500–1000 mg q4–6h or ibuprofen (Advil, NSAID) 400 mg q6–8h. Underlying cause determines specific antimicrobial/antiviral treatment.
treatment_algorithm: Fever is a symptom, not a disease. Treatment of underlying infection is primary. Antipyretics: acetaminophen or NSAIDs (ibuprofen, naproxen) for comfort — do not eliminate fever in some conditions (e.g., typhoid). In children: acetaminophen preferred; ibuprofen ≥6 months. Avoid aspirin in children (Reye's syndrome). Cooling blankets for hyperthermia >41°C. Novel pyrexia management: no significant pharmacologic advances for fever per se. 2024: Debates on antipyretic benefit vs. harm in sepsis (fever may be protective).
us_prevalence: ~100 million fever episodes/yr across all age groups in US. (NCHS/CDC estimate)
patients_needing_tx: All febrile patients use OTC antipyretics; ~10 million severe fever cases require medical evaluation.
price: $5–$50 OTC (acetaminophen/ibuprofen); novel IV antipyretic for ICU: $500–$1,000 per course.
tam_gross: Symptom management: $500M–$1B OTC antipyretic market in US.
peak_sales: OTC market dominated by generics; novel IV antipyretic for ICU: $50–100M.
registrational_endpoint: N/A for fever as isolated indication; underlying disease endpoint applicable.

symptoms: Fever >38.3°C ×3 weeks, no identified cause after 1 week of investigation
treatment_goal: Identify underlying cause; targeted treatment when etiology found
first_line_soc: Empirical treatment not recommended until etiology found (risk of obscuring diagnosis). Exception: high suspicion for infection in ill patients may warrant empiric antibiotics or anti-TB therapy pending workup.
treatment_algorithm: Diagnostic workup > empirical treatment. CT PET scan increasingly used. If culture/serology confirms infection: appropriate antibiotic/antiviral. If immune/autoinflammatory FUO: corticosteroids or NSAIDs. Anti-TB empiric therapy if tuberculosis strongly suspected (AFB negative does not exclude). No pharmacologic treatment is 'first-line' until diagnosis established. 2024: PET-CT expanding diagnostic yield; naproxen test (fever suppression suggests infectious cause) used in some centers.
us_prevalence: ~5,000–10,000 classic FUO cases/yr in US per IDSA definition (3-week duration, no cause after 1-week inpatient investigation). (Literature estimate)
patients_needing_tx: ~5,000–8,000 patients/yr ultimately receive targeted treatment once etiology found; empirical pharmacologic treatment not a separate category.
price: N/A as FUO-specific drug; dependent on identified etiology.
peak_sales: N/A — FUO is a diagnostic syndrome, not a pharmacologic target per se.
registrational_endpoint: N/A — no FUO-specific registration pathway.

symptoms: Perianal itching (especially at night), disturbed sleep, irritability in children
treatment_goal: Eradicate Enterobius vermicularis, relieve symptoms, prevent household reinfestation
first_line_soc: Mebendazole (Emverm, benzimidazole anthelmintic) 100 mg single dose, repeated at 2 weeks; or albendazole (Albenza) 400 mg single dose, repeated at 2 weeks; or pyrantel pamoate (OTC, Pin-X) 11 mg/kg single dose × 2 doses 2 weeks apart.
treatment_algorithm: Single dose of mebendazole or albendazole 400 mg + repeat at 14 days. Treat all household members simultaneously. Pyrantel pamoate available OTC. Strict hygiene (handwashing, daily underwear/bedding laundering) to prevent autoinfection. Cure rate ~95% with 2 doses. 2024: No new agents needed.
us_prevalence: ~30–50 million US cases/yr (most common helminth in US children; 5–10% prevalence in school-age children). (CDC parasitology estimate)
patients_needing_tx: ~10–15 million US patients/yr actively seek treatment.
price: $20–$100 per course (OTC pyrantel pamoate $10–$30; Rx mebendazole $30–$60).
peak_sales: $50–100M for branded novel single-dose anthelmintic with improved household transmission prevention.
registrational_endpoint: Tape test negativity at Day 21 (parasitological cure); symptom resolution (perianal itch VAS = 0) at Day 14.
btd_threshold_vs_soc: Single-dose cure rate ≥97% vs. ~90–95% for single mebendazole (before required retreat); or no retreat requirement demonstrating 98% cure.

symptoms: Skin rash, fever, abdominal pain, bloody stool or urine, liver enlargement
treatment_goal: Eliminate Schistosoma worms, prevent fibrosis and organ damage
first_line_soc: Praziquantel (Biltricide, isoquinoline anthelmintic) 40–60 mg/kg/day in 2–3 divided doses × 1 day — single-day treatment for all Schistosoma species.
treatment_algorithm: Praziquantel single-day treatment (40 mg/kg for S. haematobium/S. mansoni; 60 mg/kg for S. japonicum). Oxamniquine (alternative for S. mansoni, less available). Artesunate (anti-malarial) has schistosomicidal activity against juvenile schistosomes — combined with praziquantel in some protocols. Corticosteroids for Katayama fever (acute hypersensitivity). Surgical management for obstructive uropathy or portal hypertension. 2024: Arpraziquantel (pediatric formulation developed by MMV/Merck KGaA) approved in selected countries for children <6 yrs; pipeline thin.
us_prevalence: ~300,000–500,000 US cases (immigrants/travelers from endemic areas); ~240 million globally. (CDC 2024)
patients_needing_tx: ~400,000 US patients warranting praziquantel treatment; essentially 100% of diagnosed cases require treatment.
price: $500–$3,000 per course (per-course for novel agent addressing praziquantel resistance or active juvenile forms).
peak_sales: ~$50–150M for novel agent with activity against juvenile worms or praziquantel-resistant strains at 20% share.
registrational_endpoint: Parasitological cure (egg count = 0) at 4 weeks post-treatment; reduction in egg count ≥95% at 4 weeks.
btd_threshold_vs_soc: Cure rate ≥90% vs. ~70–80% for praziquantel single-dose; activity against juvenile schistosomes reducing Katayama fever incidence.

symptoms: Abdominal pain, malnutrition, intestinal obstruction, cough
treatment_goal: Eliminate Ascaris worms, restore nutrition, clear airways
first_line_soc: Albendazole (Albenza, benzimidazole anthelmintic) 400 mg single dose OR mebendazole 100 mg BID × 3 days.
treatment_algorithm: Step 1: Single-dose albendazole 400 mg or ivermectin 150–200 mcg/kg. Step 2: Mebendazole 100 mg BID × 3 days as alternative. Step 3: For complications (intestinal obstruction): IV fluids, NG suction, surgical intervention if needed. Repeat treatment in heavy-burden settings. 2024: No new agents; benzimidazoles remain standard. Mass drug administration (MDA) programs in endemic regions.
us_prevalence: ~4 million US cases (mostly immigrants, low-income populations in Southeast); globally ~800 million infected. (CDC)
patients_needing_tx: ~1 million US patients/yr may need treatment; heavy infections and symptomatic cases prioritized. Many mild cases in US detected incidentally.
price: $150–$500 per course (novel once-daily oral agent with improved efficacy vs. albendazole resistance); NTD pricing pressure limits premium.
peak_sales: ~$50–100M at 10–15% US/global market share (generic pressure dominant; novel drug premium limited).
registrational_endpoint: Egg reduction rate (ERR) ≥95% and cure rate ≥80% by Day 14 stool O&P; fecal egg count as primary endpoint (WHO STH drug efficacy standard).
btd_threshold_vs_soc: Cure rate ≥90% and ERR ≥99% vs. albendazole's ~72% cure rate for Ascaris.

symptoms: Abdominal pain, diarrhea, rectal prolapse (heavy infection), anemia, malnutrition
treatment_goal: Eliminate Trichuris trichiura whipworm, reverse malnutrition
first_line_soc: Albendazole 400 mg daily × 3 days OR mebendazole 500 mg single dose; ivermectin combination improves cure rates for heavy infection.
treatment_algorithm: Albendazole 400 mg/day × 3 days: ~30–90% cure rate (lower efficacy vs. other helminths). Mebendazole 500 mg single dose or 100 mg BID × 3 days: ~20–60% cure. Combination albendazole + ivermectin or albendazole + oxantel pamoate: significantly improved cure rates (~60–85%). Oxantel pamoate (available in combination, limited as standalone in US). Nitazoxanide: some activity. Mass drug administration (MDA) programs use albendazole or mebendazole for school-age children in endemic regions. 2024: Tribendimidine and emodepside (Phase 2) showing activity against soil-transmitted helminths including Trichuris.
us_prevalence: ~2.2 million US cases (primarily Appalachia, immigrants); 477 million globally. (CDC Trichuriasis data)
patients_needing_tx: ~500,000 US patients with moderate-heavy infection requiring treatment; light infections may be monitored without intervention.
price: $300–$1,000 per course for novel anthelmintic with superior single-dose Trichuris cure.
peak_sales: ~$30–80M for novel agent with ≥85% single-dose cure vs. ~30% for albendazole monotherapy.
registrational_endpoint: Egg reduction rate (ERR ≥99%) and cure rate (stool egg count = 0) at Day 21.
btd_threshold_vs_soc: Cure rate ≥80% vs. ~30% for albendazole single dose; egg reduction rate ≥99% at Day 21 in heavy-intensity infections.

symptoms: Skin rash, abdominal pain, diarrhea, cough; hyperinfection in immunocompromised
treatment_goal: Eradicate Strongyloides larvae, prevent fatal hyperinfection syndrome
first_line_soc: Ivermectin (Stromectol, macrocyclic lactone) 200 mcg/kg/day × 2 days — superior to albendazole for strongyloidiasis.
treatment_algorithm: Uncomplicated: ivermectin 200 mcg/kg/day × 2 days; repeat in 2 weeks for immunocompromised or persistent infection. Albendazole 400 mg BID × 7 days (inferior to ivermectin, 60% vs. 97% cure; use if ivermectin unavailable). Hyperinfection syndrome: ivermectin daily until stool/sputum negative × 2 weeks or more; reduce immunosuppression (especially corticosteroids). Disseminated strongyloidiasis: ivermectin + albendazole combination; mortality ~80% despite treatment. Supportive care for hyperinfection. 2024: Screening immunocompromised patients (transplant, hematology) for latent Strongyloides increasingly recommended.
us_prevalence: ~100,000–370,000 US cases (immigrants from endemic areas, Appalachia); ~40 million globally. (CDC estimates)
patients_needing_tx: ~200,000 US patients/yr needing treatment (includes latent pre-immunosuppression screening); all diagnosed cases require treatment.
price: $300–$1,500 per course; ivermectin is generic (~$30/course), so novel drug premium requires superior efficacy in hyperinfection/resistant cases.
peak_sales: ~$30–80M for novel agent with efficacy in hyperinfection syndrome at 20–30% share.
registrational_endpoint: Parasitological cure (serial stool examinations negative) at Day 28; clinical resolution of hyperinfection as secondary.
btd_threshold_vs_soc: Cure rate in hyperinfection syndrome ≥50% vs. ~20% for ivermectin alone; reduction in 30-day mortality from hyperinfection below 40% vs. ~80% historical.

symptoms: Severe itching, skin depigmentation, eye lesions, progressive vision loss, blindness
treatment_goal: Kill microfilariae, reduce ocular inflammation, prevent blindness
first_line_soc: Ivermectin (Stromectol, macrocyclic lactone) 150 mcg/kg single oral dose annually or semi-annually (kills microfilariae, not adult worms); mass drug administration (MDA) program. Doxycycline 200 mg/day × 6 weeks (anti-Wolbachia) kills adult worms — more complete cure but difficult to implement.
treatment_algorithm: MDA with annual ivermectin 150 mcg/kg controls transmission and prevents blindness. Doxycycline 200 mg/day × 4–6 weeks (anti-Wolbachia therapy) sterilizes adult female worms and causes slow macrofilaricidal death — used where MDA inadequate. Novel: moxidectin (Moxidectin Tablet, oral; FDA-approved 2018 for onchocerciasis) superior to ivermectin in microfilaria suppression duration (12 vs. 3 months). Emodepside (Phase 3, ABBV-4083) macrofilaricidal. 2024: Moxidectin MDA programs expanding in Africa; emodepside Phase 3 enrollment ongoing.
us_prevalence: ~1,000 imported US cases (travelers/immigrants from Africa); ~21 million infected globally. (WHO/CDC onchocerciasis data)
patients_needing_tx: ~1,000 US patients/yr + global MDA (250 million doses/yr globally donated by Merck).
price: Moxidectin: $50–$200/dose (US commercial; donated globally); emodepside: $500–$2,000/course anticipated.
tam_gross: 1,000 US patients × $150 = $150K (US negligible); global commercial market if private sector: $100M potential.
peak_sales: $30–100M for moxidectin in commercial markets (travel medicine + endemic wealthy populations); emodepside if macrofilaricidal: $50–150M global private.
registrational_endpoint: Skin microfilaria density reduction ≥95% at Month 12 (FDA moxidectin endpoint); or adult worm viability (assessed by Wolbachia PCR) for macrofilaricidal agents.
btd_threshold_vs_soc: Skin mf density <1/mg skin snip at 12 months in ≥80% of patients vs. ~55% for ivermectin annual dosing; or adult worm death ≥80% at 18 months vs. <5% for ivermectin.

symptoms: Itchy skin rash, anemia, fatigue, abdominal pain, iron deficiency
treatment_goal: Eradicate hookworm, reverse anemia, replete iron stores
first_line_soc: Albendazole (Albenza, benzimidazole anthelmintic) 400 mg single oral dose; or mebendazole 500 mg single dose. Iron supplementation adjunct.
treatment_algorithm: Step 1: Albendazole 400 mg × 1 dose (or × 3 days for heavy infection); or mebendazole 100 mg BID × 3 days or 500 mg × 1 dose. Step 2: Iron supplementation for anemia. Step 3: Severe anemia: IV iron or blood transfusion before anthelmintic. Step 4: Mass drug administration (MDA) programs in endemic regions. Novel hookworm vaccine (Na-GST-1 + Na-APR-1, GeoVax/GAVI) in Phase 2. 2024: benzimidazole resistance emerging in veterinary setting; human trials of novel benzimidazoles ongoing.
us_prevalence: ~700,000 US cases (mostly immigrants/travelers); rare in domestic US. Global: ~500 million infected.
patients_needing_tx: ~200,000–300,000 US patients/yr with symptomatic hookworm need treatment; imported disease primarily.
price: $100–$300 per course (novel benzimidazole or combination for resistant strains).
tam_gross: 300,000 patients × $200 = $60M (US); global NTD market primarily MDA-funded.
peak_sales: $20–50M for novel anthelmintic active against benzimidazole-resistant hookworm, or prophylactic vaccine (US/Europe travelers).
registrational_endpoint: Cure rate (stool PCR/egg count = 0) at Day 21; or egg reduction rate (ERR) ≥95% for MDA context.
btd_threshold_vs_soc: Cure rate ≥90% single dose vs. ~72% for albendazole single dose; or vaccine prevention ≥75% of hookworm acquisition over 12 months.

symptoms: Mild abdominal discomfort, proglottids in stool, weight loss, vitamin B12 deficiency
treatment_goal: Eliminate adult tapeworm, prevent cystic complications
first_line_soc: Praziquantel (Biltricide, isoquinoline) 5–25 mg/kg single dose for most Taenia species; niclosamide 2g single dose for intestinal tapeworms.
treatment_algorithm: General tapeworm treatment: praziquantel single dose highly effective for most cestodes (T. saginata, T. asiatica, T. solium intestinal, H. nana, D. latum). Niclosamide alternative for intestinal tapeworms. Echinococcosis (hydatid cyst): albendazole long-course ± surgery (separate entry). Cysticercosis: albendazole + steroids. Sparganosis: surgical. Hymenolepis nana: praziquantel 25 mg/kg single dose. Diphyllobothrium: praziquantel 25 mg/kg; B12 supplementation.
us_prevalence: ~50,000–100,000 total tapeworm infections/yr in US (all species combined); most imported. (CDC)
patients_needing_tx: ~80,000 patients/yr require anthelmintic treatment.
price: $300–$1,000 per course for novel broad-spectrum anthelmintic.
peak_sales: ~$10–25M for broad-spectrum anthelminthic with improved activity and palatability.
registrational_endpoint: Parasitological cure (stool ova/proglottid negative) at Day 28 for intestinal tapeworms.
btd_threshold_vs_soc: Cure rate ≥98% for H. nana (hardest to treat cestode) vs. ~90% for praziquantel; or oral pediatric formulation with similar efficacy.

symptoms: Fever, wheezing, enlarged liver, eye pain/vision loss (ocular larva migrans)
treatment_goal: Kill Toxocara larvae, prevent permanent vision loss and organ damage
first_line_soc: Albendazole (benzimidazole) 400 mg BID × 5 days for visceral/covert toxocariasis; corticosteroids added for severe organ involvement. Ocular toxocariasis: periocular corticosteroids ± laser photocoagulation; albendazole debated.
treatment_algorithm: Systemic (visceral larva migrans): albendazole 400 mg BID × 5 days or mebendazole 100–200 mg BID × 5 days. Adjunct prednisone 1 mg/kg/day for eosinophilic lung/myocarditis. Ocular larva migrans: corticosteroids (topical + periocular) to reduce inflammation; antihelmintics may cause worsening due to dead larva inflammation; laser/cryotherapy for peripheral lesions. Neurotoxocariasis: albendazole + steroids × 3–4 weeks. 2024: Improved pediatric formulations needed; significant NTD in US urban poor.
us_prevalence: ~2.8 million Americans seropositive for Toxocara; ~10,000–14,000 symptomatic cases/yr; ~700 ocular toxocariasis cases/yr. (CDC National Toxocariasis Survey)
patients_needing_tx: ~10,000 symptomatic patients/yr requiring albendazole; ~700 with ocular disease requiring specialized treatment.
price: $500–$2,000 per course for improved anthelminthic with confirmed larval activity.
peak_sales: ~$5–15M; small US market. Global neglected disease context.
registrational_endpoint: Resolution of eosinophilia at Day 28; improvement in eosinophil count ≥50% from baseline; resolution of pulmonary infiltrates on CT.
btd_threshold_vs_soc: Complete eosinophil normalization at Day 28 in ≥75% vs. ~55% for albendazole; ocular cure (lesion stability) ≥80% vs. ~60% for corticosteroids alone.

disease: Taenia Solium (Pork Tapeworm) Infection and Cysticercosis
symptoms: Intestinal: mild GI symptoms; Neurocysticercosis: seizures, headache, hydrocephalus
treatment_goal: Eliminate Taenia solium, resolve CNS cysts, control seizures
first_line_soc: Intestinal tapeworm: praziquantel (isoquinoline) 5–10 mg/kg single dose or niclosamide. Neurocysticercosis: albendazole 7.5 mg/kg BID × 8–30 days + dexamethasone (to manage inflammation); praziquantel 50 mg/kg/day × 15 days alternative.
treatment_algorithm: Intestinal T. solium: praziquantel 5–10 mg/kg single dose; niclosamide (less available in US). Neurocysticercosis (NCC): IDSA/ASTMH guidelines: albendazole 15 mg/kg/day (max 1,200 mg/day) × 8–30 days + dexamethasone 6–12 mg/day. Multiple/disseminated NCC: albendazole + praziquantel combination. Single enhancing lesion: albendazole × 8–15 days debated. Calcified cysts: antiparasitics controversial; anti-epileptics for seizure control. Hydrocephalus: CSF diversion (ventriculoperitoneal shunt). 2024: Ongoing IDSA NCC guideline refinement.
us_prevalence: ~1,000–2,000 NCC cases/yr in US (immigrants, especially Latin American); leading parasitic cause of epilepsy in endemic regions. (CDC)
patients_needing_tx: ~1,500 US patients/yr with NCC requiring antiparasitic treatment; intestinal T. solium ~500 cases/yr.
price: $2,000–$8,000 per course for improved NCC treatment (novel agent with better CNS penetration and fewer adverse effects).
peak_sales: ~$5–15M for novel NCC therapy; small US market but significant global NTD burden.
registrational_endpoint: Resolution of viable cysts on MRI at 6 months; reduction in cyst number ≥50%; seizure-free rate at 12 months.
btd_threshold_vs_soc: Complete cyst resolution ≥60% at 6 months vs. ~40% for albendazole monotherapy; seizure recurrence <20% at 12 months vs. ~30–40%.

symptoms: Liver/lung cysts, abdominal fullness, allergic reactions, cyst rupture
treatment_goal: Kill Echinococcus, remove/collapse cysts, prevent anaphylaxis
first_line_soc: Albendazole (benzimidazole) 400 mg BID × cycles (e.g., 28 days on/14 days off) alone or with PAIR procedure (Puncture-Aspiration-Injection-Re-aspiration); surgery for large/complicated cysts.
treatment_algorithm: Cystic echinococcosis (CE): PAIR + albendazole (perioperative); surgery for complicated cysts; albendazole monotherapy for inoperable cysts. Alveolar echinococcosis (AE): radical surgery + albendazole; long-term albendazole (years to lifelong) for inoperable AE. Adjunct: praziquantel sometimes added. Modified catheterization techniques (PAIR-based). 2024: ERCE (European Register of Cystic Echinococcosis) guiding treatment selection. Nitazoxanide studied but inferior.
us_prevalence: ~200–500 US cases/yr (immigrants, livestock workers, states with sheep); globally ~3 million. (CDC/literature)
patients_needing_tx: ~400 US patients/yr require albendazole treatment ± surgery.
price: $5,000–$20,000 per course/year (novel cysticidal agent achieving cure without surgery; orphan pricing).
peak_sales: $5–10M US; global NTD context with larger grant/procurement market.
registrational_endpoint: Cyst volume reduction ≥50% at 12 months + CE4/CE5 stage cyst classification; treatment success by WHO-IWGE criteria.
btd_threshold_vs_soc: Radiologic cure ≥50% at 12 months vs. ~20–30% for albendazole alone; or elimination of surgery requirement in 70% of CE patients.

symptoms: Lymphedema, swelling, nodules, vision loss, pruritis (varies by species)
treatment_goal: Kill microfilariae and adult worms, reduce lymphedema and blindness
first_line_soc: Depends on species: ivermectin (Stromectol) for onchocerciasis/loiasis; DEC for Bancroftian/Brugian filariasis; albendazole adjunct for all; doxycycline (anti-Wolbachia) for macrofilaricidal activity.
treatment_algorithm: Lymphatic filariasis: DEC + albendazole ± ivermectin (IDA triple therapy). Onchocerciasis: ivermectin biannually. Loiasis: DEC or single-dose ivermectin (caution high mf burden). Anti-Wolbachia: doxycycline 200 mg/day × 4–6 weeks kills adult worms via endosymbiont depletion. ABBV-4083 (tylosin-based anti-Wolbachia) Phase 2 in progress (2024) for shorter-course macrofilaricidal activity. IDA (ivermectin-DEC-albendazole) now WHO standard MDA for LF where onchocerciasis not co-endemic. 2024: Multiple anti-Wolbachia programs advancing.
us_prevalence: ~2,000–5,000 US cases (immigrants from endemic regions; all filarial types). Global: ~800 million at risk, 51 million infected with LF. (CDC/WHO 2024)
patients_needing_tx: ~3,000 US patients/yr need antifilarial therapy; global MDA serves 300+ million/yr.
price: $500–$2,000 per course in US (anti-Wolbachia or macrofilaricidal agent, replacing multi-year ivermectin); NTD pricing elsewhere.
peak_sales: $5–15M US; global NTD drug market dominated by donations. Anti-Wolbachia commercial potential larger if treatment-as-cure achievable in <6 weeks.
registrational_endpoint: Microfilaremia clearance at 12 months + antigenemia clearance (for LF); macrofilaricidal activity (adult worm death by ultrasound) at 12 months.
btd_threshold_vs_soc: Adult worm death ≥80% at 12 months vs. ~0% for ivermectin/DEC alone (microfilaricidal only); macrofilaricidal activity would represent breakthrough.

symptoms: Eye worm (Loa loa migration across eye), skin swellings (Calabar swellings), itching
treatment_goal: Eradicate Loa loa microfilariae and adult worms, prevent encephalopathy
first_line_soc: Diethylcarbamazine (DEC, Hetrazan, antiparasitic) 6–8 mg/kg/day × 21 days; for very high microfilaremia (>8,000 mf/mL), albendazole or apheresis first to reduce load before DEC (prevents encephalopathy).
treatment_algorithm: Low microfilaremia (<8,000 mf/mL): DEC 6–8 mg/kg/day in divided doses × 21 days with gradual dose escalation. Very high microfilaremia: albendazole 200 mg BID × 21 days to reduce load, then DEC; or plasmapheresis to reduce microfilaria before DEC. Corticosteroids to manage DEC reactions. Note: ivermectin CONTRAINDICATED in Loa loa coinfection (causes fatal encephalopathy). Surgical removal of adult worm crossing the eye. 2024: No new agents.
us_prevalence: ~300–500 imported US cases/yr (travelers/immigrants from Central/West Africa). (CDC)
patients_needing_tx: ~400 US patients/yr; all require DEC treatment.
price: $2,000–$8,000 per course (novel macrofilaricide with safer encephalopathy profile vs. DEC).
peak_sales: <$5M; primarily global/NTD market. AstraZeneca/DNDi collaboration investigating emodepside.
registrational_endpoint: Microfilaria clearance (blood count <1 mf/mL) at Month 12; adult worm killing (DEC-PC antibody surrogate or eye migration cessation).
btd_threshold_vs_soc: Microfilaria clearance ≥90% at Month 12 with no encephalopathy (vs. ~85% for DEC with encephalopathy risk of 0.1–1%); or single-dose regimen equivalence.

symptoms: Itching, skin rash, joint pain, headache, fever (often asymptomatic)
treatment_goal: Eradicate Mansonella filariae, relieve symptoms
first_line_soc: Diethylcarbamazine (DEC) 6 mg/kg/day × 21 days for Mansonella ozzardi; no highly effective drug for Mansonella perstans. Doxycycline 200 mg/day × 6 weeks (anti-Wolbachia strategy) shows efficacy for M. perstans.
treatment_algorithm: M. ozzardi: DEC or ivermectin (single dose 150 mcg/kg). M. perstans (most resistant to DEC/ivermectin): doxycycline 200 mg/day × 6 weeks (anti-Wolbachia endosymbiont therapy) or mebendazole 100 mg BID × 30 days. M. streptocerca: DEC 6 mg/kg/day × 21 days or ivermectin. Novel: ABBV-4083 (Abbvie, emodepside) Phase 3. 2024: DNDi emodepside Phase 3 data expected.
us_prevalence: <500 US cases/yr (immigrants from endemic Caribbean/Africa). Global: tens of millions infected.
patients_needing_tx: <500 US patients/yr; all symptomatic cases need treatment.
peak_sales: <$5M US; global NTD context; emodepside broader NTD indication more commercially viable.
registrational_endpoint: Microfilaria clearance (blood smear negative) at Month 12; symptom resolution score at Month 6.
btd_threshold_vs_soc: Microfilaria clearance ≥90% at Month 12 vs. ~60% for 6-week doxycycline; or single-dose treatment achieving equivalent clearance.

symptoms: Right upper quadrant pain, fever, eosinophilia, jaundice, biliary obstruction
treatment_goal: Eliminate Fasciola hepatica/gigantica flukes, restore biliary function
first_line_soc: Triclabendazole (Egaten, halogenated benzimidazole, FDA-approved 2019) 10 mg/kg single or two doses 12 hours apart with food.
treatment_algorithm: Triclabendazole 10 mg/kg single dose OR two doses 12 hrs apart (greater efficacy). Treatment efficacy ~90%; some resistance emerging. Nitazoxanide as salvage (limited data). Biliary endoscopy/surgery if obstruction persists. Note: praziquantel NOT effective against Fasciola. 2024: Triclabendazole available via FDA REMS after previous investigational status.
us_prevalence: ~200–500 US cases/yr (mostly imported; watercress-eating, immigrants). Globally ~17 million. (CDC/WHO)
patients_needing_tx: ~400 patients/yr in US require triclabendazole treatment.
price: $1,000–$3,000 per course (novel flukicide with single-dose activity vs. triclabendazole-resistant strains; per-course price).
registrational_endpoint: Fecal egg negativity at Day 90 + eosinophilia normalization; serology titer reduction at 6 months.
btd_threshold_vs_soc: Cure rate ≥95% at Day 90 vs. ~90% for standard triclabendazole; or active vs. triclabendazole-resistant Fasciola strains.

symptoms: Mild abdominal discomfort, weight loss, visible proglottids in stool
treatment_goal: Eliminate Taenia saginata from intestine, confirm scolex passage
first_line_soc: Praziquantel 5–10 mg/kg single dose; niclosamide 2g single dose as alternative.
treatment_algorithm: Praziquantel 5–10 mg/kg single dose — highly effective. Niclosamide 2g single dose (alternative). No tissue invasion or cysticercosis risk with T. saginata (unlike T. solium). Confirm cure by absence of proglottids/ova at 4 weeks. 2024: Rare in US; imported cases.
us_prevalence: ~1,000–2,000 US cases/yr; worldwide 60 million cases. (CDC)
patients_needing_tx: ~1,500 US patients/yr; all require single-dose treatment.
price: $300–$500 per course.
registrational_endpoint: Parasitological cure (absence of ova/proglottids) at Day 28; scolex recovery from stool.
btd_threshold_vs_soc: Cure rate ≥98% at Day 28 vs. ~95% for praziquantel; single-dose with broader tapeworm activity.

symptoms: Severe headache, neck stiffness, eosinophilic meningitis
