treatment_goal: Reduce inflammation, manage eosinophilic meningitis symptoms
first_line_soc: Supportive care (analgesics, corticosteroids for inflammation); no proven anthelmintic benefit — albendazole (Albenza) sometimes used but evidence limited.
treatment_algorithm: Step 1: Supportive care — analgesics, repeat lumbar puncture for pressure relief. Step 2: Corticosteroids (prednisone 60 mg/day) to reduce eosinophilic inflammation — shown to reduce duration/severity in trials. Step 3: Albendazole 400 mg BID × 14 days sometimes used; may worsen symptoms if larval die-off exacerbates inflammation. Step 4: Self-limiting in most immunocompetent patients (weeks to months). 2024: No FDA-approved anthelmintic specifically for Angiostrongylus.
us_prevalence: ~30–50 US cases/yr (Hawaii, travelers). (CDC MMWR case reports)
patients_needing_tx: ~40 patients/yr require treatment; all symptomatic cases need corticosteroids and supportive management.
price: $2,000–$5,000 per course (novel oral anthelmintic with anti-inflammatory activity); orphan pricing.
registrational_endpoint: Duration and severity of eosinophilic meningitis (headache VAS score); CSF eosinophil clearance at Day 14.
btd_threshold_vs_soc: N/A — insufficient US patient volume for BTD-qualifying trial; would need global enrollment.

symptoms: Severe abdominal pain, nausea, vomiting after raw fish ingestion
treatment_goal: Remove Anisakis larvae, relieve gastrointestinal symptoms
first_line_soc: Endoscopic removal of larvae (definitive treatment); pharmacologic therapy has limited evidence. Albendazole sometimes used but efficacy unproven.
treatment_algorithm: Step 1: Endoscopic extraction of Anisakis larvae from stomach/intestine. Step 2: If endoscopy not possible or small bowel: supportive care (analgesics, antihistamines for allergic component). Step 3: Albendazole 400 mg BID × 3–21 days in select cases (limited evidence; some case reports show resolution). Step 4: Surgery for intestinal obstruction or eosinophilic granuloma. No FDA-approved drug for anisakiasis. 2024: No new developments.
us_prevalence: ~100–200 US cases/yr (likely underdiagnosed; actual ~1,000+ mild cases). (FDA/CDC estimates)
patients_needing_tx: ~100–200 patients/yr require endoscopic/medical intervention; most resolve spontaneously.
price: $500–$2,000 per course (oral antiparasitic); orphan-level niche.
peak_sales: <$500K; global market modestly larger (Japan is high-incidence) but not commercial opportunity for US pharma.
registrational_endpoint: Resolution of abdominal symptoms by Day 7 + absence of larvae on follow-up endoscopy; patient-reported pain VAS as primary endpoint.
btd_threshold_vs_soc: N/A — too few US cases for conventional BTD threshold comparison.

disease: Bancroftian Lymphatic Filariasis and Brugian Lymphatic Filariasis
symptoms: Lymphedema, elephantiasis, genital swelling, recurrent fever
treatment_goal: Kill microfilariae, reduce lymphedema, prevent progression
first_line_soc: Diethylcarbamazine (DEC, antifilarial) 6 mg/kg/day × 12 days OR single-dose DEC + albendazole (MDA regimen); ivermectin + albendazole used in areas co-endemic with onchocerciasis.
treatment_algorithm: Step 1: Single-dose DEC (6 mg/kg) + albendazole (400 mg) annually for 4–6 years (WHO MDA). Step 2: Triple drug therapy (TDT): IDA = ivermectin + DEC + albendazole — more rapid microfilaremia clearance. Step 3: Doxycycline (anti-Wolbachia) 200 mg/day × 6 weeks kills adult worms via endosymbiont elimination. Step 4: Surgery/physiotherapy for established lymphedema and hydrocele. 2024: WHO GPELF targeting elimination; IDA advancing globally.
us_prevalence: ~1,000–2,000 US cases (immigrants from endemic regions); global 51 million microfilaremic. (CDC/WHO LF atlas 2024)
patients_needing_tx: ~1,500 US patients/yr need treatment; global MDA programs serve hundreds of millions.
price: $200–$1,000 per course in US orphan context; NTD pricing globally near zero (donated drugs).
tam_gross: 1,500 US × $600 = $900K (negligible commercial US market). Global NTD grant-funded.
peak_sales: <$2M US; global NTD context driven by donations/government contracts.
registrational_endpoint: Microfilaremia clearance at 12 months + antigenemia reduction; WHO defines threshold: mf density <1/mL.
btd_threshold_vs_soc: Antigenemia clearance ≥80% at 12 months vs. ~50% for single-dose DEC+ALB; or 90% microfilaremia reduction at 6 months.

symptoms: Neurological damage, eye inflammation, encephalitis (larvae in CNS)
treatment_goal: Kill Baylisascaris larvae, prevent CNS/ocular damage
first_line_soc: Albendazole (benzimidazole) 25–50 mg/kg/day × 10–20 days (no approved regimen; empirical); corticosteroids adjunctive to reduce inflammation.
treatment_algorithm: Step 1: High-dose albendazole initiated immediately upon diagnosis. Step 2: Corticosteroids (dexamethasone) to reduce larval migration-induced inflammation. Step 3: Supportive care (anticonvulsants, physiotherapy). Extremely high mortality/morbidity — most patients diagnosed postmortem or with permanent neurologic deficit. No FDA-approved treatment. 2024: Case reports only; no clinical trials.
us_prevalence: <25 confirmed US cases ever (mostly children, raccoon roundworm exposure); extremely rare. (CDC case series)
patients_needing_tx: <5 US cases/yr; all require immediate empirical treatment.
price: $2,000–$5,000 per course (compassionate use/orphan pricing); market size negligible.
registrational_endpoint: Neurologic functional outcome (modified Rankin Scale) at 6 months + reduction in serum/CSF eosinophilia. Too rare for formal RCT.
btd_threshold_vs_soc: N/A — insufficient patient numbers for BTD threshold determination; FDA would use expanded access/compassionate use framework.

symptoms: Right upper quadrant pain, jaundice, biliary obstruction, fever
treatment_goal: Eliminate liver fluke, prevent cholangitis and cholangiocarcinoma
first_line_soc: Praziquantel (Biltricide, anthelmintic) 75 mg/kg/day divided in 3 doses × 1–2 days.
treatment_algorithm: Praziquantel 25 mg/kg TID × 1–2 days (3 × 25 mg/kg/day); cure rate >90%. Alternative: albendazole 10 mg/kg/day × 7 days (less effective, ~40–60% cure). Biliary obstruction: ERCP + biliary drainage. Long-term risk of cholangiocarcinoma in chronic disease. 2024: No new agents.
us_prevalence: ~1,000–2,000 US cases (mostly Southeast Asian and Chinese immigrants); globally ~35 million. (CDC/literature)
patients_needing_tx: ~1,500 US patients/yr require treatment.
price: $500–$2,000 per course (single-dose novel anthelmintic); NTD pricing limits US premium.
registrational_endpoint: Stool egg reduction rate (ERR) ≥90% and parasitological cure rate at Day 21 follow-up.
btd_threshold_vs_soc: Single-dose ≥95% cure rate vs. ~90% for 2-day praziquantel; or activity vs. praziquantel-resistant strains (emerging concern).

disease: Coenurosis (Taenia multiceps, Taenia serialis, or Taenia brauni Infection)
symptoms: Neurological deficits, seizures, headache, visual impairment (CNS cysts)
treatment_goal: Remove or shrink coenurus cysts, prevent fatal neurological damage
first_line_soc: Surgical excision of coenurus cysts is primary treatment; albendazole (benzimidazole) + praziquantel adjunctive with limited evidence.
treatment_algorithm: Surgical resection of cerebral/spinal cysts when accessible. Adjunctive albendazole 15 mg/kg/day × weeks and/or praziquantel 50 mg/kg/day × several weeks; corticosteroids for inflammation. Very rare; management extrapolated from neurocysticercosis and echinococcosis. No clinical trials. 2024: No new agents.
us_prevalence: <10 US cases ever reported; worldwide extremely rare (mostly sub-Saharan Africa, rare Europe). (CDC/literature)
patients_needing_tx: <5 US cases ever; surgical + antiparasitic treatment for all.
price: Compassionate use pricing; no commercial market.
registrational_endpoint: Radiographic resolution of cysts + neurological functional recovery at 6 months.
btd_threshold_vs_soc: N/A — too rare for clinical trials; FDA would use expanded access.

symptoms: Abdominal discomfort, weakness, diarrhea, B12 deficiency (rare)
treatment_goal: Expel fish tapeworm, correct B12 deficiency
first_line_soc: Praziquantel (Biltricide) 10–25 mg/kg single oral dose — cure rate >95%.
treatment_algorithm: Single-dose praziquantel 10–25 mg/kg. Alternative: niclosamide 2g single dose (not available in US). B12 supplementation if deficient. Most infections asymptomatic; treat all diagnosed. 2024: No new agents.
us_prevalence: ~500–1,000 US cases/yr (Alaska Native populations, Great Lakes, travelers). (CDC/literature estimate)
patients_needing_tx: ~800 patients/yr in US require treatment.
price: $200–$500 per course (single-dose anthelmintic); no premium opportunity over generic praziquantel.
registrational_endpoint: Stool examination negativity for proglottids/eggs at Day 14 + 30-day follow-up.
btd_threshold_vs_soc: N/A — existing praziquantel achieves >95% single-dose cure rate; no clinically meaningful gap.

symptoms: Mild GI discomfort, itching around anus, visible proglottids in stool
treatment_goal: Expel dog/cat tapeworm with single oral dose
first_line_soc: Praziquantel 10 mg/kg single oral dose — highly effective.
treatment_algorithm: Single-dose praziquantel 10 mg/kg or niclosamide 2g single dose (not US-available). Most infections in children from flea ingestion; treat the child + pet anthelmintic treatment. 2024: No new agents.
us_prevalence: <200 US cases/yr (rare; mostly children with close pet contact). (CDC/literature)
patients_needing_tx: <200 US patients/yr.
price: $200–$400 per course; negligible commercial opportunity.
registrational_endpoint: Stool egg/proglottid negativity at Day 14.
btd_threshold_vs_soc: N/A.

symptoms: Pulmonary nodule, chest pain, cough, subcutaneous nodule (dog heartworm in humans)
treatment_goal: Surgical removal of Dirofilaria worm; prevent misdiagnosis as malignancy
first_line_soc: Surgical excision of pulmonary/subcutaneous nodule (definitive). No pharmacologic treatment of proven benefit in humans.
treatment_algorithm: Surgical excision is primary treatment (often diagnostic). Pulmonary dirofilariasis typically found incidentally as 'coin lesion' on chest imaging. Surgical resection confirms diagnosis and is curative. Pharmacologic: diethylcarbamazine (DEC) and ivermectin have been used empirically but efficacy in humans unclear; no clinical trials. 2024: No new treatments.
us_prevalence: ~70–100 US cases/yr (mostly southeastern states, dog-endemic areas). (CDC/literature case series)
patients_needing_tx: ~80 patients/yr; all undergo surgical excision.
price: $1,000–$3,000 per course (antifilarial agent replacing surgery); very niche.
registrational_endpoint: Imaging resolution of nodule at 3 months; confirmed parasite elimination histologically.
btd_threshold_vs_soc: N/A — surgical removal is definitive; no pharmacologic BTD threshold applicable.

symptoms: Slow extraction of guinea worm from skin, intense burning pain, skin ulcer
treatment_goal: Extract guinea worm, prevent secondary bacterial infection
first_line_soc: No pharmacologic treatment. Mechanical: slow manual extraction over days–weeks; wound care. Metronidazole used to reduce inflammation (not kills worm); mebendazole ineffective.
treatment_algorithm: Extraction: slowly wind the worm 1–2 cm/day on a stick over weeks. Wound care: clean/dress ulcer. NSAIDs for pain. Prevention: water filtration (temephos). WHO Guinea Worm Eradication Program: <20 global cases reported 2022 (near-eradication). 2024: <15 global cases/yr; no pharmacologic drug developed.
us_prevalence: Zero US cases; globally <20 cases/yr (near-eradicated). (CDC/WHO Guinea Worm Program 2024)
patients_needing_tx: ~15 patients globally/yr; <1 US case expected.
price: N/A — negligible global cases; eradication imminent.

symptoms: Abdominal pain, diarrhea, nausea, edema, allergic reactions
treatment_goal: Eliminate Fasciolopsis buski intestinal fluke
first_line_soc: Praziquantel (Biltricide) 25 mg/kg TID × 1 day (75 mg/kg/day × 1 day); cure rate >95%.
treatment_algorithm: Praziquantel 25 mg/kg TID × 1 day. Alternative: triclabendazole (limited data). Most infections asymptomatic. 2024: No new agents.
us_prevalence: <100 US cases (Asian immigrants); globally ~10 million (SE Asia). (CDC/literature)
patients_needing_tx: <100 US patients/yr.
price: $200–$500 per course; negligible commercial opportunity.
registrational_endpoint: Stool egg negativity at Day 14.
btd_threshold_vs_soc: N/A — praziquantel achieves >95% cure; no gap.

symptoms: Diarrhea, abdominal cramps, mucus in stool, cardiac symptoms in heavy infection
treatment_goal: Eradicate intestinal/tissue trematode parasites, prevent cardiac complications
first_line_soc: Praziquantel (Biltricide, isoquinoline anthelmintic) 25 mg/kg TID × 1 day for Heterophyes and related flukes.
treatment_algorithm: Praziquantel 25 mg/kg TID × 1 day (single-day course achieves >90% cure). Alternative: niclosamide (limited availability in US). Cardiac involvement (ectopic egg embolism): no specific additional treatment. 2024: No new agents; praziquantel remains standard for most trematodes.
us_prevalence: <500 US cases/yr (mostly immigrants from endemic Asia and Middle East); praziquantel available through CDC/specialty pharmacies.
patients_needing_tx: <500 US patients/yr; all diagnosed cases require praziquantel.
price: $200–$500 per course (novel anthelmintic with improved safety profile).
registrational_endpoint: Parasitological cure (stool egg count reduction ≥95%) at Day 7; clinical resolution of diarrhea.
btd_threshold_vs_soc: Single-dose cure rate ≥95% vs. ~90% for 3-dose praziquantel; or superior cardiac safety profile.

symptoms: Usually asymptomatic; mild diarrhea, abdominal discomfort, nausea
treatment_goal: Eradicate rat tapeworm, resolve GI symptoms
first_line_soc: Praziquantel (Biltricide) 25 mg/kg single oral dose; niclosamide as alternative (not commercially available in US).
treatment_algorithm: Single dose praziquantel 25 mg/kg. Repeat dose at Day 10 if stool still positive. Niclosamide 2g oral (chewed) as alternative where available. 2024: No new agents. Extremely rare in US.
us_prevalence: <100 US cases/yr (rare zoonosis, accidental ingestion of infected fleas/beetles). Mostly children.
patients_needing_tx: <100 US patients/yr; all treated with single-dose praziquantel.
price: $100–$300 per course.
registrational_endpoint: Parasitological cure (stool egg count = 0) at Day 14.
btd_threshold_vs_soc: Single-dose cure rate ≥95% vs. ~90% for praziquantel; N/A for commercial development.

symptoms: Often asymptomatic; diarrhea, abdominal pain, irritability, weight loss in children
treatment_goal: Eradicate dwarf tapeworm, prevent autoinfection cycle
first_line_soc: Praziquantel (Biltricide) 25 mg/kg single oral dose; repeat at 10 days (autoinfection risk requires higher cure rates).
treatment_algorithm: Praziquantel 25 mg/kg single dose repeated at Day 10 (autoinfection makes single dose less reliable than for other tapeworms; ~95% cure with 2 doses). Nitazoxanide (Alinia) 500 mg BID × 3 days — effective alternative, especially in children. Treat all household members if exposure suspected. 2024: No new agents.
us_prevalence: ~1,000–5,000 US cases/yr (most common tapeworm in the US, especially institutionalized children/adults). (CDC parasitology data)
patients_needing_tx: ~2,000–3,000 US patients/yr require treatment.
price: $200–$500 per course.
registrational_endpoint: Parasitological cure (stool examination negative at Day 21).
btd_threshold_vs_soc: Single-dose cure rate ≥97% vs. ~80–85% for single praziquantel dose (eliminating need for repeat dosing).

symptoms: Biliary symptoms, jaundice, abdominal pain, cholangitis, liver fluke-related cholangiocarcinoma risk
treatment_goal: Eradicate Opisthorchis liver flukes, prevent biliary complications and cholangiocarcinoma
first_line_soc: Praziquantel (Biltricide) 25 mg/kg TID × 1 day (3 doses over 1 day; same as clonorchiasis).
treatment_algorithm: Praziquantel 25 mg/kg TID × 1 day (total 75 mg/kg); cure rate ~85–90%. Alternative: albendazole 400 mg BID × 7 days (less effective, ~40% cure). Treat biliary complications (cholangitis) with antibiotics + ERCP. Cholangiocarcinoma: oncologic management. 2024: No new agents; praziquantel remains standard.
us_prevalence: <500 US cases/yr (immigrants from Southeast Asia, Eastern Europe). Global: 10 million infected.
patients_needing_tx: <500 US patients/yr; all treated with praziquantel.
price: $200–$600 per course.
registrational_endpoint: Parasitological cure (stool egg count reduction ≥95%) at Day 14 post-treatment.
btd_threshold_vs_soc: Single-dose oral cure ≥95% vs. ~85% for 3-dose praziquantel day; N/A for commercial development.

symptoms: Chronic cough, hemoptysis, chest pain, pleural effusion (mimics TB), brain lesions
treatment_goal: Eradicate Paragonimus lung/brain flukes, heal pulmonary/CNS lesions
first_line_soc: Praziquantel (Biltricide) 25 mg/kg TID × 2 days (total 6 doses); or triclabendazole 10 mg/kg × 1–2 doses.
treatment_algorithm: Praziquantel 25 mg/kg TID × 2 days — cure rate >90% for pulmonary paragonimiasis. Cerebral paragonimiasis: praziquantel + corticosteroids (to manage inflammation from dying worms). Triclabendazole 10 mg/kg single or 2 doses (preferred by WHO for clonorchiasis/fascioliasis, also active for Paragonimus). Surgical for accessible brain lesions + cysts. 2024: No new agents.
us_prevalence: ~1,000–2,000 US cases/yr (immigrants from East Asia, West Africa; imported disease). (CDC parasitology data)
patients_needing_tx: ~1,500 US patients/yr; all require praziquantel treatment.
price: $300–$1,000 per course.
registrational_endpoint: Parasitological cure (sputum/stool egg negative) at Day 21; radiological response (chest X-ray/CT cyst resolution) at Month 3.
btd_threshold_vs_soc: Single-dose cure rate ≥95% for pulmonary paragonimiasis vs. ~90% for 2-day praziquantel; or faster resolution of cerebral lesions.

symptoms: Migratory painful subcutaneous swelling, eye swelling, nodules
treatment_goal: Surgical removal of Spirometra larvae, prevent CNS invasion
first_line_soc: Surgical excision of the sparganum larva is the primary treatment. Praziquantel has limited efficacy against spargana (unlike adult tapeworms); ethanol injection into lesion used in some cases.
treatment_algorithm: Surgical excision: definitive treatment for subcutaneous, orbital, and accessible CNS sparganosis. Praziquantel 120–150 mg/kg in 3 doses over 2 days (limited activity against plerocercoids). Albendazole (limited data). Ethanol (anhydrous) injection directly into cyst: used for inoperable deep lesions. CNS sparganosis: neurosurgical removal preferred; praziquantel + albendazole combination tried. 2024: No controlled trials; case series only.
us_prevalence: <100 US cases total (extremely rare, mostly imported); ~500–1,000 global cases/yr reported.
patients_needing_tx: <100 US patients over years; all require surgical or pharmacologic intervention.
price: $5,000–$20,000 per course (orphan drug pricing for effective oral agent).
peak_sales: <$1M; ultra-rare orphan disease with no viable commercial market.
registrational_endpoint: Complete parasite removal (radiographic and pathologic confirmation); lesion resolution at 6 months.
btd_threshold_vs_soc: Oral drug achieving parasitological cure (larval death) ≥70% vs. ~20% for praziquantel alone in CNS sparganosis.

symptoms: Mild abdominal discomfort, visible proglottids in stool, nausea
treatment_goal: Eliminate adult Taenia asiatica tapeworm from intestine
first_line_soc: Praziquantel 5–10 mg/kg single dose; niclosamide 2g single dose as alternative.
treatment_algorithm: Praziquantel 5–10 mg/kg single dose — highly effective (>95% cure rate). Niclosamide 2g single dose (not FDA-approved, limited availability). Supportive: confirmation of scolex passage to confirm cure. No CNS involvement (unlike T. solium). 2024: Essentially absent in US; cases found in East/Southeast Asian immigrants.
us_prevalence: <200 US cases/yr (imported); endemic in East Asia. (CDC case reports)
patients_needing_tx: <200 US patients/yr; all diagnosed cases require treatment.
price: $300–$500 per course; no commercial novel drug opportunity.
registrational_endpoint: Parasitological cure (stool examination negative for ova/proglottids) at Day 28.
btd_threshold_vs_soc: Cure rate ≥98% vs. ~95% for praziquantel single dose; or shorter treatment duration.

symptoms: Muscle pain, periorbital edema, fever, eosinophilia after eating undercooked pork
treatment_goal: Kill Trichinella larvae, relieve muscle inflammation
first_line_soc: Albendazole (benzimidazole) 400 mg BID × 8–14 days — most effective against early (intestinal) stages; corticosteroids (prednisone) for severe myositis and allergic reactions.
treatment_algorithm: Early intestinal phase: albendazole 400 mg BID × 8–14 days (kills adult worms and newborn larvae before muscle invasion). Muscle phase: albendazole + prednisone 30–60 mg/day (steroids reduce inflammation; antiparasitic less effective once larvae encysted). Mebendazole 200–400 mg TID × 3 days then 400–500 mg TID × 10 days: alternative. Supportive care (NSAIDs, hydration). Cardiac involvement: corticosteroids essential.
us_prevalence: ~15–20 US cases/yr (improved meat inspection; most from game meat). (CDC FoodNet/Trichinellosis surveillance 2024)
patients_needing_tx: ~15–20 patients/yr; all require albendazole + steroids.
price: $500–$2,000 per course; micro-niche market.
registrational_endpoint: Resolution of eosinophilia and CPK elevation at Day 28; resolution of muscle pain by Day 14.
btd_threshold_vs_soc: Eosinophil normalization ≥80% at Day 14 vs. ~60% for albendazole; muscle phase cure ≥70% vs. ~50%.

symptoms: Acute: fever, swollen eye (Romaña sign). Chronic: heart failure, megacolon
treatment_goal: Eliminate Trypanosoma cruzi, prevent cardiac/GI complications
first_line_soc: Benznidazole (Rochagan, nitroimidazole derivative) 5–7 mg/kg/day PO × 60 days (FDA-approved 2017 for children 2–12; used off-label in adults); nifurtimox (Lampit) 8–10 mg/kg/day × 60 days as alternative.
treatment_algorithm: Acute phase/early chronic: benznidazole 5–7 mg/kg/day × 60 days (first-line; better tolerated than nifurtimox). Alternative: nifurtimox 8–10 mg/kg/day × 60 days. Late chronic phase: antiparasitic treatment controversial but increasingly recommended per 2024 ACC/IDSA guidance for all patients without advanced cardiomyopathy. Chronic Chagasic cardiomyopathy: heart failure meds (ACE inhibitors, beta-blockers, ICD); benznidazole may slow progression (BENEFIT trial: no reduction in clinical events but some benefit in early disease). Novel agents: E1224 (ravuconazole prodrug, azole) Phase 2 failed; fexinidazole ongoing Phase 2 for Chagas (DNDi). GI disease: surgery/botulinum toxin for megaesophagus.
us_prevalence: ~300,000–400,000 US patients with Chagas disease (mostly Latin American immigrants); ~45,000 with cardiomyopathy. (CDC 2024 estimate)
patients_needing_tx: ~200,000 US patients could benefit from antiparasitic treatment; only ~20,000–30,000 currently treated due to underdiagnosis and drug access issues.
price: $5,000–$15,000 per course (improved tolerable novel agent replacing benznidazole ~$500/course generic); US premium for novel effective agent.
peak_sales: $300–600M at 15–20% penetration of treatable population (once diagnosis rates improve with novel drug story).
registrational_endpoint: Sustained parasitological cure (negative PCR at 12 months post-treatment); or reduction in serologic titer (≥2-fold decline in TESA-blot antibody titers); cardiac biomarker preservation.
btd_threshold_vs_soc: PCR negativity rate ≥80% at 12 months vs. ~50–70% for benznidazole; or shorter 30-day course equivalent to 60-day benznidazole with same cure rate.

symptoms: Diarrhea, weight loss, abdominal cramps, malabsorption, dehydration
treatment_goal: Eradicate protozoa/microsporidia, restore GI absorption, manage in immunocompromised
first_line_soc: Varies by pathogen. Giardia: tinidazole/metronidazole. Cryptosporidium: nitazoxanide (Alinia) 500 mg BID × 3 days (immunocompetent). Microsporidia (Enterocytozoon): albendazole 400 mg BID × 3+ weeks; fumagillin (outside US) for E. bieneusi. Cystoisospora: TMP-SMX. Cyclospora: TMP-SMX DS BID × 7 days.
treatment_algorithm: Giardia (see separate). Cryptosporidium: nitazoxanide 500 mg BID × 3 days (immunocompetent); no proven therapy for HIV/AIDS cryptosporidiosis except immune reconstitution via ART. Microsporidia: albendazole 400 mg BID × 21 days (systemic) or fumagillin for E. bieneusi (Europe). Cyclosporiasis: TMP-SMX DS BID × 7 days. Cystoisospora (Isospora): TMP-SMX DS BID × 10 days with suppression. Blastocystis: metronidazole. 2024: No new protozoan antiparasitics approved; nitazoxanide expanded indications being studied.
us_prevalence: ~10M annual intestinal protozoan infections; ~750,000 Cryptosporidium cases/yr (CDC); microsporidia primarily in HIV patients (~5,000/yr).
patients_needing_tx: ~500,000–700,000 US patients/yr need pharmacologic treatment; immunocompromised cohort (HIV, transplant) most critical.
price: $500–$3,000 per course for novel antiprotozoal active in immunocompromised; $200–$500 per course for immunocompetent.
peak_sales: $100–200M for novel broad-spectrum antiprotozoal active in immunocompromised at 15–20% share.
registrational_endpoint: Parasitological cure (stool PCR/antigen negative) at Day 14; or reduction in stool frequency/consistency (clinical endpoint) for immunocompromised patients.
btd_threshold_vs_soc: Cryptosporidium clearance ≥60% in HIV patients on ART vs. ~25% for nitazoxanide; or microsporidia clearance ≥80% vs. ~60% for albendazole.

symptoms: Watery diarrhea, bloating, gas, nausea, weight loss
treatment_goal: Eradicate Giardia, resolve diarrhea, prevent reinfection
first_line_soc: Metronidazole (Flagyl, nitroimidazole) 250 mg TID × 5–7 days; tinidazole (Tindamax) 2g single dose is preferred for simplicity.
treatment_algorithm: Step 1: Tinidazole 2g single dose (>90% cure, preferred) or metronidazole 250 mg TID × 5–7 days. Step 2: Nitazoxanide (Alinia) 500 mg BID × 3 days — preferred in children, safe in pregnancy consideration. Step 3: Treatment failure or refractory: combination metronidazole + quinacrine, or albendazole 400 mg/day × 5 days. Step 4: Immunocompromised (HIV/agammaglobulinemia): prolonged courses (14–21 days) or combination therapy. 2024: No new agents; mebendazole has modest activity. Paromomycin for pregnancy (poorly absorbed).
us_prevalence: ~1.2 million symptomatic US cases/yr; most common intestinal parasite in the US. (CDC parasitology surveillance)
patients_needing_tx: ~800,000–1,000,000 patients/yr require pharmacologic treatment; most symptomatic cases warrant therapy.
price: $200–$600 per course (short-course oral agent); rationale: modest premium over generic tinidazole for single-dose improved-tolerability formulation.
peak_sales: ~$50–100M at 10–15% share; dominated by generics. A novel single-dose agent with superior tolerability could capture $80–120M.
registrational_endpoint: Parasitological cure (stool antigen test or microscopy negative) at Day 14; clinical cure (resolution of diarrhea) as co-primary.
btd_threshold_vs_soc: Single-dose cure rate ≥95% vs. ~90% for tinidazole; or superior tolerability (GI AEs <5% vs. ~20% for metronidazole).

symptoms: Flu-like illness (immunocompetent), encephalitis, chorioretinitis (immunocompromised/congenital)
treatment_goal: Eliminate Toxoplasma gondii, prevent CNS disease reactivation, protect fetal brain
first_line_soc: Pyrimethamine (Daraprim, antifolate) + sulfadiazine + leucovorin (folinic acid) × 6 weeks for toxoplasmic encephalitis in HIV/immunocompromised; TMP-SMX is equivalent per recent data.
treatment_algorithm: Immunocompetent acute toxoplasmosis: usually no treatment needed (self-limiting). Toxoplasmic encephalitis (HIV/AIDS): pyrimethamine 200 mg loading then 75 mg/day + sulfadiazine 1–1.5g QID + leucovorin 10–25 mg/day × 6 weeks; then suppressive therapy with half-doses until CD4 >200 on ART. TMP-SMX DS BID shown equivalent to classic regimen (simpler, preferred by some). Ocular toxoplasmosis: pyrimethamine + sulfadiazine + prednisone. Congenital toxoplasmosis: pyrimethamine + sulfadiazine × 12 months. Pregnancy: spiramycin (for maternal infection before fetal transmission); pyrimethamine + sulfadiazine after 18 weeks if fetal infection confirmed. 2024: Clindamycin + pyrimethamine alternative for sulfa allergy.
us_prevalence: ~11% US seroprevalence; ~750–1,500 active toxoplasmic encephalitis cases/yr (primarily HIV/AIDS, transplant); ~400–500 congenital cases/yr. (CDC; NHSN)
patients_needing_tx: ~2,000–3,000 US patients/yr require pharmacologic treatment (immunocompromised + congenital); immunocompetent cases rarely need treatment.
price: $2,000–$8,000 per course (novel oral agent active against tissue cysts, addressing chronic infection); annual suppressive ~$3,000–$8,000.
tam_gross: 3,000 × $5,000 = $15M; chronic suppressive in HIV adds ~$8,000/yr × 10,000 patients = $80M
peak_sales: ~$30–80M for novel agent effective against latent Toxoplasma cysts (addressing unmet need in immunocompromised); includes ocular and congenital market.
registrational_endpoint: Resolution of CNS lesions on MRI at 6 weeks (>50% size reduction); clinical success (neurological improvement, defervescence) at Day 42; prevention of relapse at 12 months.
btd_threshold_vs_soc: Complete radiological response ≥50% at 6 weeks vs. ~40% for pyrimethamine + sulfadiazine; or relapse rate <10% at 12 months vs. ~20–30% without suppressive therapy.

symptoms: Watery diarrhea (profuse), stomach cramps, nausea, low-grade fever
treatment_goal: Clear parasite, restore hydration, prevent wasting in immunocompromised
first_line_soc: Nitazoxanide (Alinia, thiazolide) 500 mg BID × 3 days for immunocompetent adults. In HIV/immunocompromised: ART optimization is key; nitazoxanide has limited efficacy in severe immunosuppression.
treatment_algorithm: Immunocompetent: nitazoxanide 500 mg BID × 3 days; supportive rehydration. Immunocompromised (HIV): ART to raise CD4 is most important intervention; nitazoxanide less effective; paromomycin (aminoglycoside) 500 mg QID × 14–28 days as salvage. Malnourished children: nitazoxanide 100 mg BID × 3 days (pediatric dose). 2024: No new approved agents; SLV213 (clofazimine derivative) and nitazoxanide combinations under study. Halofuginone (veterinary) has human activity but not approved.
us_prevalence: ~800,000–1 million US cases/yr; second most common cause of waterborne diarrhea after norovirus. (CDC 2024 estimate)
patients_needing_tx: ~100,000 patients/yr require pharmacologic treatment (immunocompromised + severe cases); mild immunocompetent cases self-resolve.
price: $300–$1,500 per course (improved nitazoxanide-like agent effective in immunocompromised; per-course price).
peak_sales: $30–60M at 20–30% share for novel anti-cryptosporidial agent with HIV-positive activity.
registrational_endpoint: Oocyst clearance at Day 7 (stool O&P or PCR negativity); reduction in stool frequency to <3/day at Day 5.
btd_threshold_vs_soc: Oocyst clearance ≥70% in HIV+ (CD4 <100) patients at Day 14 vs. ~10–20% for nitazoxanide in severely immunocompromised.

symptoms: Chronic diarrhea, wasting, sinusitis, keratitis, muscle inflammation in immunocompromised
treatment_goal: Eradicate microsporidian parasite, restore immunity in HIV/transplant patients
first_line_soc: Albendazole (Albenza, benzimidazole) 400 mg BID × 3+ weeks for Encephalitozoon species. ART initiation (immune reconstitution) is key for HIV patients. Fumagillin (not US-approved) for E. bieneusi intestinal microsporidiosis.
treatment_algorithm: Encephalitozoon intestinalis: albendazole 400 mg BID × 21 days. Enterocytozoon bieneusi: fumagillin (TNP-470) most effective but unavailable in US; albendazole less effective; nitazoxanide off-label. Disseminated: albendazole with ART initiation. Ocular: topical fumagillin; azithromycin adjunct; topical voriconazole off-label. Severely immunocompromised: continue albendazole until immune reconstitution. 2024: No new approved agents.
us_prevalence: ~5,000–10,000 US microsporidiosis cases/yr (primarily HIV/AIDS patients with CD4 <100; also transplant). (CDC/MMWR estimates)
patients_needing_tx: ~5,000 HIV/immunocompromised patients/yr need albendazole and/or ART-induced immune recovery.
price: $3,000–$15,000 per course (US-approved fumagillin equivalent or novel anti-microsporidial).
peak_sales: $20–50M for US-approved fumagillin equivalent or novel agent at 30% share of immunocompromised microsporidiosis.
registrational_endpoint: Stool microsporidian spore count reduction to undetectable by PCR at Week 12; resolution of diarrhea (stool frequency ≤2/day) at Week 4.
btd_threshold_vs_soc: Spore clearance ≥70% at Week 12 for E. bieneusi vs. ~30–40% for albendazole; or fumagillin-equivalent achieving ≥80% symptom resolution.

symptoms: Hemolytic anemia, fever, fatigue, dark urine, chills
treatment_goal: Clear Babesia parasitemia, prevent severe hemolysis/death
first_line_soc: Atovaquone 750 mg BID + azithromycin 500 mg × 1 then 250 mg/day × 7–10 days for mild–moderate disease; clindamycin + quinine for severe disease.
treatment_algorithm: Mild–moderate: atovaquone + azithromycin × 7–10 days (standard combination). Severe (immunocompromised, asplenic, elderly): clindamycin 600 mg IV q8h + quinine 650 mg PO q6–8h × 7–10 days; exchange transfusion for parasitemia >10% or severe hemolysis. 2024 IDSA babesiosis guidelines (published 2020, reaffirmed 2024): atovaquone + azithromycin preferred for most; extend to 6 weeks in immunocompromised. Drug-resistant cases: combination strategies under study.
us_prevalence: ~3,000–4,000 reported US cases/yr; actual ~10,000–20,000 (significant underreporting). Tick-borne, Northeast/Upper Midwest. (CDC ArboNET)
patients_needing_tx: ~15,000 US patients/yr estimated to need pharmacologic treatment; most symptomatic cases require antibiotics.
price: $1,500–$3,500 per course (novel antiparasitic vs. generic atovaquone/azithromycin combination); premium for drug-resistant cases.
peak_sales: ~$20–40M at 30–40% share targeting severe/refractory babesiosis segment.
registrational_endpoint: Parasite clearance by Day 7 (peripheral blood smear negativity); symptom resolution composite; recrudescence rate at Day 42.
btd_threshold_vs_soc: Parasite clearance ≥90% by Day 7 vs. ~80–85% for atovaquone/azithromycin; recrudescence rate <5% vs. ~15% in immunocompromised.

symptoms: Skin ulcers (cutaneous), fever, weight loss, spleen/liver enlargement (visceral), mucosal lesions
treatment_goal: Eradicate Leishmania parasite, heal lesions, prevent relapse and death
first_line_soc: Visceral leishmaniasis (VL): liposomal amphotericin B (AmBisome) 3–5 mg/kg IV × 5 days (FDA-approved for VL). Cutaneous (CL): miltefosine (Impavido, alkylphospholipid) oral × 28 days or pentamidine/meglumine antimoniate (Glucantime) IM.
treatment_algorithm: VL (kala-azar): liposomal AmBisome single or multi-dose (WHO-recommended); miltefosine 2.5 mg/kg/day × 28 days (oral, FDA-approved). Indian VL: AmBisome single-dose 10 mg/kg. Old World CL: sodium stibogluconate (Pentostam, antimonial) IM/IV × 20 days or miltefosine. New World CL/MCL: miltefosine 28 days or pentamidine. HIV-VL: AmBisome induction + secondary prophylaxis with pentamidine or miltefosine. Novel: LXE408 (Novartis, CRK12 inhibitor) Phase 2; DNDi pipeline includes multiple leads. 2024: Combination regimens (miltefosine + SSG) under evaluation.
us_prevalence: ~1,000–2,000 US CL cases/yr (travelers/immigrants); <50 VL cases/yr. Global: 700,000–1M new CL + 50,000–90,000 VL cases/yr. (WHO/CDC)
patients_needing_tx: ~1,500 US patients/yr need pharmacologic treatment; all VL patients and most CL patients require therapy.
price: $10,000–$50,000 per course for VL (AmBisome comparable); oral VL agent $2,000–$10,000/course.
peak_sales: $50–100M for novel oral VL agent (CRK12 inhibitor) if approved globally with premium pricing; global access pricing constrains commercial potential.
registrational_endpoint: Initial cure at Day 28 (absence of parasitemia) + definitive cure at 6-month follow-up (no relapse); splenic aspirate/PCR negativity for VL.
btd_threshold_vs_soc: 6-month definitive cure ≥95% for VL vs. ~90% for AmBisome single dose; or all-oral 14-day regimen achieving equivalent cure (eliminating IV requirement).

symptoms: Granulomatous encephalitis, skin lesions, sinusitis (Acanthamoeba, Balamuthia, Naegleria)
treatment_goal: Kill free-living ameba, preserve neurological function
first_line_soc: No well-established SoC. Naegleria fowleri (PAM): amphotericin B IV + miltefosine + rifampin combination. Balamuthia: combination of pentamidine + sulfadiazine + fluconazole + azithromycin + flucytosine + miltefosine.
treatment_algorithm: Naegleria fowleri (primary amebic meningoencephalitis): IV amphotericin B + miltefosine (Impavido) oral 50 mg TID × 28 days + rifampin + fluconazole. Mortality ~97% historically; miltefosine added after US survivor cases 2013–2016. Balamuthia GAE: pentamidine + sulfadiazine + fluconazole + flucytosine + clarithromycin + miltefosine. Acanthamoeba GAE: combination immunosuppression reduction + miltefosine + fluconazole/pentamidine. 2024: Nitroxoline emerging in vitro data; no new approved agents.
us_prevalence: Naegleria PAM: ~3–5 US cases/yr; Balamuthia GAE: ~5–10/yr; Acanthamoeba GAE: ~100–200/yr (mostly immunocompromised). (CDC surveillance 2024)
patients_needing_tx: ~150–200 US patients/yr; all require aggressive multimodal treatment.
price: $10,000–$50,000 per course (miltefosine + combination; per-course acute intensive treatment).
peak_sales: $5–10M for miltefosine/novel amebicide in FDA-approved indication for free-living ameba.
registrational_endpoint: Survival at 30 days (primary for PAM given near-universal lethality); neurological functional outcome (GCS/mRS) at Day 30.
btd_threshold_vs_soc: Survival ≥40% at 30 days for Naegleria PAM vs. ~3–5% historical; or Balamuthia GAE survival ≥50% vs. <20% historical.

symptoms: Cyclical fever/chills, sweating, headache, vomiting, anemia, splenomegaly
treatment_goal: Rapidly clear parasitemia, prevent severe malaria and death, prevent relapse
first_line_soc: Uncomplicated P. falciparum: artemisinin-based combination therapy (ACT) — artemether-lumefantrine (Coartem, CDC recommended in US) or atovaquone-proguanil (Malarone). P. vivax/ovale: chloroquine + primaquine (to clear hypnozoites) or tafenoquine (Arakoda) single dose.
treatment_algorithm: Uncomplicated falciparum malaria: artemether-lumefantrine (Coartem) × 3 days or atovaquone-proguanil (Malarone) × 3 days. Severe falciparum: IV artesunate (Artesun, CDC-distributed for severe malaria in US) 2.4 mg/kg IV at 0, 12, 24 hrs then daily × minimum 24 hrs, then oral ACT. Chloroquine-sensitive P. vivax: chloroquine 1g then 500 mg at 6/24/48 hrs. Radical cure (vivax/ovale hypnozoites): primaquine 0.5 mg/kg/day × 14 days or tafenoquine (Krintafel) 300 mg single dose (adults; G6PD normal). Chemoprophylaxis: atovaquone-proguanil daily or mefloquine weekly or doxycycline. Artemisinin-resistant P. falciparum (Southeast Asia, expanding to Africa): triple ACT (KAF156+lumefantrine, GSK3537872, MMV390048) in trials. Novel: KAF156 (novartis ganaplacide), MMV390048 (cipargamin-like), M5717 (PfEF2 inhibitor). 2024: partial artemisinin resistance (kelch13 mutations) documented in East Africa; WHO emergency alert.
us_prevalence: ~2,200 imported US malaria cases/yr (CDC 2024); ~2,000 P. falciparum. Global: ~240 million cases/yr; 608,000 deaths/yr (WHO 2024).
patients_needing_tx: ~2,200 US patients/yr need treatment; globally all symptomatic cases require ACT.
price: $500–$3,000 per treatment course (novel ACT-replacing agent in US/Europe); mass treatment in Africa $1–10/course via GAVI/AMC.
tam_gross: 2,200 US patients × $2,000 = $4.4M (US only); global commercial potential $1–2B for novel anti-malarials in private markets.
peak_sales: $200–500M for novel single-dose or short-course anti-malarial with ACT-resistance coverage (ganaplacide + lumefantrine 3-day); global ACT market $500M–$1B including endemic private market.
registrational_endpoint: Adequate Clinical and Parasitological Response (ACPR) at Day 28 for uncomplicated P. falciparum (PCR-corrected); parasite clearance time; Day 3 parasite positivity rate.
btd_threshold_vs_soc: ACPR ≥95% at Day 28 (PCR-corrected) including artemisinin-partial-resistant strains vs. ~75–80% for ACT in kelch13-mutant regions; or true single-dose cure ≥90% vs. 3-day ACT.

symptoms: Bloody diarrhea, abdominal cramps, fever, liver pain
treatment_goal: Eliminate Entamoeba histolytica, prevent liver abscess
first_line_soc: Metronidazole (Flagyl, nitroimidazole) 500–750 mg TID × 7–10 days followed by luminal agent paromomycin (Humatin, aminoglycoside) or iodoquinol to eliminate cysts.
treatment_algorithm: Step 1: Metronidazole (or tinidazole — better tolerated, 2g/day × 3–5 days) for invasive amebiasis. Step 2: Luminal cyst eradication with paromomycin 25–35 mg/kg/day × 7 days or iodoquinol 650 mg TID × 20 days. Step 3: Amebic liver abscess — metronidazole + percutaneous drainage if >5 cm or no improvement in 72 hrs. Asymptomatic intestinal carriage: treat with luminal agent only. 2024: No new agents.
us_prevalence: ~3,000–5,000 symptomatic US cases/yr; mostly immigrants and travelers. (CDC)
patients_needing_tx: ~4,000 US patients/yr require metronidazole treatment; asymptomatic carriers (~10,000) may need luminal agents.
price: $500–$1,500 per course (short-course oral agent); rationale: modest premium over generics for single-agent cure without luminal retreatment.
peak_sales: ~$5–10M at 30–40% share; small market. Global NTD context.
registrational_endpoint: Parasitological cure (stool antigen test negative) at Day 28; clinical response (resolution of diarrhea/abscess) as secondary.
btd_threshold_vs_soc: Single-agent cure rate ≥90% (eliminating need for sequential luminal agent) vs. ~85% for metronidazole + paromomycin sequential therapy.

symptoms: Severe eye pain, photophobia, blurred vision, red eye
treatment_goal: Eliminate Acanthamoeba from cornea, preserve vision
first_line_soc: Topical polyhexamethylene biguanide (PHMB 0.02%) and/or chlorhexidine 0.02% combined with topical propamidine isethionate (Brolene) — antiseptic/biguanide combination.
treatment_algorithm: Step 1: Intensive topical PHMB + propamidine (every 1 hour initially for 48 hrs, then tapered). Step 2: Add topical neomycin or hexamidine in refractory cases. Step 3: Oral voriconazole (Vfend) or miltefosine (Impavido) used off-label for refractory/deep keratitis. Step 4: Penetrating keratoplasty (corneal transplant) for scarring. Prolonged treatment 3–12 months required. 2024–2025: Miltefosine gaining traction; no FDA-approved agent for this indication.
us_prevalence: ~200–250 US cases/yr; mostly contact lens wearers. (CDC/NEI estimate)
patients_needing_tx: ~200–250 patients/yr all require treatment (progressive, vision-threatening if untreated).
price: $5,000–$20,000 per course (topical ophthalmic drug + systemic adjunct for 3–6 months); orphan-level pricing.
peak_sales: $2–5M at 50% share; very rare disease, orphan designation likely.
registrational_endpoint: Corneal healing (slit-lamp resolution of infiltrate) at 3 months + negative corneal scraping culture; visual acuity improvement.
btd_threshold_vs_soc: Cure rate ≥70% without keratoplasty vs. ~50% for current PHMB-based regimen at 3 months.

symptoms: Watery diarrhea, fatigue, nausea, loss of appetite, cramping
treatment_goal: Eradicate Cyclospora, resolve diarrheal illness
first_line_soc: TMP-SMX DS (trimethoprim/sulfamethoxazole, antifolate) 160/800 mg BID × 7 days.
treatment_algorithm: TMP-SMX DS BID × 7 days; immunocompromised: 10 days. Sulfonamide-allergic patients: ciprofloxacin 500 mg BID × 7 days (less effective; ~50% response). Nitazoxanide not effective. Self-limiting in immunocompetent but prolonged course without treatment (months). 2024: No new agents. Outbreak tracking FDA/CDC (imported fresh produce).
us_prevalence: ~300–500 reported US cases/yr; actual ~3,000–5,000 (significant underreporting; food-borne outbreaks). (CDC 2024 Cyclosporiasis surveillance)
patients_needing_tx: ~3,000–4,000 patients/yr need TMP-SMX treatment.
price: $300–$800 per course (novel sulfonamide-alternative for allergic patients; per-course price).
tam_gross: 4,000 patients × $500 = $2M. Very small US commercial opportunity.
registrational_endpoint: Oocyst clearance at Day 14 + clinical cure (cessation of diarrhea); stool PCR negativity.
btd_threshold_vs_soc: Cure rate ≥90% in sulfonamide-allergic patients vs. ~50% for ciprofloxacin (the only alternative).

symptoms: Watery non-bloody diarrhea, cramping, weight loss (especially HIV patients)
treatment_goal: Eradicate Cystoisospora belli, restore bowel function
first_line_soc: TMP-SMX DS 160/800 mg QID × 10 days (loading), then BID × 3 weeks; alternatives: pyrimethamine + folinic acid.
treatment_algorithm: TMP-SMX DS QID × 10 days then BID × 3 weeks. HIV patients: secondary prophylaxis with TMP-SMX DS 3× weekly indefinitely until CD4 >200. Sulfonamide-allergic: pyrimethamine 50–75 mg/day + folinic acid × 3–4 weeks; ciprofloxacin less effective. Nitazoxanide and metronidazole not effective. 2024: No new agents.
us_prevalence: ~500–1,000 US cases/yr (mostly HIV+ immigrants from tropical regions); globally more common. (CDC/literature)
patients_needing_tx: ~800 US patients/yr require treatment; all diagnosed symptomatic cases need therapy.
price: $500–$1,500 per course (novel anti-coccidial agent); orphan-level niche.
registrational_endpoint: Oocyst clearance at Day 21 + diarrhea cessation; clinical cure at end of treatment.
btd_threshold_vs_soc: Cure rate ≥90% at Day 21 vs. ~85% for TMP-SMX; or single shorter course with equal efficacy (reducing 3–4 week regimen).

symptoms: Fever, headache, sleep disturbances, neuropsychiatric symptoms
treatment_goal: Eliminate parasite from blood and CNS, prevent coma/death
first_line_soc: Stage 1 (hemolymphatic): pentamidine (Pentam, for T.b. gambiense) or suramin (T.b. rhodesiense). Stage 2 (CNS): eflornithine + nifurtimox combination (NECT) for gambiense; melarsoprol for rhodesiense.
treatment_algorithm: Stage 1 gambiense: pentamidine IM 7 days. Stage 1 rhodesiense: suramin IV. Stage 2 gambiense: NECT (nifurtimox + eflornithine, WHO-recommended) or fexinidazole (Fexinidazole oral, WHO-approved 2019, DNDi-developed) — now first-line for many stage 2 gambiense cases. Stage 2 rhodesiense: melarsoprol IV (toxic, arsenic-based). 2024–2025: Acoziborole (oral, single-dose, DNDi) in Phase 3 showing >90% cure rate for gambiense stage 1 and 2.
us_prevalence: ~2–5 US cases/yr (imported, travelers/immigrants). Global: ~6,000 cases/yr. (CDC/WHO 2024)
patients_needing_tx: ~2–5 US patients/yr; all require treatment (fatal if untreated).
price: $500–$2,000 per course in US orphan drug context; DNDi drugs often donated/subsidized globally.
tam_gross: ~5 US patients × $1,500 = $7,500/yr (US only; global NTD market minimal commercial value)
peak_sales: <$1M US; global neglected tropical disease market — primarily grant/procurement funded, not commercial.
registrational_endpoint: Cure at 18-month follow-up (no parasite detection in blood/CSF); FDA would accept composite parasitological cure endpoint.
btd_threshold_vs_soc: Cure rate ≥90% at 18 months vs. ~80% for NECT; or single oral dose achieving equivalent cure to multi-day IV regimen.

symptoms: Headache, confusion, seizures, focal neurological deficits, altered mental status
treatment_goal: Eliminate Acanthamoeba/Balamuthia from CNS, prevent death
first_line_soc: No FDA-approved standard; combination regimens used empirically: miltefosine (Impavido) + fluconazole + albendazole ± azithromycin for Balamuthia; miltefosine + voriconazole + azithromycin for Acanthamoeba.
treatment_algorithm: Balamuthia mandrillaris GAE: miltefosine (Impavido, FDA-approved for leishmaniasis, used compassionately) + fluconazole + albendazole ± flucytosine ± azithromycin. Acanthamoeba GAE: voriconazole + miltefosine + azithromycin + trimethoprim-sulfamethoxazole. CDC Emergency Operations consult recommended for all US cases. Surgical biopsy/resection for localized lesions. Prognosis extremely poor (~90% mortality). 2024: Small case series supporting miltefosine-containing combos; no controlled trials feasible.
us_prevalence: ~20–30 US cases/yr (Balamuthia ~30, Acanthamoeba GAE rare but distinct from keratitis). (CDC ameba surveillance)
patients_needing_tx: ~20–30 patients/yr; all need treatment (universally fatal without therapy).
price: $10,000–$50,000 per course (orphan ultra-rare; comparable to miltefosine pricing).
peak_sales: <$5M; orphan/neglected disease; CDC-assisted compassionate use pathway.
registrational_endpoint: Survival at Day 90; neurological outcome score (modified Rankin Scale) improvement vs. historical controls.
btd_threshold_vs_soc: 90-day survival ≥40% vs. ~10% historical; or mRS improvement ≥2 points in survivors.

symptoms: Sudden severe headache, fever, neck stiffness, confusion, seizures, rapid death
treatment_goal: Immediate CNS ameba eradication; prevent fulminant death (>97% fatal without treatment)
first_line_soc: Miltefosine (Impavido) + amphotericin B (AmBisome) IV + azithromycin + fluconazole (CDC-recommended combination for Naegleria fowleri PAM); all available via CDC Emergency Operations.
treatment_algorithm: Naegleria fowleri PAM (>97% mortality): immediate IV AmBisome 1.5 mg/kg/day + miltefosine 50 mg TID + azithromycin 500 mg/day + fluconazole 100 mg/day + dexamethasone. IV and intrathecal AmBisome used together. Hypothermia (therapeutic cooling) used in some cases. Only ~5 reported US survivors ever. CDC coordinates emergency drug access. 2024–2025: Climate change expanding N. fowleri geographic range (warmer waters); 5 US cases/yr average.
us_prevalence: ~5 US cases/yr (swimming in warm freshwater; expanding to northern states). Essentially 100% fatal. (CDC NAEGLERIA)
patients_needing_tx: ~5 patients/yr; all require emergency multi-drug combination.
price: $20,000–$100,000 per treatment (orphan/ultra-rare; miltefosine ~$45K/course).
registrational_endpoint: Survival at Day 30; CSF Naegleria clearance (PCR negative at Day 7).
btd_threshold_vs_soc: Survival ≥30% with novel combination vs. <3% historical; CSF PCR negativity by Day 5 vs. ~Day 7+ for current protocol.

symptoms: Fever, headache, muscle aches, low white blood cell count, tick bite
treatment_goal: Eradicate Ehrlichia/Anaplasma, prevent severe disease and death
first_line_soc: Doxycycline (tetracycline) 100 mg BID × 5–10 days — first-line for all ages including children (benefits outweigh dental risk in severe illness per AAP/CDC).
treatment_algorithm: Doxycycline 100 mg BID × minimum 5–7 days (Ehrlichia) or 10 days (Anaplasma) — must continue until afebrile ≥3 days. Children: doxycycline 2.2 mg/kg BID. Rifampin alternative in doxycycline-allergic pregnant patients. Start empirically in endemic areas without waiting for serology. 2024 CDC tick-borne disease guidelines updated. Avoid fluoroquinolones (ineffective). Do not delay treatment.
us_prevalence: ~2,000 Ehrlichiosis + ~3,000 Anaplasmosis cases/yr reported; true incidence ~10,000+/yr. Tick-borne; expanding geographic range. (CDC MMWR 2024)
patients_needing_tx: ~8,000–10,000 patients/yr need doxycycline treatment; all symptomatic cases require immediate treatment.
price: $200–$800 per course (novel tetracycline alternative or tick-borne pathogen-specific agent); limited differentiation over generic doxycycline. Per-course price.
peak_sales: $2–5M for novel agent; limited commercial opportunity over cheap generic doxycycline.
registrational_endpoint: Fever resolution at 48 hours + normalization of CBC (WBC, platelets) at Day 7; clinical cure at end of treatment.
btd_threshold_vs_soc: Fever resolution by 24 hrs vs. ~36–48 hrs for doxycycline; or single-dose oral agent with equivalent efficacy.

symptoms: Fever, petechial rash starting wrists/ankles, headache, tick bite
treatment_goal: Rapidly eliminate Rickettsia rickettsii, prevent vasculitis-related mortality
first_line_soc: Doxycycline (tetracycline) 100 mg BID × 7–14 days, initiated empirically within 5 days of symptom onset; delayed treatment dramatically increases mortality.
treatment_algorithm: Empiric doxycycline 100 mg BID IV/PO immediately upon clinical suspicion — do not await confirmatory testing. Severity guides duration: mild-moderate 7 days (3 days after defervescence); severe 14 days. Pediatric: doxycycline 2.2 mg/kg BID (AAP endorses in all ages). Chloramphenicol: inferior outcomes (mortality 5× higher than doxycycline), used only in documented doxycycline anaphylaxis. Tick removal, supportive care. 2024: CDC emphasizes doxycycline superiority in all ages; azithromycin not recommended.
us_prevalence: ~2,500–4,000 cases/yr; ~3–5% case fatality rate (higher with delayed treatment); hotspots in Arkansas, Missouri, Oklahoma, North Carolina. (CDC NNDSS 2024)
patients_needing_tx: ~3,500 patients/yr; all require immediate doxycycline treatment (fatal without treatment in 8–10 days).
price: $500–$2,000 per course; premium justified only for severe/cerebral RMSF or doxycycline-refractory cases.
peak_sales: ~$5–10M for severe RMSF rescue therapy (novel IV agent for cerebral RMSF); limited niche.
registrational_endpoint: All-cause mortality at Day 30; or clinical cure (defervescence + normalization of platelet count/transaminases) at Day 14.
btd_threshold_vs_soc: Case fatality rate <1% vs. ~3–5% historical for doxycycline-treated RMSF; or faster defervescence <24 hrs vs. ~48 hrs for doxycycline.

symptoms: High fever, severe headache, fatigue, pneumonia, hepatitis
treatment_goal: Eradicate Coxiella burnetii, prevent chronic Q fever/endocarditis
first_line_soc: Doxycycline (tetracycline class) 100 mg BID × 14 days for acute Q fever; chronic Q fever/endocarditis: doxycycline + hydroxychloroquine × 18 months (valvular) or 12 months (non-valvular).
treatment_algorithm: Acute Q fever: doxycycline 100 mg BID × 14 days — first-line per IDSA/CDC. Pregnancy: TMP-SMX (teratogenicity concern with doxycycline). Alternative: fluoroquinolones (ciprofloxacin, levofloxacin) × 14 days. Chronic Q fever (endocarditis, vascular infection): doxycycline 100 mg BID + hydroxychloroquine 200 mg TID × 18–36 months; monitor doxycycline serum levels. Pediatric: TMP-SMX or azithromycin. Valve surgery often required for endocarditis. 2024: No new agents; serologic monitoring and duration debates ongoing.
us_prevalence: ~100–200 acute US cases reported/yr (highly underreported; CDC estimates true incidence 10–100× higher); ~1,000–2,000 true annual acute cases. Chronic Q fever ~200 prevalent cases. (CDC Q Fever surveillance 2024)
patients_needing_tx: ~2,000 patients/yr with acute Q fever requiring doxycycline; ~200 patients with chronic Q fever requiring prolonged combination therapy.
price: $500–$2,000 per course (acute); rationale: small niche market limits novel drug premium; orphan pricing for chronic endocarditis indication.
peak_sales: ~$2–5M; negligible commercial opportunity absent bioterrorism/outbreak scenario.
registrational_endpoint: Defervescence by Day 3–5 and clinical cure at Day 14 (absence of fever + normalization of liver enzymes); seroconversion for PCR clearance as secondary.
btd_threshold_vs_soc: Fever clearance by Day 3 in ≥80% vs. ~60% for doxycycline monotherapy; or chronic Q fever serological cure (Phase I IgG <1:200) at 12 months ≥50% vs. ~30%.

symptoms: Fever, headache, rash, myalgias, tick or mite exposure history
treatment_goal: Eradicate obligate intracellular bacteria, prevent organ failure
first_line_soc: Doxycycline (tetracycline) 100 mg BID × 5–7 days is first-line for all rickettsial infections across all age groups including children (per CDC/IDSA).
treatment_algorithm: Doxycycline 100 mg BID (IV or oral) × 5–7 days for mild-moderate; severe: up to 10–14 days and IV formulation. Chloramphenicol is second-line (used when doxycycline contraindicated — pregnancy in 3rd trimester, severe allergy); inferior outcomes for RMSF. Rifampin active in vitro; used in some scrub typhus resistance settings. Fluoroquinolones (ciprofloxacin) active for some rickettsia but generally inferior. 2024: Doxycycline remains unchallenged; no novel agents in pipeline.
us_prevalence: ~4,000–6,000 rickettsial infections/yr in US (RMSF, ehrlichiosis, anaplasmosis, rickettsialpox, murine typhus). (CDC NNDSS 2024)
patients_needing_tx: ~5,000 patients/yr; all require antibiotic treatment (significant mortality if delayed).
price: $300–$1,000 per course; commodity antibiotic class — minimal novel drug opportunity.
peak_sales: <$5M; no meaningful commercial opportunity unless bioterrorism indication.
registrational_endpoint: Defervescence within 48–72 hours of treatment initiation; clinical cure at Day 14.
btd_threshold_vs_soc: Fever clearance <36 hrs in ≥85% vs. ~70% for doxycycline; or superior CNS penetration reducing RMSF mortality below 1% vs. ~3–5%.

symptoms: Fever, headache, rash (petechial or maculopapular), tick bite eschar, myalgia
treatment_goal: Eradicate Rickettsia species, prevent severe vasculitis and organ failure
first_line_soc: Doxycycline (tetracycline) 100 mg BID × 5–7 days (until afebrile × 3 days) — first-line for all spotted fever group rickettsiae (SFG), including R. africae, R. conorii (Mediterranean), R. akari, etc.
treatment_algorithm: Doxycycline 100 mg BID × 5–7 days for SFG rickettsiae (R. africae African tick bite fever, R. conorii Mediterranean spotted fever, R. parkeri, etc.). Chloramphenicol if doxycycline contraindicated. Azithromycin option for children/pregnancy with milder SFG. Early treatment critical — delay increases mortality. 2024: Spotted fever rickettsiae underdiagnosed; travel medicine awareness important.
us_prevalence: ~2,000–3,000 US cases of non-RMSF SFG rickettsiae/yr (mostly travel-imported; some domestic); RMSF separate entry. (CDC rickettsial surveillance)
patients_needing_tx: ~2,000 patients/yr; all require doxycycline.
price: $200–$600 per course.
registrational_endpoint: Time to fever resolution and clinical cure at Day 7; eschar healing at Day 14.
btd_threshold_vs_soc: Fever resolution in <24 hours vs. ~48–72 hours for doxycycline; or single-dose agent equivalent to 7-day doxycycline course.

symptoms: High fever, severe headache, body louse-associated rash, confusion
treatment_goal: Eradicate Rickettsia prowazekii, reduce mortality
first_line_soc: Doxycycline (tetracycline) 200 mg single dose OR 100 mg BID × 7 days; chloramphenicol alternative for children/pregnant women.
treatment_algorithm: Doxycycline 100 mg BID × 7 days or single 200 mg dose (WHO). Chloramphenicol 50 mg/kg/day × 7 days for those who cannot take doxycycline (children <8, pregnancy). Louse elimination (permethrin) for epidemic control. 2024: No new agents. Rare in US (imported; Brill-Zinsser recrudescent in elderly immigrants).
us_prevalence: <5 US cases/yr (occasional imported Brill-Zinsser reactivation). Global: endemic in refugee/famine settings (Africa, Andes). (CDC)
patients_needing_tx: <5 US patients/yr; all require doxycycline treatment.
price: $200–$500 per course; no commercial opportunity.
registrational_endpoint: Fever resolution at Day 3–5; clinical cure at end of treatment.
btd_threshold_vs_soc: N/A — doxycycline achieves near-100% cure; no meaningful BTD gap.

symptoms: Fever, headache, rash, muscle aches, nausea (milder than epidemic typhus)
treatment_goal: Eradicate Rickettsia typhi, resolve fever, prevent complications
first_line_soc: Doxycycline (tetracycline) 100 mg BID × 7–10 days (first-line for all rickettsial infections including murine typhus).
treatment_algorithm: Doxycycline 100 mg BID × 7–10 days (until afebrile × 3 days). Alternatives for pregnancy/children: chloramphenicol (more toxic; only if doxycycline contraindicated); azithromycin off-label. Fluoroquinolones (ciprofloxacin) show limited activity but inferior to doxycycline. Supportive care. 2024: No new agents; doxycycline remains definitive.
us_prevalence: ~300–500 US cases/yr (Southern California, Texas, Hawaii — flea/rat/possum vector). (CDC rickettsial surveillance)
patients_needing_tx: ~400 US patients/yr; all require doxycycline.
price: $200–$500 per course.
registrational_endpoint: Time to fever defervescence and clinical resolution; doxycycline resistance testing (not yet a clinical problem).
btd_threshold_vs_soc: Fever resolution in <24 hours vs. ~2–3 days for doxycycline; or single-dose oral agent achieving equivalent cure rate ≥95%.

symptoms: Eschar at bite site, fever, papulovesicular rash, headache
treatment_goal: Eradicate Rickettsia akari, resolve rash and fever
first_line_soc: Doxycycline 100 mg BID × 5–7 days (self-limiting disease, short course sufficient).
treatment_algorithm: Mild, self-limiting disease in most immunocompetent patients; doxycycline × 5–7 days accelerates resolution. Tetracycline 500 mg QID × 5–7 days as alternative. Chloramphenicol if tetracyclines contraindicated. No hospitalization typically required. Rodent vector control (house mouse mite Liponyssoides sanguineus) is primary prevention.
us_prevalence: ~50–100 cases/yr, mainly urban northeastern US (NYC); underreported. (CDC surveillance)
patients_needing_tx: ~100 patients/yr requiring antibiotic treatment (all diagnosed cases).
price: $300–$500 per course; micro-niche disease with no commercial potential.
registrational_endpoint: Clinical cure (resolution of fever, eschar, rash) at Day 14; defervescence by Day 3.
btd_threshold_vs_soc: N/A — disease too rare and self-limiting to justify breakthrough threshold criteria; doxycycline is highly effective.

symptoms: Fever, eschar, headache, rash, lymphadenopathy after mite exposure
treatment_goal: Eradicate Orientia tsutsugamushi, prevent multi-organ failure
first_line_soc: Doxycycline 100 mg BID × 7 days; azithromycin 500 mg daily × 3–5 days is equally effective and preferred in pregnancy and children.
treatment_algorithm: Doxycycline 100 mg BID × 7 days (standard). Azithromycin 500 mg daily × 3–5 days (equally effective, preferred in pregnancy and pediatrics). Tetracycline 500 mg QID × 7 days (alternative). Rifampin (doxycycline + rifampin combination) for severe/complicated scrub typhus. Chloramphenicol if above unavailable. Azithromycin-resistant strains reported in Asia. 2024: Doxycycline-resistant strains in Thailand/India documented; combination therapy explored.
us_prevalence: Extremely rare in US mainland; ~50–100 imported cases/yr. Endemic in Asia-Pacific. No significant US market.
patients_needing_tx: ~100 US patients/yr; globally ~1 million cases/yr requiring treatment.
price: $500–$2,000 per course (US orphan market); global generic pricing dominates.
tam_gross: 100 US patients × $1,000 = $100K (US only); negligible commercial US market.
registrational_endpoint: Defervescence by Day 3–5; clinical cure at Day 14 (resolution of eschar, fever, rash).
btd_threshold_vs_soc: Fever clearance within 24 hrs in ≥85% vs. ~70% for doxycycline; efficacy against doxycycline-resistant strains (cure rate ≥90%).

symptoms: Painful genital blisters, ulcers, burning, recurrent outbreaks
treatment_goal: Suppress HSV replication, reduce outbreak frequency, prevent transmission
first_line_soc: Valacyclovir (Valtrex, nucleoside analog prodrug) 1g BID × 7–10 days (first episode) or 500 mg BID × 3–5 days (recurrence); 500 mg daily suppressive therapy to reduce recurrences.
treatment_algorithm: First episode: valacyclovir 1g BID × 7–10 days or acyclovir 400 mg TID × 7–10 days or famciclovir 250 mg TID × 7–10 days. Recurrent: episodic (valacyclovir 500 mg BID × 3 days) or suppressive (valacyclovir 500–1000 mg/day indefinitely). Severe/immunocompromised: IV acyclovir 5–10 mg/kg q8h. HSV vaccine: prophylactic/therapeutic vaccines in development (Genocea, Moderna mRNA HSV-2 vaccine in Phase 1–2 as of 2024). Novel antiherpetic: pritelivir (helicase-primase inhibitor) Phase 3 for immunocompromised; amenamevir (Japan-approved). Bulevirtide and other novel agents in pipeline. Aimvax (amenamevir, Astellas) gaining US IND data.
us_prevalence: ~572,000 new HSV-2 infections/yr; ~18–25 million Americans with genital herpes (HSV-2 prevalence 12% adults); many undiagnosed. (CDC STI surveillance 2024)
patients_needing_tx: ~5–6 million Americans use suppressive antiviral therapy; ~2 million seek episodic treatment annually.
price: $500–$2,000/yr suppressive (novel agent vs. generic valacyclovir ~$200/yr); per-outbreak episodic: $100–$300. Annual price for chronic suppression.
peak_sales: $300–600M for novel suppressive agent (pritelivir, if approved) with reduced transmission vs. valacyclovir; or HSV-2 therapeutic vaccine $500M–$1.5B.
registrational_endpoint: Reduction in viral shedding rate (days of genital HSV shedding by PCR); time to healing of recurrent genital herpes lesions; recurrence rate per year for suppressive therapy.
btd_threshold_vs_soc: Viral shedding rate <2% of days/year vs. ~10% for valacyclovir 500 mg/day suppression; or recurrence rate <1 episode/year vs. ~1–2/year with valacyclovir.

symptoms: Genital ulcers, discharge, dysuria, rash, pelvic pain
treatment_goal: Eradicate causative pathogen, prevent transmission and long-term complications
first_line_soc: Pathogen-specific: gonorrhea (ceftriaxone 500 mg IM single dose); chlamydia (doxycycline 100 mg BID × 7 days); syphilis (benzathine penicillin G 2.4 million units IM); herpes (valacyclovir); trichomoniasis (metronidazole 2g single dose).
treatment_algorithm: Comprehensive STI framework: (1) Gonorrhea: ceftriaxone 500 mg IM single dose; cefixime alternative; dual therapy with doxycycline if chlamydia co-infection possible. (2) Chlamydia: doxycycline 100 mg BID × 7 days (superior to azithromycin single dose per 2021 CDC STI guidelines). (3) Syphilis: benzathine penicillin G 2.4M units IM × 1 dose (primary/secondary); × 3 doses (tertiary). (4) Herpes: acyclovir/valacyclovir/famciclovir. (5) Trichomoniasis: metronidazole 2g or tinidazole 2g single dose. (6) PID: ceftriaxone + doxycycline ± metronidazole. (7) BV: metronidazole gel. 2024–2025: Syphilis epidemic in US (highest since 1950); XDR Neisseria gonorrhoeae emerging.
us_prevalence: ~2.5 million new chlamydia cases/yr; ~660,000 gonorrhea cases/yr; ~210,000 syphilis cases/yr; ~8 million trichomoniasis cases/yr. (CDC STI Surveillance 2024)
patients_needing_tx: ~12–15 million US patients/yr need pharmacologic STI treatment; nearly 100% of diagnosed STI cases require treatment.
price: $300–$2,000 per course depending on indication; gonorrhea novel agent $1,000–$1,500 per course given resistance burden.
tam_gross: ~12M patients × $500 avg = $6B broad STI market; gonorrhea/MDR STI segment $660K × $1,200 = $800M
peak_sales: Novel gonorrhea agent (zoliflodacin, gepotidacin Phase 3): ~$200–400M peak; novel syphilis agent: $100–200M.
registrational_endpoint: Microbiological cure (culture/NAAT negative) at Day 7 for gonorrhea/chlamydia; clinical + serological cure at 3 months for syphilis.
btd_threshold_vs_soc: Microbiological cure ≥95% for MDR gonorrhea vs. ~85% for ceftriaxone; or single-dose oral agent non-inferior to IM ceftriaxone (lower bound of 95% CI > -10%).

symptoms: Dysuria, urethral/vaginal discharge, often asymptomatic, pelvic pain
treatment_goal: Eradicate Chlamydia trachomatis, prevent PID, infertility, and neonatal infection
first_line_soc: Doxycycline 100 mg BID × 7 days — preferred over azithromycin 1g single dose per 2021 CDC STI guidelines (lower treatment failure for urogenital chlamydia).
treatment_algorithm: First-line: doxycycline 100 mg BID × 7 days (preferred; ~98% cure rate). Azithromycin 1g single dose (alternative; ~90% cure rate — inferior; reserved for adherence-concern situations). Pregnancy: azithromycin 1g single dose or amoxicillin 500 mg TID × 7 days. Rectal chlamydia: doxycycline 100 mg BID × 7 days. LGV proctitis: doxycycline × 21 days. Partner notification and treatment mandatory. Test of cure not routinely recommended (except pregnancy, rectal, or LGV). 2024: Rising doxycycline-resistant Chlamydia hypothetical concern; no confirmed resistance to date.
us_prevalence: ~1.65 million reported cases/yr (most common bacterial STI); estimated true incidence 2.5–3 million/yr with underreporting. (CDC STI Surveillance 2024)
patients_needing_tx: ~2.5–3 million US patients/yr requiring doxycycline; nearly 100% of diagnosed cases need treatment.
price: $200–$1,000 per course for novel single-dose oral agent with superior cure rate; per-course pricing.
peak_sales: ~$200–400M for novel chlamydia-specific agent with superior single-dose efficacy vs. doxycycline 7-day course; adherence advantage.
registrational_endpoint: Microbiological cure (NAAT negative) at Day 14–21 post-treatment; clinical cure (symptom resolution) at Day 7.
btd_threshold_vs_soc: Microbiological cure ≥98% single-dose vs. ~90% for azithromycin or ~97% for 7-day doxycycline; superiority on test of cure + lower pelvic inflammatory disease incidence.

symptoms: Urethritis, vaginal discharge, pelvic pain, infertility, often asymptomatic
treatment_goal: Eradicate Mycoplasma genitalium, prevent treatment failure and antibiotic resistance
first_line_soc: Azithromycin 1g single dose (historically); 2020–2024 CDC shift to doxycycline 100 mg BID × 7 days (reduces azithromycin resistance selection) followed by azithromycin 500 mg Day 1 then 250 mg × 4 days if M. genitalium confirmed. Moxifloxacin 400 mg × 7–14 days for macrolide-resistant strains.
treatment_algorithm: Urethritis (presumptive): doxycycline × 7 days (empiric). Confirmed M. genitalium: resistance-guided therapy — if macrolide-susceptible: azithromycin 500 mg Day 1 then 250 mg × 4 days (extended regimen). Macrolide-resistant: moxifloxacin 400 mg/day × 7–14 days (>95% cure). Moxifloxacin-resistant (fluoroquinolone + macrolide resistance): pristinamycin (not US-available), minocycline, or investigational. M. hominis: tetracyclines or clindamycin. Ureaplasma urealyticum: doxycycline. 2024–2025: Lefamulin (pleuromutilin, Xenleta) activity against M. genitalium; omadacycline explored; resistance-guided therapy increasingly recommended.
us_prevalence: ~1–2 million US M. genitalium infections/yr (estimated; underdiagnosed due to limited FDA-cleared NAAT testing). (CDC STI Surveillance 2024; BASHH)
patients_needing_tx: ~500,000–1 million US patients/yr with symptomatic or confirmed M. genitalium requiring treatment.
price: $500–$2,000 per course for novel oral agent active against macrolide/fluoroquinolone-resistant M. genitalium; per-course pricing.
peak_sales: ~$100–200M for novel M. genitalium-active agent (lefamulin, zoliflodacin) with resistance-sparing profile; market growing with expanded NAAT testing.
registrational_endpoint: Microbiological cure (NAAT negative for M. genitalium) at Day 21–28; resolution of urethritis symptoms at Day 7.
btd_threshold_vs_soc: Microbiological cure ≥95% for macrolide-resistant M. genitalium vs. ~40% for azithromycin (resistant); or ≥90% for all-comers vs. ~80% for moxifloxacin.

symptoms: Genital warts, abnormal Pap smear, cervical/oropharyngeal/anal dysplasia
treatment_goal: Remove warts, prevent cancer progression, eradicate persistent infection
first_line_soc: No antiviral pharmacologic cure for HPV infection itself. Genital warts: patient-applied imiquimod (Aldara, immune response modifier) 5% cream 3×/wk × 16 weeks or podofilox 0.5% solution BID × 3 days/wk × 4 weeks; provider-applied trichloroacetic acid or cryotherapy. Prevention: Gardasil 9 (9vHPV vaccine, Merck) for 9–45 years.
treatment_algorithm: Genital warts: imiquimod 5% or 3.75%, podofilox, sinecatechins (Veregen — green tea extract, FDA-approved). Provider: cryotherapy, TCA, surgical/laser excision. Cervical dysplasia (CIN 2/3): LEEP, cone biopsy. Cancer: standard oncology protocols. Prevention: Gardasil 9 (9-valent) covers HPV 6/11/16/18/31/33/45/52/58 — >90% of cervical cancers. Therapeutic HPV vaccines in development (ISA101b, Shanchol, mRNA-based for CIN/cancer). 2024–2025: No new antiviral HPV drugs; therapeutic vaccine trials ongoing.
us_prevalence: ~43 million Americans with active HPV infection at any time; ~400,000 genital wart cases/yr treated; ~14,000 cervical cancer cases/yr. (CDC HPV surveillance)
patients_needing_tx: ~400,000 genital wart patients/yr need pharmacologic/ablative treatment; ~200,000 CIN 2/3 require intervention; cervical cancer: 14,000.
price: Therapeutic HPV vaccine: $1,000–$5,000 per course (series); topical antiviral: $200–$800 per course.
peak_sales: $500M–$2B for therapeutic HPV vaccine (if approved for CIN/oropharyngeal cancer) at 30–40% penetration of high-grade dysplasia market.
registrational_endpoint: Regression of CIN 2/3 to CIN 1 or normal histology at Month 12 (for therapeutic vaccine); complete clearance of genital warts at Week 16 (for topical agent).
btd_threshold_vs_soc: CIN 2/3 regression ≥70% at Month 12 vs. ~20–30% spontaneous regression; or genital wart clearance ≥80% vs. ~50% for imiquimod.

symptoms: Vaginal itching, yellow-green discharge, dysuria, asymptomatic in many men
treatment_goal: Eradicate Trichomonas vaginalis, prevent recurrence, reduce HIV risk
first_line_soc: Metronidazole (nitroimidazole) 2g oral single dose OR 500 mg BID × 7 days (preferred for women per 2021 CDC STI guidelines — lower recurrence); tinidazole 2g single dose as alternative.
treatment_algorithm: Women: metronidazole 500 mg BID × 7 days (preferred — lower recurrence than single 2g dose) or tinidazole 2g single dose. Men: metronidazole 2g single dose. Partner treatment mandatory (concurrent). Metronidazole resistance (<5%): tinidazole 2g/day × 7 days. Severe nitroimidazole resistance (rare): vaginal boric acid, paromomycin cream as salvage. Pregnancy: metronidazole 2g single dose (safe; tinidazole safety data limited in 1st trimester). HIV co-infection: treat with 7-day course. 2024: Secnidazole 2g single dose FDA-approved for trichomoniasis (Solosec) — equivalent efficacy with single-dose convenience.
us_prevalence: ~2.6 million new US Trichomonas infections/yr; most prevalent non-viral STI in US. (CDC STI Surveillance 2024)
patients_needing_tx: ~2.6 million US patients/yr; nearly all require pharmacologic treatment (persistent/recurrent if untreated).
price: $100–$500 per course (per-course for novel single-dose oral agent); secnidazole (Solosec) ~$350/course.
peak_sales: ~$100–200M for novel nitroimidazole-resistant Trichomonas agent or improved formulation; limited by generic metronidazole dominance.
registrational_endpoint: Microbiological cure (NAAT negative) at Day 7–14 for women; symotomatic resolution at Day 7.
btd_threshold_vs_soc: Microbiological cure ≥95% at Day 14 vs. ~90% for 7-day metronidazole; cure of metronidazole-resistant trichomoniasis ≥85% vs. ~50% for salvage regimens.

symptoms: Urethral/vaginal discharge, dysuria, pelvic pain, rectal discharge
treatment_goal: Eradicate Neisseria gonorrhoeae, prevent PID, prevent transmission
first_line_soc: Ceftriaxone (Rocephin, 3rd-gen cephalosporin) 500 mg IM single dose (if weight ≥150 kg: 1g); if chlamydia not excluded, add doxycycline 100 mg BID × 7 days.
treatment_algorithm: Step 1: Ceftriaxone 500 mg IM (CDC 2021 guidelines) + doxycycline 100 mg BID × 7 days if chlamydia coinfection possible. Step 2: Ceftriaxone-resistant (rare): gentamicin 240 mg IM + azithromycin 2g oral. Step 3: Pharyngeal gonorrhea: same ceftriaxone regimen but test-of-cure in 14 days. Step 4: Disseminated gonococcal infection (DGI): ceftriaxone 1g IV daily × 7 days. Step 5: Novel agents: zoliflodacin (topoisomerase II inhibitor, Phase 3 completed 2023, non-inferior to ceftriaxone) and gepotidacin (Phase 3 completed 2023, FDA filing expected 2025) provide oral options for resistant strains. 2024–2025: CEPH-R strains increasing; GISP data showing ceftriaxone MIC creep.
us_prevalence: ~700,000–800,000 new US gonorrhea cases/yr (CDC STI surveillance 2024); rising trend with increasing AMR.
patients_needing_tx: ~700,000 patients/yr require antibiotic treatment; all diagnosed cases mandate treatment.
price: $300–$800 per course (single-dose oral agent for gonorrhea); rationale: premium over IM ceftriaxone for oral convenience and resistant strains.
peak_sales: ~$150–300M at 25–35% share for zoliflodacin or gepotidacin (oral option driving uptake vs. IM ceftriaxone).
registrational_endpoint: Microbiological cure (culture negativity) at Test-of-Cure Day 4–8 for urogenital/rectal/pharyngeal sites (FDA SSTI/gonorrhea guidance).
btd_threshold_vs_soc: Microbiological cure ≥95% at all anatomical sites (urogenital + pharyngeal) vs. ~93% for ceftriaxone; or cure of ceftriaxone-resistant strains ≥90%.

symptoms: Painless genital ulcer (primary), body rash including palms/soles (secondary), neurological/cardiac damage (tertiary)
treatment_goal: Eradicate Treponema pallidum, prevent neurosyphilis, cardiovascular syphilis, and congenital transmission
first_line_soc: Benzathine penicillin G (Bicillin L-A) 2.4 million units IM single dose for primary, secondary, and early latent syphilis — the only proven treatment; benzathine penicillin G 2.4 million units IM weekly × 3 doses for late latent/tertiary.
treatment_algorithm: Primary/secondary/early latent: benzathine penicillin G 2.4M units IM × 1 dose. Late latent/tertiary (non-neurological): benzathine penicillin G 2.4M units IM weekly × 3 doses. Neurosyphilis: aqueous crystalline penicillin G 18–24 million units/day IV × 10–14 days. Penicillin allergy: doxycycline 100 mg BID × 14 days (early) or 28 days (late); efficacy inferior to penicillin. Congenital syphilis: aqueous penicillin G IV × 10 days. Syphilis in HIV: same regimen, closer follow-up. 2024–2025: US syphilis epidemic — 209,000+ cases/yr, congenital syphilis ~3,000+ cases/yr (highest since 1950); Bicillin shortage ongoing concern; doxycycline prophylaxis (DoxyPEP) post-exposure reduces STI risk.
us_prevalence: ~210,000 new syphilis cases/yr; ~3,000+ congenital syphilis cases/yr. (CDC STI Surveillance 2024)
patients_needing_tx: ~210,000 US patients/yr requiring penicillin/doxycycline treatment; all diagnosed cases require treatment.
price: $500–$2,000 per course (novel oral agent eliminating need for IM injection and addressing penicillin shortage); per-course pricing.
peak_sales: ~$100–200M for oral syphilis agent non-inferior to penicillin; doxycycline PEP market ~$50–100M.
registrational_endpoint: Serological cure (≥4-fold RPR titer decline) at 12 months; or clinical treatment success (resolution of primary/secondary manifestations + RPR decline) at 3 months.
btd_threshold_vs_soc: Serological cure ≥80% at 12 months vs. ~75% for benzathine penicillin; or oral agent with equivalent cure rate to IM penicillin (non-inferiority, lower bound >-10%).

symptoms: Diarrhea, proctitis, rectal pain, rectal discharge, nausea
treatment_goal: Eradicate enteric STI pathogens, prevent complications and transmission
first_line_soc: Depends on causative organism: Shigella (azithromycin 1g single dose for susceptible strains, or ceftriaxone 1g IM for MDR); LGV/chlamydial proctitis (doxycycline 100 mg BID × 21 days); Campylobacter (azithromycin 500 mg × 3 days); Giardia (metronidazole 500 mg TID × 7 days).
treatment_algorithm: Proctitis from gonorrhea: ceftriaxone 500 mg IM single dose + doxycycline (if chlamydia not excluded). Shigella proctitis: azithromycin 1g or ceftriaxone (MDR strains common in MSM). LGV: doxycycline 100 mg BID × 21 days. Intestinal amebiasis: metronidazole + paromomycin.
