CMV colitis (HIV): ganciclovir/valganciclovir. 2024: Shigella XDR strains among MSM — emerging public health crisis; ceftriaxone failing in some cases.
us_prevalence: ~100,000–200,000 STI-associated enteric infections/yr in US (MSM predominant); Shigella among MSM ~30,000/yr. (CDC STI Surveillance 2024)
patients_needing_tx: ~150,000 patients/yr require antibiotic treatment; all cases with confirmed STI etiology.
price: $300–$1,500 per course; limited novel drug opportunity except for XDR Shigella indication.
peak_sales: ~$30–60M for novel agent active against XDR Shigella in MSM population at 25% share.
registrational_endpoint: Microbiological cure (stool culture negative) at Day 7; proctitis symptom resolution score at Day 5.
btd_threshold_vs_soc: Microbiological cure ≥90% for XDR Shigella vs. ~50–60% for azithromycin (increasingly resistant); clinical cure at Day 3 vs. Day 5 for azithromycin.

symptoms: Painless genital ulcer, inguinal lymphadenopathy (buboes), rectal pain, fever
treatment_goal: Eradicate Chlamydia trachomatis L1-L3, resolve adenopathy, prevent rectal strictures
first_line_soc: Doxycycline (tetracycline) 100 mg BID × 21 days (CDC STI guidelines 2021).
treatment_algorithm: Step 1: Doxycycline 100 mg BID × 21 days (first-line; longer course than chlamydia urethritis). Step 2: Alternative: azithromycin 1g/week × 3 weeks (less data). Step 3: Needle aspiration of fluctuant buboes (do NOT incise). Step 4: Proctocolitis (rectal LGV): doxycycline × 21 days; evaluate with sigmoidoscopy. Step 5: Test for HIV and other STIs. Sexual partners treated for standard chlamydia. 2024: Increasing LGV rates in MSM; rectal LGV often severe.
us_prevalence: ~2,000–4,000 US LGV cases/yr (rising among MSM); underdiagnosed due to lack of routine genotyping. (CDC STI surveillance 2024)
patients_needing_tx: ~3,000 patients/yr; all diagnosed cases require 21-day doxycycline.
price: $300–$800 per course (novel chlamydia agent enabling shorter, single-dose therapy).
tam_gross: 3,000 patients × $500 = $1.5M (negligible standalone market; part of broader chlamydia/STI market)
peak_sales: <$5M standalone; novel chlamydia agent addressing LGV as sub-indication.
registrational_endpoint: Clinical cure (resolution of bubo, inguinal adenopathy regression, no new lesions) at Day 21 and Day 42; C. trachomatis L-type PCR negativity.
btd_threshold_vs_soc: Clinical cure ≥90% with 7-day course vs. ~85% with 21-day doxycycline; single-dose achieving equivalent LGV cure.

symptoms: Painful genital ulcer, inguinal lymphadenopathy, suppurative bubo
treatment_goal: Eradicate Haemophilus ducreyi, heal ulcer, prevent HIV co-transmission
first_line_soc: Azithromycin (macrolide) 1g single oral dose OR ceftriaxone 250 mg IM single dose (both equivalent per CDC 2021 STI guidelines).
treatment_algorithm: Single-dose: azithromycin 1g PO or ceftriaxone 250 mg IM. Alternative: ciprofloxacin 500 mg BID × 3 days or erythromycin base 500 mg TID × 7 days. Aspiration of fluctuant buboes may be needed. HIV-positive patients may need longer therapy; test for HIV/syphilis/gonorrhea. 2024: Extremely rare in US now; CDC STI guidelines reaffirm single-dose approaches.
us_prevalence: ~20–100 US cases/yr (very rare; <10 per year reported recently to CDC in some years). (CDC STI surveillance 2024)
patients_needing_tx: <100 US patients/yr; all require antibiotic treatment.
price: $200–$500 per course (single-dose oral agent); market is negligible in the US.
tam_gross: 100 patients × $300 = $30K/yr. No commercial opportunity in US.
registrational_endpoint: Ulcer healing at Day 7 + clinical cure (no suppurative nodes) at Day 14; microbiologic eradication.
btd_threshold_vs_soc: N/A — existing single-dose regimens achieve >95% cure; no viable BTD threshold gap.

symptoms: Painless genital ulcers, beefy-red lesions, progressive tissue destruction
treatment_goal: Eradicate Klebsiella granulomatis, heal genital ulcers
first_line_soc: Azithromycin (macrolide) 1g oral weekly × 3 weeks or 500 mg/day × 3 weeks; or doxycycline 100 mg BID × 3 weeks.
treatment_algorithm: Step 1: Azithromycin 1g weekly × 3+ weeks (CDC preferred) or doxycycline 100 mg BID × 21 days. Step 2: Alternative: TMP-SMX DS BID × 21 days or ciprofloxacin 750 mg BID × 21 days or erythromycin 500 mg QID × 21 days. Step 3: HIV-positive: add azithromycin to regimen. Step 4: Surgical excision for large or persistent lesions. Treat until all lesions fully healed. 2024: No new agents.
us_prevalence: <100 US cases/yr (extremely rare, endemic in tropical regions); globally more prevalent. (CDC STI data)
patients_needing_tx: <100 US patients/yr; all require antibiotic treatment.
price: $500–$2,000 per course (rare STI; orphan pricing context).
peak_sales: <$1M; no commercial development interest; public health/global health focus.
registrational_endpoint: Complete lesion healing at Day 21 and Day 42; microbiological cure (PCR negativity for K. granulomatis).
btd_threshold_vs_soc: Lesion healing ≥90% at Day 21 vs. ~75% for doxycycline at 3 weeks; or single-dose oral regimen achieving equivalent cure.

symptoms: Burning urination, frequency, urgency, pelvic pain, fever
treatment_goal: Eradicate uropathogen, relieve symptoms, prevent pyelonephritis
first_line_soc: Uncomplicated UTI: nitrofurantoin macrocrystals (Macrobid, nitrofuran) 100 mg BID × 5 days OR TMP-SMX DS × 3 days. Complicated/pyelonephritis: oral ciprofloxacin 500 mg BID × 7 days or IV ceftriaxone.
treatment_algorithm: Uncomplicated cystitis: nitrofurantoin, TMP-SMX (if local resistance <20%), or fosfomycin 3g single dose. Complicated UTI: fluoroquinolone (ciprofloxacin) or cephalosporin, 7–14 days. ESBL UTI: ertapenem or oral fosfomycin. Acute pyelonephritis: IV ceftriaxone then oral step-down. Recurrent UTI: methenamine hippurate prophylaxis. 2024 AUA/IDSA guidelines emphasize stewardship. Novel agents: pivmecillinam (Selincro) FDA-approved 2024 for uncomplicated UTI (new option); gepotidacin (GSK) and zoliflodacin FDA-approved 2024 for uncomplicated UTI caused by resistant organisms.
us_prevalence: ~8–10 million US UTI cases/yr in women alone; ~3 million complicated UTIs; total ambulatory visits ~11 million. (CDC/AHRQ)
patients_needing_tx: ~10 million patients/yr need antibiotics (virtually all symptomatic UTI cases require pharmacologic treatment).
price: $300–$1,500 per course (novel oral agent); uncomplicated UTI oral drugs priced modestly; novel MDR-active agents command higher price ($500–$1,500). Noting per-course price.
peak_sales: ~$500M–$1.2B for novel oral agent targeting fluoroquinolone-resistant/ESBL UTI (15–20% of market); example: gepotidacin projected ~$500–800M peak.
registrational_endpoint: Composite microbiological and clinical cure at Test of Cure (Day 7 post-treatment end); FDA GUDID/ABSSSI-analogous UTI guidance requires per-pathogen microbiological eradication ≥70% lower bound.
btd_threshold_vs_soc: Composite cure rate ≥75% in fluoroquinolone-resistant/ESBL-positive UTI vs. ~50–60% for nitrofurantoin/fosfomycin comparator in MDR setting.

symptoms: Pyuria with yeast, UTI symptoms, asymptomatic funguria in catheterized patients
treatment_goal: Eradicate Candida from urinary tract, remove predisposing factors
first_line_soc: Fluconazole (azole) 200–400 mg/day × 7–14 days for symptomatic candiduria (IDSA 2016 guidelines). Amphotericin B bladder irrigation for fluconazole-resistant Candida. Remove catheter if present.
treatment_algorithm: Asymptomatic candiduria: do NOT treat (IDSA; only treat pre-procedure or immunocompromised). Symptomatic: fluconazole 400 mg/day × 7–14 days. Fluconazole-resistant (C. glabrata/krusei): amphotericin B bladder irrigation 50 mg/mL × 5 days, or systemic echinocandin. C. auris UTI: echinocandin ± amphotericin B (poor response to azoles). Remove catheter/stents. 2024: C. auris expanding in US hospitals; IDSA guidance update pending.
us_prevalence: ~100,000–150,000 symptomatic fungal UTI cases/yr in catheterized/immunocompromised patients. (CDC NHSN estimate)
patients_needing_tx: ~50,000–80,000 patients/yr need antifungal treatment (symptomatic cases + C. auris/resistant Candida; exclude asymptomatic bacteriuria).
price: $5,000–$20,000 per course (novel echinocandin/azole with urinary penetration for C. auris; per-course price).
peak_sales: $100–200M for novel agent with established urinary penetration + C. auris activity at 15–20% share.
registrational_endpoint: Mycological eradication (urine culture <10³ CFU/mL) at Test of Cure Day 14; clinical cure composite.
btd_threshold_vs_soc: Mycological eradication ≥75% for C. auris UTI vs. <30% for fluconazole/echinocandin monotherapy; or oral agent non-inferior to IV for fungal UTI.

symptoms: Recurrent UTI, flank pain, hypertension, progressive kidney scarring
treatment_goal: Prevent recurrent UTI, halt renal scarring, control hypertension
first_line_soc: Long-term low-dose antibiotic prophylaxis with TMP-SMX (co-trimoxazole) or nitrofurantoin; acute pyelonephritis episodes treated with fluoroquinolones (ciprofloxacin, levofloxacin) or TMP-SMX × 7–14 days.
treatment_algorithm: Acute episodes: fluoroquinolone (ciprofloxacin 500 mg BID × 7 days) or TMP-SMX × 14 days; IV ceftriaxone or aminoglycoside for hospitalized patients. Prophylaxis: TMP-SMX 40/200 mg daily or nitrofurantoin 50 mg daily for recurrent UTI. Vesicoureteral reflux (VUR): surgical correction (ureteral reimplantation or endoscopic injection) in severe cases. Supportive: antihypertensives (ACE inhibitors) to preserve renal function. 2024: Ongoing debate about antibiotic prophylaxis value vs. resistance risk; RIVUR trial outcomes influencing practice.
us_prevalence: ~750,000 US patients with reflux nephropathy/chronic pyelonephritis; ~80,000 new diagnoses/yr. (NIDDK/USRDS estimates)
patients_needing_tx: ~500,000 patients with active disease requiring ongoing UTI management; ~100,000 on antibiotic prophylaxis.
price: $500–$2,000/yr (novel prophylactic agent); rationale: modest premium for once-daily oral agent with improved safety/resistance profile vs. TMP-SMX.
peak_sales: ~$100–200M at 15–20% share for novel prophylaxis agent with better tolerability.
registrational_endpoint: Rate of recurrent UTI per patient-year at 12 months; time to first UTI recurrence as secondary endpoint.
btd_threshold_vs_soc: Recurrent UTI rate <0.5 episodes/yr vs. ~1.5/yr on TMP-SMX prophylaxis; or reduction in renal scarring progression on DMSA scan at 2 years.

symptoms: Fever, dysuria, pyuria, cloudy urine with urinary catheter in place
treatment_goal: Eradicate uropathogen, remove catheter, prevent urosepsis
first_line_soc: Catheter removal/exchange + antibiotic based on culture/susceptibility: ciprofloxacin, TMP-SMX, or nitrofurantoin (ESBL-negative); carbapenems for ESBL-producing organisms. 7-day regimen for women, 14 days for men.
treatment_algorithm: Step 1: Remove/replace catheter ASAP. Step 2: Culture-directed therapy. Empiric: fluoroquinolone (ciprofloxacin) or cephalosporin (ceftriaxone). ESBL: ertapenem or IV ertapenem/meropenem. Candida CAUTI: fluconazole 200 mg/day × 14 days if Candida; amphotericin bladder irrigation rarely needed. Duration: 7–14 days. Asymptomatic bacteriuria in catheterized patients: do NOT treat (IDSA 2019 guidelines). 2024: SHEA guidelines update on CAUTI bundle. Novel IV-to-oral regimens for ESBL CAUTI.
us_prevalence: ~100,000–200,000 symptomatic CAUTI cases/yr in hospitalized patients (catheterized population ~1 million US inpatients/yr). (CDC NHSN 2024)
patients_needing_tx: ~150,000 patients/yr with symptomatic CAUTI require antibiotic treatment; asymptomatic bacteriuria should NOT be treated.
price: $500–$3,000 per course IV-to-oral (ESBL-active oral agent); per-course price.
peak_sales: $100–200M for ESBL-active oral agent in CAUTI/complicated UTI indication at 25–30% share.
registrational_endpoint: Microbiological eradication (≤10³ CFU/mL) at Test of Cure Day 5–9; composite clinical cure + microbiological eradication.
btd_threshold_vs_soc: Composite cure ≥75% vs. ~55% for carbapenems in ESBL-producing CAUTI; or clinical cure equivalent to IV ertapenem with oral-only regimen.

symptoms: Sudden fever, severe joint pain/swelling, rash, myalgia
treatment_goal: Relieve joint pain, reduce fever, prevent chronic arthralgia
first_line_soc: No approved antiviral. Supportive: NSAIDs (ibuprofen, naproxen) for fever and arthralgia; chloroquine for chronic joint symptoms (evidence limited).
treatment_algorithm: Acute phase: NSAIDs, acetaminophen for pain/fever. Severe/joint disease: corticosteroids for severe polyarthritis (short course). Chronic phase (>3 months): hydroxychloroquine (Plaquenil) per some guidelines; methotrexate for destructive arthritis; physical therapy. Novel antivirals: ribavirin has in vitro activity; no approved agent. CHIKV vaccine: Ixchiq (Valneva/Bavarian Nordic, live-attenuated) FDA-approved June 2023 for adults ≥18 traveling to endemic regions. 2024–2025: Outbreak activity in Americas continues.
us_prevalence: ~300–1,000 US cases/yr (imported travelers); locally acquired cases rare. (CDC ArboNET 2024)
patients_needing_tx: ~800 US patients/yr require supportive treatment; no pharmacologic cure. Globally ~5 million/yr.
price: $1,000–$3,000 per course (novel antiviral with proven activity on duration/chronicity); per-course acute price.
peak_sales: $100–300M globally for novel antiviral if proven to prevent chronic arthritis (unmet need valued); US commercial market small.
registrational_endpoint: Joint pain VAS score reduction at Day 14; time to freedom from fever; proportion with chronic arthralgia at 3 months.
btd_threshold_vs_soc: Chronic arthralgia at 3 months <20% vs. ~40–50% in untreated; or VAS pain score reduction ≥50% at Day 7 vs. NSAIDs alone.

symptoms: High fever, severe headache, pain behind eyes, rash, muscle/joint aches
treatment_goal: Supportive hydration, antipyresis, monitor for severe dengue
first_line_soc: No approved antiviral. Supportive: acetaminophen (avoid NSAIDs/aspirin — platelet effects); IV fluids for severe dengue. Dengvaxia (CYD-TDV, Sanofi) licensed for seropositive individuals ≥9 years in endemic areas.
treatment_algorithm: Supportive care: oral/IV fluids per WHO dengue guidelines (careful fluid management critical). Antipyretics: paracetamol only (no NSAIDs). Blood transfusion for severe hemorrhage. No approved antivirals; investigational: JNJ-1802 (dengue NS5 polymerase inhibitor, Janssen) Phase 2 results expected 2024; favipiravir (RNA polymerase inhibitor) active in vitro; presatovir terminated. 2024: TAK-003 (Qdenga, live-attenuated tetravalent vaccine) approved in 40+ countries; Dengvaxia use restricted to seropositive. Novel dengue antivirals in Phase 2. WHO 2024: 10 million+ global cases/yr, dengue expanding geographically.
us_prevalence: ~1,000–2,000 imported US cases/yr; local transmission Florida/Texas sporadic. Global: ~400 million infections/yr with ~100 million symptomatic. (CDC ArboNET 2024)
patients_needing_tx: ~2,000 US patients/yr; globally ~100 million symptomatic cases. Novel antiviral would target severe dengue (~500,000 hospitalizations globally/yr).
price: $500–$1,500 per course (oral antiviral for dengue, global health pricing pressure; per-course price).
peak_sales: $200–500M globally for first dengue antiviral (JNJ-1802 if successful); primarily global pharma strategy.
registrational_endpoint: Time to resolution of fever; absence of severe dengue at Day 7; viral load reduction at 48 hours.
btd_threshold_vs_soc: Severe dengue rate <1% vs. ~3–5% in untreated hospitalized dengue; fever resolution by Day 2 vs. Day 4 for placebo.

symptoms: Sudden fever, muscle aches, diarrhea, vomiting, hemorrhagic bleeding, shock
treatment_goal: Reduce viral replication, prevent hemorrhagic shock and death
first_line_soc: Ebola (EBOV): Atoltivimab/maftivimab/odesivimab-ebgn (Inmazeb, triple mAb cocktail, FDA-approved 2020) IV; or ansuvimab-zykl (Ebanga, mAb, FDA-approved 2020). Marburg: no FDA-approved treatment; remdesivir (Veklury, nucleoside prodrug) and MR191 mAb used investigationally.
treatment_algorithm: Ebola (EBOV/Zaire): Inmazeb (atoltivimab/maftivimab/odesivimab) first-line single IV infusion, or Ebanga (ansuvimab) single IV infusion — FDA-approved per PALM trial/EBOLA-Rx data. Sudan ebolavirus: no approved mAb; MV-SERO-Sudan (vaccine) used under EUA; remdesivir off-label. Marburg: supportive care; cAb3403 mAb + remdesivir + favipiravir compassionate use; no approved therapy. Ebola vaccines: rVSV-ZEBOV (Ervebo, Merck) FDA-approved for Zaire prevention; Ad26.ZEBOV + MVA-BN-Filo (Zabdeno/Mvabea, Johnson & Johnson) approved in EU. Supportive: aggressive fluid replacement, organ support. 2024: Mpox and Marburg outbreaks driving expanded pipeline; MR191 Marburg mAb in Phase 2.
us_prevalence: <5 imported US Ebola cases since 2014; no endemic US cases. Outbreak risk: periodic West/Central Africa outbreaks. (CDC VHF tracking)
patients_needing_tx: <5 US patients/yr typically; outbreak scenario could involve hundreds. Biodefense/outbreak-preparedness context.
price: $50,000–$200,000 per patient treatment (biodefense/orphan; Inmazeb estimated $500K/course); BARDA stockpile pricing varies.
tam_gross: 5 US patients × $100,000 = $500K (US commercial); BARDA procurement: $100M–$500M stockpile contracts.
peak_sales: $50–200M via BARDA/government stockpile contracts; commercial market negligible without outbreak.
registrational_endpoint: 28-day all-cause mortality; viral load reduction at Day 7; organ function recovery score.
btd_threshold_vs_soc: 28-day survival ≥60% vs. ~50% for supportive care (historical Ebola CFR 50–70%); or mAb achieving survival ≥70% vs. ~65% for currently approved mAbs.

symptoms: Fever, rash, joint pain, encephalitis, hemorrhagic fever (varies by arbovirus)
treatment_goal: Supportive care; manage encephalitis and hemorrhagic fever complications
first_line_soc: No FDA-approved antiviral for most arboviruses. Supportive care (IV fluids, antipyretics, analgesics, ICU care). Ribavirin used off-label for some hemorrhagic fever arboviruses. Immunotherapy for severe encephalitis.
treatment_algorithm: Most arboviruses (Ross River, Barmah Forest, Oropouche, Mayaro, EEEV, WEEV, VEEV): supportive care only. Eastern/Western/Venezuelan equine encephalitis (EEE/WEE/VEE): intensive CNS supportive care; no proven antivirals. IVIG and corticosteroids used for severe arboviral encephalitis. Oropouche (emerging, 2024 outbreak in Brazil): supportive. Novel: CDC/BARDA developing medical countermeasures for alphaviral encephalitis (VEEV vaccines, EEEV mAbs). 2024: Oropouche virus emergence in Americas; multiple arboviruses expanding geographic range due to climate change.
us_prevalence: ~200–500 EEE/WEE/VEE cases/yr in US; hundreds of other arboviral cases from travel. (CDC ArboNET)
patients_needing_tx: ~500 US patients/yr with significant arboviral disease need medical management; ICU care for encephalitis.
price: $10,000–$50,000 per course (biodefense-priced arboviral antiviral or BARDA-funded mAb).
peak_sales: $20–50M for BARDA-funded EEEV/VEEV medical countermeasure; commercial market minimal.
registrational_endpoint: Neurological outcome (Glasgow Outcome Scale ≥4) at Day 90 for encephalitic arboviruses; 30-day survival as primary for VHF arboviruses.
btd_threshold_vs_soc: Neurological recovery ≥60% (GOS ≥4) at Day 90 for EEEV encephalitis vs. ~25–40% historical; or mortality <10% vs. ~33% for EEEV.

symptoms: Most asymptomatic; fever, headache, myalgias; severe: encephalitis, flaccid paralysis
treatment_goal: Supportive care, reduce neuroinvasive disease mortality, neuroprotection
first_line_soc: No FDA-approved antiviral for West Nile Virus. Treatment is entirely supportive (IV fluids, antipyretics, mechanical ventilation for encephalitis). IVIG and interferon-alpha have been used off-label with limited evidence.
treatment_algorithm: Supportive care: hospitalization for neuroinvasive disease, ICU for encephalitis/meningitis. Experimental: ribavirin (no proven benefit), interferon-alpha 2b (case series only), IVIG with high WNV-specific titers. No approved antiviral or monoclonal antibody. Prevention: DEET-based mosquito repellents, blood supply screening. Equine vaccine exists but no human vaccine approved. 2024: humanized anti-WNV mAb (MGAWN1) studied in Phase 2 — negative results. No active large trials.
us_prevalence: ~2,000–3,000 symptomatic US cases/yr; ~150–200 deaths/yr; ~400,000 cumulative US cases since 1999 (mostly asymptomatic). (CDC ArboNET 2024)
patients_needing_tx: ~600–800 patients/yr with neuroinvasive WNV requiring hospitalization; no pharmacologic treatment available.
price: $10,000–$50,000 per course for novel anti-WNV agent (mAb or antiviral); premium justified by severe neurological outcomes.
peak_sales: ~$20–50M for novel WNV antiviral/mAb at 50% neuroinvasive market share; limited by small treatable population.
registrational_endpoint: Neurological outcome (Modified Rankin Scale ≤2) at 90 days; all-cause mortality at Day 30; viral RNA clearance in CSF/serum by Day 14.
btd_threshold_vs_soc: mRS ≤2 at 90 days ≥60% vs. ~40% with supportive care; or 30-day mortality <5% vs. ~8–10% for neuroinvasive WNV.

symptoms: Fever, muscle aches, respiratory failure, low platelets, shock
treatment_goal: Supportive ICU care; prevent cardiopulmonary collapse and death
first_line_soc: No FDA-approved antiviral for hantavirus (HPS or HFRS). Ribavirin (Virazole, nucleoside analog) used IV off-label for HFRS (Old World hantaviruses); NOT proven effective for HPS. Supportive care including mechanical ventilation and ECMO.
treatment_algorithm: HPS (Sin Nombre virus, US): aggressive supportive care — early ECMO referral, avoid fluid overload, vasopressors, lung-protective ventilation. IV ribavirin studied but no survival benefit shown in HPS. HFRS (Hantaan, Seoul — international): IV ribavirin reduces renal mortality if given early in the illness. 2024–2025: Favipiravir and other broad-spectrum antivirals under investigation; no Phase 3 data for HPS.
us_prevalence: ~17–50 US HPS cases/yr (CDC); ~250 cases cumulative 2020–2025. Case fatality rate ~38%. HFRS rare in US.
patients_needing_tx: ~35 US HPS patients/yr; all require ICU-level supportive care.
price: $10,000–$50,000 per course (IV antiviral for HPS; rare/orphan disease).
peak_sales: <$5M; pure orphan/biodefense market with limited commercial viability.
registrational_endpoint: 28-day survival; time on mechanical ventilation; requirement for ECMO (composite severity endpoint).
btd_threshold_vs_soc: 28-day survival ≥75% vs. ~62% historical for ECMO-capable centers; or no-ECMO survival ≥50% vs. ~25% historical without ECMO.

symptoms: Mild fever, rash, joint pain, conjunctivitis; severe: microcephaly in fetus, Guillain-Barré
treatment_goal: Supportive care; prevent fetal microcephaly in pregnant women
first_line_soc: No FDA-approved antiviral for Zika. Treatment is supportive (acetaminophen, fluids, rest). Aspirin and NSAIDs avoided (dengue co-infection risk). Prevention: mosquito control, barrier contraception for sexual transmission.
treatment_algorithm: Supportive care: acetaminophen for fever/pain; rest and hydration. Sexual transmission prevention: barrier contraception 3–6 months in exposed individuals. Pregnancy: serial fetal ultrasound monitoring. Guillain-Barré syndrome: IVIG 2g/kg or plasmapheresis. No approved antivirals. Investigational: NITD008 (adenosine analog), sofosbuvir, ribavirin (all pre-clinical/early clinical). Vaccine pipeline: mRNA ZIKV vaccines (Moderna Phase 1), DNA vaccine (IAVI Phase 2). 2024–2025: No Zika vaccine approved; transmission low in continental US; Puerto Rico/travel cases ongoing.
us_prevalence: ~80–200 US cases/yr (travel-associated), down from 2016–2017 outbreak (5,000+ cases). (CDC ArboNET 2024)
patients_needing_tx: ~150 US patients/yr needing supportive care; pregnant women with Zika exposure (~50–100/yr) require intensive fetal monitoring.
price: $2,000–$10,000 per course for antiviral; $100–300/dose for ZIKV vaccine.
tam_gross: 150 US patients × $5,000 = $750K; global outbreak potential 500,000+ cases × $500 = $250M
peak_sales: ~$50–200M for Zika vaccine in outbreak scenario; antiviral ~$20–50M in pregnancy/endemic indication. Largely outbreak-dependent.
registrational_endpoint: Reduction in viremia AUC by Day 7; prevention of mother-to-child transmission (MTCT) rate in pregnant women; Guillain-Barré incidence reduction.
btd_threshold_vs_soc: Viral clearance by Day 5 in ≥80% vs. no clearance with supportive care; MTCT rate <5% vs. ~29% in asymptomatic maternal infection; GBS incidence reduction ≥50%.

symptoms: Severe bleeding, plasma leakage, shock, multiorgan failure
treatment_goal: Restore plasma volume, prevent shock, control hemorrhage
first_line_soc: No approved pharmacologic treatment; strict fluid management per WHO protocol; platelet transfusion for severe thrombocytopenia with bleeding.
treatment_algorithm: ICU-level fluid management per WHO DHF guidelines: crystalloid boluses, careful monitoring of hematocrit, avoidance of fluid overload. Platelet transfusion if <10,000/μL with active bleeding. Fresh frozen plasma/cryoprecipitate for coagulopathy. Corticosteroids studied but not proven beneficial (multiple RCTs negative). IVIG not proven. No approved pharmacotherapy. 2024: Same supportive management as dengue.
us_prevalence: ~50–100 US DHF/DSS cases/yr (imported); globally ~500,000 severe dengue/yr requiring hospitalization. (CDC/WHO)
patients_needing_tx: ~100 US patients/yr; globally ~500,000. All require supportive ICU treatment. Novel pharmacologic agent for dengue vascular leak/coagulopathy is unmet need.
price: $2,000–$10,000 per ICU course (plasma leak inhibitor or novel hemostatic agent; per-course price).
peak_sales: $200–500M globally for novel dengue vascular permeability inhibitor (statins, anti-NS1 antibodies in trials).
registrational_endpoint: Prevention of dengue shock (DSS) in hospitalized DHF patients; hematocrit normalization at 48 hours; reduction in plasma leakage (protein-to-creatinine ratio).
btd_threshold_vs_soc: DSS rate <5% vs. ~20% in control group of severe dengue; or hematocrit normalization within 24 hours vs. ~48 hours with fluid management alone.

symptoms: Fever, weakness, headache, sore throat, chest pain, hemorrhagic manifestations
treatment_goal: Reduce viral replication, prevent hemorrhagic shock and death
first_line_soc: Ribavirin (Virazole, nucleoside analog) IV/oral if given within 6 days of onset — reduces mortality from ~50% to ~5% in high-risk patients (per WHO data). IV ribavirin 2g loading, then 1g q6h × 4 days, then 0.5g q8h × 6 days.
treatment_algorithm: IV ribavirin (preferred) or oral ribavirin for mild/moderate Lassa fever if within 6 days of symptom onset. Supportive: fluid resuscitation, antipyretics, correction of electrolyte imbalances, blood products for coagulopathy. Novel: favipiravir showed activity in animal models but no human trials completed. Lassa vaccine candidates (VSV-LASV, IAVI Lassa vaccine) in Phase 1–2 trials 2024. Convalescent plasma in limited use. 2024–2025: No new FDA-approved agents; ribavirin remains standard.
us_prevalence: ~1–5 imported US cases/yr; endemic West Africa (100,000–300,000 cases/yr globally). (CDC Lassa fever reports)
patients_needing_tx: ~1–5 US patients/yr; all severe cases require IV ribavirin.
price: $10,000–$50,000 per course (IV antiviral for VHF; orphan/biodefense pricing); vaccine $50–$150/dose (public health).
tam_gross: <5 US patients × $30,000 = $150K (US commercial value negligible); biodefense/BARDA funding context.
peak_sales: <$5M US; primarily BARDA/government procurement; global neglected disease.
registrational_endpoint: 28-day survival; reduction in Lassa viral load (RT-PCR) by Day 7; clinical improvement score.
btd_threshold_vs_soc: Mortality <5% vs. ~50% untreated; or ≥50% faster viral clearance vs. ribavirin; or vaccine efficacy ≥75% in endemic population.

symptoms: Fever, jaundice, hemorrhage, renal failure, black vomit (severe/toxic phase)
treatment_goal: Supportive care, organ support; vaccination is definitive prevention
first_line_soc: No specific antiviral treatment exists. Treatment is entirely supportive: IV fluids, vasopressors, renal replacement therapy, fresh frozen plasma for coagulopathy. No antivirals with proven benefit. Vaccination (17D live-attenuated vaccine) is highly effective prevention.
treatment_algorithm: Supportive: hospitalization with ICU for toxic phase. Renal replacement therapy, vasopressors, FFP for hemorrhagic diathesis. Ribavirin (no clinical benefit established). Sofosbuvir (HCV NS5B inhibitor) active against YFV in vitro — no clinical trial data. Recombinant interferon studied experimentally. 17D vaccine (YF-Vax, Stamaril): >99% protective, single lifetime dose in most travelers. 2024: No novel antivirals in Phase 2/3; vaccine supply concerns in endemic regions.
us_prevalence: ~0–2 imported US cases/yr (exceptionally rare); endemic in sub-Saharan Africa and South America. (CDC 2024)
patients_needing_tx: 0–2 US patients/yr; essentially no US commercial treatment market.
price: $5,000–$20,000 per course for hypothetical antiviral (per-course); government procurement for biodefense if developed.
registrational_endpoint: All-cause survival at Day 28 for severe yellow fever; viral RNA clearance from serum by Day 7; ALT/AST normalization and resolution of jaundice.
btd_threshold_vs_soc: Survival ≥60% in toxic-phase yellow fever vs. ~50% historical with supportive care; or viral clearance ≥80% by Day 7 vs. no clearance with supportive care.

symptoms: Painful dermatomal rash, vesicular blisters, burning pain, postherpetic neuralgia
treatment_goal: Reduce viral replication, accelerate healing, prevent postherpetic neuralgia
first_line_soc: Valacyclovir (Valtrex, nucleoside analog prodrug) 1g TID × 7 days or famciclovir 500 mg TID × 7 days; both superior to acyclovir due to pharmacokinetics. Prevention: Shingrix (recombinant zoster vaccine, adjuvanted, GSK) >90% efficacy.
treatment_algorithm: Acute zoster: valacyclovir 1g TID × 7 days (within 72 hrs of rash); famciclovir 500 mg TID × 7 days or acyclovir 800 mg 5×/day × 7 days. Immunocompromised: IV acyclovir 10 mg/kg q8h × 7 days. Postherpetic neuralgia (PHN) treatment: gabapentin, pregabalin, tricyclic antidepressants, lidocaine patches, capsaicin. Zoster ophthalmicus: IV or high-dose oral acyclovir + ophthalmology. Prevention: Shingrix 2-dose series (97% efficacy in 50–69 yr). Novel: pritelivir, amenamevir (shorter courses). 2024: Shingrix supply normalized; boosted vaccination campaigns.
us_prevalence: ~1 million US zoster cases/yr (declining with Shingrix expansion); ~100,000–130,000 PHN cases/yr. (CDC shingles data)
patients_needing_tx: ~900,000 acute zoster patients/yr need antivirals; ~130,000 PHN patients need chronic pain management.
price: $500–$1,500 per acute course (novel antiviral with proven PHN prevention vs. valacyclovir); suppressive $600–$1,200/yr for immunocompromised.
peak_sales: $150–400M for novel zoster antiviral with PHN prevention differentiation vs. valacyclovir at 15–25% share.
registrational_endpoint: Incidence of postherpetic neuralgia at Day 90; time to cessation of new lesion formation; pain score (VAS/NRS) reduction at Day 7.
btd_threshold_vs_soc: PHN incidence <5% at Day 90 vs. ~13–20% for valacyclovir; or ≥50% reduction in pain NRS score at Day 7 vs. ~30% for valacyclovir.

symptoms: Prodrome fever, then pustular rash covering entire body including palms/soles
treatment_goal: Antiviral treatment, supportive care, ring vaccination to halt outbreak
first_line_soc: Tecovirimat (TPOXX/ST-246, viral egress inhibitor) 600 mg BID × 14 days — FDA-approved under animal rule for smallpox; first-line per CDC Strategic National Stockpile protocol.
treatment_algorithm: First-line: tecovirimat (TPOXX) oral 600 mg BID × 14 days or IV for severe/unable to swallow. Cidofovir (Vistide) IV: licensed alternative in SNS; nephrotoxic (requires probenecid + hydration). Brincidofovir (Tembexa): FDA-approved 2021 for smallpox (animal rule); reduced nephrotoxicity vs. cidofovir. Vaccinia immune globulin (VIG): adjunct for vaccine complications, severe eczema vaccinatum. Supportive care: IV fluids, pain management, antibiotics for secondary bacterial superinfection. Ring vaccination with ACAM2000 or JYNNEOS (modified vaccinia Ankara). 2024–2025: SNS stockpile of tecovirimat; WHO smallpox eradication maintained.
us_prevalence: Zero natural cases (eradicated 1980). US preparedness for bioterrorist event or lab accident; SNS protocol for mass casualty scenario.
patients_needing_tx: Zero current cases; preparedness stockpile for hypothetical outbreak. SNS maintains 1.7 million courses of tecovirimat.
price: $500–$2,000 per course (government stockpile pricing); BARDA contract drives market.
tam_gross: Hypothetical outbreak: 10,000 initial cases × $1,500 = $15M acute; preparedness stockpile contracts ~$100–500M total value.
peak_sales: Government procurement contracts ($200–500M) dominate; no commercial market under natural epidemiologic conditions.
registrational_endpoint: Animal rule: survival in lethal orthopoxvirus animal models (NHP); human endpoints via ring vaccination RCT impossible. For mpox surrogate: time to lesion resolution, viral load reduction.
btd_threshold_vs_soc: N/A in classical sense; animal rule approval: ≥60% survival in NHP model vs. ~0% untreated; lesion count reduction ≥50% in mpox human data.

symptoms: Painful rash/vesicles on genitals/skin, fever, lymphadenopathy, myalgia
treatment_goal: Accelerate lesion healing, reduce severe disease, prevent transmission
first_line_soc: Tecovirimat (TPOXX, Orthopoxvirus CDC-506 inhibitor, SIGA Technologies) — FDA-approved for smallpox, used under EUA/IND for mpox; 600 mg BID × 14 days (adult, oral). Brincidofovir (Tembexa) or cidofovir as alternatives for severe disease.
treatment_algorithm: Mild mpox (immunocompetent): supportive care (wound care, pain management); antivirals reserved for severe/high-risk. Severe/immunocompromised: tecovirimat 600 mg BID × 14 days oral or IV. Brincidofovir (Tembexa) 200 mg oral 2×/week for renal/hepatic concerns. IV cidofovir as salvage. Vaccinia Immune Globulin Intravenous (VIGIV) for severe disease. Prevention: JYNNEOS vaccine (Bavarian Nordic) 2-dose subcutaneous — FDA-approved; ACAM2000 as alternative. 2024–2025: Clade I mpox outbreak in DRC and Africa; JYNNEOS deployed; tecovirimat PALM007 trial data under review. FDA IND requirement for tecovirimat mpox use remains controversial.
us_prevalence: ~30,000 cumulative US mpox cases since 2022; ~5,000–10,000 new cases/yr (2025 estimates with ongoing clade IIb transmission). (CDC mpox tracking 2025)
patients_needing_tx: ~2,000–5,000 US patients/yr with severe/immunocompromised mpox need antivirals; ~500 critically ill (HIV coinfection).
price: Tecovirimat (TPOXX) ~$2,000–$5,000 per course (commercial pricing TBD post-FDA approval for mpox); new orthopoxvirus agents $3,000–$10,000.
tam_gross: 5,000 severe patients × $4,000 = $20M; mild disease if routinely treated: 10,000 × $4,000 = $40M. JYNNEOS vaccine market $100M+/yr.
peak_sales: $50–150M for tecovirimat (FDA approval for mpox indication) + JYNNEOS vaccine ~$200M/yr US; global outbreak scenario could drive $500M peak.
registrational_endpoint: Time to lesion resolution (all lesions crusted/healed) — primary endpoint for mpox antiviral; maximum lesion count reduction at Day 7.
btd_threshold_vs_soc: Time to lesion resolution ≤14 days vs. ~21 days for placebo; or ≥50% reduction in mpox hospitalizations in HIV-positive patients vs. supportive care.

symptoms: Itchy blistering rash, fever, headache, generalized malaise
treatment_goal: Reduce severity, shorten duration, prevent complications
first_line_soc: Acyclovir (Zovirax, nucleoside analog) 800 mg 5× daily × 5–7 days for adolescents/adults and high-risk children; valacyclovir (Valtrex) 1g TID × 5–7 days preferred for better bioavailability.
treatment_algorithm: Immunocompetent children: supportive (antihistamines, oatmeal baths); treatment beneficial within 24 hrs if adolescent/adult. Adolescents/adults: valacyclovir 1g TID × 5 days or acyclovir 800 mg 5×/day × 5–7 days. Immunocompromised: IV acyclovir 10–15 mg/kg q8h × 7–10 days. Neonatal varicella: IV acyclovir 10 mg/kg q8h × 10 days. Prevention: varicella-zoster immune globulin (VariZIG) post-exposure in high-risk. VZV vaccine (Varivax) primary prevention. 2024: Universal vaccination has reduced incidence >90% since 1995.
us_prevalence: ~350,000 US cases/yr (down from 4 million pre-vaccine era); mostly unvaccinated/immunocompromised. (CDC VPD surveillance 2024)
patients_needing_tx: ~100,000–150,000 US patients/yr require antiviral treatment (adults, adolescents, immunocompromised, pregnant women); mild pediatric cases managed supportively.
price: $200–$500 per course (novel antiviral vs. generic valacyclovir ~$30–50); minimal differentiation opportunity. Noting per-course price.
peak_sales: $10–20M for novel anti-VZV agent at 10% share; strong generic competition.
registrational_endpoint: Time to lesion crusting (50% of lesions crusted vs. baseline); fever resolution; viral titer reduction.
btd_threshold_vs_soc: Lesion crusting 2 days faster than acyclovir/valacyclovir; or viral titer clearance by Day 3 vs. Day 5 with current SoC.

symptoms: Fever, cough, shortness of breath, fatigue, loss of smell/taste
treatment_goal: Prevent severe disease, hospitalization, and death
first_line_soc: Nirmatrelvir-ritonavir (Paxlovid, 3CL protease inhibitor) 300/100 mg BID × 5 days for high-risk outpatients within 5 days of symptom onset; remdesivir (Veklury, nucleoside analog) IV × 3–5 days for hospitalized patients.
treatment_algorithm: High-risk outpatients: nirmatrelvir-ritonavir (Paxlovid) first-line; molnupiravir (Lagevrio) if Paxlovid contraindicated (drug interactions). Hospitalized, no O2: remdesivir IV × 5 days. Hospitalized, on O2: remdesivir + dexamethasone 6 mg/day × 10 days. ICU/mechanical ventilation: dexamethasone + baricitinib (Olumiant, JAK inhibitor) or tocilizumab (Actemra, IL-6 antagonist) for cytokine storm. Bebtelovimab and other monoclonal antibodies lost activity vs. current variants (XBB/JN.1 era 2024). 2024–2025 variants: Paxlovid retains activity; new nirmatrelvir formulations under study. Long COVID: no approved pharmacotherapy; leronlimab and other agents in trials.
us_prevalence: ~5–10 million active/recent COVID-19 cases at any time (endemic, seasonal peaks); ~3–5 million annual hospitalizations declined substantially. Long COVID: ~6 million Americans (Census Bureau/CDC 2025).
patients_needing_tx: ~10 million high-risk outpatients/yr meet Paxlovid eligibility; ~1 million hospitalizations requiring remdesivir/dexamethasone.
price: $500–$1,500 per outpatient course; $3,000–$8,000 per inpatient course (per-course acute disease price). Long COVID chronic therapy: $10,000–$30,000/yr if approved.
peak_sales: Paxlovid ~$8B peak (2022); sustained market at $2–3B/yr in endemic phase. Novel antiviral next-gen: $1–3B peak; Long COVID drug: $3–10B potential.
registrational_endpoint: COVID-19-related hospitalization or death through Day 28 (primary endpoint for outpatient trials); for inpatient: time to recovery (6-ordinal scale Day 28) as per FDA COVID antiviral guidance.
btd_threshold_vs_soc: Hospitalization/death rate ≤1% vs. ~5–6% for placebo in high-risk unvaccinated/immunocompromised; or ≥35% relative risk reduction vs. Paxlovid in Paxlovid-ineligible patients.

symptoms: Runny nose, sore throat, sneezing, mild cough, congestion
treatment_goal: Symptom relief; reduce duration (no cure)
first_line_soc: None pharmacologically curative. Symptomatic: antihistamines (loratadine), decongestants (pseudoephedrine), NSAIDs/acetaminophen, zinc lozenges (modest evidence for duration reduction).
treatment_algorithm: Symptomatic relief: pseudoephedrine/oxymetazoline for congestion; antihistamines (first-generation more effective for cold symptoms); throat lozenges; NSAIDs. Zinc acetate lozenges initiated ≤24 hrs may reduce duration by ~1 day (Cochrane 2015 but variable evidence). Vitamin C supplementation: modest benefit if pre-exposure. Intranasal ipratropium (Atrovent) for rhinorrhea. Novel antivirals: pleconaril (Schering-Plough) failed FDA approval; no approved antirhinoviral exists. BTA798 (vapendavir, rhinovirus capsid inhibitor) in Phase 2/3 historically. Gesifovir and other candidates in development. 2024: No new approved agents.
us_prevalence: ~1 billion US episodes/yr (adults average 2–3 colds/yr; children 6–8/yr). (NIAID estimate)
patients_needing_tx: OTC symptomatic treatment: ~400–500 million treatment episodes/yr; novel antiviral (not yet available) would target ~100–200 million adults seeking faster recovery.
price: $50–$150 per course (rhinovirus-specific oral antiviral, 5-day course; per-course price); pricing constrained by OTC competition and self-limiting nature.
peak_sales: $500M–$2B for first approved cold antiviral shortening duration by ≥2 days at 5–10% market penetration (precedent: Tamiflu for flu).
registrational_endpoint: Time to alleviation of cold symptoms (composite score including nasal discharge, sore throat, sneezing, cough, nasal obstruction); Jackson score at Day 5.
btd_threshold_vs_soc: Symptom resolution by Day 3 vs. ~Day 7 for placebo; viral load reduction ≥2 log10 by Day 2 vs. <0.5 log10 for placebo.

symptoms: Sudden fever, body aches, cough, fatigue, headache, chills
treatment_goal: Reduce severity and duration, prevent complications, limit transmission
first_line_soc: Oseltamivir (Tamiflu, neuraminidase inhibitor) 75 mg BID × 5 days within 48 hrs of symptom onset; or baloxavir marboxil (Xofluza, cap-dependent endonuclease inhibitor) 40–80 mg single dose for uncomplicated influenza.
treatment_algorithm: Uncomplicated influenza: oseltamivir 75 mg BID × 5 days or baloxavir single dose (preferred for simplicity). Hospitalized/severe: IV oseltamivir or IV peramivir (Rapivab) 600 mg single dose. Oseltamivir-resistant (H275Y): IV zanamivir (Relenza) or peramivir. Prophylaxis: oseltamivir 75 mg/day × 10 days (post-exposure) or 6 weeks (seasonal). Influenza vaccines (IIV4, LAIV4, high-dose, adjuvanted for elderly, recombinant — Flublok) primary prevention. Novel: HA stalk-targeting mAbs (VIS410, CT-P27) in trials; mRNA universal flu vaccine pipeline (Moderna, Sanofi). 2024–2025 flu season: baloxavir uptake increasing; oseltamivir resistance monitoring ongoing. New IDSA 2024 guidelines support earlier treatment.
us_prevalence: ~20–40 million US influenza cases/yr (varies by season); ~400,000 hospitalizations/yr; ~15,000–50,000 deaths/yr. (CDC FluView)
patients_needing_tx: ~10–15 million US patients/yr warrant antiviral treatment (high-risk patients, hospitalized, or within 48 hrs of symptom onset).
price: $200–$600 per course (acute antiviral); branded IV agent $3,000–$8,000 per hospitalized course. Annual maintenance (prophylaxis): $1,500–$3,000/yr for immunocompromised.
tam_gross: 15M treated patients × $400 avg = $6B; novel IV 400,000 hospitalized × $5,000 = $2B. Total ~$8B.
peak_sales: $800M–$2B for novel mechanism antiviral (pan-influenza activity, less resistance) at 10–15% share; mRNA universal vaccine $2–5B peak.
registrational_endpoint: Time to alleviation of symptoms (TTAS) — time to all 7 IDSA-defined influenza symptoms rated ≤1; or Time to Clinically Meaningful Improvement. FDA influenza guidance 2024.
btd_threshold_vs_soc: TTAS ≤54 hours vs. ~80 hours for oseltamivir; or 50% reduction in hospitalizations vs. placebo for high-risk patients; viral titer reduction ≥2 log₁₀ by Day 2.

symptoms: High fever, severe respiratory distress, cough, conjunctivitis
treatment_goal: Reduce viral load, prevent respiratory failure, prevent death
first_line_soc: Oseltamivir (Tamiflu, neuraminidase inhibitor) 75 mg BID × 5 days (double-dose 150 mg BID sometimes used for H5N1); start within 48 hrs. IV peramivir or inhaled zanamivir alternatives.
treatment_algorithm: Step 1: High-dose oseltamivir (75–150 mg BID × 10 days) as soon as suspected H5N1/H7N9 exposure. Step 2: Baloxavir marboxil (Xofluza, cap-dependent endonuclease inhibitor) — active against avian strains; added in 2024 US outbreak H5N1 cases. Step 3: IV peramivir for hospitalized patients unable to take orally. Step 4: Supportive ICU care — mechanical ventilation, ECMO for ARDS. Step 5: Avoid corticosteroids (worsen outcomes in H5N1). Anti-PD1 immunotherapy under investigation for cytokine storm. 2025: H5N1 clade 2.3.4.4b active in US dairy cattle/poultry; human cases via direct animal contact reported. mRNA H5 vaccines in development (Moderna, NIAID).
us_prevalence: ~70+ confirmed US human cases 2024–2025 (mostly H5N1 from cattle/poultry exposure); pandemic risk elevated. (CDC H5 tracker 2025)
patients_needing_tx: All confirmed/probable human avian flu cases require immediate antiviral treatment (~100–500 US patients/yr currently; pandemic scenario: millions).
price: $500–$2,000 per course (per-course price); in pandemic context, government stockpile pricing. Novel broad-spectrum antiviral could command $1,500–$3,000/course.
tam_gross: Current epidemic: 500 patients × $1,500 = $750K; pandemic scenario: 5M patients × $1,500 = $7.5B.
peak_sales: Pandemic scenario: $2–5B peak (government procurement + commercial). Endemic/sporadic: $50–100M/yr.
registrational_endpoint: Time to alleviation of influenza symptoms (IARI/IASRS score) by Day 5; viral load reduction by 48 hrs; 28-day mortality for severe disease.
btd_threshold_vs_soc: Symptom resolution by Day 3 vs. Day 4.2 for oseltamivir; or 48-hr viral clearance ≥2 log10 reduction vs. ~1 log10 for comparator.

symptoms: Runny nose, sore throat, cough, fever, shortness of breath
treatment_goal: Reduce viral replication, shorten illness, prevent hospitalization
first_line_soc: Influenza: oseltamivir (Tamiflu, neuraminidase inhibitor) 75 mg BID × 5 days. RSV (in infants/elderly): nirsevimab (Beyfortus, mAb prophylaxis) or palivizumab for high-risk infants; abrysvo/mResvia vaccines for adults. COVID-19: nirmatrelvir/ritonavir (Paxlovid) × 5 days. Most other viral URIs (rhinovirus, parainfluenza): supportive care only.
treatment_algorithm: Broad category. Influenza: oseltamivir 75 mg BID × 5 days (zanamivir, baloxavir alternatives). RSV in adults/elderly: no approved direct antiviral; abrysvo/mResvia vaccine. RSV hospitalized infants: supportive; compassionate ribavirin. Parechovirus, enteroviruses: no specific antiviral. Rhinovirus: no approved antiviral; ruprintrivir (early failed trials). Parainfluenza, hMPV: supportive. HPIV in immunocompromised: ribavirin aerosol (limited evidence). 2024–2025: Novel RSV antivirals (ziresovir — Phase 3; presatovir failed); broad-spectrum antivirals (obeldesivir, galidesivir) in development.
us_prevalence: ~1 billion viral URI episodes/yr in US (rhinovirus/common cold dominant); ~35–40 million influenza cases/yr; ~3–4 million RSV hospitalizations (all ages). (CDC)
patients_needing_tx: ~20–30 million patients/yr seek treatment; ~5–8 million high-risk patients receive antivirals (oseltamivir, Paxlovid). Vast majority treated supportively.
price: $100–$500 per course for common cold/rhinovirus agent; $1,500 per course for severe viral LRI agent; per-course pricing.
peak_sales: ~$500M–1.5B for novel broad-spectrum antiviral covering rhinovirus/RSV/parainfluenza in outpatient high-risk setting.
registrational_endpoint: Time to alleviation of all symptoms (TAAS) in outpatient viral LRTI; hospitalization rate at Day 28 for high-risk adults with RSV/influenza; viral load AUC by Day 5 for antiviral trials.
btd_threshold_vs_soc: TAAS reduction by ≥3 days vs. placebo for rhinovirus; RSV hospitalizations in adults reduced ≥40% vs. placebo; or influenza TAAS <4 days vs. ~6 days placebo.

symptoms: Croup, hoarse voice, barking cough, stridor, bronchiolitis, pneumonia in children
treatment_goal: Supportive airway management; prevent respiratory failure in immunocompromised
first_line_soc: No FDA-approved antiviral for parainfluenza virus (PIV). Supportive care: dexamethasone (steroid) 0.6 mg/kg IM/oral × 1 dose for croup reduces edema; nebulized epinephrine for severe stridor.
treatment_algorithm: Croup (mild): mist therapy (limited evidence), humidification. Moderate/severe croup: dexamethasone 0.6 mg/kg IM/oral single dose + nebulized epinephrine 0.5 mL of 2.25% racemic epi. Hospitalized severe: helium-oxygen (heliox) inhaled mixture. Immunocompromised PIV pneumonia: IV ribavirin + IVIG off-label. DAS181 (sialidase fusion protein, recombinant) in Phase 3 trials for PIV in HSCT patients (Ansun Biopharma) — showed clinical benefit in 2023 Phase 3. 2024: DAS181 BLA submission pending.
us_prevalence: ~5 million US PIV infections/yr (mainly children); ~200–500 severe immunocompromised cases/yr. (CDC respiratory virus surveillance)
patients_needing_tx: ~200,000 children/yr with moderate/severe croup need dexamethasone; ~500 immunocompromised adults need antiviral.
price: $10,000–$30,000 per course for DAS181 (HSCT/immunocompromised PIV); pediatric antiviral $200–$500.
peak_sales: $30–60M for DAS181 (Ansun Biopharma) in HSCT PIV indication; pediatric antiviral market $50–100M if approved.
registrational_endpoint: Clinical failure rate (progression to mechanical ventilation or death) at Day 28 in HSCT patients; time to viral load ≤2 log₁₀ copies/mL; Croup Score improvement for pediatric endpoint.
btd_threshold_vs_soc: Clinical failure rate <15% vs. ~35% for supportive care in HSCT PIV (DAS181 PHASE 3 data); or PIV RNA negativity ≥60% at Day 14 vs. ~15% for placebo.

symptoms: Fever, sore throat, conjunctivitis, respiratory illness, diarrhea
treatment_goal: Supportive care; prevent dissemination in immunocompromised
first_line_soc: No FDA-approved antiviral for adenovirus. Cidofovir (Vistide, nucleotide analog) used off-label for severe/disseminated disease in immunocompromised patients.
treatment_algorithm: Step 1: Supportive care for immunocompetent patients (self-limiting). Step 2: Immunocompromised (transplant, HIV): off-label cidofovir IV with probenecid and aggressive hydration to reduce nephrotoxicity. Step 3: Brincidofovir (CMX001/Tembexa) — FDA-approved 2021 for smallpox, has adenovirus activity, used compassionately. Step 4: IVIG adjunct in severe cases. Step 5: Reduction of immunosuppression if feasible. 2024–2025: CMX001 compassionate use data accumulating; no Phase 3 trials completed for adenovirus.
us_prevalence: ~10 million symptomatic US cases/yr overall; ~600 severe/disseminated cases in immunocompromised patients annually. (CDC surveillance)
patients_needing_tx: ~600–1,000 immunocompromised patients/yr need antiviral treatment; majority of immunocompetent cases need only supportive care.
price: $5,000–$15,000 per course (IV antiviral, 2-3 week course for immunocompromised); comparable to brincidofovir pricing framework.
peak_sales: ~$15–30M at 50–60% share in immunocompromised segment (~600 patients × $10,000 × 50% = $3M; modest orphan premium pricing could push to $20–30M)
registrational_endpoint: Adenovirus viremia clearance (PCR negativity) by Day 21 in immunocompromised patients; or 42-day all-cause mortality.
btd_threshold_vs_soc: Viral clearance ≥60% by Day 21 vs. ~35% for cidofovir; or mortality reduction ≥30% relative risk reduction vs. historical.

disease: Coronaviruses and Acute Respiratory Syndromes (MERS and SARS)
symptoms: High fever, severe pneumonia, respiratory failure, multiorgan dysfunction
treatment_goal: Reduce viral replication, prevent respiratory failure and death
first_line_soc: No FDA-approved specific treatment for MERS-CoV or SARS-CoV-1. Supportive ICU care; remdesivir (Veklury) used off-label for MERS based on in vitro/primate data; ribavirin + interferon used historically for MERS with limited benefit.
treatment_algorithm: Supportive: mechanical ventilation, ECMO for ARDS. MERS-CoV: Remdesivir (off-label, based on nCoV data); lopinavir/ritonavir + ribavirin + interferon (MIRACLE trial for MERS showed limited benefit). SARS-CoV-1: lopinavir/ritonavir, ribavirin, corticosteroids used historically with uncertain benefit. MERS: anti-MERS-CoV monoclonal antibodies (REGN3048+3051) completed Phase 2 (LY-CoV3256 under study). Convalescent plasma historically used. 2024: No active SARS-CoV-1 cases globally. MERS-CoV ~200 cases/yr globally, mainly Saudi Arabia.
us_prevalence: SARS: zero cases since 2004. MERS: <5 imported US cases (last 2014); 0–2 US cases expected in 2025. Global MERS: ~200–250 cases/yr (Saudi Arabia). (WHO MERS surveillance 2024)
patients_needing_tx: <5 US patients in any given year for MERS; all would require treatment.
price: $10,000–$50,000 per course (anti-MERS mAb or broad-spectrum coronavirus antiviral; per-course price); primarily government procurement/pandemic preparedness.
tam_gross: 5 US × $30,000 = $150K. Primarily BARDA/government pandemic preparedness value.
peak_sales: Pandemic preparedness: government contracts $50–200M potential. Commercial market negligible at current MERS/SARS incidence.
registrational_endpoint: 28-day all-cause mortality; time to clinical improvement (WHO ordinal scale) in MERS hospitalized patients.
btd_threshold_vs_soc: 28-day mortality ≤20% vs. ~35–40% historical for MERS-CoV severe disease with supportive care only.

symptoms: Fatigue, weight loss, recurrent infections, lymphadenopathy, immunodeficiency
treatment_goal: Suppress HIV viral load to undetectable, restore CD4 count, prevent AIDS
first_line_soc: Bictegravir/tenofovir alafenamide/emtricitabine (Biktarvy, INSTI + NRTI backbone) single-tablet regimen once daily; or dolutegravir/abacavir/lamivudine (Triumeq) as alternative.
treatment_algorithm: First-line: Biktarvy (BIC/TAF/FTC) once daily (preferred per DHHS 2024 guidelines) or dolutegravir-based STRs. Alternative: darunavir/cobicistat/TAF/FTC (Symtuza). Long-acting injectable: cabotegravir + rilpivirine (Cabenuva) Q2M IM injections (ViiV/Janssen) — preferred for adherence challenges. Islatravir + lenacapavir (capsid inhibitor, Sunlenca) — lenacapavir 6-monthly SC injection Phase 3 demonstrating >90% suppression (PURPOSE trials). Pre-exposure prophylaxis (PrEP): emtricitabine/tenofovir (Truvada/Descovy) daily oral or cabotegravir LA IM Q2M (Apretude). Treatment-experienced: lenacapavir (Sunlenca) + optimized background. 2024–2025: lenacapavir PURPOSE 1 & 2 trials showed >90% HIV prevention; islatravir combinations advancing.
us_prevalence: ~1.2 million Americans living with HIV; ~35,000 new infections/yr. ~87% diagnosed; ~67% on treatment; ~57% virologically suppressed. (CDC HIV surveillance 2024)
patients_needing_tx: ~1.2 million patients need ART; all with diagnosed HIV warrant treatment regardless of CD4 count.
price: $30,000–$50,000/yr (branded long-acting injectables; Biktarvy ~$45K/yr WAC; lenacapavir ~$42K/yr). Long-acting SC lenacapavir 2×/yr $40,000–$60,000/yr.
peak_sales: $3–6B peak for next-generation long-acting regimen (e.g., lenacapavir + islatravir 2×/yr); PrEP market additional $2–4B.
registrational_endpoint: HIV-1 RNA <50 copies/mL at Week 48 (virological suppression; FDA Snapshot algorithm); CD4 count increase as secondary endpoint.
btd_threshold_vs_soc: Virological suppression ≥92% at Week 48 vs. ~89% for Biktarvy; or long-acting Q6M regimen with suppression non-inferior to daily oral ± <5% treatment discontinuation for AEs.

symptoms: Oral/genital blisters, painful ulcers, fever, encephalitis in severe cases
treatment_goal: Suppress HSV replication, heal lesions, prevent severe complications
first_line_soc: Acyclovir (Zovirax, nucleoside analog) or valacyclovir (Valtrex, prodrug) for primary and recurrent episodes; IV acyclovir 10 mg/kg q8h for HSV encephalitis and neonatal herpes.
treatment_algorithm: Orolabial/genital HSV: oral acyclovir 400 mg TID × 7–10 days (primary) or valacyclovir 1g BID × 7–10 days or famciclovir 250 mg TID. Suppressive: valacyclovir 500 mg–1g/day indefinitely. Severe/encephalitis: IV acyclovir 10 mg/kg q8h × 14–21 days. Neonatal HSV: IV acyclovir 20 mg/kg q8h × 14–21 days; high-dose oral acyclovir suppression post-treatment. Acyclovir-resistant HSV (foscarnet 40 mg/kg q8h IV; cidofovir). Novel: pritelivir (helicase-primase inhibitor) Phase 3 for immunocompromised; amenamevir (Japan/Asia approved). 2024–2025: Pritelivir NDA anticipated.
us_prevalence: HSV-1: ~67% US adults seropositive; HSV-2: ~12% adults (~38M); active symptomatic HSV ~25M patients needing treatment. (CDC/NHANES)
patients_needing_tx: ~7–8 million active HSV patients seek treatment annually; ~5M on suppressive therapy.
price: $600–$2,000/yr suppressive; $150–$400 per acute course; novel agent (pritelivir) expected $1,500–$3,000/yr.
peak_sales: $500M–$1.5B for novel agent (pritelivir or mRNA vaccine) at 10–15% share of total treatable HSV population.
registrational_endpoint: Viral shedding rate (% days with positive HSV PCR) for suppression; time to lesion healing for acute; CSF PCR negativity for encephalitis.
btd_threshold_vs_soc: Shedding rate <2%/yr vs. ~10% for valacyclovir (suppressive); or pritelivir superior reduction in viral shedding by ≥50% relative to valacyclovir.

symptoms: Varies by virus: fever, rash, lymphadenopathy, encephalitis, organ involvement
treatment_goal: Suppress herpesvirus replication, prevent end-organ damage and death
first_line_soc: Class-dependent: acyclovir/valacyclovir (HSV-1/2, VZV); ganciclovir/valganciclovir (CMV); foscarnet (resistant CMV/HSV). EBV, HHV-6/7/8: no approved targeted antiviral; ganciclovir/foscarnet used off-label.
treatment_algorithm: HSV-1/2: valacyclovir or acyclovir (see HSV entry). VZV: valacyclovir/famciclovir/IV acyclovir. CMV: valganciclovir (Valcyte) prophylaxis/preemptive; ganciclovir IV for disease; letermovir (Prevymis) CMV prophylaxis in HSCT. EBV: no antiviral approved; supportive care; rituximab for EBV-PTLD. HHV-6 (encephalitis in HSCT): foscarnet or ganciclovir. HHV-8 (Kaposi's): HAART ± chemotherapy. Novel: maribavir (Livtencity, UL97 kinase inhibitor) FDA-approved 2021 for refractory CMV in transplant. 2024: Maribavir expanding real-world use; letermovir prophylaxis post-SOT trials ongoing.
us_prevalence: Herpesvirus family infections affect >90% of US adults (seroprevalence); clinically significant disease: CMV in ~50K transplant patients, HHV-6 encephalitis ~1,000 HSCT patients/yr, EBV-PTLD ~2,000 patients/yr.
patients_needing_tx: ~50,000–70,000 patients/yr with clinically significant non-HSV/VZV herpesvirus disease need targeted antivirals.
price: $20,000–$80,000/yr for antiviral prophylaxis/treatment (comparable to letermovir ~$35K/yr, maribavir ~$40K/course).
peak_sales: $300–600M for a novel pan-herpesvirus agent (maribavir expanded indication + HHV-6 coverage at 20–30% share).
registrational_endpoint: CMV viremia clearance by Week 8; or HHV-6/EBV viremia clearance; transplant survival composite endpoint.
btd_threshold_vs_soc: CMV clearance ≥55% at Week 8 vs. ~44% for investigator-initiated therapy (as shown in SOLSTICE trial for maribavir); or HHV-6 clearance ≥70% vs. ~40% for foscarnet.

disease: Cancers Common in Patients With HIV Infection
symptoms: Lymphoma, Kaposi sarcoma lesions, cervical cancer, weight loss
treatment_goal: Treat AIDS-defining malignancy, optimize antiretroviral therapy
first_line_soc: Antiretroviral therapy (ART) optimization is foundational. AIDS-defining cancers: KS — liposomal doxorubicin (Doxil) ± paclitaxel; NHL — R-CHOP (rituximab-cyclophosphamide-doxorubicin-vincristine-prednisone); cervical cancer — standard oncology SoC (surgery, radiation, cisplatin).
treatment_algorithm: Kaposi sarcoma: ART alone for early disease; advanced disease: liposomal doxorubicin (Doxil) 20 mg/m² q3w or paclitaxel; pomalidomide (Pomalyst) FDA-approved 2020 for KS. Primary CNS lymphoma: high-dose methotrexate ± rituximab ± WBRT + ART. Systemic AIDS-related NHL: R-CHOP or DA-EPOCH-R (superior in HTLV-associated). Cervical cancer: cisplatin-based chemoradiotherapy ± pembrolizumab. Anal cancer: 5-FU/mitomycin + radiation. 2024: Immune checkpoint inhibitors (nivolumab, pembrolizumab) showing activity in HIV+ patients with viral suppression.
us_prevalence: ~1.2 million HIV+ in US; ~15,000 AIDS-defining cancers/yr (KS ~2,000, NHL ~6,000, cervical ~1,500) plus non-AIDS-defining cancers (lung, liver, anal). (CDC HIV surveillance 2024)
patients_needing_tx: ~15,000–20,000 HIV+ patients/yr needing cancer-specific pharmacologic treatment beyond ART.
price: $50,000–$200,000/yr (oncology + ART combination pricing); KS novel agent $100,000/yr; checkpoint inhibitor $150,000/yr.
peak_sales: $200–500M for novel HIV-specific oncology agent (e.g., expanded pomalidomide indication or HIV-specific checkpoint inhibitor).
registrational_endpoint: Objective response rate (ORR) for KS/lymphoma; progression-free survival (PFS) for lymphoma; overall survival (OS) for cervical cancer. FDA oncology endpoints apply.
btd_threshold_vs_soc: ORR ≥60% for KS vs. ~50% for liposomal doxorubicin; or PFS improvement of ≥3 months median for AIDS-NHL vs. R-CHOP.

symptoms: Oral ulcers, rash on hands/feet, fever, sore throat in children
treatment_goal: Supportive care; prevent severe neurological complications in EV-A71
first_line_soc: No antiviral pharmacologic SoC; supportive care (hydration, antipyretics, topical analgesics) is standard. No FDA-approved antiviral for HFMD.
treatment_algorithm: Supportive: hydration, acetaminophen/ibuprofen, viscous lidocaine (off-label) for oral ulcer pain. For EV-A71 severe neurological complications (brainstem encephalitis, pulmonary edema): IV immunoglobulin (IVIG) off-label, milrinone for autonomic dysfunction. EV-A71 vaccines (approved in China, not US): Sinovac/Chendu bivalent vaccines. 2024–2025: No FDA-approved EV-A71 vaccine or antiviral. Pocapavir and other enterovirus inhibitors studied but no approval.
us_prevalence: ~3–5 million US HFMD cases/yr (endemic in children <5 years); mostly mild and self-limiting. (CDC HFMD tracking)
patients_needing_tx: ~200,000–500,000 children with moderate disease need symptomatic treatment; <1,000 severe neurological/cardiorespiratory cases need IVIG/ICU care.
price: $500–$1,500 per course (oral antiviral for HFMD, pediatric formulation); or EV-A71 vaccine $50–$100/dose.
peak_sales: $100–200M for novel HFMD antiviral at 20% penetration in moderate/severe pediatric cases; EV-A71 vaccine $150–300M if FDA-approved.
registrational_endpoint: Time to resolution of oral lesions and fever (clinical cure at Day 5); or prevention of severe EV-A71 complications (for vaccine: attack rate reduction).
btd_threshold_vs_soc: Fever resolution by Day 2 vs. Day 3–4 for supportive care; or ≥80% reduction in severe EV-A71 neurological complications vs. placebo.

symptoms: Progressive muscle weakness, fatigue, pain, years after initial polio infection
treatment_goal: Manage symptoms, maintain function, slow decline; no curative pharmacologic treatment
first_line_soc: No pharmacologic SoC. Symptomatic management: physical/occupational therapy, energy conservation, non-fatiguing exercise, assistive devices. NSAIDs/acetaminophen for pain. Pyridostigmine investigated but no proven benefit in RCTs.
treatment_algorithm: Physical/occupational therapy is mainstay — low-intensity exercise, breathing exercises, weight management. Pain: NSAIDs, tramadol for neuropathic pain, low-dose amitriptyline. Pyridostigmine (acetylcholinesterase inhibitor) studied; one trial showed modest benefit in muscle strength but not replicated. IVIG (intravenous immunoglobulin): small studies showed reduction in pain/fatigue but no Phase 3 data. Novel neurotrophic approaches (CNTF, IGF-1) in exploratory research. 2024: No pharmacologic SoC established.
us_prevalence: ~300,000–500,000 US postpolio survivors with post-polio syndrome (PPS). (PPS Research Foundation; estimated 25–40% of ~1M polio survivors).
patients_needing_tx: ~300,000 US patients with PPS; most managed non-pharmacologically; ~50,000 with severe fatigue/pain may benefit from pharmacologic intervention.
price: $5,000–$30,000/yr (novel neuroprotective agent for PPS maintenance).
peak_sales: $100–250M for novel neuroprotective/symptomatic agent proven in Phase 3 PPS trial at 20–30% severe-PPS market share.
registrational_endpoint: Isometric muscle strength (dynamometry) change at Month 12; fatigue score (FSS — Fatigue Severity Scale) change from baseline at Month 12; pain NRS.
btd_threshold_vs_soc: FSS improvement ≥2 points at Month 12 vs. no change for placebo; or muscle strength preservation (no decline) in 80% vs. ~50% for placebo over 12 months.

symptoms: Fever, fatigue, retinitis, colitis, pneumonitis (in immunocompromised)
treatment_goal: Suppress CMV replication, preserve vision, prevent graft failure
first_line_soc: Valganciclovir (Valcyte, nucleoside analog, ganciclovir prodrug) 900 mg BID × 3 weeks (induction) then 900 mg daily (maintenance); IV ganciclovir for severe disease.
treatment_algorithm: Retinitis/severe: IV ganciclovir 5 mg/kg q12h × 21 days, then oral valganciclovir maintenance 900 mg/day. Alternative: IV foscarnet (Foscavir) for ganciclovir-resistant CMV. IV cidofovir every 2 weeks for resistant cases (nephrotoxic). Maribavir (Livtencity, kinase inhibitor) FDA-approved Oct 2021 for CMV infections refractory/resistant to ganciclovir/foscarnet/cidofovir in transplant recipients. Letermovir (Prevymis) — primary prophylaxis post-allogeneic HCT, FDA-approved 2017. 2024–2025: Maribavir expanding use; letermovir prophylaxis data in solid organ transplant (Merck).
us_prevalence: ~5,000–8,000 clinically significant CMV infections/yr in transplant recipients + HIV+ patients; CMV seropositive prevalence 40–80% US adults. (ASBMT/ASTS CMV surveillance 2024)
patients_needing_tx: ~8,000–10,000 transplant + HIV+ patients/yr require antiviral treatment for active CMV disease.
price: $40,000–$80,000 per course (novel antiviral for resistant/refractory CMV); maribavir ~$48,000 per 8-week course.
peak_sales: Maribavir (Livtencity): $200–400M peak sales projected. Novel prophylaxis agent: $200–500M. Total CMV market ~$600–800M.
registrational_endpoint: CMV viremia clearance (PCR <LLOQ) at Week 8; all-cause mortality or treatment failure (primary endpoint for refractory/resistant CMV per FDA guidance).
btd_threshold_vs_soc: CMV clearance ≥55% at Week 8 vs. ~25% for investigator-assigned therapy (maribavir trial SOLSTICE result: 55.7% vs. 23.9%) — established threshold achieved.

symptoms: Hand-foot-mouth sores, meningitis, conjunctivitis, flaccid paralysis, myocarditis
treatment_goal: Supportive care; prevent severe complications (myocarditis, paralysis)
first_line_soc: No approved antiviral for enteroviruses. Supportive care. IVIG used for neonatal/immunocompromised severe enteroviral disease.
treatment_algorithm: Supportive care for most patients. Severe neonatal enteroviral sepsis: high-dose IVIG (controversial benefit). Immunocompromised with chronic enteroviral encephalitis: IVIG. Novel antivirals: pleconaril (failed FDA) targeting capsid; pocapavir (V-073, capsid inhibitor) — Phase 2 for poliovirus and enterovirus post-transplant; vapendavir (BTA798) in Phase 2. EV-D68 (acute flaccid myelitis): IVIG ± methylprednisolone studied per 2024 CDC AFM guidance — uncertain benefit; no approved treatment. 2024–2025: EV-D68 AFM continues to cause paralysis in children; unmet need for antiviral.
us_prevalence: ~30–50 million US enteroviral illnesses/yr; ~10,000 aseptic meningitis cases; ~100–200 EV-D68 AFM cases in outbreak years. (CDC enterovirus surveillance 2024)
patients_needing_tx: ~5,000–10,000 patients/yr with severe complications need treatment (immunocompromised, neonates, AFM); most are self-limiting.
price: $5,000–$20,000 per course (novel capsid inhibitor for severe enteroviral disease, especially AFM); per-course price.
peak_sales: $50–150M for first approved enterovirus antiviral targeting EV-D68/EV-71 AFM/severe disease at 30–50% share.
registrational_endpoint: For AFM: motor function recovery (modified Rankin Scale 0–2) at 6 months + MRI lesion improvement; for neonatal enteroviral sepsis: 28-day survival.
btd_threshold_vs_soc: AFM full motor recovery ≥50% at 6 months vs. ~20–30% historical without treatment; or EV-D68 viral clearance by Day 5 vs. no clearance with supportive care.

symptoms: Extreme fatigue, sore throat, swollen lymph nodes, fever, enlarged spleen
treatment_goal: Supportive care; prevent splenic rupture, manage complications
first_line_soc: No pharmacologic SoC; supportive care with rest, hydration, NSAIDs/acetaminophen for fever/pain. Corticosteroids (prednisone) reserved for severe complications (airway compromise, thrombocytopenia, hemolytic anemia).
treatment_algorithm: Supportive: rest (avoid contact sports ≥3–4 weeks to prevent splenic rupture), antipyretics, hydration. Avoid amoxicillin/ampicillin (causes maculopapular rash ~80%). Antivirals (acyclovir, ganciclovir) reduce viral shedding but do NOT shorten clinical illness — not recommended. Corticosteroids: prednisone 40–60 mg/day taper × 5–7 days for severe pharyngotonsillitis with airway obstruction. IV dexamethasone for impending airway emergency. EBV-PTLD: rituximab ± reduction of immunosuppression. 2024: No new antiviral EBV-specific therapies approved.
us_prevalence: ~500,000 new US infectious mononucleosis cases/yr (EBV primary infection; peaks age 15–24). (CDC EBV data)
patients_needing_tx: ~50,000–100,000 patients/yr with severe IM need corticosteroids or other pharmacologic intervention; vast majority managed supportively.
price: $500–$2,000 per course (EBV-specific antiviral or immunomodulator for severe IM/PTLD).
peak_sales: $50–150M for EBV-specific antiviral with proven efficacy for severe IM; up to $500M if also indicated for EBV-PTLD (transplant market).
registrational_endpoint: Time to resolution of severe pharyngitis and fever (Days to symptom resolution); return to normal activity; no requirement for corticosteroids by Day 7.
btd_threshold_vs_soc: Time to fever resolution ≤3 days vs. ~7–14 days for supportive care; or avoidance of corticosteroids in ≥70% of patients with severe pharyngitis vs. ~50% for supportive alone.

symptoms: Sudden fever, painful oral blisters, sore throat, difficulty swallowing in children
treatment_goal: Supportive care; maintain hydration, relieve oral pain
first_line_soc: No pharmacologic SoC; supportive care with analgesics (acetaminophen/ibuprofen), topical anesthetics (viscous lidocaine off-label), and hydration.
treatment_algorithm: Supportive: antipyretics, hydration (oral or IV if unable to swallow), viscous lidocaine or 'magic mouthwash' for pain. Self-limiting in 7–10 days. No antiviral approved. Distinction from HFMD: herpangina lesions confined to posterior oropharynx; caused by Coxsackie A viruses and other enteroviruses. 2024: No new therapeutic agents.
us_prevalence: ~1–2 million US cases/yr (mainly children; peaks in summer). Often unreported.
patients_needing_tx: ~100,000–200,000 children need medical attention for hydration support; most self-managed at home.
price: $200–$500 per course (pediatric oral antiviral formulation).
peak_sales: $10–20M for a novel enterovirus antiviral with herpangina indication (small submarket of broader enterovirus antiviral).
registrational_endpoint: Time to resolution of oral lesions and fever (Day 5 clinical cure); reduction in pain score (VAS) at 24–48 hours.
btd_threshold_vs_soc: Lesion resolution by Day 3 vs. Day 5–7 for supportive care alone; or fever resolution <24 hours vs. ~48–72 hours.

symptoms: Sudden severe chest/abdominal muscle pain, fever, brief spasms (Bornholm disease)
treatment_goal: Symptomatic relief; self-limiting illness (NSAIDs, rest)
first_line_soc: None — supportive only. NSAIDs and analgesics for pain relief; self-limiting.
treatment_algorithm: Supportive: NSAIDs, acetaminophen, rest, heat application to chest/abdomen. Caused by Coxsackie B virus (enterovirus); no antiviral approved. Most resolve in days to 2 weeks. 2024: No pharmacologic treatment advances.
us_prevalence: ~1,000–5,000 US cases/yr (often during summer enterovirus outbreaks); actual incidence likely underreported. (Literature estimate)
patients_needing_tx: All cases require symptomatic treatment only; no pharmacologic antivirals needed.
price: $500–$2,000 per course (if enterovirus antiviral were approved; per-course price).
tam_gross: 5,000 × $1,000 = $5M (if antiviral approved — currently no SoC pharmacotherapy).
peak_sales: $1–3M, subsumed within broader enteroviral antiviral market.
registrational_endpoint: Pain VAS resolution at Day 5; fever resolution within 72 hours of treatment.
btd_threshold_vs_soc: Pain resolution by Day 3 vs. ~Day 7 for NSAIDs alone; fever elimination within 24 hours.

symptoms: Fever, headache, stiff neck, photophobia, nausea, aseptic meningitis
treatment_goal: Supportive care; manage meningitis symptoms, prevent complications in immunocompromised
first_line_soc: No FDA-approved antiviral for LCMV. Supportive care (analgesics, corticosteroids for severe meningitis). Ribavirin used off-label in severe/transplant-associated cases.
treatment_algorithm: Immunocompetent: self-limiting; supportive care (NSAIDs, hydration, rest). Severe neurological disease: corticosteroids. Transplant-associated LCMV (donor-derived, rare): ribavirin IV/oral off-label + reduction of immunosuppression; very high mortality in transplant. 2024: No new antivirals for LCMV. Prevention: rodent (hamster/mouse) avoidance.
us_prevalence: ~5,000 estimated US LCMV infections/yr (mostly unreported/mild); <10 transplant-associated severe cases/yr. (CDC)
patients_needing_tx: ~100–500 US patients/yr with severe/symptomatic LCMV meningitis need medical management; <10 transplant cases need ribavirin.
price: $5,000–$15,000 per course (antiviral treatment for severe LCMV).
peak_sales: <$5M; no commercial development interest; public health focus.
registrational_endpoint: Time to CSF pleocytosis resolution; fever resolution; modified Rankin Scale at Day 30 for neurological cases.
btd_threshold_vs_soc: Neurological recovery (mRS ≤1) at Day 30 in ≥80% vs. ~60% supportive care; or transplant LCMV survival ≥50% vs. ~25% historical.

symptoms: Fever, limb weakness, paralysis, respiratory failure (bulbar/spinal polio), meningitis
treatment_goal: Supportive care; prevent respiratory failure; no antiviral curative treatment
first_line_soc: No antiviral pharmacologic SoC. Supportive care: respiratory support (mechanical ventilation), physiotherapy, pain management. Prevention: IPV (inactivated poliovirus vaccine) or OPV.
treatment_algorithm: Supportive: bed rest, pain management, respiratory support (iron lung historically; now mechanical ventilation). Physical/occupational therapy for paralysis rehabilitation. No antiviral approved for polio treatment. Pocapavir (an anti-enteroviral capsid inhibitor) showed antiviral activity against poliovirus in early trials but not advanced for treatment. Vaccine-derived poliovirus (VDPV) outbreaks: IPV used to boost immunity. 2024–2025: Poliovirus detected in wastewater NYC 2022; vaccination campaigns; nOPV2 for circulating type 2 VDPV outbreaks.
us_prevalence: Wild poliovirus: 0 US cases since 1979 (last imported 1993). VDPV: occasional cases (~1–3/yr). Global: near eradication (6 WPV1 cases in 2021). (GPEI data 2024)
patients_needing_tx: <5 US paralytic polio cases/yr; all supported with ICU/respiratory care.
price: $5,000–$20,000 per course for antiviral active against poliovirus (biodefense/eradication context).
peak_sales: <$5M US; primary global public health value for outbreak control (pocapavir-type agent for VDPV outbreaks).
registrational_endpoint: Viral load reduction in stool (PCR) by Day 14; virological cure (stool negative by Day 28) for VDPV; prevention of paralytic disease as primary for antiviral.
btd_threshold_vs_soc: VDPV stool clearance ≥80% at Day 14 vs. ~30% spontaneous clearance; prevention of paralytic progression in vaccine-immunocompromised patients ≥90%.

symptoms: Silent bone loss; fragility fractures (hip, vertebral, wrist); low T-score on DXA scan
treatment_goal: Increase bone mineral density; prevent fragility fractures; reduce fracture-related mortality
first_line_soc: Bisphosphonates — alendronate (Fosamax) 70 mg/week PO or zoledronate (Reclast) 5 mg IV annually — are first-line pharmacologic therapy.
treatment_algorithm: Calcium + vitamin D (foundational). Bisphosphonates: alendronate, risedronate, ibandronate (oral); zoledronate IV. RANK-L inhibitor: denosumab (Prolia) 60 mg SC every 6 months — highly effective. Anabolic agents: teriparatide (Forteo, PTH 1-34) 20 mcg/day SC; abaloparatide (Tymlos). Romosozumab (Evenity, anti-sclerostin) — 12-month anabolic then anti-resorptive. Raloxifene (SERM) for postmenopausal women. High fracture risk: romosozumab → zoledronate (FRAME trial). 2024: sequential therapy algorithms widely adopted.
us_prevalence: ~10–12 million US adults with osteoporosis; ~43 million with low bone mass. NOF 2025 data.
patients_needing_tx: ~5–6 million patients currently on pharmacologic therapy; 50–60% undertreated.
price: $1,500–$5,000/year (generic alendronate); $3,000–$15,000/year (branded); romosozumab ~$20,000–$25,000/year.
peak_sales: Romosozumab (Evenity): ~$1B annual sales 2024. Novel next-gen anabolic: ~$2–3B peak sales.
registrational_endpoint: Change in BMD at lumbar spine and total hip at 24 months; new vertebral fracture rate; hip fracture rate.
btd_threshold_vs_soc: ≥10% BMD increase at lumbar spine; vertebral fracture risk reduction ≥50% vs. zoledronate at 24 months.

symptoms: Bone pain, deformity (bowing of tibia), fractures; elevated alkaline phosphatase; focal bone overgrowth
treatment_goal: Suppress osteoclast activity; normalize ALP; relieve pain; prevent deformity
first_line_soc: Zoledronate (Reclast) 5 mg IV single infusion is the most effective bisphosphonate first-line agent; alendronate oral as alternative.
treatment_algorithm: Zoledronate (Reclast) 5 mg IV × 1 infusion: most potent; normalizes ALP in ~90% for years. Oral bisphosphonates: risedronate 30 mg/day × 2 months; alendronate 40 mg/day × 6 months. Calcitonin (salmon): less effective, now rarely used. NSAIDs for bone pain. Calcium + vitamin D supplementation. Orthopedic surgery for fractures or deformity correction.
us_prevalence: ~1–3 million US adults; predominantly White males >50. Declining incidence trend noted since 1990s.
patients_needing_tx: ~400,000–600,000 patients requiring pharmacologic treatment.
peak_sales: Zoledronate generic dominant. Novel RANK-L inhibitor or anti-sclerostin for Paget: ~$100–200M peak sales.
registrational_endpoint: Serum alkaline phosphatase normalization rate at 6 months; ALP reduction from baseline; bone scan improvement.
btd_threshold_vs_soc: ALP normalization in ≥90% at 6 months; duration of remission >5 years vs. ~3–5 years with zoledronate.

symptoms: Deep joint pain (femoral head most common), collapse of bone, progressive articular destruction
treatment_goal: Preserve femoral head; prevent collapse; relieve pain
first_line_soc: Core decompression (surgical) for pre-collapse stage. Bisphosphonates (alendronate, zoledronate) reduce osteoclast activity and may prevent progression.
treatment_algorithm: Non-collapsed (Ficat I–II): core decompression ± bone grafting. Bisphosphonates: alendronate 70 mg/week or zoledronate 5 mg IV annual — reduce collapse risk. Iloprost (prostacyclin analogue) — IV: Phase 3 data in Europe showing pain relief and potential disease modification. Hyperbaric oxygen. NSAIDs for pain management. Collapsed (Ficat III–IV): total hip arthroplasty. Statin therapy to reduce fat emboli (secondary prevention). Anticoagulation for hypercoagulable-related ON.
