us_prevalence: ~20,000 new cases/year; ~100,000 prevalent; 10,000–20,000 hip replacements/year for ON. NIAMS data.
patients_needing_tx: ~50,000 patients requiring pharmacologic management.
price: $10,000–$50,000/year for a novel anabolic bone-building agent.
peak_sales: ~$150–250M peak sales for a novel bone-protective agent preventing femoral head collapse.
registrational_endpoint: Collapse-free survival at 24 months; MRI lesion volume change at 12 months; VAS hip pain.
btd_threshold_vs_soc: Collapse-free rate ≥70% at 24 months vs. ~50% for core decompression alone; MRI volume reduction ≥30%.

symptoms: Widespread musculoskeletal pain, fatigue, sleep disturbance, cognitive dysfunction ('fibro fog')
treatment_goal: Reduce pain; improve sleep; restore function and quality of life
first_line_soc: Duloxetine (Cymbalta) SNRI — FDA-approved; or pregabalin (Lyrica) — FDA-approved; or milnacipran (Savella) — FDA-approved. Aerobic exercise combined with pharmacotherapy.
treatment_algorithm: FDA-approved agents: duloxetine (Cymbalta) 60 mg/day; pregabalin (Lyrica) 300–450 mg/day; milnacipran (Savella) 100 mg BID. Low-dose tricyclics (amitriptyline 10–25 mg QHS) for sleep/pain. Cyclobenzaprine for muscle pain/sleep. Tramadol for refractory pain. Cognitive behavioral therapy (CBT). Aerobic exercise. Avoid opioids (not effective, worsen central sensitization). Sleep hygiene. Multidisciplinary pain management. 2024 research: low-dose naltrexone showing promise (Phase 3 trials ongoing).
us_prevalence: ~10–12 million US patients. NFA prevalence data 2024; 2–3% of US adults.
patients_needing_tx: ~6–8 million patients requiring pharmacologic treatment.
price: $8,000–$25,000/year for novel central sensitization agent.
peak_sales: Highly competitive vs. generic duloxetine/pregabalin. Novel mechanism (e.g., low-dose naltrexone, TrkA inhibitor): ~$500M–1.5B peak sales.
registrational_endpoint: Brief Pain Inventory (BPI) average pain severity at 12 weeks; Patient Global Impression of Change (PGIC); Fibromyalgia Impact Questionnaire (FIQ).
btd_threshold_vs_soc: ≥30% BPI pain reduction; PGIC 'much improved' ≥50% responders vs. ~20% placebo at 12 weeks.

symptoms: Localized tendon pain, swelling, stiffness; worsened with activity; common at shoulder, Achilles, wrist
treatment_goal: Reduce inflammation; relieve pain; restore tendon function
first_line_soc: Rest, ice, NSAIDs (ibuprofen or naproxen). Eccentric exercises. Corticosteroid injection for persistent cases.
treatment_algorithm: Conservative: activity modification, ice, NSAIDs. Eccentric loading exercises (key for Achilles, patellar tendinopathy). Corticosteroid injections (short-term benefit; avoid in Achilles due to rupture risk). Physical therapy. Platelet-rich plasma (PRP) injections — evidence emerging. Extracorporeal shockwave therapy (ESWT). Topical NSAIDs (diclofenac gel). Surgical tendon debridement for refractory cases. Tenosynovitis: antibiotics for infectious cause (dicloxacillin for Staph).
us_prevalence: ~25–30 million US cases/year; rotator cuff tendinitis, Achilles tendinopathy, and De Quervain syndrome most common.
patients_needing_tx: ~10–15 million patients requiring pharmacologic/injection therapy.
price: $3,000–$10,000/course for novel biologic (e.g., anti-IL-17 for enthesitis; PRP-derived growth factor).
peak_sales: Fragmented; generic NSAIDs dominate. Novel tendon-targeted biologic or growth factor: ~$300–600M peak sales.
registrational_endpoint: VISA-A score (Achilles) or DASH score (upper extremity) at 12 weeks; VAS pain; recurrence at 6 months.
btd_threshold_vs_soc: VISA-A improvement ≥20 points; VAS reduction ≥30 mm vs. eccentric exercise + placebo injection at 12 weeks.

symptoms: Posterior knee swelling, stiffness, pain; fluid-filled popliteal cyst
treatment_goal: Reduce cyst size; treat underlying joint pathology; relieve pain
first_line_soc: Treat underlying cause (OA, RA, meniscal tear). NSAIDs (ibuprofen, naproxen) for pain/inflammation. Intra-articular corticosteroid injection for rapid relief.
treatment_algorithm: Treat underlying intra-articular pathology: meniscal repair, OA management, RA DMARDs. NSAIDs for symptomatic relief. Intra-articular corticosteroid injection (reduces cyst volume by reducing joint effusion). Aspiration of the cyst with or without steroid injection. Physical therapy. Surgical excision for refractory large cysts. Hyaluronic acid injection for OA-related cysts.
us_prevalence: ~5–10 million US adults with Baker cysts; mostly secondary to OA or meniscal pathology.
patients_needing_tx: ~2–3 million patients seeking pharmacologic or injection therapy annually.
price: $500–$5,000/injection or $2,000–$10,000/year for novel intra-articular agent.
peak_sales: Treated with generics and procedures; novel intra-articular agent: ~$150–300M peak sales.
registrational_endpoint: Cyst volume reduction (ultrasound) at 12 weeks; VAS pain score; recurrence at 6 months.
btd_threshold_vs_soc: ≥50% cyst volume reduction; VAS pain reduction ≥30 mm at 12 weeks vs. placebo injection.

symptoms: Localized joint pain, tenderness, swelling over bursae; worsened with movement
treatment_goal: Reduce inflammation; relieve pain; restore range of motion
first_line_soc: Rest, ice, NSAIDs (ibuprofen 400–600 mg TID). Corticosteroid injection for refractory cases.
treatment_algorithm: RICE (rest, ice, compression, elevation). NSAIDs: ibuprofen, naproxen. Corticosteroid injection (methylprednisolone or triamcinolone) into bursa for persistent cases. Aspiration for septic or large bursa. IV/oral antibiotics (dicloxacillin, MRSA: TMP-SMX or vancomycin) for septic bursitis. Physical therapy. Surgery (bursectomy) for refractory chronic bursitis.
us_prevalence: ~8–10 million US cases/year across all types; olecranon and prepatellar most common.
patients_needing_tx: ~5–6 million patients requiring pharmacologic therapy (NSAIDs or injections).
price: $500–$5,000/injection course.
peak_sales: Generic-dominated market. Novel long-acting corticosteroid microsphere: ~$100–200M peak sales.
registrational_endpoint: VAS pain score at 4 weeks; patient global assessment; time to return to activity.
btd_threshold_vs_soc: VAS pain reduction ≥40 mm vs. placebo injection; recurrence rate <15% at 3 months.

symptoms: Inferior heel pain worst with first morning steps; tenderness at calcaneal insertion
treatment_goal: Reduce plantar fascial inflammation; restore pain-free ambulation
first_line_soc: Stretching exercises (Achilles/plantar fascia), heel cup, NSAIDs. Corticosteroid injection for persistent cases.
treatment_algorithm: Stretching: plantar fascia-specific stretches + calf stretching. Heel cup or custom orthotics. NSAIDs (naproxen, ibuprofen). Night splints. Corticosteroid injection (landmark-guided or US-guided): effective for 1–3 months. PRP injection: TOPAZ RCT (2024) showed superiority to corticosteroid at 6 months. ESWT: FDA-cleared for chronic plantar fasciitis. Physical therapy. Walking boot for severe refractory. Surgery (plantar fasciotomy) <5% need surgery.
us_prevalence: ~3–5 million new cases/year; ~10–15 million prevalent US patients. Most common cause of heel pain.
patients_needing_tx: ~8–10 million patients seeking treatment; ~3–4 million require injection therapy.
price: $3,000–$15,000/injection course; $500–$2,000/year for systemic analgesics.
peak_sales: Highly competitive; generic injections dominate. Novel single-injection biological cure: ~$300–600M peak sales.
registrational_endpoint: VAS heel pain at 12 weeks; Foot Function Index; first-step pain score.
btd_threshold_vs_soc: VAS reduction ≥40 mm; pain-free with first steps in ≥60% at 12 weeks vs. corticosteroid injection.

symptoms: Posterior heel and mid-Achilles pain, morning stiffness, tenderness on palpation
treatment_goal: Restore tendon loading capacity; reduce pain; prevent rupture
first_line_soc: Eccentric heel drop exercises (Alfredson protocol) are first-line. NSAIDs for acute pain phase.
treatment_algorithm: Eccentric exercise program (12-week Alfredson protocol): most effective conservative treatment. NSAIDs short-term. Corticosteroid injection — use with caution (rupture risk). PRP injection — growing evidence (TOPAZ trial positive data 2024). ESWT for chronic insertional tendinopathy. Topical glyceryl trinitrate patches (evidence mixed). Night splints. Surgery (debridement) for refractory cases. Autologous blood injection as alternative to PRP.
us_prevalence: ~2–3 million US patients; incidence ~2.35/1,000/year. Primarily athletic and middle-aged adults.
patients_needing_tx: ~1.5–2 million patients seeking pharmacologic or injection therapy.
price: $3,000–$15,000/course (injection therapy) or $10,000–$30,000/year (systemic biologic).
peak_sales: Primarily procedure/exercise-based. Novel tendon-healing biologic: ~$200–400M peak sales.
registrational_endpoint: VISA-A score at 12 weeks; VAS pain with activity; tendon thickness by ultrasound at 12 weeks.
btd_threshold_vs_soc: VISA-A improvement ≥20 points vs. eccentric exercise; pain reduction ≥30 mm VAS at 12 weeks.

symptoms: Medial ankle/arch pain, progressive flat foot deformity, difficulty standing on tiptoe
treatment_goal: Halt tendon degeneration; restore arch support; prevent flatfoot progression
first_line_soc: Custom orthotics (medial arch support), NSAIDs, physical therapy with eccentric strengthening.
treatment_algorithm: Medial arch support orthotic or AFO (ankle-foot orthosis). NSAIDs. Eccentric tibialis posterior strengthening. Corticosteroid injection — limited to peritendinous sheath (avoid intratendinous). PRP injection for degeneration. Walking boot for acute inflammatory phase. Surgery (flexor digitorum longus transfer + osteotomy) for Stage III/IV flatfoot.
us_prevalence: ~3–5 million US adults with tibialis posterior tendon dysfunction; most common cause of adult acquired flatfoot.
patients_needing_tx: ~1.5–2 million patients requiring pharmacologic/injection therapy.
peak_sales: ~$100–200M peak sales for novel tendon-healing or anti-fibrotic injection.
registrational_endpoint: FAOS (Foot and Ankle Outcome Score); VAS pain; subtalar single-heel rise test at 12 weeks.
btd_threshold_vs_soc: FAOS improvement ≥15 points; VAS reduction ≥30 mm; heel-rise ability in ≥60% at 12 weeks.

symptoms: Bony prominence at first MTP joint, pain with footwear, hallux valgus deformity
treatment_goal: Relieve pain; correct alignment; prevent deformity progression
first_line_soc: Wide toe-box shoes, bunion pads, orthotics. NSAIDs for pain. No pharmacologic disease-modifying therapy.
treatment_algorithm: Conservative: wide-toe footwear, bunion splints, orthotics, NSAIDs. Corticosteroid injection for associated bursitis. Surgery (bunionectomy/osteotomy) for deformity correction — only definitive treatment. Physical therapy. No pharmacologic agent modifies hallux valgus progression.
us_prevalence: ~64–67 million US adults with bunions; ~30% of adults. AAOS podiatry data.
patients_needing_tx: ~5–8 million patients seeking pharmacologic pain management; ~300,000 surgeries/year.
price: $1,000–$5,000/year for pharmacologic pain management.
peak_sales: Predominantly surgical/orthotic condition. Novel intra-articular anti-inflammatory: ~$100–200M peak sales.
registrational_endpoint: VAS pain; Manchester-Oxford Foot Questionnaire (MOXFQ); hallux valgus angle reduction at 6 months.
btd_threshold_vs_soc: VAS reduction ≥30 mm; MOXFQ improvement ≥15 points vs. footwear alone at 6 months.

symptoms: Insertional Achilles pain at calcaneus, bone spurs, morning stiffness, pain with first steps
treatment_goal: Reduce entheseal inflammation; restore pain-free ambulation
first_line_soc: NSAIDs, heel wedge insoles, stretching, ESWT. Corticosteroid injection avoided at insertion site (rupture risk).
treatment_algorithm: Conservative: activity modification, heel wedge, stretching program. NSAIDs (naproxen, ibuprofen). ESWT (shockwave therapy) — superior to placebo for insertional enthesopathy. Topical diclofenac. PRP injection at enthesis. Intratendinous injection of polidocanol (sclerosant) for neovascularization. Surgery (calcaneal exostectomy) for refractory Haglund deformity. Anti-TNF therapy (adalimumab, etanercept) for enthesitis in spondyloarthritis.
us_prevalence: ~1–2 million US patients; common in runners, ankylosing spondylitis, and obese patients.
patients_needing_tx: ~800,000 patients requiring pharmacologic or injection treatment.
peak_sales: ~$100–200M peak sales for novel enthesis-targeted drug.
registrational_endpoint: VISA-A score; VAS pain; enthesis thickness by MRI at 12 weeks.
btd_threshold_vs_soc: VISA-A improvement ≥20 points; VAS reduction ≥30 mm at 12 weeks vs. NSAID + ESWT.

symptoms: Pain and swelling at ball of foot; stiffness in toe joints; hallux rigidus or synovitis
treatment_goal: Reduce synovitis; preserve joint function; relieve pain
first_line_soc: NSAIDs (ibuprofen, naproxen). Intra-articular corticosteroid injection for synovitis.
treatment_algorithm: NSAIDs. Intra-articular corticosteroid injection. Orthotics/metatarsal bar. Footwear modification. Hyaluronic acid injection for hallux rigidus (off-label). Cheilectomy (bone spur removal) for hallux rigidus. Fusion for end-stage hallux rigidus. RA-associated MTP synovitis: DMARDs (methotrexate, biologics).
us_prevalence: ~5–8 million US patients with symptomatic MTP joint pain.
patients_needing_tx: ~2–3 million patients seeking pharmacologic or injection treatment.
peak_sales: ~$100–200M peak sales for novel intra-articular agent.
registrational_endpoint: VAS pain; AOFAS forefoot score; MTP joint range of motion at 12 weeks.
btd_threshold_vs_soc: VAS reduction ≥30 mm; AOFAS improvement ≥15 points vs. NSAIDs alone at 12 weeks.

symptoms: Forefoot pain under metatarsal heads; worsened with walking and standing
treatment_goal: Redistribute forefoot pressure; reduce metatarsal head pain; maintain activity
first_line_soc: Metatarsal pad/orthotic, wide-toe shoes, NSAIDs for pain.
treatment_algorithm: Metatarsal dome pad (redistributes pressure). Wide-toe-box shoes with extra depth. NSAIDs (ibuprofen, naproxen). Corticosteroid injection for associated capsulitis or bursitis. Physical therapy. Weight reduction. Surgery (Weil osteotomy) for structural deformity.
us_prevalence: ~15–20 million US adults with metatarsalgia; highly prevalent in runners and older adults.
patients_needing_tx: ~3–5 million patients requiring pharmacologic or injection therapy.
peak_sales: Generic-dominated; novel intra-articular treatment: ~$100–200M peak sales.
registrational_endpoint: VAS forefoot pain; MOXFQ score; step count/activity at 12 weeks.
btd_threshold_vs_soc: VAS reduction ≥30 mm; MOXFQ improvement ≥15 points vs. orthotics alone at 12 weeks.

symptoms: Curled/bent toe deformity, corns, pain with footwear, reduced toe extension
treatment_goal: Relieve pain; prevent fixed deformity; correct toe alignment
first_line_soc: Wide toe-box shoes, toe pads, metatarsal insoles, NSAIDs for associated pain. Corticosteroid injection for MTP synovitis.
treatment_algorithm: Conservative: footwear modification, toe splints, metatarsal pads. NSAIDs. Corticosteroid injection for associated MTP synovitis. Toe exercises for flexible hammer toe. Surgery (flexor-extensor tendon transfer, proximal interphalangeal joint fusion) for fixed deformity. No pharmacologic disease-modifying agent.
us_prevalence: ~7–10 million US adults with hammer toes requiring treatment; prevalence increases with age.
patients_needing_tx: ~3–4 million patients seeking pharmacologic or injection-based management.
peak_sales: Surgical/orthotic condition primarily. Pharmacologic peak sales ~$50–100M.
registrational_endpoint: VAS pain; Manchester-Oxford Foot Questionnaire; toe range of motion at 12 weeks.
btd_threshold_vs_soc: VAS reduction ≥30 mm; MOXFQ improvement ≥15 points vs. footwear alone at 12 weeks.

symptoms: Medial ankle pain, plantar foot numbness/tingling; positive Tinel sign at tarsal tunnel
treatment_goal: Decompress posterior tibial nerve; relieve neuropathic pain; restore function
first_line_soc: Orthotics (medial arch support), NSAIDs, corticosteroid injection at tarsal tunnel. Gabapentin/pregabalin for neuropathic pain.
treatment_algorithm: Medial arch support orthotics. NSAIDs. Ultrasound-guided corticosteroid + local anesthetic injection at tarsal tunnel. Gabapentin 300–1,800 mg/day or pregabalin for neuropathic symptoms. Night splints. Physical therapy. Surgical tarsal tunnel release for refractory cases (NCV-confirmed).
us_prevalence: ~500,000–800,000 US patients with confirmed tarsal tunnel syndrome.
patients_needing_tx: ~400,000 patients requiring pharmacologic management.
price: $3,000–$15,000/year for neuropathic pain treatment.
peak_sales: ~$80–150M peak sales for novel peripheral nerve decompression drug.
registrational_endpoint: VAS neuropathic pain score; nerve conduction velocity improvement; Foot Function Index at 12 weeks.
btd_threshold_vs_soc: VAS reduction ≥30 mm; NCV improvement ≥10 m/s; symptom resolution in ≥50% vs. injection alone.

symptoms: Medial ankle swelling, pain along tibialis posterior tendon sheath, crepitus with movement
treatment_goal: Reduce tenosynovial inflammation; prevent tendon rupture; restore function
first_line_soc: Immobilization (boot/cast), NSAIDs, peritendinous corticosteroid injection.
treatment_algorithm: Walking boot or cast immobilization 4–6 weeks. NSAIDs. Ultrasound-guided peritendinous corticosteroid injection (avoid intratendinous). Physical therapy. Custom orthotics after acute resolution. Surgery if tenosynovitis progresses to Stage II–III PTTD (posterior tibial tendon dysfunction). Same principles as tibialis posterior tendinosis management.
us_prevalence: ~500,000–1,000,000 US patients; overlaps significantly with tibialis posterior tendinosis.
patients_needing_tx: ~400,000 patients requiring pharmacologic or injection treatment.
registrational_endpoint: VAS pain; FAOS score; tenosynovial thickness by ultrasound at 8 weeks.
btd_threshold_vs_soc: VAS reduction ≥30 mm; ≥40% reduction in tenosynovial sheath thickness at 8 weeks vs. boot alone.

symptoms: Firm, painless plantar nodule(s); slowly enlarging fibrotic lesions on plantar fascia
treatment_goal: Reduce nodule size; prevent functional impairment; relieve pain
first_line_soc: Orthotics/offloading pads. Corticosteroid injections (temporary benefit). Verapamil topical/injection — off-label. No FDA-approved pharmacologic therapy.
treatment_algorithm: Orthotics to offload nodule. Corticosteroid injection (limited, temporary). Verapamil 15% topical gel or intralesional injection (calcium channel blocker — anti-fibrotic; case series evidence). Collagenase clostridium histolyticum (Xiaflex — approved for Dupuytren, used off-label for plantar fibromatosis). ESWT. Radiation therapy for aggressive/rapidly growing lesions. Surgical fasciectomy for progressive deformity.
us_prevalence: ~200,000–400,000 US patients; often bilateral; associated with Dupuytren disease.
patients_needing_tx: ~150,000 patients requiring pharmacologic or injection treatment.
price: $5,000–$20,000/injection course.
peak_sales: ~$50–100M for a novel anti-fibrotic injection approved specifically for plantar fibromatosis.
registrational_endpoint: Nodule volume (ultrasound) at 12 weeks; VAS pain; Foot Function Index.
btd_threshold_vs_soc: ≥40% nodule volume reduction; VAS reduction ≥20 mm at 12 weeks vs. placebo injection.

symptoms: Burning forefoot pain between 3rd-4th toes (Morton neuroma); worsened with tight shoes
treatment_goal: Reduce perineural fibrosis pain; decompress nerve; prevent recurrence
first_line_soc: Wide toe-box shoes, metatarsal pad, NSAIDs. Corticosteroid injection is highly effective first-line injection therapy.
treatment_algorithm: Wide-toe footwear, metatarsal dome pad. NSAIDs. Corticosteroid injection (3–4 injections, 6–8 weeks apart): 50–80% initial success. Alcohol sclerosant injection (4% alcohol) — alternative. Cryotherapy. RF ablation. PRP injection. Surgical neurectomy for refractory cases (permanent but risk of stump neuroma).
us_prevalence: ~500,000–1,000,000 US patients; predominantly women aged 30–60.
patients_needing_tx: ~500,000 patients requiring pharmacologic or injection treatment annually.
price: $1,000–$5,000/injection course.
peak_sales: ~$50–100M for novel long-acting formulation or nerve-targeted therapy.
registrational_endpoint: VAS forefoot pain at 12 weeks; neuroma diameter (ultrasound); patient satisfaction score.
btd_threshold_vs_soc: VAS reduction ≥40 mm; neuroma diameter reduction ≥25% at 12 weeks vs. standard corticosteroid.

disease: Medial and Lateral Plantar Nerve Entrapment
symptoms: Heel and plantar foot pain with numbness; nerve entrapment at tarsal tunnel or arch
treatment_goal: Decompress nerve; relieve pain and paresthesias; restore nerve conduction
first_line_soc: Rest, orthotics (arch support), NSAIDs. Corticosteroid injection near entrapment site.
treatment_algorithm: Conservative: rest, orthotics, NSAIDs. Corticosteroid + local anesthetic injection at entrapment site. Night splints. Physical therapy. Surgical decompression of tarsal tunnel or plantar fascial band for refractory cases. Gabapentin or pregabalin for neuropathic pain component.
us_prevalence: ~200,000–400,000 US patients with plantar nerve entrapment distinct from plantar fasciitis.
patients_needing_tx: ~150,000 patients requiring pharmacologic management.
price: $2,000–$10,000/year for a neuropathic pain agent.
registrational_endpoint: VAS pain; nerve conduction study improvement; Foot Function Index at 12 weeks.
btd_threshold_vs_soc: VAS reduction ≥30 mm; nerve conduction normalization ≥40% improvement at 12 weeks vs. conservative care.

symptoms: Posterior heel swelling and pain at superior calcaneal prominence; associated with Haglund deformity
treatment_goal: Reduce retrocalcaneal bursal inflammation; relieve Haglund-related pain
first_line_soc: Heel lift, open-back shoes, NSAIDs. Ultrasound-guided corticosteroid injection into retrocalcaneal bursa.
treatment_algorithm: Heel lift and shoe modification (avoid heel counters). NSAIDs. Ultrasound-guided corticosteroid injection (retrocalcaneal bursa — avoid Achilles tendon injection due to rupture risk). Physical therapy and stretching. ESWT. Surgical bursectomy + Haglund's resection for refractory cases.
us_prevalence: ~500,000–800,000 US patients; often comorbid with Achilles tendinopathy.
patients_needing_tx: ~400,000 patients requiring pharmacologic or injection treatment.
price: $500–$3,000/injection course.
peak_sales: Minimal novel drug opportunity; ~$15–30M peak sales.
registrational_endpoint: VAS posterior heel pain; bursal thickness by ultrasound; VISA-A score at 8 weeks.
btd_threshold_vs_soc: VAS reduction ≥40 mm; bursal thickness reduction ≥40% at 8 weeks vs. shoe modification.

symptoms: Forefoot pain and swelling at 2nd or 3rd metatarsal head; osteonecrosis of metatarsal epiphysis
treatment_goal: Relieve pain; prevent metatarsal head collapse; preserve joint function
first_line_soc: Activity modification, metatarsal pad, NSAIDs, walking boot. No disease-modifying pharmacotherapy.
treatment_algorithm: Conservative: metatarsal dome pad, stiff-soled shoe or rocker-bottom, activity restriction, NSAIDs. Walking boot for 4–6 weeks for severe cases. Bisphosphonates or prostanoids under investigation for osteonecrosis (limited evidence). Intra-articular corticosteroid or hyaluronic acid injection. Surgery (core decompression, osteotomy, joint resection) for advanced disease (Smillie stage III–V).
us_prevalence: ~50,000–100,000 US cases; predominantly adolescent females. Rare condition.
patients_needing_tx: ~40,000–70,000 patients requiring pharmacologic management.
price: $5,000–$20,000/year for a novel bone-protective or anti-osteonecrosis agent.
registrational_endpoint: VAS pain; radiographic Smillie staging progression; AOFAS forefoot score at 6 months.
btd_threshold_vs_soc: Halt in Smillie stage progression; VAS reduction ≥30 mm at 6 months vs. conservative management.

symptoms: Heel pain in active children aged 8–14 during growth spurts; Sever disease; bilateral in 60%
treatment_goal: Relieve pain; reduce apophyseal inflammation; maintain activity
first_line_soc: Activity modification, heel cup insoles, calf stretching, NSAIDs (ibuprofen) for pain relief.
treatment_algorithm: Heel cup or silicone insert (offloads the apophysis). Activity modification (reduce impact sports temporarily). Calf stretching and strengthening. NSAIDs (ibuprofen 10 mg/kg TID) for acute pain. Ice after activity. Walking boot for severe cases. Prognosis excellent: resolves with skeletal maturity. No injection or surgical treatment needed.
us_prevalence: ~800,000–1,500,000 US children/year (most common cause of heel pain in children). AAP data.
patients_needing_tx: ~1,000,000 children requiring NSAIDs or supportive pharmacologic management.
price: $200–$1,000/episode (pediatric NSAIDs).
peak_sales: Self-limiting pediatric condition. Novel pediatric anti-inflammatory: ~$20–40M peak sales.
registrational_endpoint: Pediatric VAS pain score at 4 weeks; PODCI functional score; return to sport.
btd_threshold_vs_soc: VAS reduction ≥40 mm; return-to-sport at 4 weeks in ≥70% vs. 40% with standard care.

symptoms: Deep anterior heel pain, swelling at Achilles insertion, Haglund deformity association
treatment_goal: Reduce bursal inflammation; relieve pain; prevent recurrence
first_line_soc: Heel pad, shoe modification, NSAIDs, ice. Corticosteroid injection into retrocalcaneal bursa.
treatment_algorithm: Heel pad/silicone insert, open-back shoes. NSAIDs (ibuprofen, naproxen). Ultrasound-guided corticosteroid injection into retrocalcaneal bursa (accurate targeting essential). Physical therapy. ESWT for chronic cases. Surgical bursectomy + Haglund deformity resection for refractory cases.
us_prevalence: ~500,000 US patients annually; often associated with Haglund deformity.
patients_needing_tx: ~300,000 patients requiring injection or pharmacologic treatment.
price: $500–$3,000/injection course.
peak_sales: Generic NSAIDs dominate; injection market. Peak sales ~$15–30M for novel long-acting steroid formulation.
registrational_endpoint: VAS pain score; bursal thickness by ultrasound; time to pain-free walking.
btd_threshold_vs_soc: VAS reduction ≥40 mm; ≥50% reduction in bursal thickness at 4 weeks vs. placebo injection.

symptoms: Heel pad pain beneath calcaneus, especially with standing and walking; subcalcaneal bursitis
treatment_goal: Reduce bursal inflammation; relieve heel pain; restore ambulation
first_line_soc: Heel cup/cushion, NSAIDs, corticosteroid injection into inferior calcaneal bursa.
treatment_algorithm: Heel cup with cushioning. NSAIDs (ibuprofen 400 mg TID). Ice application. Ultrasound-guided corticosteroid injection (triamcinolone/betamethasone). Physical therapy. Tape strapping. ESWT for refractory chronic cases. Surgical bursectomy rare.
us_prevalence: ~500,000 US cases/year; often overlaps with plantar fasciitis.
patients_needing_tx: ~300,000 patients requiring pharmacologic or injection therapy.
peak_sales: Minimal novel drug opportunity; generic NSAIDs and generic corticosteroids dominate. Peak sales ~$10–20M.
registrational_endpoint: VAS heel pain at 4 weeks; Foot Function Index; return to full weight-bearing.
btd_threshold_vs_soc: VAS reduction ≥40 mm; ≥60% pain-free ambulation at 4 weeks vs. heel cup alone.

symptoms: Pain beneath first metatarsal head at ball of foot; tender sesamoid bones; worse with push-off
treatment_goal: Reduce sesamoid bone inflammation; offload pressure; prevent osteonecrosis
first_line_soc: Sesamoid pad/dancer's pad, activity reduction, NSAIDs. Stiff-soled shoe or walking boot for severe cases.
treatment_algorithm: Dancer's pad (felt pad with sesamoid cutout). NSAIDs. Walking boot 4–6 weeks. Corticosteroid injection — use cautiously (risk of avascular necrosis). Physical therapy. Padding and taping. Surgery (sesamoidectomy) for osteonecrosis or chronic refractory sesamoiditis.
us_prevalence: ~200,000–500,000 US cases/year; predominantly dancers, runners, and high-heeled shoe wearers.
patients_needing_tx: ~200,000 patients requiring pharmacologic management.
peak_sales: Minimal novel drug market. Peak sales ~$5–15M.
registrational_endpoint: VAS forefoot pain; AOFAS hallux score; return to activity at 8 weeks.
btd_threshold_vs_soc: VAS reduction ≥30 mm; return to full activity in ≥70% at 8 weeks vs. padding alone.

symptoms: Joint pain, stiffness, Heberden/Bouchard nodes at DIP/PIP joints; grip weakness; morning stiffness
treatment_goal: Reduce pain; maintain hand function; slow cartilage loss
first_line_soc: Topical NSAIDs (diclofenac gel 1% — Voltaren Arthritis Pain) are first-line pharmacologic therapy. Oral NSAIDs for additional pain control.
treatment_algorithm: Topical diclofenac (Voltaren Arthritis Pain gel) 4×/day: FDA-approved, first-line. Oral NSAIDs (naproxen, ibuprofen). Intra-articular corticosteroid for CMC or thumb base OA. Intra-articular hyaluronic acid (CMC joint, off-label). Hydroxychloroquine for erosive hand OA (limited trial evidence). Splinting for thumb base OA (CMC splint). Occupational therapy. Sprifermin (recombinant FGF-18) in trial for hand OA. Lorecivivint (WNT inhibitor) in Phase 3 for hand OA. Surgery (trapeziectomy ± ligament reconstruction for thumb CMC OA Stage IV).
us_prevalence: ~27–30 million US adults with hand OA; accounts for ~50% of all OA patients. ACR 2024 guidelines.
patients_needing_tx: ~15–18 million patients requiring pharmacologic treatment.
price: $15,000–$40,000/year for a novel structure-modifying OA drug (SMOAD).
tam_gross: 16M × $25,000 = $400B (includes total hand OA); brand new SMOAD: ~$30–50B TAM (addressable hand OA subset)
peak_sales: Novel SMOAD (structure-modifying): ~$1–3B peak sales for hand OA indication.
registrational_endpoint: VAS/NRS hand pain; AUSCAN score; Joint Space Width (JSW) change at 24 months; erosion score on X-ray.
btd_threshold_vs_soc: JSW preservation vs. narrowing; AUSCAN improvement ≥20% vs. NSAID; erosion score improvement at 24 months.

symptoms: Hand numbness, tingling (median nerve distribution), nocturnal symptoms, thenar wasting in severe cases
treatment_goal: Decompress median nerve; relieve symptoms; prevent permanent nerve damage
first_line_soc: Wrist splint (neutral position) at night. Oral NSAIDs or corticosteroids for short-term relief. Corticosteroid injection provides medium-term benefit.
treatment_algorithm: Wrist splint. Corticosteroid injection (methylprednisolone + lidocaine): 50–80% response at 1 month. NSAIDs for mild symptoms. Ultrasound-guided injection superior to landmark. B6 (pyridoxine) — limited evidence. Surgical carpal tunnel release (endoscopic or open): gold standard for persistent/severe cases. Novel: ultrasound-guided percutaneous needle hydrodissection (PNH) emerging as alternative to surgery.
us_prevalence: ~10–12 million US patients; most common peripheral neuropathy. ~500,000 surgeries/year.
patients_needing_tx: ~6–8 million patients requiring pharmacologic or injection treatment.
price: $1,000–$5,000/injection course; $500–$2,000/year for ongoing management.
peak_sales: Primarily surgical + injection market. Novel disease-modifying injection: ~$200–400M peak sales.
registrational_endpoint: Boston Carpal Tunnel Questionnaire (BCTQ); nerve conduction velocity; VAS pain at 12 weeks.
btd_threshold_vs_soc: BCTQ improvement ≥1.5 points; NCV improvement ≥5 m/s; ≥70% responder rate vs. corticosteroid injection alone at 12 weeks.

symptoms: PIP joint flexion contracture with DIP hyperextension; follows extensor tendon injury or RA
treatment_goal: Restore extensor mechanism; prevent fixed deformity; maintain grip function
first_line_soc: PIP extension splinting. Physical therapy. Treat underlying RA (DMARDs/biologics) to prevent progression.
treatment_algorithm: PIP extension splinting 6–8 weeks for acute/flexible deformity. Serial casting for fixed deformity. Physical therapy (AROM exercises). Treat underlying RA: methotrexate + biologics (see RA section). Corticosteroid injection into PIP joint for inflammatory cases. Surgery (extensor tendon reconstruction or PIP fusion) for fixed/end-stage deformity.
us_prevalence: ~500,000–1,000,000 US patients (RA-related and traumatic); RA accounts for ~70%.
patients_needing_tx: ~400,000 patients requiring pharmacologic management.
price: $5,000–$30,000/year (DMARDs/biologics for RA-related).
peak_sales: Managed under RA biologics market; no stand-alone drug market. Novel extensor tendon repair biologic: ~$50–100M peak sales.
registrational_endpoint: PIP extension deficit at 12 weeks; DASH score; grip strength.
btd_threshold_vs_soc: PIP extension improvement ≥30°; DASH score improvement ≥15 points vs. splinting alone.

symptoms: PIP hyperextension with DIP flexion; characteristic 'S-shape' deformity; seen in RA and connective tissue disease
treatment_goal: Restore normal PIP/DIP motion balance; maintain grip; treat underlying RA
first_line_soc: Silver ring splint to limit PIP hyperextension. Treat underlying RA with DMARDs/biologics.
treatment_algorithm: PIP ring splint (silver ring splint/Oval-8 splint) to block hyperextension. Treat underlying RA: methotrexate + TNF inhibitor or JAK inhibitor. Soft tissue procedures (FDS slip tenodesis) for flexible deformity. PIP arthroplasty or fusion for fixed end-stage deformity. Hand occupational therapy.
us_prevalence: ~500,000–1,000,000 US patients (RA-related); also seen in SLE, EDS, and post-trauma.
patients_needing_tx: ~400,000 patients with symptomatic swan-neck deformity requiring pharmacologic management.
price: $5,000–$30,000/year (RA biologics).
peak_sales: Managed under RA biologics. Deformity-specific surgery/device market. Peak sales ~$30–60M for novel joint-preserving biologic.
registrational_endpoint: PIP extension deformity angle; DASH score; grip strength at 12 weeks.
btd_threshold_vs_soc: PIP hyperextension reduction ≥30°; DASH improvement ≥15 points vs. splinting alone at 12 weeks.

symptoms: Smooth cystic swelling arising from wrist/finger joint or tendon sheath; may cause pain or weakness
treatment_goal: Relieve pain; eliminate cyst; prevent recurrence
first_line_soc: Observation (many resolve spontaneously). Aspiration (cyst decompression). No pharmacologic SoC.
treatment_algorithm: Observation (30–50% spontaneous resolution). Aspiration under local anesthesia: simple (recurrence ~50%) or aspiration + steroid injection. Surgical excision: lower recurrence (~10%) but risks including scar, stiffness. Ultrasound-guided aspiration. Intralesional steroid after aspiration (mild benefit on recurrence). No approved pharmacologic agent.
us_prevalence: ~10–15 million US adults with ganglion cysts; most common hand/wrist tumor.
patients_needing_tx: ~2–3 million patients seeking treatment annually.
price: $1,000–$5,000/injection course.
peak_sales: Procedure-dominant condition. Novel sclerosant or cyst-ablating injection: ~$100–200M peak sales.
registrational_endpoint: Cyst recurrence at 6 months; VAS pain; wrist range of motion at 4 weeks.
btd_threshold_vs_soc: Recurrence rate ≤15% at 12 months vs. ~50% for aspiration alone; VAS reduction ≥30 mm at 4 weeks.

symptoms: Catching, clicking, or locking of finger during flexion/extension; A1 pulley inflammation
treatment_goal: Free tendon gliding; eliminate triggering; reduce A1 pulley inflammation
first_line_soc: Corticosteroid injection into A1 pulley/tendon sheath: ~60–70% success rate. Splinting as adjunct.
treatment_algorithm: Corticosteroid injection (triamcinolone or methylprednisolone into A1 pulley): first-line, 60–70% initial success; 50% recurrence at 1 year. Second injection if first fails. NSAIDs + splinting for mild cases. Percutaneous needle trigger finger release (PNTFR): office procedure, highly effective. Surgical A1 pulley release: definitive; recurrence <5%. Collagenase clostridium histolyticum injection (Xiaflex) under investigation for trigger finger.
us_prevalence: ~3–5 million US patients; incidence ~2.6% in general population; more common in diabetics.
patients_needing_tx: ~2–3 million patients seeking injection or surgical treatment.
price: $500–$3,000/injection; $5,000–$15,000 for novel biologic.
peak_sales: Generic steroid injections dominate. Novel long-acting or anti-fibrotic injection: ~$200–400M peak sales.
registrational_endpoint: Triggering resolution at 4 weeks; recurrence at 12 months; QuickDASH score.
btd_threshold_vs_soc: Triggering resolution in ≥80% at 4 weeks; recurrence ≤15% at 12 months vs. ~50% for corticosteroid.

symptoms: Elbow/medial forearm pain, hand numbness (ring/little fingers), ulnar nerve compression
treatment_goal: Decompress ulnar nerve; relieve neuropathic pain; preserve intrinsic hand strength
first_line_soc: Elbow extension splint at night, activity modification (avoid elbow flexion >90°). NSAIDs. Corticosteroid injection around cubital tunnel for mild cases.
treatment_algorithm: Elbow splint (extension at night). NSAIDs. Activity modification. Corticosteroid injection near ulnar nerve at elbow. Ergonomic modifications. Surgical: simple decompression, medial epicondylectomy, or ulnar nerve transposition for moderate-severe cases. Gabapentin/pregabalin for neuropathic pain.
us_prevalence: ~1–2 million US patients; second most common peripheral entrapment neuropathy after CTS.
patients_needing_tx: ~700,000 patients requiring pharmacologic management.
peak_sales: ~$80–150M peak sales for novel peripheral nerve decompression drug.
registrational_endpoint: ULDS (Ulnar Neuropathy at the Elbow assessment); NCV; grip/pinch strength at 12 weeks.
btd_threshold_vs_soc: NCV improvement ≥5 m/s; grip strength improvement ≥20%; symptom resolution in ≥50% vs. splinting.

symptoms: Radial wrist pain, tenderness at first dorsal compartment; positive Finkelstein test
treatment_goal: Reduce tenosynovitis; relieve pain; restore thumb and wrist function
first_line_soc: Corticosteroid injection into first dorsal compartment tendon sheath is the most effective first-line treatment. Thumb spica splint + NSAIDs.
treatment_algorithm: Thumb spica splint. NSAIDs (ibuprofen, naproxen). Corticosteroid injection (1–2 injections of methylprednisolone/triamcinolone into first compartment sheath): ~80% success rate. Ultrasound-guided injection (higher accuracy). Physical therapy. Surgery (first dorsal compartment release) for refractory cases.
us_prevalence: ~2–3 million US cases/year; extremely common in postpartum mothers and racket sport players.
patients_needing_tx: ~1.5 million patients requiring injection or pharmacologic therapy.
price: $500–$3,000/injection course.
peak_sales: Generic corticosteroid injections dominate. Novel long-acting biologic injection: ~$100–200M peak sales.
registrational_endpoint: VAS pain at 6 weeks; Finkelstein test positivity; DASH score.
btd_threshold_vs_soc: VAS reduction ≥40 mm; DASH improvement ≥15 points; recurrence-free at 6 months in ≥75% vs. standard injection.

symptoms: Progressive fibrous cord formation in palm; finger flexion contracture; inability to extend fingers
treatment_goal: Restore finger extension; break down fibrous cords; prevent recurrence
first_line_soc: Collagenase clostridium histolyticum (Xiaflex/Qwo) — FDA-approved 2010 for Dupuytren with palpable cord; enzymatic fasciotomy by injection.
treatment_algorithm: Stage I–II (no contracture): observation, passive stretching. Cord with ≥30° MCP contracture: collagenase clostridium histolyticum (Xiaflex) injection → 24h later manipulation. Needle aponeurotomy (percutaneous fasciotomy) — office procedure. Surgical fasciotomy/fasciectomy for severe or recurrent contractures. Radiation therapy for early Dupuytren (prevents progression). Tamoxifen and interferon investigated — limited evidence. Anti-TGF-β biologics in trials.
us_prevalence: ~2–3 million US patients with Dupuytren disease; ~200,000 procedures/year.
patients_needing_tx: ~500,000 patients with contracture requiring Xiaflex or surgical intervention.
price: $3,000–$6,000/injection series (Xiaflex current pricing ~$3,300–$4,200/vial).
peak_sales: Xiaflex (Endo) ~$200M annual sales. Novel anti-recurrence biologic: ~$200–400M peak sales.
registrational_endpoint: Reduction in contracture ≥50% from baseline; active range of motion improvement; clinical success rate at 4 weeks.
btd_threshold_vs_soc: Clinical success (≤5° contracture) in ≥60% at 4 weeks vs. ~44% for Xiaflex; lower 2-year recurrence rate.

symptoms: Wrist pain, stiffness, swelling; osteonecrosis of lunate bone; may progress to carpal collapse
treatment_goal: Preserve or restore lunate vascularity; prevent carpal collapse; maintain wrist function
first_line_soc: Stage I–IIIa: wrist immobilization + unloading (radial shortening osteotomy or external fixator). NSAIDs for pain. No pharmacologic disease-modifying therapy established.
treatment_algorithm: Conservative: immobilization + NSAIDs for early stages. Surgical: radial shortening osteotomy (reduces lunate load), ulnar lengthening, capitate shortening. Revascularization procedures (pedicled bone grafts). Proximal row carpectomy or wrist fusion for advanced (Stage IV) collapse. Bisphosphonates and prostanoids under investigation. No FDA-approved pharmacologic therapy.
us_prevalence: ~50,000–100,000 US patients; predominantly young males aged 20–40. Rare.
patients_needing_tx: ~40,000 patients requiring pharmacologic or surgical treatment.
price: $10,000–$30,000/year for bone-protective/vascular biologic.
peak_sales: Primarily surgical condition. Novel revascularization drug: ~$30–60M peak sales.
registrational_endpoint: MRI lunate viability score; VAS wrist pain; Mayo Wrist Score at 12 months.
btd_threshold_vs_soc: Halt radiographic stage progression; MRI viability improvement ≥30%; VAS reduction ≥30 mm at 12 months.

symptoms: Lateral elbow/proximal forearm pain; no motor weakness; compressive radial nerve neuropathy
treatment_goal: Decompress radial nerve; relieve pain; restore forearm function
first_line_soc: Rest, activity modification, NSAIDs. Corticosteroid injection near radial tunnel. Physical therapy with forearm stretching.
treatment_algorithm: Activity modification (avoid repetitive supination/forearm rotation). NSAIDs (ibuprofen, naproxen). Corticosteroid injection at radial tunnel. Physical therapy (forearm and wrist stretching, IASTM). Surgical decompression of radial nerve at supinator arcade for refractory cases (>3 months of failed conservative care).
us_prevalence: ~200,000–400,000 US patients; frequently misdiagnosed as lateral epicondylitis.
patients_needing_tx: ~200,000 patients requiring pharmacologic management.
registrational_endpoint: VAS lateral elbow/forearm pain; DASH score; radial nerve NCV at 12 weeks.
btd_threshold_vs_soc: VAS reduction ≥30 mm; DASH improvement ≥15 points vs. corticosteroid injection at 12 weeks.

symptoms: Kanavel's four signs: finger held in flexion, symmetric swelling, tenderness along sheath, pain with passive extension
treatment_goal: Urgent drainage of tendon sheath; eradicate bacteria; preserve flexor function
first_line_soc: Urgent surgical drainage + irrigation of flexor tendon sheath. IV antibiotics: vancomycin + ceftriaxone empirically covering Staph, Strep, gram-negatives.
treatment_algorithm: Emergency surgical drainage (open or catheter irrigation). IV antibiotics: vancomycin + ceftriaxone or piperacillin-tazobactam. Narrow based on culture. IV → oral antibiotics × 10–14 days. Splinting in position of function post-surgery. Aggressive hand therapy after acute infection resolves. Failure to treat urgently results in flexor tendon necrosis and permanent disability.
us_prevalence: ~30,000–50,000 cases/year in US emergency departments.
patients_needing_tx: ~40,000 patients/year requiring IV antibiotics + surgical drainage.
peak_sales: ~$20–50M peak sales for novel antibiotic with tendon sheath penetration.
registrational_endpoint: Clinical cure (preserved tendon function) at 4 weeks; reoperation rate; amputation rate.
btd_threshold_vs_soc: Preserved tendon function in ≥90%; reoperation rate ≤5% vs. ~15% historical.

disease: Infected Bite Wounds of the Hand
symptoms: Painful, swollen hand wound after human/animal bite; progressive cellulitis, abscess, or septic arthritis risk
treatment_goal: Eradicate infection; prevent septic arthritis and osteomyelitis; wound healing
first_line_soc: Amoxicillin-clavulanate (Augmentin) 875/125 mg BID × 5–7 days for outpatient bite wound infection. IV ampicillin-sulbactam for severe infections.
treatment_algorithm: Wound care: irrigate thoroughly, debride devitalized tissue. Avoid primary closure for most bite wounds (except facial). Oral: amoxicillin-clavulanate (covers Eikenella corrodens for human bites; Pasteurella multocida for cat/dog). Cat bites: very high infection risk, prophylactic antibiotics mandatory. Severe/deep: IV ampicillin-sulbactam; piperacillin-tazobactam. Tetanus prophylaxis. Rabies post-exposure prophylaxis for high-risk animal bites. MRSA consideration: add doxycycline or TMP-SMX.
us_prevalence: ~300,000–500,000 infected bite wounds treated/year in the US (human + animal).
patients_needing_tx: ~400,000 patients/year requiring antibiotic therapy.
price: $200–$1,000/antibiotic course.
peak_sales: Generic-dominated. Novel antibiotic for polymicrobial bite infections: ~$10–20M peak sales.
registrational_endpoint: Clinical cure at day 7; wound infection rate at day 3–4; hospitalization rate.
btd_threshold_vs_soc: Clinical cure ≥90%; hospitalization rate ≤5% vs. ~15% without prophylaxis.

symptoms: Tense, throbbing fingertip pain; abscess in distal finger pulp space; purulent collection
treatment_goal: Drain abscess; eradicate infection; preserve fingertip sensation and function
first_line_soc: Surgical incision and drainage (I&D) is the cornerstone. Antibiotics (dicloxacillin or cephalexin for MSSA; TMP-SMX for MRSA coverage) post-I&D.
treatment_algorithm: I&D under digital block anesthesia (high-lateral or through-and-through incision). Antibiotics: dicloxacillin 500 mg QID × 5 days; TMP-SMX DS BID for MRSA risk. Warm soaks. Wound packing/irrigation. Culture-directed therapy if needed. Complication (osteomyelitis): IV antibiotics ± bone debridement.
patients_needing_tx: ~300,000 patients requiring antibiotic therapy post-drainage.
price: $200–$1,000/antibiotic course.
peak_sales: Generic antibiotic-dominated; minimal novel drug opportunity. Peak sales ~$5–10M.
registrational_endpoint: Clinical cure at day 7 post-I&D; infection recurrence at 30 days.
btd_threshold_vs_soc: Clinical cure ≥90% at day 7; MRSA eradication rate ≥85% vs. standard antibiotics.

symptoms: Fluctuant swelling with pain in midpalmar or thenar space; limited finger motion
treatment_goal: Drain abscess; eradicate infection; preserve hand function
first_line_soc: Surgical incision and drainage (I&D) is essential. Antibiotics: dicloxacillin/cephalexin (outpatient) or vancomycin + piperacillin-tazobactam (inpatient, severe cases).
treatment_algorithm: I&D (thenar or midpalmar space): requires precise anatomic knowledge. IV antibiotics: vancomycin + piperacillin-tazobactam for deep space infections (polymicrobial, MRSA risk). Oral step-down: amoxicillin-clavulanate or TMP-SMX. Wound packing and irrigation. Hand therapy after acute resolution. Diabetic and immunocompromised patients: extended IV treatment.
us_prevalence: ~10,000–30,000 US cases/year of deep palm space infections.
patients_needing_tx: ~20,000 patients/year requiring IV antibiotics.
registrational_endpoint: Clinical cure at 7 days; re-infection rate at 30 days; hand function score.
btd_threshold_vs_soc: Clinical cure ≥90% at day 7; reoperation rate ≤5% vs. ~15% historical.

symptoms: Painful vesicular eruption on finger; HSV-1 or HSV-2 infection of the fingertip
treatment_goal: Shorten episode duration; relieve pain; prevent transmission and recurrence
first_line_soc: Oral acyclovir 400 mg 5×/day × 7–10 days (or valacyclovir 1 g BID × 7 days) for primary episode.
treatment_algorithm: Primary episode: oral acyclovir 400 mg 5×/day × 10 days; valacyclovir 1 g BID × 7 days (better bioavailability). Famciclovir 500 mg BID × 7 days. Recurrent: acyclovir 400 mg TID × 5 days; valacyclovir 500 mg BID × 3 days. Topical acyclovir (limited benefit for finger lesions). Avoid I&D — worsens outcome. Pain management: NSAIDs, topical lidocaine. Suppressive therapy for frequent recurrences: acyclovir 400 mg BID.
us_prevalence: ~30,000–50,000 cases/year; mostly healthcare workers exposed to oral secretions.
patients_needing_tx: ~40,000 patients/year requiring antiviral treatment.
price: $100–$500/acute course (generics available).
peak_sales: Generic antiviral market; minimal novel drug opportunity. Peak sales ~$5–10M.
registrational_endpoint: Lesion healing time; pain VAS at day 5; recurrence at 6 months.
btd_threshold_vs_soc: ≥2-day reduction in lesion healing vs. acyclovir; recurrence-free at 6 months in ≥70%.

symptoms: Periprosthetic joint pain, swelling, loosening; fever; elevated ESR/CRP after arthroplasty
treatment_goal: Eradicate biofilm infection; preserve or salvage prosthesis; prevent systemic sepsis
first_line_soc: Two-stage exchange arthroplasty (prosthesis removal + interval antibiotic spacer + reimplantation) + prolonged antibiotics. Rifampin-based oral regimens for biofilm-active therapy post-débridement.
treatment_algorithm: Acute (<4 weeks post-implant or <3 weeks symptoms): DAIR (debridement, antibiotics, implant retention) + rifampin combination. Chronic: two-stage exchange arthroplasty. Antibiotics: IV vancomycin (MRSA) or ceftriaxone + rifampin 300–450 mg BID (biofilm-active) × 3–6 months. Oral step-down: fluoroquinolone + rifampin for susceptible organisms (IDSA guidelines). New: ceftaroline or dalbavancin for prolonged MRSA suppression. Antibiotic-loaded bone cement spacers.
us_prevalence: ~50,000–100,000 periprosthetic joint infections/year as arthroplasty volume grows. AAOS 2024.
patients_needing_tx: ~75,000 patients/year requiring prolonged antibiotic therapy.
peak_sales: ~$200–400M peak sales for novel anti-biofilm antibiotic or prevention agent.
registrational_endpoint: Infection cure at 12 months post-treatment; prosthesis retention rate; reoperation rate.
btd_threshold_vs_soc: Infection cure ≥80%; prosthesis retention ≥70% at 12 months vs. ~60–65% with DAIR + standard antibiotics.

symptoms: Bone pain, tenderness, fever; elevated CRP/ESR; MRI shows bone marrow edema
treatment_goal: Eradicate bone infection; preserve bone integrity; prevent chronic osteomyelitis
first_line_soc: IV antibiotics: nafcillin/cefazolin for MSSA; vancomycin for MRSA; ceftriaxone for gram-negatives. Surgical debridement for chronic or hardware-associated osteomyelitis.
treatment_algorithm: IV antibiotics: vancomycin (MRSA); nafcillin or cefazolin (MSSA); ceftriaxone (gram-negatives); piperacillin-tazobactam (polymicrobial). Duration: 4–6 weeks IV (option for early oral switch per OVIVA trial). Early oral switch: rifampin-based regimens for S. aureus. Surgical debridement for necrotic bone. Bone flap/free flap for defects. Hyperbaric oxygen as adjunct for ischemic osteomyelitis. Novel: oritavancin (single-dose IV) for MRSA osteomyelitis (Phase 3 evidence).
us_prevalence: ~50,000–80,000 hospitalizations/year; chronic osteomyelitis ~100,000 prevalent cases.
patients_needing_tx: ~60,000 patients/year requiring IV or prolonged oral antibiotics.
peak_sales: ~$200–400M peak sales for a novel long-acting lipoglycopeptide or anti-biofilm antibiotic.
registrational_endpoint: Clinical remission at 12 months; absence of relapse; CRP/ESR normalization.
btd_threshold_vs_soc: Remission rate ≥80% at 12 months; relapse rate ≤10% vs. ~20% with standard IV therapy.

symptoms: Sudden hot, swollen monoarthritis; fever; elevated WBC/ESR; joint aspirate shows bacteria
treatment_goal: Eradicate joint infection; preserve joint function; prevent systemic sepsis
first_line_soc: Urgent joint aspiration + IV antibiotics: nafcillin/oxacillin for MSSA; vancomycin for MRSA; ceftriaxone for N. gonorrhoeae; gram-negative coverage with cefepime or piperacillin-tazobactam.
treatment_algorithm: Aspiration (arthrocentesis) for diagnosis and drainage. Empiric IV antibiotics: vancomycin (MRSA coverage) + ceftriaxone (gram-negative, gonococcal). Culture-directed narrowing. Surgical washout (arthroscopic lavage) if inadequate drainage. Duration: 2–4 weeks IV then oral completion. Total antibiotics 4–6 weeks for S. aureus. Dexamethasone adjunct controversial. Post-infectious rehabilitation.
us_prevalence: ~20,000–30,000 hospitalized cases/year. Predominantly S. aureus (40–50%) and gram-negatives.
patients_needing_tx: ~25,000 patients/year requiring IV antibiotics.
peak_sales: ~$50–100M peak sales for novel anti-biofilm or joint-penetrating antibiotic.
registrational_endpoint: Clinical cure at day 14; joint function recovery at 6 weeks; bacteriological eradication.
btd_threshold_vs_soc: Clinical cure ≥90%; functional recovery ≥60% at 6 weeks vs. ~40–50% historical with standard IV antibiotics.

symptoms: Persistent joint pain, swelling, and restriction >6 weeks; mycobacterial, fungal, or Lyme etiology
treatment_goal: Eradicate chronic pathogen; preserve joint integrity; prevent recurrence
first_line_soc: Pathogen-specific regimen: rifampin-based for TB arthritis (RIPE × 6–9 months); doxycycline for Lyme arthritis; antifungals for fungal arthritis (fluconazole, itraconazole, voriconazole).
treatment_algorithm: TB arthritis: isoniazid + rifampin + pyrazinamide + ethambutol × 2 months, then INH + rifampin × 4 months. Lyme arthritis: doxycycline 100 mg BID × 28 days (or amoxicillin); ceftriaxone IV for refractory. Fungal (Coccidioides, Candida): fluconazole, itraconazole, or voriconazole 6–12 months. Brucella: doxycycline + rifampin × 6 weeks–3 months. Surgical debridement for extensive destruction.
us_prevalence: ~10,000–20,000 US cases/year; Lyme arthritis most common, followed by TB and fungal.
patients_needing_tx: ~15,000 patients requiring prolonged antimicrobial therapy.
price: $3,000–$30,000/course depending on pathogen.
peak_sales: ~$20–50M peak sales for novel pathogen-specific agent.
registrational_endpoint: Clinical cure; joint function score at 6 months; pathogen eradication.
btd_threshold_vs_soc: Clinical cure ≥90% at 6 months; joint function preservation ≥80% vs. ~60% historical.

symptoms: Joint pain, stiffness, crepitus, reduced range of motion; worse with activity; commonly knee, hip, hand
treatment_goal: Reduce pain; improve function; slow structural progression
first_line_soc: Topical NSAIDs (diclofenac gel) for hand/knee OA; oral NSAIDs (naproxen, ibuprofen, celecoxib) for moderate-severe pain. Exercise + weight loss as cornerstone.
treatment_algorithm: Non-pharmacologic: exercise, weight loss, physiotherapy. Topical diclofenac (Voltaren). Oral NSAIDs; COX-2 inhibitors (celecoxib). Duloxetine (Cymbalta) — FDA-approved for OA pain. Intra-articular corticosteroids (short-term relief). Intra-articular hyaluronic acid (Synvisc, Euflexxa — viscosupplementation). PRP injection — growing evidence. Novel: tanezumab (NGF inhibitor) — Phase 3 completed, mixed FDA review; lorecivivint (WNT inhibitor) Phase 3 ongoing; sprifermin (FGF-18) Phase 3. Joint replacement surgery for end-stage.
us_prevalence: ~32–36 million US adults with symptomatic OA. Arthritis Foundation 2024 data.
patients_needing_tx: ~20–25 million patients requiring pharmacologic therapy.
price: $15,000–$50,000/year for a novel SMOAD; injectable: $3,000–$15,000/injection series.
peak_sales: First approved SMOAD (structure-modifying OA drug): ~$3–8B peak sales. Current pain management market dominated by generics.
registrational_endpoint: WOMAC pain and function subscale; joint space width/cartilage thickness change (radiographic/MRI) at 24 months; OARSI responder rate.
btd_threshold_vs_soc: JSW preservation vs. placebo; WOMAC pain improvement ≥30%; cartilage thickness maintained on MRI at 24 months.

symptoms: Painless or relatively painless joint destruction; massive joint swelling; associated with DM, syphilis, or syringomyelia (Charcot joint)
treatment_goal: Offload joint; prevent further destruction; manage underlying neuropathy
first_line_soc: Total contact casting (TCC) for Charcot foot neuropathy. Bisphosphonates (IV pamidronate or zoledronate) to reduce osteoclast activity. Treat underlying diabetes aggressively.
treatment_algorithm: Acute Charcot: total contact cast or walker boot for 3–12 months. IV bisphosphonates (pamidronate 90 mg or zoledronate 5 mg) — reduce resorption, improve healing (ACR 2024 off-label use). Optimize glycemic control. NSAIDs for inflammatory phases. Surgical reconstruction (Charcot reconstruction) for deformity/instability. Calcitonin as alternative to bisphosphonates. Anti-RANKL (denosumab) investigated for Charcot prevention.
us_prevalence: ~1–2 million US patients with neuropathic arthropathy; diabetic Charcot foot most common (~0.5% of diabetics).
patients_needing_tx: ~500,000 patients requiring pharmacologic management.
peak_sales: ~$200–400M peak sales for a novel bone-protective agent proven to halt Charcot progression.
registrational_endpoint: Foot temperature asymmetry (≤2°C) achievement; radiographic destruction score at 12 months; time to consolidation (casting weeks).
btd_threshold_vs_soc: ≥50% faster consolidation; radiographic destruction score ≤30% progression vs. casting alone at 12 months.

symptoms: Lower back and leg pain (sciatica), dermatomal numbness/weakness; worse with sitting/Valsalva
treatment_goal: Relieve radicular pain; reduce disc herniation; restore lumbar function
first_line_soc: NSAIDs (naproxen, ibuprofen, diclofenac) + muscle relaxants. Lumbar epidural steroid injection for moderate-severe radiculopathy.
treatment_algorithm: NSAIDs. Short-course oral corticosteroids (prednisone taper). Muscle relaxants (cyclobenzaprine, baclofen). Lumbar epidural steroid injection (interlaminal or transforaminal LESI): highly effective for short-term radiculopathy relief. Gabapentin/pregabalin for neuropathic pain. Physical therapy: McKenzie method, core stabilization. Surgery: microdiscectomy for failed conservative treatment (>6 weeks) or motor deficit. Nucleus replacement biologics in trial (GelStix, NP Therapeutics).
us_prevalence: ~5–6 million new symptomatic lumbar disc herniations/year; ~500,000 microdiscectomies/year.
patients_needing_tx: ~4–5 million patients requiring pharmacologic management.
peak_sales: ~$300–600M peak sales for a novel disc regeneration/sustained epidural drug.
registrational_endpoint: NRS leg pain at 6 weeks; ODI (Oswestry Disability Index) at 3 months; MRI disc extrusion size at 6 weeks.
btd_threshold_vs_soc: ≥50% leg pain NRS reduction at 6 weeks; ODI improvement ≥20 points vs. epidural steroid injection alone.

symptoms: Low back pain, neurogenic claudication, radiculopathy; one vertebra slipped forward on another
treatment_goal: Stabilize slippage; relieve nerve compression; reduce pain
first_line_soc: NSAIDs + physical therapy (core stabilization, extension exercises). Epidural steroid injection for radiculopathy. Surgery (spinal fusion) for high-grade or refractory cases.
treatment_algorithm: NSAIDs. Physical therapy (lumbar stabilization). Lumbar ESI for radiculopathy. Bracing (lumbosacral orthosis). Surgical: posterolateral fusion ± interbody fusion (PLIF/TLIF) for Grade II+ slippage or neurological compromise. Isthmic spondylolisthesis in athletes: activity modification + physiotherapy. No pharmacologic disease-modifying agent for spondylolisthesis per se.
us_prevalence: ~6–8 million US adults with spondylolisthesis; ~60% of spinal fusion surgeries include spondylolisthesis.
patients_needing_tx: ~3–4 million patients requiring pharmacologic management.
peak_sales: ~$150–300M for novel biomechanical stabilizing drug or bone fusion enhancer.
registrational_endpoint: ODI improvement; NRS back/leg pain at 3 months; slip reduction (% anterior vertebral translation).
btd_threshold_vs_soc: ODI improvement ≥20 points; slip reduction ≥50% at 6 months without surgical fusion in mild cases.

symptoms: Shooting leg pain, numbness in dermatomal pattern, weakness; positive straight leg raise; L4-S1 most common
treatment_goal: Relieve radicular pain; restore neurological function; prevent chronification
first_line_soc: NSAIDs + short course oral steroids. Lumbar transforaminal epidural steroid injection for persistent (>6 weeks) radiculopathy.
treatment_algorithm: NSAIDs (naproxen, diclofenac). Short oral corticosteroid taper. Gabapentin/pregabalin (Lyrica) for neuropathic pain. Muscle relaxants. Lumbar transforaminal ESI: most effective short-term intervention. Physical therapy. Surgery (microdiscectomy, foraminotomy) for refractory motor deficit or bowel/bladder involvement. Novel: intradiscal biological therapies (growth factor injection, notochordal cell-derived conditioned medium).
us_prevalence: ~3–5 million US adults with active lumbosacral radiculopathy.
patients_needing_tx: ~3 million patients requiring pharmacologic/injection treatment.
peak_sales: ~$200–400M peak sales for novel neuroinflammation-targeting agent.
registrational_endpoint: NRS leg pain at 6 weeks; Dolo Osteoarthritis Index; neurological recovery; ODI.
btd_threshold_vs_soc: ≥50% NRS leg pain reduction; neurological improvement ≥1 ASIA grade at 6 weeks vs. NSAIDs + ESI.

symptoms: Neck/arm pain (radiculopathy), numbness/weakness in dermatomal distribution; worsened with neck movement
treatment_goal: Relieve radicular pain; decompress nerve root; restore cervical function
first_line_soc: NSAIDs (naproxen, ibuprofen) + muscle relaxants (cyclobenzaprine). Oral corticosteroid taper (medrol dose pack) for acute radiculopathy. Cervical epidural steroid injection for persistent radiculopathy.
treatment_algorithm: NSAIDs. Muscle relaxants (cyclobenzaprine, methocarbamol). Oral corticosteroid taper (methylprednisolone dose pack × 6 days). Cervical epidural steroid injection (interlaminal or transforaminal): significant pain relief for 6–12 weeks. Gabapentin/pregabalin for neuropathic component. Physical therapy. Soft cervical collar short-term. Surgery (ACDF or posterior foraminotomy) for motor deficit, myelopathy, or >6 weeks failed conservative therapy.
us_prevalence: ~1–2 million new symptomatic cervical disc herniations/year; ~500,000 ACDF surgeries/year.
patients_needing_tx: ~1.5 million patients requiring pharmacologic management.
peak_sales: ~$150–300M peak sales for a novel disc-regenerating biologic or sustained-release epidural drug.
registrational_endpoint: NRS arm pain score at 6 weeks; NDI (Neck Disability Index) at 3 months; neurological recovery.
btd_threshold_vs_soc: ≥50% arm pain NRS reduction; NDI improvement ≥15 points vs. epidural steroid injection at 6 weeks.

symptoms: Neurogenic claudication (leg pain/weakness with walking); relieved by sitting or forward flexion
treatment_goal: Relieve neurogenic claudication; increase walking distance; prevent surgical escalation
first_line_soc: NSAIDs + physical therapy (flexion-based exercises). Epidural steroid injections for short-term claudication relief.
treatment_algorithm: NSAIDs, acetaminophen, duloxetine for pain. Lumbar epidural steroid injection: modest short-term benefit (SPORT trial). Gabapentin/pregabalin for neuropathic leg pain. Physical therapy (aquatic therapy, stationary bike). Calcitonin intranasal (Miacalcin) — some evidence for neurogenic claudication but not FDA-approved for LSS. Surgical decompression (laminectomy): superior to epidurals for claudication at 2 years. Minimally invasive: percutaneous lumbar decompression (mild procedure, FDA cleared 2015 for ligamentum flavum hypertrophy).
us_prevalence: ~600,000–800,000 symptomatic US patients; most common cause of spinal surgery in adults >65.
patients_needing_tx: ~500,000 patients requiring pharmacologic/injection management.
peak_sales: ~$150–300M for novel neurogenic claudication drug (non-surgical option).
registrational_endpoint: Zurich Claudication Questionnaire (ZCQ); treadmill walking distance at 12 weeks; NRS leg pain.
btd_threshold_vs_soc: Walking distance increase ≥50%; ZCQ satisfaction ≥75% at 12 weeks vs. epidural steroid injection.

symptoms: Bilateral hand numbness, gait disturbance, bladder dysfunction; cervical myelopathy in severe cases
treatment_goal: Decompress spinal cord; prevent myelopathic progression; relieve radicular symptoms
first_line_soc: For myelopathy: surgical decompression (ACDF or laminoplasty) is first-line. For symptomatic radiculopathy without myelopathy: NSAIDs + cervical epidural steroid injections.
treatment_algorithm: Radiculopathy without myelopathy: NSAIDs, gabapentin/pregabalin, cervical ESI, physical therapy. Myelopathy: early surgical decompression (ACDF, posterior cervical laminoplasty or laminectomy + fusion). No disease-modifying pharmacotherapy for stenosis itself. Post-surgical: rehabilitation. Neuroprotective agents under investigation (riluzole — Phase 3 AOSpine trials completed, not FDA approved for CSM).
us_prevalence: ~600,000–800,000 US patients with symptomatic cervical spinal stenosis; ~100,000 surgeries/year.
patients_needing_tx: ~400,000 patients seeking pharmacologic pain management.
price: $5,000–$20,000/year for pharmacologic + injection treatment.
peak_sales: Primarily surgical. Novel neuroprotective: ~$100–200M peak sales.
registrational_endpoint: mJOA score improvement at 12 months (myelopathy); NDI; grip strength.
btd_threshold_vs_soc: mJOA improvement ≥2 points; NDI improvement ≥15 points at 12 months vs. conservative management.

symptoms: Pain, instability, or deformity of the coccyx; worse with sitting or standing transitions
treatment_goal: Eliminate pain; restore mobility; prevent chronic pain syndrome
first_line_soc: Conservative: NSAIDs, coccyx cushion, corticosteroid injections. Same as coccygeal pain management.
treatment_algorithm: Identical to coccygeal area pain: NSAIDs, corticosteroid/local anesthetic injections, ganglion impar block, physical therapy, surgical coccygectomy for refractory coccydynia. Fluoroscopic-guided injections improve accuracy. For hypermobility-related coccyx disorders: stabilization exercises.
us_prevalence: ~1–2 million US patients with chronic coccyx-related pain; many post-childbirth or trauma.
patients_needing_tx: ~500,000 patients requiring ongoing pharmacologic or interventional treatment.
price: $1,000–$5,000/treatment course.
peak_sales: Treated with generics. Novel long-acting local anesthetic or neurotoxin: ~$30–60M peak sales.
registrational_endpoint: VAS pain score at rest and sitting at 8 weeks; patient satisfaction; Oswestry score.
btd_threshold_vs_soc: VAS pain reduction ≥40 mm; ≥60% patient satisfaction at 8 weeks vs. standard injection.

symptoms: Neck pain, headache, C2 neurological symptoms; upper cervical instability; risk of spinal cord compression
treatment_goal: Stabilize C1-C2 articulation; prevent cord compression; relieve pain
first_line_soc: Cervical collar immobilization. Treat underlying RA/Down syndrome aggressively with DMARDs. Surgical fusion (C1-C2 arthrodesis) for significant instability or neurologic compromise.
treatment_algorithm: Treat underlying cause: RA — MTX + biologics (reduce pannus); Down syndrome — surgical planning. Rigid cervical orthosis (Halo brace) for acute stabilization. NSAIDs + muscle relaxants for pain. Surgical C1-C2 posterior fusion (Magerl or Goel-Harms technique) for atlantoaxial instability with ≥3–4 mm ADI. Corticosteroid injection (C1-C2 joint) for inflammatory cases.
us_prevalence: ~100,000–200,000 US patients; RA-related most common (~50%), Down syndrome (~15%), post-traumatic.
patients_needing_tx: ~80,000 patients requiring pharmacologic management; ~20,000 needing surgical stabilization.
price: $5,000–$30,000/year for biologics to control RA-related pannus.
peak_sales: ~$50–100M for novel pannus-reducing biologic specific to C1-C2 instability.
registrational_endpoint: Atlantodental interval (ADI) normalization; VAS neck pain; neurological function scale at 12 months.
btd_threshold_vs_soc: ADI normalization ≤3 mm in ≥60%; neurological improvement ≥1 mJOA grade at 12 months vs. standard RA therapy.

symptoms: Tailbone pain worsened by sitting; localized tenderness at coccyx
treatment_goal: Relieve pain; restore sitting tolerance; prevent chronification
first_line_soc: Donut/coccyx cushion, NSAIDs (ibuprofen), local corticosteroid injection.
treatment_algorithm: Coccyx cushion (donut-shaped). NSAIDs for acute pain. Corticosteroid + local anesthetic injection into the sacrococcygeal joint (effective in ~80%). Ganglion impar block for refractory pain. Physical therapy and stretching. Manipulation (manual therapy). Coccygectomy (surgical removal) for refractory cases; high satisfaction rates. Topical diclofenac.
us_prevalence: ~500,000 new cases/year; trauma (falls, childbirth) most common cause.
patients_needing_tx: ~300,000 patients requiring pharmacologic or injection therapy.
price: $500–$3,000/injection course.
peak_sales: Generic-dominated; minimal novel drug opportunity. Peak sales ~$20–40M.
registrational_endpoint: VAS pain sitting score at 6 weeks; Oswestry Disability Index; time to pain resolution.
btd_threshold_vs_soc: VAS reduction ≥50% vs. placebo injection; pain-free sitting ≥1 hour at 6 weeks in ≥60% of patients.

symptoms: Sudden severe joint pain and swelling (big toe, ankle, knee); tophi; hyperuricemia
treatment_goal: Terminate acute flares; lower serum uric acid; prevent tophi and joint damage
first_line_soc: Acute flare: colchicine (0.6 mg BID); NSAIDs (indomethacin); corticosteroids. Urate-lowering therapy (ULT): allopurinol is first-line ULT (target sUA <6 mg/dL).
treatment_algorithm: Acute: colchicine (Colcrys) 1.2 mg then 0.6 mg 1h later; NSAIDs; oral/intra-articular steroids. IL-1 inhibitor canakinumab (Ilaris) or anakinra for refractory/contraindicated cases. ULT: allopurinol (start 100 mg/day, titrate); febuxostat (Uloric) — xanthine oxidase inhibitor, 40–80 mg/day. Probenecid for uricosuric therapy. Lesinurad (Zurampic) — uricosuric, combined with allopurinol (FDA-approved, now discontinued). Pegloticase (Krystexxa) — IV PEGylated uricase for chronic refractory gout; now often combined with methotrexate to reduce immunogenicity. Prophylaxis: colchicine 0.6 mg/day × 3–6 months during ULT initiation.
us_prevalence: ~9–10 million US adults with gout; ~4% US adults. ACR Gout Management Guidelines 2020; NHANES 2024 update.
patients_needing_tx: ~7–8 million patients requiring urate-lowering therapy; ~300,000 with refractory gout needing pegloticase.
price: $15,000–$30,000/year for novel ULT; pegloticase ~$200,000/year.
tam_gross: 7.5M × $20,000 = $150B TAM (including OTC/generic allopurinol territory); novel branded: ~$15B TAM
peak_sales: New mechanism ULT or novel IL-1 blocker for recurrent gout: ~$500M–1.5B peak sales.
registrational_endpoint: Serum uric acid <6 mg/dL responder rate at 6 months; annual flare rate; tophi resolution at 12 months.
btd_threshold_vs_soc: sUA <5 mg/dL in ≥60% at 6 months; annual flare reduction ≥50% vs. allopurinol standard dosing.

disease: Basic Calcium Phosphate Crystal Deposition Disease
symptoms: Acute periarticular pain, swelling at shoulder (calcific tendinitis); can mimic gout
treatment_goal: Relieve acute pain; dissolve calcium deposits; prevent recurrence
first_line_soc: NSAIDs (indomethacin, naproxen) for acute flares. Corticosteroid injection for rapid relief. Colchicine as alternative.
treatment_algorithm: Acute attack: NSAIDs (indomethacin 50 mg TID); colchicine 0.6 mg BID. Corticosteroid injection into affected bursa/joint. Ultrasound-guided needling and lavage (barbotage) for calcific tendinitis. Extracorporeal shockwave therapy (ESWT) for shoulder calcific deposits. Arthroscopic removal for large deposits. Long-term: no urate-lowering equivalent — no approved drug to prevent BCP deposition.
us_prevalence: ~3–5 million US patients; shoulder calcific tendinitis most common manifestation.
patients_needing_tx: ~1.5–2 million patients seeking pharmacologic treatment for acute or recurrent episodes.
price: $3,000–$15,000/year for a novel calcium-solubilizing or anti-inflammatory agent.
peak_sales: ~$150–300M peak sales for a novel preventive or acute therapy.
registrational_endpoint: VAS pain at 4 weeks; crystal deposit size on ultrasound at 12 weeks; time to first recurrence.
btd_threshold_vs_soc: ≥50% reduction in calcium deposit size; VAS pain reduction ≥30 mm vs. placebo at 4 weeks.

symptoms: Acute pseudogout (knee, wrist swelling), chondrocalcinosis on X-ray, chronic arthropathy
treatment_goal: Terminate acute flares; prevent recurrence; manage underlying metabolic cause
first_line_soc: NSAIDs (indomethacin, naproxen) or colchicine for acute pseudogout. Intra-articular corticosteroid injection for monoarticular flares.
treatment_algorithm: Acute pseudogout: NSAIDs (indomethacin 50 mg TID × 7–10 days); colchicine 0.6 mg BID; intra-articular corticosteroid. Refractory/polyarticular: oral prednisone. Prophylaxis: colchicine 0.6 mg QD–BID. No approved pyrophosphate-lowering therapy. Treat underlying: hyperparathyroidism, hemochromatosis, hypomagnesemia, hypophosphatasia. Magnesium supplementation for hypomagnesemia-related CPPD. Investigational: hydroxychloroquine, methotrexate for chronic inflammatory CPPD (ongoing RCTs).
us_prevalence: ~500,000–1,000,000 symptomatic US patients; radiographic prevalence much higher (>50% of adults >85 years). Arthritis Rheum 2023.
patients_needing_tx: ~400,000 patients requiring pharmacologic therapy for acute or chronic CPPD.
price: $10,000–$30,000/year for a novel CPPD-specific agent (no such drug currently approved).
peak_sales: ~$300–600M peak sales for first approved disease-modifying CPPD therapy.
registrational_endpoint: Flare rate per year; VAS pain at 72 hours for acute pseudogout; synovial fluid crystal burden.
btd_threshold_vs_soc: ≥50% reduction in annual flare rate vs. placebo; VAS pain reduction ≥30 mm at 72 hours.

symptoms: Acute arthritis, chondrocalcinosis in patients with renal failure or primary hyperoxaluria
treatment_goal: Manage acute arthritis; reduce oxalate burden; treat underlying cause
first_line_soc: NSAIDs or colchicine for acute attacks. Treat underlying cause: dialysis optimization, vitamin C restriction, pyridoxine for hyperoxaluria.
treatment_algorithm: Acute attacks: NSAIDs (indomethacin), colchicine, or intra-articular corticosteroids. Prevent further oxalate deposition: optimize dialysis (removes oxalate), restrict vitamin C intake, pyridoxine (vitamin B6) for type 1 hyperoxaluria. Lumasiran (siRNA targeting liver glycolate oxidase) — FDA-approved 2020 for primary hyperoxaluria type 1 (PH1); reduces oxalate burden. Nedosiran (siRNA) — FDA-approved 2023 for PH1/PH2. Liver/combined liver-kidney transplant for primary hyperoxaluria.
us_prevalence: ~50,000–100,000 patients with renal failure-associated calcium oxalate arthropathy; primary hyperoxaluria ~2,000–3,000.
patients_needing_tx: ~30,000 patients requiring systemic treatment; ~1,500 with PH1 eligible for lumasiran/nedosiran.
price: $300,000–$600,000/year for siRNA therapies (lumasiran, nedosiran); $2,000–$10,000/year for general management.
peak_sales: Lumasiran (Oxlumo) peak sales ~$200–400M globally; nedosiran competes. Arthropathy alone: ~$20–40M.
registrational_endpoint: Urine oxalate excretion reduction at 6 months (PH); acute arthritis episode rate (arthropathy).
btd_threshold_vs_soc: ≥50% reduction in urine oxalate at 6 months vs. placebo (PH1/siRNA endpoint per FDA approval).

symptoms: Multi-organ fibrosis and inflammation; pancreatic swelling (autoimmune pancreatitis), salivary gland enlargement, orbital disease
treatment_goal: Induce remission; prevent fibrosis progression; reduce mass lesions
first_line_soc: Prednisone (0.6–1 mg/kg/day) is first-line induction; rituximab (anti-CD20) increasingly used upfront for relapsing-remitting or steroid-refractory cases.
treatment_algorithm: Induction: prednisone 0.6–1 mg/kg/day × 4 weeks, then taper over 3–6 months. Maintenance: low-dose prednisone or immunosuppressant (azathioprine, mycophenolate, 6-mercaptopurine). Rituximab (Rituxan) 375 mg/m² × 4 doses for relapsing disease — highly effective (ACR/EULAR guidelines 2024). Inebilizumab (anti-CD19) — positive Phase 3 MITIGATE trial 2024; FDA breakthrough designation. Zilucoplan (anti-C5) investigated. Surgery rarely needed (for mass effect).
us_prevalence: ~20,000–50,000 US patients. Orphan-level prevalence estimated; Asian populations disproportionately affected.
patients_needing_tx: ~25,000–35,000 patients requiring systemic immunosuppression.
price: $50,000–$150,000/year for an approved biologic (rituximab ~$50–100K/year biosimilars).
peak_sales: ~$300–600M peak sales for inebilizumab or similar novel biologic if approved.
registrational_endpoint: IgG4-RD Responder Index (IgG4-RD RI) at 52 weeks; relapse-free survival; serum IgG4 normalization.
btd_threshold_vs_soc: IgG4-RD RI improvement ≥4 points; relapse rate ≤20% at 52 weeks vs. ~50% with corticosteroid alone.

symptoms: Inflammatory joint pain, skin psoriasis, dactylitis, enthesitis, nail pitting; axial involvement
treatment_goal: Inhibit joint inflammation and structural damage; clear skin; restore function
first_line_soc: TNF inhibitors (adalimumab, etanercept, golimumab, infliximab, certolizumab) are most established first-line for moderate-severe PsA. Methotrexate for peripheral arthritis.
treatment_algorithm: Mild PsA: NSAIDs. Peripheral arthritis: methotrexate. Biologics: TNF inhibitors (adalimumab/Humira, etanercept/Enbrel); IL-17A inhibitors (secukinumab/Cosentyx, ixekizumab/Taltz); IL-12/23 inhibitor (ustekinumab/Stelara); IL-23 inhibitors (guselkumab/Tremfya, risankizumab/Skyrizi); JAK inhibitors (tofacitinib/Xeljanz, upadacitinib/Rinvoq). PDE4 inhibitor: apremilast (Otezla — oral, FDA-approved). Bimekizumab (Bimzelx, anti-IL-17A/F) FDA-approved for PsA 2024.
us_prevalence: ~1.5–2.5 million US patients; ~30% of psoriasis patients develop PsA. NPF 2024.
patients_needing_tx: ~1–1.5 million patients requiring biologic or advanced oral therapy.
peak_sales: ~$500M–2B peak sales per novel biologic; highly competitive market.
registrational_endpoint: ACR20 response at 12 weeks; PASI 75 skin response; HAQ-DI at 24 weeks; mTSS (structural) at 52 weeks.
btd_threshold_vs_soc: ACR20 ≥50% vs. ~20% placebo; mTSS non-progression (≤0.5 unit change) at 52 weeks.

symptoms: Symmetric small joint swelling/stiffness, morning stiffness >1 hour, RF/anti-CCP positive, extra-articular manifestations
treatment_goal: Achieve remission or low disease activity; prevent joint erosion and disability
first_line_soc: Methotrexate 15–25 mg/week is the anchor DMARD. Add biologic (TNF inhibitor or JAK inhibitor) for inadequate MTX response.
treatment_algorithm: Step 1: MTX + folic acid; hydroxychloroquine + sulfasalazine as alternatives or triple DMARD therapy. Step 2: TNF inhibitors — adalimumab (Humira/biosimilars), etanercept (Enbrel), infliximab, certolizumab, golimumab; or abatacept (Orencia); or rituximab (Rituxan); or IL-6R inhibitors — tocilizumab (Actemra), sarilumab (Kevzara). JAK inhibitors: tofacitinib (Xeljanz), baricitinib (Olumiant), upadacitinib (Rinvoq). 2024 updates: extensive biosimilar uptake reducing costs; upadacitinib vs. adalimumab head-to-head superiority established.
us_prevalence: ~1.3–1.5 million US adults with RA. Arthritis Foundation 2024 data.
patients_needing_tx: ~1.0–1.2 million patients requiring biologic or advanced therapy.
price: $20,000–$50,000/year (biosimilar TNF-i); $25,000–$60,000 (novel JAK-i, IL-6R-i).
