peak_sales: ~$500M–3B peak sales for novel mechanism; competition intense with biosimilars.
registrational_endpoint: ACR20/50/70 response at 12–24 weeks; DAS28-CRP/ESR; mTSS at 52 weeks; CDAI/SDAI remission rate.
btd_threshold_vs_soc: ACR50 ≥40% at 12 weeks; DAS28 remission ≥30% at 24 weeks; mTSS non-progression at 52 weeks.

symptoms: Inflammatory back pain, peripheral arthritis, enthesitis, uveitis; RF negative; HLA-B27 positive
treatment_goal: Control inflammation; prevent ankylosis; maintain spinal mobility
first_line_soc: NSAIDs (continuous use for axial disease) + physical therapy. TNF inhibitors or IL-17A inhibitors for NSAID-refractory.
treatment_algorithm: NSAIDs: first-line for all spondyloarthropathies. TNF inhibitors: adalimumab, certolizumab, golimumab (all approved for SpA). IL-17A inhibitors: secukinumab (Cosentyx), ixekizumab (Taltz). JAK inhibitors: upadacitinib (Rinvoq) — FDA-approved 2022 for PsA and AS; tofacitinib. Bimekizumab (anti-IL-17A/F) FDA-approved 2023. Sulfasalazine for peripheral arthritis. Includes reactive arthritis, IBD-associated arthritis, undifferentiated SpA.
us_prevalence: ~1.5–2.5 million US adults across all spondyloarthropathy subtypes.
patients_needing_tx: ~800,000 patients requiring biologic therapy.
peak_sales: ~$500M–1.5B peak sales for novel mechanism (IL-17, JAK-i, etc.).
registrational_endpoint: ASAS40 at 16 weeks; BASDAI 50% response; PASI 75 for psoriatic subtype.
btd_threshold_vs_soc: ASAS40 ≥40% at 16 weeks; ASDAS inactive disease ≥30% at 16 weeks vs. ~10% placebo.

symptoms: Chronic inflammatory back pain, morning stiffness >1 hour, sacroiliitis; improves with exercise
treatment_goal: Reduce spinal inflammation; prevent ankylosis; improve mobility and function
first_line_soc: NSAIDs (indomethacin, naproxen, diclofenac, celecoxib) are first-line: anti-inflammatory + may slow radiographic progression. TNF inhibitors (adalimumab, etanercept, certolizumab) for NSAID-inadequate response.
treatment_algorithm: Step 1: NSAIDs continuously (not PRN) — reduce CRP, improve BASDAI. Step 2: TNF inhibitors — adalimumab (Humira), etanercept (Enbrel), infliximab (Remicade), golimumab (Simponi), certolizumab (Cimzia). Step 3: IL-17A inhibitors — secukinumab (Cosentyx, 2016 FDA approval), ixekizumab (Taltz) — equivalent efficacy to TNF-i; preferred when comorbid IBD absent. JAK inhibitors: upadacitinib (Rinvoq) FDA-approved 2022 for AS; tofacitinib (XELJANZ). Bimekizumab (Bimzelx — anti-IL-17A/F) FDA-approved 2023 for AS.
us_prevalence: ~1–1.4 million US adults with ankylosing spondylitis. ACR AS data 2024.
patients_needing_tx: ~600,000 patients requiring biologics or JAK inhibitors.
price: $30,000–$70,000/year for biosimilar TNF-i; $50,000–$80,000 for novel IL-17/JAK inhibitor.
peak_sales: Competitive biologic market. New class (JAK-i + IL-17): ~$500M–1.5B peak sales per agent.
registrational_endpoint: ASAS40 response rate at 16 weeks; BASDAI 50% improvement; ASDAS-CRP change.
btd_threshold_vs_soc: ASAS40 response ≥40% vs. ~25% placebo; ASDAS major improvement (decrease ≥2.0 from baseline) at 16 weeks.

symptoms: Asymmetric oligoarthritis following GI or urogenital infection; urethritis, conjunctivitis triad (Reiter syndrome)
treatment_goal: Control inflammation; eliminate triggering pathogen; prevent chronification
first_line_soc: NSAIDs (indomethacin, naproxen) are first-line for acute arthritis. Antibiotics for active C. trachomatis or enteric infection (doxycycline × 3 months for chlamydial reactive arthritis).
treatment_algorithm: NSAIDs for acute joint inflammation. Antibiotics: doxycycline 100 mg BID × 3 months (evidence for chlamydial reactive arthritis). Intra-articular corticosteroids. Systemic corticosteroids for severe cases. Sulfasalazine for chronic/refractory cases (1–2 g/day). Methotrexate for refractory peripheral arthritis. TNF inhibitors (off-label) for chronic reactive arthritis resistant to DMARDs.
us_prevalence: ~150,000–250,000 new cases/year; ~10% become chronic.
patients_needing_tx: ~200,000 patients/year requiring pharmacologic therapy; ~20,000 with chronic disease needing DMARDs.
price: $1,000–$20,000/year depending on severity.
peak_sales: ~$100–200M peak sales for a novel targeted anti-inflammatory for chronic reactive arthritis.
registrational_endpoint: ACR20 response at 12 weeks; swollen joint count reduction; chlamydial DNA eradication.
btd_threshold_vs_soc: ACR20 ≥50% at 12 weeks; chronic reactive arthritis remission rate ≥60% at 6 months vs. ~20–30% with NSAIDs.

symptoms: Dry eyes (keratoconjunctivitis sicca), dry mouth (xerostomia), fatigue, arthralgia; anti-SSA/SSB antibodies
treatment_goal: Relieve sicca symptoms; prevent systemic complications; manage fatigue
first_line_soc: Artificial tears + pilocarpine (Salagen) or cevimeline (Evoxac) for dry eye/mouth. Hydroxychloroquine for systemic/articular manifestations.
treatment_algorithm: Dry eyes: artificial tears, cyclosporine ophthalmic (Restasis), lifitegrast (Xiidra). Dry mouth: pilocarpine (Salagen) 5 mg QID; cevimeline (Evoxac) 30 mg TID. Systemic: hydroxychloroquine 200–400 mg/day. Immunosuppressants for organ involvement: methotrexate, azathioprine. Rituximab for lymphoma or severe systemic disease. Novel: iscalimab (anti-CD40L, COMET trial 2024); ianalumab (anti-BAFF-R, Phase 3 TWINSS trial); remibrutinib (BTK inhibitor); VAY736 (BAFF inhibitor). Multiple Phase 3 programs active 2024–2025.
us_prevalence: ~3–4 million US patients; predominantly middle-aged women; SSF Foundation data 2024.
patients_needing_tx: ~2–2.5 million patients requiring systemic pharmacotherapy.
price: $30,000–$80,000/year for a novel approved biologic.
peak_sales: ~$1–3B peak sales for first approved systemic biologic for Sjögren syndrome.
registrational_endpoint: ESSDAI (EULAR Sjögren Syndrome Disease Activity Index) at 24 weeks; ESSPRI (patient-reported); salivary flow; anti-SSA titer reduction.
btd_threshold_vs_soc: ESSDAI improvement ≥3 points; ESSPRI improvement ≥1 point vs. hydroxychloroquine at 24 weeks.

symptoms: Multi-organ inflammation; malar rash, arthritis, nephritis, serositis; ANA + ds-DNA antibodies
treatment_goal: Induce remission; prevent flares and organ damage; protect kidneys
first_line_soc: Hydroxychloroquine (Plaquenil) 200–400 mg/day is foundational. Oral corticosteroids for flares. Belimumab (Benlysta, anti-BLyS) FDA-approved for active SLE.
treatment_algorithm: Hydroxychloroquine (universal foundational therapy). Corticosteroids (prednisone) for flares. Immunosuppressants: methotrexate (skin/joints), azathioprine (maintenance), mycophenolate mofetil (nephritis). Biologics: belimumab (Benlysta, IV/SC — 2011/2020 approvals); anifrolumab (Saphnelo, anti-IFN-αR — FDA approved 2021). Voclosporin (Lupkynis) + mycophenolate for lupus nephritis — FDA approved 2021. Obinutuzumab (anti-CD20, Phase 3 REGENCY trial 2024). Avacopan (complement inhibitor) for lupus nephritis in trials. Novel: deucravacitinib (TYK2 inhibitor, Phase 3 POETYK-LN) showing promise.
us_prevalence: ~1.5–2.0 million US patients. LFA National Data 2024; predominantly women of color.
patients_needing_tx: ~1.2–1.5 million patients requiring pharmacologic therapy.
price: $30,000–$80,000/year for novel biologics.
peak_sales: ~$500M–2B peak sales per novel mechanism. TYK2 inhibitor or B-cell depleter: highest potential.
registrational_endpoint: SLE Responder Index-4 (SRI-4) at 52 weeks; BILAG-based Composite Lupus Assessment (BICLA); annualized flare rate; eGFR at 52 weeks for nephritis.
btd_threshold_vs_soc: SRI-4 response ≥50% at 52 weeks vs. ~35% for SoC; flare rate reduction ≥40%; lupus nephritis complete renal response ≥40% at 52 weeks.

symptoms: Proximal muscle weakness, elevated CK, skin rash (dermatomyositis), interstitial lung disease risk
treatment_goal: Restore muscle strength; prevent ILD progression; induce remission
first_line_soc: High-dose corticosteroids (prednisone 1 mg/kg/day) + methotrexate or azathioprine as steroid-sparing agents. IVIG for refractory dermatomyositis.
treatment_algorithm: Induction: prednisone 1 mg/kg/day. Steroid-sparing: methotrexate 15–25 mg/week; azathioprine 2–3 mg/kg/day. IVIG 2 g/kg/month for refractory dermatomyositis — FDA-approved indication (Octagam, 2021). Rituximab for anti-synthetase syndrome/refractory IIM (RIM trial evidence). Tacrolimus for IIM-associated ILD. JAK inhibitors (ruxolitinib, baricitinib, tofacitinib) — emerging evidence for DM including MDA5-positive. Novel: efgartigimod (FcRn inhibitor) in Phase 3 for IIM 2024.
us_prevalence: ~100,000–200,000 US patients across all IIM subtypes. NIH/NIAMS data.
patients_needing_tx: ~80,000–120,000 patients requiring pharmacologic treatment.
price: $50,000–$200,000/year for targeted biologic.
peak_sales: ~$400–800M peak sales for a novel targeted therapy (anti-JAK, FcRn inhibitor).
registrational_endpoint: MMT8 (manual muscle testing) score; IMACS Total Improvement Score (TIS); CK normalization at 24 weeks.
btd_threshold_vs_soc: TIS ≥50% improvement; MMT8 improvement ≥8 points vs. corticosteroids alone at 24 weeks.

symptoms: Overlapping features of SLE, SSc, and myositis; Raynaud's, swollen hands, U1-RNP antibodies positive
treatment_goal: Control inflammation; treat dominant organ manifestation; prevent vascular/pulmonary complications
first_line_soc: Hydroxychloroquine (Plaquenil) as baseline therapy + low-dose corticosteroids. Treatment of dominant phenotype: SLE-like → immunosuppressants; SSc-like → vasodilators; myositis → steroids/MTX.
treatment_algorithm: Hydroxychloroquine 200–400 mg/day (foundational). Low-dose prednisone. For Raynaud's/vascular: calcium channel blockers (nifedipine, amlodipine), bosentan, sildenafil. PAH: PAH-specific therapy (see PH section). For arthritis: methotrexate, sulfasalazine. For ILD: mycophenolate mofetil, nintedanib. For myositis: IVIG, azathioprine. Belimumab (anti-BLyS) and voclosporin for SLE features. Rituximab for refractory MCTD.
us_prevalence: ~100,000–200,000 US patients. Orphan-level estimates; actual diagnosis underrepresented.
patients_needing_tx: ~100,000–150,000 patients requiring systemic immunosuppression.
peak_sales: ~$200–400M for a novel MCTD-specific biologic addressing U1-RNP pathway.
registrational_endpoint: Physician Global Assessment; Raynaud's Condition Score; change in dominant manifestation activity score at 52 weeks.
btd_threshold_vs_soc: PGA improvement ≥50%; Raynaud's attack frequency reduction ≥40%; dominant organ response ≥60% at 52 weeks.

symptoms: Raynaud's phenomenon, skin thickening (fibrosis), ILD, pulmonary hypertension, GI dysmotility
treatment_goal: Control Raynaud's; slow skin/lung fibrosis; prevent vascular crises
first_line_soc: Raynaud's: dihydropyridine CCBs (nifedipine). ILD: mycophenolate mofetil (SLS II trial) or nintedanib (Ofev, FDA-approved 2019 for SSc-ILD). Scleroderma renal crisis: ACE inhibitors (captopril).
treatment_algorithm: Raynaud's: nifedipine extended-release; bosentan (Tracleer) to prevent digital ulcers (FDA/EMA). Digital ulcers: sildenafil, epoprostenol. ILD: nintedanib (Ofev) or mycophenolate mofetil + nintedanib (SENSCIS+ trial). PAH: PAH-specific therapy (see PAH section). Scleroderma renal crisis: ACE inhibitors (captopril). Skin fibrosis: tocilizumab (Actemra, FDA-approved for SSc-ILD 2021); rituximab. GI: proton pump inhibitors, prokinetics (domperidone, pyridostigmine). Antifibrotic trials: ROCK inhibitors, lysophospholipid receptor antagonists in Phase 2–3.
us_prevalence: ~100,000 US patients; ~25–30 per 100,000. Scleroderma Foundation data 2024.
patients_needing_tx: ~70,000–80,000 patients requiring systemic immunosuppression or anti-fibrotic.
price: $50,000–$150,000/year for novel anti-fibrotic or immunomodulator.
peak_sales: ~$500M–1.5B peak sales for novel SSc-specific agent addressing both fibrosis and vascular disease.
registrational_endpoint: Modified Rodnan Skin Score (mRSS) change at 48 weeks; FVC change; HAQ-DI; time to SSc-related death.
btd_threshold_vs_soc: mRSS reduction ≥5 units; FVC decline ≤3% vs. >5% for placebo at 48 weeks.

symptoms: Recurrent inflammation of cartilage (ears, nose, trachea); auricular chondritis, saddle nose, airway involvement
treatment_goal: Suppress cartilaginous inflammation; prevent structural collapse; manage airway risk
first_line_soc: NSAIDs for mild auricular chondritis. Oral corticosteroids (prednisone 1 mg/kg/day) for systemic or severe cases. Dapsone as steroid-sparing agent.
treatment_algorithm: Mild: NSAIDs, colchicine, dapsone 50–100 mg/day. Moderate-severe: prednisone 1 mg/kg/day. Steroid-sparing: methotrexate, azathioprine, cyclosporine, leflunomide. Refractory: TNF inhibitors (infliximab, adalimumab — case series evidence), tocilizumab (IL-6R). JAK inhibitors (baricitinib, ruxolitinib) — emerging evidence (2024 case series). Airway involvement: tracheostomy if subglottic stenosis severe. Aortic valvular replacement for cardiac complications.
us_prevalence: ~5,000–10,000 US patients. Orphan disease. NORD estimates.
patients_needing_tx: ~5,000–8,000 patients requiring systemic pharmacotherapy.
price: $50,000–$150,000/year for an orphan biologic.
peak_sales: ~$80–150M peak sales for a novel biologic approved specifically for relapsing polychondritis.
registrational_endpoint: Disease Activity Index for Relapsing Polychondritis (DAIRP); auricular chondritis flare rate; respiratory function (FVC, peak expiratory flow).
btd_threshold_vs_soc: Flare reduction ≥50%; DAIRP improvement ≥30%; steroid dose reduction ≥50% vs. prednisone alone at 52 weeks.

symptoms: Woody skin induration, peau d'orange texture, eosinophilia, limb swelling sparing skin over digits
treatment_goal: Suppress eosinophilic inflammation; prevent fascial fibrosis; restore limb mobility
first_line_soc: High-dose corticosteroids (prednisone 1 mg/kg/day) are the cornerstone of therapy; most patients respond well.
treatment_algorithm: High-dose prednisone 1 mg/kg/day (up to 60 mg/day) × 4–6 weeks; then taper over 12–24 months. Steroid-refractory: hydroxychloroquine, methotrexate, cyclosporine, D-penicillamine. Newer agents: dupilumab (IL-4/13 inhibitor) — case reports of dramatic response; Phase 2 trials planned 2024. Cimetidine (H2 blocker) — occasional use, mechanism unclear. IVIG for severe refractory cases. Physiotherapy for joint contracture.
us_prevalence: ~3,000–5,000 US cases; rare connective tissue disease. No registry data.
patients_needing_tx: ~3,000 patients requiring systemic immunosuppression.
price: $50,000–$150,000/year for a novel biologic.
peak_sales: ~$30–60M peak sales for a novel steroid-sparing biologic.
registrational_endpoint: Modified Rodnan Skin Score (mRSS) improvement; range-of-motion recovery at 24 weeks; eosinophil count normalization.
btd_threshold_vs_soc: mRSS improvement ≥30%; ROM recovery ≥20° in affected joints vs. prednisone alone at 24 weeks.

symptoms: Bilateral shoulder and pelvic girdle pain and stiffness, morning stiffness >45 min, elevated ESR/CRP; age >50
treatment_goal: Rapid symptom relief; prevent GCA co-occurrence; minimize steroid toxicity
first_line_soc: Low-dose prednisone (15–20 mg/day) provides dramatic, rapid improvement (diagnostic response) — first-line treatment.
treatment_algorithm: Prednisone 15–20 mg/day tapering over 12–24 months (ACR/EULAR 2015 guidelines). Methotrexate as steroid-sparing for relapsers. Tocilizumab (Actemra) — positive Phase 3 TENOR trial 2023 as steroid-sparing in PMR; FDA consideration pending 2024. IL-6R pathway key. Sarilumab also in Phase 3 trials for PMR (SAPHYR trial). Calcium + vitamin D with steroids. Aspirin for GCA monitoring.
us_prevalence: ~700,000–900,000 US patients; most common inflammatory rheumatic disease in adults >50. ACR 2024.
patients_needing_tx: ~600,000 patients requiring pharmacologic management.
price: $20,000–$60,000/year for novel steroid-sparing biologic.
peak_sales: ~$500M–1.5B peak sales for an approved IL-6R inhibitor with PMR indication.
registrational_endpoint: PMR-AS (Activity Score) at 12 weeks; prednisone dose at 24 weeks; relapse rate at 52 weeks.
btd_threshold_vs_soc: PMR-AS <10 at 12 weeks; prednisone ≤5 mg/day in ≥50% at 24 weeks; relapse rate ≤20% at 52 weeks vs. ~50% with prednisone alone.

symptoms: Temporal headache, jaw claudication, visual loss risk, elevated ESR/CRP; age >50
treatment_goal: Prevent blindness and aortic complications; suppress granulomatous inflammation; taper steroids
first_line_soc: High-dose oral corticosteroids (prednisone 40–60 mg/day; IV pulse for visual symptoms). Tocilizumab (Actemra) — IL-6R inhibitor — FDA-approved 2017 as steroid-sparing adjunct (GiACTA trial).
treatment_algorithm: Prednisone 40–60 mg/day (or IV methylprednisolone 1 g × 3 days for visual symptoms). Tocilizumab (Actemra) SC 162 mg/week: FDA-approved as steroid-sparing in GCA — reduces relapse rate, enables faster steroid taper. Aspirin 81 mg/day (reduces ischemic events). Methotrexate as steroid-sparing alternative (modest evidence). Novel: avacopan (C5a receptor inhibitor, Phase 3 ROCK-GCA trial completed 2024 — positive); secukinumab in Phase 3 (TitAIN trial 2024 — positive for sustained remission). Upadacitinib in trials.
us_prevalence: ~200,000–250,000 US patients; most common vasculitis in adults >50 years. ACR 2024.
patients_needing_tx: ~150,000 patients requiring active pharmacotherapy.
price: $25,000–$80,000/year for novel biologic (tocilizumab ~$30,000–$50,000/year).
peak_sales: ~$300–600M peak sales for novel GCA-specific biologic competing with tocilizumab.
registrational_endpoint: Sustained remission rate at 52 weeks without ≥10 mg prednisone; relapse-free survival; GCA flare rate.
btd_threshold_vs_soc: Sustained remission ≥50% at 52 weeks vs. ~23% placebo; relapse reduction ≥50% vs. prednisone alone (GiACTA benchmark).

symptoms: Sinusitis, pulmonary infiltrates/nodules, rapidly progressive glomerulonephritis; ANCA-PR3 positive
treatment_goal: Induce ANCA-associated vasculitis remission; preserve renal function; prevent relapse
first_line_soc: Rituximab (Rituxan, anti-CD20) + high-dose methylprednisolone for induction (RAVE trial superiority over cyclophosphamide). Avacopan (Tavneos) replaces high-dose corticosteroids (FDA-approved 2021).
treatment_algorithm: Induction: rituximab 375 mg/m² × 4 doses (or 1g × 2 doses) + IV/oral corticosteroids. Alternatively: cyclophosphamide IV + corticosteroids. Avacopan (Tavneos) 30 mg BID — FDA approved October 2021 — replaces high-dose prednisone in induction; reduces serious adverse events. Maintenance: rituximab 500 mg × 2 doses at 6 months, then 500 mg every 6 months (MAINRITSAN trials). Alternative maintenance: azathioprine. Novel: obinutuzumab (stronger B-cell depletion) in trials; belimumab add-on; PI3Kδ inhibitors.
us_prevalence: ~20,000–30,000 US patients. VCRC/ACR ANCA-AAV registry.
patients_needing_tx: ~20,000 patients requiring systemic immunosuppression.
peak_sales: ~$200–400M peak sales for a novel next-generation ANCA-AAV agent; avacopan (Tavneos) peaked ~$200M by 2024.
registrational_endpoint: BVAS/WG remission (≤1) at 6 months; eGFR change at 12 months; time to relapse; VDI at 12 months.
btd_threshold_vs_soc: Remission rate ≥70% at 6 months; eGFR preservation ≥80% vs. ~60% with cyclophosphamide; relapse reduction ≥40%.

symptoms: Rapidly progressive glomerulonephritis, pulmonary hemorrhage, mononeuritis multiplex; ANCA-MPO positive
treatment_goal: Induce remission; preserve renal function; prevent relapse
first_line_soc: Rituximab + high-dose methylprednisolone (RAVE/RITUXVAS) or cyclophosphamide + methylprednisolone. Avacopan (Tavneos) as steroid-sparing agent in induction.
treatment_algorithm: Same as GPA/ANCA-AAV protocol. Induction: rituximab (preferred for MPO-ANCA MPA per current guidelines) + avacopan (steroid-replacing). Cyclophosphamide alternative. Plasmapheresis for severe alveolar hemorrhage. Maintenance: rituximab 500 mg q6 months; azathioprine alternative. Dialysis for end-stage renal disease; transplant consideration.
us_prevalence: ~10,000–15,000 US patients. Smaller than GPA; MPO-ANCA predominant.
patients_needing_tx: ~10,000 patients requiring active systemic therapy.
peak_sales: ~$100–200M peak sales; often bundled with GPA in ANCA-AAV programs.
registrational_endpoint: BVAS/WG ≤1 at 6 months; eGFR preservation; dialysis-free survival at 12 months.
btd_threshold_vs_soc: Remission rate ≥70% at 6 months; dialysis-free rate ≥80% vs. ~65% with cyclophosphamide alone.

symptoms: Recurrent oral aphthous ulcers, genital ulcers, uveitis, skin lesions; pathergy positive
treatment_goal: Prevent recurrent ulcers and ocular damage; control systemic inflammation
first_line_soc: Colchicine (0.5–2 mg/day) for mucocutaneous manifestations. Azathioprine + corticosteroids for ocular involvement. Apremilast (Otezla) FDA-approved in 2023 for Behçet oral ulcers.
treatment_algorithm: Mucocutaneous: colchicine; apremilast (Otezla 30 mg BID — FDA approved 2023 for oral ulcers). Ocular: azathioprine + corticosteroids; infliximab (TNF-α inhibitor) for posterior uveitis (most effective). Tocilizumab for refractory uveitis. Neurological/vascular Behçet: high-dose corticosteroids + cyclophosphamide or azathioprine. Secukinumab (anti-IL-17) showing promise in trials. Thalidomide for oral ulcers (teratogenic, limited use). JAK inhibitors under investigation.
us_prevalence: ~15,000–25,000 US patients; Middle Eastern/Mediterranean and Asian populations disproportionately affected.
patients_needing_tx: ~15,000 patients requiring systemic pharmacotherapy.
peak_sales: ~$100–200M peak sales for a novel Behçet-specific biologic.
registrational_endpoint: Oral ulcer count at 12 weeks (BDCAF — Behçet Disease Current Activity Form); uveitis flare rate; genital ulcer resolution.
btd_threshold_vs_soc: ≥50% reduction in oral ulcer count; ≥60% uveitis flare reduction vs. colchicine alone at 12 weeks.

symptoms: Palpable purpura (lower extremities), arthritis, abdominal pain, renal involvement (IgA nephropathy); Henoch-Schönlein Purpura
treatment_goal: Control skin/GI/joint symptoms; prevent nephritis progression; maintain renal function
first_line_soc: Corticosteroids (prednisone 1 mg/kg/day) for significant GI or renal involvement. NSAIDs for arthritis. Supportive care for mild cases.
treatment_algorithm: Mild: supportive care, NSAIDs for arthritis, hydration. GI involvement: prednisone 1–2 mg/kg/day. Nephritis: corticosteroids ± azathioprine or cyclophosphamide for severe nephritis. IgA nephropathy-specific: sparsentan (Filspari, FDA-approved 2023 for proteinuric IgA nephropathy) — dual endothelin/angiotensin receptor antagonist; SGLT2 inhibitors (empagliflozin, dapagliflozin) for nephroprotection. Iptacopan (anti-factor B complement inhibitor) FDA-approved 2024 for IgA nephropathy. Novel: atrasentan, zigakibart in trials.
us_prevalence: ~30,000–50,000 US cases/year; mostly children aged 3–10 but also adults.
patients_needing_tx: ~20,000 patients requiring pharmacologic treatment; ~5,000 with nephritis needing aggressive therapy.
price: $50,000–$150,000/year for novel nephroprotective agent; $5,000–$20,000 for corticosteroids.
peak_sales: ~$100–200M peak sales for novel IgAV-specific nephroprotective agent; significant overlap with IgA nephropathy market.
registrational_endpoint: eGFR change at 12 months; proteinuria reduction (urine protein:creatinine ratio) at 9 months; complete renal remission rate.
btd_threshold_vs_soc: eGFR preservation vs. decline; proteinuria reduction ≥40%; complete renal remission ≥40% at 12 months vs. standard corticosteroids.

symptoms: Palpable purpura on lower extremities; possible arthralgias; skin biopsy shows leukocytoclastic vasculitis
treatment_goal: Control skin inflammation; identify and remove triggering cause; prevent systemic extension
first_line_soc: Remove trigger (drug, infection). For persistent cases: oral corticosteroids (prednisone 20–40 mg/day). Colchicine or dapsone for chronic limited cutaneous vasculitis.
treatment_algorithm: Identify and treat trigger (stop offending drug, treat infection). Self-limited: supportive care, leg elevation. Persistent/recurrent: colchicine 0.6 mg BID; dapsone 50–100 mg/day; pentoxifylline. Corticosteroids for rapid control. Severe/systemic extension: azathioprine, mycophenolate, cyclophosphamide. Rituximab for B-cell-associated cutaneous vasculitis. Intravenous immunoglobulin (IVIG) for severe cases.
us_prevalence: ~50,000–100,000 US cases/year; many are drug-induced or infection-associated.
patients_needing_tx: ~40,000 patients requiring pharmacologic treatment.
peak_sales: ~$50–100M peak sales for a novel targeted anti-vasculitis agent.
registrational_endpoint: Purpura resolution at 4 weeks; renal involvement rate; Vasculitis Damage Index (VDI) at 12 months.
btd_threshold_vs_soc: Purpura complete resolution ≥80% at 4 weeks; renal involvement rate ≤5% vs. ~15% untreated at 12 months.

symptoms: Asthma, peripheral eosinophilia, sinusitis, granulomatous vasculitis; peripheral neuropathy; cardiac involvement
treatment_goal: Induce remission; control eosinophilic inflammation; preserve cardiac/nerve function
first_line_soc: High-dose corticosteroids (prednisone 1 mg/kg/day) ± cyclophosphamide for organ-threatening disease. Mepolizumab (Nucala, anti-IL-5) FDA-approved 2017 for EGPA — reduces eosinophilia and relapse rate.
treatment_algorithm: Induction: prednisone 1 mg/kg/day ± cyclophosphamide (or rituximab) for FFS ≥1 disease. Remission maintenance: azathioprine, methotrexate. Mepolizumab (Nucala) 300 mg SC monthly — steroid-sparing; FDA approved for EGPA 2017 (MIRRA trial). Benralizumab (Fasenra, anti-IL-5Rα) — Phase 3 MANDARA trial 2024: superior to mepolizumab in some endpoints. Dupilumab for refractory asthma component. IVIG for neuropathy.
us_prevalence: ~5,000–10,000 US patients. Orphan disease. VCRC registry.
patients_needing_tx: ~5,000–7,000 patients requiring active systemic therapy.
peak_sales: ~$100–200M peak sales for next-generation eosinophil-targeting biologic.
registrational_endpoint: Remission rate at 36 weeks (BVAS/WG score); relapse-free survival; accrued prednisone dose reduction.
btd_threshold_vs_soc: Remission rate ≥60% at 36 weeks vs. ~35% for mepolizumab; prednisone ≤4 mg/day in ≥50% at 52 weeks.

symptoms: Upper extremity claudication, pulselessness, hypertension, carotid/aortic bruits; young women; large-vessel vasculitis
treatment_goal: Suppress aortic wall inflammation; prevent ischemic complications; enable revascularization
first_line_soc: High-dose corticosteroids (prednisone 1 mg/kg/day). Methotrexate or azathioprine as steroid-sparing agents. Tocilizumab increasingly used for refractory cases.
treatment_algorithm: Prednisone 1 mg/kg/day × 4 weeks, then taper. Methotrexate 15–25 mg/week; azathioprine 2 mg/kg/day as steroid-sparing. Tocilizumab (Actemra) — retrospective/open-label evidence; Phase 3 TOCITAKA trial in progress 2024. Secukinumab in trials. Anti-TNF agents (infliximab, adalimumab) for refractory cases. Antiplatelet therapy (aspirin) for ischemic risk. Surgical/endovascular revascularization when disease is quiescent.
us_prevalence: ~10,000–15,000 US patients; predominantly young women of Asian descent.
patients_needing_tx: ~8,000 patients requiring active systemic therapy.
peak_sales: ~$80–150M peak sales for novel Takayasu-approved biologic.
registrational_endpoint: Indian Takayasu Activity Score (ITAS2010); PET-CT aortic inflammation score; prednisone dose at 52 weeks; revascularization rate.
btd_threshold_vs_soc: ITAS remission (ITAS ≤4) in ≥50%; prednisone ≤5 mg/day in ≥50% at 52 weeks vs. ~25–30% with prednisone + methotrexate.

symptoms: Fever, weight loss, renal involvement, peripheral neuropathy, abdominal pain, hypertension; medium-vessel vasculitis
treatment_goal: Suppress medium-vessel inflammation; prevent organ damage; achieve remission
first_line_soc: High-dose prednisone (1 mg/kg/day) is first-line. Cyclophosphamide added for severe/renal involvement (FFS ≥1).
treatment_algorithm: Prednisone 1 mg/kg/day. Cyclophosphamide IV or oral for FFS ≥1 disease (5-factor score: proteinuria, renal insufficiency, cardiomyopathy, GI involvement, CNS). Hepatitis B-associated PAN: antiviral therapy (tenofovir, entecavir) + short-course steroids + plasma exchange. Rituximab for refractory cases (limited evidence; ANCA-positive variant more responsive). Azathioprine/methotrexate for maintenance.
us_prevalence: ~5,000–10,000 US patients; rare. VCRC registry.
patients_needing_tx: ~6,000 patients requiring systemic immunosuppression.
peak_sales: ~$60–120M peak sales for a novel PAN-targeted immunosuppressant.
registrational_endpoint: BVAS/PAN remission at 6 months; relapse-free survival at 18 months; eGFR preservation.
btd_threshold_vs_soc: Remission rate ≥70%; relapse-free survival ≥60% at 18 months vs. ~40–50% with cyclophosphamide + prednisone.

symptoms: Orthostatic hypotension, bowel/bladder dysfunction, anhidrosis, tachycardia
treatment_goal: Manage orthostatic hypotension, improve autonomic symptoms
first_line_soc: Midodrine (ProAmatine) — alpha-1 agonist — or fludrocortisone (Florinef) — mineralocorticoid — for orthostatic hypotension.
treatment_algorithm: Non-pharmacologic: compression stockings, salt loading, head-of-bed elevation. Midodrine 2.5–10 mg TID (avoid supine hypertension). Fludrocortisone 0.1–0.4 mg/day. Droxidopa (Northera) — norepinephrine precursor — FDA-approved for neurogenic orthostatic hypotension. Pyridostigmine (30–60 mg) as add-on. Treat underlying cause (diabetes, amyloid, immune).
us_prevalence: ~1–2 million Americans with clinically significant autonomic neuropathy; ~500,000 severe cases.
patients_needing_tx: ~500,000 require pharmacologic management.
price: $5,000–$20,000/year for a novel autonomic stabilizer.
registrational_endpoint: Orthostatic hypotension magnitude (systolic BP drop) and OHSA composite score at 12 weeks vs. midodrine.
btd_threshold_vs_soc: ≥15 mmHg less orthostatic BP drop vs. midodrine, or superior OHSA composite score.

symptoms: Parkinsonism, cerebellar ataxia, autonomic failure, rapid progression
treatment_goal: Manage autonomic symptoms, maintain function, slow progression
first_line_soc: No disease-modifying therapy — symptomatic: midodrine or fludrocortisone for orthostatic hypotension; levodopa (limited benefit).
treatment_algorithm: Orthostatic hypotension: midodrine, fludrocortisone, droxidopa. Parkinsonism: trial of levodopa (often minimal response). Urinary dysfunction: anticholinergics (oxybutynin) or α-blockers (tamsulosin). Constipation: polyethylene glycol. REM sleep disorder: clonazepam or melatonin. No disease-modifying agents approved. Investigational: moxifloxacin, rifampicin (anti-aggregation), mesenchymal stem cells.
us_prevalence: ~15,000–20,000 MSA patients in the US (prevalence ~4–5 per 100,000).
patients_needing_tx: ~18,000 require pharmacologic symptomatic management.
price: $50,000–$150,000/year for a disease-modifying agent.
registrational_endpoint: Unified MSA Rating Scale (UMSARS) total score change at 52 weeks vs. placebo.
btd_threshold_vs_soc: ≥4-point improvement in UMSARS total score vs. placebo at 52 weeks.

symptoms: Orthostatic hypotension, syncope, bladder dysfunction, constipation, without somatic neuropathy
treatment_goal: Prevent syncopal falls, manage orthostatic hypotension
first_line_soc: Midodrine (2.5–10 mg TID) — alpha-1 agonist — or droxidopa (Northera 100–600 mg TID) for neurogenic orthostatic hypotension.
treatment_algorithm: Non-pharmacologic: increased salt/fluid intake, compression garments. Midodrine + fludrocortisone ± pyridostigmine. Droxidopa (Northera) — FDA-approved for neurogenic OH. Octreotide for postprandial hypotension. Head-of-bed elevation.
us_prevalence: ~5,000–10,000 PAF patients in the US (rare; may evolve to MSA or Parkinson's).
patients_needing_tx: ~8,000 require pharmacologic management.
price: $20,000–$60,000/year for a novel autonomic stabilizer.
registrational_endpoint: Standing SBP and OHSA score at 8 weeks vs. midodrine or droxidopa.
btd_threshold_vs_soc: ≥10 mmHg improvement in standing SBP or superior OHSA composite vs. droxidopa.

symptoms: Ptosis, miosis, anhidrosis (ipsilateral), with underlying cause symptoms
treatment_goal: Identify and treat underlying cause (e.g., carotid dissection, lung tumor)
first_line_soc: None pharmacologic for Horner itself — treatment targets underlying etiology (e.g., anticoagulation for carotid dissection, tumor treatment).
treatment_algorithm: None for Horner per se. Workup: MRI, CT angiography, chest imaging. Carotid dissection: anticoagulation (heparin → warfarin) or antiplatelet. Lung apex tumor (Pancoast): chemoradiation. Neck mass: biopsy + treatment. Ptosis cosmetic: phenylephrine drops (diagnosis) → surgery (Müller's muscle conjunctival resection).
us_prevalence: ~20,000–30,000 new cases/year; prevalence ~50,000 (many are incidental findings).
patients_needing_tx: ~20,000 require workup and treatment of underlying cause.
price: Targeting underlying cause; N/A for Horner syndrome itself.

symptoms: Fever, headache, altered mental status, seizures, focal deficits
treatment_goal: Eradicate causative pathogen, reduce inflammation, prevent death
first_line_soc: Acyclovir 10 mg/kg IV q8h — empiric for HSV encephalitis (most common treatable viral cause); started empirically while awaiting CSF results.
treatment_algorithm: Acyclovir (HSV-1/2, VZV). Ganciclovir/valganciclovir for CMV encephalitis. Antibiotics (empiric ceftriaxone + vancomycin) for bacterial coverage. IVIG or plasma exchange for autoimmune encephalitis (anti-NMDAR, anti-LGI1). Methylprednisolone 1g/day for autoimmune. Rituximab for refractory autoimmune. ICU support, anti-epileptics.
us_prevalence: ~20,000 encephalitis hospitalizations/year in the US; ~1,200 deaths.
patients_needing_tx: ~20,000 require active pharmacologic management.
price: $20,000–$50,000 per course for a novel antiviral or immunomodulatory agent.
registrational_endpoint: Modified Rankin Scale at 90 days and mortality vs. acyclovir + supportive care.
btd_threshold_vs_soc: ≥20% reduction in 90-day mortality or ≥0.5 point improvement in mRS vs. acyclovir standard care.

symptoms: Progressive cognitive decline, motor weakness, vision loss, ataxia
treatment_goal: Restore immune function, suppress JC virus replication
first_line_soc: No approved antiviral — primary strategy is immune reconstitution: discontinue immunosuppressant (natalizumab, fingolimod); use plasma exchange (PLEX) to clear natalizumab.
treatment_algorithm: Immune reconstitution: stop natalizumab → PLEX (5 exchanges). Cidofovir/cytarabine (poor evidence). Pembrolizumab (PD-1 inhibitor) promising in case series and Phase I data for PML — experimental. Mefloquine (failed). IVIG. Supportive care, anti-epileptics, PT/OT. Combination ART for HIV-associated PML.
us_prevalence: ~1,000–2,000 PML cases/year in the US (MS patients on natalizumab, HIV, hematologic malignancy).
patients_needing_tx: ~1,500 require active management.
price: $50,000–$200,000 per course for a novel anti-JC virus therapy.
registrational_endpoint: JC viral load in CSF and mRS at 6 months vs. standard immune reconstitution.
btd_threshold_vs_soc: ≥50% reduction in CSF JC viral load and ≥1 point mRS improvement vs. standard immune reconstitution.

symptoms: Headache, fever, focal deficits, seizures, altered consciousness
treatment_goal: Eradicate infection, reduce mass effect, prevent rupture
first_line_soc: Metronidazole 500 mg IV q8h + ceftriaxone 2g IV q12h (empiric) — broad-spectrum antibiotic combination covering anaerobes, streptococci, gram-negatives.
treatment_algorithm: IV metronidazole + ceftriaxone (empiric 4–8 weeks). Adjust to culture. Corticosteroids (dexamethasone) for severe edema/herniation (controversial). Neurosurgical aspiration or excision for abscesses >2.5 cm or refractory cases. Anti-epileptics for seizures. Duration: 6–8 weeks IV.
us_prevalence: ~3,000–5,000 brain abscess cases/year in the US.
patients_needing_tx: ~4,000 require IV antibiotics + possible surgery.
price: $10,000–$30,000 per course for a novel CNS-penetrating antibiotic.
registrational_endpoint: Clinical cure rate and radiographic resolution at 8 weeks vs. metronidazole + ceftriaxone.
btd_threshold_vs_soc: ≥90% clinical cure rate with shorter duration vs. standard 6–8 week regimen.

disease: Intracranial Epidural Abscess and Subdural Empyema
symptoms: Severe headache, fever, focal deficits, altered consciousness, rapid deterioration
treatment_goal: Urgent surgical drainage, eradicate infection, prevent death
first_line_soc: Neurosurgical drainage (craniotomy or burr hole) + empiric IV antibiotics: vancomycin + ceftriaxone + metronidazole.
treatment_algorithm: Urgent surgical drainage (craniotomy preferred for empyema). IV antibiotics: vancomycin 25–30 mg/kg q12h + ceftriaxone 2g q12h + metronidazole 500 mg q8h for 4–8 weeks. Corticosteroids controversial. Anti-epileptics for seizures. Serial imaging for drainage adequacy.
us_prevalence: ~500–1,000 intracranial empyema cases/year in the US (rare, high mortality ~10–30%).
patients_needing_tx: ~750 require urgent combined medical-surgical management.
price: $15,000–$30,000 per course for a novel CNS-penetrating antibiotic combination.
registrational_endpoint: Clinical cure and 90-day mortality vs. standard vancomycin + ceftriaxone + metronidazole.
btd_threshold_vs_soc: ≥30% reduction in 90-day mortality vs. standard triple-antibiotic therapy.

symptoms: Encephalitis, hydrophobia, aerophobia, agitation, progresses to coma
treatment_goal: Prevent disease with PEP; no cure once encephalitic phase begins
first_line_soc: Post-exposure prophylaxis (PEP): rabies immune globulin (RIG, Imogam Rabies-HT) + rabies vaccine series (days 0, 3, 7, 14) — essentially 100% effective if given promptly.
treatment_algorithm: PEP: wound wash + RIG infiltration + vaccine × 4 doses (unvaccinated). If vaccinated: vaccine × 2 doses. No proven treatment once clinical rabies develops. Milwaukee Protocol (ketamine, benzodiazepines, antiviral) — rare survivals reported but inconsistent. Supportive ICU care for clinical rabies.
us_prevalence: ~5,000 post-exposure prophylaxis courses/year; clinical rabies <5 US cases/year.
patients_needing_tx: ~5,000 require PEP annually.
price: $2,000–$5,000 per PEP course (current RIG ~$1,500 + vaccine).
registrational_endpoint: Seroconversion rate (≥0.5 IU/mL neutralizing antibody) at day 14 post-PEP vs. current RIG + vaccine regimen.
btd_threshold_vs_soc: ≥99% seroconversion with fewer doses or improved clinical protection profile vs. current 4-dose regimen.

symptoms: Seizures, headache, focal deficits, hydrocephalus (neurocysticercosis most common)
treatment_goal: Kill parasites, reduce inflammation, control seizures
first_line_soc: Albendazole 15 mg/kg/day (max 1200 mg/day) × 10–28 days — antiparasitic agent, first-line for neurocysticercosis.
treatment_algorithm: Albendazole ± praziquantel for neurocysticercosis. Corticosteroids (dexamethasone) to prevent inflammation from dying larvae. Anti-epileptics for seizures (levetiracetam). Shunt for hydrocephalus. Praziquantel for cerebral cysticercosis. Treatment decision based on viability/number of lesions on MRI.
us_prevalence: ~1,500–2,000 neurocysticercosis cases/year in the US (primarily in immigrants from endemic regions).
patients_needing_tx: ~1,500 require antiparasitic therapy.
price: $5,000–$15,000 per course for a novel antiparasitic + anti-inflammatory agent.
peak_sales: $1M–$3M at 10–25% share (small US market; significant global market).
registrational_endpoint: Seizure freedom rate and lesion resolution on MRI at 6 months vs. albendazole ± praziquantel.
btd_threshold_vs_soc: ≥80% seizure freedom and complete imaging resolution vs. standard dual therapy at 6 months.

symptoms: Unresponsive, eyes closed, no purposeful movement, absent sleep-wake cycle
treatment_goal: Identify and treat underlying cause, support vital functions, promote recovery
first_line_soc: Thiamine 100 mg IV (Wernicke's prevention) + dextrose 50% + naloxone 0.4–2 mg IV — empiric 'coma cocktail'; treat specific identified etiology.
treatment_algorithm: Coma cocktail: thiamine + glucose + naloxone (flumazenil for benzo overdose). Treat etiology: mannitol/surgery for herniation, antibiotics for CNS infection, glucose for hypoglycemia. ICU hemodynamic/respiratory support. Neuroprotective cooling for post-cardiac arrest. Amantadine for disorders of consciousness. PT/OT/speech therapy for recovery.
us_prevalence: ~250,000 coma admissions/year in the US across multiple etiologies.
patients_needing_tx: ~200,000 require active pharmacologic management.
price: $20,000–$100,000 per course for a novel neurological recovery agent.
registrational_endpoint: GCS and CRS-R (Coma Recovery Scale-Revised) score at 28 days and 90-day mortality vs. standard supportive care.
btd_threshold_vs_soc: ≥2-point GCS improvement or ≥20% reduction in 90-day mortality vs. standard supportive care.

disease: Vegetative State and Minimally Conscious State
symptoms: No conscious awareness (VS) or minimal signs of awareness (MCS), preserved sleep-wake cycles
treatment_goal: Facilitate recovery of consciousness and functional communication
first_line_soc: Amantadine 100–200 mg BID — FDA-recognized treatment (NEJM 2012 trial showed improved DOC recovery) for post-traumatic VS/MCS.
treatment_algorithm: Amantadine 100–200 mg BID (4 weeks). Zolpidem 10 mg (paradoxical awakening in some MCS patients — rare). Deep brain stimulation (CM-PF nucleus) investigational. Transcranial magnetic stimulation (rTMS). Transcranial direct current stimulation (tDCS). PT/OT/speech. Sensory stimulation programs. Hyperbaric oxygen (investigational).
us_prevalence: ~270,000–600,000 Americans in persistent VS/MCS; ~100,000 in permanent VS.
patients_needing_tx: ~100,000 require active pharmacologic management.
price: $20,000–$100,000/year for a novel CNS stimulant or consciousness-restoring agent.
registrational_endpoint: CRS-R (Coma Recovery Scale-Revised) total score at 4 and 12 weeks vs. amantadine.
btd_threshold_vs_soc: ≥3-point improvement in CRS-R total score vs. amantadine at 4 weeks.

symptoms: Deteriorating consciousness, Cushing's triad, posturing, fixed dilated pupil
treatment_goal: Reduce ICP urgently, prevent brainstem compression, save life
first_line_soc: Mannitol 20% 1 g/kg IV bolus or hypertonic saline 23.4% 30 mL IV — for emergent ICP reduction. Dexamethasone for vasogenic edema (tumor-related).
treatment_algorithm: Emergent: head elevation 30°, hyperventilation (pCO2 35–40 mmHg). Mannitol 0.25–1 g/kg or hypertonic saline 3%–23.4%. Dexamethasone for tumor edema. Barbiturate coma (pentobarbital) for refractory ICP. Decompressive craniectomy (surgical). Treat underlying cause (hematoma evacuation, tumor resection).
us_prevalence: ~50,000 cases/year of clinically significant brain herniation across TBI, stroke, tumor, infection.
patients_needing_tx: ~50,000 require emergent ICP management.
price: $10,000–$50,000 per episode for a novel ICP reduction agent.
registrational_endpoint: ICP reduction (to <20 mmHg) and 90-day GOS-E vs. mannitol/hypertonic saline.
btd_threshold_vs_soc: ≥20% faster ICP reduction to <20 mmHg or ≥20% improvement in favorable neurological outcome at 90 days.

symptoms: Quadriplegia, anarthria, preserved consciousness, only vertical eye movements
treatment_goal: Communication support, prevent complications, improve functional outcomes
first_line_soc: None pharmacologic — communication aids (eye-tracking, BCI); prevention of complications (DVT prophylaxis, nutrition).
treatment_algorithm: None — augmentative/alternative communication (AAC). DVT prophylaxis (LMWH, SCDs). Spasticity: baclofen (intrathecal), botulinum toxin. Tracheostomy for airway. Enteral nutrition. Neuroprotective strategies for acute pontine stroke (tPA if within window). Investigational: BCI for communication.
us_prevalence: ~1,000–2,000 LIS cases living in the US; very rare (~50–150 new cases/year from pontine stroke).
patients_needing_tx: ~1,500 patients; predominantly supportive care.
price: $50,000–$200,000/year for a novel neurostimulation or neuroprotective biologic.
registrational_endpoint: CRS-R score and motor recovery (AIS) at 6 months vs. standard supportive care.
btd_threshold_vs_soc: ≥30% patients achieving any motor function recovery vs. standard rehabilitation.

symptoms: Absence of all brain function, fixed/dilated pupils, no brainstem reflexes, apnea
treatment_goal: Organ donor management; no treatment for brain death itself
first_line_soc: None — brain death is irreversible; management focuses on organ preservation for donation.
treatment_algorithm: None — confirmation per AANCP criteria (2 examinations). Organ preservation: hemodynamic management, hormone replacement (T3/vasopressin/cortisol for DI). Family counseling. No pharmacologic treatment applicable.
us_prevalence: ~20,000 brain death diagnoses/year in the US (~50% become organ donors).
patients_needing_tx: No pharmacologic treatment needed; organ preservation management only.

symptoms: Neck pain, myelopathy, ataxia, lower cranial nerve deficits, respiratory failure
treatment_goal: Decompress neural structures, stabilize craniocervical junction
first_line_soc: None pharmacologic — surgical decompression (foramen magnum decompression) ± occipitocervical fusion for instability.
treatment_algorithm: None — Chiari I: observation if asymptomatic; posterior fossa decompression ± duraplasty for symptomatic. Basilar invagination/odontoid: occipitocervical fusion. Atlantoaxial instability: C1-C2 fusion (Harms technique). Anti-inflammatories/analgesics (NSAIDs, gabapentin) for pain management. Physical therapy.
us_prevalence: ~100,000 symptomatic craniocervical junction abnormalities in the US (including Chiari I malformation ~0.5–1% prevalence).
patients_needing_tx: ~20,000/year require surgical evaluation; ~10,000 undergo surgery.
price: $20,000–$60,000/year for a novel neuroprotective agent.
registrational_endpoint: Modified Japanese Orthopaedic Association (mJOA) score and Chiari Symptom Profile at 12 months vs. surgery alone.
btd_threshold_vs_soc: ≥3-point improvement in mJOA score vs. standard decompression surgery alone.

symptoms: Memory loss, cognitive decline, personality changes, functional impairment
treatment_goal: Slow cognitive decline, manage behavioral symptoms, maintain independence
first_line_soc: Donepezil (Aricept) — acetylcholinesterase inhibitor — all stages; memantine (Namenda) for moderate-severe AD.
treatment_algorithm: AChEIs (donepezil, rivastigmine, galantamine) — all AD stages. Memantine (moderate-severe). Combined Namzaric for moderate-severe. Lecanemab/donanemab for early AD specifically. BPSD: brexpiprazole, SSRIs, quetiapine. Anti-dementia drugs for non-AD dementias (rivastigmine for DLB, galantamine for vascular).
us_prevalence: ~6–7 million dementia patients in the US; 60–70% are Alzheimer's type.
patients_needing_tx: ~5 million on pharmacologic management.
price: $5,000–$30,000/year for novel symptomatic or disease-modifying agents.
registrational_endpoint: ADAS-Cog change and CDR-SB at 6 months vs. donepezil (symptomatic) or 18 months vs. placebo (DMT).
btd_threshold_vs_soc: ≥3-point improvement on ADAS-Cog vs. donepezil (symptomatic), or ≥35% CDR-SB slowing vs. placebo (DMT).

symptoms: Memory loss, confusion, language problems, behavioral changes, inability to care for self
treatment_goal: Slow amyloid accumulation, preserve cognitive function
first_line_soc: Lecanemab (Leqembi, Eisai/Biogen, FDA-approved 2023) — anti-amyloid monoclonal antibody — for early AD; acetylcholinesterase inhibitors (donepezil, rivastigmine) for symptomatic management.
treatment_algorithm: Disease modification: lecanemab (Leqembi) 10 mg/kg biweekly IV or donanemab (Kisunla, Eli Lilly, FDA-approved 2024) for early AD. Symptomatic: donepezil 5–23 mg/day (AChEI), rivastigmine (Exelon), galantamine; memantine (NMDA antagonist) for moderate-severe; combined donepezil + memantine (Namzaric). BPSD management: quetiapine, SSRIs. Caregiver support.
us_prevalence: ~7.2 million Americans with Alzheimer's dementia (Alzheimer's Association, 2025).
patients_needing_tx: ~1 million in early AD (lecanemab/donanemab eligible); ~6 million on symptomatic therapy.
price: $26,500/year (lecanemab current WAC); novel pipeline agents $30,000–$80,000/year.
peak_sales: $2B–$5B at 5–12% share of eligible early AD population.
registrational_endpoint: CDR-SB (Clinical Dementia Rating Sum of Boxes) change at 18 months vs. placebo.
btd_threshold_vs_soc: ≥35% slowing of CDR-SB progression vs. placebo at 18 months (lecanemab showed ~27%; breakthrough threshold = >30%).

disease: Dementia with Lewy Bodies and Parkinson Disease Dementia
symptoms: Fluctuating cognition, visual hallucinations, Parkinsonism, REM sleep disorder
treatment_goal: Manage cognitive symptoms, control hallucinations, treat motor features
first_line_soc: Rivastigmine (Exelon) — AChEI — only FDA-approved drug for Parkinson's disease dementia; used off-label for DLB.
treatment_algorithm: Rivastigmine 1.5–12 mg/day or 4.6–13.3 mg/24h patch for PDD/DLB. Donepezil (off-label for DLB — evidence from trials). Memantine (modest benefit). Avoid antipsychotics (severe sensitivity in DLB — risk of NMS-like reaction); quetiapine lowest risk if needed. REM sleep disorder: clonazepam 0.25–0.5 mg or melatonin 3–12 mg. Carbidopa/levodopa for motor features (lower doses).
us_prevalence: ~1.4 million DLB + 700,000 PDD patients in the US (~2.1 million combined).
patients_needing_tx: ~1.5 million require pharmacologic management.
price: $10,000–$50,000/year for a novel alpha-synuclein targeted therapy.
registrational_endpoint: MMSE and NPI-10 at 24 weeks vs. rivastigmine or placebo.
btd_threshold_vs_soc: ≥3-point improvement in MMSE and ≥30% NPI-10 reduction vs. rivastigmine at 24 weeks.

disease: Behavioral and Psychologic Symptoms of Dementia
symptoms: Agitation, aggression, psychosis, depression, wandering in dementia patients
treatment_goal: Reduce agitation and psychosis, improve caregiver burden
first_line_soc: Non-pharmacologic first; brexpiprazole (Rexulti, FDA-approved 2023) — atypical antipsychotic — first FDA-approved drug specifically for Alzheimer's-related agitation.
treatment_algorithm: Non-pharmacologic: structured activities, environment modification. Pharmacologic: brexpiprazole 0.5–3 mg/day (Rexulti, 2023 FDA-approved for AD agitation). Quetiapine, olanzapine, risperidone (off-label, black box warning). SSRIs (citalopram, sertraline) for irritability/depression. Valproate for severe agitation. Dextromethorphan/quinidine (Nuedexta) for pseudobulbar affect.
us_prevalence: ~90% of Alzheimer's patients develop BPSD at some point; ~3–4 million actively affected.
patients_needing_tx: ~1.5 million require pharmacologic management.
price: $15,000–$40,000/year for a novel BPSD-specific agent.
registrational_endpoint: NPI (Neuropsychiatric Inventory) Agitation/Aggression domain score at 12 weeks vs. placebo.
btd_threshold_vs_soc: ≥30% greater reduction in NPI Agitation/Aggression score vs. brexpiprazole at 12 weeks.

symptoms: Stepwise cognitive decline, memory impairment, executive dysfunction, focal deficits
treatment_goal: Prevent further strokes, manage risk factors, slow cognitive decline
first_line_soc: Antiplatelet therapy (aspirin 81 mg or clopidogrel) + aggressive vascular risk factor management (antihypertensives, statins); no approved cognitive-specific pharmacotherapy.
treatment_algorithm: Antiplatelet (aspirin, clopidogrel). Antihypertensives (target SBP <130 mmHg). High-intensity statins (atorvastatin 40–80 mg). Anticoagulation for AF. Donepezil/rivastigmine (modest evidence, off-label). Memantine (modest evidence). Smoking cessation. Diabetes control. Investigational: cilostazol, phosphodiesterase inhibitors.
us_prevalence: ~2–3 million vascular dementia patients in the US; 20–30% of all dementias.
patients_needing_tx: ~2 million require pharmacologic risk factor management.
price: $5,000–$20,000/year for a novel vascular-cognitive protective agent.
registrational_endpoint: ADAS-Cog and MoCA at 12 months vs. standard antiplatelet + vascular risk management.
btd_threshold_vs_soc: ≥3-point improvement in ADAS-Cog vs. standard antiplatelet + vascular risk management at 12 months.

symptoms: Acute confusion, fluctuating consciousness, inattention, disorganized thinking
treatment_goal: Identify and treat precipitating cause, restore orientation, prevent complications
first_line_soc: Non-pharmacologic reorientation, sleep hygiene, sensory correction; haloperidol 0.25–0.5 mg IV/PO for hyperactive delirium symptoms — widely used though evidence base is limited.
treatment_algorithm: Non-pharmacologic first (Hospital Elder Life Program, HELP). Treat underlying cause. Haloperidol 0.25–0.5 mg IV/IM for agitation. Quetiapine 12.5–25 mg for sleep. Lorazepam for alcohol/benzo withdrawal delirium only. Ramelteon for circadian realignment. Avoid anticholinergics/benzodiazepines (worsen delirium). Suvorexant (Belsomra) for ICU delirium prevention (emerging evidence).
us_prevalence: ~7 million hospital admissions/year involve delirium in the US; ~30–40% of ICU patients.
patients_needing_tx: ~3 million require active pharmacologic management.
price: $1,000–$5,000 per episode for a novel delirium prevention/treatment agent.
registrational_endpoint: CAM-ICU (Confusion Assessment Method) delirium-free days at 14 days vs. haloperidol or placebo.
btd_threshold_vs_soc: ≥2 additional delirium-free days per 14-day ICU stay vs. haloperidol.

symptoms: Gait instability, urinary incontinence, cognitive decline (Hakim's triad)
treatment_goal: Restore CSF flow, improve gait and cognition
first_line_soc: None pharmacologic — ventriculoperitoneal (VP) shunt or lumboperitoneal shunt for symptomatic NPH.
treatment_algorithm: None — VP shunt (definitive treatment). Serial lumbar punctures (CSF tap test) for diagnosis and temporary relief. Programmable valves to optimize shunt pressure. Endoscopic third ventriculostomy (ETV) in select cases. No pharmacologic treatment approved; acetazolamide reduces CSF production but not validated for NPH.
us_prevalence: ~700,000–1 million NPH patients in the US; many undiagnosed.
patients_needing_tx: ~100,000 undergo VP shunting annually; ~500,000 have symptomatic disease.
price: $5,000–$20,000/year for a novel CSF regulation agent.
registrational_endpoint: 10-meter walk test and NPH Grading Scale at 6 months vs. VP shunt.
btd_threshold_vs_soc: Non-inferior to VP shunting on gait speed and urinary symptom measures at 6 months.

symptoms: Personality changes, disinhibition, apathy, language difficulties, poor judgment
treatment_goal: Manage behavioral symptoms; no disease-modifying treatments available
first_line_soc: No FDA-approved drug for FTD — SSRIs (sertraline 50–200 mg/day) for behavioral/emotional symptoms (best-studied off-label agent).
treatment_algorithm: SSRIs (sertraline, trazodone, paroxetine) for behavioral FTD. Trazodone 200–300 mg for agitation/sleep. Quetiapine for severe behavioral symptoms. Memantine (minimal evidence). Acetylcholinesterase inhibitors (not recommended in bvFTD — may worsen). Investigational: AL001 (tividenafil) — RESULTS Phase III trial read out 2024 for GRN mutation FTD; antisense oligonucleotides for C9orf72.
us_prevalence: ~50,000–60,000 FTD patients in the US; onset typically 45–65 years.
patients_needing_tx: ~50,000 require pharmacologic behavioral management.
price: $50,000–$200,000/year for a gene-targeted therapy (GRN, C9orf72, MAPT mutations).
registrational_endpoint: CDR-FTLD Sum of Boxes and NPI at 12–18 months vs. placebo.
btd_threshold_vs_soc: ≥25% slowing of CDR-FTLD SB progression vs. placebo at 12 months.

symptoms: Cognitive decline, mood changes, aggression, depression, progressive dementia
treatment_goal: No disease-modifying treatment; manage behavioral and cognitive symptoms
first_line_soc: None — no FDA-approved treatment; symptomatic management: SSRIs for depression, AChEI for cognitive symptoms (off-label), antipsychotics for aggression.
treatment_algorithm: None approved. Symptomatic: donepezil/memantine (off-label, as per AD SoC). SSRIs (sertraline/escitalopram) for depression/irritability. Low-dose risperidone or quetiapine for severe behavioral symptoms. Amantadine for cognitive symptoms (investigational). Tau-targeted therapies in clinical trials (e.g., anti-tau antibodies).
us_prevalence: ~100,000–300,000 estimated US CTE cases (post-mortem diagnosis only); ~5,000 symptomatic patients seeking care for suspected CTE.
patients_needing_tx: ~50,000 receive symptomatic management (diagnosed as possible CTE or post-concussive dementia).
price: $30,000–$80,000/year for a novel tau-targeting drug.
registrational_endpoint: CDS (Clinical Dementia Rating) sum of boxes and tau PET SUVR at 18 months vs. placebo.
btd_threshold_vs_soc: ≥25% slowing of CDR-SB progression or ≥20% reduction in tau PET SUVR vs. placebo at 18 months.

symptoms: Fatigue, visual disturbance, numbness, weakness, balance problems, cognitive fog
treatment_goal: Reduce relapse rate, slow disability progression, manage symptoms
first_line_soc: High-efficacy DMTs recommended early: ocrelizumab (Ocrevus, Roche) or natalizumab (Tysabri) for high-disease-activity RRMS; interferons/glatiramer acetate for mild-moderate.
treatment_algorithm: Interferons (IFN-β-1a/b: Avonex, Rebif, Betaseron). Glatiramer acetate (Copaxone/Glatopa). Oral agents: dimethyl fumarate (Tecfidera), teriflunomide (Aubagio), siponimod (Mayzent), ozanimod (Zeposia), ponesimod. High-efficacy: natalizumab (Tysabri), ocrelizumab (Ocrevus), ofatumumab (Kesimpta), ublituximab (Briumvi), alemtuzumab (Lemtrada). Tolebrutinib (BTKi — pending FDA 2025/2026). Symptomatic: baclofen, modafinil, dalfampridine.
us_prevalence: ~1 million MS patients in the US (NMSS); ~85% RRMS.
price: $50,000–$100,000/year (current anti-CD20 agents ~$70,000–$95,000/year WAC).
registrational_endpoint: Annual relapse rate (ARR) and 6-month confirmed disability progression (CDP) vs. placebo or active comparator.
btd_threshold_vs_soc: ≥50% ARR reduction vs. placebo, or ≥30% CDP reduction vs. interferon/GA for RRMS.

symptoms: Optic neuritis, transverse myelitis, severe attacks, area postrema syndrome
treatment_goal: Prevent attacks, reduce disability accumulation
first_line_soc: Inebilizumab (Uplizna, CD19 mAb, FDA-approved 2020) or satralizumab (Enspryng, IL-6R mAb, FDA-approved 2020); eculizumab (Soliris, FDA-approved 2019) for AQP4+ NMOSD.
treatment_algorithm: AQP4-IgG+: eculizumab (Soliris) 900 mg q2w → 1200 mg q2w; inebilizumab (Uplizna) 300 mg IV q6 months; satralizumab (Enspryng) 120 mg SC q4w. Ublituximab (NMOSD — off-label). Acute attacks: high-dose IVMP 1g/day × 5 days; plasma exchange (5–7 cycles). MOG-IgG+: IVIG, low-dose steroids. Azathioprine/mycophenolate (older off-label maintenance).
us_prevalence: ~15,000–20,000 NMOSD patients in the US (~70% AQP4-IgG+).
patients_needing_tx: ~18,000 require maintenance attack-prevention therapy.
price: $120,000–$350,000/year (eculizumab ~$750K/year; inebilizumab ~$200K/year).
registrational_endpoint: Time to first adjudicated relapse vs. placebo (attack prevention superiority).
btd_threshold_vs_soc: ≥60% risk reduction in time to first relapse vs. placebo.

symptoms: Brain fog, fatigue, depression, anxiety, memory problems, headache
treatment_goal: Reduce neuroinflammation, restore cognitive and psychiatric function
first_line_soc: No FDA-approved treatment for neuro-COVID/Long COVID — symptomatic: SSRIs for depression/anxiety, low-dose naltrexone for fatigue (investigational), antihistamines for mast cell activation.
treatment_algorithm: Symptomatic: SSRIs/SNRIs for mood. Low-dose naltrexone 1.5–4.5 mg (LDN) — reducing neuroinflammation (investigational). Antihistamines (cetirizine, famotidine) for mast cell activation. Paxlovid (nirmatrelvir/ritonavir) for acute — may prevent neuro-Long COVID. Metformin (COVID-OUT trial: reduced Long COVID). Cognitive rehabilitation. Structured exercise. Investigational: BC007 (autoantibody removal).
us_prevalence: ~3–4 million Americans with significant Long COVID neuropsychiatric symptoms.
patients_needing_tx: ~2 million require pharmacologic management.
price: $5,000–$30,000/year for a novel Long COVID neurotherapeutic.
registrational_endpoint: PROMIS-29 cognitive function domain and fatigue scale at 12 weeks vs. placebo.
btd_threshold_vs_soc: ≥5-point improvement in PROMIS-29 cognitive T-score vs. placebo at 12 weeks.

symptoms: Impaired speaking, comprehension, reading, writing after brain injury
treatment_goal: Restore communication through speech therapy, improve quality of life
first_line_soc: Speech-language therapy (SLT) — intensive aphasia therapy is the cornerstone of management; no pharmacologic first-line SoC.
treatment_algorithm: SLT (constraint-induced aphasia therapy, CIAT). Noninvasive brain stimulation (tDCS, rTMS over Broca's area) adjunctive. Donepezil (5–10 mg — off-label; evidence from RCT showing improved naming). Memantine (investigational). Piracetam (limited evidence, off-label). Treat underlying stroke (thrombolysis for acute, secondary prevention).
us_prevalence: ~2 million Americans living with aphasia (National Aphasia Association); ~180,000 new cases/year (mainly post-stroke).
patients_needing_tx: ~500,000 receive active aphasia treatment.
price: $5,000–$20,000/year for a novel neuroplasticity-enhancing agent.
registrational_endpoint: WAB-R (Western Aphasia Battery-Revised) Aphasia Quotient at 6 months vs. SLT alone.
btd_threshold_vs_soc: ≥10-point improvement in WAB-R AQ vs. SLT alone at 6 months.

symptoms: Memory impairment (anterograde or retrograde), confabulation, disorientation
treatment_goal: Identify and treat underlying cause; cognitive rehabilitation
first_line_soc: Thiamine 100 mg IV/IM for Wernicke-Korsakoff syndrome. No pharmacologic treatment for other amnesias.
treatment_algorithm: Wernicke-Korsakoff: thiamine 500 mg IV TID × 3 days, then 250 mg/day × 5 days. Memory rehabilitation (errorless learning, spaced retrieval). Donepezil (off-label, limited evidence for post-encephalitic amnesia). Treat underlying cause (tumor, stroke, herpes encephalitis).
us_prevalence: ~50,000–100,000 Americans with significant amnestic syndromes (Korsakoff ~25,000; post-encephalitic ~10,000; other ~65,000).
patients_needing_tx: ~35,000 require pharmacologic management (thiamine, AChEIs).
price: $5,000–$20,000/year for a novel memory restoration agent.
registrational_endpoint: Wechsler Memory Scale (WMS) score change at 6 months vs. standard rehabilitation.
btd_threshold_vs_soc: ≥10-point improvement in WMS composite score vs. rehabilitation alone.

symptoms: Inability to recognize objects, faces, or sounds despite intact senses
treatment_goal: Compensatory strategies, treat underlying cause, cognitive rehabilitation
first_line_soc: None pharmacologic — cognitive rehabilitation and compensatory strategy training.
treatment_algorithm: None — identify and treat underlying etiology (stroke, tumor, dementia). Cognitive rehabilitation. Occupational therapy for ADL compensation. No pharmacologic agent approved or validated.
us_prevalence: ~100,000–200,000 Americans with significant agnosia; most associated with stroke or neurodegenerative disease.
patients_needing_tx: ~50,000 receive active cognitive rehabilitation; minimal pharmacologic need.

symptoms: Unable to perform skilled movements on command despite intact motor/sensory function
treatment_goal: Restore functional motor planning through rehabilitation
first_line_soc: None pharmacologic — occupational and speech therapy for limb and speech apraxia.
treatment_algorithm: None — limb apraxia: OT with gesture imitation therapy, ADL training. Speech apraxia (AOS): SLT, DIVA/MDRP treatment. Treat underlying cause (stroke, FTD). No pharmacologic agent validated.
us_prevalence: ~400,000–600,000 Americans with significant apraxia (predominantly post-stroke and FTD-related).
patients_needing_tx: ~200,000 receive active rehabilitation.

symptoms: Sudden onset anterograde amnesia, repetitive questioning, confusion, resolves in 24h
treatment_goal: Reassurance, rule out TIA/stroke, observe for resolution
first_line_soc: None pharmacologic — supportive care; observation for 24 hours. No treatment required.
treatment_algorithm: None — observation, reassurance. Rule out TIA with MRI DWI. Echocardiogram/Holter to exclude cardiac source. Aspirin if stroke risk present. Recurrence rate ~5–10%; antiplatelet if vascular risk factors.
us_prevalence: ~5,000–10,000 TGA episodes/year in the US.
patients_needing_tx: ~5,000 require ED evaluation; no pharmacologic treatment needed.

symptoms: Severe unilateral throbbing headache, nausea, photo/phonophobia, aura in some
treatment_goal: Abort attacks, prevent monthly migraine days, restore function
first_line_soc: Acute: sumatriptan (Imitrex) or ubrogepant (Ubrelvy, CGRP receptor antagonist) or rimegepant (Nurtec). Preventive: erenumab (Aimovig), fremanezumab (Ajovy), galcanezumab (Emgality), or eptinezumab (Vyepti) — anti-CGRP mAbs.
treatment_algorithm: Acute: triptans (sumatriptan, rizatriptan, eletriptan); gepants (ubrogepant, rimegepant, atogepant); ditans (lasmiditan/Reyvow); NSAIDs + caffeine (Excedrin); antiemetics (metoclopramide IV). Preventive (monthly injections): erenumab 70/140 mg SC, fremanezumab 225 mg SC/675 mg q3mo, galcanezumab 240 mg load → 120 mg SC; oral preventive: atogepant 10–60 mg/day, rimegepant 75 mg QOD; legacy: topiramate, amitriptyline, propranolol, valproate. Botulinum toxin A (Botox) 155–195 units q12 weeks for chronic migraine.
us_prevalence: ~39 million US migraine sufferers; ~15% of population.
patients_needing_tx: ~15 million require prescription treatment (acute or preventive); ~4 million on CGRP-targeted preventives.
price: $8,000–$18,000/year for CGRP mAbs; $5,000–$12,000/year for oral gepants.
peak_sales: $1B–$4B at 0.7–3% of addressable market per novel agent (very crowded).
registrational_endpoint: Monthly migraine days (MMD) reduction at 3 months vs. placebo (preventive); pain freedom at 2 hours (acute).
btd_threshold_vs_soc: ≥50% MMD responder rate superior to CGRP mAb comparator (preventive), or ≥50% pain freedom at 2h (acute) vs. triptan.

symptoms: Bilateral pressing/tightening mild-moderate headache, no nausea, no photo/phonophobia
treatment_goal: Relieve acute headache, prevent frequent episodic/chronic TTH
first_line_soc: Aspirin 500–1000 mg or ibuprofen 200–400 mg or acetaminophen 1000 mg — for acute TTH. Amitriptyline 10–75 mg/day for chronic TTH prevention.
treatment_algorithm: Acute: NSAIDs (ibuprofen, naproxen), aspirin, acetaminophen (OTC, first-line). Chronic TTH prevention: amitriptyline 10–75 mg/day. Mirtazapine, venlafaxine (second-line). Topiramate. Botulinum toxin (limited evidence for TTH vs. migraine). Non-pharmacologic: biofeedback, CBT, relaxation, PT.
us_prevalence: ~78 million Americans affected; most common headache type worldwide.
patients_needing_tx: ~10 million require prescription treatment (chronic/frequent episodic).
price: $2,000–$8,000/year for a novel preventive agent.
registrational_endpoint: Headache days/month reduction at 3 months vs. amitriptyline.
btd_threshold_vs_soc: ≥4 fewer headache days/month vs. amitriptyline at 3 months.

symptoms: Daily or near-daily headache, worsening with frequent analgesic use, rebound pattern
treatment_goal: Detoxify from overused medication, prevent transformation to chronic migraine
first_line_soc: Medication withdrawal (first-line); bridge therapy with NSAIDs, naratriptan, or prednisone during withdrawal; preventive migraine therapy (topiramate, amitriptyline).
treatment_algorithm: Abrupt or gradual withdrawal of overused medication. Bridge: naproxen sodium 550 mg BID × 2 weeks; short oral prednisone taper. Preventive: topiramate 50–100 mg/day, amitriptyline 25–75 mg, propranolol. CGRP mAbs (fremanezumab, erenumab) effective for prevention post-withdrawal. Behavioral counseling.
us_prevalence: ~4–5 million MOH patients in the US (~1–2% of population).
patients_needing_tx: ~3 million require active management.
price: $8,000–$20,000/year for a novel detoxification or preventive agent.
registrational_endpoint: Headache days/month at 3 months post-withdrawal vs. standard detoxification protocol.
btd_threshold_vs_soc: ≥4 fewer headache days/month post-withdrawal vs. standard behavioral detoxification.

symptoms: Unilateral periorbital excruciating pain, lacrimation, rhinorrhea, autonomic symptoms, clustered attacks
treatment_goal: Abort attacks quickly, prevent cluster periods
first_line_soc: Sumatriptan 6 mg SC (triptan) for acute attack abort + high-flow O2 (100% via NRB) — both first-line for acute attacks.
treatment_algorithm: Acute: sumatriptan 6 mg SC or zolmitriptan nasal spray 5 mg; high-flow 100% O2 (10–15 L/min × 15–20 min). Preventive: verapamil 240–960 mg/day (first-line); lithium carbonate; topiramate; melatonin. Short-term bridging: prednisone taper or suboccipital corticosteroid injection. Galcanezumab (Emgality 300 mg SC) — FDA-approved 2019 for episodic cluster. SPG stimulation (neuromodulation) for refractory.
us_prevalence: ~400,000 cluster headache patients in the US (~0.1% prevalence); ~200,000 episodic, ~200,000 chronic.
patients_needing_tx: ~380,000 require pharmacologic management.
price: $15,000–$40,000/year for a novel preventive agent.
registrational_endpoint: Attack frequency during cluster period (attacks/week) vs. placebo — primary endpoint for preventive; time to pain freedom within 30 minutes for acute treatment.
btd_threshold_vs_soc: ≥50% reduction in attack frequency during cluster period vs. verapamil (preventive); ≥65% pain freedom at 15 min (acute).

symptoms: Headache, pulsatile tinnitus, transient visual obscurations, papilledema, visual loss
treatment_goal: Reduce CSF pressure, preserve vision, resolve headache
first_line_soc: Acetazolamide (Diamox) — carbonic anhydrase inhibitor — 250–2000 mg/day; weight loss for obese patients.
treatment_algorithm: Acetazolamide 500 mg–2 g/day (NORDIC IIH Trial SoC). Topiramate as alternative (dual mechanism: CA inhibitor + weight loss). Furosemide. Optic nerve sheath fenestration (ONSF) for vision-threatening. CSF shunting (VP or LP shunt) for refractory. IIH:WT (IIH: Weight Trial) — bariatric surgery. Investigational: 11β-HSD1 inhibitor (AZD4017 — randomized IIH trial showed benefit).
us_prevalence: ~100,000–120,000 IIH patients in the US; predominantly obese women of childbearing age.
patients_needing_tx: ~100,000 require pharmacologic management.
price: $10,000–$30,000/year for a novel CSF pressure reduction agent.
registrational_endpoint: CSF opening pressure reduction (mmH2O) and VFMD (visual field mean deviation) at 12 weeks vs. acetazolamide.
btd_threshold_vs_soc: ≥6 cmH2O greater CSF pressure reduction vs. acetazolamide, or superior visual field preservation.

disease: Postlumbar Puncture and Other Low–Pressure Headaches
symptoms: Positional headache (worse upright, better supine), neck stiffness, nausea after LP
treatment_goal: Seal CSF leak, relieve headache, restore CSF pressure
first_line_soc: Conservative: caffeine 300 mg oral/IV, bedrest, IV fluids. Epidural blood patch (EBP) — 15–20 mL autologous blood — for refractory cases (most effective definitive treatment).
treatment_algorithm: Conservative 24–48h: bed rest, IV fluids, caffeine (300 mg oral BID or 500 mg IV). NSAIDS/acetaminophen. Epidural blood patch for failure of conservative care (~90% success). Theophylline (alternative to caffeine). Gabapentin adjunctive. Intrathecal saline/fibrin glue for spontaneous intracranial hypotension refractory to EBP.
us_prevalence: ~500,000–1 million post-LP headaches/year in the US (PDPH occurs in 1–40% of LPs depending on needle size).
patients_needing_tx: ~200,000 require pharmacologic management; ~50,000 undergo blood patch.
price: $500–$2,000 per course for a novel CSF pressure restoration agent.
registrational_endpoint: VAS headache score at 24 hours (postural) vs. caffeine + standard care.
btd_threshold_vs_soc: ≥3-point reduction in positional VAS headache score vs. caffeine alone at 24 hours.

disease: Short-Lasting Unilateral Neuralgiform Headache With Conjunctival Injection and Tearing (SUNCT)
symptoms: Brief stabbing periorbital pain attacks, conjunctival injection, tearing, hundreds of attacks/day
treatment_goal: Prevent attacks, abort individual attacks
first_line_soc: Lamotrigine 25–400 mg/day — first-line preventive for SUNCT (only agent with meaningful evidence).
treatment_algorithm: Preventive: lamotrigine (titrated to 200–400 mg/day). Second-line: topiramate, gabapentin. Carbamazepine (less effective than in trigeminal neuralgia). Acute bridge: IV lidocaine infusion. Occipital nerve stimulation for refractory. Microvascular decompression (MVD) reported in case series.
us_prevalence: ~5,000–10,000 SUNCT patients in the US (very rare trigeminal autonomic cephalalgia).
patients_needing_tx: ~8,000 require preventive management.
price: $15,000–$50,000/year for a novel CGRP or pituitary adenylate cyclase-targeted agent.
registrational_endpoint: Attack frequency (attacks/day) at 12 weeks vs. lamotrigine.
btd_threshold_vs_soc: ≥50% attack frequency reduction vs. lamotrigine at 12 weeks.

symptoms: Headache, fever, stiff neck, photophobia; benign, self-limited course
treatment_goal: Supportive symptom management; acyclovir for HSV
first_line_soc: Acyclovir 10 mg/kg IV q8h empirically until HSV confirmed or excluded — most viral causes (enteroviruses) are self-limited and require only supportive care.
treatment_algorithm: Supportive: IV fluids, analgesics (acetaminophen/NSAIDs), antiemetics. Acyclovir for HSV-2 meningitis. Avoid steroids unless strongly suspected bacterial/autoimmune. Pleconaril (investigational, enteroviral) — not FDA-approved. Most cases resolve in 7–10 days without specific therapy.
us_prevalence: ~30,000–75,000 viral meningitis cases/year in the US (enteroviruses most common).
patients_needing_tx: ~30,000 require hospitalization and supportive care.
price: $5,000–$15,000 per course for a novel broad-spectrum antiviral for CNS viruses.
registrational_endpoint: Time to clinical recovery and CSF pleocytosis resolution at 14 days vs. acyclovir + supportive care.
btd_threshold_vs_soc: ≥3-day faster recovery vs. standard supportive care in enteroviral meningitis.

symptoms: Headache, meningismus, pleocytosis from drug, malignancy, or systemic disease
treatment_goal: Remove causative agent, reduce inflammation
first_line_soc: Discontinue causative drug (NSAIDs, antibiotics, IVIG — most common causes) or treat underlying malignancy/systemic disease; corticosteroids for inflammatory/neoplastic meningitis.
treatment_algorithm: Drug-induced: discontinue offending agent (NSAIDs, TMP-SMX, IVIG). Autoimmune/inflammatory: oral or IV corticosteroids. Neoplastic (carcinomatous meningitis): intrathecal chemotherapy (methotrexate, cytarabine) + systemic chemotherapy. No specific antibiotic unless infection excluded.
us_prevalence: ~10,000–20,000 cases/year of aseptic/noninfectious meningitis in the US.
patients_needing_tx: ~15,000 require active management.
price: $10,000–$30,000 per course for a novel intrathecal anti-inflammatory.
registrational_endpoint: Symptom resolution rate and CSF cell count normalization at 14 days vs. standard corticosteroid ± removal of cause.
btd_threshold_vs_soc: ≥30% faster CSF normalization vs. corticosteroid + cause removal.

symptoms: Gradual-onset headache, meningismus, cranial nerve palsies, weeks to months course
treatment_goal: Eradicate pathogen (TB, fungal) or treat inflammatory/malignant cause
first_line_soc: Tuberculous: isoniazid + rifampin + pyrazinamide + ethambutol (RIPE) × 2 months, then INH + RIF × 9–12 months + dexamethasone. Cryptococcal: liposomal amphotericin B + flucytosine × 2 weeks, then fluconazole.
treatment_algorithm: TB meningitis: RIPE × 2 months → INH + RIF × 10 months; dexamethasone 0.4 mg/kg/day tapering × 6 weeks. Cryptococcal: AmBisome 3–4 mg/kg/day + flucytosine 25 mg/kg q6h × 2 weeks; fluconazole 400–800 mg maintenance. Coccidioidal: fluconazole 400–800 mg lifelong. Sarcoid/inflammatory: prednisone.
us_prevalence: ~5,000–8,000 cases/year of subacute/chronic meningitis (TB, fungal, sarcoid, malignant).
patients_needing_tx: ~6,000 require prolonged pharmacologic therapy.
price: $10,000–$30,000 per course for a novel CNS anti-infective or anti-inflammatory.
registrational_endpoint: CSF sterility/pleocytosis resolution and clinical outcome (mRS) at 6 months vs. RIPE + dexamethasone (TB) or AmBisome + fluconazole (fungal).
btd_threshold_vs_soc: ≥20% improvement in 6-month favorable outcome (mRS ≤2) vs. standard regimen.

symptoms: Severe headache, stiff neck, fever, photophobia, altered consciousness
treatment_goal: Eradicate bacterial infection, prevent death and neurological sequelae
first_line_soc: Ceftriaxone 2g IV q12h + vancomycin 15–20 mg/kg q6–12h (empiric for all ages) + dexamethasone 0.15 mg/kg q6h × 4 days (to reduce inflammation).
treatment_algorithm: Empiric: ceftriaxone + vancomycin (add ampicillin for Listeria risk: elderly, immunocompromised). Dexamethasone 0.15 mg/kg q6h × 4 days (reduces mortality/hearing loss for S. pneumoniae). Adjust to culture/sensitivity. Duration: S. pneumoniae 10–14 days; N. meningitidis 5–7 days; L. monocytogenes 21 days. ICU support. Treat septic shock.
us_prevalence: ~3,000–5,000 bacterial meningitis cases/year in the US (vaccines have reduced incidence significantly).
patients_needing_tx: ~4,000 require IV antibiotic + dexamethasone treatment.
price: $10,000–$30,000 per course for a novel anti-inflammatory or antimicrobial adjunct.
registrational_endpoint: 14-day mortality and hearing loss rate vs. ceftriaxone + vancomycin + dexamethasone.
btd_threshold_vs_soc: ≥20% reduction in neurological sequelae or 14-day mortality vs. standard antibiotics + dexamethasone.

symptoms: ≥2 episodes of meningitis; fever, headache, meningismus recurrence
treatment_goal: Prevent recurrent episodes by treating underlying anatomic defect or immune deficiency
first_line_soc: Treat underlying cause: antibiotics for bacterial (vancomycin + ceftriaxone); surgical correction of CSF leak; IVIG for immune deficiency; acyclovir for Mollaret's (HSV-associated).
treatment_algorithm: Identify underlying cause (CSF leak, cholesteatoma, dermal sinus, immune deficiency). Antibiotics targeting specific pathogen. Acyclovir 400 mg PO BID long-term for HSV-2 Mollaret's meningitis. IVIG/SCIg for immunodeficiency. Surgical correction of anatomic defects. Eculizumab for complement deficiency-associated.
us_prevalence: ~2,000–5,000 recurrent meningitis cases/year in the US.
patients_needing_tx: ~3,000 require long-term prophylactic pharmacologic management.
price: $5,000–$20,000/year for a novel prophylactic agent.
registrational_endpoint: Time to next meningitis episode and number of episodes at 12 months vs. standard antibiotic prophylaxis.
btd_threshold_vs_soc: ≥50% reduction in recurrent episodes vs. standard prophylaxis at 12 months.

symptoms: Tremor, rigidity, bradykinesia, postural instability
treatment_goal: Restore dopamine, manage motor symptoms, slow progression
first_line_soc: Levodopa/carbidopa (Sinemet, Rytary) — dopamine precursor — the most effective symptomatic therapy; dopamine agonists (pramipexole, ropinirole) as early alternatives.
treatment_algorithm: Early: levodopa/carbidopa (Sinemet), dopamine agonists (pramipexole/Mirapex, ropinirole/Requip, rotigotine/Neupro patch). MAO-B inhibitors (rasagiline/Azilect, selegiline, safinamide/Xadago) as adjunct. COMT inhibitors (entacapone/Comtan, opicapone/Ongentys) for motor fluctuations.
