Amantadine (Gocovri ER) for dyskinesia. Device-aided: DBS, levodopa-carbidopa intestinal gel (Duopa). Prasinezumab (anti-α-synuclein Ab) and other DMTs in trials. Prasinezimuab failed 2025 Phase 2b.
us_prevalence: ~1 million US patients with PD; ~90,000 new diagnoses/year. Parkinson's Foundation 2025 data.
patients_needing_tx: ~950,000 require pharmacologic treatment; virtually all symptomatic PD patients need dopaminergic therapy.
price: $50,000–$150,000/year for a novel disease-modifying agent; symptomatic generics are cheap (~$500/year levodopa). Disease-modifier analogy: $80,000–$120,000/year.
peak_sales: $1B–$3B for a disease-modifying therapy at 5–15% share; math: 950,000 × $100,000 × 0.05–0.10.
registrational_endpoint: MDS-UPDRS total score change from baseline at 52–104 weeks vs. placebo (disease modification); or part III motor score for symptomatic.
btd_threshold_vs_soc: ≥25% slowing of MDS-UPDRS total score progression at 52 weeks vs. placebo, sustained to 104 weeks.

symptoms: Rhythmic involuntary shaking of hands, head, voice, or limbs
treatment_goal: Reduce tremor amplitude, improve daily function
first_line_soc: Propranolol (non-selective beta-blocker) or primidone (anticonvulsant) — first-line for essential tremor.
treatment_algorithm: Essential tremor: propranolol 40–320 mg/day, primidone 50–250 mg/day, gabapentin, topiramate. CX-8998 (T-type calcium channel blocker) — SAGE-547 failed. Focused ultrasound thalamotomy (Exablate Neuro, FDA-approved 2016/2020). DBS of VIM nucleus for refractory. Enhanced tremor: treat underlying cause (thyroid, medication review). Orthostatic tremor: clonazepam.
us_prevalence: ~7 million US patients with essential tremor alone. IETF estimates.
patients_needing_tx: ~2.5–3 million require pharmacologic treatment; many have mild disease managed with observation.
price: $5,000–$20,000/year for a novel oral agent; rationale: large addressable population but generic competition; focused ultrasound procedure $15,000–$25,000.
registrational_endpoint: Fahn-Tolosa-Marin Tremor Rating Scale (TRS) part A+B total score change at 12 weeks vs. placebo.
btd_threshold_vs_soc: ≥3-point improvement in TRS total score and ≥25% reduction in postural tremor amplitude vs. propranolol comparator.

symptoms: Sustained muscle contractions, twisting postures, repetitive movements
treatment_goal: Reduce abnormal postures, relieve pain, improve function
first_line_soc: Botulinum toxin A (onabotulinumtoxinA/Botox, abobotulinumtoxinA/Dysport) — chemodenervation — first-line for focal dystonias (cervical, blepharospasm, limb).
treatment_algorithm: Focal: botulinum toxin injections q12 weeks (Botox, Dysport, Xeomin). Generalized: trihexyphenidyl (anticholinergic), baclofen (intrathecal for severe generalized), benzodiazepines, tetrabenazine. DBS (deep brain stimulation) of GPi for medication-refractory generalized dystonia (FDA-approved HDE). Genetic counseling for DYT mutations.
us_prevalence: ~300,000 US patients with dystonia (all forms); focal dystonias most common. Dystonia Medical Research Foundation estimate.
patients_needing_tx: ~280,000 require pharmacologic or botulinum toxin treatment; ~5,000–10,000 are candidates for DBS.
price: $50,000–$100,000/year for a novel oral agent targeting basal ganglia circuitry; rationale: orphan-like pricing for generalized forms; botulinum toxin ~$3,000–$6,000/injection cycle.
registrational_endpoint: Burke-Fahn-Marsden Dystonia Rating Scale (BFMDRS) movement subscore change from baseline at 24 weeks vs. placebo.
btd_threshold_vs_soc: ≥25% improvement in BFMDRS movement score vs. placebo at 24 weeks.

symptoms: Brief, sudden muscle jerks, involuntary, can impair coordination
treatment_goal: Reduce jerk frequency and severity, improve function
first_line_soc: Clonazepam (benzodiazepine) or valproate (sodium channel/GABA enhancer) — first-line symptomatic agents for most myoclonus subtypes.
treatment_algorithm: Clonazepam 0.5–2 mg TID; valproic acid 500–2000 mg/day. Levetiracetam for cortical myoclonus. Piracetam (widely used outside US). Zonisamide, primidone as alternatives. 5-hydroxytryptophan for post-hypoxic myoclonus. Treat underlying cause. No approved disease-modifying therapies.
us_prevalence: ~200,000–300,000 US patients with clinically significant myoclonus (all causes combined); exact figures not well-reported.
patients_needing_tx: ~150,000 require pharmacologic management.
price: $30,000–$80,000/year for a novel CNS-targeted agent; rationale: partially orphan indication depending on etiology.
peak_sales: $75M–$200M at 1–3% share (fragmented market, generic dominates); math: 150,000 × $50,000 × 0.01–0.03.
registrational_endpoint: Unified Myoclonus Rating Scale (UMRS) or jerk count per hour reduction at 12 weeks vs. placebo.
btd_threshold_vs_soc: ≥50% reduction in UMRS functional score or jerk count vs. placebo at 12 weeks.

symptoms: Parkinsonism plus additional features (e.g., autonomic failure, dementia, gaze palsy)
treatment_goal: Manage motor symptoms, treat underlying cause, prevent complications
first_line_soc: Levodopa/carbidopa — empiric trial in all suspected parkinsonism; response helps differentiate PD from atypical forms.
treatment_algorithm: Levodopa trial (usually poor response in MSA/PSP/CBD). Dopamine agonists. Clonazepam for REM sleep behavior disorder (MSA). Midodrine/fludrocortisone for orthostatic hypotension (MSA). Speech therapy. No approved disease-modifying agents for MSA, PSP, or CBD.
us_prevalence: ~50,000–70,000 US patients with atypical parkinsonism (MSA ~25,000; PSP ~20,000; CBD ~5,000; drug-induced ~variable).
patients_needing_tx: ~55,000 require pharmacologic management.
price: $100,000–$200,000/year for a disease-modifying agent targeting alpha-synuclein (MSA) or tau (PSP/CBD).
registrational_endpoint: Disease-specific severity scale (MSA-Rating Scale or PSPRS) change at 52 weeks vs. placebo.
btd_threshold_vs_soc: ≥25% reduction in rate of UMSARS (MSA) or PSPRS progression vs. placebo at 52 weeks.

symptoms: Chorea, cognitive decline, psychiatric symptoms, weight loss
treatment_goal: Suppress chorea, manage psychiatric symptoms, slow decline
first_line_soc: Deutetrabenazine (Austedo/Austedo XR) or valbenazine (Ingrezza) — VMAT2 inhibitors — FDA-approved for Huntington's chorea; tetrabenazine (Xenazine) is also approved.
treatment_algorithm: Chorea: deutetrabenazine (Austedo XR, 2017), valbenazine (Ingrezza, approved for tardive dyskinesia; off-label for HD chorea), tetrabenazine (Xenazine). Psychiatric: SSRI/SNRI for depression; antipsychotics (olanzapine, quetiapine) for agitation. Riluzole off-label; no approved disease-modifying therapy. Tominersen (HTT-lowering ASO) failed Phase III 2021. PTC518, AMT-130 gene therapy in trials.
us_prevalence: ~30,000 US patients with manifest HD; ~200,000 at risk (autosomal dominant). HDSA prevalence data.
patients_needing_tx: ~28,000 require pharmacologic treatment for chorea and/or psychiatric symptoms.
price: $100,000–$200,000/year for a novel HTT-lowering or disease-modifying agent; current Austedo XR WAC ~$90,000/year.
registrational_endpoint: Total Maximal Chorea (TMC) score from UHDRS, or Composite Unified Huntington's Disease Rating Scale (cUHDRS) change at 15–18 months vs. placebo.
btd_threshold_vs_soc: ≥3-point improvement in TMC score OR ≥0.12-point improvement in cUHDRS vs. placebo at 15 months.

symptoms: Ataxia, dysmetria, dysarthria, nystagmus, intention tremor, unsteady gait
treatment_goal: Treat underlying cause, reduce ataxia, prevent falls
first_line_soc: Treat underlying cause — riluzole 50 mg BID — modestly effective in multiple cerebellar ataxias (ReSPECT trial showed benefit); 4-aminopyridine for downbeat nystagmus/episodic ataxia type 2.
treatment_algorithm: Cause-directed: thiamine for Wernicke, immunotherapy for immune ataxias (IVIG, steroids). Riluzole 100 mg/day (SARA scale benefit). 4-Aminopyridine (4-AP, dalfampridine) for EA2 and downbeat nystagmus. Acetazolamide for EA2 attacks. Physiotherapy for balance. SCA-specific: no disease-modifying agents approved; antisense oligonucleotides in trials.
us_prevalence: ~150,000 Americans with significant cerebellar disorders (hereditary + acquired).
patients_needing_tx: ~100,000 require active pharmacologic management.
price: $20,000–$80,000/year for a disease-modifying ataxia agent.
registrational_endpoint: SARA (Scale for Assessment and Rating of Ataxia) total score change at 12 months vs. placebo.
btd_threshold_vs_soc: ≥2.5-point improvement in SARA score vs. placebo at 12 months.

symptoms: Falls, vertical gaze palsy, parkinsonism, dementia, dysarthria
treatment_goal: Manage motor and bulbar symptoms, prevent falls
first_line_soc: None disease-modifying. Levodopa/carbidopa — modest and usually temporary benefit for parkinsonism in some PSP subtypes.
treatment_algorithm: None — levodopa trial (often fails or temporary benefit); amantadine; baclofen for axial rigidity; botulinum toxin for blepharospasm; SSRIs for emotional lability/depression; midodrine/fludrocortisone for orthostatic hypotension. PT/speech therapy critical. Multiple clinical trials failed (tideglusib/tau; davunetide). Semaglutide and other tau-targeting agents in trials.
us_prevalence: ~20,000–25,000 US patients with PSP. CurePSP prevalence estimates; incidence ~1.1/100,000/year.
patients_needing_tx: ~20,000 require symptomatic pharmacologic management.
price: $100,000–$200,000/year for a disease-modifying anti-tau agent; rationale: ultra-orphan pricing, unmet need.
registrational_endpoint: PSP Rating Scale (PSPRS) change at 52 weeks vs. placebo (primary); time to PSP-RS ≥9 point worsening as secondary.
btd_threshold_vs_soc: ≥3.5-point reduction in PSPRS change from baseline at 52 weeks vs. placebo.

symptoms: Involuntary dance-like movements (chorea), writhing (athetosis), violent flinging (hemiballismus)
treatment_goal: Suppress abnormal movements, treat underlying cause
first_line_soc: Tetrabenazine (Xenazine) or deutetrabenazine (Austedo) — VMAT2 inhibitors — FDA-approved for Huntington's chorea; first-line for symptomatic chorea.
treatment_algorithm: VMAT2 inhibitors: tetrabenazine, deutetrabenazine (Austedo), valbenazine. Dopamine antagonists (haloperidol, fluphenazine, quetiapine) for refractory. Treat underlying cause: immunotherapy for Sydenham's, antipsychotics for tardive. Hemiballismus: haloperidol/valproate (usually self-limited after subthalamic stroke). PT.
us_prevalence: ~50,000 symptomatic chorea patients in the US (Huntington's ~30,000; other causes ~20,000).
patients_needing_tx: ~45,000 require pharmacologic management.
price: $50,000–$100,000/year (current deutetrabenazine ~$90,000/year WAC).
registrational_endpoint: Total Maximal Chorea (TMC) subscale of UHDRS change at 12 weeks vs. placebo.
btd_threshold_vs_soc: ≥2-point greater reduction in TMC score vs. placebo at 12 weeks, sustained at 26 weeks.

symptoms: Involuntary neck muscle contractions, abnormal head posturing, pain
treatment_goal: Reduce abnormal neck movements, relieve pain, improve function
first_line_soc: Botulinum toxin A/B injection (onabotulinumtoxinA/Botox; abobotulinumtoxinA/Dysport; rimabotulinumtoxinB/Myobloc) q12 weeks — FDA-approved first-line for cervical dystonia.
treatment_algorithm: Botulinum toxin injections q12 weeks (first-line, ~70% response). Oral anticholinergics (trihexyphenidyl 2–20 mg/day) second-line. Clonazepam, baclofen (modest). Deep brain stimulation (GPi-DBS) for refractory. Selective peripheral denervation surgery.
us_prevalence: ~60,000–90,000 cervical dystonia patients in the US (most common focal dystonia).
patients_needing_tx: ~80,000 require treatment.
price: $2,000–$8,000 per injection cycle; novel sustained-release agents could achieve $15,000–$30,000/year.
registrational_endpoint: TWSTRS (Toronto Western Spasmodic Torticollis Rating Scale) total score reduction at 12 weeks vs. botulinum toxin.
btd_threshold_vs_soc: Non-inferior to botulinum toxin on TWSTRS with longer duration of effect (>16 weeks) or oral bioavailability.

symptoms: Intention tremor, gait ataxia, parkinsonism, cognitive decline in older males
treatment_goal: Manage tremor, stabilize gait, slow neurodegeneration
first_line_soc: None disease-modifying. Primidone or propranolol (beta-blocker) — first-line for symptomatic tremor; same as essential tremor management.
treatment_algorithm: None — symptomatic: beta-blockers (propranolol), primidone for tremor; levodopa/carbidopa for parkinsonian features (modest benefit); clonazepam for tremor/anxiety; memantine trials (no proven benefit). PT/OT for gait. No approved disease-modifying therapy. Clinical trials targeting RNA toxicity (CGG repeat).
us_prevalence: ~20,000 US males with FXTAS (estimated 1:3,000 males are FMR1 premutation carriers; ~5–15% develop FXTAS after age 50). NFXF estimates.
patients_needing_tx: ~15,000 have symptomatic disease requiring pharmacologic management.
price: $80,000–$150,000/year for a novel RNA-targeting agent; rationale: rare disease, CGG-repeat RNA toxicity target, comparable to other RNA-targeting therapeutics.
registrational_endpoint: Fahn-Tolosa-Marin Tremor Rating Scale (TRS) score or Clinical Tremor Rating Scale change at 52 weeks vs. placebo.
btd_threshold_vs_soc: ≥30% reduction in TRS total score and ≥1-point improvement in SARA (ataxia scale) at 52 weeks vs. placebo.

symptoms: Sudden severe electric-shock face pain, triggered by touch, eating, or talking
treatment_goal: Prevent pain attacks, achieve long-term remission
first_line_soc: Carbamazepine (Tegretol) — sodium channel blocker — FDA-approved first-line; reduces attack frequency by ~70% in early treatment.
treatment_algorithm: Carbamazepine 200–1200 mg/day (first-line, FDA-approved). Oxcarbazepine 300–1800 mg/day (better tolerated). Baclofen, gabapentin, lamotrigine, pregabalin as second-line. Interventional: percutaneous rhizotomy (glycerol, radiofrequency, balloon compression), Gamma Knife radiosurgery, microvascular decompression (MVD). Novel: Nav1.7 inhibitors (suzetrigine/Journavx, approved 2025 for MSP but not TN yet).
us_prevalence: ~150,000 US patients with trigeminal neuralgia; incidence ~4–5/100,000/year. TNA Facial Pain Association data.
patients_needing_tx: ~130,000 require pharmacologic treatment; ~30,000–40,000 proceed to interventional/surgical.
price: $20,000–$60,000/year for a novel Nav inhibitor; rationale: TN is treatment-refractory in ~30%, high unmet need.
registrational_endpoint: Daily pain attack frequency and BPI (Brief Pain Inventory) pain intensity score at 12 weeks vs. carbamazepine.
btd_threshold_vs_soc: ≥50% responder rate (≥50% reduction in daily attack frequency) at 12 weeks vs. carbamazepine (~70% responder rate initially), with sustained effect at 52 weeks and superior tolerability.

symptoms: Unilateral involuntary facial muscle twitching, eye closure spasms, cheek pulling
treatment_goal: Eliminate or reduce facial spasms, improve quality of life
first_line_soc: OnabotulinumtoxinA (Botox) — FDA-approved for hemifacial spasm — injections every 12–16 weeks into affected facial muscles.
treatment_algorithm: Botulinum toxin type A (onabotulinumtoxinA/Botox, abobotulinumtoxinA/Dysport) q12–16 weeks — mainstay of treatment (~85–90% efficacy). Carbamazepine or gabapentin for mild cases. Microvascular decompression (MVD) surgery for patients who prefer curative intervention. Clonazepam for anxious patients.
us_prevalence: ~80,000–100,000 US patients with hemifacial spasm; incidence ~11/100,000.
patients_needing_tx: ~75,000 require botulinum toxin or pharmacologic treatment.
price: $15,000–$30,000/year for botulinum toxin cycles; novel oral agents could price at $20,000–$50,000/year.
registrational_endpoint: Jankovic Rating Scale (JRS) total score change at 12 weeks vs. placebo.
btd_threshold_vs_soc: ≥2-point improvement in JRS total score at 12 weeks, with ≥3-month duration of effect vs. placebo.

symptoms: Impaired adduction of one eye with contralateral nystagmus, diplopia
treatment_goal: Treat underlying cause (usually MS), relieve diplopia
first_line_soc: None specific for INO — treat underlying cause: high-dose IV methylprednisolone (Solu-Medrol) for MS-related INO.
treatment_algorithm: IV methylprednisolone 1g/day × 3–5 days for MS relapse. Prism glasses for persistent diplopia. Patching. DMT for MS to prevent recurrence (see MS SoC). No specific pharmacologic therapy for INO itself.
us_prevalence: ~100,000–150,000 cumulative US patients (most associated with MS; also vascular, drug causes). Exact incidence ~1/100,000/year.
patients_needing_tx: ~80,000 require treatment of underlying condition.
price: $5,000–$15,000 per acute course; ongoing DMT $50,000–$100,000/year for MS prevention.
registrational_endpoint: Resolution of INO (full adduction recovery) at 6 weeks vs. placebo.
btd_threshold_vs_soc: ≥30% absolute improvement in adduction velocity or complete INO resolution rate at 6 weeks vs. placebo.

symptoms: Stabbing throat and ear pain triggered by swallowing, talking, coughing
treatment_goal: Abort and prevent paroxysmal pain episodes
first_line_soc: Carbamazepine (Tegretol) — sodium channel blocker — first-line anticonvulsant as in trigeminal neuralgia.
treatment_algorithm: Carbamazepine 100–800 mg/day (first-line); oxcarbazepine as better-tolerated alternative; gabapentin, phenytoin, baclofen as second-line. Topical local anesthetics to trigger zone. Microvascular decompression (MVD) surgery for refractory cases. Glossopharyngeal nerve block for diagnostic/therapeutic use.
us_prevalence: ~10,000–15,000 US patients; incidence ~0.7/100,000/year (much rarer than trigeminal neuralgia).
patients_needing_tx: ~12,000 require pharmacologic management.
price: $20,000–$60,000/year for a novel Nav1.7/Nav1.3 inhibitor; rationale: orphan-like indication, analogous to trigeminal neuralgia pipeline.
registrational_endpoint: Pain attack frequency per day and NRS pain score reduction at 8 weeks vs. carbamazepine or placebo.
btd_threshold_vs_soc: ≥50% reduction in daily pain attack frequency at 8 weeks vs. carbamazepine.

symptoms: Inability to move eyes together horizontally or vertically
treatment_goal: Treat underlying cause, relieve diplopia
first_line_soc: None pharmacologic for the palsy itself. Treat underlying etiology: antibiotics for infection, steroids for inflammation, anticoagulation for vascular cause.
treatment_algorithm: None specific — treat underlying cause (stroke, MS, tumor, Wernicke's encephalopathy). Prism glasses for diplopia. Thiamine IV for Wernicke's. PT/neuro-ophthalmology follow-up. Botulinum toxin for persistent misalignment.
us_prevalence: ~10,000–20,000 new cases/year across all causes; true prevalence unknown.
patients_needing_tx: ~15,000 require active management of underlying cause.
price: $5,000–$30,000 per treatment course depending on etiology.
registrational_endpoint: Recovery of full conjugate gaze range (degrees of ocular excursion) at 12 weeks vs. standard care.
btd_threshold_vs_soc: ≥50% reduction in gaze limitation degrees vs. placebo at 12 weeks.

symptoms: Horizontal diplopia, inability to abduct eye, inward eye deviation (esotropia)
treatment_goal: Restore eye movement, relieve diplopia, identify/treat underlying cause
first_line_soc: None pharmacologic — observation for spontaneous recovery in microvascular cases; treat underlying cause (hypertension, diabetes, mass lesion).
treatment_algorithm: None — microvascular: observation × 3 months (most resolve). Prism glasses for diplopia. Botulinum toxin to ipsilateral medial rectus to prevent contracture. Strabismus surgery for persistent palsy. Treat underlying: antihypertensives, glucose control, corticosteroids for inflammatory causes.
us_prevalence: ~15,000–25,000 new cases/year in the US; most common isolated CN palsy.
patients_needing_tx: ~20,000 require active management.
registrational_endpoint: Abduction range (degrees) and diplopia resolution at 12 weeks vs. observation.
btd_threshold_vs_soc: ≥40% complete resolution rate at 12 weeks vs. observation (spontaneous recovery rate ~50%).

symptoms: Unilateral facial weakness, drooping, inability to close eye, drooling
treatment_goal: Speed recovery, prevent corneal injury, restore facial function
first_line_soc: Prednisolone (corticosteroid) — 25 mg BID × 10 days — first-line for Bell's palsy within 72 hours of onset; significantly improves recovery rate.
treatment_algorithm: Corticosteroids: prednisolone 50–60 mg/day × 10 days (tapered). Antivirals (valacyclovir 1g TID × 7 days) added to steroids if herpes zoster suspected (Ramsay Hunt). Eye lubricants/tape/moisture chamber for corneal protection. PT with mime therapy for refractory cases. Botulinum toxin for synkinesis. Surgical decompression rarely.
us_prevalence: ~40,000 new Bell's palsy cases/year in the US; additional from Lyme, Ramsay Hunt, trauma, tumors. Incidence ~25/100,000/year.
patients_needing_tx: ~35,000 require corticosteroid treatment annually.
price: $1,000–$5,000 per course for a novel neuroprotective agent; comparator: prednisone is essentially free. Novel agent must show added benefit.
peak_sales: $15M–$40M at 17–46% share if showing clear superiority.
registrational_endpoint: House-Brackmann facial nerve grading scale (Grade I/II vs. III–VI) at 3 months post-onset vs. steroid alone.
btd_threshold_vs_soc: ≥20% absolute increase in proportion achieving House-Brackmann Grade I or II at 3 months vs. steroid monotherapy.

symptoms: Vertical diplopia, head tilt, inability to look down-and-inward
treatment_goal: Resolve diplopia, correct head tilt, restore binocular vision
first_line_soc: None pharmacologic — management is prism glasses for diplopia relief; observation for spontaneous recovery (congenital or microvascular cases).
treatment_algorithm: None — observation × 3–6 months for microvascular (diabetic/hypertensive) cases; vertical prism glasses for acute diplopia. Strabismus surgery (superior oblique tenectomy or superior rectus recession) for persistent palsy >6 months. Botulinum toxin injection to ipsilateral inferior oblique for refractory cases.
us_prevalence: ~15,000–20,000 new cases/year in the US (most common isolated cranial nerve palsy causing vertical diplopia).
patients_needing_tx: ~15,000 require active management (prism, surgery, or botulinum toxin).
price: $2,000–$10,000 per course for a novel neuroprotective/regenerative agent.
registrational_endpoint: Binocular visual acuity (diplopia resolution rate) and vertical fusion range at 12 weeks vs. prism alone.
btd_threshold_vs_soc: ≥40% complete diplopia resolution rate at 12 weeks vs. prism observation.

symptoms: Ptosis, eye turned down and out, dilated pupil (complete palsy), diplopia
treatment_goal: Treat underlying cause urgently, relieve ptosis/diplopia
first_line_soc: None pharmacologic — surgical emergency if pupil-involving (rule out posterior communicating artery aneurysm); treat underlying vascular/inflammatory cause.
treatment_algorithm: None — pupil-involved: urgent neurovascular imaging; surgical clipping/coiling of aneurysm. Pupil-spared (microvascular): observation × 3 months. Eyelid tape/crutch for ptosis. Strabismus surgery for permanent palsy. Corticosteroids for inflammatory (orbital disease, sarcoid).
us_prevalence: ~10,000–15,000 new CN III palsy cases/year in the US.
patients_needing_tx: ~12,000 require active management.
registrational_endpoint: Resolution of ptosis and extraocular movement at 12 weeks vs. observation.
btd_threshold_vs_soc: ≥35% complete resolution rate at 12 weeks vs. observation.

symptoms: Diffuse muscle and joint pain, fatigue, sleep disturbance, cognitive fog
treatment_goal: Reduce pain, improve function, restore sleep and quality of life
first_line_soc: Duloxetine (Cymbalta) — SNRI — or pregabalin (Lyrica) — alpha-2-delta ligand — both FDA-approved for fibromyalgia, the prototypical centralized pain syndrome.
treatment_algorithm: Approved: duloxetine (Cymbalta 30–120 mg), milnacipran (Savella 50–100 mg BID), pregabalin (Lyrica 150–450 mg/day) — all FDA-approved for fibromyalgia. Low-dose naltrexone (off-label). CBT, exercise. Low-dose amitriptyline for sleep. Novel: sodium oxybate (Xyrem — shown benefit; not FDA-approved for fibromyalgia), TNX-102 SL (cyclobenzaprine SL) — FDA-approved 2023 as Tonmya for fibromyalgia.
us_prevalence: ~10 million US fibromyalgia patients; broader centralized pain ~20–25 million. NIH/ACR estimates.
patients_needing_tx: ~8 million require pharmacologic treatment.
price: $5,000–$20,000/year for a novel mechanistic agent; branded Tonmya ~$15,000/year.
peak_sales: $500M–$1.5B at 0.6–1.9% share (very crowded, generic-heavy market); math: 8,000,000 × $10,000 × 0.006–0.019.
registrational_endpoint: Patient Global Impression of Change (PGIC), Fibromyalgia Impact Questionnaire Revised (FIQR) total score, and NRS pain at 12 weeks vs. placebo.
btd_threshold_vs_soc: ≥14% improvement in FIQR total score AND ≥30% NRS pain reduction, with ≥60% PGIC 'much improved' rate at 12 weeks vs. placebo.

symptoms: Burning, shooting, electric pain; allodynia; numbness in nerve distribution
treatment_goal: Reduce pain intensity, improve function and sleep
first_line_soc: Gabapentin (Neurontin) or pregabalin (Lyrica) — alpha-2-delta calcium channel ligands — and/or duloxetine (Cymbalta) — SNRI — as first-line per NeuPSIG guidelines.
treatment_algorithm: First-line: gabapentinoids (pregabalin, gabapentin), SNRIs (duloxetine, venlafaxine), TCAs (amitriptyline, nortriptyline). Second-line: lidocaine patch 5% (Lidoderm — postherpetic neuralgia), capsaicin 8% patch (Qutenza), opioids (tramadol). For specific types: mexiletine (diabetic neuropathy); suzetrigine (Journavx, Nav1.8 inhibitor, FDA-approved Jan 2025 for moderate-to-severe acute pain). Resiniferatoxin, Nav channel inhibitors in trials.
us_prevalence: ~20–25 million US adults with chronic neuropathic pain (diabetic peripheral neuropathy ~8M; postherpetic neuralgia ~1M; others). NIH/NeuPSIG.
patients_needing_tx: ~15 million require pharmacologic treatment.
price: $15,000–$50,000/year for a novel mechanism agent (e.g., Nav1.7/Nav1.8 inhibitor); branded comparators $3,000–$12,000/year.
peak_sales: $1B–$3B for a breakthrough agent at ~2–6% of treatment-refractory segment; math: 2,000,000 × $25,000 × 0.02–0.06.
registrational_endpoint: NRS average pain score (0–10) change from baseline at 12 weeks vs. placebo (≥2-point difference = clinically meaningful).
btd_threshold_vs_soc: ≥30% responder rate advantage over comparator (pregabalin or duloxetine) at 12 weeks, with NRS change ≥2.0 points vs. placebo.

symptoms: Severe burning pain, skin color/temperature changes, swelling, hypersensitivity after injury
treatment_goal: Relieve pain, restore function, prevent disease spread
first_line_soc: None FDA-approved specifically. NSAIDs and corticosteroids (for early inflammatory phase) combined with physical therapy — clinical guideline first-line.
treatment_algorithm: None approved. First-line: NSAIDs, corticosteroids (early), PT/OT (graded motor imagery, mirror therapy). Second-line: gabapentin/pregabalin, SNRIs, TCAs, bisphosphonates (alendronate, pamidronate — off-label, evidence-supported). Opioids discouraged. Spinal cord stimulation (SCS) for refractory. Ketamine infusions (off-label). Calcitonin, IV immunoglobulin, and low-dose naltrexone (emerging evidence).
us_prevalence: ~200,000 US patients with CRPS; incidence ~5.5–26/100,000/year. Dutch/RSDSA estimates.
patients_needing_tx: ~150,000 require active pharmacologic or interventional management.
price: $20,000–$80,000/year for a novel neuroinflammatory-targeting agent; high unmet need.
registrational_endpoint: NRS pain score change and clinician-assessed CRPS severity score at 12 weeks vs. placebo.
btd_threshold_vs_soc: ≥2-point NRS pain reduction AND ≥30% improvement in CRPS severity score at 12 weeks vs. placebo.

symptoms: Distal numbness, tingling, burning pain, weakness, loss of reflexes
treatment_goal: Treat underlying cause, relieve neuropathic pain, prevent falls
first_line_soc: Treat underlying cause (glucose control for diabetic neuropathy; B12 replacement; chemotherapy dose modification). Duloxetine (Cymbalta) — FDA-approved for diabetic peripheral neuropathic pain.
treatment_algorithm: Etiology-specific: optimize glycemia (diabetic), replace B12/folate (nutritional), treat monoclonal protein (MGUS-associated). Pain: duloxetine (Cymbalta, FDA-approved DPNP), pregabalin (Lyrica), gabapentin, TCAs. Topicals: capsaicin 8% patch (Qutenza), lidocaine 5% patch. Opioids last resort. Tavanta-C (benfotiamine) data limited. Patisiran/givosiran for hereditary ATTR neuropathy.
us_prevalence: ~20 million US adults with peripheral neuropathy (all causes); diabetic peripheral neuropathy ~8 million. American Chronic Pain Association.
patients_needing_tx: ~12 million require pharmacologic treatment for symptomatic relief.
price: $10,000–$50,000/year for a novel neuroprotective/regenerative agent; ATTR neuropathy agents ~$300,000/year.
tam_gross: 12,000,000 × $20,000 = $240B (theoretical); realistically ~$24B for treated population at current prices
registrational_endpoint: NRS average pain score change at 12 weeks vs. placebo (FDA neuropathic pain guidance).
btd_threshold_vs_soc: ≥30% responder rate advantage over duloxetine at 12 weeks.

symptoms: Symmetric distal weakness, sensory loss, areflexia, length-dependent pattern
treatment_goal: Treat etiology, prevent progression, manage pain and functional impairment
first_line_soc: Etiology-specific: patisiran (Onpattro) or vutrisiran (Amvuttra) for hereditary transthyretin (hATTR) polyneuropathy; inotersen (Tegsedi) — ASO — also approved for hATTR.
treatment_algorithm: hATTR: patisiran (Onpattro, siRNA), vutrisiran (Amvuttra, siRNA), inotersen (Tegsedi, ASO), eplontersen (Wainua, ASO, FDA-approved 2023). Symptomatic polyneuropathy: treat underlying (diabetes, alcohol, toxin). IVIg/plasma exchange if immune-mediated. Neuropathic pain: gabapentinoids, duloxetine. ATTR stabilizers: tafamidis (Vyndaqel/Vyndamax) for cardiomyopathy variant.
us_prevalence: ~20 million with polyneuropathy (all causes); ~50,000 with hATTR polyneuropathy.
patients_needing_tx: ~50,000 hATTR patients for novel agents; broader symptomatic polyneuropathy ~10 million.
price: $300,000–$500,000/year for hATTR-specific gene silencing therapy; $10,000–$30,000/year for symptomatic broad-polyneuropathy agents.
peak_sales: $400M–$1.5B for hATTR-specific therapy at 8–30% share; math: 50,000 × $400,000 × 0.08–0.30.
registrational_endpoint: mNIS+7 (modified Neuropathy Impairment Score) change and Norfolk QOL-DN questionnaire at 18 months vs. placebo.
btd_threshold_vs_soc: ≥50% reduction in mNIS+7 score progression rate at 18 months vs. placebo (patisiran achieved 34-point improvement).

symptoms: Fatigable ptosis, diplopia, dysphagia, limb weakness, respiratory failure risk
treatment_goal: Achieve minimal symptom expression, prevent myasthenic crisis
first_line_soc: Pyridostigmine (Mestinon) — acetylcholinesterase inhibitor — symptomatic first-line; prednisone for immunosuppression initiation.
treatment_algorithm: Pyridostigmine 30–120 mg q3–6h (symptomatic). Prednisone + long-term IS: azathioprine, MMF, tacrolimus. Thymectomy for thymomatous and nonthymomatous gMG (shown benefit in MGTX trial). Complement inhibitors: eculizumab (Soliris, AChR+), ravulizumab (Ultomiris). FcRn blockers: efgartigimod alfa (Vyvgart Hytrulo, 2023), rozanolixizumab (Rystiggo, 2023), nipocalimab (Imaavry, April 2025). IVIg/PLEX for crisis. Rituximab for MuSK+ and refractory.
us_prevalence: ~65,000 US patients with generalized MG; ~20 per 100,000. MGFA prevalence data.
patients_needing_tx: ~60,000 require pharmacologic treatment; ~20,000 have generalized MG needing advanced biologics.
price: $150,000–$300,000/year for complement inhibitors/FcRn blockers; eculizumab (Soliris) WAC ~$700,000/year.
peak_sales: $500M–$1.5B for a best-in-class biologic at 4–12% share; math: 60,000 × $200,000 × 0.04–0.12.
registrational_endpoint: MG-ADL score (≥3-point improvement) responder rate at 26 weeks vs. placebo.
btd_threshold_vs_soc: ≥50% MG-ADL responder rate (≥3-point improvement) at 26 weeks vs. placebo (~15–20% placebo rate).

symptoms: Progressive proximal muscle weakness, hypotonia, respiratory insufficiency (most severe in infants)
treatment_goal: Restore SMN protein expression, preserve motor function, prevent respiratory failure
first_line_soc: Nusinersen (Spinraza) — intrathecal ASO targeting SMN2 splicing — first approved therapy (2016); onasemnogene abeparvovec (Zolgensma) — gene therapy — for patients <2 years (2019).
treatment_algorithm: Nusinersen (Spinraza): IT injection q4 months after loading. Onasemnogene abeparvovec (Zolgensma, AV9-SMN1): single IV infusion (<2 years). Risdiplam (Evrysdi): oral SMN2 splicing modifier, FDA-approved 2020, for all types/ages. Supportive: ventilation, PT, scoliosis management. All three approved; risdiplam preferred for older patients/adults. Gene therapy trials for SMA type 3.
us_prevalence: ~25,000–30,000 US patients with SMA (all types); ~1/10,000 live births. SMA Foundation data.
patients_needing_tx: ~25,000 require pharmacologic disease-modifying therapy.
price: $340,000–$2,125,000 (onasemnogene/Zolgensma WAC $2.125M one-time; nusinersen ~$750,000/year; risdiplam ~$340,000/year).
peak_sales: $1B–$2.5B across all three approved agents (combined SMA market); single novel agent: $200M–$500M at 8–20% share.
registrational_endpoint: HINE-2 (Hammersmith Infant Neurological Examination) motor milestone achievement or HFMSE change at 15 months vs. sham.
btd_threshold_vs_soc: ≥2-point greater improvement in HFMSE score and ≥3 HINE-2 motor milestone achievements vs. sham at 15 months.

symptoms: Fatigable weakness, ptosis, diplopia, dysphagia, respiratory weakness
treatment_goal: Restore neuromuscular junction function, prevent crisis
first_line_soc: Pyridostigmine (Mestinon) — acetylcholinesterase inhibitor — symptomatic first-line for most NMJ disorders including myasthenia gravis and Lambert-Eaton.
treatment_algorithm: Pyridostigmine 30–120 mg q3–6h. Amifampridine (Firdapse/Ruzurgi) — voltage-gated calcium channel enhancer — FDA-approved for Lambert-Eaton Myasthenic Syndrome (LEMS). Immunosuppression: steroids, azathioprine, MMF. FcRn blockers (efgartigimod, rozanolixizumab) for MG. Complement inhibitors (eculizumab/Soliris, ravulizumab/Ultomiris, zilucoplan/Zilbrysq) for AChR+ gMG. IVIg/PLEX for crisis.
us_prevalence: ~70,000 NMJ disorder patients (MG ~65,000; LEMS ~3,000; congenital myasthenic syndromes ~2,000).
patients_needing_tx: ~68,000 require pharmacologic treatment.
price: $80,000–$200,000/year for novel biologics; pyridostigmine generic ~$500/year.
registrational_endpoint: MG-ADL (Activities of Daily Living) score or QMG (Quantitative MG) score change at 24–26 weeks vs. placebo.
btd_threshold_vs_soc: ≥1.9-point improvement in MG-ADL at 24 weeks vs. placebo (threshold used in ADAPT and ADAPT+ trials).

symptoms: Slowly progressive distal muscle weakness, foot deformity, loss of sensation, areflexia
treatment_goal: Slow progression, preserve ambulation, manage pain
first_line_soc: None disease-modifying approved. PXT3003 (RS-STIGMASTEROL combination) — in Phase 3 for CMT1A (Charcot-Marie-Tooth).
treatment_algorithm: None FDA-approved. PT/OT, orthotics/AFOs for foot drop. Pain: gabapentinoids, SNRIs. Avoid neurotoxic drugs (vincristine, isoniazid). Surgical foot correction. Emerging: PMP22-targeting RNAi (patisiran analog), gene therapy. Ascorbic acid failed in CMT1A trials. PXT3003 showed modest benefit in Phase 3.
us_prevalence: ~150,000 US patients with CMT (all forms); ~100,000 with CMT1A. CMTA (Charcot-Marie-Tooth Association) data.
patients_needing_tx: ~100,000 could benefit from disease-modifying treatment; ~150,000 need symptomatic management.
price: $50,000–$300,000/year for a gene therapy; oral disease-modifying agents $10,000–$50,000/year.
peak_sales: $150M–$500M at 2–7% share (gene therapy) or $200M–$600M for oral agent; math: 100,000 × $75,000 × 0.02–0.07.
registrational_endpoint: CMT Neuropathy Score version 2 (CMTNSv2) change from baseline at 52 weeks vs. placebo.
btd_threshold_vs_soc: ≥2-point improvement in CMTNSv2 at 52 weeks vs. placebo.

symptoms: Radicular pain, weakness, numbness following dermatomal/myotomal distribution
treatment_goal: Relieve radicular pain, decompress nerve root, restore function
first_line_soc: NSAIDs and short-course oral corticosteroids — first-line pharmacologic for acute radiculopathy; epidural steroid injections for subacute/chronic.
treatment_algorithm: NSAIDs (naproxen, ibuprofen, diclofenac), short oral prednisone taper (Medrol dose pack). Gabapentinoids (gabapentin, pregabalin) for neuropathic component. Muscle relaxants (cyclobenzaprine). Epidural steroid injections (ESIs) for subacute. PT/traction. Surgery (discectomy, laminectomy) for neurologic deficits or failure of conservative care.
us_prevalence: ~3–4 million US adults with symptomatic radiculopathy requiring medical care annually; lifetime prevalence ~40%.
patients_needing_tx: ~3 million require pharmacologic treatment annually.
price: $1,000–$5,000 per course for a novel acute radiculopathy agent.
registrational_endpoint: NRS leg pain score change and Oswestry Disability Index (ODI) at 6 weeks vs. NSAIDs.
btd_threshold_vs_soc: ≥2-point NRS leg pain reduction at 6 weeks vs. NSAIDs, with ≥20% improvement in ODI.

disease: Amyotrophic Lateral Sclerosis (ALS) and Other Motor Neuron Diseases (MNDs)
symptoms: Progressive muscle weakness, atrophy, spasticity, dysarthria, respiratory failure
treatment_goal: Slow disease progression, extend survival, maintain function
first_line_soc: Riluzole (Rilutek) — glutamate antagonist — FDA-approved 1995; extends survival ~2–3 months. Edaravone (Radicava/Radicava ORS) — free radical scavenger — FDA-approved 2017 for early-stage ALS.
treatment_algorithm: Riluzole 50 mg BID. Edaravone (Radicava IV/Radicava ORS oral suspension). Tofersen (Qalsody, ASO targeting SOD1) — FDA-approved 2023 for SOD1-ALS. AMX0035 (Relyvrio/sodium phenylbutyrate+TUDCA) — approved 2022, withdrawn 2024 after failed confirmatory trial. Symptomatic: baclofen/tizanidine for spasticity; anticholinergics for sialorrhea; mexiletine for cramps. PEG tube, NIV, hospice. Multiple trials ongoing (gene therapy, antisense oligos for FUS, TDP-43).
us_prevalence: ~32,000–36,000 US ALS patients in 2025; ~5,800 new cases/year. CDC/ATSDR ALS Surveillance System; PubMed 2025 projection study.
patients_needing_tx: ~32,000 require pharmacologic treatment; virtually all patients with confirmed ALS are treated.
price: $150,000–$300,000/year for a disease-modifying therapy; tofersen (Qalsody) WAC ~$187,000/year; riluzole generic ~$500/year.
peak_sales: $500M–$1.5B for a broadly applicable DMT at 8–23% share; math: 32,000 × $200,000 × 0.08–0.23.
registrational_endpoint: ALSFRS-R (ALS Functional Rating Scale-Revised) slope change from baseline at 48 weeks vs. placebo; joint primary endpoint with survival or respiratory decline.
btd_threshold_vs_soc: ≥20% reduction in ALSFRS-R slope decline (e.g., –0.5 vs. –0.6 points/month) AND ≥20% reduction in mortality/ventilation-free survival at 48 weeks vs. placebo.

symptoms: Progressive limb weakness, sensory loss, areflexia, gait difficulty
treatment_goal: Halt immune attack on myelin, restore strength and function
first_line_soc: IVIg (intravenous immunoglobulin, e.g., Privigen, Gamunex) — first-line; corticosteroids (prednisone 1 mg/kg/day) or plasma exchange are alternatives.
treatment_algorithm: IVIg 2g/kg loading then 1g/kg q3–4 weeks. Subcutaneous IG (Hizentra, Cuvitru) for maintenance. Corticosteroids. Plasma exchange. Efgartigimod alfa (Vyvgart/Argenx) — FcRn blocker — FDA-approved for CIDP June 2024 (Vyvgart Hytrulo SC). Rituximab for refractory. Azathioprine, MMF, cyclosporine for steroid-sparing.
us_prevalence: ~40,000 US patients with CIDP; prevalence ~8–9/100,000. GBS/CIDP Foundation data.
patients_needing_tx: ~38,000 require active pharmacologic treatment.
price: $100,000–$200,000/year; efgartigimod (Vyvgart Hytrulo) WAC ~$125,000/year.
registrational_endpoint: Adjusted INCAT disability scale (aINCAT) responder rate (≥1-point improvement) or I-RODS change at 24 weeks vs. placebo.
btd_threshold_vs_soc: ≥20% absolute improvement in aINCAT responder rate vs. IVIg at 24 weeks, or sustained remission at 52 weeks.

symptoms: Focal pain, weakness, numbness in single nerve distribution
treatment_goal: Relieve pain, restore function, prevent nerve compression damage
first_line_soc: None pharmacologic for most (carpal tunnel: splinting first; NSAIDs for pain). Corticosteroid injection — first-line for carpal tunnel syndrome, the most common mononeuropathy.
treatment_algorithm: Carpal tunnel: wrist splinting, corticosteroid injection (triamcinolone), surgical decompression for persistent/severe. Ulnar neuropathy: elbow padding, surgical transposition. Peroneal palsy: AFO, observation. NSAIDs/gabapentinoids for pain. Corticosteroids for traumatic inflammation.
us_prevalence: ~5 million US patients with symptomatic mononeuropathies; carpal tunnel syndrome ~3.7 million. NIAMS data.
patients_needing_tx: ~3 million require active treatment (injection, surgery, or Rx medication).
price: $200–$2,000 per injection or short course.
registrational_endpoint: Boston Carpal Tunnel Questionnaire (BCTQ) score and nerve conduction velocity improvement at 12 weeks vs. splint+placebo.
btd_threshold_vs_soc: ≥15% greater improvement in BCTQ score vs. corticosteroid injection at 12 weeks.

disease: Hereditary Motor Neuropathy With Liability to Pressure Palsies (HNPP)
symptoms: Recurrent painless focal motor weakness after minor pressure on nerves
treatment_goal: Prevent pressure palsies, relieve symptoms during episodes
first_line_soc: None pharmacologic — primary treatment is avoidance of pressure on susceptible nerves; supportive care for acute episodes.
treatment_algorithm: None — avoidance of prolonged pressure (nerve padding). PT/OT for recovery after episodes. Pain management if present (gabapentinoids). No proven pharmacologic disease-modifying treatment. Caused by PMP22 deletion; gene therapy in preclinical stage.
us_prevalence: ~40,000–50,000 US patients (estimated prevalence 16/100,000); underdiagnosed. HNPP is the 2nd most common hereditary neuropathy.
patients_needing_tx: ~15,000 have symptomatic disease requiring active management.
price: $50,000–$200,000/year for a gene therapy or PMP22-targeted RNA therapy.
registrational_endpoint: Frequency of pressure palsy episodes per year and NCS (nerve conduction studies) parameters at 52 weeks vs. placebo.
btd_threshold_vs_soc: ≥50% reduction in annual palsy episode frequency vs. placebo.

disease: Brachial Plexus and Lumbosacral Plexus Disorders
symptoms: Arm or leg weakness, pain, numbness following injury, cancer, or inflammation
treatment_goal: Relieve pain, preserve/restore motor function, treat underlying cause
first_line_soc: None pharmacologic for the plexopathy itself. Corticosteroids for inflammatory plexopathy (Parsonage-Turner syndrome).
treatment_algorithm: None — traumatic: surgical exploration/neurolysis/nerve grafting for severe injury. Inflammatory (Parsonage-Turner): corticosteroids (prednisone 60 mg/day tapering); IVIG for refractory. Neoplastic: treat underlying malignancy. Pain: gabapentinoids, SNRIs, TCAs. PT/OT essential. IVIg in inflammatory subtypes.
us_prevalence: ~20,000–30,000 new brachial plexopathy cases/year; lumbosacral plexopathy less common. Parsonage-Turner ~1–3/100,000/year.
patients_needing_tx: ~25,000 require pharmacologic pain and/or anti-inflammatory management.
registrational_endpoint: NRS pain score and MRC muscle strength scale change at 12 weeks vs. placebo.
btd_threshold_vs_soc: ≥30% reduction in NRS pain and ≥1 MRC grade improvement vs. placebo at 12 weeks.

symptoms: Sequential/simultaneous involvement of multiple named nerves, pain, weakness, numbness
treatment_goal: Treat underlying vasculitis or systemic disease, relieve neuropathic pain
first_line_soc: High-dose corticosteroids (prednisone 1 mg/kg/day) — first-line for vasculitic mononeuritis multiplex (most common underlying cause).
treatment_algorithm: Treat underlying cause: corticosteroids ± cyclophosphamide for vasculitic; rituximab for ANCA-vasculitis. Antiretrovirals for HIV-associated. Dapsone for leprosy. Neuropathic pain: gabapentinoids, SNRIs. Plasma exchange for cryoglobulinemia. IVIG.
us_prevalence: ~20,000–30,000 US patients with mononeuritis multiplex; vasculitic cause most common.
patients_needing_tx: ~25,000 require immunosuppressive or disease-specific treatment.
price: $30,000–$100,000/year for a novel targeted anti-vasculitic agent.
registrational_endpoint: Neuropathy symptom/disability score and vasculitis activity score (BVAS) at 24 weeks vs. standard immunosuppression.
btd_threshold_vs_soc: ≥30% greater reduction in neuropathy disability score vs. standard corticosteroids at 24 weeks.

symptoms: Progressive stiffness, painful spasms of axial muscles, hyperlordosis, startle sensitivity
treatment_goal: Reduce muscle stiffness and spasms, suppress autoimmunity
first_line_soc: Diazepam (GABA-A agonist) — first-line symptomatic; baclofen (GABA-B agonist) as adjunct or alternative.
treatment_algorithm: Diazepam 5–30 mg/day; baclofen 10–80 mg/day (intrathecal baclofen for severe). Clonazepam, tizanidine. Immunotherapy: IVIg — shown effective in RCT (NIH 2005); plasma exchange; rituximab; corticosteroids. GAD65-antibody testing to guide immunotherapy. Emerging: phase 2 trials of anti-CD20 and anti-CD19 CAR-T.
us_prevalence: ~5,000–10,000 US patients (estimated 1–2/million prevalence). True prevalence likely underestimated.
patients_needing_tx: ~7,500 require pharmacologic and/or immunologic treatment.
price: $100,000–$300,000/year for a novel GAD65 antibody-targeting immunotherapy.
registrational_endpoint: Visual Analog Scale (VAS) stiffness score and anti-GAD65 antibody titer change at 24 weeks vs. placebo.
btd_threshold_vs_soc: ≥30% reduction in VAS stiffness at 24 weeks AND ≥50% reduction in anti-GAD65 titer vs. placebo.

symptoms: Ascending paralysis, areflexia, paresthesias, respiratory failure risk
treatment_goal: Halt immune attack, accelerate recovery, prevent respiratory failure
first_line_soc: IVIg (2g/kg over 5 days) — IV immunoglobulin — equal efficacy to plasma exchange; standard first-line in most centers.
treatment_algorithm: IVIg 2g/kg over 2–5 days OR plasma exchange (4–6 exchanges over 1–2 weeks) — equally effective, not additive. ICU monitoring, ventilator support as needed. No corticosteroids (not beneficial). Pain: opioids, gabapentinoids. Eculizumab (complement inhibitor) evaluated in trials for severe GBS. Imlifidase (IgG-cleaving enzyme) in Phase 2 trials.
us_prevalence: ~10,000–15,000 new GBS cases/year in the US; incidence ~1–2/100,000/year.
patients_needing_tx: ~12,000 require IVIg or plasma exchange annually.
price: $50,000–$150,000 per acute course for a novel complement inhibitor or neuroprotective agent; IVIg course ~$15,000–$30,000.
registrational_endpoint: GBS Disability Scale score and time to walk unaided at 4 weeks vs. IVIg alone.
btd_threshold_vs_soc: ≥25% reduction in proportion with GBS Disability Scale ≥3 at 4 weeks vs. IVIg alone.

symptoms: Continuous muscle rippling, cramps, stiffness, hyperhidrosis, myokymia
treatment_goal: Reduce neuromuscular hyperexcitability, suppress autoimmunity
first_line_soc: Carbamazepine or mexiletine — membrane stabilizers — for symptomatic myokymia and cramps. Immunotherapy (IVIG or plasma exchange) for CASPR2/VGKC antibody-positive cases.
treatment_algorithm: Membrane stabilizers: carbamazepine, phenytoin, mexiletine. Immunosuppression: oral prednisone, azathioprine, MMF, rituximab for refractory. IVIg/plasma exchange for severe cases. Treat underlying thymoma if present. No FDA-approved specific agent.
us_prevalence: ~5,000–8,000 US patients (rare, often underdiagnosed); true prevalence unknown. Orphan condition.
patients_needing_tx: ~6,000 require pharmacologic management.
price: $100,000–$300,000/year for a novel CASPR2/VGKC-targeted immunotherapy.
registrational_endpoint: Clinical Assessment Scale for Autoimmune Encephalitis (CASE) or neuromuscular symptom score change at 24 weeks vs. placebo.
btd_threshold_vs_soc: ≥30% reduction in myokymia discharge frequency on EMG and ≥2-point reduction in CASE score at 24 weeks.

symptoms: Arm pain, numbness, weakness; upper extremity swelling; positional symptoms
treatment_goal: Decompress thoracic outlet, relieve pain, restore arm function
first_line_soc: None pharmacologic — physical therapy (postural correction, shoulder girdle strengthening) is first-line conservative management.
treatment_algorithm: None pharmacologic as primary. PT first-line for neurogenic TOS (6–12 weeks). NSAIDs for pain. Botulinum toxin injection into anterior scalene for neurogenic TOS. Anticoagulation ± thrombolysis for subclavian vein thrombosis (Paget-Schroetter). Surgical: first rib resection ± scalenectomy for persistent neurogenic or vascular TOS.
us_prevalence: ~80,000–100,000 US patients with symptomatic TOS annually; neurogenic TOS accounts for ~95%.
patients_needing_tx: ~70,000 require active management.
registrational_endpoint: Disability of the Arm, Shoulder and Hand (DASH) score and NRS pain change at 12 weeks vs. PT alone.
btd_threshold_vs_soc: ≥10-point improvement in DASH score vs. PT alone at 12 weeks.

symptoms: Rapidly progressive dementia, myoclonus, ataxia, visual disturbance, coma
treatment_goal: Palliative care; no curative treatment exists
first_line_soc: None — no effective pharmacologic treatment. Supportive/palliative care.
treatment_algorithm: None — all symptomatic: clonazepam/valproate for myoclonus; antipsychotics for agitation; opioids for pain. Hospice. Quinacrine and flupirtine failed in trials. PRN100 (monoclonal antibody) in Phase 1 trial (UK). Pentosan polysulfate, doxycycline — no proven benefit. Prion-directed ASOs and small molecules in early research.
us_prevalence: ~500 new sporadic CJD cases/year in the US; prevalence ~1–2/million. CDC National Prion Disease Pathology Surveillance Center.
patients_needing_tx: ~500 symptomatic patients/year; disease duration 4–8 months (mean).
price: $200,000–$500,000 per episode for a novel anti-prion agent; ultra-orphan pricing.
peak_sales: $30M–$100M at 17–57% share (unmet need so high that penetration could be significant).
registrational_endpoint: Survival at 6 months vs. placebo/natural history; or MRC Scale score decline rate.
btd_threshold_vs_soc: ≥50% improvement in 6-month survival rate vs. historical control (~20%).

symptoms: Slowly progressive cerebellar ataxia, late dementia, family history
treatment_goal: Palliative care; no effective pharmacologic treatment
first_line_soc: None — supportive and palliative care only.
treatment_algorithm: None — symptomatic management: anti-ataxia agents (clonazepam for myoclonus), PT/OT, speech therapy. Mean survival 5 years after onset. No proven therapies. Same investigational landscape as sporadic CJD.
us_prevalence: <100 US patients at any time; incidence ~1–10 per 100 million. Autosomal dominant PRNP mutation.
patients_needing_tx: <100 patients.
price: $500,000+ per year (ultra-orphan).
registrational_endpoint: Single-arm: survival time or SARA (Scale for Assessment and Rating of Ataxia) slope vs. natural history.
btd_threshold_vs_soc: N/A — any meaningful survival extension vs. natural history would qualify.

symptoms: Progressive insomnia, autonomic dysfunction, dementia, hallucinations, coma
treatment_goal: Palliative support; no effective treatment
first_line_soc: None — no effective pharmacologic or disease-modifying treatment.
treatment_algorithm: None — symptomatic: sedatives (clonazepam, lorazepam), opioids for pain, antipsychotics. Palliative/hospice care. Fatal within 6–36 months. No proven therapeutic intervention.
us_prevalence: <10 US cases/year (sporadic fatal insomnia); ~30–40 FFI families worldwide. CJD Foundation data.
patients_needing_tx: <10 patients/year in the US.
price: $500,000+ per patient for a compassionate-use anti-prion gene silencer.
peak_sales: Negligible commercially; <$5M. Research/compassionate use model only.
registrational_endpoint: N/A (insufficient patient numbers for RCT); single-arm survival or PSG sleep parameter improvement vs. natural history.
btd_threshold_vs_soc: N/A — any clinically meaningful improvement in survival would qualify given 100% fatality.

disease: Prion Disease Associated With Diarrhea and Autonomic Neuropathy
symptoms: Severe diarrhea, autonomic dysfunction (orthostatic hypotension), later ataxia and dementia
treatment_goal: Palliative autonomic and GI symptom management; no disease-modifying therapy
first_line_soc: None — supportive care for autonomic and GI manifestations.
treatment_algorithm: None — autonomic: fludrocortisone/midodrine for orthostasis; antidiarrheals; TPN for severe malnutrition. PRNP Y163X mutation. Extremely rare.
us_prevalence: <50 reported cases globally; ~0–5 US patients. Ultra-rare PRNP mutation.
patients_needing_tx: <5 in the US.
price: N/A commercially.

symptoms: Psychiatric symptoms, cognitive decline, speech difficulty; slower than CJD
treatment_goal: Palliative care; no disease-modifying therapy
first_line_soc: None — supportive/palliative care.
treatment_algorithm: None — symptomatic management same as sporadic CJD. Rare sporadic prion disease (PRNP 129 polymorphism). ~50–100 cases worldwide.
us_prevalence: <50 US cases ever reported; ~5–10 per year globally.
patients_needing_tx: <10 patients/year in the US.
price: N/A commercially.

symptoms: Cerebellar ataxia, tremor, laughing spasms, dementia — now virtually eradicated
treatment_goal: Palliative; disease is historically extinct (cannibalism-transmitted)
first_line_soc: None — no effective treatment; disease is essentially eliminated.
treatment_algorithm: None — historically palliative only. All cases linked to ritual cannibalism in Papua New Guinea. No cases reported in US. Prion research derived from Kuru laid groundwork for all prion biology.
us_prevalence: 0 US cases; disease is extinct in source population. Historical condition.
price: N/A — no commercially relevant market.

symptoms: Episodic loss of awareness, convulsions, sensory disturbances, automatisms
treatment_goal: Achieve seizure freedom, minimize drug side effects
first_line_soc: Levetiracetam (Keppra) — broad-spectrum antiseizure medication — or lamotrigine (Lamictal), carbamazepine (focal), or valproate (generalized) depending on seizure type.
treatment_algorithm: Monotherapy first: levetiracetam, lamotrigine, carbamazepine, oxcarbazepine (focal); valproate, lamotrigine, levetiracetam (generalized). Add-on: lacosamide (Vimpat), brivaracetam (Briviact), cenobamate (Xcopri — 2019 approval, high seizure-free rates), perampanel (Fycompa). Refractory: vagus nerve stimulation, responsive neurostimulation (RNS), anterior thalamic DBS. Dietary: ketogenic diet. Fenfluramine (Fintepla) for Dravet; ganaxolone (Ztalmy) for CDKL5.
us_prevalence: ~3.4 million US patients with epilepsy/seizure disorders. CDC data; Epilepsy Foundation.
patients_needing_tx: ~3.2 million require antiseizure medication; ~900,000 (~30%) have drug-resistant epilepsy.
price: $15,000–$80,000/year for a novel ASM; cenobamate (Xcopri) WAC ~$20,000/year; targeted gene therapies $500,000+.
peak_sales: $500M–$2B for a novel broad-spectrum ASM at 1.5–6% total market share; math: 3,200,000 × $25,000 × 0.015–0.06.
registrational_endpoint: 50% responder rate (proportion with ≥50% reduction in seizure frequency) and seizure-free rate at 12 weeks vs. placebo in add-on RCT.
btd_threshold_vs_soc: ≥30% absolute improvement in 50%-responder rate AND ≥15% seizure-free rate at 12 weeks vs. placebo add-on.

symptoms: Difficulty initiating/maintaining sleep, daytime fatigue, impaired concentration, irritability
treatment_goal: Restore normal sleep, reduce daytime impairment, avoid dependence
first_line_soc: Cognitive Behavioral Therapy for Insomnia (CBT-I) — non-pharmacologic first-line per ACP guidelines. Pharmacologically: suvorexant (Belsomra) or lemborexant (Dayvigo) — dual orexin receptor antagonists — preferred over benzodiazepines.
treatment_algorithm: CBT-I first-line. DORAs: suvorexant (Belsomra 5–20 mg), lemborexant (Dayvigo 5–10 mg). BzRAs: zolpidem (Ambien), eszopiclone (Lunesta), zaleplon (Sonata) — for short-term. Doxepin low-dose (Silenor 3–6 mg). Ramelteon (Rozerem) for sleep onset. Trazodone off-label. Avoid long-acting benzodiazepines. EDS treatment: modafinil/armodafinil, solriamfetol (Sunosi), pitolisant (Wakix).
us_prevalence: ~50–70 million US adults with chronic insomnia; ~10–20% prevalence. NIH estimates.
patients_needing_tx: ~20 million use pharmacologic therapy; EDS ~5 million.
price: $3,000–$15,000/year for a novel agent; branded DORAs ~$4,000–$8,000/year.
peak_sales: $500M–$2B at 0.25–1% share in the highly competitive insomnia market.
registrational_endpoint: Subjective sleep onset latency (sSOL) and Wake After Sleep Onset (WASO) by daily sleep diary at 4 weeks vs. placebo.
btd_threshold_vs_soc: ≥30-minute reduction in sSOL AND ≥30-minute reduction in WASO vs. placebo at 4 weeks, superior to DORA comparator.

disease: Periodic Limb Movement Disorder (PLMD) and Restless Legs Syndrome (RLS)
symptoms: Uncomfortable urge to move legs at rest/nighttime, leg kicking during sleep, sleep disruption
treatment_goal: Eliminate RLS symptoms, normalize periodic limb movements, restore sleep
first_line_soc: Dopamine agonists: pramipexole (Mirapex) or ropinirole (Requip) — FDA-approved for moderate-to-severe RLS — or alpha-2-delta ligands (gabapentin enacarbil [Horizant], pregabalin) as preferred due to lower augmentation risk.
treatment_algorithm: Iron supplementation (serum ferritin <75 µg/L). Gabapentin enacarbil (Horizant — FDA-approved for moderate-severe RLS), pregabalin. Dopamine agonists (pramipexole, ropinirole) — second-line due to augmentation. Low-dose opioids for refractory. Non-pharmacologic: exercise, sleep hygiene. PLMD: same agents; treat associated conditions.
us_prevalence: ~5–8 million US patients with moderate-to-severe RLS requiring treatment; PLMD prevalence ~4–11% of adults. RLSF/NSF data.
patients_needing_tx: ~5 million require pharmacologic treatment for RLS; ~2 million for PLMD.
price: $3,000–$15,000/year; branded Horizant ~$3,500/year.
registrational_endpoint: International Restless Legs Syndrome (IRLS) rating scale change from baseline and Clinical Global Impression Improvement (CGI-I) at 12 weeks vs. placebo.
btd_threshold_vs_soc: ≥3-point greater reduction in IRLS score vs. pramipexole at 12 weeks with ≤10% augmentation rate at 52 weeks.

symptoms: Misaligned sleep-wake timing, insomnia at night, excessive daytime sleepiness
treatment_goal: Re-entrain circadian rhythm, restore normal sleep timing
first_line_soc: Melatonin — chronobiotic — timed low-dose (0.5–3 mg) for DSPD and jet lag; light therapy combined with melatonin for most circadian disorders.
treatment_algorithm: Melatonin (low-dose, timed). Light therapy (10,000 lux morning for DSPD; evening for ASPD). Tasimelteon (Hetlioz) — melatonin receptor agonist — FDA-approved for Non-24-Hour Sleep-Wake Disorder in blind individuals (2014) and Smith-Magenis syndrome (2023). Ramelteon (Rozerem). Chronotherapy. Suvorexant (Belsomra) as adjunct for insomnia component.
us_prevalence: ~5–10 million US adults with circadian rhythm disorders (DSPD ~1–3%, shift work SWD ~10–15% of shift workers).
patients_needing_tx: ~3 million require pharmacologic intervention.
price: $5,000–$20,000/year for a novel circadian-targeting compound.
registrational_endpoint: Sleep diary-derived sleep onset time (lights out to sleep latency) and Total Sleep Time change vs. placebo over 4 weeks.
btd_threshold_vs_soc: ≥30-minute advance in sleep onset time AND ≥45-minute increase in TST vs. placebo at 4 weeks.

symptoms: Sleepwalking, night terrors, sleep paralysis, REM sleep behavior disorder (RBD)
treatment_goal: Prevent injury during episodes, reduce frequency of disruptive behaviors
first_line_soc: Clonazepam 0.25–2 mg at bedtime — first-line for REM sleep behavior disorder (RBD) and some NREM parasomnias.
treatment_algorithm: RBD: clonazepam (first-line), melatonin 3–12 mg (better tolerated, evidence-supported). NREM parasomnias (sleepwalking/night terrors): benzodiazepines (clonazepam, diazepam), SSRI/TCAs for psychophysiologic triggers, safety measures. Nightmare disorder: prazosin (alpha-blocker, off-label, evidence-supported). Sodium oxybate for confusional arousals. Non-pharmacologic: sleep hygiene, scheduled awakenings.
us_prevalence: ~20–30 million US adults with parasomnia episodes; RBD prevalence ~0.5–1% (~1–3 million). AASM data.
patients_needing_tx: ~3 million require pharmacologic management.
peak_sales: $50M–$150M at 0.2–0.6% share (mostly managed with cheap generics).
registrational_endpoint: Video-PSG-confirmed RBD episode frequency per night and RBDQ-HK questionnaire score change at 12 weeks vs. placebo.
btd_threshold_vs_soc: ≥50% reduction in RBD event frequency and ≥10-point improvement in RBDQ-HK score vs. placebo at 12 weeks.

symptoms: Excessive daytime sleepiness, cataplexy, sleep paralysis, hypnagogic hallucinations
treatment_goal: Eliminate cataplexy attacks, reduce daytime sleepiness, restore normal REM
first_line_soc: Sodium oxybate (Xyrem/Lumryz) — GHB derivative — FDA-approved for excessive daytime sleepiness (EDS) and cataplexy in narcolepsy type 1; pitolisant (Wakix) for EDS and cataplexy.
treatment_algorithm: Cataplexy: sodium oxybate (Xyrem, low-sodium Lumryz), pitolisant (Wakix). EDS: modafinil (Provigil), armodafinil (Nuvigil), methylphenidate, amphetamines, solriamfetol (Sunosi). TCAs/venlafaxine for cataplexy (second-line). Combination approaches. TAK-861 (orexin receptor 2 agonist) in Phase 3 — positive results 2025.
us_prevalence: ~175,000–200,000 US patients with narcolepsy (type 1 and 2). Narcolepsy Network; ~1/2,000 prevalence.
patients_needing_tx: ~180,000 require pharmacologic treatment.
price: $50,000–$150,000/year; Xyrem WAC ~$100,000/year; orexin agonists expected ~$80,000–$120,000/year.
peak_sales: $300M–$1B for an orexin 2 receptor agonist at 2–7% share; math: 180,000 × $80,000 × 0.02–0.07.
registrational_endpoint: Weekly cataplexy attack rate and Epworth Sleepiness Scale (ESS) score at 8–12 weeks vs. placebo.
btd_threshold_vs_soc: ≥50% reduction in weekly cataplexy rate AND ≥3-point improvement in ESS at 8 weeks vs. placebo.

symptoms: Loud upper airway noise during sleep, partner-reported, disrupted partner sleep
treatment_goal: Reduce snoring intensity, improve airway patency, prevent OSA progression
first_line_soc: None pharmacologic — behavioral modification (weight loss, positional therapy, alcohol avoidance); mandibular advancement devices (MADs); CPAP if concurrent OSA.
treatment_algorithm: None proven pharmacologically. Behavioral: weight loss, positional therapy, alcohol avoidance. Devices: MAD, CPAP for concurrent OSA. Nasal steroids for congestion-related snoring. Surgical: uvulopalatopharyngoplasty (UPPP), palatal implants, radiofrequency ablation. No approved pharmacologic therapy. Dasotraline and other compounds studied without success.
us_prevalence: ~90 million US adults snore; ~37 million habitual snorers. NSF/AASM data.
patients_needing_tx: ~15–20 million seek treatment; ~5 million use Rx intervention.
price: $500–$3,000/year for a novel topical upper airway agent.
registrational_endpoint: Snore intensity (dB) reduction measured by validated bedside monitor and Snore Outcomes Survey score at 12 weeks vs. placebo.
btd_threshold_vs_soc: ≥30% reduction in snore intensity (dB) and ≥2-point improvement in partner-reported sleep quality at 12 weeks vs. placebo.

symptoms: Excessive daytime sleepiness despite adequate sleep, long sleep times, sleep inertia (grogginess)
treatment_goal: Reduce daytime sleepiness, improve wakefulness and cognitive function
first_line_soc: Low-sodium oxybate (Lumryz) — calcium, magnesium, potassium, sodium oxybate — FDA-approved August 2023 for idiopathic hypersomnia.
treatment_algorithm: Low-sodium oxybate (Lumryz, once-nightly). Modafinil (Provigil, off-label) or armodafinil (Nuvigil, off-label) as wake-promoters. Sodium oxybate (Xyrem, off-label). Clarithromycin (off-label, modulates GABA-A). Flumazenil (off-label). Methylphenidate, amphetamines (off-label stimulants). Clinical trials: pitolisant (Wakix) for IH.
us_prevalence: ~37,000–170,000 US patients (prevalence uncertain; 0.01–0.05%); Sleep Foundation estimates.
patients_needing_tx: ~100,000 require pharmacologic treatment.
registrational_endpoint: Epworth Sleepiness Scale (ESS) score change and Patient Global Impression of Change at 8 weeks vs. placebo (as used in L-SXB IH approval study).
btd_threshold_vs_soc: ≥3-point improvement in ESS score at 8 weeks vs. placebo (L-SXB achieved 3.5 points vs. placebo).

disease: Cervical Spondylosis and Spondylotic Cervical Myelopathy
symptoms: Neck pain, arm/hand numbness, gait difficulty, clumsiness, bladder issues
treatment_goal: Halt myelopathy progression, relieve radiculopathy, restore function
first_line_soc: None pharmacologic for myelopathy — surgical decompression (ACDF or laminoplasty) for progressive myelopathy; NSAIDs and physical therapy for cervical radiculopathy.
treatment_algorithm: Radiculopathy: NSAIDs, gabapentinoids, short oral steroid taper, epidural injections, PT. Myelopathy: surgical decompression (ACDF/laminoplasty) — definitive treatment. No pharmacologic disease-modifying therapy for myelopathy. Neuroprotective strategies (riluzole for myelopathy) — limited evidence. Post-op: PT for functional recovery.
us_prevalence: ~10 million US adults with symptomatic cervical spondylosis; ~200,000–400,000 with myelopathy. NASS/AAO-NS data.
patients_needing_tx: ~5 million require pharmacologic pain/symptom management; ~300,000 for myelopathy (surgical or combined).
price: $5,000–$30,000/year for a novel neuroprotective agent for myelopathy.
registrational_endpoint: mJOA (modified Japanese Orthopaedic Association) score change at 12 months vs. surgery alone or placebo.
btd_threshold_vs_soc: ≥1.5-point improvement in mJOA score at 12 months vs. placebo add-on to standard care.

symptoms: Progressive lower limb stiffness, spastic gait, hyperreflexia, urinary urgency
treatment_goal: Reduce spasticity, preserve ambulation, improve quality of life
first_line_soc: Baclofen (oral or intrathecal) — GABA-B agonist — first-line for spasticity management.
treatment_algorithm: Baclofen oral 10–80 mg/day; intrathecal baclofen pump for severe. Tizanidine (alpha-2 agonist). Botulinum toxin for focal spasticity (gastrocnemius, hip adductors). Physical therapy. Oxybutynin/mirabegron for bladder. Gene therapy in research (SPG4/SPAST targeting). No disease-modifying agents approved.
us_prevalence: ~20,000–30,000 US patients with HSP (all genetic forms); prevalence ~1–10/100,000. HSP Research Group.
patients_needing_tx: ~25,000 require antispastic pharmacologic management.
price: $50,000–$200,000/year for a gene therapy or disease-modifying agent.
registrational_endpoint: Spastic Paraplegia Rating Scale (SPRS) change and 6-minute walk distance at 52 weeks vs. placebo.
btd_threshold_vs_soc: ≥2-point improvement in SPRS total score at 52 weeks vs. placebo.

symptoms: Progressive weakness, sensory loss, bladder/bowel dysfunction, depending on level
treatment_goal: Emergency decompression, preserve neurologic function, treat underlying cause
first_line_soc: High-dose dexamethasone (10 mg IV loading, then 4 mg q6h) — corticosteroid — for metastatic spinal cord compression; surgical decompression for structural causes.
treatment_algorithm: Dexamethasone for metastatic epidural SCC (MESCC) × 5–7 days. Radiotherapy for MESCC (definitive or post-surgical). Surgical: laminectomy/vertebroplasty for structural. Biphosphonates/denosumab for bone metastases preventing recurrence. Cervical/thoracic/lumbar surgical decompression for disc or bony causes.
us_prevalence: ~20,000 new cases of MESCC/year; structural SCC ~100,000/year (disc, spondylosis).
patients_needing_tx: ~120,000 require pharmacologic and/or surgical decompression annually.
price: $5,000–$30,000 per acute course for a novel neuroprotective agent.
registrational_endpoint: ASIA Impairment Scale change and ambulatory status at 6 months vs. standard decompression ± dexamethasone.
btd_threshold_vs_soc: ≥20% improvement in ambulatory rate at 6 months vs. standard care.

symptoms: Slowly progressive spastic paraparesis, bladder dysfunction, back pain, sensory changes
treatment_goal: Slow progression of spastic paraparesis, manage spasticity and bladder
first_line_soc: None disease-modifying approved. Corticosteroids (prednisolone) — empirically used for active inflammatory phase; modest short-term benefit.
treatment_algorithm: No proven disease-modifying treatment. Corticosteroids (short courses). Interferon-alpha (INF-α) — some evidence in trials. Zidovudine, lamivudine (antiretrovirals — minimal benefit). Spasticity: baclofen, tizanidine. Bladder: anticholinergics. Mogamulizumab (anti-CCR4 mAb) in Phase 2 trials for HAM/TSP.
us_prevalence: ~5,000–10,000 US patients (mainly Caribbean/Latin American/Japanese American immigrants); HTLV-1 seroprevalence ~1/100,000 in US general population.
patients_needing_tx: ~7,000 require pharmacologic management.
price: $50,000–$150,000/year for a novel anti-HTLV-1 or anti-inflammatory biologic.
registrational_endpoint: OPCA (Osame Motor Disability Scale) or Expanded Disability Status Scale (EDSS) change at 52 weeks vs. placebo.
btd_threshold_vs_soc: ≥20% reduction in EDSS progression rate at 52 weeks vs. placebo.

disease: Syrinx of the Spinal Cord or Brain Stem
symptoms: Cape-like dissociated sensory loss, arm weakness, spasticity, pain — cavitation within cord
treatment_goal: Treat underlying cause (Chiari, tumor), drain syrinx, halt expansion
first_line_soc: None pharmacologic — surgical treatment of underlying cause (Chiari decompression, tumor resection) is definitive.
treatment_algorithm: None pharmacologic. Surgical: posterior fossa decompression for Chiari I, syrinx shunting (syringo-subarachnoid or syringo-peritoneal shunt). Pain: gabapentinoids, TCAs. Nonsteroidal analgesics. PT. Monitor with serial MRI. No pharmacologic disease-modifying therapy.
us_prevalence: ~21,000 US patients with syringomyelia; prevalence ~8.4/100,000. American Syringomyelia & Chiari Alliance Project.
patients_needing_tx: ~10,000 require surgical or pharmacologic pain management.
price: $20,000–$80,000/year for a novel antisecretory or neuroprotective agent.
registrational_endpoint: Syrinx width reduction on MRI and NRS pain score at 12 months vs. standard surgical care.
btd_threshold_vs_soc: ≥20% reduction in syrinx diameter AND ≥1.5-point NRS pain improvement at 12 months vs. sham/standard care.

symptoms: Sudden severe hypertension, sweating, bradycardia, headache in spinal cord injury patients
treatment_goal: Rapidly lower blood pressure, identify and remove triggering stimulus
first_line_soc: Nitroglycerine paste or nifedipine (immediate-release) — vasodilators — for acute BP reduction; first-line emergency management per SCIRE guidelines.
treatment_algorithm: Acute: sit patient upright, remove trigger (bladder/bowel obstruction, pressure). Antihypertensives: nitroglycerin paste 2%, nifedipine 10 mg sublingual (if >150 mmHg systolic), captopril. Refractory: IV hydralazine, labetalol. Prophylaxis for recurrent AD: alpha-blockers (terazosin, prazosin), sildenafil. Baclofen to reduce spasms. Botulinum toxin for bladder hyper-reflexia.
us_prevalence: ~50–80% of patients with spinal cord injury at T6 or above experience AD; ~250,000 relevant SCI patients in the US. Christopher & Dana Reeve Foundation.
patients_needing_tx: ~150,000 US SCI patients at risk of AD episodes; ~50,000 require chronic preventive management.
price: $2,000–$10,000/year for chronic prophylactic agent.
registrational_endpoint: Frequency of AD episodes per month and peak systolic BP during triggered AD at 12 weeks vs. placebo.
btd_threshold_vs_soc: ≥50% reduction in monthly AD episode frequency and ≥30 mmHg reduction in peak SBP during AD vs. placebo.

symptoms: Progressive myelopathy, back/leg pain, acute hemorrhage, bladder dysfunction
treatment_goal: Prevent hemorrhage, eliminate AVM, preserve neurologic function
first_line_soc: None pharmacologic — definitive treatment is endovascular embolization or surgical resection/radiosurgery.
treatment_algorithm: None pharmacologic. Interventional: endovascular embolization (Onyx, NBCA liquid embolic). Surgical resection. Stereotactic radiosurgery for deep lesions. Corticosteroids for peri-treatment edema. Anti-epileptics if seizures. PT post-treatment rehabilitation.
us_prevalence: ~3,000–5,000 US patients with spinal cord AVMs; 0.5–1/100,000 prevalence.
patients_needing_tx: ~4,000 require active intervention.
price: $20,000–$80,000 per treatment course.
registrational_endpoint: ASIA Impairment Scale improvement and hemorrhage recurrence rate at 24 months vs. observation.
btd_threshold_vs_soc: ≥50% reduction in AVM obliteration failure rate at 24 months vs. standard embolization.

symptoms: Sudden bilateral leg weakness, sensory level, bladder/bowel dysfunction
treatment_goal: Halt demyelination, speed recovery, prevent permanent disability
first_line_soc: IV methylprednisolone (Solu-Medrol) 1 g/day × 3–5 days — high-dose corticosteroids as first-line for acute inflammatory transverse myelitis.
treatment_algorithm: IV methylprednisolone 1g/day × 5 days. Plasma exchange (5–7 exchanges) for steroid-refractory. Cyclophosphamide for fulminant cases. IVIg for parainfectious etiologies. AQP4-IgG positive: treat underlying NMOSD (eculizumab, inebilizumab). MOG-IgG positive: mycophenolate, azathioprine for prevention. PT/OT/bladder management. Rehabilitation.
us_prevalence: ~1,400–4,600 new cases/year in the US; incidence 1–8/million/year. Transverse Myelitis Association.
patients_needing_tx: ~3,000 require acute pharmacologic treatment/year.
price: $30,000–$100,000 per acute episode for a novel neuroprotective agent.
registrational_endpoint: ASIA Impairment Scale improvement and Expanded Disability Status Scale (EDSS) score at 6 months vs. standard steroid.
btd_threshold_vs_soc: ≥25% improvement in ASIA grade and ≥0.5-point EDSS reduction at 6 months vs. methylprednisolone alone.

symptoms: Bilateral leg weakness, loss of position/vibration sense, spasticity, cognitive changes — B12 deficiency
treatment_goal: Replenish B12, halt and reverse myelopathy
first_line_soc: Intramuscular vitamin B12 (cyanocobalamin or hydroxocobalamin) 1,000 µg/day × 7 days, then weekly × 4 weeks, then monthly — definitive treatment.
treatment_algorithm: IM cyanocobalamin 1,000 µg/day × 7 days → weekly × 4 → monthly lifelong. High-dose oral B12 (1,000–2,000 µg/day) if no pernicious anemia. Folate if deficient. Treat underlying cause (pernicious anemia: intrinsic factor antibody positive — requires IM; gastrectomy, malabsorption). PT for gait rehabilitation.
us_prevalence: ~5,000–10,000 new SCD cases/year in the US; prevalence unknown. Pernicious anemia affects ~0.1% of US adults (~300,000).
patients_needing_tx: ~7,500/year require parenteral B12 treatment.
price: $500–$3,000 per course (B12 is generic); novel neuroprotective agents $10,000–$30,000 per course.
peak_sales: $10M–$30M at 9–27% share (small market dominated by cheap generics).
registrational_endpoint: Neurological improvement score (sensory/motor exam) and serum methylmalonic acid normalization at 12 weeks vs. standard IM B12.
btd_threshold_vs_soc: ≥25% faster neurologic recovery score improvement at 12 weeks vs. standard IM B12.

symptoms: Severe back pain, fever, neurologic deficit, spinal tenderness — MRSA common cause
treatment_goal: Eradicate infection, prevent paralysis, emergency decompression if neurologic deficit
first_line_soc: IV anti-staphylococcal antibiotics (vancomycin + anti-Gram-negative coverage) — empiric first-line while awaiting culture; surgical decompression for neurologic deficit.
treatment_algorithm: Empiric IV: vancomycin (for MRSA) + ceftriaxone or piperacillin-tazobactam (for Gram-negatives). Culture-directed therapy × 6 weeks. Surgical: laminectomy + drainage for progressive neurologic deficit or failed medical treatment. Immobilization. Blood cultures before antibiotics. Post-treatment PT.
us_prevalence: ~5,000–10,000 new spinal epidural abscess cases/year in the US; incidence rising (opioid epidemic, spinal interventions). ~0.2–2/10,000 hospital admissions.
patients_needing_tx: ~7,500 require IV antibiotics ± surgery annually.
price: $5,000–$20,000 per course for a novel anti-biofilm/anti-MRSA agent.
registrational_endpoint: Neurologic recovery (Frankel/ASIA grade) at 3 months and microbiologic cure rate vs. standard antibiotics.
btd_threshold_vs_soc: ≥30% improvement in neurologic recovery rate at 3 months vs. vancomycin + standard care.

symptoms: Saddle anesthesia, bladder/bowel dysfunction, bilateral leg weakness from lumbar disc compression
treatment_goal: Emergency decompression to prevent permanent bladder/bowel paralysis
first_line_soc: None pharmacologic — emergency surgical decompression (laminectomy/discectomy) is the definitive treatment within 48 hours of symptom onset.
treatment_algorithm: None pharmacologic. Emergency surgical decompression within 24–48 hours of onset. Corticosteroids perioperatively (controversial). Bladder management (catheterization). PT/OT rehabilitation. Neuropathic pain management post-operatively.
us_prevalence: ~2,000–4,000 new cases/year in the US; incidence ~1–3.4/100,000/year.
patients_needing_tx: ~3,000 require emergency intervention annually.
price: $10,000–$30,000 per episode for a novel neuroprotective agent adjunct to surgery.
registrational_endpoint: Bladder function recovery (cystometry normalization) and ASIA score change at 6 months vs. surgery alone.
btd_threshold_vs_soc: ≥25% improvement in bladder function recovery rate at 6 months vs. surgery alone.

symptoms: Sudden flaccid paralysis, sensory loss below lesion, bladder/bowel retention — vascular onset
treatment_goal: Limit ischemic damage, maintain perfusion, prevent complications
first_line_soc: None proven pharmacologic. Supportive: optimize mean arterial pressure (MAP ≥85 mmHg), aspirin for atherothrombotic cause.
treatment_algorithm: None — emergency: MAP optimization (vasopressors if needed). Aspirin/anticoagulation for cardioembolic or atherothrombotic cause (after hemorrhage excluded). CSF drainage for aortic surgery-related SCI. No thrombolytics (risk > benefit). PT/OT/bladder rehabilitation critical. No FDA-approved neuroprotective therapy.
us_prevalence: ~500–1,500 new spinal cord infarction cases/year in the US; represents ~1% of all strokes.
patients_needing_tx: ~1,000/year require acute management and rehabilitation.
price: $20,000–$80,000 per episode for a novel neuroprotective agent.
registrational_endpoint: ASIA Impairment Scale improvement and motor function score change at 6 months vs. best medical management.
btd_threshold_vs_soc: ≥25% improvement in ASIA grade at 6 months vs. supportive care alone.

symptoms: Sudden severe back pain, rapidly progressive myelopathy, bladder/bowel dysfunction
treatment_goal: Emergency surgical evacuation to prevent permanent paralysis
first_line_soc: None pharmacologic — emergency surgical evacuation (laminectomy/decompression) is the primary and urgent treatment.
