treatment_algorithm: None — reverse anticoagulation emergently (vitamin K, PCC, FFP, protamine for heparin). Emergency laminectomy/drainage. ICU monitoring. PT rehabilitation. Conservative management only for spontaneous resolution with no neurologic deficit.
us_prevalence: ~1,000–2,000 new cases/year in the US (rising with anticoagulant use); spinal subdural hematoma is rarer than epidural.
patients_needing_tx: ~1,500/year require acute intervention.
price: $5,000–$20,000 per episode.
registrational_endpoint: Neurologic outcome (ASIA grade) at 3 months vs. standard surgical evacuation.
btd_threshold_vs_soc: ≥20% improvement in complete neurologic recovery rate at 3 months vs. surgery alone.

symptoms: Sudden facial drooping, arm weakness, speech difficulty, dizziness, vision loss (FAST)
treatment_goal: Restore blood flow rapidly, limit infarct, prevent recurrence
first_line_soc: IV alteplase (tPA, Activase) — thrombolytic — within 4.5 hours of onset, per AHA/ASA guidelines; tenecteplase (TNKase) now preferred over alteplase in many centers; mechanical thrombectomy for large vessel occlusion within 24 hours.
treatment_algorithm: Acute reperfusion: IV alteplase (0.9 mg/kg, max 90 mg) or tenecteplase (0.25 mg/kg). Mechanical thrombectomy for LVO (anterior/posterior circulation, extended window). Secondary prevention: aspirin 81–325 mg + clopidogrel (dual antiplatelet × 21 days for minor stroke); anticoagulation (apixaban, rivaroxaban) for AF-related. BP, statin (high-intensity atorvastatin), glucose control. Neuroprotectants: citicoline, NXY-059, edaravone all failed; argatroban, tenecteplase NXU data promising.
us_prevalence: ~795,000 new strokes/year (~610,000 ischemic first events + recurrences); ~7 million stroke survivors. AHA/ASA 2025 Statistics.
patients_needing_tx: ~610,000 new acute ischemic strokes/year; ~150,000 eligible for IV thrombolysis; ~30,000–40,000 receive thrombectomy.
price: $10,000–$50,000 per acute episode for a novel neuroprotective/thrombolytic; ongoing secondary prevention ~$5,000–$20,000/year.
peak_sales: $500M–$2B for a novel neuroprotective agent at 3–13% acute market share; math: 610,000 × $25,000 × 0.03–0.13.
registrational_endpoint: mRS 0–2 at 90 days (favorable outcome) vs. tPA or tenecteplase; or 90-day mRS shift analysis.
btd_threshold_vs_soc: ≥6% absolute improvement in mRS 0–2 at 90 days vs. IV tPA (tPA achieves ~39–55% favorable outcome).

symptoms: Usually silent until rupture; rupture: thunderclap headache, loss of consciousness, vomiting
treatment_goal: Prevent rupture, eliminate aneurysm, prevent rebleeding
first_line_soc: None pharmacologic — definitive treatment is endovascular coiling or surgical clipping.
treatment_algorithm: None pharmacologic for aneurysm elimination. Endovascular coiling or surgical clipping. Blood pressure control (nicardipine IV) peri-procedurally. Unruptured: watchful waiting vs. intervention based on size/location. Post-SAH management overlaps with subarachnoid hemorrhage SoC.
us_prevalence: ~6 million Americans have unruptured intracranial aneurysms (~2% prevalence); ~30,000 rupture annually. Brain Aneurysm Foundation.
patients_needing_tx: ~100,000/year undergo interventional treatment; ~500,000 are monitored with watchful waiting.
price: $10,000–$50,000 per treatment course for a pharmacologic agent targeting aneurysm wall biology.
registrational_endpoint: Aneurysm rupture rate at 24 months and aneurysm volume change on CTA/MRA vs. observation alone.
btd_threshold_vs_soc: ≥50% reduction in annual rupture rate in untreated unruptured aneurysms vs. natural history (~1–2%/year).

symptoms: Intracerebral hemorrhage, seizures, headache, neurologic deficit — vascular malformation
treatment_goal: Eliminate AVM to prevent hemorrhage, control seizures
first_line_soc: None pharmacologic — definitive treatment is surgical resection, stereotactic radiosurgery (Gamma Knife), or endovascular embolization (often combined).
treatment_algorithm: None pharmacologic for AVM obliteration. Surgery for accessible, ruptured AVMs. Stereotactic radiosurgery (Gamma Knife) for small/deep AVMs. Endovascular embolization (Onyx, NBCA) as adjunct. Anti-epileptics for seizures. Medical management only for patients who decline/are unfit for intervention (ARUBA trial: medical management showed lower event rate in unruptured AVMs). Bevacizumab for hereditary hemorrhagic telangiectasia (off-label).
us_prevalence: ~300,000 US patients with brain AVMs; ~1/500–1/200 prevalence. ANGIO/AVM Foundation.
patients_needing_tx: ~100,000 patients require active intervention or management.
price: $20,000–$100,000 per course for a novel anti-angiogenic or vascular targeting agent.
registrational_endpoint: AVM obliteration rate and hemorrhage recurrence at 24 months vs. standard radiosurgery or medical management.
btd_threshold_vs_soc: ≥30% improvement in obliteration rate at 24 months vs. current radiosurgery alone.

symptoms: Sudden focal neurologic deficit, severe headache, vomiting, altered consciousness — bleeding in brain parenchyma
treatment_goal: Limit hematoma expansion, reduce ICP, prevent rebleeding, early rehabilitation
first_line_soc: Blood pressure lowering to SBP <140 mmHg (intensive; labetalol, nicardipine IV) and reversal of anticoagulation — first-line pharmacologic management per AHA/ASA guidelines.
treatment_algorithm: BP control: IV labetalol or nicardipine to SBP <140 mmHg. Anticoagulation reversal: andexanet alfa (Andexxa) for apixaban/rivaroxaban; idarucizumab (Praxbind) for dabigatran; PCC4 for warfarin; protamine for heparin. Tranexamic acid (TXA) — not proven to improve outcomes in ICH but studied. Surgical: stereotactic aspiration/evacuation for cerebellar ICH or large lobar. ICP management (mannitol, hypertonic saline). MISTIE trials. No FDA-approved pharmacologic hemostatic agent for spontaneous ICH.
us_prevalence: ~85,000 new ICH cases/year in the US; represents ~15% of all strokes. AHA/ASA 2025 statistics.
patients_needing_tx: ~85,000 require acute pharmacologic and/or surgical management annually.
price: $20,000–$80,000 per episode for a novel hemostatic neuroprotective agent.
registrational_endpoint: Hematoma volume change at 24 hours and mRS (modified Rankin Scale) 0–2 at 90 days vs. standard care.
btd_threshold_vs_soc: ≥15% absolute increase in mRS 0–2 at 90 days vs. standard intensive BP management.

symptoms: Sudden brief focal neurologic symptoms (weakness, speech, vision) resolving within 24 hours — no infarction
treatment_goal: Prevent early stroke, identify high-risk patients, initiate antithrombotic therapy
first_line_soc: Aspirin 81–325 mg + clopidogrel 75 mg (DAPT) for 21–90 days — dual antiplatelet therapy per AHA/ASA TIA guidelines (POINT trial); ticagrelor + aspirin alternative.
treatment_algorithm: DAPT: aspirin + clopidogrel × 21–90 days (then aspirin monotherapy or clopidogrel monotherapy). Anticoagulation (apixaban/rivaroxaban) for AF. High-intensity statin (atorvastatin 40–80 mg). Antihypertensives. Urgent evaluation (ABCD2 score, MRI DWI, carotid imaging). Carotid endarterectomy or stenting for ≥50% symptomatic stenosis within 2 weeks. Colchicine for inflammatory atherosclerosis (CONVINCE trial).
us_prevalence: ~200,000–350,000 TIA events/year in the US; annual incidence ~83/100,000. AHA/ASA 2025 statistics.
patients_needing_tx: ~300,000 require antithrombotic and risk factor management annually.
price: $2,000–$10,000 per acute course; chronic secondary prevention ~$3,000–$10,000/year.
peak_sales: $100M–$300M for a novel anti-inflammatory or antiplatelet agent at 3–10% share; math: 300,000 × $5,000 × 0.03–0.10.
registrational_endpoint: Stroke at 90 days (primary prevention endpoint) and NIHSS change vs. aspirin + clopidogrel (DAPT); non-inferiority or superiority design.
btd_threshold_vs_soc: ≥25% relative reduction in 90-day stroke rate vs. DAPT (DAPT 90-day stroke rate ~5–7% in high-risk TIA).

symptoms: Thunderclap headache ('worst of my life'), neck stiffness, photophobia, LOC
treatment_goal: Prevent rebleeding, prevent cerebral vasospasm, manage hydrocephalus
first_line_soc: Nimodipine (Nimotop) — calcium channel blocker — oral 60 mg q4h × 21 days — FDA-approved to prevent vasospasm-related ischemic deficits following aneurysmal SAH.
treatment_algorithm: Nimodipine 60 mg PO/NG q4h × 21 days (standard of care). Aneurysm securing: endovascular coiling or surgical clipping within 24 hours (rebleeding prevention). Triple-H therapy (hypertension, hypervolemia, hemodilution) for vasospasm — controversial. CSF diversion (EVD) for hydrocephalus. Statins (atorvastatin) and magnesium — studied but not conclusively beneficial. Clazosentan (endothelin antagonist) — shown to reduce angiographic vasospasm but not outcome; Phase 3 ongoing.
us_prevalence: ~30,000 aneurysmal SAH cases/year in the US; ~3% of all strokes. AHA/ASA data.
patients_needing_tx: ~30,000 require nimodipine and acute multi-modal management annually.
price: $20,000–$80,000 per episode for a novel anti-vasospasm agent; nimodipine oral course ~$500–$2,000.
registrational_endpoint: Modified Rankin Scale (mRS) 0–2 at 90 days and angiographic vasospasm rate at 7 days vs. nimodipine.
btd_threshold_vs_soc: ≥10% absolute improvement in mRS 0–2 at 90 days vs. nimodipine alone (nimodipine baseline ~40–55% favorable outcome).

symptoms: Intense fear of social situations, embarrassment, avoidance
treatment_goal: Reduce social fear; expand engagement in work and social life
first_line_soc: Paroxetine, sertraline (SSRIs — FDA-approved for social anxiety disorder), and venlafaxine ER (SNRI — FDA-approved) are first-line. CBT is equally effective.
treatment_algorithm: Step 1: SSRI (paroxetine 20–60 mg, sertraline 50–200 mg — FDA-approved; escitalopram off-label) or venlafaxine ER (75–225 mg — FDA-approved) + CBT. Step 2: Switch SSRI/SNRI; pregabalin (off-label). Step 3: MAOIs (phenelzine — highly effective but limited by side effects). Gabapentin off-label. Beta-blockers (propranolol) for performance subtype only. Buspirone as augmentation.
us_prevalence: ~15 million US adults (~7% prevalence; ADAA/NIMH); most common anxiety disorder.
patients_needing_tx: ~7 million patients seeking pharmacotherapy.
peak_sales: Large, competitive market. Novel mechanism: 5–8% share. Peak sales ~$1.5–3.0B.
registrational_endpoint: LSAS ≥30% reduction at 12 weeks; CGI-I responder rate ≥50%.
btd_threshold_vs_soc: LSAS ≥10-point superiority vs. paroxetine; full remission rate ≥30% absolute improvement.

symptoms: Persistent excessive worry, tension, fatigue, difficulty concentrating
treatment_goal: Reduce anxiety to manageable levels; restore daily functioning
first_line_soc: SSRIs (escitalopram, paroxetine) and SNRIs (venlafaxine, duloxetine) are first-line. Buspirone is an alternative first-line agent.
treatment_algorithm: Step 1: SSRI (escitalopram 10–20 mg, paroxetine ER, sertraline) or SNRI (venlafaxine ER 75–225 mg, duloxetine 60–120 mg). Step 2: Buspirone augmentation or switch; pregabalin (Europe-approved, off-label US). Step 3: TCAs; hydroxyzine for acute anxiety. Benzodiazepines short-term only. CBT gold standard for psychotherapy. 2024: zuranolone (Zurzuvae, GABA-A) approved for MDD — under study for GAD.
us_prevalence: ~6.8 million US adults (~3.1% prevalence; NIMH); lifetime prevalence ~5.7%.
patients_needing_tx: ~4.5 million patients receiving pharmacotherapy.
price: $12,000–$22,000/year; premium over generic escitalopram (~$500/yr).
peak_sales: Large competitive market. Novel mechanism: 5–10% share. Peak sales ~$1.5–3.0B.
registrational_endpoint: GAD-7 score ≥50% reduction; HAM-A total score ≥10-point improvement at 8 weeks.
btd_threshold_vs_soc: ≥3-point superiority on HAM-A vs. SSRI/SNRI comparator; faster onset (≤2 weeks).

symptoms: Sudden intense fear episodes, heart racing, breathlessness, terror
treatment_goal: Eliminate panic attacks; reduce anticipatory anxiety
first_line_soc: SSRIs (paroxetine, sertraline — FDA-approved) and SNRIs (venlafaxine) are first-line. Combined with CBT/panic-focused exposure therapy.
treatment_algorithm: Step 1: SSRI (paroxetine, sertraline, fluoxetine — FDA-approved for panic disorder) + CBT. Step 2: Venlafaxine ER (FDA-approved). Step 3: TCAs (imipramine, clomipramine); clonazepam (benzodiazepine) short-term. Alprazolam second-line (high dependency risk). Combination therapy superior. Full remission achievable in 70–90% with combined treatment.
us_prevalence: ~6 million US adults with panic disorder (~2.7% prevalence; NIMH); 15–30% of primary care patients have panic attacks.
patients_needing_tx: ~3.5 million patients needing pharmacotherapy.
peak_sales: Well-established SSRI market. Novel mechanism: 8–12% share. Peak sales ~$1.0–2.0B.
registrational_endpoint: Panic attack frequency ≥50% reduction; PDSS (Panic Disorder Severity Scale) ≥35% reduction at 10 weeks.
btd_threshold_vs_soc: ≥50% PDSS reduction vs. SSRI; superior full remission rate (≥30% absolute difference).

symptoms: Intense fear of specific object or situation; immediate anxiety and avoidance
treatment_goal: Eliminate phobic response; restore ability to face feared situations
first_line_soc: Exposure therapy (CBT) is first-line and most effective. Pharmacotherapy is adjunctive: D-cycloserine (memory consolidation augment) or SSRIs for comorbid anxiety.
treatment_algorithm: Exposure therapy: in vivo or virtual reality-based. Pharmacologic (adjunctive, off-label): D-cycloserine (50 mg before sessions — augments extinction learning); SSRIs for generalized anxiety component; benzodiazepines only acutely (may impair extinction). Propranolol pre-exposure for performance anxiety subtype.
us_prevalence: ~19.3 million US adults (~8.7% prevalence; NIMH); most common anxiety disorder.
patients_needing_tx: ~2 million patients seeking pharmacologic augmentation.
price: $5,000–$12,000/year; mostly generic/OTC drugs used.
peak_sales: Majority treated with therapy alone. Pharmacotherapy adjunct: 5–8% share. Peak sales ~$300–500M.
registrational_endpoint: Fear/avoidance composite scale ≥40% reduction; BAT (Behavioral Approach Test) completion rate at 8 weeks.
btd_threshold_vs_soc: ≥40% improvement in BAT completion; phobia severity scale ≥35% vs. placebo + exposure therapy.

symptoms: Fear and avoidance of open spaces, crowds, public transport
treatment_goal: Expand mobility, reduce avoidance, achieve independence
first_line_soc: SSRIs (sertraline, paroxetine) or SNRIs (venlafaxine) are first-line pharmacotherapy, combined with CBT/exposure therapy.
treatment_algorithm: Step 1: SSRI (paroxetine, sertraline, escitalopram) + CBT. Step 2: SNRI (venlafaxine ER). Step 3: Tricyclic antidepressants (imipramine) or MAOIs for refractory cases. Benzodiazepines adjunctive short-term. Combination therapy (drug + CBT) superior to monotherapy.
us_prevalence: ~1.3–1.8% prevalence; ~3–4 million US adults (NIMH). Often co-occurs with panic disorder.
patients_needing_tx: ~2.5 million patients; ~60–70% seek treatment, ~50% use pharmacotherapy. Pharmacologic pool ~1.5 million.
price: $8,000–$14,000/year; comparable to anxiety disorder drugs (Lexapro generic ~$500/yr limits premium).
peak_sales: 10–15% of pharmacologically treated patients. Peak sales ~$600M–1.0B.
registrational_endpoint: Mobility Inventory for Agoraphobia (MIA) score and CGI-I at 12 weeks.
btd_threshold_vs_soc: ≥30% superiority on MIA total score vs. SSRI comparator.

symptoms: Feeling detached from one's body or surroundings; emotional numbness
treatment_goal: Reduce dissociative episodes; restore sense of reality
first_line_soc: CBT (specifically tailored for DPDR) is first-line. No FDA-approved pharmacotherapy. SSRIs (fluoxetine, sertraline) and lamotrigine used off-label.
treatment_algorithm: CBT for DPDR (Hunter Protocol) is most evidence-based treatment. Pharmacologic (off-label): SSRIs (fluoxetine most studied); lamotrigine (has small RCT evidence); naltrexone (shows promise); SSRIs + lamotrigine combination. Benzodiazepines worsen symptoms. Opioid antagonists under investigation.
us_prevalence: ~1–2% prevalence; ~3–5 million US adults transiently; chronic disorder ~750,000.
patients_needing_tx: ~300,000 patients with chronic DPDR needing pharmacotherapy.
registrational_endpoint: Cambridge Depersonalization Scale (CDS) ≥35% reduction at 12 weeks.
btd_threshold_vs_soc: ≥40% CDS reduction vs. placebo; CGI-I 'much improved.'

symptoms: Multiple distinct identity states, memory gaps, dissociative episodes
treatment_goal: Integrate identity states; reduce dissociation and trauma symptoms
first_line_soc: Psychotherapy (trauma-focused, phase-oriented treatment, ISSTD guidelines) is sole primary treatment. No FDA-approved pharmacotherapy. SSRIs/antipsychotics treat comorbid symptoms.
treatment_algorithm: Phase-oriented trauma therapy (ISSTD guidelines): stabilization, trauma processing, integration. Pharmacologic (all off-label, target comorbidities): SSRIs for depression/PTSD comorbidity; low-dose antipsychotics for severe dissociation/paranoia; mood stabilizers; naltrexone for self-harm. No drug addresses DID itself.
us_prevalence: ~1–3% general population; ~1.5–3 million US adults; treatment-seeking ~300,000.
patients_needing_tx: ~200,000 patients using pharmacotherapy adjunctively.
peak_sales: Highly specialized market. 15–20% uptake if approved. Peak sales ~$100–200M.
registrational_endpoint: Dissociative Experiences Scale (DES-II) ≥40% reduction; MID severity score at 16 weeks.
btd_threshold_vs_soc: ≥40% DES reduction vs. placebo; significant reduction in alter-switching frequency.

symptoms: Inability to recall important personal information, usually trauma-related
treatment_goal: Recover memory continuity; process underlying trauma
first_line_soc: Psychotherapy (trauma-focused CBT, EMDR, hypnotherapy) is primary. No FDA-approved pharmacotherapy; sodium amytal (amobarbital) historically used as abreactive interview agent.
treatment_algorithm: Psychotherapy: trauma-focused CBT, EMDR. Supportive therapy in safe environment. Pharmacologic (no standard): benzodiazepines for anxiety only; historical use of amobarbital narcosynthesis; no evidence-based drug treatments for core amnesia. Treat comorbid PTSD/depression with SSRIs.
us_prevalence: ~1.8% lifetime prevalence; ~1–3 million US adults (including fugue subtype); chronic/treatment-seeking ~200,000.
patients_needing_tx: ~100,000 patients needing pharmacologic support adjunctive to therapy.
peak_sales: Very small market. Novel drug: 15–20% uptake. Peak sales ~$50–100M.
registrational_endpoint: Autobiographical Memory Test (AMT) improvement; DES-II (dissociation scale) reduction at 12 weeks.
btd_threshold_vs_soc: ≥35% reduction in DES-II; significant improvement in AMT recall.

symptoms: Recurrent uncontrolled overeating, shame, distress without purging
treatment_goal: Reduce binge frequency, improve psychological distress
first_line_soc: Vyvanse (lisdexamfetamine, AMPHETAMINE) — the only FDA-approved drug for BED (2015). Indicated for moderate-to-severe BED in adults.
treatment_algorithm: FDA-approved: lisdexamfetamine (Vyvanse) 50–70 mg/day — first-line pharmacotherapy. Off-label: topiramate (effective but poorly tolerated); SSRIs (fluoxetine, sertraline) for comorbid depression; CBT is gold standard psychotherapy, often combined with pharmacotherapy. Second-line: naltrexone/bupropion (Contrave) off-label.
us_prevalence: ~2.8 million US adults (NIMH); lifetime prevalence ~2.6% women, 0.8% men.
patients_needing_tx: ~1.2 million patients seeking pharmacotherapy; ~50% of those diagnosed consider medication.
price: $15,000–$20,000/year; Vyvanse for BED ~$16,000/year WAC benchmark.
peak_sales: Lisdexamfetamine captured ~$400M at peak for BED indication. Novel drug: 10–20% share. Peak sales ~$800M–1.5B.
registrational_endpoint: Binge eating days/week reduction ≥50% at 12 weeks; 4-week cessation of binge eating.
btd_threshold_vs_soc: Superiority vs. placebo: ≥50% reduction in binge days/week and ≥30% improvement on CGI-I.

symptoms: Recurrent binge-purge cycles, shame, dental erosion, electrolyte imbalance
treatment_goal: Eliminate binge-purge cycle; restore healthy eating patterns
first_line_soc: Fluoxetine (Prozac) 60 mg/day is the only FDA-approved drug for bulimia nervosa. Combined with CBT for optimal outcomes.
treatment_algorithm: FDA-approved: fluoxetine 60 mg/day. Step 2: Other SSRIs (sertraline, citalopram) off-label. Topiramate has evidence (weight control, binge reduction) off-label. Avoid bupropion (seizure risk in purging patients). CBT-BN is gold standard psychotherapy. Combination CBT + fluoxetine superior to either alone.
us_prevalence: ~1.5–2.1 million US adults (0.9–1.5% lifetime prevalence; NIMH/NEDA).
patients_needing_tx: ~800,000 patients currently symptomatic needing pharmacotherapy.
price: $12,000–$18,000/year; eating disorder premium over generic fluoxetine.
peak_sales: Better-established market than anorexia. Novel mechanism: 15–20% uptake. Peak sales ~$500–900M.
registrational_endpoint: Binge and purge frequency: ≥50% reduction per week at 8 weeks; 4-week abstinence rate.
btd_threshold_vs_soc: ≥50% reduction in binge/purge episodes vs. placebo; superiority vs. fluoxetine 60 mg.

symptoms: Extreme food selectivity, fear of eating, weight loss, nutritional deficiency
treatment_goal: Expand food variety, restore nutrition, reduce distress
first_line_soc: No FDA-approved pharmacotherapy. Behavioral/CBT-based exposure therapy (ARFID-specific CBT) and nutritional counseling are standard. Cyproheptadine and SSRIs used off-label.
treatment_algorithm: Primary: ARFID-specific CBT-AR (cognitive behavioral therapy for ARFID). Pharmacologic (off-label): cyproheptadine (appetite stimulant); buspirone (sensory sensitivity); SSRIs (anxiety component, sertraline); mirtazapine for weight gain/anxiety. D-cycloserine investigated in trials.
us_prevalence: ~0.5–1.0% of US population (~1.5–3 million); higher in pediatric populations (2–3.2% of children; JAMA Pediatrics 2022).
patients_needing_tx: ~500,000–800,000 patients needing pharmacologic support; significant pediatric/adolescent component.
price: $12,000–$25,000/year; pediatric premium; rare/unmet need pricing.
peak_sales: Growing recognition; no approved drug. 15–25% penetration if approved. Peak sales ~$600M–1.2B.
registrational_endpoint: Food neophobia scale reduction; weight/BMI improvement; PARDI severity score at 16 weeks.
btd_threshold_vs_soc: ≥30% increase in dietary variety score; clinically meaningful weight gain vs. placebo.

symptoms: Extreme food restriction, distorted body image, severe underweight
treatment_goal: Restore healthy weight, normalize eating, prevent relapse
first_line_soc: No FDA-approved pharmacotherapy. Nutritional rehabilitation and psychotherapy (CBT, FBT for adolescents) are standard. Olanzapine used off-label for weight gain and anxiolysis.
treatment_algorithm: Nutritional rehabilitation is mandatory first step. Psychotherapy: family-based treatment (adolescents), CBT (adults). Pharmacologic (all off-label): olanzapine (most evidence for weight gain); SSRIs for comorbid anxiety/depression after weight restoration; zinc supplementation adjunctive. Pimavanserin and other novel agents in trials. Severe cases require inpatient/residential programs.
us_prevalence: ~1 million US patients; lifetime prevalence 0.3–0.9% in females (ANAD/NEDA data).
patients_needing_tx: ~400,000 patients needing active pharmacologic support (severe/chronic); most remain in behavioral programs.
price: $15,000–$35,000/year; premium justified by high medical burden and lack of approved options.
peak_sales: High unmet need but small marketable population. 20–30% uptake in treatment-seeking patients. Peak sales ~$500M–1.0B.
registrational_endpoint: BMI gain ≥1.0 kg/m² and maintenance at 12 months; EDE-Q global score improvement.
btd_threshold_vs_soc: Statistically significant BMI gain ≥1.5 kg/m² vs. placebo at 6 months; >30% reduction in relapse rate.

symptoms: Compulsive eating of non-nutritive, non-food substances (dirt, chalk, clay)
treatment_goal: Eliminate pica behavior; address nutritional deficiencies; ensure safety
first_line_soc: Behavioral interventions (ABA, habit reversal) are primary. Iron/zinc supplementation if deficient. No FDA-approved pharmacotherapy.
treatment_algorithm: Nutritional assessment and deficiency correction (iron, zinc — deficiency can drive pica). Behavioral: ABA, differential reinforcement. Pharmacologic (off-label): SSRIs; olanzapine for comorbid psychotic/ASD features; naltrexone (self-injurious behavior reduction in ASD-pica). Address underlying intellectual disability or ASD.
us_prevalence: ~10–30% of young children (typically benign/transient); clinical disorder in IDD/ASD populations ~200,000–500,000.
patients_needing_tx: ~150,000 patients with clinical pica receiving pharmacotherapy.
registrational_endpoint: Pica behavior frequency scale; safety events per month at 12 weeks.
btd_threshold_vs_soc: ≥50% reduction in pica episodes; zero serious ingestion events vs. placebo.

symptoms: Repeated regurgitation and re-chewing of food without nausea or disgust
treatment_goal: Eliminate regurgitation; restore normal eating and weight
first_line_soc: Behavioral intervention (diaphragmatic breathing, HRT) is first-line. No FDA-approved pharmacotherapy.
treatment_algorithm: Diaphragmatic breathing training and behavioral therapy (first-line, high efficacy). Pharmacologic (off-label): baclofen (reduces transient lower esophageal sphincter relaxation); buspirone; proton pump inhibitors for esophagitis. Rarely, antidepressants for comorbid anxiety.
us_prevalence: ~1–3% prevalence (higher in IDD populations); treatment-seeking adults ~200,000–400,000.
patients_needing_tx: ~100,000 patients needing pharmacotherapy.
registrational_endpoint: Rumination event frequency per meal (diary-based); weight gain at 8 weeks.
btd_threshold_vs_soc: ≥70% reduction in rumination episodes vs. placebo; complete cessation in ≥40% of patients.

symptoms: Persistent sadness, loss of interest, fatigue, suicidal thoughts
treatment_goal: Achieve and maintain remission; restore daily functioning
first_line_soc: SSRIs (escitalopram, sertraline) are first-line pharmacotherapy. SNRIs (venlafaxine, duloxetine) are also first-line alternatives.
treatment_algorithm: Step 1: SSRI (escitalopram, sertraline, fluoxetine) or SNRI (venlafaxine, duloxetine). Step 2: Switch SSRI class, add augmentation (aripiprazole, brexpiprazole, quetiapine, lithium, thyroid hormone). Step 3: TCAs (nortriptyline), MAOIs; esketamine (Spravato) for TRD (FDA 2019); dextromethorphan/bupropion (Auvelity, FDA 2022); psilocybin (investigational). Psychotherapy (CBT) combined. 2024–2025 approvals: gepirone (FDA 2023), dextromethorphan/bupropion (Auvelity). TMS, ECT for refractory.
us_prevalence: ~21 million US adults with MDD annually (~8.3% prevalence; NIMH 2024).
patients_needing_tx: ~14 million patients seeking pharmacotherapy.
price: $20,000–$40,000/year for novel mechanisms (NMDA/psychedelic); generic SSRI benchmark $500/yr.
peak_sales: Massive market but competitive. Novel mechanism: 5–8% of TRD submarket. Peak sales ~$3.0–6.0B.
registrational_endpoint: MADRS or HAM-D17 ≥50% reduction (response) and remission rate at 6–8 weeks.
btd_threshold_vs_soc: MADRS ≥3-point superiority over placebo; remission rate ≥15% absolute difference; faster onset (≤2 weeks).

symptoms: Alternating mania/depression, mood instability, impaired judgment
treatment_goal: Stabilize mood, prevent relapse, restore functioning
first_line_soc: Lithium (mood stabilizer) for maintenance; valproate/quetiapine for acute mania; quetiapine or lurasidone for bipolar depression.
treatment_algorithm: Acute Mania: lithium, valproate, carbamazepine, atypical antipsychotics (quetiapine, aripiprazole, risperidone, olanzapine, asenapine, cariprazine). Bipolar Depression: quetiapine, lurasidone, cariprazine (Vraylar), lumateperone (Caplyta—FDA 2021). Maintenance: lithium, lamotrigine (depressive prevention), quetiapine, aripiprazole. 2024–2025: dextromethorphan/bupropion (Auvelity) for MDD comorbidity; ongoing trials with psilocybin. Key brands: Seroquel, Latuda, Vraylar, Caplyta.
us_prevalence: ~5.7 million US adults with bipolar I/II (~2.4% prevalence; NIMH 2024).
patients_needing_tx: ~4.5 million patients needing pharmacotherapy; ~80% of diagnosed patients require medications.
price: $15,000–$30,000/year; atypical antipsychotics for bipolar run $15,000–$25,000/yr WAC.
peak_sales: Highly competitive market. Novel mechanism: 8–12% share. Peak sales ~$2.0–4.0B.
registrational_endpoint: YMRS score reduction ≥50% (mania); MADRS score reduction ≥50% (depression) at 3–4 weeks.
btd_threshold_vs_soc: YMRS ≥5 point superiority over placebo; or MADRS ≥3 point superiority in bipolar depression.

symptoms: Suicidal ideation, planning, or attempts; self-harm with intent to die
treatment_goal: Eliminate suicidal ideation; ensure safety; address root psychiatric disorders
first_line_soc: Lithium (reduces suicide risk — most robust evidence); clozapine (FDA-approved for reducing suicidal behavior in schizophrenia/schizoaffective). Esketamine (Spravato) has rapid antisuicidal effects.
treatment_algorithm: Lithium — strongest evidence for suicide prevention in mood disorders. Clozapine — FDA-approved for suicidality in schizophrenia. Esketamine (Spravato) — FDA-approved for TRD, with rapid antisuicidal effect. Ketamine infusions. SSRIs/SNRIs for underlying depression. Safety planning + DBT + crisis intervention. Emerging: psilocybin, MDMA (investigational). Ketamine nasal spray for acute SI in ED setting.
us_prevalence: ~47,000 suicides/year; ~1.6 million attempts; ~12 million with serious suicidal ideation (CDC 2024).
patients_needing_tx: ~3 million patients with moderate-severe suicidal ideation needing active pharmacotherapy.
price: $20,000–$50,000/year for acute/maintenance anti-suicidal drug.
peak_sales: Enormous unmet need. Anti-suicidal specific drug: 10–15% share. Peak sales ~$2.0–4.0B.
registrational_endpoint: Columbia Suicide Severity Rating Scale (C-SSRS) ≥2-point reduction; SIQ-JR score at 4 weeks; suicide attempt rate.
btd_threshold_vs_soc: ≥50% reduction in C-SSRS composite score vs. placebo/SSRI; rapid onset (≤72 hours); ≥50% reduction in attempt rate.

symptoms: Mild mood swings between hypomania and mild depression for ≥2 years
treatment_goal: Stabilize mood; prevent progression to bipolar disorder
first_line_soc: Psychotherapy (CBT) is typically first-line. Lithium and valproate are used off-label for mood stabilization; no FDA-approved drug specific to cyclothymia.
treatment_algorithm: Psychotherapy: CBT for cyclothymia. Pharmacologic (off-label): lithium (prevents escalation to BD I/II); valproate; lamotrigine for depressive phases; quetiapine at low doses. Avoid antidepressants without mood stabilizer cover. Long-term monitoring essential.
us_prevalence: ~1% prevalence; ~2.5–3 million US adults.
patients_needing_tx: ~600,000 patients needing pharmacotherapy.
price: $10,000–$16,000/year; mood stabilizer pricing range.
peak_sales: Small niche, often managed with existing bipolar drugs. 8–12% share. Peak sales ~$200–400M.
registrational_endpoint: HAM-D / YMRS combined score; mood cycle frequency reduction at 6 months.
btd_threshold_vs_soc: ≥40% reduction in mood episode frequency vs. placebo; no conversion to BD I/II.

symptoms: Intrusive obsessive thoughts, repetitive compulsions, severe functional impairment
treatment_goal: Reduce obsession/compulsion severity; restore functioning
first_line_soc: SSRIs (fluoxetine, sertraline, fluvoxamine, paroxetine — all FDA-approved) plus ERP (exposure and response prevention) therapy are first-line.
treatment_algorithm: Step 1: SSRI at high dose (fluoxetine 40–80 mg, sertraline 100–200 mg, fluvoxamine 100–300 mg, paroxetine 40–60 mg) + ERP. Step 2: Clomipramine (TCA — FDA-approved for OCD). Step 3: Augmentation with antipsychotics (aripiprazole, risperidone, olanzapine); glutamate modulators (riluzole, N-acetylcysteine). Deep TMS (NeuroStar — FDA-cleared for OCD 2018). Ketamine for acute TRD-OCD. Emerging: CGRP/glutamate agents.
us_prevalence: ~3.3 million US adults (~1.2% prevalence; NIMH); lifetime ~2.3%.
patients_needing_tx: ~2.5 million patients needing pharmacotherapy.
price: $15,000–$25,000/year; OCD drugs like fluvoxamine CR (Luvox CR) set pricing precedent.
peak_sales: Active R&D (glutamate, psychedelics). Novel mechanism: 10–15% share. Peak sales ~$1.0–2.0B.
registrational_endpoint: Y-BOCS (Yale-Brown Obsessive Compulsive Scale) ≥35% reduction at 12 weeks.
btd_threshold_vs_soc: ≥35% Y-BOCS reduction vs. SSRI; or Y-BOCS <8 (subclinical) response rate ≥30%.

symptoms: Obsessive preoccupation with perceived physical flaw, ritualistic checking
treatment_goal: Reduce obsessive thinking, improve functioning and quality of life
first_line_soc: High-dose SSRIs (fluoxetine, fluvoxamine, escitalopram) are first-line pharmacotherapy. Clomipramine (TCA) is second-line.
treatment_algorithm: Step 1: High-dose SSRI (fluoxetine 60–80 mg, fluvoxamine 300 mg, escitalopram 40 mg) for 12–16 weeks. Step 2: Augmentation with buspirone, aripiprazole, or clomipramine. Step 3: Switch to clomipramine or different SSRI. CBT (ERP-BDD) combined with pharmacotherapy is optimal. Antipsychotic augmentation for delusional variant.
us_prevalence: ~5–7.5 million US adults (1.7–2.4% prevalence; BDD Foundation/NIMH).
patients_needing_tx: ~2 million patients needing pharmacotherapy; BDD is underdiagnosed.
price: $10,000–$18,000/year; OCD-spectrum pricing benchmark.
peak_sales: High unmet need. 10–15% penetration with novel agent. Peak sales ~$700M–1.2B.
registrational_endpoint: Yale-Brown Obsessive Compulsive Scale Modified for BDD (BDD-YBOCS) ≥30% reduction at 12 weeks.
btd_threshold_vs_soc: ≥35% reduction in BDD-YBOCS vs. SSRI comparator; CGI-I 'much improved.'

symptoms: Compulsive hair pulling, skin picking, or nail biting causing distress
treatment_goal: Reduce repetitive behaviors; improve self-control and quality of life
first_line_soc: Habit Reversal Training (HRT) behavioral therapy is first-line. N-acetylcysteine (OTC supplement) has best pharmacologic evidence; no FDA-approved drug.
treatment_algorithm: Behavioral: HRT, Comprehensive Behavioral Treatment (ComB). Pharmacologic (off-label): N-acetylcysteine (1,200–2,400 mg/day) — best RCT evidence; clomipramine for trichotillomania subtype; SSRIs (limited evidence); olanzapine adjunctive. Opioid modulators under investigation.
us_prevalence: ~10–15 million US adults across all subtypes (excoriation ~2%, trichotillomania ~1–2%, onychophagia ~20%).
patients_needing_tx: ~1–2 million patients seeking pharmacologic treatment for moderate-to-severe BFRB.
price: $10,000–$18,000/year; OCD-adjacent pricing.
peak_sales: BFRB includes trichotillomania (see separate) and excoriation. Umbrella drug: 10–15% uptake. Peak sales ~$600M–1.0B.
registrational_endpoint: Massachusetts General Hospital Hair Pulling Scale (MGH-HPS) or Skin Picking Scale-Revised (SPS-R) ≥35% reduction at 12 weeks.
btd_threshold_vs_soc: ≥40% reduction in primary behavior scale vs. placebo; superiority vs. N-acetylcysteine.

symptoms: Recurrent skin picking causing lesions, distress, and functional impairment
treatment_goal: Reduce picking frequency; heal skin; restore quality of life
first_line_soc: Habit Reversal Training (HRT) behavioral therapy is first-line. N-acetylcysteine is most evidence-based pharmacologic option (off-label); no FDA-approved drug.
treatment_algorithm: Behavioral: HRT, Comprehensive Behavioral Treatment (ComB), CBT. Pharmacologic (off-label): N-acetylcysteine (1,200–2,400 mg/day — best evidence from RCTs); SSRIs (fluoxetine, escitalopram) for anxiety/OCD dimension; lamotrigine; inositol. Naltrexone under investigation.
us_prevalence: ~5.4 million US adults (~1.4–5.4% prevalence; Skin Picking Research Center).
patients_needing_tx: ~1.5 million patients seeking pharmacologic treatment.
price: $10,000–$18,000/year; OCD-spectrum pricing.
peak_sales: Grouped with trichotillomania/BFRB; no approved drug. 15–20% uptake. Peak sales ~$500–900M.
registrational_endpoint: Skin Picking Scale-Revised (SPS-R) ≥35% reduction at 12 weeks; CGI-I.
btd_threshold_vs_soc: ≥40% SPS-R reduction vs. placebo; significant functional improvement on SDS.

symptoms: Recurrent compulsive hair pulling, hair loss, distress or functional impairment
treatment_goal: Reduce hair pulling; restore hair and self-image; improve quality of life
first_line_soc: Habit Reversal Training (HRT) is first-line behavioral treatment. N-acetylcysteine (1,200–2,400 mg/day) is most evidence-based pharmacologic option (off-label); no FDA-approved drug.
treatment_algorithm: Behavioral: HRT, ComB (Comprehensive Behavioral Treatment). Pharmacologic (off-label): N-acetylcysteine — best RCT evidence; clomipramine (TCA) has efficacy but poor tolerability; naltrexone; SSRIs (limited evidence for core pulling); olanzapine small evidence. Combination HRT + pharmacotherapy may be superior.
us_prevalence: ~1–2% prevalence; ~3–4 million US adults (NIMH/TLC Foundation).
patients_needing_tx: ~1.5 million patients needing pharmacotherapy.
peak_sales: No approved drug. High unmet need. 15–20% uptake. Peak sales ~$600M–1.0B.
registrational_endpoint: Massachusetts General Hospital Hair Pulling Scale (MGH-HPS) ≥35% reduction at 12 weeks; NIMH-TSS.
btd_threshold_vs_soc: ≥35% MGH-HPS reduction vs. placebo; clinically meaningful improvement in NIMH-TSS.

symptoms: Difficulty discarding possessions, extreme clutter, distress at discarding
treatment_goal: Reduce acquisition and improve discard ability; improve functioning
first_line_soc: Cognitive behavioral therapy for hoarding (CBT-HD) is first-line. SSRIs/venlafaxine used off-label; no FDA-approved drug.
treatment_algorithm: CBT-HD: motivation enhancement, sorting/discarding training, cognitive restructuring. Pharmacologic (off-label): SSRIs (paroxetine, sertraline — small trials showing benefit); venlafaxine; methylphenidate for ADHD comorbidity. Combination therapy may be superior. Clutter Image Rating as outcome measure.
us_prevalence: ~2.5–6% prevalence; ~5–8 million US adults (IOCDF/DSM-5 data).
patients_needing_tx: ~1.5 million patients seeking pharmacotherapy.
peak_sales: OCD-adjacent market. Novel drug: 8–12% penetration. Peak sales ~$400–700M.
registrational_endpoint: Hoarding Rating Scale-Interview (HRS-I) ≥35% reduction at 16 weeks; Clutter Image Rating.
btd_threshold_vs_soc: ≥35% HRS-I total score reduction vs. placebo; CGI-I 'much improved' in ≥40% of patients.

symptoms: Preoccupation with emitting a bad odor others find offensive; shame and avoidance
treatment_goal: Reduce olfactory preoccupation; improve social functioning
first_line_soc: CBT (OCD/BDD-adapted with ERP) is primary. SSRIs at high dose (OCD dosing) are pharmacologic first-line (off-label).
treatment_algorithm: CBT with ERP components. Pharmacologic (off-label): high-dose SSRIs (fluoxetine, fluvoxamine) — OCD dosing protocols; antipsychotic augmentation for delusional intensity. Overlap with olfactory hallucination requires antipsychotic evaluation.
us_prevalence: Very rare; estimated ~50,000–200,000 US patients (prevalence data limited).
patients_needing_tx: ~50,000 patients receiving pharmacotherapy.
registrational_endpoint: BDD-YBOCS modified for olfaction; CGI-I at 12 weeks.
btd_threshold_vs_soc: ≥35% reduction in olfactory obsession scale vs. placebo.

symptoms: Distress from mismatch between gender identity and assigned sex at birth
treatment_goal: Reduce dysphoria; align physical presentation with gender identity
first_line_soc: Gender-affirming hormone therapy (testosterone for trans males; estradiol + antiandrogens for trans females) is first-line medical treatment. Psychotherapy supportive.
treatment_algorithm: Puberty blockers (GnRH agonists: leuprolide, histrelin) for adolescents. Gender-affirming hormones: testosterone (trans male); estradiol + spironolactone/cyproterone (trans female). Surgical options (gender-affirming surgery) for adults. Psychological support and social transition. Mental health treatment for comorbid depression/anxiety with SSRIs/SNRIs.
us_prevalence: ~1.6 million US adults identify as transgender (~0.6% prevalence; Williams Institute 2022); treatment-seeking ~600,000–800,000.
patients_needing_tx: ~700,000 patients using hormone therapy; growing market.
price: $5,000–$12,000/year for hormones; GnRH agonists $10,000–$30,000/year.
peak_sales: Established hormonal market. Novel formulations or blockers: 10–15% share. Peak sales ~$300–600M.
registrational_endpoint: Gender Congruence and Life Satisfaction Scale (GCLS); Utrecht Gender Dysphoria Scale reduction at 6 months.
btd_threshold_vs_soc: ≥40% UGDS reduction; significant improvement in GCLS total score vs. control.

symptoms: Recurrent urge to expose genitals to non-consenting persons causing distress
treatment_goal: Reduce paraphilic urges; prevent harmful behaviors
first_line_soc: CBT and relapse prevention therapy are first-line. Antiandrogens (medroxyprogesterone acetate, leuprolide) and SSRIs used off-label for urge reduction.
treatment_algorithm: Psychotherapy: CBT, relapse prevention, sex offender treatment programs. Pharmacologic (off-label): SSRIs (reduce sexual obsessions); antiandrogens — medroxyprogesterone acetate (MPA), cyproterone acetate, leuprolide (GnRH agonist) for severe cases; naltrexone. Treatment usually court-ordered.
us_prevalence: ~2–4% prevalence (paraphilia); treatment-seeking population ~500,000 (often involuntary).
patients_needing_tx: ~200,000 patients in treatment programs using pharmacotherapy.
peak_sales: Forensic/correctional market. Small commercial opportunity. Peak sales ~$50–100M.
registrational_endpoint: Sexual Interest and Desire Inventory (SIDI) and offense recidivism rate at 12 months.
btd_threshold_vs_soc: ≥50% reduction in paraphilic urge score; zero-offense rate vs. control.

symptoms: Recurrent sexual attraction to prepubescent children causing distress or offending behavior
treatment_goal: Prevent offending; reduce pedophilic urges and recidivism
first_line_soc: Antiandrogens (medroxyprogesterone acetate, leuprolide) for high-risk/acting-out individuals; SSRIs for non-offending pedophilia with distress. Combined with psychotherapy.
treatment_algorithm: Psychotherapy: CBT, relapse prevention, acceptance-based approaches (non-offending). Pharmacologic: SSRIs (libido modulation) for non-offending with distress; antiandrogens — MPA (Depo-Provera), leuprolide (GnRH agonist), cyproterone acetate for acting-out; naltrexone adjunctive. Chemical castration in high-risk cases (court-ordered).
us_prevalence: Prevalence ~1% of males (~1.5 million); acting-out/offending ~200,000–400,000 (treatment-mandated).
patients_needing_tx: ~200,000 in structured pharmacological treatment programs.
peak_sales: Forensic/correctional market with ethical complexity. Peak sales ~$50–100M.
registrational_endpoint: Sexual interest in children (penile plethysmography or SCI-2R); recidivism rate at 24 months.
btd_threshold_vs_soc: ≥50% reduction in pedophilic arousal measure; zero recidivism vs. control.

symptoms: Recurrent urge to touch/rub against non-consenting persons causing distress
treatment_goal: Reduce paraphilic urges; prevent offending behavior
first_line_soc: CBT and relapse prevention therapy first-line. SSRIs and antiandrogens used off-label.
treatment_algorithm: Psychotherapy: CBT, relapse prevention. Pharmacologic (off-label): SSRIs (fluoxetine, sertraline) for urge reduction; antiandrogens (MPA, leuprolide) for severe cases. Often court-mandated treatment.
us_prevalence: ~10–14% of men report frotteuristic fantasies; clinical disorder (distress/impairment) ~500,000 treatment-presenting.
patients_needing_tx: ~150,000 in treatment programs receiving pharmacotherapy.
registrational_endpoint: Paraphilic urge scale; offense recidivism rate at 12 months.
btd_threshold_vs_soc: ≥50% reduction in urge frequency; zero recidivism rate vs. control.

symptoms: Recurrent arousal from watching unsuspecting persons undress/engage in sexual activity; distress or offending
treatment_goal: Prevent offending; reduce voyeuristic urges and recidivism
first_line_soc: CBT and relapse prevention therapy (often mandatory for offenders). SSRIs for compulsive dimension; antiandrogens for high-risk offenders.
treatment_algorithm: Psychotherapy: CBT, covert sensitization, relapse prevention. Pharmacologic (off-label): SSRIs (obsessive urge reduction); antiandrogens (MPA, leuprolide) for high-risk acting-out cases; naltrexone adjunctive.
us_prevalence: ~12% of males report voyeuristic behavior; clinical disorder (distress/impairment/offending) ~500,000 treatment-presenting.
patients_needing_tx: ~150,000 in treatment programs receiving pharmacotherapy.
registrational_endpoint: Voyeuristic urge scale; offense recidivism rate at 12 months.
btd_threshold_vs_soc: ≥50% urge reduction; zero offense recidivism vs. control.

symptoms: Recurrent sexual arousal to non-living objects or body parts causing distress
treatment_goal: Reduce distress; manage paraphilic urges if problematic
first_line_soc: Psychotherapy (CBT) when distress or impairment present. SSRIs for obsessive-compulsive dimension; antiandrogens for severe cases (off-label).
treatment_algorithm: CBT (covert sensitization, aversion therapy). Pharmacologic (off-label): SSRIs (reduce sexual obsession intensity); antiandrogens (MPA, leuprolide) for severe cases. Most cases do not require treatment unless distress is significant.
us_prevalence: Estimated ~1–3% of population with fetishistic interests; clinical disorder (causing distress/impairment) ~200,000–400,000.
patients_needing_tx: ~100,000 patients seeking pharmacologic support.
registrational_endpoint: Paraphilia questionnaire and SIDI score ≥30% reduction at 12 weeks.
btd_threshold_vs_soc: ≥40% reduction in fetishistic urge severity vs. placebo.

symptoms: Recurrent arousal from inflicting pain/humiliation on others; may lead to offending
treatment_goal: Prevent harm; reduce sadistic urges; prevent recidivism
first_line_soc: Antiandrogens (leuprolide, MPA) for acting-out/dangerous cases. SSRIs for non-offending distress. CBT mandatory.
treatment_algorithm: CBT, relapse prevention (mandatory for offenders). Pharmacologic: antiandrogens (leuprolide, MPA) — chemical castration; SSRIs for compulsive dimension; naltrexone adjunctive. Often court-mandated in forensic settings.
us_prevalence: ~2–5% of males report sadistic fantasies; clinical disorder ~200,000–400,000 (including forensic).
patients_needing_tx: ~100,000 patients in treatment programs.
registrational_endpoint: Sadistic urge scale; recidivism rate at 24 months.
btd_threshold_vs_soc: ≥50% reduction in sadistic arousal index; zero violent sexual recidivism.

symptoms: Recurrent distress or impairment from sexual arousal to receiving pain/humiliation
treatment_goal: Reduce paraphilic distress and impairment; improve safety
first_line_soc: Psychotherapy (CBT, acceptance-based) when distress/impairment present. SSRIs for obsessive sexual thoughts; antiandrogens if behaviors are dangerous.
treatment_algorithm: CBT (covert sensitization when needed). Most cases non-clinical. Pharmacologic (off-label): SSRIs for compulsive dimension; antiandrogens (MPA, leuprolide) if dangerous behaviors. Usually only treated when distress or risk is significant.
us_prevalence: Paraphilic interests ~5–10% adults; clinical disorder (distress/impairment) ~200,000–500,000.
patients_needing_tx: ~50,000 patients seeking pharmacotherapy.
registrational_endpoint: Paraphilia questionnaire; SIDI score ≥30% reduction at 12 weeks.
btd_threshold_vs_soc: ≥40% reduction in paraphilic distress score vs. placebo.

symptoms: Distress or impairment from arousal to cross-dressing
treatment_goal: Reduce distress; improve quality of life if impairment present
first_line_soc: Psychotherapy (CBT) for distress/impairment cases. No FDA-approved pharmacotherapy. SSRIs for OCD dimension.
treatment_algorithm: Most cases: no treatment needed. If distress present: CBT, acceptance-based therapies. Pharmacologic (off-label): SSRIs for compulsive urge reduction; anti-androgens in rare severely distressing cases.
us_prevalence: ~2–3% of males; clinical disorder (distress/impairment) ~200,000–400,000.
patients_needing_tx: ~50,000 patients seeking pharmacotherapy.
registrational_endpoint: Paraphilic distress scale; SIDI score ≥30% reduction at 12 weeks.
btd_threshold_vs_soc: ≥40% reduction in distress/impairment score vs. placebo.

symptoms: Emotional instability, impulsivity, self-harm, unstable relationships, identity issues
treatment_goal: Reduce self-harm, stabilize emotions, improve relationships
first_line_soc: No FDA-approved pharmacotherapy. Dialectical Behavior Therapy (DBT) is first-line psychological treatment. Atypical antipsychotics and mood stabilizers used off-label.
treatment_algorithm: Psychotherapy: DBT (gold standard), MBT, TFP. Pharmacologic (all off-label): atypical antipsychotics (quetiapine, olanzapine, aripiprazole) for anger/impulsivity; mood stabilizers (valproate, lamotrigine, topiramate); SSRIs for comorbid depression; naltrexone for self-harm/dissociation. Avoid long-term benzodiazepines. No drug improves BPD globally.
us_prevalence: ~1.6–5.9% prevalence; ~3–7 million US adults (NIMH/McLean Hospital data).
patients_needing_tx: ~2 million patients receiving pharmacotherapy adjunctively.
price: $12,000–$20,000/year; high unmet need warrants premium.
peak_sales: Regulatory pathway challenging (no established endpoint). 10–15% penetration. Peak sales ~$800M–1.5B.
registrational_endpoint: Zanarini Rating Scale for BPD (ZAN-BPD) ≥30% reduction at 12 weeks; CGI-BPD.
btd_threshold_vs_soc: ≥35% ZAN-BPD reduction vs. placebo; significant reduction in self-harm frequency.

symptoms: Social inhibition, feelings of inadequacy, extreme rejection sensitivity
treatment_goal: Reduce avoidance; improve social engagement and self-esteem
first_line_soc: Psychotherapy (CBT, schema therapy) is primary. SSRIs/SNRIs used off-label for social anxiety dimension (paroxetine, venlafaxine have best evidence).
treatment_algorithm: CBT and schema-focused therapy are primary. Pharmacologic (off-label): SSRIs (paroxetine, sertraline) and SNRIs (venlafaxine) for anxiety component; MAOIs historically effective; quetiapine low-dose adjunctive in some cases. Overlap with social anxiety disorder guides treatment.
us_prevalence: ~2.1–2.4% population; ~5–6 million US adults (NESARC/NIMH data).
patients_needing_tx: ~1.5 million patients seeking pharmacologic augmentation; many co-managed under social anxiety disorder diagnosis.
price: $8,000–$14,000/year; grouped with anxiety disorder pricing.
peak_sales: Likely absorbed under social anxiety umbrella. Dedicated drug: 5–8% uptake. Peak sales ~$400–700M.
registrational_endpoint: Liebowitz Social Anxiety Scale (LSAS) and SDS at 12 weeks.
btd_threshold_vs_soc: ≥35% reduction in LSAS vs. placebo; significant improvement in global functioning.

symptoms: Disregard for rules, aggression, deceitfulness, lack of remorse
treatment_goal: Reduce aggression, impulsivity; improve social functioning
first_line_soc: No FDA-approved pharmacotherapy. Psychotherapy (DBT-adapted, MBT, CBT for offenders) is standard. Mood stabilizers (lithium, valproate) and atypical antipsychotics used off-label for aggression/impulsivity.
treatment_algorithm: Primarily psychosocial: CBT, schema therapy, MBT. Pharmacologic (all off-label): mood stabilizers (lithium, valproate) for aggression; atypical antipsychotics (quetiapine, risperidone) for impulsivity/anger; SSRIs for comorbid depression. No clear evidence base. Management often occurs in forensic/correctional settings.
us_prevalence: ~3–5% general population; ~6–10 million US adults (DSM-5/NIMH). Higher in correctional settings (>50%).
patients_needing_tx: ~1 million patients in psychiatric settings seeking pharmacologic support; many untreated.
price: $8,000–$12,000/year; limited commercial attractiveness given population characteristics.
peak_sales: Poor treatment adherence, forensic population. 5–8% peak penetration. Peak sales ~$300–500M.
registrational_endpoint: Modified Overt Aggression Scale (MOAS) reduction ≥50% at 12 weeks; CGI-I.
btd_threshold_vs_soc: ≥40% reduction in MOAS vs. placebo; significant reduction in violent incidents.

symptoms: Odd beliefs, magical thinking, social isolation, perceptual distortions
treatment_goal: Reduce psychotic-like symptoms; improve social functioning
first_line_soc: Low-dose atypical antipsychotics (risperidone, olanzapine — off-label) are most commonly used. SSRIs for anxiety/depression components.
treatment_algorithm: Psychotherapy: CBT, supportive therapy. Pharmacologic (off-label): low-dose atypicals (risperidone 0.5–2 mg) for cognitive-perceptual symptoms; SSRIs for social anxiety component; anti-anxiety agents. Higher doses used if spectrum nears schizophrenia.
us_prevalence: ~3.9% prevalence; ~5–8 million US adults.
patients_needing_tx: ~600,000 patients receiving pharmacotherapy.
peak_sales: Managed within schizophrenia/anxiety drug market. Peak sales ~$200–400M.
registrational_endpoint: STPD features scale (SPQ); CGI-I at 12 weeks.
btd_threshold_vs_soc: ≥30% SPQ total score reduction; significant social functioning improvement vs. placebo.

symptoms: Perfectionism, rigidity, preoccupation with order and control, inflexibility
treatment_goal: Reduce rigidity; improve flexibility and interpersonal relationships
first_line_soc: Psychotherapy (CBT) is primary. No FDA-approved drug. SSRIs helpful for OCD-dimensional overlap.
treatment_algorithm: CBT: cognitive restructuring, behavioral experiments. Pharmacologic (off-label): SSRIs if OC symptoms present; no specific evidence base for OCPD core features vs. OCD.
us_prevalence: ~2.1–7.9% prevalence; ~5–8 million US adults.
patients_needing_tx: ~800,000 patients receiving pharmacotherapy.
peak_sales: Overlaps with OCD market. Peak sales ~$150–300M.
registrational_endpoint: OCPD Interview Scale; PDQ-4 OCPD subscale ≥30% at 12 weeks.
btd_threshold_vs_soc: ≥30% reduction in rigidity/perfectionism scale; significant functional improvement.

symptoms: Pervasive distrust and suspiciousness of others without psychosis
treatment_goal: Reduce suspiciousness; improve social functioning and relationships
first_line_soc: Psychotherapy (CBT) is first-line. No FDA-approved pharmacotherapy. Low-dose antipsychotics for severe paranoia.
treatment_algorithm: CBT with trust-building approach. Pharmacologic (off-label): low-dose atypical antipsychotics (quetiapine, risperidone) for paranoid ideation; SSRIs for comorbid anxiety; mood stabilizers for irritability.
us_prevalence: ~2.3–4.4% prevalence; ~4–8 million US adults.
patients_needing_tx: ~500,000 patients receiving pharmacotherapy.
registrational_endpoint: Revised Paranoia Scale; CGI-PPD score ≥30% improvement at 12 weeks.
btd_threshold_vs_soc: ≥35% paranoia scale reduction vs. low-dose antipsychotic comparator.

symptoms: Excessive emotionality, attention-seeking, shallow affect, seductiveness
treatment_goal: Improve self-awareness; build healthier relationships and coping skills
first_line_soc: Psychotherapy (psychodynamic therapy, CBT) is primary. No FDA-approved pharmacotherapy. SSRIs for comorbid anxiety/depression; antipsychotics for brief psychotic episodes.
treatment_algorithm: Psychodynamic therapy, CBT. Pharmacologic (off-label): SSRIs for mood/anxiety; antipsychotics if brief psychosis; mood stabilizers for emotional dysregulation. Long-term treatment needed. Prognosis better than cluster A/B personality disorders.
us_prevalence: ~1.8–3% prevalence; ~4–6 million US adults.
patients_needing_tx: ~500,000 patients receiving pharmacotherapy for comorbidities.
peak_sales: No clear pharmacologic market. Peak sales ~$100–150M.
registrational_endpoint: PDQ-4 personality disorder scale; SDS at 16 weeks.
btd_threshold_vs_soc: ≥30% PDQ-4 reduction; significant CGI-I improvement vs. placebo.

symptoms: Excessive need for others' care, submissiveness, fear of abandonment
treatment_goal: Build autonomy; reduce dependent behavior and separation anxiety
first_line_soc: Psychotherapy (CBT, psychodynamic therapy) is primary. No FDA-approved pharmacotherapy. SSRIs may address comorbid anxiety/depression.
treatment_algorithm: Primary: CBT focused on autonomy-building, assertiveness training. Pharmacologic (off-label): SSRIs for anxiety/depression comorbidity; SNRIs for anxiety component; anxiolytics only short-term. Personality disorders generally require long-term psychological intervention.
us_prevalence: ~0.5–1% prevalence; ~1.5–2 million US adults.
patients_needing_tx: ~400,000 patients receiving pharmacologic augmentation.
peak_sales: Small market dominated by psychotherapy. Peak sales ~$100–200M.
registrational_endpoint: Sheehan Disability Scale and DPD-specific structured interview ≥30% at 12 weeks.
btd_threshold_vs_soc: ≥35% reduction in dependency measures vs. placebo; improved functioning scores.

symptoms: Grandiosity, lack of empathy, entitlement, need for admiration
treatment_goal: Improve empathy, reduce interpersonal dysfunction and comorbid depression
first_line_soc: Psychotherapy (psychodynamic, CBT) is primary. No FDA-approved pharmacotherapy. SSRIs for comorbid depression; low-dose antipsychotics for anger/agitation.
treatment_algorithm: Psychodynamic therapy; CBT for NPD. Pharmacologic (off-label): SSRIs for comorbid depression/anxiety; mood stabilizers for emotional dysregulation; antipsychotics for paranoia/agitation. Treatment-seeking is rare in NPD.
us_prevalence: ~0.5–6.2% prevalence; ~1–5 million US adults; treatment-seeking very low.
patients_needing_tx: ~200,000 patients receiving pharmacotherapy for comorbidities.
registrational_endpoint: Narcissistic Personality Inventory (NPI-40) + interpersonal functioning scale at 16 weeks.
btd_threshold_vs_soc: ≥25% NPI reduction; significant improvement in empathy measures vs. placebo.

symptoms: Detachment from social relationships, restricted emotional expression, preference for solitude
treatment_goal: Improve social engagement if desired; reduce comorbid distress
first_line_soc: Individual psychotherapy (CBT, supportive) is primary when treatment sought. No FDA-approved pharmacotherapy.
treatment_algorithm: Patients rarely seek treatment. CBT for social skills building. Pharmacologic (off-label): low-dose antipsychotics for any transient psychotic features; SSRIs for comorbid depression; no strong evidence base for core schizoid features.
us_prevalence: ~3.1–4.9% prevalence; ~4–7 million US adults.
patients_needing_tx: ~200,000 patients receiving any pharmacotherapy.
registrational_endpoint: Social functioning scale; SCID-personality inventory schizoid subscale at 16 weeks.
btd_threshold_vs_soc: ≥25% social engagement improvement vs. placebo.

symptoms: Hallucinations, delusions, disorganized thinking, negative symptoms, cognitive deficits
treatment_goal: Achieve symptom control; prevent relapse; improve functioning
first_line_soc: Atypical antipsychotics (risperidone, olanzapine, aripiprazole, quetiapine) are first-line. Long-acting injectable (LAI) formulations improve adherence.
treatment_algorithm: Step 1: Atypical antipsychotic — aripiprazole, risperidone, quetiapine, ziprasidone, olanzapine, paliperidone. LAI options: Abilify Maintena, Aristada, Invega Sustenna/Trinza. Step 2: Switch antipsychotic or add mood stabilizer. Step 3: Clozapine (TRS). 2024–2025 approvals: xanomeline/trospium (KarXT/Cobenfy — FDA approved 2024; first muscarinic mechanism); emraclidine in Phase 2. First-generation: haloperidol, fluphenazine. Psychosocial: CSC programs, CBTp, family therapy.
us_prevalence: ~3.7 million US adults (~1.1% prevalence; NIMH/Schizophrenia International Research Society 2024).
patients_needing_tx: ~3.2 million patients needing pharmacotherapy.
peak_sales: Competitive but KarXT differentiation for negative symptoms. New mechanism: 10–15% share. Peak sales ~$2.0–5.0B.
registrational_endpoint: PANSS total score ≥20% reduction (responder) at 5–6 weeks; PANSS negative subscale.
btd_threshold_vs_soc: PANSS total ≥5-point superiority vs. active comparator; ≥20% improvement in negative symptoms scale (PANSS-N or BNSS).

symptoms: Psychosis plus concurrent mood disorder (depression or mania/hypomania)
treatment_goal: Control psychosis and stabilize mood; maximize functioning
first_line_soc: Paliperidone (Invega — FDA-approved for schizoaffective disorder) is the primary approved agent. Combined antipsychotic + mood stabilizer/antidepressant for respective subtypes.
treatment_algorithm: FDA-approved: paliperidone (oral/ER and LAI formulations — Invega Sustenna/Trinza for schizoaffective). Off-label atypicals (olanzapine, quetiapine) with mood stabilizer (valproate, lithium) for manic subtype; antidepressant + antipsychotic for depressive subtype. Aripiprazole, ziprasidone, clozapine (TRS). Psychotherapy: CBT for psychosis; family education. LAI antipsychotics improve adherence.
us_prevalence: ~0.3% prevalence; ~750,000–1 million US adults.
patients_needing_tx: ~700,000 patients needing chronic pharmacotherapy.
price: $15,000–$30,000/year; comparable to schizophrenia LAIs (Invega Sustenna ~$25,000/yr WAC).
peak_sales: Specialized market. Novel drug: 10–15% share. Peak sales ~$500M–1.0B.
registrational_endpoint: PANSS total + MADRS or YMRS composite reduction ≥30% at 6 weeks.
btd_threshold_vs_soc: ≥30% composite PANSS+MADRS/YMRS reduction vs. paliperidone; superior relapse prevention.

disease: Other Schizophrenia Spectrum and Psychotic Disorders
symptoms: Psychotic features (hallucinations, delusions, disorganization) not meeting full criteria
treatment_goal: Control psychotic symptoms; prevent functional decline
first_line_soc: Atypical antipsychotics (risperidone, olanzapine, aripiprazole, quetiapine) are first-line.
treatment_algorithm: Atypical antipsychotics are the backbone. Selection based on side-effect profile. Psychoeducation and case management. Treat to SSD/schizophreniform/other spectrum criteria using PANSS/BPRS monitoring. Clozapine for treatment-resistant. Coordinated Specialty Care (CSC) programs for first-episode psychosis.
us_prevalence: ~500,000–800,000 US adults across spectrum disorders excluding schizophrenia proper.
patients_needing_tx: ~450,000 patients needing pharmacotherapy.
peak_sales: Bundled with schizophrenia market. Peak sales ~$300–600M.
registrational_endpoint: PANSS total score ≥20% reduction at 6 weeks.
btd_threshold_vs_soc: ≥30% PANSS reduction; PANSS positive subscale superiority vs. active comparator.

symptoms: Hallucinations/delusions during or after substance use or withdrawal
treatment_goal: Resolve acute psychosis; prevent substance use recurrence
first_line_soc: Short-term atypical antipsychotics (quetiapine, risperidone, olanzapine) for psychosis management. Treat underlying SUD.
treatment_algorithm: Acute stabilization: atypical antipsychotics (quetiapine, olanzapine) — lower doses often effective. Avoid long-term antipsychotics if substance-related. Treat underlying SUD with appropriate pharmacotherapy (naltrexone, buprenorphine). Monitor for conversion to primary psychotic disorder. Psychosocial treatment for SUD.
us_prevalence: ~800,000–1.5 million episodes annually; cannabis-induced psychosis most common (~500,000).
patients_needing_tx: ~600,000 patients needing acute pharmacotherapy annually.
price: $8,000–$15,000/acute episode or year of maintenance.
peak_sales: Managed within existing antipsychotic market. Peak sales ~$150–300M.
registrational_endpoint: PANSS positive subscale ≥40% reduction; time to psychosis resolution.
btd_threshold_vs_soc: ≥50% faster psychosis resolution vs. quetiapine; lower conversion to primary psychosis at 12 months.

symptoms: Fixed false beliefs without hallucinations; daily functioning relatively preserved
treatment_goal: Reduce delusional conviction; improve social functioning
first_line_soc: Antipsychotics (pimozide historically, now atypical antipsychotics — risperidone, olanzapine) are first-line. No FDA-approved drug specifically for delusional disorder.
treatment_algorithm: Atypical antipsychotics: risperidone (1–6 mg) or olanzapine (5–15 mg) first choice. Pimozide historically used for somatic type. Psychotherapy (CBT for psychosis) as adjunct. Full response rare; partial response/functional improvement common goal. Treatment often long-term.
us_prevalence: ~0.2% population; ~500,000–650,000 US adults.
patients_needing_tx: ~300,000 patients receiving pharmacotherapy.
price: $12,000–$20,000/year; antipsychotic pricing range.
peak_sales: Niche indication. 10–15% novel penetration. Peak sales ~$150–300M.
registrational_endpoint: PANSS positive subscale; Delusional Beliefs Questionnaire ≥30% improvement at 8 weeks.
btd_threshold_vs_soc: ≥35% reduction in delusional conviction score; CGI-I 'much improved' vs. placebo.

disease: Psychotic Disorder Due to Another Medical Condition
symptoms: Hallucinations/delusions directly caused by a medical illness
treatment_goal: Treat underlying condition; manage acute psychosis
first_line_soc: Treat underlying medical cause first. Atypical antipsychotics (quetiapine, risperidone, olanzapine) for acute psychosis management.
treatment_algorithm: Identify and treat the medical etiology (e.g., autoimmune encephalitis, CNS tumor, metabolic disorder). Atypical antipsychotics for symptom control — quetiapine, olanzapine, risperidone. Avoid antipsychotics in Lewy Body Dementia (use clozapine or pimavanserin). IV immunotherapy for autoimmune encephalitis.
us_prevalence: ~200,000–400,000 US cases annually (varied etiology); chronic/persistent ~100,000.
patients_needing_tx: ~200,000 patients needing pharmacotherapy annually.
peak_sales: Managed within existing antipsychotic/neurology market. Peak sales ~$100–200M.
registrational_endpoint: PANSS positive subscale ≥40% reduction; resolution of specific psychotic features at 6 weeks.
btd_threshold_vs_soc: ≥50% PANSS positive score reduction vs. placebo; faster resolution vs. standard antipsychotic.

symptoms: Schizophrenia-like psychosis lasting 1–6 months; may resolve completely
treatment_goal: Resolve psychosis; prevent conversion to schizophrenia
first_line_soc: Atypical antipsychotics (risperidone, olanzapine, quetiapine) are first-line for symptom control.
treatment_algorithm: Acute treatment with atypical antipsychotics; duration 12–24 months. Close monitoring for conversion to schizophrenia (60–80% do). CSC programs recommended. If remission achieved, gradual taper trial at 12 months. Same drug classes as schizophrenia.
us_prevalence: Incidence ~0.01–0.02%; active prevalent cases ~100,000–200,000 (many convert to schizophrenia).
patients_needing_tx: ~150,000 patients on active pharmacotherapy.
peak_sales: Managed within schizophrenia market. Peak sales ~$80–150M.
registrational_endpoint: PANSS ≥20% reduction; conversion to schizophrenia rate at 12 months.
btd_threshold_vs_soc: ≥50% reduction in schizophrenia conversion rate vs. standard antipsychotic.

symptoms: Sudden hallucinations, delusions, disorganized speech lasting <1 month
treatment_goal: Resolve psychosis rapidly; prevent recurrence
first_line_soc: Atypical antipsychotics (risperidone, olanzapine, quetiapine) are first-line. Short-course benzodiazepines for acute agitation.
treatment_algorithm: Acute: low-dose atypical antipsychotics (risperidone 1–4 mg, olanzapine 5–10 mg, quetiapine 200–400 mg). Benzodiazepines (lorazepam) for acute behavioral control. Duration typically 1–4 weeks. Taper and discontinue after 3–6 months if no recurrence. Identify precipitating stressors; psychotherapy post-resolution.
us_prevalence: ~0.05–0.1% incidence; ~100,000–200,000 episodes annually. Low chronic prevalence due to brief duration.
patients_needing_tx: ~150,000 patients annually requiring acute pharmacotherapy.
price: $8,000–$15,000/course; acute/episodic market.
peak_sales: Very small market; managed mostly with existing antipsychotics. Peak sales ~$50–100M.
registrational_endpoint: PANSS total score reduction ≥40% at 2 weeks; time to symptom resolution.
btd_threshold_vs_soc: ≥50% PANSS reduction at 1 week vs. placebo; rapid resolution within 7 days.

disease: Psychological Factors Affecting Other Medical Conditions
symptoms: Psychological/behavioral factors worsen an existing medical condition
treatment_goal: Reduce psychological impact on medical illness; improve health outcomes
first_line_soc: Integrated psychotherapy (CBT, mindfulness-based CBT) addressing medical illness. Pharmacotherapy targets comorbid depression/anxiety — SSRIs/SNRIs.
treatment_algorithm: CBT for chronic illness; health psychology interventions. Pharmacologic: SSRIs/SNRIs for comorbid depression/anxiety; mind-body approaches; consultation-liaison psychiatry for complex cases. No specific drug class for the diagnosis itself.
us_prevalence: ~20–30% of medically ill patients have significant psychological factors; estimated 15–25 million US patients.
patients_needing_tx: ~3 million patients receiving dedicated pharmacotherapy.
peak_sales: Overlaps with depression/anxiety market. Dedicated drug unlikely. Peak sales ~$300–500M.
registrational_endpoint: Disease-specific outcome improvement (e.g., A1c reduction in diabetes) plus PHQ-9 improvement.
btd_threshold_vs_soc: Significant improvement in primary medical outcome AND ≥40% PHQ-9 reduction vs. placebo.

symptoms: Excessive distress over physical symptoms; disproportionate health anxiety
treatment_goal: Reduce somatic distress; improve functioning and reduce over-medicalization
first_line_soc: CBT for somatic symptoms is first-line. SSRIs/SNRIs (off-label) used for comorbid anxiety/depression.
treatment_algorithm: CBT for SSD; mindfulness-based cognitive therapy. Pharmacologic (off-label): SSRIs (paroxetine, fluoxetine); SNRIs (duloxetine — evidence for pain component); tricyclics for pain management. Scheduled primary care appointments reduce unnecessary testing. Antidepressants reduce somatic amplification.
us_prevalence: ~5–7% population; ~12–15 million US adults.
patients_needing_tx: ~2 million patients receiving pharmacotherapy.
peak_sales: Overlaps with depression/pain market. Novel drug: 5–8% share. Peak sales ~$400–700M.
registrational_endpoint: Patient Health Questionnaire-15 (PHQ-15) ≥35% reduction; SSD-12 score at 12 weeks.
btd_threshold_vs_soc: ≥40% PHQ-15 reduction vs. SSRI; significant reduction in healthcare utilization.

symptoms: Excessive preoccupation with having a serious illness without physical cause
treatment_goal: Reduce health-related anxiety; decrease medical utilization
first_line_soc: CBT for health anxiety is first-line. SSRIs (paroxetine, fluoxetine) are most commonly used pharmacologically (off-label).
treatment_algorithm: CBT (health anxiety-specific protocols). Pharmacologic (off-label): SSRIs (paroxetine, fluoxetine) — have small trial evidence; SNRIs; venlafaxine. Scheduled regular appointments (rather than as-needed) reduce unnecessary testing. Clomipramine for OCD-like presentations.
us_prevalence: ~1.3–10% prevalence (broad definition); treatment-presenting ~1–2 million.
patients_needing_tx: ~500,000 patients using pharmacotherapy.
peak_sales: Modest market overlapping with anxiety disorders. Peak sales ~$150–300M.
registrational_endpoint: Health Anxiety Inventory (HAI-18) ≥35% reduction at 12 weeks.
btd_threshold_vs_soc: ≥40% HAI-18 reduction vs. SSRI; significant reduction in medical visits.

symptoms: Neurological symptoms (weakness, seizures, blindness) without structural cause
treatment_goal: Restore neurological function; address psychological triggers
first_line_soc: Physiotherapy/occupational therapy and psychotherapy (CBT) are first-line. SSRIs used for comorbid depression/anxiety (off-label for core symptoms).
treatment_algorithm: Explanation and psychoeducation first. Physical rehabilitation: physiotherapy, occupational therapy. CBT specifically for FND. Pharmacologic (for comorbidities): SSRIs for depression/anxiety; GABAergic agents for functional seizures (limited evidence); no drug addresses core FND. MDMA-assisted therapy under investigation.
us_prevalence: ~50–100 per 100,000; ~150,000–300,000 prevalent US cases of functional neurological disorder (Neurology 2020).
patients_needing_tx: ~100,000 patients using pharmacotherapy for comorbid symptoms.
peak_sales: Challenging market; heterogeneous endpoints. 10–15% penetration. Peak sales ~$100–200M.
registrational_endpoint: Clinical Global Impression-Improvement (CGI-I); SF-36 physical function score at 12 weeks.
btd_threshold_vs_soc: ≥35% improvement in CGI-I vs. physiotherapy alone; significant disability score reduction.

symptoms: Deliberate feigning or inducing illness in oneself for sick role; no external gain
treatment_goal: Address underlying psychiatric comorbidities; prevent self-harm
first_line_soc: No FDA-approved pharmacotherapy. Psychotherapy (CBT, motivational interviewing) is recommended. Treat underlying depression, trauma, or personality disorder with SSRIs/antipsychotics.
treatment_algorithm: Confrontational management is controversial; non-confrontational engagement preferred. Psychotherapy: CBT. Pharmacologic: SSRIs for depression/anxiety comorbidity; antipsychotics for psychotic features; mood stabilizers for BPD co-occurring. No specific drug.
us_prevalence: ~0.5–2% hospital admissions; ~50,000–100,000 active cases in the US.
patients_needing_tx: ~30,000 patients receiving pharmacotherapy for comorbid conditions.
peak_sales: No viable stand-alone pharmacotherapy market. Peak sales ~$20–40M (comorbidity treatment only).

symptoms: Caregiver fabricates/induces illness in another (usually child) for attention
treatment_goal: Protect victim; address caregiver's psychological disorder
first_line_soc: No pharmacotherapy for the perpetrator. Child protection and psychiatric assessment are primary. Psychotherapy for perpetrator if they accept treatment.
treatment_algorithm: Child protection services involvement is mandatory. Psychiatric evaluation of perpetrator; voluntary psychotherapy if willing. Pharmacologic treatment of any underlying psychiatric disorder (depression, personality disorder) using standard drugs. No specific drug treatment.
us_prevalence: Rare; estimated ~1,000–1,500 confirmed cases annually (mainly pediatric; pediatric estimates 0.5–2 per 100,000 children).
patients_needing_tx: ~500 patients receiving any pharmacotherapy; primarily managed through forensic/child psychiatry.
price: N/A — no pharmacologic market.
peak_sales: No commercial opportunity for a dedicated drug. Peak sales ~$5–10M (effectively zero).

symptoms: Loss of control over substance use, craving, withdrawal, continued use despite harm
treatment_goal: Achieve abstinence or harm reduction; prevent relapse
first_line_soc: Varies by substance: naltrexone (opioid/alcohol — FDA-approved), buprenorphine/naloxone (Suboxone — opioid), methadone (opioid), acamprosate (alcohol), varenicline (nicotine), bupropion (nicotine).
treatment_algorithm: Opioid Use Disorder: buprenorphine/naloxone (Suboxone, Sublocade injectable), methadone, extended-release naltrexone (Vivitrol). Alcohol Use Disorder: naltrexone, acamprosate, disulfiram; nalmefene (Europe). Nicotine: varenicline (Chantix/Champix — most effective), bupropion SR, NRT. Stimulant/Cannabis: no approved drugs; contingency management. 2024–2025: semaglutide (GLP-1) showing promising data for alcohol/opioid craving reduction in trials.
us_prevalence: ~48 million Americans have a SUD in past year (~17% prevalence; SAMHSA 2023 survey).
patients_needing_tx: ~15 million patients seeking pharmacotherapy (opioid ~3M, alcohol ~5M, nicotine ~7M).
price: $10,000–$25,000/year; varies by substance — Sublocade ~$14,000/yr; Vivitrol ~$14,000/yr.
peak_sales: Massive market. GLP-1s for SUD: 5–10% crossover penetration. Novel drug: Peak sales ~$2.0–5.0B.
registrational_endpoint: Days abstinent; relapse rate at 12 weeks; urine drug screen negativity (opioid); AUDIT-C score (alcohol).
btd_threshold_vs_soc: ≥30% improvement in abstinence rate vs. naltrexone/bupropion; ≥40% reduction in craving scale.

symptoms: Psychiatric symptoms (depression, anxiety, psychosis) caused or worsened by substance use
treatment_goal: Treat psychiatric symptoms; achieve sobriety; prevent recurrence
first_line_soc: Treat underlying SUD first; allow 4-week washout before diagnosing independent psychiatric disorder. SSRIs for persistent depression/anxiety post-sobriety.
treatment_algorithm: SUD treatment first (MAT with buprenorphine/naltrexone/methadone). If symptoms persist beyond 4 weeks of abstinence: SSRIs/SNRIs for depression/anxiety; antipsychotics for psychotic features; mood stabilizers for bipolar. Integrated dual diagnosis treatment is optimal. CBT and motivational interviewing.
us_prevalence: ~9–10 million Americans with co-occurring SUD and mental illness (SAMHSA 2023).
patients_needing_tx: ~5 million patients needing combined SUD + psychiatric pharmacotherapy.
price: $10,000–$20,000/year for an integrated dual-diagnosis agent.
peak_sales: Managed across multiple drug classes. Integrated novel drug: 3–5% share. Peak sales ~$500M–1.5B.
registrational_endpoint: AUDIT-C or DAST score reduction PLUS MADRS/GAD-7 improvement at 12 weeks.
btd_threshold_vs_soc: ≥30% improvement in both SUD and psychiatric outcomes vs. monotherapy comparator.

symptoms: Overdose: coma, miosis, respiratory depression; withdrawal: pain, diarrhea, vomiting
treatment_goal: Reverse overdose immediately; manage withdrawal for detoxification
first_line_soc: Naloxone (opioid antagonist, Narcan) intranasal or IV is first-line emergency antidote for opioid overdose. Opioid withdrawal: methadone or buprenorphine taper; clonidine (alpha-2 agonist) for autonomic symptoms.
treatment_algorithm: Opioid overdose: IV/IN/IM naloxone 0.4–2 mg, repeat every 2–3 min (total up to 10 mg); endotracheal intubation if persistent apnea; naloxone infusion for long-acting opioids/fentanyl. OTC naloxone available. Opioid withdrawal management (COWS scale-guided): (1) Buprenorphine (partial agonist) preferred for transition to MOUD — reduces COWS ≥8; (2) Methadone taper in OTP settings; (3) Clonidine 0.1–0.3 mg TID for autonomic symptoms; (4) Loperamide for diarrhea; (5) NSAIDs for myalgias; (6) Hydroxyzine or ondansetron for nausea; (7) Lofexidine (Lucemyra) — FDA-approved alpha-2 agonist for opioid withdrawal, not euphoric.
us_prevalence: ~5.9 million Americans 12+ with opioid use disorder (NCDAS 2025); ~80,000–90,000 overdose deaths/year (AMA 2025). ~2.3 million received MOUD in 2023.
patients_needing_tx: ~5.9 million with OUD; ~2.3 million receiving MOUD; acute withdrawal treatment (inpatient detox): ~500,000–800,000 episodes/year. Naloxone: distributed to millions.
price: Naloxone: ~$50–$150/dose (OTC); novel extended-release antidote: $500–$2,000/episode. Maintenance MOUD: $6,000–$18,000/year (buprenorphine branded).
tam_gross: ~5.9 million OUD patients × $12,000/year MOUD = ~$71 billion TAM (including withdrawal management). (5,900,000 × $12,000 = $70,800,000,000)
peak_sales: US OUD drug market ~$1.4 billion in 2023 (DelveInsight); novel extended-release buprenorphine (Sublocade): ~$300–400M/year. New entrant at 8% share: ~$700M–$1B. (5,900,000 × 8% × $12,000 = $5.7B ceiling; realistic capture ~$700M–$1B.)
registrational_endpoint: Clinical Opiate Withdrawal Scale (COWS) total score at 72 hours for withdrawal; time to reversal of opioid-induced respiratory depression (SpO2 recovery) for antidote.
btd_threshold_vs_soc: COWS ≤8 at 24 hours in ≥60% of patients vs. ≤30% with placebo; or SpO2 ≥95% within 3 minutes in overdose reversal trial.
