symptoms: Compulsive opioid use, craving, withdrawal, overdose risk, functional decline
treatment_goal: Achieve remission, prevent overdose death, restore social function
first_line_soc: Buprenorphine/naloxone (Suboxone, partial mu-agonist/antagonist, sublingual or buccal film) is first-line outpatient MOUD; methadone in opioid treatment program (OTP) is first-line for severe/high-risk cases.
treatment_algorithm: Three FDA-approved MOUD: (1) Buprenorphine/naloxone (Suboxone 8/2–16/4 mg/day SL or Sublocade 300 mg SC monthly) — outpatient, preferred post-DATA 2000/SUPPORT Act; (2) Methadone (60–120 mg/day oral in OTP) — highest efficacy for severe OUD; (3) Naltrexone (Vivitrol 380 mg IM monthly) — for abstinent patients, effective in motivated patients. All combined with behavioral counseling. Transitional care, contingency management adjuncts. Long-acting injectable buprenorphine (Sublocade, Brixadi) reducing diversion risk. Naloxone co-prescribing for all OUD patients.
us_prevalence: ~5.9 million with OUD (NCDAS 2025); ~2.3 million receiving MOUD; ~80,000 overdose deaths in 2024 (AMA). OUD market ~$1.4B US in 2023 (DelveInsight), ~$2B global.
patients_needing_tx: ~5.9 million with OUD; only ~2.3 million receiving MOUD (~39% treatment rate). Untreated gap: ~3.5 million. Realistic active pharmacotherapy pool: ~2.5–3 million with improved access.
price: $15,000–$25,000/year for a novel long-acting or implantable MOUD (e.g., 6-month buprenorphine implant or once-yearly depot). Branded Sublocade ~$15,000–$20,000/year.
tam_gross: ~3 million undertreated OUD patients × $18,000/year = ~$54 billion TAM. (3,000,000 × $18,000 = $54,000,000,000)
peak_sales: ~$2–3 billion for a differentiated long-acting MOUD at 5–8% share. (3,000,000 × 6% × $18,000 = $3.24B ceiling; realistic ~$2–2.5B). Market leader Sublocade ~$400M/year currently.
registrational_endpoint: Proportion of opioid-negative urine samples over treatment period (e.g., Weeks 1–24); proportion retained in treatment at 24 weeks; opioid overdose rate reduction.
btd_threshold_vs_soc: ≥15% absolute increase in proportion with ≥80% opioid-negative urines at Week 24 vs. active comparator (buprenorphine IR); overdose event rate <2% vs. ≥5% in control.

symptoms: Compulsive drinking, craving, impaired control, withdrawal on cessation
treatment_goal: Achieve and maintain abstinence or reduced drinking, prevent relapse
first_line_soc: Naltrexone (opioid antagonist, oral or monthly injectable) is the most widely used FDA-approved first-line agent; reduces craving and reward. Acamprosate (GABA/glutamate modulator) is preferred for patients with abstinence as goal.
treatment_algorithm: Three FDA-approved pharmacotherapies: (1) Naltrexone 50 mg/day PO or Vivitrol 380 mg IM monthly — reduces heavy drinking days; (2) Acamprosate 666 mg TID — reduces abstinence relapse; (3) Disulfiram 250–500 mg/day — aversion therapy (supervised use preferred). All combined with behavioral intervention (CBT, motivational enhancement, 12-step facilitation). Off-label: topiramate (evidence base strong), gabapentin (adjunct for cravings/sleep). Nalmefene (approved EU only) for as-needed reduction. Residential rehabilitation for severe or treatment-refractory cases.
us_prevalence: ~28.9 million Americans aged 12+ had AUD in 2023 (NIAAA 2025 update); ~28 million adults 18+ affected. Only ~10% receive treatment; ~2% access FDA-approved medications (Pew 2024).
patients_needing_tx: ~28 million with AUD; ~10% actively seeking/receiving treatment = ~2.8 million. Pharmacotherapy-eligible treatment pool: ~1.5–2 million (those in formal treatment programs). Medicated currently: ~560,000–700,000.
price: $8,000–$15,000/year. Rationale: Brand naltrexone (Vivitrol) ~$1,800/month injectable; novel agent would need to demonstrate superiority to generic oral naltrexone (~$50/month) to price in $8K–$15K range.
peak_sales: ~$1.5–2 billion at 6–8% of treatment-seeking patients. (2,000,000 × 7% × $12,000 = $1.68B). Large market but generic competition and stigma limit uptake.
registrational_endpoint: Percentage of heavy drinking days (HDD) over 24 weeks OR time to first heavy drinking day; WHO Level IV endpoint acceptable to FDA for AUD indication.
btd_threshold_vs_soc: ≥15% absolute reduction in percentage of heavy drinking days vs. placebo at Week 24, or ≥20% improvement in total abstinence rate, representing meaningful improvement over naltrexone (~10–15% HDD reduction in trials).

symptoms: Intoxication, anxiety, psychosis, withdrawal with chronic use, impaired memory
treatment_goal: Achieve cessation, manage withdrawal, address cannabis use disorder
first_line_soc: No FDA-approved pharmacotherapy for cannabis use disorder. Behavioral therapies (CBT, motivational enhancement) are standard of care. Acute anxiety/panic: benzodiazepines if needed.
treatment_algorithm: No approved pharmacotherapy. Cannabis withdrawal syndrome: insomnia, irritability, appetite loss, anxiety — treated symptomatically with clonazepam, trazodone, or melatonin. Off-label options investigated: dronabinol (synthetic THC) for withdrawal; N-acetylcysteine for adolescents (modest evidence); gabapentin; zolpidem for sleep disruption. CBD (nabiximols, Sativex) under investigation for withdrawal in EU/Canada. Cannabis hyperemesis syndrome: topical capsaicin or haloperidol for refractory vomiting. Behavioral: CBT + motivational enhancement is the gold standard.
us_prevalence: ~52 million Americans (18.4%) used cannabis in the past year; ~22.9% of adults 18+ are current users (NCDAS 2025). Cannabis use disorder affects ~8–10 million Americans (16% of users).
patients_needing_tx: ~8–10 million with cannabis use disorder; ~1.5–2 million seeking treatment. Pharmacotherapy-treatable pool (if agent approved): ~1–2 million.
price: $8,000–$15,000/year. A validated agent targeting endocannabinoid system (e.g., FAAH inhibitor, CB1 partial agonist) would price similarly to AUD agents.
peak_sales: ~$1.2–1.8 billion at 6–8% share. (2,000,000 × 7% × $10,000 = $1.4B). Growing market with legalization increasing use prevalence.
registrational_endpoint: Proportion abstinent from cannabis at Week 12 (confirmed by urine THC-COOH <50 ng/mL); Cannabis Withdrawal Scale (CWS) total score reduction.
btd_threshold_vs_soc: ≥15% absolute improvement in cannabis abstinence rate vs. placebo at Week 12; ≥30% reduction in Cannabis Use Disorder Identification Test-Revised (CUDIT-R) score.

symptoms: Euphoria, tachycardia, hypertension, paranoia, psychosis, crash on cessation
treatment_goal: Manage acute toxicity, reduce use, prevent cardiovascular harm
first_line_soc: No FDA-approved pharmacotherapy for amphetamine use disorder. Acute toxicity: benzodiazepines (lorazepam) for agitation, seizures; antihypertensives for hypertension; supportive care.
treatment_algorithm: Acute intoxication/toxicity: benzodiazepines first-line for agitation/seizures; phentolamine or nitroprusside for hypertensive crisis; avoid beta-blockers alone (risk of unopposed alpha stimulation). Cooling measures for hyperthermia. No FDA-approved pharmacologic maintenance treatment for stimulant use disorder. Off-label options under investigation: bupropion, naltrexone combination (COMBINE-type); mirtazapine; topiramate; N-acetylcysteine. Behavioral: contingency management is most evidence-based for stimulant use disorder.
us_prevalence: ~5 million Americans 12+ misused prescription stimulants in 2022; ~2.6 million had amphetamine use in past year; ~1.5 million with stimulant use disorder (SAMHSA/NCDAS 2025). Methamphetamine use ~2.6 million/year.
patients_needing_tx: ~1.5–2 million with stimulant use disorder needing treatment; treatment uptake for pharmacotherapy is minimal (<5%) given no approved agent. Potential pharmacotherapy pool: ~500,000–800,000 if an approved drug existed.
price: $10,000–$18,000/year. Rationale: High unmet need with no approved agents; first-in-class would command premium pricing similar to OUD agents, potentially $10K–$18K/year.
peak_sales: ~$800 million–$1.2 billion at 8–10% penetration in first 5 years post-approval. (750,000 × 9% × $14,000 = $945M). First-mover advantage massive given no approved competitor.
registrational_endpoint: Percentage of methamphetamine/amphetamine-negative urine drug screens over the last 2 weeks of a 12-week treatment period (FDA CDER stimulant use disorder endpoint guidance 2021).
btd_threshold_vs_soc: ≥15% absolute increase in negative urine drug screen rate vs. placebo during Weeks 10–12; sustained abstinence rate >20% at Week 24.

symptoms: Euphoria, chest pain, cardiovascular toxicity, paranoia, crash/depression
treatment_goal: Manage cardiovascular toxicity, achieve sustained abstinence
first_line_soc: No FDA-approved pharmacotherapy for cocaine use disorder. Acute intoxication: benzodiazepines for cardiovascular effects and agitation; nitrates/phentolamine for coronary vasospasm/hypertensive crisis.
treatment_algorithm: Acute toxicity: benzodiazepines first-line for chest pain, agitation, hypertension; avoid beta-blockers (unopposed alpha stimulation risk); phentolamine or nitrates for severe hypertension/coronary vasospasm; aspirin for chest pain. No pharmacologic maintenance therapy is FDA-approved for cocaine use disorder. Best evidence off-label: topiramate (moderate evidence), disulfiram (modest), modafinil, N-acetylcysteine. Contingency management (behavioral) is most effective documented intervention. Vaccines in development (e.g., dAd5GNE cocaine vaccine) — not approved.
us_prevalence: ~5 million cocaine users in past year (1.8% of 12+) per 2023 NSDUH (Cureus 2025); ~1.3 million with cocaine use disorder. Cocaine-related deaths rose to 27,569 in 2022 (CDC).
patients_needing_tx: ~1.3 million with cocaine use disorder; ~300,000–400,000 seeking treatment annually. Pharmacotherapy pool: ~400,000 if approved agent available.
price: $10,000–$18,000/year. First approved cocaine use disorder drug would command substantial premium; comparable to AUD premium branded agents.
peak_sales: ~$500–800 million at 8–12% penetration within 5 years. (400,000 × 10% × $14,000 = $560M). High unmet need; first mover captures majority share.
registrational_endpoint: Proportion of cocaine-negative urine samples over last 2 weeks of 12-week treatment OR proportion achieving 3 consecutive negative screens (FDA Guidance, Stimulant Use Disorder).
btd_threshold_vs_soc: ≥15% absolute improvement in cocaine-negative urine sample rate vs. placebo during Weeks 10–12, with ≥20% sustained abstinence at Week 24.

symptoms: Tremor, seizures, hallucinations, autonomic instability on alcohol cessation
treatment_goal: Prevent seizures and delirium tremens, ensure safe detoxification
first_line_soc: Benzodiazepines (e.g., chlordiazepoxide or lorazepam, GABA-A agonists) are first-line for alcohol withdrawal symptom management and seizure prevention.
treatment_algorithm: Symptom-triggered benzodiazepine dosing guided by CIWA-Ar scale (chlordiazepoxide, diazepam, lorazepam, or oxazepam) for mild-to-moderate withdrawal; fixed-schedule dosing for severe withdrawal/ICU patients. IV thiamine 500 mg TID × 3 days for all inpatients (Wernicke's prophylaxis). Phenobarbital as adjunct for refractory withdrawal. Beta-blockers (propranolol, atenolol) or alpha-2 agonists (clonidine, dexmedetomidine) for autonomic symptoms. Antipsychotics (haloperidol) for hallucinations; avoid as monotherapy — seizure threshold lowering risk. Inpatient monitoring mandatory for CIWA ≥10 or prior seizure history.
us_prevalence: ~3–4 million acute alcohol withdrawal episodes/year in the US; ~15–20% of hospitalized alcoholic patients develop clinically significant withdrawal. Alcohol use disorder affects ~28–30 million Americans (NIAAA 2025).
patients_needing_tx: ~500,000–700,000 patients annually receive inpatient or intensive outpatient pharmacologic treatment for alcohol withdrawal; ~2–3 million require at least outpatient benzodiazepine taper.
price: $5,000–$10,000/course (acute treatment). Rationale: Acute withdrawal is episodic, not chronic; a novel non-benzo agent (e.g., selective GABA modulator with no abuse potential) would price per treatment course in the $5K–$10K range.
peak_sales: ~$300–500 million at 5–8% share of treated inpatient episodes. (700,000 × 6% × $7,500 = $315M). Generics dominate; differentiation requires safety or abuse-deterrence profile.
registrational_endpoint: Maximum CIWA-Ar score or proportion of patients requiring rescue benzodiazepines during 72-hour withdrawal period; FDA endpoint for alcohol withdrawal trials.
btd_threshold_vs_soc: ≥30% reduction in peak CIWA-Ar score vs. active comparator (lorazepam), or ≥50% reduction in rescue benzodiazepine use, with ≤2% seizure rate.

symptoms: CNS/respiratory depression, overdose coma, severe withdrawal (seizures, delirium)
treatment_goal: Reverse acute toxicity, manage life-threatening withdrawal safely
first_line_soc: Benzodiazepine overdose: flumazenil (benzodiazepine antagonist) is the reversal agent; use cautiously (seizure risk in chronic users). Barbiturate/sedative withdrawal: benzodiazepine taper or phenobarbital substitution taper.
treatment_algorithm: Benzodiazepine overdose: flumazenil 0.2 mg IV (up to 1 mg total); supportive care, airway management; flumazenil should not be given if chronic benzo use (precipitates seizures). Barbiturate overdose: supportive care, alkalinization of urine for phenobarbital clearance; no specific antidote. Sedative-hypnotic withdrawal: (1) Phenobarbital substitution taper (preferred for polysubstance); (2) Equivalent benzodiazepine taper with gradual reduction; (3) Valproate adjunct; (4) ICU for severe withdrawal with autonomic instability. No approved pharmacologic maintenance treatment for sedative use disorder beyond standard taper protocols.
us_prevalence: ~5 million Americans misuse prescription sedatives annually; ~4–5 million misuse benzodiazepines. ~450,000–500,000 with sedative use disorder (SAMHSA 2025). Benzo-involved overdose deaths ~12,000–13,000/year.
patients_needing_tx: ~500,000 with sedative use disorder; ~200,000–300,000 requiring medically supervised withdrawal/treatment. Acute overdose: ~50,000–80,000 ER visits/year with flumazenil consideration.
price: $3,000–$8,000/course for withdrawal management; novel maintenance agent: $10,000–$18,000/year.
registrational_endpoint: Proportion completing medically supervised taper without seizure or delirium; sedative-free urine screen rate at 12 weeks for use disorder indication.
btd_threshold_vs_soc: ≥40% reduction in withdrawal seizure/delirium rate vs. standard phenobarbital taper; ≥20% improvement in 12-week abstinence rate vs. comparator.

symptoms: Severe memory impairment, confabulation, inability to form new memories
treatment_goal: Stabilize thiamine levels, limit further memory deterioration, rehabilitate
first_line_soc: High-dose thiamine (vitamin B1, water-soluble vitamin) IV or IM is the primary treatment: 500 mg IV TID × 3–5 days (Pabrinex/parenteral thiamine), then oral thiamine 100 mg TID ongoing.
treatment_algorithm: Korsakoff psychosis is the chronic amnestic phase following inadequately treated Wernicke encephalopathy. Treatment: (1) High-dose parenteral thiamine (500 mg IV TID × 3–7 days) — limits further damage; improvement in established Korsakoff is limited; (2) Oral thiamine 100 mg TID indefinitely; (3) Nutritional repletion (multivitamins, magnesium, B12, folate); (4) Abstinence from alcohol (mandatory); (5) Cognitive rehabilitation — reality orientation, memory aids, structured environment; (6) Long-term supervised care typically required (<10% fully recover after discharge). Pharmacologic interventions for cognitive symptoms are largely empirical (memantine, donepezil — off-label, limited evidence).
us_prevalence: ~200,000–300,000 Americans have Wernicke-Korsakoff syndrome (WKS) at any time; Korsakoff psychosis is the chronic manifestation in ~80% of untreated WE survivors. US autopsy prevalence ~2.2% in alcohol users (Psychiatrist.com data).
patients_needing_tx: ~150,000–200,000 with established Korsakoff psychosis; most are institutionalized. Active pharmacotherapy pool (thiamine + cognitive agents): ~100,000–150,000.
price: $15,000–$30,000/year. Rationale: Rare, institutionalized population; a disease-modifying cognitive agent targeting thiamine-dependent neurotoxicity pathways would price as a specialty orphan-adjacent product.
peak_sales: ~$200–350 million at 7–10% share. (150,000 × 8% × $20,000 = $240M). Institutionalized population; limited prescribing dynamics.
registrational_endpoint: Change from baseline in Wechsler Memory Scale (WMS-IV) General Memory Index at 6 months; proportion showing ≥10-point improvement in WMS-IV.
btd_threshold_vs_soc: ≥10-point improvement in WMS-IV General Memory Index vs. placebo at 24 weeks; statistically significant reduction in confabulation frequency on standardized assessment.

symptoms: Dissociation, agitation, hypertension, hallucinations, anesthesia (K-hole)
treatment_goal: Manage acute agitation and cardiovascular toxicity; treat use disorder
first_line_soc: No FDA-approved agent for ketamine/PCP use disorder. Acute intoxication: benzodiazepines (lorazepam or diazepam) are first-line for agitation and neurotoxicity; low-stimulation environment.
treatment_algorithm: Acute PCP/ketamine toxicity: benzodiazepines first-line (large doses may be needed for PCP); avoid physostigmine; haloperidol for severe psychosis after benzodiazepine; propranolol/labetalol for hypertension; gastric lavage if oral PCP ingestion recent. Acidification of urine (ammonium chloride) to hasten PCP elimination — limited clinical use. No approved maintenance pharmacotherapy for ketamine or PCP use disorder. Behavioral treatment (CBT) and abstinence-focused rehabilitation are standard. Note: esketamine (Spravato) is FDA-approved for TRD/MDD — a distinct, therapeutic use.
us_prevalence: ~500,000–1 million ketamine users annually in the US (including recreational misuse of medical ketamine); PCP use is much lower (~100,000–200,000). Ketamine use disorder affects an estimated 100,000–200,000.
patients_needing_tx: ~150,000–250,000 with problematic ketamine or PCP use needing pharmacotherapy; current pharmacotherapy demand is minimal due to lack of approved agents.
price: $10,000–$15,000/year for a maintenance therapy; acute antagonist ~$1,000–$3,000/use.
registrational_endpoint: Proportion with sustained ketamine abstinence (urine-confirmed) at Weeks 10–12; reduction in dissociative symptom scale score.
btd_threshold_vs_soc: ≥15% absolute increase in sustained abstinence vs. placebo at Week 12; ≥30% improvement on Brief Psychiatric Rating Scale (BPRS) agitation/psychosis subscale.

symptoms: Euphoria, hyperthermia, hyponatremia, serotonin syndrome, neurotoxicity
treatment_goal: Manage acute toxicity (hyperthermia, serotonin syndrome, hyponatremia)
first_line_soc: No FDA-approved pharmacotherapy for MDMA use disorder. Acute toxicity: active cooling and supportive care for hyperthermia; cyproheptadine (serotonin antagonist) and benzodiazepines for serotonin syndrome.
treatment_algorithm: Acute MDMA intoxication/toxicity: (1) Aggressive cooling (ice packs, mist-fan, cold IV fluids) for hyperthermia — most life-threatening complication; (2) Fluid restriction or hypertonic saline for hyponatremia (dilutional, from excess water intake + SIADH); (3) Benzodiazepines for seizures/agitation; (4) Cyproheptadine for serotonin syndrome; (5) Dantrolene only if rhabdomyolysis-related hyperthermia severe. Note: MDMA-assisted psychotherapy was declined by FDA for PTSD (August 2024) due to trial design concerns; research ongoing. No maintenance pharmacotherapy for MDMA use disorder.
us_prevalence: ~2.2 million Americans used MDMA in the past year; problematic use affects ~400,000–600,000. Acute toxicity ER visits ~5,000–10,000/year (DAWN data).
patients_needing_tx: ~300,000–500,000 with MDMA use disorder or requiring ongoing medical management. Pharmacotherapy pool (hypothetical): ~100,000–200,000.
price: $8,000–$15,000/year for maintenance; acute reversal agent ~$500–$2,000/episode.
registrational_endpoint: MDMA-negative urine drug screen sustained abstinence rate at Weeks 10–12; reduction in neurotoxicity biomarkers (e.g., plasma serotonin transporter activity) at 24 weeks.
btd_threshold_vs_soc: ≥20% sustained abstinence at 12 weeks vs. placebo; ≥25% reduction in validated MDMA craving scale score.

symptoms: Euphoria, arrhythmia, CNS depression, hepatotoxicity, neurotoxicity
treatment_goal: Prevent sudden sniffing death, manage arrhythmia and organ toxicity
first_line_soc: No specific antidote or approved pharmacologic treatment. Acute toxicity: avoid catecholamine stimulation (risk of 'sudden sniffing death' via sensitized myocardium); supportive care; cardiac monitoring and anti-arrhythmics as needed.
treatment_algorithm: Acute inhalant toxicity: (1) Remove from exposure and ensure fresh air; (2) Avoid epinephrine — fatal ventricular arrhythmia risk (sensitized myocardium); use lidocaine/beta-blockers for arrhythmias if needed; (3) Supportive care: oxygen, IV fluids, airway management; (4) N-acetylcysteine for hepatotoxicity (toluene, chlorinated solvents); (5) ICU monitoring for renal failure, rhabdomyolysis, hepatitis. Inhalant use disorder treatment: behavioral (CBT, family therapy, contingency management); no approved pharmacologic agent. N-acetylcysteine studied but not approved for inhalant use disorder.
us_prevalence: ~2.2–2.5 million Americans (mostly adolescents) used inhalants in the past year (SAMHSA/NCDAS); ~600,000–800,000 with inhalant use disorder. ~100–150 sudden sniffing deaths/year.
patients_needing_tx: ~600,000 with inhalant use disorder; ~300,000 with ongoing harmful use requiring treatment. Pharmacotherapy pool: ~100,000–200,000 if agent approved.
price: $8,000–$15,000/year for a maintenance/anti-craving agent.
peak_sales: ~$80–150 million at 4–6% share. (200,000 × 5% × $10,000 = $100M). Primarily adolescent population; limited commercial incentive.
registrational_endpoint: Inhalant-free urine confirmatory test plus ASSIST (Alcohol, Smoking and Substance Involvement Screening Test) inhalant score reduction at 12 weeks.
btd_threshold_vs_soc: ≥20% absolute improvement in 12-week abstinence rate vs. behavioral therapy alone; ≥30% reduction in ASSIST inhalant score.

symptoms: Tachycardia, agitation, psychosis, vomiting, seizures, unpredictable toxicity
treatment_goal: Manage acute toxicity, treat psychosis, support cessation
first_line_soc: No FDA-approved antidote or pharmacologic treatment for synthetic cannabinoid use disorder. Acute toxicity: benzodiazepines for agitation/seizures; antipsychotics (haloperidol, olanzapine) for psychosis.
treatment_algorithm: No approved treatment. Acute toxicity management: supportive care — IV fluids, benzodiazepines (lorazepam) for agitation and seizures, antipsychotics for psychotic symptoms, antiemetics for intractable vomiting, cooling for hyperthermia. No specific antidote exists. Chronic use/dependence: behavioral approaches (CBT, motivational interviewing); no validated pharmacotherapy. Monitor for rhabdomyolysis, renal failure, and coagulopathy.
us_prevalence: Synthetic cannabinoid use is difficult to track; SAMHSA/NCDAS estimates several hundred thousand annual users; poison control calls ~8,000–12,000/year. Prevalence declining from 2015 peak but still significant in adolescents and incarcerated populations.
patients_needing_tx: ~200,000–400,000 persons with problematic synthetic cannabinoid use; ~50,000–100,000 seeking formal treatment annually. Pharmacotherapy pool (if drug approved): ~100,000–150,000.
price: $8,000–$15,000/year (maintenance); acute antidote ~$2,000–$5,000/episode.
peak_sales: ~$100–200 million at 7–10% share. (150,000 × 8% × $10,000 = $120M). Small market; primarily ER/acute hospital use of any antidote.
registrational_endpoint: Sustained abstinence rate (% negative urine screens for synthetic cannabinoid metabolites) over Weeks 8–12; reduction in acute toxicity episodes requiring ER/hospitalization.
btd_threshold_vs_soc: ≥20% sustained abstinence at Week 12 vs. placebo, or ≥50% reduction in ER utilization compared to control group.

symptoms: Perceptual distortions, panic, dissociation, HPPD; rarely severe toxicity
treatment_goal: Manage acute panic/psychosis, treat persistent perception disorder
first_line_soc: No FDA-approved pharmacotherapy for hallucinogen use disorder or acute intoxication beyond supportive care. Benzodiazepines (lorazepam) for acute agitation/panic ('bad trip') is the clinical standard.
treatment_algorithm: Acute intoxication: calm environment ('talk-down'), benzodiazepines for severe anxiety or agitation; avoid antipsychotics if possible (may worsen dissociation with PCP-like drugs); haloperidol if psychosis is severe and unresponsive. Hallucinogen persisting perception disorder (HPPD): clonazepam and/or lamotrigine; clonidine off-label. No established pharmacologic maintenance for use disorder. Behavioral approaches (CBT) for HPPD and psychological sequelae. Note: psilocybin and MDMA are under FDA review for therapeutic applications.
us_prevalence: LSD use 11.6% lifetime among adults 18+; past-year use ~1–2 million Americans. Psilocybin use growing with decriminalization trend. Classic hallucinogen use disorder is rare (<100,000 with severe/chronic use disorder).
patients_needing_tx: ~100,000–200,000 persons with HPPD or recurrent hallucinogen-related psychopathology needing pharmacotherapy; acute ER visits ~50,000–100,000/year.
price: $8,000–$15,000/year for HPPD maintenance therapy; acute intervention ~$500–$2,000/episode.
registrational_endpoint: HPPD symptom severity scale improvement at 12 weeks (validated HPPD assessment scale); reduction in visual disturbance frequency/severity.
btd_threshold_vs_soc: ≥30% reduction in validated HPPD severity score vs. placebo at 12 weeks; ≥40% responder rate (patients with ≥50% symptom reduction).

symptoms: Methemoglobinemia, hypotension, headache, eye damage, immunosuppression
treatment_goal: Treat methemoglobinemia; prevent ongoing use complications
first_line_soc: Methylene blue (methylthioninium chloride) 1–2 mg/kg IV is first-line antidote for significant methemoglobinemia (MetHb >30% or symptomatic). Supportive oxygen for mild cases.
treatment_algorithm: Acute methemoglobinemia: methylene blue 1–2 mg/kg IV over 5 min; repeat if MetHb does not fall within 1 hour; ascorbic acid as adjunct if methylene blue unavailable. Exchange transfusion for refractory cases or G6PD deficiency. Retinal and eye toxicity: ophthalmology referral; no proven pharmacologic treatment for volatile nitrite maculopathy — cessation essential. No pharmacologic treatment for volatile nitrite use disorder; behavioral/psychosocial treatment. Public health: FDA banned alkyl nitrites for human use (except amyl nitrite, Rx).
us_prevalence: Volatile nitrites ('poppers') used by an estimated 2–5 million Americans, primarily among MSM (gay/bisexual men). Methemoglobinemia cases: several hundred per year requiring treatment. Use disorder is uncommon.
patients_needing_tx: ~500–1,000 acute methemoglobinemia cases/year requiring methylene blue; ~50,000–100,000 with retinal toxicity or other harms from regular use.
price: Methylene blue ~$100–$500/episode (generic). A novel retinal neuroprotectant for nitrite maculopathy: $5,000–$15,000/course.
peak_sales: ~$50–100 million for a retinal protection agent. (100,000 × 5% × $10,000 = $50M). Methylene blue already generic.
registrational_endpoint: Improvement in best-corrected visual acuity (BCVA) at 6 months vs. placebo for retinal protection indication.
btd_threshold_vs_soc: ≥5-letter improvement in ETDRS BCVA at 6 months vs. untreated observation in patients with acute nitrite maculopathy.

symptoms: Agitation, psychosis, hyperthermia, tachycardia, violent behavior
treatment_goal: Manage acute toxicity, prevent cardiovascular and neurological complications
first_line_soc: No FDA-approved pharmacologic treatment. Acute toxicity: benzodiazepines (lorazepam) first-line for agitation, seizure control, and cardiovascular stabilization.
treatment_algorithm: No approved treatment. Acute toxicity: (1) Benzodiazepines (lorazepam 2–4 mg IV/IM) — agitation, tachycardia, seizure prevention; (2) Antipsychotics (droperidol, haloperidol) — adjunct for psychosis; (3) Aggressive cooling — hyperthermia can be fatal; (4) IV fluids — hydration and rhabdomyolysis prevention; (5) Cyproheptadine — if serotonin syndrome features present. No antidote; no FDA-approved maintenance treatment for cathinone use disorder. Behavioral treatment mirrors stimulant use disorder protocols.
us_prevalence: Mephedrone and other cathinones ('bath salts') peaked ~2011–2013; US prevalence now estimated <500,000 annual users; poison control reports several thousand cases/year. Exact data limited by novel analogue proliferation.
patients_needing_tx: ~100,000–200,000 with problematic use annually; pharmacotherapy pool (hypothetical): ~50,000–80,000.
price: $8,000–$12,000/year for a maintenance therapy; acute antidote ~$1,000–$3,000/episode.
peak_sales: ~$50–80 million at 6–8% share. (80,000 × 7% × $10,000 = $56M). Very small market.
registrational_endpoint: Reduction in acute toxicity hospitalization rate and sustained urine drug screen negativity at 8 weeks.
btd_threshold_vs_soc: ≥40% reduction in acute toxicity admissions vs. supportive care alone; ≥20% abstinence at Week 12.

symptoms: CNS depression, respiratory depression, amnesia, coma at high doses
treatment_goal: Manage airway/respiratory failure, treat withdrawal safely
first_line_soc: No antidote exists. Supportive care is standard: airway protection (intubation if needed), monitoring, and hemodynamic support. GHB withdrawal treated with benzodiazepines ± barbiturates in ICU.
treatment_algorithm: Acute intoxication: supportive care only — airway management (endotracheal intubation for apnea), IV fluids, hemodynamic support; atropine for bradycardia; no specific reversal agent. GHB withdrawal (potentially life-threatening): high-dose benzodiazepines; baclofen (GABA-B agonist) has emerging evidence for craving and withdrawal management; phenobarbital for refractory cases. ICU admission for severe withdrawal. Note: Pharmaceutical GHB (sodium oxybate/Xyrem) is Schedule III/FDA-approved for narcolepsy; illicit use is Schedule I.
us_prevalence: GHB misuse affects an estimated 100,000–300,000 Americans annually; most prevalent in club/party settings. Exact epidemiology poorly captured; poison control reports several thousand annual calls.
patients_needing_tx: ~50,000–100,000 persons with problematic GHB use or dependence requiring treatment per year; most managed in ED/ICU for acute episodes.
price: $5,000–$10,000/episode (acute antidote); $8,000–$12,000/year for maintenance. A validated antidote (e.g., MCT1 inhibitor) would price as an acute rescue agent.
registrational_endpoint: Time to full neurological recovery (GCS = 15) OR proportion requiring mechanical ventilation in GHB overdose RCT; time to resolution of withdrawal symptoms on standardized scale.
btd_threshold_vs_soc: ≥30% reduction in proportion requiring mechanical ventilation vs. supportive care; or ≥2-hour faster return to GCS 15 vs. placebo.

symptoms: Ataxia, ophthalmoplegia, confusion — classic triad; often incomplete
treatment_goal: Rapidly restore thiamine to prevent Korsakoff psychosis and death
first_line_soc: High-dose intravenous thiamine (vitamin B1) 500 mg IV TID × 3–5 days (Royal College of Physicians / European guidelines) is the established first-line treatment; prevents progression to Korsakoff syndrome.
treatment_algorithm: Medical emergency: (1) Do NOT give glucose before thiamine (worsens Wernicke's); (2) Thiamine 500 mg IV in 100 mL NS over 30 min TID × 3–5 days; reduce to 250 mg IV/IM daily for 3–5 additional days if improving; then oral thiamine 100 mg TID ongoing; (3) Correct hypomagnesemia (required for thiamine utilization); (4) Nutritional repletion: multivitamins, B12, folate; (5) Alcohol abstinence; (6) Monitor for WE in all patients with: altered mental status + alcohol history, malnutrition, eating disorders, bariatric surgery, prolonged IV nutrition; (7) Oral supplementation alone is insufficient for treatment-level dosing in alcoholic patients.
us_prevalence: US prevalence 2.2% in alcoholic population by autopsy (Psychiatrist.com data); ~400,000–600,000 clinical episodes/year estimated; most unrecognized (only 20–30% clinically diagnosed antemortem). ~80,000–100,000 inpatient cases/year.
patients_needing_tx: ~80,000–150,000 inpatient cases/year recognized and treated; larger unrecognized burden. Active treatment population: ~100,000 annually.
price: $3,000–$8,000/episode for a novel neuroprotective or formulation advantage product; standard IV thiamine is ~$100–$500/course (generic). A lipid-soluble thiamine analogue (e.g., benfotiamine) at improved brain penetrance could command $2,000–$5,000/course.
peak_sales: ~$50–100 million for a novel thiamine formulation with superior brain delivery. (100,000 × 15% × $5,000 = $75M).
registrational_endpoint: Proportion achieving complete resolution of oculomotor abnormalities + ataxia at 7 days; MMSE ≥24 at 14 days; MRI WE lesion resolution at 30 days.
btd_threshold_vs_soc: Complete oculomotor resolution in ≥70% at Day 7 vs. ≤40% with standard 100 mg/day IV thiamine; prevention of Korsakoff progression in ≥80% of treated patients (vs. ~20% with standard dose).

symptoms: Dementia, dysarthria, gait ataxia, corpus callosum demyelination from alcohol
treatment_goal: Limit demyelination progression with thiamine and nutritional support
first_line_soc: High-dose parenteral thiamine (1,500 mg/day IV × 7 days) based on case reports and expert consensus; oral B-complex vitamins and folate concurrently. No randomized controlled trial data exists.
treatment_algorithm: No proven pharmacologic treatment; management guided by case reports. (1) High-dose IV thiamine 500–1,500 mg/day × 7–14 days (superior to standard dosing in case series); (2) B12 (cyanocobalamin 1 mg IM daily) and folate 5 mg/day; (3) Corticosteroids (methylprednisolone) for acute forms with brain edema — limited evidence; (4) Amantadine for extrapyramidal symptoms in some cases; (5) Alcohol abstinence — mandatory; (6) Nutritional rehabilitation; (7) Supportive care and neurorehabilitation. Prognosis ranges from full recovery to permanent vegetative state depending on form (Type A acute vs. Type B subacute).
us_prevalence: Extremely rare: estimated <1,000–2,000 cases in the US at any given time; likely underdiagnosed. MBD is found in ~12–15% of Wernicke's disease autopsies, suggesting co-prevalence (PMC data). Most epidemiological data from European and Japanese case series.
patients_needing_tx: <2,000 diagnosed US cases per year; almost all require acute inpatient treatment. True pharmacotherapy pool: ~500–1,000 patients annually.
price: $50,000–$150,000/course. Rationale: Ultra-rare disease; a validated myelin repair or neuroprotective agent would achieve ultra-orphan pricing.
peak_sales: ~$20–50 million at 20–50% capture. (1,000 × 30% × $100,000 = $30M). Ultra-rare; breakthrough designation likely.
registrational_endpoint: MRI corpus callosum lesion volume reduction at 3 months; clinical improvement on Global Clinical Impression (CGI) scale or Barthel Index.
btd_threshold_vs_soc: ≥30% reduction in MRI corpus callosum T2-FLAIR lesion volume at 12 weeks vs. standard thiamine alone, with ≥1 CGI-I rating improvement.

symptoms: Flashbacks, nightmares, hypervigilance, avoidance after trauma exposure
treatment_goal: Reduce PTSD symptoms; achieve functional remission
first_line_soc: Sertraline (Zoloft) and paroxetine (Paxil) are the only FDA-approved drugs for PTSD. Trauma-focused CBT (CPT, PE) and EMDR are first-line psychotherapies.
treatment_algorithm: Step 1: SSRI (sertraline 50–200 mg, paroxetine 20–60 mg — FDA-approved) or SNRI (venlafaxine ER — off-label, Level A evidence). Step 2: Prazosin for nightmares; augmentation with atypical antipsychotics (quetiapine, olanzapine, risperidone). Trauma-focused psychotherapy: CPT, PE, EMDR. 2024: MDMA-assisted therapy Phase 3 trials completed (FDA advisory panel voted no 2024 due to trial design concerns); brexanolone/zuranolone in trials; nabilone for nightmares off-label. Emerging: stellate ganglion block.
us_prevalence: ~12–13 million US adults (~4.7% past-year prevalence; NIMH/VA data 2024); lifetime ~20 million.
patients_needing_tx: ~7 million patients needing pharmacotherapy.
price: $15,000–$30,000/year; esketamine-type pricing precedent for novel mechanisms.
peak_sales: Massive unmet need. Novel mechanism: 8–12% share. Peak sales ~$2.0–5.0B.
registrational_endpoint: CAPS-5 total score ≥10-point reduction; PCL-5 ≥10-point reduction at 12 weeks.
btd_threshold_vs_soc: CAPS-5 ≥12-point superiority vs. placebo; response rate (≥30% reduction) ≥50% vs. sertraline.

disease: Dissociative Subtype of Posttraumatic Stress Disorder
symptoms: PTSD with depersonalization/derealization; emotional numbing, detachment
treatment_goal: Reduce dissociative symptoms; process trauma safely
first_line_soc: SSRIs (sertraline, paroxetine — FDA-approved for PTSD) are first-line; trauma-focused CBT with dissociation-adapted protocols (phase-based treatment) is recommended.
treatment_algorithm: Phase-based psychotherapy (stabilization before trauma processing). Pharmacologic: SSRIs (sertraline, paroxetine) — FDA-approved for PTSD broadly; prazosin for nightmares; MDMA-assisted therapy showed promise in trials but FDA rejected psilocybin/MDMA NDA in 2024; venlafaxine SNRI off-label; propranolol memory reconsolidation adjunct. Avoid benzodiazepines.
us_prevalence: ~15–30% of PTSD patients have dissociative subtype; PTSD prevalence ~12–13 million; dissociative subtype ~2–4 million.
patients_needing_tx: ~1.5 million patients with dissociative PTSD needing specialized pharmacotherapy.
price: $15,000–$25,000/year; PTSD-focused drug pricing benchmark.
peak_sales: Subtype within PTSD. Novel targeted drug: 10–15% uptake. Peak sales ~$600M–1.2B.
registrational_endpoint: CAPS-5 dissociation subscale + overall PTSD score; DES-PD score ≥35% reduction at 12 weeks.
btd_threshold_vs_soc: ≥35% CAPS-5 improvement AND ≥40% DES-PD reduction vs. SSRI comparator.

symptoms: Emotional distress, anxiety, or depression after identifiable stressor
treatment_goal: Restore baseline functioning; prevent mood disorder progression
first_line_soc: Psychotherapy (CBT, supportive therapy) is first-line. No FDA-approved pharmacotherapy exists; SSRIs/SNRIs used off-label for anxiety/depressive subtypes.
treatment_algorithm: Psychotherapy is mainstay. Pharmacologic management is off-label and subtype-driven: SSRIs/SNRIs for depressive/anxious presentations; buspirone for anxiety; short-course benzodiazepines only for acute agitation. Duration typically limited to stressor resolution (usually <6 months).
us_prevalence: ~5–20% of outpatient psychiatric patients; 5–8 million US adults affected annually (APA/epidemiologic data).
patients_needing_tx: ~2 million patients annually receive some pharmacologic augmentation to therapy; most managed without drugs. Pharmacologic pool ~25% of prevalent cases.
price: $6,000–$10,000/year; low willingness to pay given benign prognosis and generic alternatives.
peak_sales: No clear drug class focus; any novel agent would capture narrow niche. ~2–3% share. Peak sales ~$200–400M.
registrational_endpoint: HAM-A or PHQ-9 score reduction ≥50% at 8 weeks vs. placebo.
btd_threshold_vs_soc: ≥35% improvement on primary symptom scale vs. active comparator or placebo.

symptoms: Physical injury, fear, psychological trauma, isolation
treatment_goal: Ensure safety, treat trauma and comorbid PTSD/depression
first_line_soc: No pharmacologic SoC for domestic violence itself. For resultant PTSD: sertraline or paroxetine (SSRIs) are FDA-approved first-line agents.
treatment_algorithm: None for domestic violence per se. Management is primarily safety planning, crisis intervention, shelter referral, and legal advocacy. Comorbid PTSD is treated with SSRIs (sertraline, paroxetine — FDA-approved) as first-line pharmacotherapy; SNRIs (venlafaxine) as second-line. Depression treated per standard guidelines. Prazosin may be added for nightmares. Psychotherapy (trauma-focused CBT, EMDR) is cornerstone treatment.
us_prevalence: ~10–12 million adults experience intimate partner violence annually in the US; cumulative lifetime exposure ~1 in 4 women and 1 in 9 men (CDC data). Roughly 9 million Americans have PTSD at any given time (SingleCare 2025).
patients_needing_tx: ~3–4 million DV survivors annually who develop PTSD or major depression requiring pharmacotherapy; treatment uptake is ~33% for pharmacotherapy in those with PTSD — yielding ~1–1.3 million active Rx patients.
price: $8,000–$15,000/year. Rationale: PTSD is a competitive market with generic SSRIs; a novel trauma-specific agent (e.g., MDMA-assisted therapy adjunct or targeted neuropeptide) would price in the $8K–$15K range, below rare-disease pricing but above generic levels.
peak_sales: ~$1.0–1.5 billion at ~10–12% share. (1,300,000 × 10% × $10,000 = $1.3B). Competitive PTSD space limits share.
registrational_endpoint: CAPS-5 (Clinician-Administered PTSD Scale for DSM-5) total symptom score reduction from baseline at 12 weeks; FDA-accepted primary endpoint for PTSD trials.
btd_threshold_vs_soc: ≥10-point reduction in CAPS-5 total score vs. placebo at Week 12, in patients with DV-related PTSD (CAPS-5 ≥25 at baseline).

symptoms: Intense fear, flashbacks, dissociation after trauma
treatment_goal: Prevent PTSD, restore daily functioning quickly
first_line_soc: Trauma-focused cognitive behavioral therapy (TF-CBT) is first-line; no FDA-approved pharmacotherapy specifically for ASD, though SSRIs (sertraline, paroxetine) are used off-label.
treatment_algorithm: First-line: trauma-focused CBT within 2–3 weeks of trauma. Pharmacology is adjunctive/off-label: SSRIs (sertraline, paroxetine) for anxiety/sleep; propranolol investigated for memory reconsolidation but not approved. Benzodiazepines generally avoided due to potential PTSD risk. Sleep disturbance may be addressed with prazosin off-label.
us_prevalence: ~1–2% of trauma-exposed individuals; estimated 1–3 million cases annually given trauma rates, but short-duration (resolves/converts within 4 weeks). Active point prevalence ~500,000 (NIMH/epidemiologic estimates).
patients_needing_tx: ~300,000 patients seeking pharmacologic support annually; most cases are short-lived and managed with therapy. Pharmacologic pool represents ~30% of prevalent cases.
price: $8,000–$15,000/year; comparable to anxiety/PTSD drugs; limited chronicity constrains price.
peak_sales: Market penetration limited by acute/self-resolving nature and therapy primacy. ~5–8% share in pharmacologically treated subset. Peak sales ~$150–250M.
registrational_endpoint: CAPS-5 score reduction vs. placebo at 4–6 weeks; or prevention of PTSD diagnosis at 3 months.
btd_threshold_vs_soc: ≥30% reduction in CAPS-5 vs. placebo, or ≥40% reduction in PTSD conversion rate vs. control.

symptoms: Breast lump, skin changes, nipple discharge, axillary swelling
treatment_goal: Achieve pathologic complete response; prevent metastasis; extend survival
first_line_soc: Early HR+/HER2- breast cancer: chemotherapy (AC-T or TC) + CDK4/6 inhibitor (abemaciclib) for high-risk; then endocrine therapy (tamoxifen or aromatase inhibitor). HER2+: pertuzumab + trastuzumab + docetaxel (TCHP). TNBC: neoadjuvant chemotherapy ± pembrolizumab. Metastatic HR+: CDK4/6 inhibitor (palbociclib/ribociclib/abemaciclib) + aromatase inhibitor as first-line.
treatment_algorithm: HR+/HER2- early: adjuvant endocrine therapy (AI for postmenopausal, tamoxifen ± OFS for premenopausal) ± CDK4/6 inhibitor (abemaciclib, monarchE). HER2+ early: trastuzumab + pertuzumab ± neratinib. TNBC early: pembrolizumab + chemotherapy (KEYNOTE-522). Metastatic HR+: CDK4/6i + AI; second-line: PI3K inhibitor (alpelisib for PIK3CA-mutant) or elacestrant (ER degrader). Metastatic HER2+: T-DM1, T-DXd (trastuzumab deruxtecan). Metastatic TNBC: sacituzumab govitecan, pembrolizumab. BRCA1/2: olaparib, talazoparib. Radiation, surgery per staging.
us_prevalence: ~3.8 million US women living with breast cancer (all stages); ~310,000 new cases/year (invasive + DCIS). Source: ACS Cancer Facts & Figures 2024.
patients_needing_tx: ~3.5 million on active surveillance or treatment; ~150,000–200,000 with metastatic breast cancer on systemic therapy; ~200,000–250,000 new early-stage patients/year needing adjuvant systemic therapy.
price: $150,000–$350,000/year for novel ADCs, PROTAC ER degraders, or next-gen CDK4/6 inhibitors. Trastuzumab deruxtecan ~$170,000/year as benchmark.
tam_gross: ~500,000 patients actively needing novel systemic therapy × $200,000 = $100B
peak_sales: $3B–$8B at 5–10% share for a novel mechanism; T-DXd (Enhertu) ~$3B in 2024 as comp.
registrational_endpoint: Event-free survival (EFS) or invasive disease-free survival (iDFS) in early stage; progression-free survival (PFS) and/or overall survival (OS) in metastatic setting. FDA co-primary: PFS + OS or OS alone.
btd_threshold_vs_soc: HR 0.65–0.70 vs. SoC (e.g., CDK4/6i + AI) for PFS in metastatic HR+; HR 0.50–0.60 for OS in TNBC second-line.

symptoms: Large rapidly growing breast mass, skin stretching, asymmetry
treatment_goal: Achieve R0 resection; prevent local recurrence in malignant type
first_line_soc: Surgery (wide local excision with 1–2 cm margins) is the primary treatment. No standard systemic pharmacologic SoC for non-metastatic phyllodes. For metastatic malignant phyllodes: ifosfamide-based chemotherapy (similar to sarcoma protocols) or doxorubicin + dacarbazine.
treatment_algorithm: Benign and borderline: complete surgical excision with negative margins. Malignant phyllodes, metastatic: anthracycline-based or ifosfamide-based chemotherapy (gemcitabine + docetaxel also used). Radiation may be considered post-surgery for close margins. No FDA-approved drug specifically for phyllodes tumors.
us_prevalence: ~500–1,000 new US cases/year; prevalence ~2,000–3,000 (includes benign, borderline, malignant). Malignant type: ~150–200/year. Source: SEER, NCCN.
patients_needing_tx: ~150–300 patients with malignant/metastatic phyllodes needing systemic therapy.
price: $80,000–$200,000/year for a novel targeted agent (e.g., CDK4/6i, MEK inhibitor, anti-PDL1 for malignant).
peak_sales: $5–15M at 20–30% share; ultra-rare tumor, sarcoma-classified treatment.
registrational_endpoint: Objective response rate (ORR) ≥20% and progression-free survival vs. historical control in metastatic malignant phyllodes (single-arm, basket trial eligible).
btd_threshold_vs_soc: ORR ≥30% with durable responses (≥6 months) in malignant metastatic phyllodes.

symptoms: Severe unintentional weight loss, muscle wasting, fatigue, anorexia
treatment_goal: Stabilize weight, preserve muscle mass, improve functional status
first_line_soc: None established pharmacologically. Nutritional support + appetite stimulants (megestrol acetate, dronabinol) used off-label.
treatment_algorithm: Megestrol acetate (Megace): increases appetite but not muscle mass. Corticosteroids: short-term appetite stimulation. Investigational: anamorelin (Adlumiz—approved Japan/EU 2021 for cancer cachexia; FDA Complete Response Letter 2016). Selective androgen receptor modulators (SARMs): enobosarm. Anti-inflammatory: thalidomide, omega-3. No FDA-approved drug in US as of 2025.
us_prevalence: ~500,000–800,000 US cancer patients experience cachexia annually; ~150,000 have severe cachexia.
patients_needing_tx: ~200,000 patients with clinically significant cancer cachexia could benefit from pharmacologic treatment.
price: $40,000–$80,000/year for an approved cachexia drug in US. $60,000 est.
peak_sales: 15% market penetration in addressable patients: ~$1.8B peak
registrational_endpoint: Lean body mass (LBM) gain ≥1 kg AND handgrip strength improvement vs. placebo at 12 weeks
btd_threshold_vs_soc: LBM gain ≥1.5 kg AND >15% improvement in 6MWT vs. placebo

symptoms: Neurological deficits, Cushing's, SIADH, hypercalcemia, DIC
treatment_goal: Treat underlying cancer; suppress paraneoplastic immune response
first_line_soc: Treat underlying malignancy (tumor-directed therapy is cornerstone). Immunosuppression (corticosteroids, IVIG, plasmapheresis) for neurological syndromes. Octreotide for carcinoid/ectopic ACTH.
treatment_algorithm: Neurological PNS: remove tumor + corticosteroids → IVIG → rituximab → cyclophosphamide for refractory. SIADH: fluid restriction + tolvaptan (Samsca, V2-receptor antagonist). Hypercalcemia: bisphosphonates (zoledronic acid), denosumab. Ectopic ACTH: ketoconazole/metyrapone. Dermatomyositis: IVIG + steroids. No single pharmacologic first-line — highly syndrome-dependent.
us_prevalence: ~150,000–200,000 cancer patients with clinically significant PNS annually.
patients_needing_tx: ~100,000 patients requiring pharmacologic management of their PNS syndrome.
price: $50,000–$150,000/year for novel immunosuppressant/targeted agent. $100,000.
peak_sales: 15% novel agent share for syndrome-specific therapy: ~$1.5B peak
registrational_endpoint: Syndrome-specific: neurological disability score improvement; ACTH/cortisol normalization rate
btd_threshold_vs_soc: ≥50% improvement in disability score vs. corticosteroids alone; cortisol normalization ≥80%

symptoms: Changing mole (ABCDE), ulceration, nodal swelling, constitutional symptoms in advanced disease
treatment_goal: Complete resection of early disease; durable response in metastatic disease
first_line_soc: Stage III/IV BRAF-mutant: BRAF + MEK inhibitor (dabrafenib + trametinib, or vemurafenib + cobimetinib, or encorafenib + binimetinib). BRAF-wild type: anti-PD-1 (pembrolizumab or nivolumab) ± anti-CTLA-4 (ipilimumab). Adjuvant: pembrolizumab or nivolumab for resected stage III.
treatment_algorithm: Stage I–II: wide local excision ± sentinel lymph node biopsy; adjuvant pembrolizumab for high-risk stage IIB/C. Stage III: adjuvant dabrafenib + trametinib (BRAF-mutant) or pembrolizumab/nivolumab. Stage IV: dual checkpoint (nivo + ipi, CheckMate-067); BRAF-mutant combo; tumor-infiltrating lymphocytes (lifileucel, FDA-approved 2024); T-VEC (intralesional oncolytic virus); LAG-3 inhibitor (relatlimab + nivolumab in stage III). Brain metastases: checkpoint inhibitors, targeted therapy, stereotactic radiosurgery.
us_prevalence: ~1.4 million US patients living with melanoma; ~100,000 new cases/year; ~8,300 deaths/year. Source: ACS 2024, SEER.
patients_needing_tx: ~200,000–300,000 patients requiring systemic therapy (adjuvant + metastatic); ~50,000–60,000 with active metastatic melanoma.
price: $150,000–$350,000/year; nivo + ipi combo ~$300,000/year; lifileucel ~$515,000 one-time.
peak_sales: $2B–$5B at 5–10% share for a novel mechanism; Opdivo/Yervoy ~$4B combined 2024.
registrational_endpoint: PFS and OS in metastatic melanoma; RFS (relapse-free survival) in adjuvant stage III setting. FDA expects OS or PFS with mature OS trend.
btd_threshold_vs_soc: HR ≤0.65 for PFS vs. PD-1 monotherapy in metastatic melanoma 1L; OS HR ≤0.70 in adjuvant stage III.

symptoms: Irregular firm lesion, ulceration, crusting on sun-exposed skin or mucosa
treatment_goal: Complete excision; prevent nodal spread and distant metastasis
first_line_soc: Localized: Mohs surgery or standard excision. Advanced/metastatic: cemiplimab (Libtayo, anti-PD-1) — FDA-approved 2018 as first-line. Pembrolizumab (Keytruda) — FDA-approved 2020 for locally advanced/metastatic cSCC.
treatment_algorithm: Early: Mohs surgery (standard); radiation as alternative. Advanced: cemiplimab 350 mg q3w IV (first-line by FDA approval); pembrolizumab 200 mg q3w. Second-line: platinum-based chemotherapy (cisplatin/5-FU) or EGFR inhibitor (cetuximab). Head/neck SCC: concurrent chemoradiation for unresectable disease.
us_prevalence: ~1–2 million new cSCC diagnoses/year; ~15,000–20,000 with locally advanced or metastatic disease requiring systemic therapy. Source: ACS, Skin Cancer Foundation.
patients_needing_tx: ~15,000–25,000 patients/year with advanced cSCC needing systemic therapy.
price: $150,000–$250,000/year; cemiplimab WAC ~$170,000/year.
peak_sales: $500M–$1.5B at 20–30% share; cemiplimab ~$1B+ in all indications, cSCC ~$400M.
registrational_endpoint: ORR, DoR, and OS in locally advanced or metastatic cSCC; cemiplimab showed 47% ORR in metastatic cSCC.
btd_threshold_vs_soc: ORR ≥50% with CR ≥10% and DoR ≥6 months in PD-L1+ advanced cSCC.

symptoms: Rapidly growing keratin-filled nodule on sun-exposed skin, spontaneous regression
treatment_goal: Complete excision; distinguish from SCC; prevent local tissue destruction
first_line_soc: Surgical excision or Mohs surgery (first-line for diagnosis and treatment). No FDA-approved systemic pharmacologic SoC; classified on spectrum with SCC.
treatment_algorithm: Excisional biopsy (diagnostic and therapeutic). For multiple/eruptive KA (Ferguson-Smith syndrome, Grzybowski): systemic retinoids (acitretin, isotretinoin) or methotrexate. Intralesional 5-FU or methotrexate for selected cases. Observation for classic solitary KA in poor surgical candidates.
us_prevalence: ~250,000–400,000 new KA diagnoses/year in US (often classified with SCC). Source: dermatology literature, SEER estimates.
patients_needing_tx: ~50,000–100,000 patients requiring pharmacologic treatment (systemic retinoids, intralesional therapy) for multiple/aggressive KA.
price: $20,000–$80,000/year for a novel systemic anti-KA agent.
peak_sales: $50–100M at 5% share; most KA managed surgically.
registrational_endpoint: Rate of complete clearance and time to resolution vs. observation in high-risk KA patients.

symptoms: Pearly skin nodule, central ulceration, telangiectasia on sun-exposed areas
treatment_goal: Achieve complete tumor removal; prevent recurrence in high-risk/advanced disease
first_line_soc: Surgery (Mohs or excision) or radiation for localized disease. Advanced/metastatic BCC: hedgehog pathway inhibitor vismodegib (Erivedge, 150 mg/day) or sonidegib (Odomzo, 200 mg/day).
treatment_algorithm: Localized: Mohs surgery (>99% cure), excision, radiation, topical imiquimod/5-FU for superficial BCC. Advanced/metastatic: vismodegib or sonidegib (first-line); cemiplimab (Libtayo, PD-1 inhibitor) for hedgehog-refractory/intolerant. Gorlin syndrome: continuous SMO inhibitor.
us_prevalence: ~3–4 million new BCC diagnoses/year (most common human cancer); ~10,000–20,000 with advanced/metastatic requiring systemic therapy. Source: ACS, Skin Cancer Foundation.
patients_needing_tx: ~15,000–25,000 patients/year with advanced BCC needing systemic hedgehog inhibitors or immunotherapy.
price: $80,000–$150,000/year; vismodegib WAC ~$100,000/year.
peak_sales: $300–600M at 20–30% share for a next-gen SMO inhibitor or combo; cemiplimab in BCC ~$200M/year.
registrational_endpoint: Objective response rate (ORR) and duration of response (DoR) in advanced/metastatic BCC; cemiplimab showed 31% ORR in vismodegib-refractory patients.
btd_threshold_vs_soc: ORR ≥30% with DoR >6 months in hedgehog inhibitor-refractory advanced BCC.

symptoms: Erythematous scaly patch (Bowen's disease), persistent on sun-exposed skin
treatment_goal: Eradicate in-situ lesion; prevent progression to invasive SCC
first_line_soc: Topical 5-fluorouracil (5-FU) cream 5% applied BID × 2–4 weeks, or topical imiquimod 5% cream 3–5× weekly × 16 weeks. Cryotherapy or curettage + electrodesiccation also standard.
treatment_algorithm: First-line: topical 5-FU or imiquimod. PDT (photodynamic therapy with 5-ALA or MAL) for extensive disease. Cryotherapy for isolated lesions. Surgical excision for persistent/recurrent. Radiation for inoperable. Nicotinamide (B3) 500 mg BID reduces incidence in high-risk patients.
us_prevalence: ~200,000–300,000 new SCC in situ (Bowen's) diagnoses/year in US. Source: Dermatology literature, SEER.
patients_needing_tx: ~150,000–250,000 patients/year needing pharmacologic topical treatment.
price: $2,000–$10,000/course for a novel topical or intralesional agent.
peak_sales: $50–150M at 10% share for a novel topical SCC-in-situ agent.
registrational_endpoint: Complete clearance rate at 12 weeks vs. vehicle cream or cryotherapy; imiquimod achieves ~73% complete clearance.
btd_threshold_vs_soc: Complete clearance rate ≥85% at 12 weeks vs. ~73% for imiquimod in SCC in situ.

symptoms: Eczema-like nipple changes, erosion, discharge, underlying breast mass
treatment_goal: Complete surgical excision; treat underlying breast cancer
first_line_soc: Surgery (mastectomy or breast-conserving surgery + radiation) depending on underlying DCIS/invasive cancer. Systemic therapy per underlying breast cancer (HR+: endocrine therapy; HER2+: trastuzumab-based). No pharmacologic SoC specific to Paget disease itself.
treatment_algorithm: Surgical resection of nipple-areolar complex. Systemic treatment dictated by co-existing invasive breast cancer (see Breast Cancer SoC). Topical imiquimod under investigation for in-situ disease. Radiation for breast-conserving approach.
us_prevalence: ~1–4% of all breast cancers; ~3,000–12,000 new US cases/year. Source: SEER, ACS.
patients_needing_tx: ~2,000–5,000 patients/year requiring systemic therapy for co-existing invasive breast cancer.
price: $100,000–$250,000/year (same as breast cancer systemic therapies).
registrational_endpoint: iDFS or pathologic complete response (pCR) per underlying breast cancer biology; or ORR for topical agent in isolated nipple Paget disease.
btd_threshold_vs_soc: pCR ≥40% in HER2+ Paget disease with neoadjuvant anti-HER2 therapy.

symptoms: Violaceous skin lesions, lymphedema, GI/pulmonary involvement in advanced disease
treatment_goal: Control lesions; preserve immune function; treat underlying HIV
first_line_soc: AIDS-KS: antiretroviral therapy (ART) as cornerstone; liposomal doxorubicin (Doxil/Caelyx, 20 mg/m² q3w) for symptomatic/advanced disease. Non-AIDS KS: observation or local therapy for indolent; systemic chemotherapy for aggressive.
treatment_algorithm: AIDS-KS: ART mandatory; local: intralesional vinblastine, radiation, cryotherapy; systemic: liposomal doxorubicin (first-line) or paclitaxel (second-line). Pomalidomide (Pomalyst) FDA-approved in 2020 for AIDS-related KS. Immunosuppression-related KS: reduce immunosuppression. Classic KS: local therapy. Endemic African KS: chemotherapy. HHV-8 targeted therapies in development.
us_prevalence: ~6,000–8,000 active US KS cases (mainly HIV/AIDS-related and transplant-related); ~3,000 new cases/year. Source: CDC, SEER.
patients_needing_tx: ~4,000–6,000 patients needing systemic therapy.
price: $80,000–$200,000/year; pomalidomide in KS ~$200,000/year WAC.
peak_sales: $100–200M at 20–30% share for a novel HHV-8 targeted or immunomodulatory agent.
registrational_endpoint: ORR (complete + partial response by modified AIDS Clinical Trials Group criteria) ≥50% at 16 weeks.
btd_threshold_vs_soc: ORR ≥50% with CR rate ≥20% in AIDS-related KS vs. ~30% ORR for liposomal doxorubicin.

symptoms: Rapidly growing painless flesh-colored nodule, lymphadenopathy
treatment_goal: Achieve complete remission; prevent rapid spread; long-term response
first_line_soc: Localized: surgery + adjuvant radiation. Advanced/metastatic: avelumab (Bavencio, PD-L1 inhibitor) — FDA-approved 2017 — or pembrolizumab (FDA-approved 2018) as first-line.
treatment_algorithm: Localized: wide excision + sentinel node biopsy + adjuvant radiation. Metastatic: avelumab or pembrolizumab (PD-1/L1 checkpoint). Second-line: chemotherapy (etoposide + carboplatin or cisplatin; topotecan) — short-lived responses. Clinical trials preferred for second-line. CAR-T and bispecifics in development.
us_prevalence: ~3,000–4,000 new US cases/year; ~12,000–15,000 prevalent cases. Source: SEER, NCI.
patients_needing_tx: ~2,500–4,000 patients/year with advanced/metastatic MCC requiring systemic therapy.
peak_sales: $150–250M at 30–40% share (small indication, high per-patient price); Bavencio in MCC ~$200M/year benchmark.
registrational_endpoint: Durable ORR (≥6 months) and OS; FDA granted accelerated approval to avelumab based on 33% ORR with 30% CR rate.
btd_threshold_vs_soc: ORR ≥40% with CR ≥20% and DoR ≥6 months vs. ~25% ORR for chemotherapy.

symptoms: Rapidly growing ulcerating nodule on sun-damaged skin of elderly
treatment_goal: Complete surgical excision; prevent local recurrence
first_line_soc: Mohs micrographic surgery or wide local excision with clear margins. No systemic pharmacologic SoC. No FDA-approved drug specifically for AFX.
treatment_algorithm: Mohs surgery first-line for optimal margin control. Standard excision with 1 cm margins if Mohs unavailable. Radiation therapy for inoperable cases. Rare metastatic AFX may be treated with checkpoint inhibitors (pembrolizumab, nivolumab) given high mutation burden, though off-label.
us_prevalence: ~300–500 new US cases/year; extremely rare. Source: dermatology literature, SEER.
patients_needing_tx: ~50–100 patients with inoperable/metastatic AFX potentially needing systemic therapy.
price: $150,000–$250,000/year for a checkpoint inhibitor used off-label or in basket trial.
registrational_endpoint: ORR ≥30% in metastatic AFX in a tumor-agnostic basket trial endpoint.
btd_threshold_vs_soc: ORR ≥30% with durable responses ≥6 months in metastatic AFX patients with high TMB.

disease: Gastrointestinal Neuroendocrine Tumors and Carcinoid Syndrome
symptoms: Flushing, diarrhea, wheezing, carcinoid heart disease (serotonin-mediated)
treatment_goal: Control carcinoid syndrome symptoms; reduce tumor burden; prevent cardiac damage
first_line_soc: Octreotide LAR (somatostatin analogue, 20–30 mg IM q28d) for symptom control and antiproliferative effect (PROMID trial). Lanreotide (Somatuline Depot, 90–120 mg SC q28d) — FDA-approved for control of GI NETs.
treatment_algorithm: SSA (octreotide LAR or lanreotide) for functional tumors and SSTR2-positive. Tumor progression on SSA: everolimus (RADIANT-4); PRRT with lutetium-177 DOTATATE (Lutathera, FDA-approved 2018) for SSTR2-positive midgut NETs. Telotristat ethyl (Xermelo, FDA-approved 2017) for carcinoid syndrome diarrhea refractory to SSA. Surgery for resectable primary/hepatic metastases. Embolization (TACE/RFA) for hepatic-dominant disease.
us_prevalence: ~170,000 US patients with GI NETs (all types); ~50,000 with midgut/carcinoid NETs and functional syndrome; ~20,000–30,000 new GI NET diagnoses/year. Source: SEER, NCI.
patients_needing_tx: ~80,000–120,000 patients on active systemic therapy for GI NETs.
price: $80,000–$250,000/year; Lutathera (PRRT) ~$130,000/2-cycle course; Xermelo ~$150,000/year.
peak_sales: $500M–$1.5B at 5–15% share for a novel NET agent; Lutathera ~$300M/year.
registrational_endpoint: PFS (RADIANT trials) or OS; for carcinoid diarrhea: reduction in bowel movements/day vs. SSA alone (Xermelo registration endpoint).
btd_threshold_vs_soc: Median PFS ≥22 months vs. ~14 months for SSA monotherapy in SSTR2+ midgut NETs.

symptoms: Severe peptic ulcers, diarrhea, abdominal pain (Zollinger-Ellison syndrome)
treatment_goal: Control gastric acid hypersecretion; control tumor growth; achieve surgical cure
first_line_soc: High-dose proton pump inhibitor (omeprazole 40–60 mg BID or lansoprazole 30–90 mg BID) for acid control. Surgical resection for localized sporadic gastrinoma. Somatostatin analogues (octreotide LAR or lanreotide) for metastatic disease.
treatment_algorithm: Antisecretory: high-dose PPI lifelong. Curative surgery for localized gastrinoma (MEN1-associated: more complex). Metastatic disease: SSA (octreotide LAR 20–30 mg q28d; lanreotide 90–120 mg q28d); everolimus (Afinitor, mTOR inhibitor) or sunitinib; PRRT (lutetium-177 DOTATATE [Lutathera]) for SSTR2+ tumors. Streptozocin + 5-FU for high-grade.
us_prevalence: ~3,000–5,000 US patients with gastrinoma (0.5–2 cases/million/year); ~60% sporadic, ~30% MEN1-associated. Source: NIH, NCI SEER.
patients_needing_tx: ~2,000–4,000 patients needing systemic pharmacologic therapy for unresectable/metastatic gastrinoma.
price: $60,000–$200,000/year for SSAs or targeted agents.
peak_sales: $50–100M at 20–30% share for a novel targeted gastrinoma agent.
registrational_endpoint: PFS vs. placebo in somatostatin receptor-positive unresectable/metastatic gastrinoma at 24 months.
btd_threshold_vs_soc: Median PFS ≥18 months vs. ~12 months for SSA monotherapy.

symptoms: Necrolytic migratory erythema, diabetes, weight loss, DVT, anemia
treatment_goal: Reduce glucagon levels; control skin lesions; achieve surgical cure
first_line_soc: Somatostatin analogues (octreotide LAR or lanreotide) for symptom control and glucagon suppression. Surgical resection for localized disease. Zinc supplementation for skin lesions.
treatment_algorithm: Symptomatic control: SSA (octreotide LAR). DVT prophylaxis mandatory. Surgical resection if localized. Unresectable/metastatic: SSA ± everolimus or sunitinib. PRRT (lutetium-177 DOTATATE) for SSTR2+. Streptozocin-based chemotherapy. Nutritional support for cachexia. Zinc/amino acid supplementation for skin lesion management.
us_prevalence: ~200–300 new US cases/year (1 in 20 million); total prevalent ~500–1,000. Source: NORD, SEER.
patients_needing_tx: ~300–600 patients needing systemic therapy.
price: $80,000–$200,000/year for novel pNET-targeted agents.
peak_sales: $15–30M at 30% share; subsumed within pancreatic NET franchise.
registrational_endpoint: PFS in basket pNET trial; tumor response (ORR) and glucagon normalization as secondary endpoints.
btd_threshold_vs_soc: Median PFS ≥18 months vs. ~12 months for SSA in metastatic glucagonoma.

symptoms: Profuse watery diarrhea, hypokalemia, achlorhydria (WDHA/Verner-Morrison syndrome)
treatment_goal: Control diarrhea; correct electrolyte imbalance; resect tumor
first_line_soc: IV fluid and electrolyte replacement urgently. Octreotide (subcutaneous or LAR) or lanreotide for immediate VIP suppression and diarrhea control. Surgical resection for localized disease.
treatment_algorithm: Urgent IV rehydration + electrolytes. Octreotide/lanreotide for symptom control. Resection if localized. Malignant/unresectable: SSA + everolimus or streptozocin-based chemo or PRRT. IFN-alpha as adjunct. Continuous monitoring of potassium, bicarbonate.
us_prevalence: ~200–300 new US cases/year; ~500–800 prevalent. Extremely rare. Source: NORD, SEER.
patients_needing_tx: ~300–500 patients needing systemic pharmacologic therapy.
price: $80,000–$200,000/year for novel VIP-receptor targeted agents.
peak_sales: $10–20M at 25% share; subsumed in pancreatic NET franchise.
registrational_endpoint: ≥50% reduction in stool output per day vs. baseline at 4 weeks; normalization of serum VIP.
btd_threshold_vs_soc: ≥70% reduction in daily stool volume vs. ~50% with SSA alone.

symptoms: Recurrent hypoglycemia, confusion, sweating, palpitations, seizures
treatment_goal: Normalize blood glucose; achieve curative surgical resection
first_line_soc: Surgery (enucleation or distal pancreatectomy) is curative in >90% of benign insulinomas. Medical: diazoxide (opens K-ATP channel, 50–300 mg/day) for inoperable cases. Octreotide/lanreotide as adjunct.
treatment_algorithm: Surgery first-line (>90% benign solitary tumors). Inoperable/malignant: diazoxide 200–400 mg/day for hypoglycemia control; octreotide LAR; everolimus (approved for PNET malignant insulinoma). Streptozocin-based chemotherapy for malignant. PRRT for SSTR2+. Continuous glucose monitoring. MEN1-associated: surgical approach based on tumor size/location.
us_prevalence: ~400–500 new US cases/year; ~90% benign; ~1,000–2,000 prevalent. Source: SEER, endocrinology literature.
patients_needing_tx: ~400–600 patients/year needing medical therapy (inoperable or malignant).
price: $50,000–$150,000/year for novel targeted agents.
peak_sales: $10–20M at 25% share; subsumed in pNET franchise.
registrational_endpoint: Normalization of fasting blood glucose and ≥50% reduction in hypoglycemic episodes at 12 weeks vs. diazoxide.
btd_threshold_vs_soc: ≥80% reduction in hypoglycemic episodes vs. ~50% with diazoxide.

symptoms: Urinary frequency, weak stream, hematuria, bone pain in advanced disease
treatment_goal: Achieve PSA undetectability; delay castration resistance; extend OS
first_line_soc: Localized: active surveillance, radiation (EBRT/brachytherapy), or radical prostatectomy. Metastatic hormone-sensitive (mHSPC): ADT + novel AR-pathway inhibitor (enzalutamide, apalutamide, darolutamide). Metastatic castration-resistant (mCRPC): enzalutamide or abiraterone (first-line); docetaxel for high-volume/aggressive.
treatment_algorithm: Non-metastatic CRPC: enzalutamide, apalutamide, or darolutamide + ADT. mCRPC: abiraterone + prednisone; enzalutamide; lutetium-177-PSMA-617 (Pluvicto, FDA 2022) for PSMA-positive; olaparib/rucaparib for BRCA1/2; docetaxel; cabazitaxel; sipuleucel-T (immunotherapy); radium-223 for bone metastases. PARP inhibitors combo with ARi (niraparib + abiraterone FDA 2023). Pembrolizumab for MSI-H/MMR-deficient.
us_prevalence: ~3.3 million US men living with prostate cancer; ~300,000 new cases/year; ~35,000 deaths/year. Source: ACS 2024, SEER.
patients_needing_tx: ~500,000–700,000 patients on active systemic therapy; ~100,000–150,000 with mCRPC; ~100,000 with mHSPC.
price: $100,000–$200,000/year for novel ARi/PARP; Pluvicto ~$42,000/dose × 6 = ~$250,000/course.
registrational_endpoint: OS in mCRPC (primary for most approvals); rPFS (radiographic PFS) in nmCRPC/mHSPC. FDA co-primary: rPFS + OS.
btd_threshold_vs_soc: OS HR ≤0.72 in mCRPC vs. enzalutamide/abiraterone SoC; rPFS HR ≤0.65 in nmCRPC.

symptoms: Painless hematuria, frequency, dysuria, pelvic pain in advanced disease
treatment_goal: Achieve complete response/preserve bladder; prevent muscle invasion; extend OS in advanced
first_line_soc: Non-muscle-invasive (NMIBC): TURBT + intravesical BCG (Bacillus Calmette-Guérin, 81 mg/dose induction + maintenance). Muscle-invasive (MIBC): neoadjuvant cisplatin-based chemotherapy (MVAC or gemcitabine/cisplatin) + radical cystectomy. Metastatic: gemcitabine + cisplatin (GC) or MVAC. PD-L1-high: pembrolizumab or atezolizumab.
treatment_algorithm: NMIBC high-risk: BCG induction + maintenance × 3 years; pembrolizumab (Keytruda) FDA-approved 2020 for BCG-unresponsive NMIBC. BCG-unresponsive: nadofaragene firadenovec (gene therapy, Adstiladrin, FDA 2022). MIBC: cystectomy ± neoadjuvant GC; adjuvant nivolumab for PD-L1+ (CheckMate-274). Metastatic 1L: enfortumab vedotin + pembrolizumab (FDA 2024, significant OS benefit vs GC). EV+pembro is new SoC for metastatic. 2L: enfortumab vedotin alone, sacituzumab govitecan, erdafitinib (FGFR3-mutant).
us_prevalence: ~900,000 US patients living with bladder cancer; ~85,000 new cases/year; ~18,000 deaths/year. Source: ACS 2024, SEER.
patients_needing_tx: ~200,000–300,000 patients on active systemic therapy (BCG, immunotherapy, chemo); ~40,000–50,000 with metastatic bladder cancer.
price: $150,000–$350,000/year for novel ADCs or combinations; enfortumab vedotin ~$300,000/year.
peak_sales: $2B–$5B at 5–15% share; EV+pembro approved 2024, likely $3B+ peak.
registrational_endpoint: OS and PFS in metastatic urothelial carcinoma (EV+pembro EV-302 study); complete response rate and disease-free survival in NMIBC.
btd_threshold_vs_soc: OS HR ≤0.65 vs. gemcitabine + cisplatin in metastatic urothelial carcinoma.

symptoms: Flank pain, hematuria, palpable mass, fatigue, weight loss
treatment_goal: Achieve complete/partial response; extend PFS and OS; maintain QoL
first_line_soc: Localized: partial or radical nephrectomy. Metastatic/advanced: nivolumab + ipilimumab (intermediate/poor risk) or pembrolizumab + axitinib (or lenvatinib + pembrolizumab) as first-line.
treatment_algorithm: Localized: surgery. Adjuvant: pembrolizumab (KEYNOTE-564, FDA 2021) for high-risk resected RCC. Metastatic 1L: nivo + ipi (favorable/intermediate/poor risk); pembro + axitinib or nivo + cabozantinib or lenvatinib + pembro. Second-line: cabozantinib, axitinib, sunitinib, pazopanib (VEGFR TKIs); nivolumab monotherapy. Non-clear-cell RCC: clinical trials preferred; cabozantinib active. Belzutifan (HIF-2α inhibitor, Welireg, FDA 2021) for VHL-mutant and now 2L+ ccRCC.
us_prevalence: ~420,000 US patients living with RCC; ~82,000 new cases/year; ~14,700 deaths/year. Source: ACS 2024, SEER.
patients_needing_tx: ~80,000–100,000 patients on active systemic therapy; ~40,000–50,000 with metastatic RCC.
price: $100,000–$250,000/year; nivo + ipi ~$250,000/year; belzutifan ~$180,000/year.
peak_sales: $1B–$3B at 10–20% share; Keytruda + Lenvima combo ~$2B+ in RCC.
registrational_endpoint: PFS and OS in metastatic RCC; DFS in adjuvant high-risk resected RCC. FDA co-primary: PFS + OS.
btd_threshold_vs_soc: OS HR ≤0.68 vs. SoC VEGFR TKI monotherapy in 1L metastatic RCC.

symptoms: Hematuria, flank pain, hydronephrosis, palpable mass
treatment_goal: Achieve radical nephroureterectomy; prevent local/distant recurrence
first_line_soc: Surgery (radical nephroureterectomy with bladder cuff). Adjuvant nivolumab (CheckMate-274, FDA 2021) for high-risk resected upper-tract urothelial carcinoma. Metastatic: same as urothelial bladder cancer — GC chemotherapy, then enfortumab vedotin + pembrolizumab.
treatment_algorithm: Localized: radical nephroureterectomy. Adjuvant: nivolumab (FDA-approved for MIBC/UTUC high-risk). Metastatic: GC or MVAC; second-line EV ± pembro; erdafitinib for FGFR3-mutant. Preservation protocols for solitary kidney cases.
us_prevalence: ~15,000 new US cases/year; ~30,000–40,000 prevalent (upper-tract urothelial carcinoma). Source: ACS, SEER.
patients_needing_tx: ~8,000–12,000 patients/year needing systemic therapy (adjuvant + metastatic).
price: $120,000–$250,000/year for novel agents.
registrational_endpoint: DFS in adjuvant resected UTUC; PFS/OS in metastatic UTUC (same endpoints as bladder cancer trials).
btd_threshold_vs_soc: DFS HR ≤0.70 vs. observation in high-risk resected UTUC.

symptoms: Penile lesion or ulcer, inguinal lymphadenopathy, pain, bleeding
treatment_goal: Achieve complete surgical resection; preserve penile function; control nodal disease
first_line_soc: Surgery (penile-sparing techniques or partial/total penectomy) for localized disease. Adjuvant chemotherapy for high-risk nodal disease: cisplatin + 5-FU or TIP (paclitaxel/ifosfamide/cisplatin).
treatment_algorithm: Localized: penile-sparing surgery (glans resurfacing, laser). Nodal disease: bilateral inguinal lymphadenectomy; adjuvant TIP chemotherapy. Advanced/metastatic: TIP (first-line); cisplatin + 5-FU + irinotecan; pembrolizumab for PD-L1+ tumors (off-label). EGFR inhibitors (cetuximab) show early activity. No FDA-approved drug specifically for penile cancer.
us_prevalence: ~2,200 new US cases/year; ~6,000–8,000 prevalent. Source: ACS, SEER.
patients_needing_tx: ~1,500–2,500 patients/year needing systemic pharmacologic therapy.
price: $100,000–$200,000/year for novel targeted or immunotherapy agent.
peak_sales: $30–70M at 15–25% share if FDA-approved for this specific indication.
registrational_endpoint: ORR ≥30% and PFS in metastatic penile cancer in a single-arm or randomized Phase 2 trial.
btd_threshold_vs_soc: ORR ≥40% with DoR ≥4 months in metastatic penile carcinoma refractory to platinum.

symptoms: Hematuria, urethral discharge, urethral obstruction, palpable mass
treatment_goal: Achieve complete excision; control locoregional spread
first_line_soc: Surgery (urethrectomy ± pelvic exenteration) for localized disease. Concurrent chemoradiation (cisplatin + radiation) for locally advanced. Systemic chemotherapy per urothelial or SCC histology.
treatment_algorithm: Anterior urethra: surgical excision. Posterior/bulbomembranous: radical resection or chemoradiation. Metastatic: cisplatin-based chemotherapy (GC or TIP); checkpoint inhibitors for PD-L1+ tumors (off-label pembrolizumab). No FDA-approved drug specifically for urethral cancer.
us_prevalence: ~2,000 new US cases/year; ~5,000–6,000 prevalent. Rare cancer. Source: SEER.
patients_needing_tx: ~1,000–2,000 patients/year needing systemic therapy.
peak_sales: $20–50M at 15–25% share in basket urothelial/SCC trial.
registrational_endpoint: ORR and PFS in advanced urethral cancer; treated as rare urothelial or SCC in basket trial.
btd_threshold_vs_soc: ORR ≥35% in advanced urethral carcinoma.

symptoms: Painless testicular mass, scrotal heaviness, gynecomastia, back pain
treatment_goal: Achieve complete remission; cure; preserve fertility
first_line_soc: Orchiectomy (diagnostic and therapeutic). Stage I: active surveillance or adjuvant chemotherapy (1× BEP). Metastatic seminoma/NSGCT: BEP (bleomycin/etoposide/cisplatin) × 3–4 cycles (gold-standard cure rate >80% even in advanced).
treatment_algorithm: Radical orchiectomy first for all. Good-risk metastatic: BEP × 3 cycles (>95% cure). Intermediate/poor risk: BEP × 4 cycles or VIP. Relapsed: salvage TIP (paclitaxel/ifosfamide/cisplatin) or VeIP; high-dose chemo + ASCT. Pembrolizumab for cisplatin-refractory (Phase 2 data). Sperm banking before chemotherapy. RPLND for residual masses.
us_prevalence: ~260,000 US survivors; ~9,800 new cases/year; ~470 deaths/year (very high cure rate). Source: ACS 2024, SEER.
patients_needing_tx: ~7,000–9,000 patients/year requiring systemic BEP; ~1,500–2,000 with relapsed/refractory disease.
price: $50,000–$150,000/year for novel second-line agents.
registrational_endpoint: PFS or OS in relapsed/refractory GCT (prior cisplatin-based chemotherapy); complete response rate as secondary.
btd_threshold_vs_soc: OS HR ≤0.70 or CR rate ≥30% vs. salvage VIP/TIP in cisplatin-refractory GCT.

symptoms: Hematuria, flank pain; symptoms vary by primary tumor type
treatment_goal: Control systemic disease; preserve renal function
first_line_soc: Treatment of the primary cancer per origin (lung, breast, colon, etc.) drives systemic therapy. Nephrectomy for solitary symptomatic renal metastasis. Local ablation (RFA, cryoablation) for selected cases.
treatment_algorithm: Systemic therapy per primary tumor. Metastasectomy for solitary/oligometastatic renal involvement. No specific pharmacologic SoC uniquely for renal metastases. Radiotherapy for palliation. Kidney function preservation during chemotherapy.
us_prevalence: ~10,000–15,000 US patients with documented renal metastases from various primary cancers. Source: SEER, urology literature.
patients_needing_tx: Managed within primary cancer treatment paradigm.
price: Priced per primary cancer indication.
peak_sales: N/A as standalone indication; commercial opportunity embedded in systemic oncology.
registrational_endpoint: N/A (treated per primary tumor type).

disease: Ovarian, Fallopian Tube, and Peritoneal Cancer
symptoms: Abdominal bloating, pelvic pain, early satiety, urinary frequency
treatment_goal: Achieve complete surgical cytoreduction; prolong PFS and OS
first_line_soc: Optimal debulking surgery + carboplatin + paclitaxel × 6 cycles. BRCA1/2-mutant: add olaparib (PARP inhibitor) as maintenance (SOLO-1). HRD+: niraparib or olaparib maintenance after first-line. Bevacizumab for high-risk stage III/IV (GOG-218).
treatment_algorithm: Standard: carboplatin/paclitaxel ± bevacizumab. BRCA1/2: olaparib maintenance (FDA 2018). HRD+: niraparib or rucaparib maintenance. Second-line: PARP inhibitor (if not previously used); trabectedin; weekly paclitaxel; topotecan; pegylated liposomal doxorubicin. Platinum-sensitive recurrence: carboplatin-based re-challenge.
