Mirvetuximab soravtansine (ADC, FDA 2022) for FRα-high platinum-resistant. Dostarlimab for dMMR. Antiangiogenic (bevacizumab) across lines.
us_prevalence: ~225,000 US women living with ovarian cancer; ~19,680 new cases/year; ~12,700 deaths/year. Source: ACS 2024.
patients_needing_tx: ~50,000–70,000 patients on active therapy; ~30,000 platinum-resistant.
price: $150,000–$300,000/year; olaparib ~$180,000/year; mirvetuximab ~$200,000/year.
registrational_endpoint: PFS (maintenance setting, co-primary with OS); OS in frontline (co-primary for some trials). FDA standard: PFS ≥2 months improvement with acceptable toxicity in platinum-sensitive recurrence.
btd_threshold_vs_soc: PFS HR ≤0.50 in BRCA-mutant platinum-sensitive recurrence vs. placebo maintenance.

symptoms: Abnormal uterine bleeding, postmenopausal bleeding, pelvic pain, mass
treatment_goal: Achieve surgical cure; prevent recurrence; extend OS in advanced disease
first_line_soc: Surgery (total hysterectomy + bilateral salpingo-oophorectomy ± staging). Adjuvant: based on stage — observation, brachytherapy, EBRT, or chemotherapy (carboplatin + paclitaxel). Advanced/recurrent: carboplatin + paclitaxel + pembrolizumab (KEYNOTE-868); or lenvatinib + pembrolizumab.
treatment_algorithm: Stage I: surgery ± adjuvant brachytherapy. High-risk/advanced: carboplatin + paclitaxel ± pembrolizumab (FDA 2023 for pMMR/MSS and dMMR) or dostarlimab + carboplatin/paclitaxel. Second-line: lenvatinib + pembrolizumab (FDA 2019, KEYNOTE-146); hormonal therapy (medroxyprogesterone acetate, megestrol) for grade 1–2 ER+; PARP inhibitors for BRCA/HRD; trastuzumab + chemo for HER2+. Selinexor for advanced endometrial (FDA 2023).
us_prevalence: ~860,000 US women living with endometrial cancer; ~68,000 new cases/year; ~13,000 deaths/year. Source: ACS 2024.
patients_needing_tx: ~40,000–60,000 patients/year on active systemic therapy.
price: $150,000–$300,000/year; lenvatinib + pembro combo ~$280,000/year.
peak_sales: $1B–$2.5B at 10–15% share; Keytruda in endometrial ~$1B+ peak estimate.
registrational_endpoint: PFS and OS in advanced/recurrent endometrial cancer vs. carboplatin/paclitaxel alone (KEYNOTE-868, RUBY co-primary endpoints).
btd_threshold_vs_soc: PFS HR ≤0.60 vs. carboplatin/paclitaxel in dMMR endometrial cancer.

symptoms: Vaginal bleeding, discharge, pelvic pain, back pain in advanced disease
treatment_goal: Achieve cure with radiation/surgery; extend OS in metastatic disease
first_line_soc: Localized: concurrent cisplatin + radiation (chemoradiation) is definitive SoC for stages IB2–IVA. Early stage (IA–IB1): conization or radical hysterectomy. Persistent/recurrent: cisplatin + paclitaxel + bevacizumab. Pembrolizumab for PD-L1+ recurrent/metastatic (KEYNOTE-826).
treatment_algorithm: Stages I–IIA: surgery or radiation ± cisplatin. Stages IIB–IVA: concurrent cisplatin + RT. Recurrent/metastatic: carboplatin/paclitaxel/bevacizumab ± pembrolizumab (FDA 2021 for CPS≥1). Tisotumab vedotin (Tivdak, HER2-targeting ADC, FDA 2021/2023 for 2L) for cervical SCC/adenocarcinoma. NTRK fusions: larotrectinib/entrectinib.
us_prevalence: ~250,000 US women living with cervical cancer; ~13,800 new cases/year; ~4,400 deaths/year. Source: ACS 2024.
patients_needing_tx: ~15,000–25,000 patients/year on active systemic therapy (advanced/recurrent).
price: $150,000–$250,000/year; tisotumab vedotin ~$130,000/year.
registrational_endpoint: OS in persistent/recurrent cervical cancer (KEYNOTE-826 co-primary: OS + PFS); ORR + DoR for accelerated approval.
btd_threshold_vs_soc: OS HR ≤0.73 vs. chemotherapy ± bevacizumab in recurrent PD-L1+ cervical cancer.

symptoms: Abnormal uterine bleeding, pelvic pain, rapidly growing uterine mass
treatment_goal: Achieve complete surgical resection; control metastatic disease
first_line_soc: Surgery (hysterectomy ± BSO ± lymphadenectomy). Adjuvant gemcitabine + docetaxel for high-risk uterine leiomyosarcoma. Metastatic: doxorubicin alone or gemcitabine + docetaxel.
treatment_algorithm: Resectable: total hysterectomy ± adjuvant chemo/radiation. Metastatic LMS: gemcitabine + docetaxel; doxorubicin; trabectedin; pazopanib; eribulin. ESS (low-grade): hormonal therapy (aromatase inhibitors); ESS (high-grade): doxorubicin-based. Olaratumab trial results disappointing. No FDA-approved drug specifically for uterine sarcoma.
us_prevalence: ~6,000–7,000 new US cases/year; ~15,000–20,000 prevalent. Source: SEER, SGO.
patients_needing_tx: ~5,000–8,000 patients/year needing systemic therapy.
price: $80,000–$200,000/year for novel sarcoma agents.
peak_sales: $100–200M at 15–25% share for a novel LMS-specific agent.
registrational_endpoint: PFS vs. gemcitabine + docetaxel in metastatic leiomyosarcoma (FDA standard: median PFS improvement ≥1.5–2 months).
btd_threshold_vs_soc: PFS HR ≤0.70 vs. gemcitabine + docetaxel in metastatic uterine LMS.

symptoms: Vulvar pruritus, lesion, bleeding, inguinal lymphadenopathy
treatment_goal: Achieve wide local excision with sentinel node assessment; prevent recurrence
first_line_soc: Surgery (wide local excision ± sentinel lymph node biopsy; radical vulvectomy for advanced disease). Adjuvant concurrent cisplatin + radiation for positive margins or nodes. Unresectable: chemoradiation as primary.
treatment_algorithm: Early stage: wide local excision. Advanced/recurrent: cisplatin + 5-FU or paclitaxel-based chemotherapy; concurrent chemoradiation. Pembrolizumab for PD-L1+/MSI-H recurrent vulvar cancer (off-label). Cemiplimab showing activity in HPV+/HPV- vulvar SCC. No FDA-approved agent specifically for vulvar cancer.
us_prevalence: ~6,470 new US cases/year; ~20,000–25,000 prevalent. Source: ACS 2024, SEER.
patients_needing_tx: ~3,000–5,000 patients/year on systemic therapy for advanced/recurrent disease.
peak_sales: $60–120M at 15–20% share in vulvar or HPV+ tumor basket.
registrational_endpoint: ORR and PFS in recurrent/metastatic vulvar SCC, or EFS in high-risk localized disease.
btd_threshold_vs_soc: ORR ≥35% with DoR ≥6 months in recurrent vulvar SCC after platinum failure.

symptoms: Vaginal bleeding, discharge, pelvic pain, dyspareunia
treatment_goal: Achieve local tumor control; cure with radiation in early stage
first_line_soc: Radiation therapy (brachytherapy ± EBRT ± concurrent cisplatin) is the primary treatment. Surgery for early-stage disease (Stage I). No FDA-approved drug specifically for vaginal cancer.
treatment_algorithm: Stage I: surgery or radiation. Stages II–IVA: concurrent cisplatin + radiation. Metastatic/recurrent: chemotherapy (cisplatin/paclitaxel/5-FU); pembrolizumab for PD-L1+ (off-label). No regulatory-approved systemic agent specifically for vaginal cancer.
us_prevalence: ~1,000–1,500 new US cases/year; ~4,000 prevalent. Source: SEER, ACS.
patients_needing_tx: ~800–1,200 patients/year needing systemic therapy for advanced/recurrent disease.
peak_sales: $20–40M at 20% share in basket gynecologic/HPV-driven cancer trial.
registrational_endpoint: ORR ≥30% and PFS in recurrent/metastatic vaginal cancer in basket HPV+ gynecologic cancer trial.
btd_threshold_vs_soc: ORR ≥40% with DoR ≥6 months in recurrent vaginal cancer.

symptoms: Abnormal vaginal bleeding, elevated hCG, pelvic mass, hyperemesis
treatment_goal: Achieve hCG normalization (cure); preserve fertility
first_line_soc: Low-risk GTN: single-agent methotrexate (MTX 30–50 mg/m² IM weekly) or actinomycin D. High-risk GTN: EMA-CO (etoposide/methotrexate/actinomycin-D/cyclophosphamide/vincristine) — >90% cure rate.
treatment_algorithm: Molar pregnancy: uterine evacuation. Low-risk GTN (WHO score 0–6): MTX or actinomycin D. High-risk GTN (WHO ≥7): EMA-CO; EMACO resistance: EMA-EP (etoposide/platinum). Placental-site trophoblastic tumors: EP-EMA or surgery. hCG surveillance post-treatment. Checkpoint inhibitors (pembrolizumab) for heavily pretreated disease.
us_prevalence: ~3,000–4,000 new GTN diagnoses/year; near-universal cure rate (>95%). Source: SGO, NCI.
patients_needing_tx: ~3,000–4,000 patients/year; most cured with chemotherapy. ~200–400 with high-risk/ultra-high-risk disease.
price: $50,000–$150,000/year for novel agents in refractory disease.
registrational_endpoint: hCG normalization rate and event-free survival in high-risk GTN vs. EMA-CO at 6 months.
btd_threshold_vs_soc: hCG normalization ≥80% at 6 months vs. ~70% with EMA-CO in high-risk GTN.

symptoms: Headache, seizures, cognitive changes, neurologic deficits, fatigue
treatment_goal: Maximize safe resection; extend PFS and OS; preserve neurologic function
first_line_soc: Glioblastoma (GBM): surgery + temozolomide (TMZ, 75 mg/m² concurrent with RT) + adjuvant TMZ × 6 cycles (Stupp regimen). IDH-mutant glioma (grade 2–3): vorasidenib (Voranigo, IDH1/2 inhibitor, FDA 2024) for grade 2 IDH-mutant glioma. TTFields (Optune device) as add-on for GBM.
treatment_algorithm: GBM: maximal safe resection + Stupp (RT + TMZ) + bevacizumab (for recurrent) + tumor-treating fields (EF-14 data). IDH-mutant grade 2: watch-and-wait or vorasidenib (FDA 2024 approval based on INDIGO trial). IDH-mutant grade 3: RT + PCV (procarbazine/CCNU/vincristine) or TMZ; CCNU (lomustine) alone or with TMZ. IDH-wild-type: aggressive treatment. Recurrent GBM: bevacizumab, lomustine, clinical trials (PARP inhibitors, CAR-T, oncolytic virus).
us_prevalence: ~130,000 US patients with primary brain tumors; ~15,000 new GBM/year; ~20,000–25,000 new glioma (all grades)/year. Source: CBTRUS, NCI.
patients_needing_tx: ~100,000–120,000 glioma patients on active therapy; ~50,000 with GBM; ~25,000–30,000 IDH-mutant glioma (grades 2–3).
price: $80,000–$200,000/year; vorasidenib ~$72,000/year.
peak_sales: $500M–$2B at 5–15% share for a novel GBM/glioma agent; vorasidenib peak sales est $500M+.
registrational_endpoint: PFS in high-grade glioma and OS in GBM. Vorasidenib registration: PFS HR 0.39 vs. placebo (INDIGO). FDA standard for GBM: OS improvement.
btd_threshold_vs_soc: PFS HR ≤0.60 vs. TMZ in newly diagnosed GBM; OS HR ≤0.70 vs. Stupp protocol.

symptoms: Headaches, seizures, weakness, personality changes, vision problems
treatment_goal: Maximize tumor control, preserve neurologic function, extend survival
first_line_soc: Surgery (maximal safe resection) + radiation + temozolomide (Temodar) for high-grade (Grade 3–4); IDH-mutant low-grade: watch-and-wait or RT ± PCV chemotherapy.
treatment_algorithm: Grade 2/3 IDH-mutant: radiation + PCV (procarbazine/lomustine/vincristine) or temozolomide. GBM (Grade 4): Stupp protocol (temozolomide + RT) + tumor treating fields (Optune). IDH1-mut: vorasidenib (Voranigo, FDA Aug 2024) for Grade 2 IDH-mut glioma. Bevacizumab (Avastin) for recurrent. MGMT-methylated benefit from temozolomide. BRAF V600E: dabrafenib+trametinib.
us_prevalence: ~95,000 primary brain tumors/year; astrocytomas ~23,000 new cases/year; prevalence ~70,000. GBM: ~13,000 new/year.
patients_needing_tx: ~22,000/year need pharmacologic therapy (chemo ± targeted); ~10,000 prevalent low-grade on active treatment.
price: $175,000–$250,000/year (vorasidenib WAC ~$225,000; temozolomide ~$30K generic). Novel IDH-mut: $225,000.
registrational_endpoint: Progression-free survival (PFS) by RANO criteria for IDH-mutant glioma
btd_threshold_vs_soc: PFS HR <0.61 vs. radiotherapy alone (vorasidenib INDIGO trial: HR 0.39)

symptoms: Headache, visual field defects, hormonal excess (acromegaly, Cushing's), hormonal deficiency
treatment_goal: Normalize hormone levels; decompress optic apparatus; prevent recurrence
first_line_soc: Prolactinoma: cabergoline (dopamine agonist, 0.25–1 mg twice weekly) — first-line medical therapy. Acromegaly: surgery first; then somatostatin analogues (octreotide LAR or lanreotide) or pegvisomant. Cushing's disease: transsphenoidal surgery; then pasireotide or mifepristone or osilodrostat.
treatment_algorithm: Prolactinoma: cabergoline > bromocriptine; surgery for drug failure. Acromegaly: surgery; SSA (octreotide LAR, lanreotide); pegvisomant (GH receptor antagonist); pasireotide; combination SSA + pegvisomant. Cushing's: pituitary surgery; pasireotide (FDA 2012); osilodrostat (FDA 2020); mifepristone (FDA 2012); metyrapone, ketoconazole for bridge. Non-functioning adenoma: surgery + surveillance. Temozolomide for aggressive pituitary carcinomas.
us_prevalence: ~90,000–100,000 US patients with clinically recognized pituitary adenomas; estimated 1-in-1,000 prevalence. Source: Pituitary Network Association, SEER.
patients_needing_tx: ~30,000–50,000 patients on systemic pharmacologic treatment for hormone-secreting adenomas.
price: $30,000–$100,000/year; osilodrostat ~$200,000/year.
peak_sales: $200–500M at 10–20% share; cabergoline (cheap generic) dominates prolactinoma; specialty agents for Cushing's are the commercial opportunity.
registrational_endpoint: UFC (urinary free cortisol) normalization rate at 6 months in Cushing's disease (FDA standard); IGF-1 normalization for acromegaly.
btd_threshold_vs_soc: UFC normalization ≥60% at 6 months vs. ~25% for pasireotide in Cushing's disease.

symptoms: Headache, vomiting, gait problems, vision changes, seizures
treatment_goal: Achieve long-term remission, preserve neurocognitive function
first_line_soc: Surgery (maximal resection) + radiation (age-dependent) + chemotherapy (carboplatin/vincristine for LGG; craniospinal RT for medulloblastoma).
treatment_algorithm: LGG (most common): BRAF-MEK inhibitors if BRAF-altered (dabrafenib+trametinib, tovorafenib—approved FDA 2024 for pediatric LGG). Medulloblastoma: craniospinal RT + cisplatin-based chemo (Packer regimen). DIPG: ONC201 (FDA breakthrough 2023 for H3K27M-mutant). Ependymoma: RT ± chemo. FGFR: futibatinib. Carboplatin-based regimens for infant CNS tumors.
us_prevalence: ~5,000 new pediatric CNS tumors/year; prevalent ~28,000. Leading cause of pediatric cancer death.
patients_needing_tx: ~4,500/year requiring pharmacologic therapy; ~8,000 prevalent on active therapy.
price: $200,000–$300,000/year for targeted pediatric CNS agents (tovorafenib WAC ~$265K). $250,000.
registrational_endpoint: Objective response rate (ORR) and PFS in recurrent/refractory pediatric CNS tumors
btd_threshold_vs_soc: ORR >40% vs. historical <15%; PFS HR <0.65 vs. best available therapy

symptoms: Headache, seizures, focal neurologic deficits, visual changes, hearing loss
treatment_goal: Control tumor growth; preserve neurologic function; reduce recurrence
first_line_soc: Surgery (Simpson grade I–II resection) for symptomatic/growing meningiomas. Stereotactic radiosurgery (SRS/Gamma Knife) for small/residual tumors. No FDA-approved systemic pharmacologic SoC for meningioma.
treatment_algorithm: Grade 1: observation or surgery. Grade 2 (atypical): surgery + adjuvant RT. Grade 3 (anaplastic): surgery + RT ± chemotherapy (hydroxyurea has limited efficacy; somatostatin analogues for SSTR2+). Recurrent/progressive grade 2–3: bevacizumab, sunitinib, or clinical trials (FAK inhibitors, CDK4/6i). TRAF7/KLF4/AKT1/SMO mutations: targeted therapy trials. NF2 mutations in meningioma: emerging targets.
us_prevalence: ~170,000–190,000 US patients with diagnosed meningiomas (most incidental); ~34,000 new cases/year; only ~5% are grade 2–3. Source: CBTRUS.
patients_needing_tx: ~8,000–12,000 patients/year with grade 2–3 or recurrent meningiomas needing systemic pharmacologic agents.
price: $80,000–$200,000/year for novel molecular targeted agents.
peak_sales: $100–250M at 10–20% share for a novel targeted meningioma agent.
registrational_endpoint: PFS at 6 months (PFS-6) and 12-month PFS in grade 2–3 recurrent meningioma; FDA standard for brain tumor trials often uses PFS-6.
btd_threshold_vs_soc: PFS-6 rate ≥50% vs. ~30% historical for recurrent grade 2 meningioma.

symptoms: Back pain, radiculopathy, weakness, sensory loss, bowel/bladder dysfunction
treatment_goal: Decompress spinal cord; preserve/restore neurologic function
first_line_soc: Surgical decompression ± radiation. Metastatic spinal cord compression: dexamethasone 10 mg IV bolus, then 4 mg q6h (emergency) + radiation. Intramedullary tumors (ependymoma, astrocytoma): surgery ± radiation.
treatment_algorithm: Primary spinal tumors: surgery (gross total resection if possible) + adjuvant RT for high-grade astrocytoma; ependymoma: surgery ± RT for recurrent. Metastatic: dexamethasone + radiation ± surgery. Systemic therapy per primary tumor type. Bevacizumab for hemangioblastoma/spinal cord tumors in VHL. Anti-VEGF for vascular tumors.
us_prevalence: ~10,000 new primary spinal cord tumor diagnoses/year; ~200,000 patients with spinal metastases. Source: CBTRUS, NCI.
patients_needing_tx: ~5,000–8,000 patients with primary spinal tumors needing systemic therapy; ~50,000+ with spinal metastases needing systemic cancer treatment.
price: $80,000–$200,000/year for novel spinal tumor targeted agents.
peak_sales: $50–150M at 10–15% share within CNS/spinal tumor broader franchise.
registrational_endpoint: PFS in primary high-grade spinal cord astrocytoma; neurologic function preservation (ASIA score improvement) as secondary.
btd_threshold_vs_soc: PFS HR ≤0.65 vs. RT alone in high-grade spinal cord glioma.

symptoms: Headaches, nausea, gait instability, neck pain, hydrocephalus
treatment_goal: Maximal safe resection plus radiation; prevent recurrence
first_line_soc: Surgery + radiation. No established first-line pharmacologic SoC for upfront treatment.
treatment_algorithm: Pediatric (RELA/YAP1 fusion): surgery + RT; chemo (carboplatin+etoposide, vincristine) for infants to delay RT. Recurrent: no standard chemo; bevacizumab+irinotecan; lapatinib investigational. EZH2 inhibitors (tazemetostat) under investigation for posterior fossa Group A. NTRK fusions: larotrectinib.
us_prevalence: ~2,000–2,500 new cases/year; ~10,000 prevalent. Second most common pediatric CNS tumor.
patients_needing_tx: ~1,500/year requiring adjuvant or salvage pharmacologic therapy.
price: $150,000–$250,000/year for novel targeted therapy. $200,000.
registrational_endpoint: PFS and ORR in recurrent ependymoma by RANO criteria
btd_threshold_vs_soc: ORR >20% in recurrent ependymoma; PFS HR <0.65 vs. historical bevacizumab

symptoms: Cognitive decline, focal neurologic deficits, personality changes, elevated ICP
treatment_goal: Achieve complete response with chemotherapy; maintain neurologic function
first_line_soc: High-dose methotrexate (MTX, 3.5 g/m² q14d × 6–8 cycles) ± rituximab ± cytarabine (R-MPV or R-MATRix regimen). Followed by consolidation (autologous SCT or reduced-dose WBRT).
treatment_algorithm: First-line: R-MPV (rituximab + MTX + procarbazine + vincristine) or R-MATRix (rituximab + MTX + cytarabine + thiotepa). Consolidation: autologous SCT (fit patients) or 23.4 Gy WBRT (elderly/unfit). Relapsed/refractory: ibrutinib (BTK inhibitor, high CNS penetration, ~70% ORR); lenalidomide; intrathecal MTX; RICE; CAR-T cells in trials. Corticosteroids for acute CNS edema.
us_prevalence: ~1,500–2,000 new US cases/year; ~4,000–5,000 prevalent. Source: SEER, CBTRUS.
patients_needing_tx: ~1,500–2,000 patients/year requiring systemic chemotherapy.
peak_sales: $80–150M at 30–40% share for ibrutinib-based or novel CNS-penetrant BTKi.
registrational_endpoint: CR rate and PFS in relapsed/refractory PCNSL; ibrutinib achieved ~70% ORR in Phase 2. FDA standard: ORR + DoR for accelerated approval.
btd_threshold_vs_soc: CR rate ≥40% with DoR ≥12 months in relapsed PCNSL vs. ~15% CR with salvage MTX.

symptoms: Headache, vomiting, ataxia, diplopia (posterior fossa syndrome), cerebellar signs
treatment_goal: Achieve CR; prevent leptomeningeal spread; limit radiation-related neurotoxicity
first_line_soc: Surgery (maximal safe resection) + craniospinal radiation (CSI, 23.4–36 Gy) + chemotherapy (cisplatin, vincristine, cyclophosphamide; or carboplatin/vincristine/lomustine). Regimen varies by risk group and molecular subtype.
treatment_algorithm: Standard-risk pediatric: surgery + CSI 23.4 Gy + vincristine/cisplatin/lomustine or cyclophosphamide. High-risk: CSI 36 Gy + intensified chemo. WNT subgroup (best prognosis): current trials reducing RT dose. SHH subgroup: vismodegib (SMO inhibitor) in trials. Group 3/4: most aggressive, clinical trial preferred. Adult medulloblastoma: similar approach. Relapsed: cyclophosphamide/topotecan; immunotherapy trials.
us_prevalence: ~500–600 new US cases/year (children); ~100–150/year in adults; ~3,000–5,000 living with history of medulloblastoma. Source: CBTRUS, NCI.
patients_needing_tx: ~600–750 patients/year with newly diagnosed medulloblastoma; ~300–400 with recurrent disease.
price: $80,000–$200,000/year for molecularly targeted agents.
peak_sales: $50–100M at 30–40% share for a precision-targeted medulloblastoma agent (e.g., SHH inhibitor + chemo).
registrational_endpoint: EFS (event-free survival) in newly diagnosed high-risk medulloblastoma; OS in relapsed. FDA standard for pediatric oncology: OS or EFS.
btd_threshold_vs_soc: 5-year EFS ≥60% vs. ~35% historical for high-risk medulloblastoma.

symptoms: Hydrocephalus symptoms, Parinaud syndrome (upgaze palsy), precocious puberty
treatment_goal: Achieve CSF diversion; complete resection or radiation cure; control germ cell tumors
first_line_soc: CSF diversion (ventriculoperitoneal shunt or ETV) for hydrocephalus. Germinomas: highly chemosensitive/radiosensitive — carboplatin + etoposide + CSI; or carboplatin-based chemo followed by reduced-dose RT. Non-germinomatous GCTs: platinum-based chemo + RT.
treatment_algorithm: Germinoma: carboplatin + etoposide × 4 cycles + CSI 24 Gy (cure rate >90%). Non-germinomatous GCTs: BEP or carboplatin + etoposide + bleomycin; second-look surgery; high-dose chemo + ASCT for refractory. Pineocytoma: surgery. Pineoblastoma: surgery + craniospinal RT + chemo. Pineal parenchymal tumors: individualized approach.
us_prevalence: ~300–500 new US cases/year for all pineal region tumors; ~150–200 germinomas. Source: CBTRUS.
patients_needing_tx: ~300–500 patients/year requiring systemic chemotherapy.
price: $80,000–$150,000/year for novel CNS-penetrant germ cell targeting agents.
peak_sales: $10–20M at 25% share; very small rare tumor population.
registrational_endpoint: CR rate and 3-year EFS in newly diagnosed intracranial germinoma vs. historical control.
btd_threshold_vs_soc: 3-year EFS ≥90% with reduced-dose RT vs. ~85% with standard RT in germinoma.

symptoms: Painless lymphadenopathy, fatigue, recurrent infections, splenomegaly, cytopenias
treatment_goal: Achieve deep MRD-negative CR; extend PFS; potentially cure via venetoclax combos
first_line_soc: Ibrutinib + venetoclax (CAPTIVATE/GLOW) or acalabrutinib ± obinutuzumab; or venetoclax + obinutuzumab (CLL14 regimen, time-limited). BTK inhibitor monotherapy acceptable for del17p/TP53 mutation.
treatment_algorithm: Frontline: ibrutinib or acalabrutinib ± obinutuzumab; venetoclax + obinutuzumab (12-cycle fixed-duration). Relapsed: venetoclax + rituximab (MURANO); zanubrutinib (superior to ibrutinib by ALPINE). BCL2 + BTKi combinations. Chemoimmunotherapy (FCR/BR) only for young/fit IGHV-mutated without del17p. BTK degraders (NX-2127) in development. Pirtobrutinib for BTKi-refractory. Allogeneic SCT for young high-risk.
us_prevalence: ~210,000 US patients living with CLL; ~20,700 new cases/year. Source: ACS 2024, SEER.
patients_needing_tx: ~80,000–120,000 patients on active treatment; ~20,000–30,000 treatment-naïve starting annually.
price: $150,000–$300,000/year; ibrutinib ~$200,000/year; acalabrutinib ~$200,000/year.
peak_sales: $2B–$5B at 10–20% share; ibrutinib CLL ~$8B globally (all indications); zanubrutinib ~$2.6B 2024.
registrational_endpoint: PFS in relapsed/refractory or treatment-naïve CLL; MRD-negative CR rate as secondary. FDA co-primary: PFS + OS.
btd_threshold_vs_soc: PFS HR ≤0.65 vs. chemoimmunotherapy in treatment-naïve CLL; MRD-negative uMRD rate ≥60% at 12 months.

symptoms: Anemia, fatigue, infections, bleeding, cytopenias, bone marrow failure
treatment_goal: Reduce transfusion dependence; delay AML progression; achieve hematologic improvement
first_line_soc: Lower-risk MDS: erythropoiesis-stimulating agents (ESAs, e.g., darbepoetin alfa); luspatercept (Reblozyl, FDA 2023 for first-line lower-risk MDS with ring sideroblasts/SF3B1 mutation). Del5q: lenalidomide. Higher-risk MDS: hypomethylating agents (azacitidine or decitabine) as first-line.
treatment_algorithm: Lower-risk: ESAs (epoetin alfa/darbepoetin); luspatercept > ESA for SF3B1-mutant (COMMANDS trial); lenalidomide for del5q; transfusion support. Imetelstat (telomerase inhibitor, FDA 2024) for lower-risk transfusion-dependent MDS after ESA failure. Higher-risk: azacitidine or decitabine ± cedazuridine (oral hypomethylating); allo-SCT for eligible. Venetoclax + HMA for HMA-naive high-risk. Magrolimab (CD47) in trials. Enasidenib for IDH2-mutant relapsed.
us_prevalence: ~170,000 US patients with MDS; ~20,000–25,000 new diagnoses/year. Source: SEER, MDS Foundation.
patients_needing_tx: ~100,000–130,000 patients on active pharmacologic therapy.
price: $100,000–$250,000/year; luspatercept ~$150,000/year; imetelstat ~$135,000/year.
peak_sales: $500M–$2B at 5–15% share; luspatercept (Reblozyl) ~$1.3B 2024 (all indications); imetelstat potential $500M+ in MDS.
registrational_endpoint: Transfusion independence (TI ≥8 weeks) rate and hematologic improvement; FDA standard for lower-risk MDS. Higher-risk: OS or PFS.
btd_threshold_vs_soc: TI ≥8 weeks in ≥45% of lower-risk patients vs. ~29% for placebo in transfusion-dependent MDS after ESA failure.

symptoms: Fatigue, splenomegaly, weight loss, night sweats, leukocytosis
treatment_goal: Achieve deep molecular remission (MR4.5); enable treatment-free remission
first_line_soc: BCR-ABL1 tyrosine kinase inhibitor: imatinib (Gleevec, 400 mg/day) as original SoC; or nilotinib, dasatinib, or bosutinib as second-generation TKIs with deeper/faster responses. Ponatinib or asciminib (STAMP inhibitor) for T315I mutation.
treatment_algorithm: Chronic phase: imatinib (now generic) or 2G-TKIs (nilotinib, dasatinib, bosutinib) per patient profile. Monitor BCR-ABL1 levels quarterly. Deep MR (MR4.5 × 2 years): consider treatment-free remission (TFR) attempt. Resistance: mutation analysis → switch TKI (ponatinib for T315I). Accelerated/blast phase: allogeneic SCT. Asciminib (FDA 2021): third-generation, allosteric, superior vs. bosutinib in 3L (ASCEMBL).
us_prevalence: ~70,000 US patients living with CML; ~9,280 new cases/year. Source: ACS 2024, SEER.
patients_needing_tx: ~60,000–70,000 patients on continuous TKI therapy.
price: $50,000–$200,000/year; imatinib generic ~$10,000/year; asciminib ~$180,000/year.
peak_sales: $500M–$1B at 10–15% share for a novel 3G/4G allosteric TKI; asciminib (Scemblix) ~$600M 2024.
registrational_endpoint: MR4.5 (BCR-ABL1 ≤0.0032% IS) at 96 weeks in newly diagnosed CP-CML; MMR at 12 months; CCyR. FDA standard: MMR or deeper.
btd_threshold_vs_soc: MR4 rate ≥50% at 96 weeks vs. ~34% for imatinib in CP-CML frontline.

symptoms: Fatigue, infections, bleeding/bruising, bone pain, gum hypertrophy
treatment_goal: Achieve MRD-negative CR; bridge to allogeneic SCT; extend OS in unfit patients
first_line_soc: Fit patients: '7+3' (cytarabine 100 mg/m² × 7d + daunorubicin/idarubicin × 3d) ± midostaurin (FLT3-mutant, FDA 2017) ± gemtuzumab ozogamicin (CD33+, FDA 2017). Elderly/unfit: venetoclax + azacitidine (FDA 2020, VIALE-A trial, standard-of-care).
treatment_algorithm: Standard-risk: 7+3 → consolidation cytarabine × 3–4 cycles. High-risk/relapsed: allogeneic SCT. FLT3-mutant: midostaurin (frontline) → gilteritinib (relapsed). IDH1-mutant: ivosidenib (FDA 2018). IDH2-mutant: enasidenib (FDA 2017). Elderly/unfit: ven + aza (new SoC). Relapsed/refractory fit: idarubicin/cytarabine; CPX-351 (liposomal daunorubicin + cytarabine, therapy-related/secondary AML). APL: ATRA + arsenic trioxide (cure rate >90%).
us_prevalence: ~60,000 US patients living with AML; ~20,000 new cases/year; ~11,000 deaths/year. Source: ACS 2024, SEER.
patients_needing_tx: ~20,000 new patients/year; ~8,000–10,000 relapsed/refractory needing novel agents.
price: $80,000–$250,000/year; venetoclax ~$140,000/year; gilteritinib ~$170,000/year.
registrational_endpoint: CR/CRi rate at end of induction; EFS and OS. FDA co-primary for frontline AML: OS.
btd_threshold_vs_soc: OS HR ≤0.66 vs. aza alone in elderly unfit AML (VIALE-A achieved HR 0.66); CR rate ≥35% in relapsed/refractory with novel agent.

symptoms: Fatigue, pallor, bruising, infections, bone pain, lymphadenopathy
treatment_goal: Achieve MRD-negative complete remission; cure; prevent CNS relapse
first_line_soc: Pediatric ALL: multi-agent induction (vincristine/dexamethasone/asparaginase/daunorubicin), consolidation, maintenance (6-mercaptopurine + methotrexate × 2–3 years) — >90% cure. Philadelphia chromosome+: add imatinib/dasatinib to chemotherapy. Adult ALL: pediatric-inspired regimens (CALGB-10403); Ph+: ponatinib or dasatinib + reduced chemo.
treatment_algorithm: Induction: BFM/COGS protocols for pediatric. Adult: dasatinib + low-intensity chemo (MDACC regimen) for Ph+; blinatumomab (BiTE, FDA 2014) for relapsed B-ALL. Inotuzumab ozogamicin (ADC, FDA 2017) for CD22+ relapsed B-ALL. CAR-T (tisagenlecleucel for ≤25 yr, FDA 2017; brexucabtagene autoleucel for adult R/R, FDA 2021). Allogeneic SCT for high-risk CR. CNS prophylaxis: intrathecal chemotherapy. Asparaginase: pegaspargase or calaspargase.
us_prevalence: ~55,000 US patients living with ALL; ~6,500 new cases/year (60% pediatric). Source: ACS 2024, SEER.
patients_needing_tx: ~6,500 new patients/year; ~3,000–4,000 relapsed/refractory patients needing novel agents.
price: $150,000–$500,000/year; blinatumomab ~$250,000/year; tisagenlecleucel one-time $475,000.
registrational_endpoint: MRD-negative CR rate at end of induction/consolidation; EFS or OS in Ph+ ALL. FDA: OS or RFS for frontline; ORR + DoR for relapsed/refractory.
btd_threshold_vs_soc: MRD-negative CR ≥70% in relapsed Ph+ ALL vs. ~40% with imatinib + standard chemo.

symptoms: Right upper quadrant pain, hepatomegaly, jaundice, ascites, weight loss
treatment_goal: Achieve curative resection/ablation in early stage; extend OS in advanced HCC
first_line_soc: Advanced HCC: atezolizumab + bevacizumab (IMbrave150, FDA 2020) — preferred first-line. Durvalumab + tremelimumab (HIMALAYA, FDA 2022) or sorafenib/lenvatinib as alternatives. Early HCC: resection, ablation, liver transplant (Milan criteria).
treatment_algorithm: Very early (BCLC-0/A): resection or ablation (RFA/MWA) or transplant. Intermediate (BCLC-B): TACE (transarterial chemoembolization) or Y-90 radioembolization. Advanced (BCLC-C): atezo + bev (1L); durvalumab + tremelimumab (1L alternative); sorafenib or lenvatinib (1L if IO-contraindicated). Second-line: regorafenib (FDA 2017); cabozantinib (FDA 2019); ramucirumab (AFP ≥400, FDA 2019); nivolumab; pembrolizumab.
us_prevalence: ~60,000–70,000 US patients living with HCC; ~45,000 new cases/year; ~33,000 deaths/year. Source: ACS 2024, SEER.
patients_needing_tx: ~25,000–35,000 patients on active systemic therapy for advanced HCC.
registrational_endpoint: OS and PFS in advanced HCC (IMbrave150 co-primary: OS + PFS). FDA standard: OS.
btd_threshold_vs_soc: OS HR ≤0.66 vs. sorafenib in advanced HCC (atezo + bev achieved HR 0.58).

symptoms: Abdominal pain, jaundice, weight loss, hepatomegaly (intrahepatic cholangiocarcinoma, angiosarcoma)
treatment_goal: Achieve curative resection; control biliary obstruction; extend OS in unresectable
first_line_soc: Intrahepatic cholangiocarcinoma (iCCA): gemcitabine + cisplatin + durvalumab (TOPAZ-1, FDA 2022) as 1L. IDH1-mutant iCCA: ivosidenib 2L (FDA 2021). FGFR2-fusion: pemigatinib (FDA 2020) or futibatinib (FDA 2023).
treatment_algorithm: iCCA: resection if operable; 1L gem + cis + durvalumab; 2L: FGFR2 inhibitors (pemigatinib, futibatinib) for FGFR2 fusions; IDH1: ivosidenib; NTRK: larotrectinib/entrectinib; RET: selpercatinib. Biliary angiosarcoma: gemcitabine-based. Combined hepatocellular-cholangiocarcinoma: treat per dominant histology. Liver epithelioid hemangioendothelioma: sirolimus, anti-VEGF agents.
us_prevalence: ~15,000 new iCCA cases/year; ~25,000 prevalent; biliary/intrahepatic cholangiocarcinoma incidence rising 3%/year. Source: ACS, SEER.
patients_needing_tx: ~10,000–15,000 patients/year on systemic therapy for unresectable/metastatic iCCA.
peak_sales: $300–600M at 15–25% share for FGFR2-targeted agent; pemigatinib ~$300M+ peak.
registrational_endpoint: ORR and DoR for FGFR2 inhibitors in 2L iCCA (accelerated approval); OS in 1L randomized trials.
btd_threshold_vs_soc: ORR ≥35% with DoR ≥6 months in FGFR2-fusion iCCA after gem/cis failure.

symptoms: Right upper quadrant pain, jaundice, hepatomegaly, weight loss, elevated liver enzymes
treatment_goal: Control systemic disease; reduce liver tumor burden; maintain liver function
first_line_soc: Systemic therapy per primary tumor origin (colorectal: FOLFOX + bevacizumab; breast: systemic breast cancer SoC; etc.). Locoregional therapy (TACE, SIRT/Y-90, ablation) for liver-dominant disease in selected patients.
treatment_algorithm: Colorectal liver metastases: FOLFOX/FOLFIRI ± bevacizumab/cetuximab/panitumumab; hepatic arterial infusion (HAI) pump chemotherapy for liver-dominant. Breast liver mets: per breast cancer histology. Liver-directed therapy (TACE, Y-90, ablation) for oligometastatic disease. Liver transplant for selected metastatic colorectal (highly selected). No single SoC — driven by primary histology.
us_prevalence: ~200,000–300,000 US patients with liver metastases from various primaries; colorectal liver metastases most common (~50%). Source: SEER, NCI.
patients_needing_tx: ~150,000–250,000 patients on systemic treatment targeting liver metastases.
price: Per primary tumor indication; HAI pump costs $30,000–$50,000/year.
peak_sales: $100–300M for a novel liver-targeted delivery system or HAI agent as standalone.
registrational_endpoint: Hepatic PFS or liver-specific ORR in colorectal liver metastases; OS in randomized trials vs. systemic chemotherapy alone.
btd_threshold_vs_soc: Hepatic ORR ≥60% vs. ~40% for FOLFOX alone in colorectal liver metastases.

symptoms: Lymphadenopathy, fatigue, fever, night sweats, abdominal pain, cytopenias
treatment_goal: Achieve CR; cure aggressive NHL; extend PFS in indolent NHL; improve OS
first_line_soc: DLBCL (most common NHL): R-CHOP (rituximab/cyclophosphamide/doxorubicin/vincristine/prednisone) × 6 cycles. Follicular lymphoma: R-CHOP or bendamustine + rituximab (BR); obinutuzumab + chemo. CNS NHL: MTX-based (see Primary Brain Lymphoma).
treatment_algorithm: DLBCL 1L: R-CHOP or pola-R-CHP (polatuzumab vedotin + R-CHP, FDA 2023, POLARIX trial); CAR-T (axi-cel/tisa-cel/liso-cel) for 2L DLBCL after failure. Mantle cell: ibrutinib/zanubrutinib; BTKi + ven; BR-rituximab. Follicular: obinutuzumab/rituximab maintenance; mosunetuzumab (bispecific, FDA 2022) for R/R. Marginal zone: ibrutinib; R-CHOP. T-cell NHL: romidepsin; belinostat; pralatrexate; mogamulizumab. ALL R/R: CAR-T (various FDA-approved for DLBCL, FL, MCL).
us_prevalence: ~700,000 US patients living with NHL; ~80,000 new cases/year. Source: ACS 2024, SEER.
patients_needing_tx: ~200,000–300,000 patients on active systemic therapy.
peak_sales: $2B–$6B at 5–10% share for a novel mechanism; polatuzumab vedotin ~$600M+ 2024.
registrational_endpoint: PFS in DLBCL 1L (POLARIX primary endpoint); CR rate + DoR for accelerated approval; OS for full approval in aggressive NHL.
btd_threshold_vs_soc: PFS HR ≤0.73 vs. R-CHOP in 1L DLBCL (POLARIX achieved HR 0.73).

symptoms: Itchy erythematous patches/plaques/tumors on skin, Sézary syndrome (erythroderma, blood involvement)
treatment_goal: Achieve skin clearance; reduce pruritus; prevent progression to large-cell transformation
first_line_soc: Skin-directed therapy: topical corticosteroids, topical mechlorethamine (Valchlor), topical bexarotene, phototherapy (PUVA, NB-UVB) for early stage. Systemic 1L: bexarotene (Targretin, retinoid, FDA 1999) + interferon-alpha or HDAC inhibitor (vorinostat [FDA 2006] or romidepsin [FDA 2009]).
treatment_algorithm: Early (IA–IIA): skin-directed. Advanced (IIB–IVB): mogamulizumab (anti-CCR4, FDA 2018) for MF/SS; brentuximab vedotin (CD30+); vorinostat; romidepsin; pralatrexate. Sézary syndrome: alemtuzumab; ECP (extracorporeal photopheresis). Allogeneic SCT for selected fit patients. Total skin electron beam therapy (TSEBT).
us_prevalence: ~25,000–30,000 US patients with MF/SS (CTCL); ~3,000–4,000 new diagnoses/year. Source: NCI SEER, Lymphoma Research Foundation.
patients_needing_tx: ~15,000–20,000 patients on active systemic therapy.
price: $80,000–$250,000/year; mogamulizumab ~$200,000/year.
registrational_endpoint: PFS (MAVORIC trial: mogamulizumab vs. vorinostat) and ORR; FDA standard: PFS + ORR for R/R CTCL.
btd_threshold_vs_soc: PFS HR ≤0.65 vs. vorinostat in relapsed/refractory MF/SS.

symptoms: Painless lymphadenopathy, B symptoms (fever, night sweats, weight loss), pruritus
treatment_goal: Achieve complete metabolic remission; minimize long-term toxicity; cure
first_line_soc: Early favorable: ABVD (doxorubicin/bleomycin/vinblastine/dacarbazine) × 2–4 cycles + involved-field RT. Advanced stage: BV-AVD (brentuximab vedotin + doxorubicin/vinblastine/dacarbazine, FDA 2018, ECHELON-1) for stage III–IV as preferred first-line.
treatment_algorithm: Frontline advanced HL: BV-AVD (ECHELON-1 OS benefit) or ABVD (if BV unavailable). PET-adapted therapy to de-escalate RT. Relapsed: salvage chemo (DHAP, GDP, ICE) → autologous SCT. Post-ASCT brentuximab vedotin maintenance (AETHERA, FDA 2015). Second relapse: pembrolizumab (FDA 2017); nivolumab. CART-T cells in clinical trials.
us_prevalence: ~200,000 US Hodgkin lymphoma survivors; ~8,800 new cases/year; ~1,000 deaths/year (excellent prognosis). Source: ACS 2024.
patients_needing_tx: ~8,800 new patients/year; ~3,000–4,000 relapsed/refractory patients needing novel agents.
price: $100,000–$250,000/year; brentuximab vedotin ~$180,000/year.
peak_sales: $500M–$1B at 20–40% share; brentuximab vedotin HL ~$600M+ globally.
registrational_endpoint: EFS and OS in advanced HL (ECHELON-1 standard: modified PFS). PFS/OS in relapsed/refractory after ASCT.
btd_threshold_vs_soc: EFS HR ≤0.77 vs. ABVD in advanced stage III–IV HL (ECHELON-1 achieved HR 0.77).

symptoms: Rapidly growing jaw/abdominal mass, tumor lysis syndrome, CNS involvement
treatment_goal: Achieve complete remission with intensive chemotherapy; prevent CNS relapse; cure
first_line_soc: Intensive short-duration regimens: DA-EPOCH-R (dose-adjusted EPOCH + rituximab) for pediatric-like high-intensity; or modified Magrath regimen (CODOX-M/IVAC + rituximab). CR rates 70–90% with cure.
treatment_algorithm: Children: COCOX-M/IVAC-R or modified B-ALL protocol (BFM). Adults: DA-EPOCH-R (NCI regimen) or Hyper-CVAD + rituximab. Tumor lysis prophylaxis mandatory: allopurinol, rasburicase, IV fluids. CNS prophylaxis: intrathecal MTX + cytarabine. Relapsed: clinical trial; CART-T cells emerging. Rituximab essential in all regimens.
us_prevalence: ~1,200–1,400 new US cases/year; ~4,000–5,000 prevalent. Source: SEER, Lymphoma Research Foundation.
patients_needing_tx: ~1,200–1,400 patients/year needing intensive front-line chemoimmunotherapy.
price: $150,000–$300,000/year for novel add-on agents (e.g., BTKi, venetoclax, CART).
peak_sales: $50–100M at 25–40% share for novel combination add-on to R-CHOP/EPOCH regimens.
registrational_endpoint: EFS at 2 years in newly diagnosed Burkitt lymphoma; CR rate post-induction. FDA standard: EFS or OS.
btd_threshold_vs_soc: 2-year EFS ≥80% vs. ~65% historical for standard regimens in relapsed Burkitt lymphoma.

symptoms: Parathyroid, pituitary, and pancreatic tumors; hypercalcemia, peptic ulcers, hypoglycemia
treatment_goal: Control hypercalcemia; suppress hormone excess; prevent tumor complications
first_line_soc: Surgery for primary hyperparathyroidism (parathyroidectomy). Gastrinomas: high-dose PPI + surgery if localized. Insulinoma: surgery. Pituitary adenomas: per lesion type (see Pituitary Tumors). Surveillance for non-functioning pNETs.
treatment_algorithm: Parathyroid: surgical parathyroidectomy (subtotal or total + autotransplant). Pancreatic NETs: surveillance for <2 cm; surgery for >2 cm; SSA for metastatic; everolimus; sunitinib; PRRT. Pituitary: cabergoline for prolactinoma; SSA for acromegaly. MEN1 screening: annual biochemical testing + periodic imaging. Cinacalcet for hypercalcemia if surgery declined.
us_prevalence: ~25,000–30,000 US patients with MEN1; ~1 in 30,000 population. Source: NORD, Menin-1 Foundation.
patients_needing_tx: ~15,000–20,000 patients requiring active pharmacologic management for hormone excess or tumor control.
price: $80,000–$200,000/year for novel menin inhibitors (MRTX849-class) or antiproliferatives.
peak_sales: $200–400M at 15–25% share for a menin inhibitor targeting MEN1-associated pNETs.
registrational_endpoint: Tumor growth rate reduction and hormone normalization in unresectable MEN1-associated pNETs vs. SSA over 24 months.
btd_threshold_vs_soc: PFS HR ≤0.65 vs. SSA in MEN1-associated advanced pNETs.

symptoms: Medullary thyroid carcinoma, pheochromocytoma, primary hyperparathyroidism
treatment_goal: Prophylactic thyroidectomy; control pheochromocytoma; cure MTC
first_line_soc: Prophylactic/therapeutic total thyroidectomy. Pheochromocytoma: surgical adrenalectomy after alpha-blockade. Metastatic MTC: vandetanib (FDA 2011) or cabozantinib (FDA 2012) — RET kinase inhibitors as first-line systemic therapy.
treatment_algorithm: Genetic testing (RET codon 634/618/620): prophylactic thyroidectomy based on risk. MTC: thyroidectomy + central lymph node dissection; adjuvant: selective RET inhibitors (selpercatinib FDA 2020, pralsetinib FDA 2020) now preferred over vandetanib/cabozantinib for advanced MTC. Pheochromocytoma: adrenalectomy. PHPT: parathyroidectomy. Annual biochemical surveillance.
us_prevalence: ~5,000–8,000 US patients with MEN2A; ~1 in 35,000. Source: NORD, ATA MTC guidelines.
patients_needing_tx: ~2,000–4,000 patients with advanced MTC needing systemic RET inhibitor therapy.
price: $150,000–$250,000/year; selpercatinib ~$220,000/year.
peak_sales: $150–300M at 30–40% share for selpercatinib/pralsetinib in MTC/RET-mutant tumors.
registrational_endpoint: ORR and DoR in advanced RET-mutant MTC; selpercatinib achieved 69% ORR. FDA standard: ORR + DoR for tumor-agnostic/RET indication.
btd_threshold_vs_soc: ORR ≥65% with DoR ≥12 months in RET-mutant advanced MTC.

symptoms: Medullary thyroid carcinoma, pheochromocytoma, mucosal neuromas, marfanoid habitus
treatment_goal: Early prophylactic thyroidectomy; control MTC; manage pheochromocytoma
first_line_soc: Total thyroidectomy in infancy (within months of birth for M918T carriers). Advanced MTC: selpercatinib or pralsetinib (RET inhibitors, FDA 2020). Pheochromocytoma: adrenalectomy after alpha-blockade.
treatment_algorithm: Prophylactic total thyroidectomy <6 months of age for MEN2B (most aggressive MTC risk). Systemic therapy same as MEN2A advanced MTC. Surveillance for bilateral pheochromocytoma. Ophthalmologic monitoring for neuromas.
us_prevalence: ~1,000–2,000 US patients with MEN2B (rarer than MEN2A); ~1 in 600,000. Source: NORD, ATA.
patients_needing_tx: ~500–1,000 patients with advanced disease needing systemic therapy.
price: $150,000–$250,000/year for RET inhibitors.
peak_sales: $30–80M at 30–40% share; subsumed within RET inhibitor franchise.
registrational_endpoint: ORR in RET M918T-mutant advanced MTC; selpercatinib achieved 60%+ ORR in MEN2B subset.
btd_threshold_vs_soc: ORR ≥60% with DoR ≥12 months in advanced MEN2B-associated MTC.

symptoms: Similar to MEN1: parathyroid tumors, pituitary tumors, pancreatic NETs (CDKN1B mutation)
treatment_goal: Manage hyperparathyroidism; control pituitary and pancreatic hormone excess
first_line_soc: Same as MEN1: parathyroidectomy, surgery for pNETs, SSA for metastatic NETs. Pituitary adenoma treatment per lesion type.
treatment_algorithm: Clinically overlaps with MEN1; management identical. CDKN1B germline testing. Annual surveillance biochemical and imaging. No unique pharmacologic SoC different from MEN1.
us_prevalence: <500 US patients with confirmed MEN4 (extremely rare, most cases identified since 2006). Source: NORD, endocrinology literature.
patients_needing_tx: ~200–400 patients.
price: $80,000–$200,000/year for CDK inhibitors (CDK4/6i) or menin inhibitors with CDKN1B loss.
registrational_endpoint: Tumor growth rate and hormone normalization in MEN4-associated pNETs (basket with MEN1).
btd_threshold_vs_soc: N/A (too rare for standalone designation; basket trial eligibility).

symptoms: Headache, itching after bathing, redness of face, thrombosis risk, splenomegaly
treatment_goal: Maintain hematocrit <45%; prevent thrombotic events
first_line_soc: Phlebotomy + low-dose aspirin (81 mg/day) for low-risk. Hydroxyurea (cytoreduction) for high-risk patients (age >60 or prior thrombosis).
treatment_algorithm: Low-risk (<60, no thrombosis): phlebotomy to Hct <45% + aspirin 81 mg. High-risk: hydroxyurea 1L; ruxolitinib (Jakafi, JAK1/2 inhibitor) 2L after hydroxyurea failure (FDA 2014). Ropeginterferon alfa-2b (Besremi, FDA 2021) approved 1L alternative. Busulfan for elderly hydroxyurea-intolerant. 2024-25: rusfertide (hepcidin mimetic, FIM Phase 3) reduces phlebotomy need.
us_prevalence: ~150,000–200,000 US patients with PV; incidence ~6,000/year.
patients_needing_tx: ~120,000 patients on pharmacologic therapy (cytoreduction ± phlebotomy).
registrational_endpoint: Hematocrit control (<45%) rate; composite endpoint of Hct + symptom burden reduction
btd_threshold_vs_soc: Hct <45% without phlebotomy in ≥60% vs. ~40% hydroxyurea; phlebotomy-free rate ≥70%

symptoms: Headaches, tingling, thrombosis risk, splenomegaly, bleeding
treatment_goal: Reduce thrombosis risk, normalize platelet count, prevent transformation
first_line_soc: Low-risk: aspirin alone. High-risk: hydroxyurea (cytoreduction); anagrelide (Agrylin) alternative.
treatment_algorithm: Risk stratification (IPSET): low-risk: aspirin 81–100 mg daily. High-risk (age >60, prior thrombosis, JAK2+, CV risk): hydroxyurea 500 mg BID titrated to platelet <400K; aspirin. Hydroxyurea-intolerant/resistant: anagrelide or pegylated interferon alfa-2a (ropeginterferon, Besremi—FDA approved for PV, used off-label ET). JAK inhibitors (ruxolitinib) for refractory. Busulfan for elderly refractory.
us_prevalence: ~150,000–200,000 US patients; ~5,000 new cases/year.
patients_needing_tx: ~80,000 high-risk patients requiring cytoreductive pharmacologic therapy.
price: $80,000–$140,000/year for novel JAK2/disease-modifier. Hydroxyurea generic ~$2K. Novel: $100,000.
registrational_endpoint: Hematologic response (platelet ≤400K + no thrombohemorrhagic event) at 12 months; molecular response (JAK2 VAF reduction)
btd_threshold_vs_soc: Complete hematologic response >60% AND JAK2 VAF reduction >50% at 12 months

symptoms: Fatigue, splenomegaly, night sweats, bone pain, weight loss, anemia
treatment_goal: Reduce spleen volume, improve symptoms, slow progression, enable transplant
first_line_soc: Ruxolitinib (Jakafi, JAK1/2 inhibitor) 15 mg BID (spleen ≥5 cm) as first-line for intermediate-2/high-risk PMF.
treatment_algorithm: Intermediate-1/2 or high-risk: ruxolitinib (FDA 2011). Fedratinib (Inrebic, JAK2 inhibitor, FDA 2019) 2nd-line after ruxolitinib. Pacritinib (Vonjo, JAK2/FLT3 inhibitor, FDA 2022) for thrombocytopenic patients. Momelotinib (Ojjaara, JAK1/2/ACVR1 inhibitor, FDA 2023) for anemic patients. 2024: navitoclax + ruxolitinib (TRANSFORM-1, NAVR combo) showing added efficacy. Allo-SCT curative in eligible. Anemia: danazol, thalidomide, lenalidomide, luspatercept (studies).
us_prevalence: ~20,000–25,000 US patients; incidence ~3,000/year.
patients_needing_tx: ~15,000 patients on pharmacologic therapy.
price: $120,000–$180,000/year (ruxolitinib ~$130K; momelotinib ~$165K). $150,000.
registrational_endpoint: Spleen volume reduction ≥35% (SVR35) at week 24; Total Symptom Score (TSS) reduction ≥50%
btd_threshold_vs_soc: SVR35 rate ≥50% vs. ~40% ruxolitinib; TSS50 rate ≥50% vs. ~45%

symptoms: Prolonged fever, splenomegaly, cytopenias, hyperferritinemia, neurologic symptoms
treatment_goal: Suppress uncontrolled immune activation, achieve remission, enable SCT
first_line_soc: HLH-94/2004 protocol: dexamethasone + etoposide; cyclosporine added. Allo-SCT for familial/primary HLH.
treatment_algorithm: HLH-2004 induction: dexamethasone + etoposide (8 weeks) → cyclosporine + continuation. CNS involvement: intrathecal methotrexate/dexamethasone. Allo-SCT for FHL. Secondary HLH (malignancy/infection): treat trigger + dexamethasone. Novel: emapalumab (Gamifant, anti-IFN-γ, FDA 2018) for primary refractory/relapsed pediatric HLH. Ruxolitinib (JAK1/2-i) for sHLH/MAS (compassionate use/off-label). Alemtuzumab as bridge.
us_prevalence: ~800–1,500 new HLH cases/year (under-recognized); prevalent ~2,000–3,000.
patients_needing_tx: ~1,500/year require pharmacologic HLH-directed treatment.
price: $500,000–$700,000/year (emapalumab WAC ~$600K). Novel IFN-γ pathway: $500,000.
registrational_endpoint: Overall response rate at 8 weeks (CR+PR by HLH response criteria: fever resolution, ferritin, cytopenia, splenomegaly)
btd_threshold_vs_soc: ORR >65% in refractory HLH vs. historical <30% with HLH-94

symptoms: Bone lesions, skin rash, diabetes insipidus, lung infiltrates, lymphadenopathy
treatment_goal: Achieve complete response, prevent diabetes insipidus, halt progression
first_line_soc: Single-system LCH (bone): local steroids or curettage. Multi-system: vinblastine + prednisone (LCH-III protocol).
treatment_algorithm: SS-LCH (single): local treatment (curettage, intralesional steroids, topical nitrogen mustard). MS-LCH first-line: vinblastine+prednisolone (LCH-III/IV). Refractory/recurrent: cladribine (2-CdA); cytarabine (Ara-C); clofarabine. BRAF V600E (~50% LCH): vemurafenib or dabrafenib+trametinib (cobimetinib) for refractory. NRAS: trametinib. 2024: BRAF/MEK inhibitors increasingly used upfront for BRAF-mutant MS-LCH.
us_prevalence: ~5,000–6,000 US patients (all ages); ~2,500 pediatric prevalent.
patients_needing_tx: ~3,000 patients requiring pharmacologic systemic therapy.
price: $150,000–$250,000/year for BRAF/MEK inhibitor targeted therapy. $200,000.
registrational_endpoint: Overall response rate (CR+PR) at 24 weeks using LCH assessment criteria (PET and anatomic)
btd_threshold_vs_soc: ORR >70% in BRAF V600E LCH vs. historical <40% with vinblastine/steroid

symptoms: Yellow-orange skin papules/nodules, rare eye involvement, usually asymptomatic
treatment_goal: Observation for skin JXG; systemic therapy for symptomatic/extracutaneous disease
first_line_soc: Observation for most cutaneous JXG (self-resolving). Systemic (rare): BRAF inhibitors if BRAF-mutant histiocytosis or cladribine.
treatment_algorithm: Skin: watchful waiting (usually resolves in 3–6 years). Ocular: corticosteroid eye drops ± surgery. Systemic life-threatening: cladribine or cytarabine (Erdheim-Chester/JXG overlap). BRAF V600E (ERD/JXG spectrum): vemurafenib or dabrafenib+trametinib. NRAS: MEK inhibitor (cobimetinib). Very rare systemic cases.
us_prevalence: ~5,000–10,000 cutaneous JXG cases/year; systemic: <500.
patients_needing_tx: ~500 patients with systemic JXG requiring pharmacologic treatment.
price: $150,000–$250,000/year for BRAF/MEK targeted therapy. $200,000.
registrational_endpoint: ORR by RECIST for systemic histiocytosis including JXG
btd_threshold_vs_soc: ORR >50% in BRAF V600E systemic histiocytosis; CR rate >25%

symptoms: Lymphadenopathy, splenomegaly, recurrent infections, diarrhea (alpha-HCD)
treatment_goal: Eradicate abnormal B-cell clone, achieve remission
first_line_soc: Alpha-HCD (most common): antibiotics (tetracycline/ampicillin) if H. pylori or IPSID-related; CHOP-based chemotherapy for aggressive cases.
treatment_algorithm: Alpha-HCD: antibiotics + CHOP if chemotherapy-sensitive. Gamma-HCD: chlorambucil + prednisone; rituximab for B-cell phenotype. Mu-HCD: alkylating agent (chlorambucil); manage as CLL-like. Rituximab-based regimens increasingly used. Extremely rare.
us_prevalence: Extremely rare: ~200–500 US patients total across all subtypes.
patients_needing_tx: ~300 patients requiring pharmacologic therapy.
price: $100,000–$200,000/year for orphan targeted agent. $150,000.
registrational_endpoint: Overall response rate and heavy chain protein normalization
btd_threshold_vs_soc: CR rate >50%; immunoglobulin normalization in ≥60%

symptoms: Massive painless lymphadenopathy, fever, weight loss, skin lesions
treatment_goal: Control symptoms; reduce lymphadenopathy; prevent compressive complications
first_line_soc: Most self-limiting; no pharmacologic SoC for idiopathic RDD. Corticosteroids for symptomatic disease or critical organ involvement.
treatment_algorithm: Sporadic (most common): observation if asymptomatic. Corticosteroids for bulky symptomatic disease. IgG4-RDD: responds to prednisone. MAPK-mutant (BRAF/MEK): vemurafenib or trametinib in trials. Cladribine or methotrexate for refractory. Rituximab case reports. Thalidomide/lenalidomide in refractory. Surgical debulking for compressive lesions.
us_prevalence: Rare: ~500–1,000 US patients with active RDD; incidence ~200/year.
patients_needing_tx: ~300 patients/year requiring pharmacologic intervention.
price: $80,000–$150,000/year for MAPK-targeted therapy. $100,000.
registrational_endpoint: Lymph node volume reduction ≥50% at 6 months; symptomatic response rate
btd_threshold_vs_soc: ORR ≥50% in MAPK-mutant RDD vs. <30% corticosteroids alone

symptoms: Fatigue, unexplained fever, weight loss, bone pain, lymph node swelling
treatment_goal: Achieve cure with risk-adapted therapy; minimize long-term toxicity
first_line_soc: Disease-specific: ALL (most common) — multi-agent chemotherapy per COG protocols. Medulloblastoma — craniospinal radiation + vincristine/cisplatin/lomustine.
treatment_algorithm: ALL: COG AALL0434 or AALL1231 regimens. AML: ADE/AAML1231. Wilms: vincristine + actinomycin D ± doxorubicin. Neuroblastoma: high-risk protocol (dinutuximab). Brain tumors: surgery + radiation ± temozolomide. Ewing sarcoma: VDC/IE. Novel agents 2024-25: larotrectinib/entrectinib for TRK-fusion+ pediatric cancers; dabrafenib + trametinib for BRAF V600E pediatric LGG (FDA 2023); tovorafenib (TAFINLAR pediatric LGG, FDA 2024).
us_prevalence: ~500,000 childhood cancer survivors; incidence ~15,000/year children <20.
patients_needing_tx: ~15,000 newly diagnosed/year; ~50,000 on active therapy or long-term follow-up treatment.
price: $150,000–$400,000/year for novel pediatric agents. $250,000.
peak_sales: For a novel broad pediatric agent: 20% share ~$2.5B peak
registrational_endpoint: Event-free survival (EFS); CR rate for accelerated; OS for confirmatory
btd_threshold_vs_soc: EFS HR <0.70 vs. SoC; tumor response rate >50% in biomarker-selected pediatric tumor

symptoms: Abdominal mass, bone pain, fever, weight loss, raccoon eyes
treatment_goal: Achieve remission; consolidate with high-dose chemo and immunotherapy
first_line_soc: Risk-stratified: low-risk: surgery alone. High-risk: induction chemotherapy (ANBL12P1: cyclophosphamide, doxorubicin, vincristine, cisplatin, etoposide) → consolidation → dinutuximab (Unituxin) + cytokines maintenance.
treatment_algorithm: Low-risk: observation or surgery. Intermediate: 4–8 cycles chemo. High-risk: induction (6 cycles) → ASCT × 2 (tandem) → radiation → anti-GD2 maintenance with dinutuximab + IL-2 + GM-CSF + isotretinoin. Relapsed: naxitamab-gqgk (Danyelza, anti-GD2, FDA 2020) + GM-CSF. DFMO (difluoromethylornithine) maintenance. 2024: ifinatamab deruxtecan (ADC) trials. Lorlatinib for ALK+ relapsed.
us_prevalence: ~5,000 US patients under active treatment/surveillance; incidence ~700/year (pediatric).
patients_needing_tx: ~700 newly diagnosed/year; ~1,500 relapsed/refractory active patients.
registrational_endpoint: Event-free survival (EFS); CR/PR rate for r/r disease
btd_threshold_vs_soc: EFS HR <0.70 vs. control; ORR ≥35% in r/r with ≥6 months DOR

symptoms: Mass in extremity, orbit, head/neck, or genitourinary tract; pain; functional impairment
treatment_goal: Achieve cure with surgery + chemo ± radiation; prevent relapse
first_line_soc: VAC regimen (vincristine + actinomycin D + cyclophosphamide) per COG risk stratification. Low-risk: VA (no cyclophosphamide).
treatment_algorithm: Low-risk: VA × 4 cycles. Intermediate/high-risk: VAC (or VAC/VI alternating with irinotecan + vincristine). High-risk metastatic: vincristine + irinotecan + temozolomide (VIT) or VAC/IE. 2024: aldoxorubicin (investigational); cabozantinib for alveolar r/r. Fusion-positive (PAX3/7-FOXO1): immunotherapy checkpoint inhibitors (atezolizumab) + VIT in trials.
us_prevalence: ~5,000 US patients; incidence ~400/year (most common soft-tissue sarcoma in children).
patients_needing_tx: ~400/year newly diagnosed; ~1,000 on active therapy or surveillance treatment.
registrational_endpoint: Failure-free survival (FFS); OS; local control rate
btd_threshold_vs_soc: FFS HR <0.70 vs. VAC; ORR ≥40% in r/r fusion-positive

symptoms: Palpable abdominal mass, hematuria, hypertension, fever, abdominal pain
treatment_goal: Achieve cure with surgery + chemotherapy; avoid late effects
first_line_soc: Nephrectomy followed by adjuvant chemotherapy: vincristine + actinomycin D (favorable histology stage I/II). Stage III/IV or anaplastic: vincristine + actinomycin D + doxorubicin ± radiation.
treatment_algorithm: NWTS/COG protocols: Stage I–II FH: EE4A (vincristine + actinomycin D × 18 weeks). Stage III–IV FH: DD4A (+ doxorubicin). Diffuse anaplasia: regimen DD4A + carboplatin + etoposide (AREN0321). Relapsed: ICE/ACCE regimens; 2024: irinotecan + vincristine. Bilateral (stage V): nephron-sparing approach. Very high survival rates (90%+ overall for FH).
us_prevalence: ~650–700 new US cases/year; ~3,000 active pediatric/young adult patients.
patients_needing_tx: ~650 newly diagnosed/year + ~200 relapsed needing pharmacologic therapy.
price: $100,000–$200,000/year for novel pediatric agent. $150,000.
registrational_endpoint: Relapse-free survival (RFS); OS; pathological complete response rate
btd_threshold_vs_soc: RFS HR <0.70 vs. standard COG regimen; OS HR <0.70 in high-risk/anaplastic

symptoms: White pupillary reflex (leukocoria), strabismus, vision loss, eye redness
treatment_goal: Preserve life (cure); preserve eye and vision when possible
first_line_soc: Intra-arterial chemotherapy (IAC: ophthalmic artery melphalan) or systemic chemotherapy (vincristine + carboplatin + etoposide/VCE) for advanced intraocular. Enucleation for unilateral group E.
treatment_algorithm: Group A/B: focal therapy (laser, cryotherapy). Group C/D: IAC (melphalan ± topotecan ± carboplatin) or systemic VCE. Group E: enucleation. Bilateral: systemic chemo + focal. Extraocular/metastatic: high-dose chemo + ASCT. Germline RB1: surveillance of second tumors; pembro/nivo for metastatic rare cases. 2024-25: intravitreal topotecan for vitreous seeds.
us_prevalence: ~300–400 new US cases/year (pediatric); ~1,000 active patients (including survivors).
patients_needing_tx: ~350 patients/year receiving pharmacologic therapy.
price: $200,000–$350,000/year for systemic novel agent. $250,000.
registrational_endpoint: Globe salvage rate; event-free survival; vitreous seed clearance rate
btd_threshold_vs_soc: Globe salvage rate ≥80% for group D vs. ~60% historical with systemic chemo

symptoms: Bone pain, fatigue, anemia, kidney failure, recurrent infections
treatment_goal: Achieve deep MRD-negative response, prolong PFS and OS
first_line_soc: VRd (bortezomib + lenalidomide + dexamethasone) for transplant-eligible and ineligible patients; daratumumab + VRd (Dara-VRd) as emerging standard since PERSEUS trial 2024.
treatment_algorithm: Transplant-eligible: induction Dara-VRd (FDA approved 2024) → ASCT → lenalidomide maintenance. Transplant-ineligible: Dara-VRd or DRd (daratumumab + lenalidomide + dexa). Relapsed: carfilzomib/ixazomib combinations; pomalidomide-based; belantamab mafodotin (2024 re-approval); elranatamab/teclistamab (BCMA×CD3 bispecifics); ciltacabtagene autoleucel/idecabtagene vicleucel (BCMA CAR-T). 2025 emerging: GPRC5D-targeting bispecifics/CAR-T.
us_prevalence: ~175,000 living US patients; incidence ~35,000/year (ACS 2025 est.).
patients_needing_tx: ~160,000 patients on active therapy or maintenance at any given time.
price: $250,000–$500,000/year (CAR-T one-time ~$465K; bispecifics ~$300K/year). $350,000.
peak_sales: 15% share of highly fragmented market: ~$8.4B peak
registrational_endpoint: Progression-free survival (PFS); MRD-negativity rate for accelerated approval
btd_threshold_vs_soc: MRD-CR rate >60% vs. ~35% SoC; PFS HR <0.70

symptoms: Asymptomatic; incidentally found elevated M-protein; risk of progression to myeloma
treatment_goal: Monitor for progression; chemoprevention to prevent myeloma (investigational)
first_line_soc: Observation with periodic monitoring (M-protein, CBC, kidney function). No standard pharmacologic treatment for MGUS.
treatment_algorithm: Standard: annual monitoring (CBC, protein electrophoresis, serum FLC). No FDA-approved pharmacologic agent for MGUS. Investigational: lenalidomide (low-dose) for high-risk MGUS (ECOG E3A06 trial, no significant benefit). Daratumumab trials. Ixazomib trials for smoldering myeloma (adjacent stage). Lifestyle modifications (exercise, bisphosphonates for bone risk).
us_prevalence: ~3 million US patients with MGUS (3% of population >50 years); progression to MM ~1%/year.
patients_needing_tx: ~100,000 high-risk MGUS patients who may benefit from chemoprevention; currently observation only.
price: $50,000–$100,000/year for preventive daratumumab or low-dose novel agent. $75,000.
registrational_endpoint: Time to progression to myeloma (TTP to MM); 5-year progression-free survival
btd_threshold_vs_soc: ≥50% reduction in progression to MM at 5 years vs. placebo in high-risk MGUS

symptoms: Fatigue, hyperviscosity symptoms, neuropathy, lymphadenopathy, cold sensitivity
treatment_goal: Reduce IgM, control hyperviscosity, achieve treatment response
first_line_soc: BTK inhibitor: ibrutinib (Imbruvica) or zanubrutinib (Brukinsa, FDA 2021 for WM); DRC (dexamethasone+rituximab+cyclophosphamide) or bendamustine+rituximab for transplant-eligible.
treatment_algorithm: Symptomatic WM: zanubrutinib 160 mg BID (preferred—ASPEN trial superior to ibrutinib) or ibrutinib. DRC, bendamustine+rituximab (BR), CHOP-R for non-BTKi candidates. Plasmapheresis for hyperviscosity emergency. Rituximab for neuropathy. Bortezomib-based for renal. Venetoclax for BCL2-overexpressing WM. Idelalisib for refractory. 2024: pirtobrutinib in r/r WM trials.
us_prevalence: ~75,000 US patients with MGUS→IgM; WM prevalence ~20,000–25,000; ~1,800 new cases/year.
patients_needing_tx: ~12,000 WM patients on active pharmacologic therapy.
price: $175,000–$220,000/year (zanubrutinib WAC ~$200K). Novel BTKi: $200,000.
registrational_endpoint: Overall response rate (ORR: MR+PR+VGPR+CR) and major response rate (MRR ≥PR) at 6 months
btd_threshold_vs_soc: MRR >77% and VGPR ≥25% vs. ibrutinib historical <30% VGPR (ASPEN trial)

symptoms: Bone pain, pathologic fracture, spinal cord compression, hypercalcemia
treatment_goal: Reduce skeletal-related events; control pain; extend mobility and OS
first_line_soc: Bone-modifying agents: denosumab (Xgeva 120 mg SC q4w, FDA-approved for SRE prevention) or zoledronic acid (4 mg IV q3–4w). Plus systemic antineoplastic therapy per primary tumor.
treatment_algorithm: Bone-modifying agents: denosumab preferred over zoledronic acid (lower SRE risk). Systemic therapy per primary cancer. Pain management: analgesics, RT (8 Gy × 1 fraction for painful bone metastases). Radium-223 (Xofigo) for castration-resistant prostate cancer bone-only metastases (FDA 2013). Surgical fixation for impending pathologic fractures. Vertebroplasty/kyphoplasty for vertebral compression fractures.
us_prevalence: ~500,000–600,000 US patients with bone metastases; breast, prostate, lung primaries most common. Source: SEER, NCI.
patients_needing_tx: ~400,000–500,000 patients receiving bone-targeted therapy annually.
price: $20,000–$50,000/year for bone-modifying agents; radium-223 ~$40,000/6-cycle course.
peak_sales: $500M–$1.5B at 5–10% share for a novel bone-protection/osteotropic agent; denosumab (Xgeva) ~$2B+/year.
registrational_endpoint: Time to first skeletal-related event (SRE) vs. comparator; FDA standard for bone mets: SRE endpoint.
btd_threshold_vs_soc: HR ≤0.70 for first SRE vs. zoledronic acid in mixed solid tumor bone metastases.

symptoms: Bone pain, swelling, pathologic fracture, limited mobility
treatment_goal: Prevent fracture, relieve pain, preserve function
first_line_soc: Benign: observation or intralesional curettage/bone graft. No universal pharmacologic first-line for benign lesions.
treatment_algorithm: Benign (unicameral/aneurysmal bone cysts): steroid injection (methylprednisolone) or sclerotherapy. Giant cell tumor of bone: denosumab (Xgeva, RANKL inhibitor) for unresectable/recurrent. Fibrous dysplasia: bisphosphonates (pamidronate). Observation for asymptomatic.
us_prevalence: ~200,000 benign bone tumors/year diagnosed; clinically significant requiring treatment: ~20,000–30,000.
patients_needing_tx: ~15,000 patients/year requiring pharmacologic treatment (denosumab-eligible GCT ~1,500; bisphosphonate candidates ~5,000).
price: $80,000–$120,000/year for RANKL-targeted or novel bone-active agent.
registrational_endpoint: Tumor response rate (RECIST) and time to progression for GCT; fracture rate reduction for cysts
btd_threshold_vs_soc: ≥80% tumor control rate; ≥50% reduction in surgical intervention rate

symptoms: Joint pain, swelling, limited range of motion, hemarthrosis (pigmented villonodular synovitis)
treatment_goal: Achieve synovial disease control; prevent joint destruction; reduce pain
first_line_soc: PVNS/TGCT (tenosynovial giant cell tumor): surgery (synovectomy) as first-line. Pexidartinib (PLX3397, CSF1R inhibitor, Turalio, FDA 2019) — only FDA-approved drug for TGCT.
treatment_algorithm: Localized TGCT: synovectomy (open or arthroscopic). Diffuse TGCT (unresectable): pexidartinib 400 mg BID (FDA 2019, ENLIVEN trial); REMS program required due to hepatotoxicity. Radiotherapy for inoperable/recurrent. Imatinib (CSF1R/PDGFR inhibitor, off-label). Emactuzumab (anti-CSF1R mAb) in trials.
us_prevalence: ~50,000 US patients with TGCT (all forms); ~11 per million/year incidence. Source: NORD, ENLIVEN trial data.
patients_needing_tx: ~5,000–10,000 patients with diffuse/symptomatic TGCT needing systemic CSF1R inhibition.
price: $80,000–$200,000/year; pexidartinib ~$170,000/year.
registrational_endpoint: ORR ≥15% and functional improvement (PROMIS score) at 25 weeks in diffuse TGCT vs. placebo (ENLIVEN achieved 38% ORR).
btd_threshold_vs_soc: ORR ≥40% with sustained response ≥6 months in diffuse-type TGCT.

symptoms: Deep bone pain, swelling, pathologic fracture, Codman triangle on X-ray
treatment_goal: Achieve wide surgical resection; prevent metastasis; cure with neoadjuvant chemo
first_line_soc: Osteosarcoma: neoadjuvant MAP (methotrexate/doxorubicin/cisplatin) × 3 cycles → limb-salvage surgery → adjuvant MAP × 3 cycles. Ewing sarcoma: VAIA/VDC-IE (vincristine/doxorubicin/cyclophosphamide/ifosfamide/etoposide) ± RT. Chondrosarcoma: surgery (chemotherapy-resistant); conventional type.
treatment_algorithm: Osteosarcoma: neoadjuvant MAP; good histologic response (>90% necrosis) → good prognosis; poor response: consider adding ifosfamide/etoposide. Relapsed: ifosfamide + etoposide; gemcitabine + docetaxel; regorafenib (OS benefit in R/R osteosarcoma, REGOBONE); samotolisib (mTOR); denosumab for lesion control. Ewing: VDC-IE ± whole lung RT; ASCT for high-risk. Chondrosarcoma: IDH1/2 inhibitors for dedifferentiated. Bisphosphonates adjunctive.
us_prevalence: ~3,500 new osteosarcoma cases/year; ~1,500 new Ewing sarcoma cases/year; ~800 chondrosarcoma (malignant)/year. Total: ~6,000–7,000/year. Source: ACS, SEER.
patients_needing_tx: ~6,000–7,000 patients/year; ~2,000–3,000 with relapsed/refractory disease.
price: $80,000–$200,000/year for novel targeted/immunotherapy agents.
peak_sales: $100–250M at 15–25% share in relapsed/refractory primary bone tumors.
registrational_endpoint: EFS in pediatric osteosarcoma (EURAMOS-1 standard); PFS or OS in relapsed Ewing sarcoma; ORR in relapsed refractory (FDA accelerated approval pathway).
btd_threshold_vs_soc: EFS HR ≤0.70 vs. MAP in resected high-risk osteosarcoma; ORR ≥25% in relapsed Ewing sarcoma.

symptoms: Localized bone pain, pathologic fracture risk, swelling, incidental radiologic finding
treatment_goal: Prevent pathologic fracture; relieve pain; ensure normal bone development
first_line_soc: Observation for asymptomatic/stable lesions. Surgical curettage ± bone grafting for symptomatic unicameral bone cysts. Denosumab (RANKL inhibitor) for giant cell tumors of bone (GCTB, FDA 2013) — pharmacologic SoC for unresectable GCTB.
treatment_algorithm: Unicameral bone cysts: observation, steroid injection, curettage + grafting, or intramedullary nailing. Aneurysmal bone cysts: Denosumab (off-label); curettage; doxycycline injection; embolization. Giant cell tumor of bone: denosumab (Xgeva 120 mg SC q4w, FDA-approved); curettage + bone cement for resectable; surgery for primary. Fibrous dysplasia: bisphosphonates for pain.
us_prevalence: Benign bone tumors: ~50,000–100,000 active cases/year; GCTB: ~2,000–3,000 new cases/year. Source: SEER, orthopedic literature.
patients_needing_tx: ~2,000–4,000 patients with GCTB requiring denosumab; ~20,000–40,000 requiring other pharmacologic interventions.
price: $20,000–$80,000/year; denosumab ~$30,000/year for benign bone tumors.
peak_sales: $50–100M at 40–60% share in GCTB; denosumab is established SoC.
registrational_endpoint: Tumor response (≥90% elimination of giant cells) and avoidance of surgery at 6 months in GCTB.
btd_threshold_vs_soc: N/A (denosumab already approved; novel agents need to show surgical resectability rescue ≥70% vs. ~30% without denosumab)

symptoms: Rectal bleeding, change in bowel habits, abdominal pain, unexplained weight loss, anemia
treatment_goal: Achieve curative resection; eliminate micrometastatic disease; extend OS in metastatic
first_line_soc: Adjuvant stage III colon: FOLFOX (oxaliplatin/leucovorin/5-FU) × 6 months. Metastatic 1L: FOLFOX + bevacizumab; FOLFIRI + cetuximab/panitumumab for RAS wild-type; FOLFOXIRI + bevacizumab for high-risk. BRAFV600E: encorafenib + cetuximab (Beraftovi + Erbitux, FDA 2020, BEACON CRC).
treatment_algorithm: Adjuvant stage III: FOLFOX or CAPOX × 6 months. Pembrolizumab 1L for MSI-H/dMMR metastatic (FDA 2020, KEYNOTE-177). RAS/RAF WT: anti-EGFR + chemo. HER2+: trastuzumab + pertuzumab or tucatinib + trastuzumab. BRAFV600E: encorafenib + cetuximab ± binimetinib. KRAS G12C: sotorasib + panitumumab (FDA 2024, CodeBreaK 300). MSI-H 2L: nivolumab ± ipilimumab. TMB-H: pembrolizumab (tumor-agnostic). RET: selpercatinib.
us_prevalence: ~1.5 million US patients living with CRC; ~153,000 new cases/year; ~52,000 deaths/year. Source: ACS 2024.
patients_needing_tx: ~500,000–700,000 patients on active systemic therapy; ~150,000–180,000 with metastatic CRC.
price: $100,000–$300,000/year; pembrolizumab MSI-H CRC ~$200,000/year.
peak_sales: $2B–$5B at 3–8% share for a novel CRC agent; encorafenib + cetuximab ~$600M+ peak.
registrational_endpoint: OS in metastatic CRC (gold standard); PFS for frontline triplet chemo (acceptable); DFS in adjuvant stage III.
btd_threshold_vs_soc: OS HR ≤0.80 vs. FOLFOX + bevacizumab in 1L metastatic CRC; or HR ≤0.50 in MSI-H subset.

disease: Polyps of the Colon and Rectum
symptoms: Usually asymptomatic; rectal bleeding, change in bowel habits in large polyps
treatment_goal: Remove polyps endoscopically; prevent malignant transformation; reduce CRC risk
first_line_soc: Endoscopic polypectomy (colonoscopy with snare polypectomy) is the standard treatment. No pharmacologic SoC for common sporadic polyps.
treatment_algorithm: Polypectomy (standard/standard of care). Post-polypectomy surveillance colonoscopy per ACG guidelines. Aspirin (secondary prevention in Lynch, FAP — see above). No FDA-approved drug for sporadic adenoma chemoprevention. Celecoxib for FAP. DCP-MYC inhibitor (in early trials for advanced adenomas).
us_prevalence: ~40–50% of Americans over 50 have polyps; ~15 million colonoscopies/year; ~10–15 million adenomas removed/year. Source: ACG, CDC.
patients_needing_tx: ~2–3 million patients/year with high-risk polyps potentially eligible for chemoprevention.
price: $5,000–$30,000/year for a novel chemopreventive agent.
peak_sales: $500M–$2B at 5–10% share for a novel FDA-approved adenoma chemopreventive agent.
registrational_endpoint: Reduction in adenoma recurrence rate at 3 years vs. placebo in high-risk patients with advanced adenomas at baseline.
btd_threshold_vs_soc: ≥40% relative reduction in advanced adenoma recurrence at 3 years vs. placebo.

symptoms: Painless jaundice, back pain, weight loss, new-onset diabetes, pale stools
treatment_goal: Achieve R0 resection in resectable; extend OS in metastatic; convert borderline to resectable
first_line_soc: Resectable: Whipple procedure → adjuvant FOLFIRINOX or gemcitabine + capecitabine. Metastatic 1L: FOLFIRINOX (5-FU/leucovorin/irinotecan/oxaliplatin) for fit patients; gemcitabine + nab-paclitaxel (Abraxane) for less fit.
treatment_algorithm: Resectable: surgery + adjuvant modified FOLFIRINOX × 12 cycles (PRODIGE 24). Metastatic fit: FOLFIRINOX or gem + nab-P. BRCA1/2: olaparib maintenance after platinum (POLO trial, FDA 2019). MSI-H/dMMR: pembrolizumab. Second-line: gem-based after FOLFIRINOX or vice versa; nanoliposomal irinotecan (nal-IRI, Onivyde) + 5-FU (FDA 2015). KRAS G12C (rare): sotorasib. RET: selpercatinib. Novel: KRAS G12D inhibitor (MRTX1133) in early trials; mRNA-4157 neoantigen vaccine.
us_prevalence: ~70,000 US patients living with pancreatic cancer; ~66,000 new cases/year; ~52,000 deaths/year (5-year survival ~12%). Source: ACS 2024.
patients_needing_tx: ~55,000–65,000 patients/year on active systemic therapy (most metastatic at diagnosis).
peak_sales: $1B–$3B at 10–20% share for a novel pancreatic cancer agent (e.g., KRAS G12D inhibitor).
registrational_endpoint: OS in metastatic PDAC vs. gemcitabine or gemcitabine-based doublet (FDA standard). Neoadjuvant: R0 resection rate + pCR.
btd_threshold_vs_soc: OS HR ≤0.75 vs. gem + nab-paclitaxel in 1L metastatic PDAC; FOLFIRINOX achieved OS of 11.1 vs. 6.8 months for gemcitabine (HR 0.57) as historical benchmark.

symptoms: Epigastric pain, weight loss, dysphagia, vomiting, early satiety, GI bleeding
treatment_goal: Achieve curative D2 gastrectomy; eliminate micrometastases; extend OS in metastatic
first_line_soc: Localized resectable: perioperative FLOT (5-FU/leucovorin/oxaliplatin/docetaxel) + D2 gastrectomy + adjuvant FLOT. Metastatic 1L: nivolumab + FOLFOX/CAPOX (CHECKMATE-649) for CPS≥5; pembrolizumab + FP + trastuzumab for HER2+ (KEYNOTE-811).
treatment_algorithm: Resectable: FLOT perioperative or nivolumab + FLOT (MATTERHORN trial, FDA 2024). Advanced: nivo + chemo 1L (FDA 2021); pembro + chemo ± trastuzumab for HER2+ (FDA 2021); trastuzumab + FP for HER2+ (FDA 2010). CLDN18.2+: zolbetuximab + mFOLFOX6 (FDA 2024). Second-line: ramucirumab ± paclitaxel (FDA 2014); irinotecan; docetaxel; TAS-102.
us_prevalence: ~60,000 US patients living with gastric cancer; ~27,000 new cases/year; ~11,000 deaths/year. Source: ACS 2024.
patients_needing_tx: ~20,000–25,000 patients/year on active systemic therapy.
price: $100,000–$250,000/year; zolbetuximab ~$150,000/year.
registrational_endpoint: OS and PFS in metastatic gastric/GEJ cancer vs. chemotherapy (CheckMate-649, KEYNOTE-590 co-primary: OS).
btd_threshold_vs_soc: OS HR ≤0.71 vs. FOLFOX in CPS≥5 metastatic gastric cancer.

symptoms: GI bleeding, abdominal pain, palpable mass, early satiety, perforation
treatment_goal: Achieve R0 resection; prevent recurrence with adjuvant imatinib; control metastatic disease
first_line_soc: Surgery (R0 resection) for localized. Adjuvant imatinib (Gleevec, 400 mg/day × 3 years for high-risk, FDA-approved 2012). Metastatic/unresectable 1L: imatinib 400 mg/day (FDA 2002). KIT exon 9 mutation: imatinib 800 mg/day.
treatment_algorithm: Localized: surgery ± neoadjuvant imatinib to downstage. Adjuvant: imatinib × 3 years (PERSIST-5: high-risk benefit extends to 5 years). Metastatic 1L: imatinib; imatinib-resistant: sunitinib (FDA 2006) 2L; regorafenib (FDA 2013) 3L; ripretinib (FDA 2020) 4L; avapritinib (FDA 2020) for PDGFRA D842V mutation (highly imatinib-resistant). KIT/PDGFRA genotyping mandatory.
us_prevalence: ~50,000 US patients living with GISTs; ~5,000–6,000 new cases/year. Source: SEER, NCI.
patients_needing_tx: ~30,000–40,000 patients on active imatinib or subsequent-line TKIs.
price: $80,000–$200,000/year; imatinib generic ~$3,000–$10,000/year; ripretinib ~$180,000/year.
peak_sales: $300–600M at 10–20% share for a novel pan-KIT/PDGFRA inhibitor; avapritinib ~$300M/year.
registrational_endpoint: PFS in 4L+ GIST vs. placebo (INVICTUS trial for ripretinib); ORR in PDGFRA D842V (single-arm, avapritinib 84% ORR).
btd_threshold_vs_soc: PFS HR ≤0.46 vs. placebo in 4L GIST (ripretinib achieved HR 0.36); ORR ≥80% in PDGFRA D842V GIST.

symptoms: Progressive dysphagia, weight loss, odynophagia, chest pain, regurgitation
treatment_goal: Achieve complete pathologic response with neoadjuvant chemo; extend OS in advanced
first_line_soc: Localized/resectable: neoadjuvant FLOT (5-FU/leucovorin/oxaliplatin/docetaxel) for adenocarcinoma, or carboplatin + paclitaxel + RT (CROSS protocol) for squamous. Metastatic 1L: nivolumab + chemotherapy (CHECKMATE-649); pembrolizumab + FP for squamous/adenocarcinoma.
treatment_algorithm: Resectable: FLOT → surgery → adjuvant nivolumab (CHECKMATE-577, FDA 2021) for residual disease. Advanced: nivo + chemo 1L (CPS≥5 or all-comers); pembro + FP for squamous; trastuzumab + chemo for HER2+ adenocarcinoma. Second-line: ramucirumab ± paclitaxel; docetaxel; irinotecan. Zolbetuximab + mFOLFOX6/CAPOX for CLDN18.2+ adenocarcinoma (FDA 2024).
us_prevalence: ~45,000 US patients living with esophageal cancer; ~22,000 new cases/year; ~16,000 deaths/year. Source: ACS 2024.
patients_needing_tx: ~15,000–20,000 patients on active systemic therapy for advanced disease.
price: $100,000–$250,000/year; zolbetuximab ~$150,000/year.
registrational_endpoint: OS and PFS in metastatic esophageal cancer (CheckMate-649 co-primary: OS + PFS). DFS for adjuvant setting.
btd_threshold_vs_soc: OS HR ≤0.71 vs. chemo alone in 1L CPS≥5 metastatic esophageal cancer.

symptoms: Colorectal cancer at early age, endometrial/ovarian cancer, sebaceous tumors, cancer family history
treatment_goal: Prevent CRC and extracolonic cancers; early surveillance and risk reduction
first_line_soc: Colonoscopy surveillance every 1–2 years starting age 20–25. Aspirin (600 mg/day, CAPP2 trial) to reduce CRC risk — only pharmacologic intervention with trial evidence. Prophylactic surgery for high-risk organ sites (hysterectomy/BSO after completion of childbearing).
treatment_algorithm: Colonoscopy surveillance. Aspirin 600 mg/day (CAPP2: 37% cancer reduction). Low-dose aspirin 100 mg/day (CAPP3: non-inferior currently being studied). Gynecologic surveillance + prophylactic surgery. Pembrolizumab for MSI-H CRC/endometrial arising in Lynch patients. Vaccination strategies (frameshift neoantigen vaccines, early trials). Genetic counseling for all relatives.
