us_prevalence: ~1 million US individuals with Lynch syndrome (1 in 279 prevalence); ~140,000 diagnosed. Source: NCI, NCCN.
patients_needing_tx: ~140,000 diagnosed Lynch patients; ~80,000–100,000 on surveillance/aspirin chemoprevention.
price: $10,000–$50,000/year for a Lynch-specific cancer vaccine or targeted chemopreventive.
peak_sales: $200–500M at 10–20% share for a novel Lynch syndrome cancer prevention vaccine or drug.
registrational_endpoint: Reduction in incident Lynch-associated cancer rate vs. placebo/aspirin at 5–10 years of surveillance.
btd_threshold_vs_soc: ≥50% relative risk reduction in incident CRC over 4 years vs. aspirin alone in Lynch syndrome.

symptoms: Abdominal pain, GI bleeding, obstruction, weight loss, intussusception
treatment_goal: Achieve complete surgical resection; prevent recurrence; control metastatic disease
first_line_soc: Surgery (resection with margins) for localized. Metastatic adenocarcinoma: FOLFOX ± bevacizumab (extrapolated from CRC). Carcinoid/NET: SSA. GIST: imatinib (per GIST guidelines). B-cell lymphoma: R-CHOP.
treatment_algorithm: Small bowel adenocarcinoma (most common malignant): FOLFOX ± bev (Phase 2 data); FOLFIRI as second-line. BRAFV600E: encorafenib + cetuximab (extrapolated). MSI-H: pembrolizumab. Small bowel NET: SSA, PRRT. Small bowel GIST: imatinib. Diagnosis by capsule endoscopy or deep enteroscopy.
us_prevalence: ~12,000 new small bowel malignant tumors/year; ~20,000–30,000 prevalent. Source: ACS 2024, SEER.
patients_needing_tx: ~6,000–10,000 patients/year on active systemic therapy.
peak_sales: $100–200M at 15–20% share (combined basket with other GI cancers).
registrational_endpoint: OS or PFS in metastatic small bowel adenocarcinoma vs. chemotherapy comparator (data from BALLAD trial, though underpowered).
btd_threshold_vs_soc: OS HR ≤0.72 vs. FOLFOX in metastatic small bowel adenocarcinoma.

symptoms: Anal bleeding, pain, mass, pruritus, change in bowel habits
treatment_goal: Achieve complete response with chemoradiation; preserve sphincter function
first_line_soc: Definitive concurrent chemoradiation: mitomycin-C + 5-FU + pelvic RT (Nigro protocol, >80% cure for localized). Metastatic: carboplatin + paclitaxel (first-line) + nivolumab.
treatment_algorithm: Localized: Nigro protocol (MMC + 5-FU + RT, 54 Gy). Early T1: local excision. Metastatic/recurrent: carboplatin + paclitaxel; pembrolizumab for MSI-H/PD-L1+ (FDA tumor-agnostic); nivolumab ± ipilimumab (InterAACT2 trial, nivolumab + ipilimumab FDA accepted). HPV-driven SCC: checkpoint inhibitors active.
us_prevalence: ~10,000 new US cases/year; ~35,000–40,000 prevalent. Source: ACS 2024.
patients_needing_tx: ~3,000–5,000 patients/year with metastatic/recurrent disease needing systemic therapy.
peak_sales: $100–200M at 20–30% share for a novel checkpoint or HPV-targeted agent.
registrational_endpoint: OS and PFS in metastatic anal SCC (InterAACT2: nivolumab + ipi vs. carboplatin + paclitaxel, OS co-primary).
btd_threshold_vs_soc: OS HR ≤0.72 vs. carboplatin + paclitaxel in 1L metastatic anal SCC.

symptoms: Hundreds of colorectal polyps, rectal bleeding, bowel changes, desmoid tumors
treatment_goal: Prevent colorectal cancer; manage desmoid tumors; reduce polyp burden
first_line_soc: Prophylactic colectomy (proctocolectomy with IPAA) is definitive. Sulindac (NSAID) 150 mg BID to reduce polyp number in retained rectum. Celecoxib (FDA-approved for FAP polyp reduction). For desmoid tumors: sulindac ± imatinib; sorafenib.
treatment_algorithm: Surgical colectomy is primary prevention. Celecoxib 400 mg BID reduces duodenal polyp burden (FDA approval 1999). Sulindac for rectal stump surveillance. Desmoid tumors: sulindac + tamoxifen; imatinib; sorafenib; liposomal doxorubicin; gamma-globulin infliximab. Screening: annual colonoscopy from age 10–12. APC gene testing.
us_prevalence: ~30,000–40,000 US patients with FAP (1 in 8,000–10,000 births). Source: Johns Hopkins Polyposis Registry, NCCN.
patients_needing_tx: ~10,000–15,000 patients on active pharmacologic management (celecoxib, sulindac, desmoid therapy).
price: $20,000–$80,000/year for a novel polyp-prevention agent (WNT pathway inhibitor, etc.).
peak_sales: $50–150M at 15–25% share for a novel FAP-targeting Wnt inhibitor or chemopreventive.
registrational_endpoint: Reduction in polyp number ≥30% and size in retained rectum/duodenum vs. placebo at 12 months; celecoxib standard for comparison.
btd_threshold_vs_soc: ≥50% reduction in polyp burden vs. ~28% for celecoxib in retained rectum at 12 months.

symptoms: Multiple colorectal polyps (10–100), CRC risk, extracolonic neoplasms
treatment_goal: Reduce polyp burden; prevent colorectal cancer; surveillance for extracolonic cancers
first_line_soc: Endoscopic polypectomy surveillance. Colectomy for unmanageable polyp burden or CRC. No FDA-approved pharmacologic SoC specifically for MAP.
treatment_algorithm: Colonoscopy every 1–2 years. Sulindac or celecoxib (off-label per FAP extrapolation) for polyp burden reduction. Colectomy when polyp burden exceeds endoscopic management. Upper GI surveillance for duodenal polyps. Similar chemoprevention agents to FAP.
us_prevalence: ~30,000–50,000 US patients with MUTYH-associated polyposis (MAP); ~1 in 10,000. Source: NCCN, hereditary CRC registries.
patients_needing_tx: ~10,000–20,000 patients on active polyp management.
price: $20,000–$60,000/year for a novel MUTYH-pathway chemopreventive.
peak_sales: $50–100M at 15–20% share for a novel MAP-targeted chemopreventive.
registrational_endpoint: Reduction in polyp number ≥30% at 12 months vs. placebo in MAP patients.
btd_threshold_vs_soc: ≥40% reduction in polyp burden vs. ~20% with sulindac in MAP.

symptoms: Mucocutaneous pigmentation, GI hamartomatous polyps, intussusception, cancer risk
treatment_goal: Prevent GI complications; reduce cancer risk through surveillance
first_line_soc: Surveillance colonoscopy/EGD every 2–3 years. Endoscopic polypectomy for large polyps. Rapamycin (sirolimus, mTOR inhibitor) has shown in vitro activity but no established clinical SoC.
treatment_algorithm: Endoscopic surveillance (capsule endoscopy for small bowel). Polypectomy for symptomatic/large polyps. Surgery for intussusception. mTOR inhibitors (everolimus, sirolimus) — promising but not established. COX-2 inhibitors under evaluation. Genetic counseling (STK11/LKB1 mutation).
us_prevalence: ~15,000–20,000 US patients with PJS (1 in 50,000–200,000 births). Source: NCCN, NORD.
patients_needing_tx: ~5,000–8,000 patients on active medical management.
price: $20,000–$80,000/year for an mTOR inhibitor or STK11-targeted agent.
peak_sales: $30–80M at 20–30% share for a novel mTOR or cancer preventive in PJS.
registrational_endpoint: Reduction in polyp burden ≥30% and intussusception rate at 24 months vs. placebo.
btd_threshold_vs_soc: ≥40% reduction in polyp burden vs. placebo in PJS with mTOR inhibitor.

symptoms: Dysphagia, regurgitation, chest discomfort, incidental endoscopic finding
treatment_goal: Relieve symptoms; confirm benign diagnosis; prevent dysphagia progression
first_line_soc: Endoscopic resection (EMR/ESD) or surgical resection for symptomatic leiomyomas or other benign esophageal tumors. No pharmacologic SoC.
treatment_algorithm: Asymptomatic small leiomyomas: observation. Symptomatic: endoscopic or surgical enucleation. No standard pharmacologic therapy. GIST of esophagus (rare): imatinib per GIST guidelines.
us_prevalence: ~2,000–5,000 new US cases/year of benign esophageal tumors (leiomyoma most common). Source: GI endoscopy literature.
patients_needing_tx: Rare patients with GIST of esophagus (<100/year) may need imatinib.
price: N/A for pharmacologic treatment of benign tumors.
tam_gross: Minimal; pharmacologic market essentially nonexistent for benign esophageal tumors.
peak_sales: <$1M for targeted benign esophageal tumor indication.

symptoms: Non-healing oral ulcer, pain, dysphagia, bleeding, neck mass
treatment_goal: Achieve negative surgical margins; prevent locoregional recurrence; extend OS
first_line_soc: Surgery for resectable early-stage (wide local excision ± neck dissection). Post-op: cisplatin + RT for high-risk features. Recurrent/metastatic: pembrolizumab (1L, KEYNOTE-048) or cetuximab + platin + 5-FU (EXTREME).
treatment_algorithm: Same as HNSCC in general (see Laryngeal Cancer). Oral cavity SCC: primary surgery preferred over CRT. Post-op adjuvant: cisplatin + RT for positive margins/extranodal extension. Pembrolizumab for PD-L1 CPS≥1 (FDA 2019). Nivolumab 2L (FDA 2016). HPV-negative: less IO-responsive; surgery + CRT preferred.
us_prevalence: ~55,000 new HNSCC cases/year (~25,000–30,000 oral cavity); ~150,000 prevalent. Source: ACS 2024.
patients_needing_tx: ~10,000–15,000 patients/year with recurrent/metastatic oral SCC.
registrational_endpoint: OS in recurrent/metastatic oral SCC vs. EXTREME (same endpoint as KEYNOTE-048 HNSCC).
btd_threshold_vs_soc: OS HR ≤0.65 vs. EXTREME in CPS≥20 recurrent oral SCC.

symptoms: Sore throat, dysphagia, neck mass, otalgia, voice changes (HPV-associated)
treatment_goal: Achieve remission with de-intensified chemoradiation (HPV+); extend OS in metastatic
first_line_soc: HPV+ (p16+) stage I–II: concurrent cisplatin + IMRT (or de-intensified chemoradiation per ECOG-3311). Stage III–IV: standard cisplatin + RT 70 Gy. Metastatic: pembrolizumab ± platin + 5-FU (KEYNOTE-048).
treatment_algorithm: HPV+ oropharynx: de-escalation trials ongoing (PATHOS, ECOG-3311). Standard CRT with cisplatin for stage III–IV. Recurrent/metastatic: pembrolizumab 1L (FDA 2019 for CPS≥1); nivolumab 2L; cetuximab + cisplatin + 5-FU. HPV-targeting vaccines (therapeutic): ISA101 in trials.
us_prevalence: ~20,000–22,000 new oropharyngeal SCC/year (70–80% HPV+, rising incidence); ~80,000 prevalent. Source: ACS, SEER.
patients_needing_tx: ~8,000–12,000 patients/year with recurrent/metastatic oropharyngeal SCC.
registrational_endpoint: OS and PFS in R/M HNSCC (KEYNOTE-048 OP subgroup); PFS in de-intensified CRT trials for early stage.
btd_threshold_vs_soc: OS HR ≤0.65 vs. EXTREME in CPS≥20 oropharyngeal SCC.

symptoms: Hoarseness, dysphagia, sore throat, neck mass, stridor, hemoptysis
treatment_goal: Achieve larynx preservation; cure with chemoradiation; extend OS in advanced
first_line_soc: Larynx preservation: concurrent cisplatin (100 mg/m² q3w) + radiation (larynx-preservation approach) for stage III–IV. Surgery (total laryngectomy) for selected cases. Metastatic/recurrent: cetuximab + platin + 5-FU (EXTREME regimen); or pembrolizumab ± chemo.
treatment_algorithm: Stage I–II glottic: laser microsurgery or RT. Stage III–IV larynx preservation: concurrent cisplatin + RT (VA Larynx trial model). Induction chemo (TPF: docetaxel/cisplatin/5-FU) for larynx preservation decision. Metastatic 1L: pembrolizumab ± platin + 5-FU (KEYNOTE-048, FDA 2019). Nivolumab 2L (CheckMate-141, FDA 2016). Cetuximab ± nivolumab.
us_prevalence: ~12,000 new US laryngeal cancer cases/year; ~50,000 prevalent. Source: ACS 2024.
patients_needing_tx: ~5,000–8,000 patients/year on active systemic therapy for locally advanced or metastatic disease.
peak_sales: $100–300M within HNSCC franchise; pembrolizumab HNSCC ~$1B+ globally.
registrational_endpoint: OS in recurrent/metastatic HNSCC (KEYNOTE-048 OS primary); larynx preservation rate at 12 months for locoregional.
btd_threshold_vs_soc: OS HR ≤0.65 vs. EXTREME in CPS≥20 recurrent/metastatic HNSCC.

symptoms: Painless parotid/submandibular swelling, facial nerve palsy, hard mass, rapid growth
treatment_goal: Achieve complete surgical resection; preserve facial nerve; control metastatic ACC
first_line_soc: Surgery (parotidectomy/submandibulectomy with appropriate margins) ± adjuvant RT for high-grade. Metastatic salivary gland carcinoma: lenvatinib (ORR ~16%); no clear SoC; androgen receptor (AR)-targeted therapy for salivary duct carcinoma (AR+).
treatment_algorithm: Benign: parotidectomy. Malignant: surgery + adjuvant RT. Metastatic adenoid cystic carcinoma (ACC): lenvatinib (FDA: not specifically approved); axitinib; larotrectinib for NTRK-fusion ACC; afatinib; lenvatinib. Salivary duct carcinoma (AR+): enz + trastuzumab (for HER2+); abiraterone; AR inhibitors. NF2-mutant ACC: emerging targets.
us_prevalence: ~15,000 new salivary gland cancer cases/year (malignant); ~50,000 prevalent. Source: ACS, SEER.
patients_needing_tx: ~5,000–8,000 patients/year with advanced/metastatic disease.
peak_sales: $100–200M at 15–25% share for a novel salivary gland targeting agent.
registrational_endpoint: ORR and DoR in metastatic ACC or salivary duct carcinoma (single-arm, FDA accelerated approval pathway).
btd_threshold_vs_soc: ORR ≥25% with DoR ≥6 months in 2L metastatic ACC.

symptoms: Nasal obstruction, epistaxis, facial pain, proptosis, diplopia, cheek swelling
treatment_goal: Achieve complete surgical resection with RT; control advanced disease
first_line_soc: Surgery + postoperative RT ± concurrent cisplatin for resectable disease. Unresectable: definitive concurrent cisplatin + RT. Metastatic: EXTREME regimen (cetuximab + platin + 5-FU) or pembrolizumab (KEYNOTE-048 for HNSCC).
treatment_algorithm: Resectable: endoscopic/open surgical resection + adjuvant RT ± cisplatin. Unresectable maxillary sinus: intra-arterial cisplatin + RT (RADPLAT). Metastatic: pembrolizumab ± chemo; cetuximab. Esthesioneuroblastoma: surgery + RT ± chemo; high chemosensitivity.
us_prevalence: ~2,500–3,000 new US paranasal sinus/nasal cavity cancer cases/year; ~8,000–10,000 prevalent. Source: ACS, SEER.
patients_needing_tx: ~1,000–2,000 patients/year with advanced/metastatic disease.
registrational_endpoint: ORR and OS in recurrent/metastatic HNSCC basket including sinonasal primaries.
btd_threshold_vs_soc: ORR ≥35% with DoR ≥6 months in sinonasal undifferentiated carcinoma or squamous type.

symptoms: Jaw swelling, loose teeth, facial asymmetry, pain, trismus (ameloblastoma, odontogenic tumors)
treatment_goal: Achieve complete surgical resection; preserve jaw function; prevent recurrence
first_line_soc: Surgery (marginal or segmental resection) for most jaw tumors. Ameloblastoma: surgical resection with 1–2 cm margins. No standard pharmacologic SoC for most benign jaw tumors.
treatment_algorithm: Ameloblastoma: surgery (standard). Recurrent/metastatic ameloblastoma: BRAF inhibitors (dabrafenib + trametinib for BRAFV600E mutation, which occurs in ~50% of ameloblastomas; preliminary data promising). Odontogenic myxoma/fibroma: surgical curettage. Bisphosphonate-related ONJ: prevent by avoiding bisphosphonates before dental procedures.
us_prevalence: ~2,000–5,000 new jaw tumor diagnoses/year (primarily odontogenic); BRAF-mutant ameloblastoma amenable to targeted therapy: ~500–1,000/year. Source: oral surgery literature.
patients_needing_tx: ~500–1,500 patients with advanced/recurrent jaw tumors potentially needing systemic therapy.
price: $80,000–$200,000/year for BRAF/MEK inhibitor combination.
peak_sales: $20–50M at 20–30% share (within broader BRAF inhibitor franchise).
registrational_endpoint: ORR ≥30% and DoR ≥6 months in BRAFV600E+ recurrent/metastatic ameloblastoma (FDA: tumor-agnostic or odontogenic basket).
btd_threshold_vs_soc: ORR ≥40% with DoR ≥6 months in BRAFV600E ameloblastoma.

symptoms: Epistaxis, hearing loss, diplopia, neck mass, headache, nasal obstruction
treatment_goal: Achieve complete remission with chemoradiation; prevent locoregional and distant relapse
first_line_soc: Concurrent cisplatin + radiation (definitive, >80% cure for stage I–III). Advanced (stage IVB): induction gemcitabine + cisplatin → concurrent chemoRT → adjuvant capecitabine (ADDX-NPC regimen). Metastatic: gemcitabine + cisplatin (GP regimen); camrelizumab + GP (Asian approvals).
treatment_algorithm: Definitive: concurrent cisplatin + RT. Advanced: induction GP → concurrent CRT → adjuvant capecitabine (ADAPT-NPC). Relapsed/metastatic: pembrolizumab (EBV+ enrichment). Nivolumab. EBV-targeted adoptive T-cell therapy promising. Famitinib + camrelizumab in Asia.
us_prevalence: ~500–700 new US cases/year (rare in US, endemic in SE Asia); ~2,000 prevalent. Source: SEER.
patients_needing_tx: ~400–700 patients/year on active systemic therapy.
registrational_endpoint: PFS or OS in recurrent/metastatic NPC; EBV titer-based response as surrogate.
btd_threshold_vs_soc: OS HR ≤0.68 vs. GP in 1L recurrent/metastatic NPC.

symptoms: Hearing loss, ear pain, facial nerve paralysis, otorrhea, tinnitus
treatment_goal: Complete surgical removal; preserve facial nerve; control locoregional disease
first_line_soc: Surgery (lateral temporal bone resection or total temporal bone resection) for primary ear malignancies. Adjuvant RT for high-risk features. No pharmacologic SoC specifically for otic tumors.
treatment_algorithm: Primary malignant otic tumors (SCC of EAC most common): surgery ± adjuvant RT ± cisplatin concurrent. Paragangliomas: surgery or stereotactic radiosurgery; antihormonal therapy for secreting tumors. Metastatic otic tumors: per primary. Rarest tumors: case-by-case basis.
us_prevalence: ~200–500 new primary malignant ear/temporal bone cancers/year. Source: SEER, otolaryngology literature.
patients_needing_tx: ~100–300 patients/year needing systemic therapy.
registrational_endpoint: ORR ≥25% in a basket head/neck SCC trial including otic primaries.

symptoms: Cough, hemoptysis, dyspnea, weight loss, chest pain
treatment_goal: Achieve cure in early stage; exploit driver mutations; extend OS
first_line_soc: NSCLC with driver mutation: osimertinib (EGFR), alectinib/lorlatinib (ALK+), selpercatinib (RET), sotorasib/adagrasib (KRAS G12C). NSCLC no driver/high PD-L1: pembrolizumab ± chemotherapy. SCLC extensive: etoposide + carboplatin + atezolizumab (IMpower133).
treatment_algorithm: Early NSCLC (I–II): surgery ± adjuvant osimertinib (EGFR) or atezolizumab; stereotactic body RT for inoperable. Stage III: chemoradiation + durvalumab (PACIFIC). Stage IV NSCLC: driver mutation → targeted therapy; no driver, PD-L1 ≥50% → pembrolizumab mono; PD-L1 <50% → carbo + pemetrexed/paclitaxel + pembrolizumab ± bevacizumab; KRAS G12C → sotorasib/adagrasib 2L; MET ex14 → tepotinib/capmatinib; RET → selpercatinib. SCLC: EP + atezolizumab 1L; lurbinectedin 2L. Brain mets: SRS, osimertinib CNS penetrant. Best supportive care + palliative RT.
us_prevalence: ~600,000 US patients living with lung cancer; ~236,000 new cases/year (NSCLC ~85%, SCLC ~15%). Leading cause of cancer death. Source: ACS Cancer Facts & Figures 2025, SEER.
patients_needing_tx: ~500,000 patients on active systemic therapy; ~120,000 stage IV NSCLC/year + ~35,000 ES-SCLC/year are primary drug-eligible pool.
price: $150,000–$300,000/year for targeted therapies and immunotherapy combinations.
tam_gross: ~500,000 patients × $200,000 = $100B (across all lines, multiple agents per patient)
peak_sales: $3–8B for a transformative novel agent; osimertinib (Tagrisso) achieved $5.7B in 2023; pembrolizumab lung franchise ~$8B/year.
registrational_endpoint: OS or PFS as primary endpoint in randomized Phase 3 (e.g., PFS with HR <0.70 vs. SoC in driver-altered NSCLC; OS with HR <0.75 in unselected NSCLC).
btd_threshold_vs_soc: PFS HR <0.60 or OS HR <0.70 vs. SoC in relevant biomarker-selected population.

symptoms: Cough, hemoptysis, carcinoid syndrome (flushing, diarrhea), Cushing syndrome
treatment_goal: Control tumor growth; manage hormonal symptoms; prolong survival
first_line_soc: Typical/atypical carcinoid: surgery (lobectomy/segmentectomy) for resectable disease; octreotide LAR (somatostatin analogue) for functional tumors or unresectable. Large-cell NEC and SCLC-like: platinum-etoposide chemotherapy.
treatment_algorithm: Well-differentiated typical carcinoid: surgery. Atypical carcinoid: surgery ± adjuvant therapy. Unresectable well-differentiated: everolimus (RADIANT-4 showed PFS benefit in lung NET), octreotide/lanreotide LAR, PRRT (Lu-177 dotatate for somatostatin receptor-positive). Large-cell NEC: EP or CAV chemotherapy ± immunotherapy. Carcinoid syndrome: octreotide LAR, telotristat (TPH inhibitor for diarrhea). ACTH-secreting tumor: metyrapone/ketoconazole for Cushing's.
us_prevalence: ~25,000–35,000 US patients with pulmonary NETs (carcinoids); ~5,000–7,000 large-cell NEC/year. Source: SEER, NANETS.
patients_needing_tx: ~20,000–30,000 patients requiring systemic pharmacologic therapy (octreotide, everolimus, PRRT, chemo).
price: $80,000–$200,000/year for everolimus/PRRT analogues; $150,000–$300,000/year for novel targeted agents.
peak_sales: $300–600M at 10–15% share for a novel PRRT or targeted agent; Lutathera (Lu-177 dotatate) ~$350M/year as benchmark.
registrational_endpoint: PFS as primary endpoint in randomized Phase 3 vs. octreotide LAR or placebo in progressive well-differentiated pulmonary NETs.
btd_threshold_vs_soc: PFS HR <0.60 vs. placebo (median PFS improvement of ≥6 months over ~14 months with octreotide).

symptoms: Chest wall pain, palpable mass, restricted breathing, rib tenderness
treatment_goal: Achieve complete surgical resection; reconstruct chest wall; control metastatic disease
first_line_soc: Surgery (wide local excision with chest wall reconstruction) for primary chest wall tumors. Systemic therapy per histology: sarcoma protocols for soft tissue/bone sarcomas; lung cancer protocols for direct lung invasion.
treatment_algorithm: Primary chest wall malignancies: surgery ± adjuvant RT. Ewing sarcoma of chest wall: VDC-IE chemotherapy + RT. Chondrosarcoma: surgery. Soft tissue sarcoma: doxorubicin-based or gemcitabine + docetaxel. Metastatic: treat per primary cancer histology. Desmoid tumors of chest: sulindac + tamoxifen; sorafenib.
us_prevalence: ~2,000–5,000 new primary malignant chest wall tumors/year; ~8,000–12,000 prevalent. Source: SEER.
patients_needing_tx: ~1,500–3,000 patients/year on active systemic therapy.
registrational_endpoint: PFS or ORR in resected high-risk/metastatic primary chest wall sarcomas.

symptoms: Dyspnea, stridor, cough, hemoptysis, voice changes
treatment_goal: Achieve complete resection; restore airway patency; control disease
first_line_soc: Surgery (tracheal resection with end-to-end anastomosis) for resectable primary tracheal tumors. Radiation therapy (60–66 Gy) for unresectable squamous cell carcinoma or adenoid cystic carcinoma.
treatment_algorithm: Resectable SCC or ACC: tracheal resection + RT. Unresectable: definitive RT ± chemotherapy (cisplatin + 5-FU for SCC). ACC: RT (proton preferred); systemic options limited—lenvatinib or cabozantinib off-label for ACC. Endoscopic palliation (bronchoscopic debulking, stenting) for airway compromise. Immunotherapy for PD-L1+ SCC of trachea. No FDA-approved drug specifically for primary tracheal cancer.
us_prevalence: Primary tracheal tumors: ~1,500–2,500 new cases/year in the US; ~5,000–8,000 prevalent. Extremely rare. Source: SEER, thoracic oncology literature.
patients_needing_tx: ~1,000–2,000 patients/year on systemic therapy (mainly unresectable or metastatic cases).
price: $80,000–$200,000/year for a novel EGFR or immunotherapy agent.
peak_sales: $20–50M at 15–25% share; typically subsumed within head and neck or lung cancer drug franchises.
registrational_endpoint: ORR and DoR in unresectable/metastatic tracheal tumors (single-arm accelerated approval); PFS in randomized setting.
btd_threshold_vs_soc: ORR ≥25% with DoR ≥6 months in unresectable/recurrent tracheal SCC or ACC.

symptoms: Blurry vision, glare, halos, faded colors, impaired night vision
treatment_goal: Restore vision clarity via lens removal and IOL implantation
first_line_soc: Surgical: phacoemulsification with intraocular lens (IOL) implantation. No FDA-approved pharmacologic agent that reverses cataracts; surgery is definitive treatment.
treatment_algorithm: Mild: observation, refraction adjustment, anti-glare glasses. Moderate-severe affecting function: phacoemulsification + IOL (standard monofocal, multifocal, or toric). Topical N-acetylcarnosine (Can-C) drops lack robust RCT evidence. Investigational: lanosterol eye drops, oxysterols targeting αA-crystallin aggregation in early trials. Post-operative: topical steroid + antibiotic.
us_prevalence: ~24.4 million Americans age 40+ have cataracts; ~4 million cataract surgeries/year in the US. Source: NEI, CDC Vision Health Initiative.
patients_needing_tx: ~4 million surgeries/year; pharmacologic pool limited to early-stage patients and investigational agents—estimated ~5–10 million with early cataracts who might use eye drops if approved.
price: $2,000–$8,000/year for a topical pharmacologic cataract-dissolving agent.
peak_sales: $500M–$2B at 5–10% share if a pharmacologic agent achieves FDA approval; currently a surgical market.
registrational_endpoint: Improvement in lens opacity grading (LOCS III) ≥1 grade and BCVA improvement ≥5 ETDRS letters at 12 months vs. vehicle in early age-related cataract.
btd_threshold_vs_soc: Reduction in lens opacity LOCS III score ≥1 grade in ≥40% of patients vs. <5% with vehicle at 12 months.

symptoms: Bilateral itching, redness, tearing, chemosis, eyelid edema
treatment_goal: Control itch and inflammation; reduce allergic mediators
first_line_soc: Topical dual-action antihistamine/mast cell stabilizer eye drops: olopatadine (Patanol/Pataday 0.1–0.7%), ketotifen (Zaditor/Alaway OTC). Seasonal: as-needed or daily use during allergy season.
treatment_algorithm: Mild-intermittent: topical antihistamine/mast cell stabilizer (olopatadine, ketotifen, azelastine). Moderate-severe: add topical NSAID (ketorolac), topical corticosteroid (loteprednol, fluorometholone) for short-term. Severe chronic (vernal/atopic keratoconjunctivitis): topical cyclosporine 0.05% (Restasis) or tacrolimus. Systemic antihistamines (loratadine, cetirizine). Allergen immunotherapy for persistent cases. Cryotherapy for giant papillae. Lifitegrast (Xiidra) has evidence for VKC.
us_prevalence: ~40 million US patients with allergic conjunctivitis (seasonal + perennial + VKC/AKC); ~6% of total population. Source: AAO, AAAAI.
patients_needing_tx: ~20–30 million patients using pharmacologic eye drops; ~2–3 million with severe VKC/AKC needing prescription therapy.
price: $500–$3,000/year for novel topical immunomodulatory agents; OTC drops $20–$100/year.
peak_sales: $500M–$2B for a novel prescription antiallergic ophthalmic; olopatadine (Pataday) ~$800M/year at peak.
registrational_endpoint: Total ocular symptom score (TOSS) improvement ≥1 unit from baseline at Day 14 in CAC (conjunctival allergen challenge) model; ocular itching VAS as co-primary.
btd_threshold_vs_soc: Ocular itching score reduction ≥1.5 units (0–4 scale) vs. <0.5 vehicle at 3 minutes post-CAC challenge.

symptoms: Yellow-white conjunctival deposit, mild irritation, cosmetic concern, dryness
treatment_goal: Relieve irritation; reduce inflammation; improve cosmesis
first_line_soc: Artificial tears and UV protection (sunglasses) for mild symptoms. Topical mild corticosteroids (loteprednol 0.5% BID–QID) or NSAIDs for symptomatic inflammation. Surgical excision for cosmetic concern or severe discomfort.
treatment_algorithm: Observation for asymptomatic. Symptomatic: preservative-free artificial tears, topical NSAID or mild steroid. Inflamed pinguecula: loteprednol or fluorometholone short course. Surgery (conjunctival excision ± amniotic membrane graft) for persistent symptoms or cosmesis. No pharmacologic SoC beyond symptom management.
us_prevalence: ~40–50% of adults have pingueculae; ~2–5 million with symptomatic pingueculae requiring treatment. Source: AAO.
patients_needing_tx: ~2–4 million patients using topical agents for symptom relief.
price: $500–$2,000/year for a novel topical anti-elastase or UV-protective ophthalmic.
peak_sales: $50–100M at 3–5% share for a novel Rx pinguecula treatment.
registrational_endpoint: Reduction in conjunctival hyperemia and patient-reported irritation VAS ≥50% at 4 weeks vs. vehicle.

symptoms: Chronic conjunctival scarring, symblepharon, dry eye, progressive vision loss
treatment_goal: Halt cicatrizing inflammation; preserve vision and conjunctival anatomy
first_line_soc: Systemic immunosuppression: dapsone 50–150 mg/day for mild-moderate disease; cyclophosphamide or mycophenolate mofetil for severe/progressive disease. IV rituximab for refractory cases.
treatment_algorithm: Mild (Stage I–II): dapsone 50–150 mg/day or sulfasalazine 500 mg–2g/day. Moderate-severe (Stage III–IV): methotrexate, mycophenolate mofetil, azathioprine. Refractory: IV cyclophosphamide (pulse) or rituximab 375 mg/m2 × 4 doses. IVIG for rapid progression. Topical: preservative-free lubricants, cyclosporine drops, scleral contact lenses for comfort. Surgery (mucous membrane grafting, symblepharon lysis) only after inflammation controlled.
us_prevalence: ~30,000–50,000 US patients with ocular MMP; incidence ~1 in 15,000–46,000. Source: AAO, NORD.
patients_needing_tx: ~25,000–40,000 patients requiring systemic immunosuppression.
price: $20,000–$80,000/year for rituximab or novel biologic targeting pemphigoid pathogenesis.
peak_sales: $100–250M at 10–15% share for a novel targeted biologic (e.g., anti-C5a, anti-IL-5 or anti-IgE class).
registrational_endpoint: Reduction in conjunctival inflammation grading ≥1 stage (Foster classification) and halting of cicatrization progression at 52 weeks vs. placebo/dapsone.
btd_threshold_vs_soc: Conjunctival staging regression in ≥40% vs. <10% with dapsone at 52 weeks.

symptoms: Fleshy conjunctival growth onto cornea, redness, irritation, astigmatism, vision blurring
treatment_goal: Excise pterygium; prevent recurrence; reduce inflammation
first_line_soc: Surgical excision with conjunctival autograft is gold-standard; topical mitomycin-C (0.02% intraoperative) reduces recurrence. Anti-VEGF (bevacizumab) perioperative injections showing benefit in trials.
treatment_algorithm: Asymptomatic small pterygium: UV protection, lubricants, observation. Symptomatic or growing onto visual axis: surgical excision with conjunctival autograft (recurrence ~5–10%) ± intraoperative MMC. Recurrence adjuvant: postoperative topical cyclosporine, bevacizumab 1.25 mg subconjunctival injection. Topical NSAIDs/steroids post-op for inflammation.
us_prevalence: ~2–4 million US patients with pterygium; ~200,000–400,000 surgeries/year. Source: AAO, NIDDK.
patients_needing_tx: ~200,000–400,000 surgical candidates/year; ~1–2 million using topical agents for symptom control.
price: $1,000–$5,000/course for anti-VEGF or anti-recurrence topical agent.
peak_sales: $50–150M at 10–15% share for a novel anti-recurrence agent (anti-VEGF, anti-fibrotic).
registrational_endpoint: Pterygium recurrence rate at 12 months post-excision vs. placebo (bare sclera or autograft alone).
btd_threshold_vs_soc: Recurrence rate <5% vs. ~15–40% with bare sclera excision alone at 12 months.

symptoms: Watery discharge, red eye, follicular reaction, photophobia, preauricular lymphadenopathy
treatment_goal: Relieve symptoms; prevent spread; manage corneal involvement
first_line_soc: Supportive care: cold compresses, preservative-free artificial tears, strict hygiene to prevent spread. No FDA-approved antiviral for most viral conjunctivitis (adenoviral). Topical corticosteroids for pseudomembranes.
treatment_algorithm: Adenoviral (most common): supportive care only; topical povidone-iodine 0.4% may reduce viral shedding. HSV conjunctivitis: topical trifluridine 1% or ganciclovir 0.15% gel + oral valacyclovir. EKC (epidemic keratoconjunctivitis): povidone-iodine, topical mild steroid for pseudomembranes and corneal infiltrates. Enteroviral: supportive. Avoid antibiotic drops (unnecessary). Strict contact precautions.
us_prevalence: ~1–2 million US cases/year; viral accounts for ~50% of acute conjunctivitis. Source: AAO, CDC.
patients_needing_tx: ~500,000–1,000,000 patients seeking medical care; ~100,000–200,000 HSV cases needing antiviral.
price: $300–$1,500/course for a novel antiadenoviral ophthalmic.
peak_sales: $50–150M for a novel topical antiadenoviral agent (povidone-iodine-based or small molecule); several in development.
registrational_endpoint: Viral eradication (culture negative) and clinical cure (hyperemia, discharge) at Day 7 vs. vehicle in adenoviral conjunctivitis.
btd_threshold_vs_soc: Clinical/microbiologic cure ≥70% at Day 7 vs. ~40% vehicle in adenoviral EKC.

symptoms: Red eye, purulent discharge, crusting, foreign body sensation
treatment_goal: Eradicate bacteria; reduce symptoms; prevent spread
first_line_soc: Topical ophthalmic antibiotics: polymyxin B/trimethoprim (Polytrim) or erythromycin ointment for mild cases; fluoroquinolone eye drops (ciprofloxacin, ofloxacin, moxifloxacin) for moderate-severe or contact lens wearers.
treatment_algorithm: Most bacterial conjunctivitis is self-limited (resolves in 7–14 days without treatment). Topical antibiotics shorten duration by ~1–2 days. Polymyxin/trimethoprim drops QID × 7–10 days. Fluoroquinolones for Pseudomonas risk (contact lens wearers) or gonorrheal conjunctivitis (IM ceftriaxone 1g single dose + topical irrigation). Neonatal gonococcal: IV ceftriaxone. Chlamydial: oral azithromycin 1g single dose.
us_prevalence: ~1–3 million US cases/year; bacterial accounts for ~50% of all acute conjunctivitis. Source: AAO, CDC.
patients_needing_tx: ~1–2 million patients/year using Rx topical antibiotics.
price: $50–$200/course for generic topical antibiotics; novel formulations $300–$800/course.
peak_sales: $50–150M for a novel antibiotic formulation (e.g., besifloxacin [Besivance] ~$80M/year as benchmark).
registrational_endpoint: Clinical cure rate (resolution of discharge and conjunctival injection) at Day 7 vs. vehicle in bacterial conjunctivitis; co-primary: microbial eradication.
btd_threshold_vs_soc: Clinical cure rate ≥85% vs. ~65% vehicle at Day 7.

symptoms: Severe deep eye pain, photophobia, red/violaceous sclera, decreased vision
treatment_goal: Control scleral inflammation; prevent scleral thinning and vision loss
first_line_soc: Oral NSAIDs (indomethacin 25–50 mg TID or ibuprofen 400–600 mg TID) for non-necrotizing scleritis. Systemic corticosteroids (prednisone 1 mg/kg/day) for NSAID-refractory or necrotizing scleritis.
treatment_algorithm: Non-necrotizing anterior: oral NSAIDs. NSAID failure: oral prednisone 1 mg/kg/day with taper. Necrotizing: high-dose systemic corticosteroids + immunosuppressant (methotrexate, mycophenolate, cyclophosphamide). Scleritis with systemic vasculitis (GPA): cyclophosphamide or rituximab. Posterior scleritis: systemic steroids ± orbital floor injection. Anti-TNF agents (infliximab, adalimumab) for refractory JIA-associated scleritis. Treat underlying systemic disease.
us_prevalence: ~14,000–30,000 US cases/year; often associated with systemic autoimmune disease in ~50%. Source: AAO, rheumatology literature.
patients_needing_tx: ~15,000–25,000 patients/year requiring systemic therapy.
price: $15,000–$50,000/year for targeted biologics (anti-TNF, anti-IL-6).
peak_sales: $50–150M at 10–20% share for a novel targeted biologic for scleritis.
registrational_endpoint: Scleritis activity score (SAS) improvement ≥2 grades and reduction in pain VAS at 12 weeks vs. placebo/NSAIDs.
btd_threshold_vs_soc: Response rate (SAS improvement ≥2) ≥60% vs. ~25% NSAID at 12 weeks.

symptoms: Chronic red eye, mucopurulent discharge, follicular conjunctivitis, lymphadenopathy
treatment_goal: Eradicate Chlamydia trachomatis; prevent corneal scarring
first_line_soc: Oral azithromycin 1g single dose or oral doxycycline 100 mg BID × 7 days. Topical tetracycline or erythromycin ointment adjunctive but insufficient alone.
treatment_algorithm: Systemic antibiotic mandatory (topical alone inadequate): azithromycin 1g PO single dose (first-line for convenience) or doxycycline 100 mg PO BID × 7 days. Treat sexual partner(s) concurrently. Screen for other STIs. Topical lubricants for comfort. Resolution expected in 2–6 weeks.
us_prevalence: ~500,000–1,000,000 US cases/year (associated with ~4 million chlamydial STI cases). Source: CDC STI Surveillance.
patients_needing_tx: ~500,000–1,000,000 patients/year requiring systemic antibiotic treatment.
price: $200–$800/course for a novel topical chlamydial agent.
peak_sales: $20–50M for a novel topical Chlamydia-targeting ophthalmic agent vs. cheap generic oral antibiotics.
registrational_endpoint: Microbiologic eradication of C. trachomatis from conjunctival swab at Day 28 vs. vehicle; clinical resolution (follicle score) as secondary.
btd_threshold_vs_soc: Microbiologic cure ≥80% at Day 28 vs. <10% vehicle.

symptoms: Painless bright red patch on sclera, typically unilateral, no vision change
treatment_goal: Reassure patient; rule out underlying coagulopathy
first_line_soc: None pharmacologic required. Self-resolving in 2–4 weeks. Artificial tears for irritation. Evaluate for hypertension or bleeding disorder if recurrent.
treatment_algorithm: Observation and reassurance for spontaneous cases. Control hypertension if elevated BP identified. Discontinue anticoagulants/NSAIDs if causative. Artificial tears for comfort. No pharmacologic treatment needed.
us_prevalence: Very common; estimated ~1–2 million US episodes/year. Typically benign. Source: AAO.
patients_needing_tx: Minimal pharmacologic treatment pool; ~100,000–200,000 with recurrent or concerning hemorrhages warrantign workup.
price: $200–$1,000/course for a topical fibrinolytic or hemostatic accelerant.
peak_sales: <$10M; no viable drug market; condition is self-limited.
registrational_endpoint: Time to complete resolution of subconjunctival hemorrhage at 14 days vs. observation.

symptoms: Localized eye redness, mild pain, tearing, no vision change
treatment_goal: Relieve pain and inflammation; prevent recurrence
first_line_soc: Topical NSAIDs (ketorolac 0.5% QID) or topical corticosteroids (fluorometholone 0.1% QID) for symptomatic relief. Mild cases: artificial tears and observation.
treatment_algorithm: Simple episcleritis: usually self-limiting in 2–3 weeks; topical NSAID or lubricants for comfort. Nodular episcleritis: topical corticosteroid ± oral NSAID (ibuprofen 400 mg TID). Systemic underlying disease (RA, IBD): treat primary condition. Systemic corticosteroids rarely needed. Recurrent episcleritis: investigate for systemic rheumatic disease.
us_prevalence: ~100,000–300,000 US cases/year; typically benign and self-limiting. Source: AAO, epidemiology literature.
patients_needing_tx: ~100,000–200,000 patients/year using topical treatments.
price: $200–$1,000/course for a novel anti-inflammatory ophthalmic.
peak_sales: $5–15M; treated with cheap generics; very limited novel drug opportunity.
registrational_endpoint: Resolution of episclerotic injection and pain VAS reduction ≥50% at Day 14 vs. vehicle.

symptoms: Follicular conjunctivitis, eyelid scarring, trichiasis, corneal opacity, blindness
treatment_goal: Eradicate Chlamydia trachomatis; prevent corneal blindness
first_line_soc: Oral azithromycin 1g single dose (community mass drug administration for endemic areas) or tetracycline eye ointment 1% BID × 6 weeks as alternative.
treatment_algorithm: WHO SAFE strategy: Surgery (trichiasis correction), Antibiotics (azithromycin mass treatment), Facial cleanliness, Environmental improvement. Single-dose azithromycin achieves >80% microbiologic cure. Topical tetracycline for areas without azithromycin. Surgical eyelid rotation (bilamellar tarsal rotation) for trichiasis. Corneal transplant for opacities. Azithromycin 1g PO annually for communities with >10% prevalence.
us_prevalence: Trachoma is essentially eliminated in the US (~100–500 cases/year in immigrant/Native American populations). Global burden: 125 million at risk. Source: CDC, WHO.
patients_needing_tx: ~300–500 US patients/year; a US drug market essentially does not exist.
price: $5–$20/dose for public health programs; ~$500–$2,000 for Rx novel topical agents.
peak_sales: <$1M in the US; global market for novel trachoma drugs exists but primarily public health/charitable context.
registrational_endpoint: Microbiologic eradication of C. trachomatis from conjunctival swab at 28 days; TF (trachomatous follicular) grade reduction.
btd_threshold_vs_soc: Single-dose cure rate ≥90% vs. ~80% azithromycin at Day 28.

symptoms: Dry gritty eyes, burning, redness, blurred vision, foreign body sensation
treatment_goal: Restore tear film; reduce ocular surface inflammation; improve symptoms
first_line_soc: Artificial tears (preservative-free) as foundation. Topical cyclosporine 0.05%/0.09% (Restasis/Cequa) BID or lifitegrast 5% (Xiidra) BID for inflammatory dry eye disease.
treatment_algorithm: Mild: preservative-free artificial tears q1–2h, omega-3 fatty acids, lid hygiene, warm compresses. Moderate: topical cyclosporine 0.05–0.09% (Restasis, Cequa) or lifitegrast 5% (Xiidra). Severe: punctal plugs, topical corticosteroid short-term (loteprednol), autologous serum tears, scleral lenses. Secretagogues: oral pilocarpine or cevimeline for aqueous deficiency (Sjögren's). Novel: perfluorohexyloctane (Miebo) drops — FDA-approved 2023 for EDE.
us_prevalence: ~16–20 million US adults with dry eye disease; ~6–8 million with moderate-severe Sjögren's or inflammatory DED needing Rx. Source: NEI, TFOS DEWS II 2017.
patients_needing_tx: ~8–12 million patients using prescription eye drops; ~3–5 million with refractory disease needing advanced therapies.
price: $3,000–$8,000/year for novel topical anti-inflammatory/secretagogue agents.
peak_sales: $500M–$3B for a novel superior DED therapy; Xiidra ~$700M/year, Restasis ~$400M/year, Miebo ~$200M (2024 ramp) as benchmarks.
registrational_endpoint: Symptom improvement (EDS VAS ≥10 mm) and sign improvement (corneal fluorescein staining reduction ≥0.5 units) at 84 days vs. vehicle — FDA requires both symptom + sign co-primary endpoints.
btd_threshold_vs_soc: Symptom + sign co-primary met with ≥10% responder advantage vs. vehicle at Day 84.

symptoms: Progressive corneal thinning, irregular astigmatism, halos, distorted vision
treatment_goal: Halt progression; improve visual acuity; avoid corneal transplant
first_line_soc: Corneal collagen cross-linking (CXL) with riboflavin + UV-A (iLink system, FDA-approved 2016) to halt progression. Rigid gas-permeable or scleral contact lenses for visual correction.
treatment_algorithm: Mild: RGP/scleral lenses for vision correction. Progressive keratoconus: CXL (riboflavin 0.1% + UV-A 365 nm) — gold standard to halt progression. Post-CXL monitoring. Advanced: intrastromal corneal ring segments (Intacs) for visual rehabilitation. Severe/scarred: deep anterior lamellar keratoplasty (DALK) or PKP. Investigational: pharmacologic corneal stiffening agents (genipin, ribose cross-linkers) and gene therapy for ectasia.
us_prevalence: ~200,000–300,000 US patients with keratoconus; ~5,000–10,000 new diagnoses/year. Source: NEI, NKCF.
patients_needing_tx: ~100,000–150,000 patients with progressive keratoconus eligible for CXL or pharmacologic intervention; ~50,000–80,000 using RGP/scleral lenses.
price: $5,000–$20,000/treatment course for a novel topical cross-linking or anti-ectasia pharmacologic agent.
peak_sales: $100–250M at 15–20% share for a novel non-UV-A pharmacologic CXL agent; iLink CXL procedure currently ~$3,000–$5,000/eye surgical.
registrational_endpoint: Halting of keratometric progression (Kmax change <1.0 D from baseline) and improvement in CDVA at 12 months vs. sham/placebo.
btd_threshold_vs_soc: Progression arrest in ≥80% vs. ~40% natural history at 12 months.

symptoms: Dendritic corneal lesion, eye pain, photophobia, tearing, decreased vision
treatment_goal: Suppress HSV replication; prevent stromal scarring and recurrence
first_line_soc: Topical ganciclovir 0.15% gel (Zirgan) 5× daily until healed (FDA-approved 2009) or trifluridine 1% drops for epithelial keratitis. Oral acyclovir/valacyclovir for stromal keratitis.
treatment_algorithm: Epithelial keratitis: topical ganciclovir 0.15% × 7–10 days; topical trifluridine + debridement alternative. Stromal keratitis: oral acyclovir 400 mg 5×/day or valacyclovir 1g BID × 10–14 days + topical prednisolone with antiviral cover. Prophylaxis of recurrence: oral acyclovir 400 mg BID or valacyclovir 500 mg/day (HEDS trial evidence). Endothelial keratitis: oral antiviral + topical steroid. PKP for severe scarring.
us_prevalence: ~400,000 US cases/year (recurrent); ~50,000 new primary episodes. Leading infectious cause of corneal blindness in developed world. Source: NEI, HEDS study.
patients_needing_tx: ~300,000–400,000 patients/year on antiviral therapy (topical or oral); ~100,000 on chronic suppressive therapy.
price: $1,500–$8,000/year for a novel topical HSV antiviral or immunomodulator.
peak_sales: $100–200M for a novel superior HSV antiviral with fewer recurrences; ganciclovir gel (Zirgan) ~$100M/year as benchmark.
registrational_endpoint: Time to healing of dendritic ulcer and proportion healed at Day 7 vs. trifluridine 1% (non-inferiority or superiority); recurrence rate at 12 months for prophylaxis studies.
btd_threshold_vs_soc: Recurrence reduction ≥50% vs. ~32% annual recurrence rate with acyclovir 400 mg BID in HEDS suppression arm.

symptoms: Unilateral facial rash (V1), eye pain, red eye, corneal involvement, risk of postherpetic neuralgia
treatment_goal: Shorten outbreak; prevent corneal and neurologic complications
first_line_soc: Oral valacyclovir 1g TID × 7 days (preferred) or acyclovir 800 mg 5×/day × 7–10 days within 72 hours of rash onset. Topical prednisolone 1% for stromal keratitis or uveitis.
treatment_algorithm: Antiviral: valacyclovir 1g TID × 7 days (reduces PHN and ocular complications). Topical therapy: lubricants, cycloplegic (cyclopentolate) for IOP and ciliary spasm, topical steroid for stromal keratitis. PHN prevention/treatment: gabapentin, pregabalin, tricyclics. Vaccination: recombinant zoster vaccine (Shingrix) for prevention (non-pharmacologic treatment but key public health). IV acyclovir for immunocompromised or severe cases.
us_prevalence: ~1 million shingles cases/year; HZO involves the eye in ~10–20%, so ~100,000–200,000 HZO cases/year. Source: CDC, NEI.
patients_needing_tx: ~100,000–200,000 patients/year requiring systemic antiviral for HZO.
price: $1,500–$8,000/course for a novel antiviral or neuroprotective agent.
peak_sales: $50–150M for a novel HZO-specific antiviral or anti-PHN agent; primarily Shingrix vaccination dominates prevention.
registrational_endpoint: Time to rash resolution and incidence of PHN at 6 months vs. standard valacyclovir in HZO.
btd_threshold_vs_soc: PHN rate <10% vs. ~18–25% with standard valacyclovir at 6 months.

symptoms: Painful blisters on cornea, blurred vision, tearing, corneal edema
treatment_goal: Reduce corneal edema; alleviate pain; restore vision with transplant
first_line_soc: Topical hypertonic saline (NaCl 5% drops QID and ointment HS) to reduce corneal edema. Endothelial keratoplasty (DMEK/DSAEK) for visual rehabilitation.
treatment_algorithm: Medical: hypertonic saline 5% drops/ointment, therapeutic soft contact lens for pain. Surgical: Descemet membrane endothelial keratoplasty (DMEK) or DSAEK for visual restoration. Penetrating keratoplasty (PK) historically, now largely replaced by EK. Novel: ROCK inhibitor drops (ripasudil, netarsudil) promote endothelial cell regeneration — approved in Japan; Phase 3 in US. RHOBTB3-targeted endothelial cell therapy under investigation.
us_prevalence: ~40,000–60,000 US patients; ~30,000 corneal transplants/year for bullous keratopathy (leading indication). Source: EBAA, AAO.
patients_needing_tx: ~40,000 patients on medical management; ~25,000–30,000/year needing surgery or novel pharmacologic corneal endothelial regeneration.
price: $5,000–$20,000/year for a topical ROCK inhibitor or endothelial cell proliferative agent.
peak_sales: $100–200M at 20–30% share for a validated ROCK inhibitor or endothelial regeneration therapy (netarsudil-class).
registrational_endpoint: Improvement in corneal endothelial cell density (≥500 cells/mm² gain) and reduction in central corneal thickness at 12 months vs. hypertonic saline.
btd_threshold_vs_soc: Central corneal thickness reduction ≥100 µm and ECD gain ≥500 cells/mm² in ≥50% of patients at 12 months.

symptoms: Multiple tiny corneal epithelial erosions, photophobia, foreign body sensation, tearing
treatment_goal: Identify and remove causative agent; promote corneal re-epithelialization
first_line_soc: Remove causative agent (preservative-containing drops, UV exposure, dry eye, contact lens over-wear). Preservative-free artificial tears QID. Topical antibiotic for bacterial etiology; antiviral for herpetic SPK.
treatment_algorithm: Treat underlying cause (DED, blepharitis, preservative toxicity, UV keratitis, trachoma). Preservative-free lubricants q1–2h. Topical NSAID for UV keratoconjunctivitis pain. Topical cyclosporine for inflammatory SPK. BCL for comfort in severe cases. Resolution expected in days to weeks with cause removed.
us_prevalence: ~200,000–500,000 US cases/year (common presenting sign rather than primary diagnosis). Source: AAO.
patients_needing_tx: ~200,000–400,000 patients/year using topical treatments.
price: $500–$2,000/course for a novel corneal healing accelerant.
peak_sales: $10–30M for a novel corneal epithelial healing agent; largely managed with cheap generics.
registrational_endpoint: SPK grade resolution (Oxford staining scale) at Day 14 vs. vehicle/standard care.

symptoms: Crescent-shaped peripheral corneal ulcer, pain, photophobia, vision loss
treatment_goal: Suppress autoimmune corneal destruction; treat underlying systemic disease
first_line_soc: Systemic immunosuppression: oral corticosteroids (prednisone 1 mg/kg/day) + methotrexate or mycophenolate. Topical preservative-free lubricants and antibiotic prophylaxis.
treatment_algorithm: Treat underlying condition (RA, GPA, SLE, relapsing polychondritis). Prednisone 1 mg/kg/day + steroid-sparing agent (methotrexate 7.5–25 mg/week or mycophenolate 2–3 g/day). Rituximab for GPA-associated PUK. Anti-TNF (infliximab) for RA-associated. Topical N-acetylcysteine (20%) may reduce collagenase. Cycloplegic. Urgent: tissue adhesive ± BCL for imminent perforation; emergency PKP for perforation.
us_prevalence: ~5,000–15,000 US cases/year; ~50% associated with systemic autoimmune disease. Source: AAO, rheumatology literature.
patients_needing_tx: ~5,000–12,000 patients requiring systemic immunosuppression.
price: $15,000–$60,000/year for targeted biologics.
peak_sales: $30–80M at 15% share for a novel targeted biologic for autoimmune corneal disease.
registrational_endpoint: Halting of peripheral corneal thinning (slit-lamp pachymetry) and reduction in ulcer size ≥50% at 12 weeks vs. corticosteroids alone.
btd_threshold_vs_soc: Ulcer resolution in ≥70% vs. ~40% with steroids alone at 12 weeks.

symptoms: Severe eye pain, photophobia, redness, purulent discharge, vision loss
treatment_goal: Eradicate infection; prevent corneal perforation; preserve vision
first_line_soc: Topical fluoroquinolone eye drops (moxifloxacin 0.5% or ciprofloxacin 0.3%) every 1–2 hours initially for bacterial ulcers. Culture-directed therapy. Fungal: topical natamycin 5% (FDA-approved); voriconazole for refractory.
treatment_algorithm: Bacterial (most common): loading dose topical fluoroquinolone or fortified antibiotics (tobramycin 14 mg/mL + cefazolin 50 mg/mL) alternating every 30–60 min initially. Adjust per culture. Fungal: topical natamycin 5% QID–8×/day; consider oral voriconazole for deep/severe. Acanthamoeba: PHMB 0.02% + propamidine isethionate 0.1% × weeks-months. Herpes: topical ganciclovir or trifluridine + oral antiviral. Surgical: penetrating keratoplasty for perforation or failed medical therapy.
us_prevalence: ~25,000–50,000 US cases/year; contact lens wearers are highest risk. Source: NEI, AAO.
patients_needing_tx: ~25,000–50,000 patients/year requiring aggressive topical and sometimes systemic antimicrobial therapy.
price: $1,000–$5,000/course for a novel broad-spectrum antimicrobial ophthalmic.
peak_sales: $20–50M at 20% share; competitive with multiple generic fluoroquinolones.
registrational_endpoint: Ulcer healing rate (complete re-epithelialization and stromal clearing) and BCVA improvement at Day 21 vs. standard fluoroquinolone.
btd_threshold_vs_soc: Healing rate ≥80% at Day 21 vs. ~60% with ciprofloxacin in bacterial corneal ulcers.

symptoms: Corneal stromal inflammation, photophobia, pain, vascularization, vision loss
treatment_goal: Treat underlying infection; suppress corneal inflammation
first_line_soc: Topical corticosteroids (prednisolone acetate 1% QID) for stromal inflammation plus systemic treatment of underlying cause (syphilis: IV/IM penicillin G; TB: RIPE therapy; HSV: oral acyclovir/valacyclovir).
treatment_algorithm: Syphilitic IK: IV penicillin G 18–24 million units/day × 10–14 days + topical prednisolone. Tuberculous IK: standard RIPE therapy + topical steroid. HSV/HZV IK: oral antiviral + topical steroid ± antiviral. Cogan syndrome: systemic steroids (see Cogan entry). Cycloplegic drops for iritis. PKP for severe corneal opacification.
us_prevalence: ~10,000–25,000 US cases/year (various etiologies). Source: AAO, CDC STI surveillance.
patients_needing_tx: ~10,000–20,000 patients/year requiring treatment.
price: $2,000–$10,000/course for a novel anti-inflammatory corneal agent.
registrational_endpoint: Reduction in corneal vascularity and stromal haze grade ≥2 units at 12 weeks vs. steroid alone.

symptoms: Interstitial keratitis, vestibuloauditory dysfunction, hearing loss, tinnitus
treatment_goal: Suppress inflammation; preserve hearing and vision
first_line_soc: High-dose systemic corticosteroids (prednisone 1–2 mg/kg/day) for acute attacks plus topical corticosteroids for corneal disease. Early treatment critical to preserve hearing.
treatment_algorithm: Acute: prednisone 1–2 mg/kg/day + topical prednisolone acetate 1% QID for IK. Steroid-sparing/refractory: methotrexate, mycophenolate, azathioprine. Refractory systemic vasculitis component: cyclophosphamide or rituximab (anti-CD20). TNF inhibitors (infliximab, adalimumab) have case series evidence. Cochlear implant for irreversible hearing loss. Vestibular rehabilitation.
us_prevalence: Very rare: ~200–400 US cases/year; ~1,000–2,000 prevalent. Source: NORD, orphan disease databases.
patients_needing_tx: ~500–1,000 patients on immunosuppressive therapy.
price: $30,000–$100,000/year for a targeted biologic.
peak_sales: $5–15M at 20% share; very rare orphan disease.
registrational_endpoint: Composite of corneal inflammation resolution (IK grade) and auditory threshold improvement (≥15 dB) at 24 weeks vs. corticosteroids alone.
btd_threshold_vs_soc: Preservation of hearing (no >15 dB loss) in ≥70% vs. ~40% with steroids alone at 24 weeks.

symptoms: Localized nodular conjunctival/corneal lesion, tearing, photophobia, eye redness
treatment_goal: Resolve phlycten; treat underlying hypersensitivity (TB, Staph)
first_line_soc: Topical corticosteroids (prednisolone acetate 1% QID) as primary treatment. Treat underlying cause: anti-TB therapy (RIPE) for TB-associated; topical antibiotic for Staphylococcal blepharitis.
treatment_algorithm: Topical prednisolone acetate 1% QID with gradual taper × 4–6 weeks. Staphylococcal blepharitis: lid hygiene + topical azithromycin 1% gel or erythromycin ointment + oral doxycycline 100 mg BID for meibomian dysfunction. TB: standard RIPE. Cyclosporine 0.05% for recurrent disease. Topical lubricants.
us_prevalence: ~5,000–15,000 US cases/year; currently more common in immunocompromised patients or those with chronic blepharitis. Source: AAO.
patients_needing_tx: ~5,000–12,000 patients/year using topical corticosteroids.
price: $500–$2,000/course for a novel topical anti-inflammatory.
peak_sales: <$2M; treated with cheap generic topical steroids.
registrational_endpoint: Phlycten resolution rate and symptom score improvement at Day 14 vs. vehicle.

symptoms: Corneal softening/melting, night blindness, xerophthalmia, risk of perforation
treatment_goal: Urgent vitamin A replacement; prevent corneal perforation and blindness
first_line_soc: High-dose vitamin A: IM/oral retinol palmitate 200,000 IU on Day 1, 2, and 14 (WHO protocol). Topical lubricants and antibiotic prophylaxis.
treatment_algorithm: Emergency vitamin A: 200,000 IU IM/oral × 3 doses (Day 1, Day 2, Day 14). Topical lubricating ointment frequently. Prophylactic topical antibiotics. Patch for comfort. Nutritional rehabilitation. Surgical: conjunctival flap or amniotic membrane transplant for perforation. PKP if cornea heals with dense scarring.
us_prevalence: Extremely rare in the US (<500 cases/year, primarily in severely malnourished or immunocompromised patients). Source: NIH, WHO.
patients_needing_tx: ~200–500 US patients/year.
price: $1,000–$5,000/course for Rx vitamin A and topical protective agents.
tam_gross: ~350 patients × $2,500 = $875K (US); global market in endemic regions is larger but not US commercial.
registrational_endpoint: Corneal re-epithelialization rate and BCVA improvement at 4 weeks vs. standard vitamin A protocol.

symptoms: Eyelid margin redness, crusting, burning, foreign body sensation, morning stickiness
treatment_goal: Control lid margin inflammation; reduce bacterial colonization and Demodex
first_line_soc: Eyelid hygiene (warm compresses + lid scrubs) BID. Topical azithromycin 1% ophthalmic solution (AzaSite) for posterior blepharitis. Oral doxycycline 100 mg BID × 4–8 weeks for refractory meibomian gland dysfunction.
treatment_algorithm: Anterior (staph): lid scrubs, topical bacitracin/erythromycin ointment, baby shampoo scrubs. Posterior/MGD: warm compresses + meibomian gland expression, topical azithromycin 1%, oral doxycycline/minocycline. Demodex blepharitis: topical lotilaner 0.25% (Xdemvy, FDA-approved 2023) BID × 6 weeks — first specific Demodex treatment. Omega-3 supplements for meibomian gland function. IPL treatment for MGD.
us_prevalence: ~25–50 million US adults with blepharitis symptoms; ~3.6 million with Demodex blepharitis. Source: AAO, Tarsus Pharmaceuticals Xdemvy prescribing data.
patients_needing_tx: ~10–20 million patients using topical/oral treatments; ~3–4 million with Demodex blepharitis eligible for lotilaner.
price: $800–$3,000/course; Xdemvy (lotilaner) ~$800/6-week course.
tam_gross: ~15,000,000 Rx patients × $2,000 = $30B (broad Rx blepharitis market); Demodex-specific: ~3,500,000 × $800 = $2.8B
peak_sales: $300–600M for Xdemvy (lotilaner) at peak (Tarsus estimates $500M+ peak sales); $200–500M for novel MGD/inflammatory blepharitis agents.
registrational_endpoint: Collarette cure rate (0 collarettes on ≥1 lid per eye) at Day 43 vs. vehicle — used in SATURN-1/2 trials for Demodex blepharitis.
btd_threshold_vs_soc: Collarette cure in ≥50% vs. ~14% vehicle at Day 43 (SATURN trial results for Xdemvy).

symptoms: Visible eyelid mass, variable pain, eyelid distortion, vision change if large
treatment_goal: Excise or treat growth; rule out malignancy; restore eyelid function
first_line_soc: Surgical excision with histopathologic examination is standard for most eyelid growths. Topical imiquimod 5% cream for select superficial BCC or sebaceous hyperplasia. Watchful waiting for clearly benign asymptomatic lesions.
treatment_algorithm: Benign (cysts, papillomas, nevi): surgical excision or observation. BCC of eyelid: Mohs surgery; vismodegib (Erivedge) for locally advanced or metastatic eyelid BCC (systemic). SCC: surgical excision + Mohs; cemiplimab for advanced SCC. Sebaceous carcinoma: wide excision ± orbital exenteration. Xanthelasma: topical trichloroacetic acid, CO2 laser, surgical excision. Dermoid cyst: surgical excision.
us_prevalence: Eyelid tumors are among the most common facial tumors; ~200,000–400,000 eyelid lesions excised/year in the US. BCC of eyelid: ~30,000–50,000/year. Source: AAO, SEER.
patients_needing_tx: ~200,000–400,000 patients/year seeking treatment; ~80,000–100,000 with malignant eyelid tumors needing pharmacologic adjuncts.
price: $3,000–$50,000/year depending on whether topical or systemic (vismodegib ~$100,000/year).
tam_gross: ~100,000 malignant patients × $30,000 = $3B (subsumed in skin cancer franchise)
peak_sales: $50–150M for a novel topical or targeted agent specific to eyelid malignancies.
registrational_endpoint: Complete response rate (CR) or ORR in locally advanced eyelid BCC or SCC at 6 months vs. Mohs/SoC.
btd_threshold_vs_soc: ORR ≥30% in locally advanced unresectable eyelid BCC (aligned with vismodegib data in Hedgehog pathway tumors).

symptoms: Outward-turning lower eyelid, epiphora, conjunctival exposure, irritation
treatment_goal: Restore eyelid position; protect cornea; relieve symptoms
first_line_soc: Surgical repair (lateral tarsal strip or medial spindle procedure) is definitive treatment. Lubricating eye drops and ointment for symptomatic relief pending surgery.
treatment_algorithm: Mild: preservative-free artificial tears, ointment HS, taping of lid. Moderate-severe: surgical correction (lateral tarsal strip for involutional ectropion; skin grafting for cicatricial). Paralytic ectropion: tarsorrhaphy or gold weight implant for lagophthalmos. No pharmacologic treatment reverses structural ectropion.
us_prevalence: ~200,000–400,000 US patients with symptomatic ectropion; predominantly elderly. Source: AAO.
patients_needing_tx: ~150,000–300,000 patients using lubricants; ~100,000–200,000/year undergoing surgical correction.
price: $1,000–$5,000/year for topical collagen-stimulating or supportive agents.
peak_sales: $10–20M for a novel Rx topical agent; primarily a surgical condition.
registrational_endpoint: Reduction in exposure keratopathy (fluorescein staining) and patient-reported tearing at 8 weeks vs. artificial tears in symptomatic ectropion.

symptoms: Painless eyelid lump, lid swelling, occasional blurring from pressure on cornea
treatment_goal: Resolve chalazion; reduce lid inflammation; prevent recurrence
first_line_soc: Warm compresses 10–15 minutes QID for 4–6 weeks (conservative management). Intralesional triamcinolone acetonide 0.1–0.2 mL injection for persistent chalazia. Surgical incision and curettage (I&C) for large non-resolving lesions.
treatment_algorithm: Conservative: warm compresses BID-QID × 4–6 weeks. If not resolved: intralesional corticosteroid (triamcinolone 40 mg/mL × 0.05–0.2 mL). I&C under local anesthesia for large or failed injection. Lid hygiene + oral doxycycline 100 mg BID for patients with recurrent chalazia and rosacea/MGD. Topical antibiotic if secondary infection (hordeolum). Biopsy if atypical features (rule out sebaceous carcinoma).
us_prevalence: ~1–2 million US cases/year; very common condition. Source: AAO.
patients_needing_tx: ~500,000–1,000,000 patients/year seeking medical treatment.
price: $300–$1,500/course for a novel topical intralesional dissolving agent.
peak_sales: $30–80M for a novel topical chalazion resolution agent (replacing I&C or steroid injection with a drop/ointment).
registrational_endpoint: Chalazion resolution (complete disappearance of mass by slit-lamp) at 8 weeks vs. warm compresses alone or vehicle.
btd_threshold_vs_soc: Complete resolution in ≥50% vs. ~30% warm compresses alone at 8 weeks.

symptoms: Painful red eyelid bump, localized swelling, tenderness, possible discharge
treatment_goal: Drain abscess; eradicate bacteria; prevent recurrence
first_line_soc: Warm compresses 10–15 min QID × 5–7 days to facilitate spontaneous drainage. Topical antibiotic ointment (bacitracin or erythromycin) for secondary infection. Incision and drainage for persistent or pointing abscess.
treatment_algorithm: Conservative: warm compresses BID-QID until pointing and draining. Topical antibiotic (bacitracin, tobramycin) for secondary infection. Oral antibiotics (doxycycline 100 mg BID) for recurrent hordeola with rosacea. I&D under local anesthesia for non-resolving. Distinguish from chalazion (chronic, non-infectious); biopsy if atypical.
us_prevalence: ~3–5 million US cases/year; very common and typically self-limited. Source: AAO.
patients_needing_tx: ~1–2 million patients seeking medical care; ~500,000 receiving topical antibiotics or I&D.
price: $200–$800/course for a novel topical antimicrobial or anti-inflammatory lid treatment.
peak_sales: $20–50M for a novel topical stye resolution product; mostly managed with cheap OTC compresses.
registrational_endpoint: Resolution of hordeolum (clinical cure: no palpable mass, pain score 0) at Day 7 vs. warm compresses alone.

symptoms: Involuntary eyelid closure, forceful blinking, functional blindness, photophobia
treatment_goal: Reduce involuntary spasm; restore functional vision and quality of life
first_line_soc: OnabotulinumtoxinA (Botox, 2.5–5 units per injection site) periocular injection every 3–4 months — gold standard FDA-approved treatment.
treatment_algorithm: Botulinum toxin type A (Botox, Dysport, Xeomin) injected into orbicularis oculi every 12–16 weeks — >90% of patients respond. Oral medications (clonazepam, baclofen, trihexyphenidyl) for refractory cases or as adjuncts. Dark tinted glasses (FL-41 tint) for photophobia. Surgical myectomy (removal of orbicularis muscle) for severe toxin-refractory cases.
us_prevalence: ~50,000–100,000 US patients with benign essential blepharospasm (BEB); ~20,000–40,000 with Meige syndrome. Source: BEBRF, AAO.
patients_needing_tx: ~60,000–90,000 patients on botulinum toxin injections every 3–4 months.
price: $500–$2,000/injection series every 3–4 months = $2,000–$8,000/year; novel topical formulation could be $3,000–$10,000/year.
peak_sales: $50–100M for a novel botulinum formulation or topical anti-spasmodic; Botox blepharospasm indication generates ~$150M/year.
registrational_endpoint: Jankovic Rating Scale (JRS) improvement ≥1 unit in severity and disability subscores at 4 weeks post-injection vs. placebo.
btd_threshold_vs_soc: JRS functional disability score 0 (no functional impairment) in ≥40% vs. ~10% placebo at 4 weeks.

symptoms: Misdirected eyelashes abrading cornea, pain, tearing, corneal ulceration
treatment_goal: Remove or redirect aberrant lashes; protect corneal surface
first_line_soc: Epilation (manual lash removal) for immediate relief. Definitive: electrolysis, cryotherapy, radiofrequency ablation, or surgical correction (eyelid rotation) to permanently eliminate misdirected lashes.
treatment_algorithm: Temporary: epilation every 4–6 weeks. Definitive options: electrolysis (low recurrence), cryotherapy (lash follicle destruction), argon laser ablation, surgical lid procedures (anterior lamellar repositioning). Treat underlying cause (trachoma, MMP, severe blepharitis). Lubricating drops and BCL to protect cornea. No pharmacologic treatment for trichiasis per se.
us_prevalence: ~200,000–400,000 US patients with symptomatic trichiasis; causes: post-infectious, autoimmune, congenital. Source: AAO.
patients_needing_tx: ~200,000–300,000 patients undergoing repeat or definitive treatments.
price: $500–$2,000/treatment course for a novel topical lash follicle growth inhibitor.
peak_sales: $20–50M for a novel topical hair follicle inhibitor preventing lash misdirection (bimatoprost reverse-analogue or anti-growth agent).
registrational_endpoint: Reduction in aberrant lash count and absence of corneal fluorescein staining at 12 weeks vs. observation.

symptoms: Inward-turning eyelid, corneal irritation from lashes, redness, pain, tearing
treatment_goal: Correct lid margin inversion; protect cornea from lash abrasion
first_line_soc: Temporary: Botox injection to orbicularis muscle or tape the lid outward. Definitive: surgical repair (horizontal lid shortening, Quickert sutures, Jones procedure). Lubricating drops and ointment for corneal protection.
treatment_algorithm: Emergency/temporary: eversion with tape or botulinum toxin 1–2 U into preseptal orbicularis. Definitive: Quickert sutures (temporary), transverse blepharotomy (Jones), or full-thickness skin grafting for cicatricial. Topical antibiotics if corneal abrasion. BCL for corneal protection.
us_prevalence: ~100,000–300,000 US patients with symptomatic entropion; predominantly elderly. Source: AAO.
patients_needing_tx: ~80,000–200,000 patients requiring definitive treatment (Botox or surgery).
price: $500–$2,000/injection course for Botox or novel botulinum formulation.
peak_sales: $15–25M from botulinum use in entropion (subsumed in broader Botox franchise).
registrational_endpoint: Lid margin position improvement (marginal reflex distance) and corneal fluorescein staining reduction at 6 weeks post-injection vs. observation.

symptoms: Epiphora (tearing), mucoid discharge, recurrent conjunctivitis, nasolacrimal duct obstruction
treatment_goal: Restore lacrimal drainage patency; prevent recurrent infections
first_line_soc: In infants: Crigler massage (digital compression of lacrimal sac) + topical antibiotic drops for secondary infection; spontaneous resolution by 12 months in ~90%. Surgical: probing and irrigation if not resolved by 12–13 months.
treatment_algorithm: Congenital NLDO: massage × 12 months, then probing under GA. Adult NLDO: probing/dilation and silicone intubation; balloon dilation (lacrimal duct dilatation); DCR if silicone intubation fails. Topical antibiotic drops for concurrent conjunctivitis. No pharmacologic treatment that relieves structural obstruction.
us_prevalence: Congenital NLDO: ~6% of newborns (~200,000–250,000/year); most resolve spontaneously. Adult NLDO: ~200,000–400,000 US cases. Source: AAO.
patients_needing_tx: ~50,000–100,000 patients/year needing surgical or pharmacologic intervention (mostly infants who fail spontaneous resolution).
price: $1,000–$5,000/course for a novel anti-fibrotic or anti-inflammatory topical agent preventing dacryostenosis.
peak_sales: $10–30M for a novel topical anti-fibrotic preventing NLDO progression; mainly surgical market.
registrational_endpoint: Fluorescein dye disappearance test normalization and resolution of tearing at 12 weeks vs. observation.

symptoms: Tender lacrimal sac swelling, epiphora, purulent discharge, periorbital redness
treatment_goal: Eradicate infection; restore lacrimal drainage
first_line_soc: Oral antibiotics targeting common pathogens (amoxicillin-clavulanate 875 mg BID or cephalexin 500 mg QID × 7–10 days) for acute dacryocystitis. IV antibiotics (oxacillin, ceftriaxone) for severe cases.
treatment_algorithm: Acute: systemic antibiotics (oral amoxicillin-clavulanate or IV ceftriaxone for severe); warm compresses; topical antibiotic drops. Abscess: incision and drainage. After resolution: dacryocystorhinostomy (DCR) to create new lacrimal drainage. Chronic dacryocystitis: DCR (endoscopic or external). Nasolacrimal probe + irrigation for dacryostenosis. Topical antibiotics adjunctive.
us_prevalence: ~50,000–100,000 US cases/year; predominantly women >40. Source: AAO, oculoplastic literature.
patients_needing_tx: ~50,000–80,000 patients/year requiring antibiotics; ~20,000–40,000/year undergoing DCR.
price: $500–$2,000/course for novel targeted ophthalmic antimicrobial.
peak_sales: $5–10M; primarily treated with cheap generic oral antibiotics.
registrational_endpoint: Resolution of acute infection signs (tenderness, discharge, swelling) and culture negativity at Day 10 vs. placebo.

symptoms: Medial eyelid redness, discharge, tearing, tender canaliculus, epiphora
treatment_goal: Eradicate pathogen; relieve obstruction; restore lacrimal drainage
first_line_soc: Surgical canaliculotomy with concretion removal plus topical antibiotic (penicillin G or cefazolin drops) for Actinomyces israelii (most common cause). Topical antibiotic irrigation of canaliculus.
treatment_algorithm: Primary: canaliculotomy (incision and curettage of sulfur granules/concretions) + penicillin G 100,000 units/mL eye drops 6×/day × 2–4 weeks. Dacryocystitis often coexists — treat simultaneously. Systemic oral penicillin V 500 mg QID × 2–4 weeks for Actinomyces. Antifungal if Candida involved. Probing and irrigation for partial obstruction.
us_prevalence: ~5,000–15,000 US cases/year; uncommon condition. Source: AAO, oculoplastic literature.
patients_needing_tx: ~5,000–12,000 patients/year requiring treatment (surgical + pharmacologic).
price: $500–$3,000/course for a novel antimicrobial irrigation solution.
peak_sales: <$2M; extremely limited drug market; primarily surgical condition.
registrational_endpoint: Resolution of canalicular discharge and improved lacrimal drainage at 4 weeks post-treatment vs. observation.

symptoms: Painless visual field loss (peripheral first), elevated IOP, optic disc cupping
treatment_goal: Lower IOP; halt optic nerve damage; preserve visual field
first_line_soc: Prostaglandin analogue eye drops: latanoprost 0.005% (Xalatan), bimatoprost 0.03% (Lumigan), travoprost 0.004% QHS — lower IOP by 25–35%. Preservative-free formulations preferred for compliance.
treatment_algorithm: Step 1: prostaglandin analogue QHS. Step 2: add beta-blocker (timolol 0.5% BID) or alpha-agonist (brimonidine 0.2% BID) or CAI (dorzolamide 2% TID). Combination drops for adherence. Step 3: laser trabeculoplasty (SLT — first-line alternative to drops). Step 4: MIGS (iStent, Hydrus) at time of cataract surgery. Step 5: trabeculectomy or tube shunt for advanced disease. Novel: netarsudil (Rhopressa, ROCK inhibitor), latanoprostene bunod (Vyzulta), bimatoprost SR implant (Durysta), sustained-release travoprost insert (iDose) for adherence.
us_prevalence: ~3 million US patients with POAG; ~2.7 million diagnosed and receiving treatment. Leading cause of irreversible blindness in the US. Source: Glaucoma Research Foundation, NEI.
patients_needing_tx: ~2.5–3 million patients on pharmacologic IOP reduction; ~1 million requiring multiple agents.
price: $3,000–$8,000/year for branded topical agents; sustained-release implants $5,000–$15,000/2-year device.
peak_sales: $500M–$2B for a transformative novel glaucoma therapy; netarsudil (Rhopressa) ~$200M/year, iDose travoprost implant (Glaukos) ~$500M projected peak.
registrational_endpoint: Mean diurnal IOP reduction ≥5 mmHg from baseline and non-inferiority or superiority to latanoprost 0.005% at 3 months on co-primary 8 AM, 10 AM, 4 PM IOP timepoints.
btd_threshold_vs_soc: Mean diurnal IOP reduction ≥7 mmHg vs. <4 mmHg placebo, AND non-inferiority to latanoprost (within 1.5 mmHg margin).

symptoms: Acute severe eye pain, halos, nausea, vomiting, markedly elevated IOP, vision loss
treatment_goal: Rapidly lower IOP; open anterior chamber angle; prevent optic nerve damage
first_line_soc: Acute attack: IV/oral acetazolamide + topical beta-blocker (timolol 0.5%) + topical alpha-agonist (apraclonidine 1% or brimonidine 0.2%) + topical steroid to break attack. Laser peripheral iridotomy (LPI) definitively prevents recurrence.
treatment_algorithm: Emergency: IV mannitol 1–2 g/kg, IV/oral acetazolamide 500 mg, topical timolol, apraclonidine, pilocarpine 1–2% (after IOP lowered). LPI for both eyes within 24–48 hours (prophylactic for fellow eye). Goniosynechialysis if PAS present. Filtration surgery (trabeculectomy) or MIGS if chronic ACG. IOP-lowering drops long-term if required. Cataract extraction (lens extraction often curative).
us_prevalence: ~500,000–600,000 US patients with angle-closure glaucoma; ~500,000 with PAC suspect (occludable angles). Acute ACG: ~30,000–50,000 attacks/year. Source: Glaucoma Research Foundation, JAMA Ophthalmol.
patients_needing_tx: ~400,000–500,000 on chronic IOP-lowering therapy; ~30,000–50,000/year with acute attacks needing emergency pharmacologic treatment.
price: $2,000–$8,000/year for topical IOP-lowering agents.
peak_sales: $100–300M for a novel IOP-lowering agent specifically for angle-closure (subsumed in broader glaucoma market).
registrational_endpoint: IOP reduction ≥30% from diurnal mean baseline at 3 months vs. timolol 0.5% BID; acute studies: IOP <21 mmHg within 2 hours.
btd_threshold_vs_soc: IOP reduction ≥35% vs. <20% with placebo at peak and trough timepoints.

symptoms: Sudden painless monocular vision loss, altitudinal visual field defect, swollen optic disc
treatment_goal: Prevent fellow eye involvement; manage underlying vascular risk factors
first_line_soc: Giant cell arteritis-related (AAION): high-dose corticosteroids URGENTLY (IV methylprednisolone 1g/day × 3 days, then oral prednisone 1 mg/kg/day) to prevent fellow eye loss. Non-arteritic NAION: no proven pharmacologic treatment; risk factor modification.
treatment_algorithm: AAION: IV methylprednisolone → oral prednisone taper × 12–18 months ± tocilizumab (Actemra, FDA-approved 2017 for GCA as steroid-sparing). NAION: no proven treatment — historical trials with corticosteroids, levodopa, aspirin not convincingly beneficial. Risk factor management (HTN, DM, sleep apnea treatment). Avoid hypotension, PDE5 inhibitors. Optic nerve decompression surgery not recommended. Investigational: CNTF gene therapy, neuroprotective agents.
us_prevalence: NAION: ~6,000–10,000 new cases/year; ~50,000–80,000 prevalent. AAION: ~2,000–3,000/year. Source: NEI, GCA epidemiology literature.
patients_needing_tx: ~8,000–12,000 new cases/year; ~50,000 prevalent NAION patients with no current approved treatment (huge unmet need).
price: $20,000–$100,000/year for a neuroprotective or vasodilatory agent; tocilizumab for GCA ~$30,000–$50,000/year.
peak_sales: $200–500M for a first approved NAION therapy; tocilizumab GCA franchise ~$400M/year; novel NAION gene therapy/neuroprotective could reach $300–600M.
registrational_endpoint: NAION: Proportion with ≥3 ETDRS letter improvement in BCVA OR visual field MD improvement at 6 months vs. placebo (currently no validated endpoint; FDA guidance needed). AAION/GCA: sustained remission without flare at 52 weeks (as per GCA endpoints).
btd_threshold_vs_soc: Visual acuity stabilization in ≥60% vs. ~35% natural history at 6 months; sustained GCA remission ≥50% vs. ~25% with prednisone alone at 52 weeks.

symptoms: Painful monocular vision loss, color desaturation, afferent pupillary defect, often MS-related
treatment_goal: Accelerate visual recovery; reduce risk of MS conversion; preserve optic nerve
first_line_soc: IV methylprednisolone 1g/day × 3 days (ONTT protocol) accelerates visual recovery but does not improve final VA. Observe if mild. Treat underlying MS per neurologist.
treatment_algorithm: IV MP 1g/day × 3 days → oral prednisone taper (ONTT protocol). High-risk for MS (CIS + MRI lesions): start DMT (interferon beta-1a, glatiramer, natalizumab, ocrelizumab) to reduce MS conversion risk. Low-risk/isolated: observe. NMO-IgG positive: aggressive immunosuppression (eculizumab, inebilizumab, satralizumab for NMOSD). Investigational for optic nerve neuroprotection/remyelination: opicinumab, MD1003 (high-dose biotin), intravitreal CNTF.
us_prevalence: ~100,000–150,000 US episodes/year; ~50–70% associated with or leading to MS. Source: NEI, ONTT epidemiology.
patients_needing_tx: ~80,000–120,000 patients/year needing IV MP; ~50,000–70,000/year considered for MS DMTs.
peak_sales: $200–500M for a novel optic nerve neuroprotectant/remyelinating agent; NMOSD agents (eculizumab, inebilizumab) generating $200–400M/year.
registrational_endpoint: Low-contrast letter acuity (2.5% Sloan) improvement at 6 months; and/or OCT retinal nerve fiber layer thickness preservation vs. fellow eye loss at 12 months.
btd_threshold_vs_soc: RNFL thickness loss <10 µm vs. ~20–30 µm natural history; OR low-contrast letter acuity improvement ≥5 letters vs. placebo at 6 months.

symptoms: Progressive painless vision loss, color desaturation, afferent pupillary defect
treatment_goal: Decompress optic nerve; preserve and restore visual function
