first_line_soc: Surgical decompression (orbitotomy, craniotomy, or endoscopic endonasal) to remove compressing mass. Systemic corticosteroids (dexamethasone) for rapid vision-threatening optic nerve compression while arranging surgery.
treatment_algorithm: Urgent surgery for acute vision loss. For thyroid eye disease-related compressive ON: IV methylprednisolone 1g/day × 3 days, then oral taper; if no improvement in 2 weeks: decompression surgery; teprotumumab (Tepezza) for TED-associated myopathy/proptosis reducing compression risk. For pituitary adenoma: transsphenoidal surgery; cabergoline/bromocriptine for prolactinoma. Radiation for residual/recurrent tumors.
us_prevalence: ~50,000–150,000 US patients with compressive optic neuropathy (various causes: TED, meningioma, pituitary tumors, orbital tumors). TED-related: ~50,000 cases/year. Source: AAO, TED-specific epidemiology.
patients_needing_tx: ~50,000–100,000 patients needing pharmacologic or surgical intervention.
price: $15,000–$100,000/year for biologics like teprotumumab ($20,000/infusion × 8 doses).
peak_sales: $500M–$1.5B for teprotumumab (Tepezza) and next-gen IGF-1R or IL-6 pathway inhibitors for TED-related CON.
registrational_endpoint: Proptosis reduction ≥2 mm (Hertel) and CAS improvement ≥2 points at Week 24 vs. placebo in TED with compressive features.
btd_threshold_vs_soc: Proptosis response (≥2 mm reduction) in ≥70% vs. ~20% placebo at Week 24.

symptoms: Bilateral optic disc swelling, headache, visual obscurations, pulsatile tinnitus
treatment_goal: Reduce ICP; prevent optic nerve damage and permanent vision loss
first_line_soc: Acetazolamide (Diamox) 500 mg–2g/day orally (carbonic anhydrase inhibitor, reduces CSF production) for idiopathic intracranial hypertension (IIH). Weight loss is essential in obese IIH patients.
treatment_algorithm: IIH: acetazolamide ± topiramate; structured weight loss (5–10% body weight critical). Recent evidence: topiramate equivalent to acetazolamide. CSF diversion (lumboperitoneal or ventriculoperitoneal shunt) for progressive vision loss. Optic nerve sheath fenestration (ONSF) for vision-threatening papilledema. Treat secondary causes: discontinue offending drugs (tetracyclines, vitamin A), treat venous thrombosis, tumor. Novel: pradigastat (lipid-lowering targeting IIH), glucagon-like peptide-1 agonists (semaglutide) for weight loss in IIH showing promise.
us_prevalence: IIH: ~250,000 US patients; incidence rising with obesity epidemic (~4–5/100,000 general population, up to 20/100,000 in obese young women). Source: IIHTT, NEI.
patients_needing_tx: ~200,000–250,000 patients on acetazolamide or topiramate for IIH.
price: $3,000–$15,000/year for a novel ICP-reducing or CSF-production-inhibiting agent.
peak_sales: $200–400M for a novel IIH agent (GLP-1 analogues gaining traction); acetazolamide is cheap generic.
registrational_endpoint: Papilledema grade improvement (Frisén scale ≥1 grade reduction) and ICP reduction ≥5 cmCSF at 24 weeks vs. placebo (IIHTT primary endpoint model); BCVA stabilization.
btd_threshold_vs_soc: Papilledema resolution in ≥60% vs. ~15% placebo at 24 weeks.

symptoms: Bilateral painless central vision loss, color vision defects, cecocentral scotoma
treatment_goal: Preserve or restore remaining vision; address mitochondrial dysfunction
first_line_soc: Idebenone (Raxone) 900 mg/day for Leber hereditary optic neuropathy (LHON) — approved in EU/UK, not FDA-approved in US; FDA declined approval. Avoid mitochondrial toxins (tobacco, alcohol, chloramphenicol). Genetic counseling.
treatment_algorithm: LHON: idebenone 300 mg TID (Raxone, EU-approved) — reduces visual loss conversion. Gene therapy (Lumevoq/LUMEVOQ, GS010 — lenadogene nolparvovec, EU conditional approval 2021) for ND4 mutation carriers. Nutritional supplements: CoQ10, riboflavin, α-lipoic acid (limited evidence). Autosomal dominant optic atrophy (ADOA/OPA1): no approved therapy; trials with idebenone and gene therapy ongoing. Avoid optic nerve stressors.
us_prevalence: LHON: ~4,000–5,000 US patients (prevalence); ~200–500 new cases/year. ADOA: ~5,000–8,000 US patients. Source: NORD, mitOHM consortium.
patients_needing_tx: ~8,000–12,000 US patients with hereditary optic neuropathies needing treatment.
price: $50,000–$200,000/year for idebenone Rx; gene therapy (Lumevoq) ~$500,000–$1M one-time treatment.
peak_sales: $200–500M at 20–50% share for gene therapy + idebenone combined; Lumevoq ~$200M projected EU peak.
registrational_endpoint: Recovery of best BCVA ≥0.2 logMAR from nadir in the affected eye AND proportion with BCVA ≥20/200 at 96 weeks vs. sham in LHON gene therapy trial.
btd_threshold_vs_soc: Visual recovery (BCVA ≥0.2 logMAR gain) in ≥40% of treated eyes vs. ~15% sham at 96 weeks.

symptoms: Bilateral painless central vision loss, color desaturation, cecocentral scotoma
treatment_goal: Remove toxic agent; supplement deficient nutrients; halt progressive vision loss
first_line_soc: Remove causative agent (ethambutol, methanol, tobacco, alcohol). B-vitamin supplementation (B1, B2, B6, B12) for nutritional deficiency. Folate supplementation. Multivitamin and nutritional support.
treatment_algorithm: Nutritional ON (B12, folate, thiamine deficiency): IM B12 1000 mcg/day × 1 week then weekly; folic acid 5 mg/day; thiamine 100 mg TID. Tobacco-alcohol amblyopia: abstinence + B vitamins + hydroxocobalamin. Ethambutol-induced ON: stop ethambutol immediately; chelation (zinc supplementation) may help. Methanol: IV fomepizole (alcohol dehydrogenase inhibitor) + hemodialysis (prevents formate formation). Visual recovery depends on cause and rapidity of treatment.
us_prevalence: ~10,000–30,000 US cases/year (various causes). Ethambutol toxicity: ~5,000–10,000/year (TB treatment). Methanol poisoning: ~1,000–2,000/year. Source: AAO, MMWR.
patients_needing_tx: ~15,000–25,000 patients/year requiring pharmacologic treatment.
price: $2,000–$10,000/course for targeted neuroprotective or antidotal agent.
peak_sales: $10–25M; largely managed with cheap generic vitamins and supportive care.
registrational_endpoint: BCVA improvement ≥5 ETDRS letters and VF MD improvement at 12 weeks vs. observation after toxic agent removed.

symptoms: Proptosis, periorbital pain, diplopia, eyelid swelling, restricted motility
treatment_goal: Suppress orbital inflammation; preserve vision and motility
first_line_soc: High-dose systemic corticosteroids (oral prednisone 1–1.5 mg/kg/day or IV methylprednisolone 1g/day × 3 days) for idiopathic orbital inflammation (orbital pseudotumor). Teprotumumab for thyroid eye disease-related orbital inflammation.
treatment_algorithm: Idiopathic orbital inflammation: prednisone 1–1.5 mg/kg/day × 4 weeks with taper — >80% response. Refractory: orbital radiation (20 Gy), methotrexate, rituximab. IgG4-related orbital disease: steroids first-line; rituximab for refractory. TED: IV MP pulse (EUGOGO protocol), teprotumumab (Tepezza) for active phase, decompression surgery for inactive phase. Lymphoma of orbit: CHOP or R-CHOP, orbital RT.
us_prevalence: ~30,000–60,000 US patients with inflammatory orbital disease (all causes including TED ~50,000, idiopathic ~10,000, IgG4 ~5,000). Source: AAO.
patients_needing_tx: ~40,000–60,000 patients requiring systemic immunosuppression.
price: $15,000–$150,000/year for targeted biologics (teprotumumab, tocilizumab, rituximab).
peak_sales: $500M–$1.5B for TED biologics; Tepezza (teprotumumab) ~$450M in 2023 and growing.
registrational_endpoint: Proptosis reduction ≥2 mm and CAS response (≥2 point reduction) at Week 24 vs. placebo in active TED.
btd_threshold_vs_soc: Proptosis response ≥70% vs. ~20% placebo at Week 24 (Tepezza OPTIC trial: 83% vs. 10%).

symptoms: Proptosis, diplopia, vision loss, palpable orbital mass, eyelid displacement
treatment_goal: Achieve complete tumor removal or control; preserve vision and globe
first_line_soc: Surgical excision for most benign orbital tumors (cavernous hemangioma, dermoid cyst, neurofibroma). For malignant tumors: surgery + RT ± chemotherapy per histology (lymphoma: R-CHOP + orbital RT; rhabdomyosarcoma: chemotherapy + RT; lacrimal gland carcinoma: surgery + RT).
treatment_algorithm: Benign: orbital surgery (anterior or lateral orbitotomy). Cavernous hemangioma: propranolol/timolol (off-label) for smaller lesions; surgery for large. Lymphoma: rituximab + CHOP; orbital RT. Rhabdomyosarcoma: VAC chemotherapy + RT (IRS protocols). ACC of lacrimal gland: surgery + RT ± chemotherapy. Metastatic orbital tumors: treat primary (e.g., breast: systemic therapy). Novel: PDR001 checkpoint inhibition for unresectable orbital malignancies.
us_prevalence: ~5,000–10,000 new orbital tumors/year; ~20,000–30,000 prevalent. Cavernous hemangioma most common benign (~30%). Source: SEER, AAO.
patients_needing_tx: ~10,000–20,000 patients/year on systemic pharmacologic therapy (malignant/lymphoma subgroup).
price: $20,000–$150,000/year depending on histology.
peak_sales: $50–150M for a novel orbital tumor-specific agent; mostly subsumed within systemic lymphoma/sarcoma franchises.
registrational_endpoint: Orbital tumor response rate (CR+PR by imaging) and vision preservation at 12 months vs. RT alone or historical controls.
btd_threshold_vs_soc: ORR ≥40% with preserved vision in ≥80% at 12 months for unresectable malignant orbital tumors.

symptoms: Proptosis, fever, restricted painful eye movement, eyelid redness and swelling
treatment_goal: Eradicate infection; prevent intracranial spread and vision loss
first_line_soc: IV antibiotics: ampicillin-sulbactam 3g Q6h or ceftriaxone 2g Q12h + metronidazole 500 mg Q8h. Vancomycin added if MRSA suspected. Surgical drainage of subperiosteal/orbital abscess when present.
treatment_algorithm: Children <9: IV ampicillin-sulbactam or ceftriaxone (gram-positive, H. influenzae coverage). Older/adults: broad-spectrum IV covering MRSA, anaerobes (vancomycin + piperacillin-tazobactam). Surgical drainage when: abscess >10 mm, no improvement in 24–48h, visual compromise, or ICU need. Transition to oral antibiotics when improving. Treat primary sinusitis. Ophthalmologic monitoring QID for vision. Cavernous sinus thrombosis vigilance.
us_prevalence: ~10,000–20,000 US cases/year; most in children via sinusitis. Source: AAO, pediatric orbital literature.
patients_needing_tx: ~10,000–20,000 patients/year requiring IV antibiotics.
price: $3,000–$15,000/inpatient course.
registrational_endpoint: Clinical cure (resolution of proptosis, fever, motility restriction) and culture eradication at Day 14 vs. standard IV antibiotics.

symptoms: Eyelid redness, swelling anterior to orbital septum, no proptosis or motility restriction
treatment_goal: Eradicate periorbital skin/soft tissue infection; prevent orbital spread
first_line_soc: Oral antibiotics for mild-moderate cases: amoxicillin-clavulanate 875 mg BID or cephalexin 500 mg QID × 7–10 days. IV antibiotics (ceftriaxone ± clindamycin) for severe cases or when orbital cellulitis cannot be excluded.
treatment_algorithm: Mild (no fever, localized eyelid swelling): oral amoxicillin-clavulanate. Moderate: oral ± monitoring or IV ceftriaxone if no improvement in 24–48h. MRSA suspected: TMP-SMX or doxycycline orally. Children: often hospitalized for IV antibiotics. Differentiate from orbital cellulitis with CT scan when unclear. Warm compresses, I&D of pointing abscess.
us_prevalence: ~40,000–80,000 US cases/year; mostly children, often post-trauma or insect bite. Source: AAO, pediatric literature.
patients_needing_tx: ~40,000–70,000 patients/year requiring antibiotic treatment.
price: $300–$1,000/course for oral antibiotics; novel agent $1,000–$5,000/course.
peak_sales: $5–15M; treated with cheap generic oral antibiotics.
registrational_endpoint: Clinical cure (complete resolution of eyelid erythema and edema) at Day 7 vs. placebo in preseptal cellulitis.

symptoms: Severe headache, fever, proptosis, ophthalmoplegia, chemosis, visual loss
treatment_goal: Eradicate infection; prevent thrombus propagation; reduce mortality
first_line_soc: IV antibiotics: vancomycin 15–20 mg/kg/dose Q8-12h + piperacillin-tazobactam 4.5g Q6h or cefotaxime 2g Q4h for broad coverage including MRSA and anaerobes. IV anticoagulation (heparin) controversial but used by many centers.
treatment_algorithm: IV vancomycin + beta-lactam (piperacillin-tazobactam or meropenem). Metronidazole added for anaerobic coverage if odontogenic source. Anticoagulation (IV heparin → warfarin) debated but may improve outcomes. Surgical drainage of primary focus (sinusitis, dental abscess). Corticosteroids controversial (no proven benefit). Monitor with serial imaging. Ophthalmologic monitoring for vision. Aggressive ICU-level care.
us_prevalence: ~1,000–2,000 US cases/year; rare but life-threatening. Source: AAO, Neurology literature.
patients_needing_tx: ~1,000–2,000 patients/year requiring emergency IV antibiotics and intensive care.
price: $5,000–$20,000/course (inpatient IV antibiotics).
peak_sales: <$5M; extremely limited standalone drug opportunity; treated with generic IV antibiotics.
registrational_endpoint: Mortality reduction and functional neurologic outcome (mRS ≤2) at 90 days vs. standard antibiotics alone.

symptoms: Blurred vision (near or distance), headaches, eye strain, squinting
treatment_goal: Correct refractive error; optimize visual acuity; prevent amblyopia
first_line_soc: Corrective lenses (spectacles or contact lenses) for all forms of refractive error. Refractive surgery (LASIK, PRK, SMILE) for eligible patients. Atropine 0.01% eye drops for myopia progression control in children.
treatment_algorithm: Myopia: spectacles/CLs; myopia control: low-dose atropine 0.01–0.05% QHS (ATOM2 evidence), orthokeratology, multifocal CLs. Hyperopia: spectacles/CLs; surgical correction with LASIK/ICL. Astigmatism: toric lenses or LASIK. Presbyopia: reading glasses, multifocal lenses; pharmacologic pilocarpine 1.25% (Vuity, FDA-approved 2021) for near vision improvement. LASIK/PRK/SMILE for younger patients. No pharmacologic agent corrects all refractive errors.
us_prevalence: ~150 million Americans wear corrective lenses; ~50 million with myopia; ~14 million with high myopia. Presbyopia: ~120 million adults >40. Source: NEI, Vision Council.
patients_needing_tx: ~150 million with corrected refractive error (mostly OTC/spectacles); ~30–40 million using pharmacologic myopia control or presbyopia drops (growing).
price: $500–$3,000/year for novel myopia progression drugs (atropine, antimuscarinic eye drops); $1,500–$4,000/year for presbyopia drops.
peak_sales: $300M–$2B for a novel myopia or presbyopia pharmacologic agent; Vuity (pilocarpine 1.25%) ~$50–100M/year (2024 ramp); atropine 0.01% generic market building.
registrational_endpoint: Myopia: axial length change ≤0.15 mm/year vs. ≥0.40 mm/year natural history over 24 months in children. Presbyopia: near DCVA improvement ≥3 lines at 1 hour with no ≥1 line loss in distance DCVA.
btd_threshold_vs_soc: Myopia progression inhibition ≥50% vs. placebo over 24 months (axial length); OR presbyopia near DCVA ≥3 lines improvement vs. vehicle at 1 hour post-dose.

symptoms: Visual blurring, floaters, vision loss, microaneurysms, neovascularization
treatment_goal: Prevent progression; reduce macular edema; preserve vision in diabetes
first_line_soc: Diabetic macular edema: intravitreal anti-VEGF (ranibizumab 0.3 mg [Lucentis], aflibercept 2 mg [Eylea], or faricimab [Vabysmo]) as first-line. Systemic: tight glycemic control (HbA1c <7%) and blood pressure control.
treatment_algorithm: DME: anti-VEGF (ranibizumab, aflibercept, faricimab, bevacizumab off-label) monthly × 6, then PRN/T&E. Ozurdex (dexamethasone implant) or fluocinolone implant (Iluvien) for chronic DME. Proliferative DR: panretinal photocoagulation (PRP) remains gold standard; anti-VEGF reduces NVD/NVE. Vitrectomy for traction RD, persistent VH. Tight systemic control: HbA1c <7%, BP <130/80. Novel: faricimab Q16W (YOSEMITE/RHINE). KSI-301 (Port Delivery System analogues), ALIMERA OTX-TKI (tyrosine kinase inhibitor sustained-release implant) in trials.
us_prevalence: ~11 million US adults with DR; ~7.7 million with NPDR; ~3.3 million with PDR or CI-DME. ~34 million Americans with DM. Source: NEI, CDC Diabetes Statistics.
patients_needing_tx: ~3–5 million patients with DME or PDR requiring pharmacologic/laser treatment; ~1.5–2 million actively receiving anti-VEGF injections.
price: $2,000–$8,000/injection; $15,000–$40,000/year for sustained-release implants.
peak_sales: $2–5B for a transformative novel agent; faricimab (Vabysmo) ~$2.5B in 2024; Eylea DR franchise ~$2B/year.
registrational_endpoint: Mean change in BCVA at 52 weeks (non-inferiority within 4 letters vs. monthly ranibizumab) and central retinal thickness reduction (OCT) vs. sham as co-primary.
btd_threshold_vs_soc: BCVA gain ≥10 letters in ≥40% with extended Q16W dosing vs. monthly ranibizumab non-inferiority at 52 weeks.

symptoms: Central vision loss, metamorphopsia, scotoma, difficulty reading
treatment_goal: Prevent wet AMD progression; stabilize or improve vision; slow geographic atrophy
first_line_soc: Wet/neovascular AMD: intravitreal anti-VEGF injections — ranibizumab (Lucentis), aflibercept (Eylea), bevacizumab (Avastin off-label), faricimab (Vabysmo 2022, anti-VEGF/Ang-2), brolucizumab (Beovu). Dry AMD: AREDS2 supplements (vitamin C, E, zinc, copper, lutein, zeaxanthin) for intermediate AMD.
treatment_algorithm: Wet AMD: anti-VEGF every 4 weeks loading × 3, then treat-and-extend or treat-and-observe. Faricimab (Vabysmo) allows up to Q16W dosing. High-dose aflibercept (8 mg, Eylea HD) Q12-16W. Wet AMD with GA: photobiomodulation (experimental). Dry AMD/GA: pegcetacoplan (Syfovre, FDA-approved 2023, C3 inhibitor) Q monthly or bimonthly; avacincaptad pegol (Izervay, FDA-approved 2023, C5 inhibitor) QM. AREDS2 supplements for intermediate dry AMD. Gene therapy (RGX-314 subretinal, ADVM-022) in trials.
us_prevalence: ~20 million US patients with AMD (all stages); ~2 million with wet AMD; ~1 million with GA. ~200,000–300,000 new wet AMD diagnoses/year. Source: NEI, BrightFocus Foundation.
patients_needing_tx: ~2 million wet AMD patients receiving anti-VEGF; ~800,000 GA patients eligible for complement inhibitors (Syfovre/Izervay).
price: $5,000–$15,000/injection for anti-VEGF; $2,000–$5,000/injection for GA treatments; $15,000–$50,000/year total.
peak_sales: $3–8B for the leading anti-VEGF agent; Eylea franchise ~$5B/year (all Regeneron); faricimab (Vabysmo) ~$2B in 2024; Syfovre/Izervay GA franchise reaching $500M–$1B.
registrational_endpoint: Wet AMD: mean change in BCVA from baseline at 12 months (non-inferiority within 4 letters vs. monthly ranibizumab, or superiority). GA: rate of GA lesion area growth (mm²/year) as primary endpoint with visual function as key secondary.
btd_threshold_vs_soc: Wet AMD: BCVA gain ≥15 letters in ≥30% of patients at 12 months. GA: lesion growth rate reduction ≥20% vs. sham at 12 months (Syfovre achieved 22%; Izervay achieved 14%).

symptoms: Metamorphopsia, blurred central vision, monocular diplopia, decreased contrast sensitivity
treatment_goal: Remove epiretinal membrane; restore retinal architecture and vision
first_line_soc: Observation for mild or minimally symptomatic ERM. Surgical pars plana vitrectomy (PPV) with membrane peeling is the only definitive treatment for visually significant ERM.
treatment_algorithm: Asymptomatic: observation. Symptomatic (BCVA ≤20/40, significant metamorphopsia): PPV + ERM + ILM peeling. No pharmacologic treatment approved. Investigational: intravitreal ocriplasmin (Jetrea) — FDA-approved for VMA but limited ERM efficacy. Pharmacologic vitreolysis to achieve PVD prior to ERM formation under study. Anti-VEGF not indicated. Steroid injections generally unhelpful.
us_prevalence: ~2–3 million US patients with ERM (all stages); ~600,000–1,000,000 with visually significant ERM. Source: NEI, retinal epidemiology.
patients_needing_tx: ~800,000 patients with visually significant ERM; ~100,000–200,000 PPV surgeries/year for ERM.
price: $5,000–$20,000/intravitreal injection for a pharmacologic vitreolysis or anti-fibrotic agent.
peak_sales: $200–500M for a validated pharmacologic ERM dissolution/prevention agent (high unmet need).
registrational_endpoint: Improvement in BCVA ≥10 ETDRS letters and reduction in central macular thickness at 6 months vs. observation in symptomatic ERM.
btd_threshold_vs_soc: BCVA improvement ≥10 letters in ≥40% vs. <10% observation at 6 months.

symptoms: Arteriovenous nicking, cotton-wool spots, flame hemorrhages, disk edema, vision changes
treatment_goal: Control blood pressure; prevent retinal and optic nerve damage
first_line_soc: Antihypertensive therapy targeting BP <130/80 mmHg: ACE inhibitors, ARBs, calcium channel blockers, or thiazide diuretics per JNC/ACC/AHA guidelines. No specific ophthalmic pharmacologic treatment.
treatment_algorithm: Systemic BP control with antihypertensive agents as primary treatment — reduces risk of progression and vision loss. Malignant hypertensive retinopathy: emergency BP reduction (IV labetalol or nicardipine) to prevent further organ damage. Optic disc edema: treat elevated ICP if concurrent. No intravitreal treatment specifically for hypertensive retinopathy. Monitor for macular edema and BVO/CVO complications.
us_prevalence: ~11 million US adults with some degree of hypertensive retinopathy (8–14% of hypertensives); malignant HTN retinopathy: ~100,000–200,000/year. Source: NHANES, hypertension epidemiology.
patients_needing_tx: ~10 million patients with hypertensive retinopathy, all treated with systemic antihypertensives; ~100,000–200,000 with severe/malignant requiring urgent treatment.
price: $2,000–$10,000/year for a retinal-protective agent or neuroprotective adjunct.
peak_sales: $100–300M for a novel retinal-protective antihypertensive adjunct (e.g., endothelin antagonist, anti-VEGF for macular sequelae).
registrational_endpoint: Improvement in Keith-Wagener-Barker grade and resolution of cotton-wool spots/hemorrhages at 12 weeks vs. antihypertensive therapy alone.

symptoms: Sectoral vision loss, metamorphopsia, retinal hemorrhages, macular edema
treatment_goal: Reduce macular edema; restore vision; prevent neovascular complications
first_line_soc: Intravitreal anti-VEGF injections: ranibizumab 0.5 mg (BRAVO trial, FDA-approved) or bevacizumab (off-label) monthly × 6, then PRN. Intravitreal dexamethasone implant (Ozurdex 0.7 mg) every 3–6 months alternative.
treatment_algorithm: Macular edema: anti-VEGF (ranibizumab, aflibercept, bevacizumab) monthly × 6 then PRN is gold standard. Ozurdex (dexamethasone implant) for anti-VEGF non-responders or pseudophakic patients. Sector laser photocoagulation for macular edema no longer first-line (BRAVO trial). Neovascularization: peripheral scatter laser. Vitrectomy for vitreous hemorrhage, tractional retinal detachment. Aspirin for systemic risk; treat HTN.
us_prevalence: ~100,000–150,000 new BRVO cases/year; ~500,000 prevalent US patients. Source: NEI, BRAVO trial demographics.
patients_needing_tx: ~100,000–150,000 patients/year on anti-VEGF or Ozurdex injections.
peak_sales: $200–400M for a novel extended-release anti-VEGF or complement inhibitor for BRVO macular edema.
registrational_endpoint: Mean change in BCVA from baseline at 12 months; macular cube volume (OCT) change as key secondary vs. sham.
btd_threshold_vs_soc: BCVA gain ≥15 letters in ≥30% of patients at 6 months vs. ~7% sham (BRAVO trial benchmark).

symptoms: Progressive night blindness, tunnel vision, bone-spicule pigmentation, ERG attenuation
treatment_goal: Slow rod/cone photoreceptor degeneration; preserve central vision
first_line_soc: Vitamin A palmitate 15,000 IU/day (slows ERG decline per Berson trial); omega-3 fatty acids adjunctive. No disease-modifying FDA-approved pharmacologic treatment for most RP.
treatment_algorithm: Vitamin A palmitate 15,000 IU/day + DHA (omega-3) supplementation — modest ERG preservation in clinical trials. Low-vision rehabilitation, UV-protective glasses. Approved gene therapy: voretigene neparvovec (Luxturna, FDA 2017) for RPE65-mutation RP (biallelic) — subretinal injection; ~1,000–2,000 eligible US patients. Argus II epiretinal prosthesis (discontinued). Ciliary neurotrophic factor (CNTF) implant Phase 3 trials ongoing. Stem cell therapies investigational.
us_prevalence: ~100,000–150,000 US patients with RP; ~1 in 4,000 worldwide. Source: Foundation Fighting Blindness, NEI.
patients_needing_tx: ~100,000–150,000 patients with RP; ~1,000–2,000 eligible for Luxturna gene therapy; broader population on vitamin A supplementation.
price: $500,000–$1,000,000 one-time for gene therapy (Luxturna ~$850,000/both eyes); $1,000–$3,000/year for pharmacologic neuroprotective agents.
tam_gross: ~1,500 Luxturna patients × $850,000 = $1.275B (gene therapy); broad pharmacologic market: ~100,000 patients × $2,000 = $200M
peak_sales: $150–300M for Luxturna (Spark Therapeutics); $100–300M for a broad-RP gene therapy or neuroprotective small molecule.
registrational_endpoint: Change in full-field stimulus testing (FST) light sensitivity and visual field III4e Goldmann area at 12 months vs. sham (Luxturna primary: multiluminance mobility test). For pharmacologic agents: ERG amplitude preservation over 24 months.
btd_threshold_vs_soc: FST improvement ≥2.5 log units in ≥50% of treated eyes vs. 0% sham (Luxturna IMPACT trial); ERG amplitude decline <20% vs. >50% natural history at 24 months.

symptoms: Sudden painless vision loss, diffuse retinal hemorrhages, disc edema, macular edema
treatment_goal: Reduce macular edema; restore vision; prevent neovascular glaucoma
first_line_soc: Intravitreal anti-VEGF injections: ranibizumab 0.5 mg (CRUISE trial) or aflibercept 2 mg (COPERNICUS/GALILEO) monthly × 6 then PRN. Intravitreal dexamethasone implant (Ozurdex) Q3 months as alternative.
treatment_algorithm: Macular edema: anti-VEGF (ranibizumab/aflibercept/bevacizumab) monthly then PRN. Ozurdex Q3–4 months for anti-VEGF failures or pseudophakic patients. CRVO-associated NVG: IOP management, panretinal laser photocoagulation, anti-VEGF. Anti-VEGF also suppresses iris/angle neovascularization. Systemic workup for hypercoagulable state if <50 years. OCTA for follow-up. No anticoagulation proven benefit for CRVO.
us_prevalence: ~50,000–80,000 new CRVO cases/year; ~400,000 prevalent US patients. Source: NEI, CRUISE trial demographics.
patients_needing_tx: ~60,000–80,000 patients/year on anti-VEGF or Ozurdex injections.
peak_sales: $200–400M for a novel extended-duration anti-VEGF or neuroprotective agent for CRVO.
registrational_endpoint: Mean change in BCVA from baseline at 6 months; macular cube volume as key secondary vs. sham.
btd_threshold_vs_soc: BCVA gain ≥15 letters in ≥40% vs. ~9% sham (CRUISE trial benchmark) at 6 months.

symptoms: Vision loss, floaters, retinal mass, leukocoria in children (retinoblastoma)
treatment_goal: Eradicate tumor; preserve vision and eye; prevent metastasis
first_line_soc: Retinoblastoma: intra-arterial chemotherapy (IAC) with melphalan ± topotecan ± carboplatin for intraocular Rb; IV carboplatin + etoposide + vincristine (CEV) for bilateral Rb. Choroidal melanoma: brachytherapy (plaque) or proton beam RT for most; enucleation for large tumors.
treatment_algorithm: Retinoblastoma: Group A/B: focal laser/cryo. Group C/D: IAC. Group E/bilateral: systemic CEV + IAC. Metastatic: MEK inhibitor (selumetinib), GNAQ/GNA11 pathway drugs in trials. Choroidal melanoma: plaque brachytherapy (COMS) or proton RT. Metastatic uveal melanoma: tebentafusp (Kimmtrak, FDA-approved 2022, first-in-class TCR bispecific, HLA-A*02:01 patients). Retinal lymphoma: see CNS lymphoma; high-dose methotrexate + ocular RT. Retinal hemangioblastoma (VHL): bevacizumab intravitreal.
us_prevalence: Retinoblastoma: ~250–300 new cases/year. Uveal melanoma: ~2,500–3,000 new cases/year. Retinal lymphoma: ~1,000–1,500/year. Source: SEER, AAO.
patients_needing_tx: ~3,500–5,000 patients/year on active pharmacologic therapy.
peak_sales: $150–300M for a novel retinal cancer agent; tebentafusp (Kimmtrak) ~$100M in 2023, building.
registrational_endpoint: OS improvement (HR <0.75) in metastatic uveal melanoma; BCVA preservation and local tumor control at 12 months for primary retinal tumors.
btd_threshold_vs_soc: OS HR <0.70 vs. investigator's choice in metastatic uveal melanoma (tebentafusp HERO trial: HR 0.51).

symptoms: Flashes, floaters, curtain-like visual field loss, shadow in peripheral vision
treatment_goal: Reattach retina; preserve central vision; prevent proliferative vitreoretinopathy
first_line_soc: Surgical: pneumatic retinopexy, scleral buckle, or pars plana vitrectomy (PPV) depending on break location, extent, and PVR. Surgery is the only treatment; no pharmacologic SoC.
treatment_algorithm: Rhegmatogenous RD: pneumatic retinopexy (gas bubble injection) for superior breaks, scleral buckle, or PPV. Tractional RD (DR): vitrectomy + membrane peeling. Exudative RD: treat underlying cause (anti-VEGF for wet AMD, steroids for VKH). Post-operative: face-down positioning, gas tamponade. PVR prevention: intravitreal 5-FU + heparin (limited trials), daunorubicin (limited evidence). No approved pharmacologic agent for primary RD.
us_prevalence: ~20,000–30,000 new rhegmatogenous RD cases/year; ~100,000 total RD of all types. Source: AAO, SEER.
patients_needing_tx: ~20,000–30,000 patients/year undergoing surgery; ~10,000 with recurrent/complex RD needing adjunctive pharmacologic PVR prevention.
price: $5,000–$20,000/course for a pharmacologic PVR prophylaxis or vitreous sealant agent.
peak_sales: $30–80M for a novel anti-PVR agent or retinal neuroprotective adjunct to surgery.
registrational_endpoint: Single-operation success rate and BCVA at 6 months vs. surgery alone; PVR development rate (≥grade C) at 3 months as secondary.
btd_threshold_vs_soc: PVR rate <5% vs. ~10–15% historical with surgery alone; single-surgery success ≥90% at 6 months.

symptoms: Sudden painless sectoral visual field loss, whitening of retinal segment
treatment_goal: Restore retinal perfusion; preserve visual field; address systemic embolic risk
first_line_soc: No proven pharmacologic treatment for BRAO itself. Anterior chamber paracentesis (IOP lowering) and ocular massage historically used; systemic evaluation for cardioembolic source. Aspirin 325 mg + systemic workup (carotid duplex, cardiac echo, lipid panel).
treatment_algorithm: Emergency: ocular massage, anterior chamber paracentesis, IV/sublingual isosorbide dinitrate (limited evidence). Most BRAO improve spontaneously; visual field recovery in ~80%. Aspirin/anticoagulation for cardioembolic prevention. Intra-arterial thrombolysis debated (EAGLE study negative for CRAO; BRAO data limited). Treat underlying risk factors (HTN, DM, AF, carotid disease). Neovascularization: laser photocoagulation.
us_prevalence: ~10,000–15,000 new BRAO cases/year in the US. Source: AAO, vascular retinology literature.
patients_needing_tx: ~10,000–15,000 patients/year; ~8,000–10,000 with persistent visual field defect needing secondary prevention.
price: $5,000–$30,000/acute episode for a thrombolytic or neuroprotective agent.
peak_sales: $30–80M for a novel tissue plasminogen activator ophthalmic formulation or neuroprotective agent for BRAO.
registrational_endpoint: Recovery of ≥10 ETDRS letters in the affected field quadrant and reduction in area of retinal whitening at 30 days vs. placebo/standard care.
btd_threshold_vs_soc: Visual field recovery in ≥50% vs. ~30% natural history at 30 days.

symptoms: Sudden complete monocular vision loss, cherry-red spot, pale retina
treatment_goal: Restore retinal arterial perfusion within therapeutic window; prevent permanent blindness
first_line_soc: No FDA-approved pharmacologic treatment. IV tissue plasminogen activator (tPA) within 4.5 hours is used at some centers (EAGLE trial negative for intra-arterial; systemic being evaluated). Ocular massage, anterior chamber paracentesis, IOP-lowering. Hemodilution (pentoxifylline) off-label.
treatment_algorithm: Emergency within 4.5 hours: ocular massage, AC paracentesis, sublingual isosorbide dinitrate. Systemic thrombolysis (IV tPA) for select patients within 4.5h (off-label, debated per EAGLE trial). Hyperbaric oxygen within 24 hours has emerging evidence. Giant cell arteritis: urgent IV methylprednisolone. Cardiovascular workup: carotid, cardiac echo, lipid panel, CBC. Aspirin for secondary prevention. Giant cell arteritis treatment to prevent fellow eye.
us_prevalence: ~5,000–8,000 new CRAO cases/year in the US. Source: AAO, AHA/ASA.
patients_needing_tx: ~5,000–8,000 patients/year; ~1,000–2,000 potentially eligible for acute thrombolysis.
price: $10,000–$50,000/acute treatment for a targeted neuroprotective, thrombolytic, or vasodilatory agent.
peak_sales: $30–80M for a first FDA-approved acute CRAO treatment (high unmet need; no approved drug).
registrational_endpoint: BCVA gain ≥15 ETDRS letters at 30 days vs. sham/standard care; proportion with BCVA ≥20/200 at Day 90.
btd_threshold_vs_soc: BCVA ≥20/200 in ≥30% vs. <10% natural history at Day 90; qualifies for Breakthrough Therapy given no approved treatment.

disease: Uveitis Caused by Systemic Rheumatic Disease
symptoms: Recurrent eye pain, redness, photophobia, floaters, vision loss linked to systemic disease
treatment_goal: Control intraocular inflammation; prevent structural complications; preserve vision
first_line_soc: Topical corticosteroids (prednisolone acetate 1% Q1–2h) + cycloplegic (cyclopentolate 1%) for acute anterior uveitis. Systemic corticosteroids (prednisone 1 mg/kg/day) for intermediate/posterior uveitis. Adalimumab (Humira, FDA-approved 2016 for non-infectious uveitis) for refractory/chronic cases.
treatment_algorithm: Anterior: topical prednisolone + cycloplegic; sub-Tenon's triamcinolone 40 mg for persistent. Intermediate/posterior: oral prednisone + steroid-sparing (methotrexate 7.5–25 mg/week, azathioprine, mycophenolate). Refractory: adalimumab 80 mg loading → 40 mg Q2W (VISUAL I/II trials); infliximab 5 mg/kg Q4–8W; golimumab; secukinumab for AS-related. JIA-associated uveitis: adalimumab + MTX. Fluocinolone acetonide implant (Retisert, Yutiq) for posterior uveitis. Intravitreal corticosteroids for refractory. Complications: glaucoma, cataract, CME management.
us_prevalence: ~300,000–500,000 US patients with uveitis associated with systemic rheumatic disease (AS, JIA, SLE, BD, sarcoidosis, IBD); ~120,000 with non-infectious uveitis on systemic therapy. Source: ACR, NEI, AAO.
patients_needing_tx: ~150,000–200,000 patients requiring systemic immunosuppression or biologic therapy.
price: $20,000–$60,000/year for biologics; adalimumab biosimilars ~$15,000–$25,000/year.
peak_sales: $500M–$1.5B for a novel biologic targeting uveitis pathogenesis (Humira uveitis indication ~$500M/year at peak; secukinumab/bimekizumab uveitis trials ongoing).
registrational_endpoint: Treatment failure rate at 6 months (time to treatment failure: worsening uveitis, BCVA loss ≥15 letters, or requiring prohibited medications) in randomized trial vs. placebo (VISUAL I/II primary endpoint model).
btd_threshold_vs_soc: Treatment failure reduction ≥40% vs. placebo at 6 months (VISUAL I: adalimumab failure 21% vs. 55% placebo; VISUAL II: 10% vs. 32%).

symptoms: Eye pain, photophobia, floaters, blurred vision, hypopyon, keratic precipitates
treatment_goal: Eradicate pathogen; control intraocular inflammation; prevent vision loss
first_line_soc: Treat specific pathogen: CMV uveitis (HIV patient): ganciclovir 5 mg/kg IV Q12h or valganciclovir 900 mg BID. Toxoplasma chorioretinitis: pyrimethamine 100 mg loading then 25 mg/day + sulfadiazine 1g QID + folinic acid. Herpetic uveitis (HSV/VZV): oral valacyclovir 1g TID.
treatment_algorithm: CMV retinitis: valganciclovir 900 mg BID × 3 weeks (induction), then 900 mg QD (maintenance); restore immune function in HIV with ART; intravitreal ganciclovir implant for refractory. Toxoplasma: pyrimethamine + sulfadiazine + folinic acid × 6 weeks; intravitreal clindamycin + dexamethasone alternative. HSV/VZV: valacyclovir; necrotizing retinitis (ARN): IV acyclovir 10 mg/kg Q8h × 10–14 days → oral valacyclovir 1g TID ≥14 weeks. Syphilitic uveitis: IV penicillin G × 14 days. Topical corticosteroid + cycloplegic for anterior uveitis component.
us_prevalence: ~50,000–100,000 US cases/year (various pathogens; CMV, toxoplasma, HSV most common). Source: AAO, HIV epidemiology.
patients_needing_tx: ~50,000–80,000 patients/year requiring systemic and/or intravitreal antimicrobials.
price: $3,000–$20,000/course for novel targeted intravitreal or systemic antimicrobial.
peak_sales: $50–100M for a novel long-acting intravitreal antiviral or antiparasitic formulation.
registrational_endpoint: Resolution of active retinitis (retinal whitening, border advancement halt) and vitreous clear-up at 4 weeks; absence of viral/parasitic PCR in aqueous at 6 weeks vs. standard-of-care.
btd_threshold_vs_soc: Retinitis arrest in ≥90% vs. ~75% valganciclovir at 4 weeks for CMV retinitis.

symptoms: Severe eye pain, vision loss, hypopyon, vitritis, periorbital swelling
treatment_goal: Eradicate intraocular infection; prevent permanent vision loss or evisceration
first_line_soc: Intravitreal antibiotics: vancomycin 1 mg/0.1 mL + ceftazidime 2.25 mg/0.1 mL (bacterial); voriconazole 100 mcg/0.1 mL or amphotericin B 10 mcg/0.1 mL for fungal. Pars plana vitrectomy for severe cases (BCVA ≤light perception per EVS trial).
treatment_algorithm: Post-surgical bacterial: immediate intravitreal vancomycin + ceftazidime; PPV for LP or worse (EVS trial). Topical fortified antibiotics (tobramycin + vancomycin). Systemic antibiotics add limited benefit for post-surgical. Endogenous endophthalmitis (Candida, Aspergillus): intravitreal + systemic antifungal (voriconazole 400 mg BID, amphotericin B IV); PPV. Post-intravitreal injection endophthalmitis: urgent intravitreal antibiotics. Intravitreal corticosteroid: dexamethasone 0.4 mg as adjunct (controversial).
us_prevalence: ~10,000–15,000 US cases/year (post-surgical ~5,000–8,000; endogenous ~2,000–3,000; post-intravitreal injection ~2,000–3,000). Source: EVS data, AAO.
patients_needing_tx: ~10,000–15,000 patients/year needing emergent intravitreal and/or systemic antimicrobials.
price: $5,000–$20,000/acute episode for novel targeted intravitreal antimicrobial.
peak_sales: $20–50M for a novel broad-spectrum intravitreal antimicrobial formulation.
registrational_endpoint: Visual acuity ≥20/100 at Day 90 and vitreous culture negativity at Day 7 vs. standard intravitreal antibiotics.
btd_threshold_vs_soc: BCVA ≥20/100 in ≥60% vs. ~50% standard intravitreal antibiotics at Day 90 (EVS data: ~53% with vitrectomy, ~41% with intravitreal alone).

symptoms: Bilateral granulomatous uveitis after penetrating eye injury, photophobia, vision loss
treatment_goal: Suppress bilateral uveitis; preserve vision in sympathizing eye
first_line_soc: High-dose systemic corticosteroids (prednisone 1–2 mg/kg/day or IV methylprednisolone 1g/day × 3 days) initiated urgently. Enucleation of injured eye within 2 weeks of injury may prevent development but not recommended after sympathetic ophthalmia develops.
treatment_algorithm: Prednisone 1–1.5 mg/kg/day × 4–6 weeks, then taper slowly over 6–12+ months. Steroid-sparing: mycophenolate mofetil 1.5–3g/day, azathioprine 1–3 mg/kg/day, or cyclosporine A 3–5 mg/kg/day. Refractory: rituximab, infliximab, adalimumab, daclizumab have case reports. Topical corticosteroid + cycloplegic for anterior segment. Long-term: monitor both eyes; average treatment ≥2 years.
us_prevalence: ~200–500 US cases/year; extremely rare. Source: AAO, FOIS.
patients_needing_tx: ~200–500 patients/year on systemic immunosuppression.
price: $20,000–$80,000/year for a targeted biologic.
peak_sales: <$5M standalone; subsumed in broader uveitis biologic market.
registrational_endpoint: Composite: BCVA ≥20/200 in sympathizing eye AND reduction in anterior chamber cells (SUN grade ≤0.5+) at 24 weeks vs. corticosteroids alone.
btd_threshold_vs_soc: Vision preservation (BCVA ≥20/200) in ≥85% vs. ~60% historical with steroids alone at 12 months.

symptoms: Progressive conductive hearing loss, tinnitus, preferential hearing in noisy environments
treatment_goal: Halt bone remodeling, restore hearing
first_line_soc: Sodium fluoride (off-label) to potentially slow progression; hearing aids for early disease; stapedectomy is definitive treatment.
treatment_algorithm: Step 1: Hearing aids for early/mild hearing loss. Step 2: Sodium fluoride 20–40 mg/day (controversial, may stabilize hearing). Step 3: Bisphosphonates (investigational). Step 4: Stapedectomy/stapedotomy — definitive surgical correction. Step 5: Cochlear implant for far-advanced otosclerosis.
us_prevalence: ~3 million Americans with clinical otosclerosis; ~1% of white population; ~10 million with histologic otosclerosis.
patients_needing_tx: ~500,000 requiring active pharmacotherapy; ~25,000 surgeries/year.
price: $5,000–$15,000/year for novel bisphosphonate or anti-RANKL biologic ear-targeted therapy.
registrational_endpoint: Pure-tone average (PTA) change and cochlear CT bone density at 24 months vs. placebo.
btd_threshold_vs_soc: ≤5 dB PTA progression vs. ~10 dB in placebo over 24 months; CT bone density stabilization.

symptoms: Painless hearing loss, ear fullness, mild conductive loss in children
treatment_goal: Restore hearing, prevent language delay
first_line_soc: Watchful waiting × 3 months (most resolve spontaneously); intranasal corticosteroids may accelerate resolution.
treatment_algorithm: Step 1: Observation × 3 months with autoinflation. Step 2: Intranasal corticosteroid spray × 3 months. Step 3: Short-course systemic steroids (controversial). Step 4: Tympanostomy tubes for persistent OME >3 months with hearing loss ≥25 dB or speech/language delay.
us_prevalence: ~2.2 million tympanostomy tube procedures annually; ~8 million children with OME at any time.
patients_needing_tx: ~3 million children/year receiving pharmacotherapy (intranasal steroids/autoinflation).
price: $400–$1,000/year for novel mucolytic, anti-inflammatory, or eustachian tube modulator.
registrational_endpoint: Tympanogram normalization rate and hearing level improvement at 12 weeks vs. placebo.
btd_threshold_vs_soc: Spontaneous resolution rate >70% at 3 months vs. ~50% for watchful waiting.

symptoms: Ear pain (primary or referred), tenderness
treatment_goal: Identify and treat underlying cause, relieve pain
first_line_soc: Analgesics (NSAIDs, acetaminophen) for symptomatic relief; antibiotics for infectious causes (AOM, OE).
treatment_algorithm: Diagnosis-driven: AOM → antibiotics; OE → topical antibiotic/steroid drops; TMJ → NSAIDs, dental referral; LPR → PPIs; referred otalgia from tonsils/tongue base → treat primary cause.
us_prevalence: ~10 million otalgia presentations/year; ~50% referred pain.
patients_needing_tx: ~5 million requiring pharmacotherapy beyond OTC analgesics.
price: $200–$500 per treatment course.
peak_sales: ~$75M at 5% novel agent share. Symptom-driven market.
registrational_endpoint: NRS pain score reduction ≥2 points at 7 days vs. placebo/comparator.
btd_threshold_vs_soc: NRS improvement ≥2 vs. <0.5 for placebo or superiority to standard analgesic.

symptoms: Ear pain, fever, hearing loss, irritability in children
treatment_goal: Eradicate bacterial infection, relieve pain
first_line_soc: Amoxicillin 80–90 mg/kg/day × 5–10 days (high dose for Streptococcus pneumoniae coverage); amoxicillin-clavulanate if treatment failure.
treatment_algorithm: Step 1: Watchful waiting for mild-moderate AOM in children ≥2 years or high-dose amoxicillin if <2 years/severe. Step 2: Amoxicillin-clavulanate for treatment failure or high penicillin-resistant S. pneumoniae. Step 3: Ceftriaxone IM ×1–3 days if oral not tolerated. Step 4: Tympanostomy tubes for recurrent AOM (≥3/6 months).
us_prevalence: ~30 million AOM episodes/year in US children; 50% of all children have ≥1 episode by age 2; ~5 million recurrent AOM cases.
patients_needing_tx: ~15 million cases needing antibiotics; ~1 million children needing tubes.
price: $50–$150 per antibiotic course (generics); novel anti-pneumococcal agent $500–$1,000.
registrational_endpoint: Clinical cure rate (symptom resolution) at Day 10–14 vs. placebo or amoxicillin (non-inferiority).
btd_threshold_vs_soc: Cure rate ≥85% at Day 10; or reduced treatment failure rate ≥20% vs. amoxicillin for resistant strains.

symptoms: Persistent ear discharge, hearing loss, perforated eardrum
treatment_goal: Eliminate infection, close perforation, preserve hearing
first_line_soc: Topical ciprofloxacin otic solution (ciprofloxacin 0.3% drops) applied after aural toilet — superior to systemic antibiotics for CSOM.
treatment_algorithm: Step 1: Aural toilet (microsuction) + topical ciprofloxacin drops BID. Step 2: Systemic antibiotics for surrounding cellulitis. Step 3: Tympanoplasty/ossiculoplasty for perforation repair once infection controlled. Step 4: Mastoidectomy for cholesteatoma.
us_prevalence: ~2 million Americans with CSOM; predominantly in disadvantaged populations; higher prevalence in indigenous communities.
patients_needing_tx: ~1.5 million needing topical pharmacotherapy; ~500,000 requiring surgery.
price: $500–$1,500 per treatment course (sustained-release antibiotic or biofilm disruptor).
registrational_endpoint: Dry ear rate (cessation of otorrhea) at Day 28 vs. placebo or comparator drops.
btd_threshold_vs_soc: Dry ear rate ≥80% at Day 14 vs. ~50% for standard ciprofloxacin drops.

symptoms: Ear discharge (watery, purulent, or bloody), ear pain, hearing loss
treatment_goal: Identify and treat underlying cause, clear infection
first_line_soc: Topical ciprofloxacin/hydrocortisone drops for infectious otorrhea; systemic antibiotics if systemic infection present.
treatment_algorithm: Cause-directed: OE → topical antibiotics; CSOM → topical ciprofloxacin + aural toilet; CSF otorrhea → neurosurgical; cholesteatoma → surgery.
us_prevalence: ~5 million otorrhea episodes/year as part of OE, CSOM, AOM, or cholesteatoma.
patients_needing_tx: ~3 million/year needing topical antibiotic therapy.
price: $100–$300 per treatment course.
registrational_endpoint: Cessation of otorrhea rate at Day 14 vs. vehicle/placebo.
btd_threshold_vs_soc: Dry ear rate ≥80% at Day 10 vs. ~60% for standard ciprofloxacin drops.

symptoms: Ear pain, hearing loss, discharge, blisters on eardrum
treatment_goal: Resolve infection, relieve pain
first_line_soc: Topical antibiotic/steroid drops (ciprofloxacin-hydrocortisone) for bullous myringitis; analgesia; oral antibiotics if bacterial otitis media coexists.
treatment_algorithm: Step 1: Analgesics for pain. Step 2: Topical ciprofloxacin-HC drops. Step 3: Oral amoxicillin if concurrent AOM. Step 4: Consider Mycoplasma: macrolide (azithromycin).
us_prevalence: ~300,000–500,000 cases/year in US; often occurs with AOM.
patients_needing_tx: ~300,000/year needing topical/oral treatment.
price: $100–$300 per treatment course.
registrational_endpoint: Resolution of bullae and pain score at Day 7 vs. placebo drops.
btd_threshold_vs_soc: Resolution rate ≥85% at Day 5 vs. ~60% for placebo.

disease: Traumatic Perforation of the Tympanic Membrane
symptoms: Sudden ear pain, hearing loss, tinnitus after trauma or blast
treatment_goal: Promote healing, prevent infection, restore hearing
first_line_soc: Conservative management (keep ear dry, no water entry); topical antibiotics if contaminated. Most heal spontaneously.
treatment_algorithm: Step 1: Observation + ear protection (dry ear precautions). Step 2: Topical ciprofloxacin drops if contaminated/infected. Step 3: Paper patch tympanoplasty for non-healing perforations. Step 4: Surgical tympanoplasty at 3–6 months if not healed.
us_prevalence: ~100,000–200,000 traumatic perforations/year (blast, Q-tip, barotrauma); ~70% heal spontaneously.
patients_needing_tx: ~60,000/year with contaminated or non-healing perforations needing pharmacotherapy.
price: $500–$2,000 for growth factor ear drops (e.g., EGF, bFGF) to accelerate healing.
registrational_endpoint: Complete tympanic membrane healing rate at 6 weeks vs. placebo drops.
btd_threshold_vs_soc: Healing rate ≥80% at 4 weeks vs. ~60% spontaneous healing.

symptoms: Ear pain, fullness, hearing loss during pressure change
treatment_goal: Equalize ear pressure, prevent tympanic membrane rupture
first_line_soc: Intranasal decongestants (oxymetazoline, pseudoephedrine) before flights/diving; auto-inflation techniques. Analgesics for pain.
treatment_algorithm: Step 1: Oxymetazoline nasal spray 30 minutes before descent. Step 2: Valsalva maneuver. Step 3: Analgesics for pain. Step 4: Tympanostomy tube for recurrent/severe barotrauma.
us_prevalence: ~10–17 million otic barotrauma episodes/year in US air travelers; mostly self-limited.
patients_needing_tx: ~2 million seeking medical treatment; ~500,000 require prescription decongestants.
price: $50–$200 per preventive dose course; OTC-dominated.
registrational_endpoint: Tympanogram normalization and pain score at Day 3 vs. placebo.
btd_threshold_vs_soc: N/A (mostly OTC and self-limiting).

symptoms: Post-auricular swelling, ear pain, fever, hearing loss
treatment_goal: Eradicate infection, prevent intracranial complications
first_line_soc: IV amoxicillin-clavulanate or ceftriaxone for acute mastoiditis; mastoidectomy for complicated cases.
treatment_algorithm: Step 1: IV ceftriaxone 50 mg/kg/day for acute coalescent mastoiditis. Step 2: Myringotomy + tympanostomy tube for drainage. Step 3: Mastoidectomy (cortical) for subperiosteal abscess or failed medical therapy. Step 4: Intracranial complication: neurosurgical consultation + IV vancomycin.
us_prevalence: ~2–5 cases/100,000/year; ~6,000–16,000 cases/year in US.
patients_needing_tx: ~10,000/year needing IV antibiotics.
price: $500–$1,500 per IV treatment course.
peak_sales: ~$3M at 30% share. Commodity market. Not a viable novel drug target.
registrational_endpoint: Clinical cure rate and time to resolution of post-auricular inflammation at Day 14.
btd_threshold_vs_soc: Cure rate ≥95% at Day 10 vs. ~80% for standard ceftriaxone.

disease: Dermatitis of the Ear Canal (Chronic Otitis Externa)
symptoms: Ear itching, scaling, discharge, pain, recurrent infections
treatment_goal: Reduce inflammation, control infection, prevent recurrence
first_line_soc: Topical corticosteroid ear drops (e.g., hydrocortisone 1%) with or without antifungal (clotrimazole) or antibiotic (ciprofloxacin-hydrocortisone otic solution).
treatment_algorithm: Step 1: Ear cleaning (microsuction), topical corticosteroid ± antibiotic drops (ciprofloxacin-hydrocortisone). Step 2: Antifungal (clotrimazole 1%) for fungal otitis externa. Step 3: Oral antibiotics for severe/spreading cellulitis. Step 4: Tacrolimus 0.1% ointment for refractory allergic/dermatitic cases.
us_prevalence: ~2.4 million chronic otitis externa cases in US; ~10 million acute OE episodes/year.
patients_needing_tx: ~1 million with chronic/recurrent dermatitis requiring maintenance pharmacotherapy.
price: $600–$1,200/year for a novel topical anti-inflammatory/antimicrobial combination.
registrational_endpoint: Change from baseline in Global Otitis Externa Severity Scale (GOSS) at Week 4 vs. placebo.
btd_threshold_vs_soc: ≥40% improvement in GOSS vs. ~20% placebo improvement; or recurrence rate <25% at 6 months.

symptoms: Hearing loss, ear fullness, pain, itching
treatment_goal: Remove obstruction, restore hearing
first_line_soc: Cerumenolytic ear drops (carbamide peroxide, docusate, mineral oil) for cerumen impaction; irrigation or manual removal.
treatment_algorithm: Step 1: Cerumenolytic drops (carbamide peroxide 6.5% or docusate sodium) × 3–5 days. Step 2: Ear irrigation with warm water. Step 3: Manual microsuction or curette by clinician for hard impactions.
us_prevalence: ~12 million cerumen impaction visits/year in US; ~2 million audiologist/physician visits for external ear obstruction.
patients_needing_tx: ~5 million self-treat annually; ~2 million seek medical cerumenolytic therapy.
price: $15–$50 OTC; prescription novel cerumenolytic $100–$300/course.
peak_sales: ~$45M at 15% Rx share. Limited novel drug opportunity (OTC-dominated).
registrational_endpoint: Complete cerumen removal rate at Day 5 vs. placebo drops.
btd_threshold_vs_soc: Removal rate ≥80% at Day 3 vs. ~50% for standard carbamide peroxide.

symptoms: Ear canal pain, tenderness, discharge, swelling
treatment_goal: Eradicate infection, relieve pain, restore canal
first_line_soc: Topical ciprofloxacin-hydrocortisone otic solution (Ciprodex) or acetic acid drops; analgesics for pain.
treatment_algorithm: Step 1: Ear canal cleaning; topical ciprofloxacin 0.3%/hydrocortisone 0.1% (Ciprodex) BID × 7 days. Step 2: Wick insertion if canal severely edematous. Step 3: Oral fluoroquinolone if periauricular cellulitis. Step 4: IV antibiotics for malignant OE (Pseudomonas, in diabetics/immunocompromised).
us_prevalence: ~2.4 million acute OE episodes/year in US; summer peak; ~600,000 physician visits.
patients_needing_tx: ~1.5 million needing topical antibiotic treatment per year.
price: $80–$200 per treatment course (largely generic market); novel sustained-release formulation ~$400.
registrational_endpoint: Clinical cure rate (pain, edema, discharge resolution) at Day 10 vs. vehicle.
btd_threshold_vs_soc: Cure rate ≥85% at Day 7 vs. ~70% for standard Ciprodex.

symptoms: Painful red swollen ear (pinna), fever, risk of cartilage destruction
treatment_goal: Eradicate Pseudomonas infection, preserve ear cartilage
first_line_soc: Oral ciprofloxacin (750 mg BID × 7–14 days) for mild-moderate perichondritis; IV fluoroquinolone for severe cases.
treatment_algorithm: Step 1: Oral ciprofloxacin for moderate disease. Step 2: IV ciprofloxacin or antipseudomonal beta-lactam for severe. Step 3: Surgical debridement if cartilage necrosis. Step 4: Preventive: cover ear piercing-related trauma.
us_prevalence: ~50,000–100,000 cases/year (rising with ear piercing prevalence).
patients_needing_tx: ~75,000/year needing antibiotic treatment.
price: $200–$500 per treatment course.
registrational_endpoint: Clinical cure (resolution of erythema, swelling) at Day 14.
btd_threshold_vs_soc: N/A (ciprofloxacin highly effective; no breakthrough opportunity).

symptoms: Severe ear pain, purulent discharge, cranial nerve palsy, fever (immunocompromised)
treatment_goal: Eradicate Pseudomonas infection, prevent intracranial spread
first_line_soc: IV ciprofloxacin or antipseudomonal beta-lactam (piperacillin-tazobactam) for 4–8 weeks; topical ciprofloxacin adjunct.
treatment_algorithm: Step 1: IV ciprofloxacin 400 mg q8h or IV cefepime for 4–8 weeks. Step 2: Surgical debridement if necrotic tissue. Step 3: Hyperbaric oxygen therapy (adjunct). Step 4: Oral ciprofloxacin for step-down therapy. Monitor: gallium/CT scanning for resolution.
us_prevalence: ~2,500–4,000 cases/year; predominantly diabetics and immunocompromised patients.
patients_needing_tx: ~3,000/year needing 4–8 weeks IV antibiotics.
price: $5,000–$10,000 per treatment course.
registrational_endpoint: Radiographic and clinical cure rate at 8 weeks vs. standard ciprofloxacin.
btd_threshold_vs_soc: Cure rate ≥90% vs. ~70% for standard IV ciprofloxacin in complicated cases.

symptoms: Ear swelling, pain, deformity after trauma
treatment_goal: Drain hematoma, prevent cauliflower ear deformity
first_line_soc: None (pharmacologic); treatment is mechanical drainage (aspiration or incision and drainage) plus pressure dressing.
treatment_algorithm: Surgical drainage (aspiration or I&D) within 24–48 hours; compression dressing; antibiotics (cephalexin) if infection suspected. No proven pharmacologic intervention to prevent re-accumulation.
us_prevalence: ~50,000–80,000 acute auricular hematoma cases/year in US (wrestlers, rugby, MMA athletes, trauma patients).
patients_needing_tx: Limited pharmacologic TAM; ~20,000/year with secondary infection or anti-inflammatory need.
price: $200–$500 for a one-time injectable anti-fibrinolytic or anti-inflammatory. Not orphan.
peak_sales: ~$2M at 30% share. TAM is negligible; no significant drug market.
registrational_endpoint: Rate of hematoma recurrence at 30 days post-drainage vs. standard drainage alone.
btd_threshold_vs_soc: Recurrence rate <10% vs. ~30% with standard drainage.

symptoms: Ear fullness, pain, hearing loss, discharge
treatment_goal: Safe removal of foreign body without trauma
first_line_soc: None (pharmacologic); mechanical removal (irrigation, suction, forceps, right-angle hook) under direct visualization.
treatment_algorithm: Mechanical extraction under otoscopy; insects: kill first with mineral oil or lidocaine then extract. Post-removal: antibiotic drops if canal traumatized.
us_prevalence: ~350,000 ER/urgent care visits/year for ear foreign bodies in US, predominantly children.
patients_needing_tx: Post-removal antibiotic pool ~100,000/year. No meaningful drug TAM.
price: N/A (procedural; no drug market).
peak_sales: Negligible. Not a viable drug target.

symptoms: Persistent ringing or noise in ears, sleep disruption, anxiety
treatment_goal: Reduce tinnitus loudness/distress, improve quality of life
first_line_soc: No FDA-approved pharmacologic treatment; cognitive behavioral therapy (CBT) and sound therapy are first-line. Hearing aids for comorbid hearing loss.
treatment_algorithm: Step 1: Hearing aids ± sound maskers. Step 2: CBT for tinnitus. Step 3: Tinnitus retraining therapy (TRT). Step 4: Low-dose amitriptyline or nortriptyline (off-label, modest effect). Step 5: Investigational: AM-101 (intratympanic NMDA antagonist), FX-322 (hair cell regeneration), neuromodulation (rTMS).
us_prevalence: ~50 million Americans with tinnitus; ~16 million with chronic bothersome tinnitus; ~2 million severely debilitated.
patients_needing_tx: ~5 million seeking pharmacologic treatment; ~2 million with severe disease.
price: $3,000–$8,000/year for novel pharmacologic agent.
registrational_endpoint: Change from baseline in Tinnitus Functional Index (TFI) and Tinnitus Handicap Inventory (THI) at 12 weeks.
btd_threshold_vs_soc: ≥13-point TFI improvement (MCID) vs. <3 for placebo; or ≥7-point THI improvement.

symptoms: Reduced hearing acuity, speech difficulty, social withdrawal
treatment_goal: Restore or compensate for hearing, prevent progression
first_line_soc: Hearing aids for sensorineural hearing loss (SNHL); cochlear implants for severe-profound SNHL. No approved pharmacologic treatment for SNHL.
treatment_algorithm: Sensorineural: hearing aids → cochlear implant for severe-profound loss. Conductive: treat underlying cause (cerumen removal, tympanostomy, ossiculoplasty). Sudden SNHL: systemic corticosteroids (prednisone) first-line; intratympanic dexamethasone for salvage. Novel: gene therapy (AAV-OTOF, DB-OTO) in trials.
us_prevalence: ~48 million Americans have some hearing loss; ~15 million with severe-profound SNHL needing device or therapy.
patients_needing_tx: ~5 million moderate-severe SNHL patients potentially eligible for a pharmacologic/gene therapy; 200,000+/year with sudden SNHL.
price: $50,000–$150,000 for gene therapy (one-time); $2,000–$5,000/year for neuroprotective small molecule.
tam_gross: For gene therapy (200K severe genetic SNHL): 200,000 × $100,000 = $20B; small molecule neuroprotection: 5M × $3,000 = $15B
peak_sales: Gene therapy peak ~$500M–$1B at 5% penetration. Small molecule ~$900M at 6% share. Combined novel therapy TAM ~$35B; peak sales ~$1B.
registrational_endpoint: Mean change from baseline in pure-tone average (PTA) at 3 months (gene therapy); SNHL prevention rate at 12 months.
btd_threshold_vs_soc: ≥10 dB improvement in PTA vs. natural history; functional hearing in ≥50% of treated ears (gene therapy).

symptoms: Progressive hearing loss, tinnitus, balance problems after drug exposure
treatment_goal: Prevent or halt cisplatin/aminoglycoside-induced hearing loss
first_line_soc: Sodium thiosulfate (STS): approved (Pedmark) for cisplatin-induced ototoxicity in pediatric patients. No approved drug for aminoglycoside ototoxicity prevention.
treatment_algorithm: Step 1: Sodium thiosulfate (Pedmark) IV for pediatric oncology patients on cisplatin regimens. Step 2: Audiological monitoring and early hearing aid fitting. Step 3: Cochlear implant for severe-profound loss. Step 4: Investigational: D-methionine, N-acetylcysteine, aspirin (clinical trials).
us_prevalence: ~30,000 pediatric cisplatin recipients/year; ~60–75% develop hearing loss. ~100,000 adults/year on aminoglycosides at risk.
patients_needing_tx: ~25,000 pediatric patients/year needing STS; ~20,000 at-risk adults needing otoprotection.
price: $15,000–$25,000/treatment course (STS/Pedmark ~$18,000/course). Pediatric orphan pricing applies.
registrational_endpoint: Proportion of patients with significant hearing threshold shift (≥10 dB at ≥2 kHz, SIOP Boston scale) vs. placebo.
btd_threshold_vs_soc: ≥30% absolute reduction in ototoxicity incidence vs. placebo (Pedmark showed ~49% reduction in pivotal trial).

symptoms: Sudden unilateral sensorineural hearing loss over ≤72 hours
treatment_goal: Recover hearing, prevent permanent loss
first_line_soc: Systemic oral prednisone (1 mg/kg/day × 10–14 days, max 60 mg) as first-line; intratympanic dexamethasone as rescue/alternative.
treatment_algorithm: Step 1: High-dose oral prednisone within 2 weeks of onset. Step 2: Intratympanic dexamethasone (4 mg/mL, 3 injections) as primary or salvage therapy. Step 3: Hyperbaric oxygen (adjunct). Step 4: Hearing aids or cochlear implant if no recovery.
us_prevalence: ~66,000 cases/year of sudden sensorineural hearing loss in US.
patients_needing_tx: ~60,000/year requiring systemic or intratympanic corticosteroid treatment.
price: $500–$2,000 per treatment course; cochlear perfusion device $15,000.
peak_sales: ~$18M at 25% share with novel drug delivery or cochlear perfusion device.
registrational_endpoint: Change from baseline in PTA (pure-tone average) at 6 weeks; proportion with ≥10 dB hearing recovery.
btd_threshold_vs_soc: ≥50% recovery rate (≥10 dB PTA improvement) vs. ~40–45% for oral steroids.

symptoms: Spinning sensation, imbalance, nausea, falls
treatment_goal: Reduce attack frequency and severity, improve balance
first_line_soc: Meclizine (antihistamine/anticholinergic) for acute vestibular episodes; betahistine for chronic vestibular disorders in some guidelines.
treatment_algorithm: Step 1: Acute attack — meclizine 25–50 mg or diazepam 5 mg. Step 2: Vestibular suppressants (prochlorperazine, ondansetron for nausea). Step 3: Betahistine (off-label US) for Meniere's-type vertigo. Step 4: Vestibular rehabilitation. Step 5: Intratympanic gentamicin/steroids for refractory Meniere's.
us_prevalence: ~69 million Americans experience vestibular dysfunction annually; ~8 million with chronic dizziness/vertigo.
patients_needing_tx: ~3 million with recurrent/chronic vertigo needing ongoing pharmacotherapy.
price: $1,500–$3,000/year for a novel vestibular modulator or H3-receptor agonist.
registrational_endpoint: Change from baseline in Dizziness Handicap Inventory (DHI) and vertigo attack frequency at 12 weeks.
btd_threshold_vs_soc: ≥15-point improvement in DHI vs. <5 for meclizine; or ≥50% reduction in monthly vertigo attacks.

symptoms: Episodic vertigo, low-frequency hearing loss, tinnitus, ear fullness
treatment_goal: Reduce vertigo attack frequency and hearing loss progression
first_line_soc: Low-sodium diet + diuretics (hydrochlorothiazide-triamterene) as first-line pharmacotherapy; betahistine widely used outside US.
treatment_algorithm: Step 1: Low-sodium diet (<2g/day) + diuretic (HCTZ 25 mg/triamterene 37.5 mg daily). Step 2: Betahistine 24 mg TID (not FDA-approved but used off-label). Step 3: Intratympanic dexamethasone for acute exacerbations. Step 4: Intratympanic gentamicin for refractory vertigo. Step 5: Endolymphatic sac surgery or labyrinthectomy.
us_prevalence: ~615,000 US patients with Meniere disease; ~45,500 new cases/year.
patients_needing_tx: ~450,000 patients needing pharmacotherapy; ~100,000 with inadequately controlled disease needing advanced therapy.
price: $3,000–$8,000/year for novel endolymphatic hydrops-targeting agent or intratympanic drug delivery system.
peak_sales: ~$450M at 20% share for refractory patients (100,000 × $8,000 × 50% + 350,000 × $3,000 × 15%). Approximately $400–$500M.
registrational_endpoint: Change in definitive vertigo attack frequency per month (2–6 month baseline vs. 6-month treatment) + AAO-HNS functional level.
btd_threshold_vs_soc: ≥50% reduction in monthly vertigo attacks vs. placebo; or <2 attacks/month vs. ~6 on placebo.

symptoms: Sudden prolonged vertigo, nausea/vomiting, gait instability, no hearing loss
treatment_goal: Control acute vertigo, accelerate vestibular compensation
first_line_soc: Methylprednisolone (oral corticosteroid) to speed recovery; vestibular suppressants (diazepam, meclizine) for acute symptom control.
treatment_algorithm: Step 1: Diazepam 5 mg IV or meclizine 25 mg for acute vestibular crisis. Step 2: Methylprednisolone 100 mg → taper × 21 days. Step 3: Antiemetics (ondansetron, promethazine). Step 4: Early vestibular rehabilitation × 4–6 weeks. Antiviral (acyclovir) — no proven benefit.
us_prevalence: ~5 million vestibular neuronitis/labyrinthitis episodes/year; second most common cause of vertigo.
patients_needing_tx: ~1.5 million/year seeking pharmacotherapy beyond OTC antiemetics.
price: $500–$1,500 per acute course; $1,000–$3,000/year for neuroprotective agent.
registrational_endpoint: DHI improvement and time to compensation (walking unsupported) at Day 7 and 30 days.
btd_threshold_vs_soc: ≥50% DHI improvement at Day 30 vs. ~35% for methylprednisolone alone.

symptoms: Progressive unilateral hearing loss, tinnitus, vertigo, facial numbness
treatment_goal: Control tumor, preserve hearing and facial nerve function
first_line_soc: None (pharmacologic for NF2-associated schwannomas: bevacizumab anti-VEGF used off-label). Primary treatment: observation (small), stereotactic radiosurgery (Gamma Knife), or microsurgical resection.
treatment_algorithm: Sporadic (<3 cm): observation with serial MRI; radiosurgery or microsurgery. NF2-associated: bevacizumab (10 mg/kg q2w) has demonstrated hearing improvement and tumor regression off-label. Anti-tumor investigational agents: lapatinib, nilotinib in NF2.
us_prevalence: ~100,000 US patients with vestibular schwannoma; ~13,000 new diagnoses/year. NF2-associated ~3,000–5,000 patients.
patients_needing_tx: ~5,000 NF2 patients + ~10,000 sporadic requiring active treatment including pharmacotherapy.
price: $80,000–$120,000/year for anti-VEGF or ERK inhibitor (NF2); pediatric orphan pricing for NF2.
peak_sales: ~$270M at 20% share for NF2-targeted agent. 5,000 NF2 × $100,000 × 50% = $250M.
registrational_endpoint: Tumor volumetric response (≥20% reduction) at 6 months and hearing preservation (Gardner-Robertson Grade I-II) at 12 months.
btd_threshold_vs_soc: Hearing improvement in ≥50% of NF2 patients vs. ≤5% with observation; tumor volume reduction ≥15%.

symptoms: Involuntary eye movements, oscillopsia, visual blurring, head bobbing
treatment_goal: Reduce nystagmus intensity, improve visual acuity
first_line_soc: Memantine (for acquired pendular nystagmus) or gabapentin (infantile nystagmus) — limited evidence, off-label use.
treatment_algorithm: Step 1: Identify and treat underlying cause (vestibular neuritis, MS, posterior fossa lesion). Step 2: Memantine 10–20 mg/day or gabapentin 300–900 mg/day for pendular nystagmus. Step 3: 4-aminopyridine for downbeat nystagmus (potassium channel blocker, off-label). Step 4: Optical aids (contact lenses, prisms). Step 5: Surgery (tenotomy) for infantile nystagmus.
us_prevalence: ~300,000 Americans with clinically significant nystagmus; ~1:1,500 births infantile nystagmus (~200,000 pediatric).
patients_needing_tx: ~100,000 with symptomatic nystagmus seeking pharmacotherapy.
price: $5,000–$15,000/year for a novel potassium channel modulator.
registrational_endpoint: Change from baseline in peak slow-phase velocity (PSPV) on eye-tracking at 12 weeks; BCVA improvement.
btd_threshold_vs_soc: ≥30% reduction in PSPV vs. placebo; or ≥2-line BCVA improvement.

symptoms: Brief spinning vertigo triggered by head position changes
treatment_goal: Resolve vertigo with canalith repositioning
first_line_soc: Canalith repositioning procedure (Epley maneuver) — non-pharmacologic; vestibular suppressants (meclizine) for acute symptomatic relief only.
treatment_algorithm: Step 1: Epley maneuver (posterior canal BPPV) or Barbecue roll (horizontal canal BPPV). Step 2: Meclizine 25 mg PRN for acute attacks (short-term only). Step 3: Vestibular rehabilitation therapy. Step 4: Canal plugging surgery for refractory cases.
us_prevalence: ~2.4 million Americans experience BPPV annually; lifetime prevalence ~2.4%; ~800,000 seek medical care.
patients_needing_tx: ~400,000/year needing pharmacotherapy (meclizine or betahistine) for symptom control; repositioning resolves most.
price: $300–$800/year for a novel vestibular modulator.
registrational_endpoint: Time to resolution of nystagmus/vertigo on Dix-Hallpike test at Day 7 vs. sham maneuver + placebo drug.
btd_threshold_vs_soc: Resolution rate >80% at 1 week vs. ~60% for Epley + placebo.

symptoms: Ear pain, vesicular rash, facial palsy, hearing loss, vertigo
treatment_goal: Accelerate healing, prevent facial nerve complications
first_line_soc: Oral valacyclovir (1g TID × 7 days) plus prednisone (60 mg tapered over 10 days); treat within 72 hours for best outcomes.
treatment_algorithm: Step 1: Valacyclovir + prednisone started within 72 hours. Step 2: Pain management (gabapentin, analgesics). Step 3: Eye care if corneal exposure. Step 4: Physical therapy for facial palsy. Prophylaxis: Shingrix (RZV) vaccine reduces risk by ~90%.
us_prevalence: ~20,000–30,000 Ramsay Hunt syndrome cases/year; ~1 million total herpes zoster cases/year in US.
patients_needing_tx: ~25,000/year needing antiviral + steroid treatment for Ramsay Hunt.
price: $500–$1,500 per treatment course; improved antiviral or nerve regeneration peptide.
peak_sales: ~$10M at 40% share. Small market; Shingrix reducing incidence.
registrational_endpoint: Rate of complete facial nerve recovery (House-Brackmann Grade I-II) at 6 months vs. standard antiviral.
btd_threshold_vs_soc: Complete recovery rate ≥75% vs. ~50% with valacyclovir + prednisone.

symptoms: Sudden profound hearing loss, severe vertigo, nausea, fever
treatment_goal: Eradicate infection, preserve cochlear function
first_line_soc: IV broad-spectrum antibiotics (ceftriaxone) targeting meningitis/otogenic source; IV dexamethasone to reduce inflammation and hearing loss.
treatment_algorithm: Step 1: IV ceftriaxone + IV dexamethasone for bacterial labyrinthitis. Step 2: Acyclovir for viral (HSV) labyrinthitis. Step 3: Vestibular rehabilitation. Step 4: Cochlear implantation for post-labyrinthitic deafness (early to prevent cochlear ossification).
us_prevalence: ~500–2,000 cases/year; rare complication of bacterial meningitis or CSOM.
patients_needing_tx: ~1,000/year needing IV antibiotic + steroid treatment.
price: $2,000–$5,000 per treatment course.
peak_sales: Negligible. Not a viable drug target.
registrational_endpoint: Hearing preservation rate at 3 months vs. antibiotic without steroids.
btd_threshold_vs_soc: ≥30% absolute reduction in profound hearing loss incidence vs. antibiotics alone.

symptoms: Low-frequency conductive hearing loss, autophony, pulsatile tinnitus, hyperacusis to bone conduction
treatment_goal: Symptom control or surgical obliteration of third window
first_line_soc: None (pharmacologic); primary treatment is surgical (plugging of superior semicircular canal dehiscence or round window reinforcement).
treatment_algorithm: Conservative: avoidance of Valsalva; lifestyle modification. Surgical: middle cranial fossa approach or transmastoid plugging for SSCD. No established pharmacotherapy.
us_prevalence: ~1% of population has SSCD on CT (most asymptomatic); ~2,000–5,000 symptomatic patients requiring treatment/year.
patients_needing_tx: ~2,000 symptomatic requiring treatment; minimal pharmacologic market.
price: N/A (surgical condition).

symptoms: Breathy/weak voice, aspiration, dysphagia, reduced cough strength
treatment_goal: Restore vocal fold function, prevent aspiration
first_line_soc: Voice therapy for unilateral VFP; no approved pharmacologic agent. Surgical: medialization laryngoplasty, arytenoid adduction, or vocal fold injection.
treatment_algorithm: Step 1: Speech/voice therapy. Step 2: Vocal fold injection (Radiesse, Gelfoam, fat, CaHA) for acute medialization. Step 3: Medialization thyroplasty (type I) for persistent unilateral VFP. Step 4: Laryngeal reinnervation for pediatric/selected cases.
us_prevalence: ~90,000–160,000 new cases/year of VFP in US; cumulative prevalence ~500,000.
patients_needing_tx: ~100,000/year requiring intervention (surgical or pharmacologic).
price: $10,000–$30,000 for laryngeal reinnervation therapy or neurotrophic factor injection.
registrational_endpoint: Voice Handicap Index (VHI-30) change and maximum phonation time (MPT) at 6 months vs. observation.
btd_threshold_vs_soc: VHI-30 improvement ≥18 points (MCID) and MPT ≥5 seconds improvement vs. observation.

symptoms: Hoarseness, voice loss, sore throat, dry cough
treatment_goal: Restore voice, relieve inflammation
first_line_soc: Voice rest and humidification for acute laryngitis; PPIs for LPR-related laryngitis. Antibiotics only for documented bacterial infection.
treatment_algorithm: Step 1: Voice rest, hydration, humidification. Step 2: PPI BID if LPR component. Step 3: Inhaled corticosteroids for chronic laryngitis. Step 4: Antibiotics (macrolide) if Mycoplasma/atypical etiology suspected. Steroids (IM/oral) for performance voice emergency.
us_prevalence: ~21 million cases/year in US; mostly self-limited viral etiology.
patients_needing_tx: ~5 million seeking pharmacologic treatment; ~1 million with chronic laryngitis.
price: $300–$800/year for topical anti-inflammatory throat formulation.
registrational_endpoint: Time to voice recovery and GRBAS scale improvement at Day 7 vs. placebo.
btd_threshold_vs_soc: Voice recovery rate ≥70% at Day 7 vs. ~50% for placebo/voice rest.

symptoms: Strained, strangled, or whispery voice; intermittent voice breaks
treatment_goal: Reduce abnormal vocal cord spasm, restore normal voice
first_line_soc: Botulinum toxin A injection (onabotulinumtoxinA, Botox) into the thyroarytenoid muscle — standard of care for adductor laryngeal dystonia.
treatment_algorithm: Step 1: EMG-guided botulinum toxin A injection (2.5–5 IU/side) q3–4 months. Step 2: Voice therapy. Step 3: Surgical: selective laryngeal adductor denervation-reinnervation (SLAD-R) for sustained benefit. No oral medications with consistent evidence.
us_prevalence: ~50,000–100,000 US patients with laryngeal dystonia (spasmodic dysphonia); prevalence ~1–4/10,000.
patients_needing_tx: ~60,000 patients receiving or needing botulinum toxin injections.
price: $5,000–$10,000/year for longer-acting botulinum toxin or oral neuromodulator.
registrational_endpoint: VHI-10 (Voice Handicap Index) improvement and voice break frequency at 12 weeks post-injection.
btd_threshold_vs_soc: ≥50% reduction in voice break frequency vs. placebo; VHI-10 improvement ≥6 points.

disease: Vocal Fold Polyps, Nodules, and Granulomas
symptoms: Hoarseness, voice fatigue, reduced pitch range, vocal effort
treatment_goal: Restore normal voice, resolve benign vocal lesion
first_line_soc: Voice therapy (behavioral modification) as first-line for nodules and small polyps; PPIs for LPR-associated granulomas.
treatment_algorithm: Step 1: Voice therapy (vocal hygiene, resonance therapy) × 8–12 weeks. Step 2: PPIs (omeprazole 40 mg BID) for granulomas. Step 3: Botulinum toxin for contact granuloma. Step 4: Microlaryngoscopic surgical excision for polyps not responding to therapy.
us_prevalence: ~1 million Americans with benign vocal fold lesions; 200,000–400,000 seeking treatment annually.
patients_needing_tx: ~200,000/year needing pharmacotherapy (PPIs for granuloma, botox for refractory) or therapy.
price: $1,000–$3,000/year for vocal fold anti-inflammatory or wound-modulating agent.
registrational_endpoint: Laryngoscopic lesion resolution and VHI-10 change at 12 weeks vs. voice therapy alone.
btd_threshold_vs_soc: Lesion resolution ≥60% at 12 weeks vs. ~30% for voice therapy alone.

symptoms: Throat pain, hoarseness, chronic throat clearing, globus sensation
treatment_goal: Heal ulcers, reduce reflux, correct voice misuse
first_line_soc: Proton pump inhibitors (PPIs, e.g., omeprazole 40 mg BID) for laryngopharyngeal reflux; voice therapy.
treatment_algorithm: Step 1: PPI therapy × 3–6 months + voice therapy for vocal hygiene. Step 2: H2-blockers if PPI insufficient. Step 3: Antireflux surgery (Nissen fundoplication) for refractory LPR. Step 4: Biopsy to exclude malignancy.
us_prevalence: ~200,000–400,000 cases of contact granuloma/ulcer per year in US; predominantly professional voice users and LPR patients.
patients_needing_tx: ~150,000/year needing PPI therapy for LPR-driven contact ulcers.
price: $1,500–$3,000/year for novel mucoprotective or anti-inflammatory agent.
registrational_endpoint: Endoscopic healing rate and voice handicap index improvement at 12 weeks vs. placebo.
btd_threshold_vs_soc: Healing rate ≥70% at 12 weeks vs. ~40% for PPI alone.

symptoms: Hoarseness, voice change, throat discomfort, dysphagia
treatment_goal: Remove tumor, restore voice and airway patency
first_line_soc: None (pharmacologic); primary treatment is surgical (microlaryngoscopy with excision). Voice therapy for vocal fold polyps/nodules.
treatment_algorithm: Step 1: Microlaryngoscopy with cold-knife or laser excision for papillomas, hemangiomas. Step 2: For recurrent respiratory papillomatosis (HPV-related): cidofovir injections, bevacizumab (off-label) intralesional, interferon-alpha (historical). Step 3: Voice therapy post-excision.
us_prevalence: ~10,000–15,000 cases/year needing surgical excision; RRP subset ~20,000 patients.
patients_needing_tx: ~5,000 RRP patients needing adjuvant pharmacotherapy; broader benign tumor pharma market limited.
price: $3,000–$8,000/year for intralesional or adjuvant biologic (e.g., anti-VEGF, anti-HPV).
registrational_endpoint: Reduction in Derkay severity score or time-to-recurrence post-surgical excision at 12 months.
btd_threshold_vs_soc: ≥50% reduction in Derkay score or ≥6-month extension of recurrence-free interval vs. placebo adjuvant.

symptoms: Progressive breathing difficulty, stridor, exercise intolerance
treatment_goal: Restore airway patency, avoid tracheostomy
first_line_soc: None (pharmacologic first-line); primary treatment is surgical (dilation, endoscopic laser, laryngotracheal reconstruction). Intralesional steroid injection (triamcinolone) used perioperatively to prevent restenosis.
treatment_algorithm: Step 1: Endoscopic balloon dilation. Step 2: Intralesional triamcinolone injection post-dilation. Step 3: CO2 laser/cold-knife incision. Step 4: Open laryngotracheal reconstruction (cricotracheal resection). Systemic steroids and antibiotics perioperatively.
us_prevalence: ~1,500–3,000 new cases/year; mostly post-intubation. Idiopathic SGS ~500 cases/year (female predominance).
patients_needing_tx: ~2,000/year needing steroid injections + procedural management.
price: $5,000–$15,000/year for anti-fibrotic biologic or TGF-beta inhibitor.
