registrational_endpoint: Time to restenosis and change in cross-sectional airway area at 6 months post-dilation.
btd_threshold_vs_soc: Restenosis rate <30% at 6 months vs. ~60% without anti-fibrotic treatment.

symptoms: Severe sore throat, drooling, stridor, high fever, difficulty swallowing
treatment_goal: Secure airway, eradicate infection
first_line_soc: IV ceftriaxone (empiric broad-spectrum antibiotic) plus airway management (intubation or tracheotomy if needed); corticosteroids adjunctive.
treatment_algorithm: Step 1: Immediate airway assessment; intubation or surgical airway if severe. Step 2: IV ceftriaxone 2g q24h (or ampicillin-sulbactam) for 7–10 days. Step 3: IV dexamethasone to reduce edema. Step 4: H. influenzae type b vaccination for prevention.
us_prevalence: ~1,500–2,500 hospitalized cases/year in US (dramatically reduced post-Hib vaccination); mainly adults now.
patients_needing_tx: ~2,000 cases/year needing IV antibiotic treatment.
price: $200–$500 per treatment course (antibiotics; no novel drug opportunity).
peak_sales: Negligible (~$200K). Not a viable commercial target.
registrational_endpoint: Time to extubation and fever resolution vs. comparator antibiotic.
btd_threshold_vs_soc: N/A (adequate therapies exist; disease too rare for BTD threshold definition).

symptoms: Neck mass, hoarseness, dysphagia, airway obstruction
treatment_goal: Remove cyst, relieve obstruction
first_line_soc: None (pharmacologic); primary treatment is surgical excision (external or endoscopic approach).
treatment_algorithm: Surgical excision; antibiotics for infected laryngocele (pyolaryngocele). No pharmacologic management for the cyst itself.
us_prevalence: Very rare; ~500–1,000 cases/year in US needing treatment.
patients_needing_tx: ~100/year needing antibiotics for infected laryngocele. Minimal drug market.
price: N/A (surgical condition).
peak_sales: Negligible. Not a viable drug target.

symptoms: Facial pain, congestion, purulent discharge, headache
treatment_goal: Eliminate infection, restore sinus drainage
first_line_soc: Intranasal saline irrigation + intranasal corticosteroids for chronic/subacute sinusitis; amoxicillin-clavulanate for documented acute bacterial sinusitis.
treatment_algorithm: Acute bacterial sinusitis: amoxicillin-clavulanate 875/125 mg BID × 5–7 days; intranasal steroids adjunct. Chronic sinusitis: intranasal corticosteroids ± saline. CRSwNP: dupilumab, mepolizumab. Refractory: FESS.
us_prevalence: ~29 million Americans diagnosed with sinusitis annually; ~11 million with chronic sinusitis.
patients_needing_tx: ~15 million needing pharmacotherapy; ~3 million with chronic/refractory sinusitis needing biologic or FESS.
price: $800–$1,500/year (antibiotics/intranasal steroids); biologic $30,000–$38,000/year.
peak_sales: ~$500M at 5% market share for novel OTC/Rx agent; biologic ~$2–3B (existing drugs). Novel agent peak ~$500M.
registrational_endpoint: SNOT-22 (Sino-Nasal Outcome Test) change from baseline at 12 weeks vs. placebo.
btd_threshold_vs_soc: ≥9-point SNOT-22 improvement (MCID) vs. ~3 for placebo; or NPS reduction ≥1.5 for polyp subtype.

symptoms: Nasal obstruction, loss of smell, chronic congestion, facial pressure
treatment_goal: Reduce polyp size, restore smell and airway
first_line_soc: Intranasal corticosteroids (mometasone, fluticasone) as primary medical treatment; short courses of oral corticosteroids for acute exacerbations.
treatment_algorithm: Step 1: Intranasal corticosteroids (mometasone 200 mcg/nostril BID). Step 2: Short-course oral prednisone for acute flares. Step 3: Biologic add-on therapy for inadequately controlled CRSwNP: dupilumab (IL-4/13), mepolizumab (IL-5), benralizumab (IL-5Rα). Step 4: Functional endoscopic sinus surgery (FESS). Step 5: Dupilumab post-FESS for recurrence prevention.
us_prevalence: ~4–5% of US population with CRSwNP (~14 million); ~1.6 million with inadequately controlled disease; ~9,000 adolescents per FDA data.
patients_needing_tx: ~1.6 million adults + 9,000 adolescents needing biologic therapy; ~5 million total on medical therapy.
price: $30,000–$40,000/year (dupilumab WAC ~$37,000; mepolizumab ~$32,000/year).
peak_sales: Dupilumab CRSwNP revenue ~$3B (2025 est.); new entrant: 1.6M × $36,000 × 15% = ~$8.6B potential. Novel agent realistic peak ~$2B at 5% share.
registrational_endpoint: Nasal Polyp Score (NPS) and Nasal Congestion Score (NCS) change from baseline at 24 weeks vs. placebo (co-primary).
btd_threshold_vs_soc: NPS reduction ≥1.5 points vs. placebo; NCS ≥1 point improvement; meets threshold used in dupilumab SINUS trials.

symptoms: Blocked nose, runny nose, sneezing, facial pressure
treatment_goal: Relieve congestion, reduce secretions
first_line_soc: Intranasal saline irrigation; intranasal corticosteroids (fluticasone, mometasone) for allergic or chronic rhinitis; oral/topical decongestants (oxymetazoline, pseudoephedrine) short-term.
treatment_algorithm: Step 1: Saline irrigation. Step 2: Intranasal corticosteroids. Step 3: Oral antihistamine for allergic component. Step 4: Oral decongestant (pseudoephedrine) short-term. Step 5: Ipratropium nasal spray for watery rhinorrhea.
us_prevalence: ~60 million Americans with allergic rhinitis; ~19 million with chronic non-allergic rhinitis.
patients_needing_tx: ~30 million seeking pharmacologic treatment for chronic/recurrent nasal congestion and rhinorrhea.
price: $500–$1,500/year for novel biologic or improved intranasal formulation.
registrational_endpoint: Total Nasal Symptom Score (TNSS) change from baseline at 4 weeks vs. placebo.
btd_threshold_vs_soc: ≥30% improvement in TNSS vs. ~15% for placebo; or superiority to fluticasone.

symptoms: Chronic nasal congestion, rhinorrhea without allergic trigger
treatment_goal: Reduce nasal symptoms, improve quality of life
first_line_soc: Intranasal corticosteroids (fluticasone) for NAR with eosinophilia; ipratropium bromide 0.03% nasal spray for rhinorrhea-predominant NAR.
treatment_algorithm: Step 1: Intranasal corticosteroids. Step 2: Ipratropium bromide for watery rhinorrhea. Step 3: Oxymetazoline PRN (short-term). Step 4: Capsaicin nasal spray (off-label) for idiopathic rhinitis. Step 5: Azelastine nasal spray for combination allergic/non-allergic rhinitis.
us_prevalence: ~19 million Americans with nonallergic rhinitis; 50–70 million with mixed rhinitis.
patients_needing_tx: ~10 million seeking consistent pharmacotherapy for NAR.
price: $800–$2,000/year for novel TRP channel antagonist or neuromodulator nasal spray.
registrational_endpoint: Change from baseline in Reflective Total Nasal Symptom Score (rTNSS) at 4 weeks vs. placebo.
btd_threshold_vs_soc: ≥30% reduction in rTNSS vs. ~10% for placebo; superiority to ipratropium in rhinorrhea subscale.

symptoms: Nosebleed, anterior or posterior bleeding
treatment_goal: Stop bleeding, prevent recurrence, treat underlying cause
first_line_soc: Direct pressure × 10–20 minutes for acute anterior epistaxis; topical tranexamic acid or oxymetazoline for vasoconstriction. Nasal cauterization or packing for persistent cases.
treatment_algorithm: Step 1: Anterior bleeding: direct pressure, topical oxymetazoline, or tranexamic acid nasal spray. Step 2: Anterior nasal pack (absorbable or non-absorbable) or cauterization. Step 3: Posterior epistaxis: posterior nasal pack, endoscopic sphenopalatine artery ligation. Step 4: Bevacizumab nasal sprays for HHT-associated epistaxis (off-label/compassionate).
us_prevalence: ~60 million Americans experience at least one nosebleed/year; ~500,000 ER visits; hereditary hemorrhagic telangiectasia (HHT) ~100,000 patients with recurrent severe epistaxis.
patients_needing_tx: ~100,000 HHT patients + ~200,000 recurrent severe epistaxis patients needing pharmacotherapy.
price: $5,000–$15,000/year for anti-VEGF nasal spray (e.g., pazopanib nasal); HHT orphan pricing.
registrational_endpoint: Monthly epistaxis severity score (ESS) reduction and number of bleeding episodes at 3 months vs. placebo.
btd_threshold_vs_soc: ≥50% reduction in ESS vs. placebo; or mean epistaxis duration <5 min vs. >10 min with placebo.

symptoms: Nasal discharge, congestion, facial pain, fever
treatment_goal: Eradicate bacteria, resolve sinusitis symptoms
first_line_soc: Amoxicillin-clavulanate (875/125 mg BID × 5–7 days) for acute bacterial rhinosinusitis; nasal saline irrigation adjunctive.
treatment_algorithm: Step 1: Amoxicillin-clavulanate first-line. Doxycycline or respiratory fluoroquinolone (moxifloxacin) for penicillin allergy. Step 2: Nasal corticosteroids as adjunct to reduce mucosal inflammation. Step 3: IV antibiotics for complicated cases (orbital, intracranial extension).
us_prevalence: ~30 million acute sinusitis episodes/year in US; ~1.3 million require antibiotics for confirmed bacterial infection.
patients_needing_tx: ~15 million/year receiving pharmacotherapy; most cases self-limited. Active treatment pool for novel anti-infectives ~2–3 million.
price: $150–$400 per treatment course (antibiotics commodity-priced; novel anti-biofilm or anti-MRSA agent could reach $600/course).
peak_sales: ~$120M at 10% market share (highly competitive generic market). 3M × $400 × 10% = $120M.
registrational_endpoint: Clinical cure rate (resolution of ≥2 cardinal symptoms) at Day 14 vs. amoxicillin-clavulanate.
btd_threshold_vs_soc: Non-inferiority with cure rate ≥85% or superiority margin ≥5% vs. amoxicillin-clavulanate.

symptoms: Nasal obstruction, crusting, bleeding, whistling noise
treatment_goal: Relieve obstruction, close perforation, prevent crusting
first_line_soc: Nasal saline irrigation and emollients (petroleum jelly, BIPP) for perforation; surgical septoplasty for symptomatic deviation.
treatment_algorithm: Deviation: septoplasty (surgical). Perforation: saline irrigation + silicone button prosthesis; surgical repair with septal flap. Topical antibiotics for crusting/infection.
us_prevalence: ~80% of adults have some degree of septal deviation; ~40,000 perforations in US.
patients_needing_tx: ~400,000 pharmacologically managed deviations/year; ~40,000 perforations needing topical treatment.
price: $1,000–$3,000/year for wound-healing/closure biologic or anti-crusting formulation.
registrational_endpoint: Nasal obstruction VAS score and perforation size change at 12 weeks vs. saline alone.
btd_threshold_vs_soc: Perforation closure rate ≥40% at 12 weeks vs. ~5% for saline + emollient.

symptoms: Unilateral nasal discharge, obstruction, foul smell
treatment_goal: Remove foreign body safely, prevent aspiration
first_line_soc: None (pharmacologic); mechanical removal (positive-pressure technique, Katz extractor, forceps).
treatment_algorithm: Mechanical extraction; topical decongestant (oxymetazoline) may reduce mucosal edema pre-extraction. Antibiotics only if secondary infection present.
us_prevalence: ~100,000–150,000 nasal foreign body removals/year in US (predominantly toddlers).
patients_needing_tx: ~20,000/year needing antibiotic treatment for secondary infection.
price: N/A (procedural).
peak_sales: Negligible. Not a viable drug target.

symptoms: Unilateral congestion, foul discharge, epistaxis in children
treatment_goal: Safe removal without aspiration or trauma
first_line_soc: None (pharmacologic); mechanical removal (positive-pressure, Katz extractor). Topical decongestant (oxymetazoline) may facilitate extraction.
treatment_algorithm: Mechanical extraction; topical vasoconstrictor pre-extraction; antibiotics only if secondary infection.
us_prevalence: ~100,000–150,000 cases/year (see Foreign Body Nose above — same condition).
patients_needing_tx: ~20,000/year for secondary infection antibiotics.
price: N/A (procedural).

symptoms: Sore throat, tonsillar swelling/exudate, fever, cervical lymphadenopathy
treatment_goal: Eradicate GAS, prevent rheumatic fever, relieve symptoms
first_line_soc: Amoxicillin 500 mg BID × 10 days or penicillin V for GAS; azithromycin 5-day course for penicillin-allergic patients.
treatment_algorithm: Step 1: Rapid strep test or culture. Step 2: Amoxicillin/penicillin for GAS. Step 3: Analgesics (NSAIDs). Step 4: IV antibiotics + dexamethasone for severe hospitalized disease. Step 5: Tonsillectomy for recurrent GAS (Paradise criteria).
us_prevalence: ~7.5 million GAS tonsillopharyngitis cases/year requiring antibiotic treatment.
patients_needing_tx: ~7.5 million/year.
price: $50–$300 per course.
registrational_endpoint: GAS eradication rate at Day 10 vs. amoxicillin (non-inferiority; ≥85%).
btd_threshold_vs_soc: Eradication rate ≥95% vs. ~85% for amoxicillin; or superior relapse prevention.

symptoms: Palpable neck swelling, pain, difficulty swallowing
treatment_goal: Diagnose etiology, treat underlying cause
first_line_soc: Antibiotics (amoxicillin-clavulanate) for reactive lymphadenopathy/lymphadenitis; otherwise diagnosis-driven (antibiotics, antifungals, chemotherapy, surgery).
treatment_algorithm: Diagnosis-driven: reactive lymphadenitis → antibiotics; Hodgkin/NHL → chemotherapy; thyroid nodule → surgery/radioiodine; abscess → I&D + antibiotics; metastatic carcinoma → surgery/chemoradiation.
us_prevalence: ~8 million neck mass presentations/year; predominantly reactive lymphadenopathy (~75%); malignant ~1–5%.
patients_needing_tx: ~3 million/year requiring antibiotic treatment for infectious etiology.
price: $200–$500 per antibiotic course.
peak_sales: ~$105M at 10% novel agent share. Largely generic market.
registrational_endpoint: Lymph node size reduction ≥50% at Day 14 and CRP normalization.
btd_threshold_vs_soc: N/A (heterogeneous condition; no single drug target).

symptoms: Throat pain, dysphagia, fever, tonsillar exudate
treatment_goal: Eradicate bacterial infection, prevent complications
first_line_soc: Amoxicillin 500 mg TID × 10 days (or benzathine penicillin IM) for Group A Streptococcal pharyngitis (Centor/McIsaac score ≥3).
treatment_algorithm: Step 1: Centor/rapid strep testing. Step 2: Amoxicillin or penicillin V for GAS. Step 3: Azithromycin or clindamycin for penicillin-allergic patients. Step 4: NSAIDs/acetaminophen for pain. Step 5: Tonsillectomy for recurrent GAS (≥7/year).
us_prevalence: ~15 million physician visits/year for sore throat; ~10 million GAS pharyngitis cases/year.
patients_needing_tx: ~10 million GAS cases/year requiring antibiotics.
price: $50–$200 per course (generic amoxicillin dominates).
peak_sales: ~$50M at 5% share for novel formulation. Commodity market.
registrational_endpoint: Clinical cure rate (symptom resolution + negative RADT) at Day 5 vs. amoxicillin.
btd_threshold_vs_soc: Non-inferiority with symptom resolution rate ≥90% vs. amoxicillin; or shorter time to pain resolution.

symptoms: Nasal obstruction, snoring, mouth breathing, recurrent infections
treatment_goal: Relieve obstruction, prevent recurrent infections
first_line_soc: Intranasal corticosteroids (e.g., fluticasone, mometasone) for mild adenoid hypertrophy; reduce inflammation and size.
treatment_algorithm: Step 1: Intranasal corticosteroids for mild-moderate hypertrophy. Step 2: Antibiotics for concurrent bacterial infection (amoxicillin-clavulanate). Step 3: Adenoidectomy for persistent obstruction, recurrent otitis media with effusion, or sleep-disordered breathing unresponsive to medical therapy.
us_prevalence: Adenoid hypertrophy affects approximately 1–2 million US children annually; adenoidectomy performed in ~130,000 children/year.
patients_needing_tx: ~500,000 children/year with symptomatic adenoid hypertrophy warranting pharmacologic therapy; surgical cases excluded from drug TAM.
price: $800–$1,500/year (intranasal biologic or anti-inflammatory; no pediatric orphan pricing applicable at this prevalence).
peak_sales: ~$120M at 20% market share (competing with generics and surgery). 500,000 × $1,200 × 20% = $120M.
registrational_endpoint: Reduction in Pediatric Sleep Questionnaire (PSQ) score or adenoid-nasopharyngeal ratio at 12 weeks vs. placebo.
btd_threshold_vs_soc: ≥30% reduction in PSQ score vs. placebo (clinically meaningful vs. current intranasal steroid ~15% improvement).

symptoms: Severe unilateral sore throat, uvular deviation, trismus, hot potato voice
treatment_goal: Drain abscess, eradicate infection, relieve airway risk
first_line_soc: Needle aspiration or incision and drainage plus amoxicillin-clavulanate or penicillin + metronidazole.
treatment_algorithm: Step 1: Needle aspiration (diagnostic + therapeutic). Step 2: Amoxicillin-clavulanate 875/125 mg BID × 10 days. Step 3: IV penicillin/clindamycin for hospitalized cases. Step 4: IV dexamethasone for pain/trismus relief. Step 5: Tonsillectomy (quinsy tonsillectomy or interval) for recurrent cases.
us_prevalence: ~45,000 cases/year in US; peak 20–30 year age group.
patients_needing_tx: ~40,000/year needing antibiotics + drainage.
price: $500–$1,500 per course.
peak_sales: ~$8M at 20% share. Not a primary drug target.
registrational_endpoint: Swallowing function recovery and fever resolution rate at Day 7.
btd_threshold_vs_soc: N/A (drainage + antibiotics highly effective).

symptoms: Bilateral floor of mouth swelling, trismus, drooling, airway threat (Ludwig's angina)
treatment_goal: Secure airway, drain infection, prevent mediastinal spread
first_line_soc: IV penicillin G + metronidazole (or ampicillin-sulbactam) plus surgical drainage; airway management critical.
treatment_algorithm: Step 1: Airway: awake fiberoptic intubation or surgical airway. Step 2: IV ampicillin-sulbactam 3g q6h. Step 3: Surgical drainage (external neck incision). Step 4: Dental extraction of offending tooth. Step 5: Monitor for descending mediastinitis.
us_prevalence: ~5,000–10,000 Ludwig's angina/submandibular space infections/year in US.
patients_needing_tx: ~7,000/year requiring IV antibiotics.
price: $1,500–$4,000 per IV course.
registrational_endpoint: Airway complication rate and infection resolution at Day 10.
btd_threshold_vs_soc: N/A (surgical emergency; drug adjunct only).

symptoms: Trismus, neck swelling, fever, dysphagia, muffled voice
treatment_goal: Drain abscess, eradicate infection, prevent spread
first_line_soc: IV broad-spectrum antibiotics (ampicillin-sulbactam or clindamycin + metronidazole) plus surgical drainage.
treatment_algorithm: Step 1: IV antibiotics (ampicillin-sulbactam 3g q6h). Step 2: Surgical drainage (external cervical or transoral). Step 3: ICU monitoring for mediastinal extension. Step 4: Anticoagulation if septic thrombophlebitis (Lemierre syndrome).
us_prevalence: ~3,000–5,000 cases/year in US.
patients_needing_tx: ~4,000/year requiring IV antibiotics.
price: $1,000–$3,000 per treatment course.
peak_sales: ~$2.4M at 30% share. Negligible novel drug opportunity.
registrational_endpoint: Clinical cure and CT abscess resolution rate at Day 14.
btd_threshold_vs_soc: N/A (standard treatment highly effective).

symptoms: Neck stiffness, fever, stridor, dysphagia, drooling in children
treatment_goal: Secure airway, drain abscess, eradicate infection
first_line_soc: IV ampicillin-sulbactam (3g q6h) or clindamycin; surgical drainage for formed abscess.
treatment_algorithm: Step 1: Airway assessment and intubation if needed. Step 2: IV ampicillin-sulbactam or clindamycin. Step 3: CT scan to confirm liquid abscess. Step 4: Transoral or external drainage. Step 5: Transition to oral antibiotics once improving.
us_prevalence: ~2,000–4,000 cases/year predominantly in children <6 years.
patients_needing_tx: ~3,000/year requiring IV antibiotics.
registrational_endpoint: Clinical cure and abscess CT resolution at Day 7.
btd_threshold_vs_soc: N/A.

symptoms: Nasal obstruction, post-nasal drip, halitosis, Eustachian tube dysfunction
treatment_goal: Relieve symptoms, drain or remove cyst
first_line_soc: None (pharmacologic); treatment is endoscopic surgical marsupializaton for symptomatic cysts.
treatment_algorithm: Most asymptomatic cysts require no treatment. Symptomatic: endoscopic marsupialization. Antibiotics for infected cyst.
us_prevalence: ~5% prevalence on MRI (mostly incidental); symptomatic cases ~50,000/year.
patients_needing_tx: ~5,000/year needing intervention; minimal pharmacologic market.
price: N/A.

symptoms: Hypernasal speech, nasal air escape, articulation errors
treatment_goal: Normalize speech, close velopharyngeal gap
first_line_soc: None (pharmacologic); primary treatment is speech therapy and surgical correction (pharyngeal flap, sphincter pharyngoplasty, posterior pharyngeal wall augmentation).
treatment_algorithm: Speech therapy (pre- and post-surgical). Surgical: pharyngeal flap or sphincter pharyngoplasty. Prosthetics: palatal obturator for non-surgical candidates.
us_prevalence: ~200,000 Americans with VPI; predominantly post-cleft palate repair (~70% of cleft population).
patients_needing_tx: ~50,000 requiring intervention; minimal pharmacologic market.
price: N/A (procedural/surgical).

symptoms: Painful salivary gland swelling, fever, purulent discharge from duct
treatment_goal: Eradicate infection, restore salivary flow
first_line_soc: Oral amoxicillin-clavulanate (875/125 mg BID × 10 days) for acute bacterial sialadenitis; IV antibiotics for severe cases.
treatment_algorithm: Step 1: Amoxicillin-clavulanate or dicloxacillin for Staphylococcal sialadenitis. Step 2: IV ampicillin-sulbactam for severe disease. Step 3: Sialagogues and warm compresses. Step 4: Sialendoscopy for ductal stricture/stone. Step 5: Parotidectomy/submandibulectomy for chronic recurrent disease.
us_prevalence: ~600,000–800,000 cases/year of acute sialadenitis; elderly and post-operative patients at highest risk.
patients_needing_tx: ~500,000/year requiring antibiotic treatment.
price: $200–$500 per treatment course.
registrational_endpoint: Clinical cure rate at Day 10 and pain VAS resolution.
btd_threshold_vs_soc: N/A (adequate standard therapy exists).

symptoms: Painful salivary gland swelling during eating, dry mouth
treatment_goal: Remove stone, restore salivary flow, prevent recurrence
first_line_soc: None (pharmacologic for stone removal); hydration, massage, sialagogues (lemon drops, vitamin C). Antibiotics for secondary sialadenitis.
treatment_algorithm: Step 1: Hydration + sialagogues. Step 2: Lithotripsy (extracorporeal shock wave) for larger stones. Step 3: Sialendoscopy with basket retrieval. Step 4: Surgical excision for large/distal stones. Antibiotics for acute infection (amoxicillin-clavulanate).
us_prevalence: ~150,000 salivary stone cases/year in US.
patients_needing_tx: ~50,000/year requiring antibiotics for secondary infection.
price: $1,000–$5,000 for a litholytic agent or dissolution therapy.
registrational_endpoint: Complete stone dissolution rate at 4 weeks vs. placebo irrigation.
btd_threshold_vs_soc: Stone dissolution ≥50% reduction in volume in ≥40% of patients vs. ~5% placebo.

symptoms: Reduced or absent smell, taste distortion, phantom odors
treatment_goal: Restore olfactory/gustatory function
first_line_soc: Olfactory training (structured twice-daily sniffing protocol) — primary evidence-based treatment. Intranasal steroids for inflammatory causes; zinc for taste disorders.
treatment_algorithm: Step 1: Olfactory training × 12+ weeks. Step 2: Intranasal corticosteroids for inflammatory anosmia. Step 3: Intranasal sodium citrate (investigational for parosmia). Step 4: Platelet-rich plasma intranasal (investigational). Step 5: Treat underlying cause (sinusitis, hypothyroidism).
us_prevalence: ~6 million Americans with chronic smell/taste dysfunction; post-COVID added millions transiently.
patients_needing_tx: ~2 million with persistent clinically significant chemosensory loss seeking pharmacotherapy.
price: $2,000–$5,000/year for novel olfactory neuroregenerative therapy.
registrational_endpoint: Change in UPSIT score and BSIT (Brief Smell Identification Test) at 16 weeks vs. olfactory training placebo.
btd_threshold_vs_soc: ≥10-point UPSIT improvement vs. ~5 for olfactory training alone.

symptoms: Complete or partial loss of smell, altered taste
treatment_goal: Restore olfactory function, improve quality of life
first_line_soc: Olfactory training (nasal steroids for inflammatory causes); short-course systemic corticosteroids for post-infectious or inflammatory anosmia.
treatment_algorithm: Step 1: Identify and treat underlying cause (sinusitis, nasal polyps → intranasal/systemic steroids; post-viral → olfactory training with essential oils × 12 weeks). Step 2: PRP or platelet-rich plasma intranasal injection (investigational). Step 3: No approved pharmacologic rescue for post-viral anosmia.
us_prevalence: ~3 million Americans have significant smell dysfunction; post-COVID anosmia added ~1.5–2 million cases (2020–2024), many partially recovered.
patients_needing_tx: ~1.5 million with persistent clinically significant anosmia seeking treatment (post-COVID and other etiologies).
price: $2,000–$5,000/year for a topical neuroregeneration or PRP-analog intranasal therapy.
peak_sales: ~$525M at 10% market share (novel therapeutic in fragmented field). 1,500,000 × $3,500 × 10% = $525M.
registrational_endpoint: Change from baseline in UPSIT (University of Pennsylvania Smell Identification Test) score at 16 weeks vs. placebo.
btd_threshold_vs_soc: ≥10-point improvement in UPSIT score vs. placebo (vs. olfactory training ~4–6 points over 12 weeks).

symptoms: Food refusal, picky eating, failure to self-feed, mealtime behavioral problems
treatment_goal: Establish healthy eating patterns, prevent nutritional deficiencies
first_line_soc: None (pharmacologic); behavioral therapy (positive reinforcement, exposure therapy, family meal coaching) is first-line.
treatment_algorithm: Behavioral: sequential oral sensory approach, feeding therapy, mealtime behavioral programs. Nutritional supplementation for deficiencies. Cyproheptadine (off-label appetite stimulant) for extreme cases. Mirtazapine for ARFID-associated anxiety.
us_prevalence: ~25–45% of toddlers/preschoolers exhibit picky eating; ~5% with Avoidant/Restrictive Food Intake Disorder (ARFID) — ~1.5 million children.
patients_needing_tx: ~500,000 with ARFID or severe eating dysfunction seeking pharmacotherapy.
price: $3,000–$8,000/year for novel appetite/feeding modulator or ARFID-targeted drug.
registrational_endpoint: Change in food variety count (ARFID DSES) and weight-for-age z-score at 16 weeks.
btd_threshold_vs_soc: ≥30% increase in accepted foods vs. ~5% for behavioral therapy alone.

disease: Violent Behavior in Children and Adolescents
symptoms: Physical aggression, property destruction, threats, self-harm
treatment_goal: Reduce aggressive behavior, ensure safety, treat underlying disorder
first_line_soc: Atypical antipsychotics (risperidone 0.5–2 mg/day) for acute aggression in ASD or conduct disorder; behavior therapy is adjunctive.
treatment_algorithm: Step 1: Safety assessment; address precipitants. Step 2: CBT, functional behavioral assessment, parent training. Step 3: Risperidone or aripiprazole for ASD-associated aggression (FDA-approved). Step 4: Clonidine for impulsive aggression. Step 5: Stimulants for ADHD-related aggression.
us_prevalence: ~5–10% of youth with clinically significant aggression; ~3–5 million children.
patients_needing_tx: ~500,000 with severe aggression requiring pharmacotherapy.
price: $3,000–$8,000/year for novel aggression-modulating agent.
registrational_endpoint: Aberrant Behavior Checklist (ABC) irritability subscale change at 8 weeks vs. placebo.
btd_threshold_vs_soc: ≥25% ABC irritability reduction vs. ~10% for placebo.

symptoms: Difficulty falling/staying asleep, night awakenings, bedtime resistance
treatment_goal: Establish regular sleep, reduce nighttime awakenings
first_line_soc: Behavioral interventions (sleep hygiene, graduated extinction) are first-line; melatonin for sleep onset disorders.
treatment_algorithm: Step 1: Sleep hygiene education and behavioral strategies. Step 2: Melatonin 0.5–5 mg at bedtime for sleep onset delay (especially ADHD/ASD). Step 3: Clonidine 0.05–0.2 mg for ADHD-related insomnia. Step 4: Off-label antihistamine (diphenhydramine) short-term.
us_prevalence: ~25–50% of children experience sleep problems; ~3–4 million with clinically significant sleep disorders.
patients_needing_tx: ~2 million children with moderate-severe sleep disorders using pharmacotherapy.
price: $500–$1,500/year for novel pediatric sleep formulation (e.g., pediatric melatonin PR, novel orexin antagonist).
registrational_endpoint: Change in sleep onset latency (actigraphy), CSHQ (Children's Sleep Habits Questionnaire) at 4 weeks.
btd_threshold_vs_soc: ≥30-minute reduction in sleep onset latency vs. ~15 min for melatonin.

symptoms: Refusal or inability to attend school, somatic complaints, anxiety, family conflict
treatment_goal: Restore regular school attendance, reduce anxiety
first_line_soc: Cognitive behavioral therapy (CBT) is first-line; SSRIs (fluoxetine, sertraline) for anxiety-driven school refusal.
treatment_algorithm: Step 1: CBT + school-based behavioral plan (graduated return). Step 2: SSRI (fluoxetine 10–20 mg or sertraline 25–100 mg) for anxiety. Step 3: Combined CBT + SSRI for severe cases. Step 4: Multidisciplinary team (school, therapist, pediatrician, family).
us_prevalence: ~2–5% of school-age children; ~1.5–3 million children affected.
patients_needing_tx: ~500,000 children with moderate-severe school avoidance needing pharmacotherapy.
price: $1,500–$3,000/year for SSRI or novel anxiolytic.
registrational_endpoint: School attendance rate (% of school days attended) at 12 weeks vs. CBT alone.
btd_threshold_vs_soc: ≥70% school attendance at 12 weeks vs. ~50% for CBT alone.

symptoms: Explosive behavioral outbursts, crying, screaming, aggression in toddlers
treatment_goal: Reduce tantrum frequency/severity, support parental coping
first_line_soc: None (pharmacologic); parental guidance, behavioral strategies (ignoring, positive reinforcement, consistent boundaries) are first-line.
treatment_algorithm: Behavioral: parent training programs (Triple P, PCIT). No pharmacologic SoC for normal developmental tantrums. For pathological explosiveness (DMDD): stimulants, atypical antipsychotics (risperidone, aripiprazole) under psychiatric care.
us_prevalence: ~80% of toddlers; ~5% with pathological explosive behavior (DMDD) — ~1.5 million children.
patients_needing_tx: ~200,000 children with DMDD or severe behavioral dysregulation needing pharmacotherapy.
price: $2,000–$5,000/year for novel pediatric behavioral modulator.
registrational_endpoint: Reduction in Affective Reactivity Index (ARI) score or CGAS improvement at 8 weeks.
btd_threshold_vs_soc: ≥30% ARI reduction vs. ~10% for behavioral therapy alone.

symptoms: Crying-triggered apnea, cyanosis or pallor, brief loss of consciousness
treatment_goal: Reassure families, prevent iron deficiency, reduce spell frequency
first_line_soc: Iron supplementation (ferrous sulfate 3 mg/kg/day) if iron-deficient or anemic — reduces spell frequency. Reassurance and behavioral guidance.
treatment_algorithm: Step 1: Reassurance (benign, self-limiting condition). Step 2: Iron supplementation for iron-deficient or low-normal iron patients. Step 3: Piracetam (not FDA-approved, used internationally) for frequent spells. Step 4: Atropine or pacemaker for cardioinhibitory type (rare).
us_prevalence: ~4–5% of children age 6 months–6 years; ~3 million children affected in US.
patients_needing_tx: ~500,000 children with frequent/severe spells needing pharmacotherapy (iron supplementation).
price: $300–$800/year for novel autonomic modulator.
registrational_endpoint: Reduction in spell frequency (spells/month) at 3 months vs. placebo.
btd_threshold_vs_soc: ≥50% reduction in spell frequency vs. ~20% for iron supplementation alone.

symptoms: Lateral spinal curvature, rib hump, uneven shoulders/hips, back pain
treatment_goal: Prevent curve progression, avoid surgery
first_line_soc: Observation for Cobb angle <20°; rigid bracing (TLSO) for 25–45° curves in growing children — non-pharmacologic. No approved pharmacologic SoC.
treatment_algorithm: Observation; bracing (Boston, Charleston); physical therapy; surgical spinal fusion for >45–50° curves or brace failure. Investigational: melatonin (low-quality evidence), anti-inflammatory agents.
us_prevalence: ~3 million US adolescents with AIS; ~28,000 scoliosis surgeries/year.
patients_needing_tx: ~500,000 with moderate AIS potentially amenable to pharmacotherapy; ~700,000 in bracing.
price: $5,000–$15,000/year for novel antifibrotic, melatonin receptor agonist, or connective tissue modulator.
registrational_endpoint: Cobb angle progression ≤5° at 2 years vs. observation; rate of surgery-avoidance.
btd_threshold_vs_soc: Curve progression ≤5° in ≥70% of patients vs. ~40% with bracing alone.

symptoms: Multiple fractures, vertebral compression, bone pain, decreased bone density
treatment_goal: Increase bone density, prevent fractures
first_line_soc: Bisphosphonates (pamidronate IV or zoledronic acid IV) for moderate-severe pediatric osteoporosis; treatment of underlying cause.
treatment_algorithm: Step 1: Calcium + Vitamin D supplementation. Step 2: IV pamidronate (0.5–1.5 mg/kg q4 months) or IV zoledronic acid. Step 3: Treat underlying cause (steroids, CF, IBD). Teriparatide not approved for children (closed growth plates only). Denosumab investigational.
us_prevalence: ~500,000 children with reduced bone density (secondary causes); severe pediatric osteoporosis ~50,000.
patients_needing_tx: ~50,000 children requiring bisphosphonate or novel osteoanabolic therapy.
price: $10,000–$50,000/year for novel pediatric osteoanabolic (anti-sclerostin, teriparatide analog). Pediatric orphan pricing.
registrational_endpoint: Lumbar spine BMD Z-score change at 24 months vs. calcium/Vitamin D.
btd_threshold_vs_soc: ≥0.5 increase in lumbar Z-score vs. ~0.2 for calcium/Vitamin D alone.

symptoms: Poor bone mineralization, fractures, tooth loss, muscle weakness, respiratory failure
treatment_goal: Restore alkaline phosphatase activity, prevent fractures and respiratory failure
first_line_soc: Asfotase alfa (Strensiq) — enzyme replacement therapy (ERT) targeting tissue-nonspecific alkaline phosphatase; approved by FDA in 2015.
treatment_algorithm: Asfotase alfa (Strensiq) 2–6 mg/kg 3× weekly subcutaneous for perinatal/infantile/juvenile-onset HPP. Supportive: respiratory support, PT/OT, seizure management with pyridoxine (B6 for pyridoxine-responsive seizures). No role for bisphosphonates (contraindicated).
us_prevalence: Severe HPP: ~1:100,000 births; ~3,000–5,000 US patients with all forms.
patients_needing_tx: ~1,500 patients with severe/moderate HPP requiring asfotase alfa.
price: $500,000–$750,000/year (asfotase alfa WAC ~$700,000/year). Pediatric orphan pricing applies.
peak_sales: ~$360M at 40% share. Asfotase alfa currently ~$200–250M global sales/year.
registrational_endpoint: Radiographic rickets score improvement at 24 weeks; ventilator-free survival at 12 months.
btd_threshold_vs_soc: Survival advantage vs. natural history (~50% mortality by 12 months for perinatal form) and rickets improvement ≥ grade 2.

symptoms: Thoracic kyphosis, back pain, wedge-shaped vertebrae in adolescents
treatment_goal: Reduce kyphosis progression, relieve pain
first_line_soc: Physical therapy and NSAIDs for pain; bracing (Milwaukee brace) for progressive curves in growing children — non-pharmacologic primary.
treatment_algorithm: NSAIDs; physical therapy; extension bracing; surgical correction (posterior fusion) for kyphosis >75° or neurologic compromise. No disease-modifying pharmacotherapy.
us_prevalence: ~0.4–8% of adolescents; ~500,000 US adolescents with clinical Scheuermann disease.
patients_needing_tx: ~200,000 receiving active pharmacotherapy (NSAIDs, bracing program).
price: $1,000–$3,000/year for novel anti-kyphosis or bone remodeling agent.
registrational_endpoint: Kyphosis Cobb angle change at 24 months and VAS pain score.
btd_threshold_vs_soc: Kyphosis stabilization (<5° progression) in ≥70% vs. ~50% with bracing.

symptoms: Dense bone thickening, cranial nerve palsies, facial deformity, marrow failure
treatment_goal: Prevent marrow failure, relieve cranial nerve compression
first_line_soc: Interferon gamma-1b (FDA-approved for osteopetrosis) to improve osteoclast function; bone marrow transplant for malignant infantile osteopetrosis.
treatment_algorithm: Malignant infantile osteopetrosis: hematopoietic stem cell transplantation (HSCT). IFN-gamma-1b for intermediate osteopetrosis. Corticosteroids for anemia. Vitamin D and calcium management. Surgical decompression for cranial nerves.
patients_needing_tx: ~500–1,000 patients requiring pharmacotherapy.
price: $100,000–$300,000/year (pediatric orphan; extreme rarity).
registrational_endpoint: Bone marrow function (hemoglobin, ANC) and skeletal radiographic changes at 12 months vs. placebo.
btd_threshold_vs_soc: ≥50% reduction in transfusion requirement or prevention of marrow failure in ≥40% of patients.

symptoms: Knee pain below kneecap during activity, tibial tubercle swelling, tenderness
treatment_goal: Relieve pain, allow growth plate healing
first_line_soc: Conservative: NSAIDs, activity modification, ice, physical therapy — self-limiting condition.
treatment_algorithm: NSAIDs; rest; ice; knee sleeve; physical therapy (quadriceps stretching). In rare refractory cases post-growth: surgical excision of ossicle. Steroid injections not recommended.
us_prevalence: ~1% of adolescents (11–14 years); ~1 million active cases in US.
patients_needing_tx: ~500,000/year seeking treatment; OTC NSAIDs dominate.
price: $200–$500/year for novel topical NSAID or bone modulator.
registrational_endpoint: Pain VAS at activity and return to sport at 8 weeks vs. activity restriction.
btd_threshold_vs_soc: N/A (self-limiting).

symptoms: Increased bone density, marrow space narrowing, cranial nerve compression, fracture risk
treatment_goal: Prevent marrow failure, relieve bone compression
first_line_soc: None (pharmacologic for most forms); treat complications (anemia, cranial nerve palsies). IFN-gamma for malignant osteopetrosis.
treatment_algorithm: Underlying etiology determines treatment. Fluorosis → remove fluoride. Drug-induced → discontinue offending drug. Myelofibrosis → ruxolitinib. HSCT for malignant infantile forms.
us_prevalence: Heterogeneous group; ~10,000 patients total with clinically significant osteosclerosis.
patients_needing_tx: ~3,000 requiring active pharmacotherapy.
price: $30,000–$100,000/year depending on orphan status.
registrational_endpoint: CT bone density change and hematologic parameters at 12 months.
btd_threshold_vs_soc: Prevention of marrow failure in ≥50% of high-risk patients.

symptoms: Hip pain, limp, limited hip rotation, avascular femoral head necrosis
treatment_goal: Preserve femoral head shape, maintain hip function, prevent arthritis
first_line_soc: Conservative: non-weight-bearing, traction, abduction bracing (Petrie casts). NSAIDs for pain. No pharmacologic SoC for the underlying necrosis.
treatment_algorithm: Observation for mild disease; abduction bracing or casting; surgical containment (Salter osteotomy, femoral osteotomy) for at-risk hips. Bisphosphonates and alendronate have been studied — inconclusive.
us_prevalence: ~15,000–20,000 children in US with LCPD; ~5,000 new cases/year.
patients_needing_tx: ~10,000 children needing active management including NSAIDs.
price: $5,000–$15,000/year for bisphosphonate, anti-RANKL, or pro-angiogenic agent.
registrational_endpoint: Radiographic femoral head sphericity (Mose criteria) at 24 months; Oxford Hip Score.
btd_threshold_vs_soc: ≥30% improvement in femoral head sphericity score vs. bracing alone.

symptoms: Skeletal abnormalities, facial deformity, cranial nerve compression
treatment_goal: Manage bone overgrowth complications, prevent nerve injury
first_line_soc: None (pharmacologic); primarily surgical decompression for cranial nerve compromise. No approved drug therapy.
treatment_algorithm: Surgical decompression for cranial nerve entrapment or increased intracranial pressure. Bisphosphonates used anecdotally. Supportive care for complications.
us_prevalence: Very rare group of disorders; <500 cases per specific subtype in US.
patients_needing_tx: <500 requiring active pharmacotherapy.
price: $50,000–$150,000/year (ultra-rare; orphan pricing).
registrational_endpoint: Radiographic bone density change and cranial nerve function at 24 months.
btd_threshold_vs_soc: Stabilization of bone density progression vs. expected worsening; ≥50% reduction in cranial nerve compression events.

symptoms: Foot pain, limping, medial foot swelling, navicular bone abnormality
treatment_goal: Relieve pain, allow navicular bone revascularization
first_line_soc: Conservative: NSAIDs + short-leg walking cast × 6–8 weeks; self-limited disease (typically resolves by age 4–5 years).
treatment_algorithm: Rest, NSAIDs, medial arch support, immobilization. Surgery not needed; condition self-resolves.
us_prevalence: ~2,000–5,000 cases/year in children aged 3–7 years.
patients_needing_tx: ~3,000/year needing NSAIDs + conservative management.
price: $200–$500 per treatment course.
peak_sales: Negligible. Not a viable drug target.
registrational_endpoint: Pain VAS and gait normalization at 8 weeks vs. NSAID + sham cast.
btd_threshold_vs_soc: N/A (self-limiting; no drug development opportunity).

symptoms: Hip/knee pain, limp, external rotation deformity, inability to walk
treatment_goal: Prevent further slip, restore hip function, avoid avascular necrosis
first_line_soc: None (pharmacologic); primary treatment is surgical pinning (in situ fixation with cannulated screw).
treatment_algorithm: Non-weight-bearing immediately; urgent surgical in situ screw fixation. Bilateral pinning for contralateral prophylaxis in endocrine causes. Post-op PT. Address underlying hypothyroidism, GHD.
us_prevalence: ~10,000–17,000 SCFE cases/year in US (rising with obesity rates).
patients_needing_tx: ~15,000/year; primarily surgical. Minimal pharmacologic market (post-op analgesics, treatment of underlying cause).
price: N/A (surgical).

symptoms: Nutritional adequacy in formula-fed infants; colic, reflux, allergy
treatment_goal: Provide complete nutrition, manage formula intolerance
first_line_soc: Standard cow's milk formula (term infants); hydrolyzed formula for mild cow's milk allergy; amino acid-based formula for severe CMPA.
treatment_algorithm: Standard formula for most. Hydrolyzed (Alimentum, Nutramigen) for CMA. Amino acid-based (Neocate, EleCare) for severe allergy/malabsorption. Soy formula (limited use). Anti-reflux formula for GER.
us_prevalence: ~3.6 million births/year; ~40% formula-fed; ~5–10% require specialized formula (~180,000–360,000 infants/year).
patients_needing_tx: ~300,000 infants/year on specialized formulas.
price: $4,000–$8,000/year for specialty amino acid formula (Neocate ~$6,000/year).
registrational_endpoint: N/A (formula is food, not drug).

symptoms: Lactation challenges, nipple pain, engorgement, low milk supply
treatment_goal: Support successful breastfeeding, increase milk supply
first_line_soc: Lactation support and education; domperidone (off-label galactagogue) or metoclopramide for insufficient milk supply in selected cases.
treatment_algorithm: Step 1: Lactation consulting; proper latch/positioning. Step 2: Pump supplementation. Step 3: Domperidone (off-label, not FDA-approved) or metoclopramide (5–10 mg TID) for augmenting milk supply. Step 4: Oxytocin nasal spray (investigational).
us_prevalence: ~4 million births/year; ~60% attempt breastfeeding; ~30% experience significant difficulty.
patients_needing_tx: ~500,000 mothers/year with clinically relevant insufficient milk supply.
price: $500–$1,500/year for approved galactagogue or oxytocin nasal spray.
registrational_endpoint: Breast milk volume (mL/day) increase at Week 2 vs. placebo; breastfeeding continuation rate at 6 months.
btd_threshold_vs_soc: ≥100 mL/day milk volume increase vs. ~30 mL for placebo.

disease: Sleeping Behaviors in Infants and Children
symptoms: Night awakenings, bedtime resistance, sleep associations, co-sleeping challenges
treatment_goal: Establish healthy sleep habits, reduce parental burden
first_line_soc: None (pharmacologic); behavioral sleep training (graduated extinction, Ferber method) is first-line.
treatment_algorithm: Behavioral: bedtime fading, graduated extinction, positive reinforcement. Melatonin for sleep onset delay in ASD or ADHD. Avoid antihistamines in infants.
us_prevalence: ~25–35% of infants/toddlers with problematic sleep behaviors.
patients_needing_tx: ~1 million children needing some form of pharmacotherapy (melatonin primarily).
registrational_endpoint: Actigraphic sleep onset latency and parental sleep diary at 4 weeks vs. placebo.
btd_threshold_vs_soc: ≥20-minute SOL reduction vs. ~10 min for placebo.

symptoms: Newborn care needs: feeding, thermoregulation, infection prevention
treatment_goal: Ensure healthy adaptation to extrauterine life
first_line_soc: Routine prophylactics: erythromycin eye ointment (ophthalmia prophylaxis), vitamin K (phytonadione 1 mg IM) for hemorrhagic disease, hepatitis B vaccine Day 1.
treatment_algorithm: Vitamin K prophylaxis; erythromycin eye ointment; Hep B vaccine; newborn screening; umbilical cord care; thermoregulation.
us_prevalence: ~3.6 million US births/year.
patients_needing_tx: ~3.6 million newborns/year receiving standard prophylactics.
price: N/A (routine prophylactics are standard of care; generic priced).
peak_sales: ~$30M for novel combination prophylactic or improved vitamin K formulation.
registrational_endpoint: N/A (supportive care, not drug development target).

symptoms: Gum pain, irritability, drooling, biting, mild temperature elevation
treatment_goal: Relieve teething discomfort
first_line_soc: Topical lidocaine/benzocaine gels are NOT recommended (FDA warning). Teething rings and acetaminophen/ibuprofen for discomfort.
treatment_algorithm: Cold teething rings; oral acetaminophen (10–15 mg/kg q4–6h) or ibuprofen (≥6 months). Avoid topical anesthetics in infants.
us_prevalence: ~4 million infants/year experiencing teething.
patients_needing_tx: ~2 million/year receiving pharmacologic pain management.
price: $50–$150 per treatment episode (OTC).
registrational_endpoint: N/A (OTC management).

disease: Initial Evaluation of the Normal Newborn
symptoms: Routine newborn assessment: APGAR, anomaly detection, vital stability
treatment_goal: Identify conditions requiring immediate intervention
first_line_soc: Clinical assessment; prophylactic medications (Vitamin K, erythromycin eye drops, Hep B vaccine).
treatment_algorithm: Routine physical exam; APGAR scoring; newborn screening (heel-stick NBS panel); hearing screen; pulse oximetry for critical CHD.
us_prevalence: ~3.6 million newborns/year.
patients_needing_tx: N/A (routine evaluation, not pharmacological treatment).

symptoms: Routine newborn physical assessment for anomalies and conditions
treatment_goal: Detect congenital anomalies and birth complications
first_line_soc: N/A (clinical examination, not pharmacologic). Vitamin K, erythromycin, and Hep B vaccine as routine pharmacologic prophylaxis.
treatment_algorithm: Physical examination protocol. Anomaly-specific management if detected.
us_prevalence: ~3.6 million US births/year.

symptoms: Detection of metabolic, endocrine, hemoglobin, and hearing disorders at birth
treatment_goal: Early identification of treatable conditions before symptoms appear
first_line_soc: N/A (diagnostic screening, not pharmacologic treatment). Early initiation of disease-specific therapy (thyroid hormone for CH, dietary management for PKU, etc.).
treatment_algorithm: NBS panel includes PKU, congenital hypothyroidism, MCAD, SCD, CF, hearing, and ~60 additional conditions. Treatment initiated based on confirmed diagnosis.
us_prevalence: ~3.6 million newborns screened/year.
patients_needing_tx: ~12,000–15,000 confirmed diagnoses requiring treatment per year across all conditions.
price: N/A (diagnostic process).
tam_gross: N/A (drug treatments are captured under specific disease entries).

symptoms: Developmental readiness for complementary feeding; feeding challenges
treatment_goal: Introduce appropriate foods, prevent allergy and nutritional deficiency
first_line_soc: None (pharmacologic); dietary guidance by AAP (6 months start, early allergen introduction for allergy prevention).
treatment_algorithm: Dietary management; early peanut introduction for high-risk infants (LEAP study); iron supplementation if needed.
us_prevalence: ~4 million US infants in complementary feeding stage annually.
patients_needing_tx: N/A (primarily nutritional/behavioral, not pharmacologic).

symptoms: Terminal illness, pain, distress, psychological burden in families
treatment_goal: Provide comfort, palliative care, family support
first_line_soc: Opioids (morphine, hydromorphone) for pain; benzodiazepines for anxiety/dyspnea; palliative sedation in refractory suffering.
treatment_algorithm: Palliative care team; opioid analgesics; anxiolytics; antiemetics; antisecretory agents (glycopyrrolate). Psychological and spiritual support.
us_prevalence: ~42,000 pediatric deaths/year in US.
patients_needing_tx: ~20,000/year receiving palliative pharmacotherapy.
price: N/A (palliative care uses existing drugs).
registrational_endpoint: N/A (palliative care).

symptoms: Temperature instability, poor feeding, respiratory distress, jaundice in sick neonates
treatment_goal: Stabilize vital functions, treat underlying illness
first_line_soc: Empiric IV antibiotics (ampicillin + gentamicin) for suspected sepsis; phototherapy for jaundice; respiratory support as needed.
treatment_algorithm: Sepsis: ampicillin + gentamicin (or cefotaxime). RDS: surfactant therapy. Jaundice: phototherapy; IVIG for Rh incompatibility. Hypoglycemia: IV dextrose. Individualized to diagnosis.
us_prevalence: ~500,000 NICU admissions/year in US.
patients_needing_tx: ~500,000/year requiring NICU pharmacotherapy.
price: N/A (condition-specific; multiple drugs).

disease: Children and Youth with Special Health Care Needs
symptoms: Complex chronic conditions; physical, developmental, behavioral, or emotional conditions
treatment_goal: Coordinate care, address medical and developmental needs
first_line_soc: Individualized care coordination; condition-specific pharmacotherapy.
treatment_algorithm: Medical home model; care coordination; specialty referrals; condition-specific treatments.
us_prevalence: ~13.9 million US children (19% of children) with SHCN.
patients_needing_tx: ~13.9 million; pharmacologic needs are condition-specific.
price: N/A (broad category).

symptoms: Chronic pain, infection, urinary complications, obstetric complications, psychological trauma
treatment_goal: Treat complications, provide psychosocial support
first_line_soc: Antibiotics for infection; analgesics for pain; psychological support. Surgical deinfibulation for urinary/obstetric complications.
treatment_algorithm: Wound care; antibiotics; analgesics; reconstructive surgery (clitoral reconstruction); psychotherapy.
us_prevalence: ~513,000 women in US at risk or living with FGM effects.
patients_needing_tx: ~100,000 seeking medical treatment for complications.
price: N/A (supportive care).

symptoms: Prevention of infectious diseases (routine immunization schedule)
treatment_goal: Achieve protective immunity, prevent vaccine-preventable diseases
first_line_soc: ACIP-recommended childhood vaccination schedule: DTaP, IPV, MMR, Varicella, Hep B, Hep A, Hib, PCV, RV, HPV, meningococcal, influenza, COVID-19.
treatment_algorithm: ACIP immunization schedule; catch-up vaccination for delayed children; travel vaccines as needed.
us_prevalence: ~72 million children (0–18 years) in US; ~4 million infants/year entering vaccination schedule.
patients_needing_tx: ~4 million infants + ~68 million children on vaccination schedule.
price: N/A (vaccines are prevention; individual vaccine pricing ~$20–$250/dose).
peak_sales: Novel vaccine (e.g., RSV pediatric, COVID booster) peak ~$500M–$2B.
registrational_endpoint: Seroconversion rate ≥90% vs. placebo at 4 weeks post-final dose; efficacy in randomized trial.
btd_threshold_vs_soc: VE ≥50% vs. placebo in RCT; or non-inferiority to existing vaccine.

symptoms: Parental delay or refusal of recommended vaccines; misinformation-driven
treatment_goal: Increase vaccination rates, address misinformation
first_line_soc: None (pharmacologic); motivational interviewing, education, trusted HCP communication.
treatment_algorithm: Presumptive vs. participatory provider communication; school mandates; social marketing. No pharmacologic intervention.
us_prevalence: ~15–25% of parents express hesitancy; ~5% refuse all vaccines.
patients_needing_tx: N/A (behavioral/public health intervention).

symptoms: Intellectual disability, hypotonia, characteristic facies, congenital heart defects, increased AML/ALL risk
treatment_goal: Improve cognitive function, manage comorbidities
first_line_soc: No approved disease-modifying pharmacotherapy. Thyroid hormone for congenital hypothyroidism (20%); early intervention/therapy programs.
treatment_algorithm: Thyroid surveillance and treatment; cardiac surgery for CHD; CPAP for OSA; early intervention programs; growth hormone for short stature; SSRI for depression; investigational: DYRK1A inhibitors, GluN2B antagonists (cognitive trials).
us_prevalence: ~270,000 Americans with Down syndrome; ~6,000 new births/year.
patients_needing_tx: ~200,000 potentially eligible for cognitive-modifying therapy if approved.
price: $15,000–$50,000/year for novel cognitive modulator (DYRK1A inhibitor, anti-amyloid).
registrational_endpoint: Cognitive function (Leiter-3, MSEL developmental quotient) change at 12 months vs. placebo.
btd_threshold_vs_soc: ≥5-point IQ equivalent improvement vs. placebo; or cognitive domain improvement ≥0.5 SD.

symptoms: Variable: intellectual disability, autism features, cardiac defects, dysmorphic features
treatment_goal: Manage specific organ complications, support development
first_line_soc: No single pharmacologic SoC; condition-specific management. Early intervention; growth hormone for some (e.g., Prader-Willi).
treatment_algorithm: Prader-Willi: growth hormone (FDA-approved), dietary management. Williams syndrome: cardiac follow-up, speech therapy. Angelman: AEDs for seizures. Individualized per syndrome.
us_prevalence: Prader-Willi: ~20,000 US; Williams: ~30,000 US; Angelman: ~15,000 US; others variable.
patients_needing_tx: ~100,000 across all syndromes needing pharmacotherapy.
price: $20,000–$200,000/year depending on orphan status.
registrational_endpoint: Syndrome-specific primary endpoint (e.g., BMI, cognitive score, seizure frequency) at 12 months.
btd_threshold_vs_soc: Complication-specific threshold per syndrome; e.g., ≥0.5 SD cognitive improvement, ≥50% reduction in seizures.

symptoms: Intellectual disability, autism features, hyperactivity, anxiety, macroorchidism
treatment_goal: Reduce hyperactivity/anxiety, improve cognitive and behavioral function
first_line_soc: No FDA-approved disease-modifying therapy; stimulants (methylphenidate) for ADHD symptoms; SSRIs for anxiety; antipsychotics for aggression.
treatment_algorithm: Step 1: Methylphenidate or amphetamine for ADHD. Step 2: SSRI (fluoxetine) for anxiety/OCD. Step 3: Antipsychotic (risperidone, aripiprazole) for aggression/ASD. Investigational: mGluR5 antagonists (mavoglurant), GABA-B agonists (arbaclofen), KMO inhibitors.
us_prevalence: ~1:4,000 males, ~1:8,000 females; ~100,000 Americans with FXS.
patients_needing_tx: ~70,000 needing pharmacotherapy for behavioral/cognitive symptoms.
price: $20,000–$50,000/year for disease-modifying agent. Pediatric orphan pricing.
registrational_endpoint: ABC-C (Aberrant Behavior Checklist — Community) and Vineland-3 change at 12 weeks.
btd_threshold_vs_soc: ≥15% ABC-C total score improvement vs. placebo; or Vineland age-equivalent gain ≥3 months in 6 months.

symptoms: Short stature, ovarian insufficiency, infertility, cardiac (coarctation), webbed neck, lymphedema
treatment_goal: Improve height, induce puberty, prevent cardiovascular complications
first_line_soc: Growth hormone (recombinant human GH, FDA-approved) for short stature; estrogen replacement for pubertal induction and hormonal maintenance.
treatment_algorithm: Step 1: GH therapy (0.375 mg/kg/week) from ~2–4 years. Step 2: Low-dose estrogen (17β-estradiol) for puberty induction at ~11–12 years, progesterone cycling thereafter. Step 3: Cardiac surveillance; hypertension treatment. Step 4: Fertility: oocyte preservation or egg donation. Step 5: Annual screening for autoimmune, thyroid, renal.
us_prevalence: ~1:2,000–2,500 females; ~80,000–100,000 US females with Turner syndrome.
patients_needing_tx: ~60,000 on active pharmacotherapy (GH + estrogen).
registrational_endpoint: Adult height SDS improvement vs. untreated controls at growth plate closure; bone age at puberty induction.
btd_threshold_vs_soc: Height gain >7–8 cm vs. untreated historical controls; SDS improvement ≥1.0.

symptoms: Variable: intellectual disability, dysmorphic features, organ anomalies, behavioral problems
treatment_goal: Maximize developmental potential, manage specific complications
first_line_soc: None (pharmacologic for the deletion itself); condition-specific management (growth hormone for 22q11, etc.).
treatment_algorithm: Early intervention; special education; growth hormone for 22q11 short stature; calcium/Vitamin D for DiGeorge-associated hypoparathyroidism; antibiotics/IVIG for immunodeficiency.
us_prevalence: 22q11 deletion: ~1:4,000 (~90,000 US patients). Many other rare deletions (<1,000 each).
patients_needing_tx: ~50,000 needing pharmacotherapy for deletion syndrome complications.
price: $5,000–$50,000/year depending on syndrome.
registrational_endpoint: Primary complication-specific endpoint (e.g., hypocalcemia normalization, immune function, growth velocity) at 12 months.
btd_threshold_vs_soc: Complication prevention rate ≥50% vs. standard care.

symptoms: Variable: sex development disorders, infertility, cognitive, cardiac, or endocrine issues
treatment_goal: Manage hormone deficiencies, support development
first_line_soc: Hormone replacement (estrogen or testosterone) based on sex assignment and karyotype; early intervention.
treatment_algorithm: Individualized hormonal management; psychological support; fertility counseling; organ-specific management.
us_prevalence: ~100,000 individuals with various X chromosome abnormalities beyond Turner/Klinefelter.
patients_needing_tx: ~50,000 needing active pharmacotherapy.
price: $5,000–$20,000/year for novel hormone replacement formulation.
registrational_endpoint: Hormone normalization and Vineland-3 at 12 months.
btd_threshold_vs_soc: N/A (heterogeneous group).

symptoms: Hypogonadism, tall stature, infertility, language delay, learning difficulties, gynecomastia
treatment_goal: Normalize testosterone, support development and fertility
first_line_soc: Testosterone replacement therapy (testosterone cypionate or enanthate IM, or transdermal) starting at puberty (~11–12 years).
treatment_algorithm: Step 1: Testosterone replacement at puberty onset. Step 2: Speech/language and occupational therapy. Step 3: Sperm retrieval (TESE) for fertility. Step 4: Psychological support.
us_prevalence: ~1:500–1,000 males; ~70,000–150,000 US males; ~50% undiagnosed.
patients_needing_tx: ~70,000 diagnosed; ~40,000 on testosterone replacement.
price: $3,000–$8,000/year for testosterone or novel androgen replacement. Existing testosterone is generic but novel formulations command premium.
registrational_endpoint: Serum testosterone normalization, testicular volume, and Vineland-3 at 12 months.
btd_threshold_vs_soc: Testosterone in normal range (300–1,000 ng/dL) in ≥90% vs. ~20% untreated.

symptoms: Premature aging: dwarfism, lipodystrophy, cardiovascular disease, atherosclerosis, death by ~14 years
treatment_goal: Extend lifespan, delay cardiovascular complications
first_line_soc: Lonafarnib (Zokinvy) — farnesyltransferase inhibitor; FDA-approved 2020 for Hutchinson-Gilford Progeria Syndrome.
treatment_algorithm: Lonafarnib 115 mg/m² BID. Aspirin for cardiovascular prevention. Statins. Physical therapy. Cardiac monitoring.
us_prevalence: ~400 children worldwide with HGPS; ~50–70 in US at any time. Pediatric orphan drug.
patients_needing_tx: ~60 US patients at any time.
price: $500,000–$1,000,000/year (Zokinvy ~$1M WAC/year). Pediatric orphan.
peak_sales: ~$18M at 40% share (captured by Zokinvy). New entrant: limited market.
registrational_endpoint: Overall survival extension vs. historical controls; change in cardiovascular endpoints at 24 months.
btd_threshold_vs_soc: ≥1.6-year survival extension vs. historical mean (~14.6 years for HGPS).

symptoms: Tall stature, mild speech/language delay, learning difficulties, behavioral issues
treatment_goal: Address developmental delays, behavioral issues
first_line_soc: None (pharmacologic); early intervention: speech/language therapy, special education support.
treatment_algorithm: Early speech and language therapy; occupational therapy; behavioral support; testosterone supplementation if hypogonadism develops (rare). No disease-specific pharmacotherapy.
us_prevalence: ~1:1,000 males; ~50,000–80,000 males in US; majority undiagnosed.
patients_needing_tx: ~20,000 diagnosed; ~5,000 requiring active pharmacotherapy for associated conditions.
price: $2,000–$5,000/year for targeted neurodevelopmental agent.
registrational_endpoint: Neurodevelopmental outcome (Vineland Adaptive Behavior Scales) at 24 months vs. baseline.
btd_threshold_vs_soc: N/A (no clear pharmacologic target identified).

symptoms: Severe brain malformation, cardiac defects, polydactyly, cleft lip/palate; most die within first year
treatment_goal: Comfort care; supportive for families choosing aggressive intervention
first_line_soc: Comfort/palliative care predominates; selected families choose cardiac surgery and intensive support.
treatment_algorithm: Comfort measures. Selected patients: cardiac surgery for survival extension; anti-epileptic drugs for seizures. G-tube for feeding.
us_prevalence: ~1:5,000–10,000 births; ~400–700 live births/year. Many fatal in utero.
patients_needing_tx: ~400/year live births; ~100–150 surviving >1 year needing pharmacotherapy.
price: N/A (palliative care focus).

symptoms: Severe ID, cardiac defects, clenched fists, growth restriction; most die within first year
treatment_goal: Comfort care; selective aggressive management
first_line_soc: Palliative/comfort care; cardiac surgery or intensive care for selected families.
treatment_algorithm: Comfort care; AEDs for seizures; cardiac surgery in selected cases. Emerging: some long-term survivors with surgical intervention.
us_prevalence: ~1:2,500–3,500 live births; ~700–900 live births/year; 10% survive >1 year.
patients_needing_tx: ~200–300 longer-term survivors requiring active pharmacotherapy.
price: N/A.

symptoms: High fever × 3–5 days followed by truncal rash, HHV-6 etiology
treatment_goal: Manage fever, prevent febrile seizures
first_line_soc: Supportive care; antipyretics (acetaminophen, ibuprofen) for fever management.
treatment_algorithm: Antipyretics; hydration. Ganciclovir/foscarnet for HHV-6 encephalitis in immunocompromised only.
us_prevalence: ~1–2 million cases/year in children <2 years.
patients_needing_tx: ~1.5 million/year with fever requiring antipyretics; ~500 immunocompromised with HHV-6 disease.
price: $20–$100 OTC antipyretic per episode.
tam_gross: ~$100M OTC. Not a novel drug opportunity.
registrational_endpoint: N/A (self-limiting).

symptoms: Slapped-cheek rash, mild fever, joint pain, reticular rash on extremities
treatment_goal: Supportive management; prevent hydrops in immunocompromised/pregnant
first_line_soc: None (pharmacologic); self-limited disease. IVIG for chronic parvovirus B19 in immunocompromised patients.
treatment_algorithm: Supportive: antipyretics, analgesics. Chronic parvovirus in immunocompromised: IVIG 400 mg/kg/day × 10 days. Hydrops fetalis: intrauterine transfusion.
us_prevalence: ~1–2 million cases/year in children (seasonal outbreaks); most self-limited.
patients_needing_tx: ~2,000 immunocompromised/year needing IVIG.
price: $2,000–$5,000 per IVIG course.
peak_sales: Negligible. Not a primary drug target.
registrational_endpoint: Viral clearance (parvovirus B19 PCR) at Day 14 in immunocompromised patients.
btd_threshold_vs_soc: N/A (self-limited in immunocompetent).

symptoms: Progressive personality change, intellectual decline, myoclonus, seizures, coma (late measles complication)
treatment_goal: Slow neurological progression; no curative treatment
first_line_soc: None curative; oral isoprinosine (inosine pranobex) + intrathecal/intraventricular interferon alfa — may temporarily slow progression.
treatment_algorithm: Isoprinosine (100 mg/kg/day) alone or with IFN-alpha (intraventricular). AEDs for myoclonus/seizures. Supportive care.
us_prevalence: ~5–10 cases/year in US (nearly eliminated by MMR vaccination); ~1,000 worldwide.
patients_needing_tx: ~5–10 US patients at any time.
price: $20,000–$100,000/year for novel antiviral (orphan).
registrational_endpoint: Neurological decline rate (EEG, MRI brain) at 12 months vs. historical controls.
btd_threshold_vs_soc: Survival extension >6 months vs. historical median; or EEG stabilization.

symptoms: High fever, cough, coryza, conjunctivitis, Koplik spots, maculopapular rash
treatment_goal: Support recovery, prevent complications (pneumonia, encephalitis)
first_line_soc: Vitamin A supplementation (200,000 IU × 2 days) reduces measles mortality; supportive care; MMR vaccination for prevention.
treatment_algorithm: Vitamin A per WHO protocol. Supportive: antipyretics, hydration, antibiotics for bacterial complications. Ribavirin for encephalitis (limited evidence). MMR vaccination primary prevention.
us_prevalence: ~200–1,200 cases/year in US (varies by outbreak year); predominantly unvaccinated populations.
patients_needing_tx: ~1,000/year requiring vitamin A + supportive treatment.
price: $100–$500 per treatment course.
peak_sales: Negligible. Vaccination prevention market much larger.
registrational_endpoint: Time to fever resolution and rash resolution vs. placebo + standard supportive care.
btd_threshold_vs_soc: N/A (vaccination prevents most disease).

symptoms: Parotid gland swelling, fever, headache, orchitis in males
treatment_goal: Relieve symptoms, prevent orchitis complications
first_line_soc: Supportive care only; analgesics/antipyretics. No antiviral treatment. MMR vaccine for prevention.
treatment_algorithm: Supportive: analgesics, hydration. Scrotal support for orchitis. NSAIDS for parotitis pain.
us_prevalence: ~500–5,000 cases/year (outbreak-dependent); vaccination has reduced incidence 99%.
patients_needing_tx: ~2,000/year requiring supportive treatment.
price: N/A (supportive).

symptoms: Slowly progressive dementia, ataxia, seizures, myoclonus (years after rubella or CRS)
treatment_goal: Slow neurological progression; no curative treatment
first_line_soc: No effective treatment; AEDs for seizures; supportive care.
treatment_algorithm: Anticonvulsants for seizures. Supportive and palliative care. Investigational: isoprinosine (off-label antiviral) — minimal evidence.
us_prevalence: Extremely rare; <10 cases documented in US since rubella vaccination. Essentially eradicated in US.
patients_needing_tx: <10 US patients.
price: N/A (effectively eradicated).

symptoms: Mild maculopapular rash, lymphadenopathy, low-grade fever, joint pain; CRS in congenital
treatment_goal: Prevent congenital rubella syndrome through vaccination
first_line_soc: Supportive care for acquired rubella. Vaccination (MMR) for prevention.
treatment_algorithm: Supportive: antipyretics, rest. No antiviral treatment. CRS management: multidisciplinary (cardiac, ophthalmology, neurology, audiiology).
us_prevalence: Acquired rubella essentially eliminated in US (<10 cases/year); CRS extremely rare.
patients_needing_tx: <10 cases/year in US.
price: N/A.

symptoms: Often asymptomatic; valve stenosis/regurgitation, aortic dilation, aortic dissection risk
treatment_goal: Prevent aortic complications, manage valve dysfunction
first_line_soc: Beta-blockers or ARBs (losartan) for aortic root dilation; surgical valve replacement for severe AS/AR.
treatment_algorithm: Surveillance MRI/Echo annually. Beta-blockers for aortic root dilation (>4 cm). Losartan for Marfan-like aortopathy in BAV. Aortic valve repair/replacement for severe disease. TAVR in adult BAV patients.
us_prevalence: ~1–2% of population; ~3–6 million Americans with BAV.
patients_needing_tx: ~500,000 on active pharmacotherapy for aortic dilation or valve disease.
price: $3,000–$8,000/year for anti-TGFbeta or novel aortopathy agent.
registrational_endpoint: Aortic root diameter change (mm/year) and aortic dissection incidence at 5 years vs. placebo.
btd_threshold_vs_soc: Aortic growth rate <1 mm/year vs. ~2–3 mm/year without treatment.

symptoms: Severe cyanosis, exercise intolerance, hemoptysis, polycythemia, clubbing
treatment_goal: Reduce pulmonary vascular resistance, improve exercise capacity, prevent death
first_line_soc: Bosentan (endothelin receptor antagonist) is FDA-approved for Eisenmenger syndrome to improve exercise capacity and WHO functional class.
treatment_algorithm: Step 1: Bosentan (62.5 mg BID titrating to 125 mg BID). Step 2: Add sildenafil or tadalafil (PDE5 inhibitors). Step 3: Parenteral prostanoids (epoprostenol, treprostinil) for severe cases. Phlebotomy for symptomatic polycythemia. Heart-lung or bilateral lung transplant + cardiac repair as last resort.
us_prevalence: ~30,000–50,000 US patients with Eisenmenger syndrome.
patients_needing_tx: ~20,000 on PAH-targeted pharmacotherapy.
price: $50,000–$100,000/year for PAH-targeted therapies (pediatric orphan pricing applies in many cases).
registrational_endpoint: 6-minute walk distance improvement and WHO functional class at 16 weeks vs. placebo.
btd_threshold_vs_soc: ≥33m improvement in 6MWD vs. placebo (BREATHE-5 showed +53m with bosentan).

symptoms: Usually asymptomatic; can cause fatigue, exercise intolerance, atrial arrhythmias, paradoxical embolism
treatment_goal: Prevent atrial arrhythmias, stroke, and pulmonary hypertension
first_line_soc: None (pharmacologic primary); transcatheter or surgical closure for significant shunts (Qp:Qs >1.5:1 or symptomatic). Medical: diuretics for CHF, antiarrhythmics.
treatment_algorithm: Small ASD: observation. Large/hemodynamically significant: transcatheter Amplatzer device closure (percutaneous) or surgical repair. Post-closure: aspirin for device endothelialization; anticoagulation for paradoxical embolism.
us_prevalence: ~1–2:1,000 live births; ~1 million Americans living with ASD; ~40,000 closures/year.
patients_needing_tx: ~100,000 with significant ASD on pharmacotherapy (diuretics, anticoagulation, antiarrhythmics).
price: $3,000–$10,000/year for post-closure pharmacotherapy or PAH prevention agent.
registrational_endpoint: PAH development prevention rate and arrhythmia burden at 24 months post-closure vs. placebo.
btd_threshold_vs_soc: PAH incidence <5% vs. ~15% without treatment; DAFNE-like trial endpoint.

symptoms: Upper extremity hypertension, weak femoral pulses, leg claudication, headache, rib notching on CXR
treatment_goal: Relieve aortic obstruction, normalize blood pressure long-term
first_line_soc: Beta-blockers (atenolol) or ACE inhibitors for hypertension management; definitive treatment is balloon angioplasty/stenting or surgical repair.
treatment_algorithm: Medical: beta-blockers (atenolol 0.5–2 mg/kg/day) or ACE inhibitors for pre/post-procedural hypertension. Definitive: balloon angioplasty ± stenting (adolescent/adult); surgical resection with end-to-end anastomosis (neonate/infant). Lifelong antihypertensive therapy for residual hypertension post-repair. Anticoagulation if stenting. Lifelong cardiology follow-up; risk of re-coarctation, aortic aneurysm.
us_prevalence: ~1:1,000–2,000 live births; ~100,000–200,000 Americans living with repaired or unrepaired CoA.
patients_needing_tx: ~80,000 on active antihypertensive pharmacotherapy post-repair or pending repair.
price: $3,000–$8,000/year for novel anti-aortopathy agent or improved antihypertensive.
registrational_endpoint: Blood pressure normalization (<95th percentile) and aortic gradient reduction (<20 mmHg) at 12 months.
btd_threshold_vs_soc: Residual gradient <10 mmHg in ≥70% vs. <40% with current therapy.

symptoms: Cyanosis (tet spells), right ventricular hypertrophy, pulmonary stenosis, VSD
treatment_goal: Complete surgical repair, prevent hypercyanotic spells, pulmonary regurgitation
first_line_soc: Beta-blockers (propranolol) to prevent tet spells pre-operatively; morphine/phenylephrine for acute tet spells. Definitive: complete surgical repair at 3–6 months.
treatment_algorithm: Pre-op: propranolol, adequate hydration. Acute tet spell: knee-chest position, morphine, phenylephrine, IV fluids, O2. Surgical: complete repair (VSD patch + RVOT reconstruction). Post-op: pulmonary valve replacement for severe PR; antiarrhythmics; ICD for VT risk.
us_prevalence: ~1:2,500 live births; ~1,600 new cases/year; ~50,000–80,000 US adult survivors of repaired ToF.
patients_needing_tx: ~30,000 adults with repaired ToF on pharmacotherapy (arrhythmia, heart failure, pre-PVR).
price: $5,000–$15,000/year for novel pulmonary valve protection or arrhythmia prevention agent.
registrational_endpoint: Reduction in right ventricular end-diastolic volume index and arrhythmia burden at 12 months.
btd_threshold_vs_soc: ≥15 mL/m² reduction in RVEDVi vs. placebo.

symptoms: Severe cyanosis at birth, shock, multi-organ failure, ductal-dependent circulation
treatment_goal: Maintain ductal patency, staged surgical palliation (Norwood), heart transplant
first_line_soc: Prostaglandin E1 (alprostadil IV) to maintain patent ductus arteriosus in neonates; definitive: staged Norwood palliation → Glenn → Fontan or cardiac transplant.
treatment_algorithm: Neonatal: PGE1 infusion continuously. Stage 1: Norwood procedure (first week). Stage 2: Hemi-Fontan/Glenn (4–6 months). Stage 3: Fontan completion (2–4 years). Post-Fontan: anticoagulation (warfarin/aspirin), ACE-I, phosphodiesterase inhibitors.
us_prevalence: ~1:4,000 live births; ~1,000 new HLHS cases/year in US; ~8,000–12,000 US survivors living with palliated HLHS.
patients_needing_tx: ~8,000 survivors on pharmacotherapy (anticoagulation, cardiac medications).
price: $10,000–$30,000/year for novel Fontan-failure prevention therapy; pediatric orphan pricing applies.
registrational_endpoint: Freedom from Fontan failure (death, transplantation, takedown) at 5 years.
btd_threshold_vs_soc: ≥30% reduction in Fontan failure vs. current standard.

symptoms: Heart murmur, heart failure, failure to thrive, recurrent pulmonary infections
treatment_goal: Prevent CHF and PAH, close VSD surgically if moderate-large
first_line_soc: Diuretics (furosemide), ACE inhibitors (captopril) for CHF symptoms; definitive surgical or catheter device closure for hemodynamically significant VSD.
treatment_algorithm: Small VSD: watchful waiting (many close spontaneously). Moderate-large VSD: medical therapy (diuretics, ACE-I, caloric supplementation) then surgical patch closure by 3–6 months. Post-surgical PAH: sildenafil. Endocarditis prophylaxis (first 6 months post-repair).
us_prevalence: Most common CHD: ~2–4/1,000 live births; ~100,000–200,000 diagnosed annually; ~1–2 million US patients living with VSD (most small and managed).
patients_needing_tx: ~50,000 with moderate-large VSD on pharmacotherapy; ~20,000 surgical closures/year.
price: $2,000–$5,000/year for novel CHF prevention agent in pediatric VSD.
registrational_endpoint: Spontaneous VSD closure rate or CHF hospitalization reduction at 12 months.
btd_threshold_vs_soc: ≥30% reduction in CHF hospitalizations vs. standard diuretic therapy.

symptoms: Severe cyanosis at birth, ductal-dependent circulation, metabolic acidosis
treatment_goal: Emergency arterial switch repair, prevent irreversible brain/organ damage
first_line_soc: Prostaglandin E1 (alprostadil) to maintain PDA; balloon atrial septostomy (Rashkind procedure) for mixing. Definitive: arterial switch operation within first 2 weeks.
treatment_algorithm: Immediate: PGE1 infusion. Balloon septostomy if inadequate mixing. Surgical: arterial switch (Jatene) operation first 2 weeks. Post-op: antiarrhythmics, coronary surveillance, ACE-I if needed.
us_prevalence: ~1:3,000–5,000 live births; ~800–1,000 new cases/year in US; ~20,000–30,000 adult survivors.
patients_needing_tx: ~15,000 adult TGA survivors on pharmacotherapy (arrhythmia, coronary, cardiac function).
price: $5,000–$15,000/year for coronary vasculopathy or arrhythmia prevention.
registrational_endpoint: Freedom from coronary obstruction and LVEF preservation at 5 years post-ASO.

symptoms: Variable: cyanosis, heart murmur, failure to thrive, arrhythmia
treatment_goal: Individualized based on anatomy; prevent mortality and morbidity
first_line_soc: Variable by lesion; typically diuretics, ACE inhibitors, prostaglandins for ductal-dependent lesions. Definitive surgical or catheter-based repair.
treatment_algorithm: Heterogeneous group; medical management of hemodynamic consequences (CHF, PAH, cyanosis) followed by anatomic correction as indicated.
us_prevalence: CHD overall affects ~1% of live births (~40,000/year). This category captures rare structural anomalies: ~5,000–10,000 US patients.
patients_needing_tx: ~5,000 on pharmacotherapy for rare/complex CHD.
price: $10,000–$50,000/year; orphan pricing applies.
registrational_endpoint: Survival and NYHA/Ross functional class at 12 months.

symptoms: Severe cyanosis, absent tricuspid valve, RV hypoplasia, heart failure
treatment_goal: Staged Fontan palliation, maintain oxygenation
first_line_soc: Prostaglandin E1 to maintain PDA in neonates; diuretics and ACE-I. Definitive: staged surgical palliation (BT shunt → Glenn → Fontan).
treatment_algorithm: Neonatal: PGE1, BT shunt. Stage 2: Glenn (4–6 months). Stage 3: Fontan (2–4 years). Post-Fontan: anticoagulation, digoxin, diuretics, phosphodiesterase inhibitors for protein-losing enteropathy.
us_prevalence: ~1:10,000 live births; ~300–400 new cases/year in US; ~5,000–8,000 Fontan survivors.
patients_needing_tx: ~5,000 Fontan survivors on pharmacotherapy.
price: $10,000–$30,000/year; pediatric orphan pricing.
registrational_endpoint: Freedom from Fontan failure and protein-losing enteropathy at 5 years.

symptoms: Cyanosis, arrhythmia (WPW), tricuspid regurgitation, heart failure
treatment_goal: Control arrhythmia, manage tricuspid regurgitation, prevent sudden death
first_line_soc: Antiarrhythmics (amiodarone, sotalol) for WPW-associated arrhythmias; diuretics for right heart failure. Surgical valve repair/replacement for severe TR.
treatment_algorithm: Medical: antiarrhythmics for SVT/WPW; diuretics + ACE-I for CHF. Catheter ablation for refractory arrhythmia. Surgical: cone reconstruction of tricuspid valve (gold standard). Fontan for complex cases. Heart transplant for end-stage.
us_prevalence: ~1:20,000 live births; ~20,000–30,000 US patients living with Ebstein anomaly.
patients_needing_tx: ~10,000 on pharmacotherapy; ~5,000 with active arrhythmia management.
price: $3,000–$10,000/year; orphan pricing applicable.
registrational_endpoint: Arrhythmia burden (24-hour Holter) and NYHA class improvement at 12 months.
btd_threshold_vs_soc: ≥50% reduction in SVT episodes vs. placebo.

symptoms: Cyanosis, heart failure, poor feeding, failure to thrive
