treatment_goal: Surgical correction, prevent cyanosis and heart failure
first_line_soc: Prostaglandin E1 (alprostadil) in neonates to maintain ductus arteriosus; diuretics and digoxin for CHF pre-operatively.
treatment_algorithm: Neonatal: PGE1 infusion to maintain ductal patency. Medical: diuretics, digoxin, ACE-I. Surgical: intraventricular repair (VSD patch routing aorta to LV). Complex anatomy may need Rastelli or Nikaidoh procedure.
us_prevalence: ~1:10,000 live births; ~500–800 new cases/year in US.
patients_needing_tx: ~600/year pre-operatively; ~5,000 US survivors with ongoing pharmacotherapy needs.
price: $5,000–$15,000/year; orphan pricing applies.
registrational_endpoint: Post-surgical hemodynamic outcomes and NYHA/Ross class at 12 months.

symptoms: Cyanosis, heart failure, pulmonary hypertension, single great vessel
treatment_goal: Surgical repair early, prevent Eisenmenger syndrome
first_line_soc: Prostaglandin E1 and diuretics/digoxin for CHF pre-operatively; definitive: complete surgical repair by 2–4 weeks of life.
treatment_algorithm: Neonatal medical stabilization: PGE1 (if associated arch anomaly), diuretics, ACE-I. Surgical: Rastelli-type repair (VSD closure + RV-PA conduit). Post-operative: PAH therapy (sildenafil) if residual PH.
us_prevalence: ~1:10,000–15,000 live births; ~250–400 new cases/year in US.
patients_needing_tx: ~300/year acutely; ~2,000–3,000 US survivors requiring ongoing pharmacotherapy.
price: $10,000–$25,000/year; pediatric orphan pricing.
registrational_endpoint: Post-operative PVR and conduit re-intervention rate at 5 years.

disease: Congenitally Corrected Transposition of the Great Arteries
symptoms: Cyanosis, arrhythmia, heart block, heart failure, poor exercise tolerance
treatment_goal: Manage arrhythmia, prevent ventricular failure, correct hemodynamics
first_line_soc: ACE inhibitors and diuretics for ventricular failure; pacemaker for complete heart block. Definitive: anatomic repair (double switch operation).
treatment_algorithm: Medical: ACE inhibitors, diuretics for systemic RV failure; beta-blockers for arrhythmia; pacemaker for CHB. Surgical: anatomic correction (atrial + arterial switch). Late: ICD, heart transplant.
us_prevalence: ~1:13,000 live births; ~3,000–5,000 US patients living with ccTGA.
patients_needing_tx: ~2,000 on pharmacotherapy (arrhythmia, heart failure management).
price: $5,000–$15,000/year for novel cardiac remodeling agent; orphan pricing applies.
peak_sales: ~$4M at 20% share (rare disease, combined cardiac market).
registrational_endpoint: Reduction in systemic ventricular dysfunction (LVEF equivalent, NYHA class) at 24 months.

symptoms: Cyanosis, pulmonary venous obstruction, respiratory distress, shock at birth
treatment_goal: Emergency surgical repair, restore pulmonary venous drainage
first_line_soc: Emergency: O2, prostaglandin (if ductal-dependent), mechanical ventilation. Definitive: urgent surgical repair.
treatment_algorithm: Medical stabilization: O2, ventilation, avoid vasodilators. Surgical: emergent repair reconnecting pulmonary veins to LA. Post-op: sildenafil for pulmonary HTN; anticoagulation if thrombosis. Long-term surveillance for PV stenosis.
us_prevalence: ~1:15,000 live births; ~200–300 new cases/year in US.
patients_needing_tx: ~2,000 US survivors; ~500 with ongoing PH pharmacotherapy.
price: $10,000–$30,000/year; orphan pricing.
registrational_endpoint: PV stenosis recurrence rate and pulmonary artery pressure normalization at 12 months post-repair.

symptoms: Congestive heart failure, poor weight gain, pulmonary hypertension, cyanosis
treatment_goal: Surgical correction, prevent PAH and Eisenmenger
first_line_soc: Diuretics (furosemide 1–4 mg/kg/day) + ACE inhibitors (captopril) pre-operatively; definitive: complete surgical repair by 3–6 months.
treatment_algorithm: Medical: diuretics, ACE-I for CHF; nutrition support. Surgical: complete AVSD repair (patch technique). PAH post-op: sildenafil, bosentan.
us_prevalence: ~1:10,000 births; ~3,500 new cases/year. Strongly associated with Down syndrome (40% of AVSD).
patients_needing_tx: ~3,000/year needing pre-op medical management; ~500 with residual PAH.
price: $5,000–$20,000/year for PAH drugs.
peak_sales: Negligible as separate market. PAH drugs captured under broader PAH.
registrational_endpoint: Post-operative hemodynamic outcome (PVR reduction) and freedom from re-operation at 5 years.
btd_threshold_vs_soc: N/A (surgical outcomes focus).

symptoms: Continuous heart murmur, wide pulse pressure, pulmonary congestion, CHF in preterm infants
treatment_goal: Close PDA, prevent pulmonary overcirculation and CHF
first_line_soc: Indomethacin (IV COX inhibitor) or ibuprofen lysine (IV) for premature neonates to achieve pharmacologic PDA closure.
treatment_algorithm: Step 1: Indomethacin 0.2 mg/kg IV q12h × 3 doses or ibuprofen IV (standard in premature neonates). Step 2: Acetaminophen IV as alternative if COX-inhibitors contraindicated. Step 3: Surgical ligation or catheter-based device closure (Amplatzer duct occluder) for failed medical therapy or in term neonates.
us_prevalence: ~3,000–5,000 symptomatic PDA cases/year in US (mostly premature infants; prevalence in VLBW infants ~30–50%).
patients_needing_tx: ~3,000 premature infants/year requiring pharmacologic closure.
price: $500–$2,000/course for novel IV formulations; not orphan-priced given acute short treatment.
peak_sales: ~$2M at 40% share (acute hospital drug, one-time treatment).
registrational_endpoint: Successful PDA closure rate on echocardiogram at 72 hours vs. placebo/comparator.
btd_threshold_vs_soc: ≥70% closure rate vs. ~50% with placebo or comparator.

symptoms: Heart failure, pulmonary hypertension, recurrent respiratory infections, poor feeding
treatment_goal: Surgical closure, prevent Eisenmenger physiology
first_line_soc: None (pharmacologic primary); urgent surgical repair shortly after diagnosis. Diuretics (furosemide) and digoxin for heart failure pre-operatively.
treatment_algorithm: Pre-op: diuretics + ACE inhibitors for CHF. Definitive: surgical patch closure. Pulmonary hypertension if delayed: sildenafil, bosentan.
us_prevalence: ~500–700 cases/year in US (rare; 0.1% of CHD).
patients_needing_tx: ~600/year; mainly surgical. ~100 with PAH needing pharmacotherapy.
price: $5,000–$15,000/year for PAH therapy.

symptoms: Small head circumference, intellectual disability, developmental delay, seizures
treatment_goal: Manage seizures, optimize development, treat underlying cause
first_line_soc: Antiepileptic drugs (levetiracetam, valproate) for seizures; no pharmacologic cure for structural microcephaly. Zika-related: supportive care.
treatment_algorithm: Seizure management: LEV, VPA, phenobarbital. Early intervention: physical, occupational, speech therapy. Treat underlying metabolic/infectious cause if identified. No disease-modifying pharmacotherapy available.
us_prevalence: ~2/10,000 live births for severe microcephaly; ~6,000–8,000 US children with clinically significant microcephaly.
patients_needing_tx: ~4,000 on antiepileptic pharmacotherapy.
price: $5,000–$20,000/year for gene therapy or neuroprotective agent; orphan pricing.
registrational_endpoint: Reduction in seizure frequency and developmental score improvement (Bayley-4) at 12 months.
btd_threshold_vs_soc: ≥50% seizure reduction vs. placebo.

symptoms: Premature skull suture fusion, abnormal head shape, increased intracranial pressure
treatment_goal: Surgical correction, prevent ICP elevation and cognitive impairment
first_line_soc: None (pharmacologic); treatment is surgical cranial vault remodeling (spring-assisted cranioplasty or open craniotomy).
treatment_algorithm: Surgical correction: endoscopic suturectomy (early) or open craniofacial reconstruction (older). Helmeting post-surgery. No pharmacologic cure. Genetic testing for syndromic forms (Apert, Crouzon: FGFR mutations).
us_prevalence: ~1:2,500 live births; ~1,500–2,000 new cases/year in US.
patients_needing_tx: ~500/year with syndromic craniosynostosis potentially benefiting from FGFR inhibitor therapy.
price: $50,000–$100,000/year for FGFR inhibitor (e.g., infigratinib analog); orphan pricing.
registrational_endpoint: Reduction in ICP and skull morphology score at 12 months in syndromic craniosynostosis.
btd_threshold_vs_soc: ≥30% reduction in reoperation rate vs. surgery-only control.

symptoms: Visual impairment, cataracts, glaucoma, coloboma, anophthalmia, nystagmus
treatment_goal: Maximize visual function, prevent amblyopia, manage secondary complications
first_line_soc: Depends on anomaly: topical antiglaucoma drops (latanoprost, timolol) for glaucoma; patching/atropine penalization for amblyopia. Surgical: cataract extraction, glaucoma surgery.
treatment_algorithm: Cataract: early surgical extraction + aphakic contact lens/glasses → patching for amblyopia. Glaucoma: topical IOP-lowering agents; goniotomy/trabeculotomy. Coloboma/anophthalmia: prosthetic eye, vision therapy.
us_prevalence: ~1–2/1,000 births with significant congenital eye anomalies; ~30,000–50,000 US children with treatable congenital eye conditions.
patients_needing_tx: ~10,000 children on pharmacologic therapy (IOP-lowering drops, cycloplegics).
price: $500–$3,000/year for topical ophthalmic therapy.
registrational_endpoint: IOP reduction and visual acuity improvement at 12 months.
btd_threshold_vs_soc: ≥30% IOP reduction vs. placebo in pediatric glaucoma.

symptoms: Enlarged head circumference, hydrocephalus, cognitive delay in some
treatment_goal: Identify underlying cause, manage hydrocephalus or metabolic condition
first_line_soc: None for benign familial macrocephaly; acetazolamide or serial LPs for communicating hydrocephalus; surgical VP shunt for obstructive hydrocephalus.
treatment_algorithm: Identify etiology (metabolic, structural, familial). Benign megalencephaly: observation. Hydrocephalus: acetazolamide short-term, VP shunting. Metabolic causes (Canavan, Alexander disease): disease-specific management.
us_prevalence: Macrocephaly (head >97th percentile): ~2–3% of children; majority benign familial. Pathologic cases: ~10,000–20,000/year.
patients_needing_tx: ~5,000 requiring pharmacologic management per year.
price: $1,000–$5,000/year for underlying metabolic disease treatment.
registrational_endpoint: Head circumference stabilization and developmental milestone achievement at 12 months.

symptoms: Facial clefting, feeding difficulty, speech problems, ear infections, dental anomalies
treatment_goal: Surgical repair, normalize feeding, speech, and hearing
first_line_soc: None (pharmacologic); primary treatment is surgical repair (lip at 3–6 months, palate at 9–18 months). Specialized feeding techniques, speech therapy, orthodontics.
treatment_algorithm: Multidisciplinary team: cleft repair surgery (lip then palate). Ear tubes for recurrent OM. Speech-language therapy. Orthodontics and orthognathic surgery in adolescence. No first-line pharmacologic therapy.
us_prevalence: ~7,000 new cases/year; ~1 in 700 live births; ~200,000 US individuals with cleft history.
patients_needing_tx: ~5,000/year requiring pharmaceutical adjuncts (antibiotics, pain management peri-op); pharmacologic TAM minimal.
price: $500–$2,000/course for adjunct peri-operative therapy.
registrational_endpoint: Wound healing score and complication rate at 4 weeks post-repair.

symptoms: Jaw malalignment, feeding/swallowing difficulties, airway obstruction, facial asymmetry
treatment_goal: Restore jaw alignment, correct airway, enable normal feeding and speech
first_line_soc: None (pharmacologic); treatment is surgical orthognathic surgery, distraction osteogenesis, and mandibular lengthening.
treatment_algorithm: Newborn: mandibular distraction for severe micrognathia/Pierre Robin. Later: orthodontics, orthognathic surgery. Airway management as needed. No pharmacologic intervention standard.
us_prevalence: ~1:5,000–10,000 births for severe jaw anomalies; ~500–1,000 new cases/year in US.
patients_needing_tx: Minimal pharmacologic treatment pool; primarily surgical.
price: $2,000–$10,000/year for growth factor or bone morphogenic protein adjunct therapy.
registrational_endpoint: Cephalometric improvement in mandibular length and bite correction at 12 months.

symptoms: Absent/malformed outer ear (microtia), hearing loss, ear canal atresia
treatment_goal: Restore hearing, reconstruct outer ear cosmetically
first_line_soc: None (pharmacologic); treatment is surgical reconstruction (rib cartilage, Medpor) and bone-anchored hearing aids (BAHA).
treatment_algorithm: Audiologic evaluation; BAHA or CROS hearing aids for atresia-related hearing loss. Surgical ear reconstruction at age 6–10. No pharmacologic treatment for structural anomalies.
us_prevalence: Microtia: ~1:6,000–10,000 births; ~500–800 new cases/year in US.
patients_needing_tx: ~500/year requiring adjunct pharmacologic support; no meaningful pharmacologic TAM.
price: $2,000–$5,000/year for hearing-loss management agents.
registrational_endpoint: Pure tone audiogram threshold improvement at 12 months.

symptoms: Progressive neonatal jaundice, pale stools, dark urine, hepatomegaly
treatment_goal: Restore bile flow, prevent cirrhosis and liver failure, bridge to transplant
first_line_soc: No pharmacologic first-line; Kasai hepatoportoenterostomy is standard of care. Post-Kasai: ursodiol (ursodeoxycholic acid) to promote bile flow.
treatment_algorithm: Early Kasai portoenterostomy (before 60–90 days of life). Post-Kasai: ursodiol, fat-soluble vitamins (A,D,E,K), prophylactic antibiotics to prevent cholangitis. If Kasai fails: liver transplantation.
us_prevalence: ~1:10,000–15,000 live births; ~300–350 new cases/year in US.
patients_needing_tx: ~3,000–5,000 US children with biliary atresia (pre/post Kasai) on ursodiol.
price: $50,000–$150,000/year for novel anti-fibrotic or bile acid modulator; orphan pricing.
registrational_endpoint: Reduction in serum bilirubin and transplant-free survival at 2 years post-Kasai.
btd_threshold_vs_soc: ≥20% improvement in transplant-free survival at 2 years vs. ursodiol alone.

symptoms: Failure to pass meconium, abdominal distension, constipation, enterocolitis
treatment_goal: Surgical resection of aganglionic bowel, restore bowel function
first_line_soc: None (pharmacologic) as primary; definitive is surgical pull-through procedure. Post-op: bowel management (enemas, laxatives for dysmotility).
treatment_algorithm: Diagnosis: suction rectal biopsy. Treatment: Swenson, Soave, or Duhamel pull-through surgery. Post-op: management of Hirschsprung-associated enterocolitis (metronidazole + bowel irrigation). Long-term: motility-promoting agents.
us_prevalence: ~1:5,000 live births; ~700–900 new cases/year in US.
patients_needing_tx: ~10,000 US patients with HD on bowel management pharmacotherapy.
price: $5,000–$20,000/year for stem cell-based or enteric nervous system restoration therapy; orphan pricing.
registrational_endpoint: Bowel continence score and enterocolitis episode frequency at 24 months post-surgery.
btd_threshold_vs_soc: ≥40% reduction in enterocolitis episodes vs. standard bowel irrigation.

symptoms: Excessive secretions, coughing, choking, inability to pass NGT, cyanosis at first feeding
treatment_goal: Surgical repair, establish esophageal continuity, prevent aspiration
first_line_soc: None (pharmacologic); emergency surgical repair (primary anastomosis or staged repair for long-gap EA). Post-op: PPI (omeprazole) for GERD, which affects >50% of EA patients.
treatment_algorithm: Surgical: primary esophageal anastomosis (type C EA); staged repair for long-gap EA (delayed anastomosis, Foker, gastric pull-up). Post-op: PPI for GERD, esophageal dilation for strictures. Long-term surveillance for dysphagia.
us_prevalence: ~1:3,000–4,500 live births; ~1,200–1,500 new cases/year in US.
patients_needing_tx: ~10,000–15,000 EA survivors on PPIs for GERD; ~5,000 on serial dilations.
price: $2,000–$10,000/year for anti-fibrotic esophageal stricture prevention therapy; orphan pricing.
registrational_endpoint: Esophageal stricture rate and dysphagia score at 12 months post-repair.
btd_threshold_vs_soc: ≥40% reduction in stricture formation rate vs. standard PPI alone.

symptoms: Respiratory distress at birth, bowel sounds in chest, mediastinal shift, pulmonary hypoplasia
treatment_goal: Surgical repair, manage pulmonary hypertension and respiratory failure
first_line_soc: Nitric oxide (iNO, inhaled) for pulmonary hypertension; mechanical ventilation. Definitive: surgical repair after stabilization.
treatment_algorithm: Stabilization: mechanical ventilation, iNO for PPHN, ECMO if severe. Surgical repair after stabilization (delay 24–48h). Post-repair: sildenafil for residual PH. ECMO bridge to surgery in severe cases.
us_prevalence: ~1:2,500 live births; ~1,500–2,000 new CDH cases/year in US.
patients_needing_tx: ~1,500/year requiring pharmacologic pulmonary hypertension management.
price: $10,000–$50,000/year for pulmonary vascular remodeling therapy; orphan pricing.
registrational_endpoint: Freedom from ECMO and survival to NICU discharge; pulmonary hypertension resolution at 30 days.
btd_threshold_vs_soc: ≥20% improvement in survival rate without ECMO vs. standard iNO + ventilation.

symptoms: Absent anal opening, absent meconium passage, abdominal distension
treatment_goal: Surgical reconstruction of anal opening, establish bowel function
first_line_soc: None (pharmacologic); primary treatment is emergency surgical repair (posterior sagittal anorectoplasty).
treatment_algorithm: Neonatal colostomy for complex anomalies. Posterior sagittal anorectoplasty (PSARP) at 3–6 months. Bowel management program (enemas, laxatives) post-op for fecal continence. No standard pharmacologic intervention.
us_prevalence: ~1:4,000–5,000 live births; ~800–1,000 new cases/year in US.
patients_needing_tx: ~10,000 US children and adults with anal atresia history requiring bowel management (laxatives, enemas).
price: $1,000–$5,000/year for bowel motility agent.
registrational_endpoint: Bowel continence score (Krickenbeck classification) at 12 months post-reconstruction.

symptoms: Abdominal wall defect with eviscerated bowel, intestinal atresia, peel/inflammation
treatment_goal: Surgical closure, bowel function recovery, prevent sepsis
first_line_soc: None (pharmacologic); emergency surgical reduction and closure. Post-op: TPN, bowel function support.
treatment_algorithm: Emergency: bowel protection (saline-moistened gauze, silo). Primary or staged closure. Post-op: TPN until bowel function returns. Monitoring for intestinal dysmotility. No pharmacologic disease-modifying treatment.
us_prevalence: ~1:2,000–3,000 live births; ~2,000–2,500 new cases/year in US (rising incidence).
patients_needing_tx: ~2,000/year perioperatively; ~5,000 survivors with bowel dysmotility on prokinetics.
price: $1,000–$5,000/year for bowel motility agent.
registrational_endpoint: Time to full enteral feeds and length of hospital stay.

symptoms: Herniation of abdominal organs through umbilicus covered by sac, associated anomalies
treatment_goal: Surgical reduction and closure, manage associated anomalies
first_line_soc: None (pharmacologic); primary treatment is surgical reduction. Topical antiseptics (silver sulfadiazine) for giant omphalocele escharization.
treatment_algorithm: Small: primary surgical closure. Giant omphalocele: staged closure or escharization with topical silver sulfadiazine followed by delayed closure. Associated anomalies (CHD, chromosomal): treat concurrently.
us_prevalence: ~1:4,000–5,000 live births; ~800–1,000 new cases/year in US.
patients_needing_tx: ~800/year needing pharmacologic adjuncts; minimal standalone pharmacologic market.
price: $1,000–$5,000/year for skin healing or anti-fibrotic adjunct.
registrational_endpoint: Complete closure rate and complication-free survival at 12 months.

symptoms: Bilious vomiting, abdominal distension, absent bowel sounds, failure to pass meconium
treatment_goal: Surgical anastomosis, restore bowel continuity
first_line_soc: None (pharmacologic); primary treatment is surgical repair (primary anastomosis or tapering enteroplasty).
treatment_algorithm: Decompression, IV fluids pre-op. Surgical: primary anastomosis or Santulli procedure. TPN post-op until bowel function. No pharmacologic disease-specific treatment.
us_prevalence: ~1:5,000–10,000 live births; ~500–800 new cases/year in US.
patients_needing_tx: Primarily surgical; pharmacologic TAM minimal.
price: $1,000–$5,000/year for short bowel syndrome management.
registrational_endpoint: Time to full enteral feeds and TPN weaning.

symptoms: Bilious vomiting in neonate, double-bubble sign on X-ray, failure to pass meconium
treatment_goal: Surgical restoration of duodenal continuity
first_line_soc: None (pharmacologic); primary treatment is surgical repair (duodenoduodenostomy).
treatment_algorithm: NG decompression, IV fluids pre-op. Surgical: duodenoduodenostomy (diamond-shaped anastomosis). Post-op: TPN then oral feeding progression. No pharmacologic intervention.
us_prevalence: ~1:5,000–10,000 live births; ~500–800 new cases/year in US. Associated with Down syndrome in 30%.
patients_needing_tx: Primarily surgical; pharmacologic TAM minimal.
price: $500–$2,000/course for perioperative support.
registrational_endpoint: Time to full oral feeds and hospital discharge after surgical repair.

symptoms: Abdominal pain, intestinal obstruction, GI bleeding, palpable mass
treatment_goal: Surgical excision to prevent obstruction and bleeding
first_line_soc: None (pharmacologic); treatment is surgical excision of the duplication.
treatment_algorithm: Surgical excision of duplication cyst. Resection with primary anastomosis if bowel involvement. Endoscopic fenestration for some thoracic duplications. No pharmacologic intervention.
us_prevalence: ~1:4,500 live births; ~600–800 new cases/year in US.
patients_needing_tx: Primarily surgical; minimal pharmacologic treatment pool.
price: $500–$2,000/course for perioperative support.
registrational_endpoint: Complication-free survival and symptom resolution at 12 months post-resection.

symptoms: Bilious vomiting, midgut volvulus, intestinal obstruction, abdominal pain
treatment_goal: Emergency surgical correction (Ladd procedure), prevent midgut loss
first_line_soc: None (pharmacologic); emergency surgical intervention (Ladd procedure) is standard.
treatment_algorithm: Emergency: IV fluids, NG decompression. Surgical: Ladd procedure (division of Ladd bands, widening mesenteric base, appendectomy). No pharmacologic intervention proven effective.
us_prevalence: ~1:500 live births (asymptomatic) but ~1:6,000 with symptomatic volvulus requiring surgery; ~600–1,000 operations/year.
patients_needing_tx: Primarily surgical; pharmacologic TAM minimal.
price: N/A for pharmacologic intervention.
registrational_endpoint: Surgical success rate (normal bowel function at 1 year) after Ladd procedure.

symptoms: Muscle weakness, hypotonia, limited joint movement, feeding difficulties
treatment_goal: Maintain function, prevent contractures, support respiratory function
first_line_soc: No universal pharmacologic SoC; physical therapy is primary. Specific disorders (e.g., nemaline myopathy): supportive care and ventilatory support.
treatment_algorithm: Physical and occupational therapy. Respiratory support (BiPAP, tracheostomy in severe cases). Nutritional support. Specific genetic therapies under investigation. No approved first-line pharmacologic agent.
us_prevalence: ~1:26,000 live births for congenital myopathies as a class; ~10,000–20,000 US patients.
patients_needing_tx: ~5,000 on supportive pharmacotherapy (respiratory agents, carnitine, CoQ10).
price: $50,000–$200,000/year for gene or antisense therapy; orphan pricing.
registrational_endpoint: Motor function measure (MFM-32) and ventilator-free survival at 12 months.
btd_threshold_vs_soc: ≥3-point improvement in MFM-32 vs. natural history.

symptoms: Multiple congenital joint contractures, muscle weakness, rigid limbs
treatment_goal: Improve joint mobility, achieve functional independence
first_line_soc: None (pharmacologic); treatment is physical therapy, splinting, and serial casting. Surgical release for severe contractures.
treatment_algorithm: Serial casting, physical/occupational therapy initiated in neonatal period. Surgical: release of contractures, tendon transfer, joint replacement in some. Assistive devices. No pharmacologic disease-modifying therapy.
us_prevalence: ~1:3,000 live births; ~1,500–2,000 new cases/year in US; ~50,000–100,000 US patients.
patients_needing_tx: Minimal pharmacologic treatment pool; pain management (~20,000 on NSAIDs/muscle relaxants).
price: $5,000–$20,000/year for muscle contracture-reducing biologics (anti-myostatin, TGF-beta); orphan pricing.
registrational_endpoint: Joint range of motion improvement (goniometry) and functional independence at 12 months.
btd_threshold_vs_soc: ≥15° improvement in knee/hip ROM vs. physical therapy alone.

symptoms: Absent, shortened, or malformed limbs; functional impairment; amputation variants
treatment_goal: Maximize function with prosthetics, corrective surgery, and rehabilitation
first_line_soc: None (pharmacologic); prosthetics, surgical reconstruction (Syme amputation, limb lengthening), occupational therapy.
treatment_algorithm: Limb deficiency: early prosthetic fitting. Polydactyly/syndactyly: surgical correction. Limb lengthening: Ilizarov technique. No pharmacologic treatment standard.
us_prevalence: ~1:2,000 live births with any limb anomaly; ~2,000–3,000 significant cases/year in US.
patients_needing_tx: Pharmacologic TAM minimal; pain management (~10,000 on analgesics).
price: $2,000–$5,000/year for adjunct pain/nerve growth.
registrational_endpoint: Functional limb use score and pain NRS at 12 months.

symptoms: Hip instability, Ortolani/Barlow sign, asymmetric skin folds, abnormal gait
treatment_goal: Restore normal hip development, prevent avascular necrosis
first_line_soc: None (pharmacologic); Pavlik harness for infants <6 months; closed/open reduction for older children.
treatment_algorithm: Birth to 6 months: Pavlik harness. 6–18 months: closed reduction + spica cast. >18 months: open reduction + osteotomy. Avascular necrosis: bisphosphonates may be considered (off-label).
us_prevalence: ~1:1,000 live births with significant DDH requiring treatment; ~4,000–5,000 new cases/year requiring intervention.
patients_needing_tx: Primarily mechanical/surgical; pharmacologic TAM negligible.
price: $500–$2,000/year for bone health adjunct.
registrational_endpoint: Acetabular index normalization and AVN rate at 2 years.

symptoms: Leg bowing or knocking, gait abnormality, knee pain in severe cases
treatment_goal: Correct alignment, prevent osteoarthritis
first_line_soc: Observation for physiologic varus/valgus (<2 years for varus, 3–5 years for valgus); rickets: vitamin D and phosphate supplementation. Surgical: guided growth (hemiepiphysiodesis) for pathologic cases.
treatment_algorithm: Physiologic cases: observation. Pathologic (Blount's disease, rickets): treat underlying cause (vitamin D, phosphate); surgical guided growth. No pharmacologic first-line for structural genu varum/valgum.
us_prevalence: ~5–10% of children have some degree of physiologic bowing; pathologic cases: ~10,000–20,000/year in US.
patients_needing_tx: ~5,000 on vitamin D/phosphate supplementation for rickets-related deformity.
price: $500–$2,000/year for vitamin D or metabolic correction.
registrational_endpoint: Mechanical axis deviation (malalignment) correction at 12 months.

symptoms: Limited neck mobility, short neck, torticollis, scoliosis, instability
treatment_goal: Prevent neurologic compromise, correct deformity, maintain spine stability
first_line_soc: Physical therapy for torticollis; cervical bracing for instability. Surgical fusion for Klippel-Feil with instability.
treatment_algorithm: Torticollis: physical therapy, botulinum toxin for refractory. Klippel-Feil: cervical bracing, fusion if unstable. Sprengel deformity: surgical omovertebral bar resection. No pharmacologic standard.
us_prevalence: Klippel-Feil: ~1:40,000 births; torticollis ~1:300 infants. Combined: ~10,000–20,000 US cases requiring active management.
patients_needing_tx: ~2,000 on botulinum toxin or pharmacologic management for refractory torticollis.
price: $1,000–$5,000/year for BTX-A and adjuncts.
registrational_endpoint: Cervical rotation range and torticollis grade at 6 months.

disease: Talipes Equinovarus (Clubfoot) and Other Congenital Foot Anomalies
symptoms: Foot turning inward and downward, rigid deformity, affecting gait
treatment_goal: Achieve plantigrade foot, normal gait
first_line_soc: None (pharmacologic); serial casting (Ponseti method) starting in neonatal period; percutaneous Achilles tenotomy; bracing.
treatment_algorithm: Ponseti method (serial casting 6–8 weeks + tenotomy). Foot abduction orthosis (Dennis Brown bar) for 3–4 years. Tibialis anterior tendon transfer for relapse. No pharmacologic standard of care.
us_prevalence: ~1:1,000 live births; ~4,000–5,000 new cases/year in US.
patients_needing_tx: Primarily mechanical; pharmacologic TAM negligible except for botulinum toxin in relapse.
price: $500–$3,000/year for BTX-A adjunct.
registrational_endpoint: Pirani score and recurrence rate at 2 years.

symptoms: Hyperextended knee at birth, inability to flex knee, fixed dislocation
treatment_goal: Serial casting, surgical reduction if needed
first_line_soc: None (pharmacologic); serial casting (Pavlik-like) or surgical reduction for persistent dislocation.
treatment_algorithm: Serial casting for grade I–II dislocation. Surgical: quadriceps lengthening, capsulotomy for grade III. Physical therapy post-reduction.
us_prevalence: ~1:50,000–100,000 births; extremely rare; ~30–50 new cases/year in US.
patients_needing_tx: Primarily surgical; negligible pharmacologic market.
price: $1,000–$3,000/year for adjunct.
registrational_endpoint: Knee ROM and Tachdjian score at 12 months after correction.

symptoms: In-toeing or out-toeing gait, tripping, pigeon-toed appearance
treatment_goal: Correct gait, prevent degenerative joint disease
first_line_soc: Observation; most cases resolve spontaneously by age 8. Severe: derotational femoral osteotomy.
treatment_algorithm: Observation for mild cases (most self-correct). Physical therapy. Derotational osteotomy for persistent severe anteversion (>3 SD from normal) with functional impairment. No pharmacologic treatment.
us_prevalence: ~10–15% of children have femoral torsion; vast majority self-resolve. ~5,000–10,000 require active intervention.
patients_needing_tx: Primarily surgical; negligible pharmacologic TAM.
registrational_endpoint: Rotational profile (CT measurement) and gait analysis at 12 months.

symptoms: In-toeing gait (internal tibial torsion), tripping in young children
treatment_goal: Allow natural correction, surgical correction for severe refractory cases
first_line_soc: Observation; spontaneous resolution by age 6–8 in most cases. Severe persistent: derotational tibial osteotomy.
treatment_algorithm: Observation (natural history favors resolution). Night splints historically used but evidence limited. Osteotomy for severe cases with functional impairment. No pharmacologic treatment.
us_prevalence: ~1–2% of children; vast majority self-resolve; ~2,000–5,000 require active monitoring/surgery.
patients_needing_tx: Negligible pharmacologic treatment pool.
registrational_endpoint: Tibio-femoral angle measurement and gait analysis at 12 months.

symptoms: Developmental regression, hand-wringing stereotypies, breathing irregularities, seizures, non-verbal
treatment_goal: Reduce seizures, improve breathing, restore neurologic function
first_line_soc: Antiepileptic drugs (valproate, lamotrigine); trofinetide (Daybue) is the first FDA-approved drug specifically for Rett syndrome (2023).
treatment_algorithm: Trofinetide (Daybue) for girls ≥2 years: reduces core RTT symptoms. AEDs for seizure management. Scoliosis management. Respiratory support for breathing irregularities. Physical and speech therapy.
us_prevalence: ~6,000–9,000 US females with Rett syndrome; ~1,200 new cases/year. Almost exclusively affects females (MECP2 mutations).
patients_needing_tx: ~6,000–8,000 eligible for trofinetide.
price: $300,000–$400,000/year (Daybue WAC ~$375,000/year; pediatric orphan pricing).
registrational_endpoint: RTT-CGAS (Clinical Global Assessment Scale) and RBSL (Rett Syndrome Behavioral Assessment) at 12 weeks vs. placebo.
btd_threshold_vs_soc: ≥3-point improvement in RTT-CGAS vs. placebo (LAVENDER trial: trofinetide showed significant improvement).

symptoms: Leg weakness/paralysis, neurogenic bladder, bowel dysfunction, hydrocephalus, Chiari malformation
treatment_goal: Prevent neurologic deterioration, manage bladder/bowel, maximize mobility
first_line_soc: Prenatal surgical closure (fetal MMC repair) shown to improve outcomes. Post-natal: VP shunt for hydrocephalus; clean intermittent catheterization (CIC); oxybutynin for neurogenic bladder.
treatment_algorithm: Prenatal: fetal MMC surgery (MOMS trial). Neonatal: surgical spinal closure. VP shunting. Neurogenic bladder: CIC + oxybutynin or solifenacin. Bowel management: bisacodyl, glycerin suppositories. Orthopedic: bracing, mobility aids. No disease-modifying pharmacotherapy for the underlying defect.
us_prevalence: ~1,600–2,000 new cases/year in US; ~166,000 US people living with spina bifida (spina bifida.org).
patients_needing_tx: ~100,000 with myelomeningocele on pharmacotherapy (anticholinergics, laxatives, antibiotics for UTI prevention).
price: $5,000–$20,000/year for novel neuroprotective or stem cell-based therapy; orphan pricing.
registrational_endpoint: Urinary continence rate and motor function (PEDI-CAT mobility domain) at 12 months.
btd_threshold_vs_soc: ≥20% improvement in urinary continence rate vs. standard CIC + oxybutynin.

symptoms: Enlarged head, bulging fontanelle, sundowning eyes, vomiting, seizures
treatment_goal: Control intracranial pressure, preserve neurodevelopment
first_line_soc: Acetazolamide (carbonic anhydrase inhibitor) for temporary ICP reduction; definitive: VP shunt placement or endoscopic third ventriculostomy (ETV).
treatment_algorithm: Medical bridge: acetazolamide + furosemide (serial LPs). Definitive: VP shunt or ETV. Shunt failure/infection: revision surgery. Post-shunt: monitoring for shunt failure, neurodevelopmental support.
us_prevalence: ~1:500–1,000 live births; ~2,000–3,000 new cases/year of congenital hydrocephalus in US.
patients_needing_tx: ~30,000–50,000 US children with hydrocephalus on pharmacologic adjuncts.
price: $10,000–$50,000/year for novel CSF secretion inhibitor or choroid plexus modulator; orphan pricing.
registrational_endpoint: ICP reduction and shunt revision rate at 12 months.
btd_threshold_vs_soc: ≥30% reduction in VP shunt revision rate vs. placebo adjunct.

symptoms: Severe intellectual disability, refractory seizures, motor deficits, cerebral malformation
treatment_goal: Control seizures, maximize developmental potential, prevent complications
first_line_soc: Antiepileptic drugs (levetiracetam, valproate, vigabatrin); ketogenic diet for refractory epilepsy. Hemispherectomy for catastrophic unilateral epilepsy.
treatment_algorithm: AEDs (polytherapy often required). Ketogenic diet. VNS (vagal nerve stimulation). Hemispherectomy/hemispherotomy for unilateral malformations. mTOR inhibitors (rapamycin) for TSC-associated hemimegalencephaly.
us_prevalence: ~5,000–10,000 US children with significant cerebral hemisphere malformations (lissencephaly, hemimegalencephaly, polymicrogyria).
patients_needing_tx: ~5,000 on multi-AED therapy.
price: $10,000–$50,000/year for mTOR inhibitor or genetic targeted therapy; orphan pricing.
registrational_endpoint: Seizure frequency reduction and developmental milestone attainment at 12 months.
btd_threshold_vs_soc: ≥50% reduction in seizure frequency vs. standard AED.

symptoms: Vision loss, pituitary hormone deficiencies, absent septum pellucidum, midline brain abnormalities
treatment_goal: Replace pituitary hormones, maximize visual function and growth
first_line_soc: Hormone replacement: growth hormone for GHD, hydrocortisone for ACTH deficiency, thyroxine for central hypothyroidism, desmopressin for DI.
treatment_algorithm: Endocrine replacement (GH, cortisol, thyroid, ADH as needed). Vision optimization (early visual intervention). Antiepileptics for seizures. Multidisciplinary management.
us_prevalence: ~1:10,000 live births; ~3,000–5,000 US patients with SOD.
patients_needing_tx: ~3,000 on hormone replacement therapy.
price: $15,000–$60,000/year for long-acting GH/pituitary hormone combinations; orphan pricing.
registrational_endpoint: Height velocity (cm/year) and hormone level normalization at 12 months.
btd_threshold_vs_soc: ≥2 cm/year improvement in height velocity vs. standard GH injection therapy.

symptoms: Fluid-filled brain cavities, seizures, motor deficits, cognitive impairment
treatment_goal: Control seizures, manage spasticity, maximize function
first_line_soc: Antiepileptic drugs (levetiracetam, oxcarbazepine) for seizures; physical therapy for motor deficits.
treatment_algorithm: AED management. Physical, occupational, speech therapy. VP shunt if communication with CSF causes ICP. No disease-modifying pharmacotherapy.
patients_needing_tx: ~3,000 on antiepileptic pharmacotherapy.
price: $5,000–$20,000/year for neuroprotective or regenerative therapy; orphan pricing.
registrational_endpoint: Seizure reduction and Gross Motor Function Classification at 12 months.
btd_threshold_vs_soc: ≥50% seizure reduction vs. standard AED.

symptoms: Neural tissue herniation through skull defect, neurologic deficits, hydrocephalus
treatment_goal: Surgical repair, reduce associated morbidity
first_line_soc: None (pharmacologic); surgical repair of encephalocele. Post-op management of hydrocephalus (VP shunt, acetazolamide).
treatment_algorithm: Surgical excision and skull repair. Post-op: acetazolamide or VP shunt for associated hydrocephalus. Antiepileptics for seizures. Neurodevelopmental support.
us_prevalence: ~1:10,000 live births; ~350–400 new cases/year in US.
patients_needing_tx: ~2,000–3,000 US survivors on pharmacotherapy (antiepileptics, ICP management).
price: $5,000–$20,000/year for neuroprotective or anti-fibrotic agent; orphan pricing.
registrational_endpoint: Seizure freedom and neurodevelopmental score at 24 months.
btd_threshold_vs_soc: ≥50% seizure reduction vs. standard AED alone.

symptoms: Absence of brain and skull; incompatible with life; stillbirth or death shortly after birth
treatment_goal: Palliative comfort care; no curative treatment
first_line_soc: None; anencephaly is lethal. Prenatal folic acid supplementation (400–800 mcg/day) is preventive.
treatment_algorithm: Prenatal: folic acid supplementation for neural tube defect prevention. Postnatal: comfort care only; no pharmacologic intervention can reverse the defect.
us_prevalence: ~1:1,000 pregnancies; ~1,000–1,500 affected births/year in US.
patients_needing_tx: No pharmacologic treatment pool (lethal condition). Prevention only.
price: Preventive folic acid: <$100/year.
registrational_endpoint: Neural tube defect prevention rate (reduction in anencephaly incidence) in supplementation trials.

symptoms: Hydronephrosis, renal cysts, horseshoe kidney, absent kidney, renal insufficiency
treatment_goal: Preserve renal function, prevent UTI, manage hypertension
first_line_soc: Antibiotic prophylaxis for associated VUR; antihypertensives (ACE inhibitors) for hypertension; surgical correction for UPJ obstruction.
treatment_algorithm: Antibiotic prophylaxis (TMP-SMX for VUR). ACE inhibitors for hypertension and CKD progression. Pyeloplasty for UPJ obstruction. Renal transplant for bilateral agenesis (Potter sequence - lethal) or CKD.
us_prevalence: ~1:500–1,000 births with significant renal anomaly; ~200,000–400,000 US children with known renal anomalies.
patients_needing_tx: ~100,000 on pharmacotherapy (antihypertensives, antibiotics, ACE inhibitors for CKD).
price: $2,000–$10,000/year for novel nephroprotective agent.
registrational_endpoint: eGFR preservation and blood pressure control at 24 months.
btd_threshold_vs_soc: ≥30% reduction in CKD progression rate vs. ACE inhibitors alone.

symptoms: Recurrent febrile UTIs, renal scarring, pyelonephritis, hypertension
treatment_goal: Prevent renal scarring, achieve VUR resolution
first_line_soc: Low-dose antibiotic prophylaxis (trimethoprim-sulfamethoxazole or nitrofurantoin) for high-grade VUR in young children.
treatment_algorithm: Step 1: Antibiotic prophylaxis for grade III–V VUR (TMP-SMX or nitrofurantoin). Step 2: Endoscopic injection (bulking agent DEFLUX/dextranomer/hyaluronic acid) for persistent VUR. Step 3: Ureteral reimplantation surgery for failed conservative management.
us_prevalence: ~1–3% of children; ~2 million US children with VUR (various grades); ~200,000 with significant grade III–V.
patients_needing_tx: ~200,000 on antibiotic prophylaxis; ~50,000 needing procedural/surgical intervention.
price: $500–$2,000/year for antibiotic prophylaxis; novel ureteral tone agent $3,000–$8,000/year.
registrational_endpoint: UTI rate and VUR resolution on VCUG at 12 months vs. antibiotic prophylaxis.
btd_threshold_vs_soc: ≥40% reduction in febrile UTI rate vs. antibiotic prophylaxis alone.

symptoms: Urinary incontinence, recurrent UTIs, bladder exstrophy, vesicoureteral reflux
treatment_goal: Surgical reconstruction, achieve urinary continence, protect kidneys
first_line_soc: None (pharmacologic) as primary; antibiotic prophylaxis (trimethoprim-sulfamethoxazole) for VUR; oxybutynin for neurogenic bladder components; surgical bladder reconstruction.
treatment_algorithm: Antibiotic prophylaxis for recurrent UTI. Oxybutynin/mirabegron for detrusor overactivity. Surgical: bladder exstrophy closure, augmentation cystoplasty, continent catheterizable channel.
us_prevalence: Bladder exstrophy: ~1:25,000–50,000; ~100–200 cases/year. Neurogenic bladder in pediatric population: ~100,000 US children.
patients_needing_tx: ~50,000 children on bladder pharmacotherapy (anticholinergics, antibiotic prophylaxis).
price: $2,000–$8,000/year for novel bladder modulator.
registrational_endpoint: Urinary continence rate and UTI frequency at 12 months.
btd_threshold_vs_soc: ≥25% improvement in continence rate vs. standard oxybutynin.

symptoms: Hydronephrosis, UTI, flank pain, ureteropelvic junction obstruction, duplex collecting system
treatment_goal: Prevent renal damage, achieve normal drainage, prevent recurrent UTIs
first_line_soc: Antibiotic prophylaxis (TMP-SMX) for UTI prevention; surgical: pyeloplasty (UPJ obstruction), ureteral reimplantation (megaureter).
treatment_algorithm: Antibiotic prophylaxis. Pyeloplasty for significant UPJ obstruction. Ureteral reimplantation for VUR or megaureter. Endoscopic incision for ureterocele.
us_prevalence: ~1:500–1,000 births; ~200,000–400,000 US children with known ureteral anomalies.
patients_needing_tx: ~50,000 on pharmacotherapy (antibiotic prophylaxis, antihypertensives).
price: $2,000–$8,000/year for novel ureteral tone or anti-inflammatory agent.
registrational_endpoint: UTI frequency and hydronephrosis resolution at 12 months.
btd_threshold_vs_soc: ≥40% reduction in UTI frequency vs. antibiotic prophylaxis alone.

symptoms: Absent testicle in scrotum, increased infertility risk, increased testicular cancer risk
treatment_goal: Achieve scrotal position, preserve fertility, reduce cancer risk
first_line_soc: Hormonal therapy (hCG or GnRH analog) attempted before age 6 months in some protocols but success rate ~20%; standard is surgical orchiopexy before age 18 months.
treatment_algorithm: Hormonal: hCG or GnRH (buserelin) to stimulate descent (partial efficacy). Surgical: orchiopexy (gold standard) before 18 months. No pharmacologic treatment achieves long-term scrotal position reliably.
us_prevalence: ~1–3% of full-term males; ~2,000–4,000 new cases needing treatment annually; ~200,000 boys with undescended testes in US at any time.
patients_needing_tx: ~50,000/year trialing hormonal therapy before orchiopexy.
price: $500–$2,000/course for hormonal treatment.
registrational_endpoint: Scrotal testis position rate at 6 months post-treatment.
btd_threshold_vs_soc: ≥50% successful descent vs. ~20% with current hCG/GnRH.

symptoms: Absent abdominal muscles, undescended testes, urinary tract dilation (mega-cystis, hydronephrosis)
treatment_goal: Prevent renal failure, achieve urinary continence, manage pulmonary complications
first_line_soc: Prophylactic antibiotics (TMP-SMX) for UTI prevention; surgical: orchiopexy + abdominal wall reconstruction; urologic reconstruction (vesicostomy, pyeloplasty).
treatment_algorithm: Antibiotic prophylaxis. CIC for bladder management. Orchiopexy. Abdominal wall reconstruction. Renal transplant for end-stage renal disease.
us_prevalence: ~1:30,000–40,000 births; ~100–200 new cases/year in US; ~5,000–8,000 US patients.
patients_needing_tx: ~3,000 on pharmacotherapy (antibiotics, diuretics, electrolyte management).
price: $5,000–$20,000/year; orphan pricing.
registrational_endpoint: UTI frequency and estimated GFR at 24 months.
btd_threshold_vs_soc: ≥50% reduction in UTI frequency vs. prophylactic antibiotics alone.

symptoms: Absent vagina (Mayer-Rokitansky syndrome), vaginal septum, imperforate hymen, cyclic pelvic pain
treatment_goal: Restore vaginal patency, enable menstrual outflow, achieve fertility if possible
first_line_soc: None (pharmacologic); imperforate hymen: surgical hymenectomy. MRKH: vaginal dilation (first-line) or surgical (McIndoe vaginoplasty). Transverse vaginal septum: surgical excision.
treatment_algorithm: Vaginal dilation techniques (Frank, Ingram) for MRKH. Surgical reconstruction as needed. Laparoscopic procedures for endometriosis/retrograde menstruation consequences. Hormone therapy for associated gonadal dysgenesis.
us_prevalence: MRKH: ~1:4,500 females; ~1,500–2,000 new diagnoses/year. Total vaginal anomaly prevalence: ~10,000–20,000 US women.
patients_needing_tx: ~5,000 on hormonal or adjunct pharmacotherapy.
price: $2,000–$10,000/year for tissue engineering or hormonal adjunct.
registrational_endpoint: Vaginal length/function score and quality of life (FSFI) at 12 months post-treatment.

symptoms: Obstructed urine stream, recurrent UTIs, incontinence, renal insufficiency (posterior urethral valves)
treatment_goal: Relieve obstruction, preserve renal function, achieve continence
first_line_soc: None (pharmacologic) as primary; emergency: catheter drainage. Surgical: endoscopic valve ablation (posterior urethral valves), urethroplasty.
treatment_algorithm: Bladder catheterization to relieve obstruction. Surgical valve ablation/urethroplasty. Post-op: ACE inhibitors for CKD prevention, antibiotic prophylaxis. Renal transplant for end-stage.
us_prevalence: Posterior urethral valves: ~1:5,000–8,000 males; ~300–500 new cases/year in US.
patients_needing_tx: ~3,000–5,000 US boys with PUV on pharmacotherapy (ACE-I, antibiotics).
price: $3,000–$10,000/year for renoprotective agent; orphan pricing.
registrational_endpoint: eGFR at 5 years and CKD progression rate post-ablation.
btd_threshold_vs_soc: ≥25% reduction in CKD progression to ESRD vs. standard care.

symptoms: Hypospadias, epispadias, chordee, micropenis, urethral displacement
treatment_goal: Surgical correction of urethral opening, achieve normal voiding and sexual function
first_line_soc: Topical testosterone cream pre-operatively to increase penile size and facilitate hypospadias repair; definitive surgical repair (urethroplasty).
treatment_algorithm: Pre-op: topical testosterone (off-label) for micropenis/hypospadias. Surgical: urethroplasty (hypospadias), chordee correction. Testosterone therapy for hypogonadism-associated micropenis.
us_prevalence: Hypospadias: ~1:200–250 males; ~8,000–10,000 cases/year in US.
patients_needing_tx: ~8,000/year with topical testosterone pre-op + post-op pharmacotherapy.
price: $500–$3,000/year for hormonal therapy and wound care.
registrational_endpoint: Successful urethroplasty outcome (meatal position, fistula rate) at 12 months.

symptoms: Scrotal swelling, testicular torsion, hydrocele, varicocele, absent testicle
treatment_goal: Preserve testicular viability, prevent infertility
first_line_soc: No pharmacologic first-line; hydrocele: observation or surgical hydrocelectomy. Torsion: emergency surgical detorsion within 6 hours. Varicocele: surgical or microsurgical repair for symptomatic.
treatment_algorithm: Torsion: emergency surgery. Hydrocele: observation (<2 years), hydrocelectomy if persistent. Varicocele: microsurgical repair or embolization for grade III or pain. No pharmacologic standard.
us_prevalence: Hydrocele: ~5% of male neonates; varicocele: ~15% of adolescent males; torsion incidence: ~4/100,000/year.
patients_needing_tx: Primarily surgical; pharmacologic TAM negligible.
registrational_endpoint: Testicular volume and semen analysis at 12 months post-varicocele repair.

symptoms: Leg bowing, growth failure, bone pain, dental abscesses, muscle weakness
treatment_goal: Normalize phosphate, prevent skeletal deformity, achieve normal growth
first_line_soc: Burosumab (Crysvita, anti-FGF23 monoclonal antibody) is FDA-approved first-line for X-linked hypophosphatemia (XLH) ≥6 months. Previously: oral phosphate + active vitamin D.
treatment_algorithm: Step 1: Burosumab SC every 2 weeks (pediatric) or every 4 weeks (adult) for XLH. Step 2: Oral phosphate + calcitriol for FGF23-independent forms. Orthopedic surgery for severe deformity.
us_prevalence: XLH: ~1:20,000; ~15,000–20,000 US patients with XLH.
patients_needing_tx: ~12,000 eligible for burosumab.
price: $200,000–$250,000/year (burosumab WAC ~$200,000+/year; pediatric orphan).
registrational_endpoint: Serum phosphorus normalization and Rickets Severity Score (RSS) improvement at 40 weeks vs. oral phosphate/calcitriol.
btd_threshold_vs_soc: ≥50% improvement in RSS vs. standard oral phosphate/calcitriol treatment.

symptoms: Recurrent kidney stones (cystine), flank pain, hematuria, renal insufficiency
treatment_goal: Prevent cystine stone formation, reduce recurrence
first_line_soc: High fluid intake, urinary alkalinization (potassium citrate or sodium bicarbonate). Tiopronin (alpha-mercaptopropionylglycine, Thiola) or D-penicillamine for severe/recurrent stone formers.
treatment_algorithm: Step 1: Aggressive hydration (>4 L/day) + urine alkalinization (pH >7). Step 2: Tiopronin or D-penicillamine to chelate cystine and increase solubility. Urologic intervention: ESWL, ureteroscopy, PCNL for obstructing stones.
us_prevalence: ~1:7,000; ~45,000–50,000 US patients with cystinuria.
patients_needing_tx: ~20,000 requiring active pharmacologic therapy (chelating agents + alkalinization).
price: $8,000–$25,000/year for cystine solubilizing agent; orphan pricing.
registrational_endpoint: 24-hour urine cystine excretion and stone recurrence rate at 12 months vs. placebo.
btd_threshold_vs_soc: ≥50% reduction in urine cystine concentration vs. alkalinization alone.

symptoms: Hypokalemia, metabolic alkalosis, salt wasting, muscle weakness, polyuria
treatment_goal: Correct electrolyte imbalances, maintain normal growth
first_line_soc: Potassium and magnesium supplementation; NSAIDs (indomethacin) for Bartter syndrome to reduce prostaglandin-mediated salt wasting; spironolactone or amiloride to conserve potassium.
treatment_algorithm: Potassium and magnesium supplements. Indomethacin (Bartter type): reduces polyuria, improves growth. Spironolactone/amiloride for K conservation. ACE inhibitors (off-label) in some. Gitelman: Mg + K supplementation lifelong.
us_prevalence: Bartter: ~1:1 million; ~350–500 US cases. Gitelman: ~1:40,000; ~8,000–10,000 US cases.
patients_needing_tx: ~10,000 (predominantly Gitelman) on lifelong electrolyte and NSAID/aldosterone antagonist therapy.
price: $5,000–$20,000/year for NKCC2/NCC transporter modulator; orphan pricing for Bartter.
registrational_endpoint: Serum K+ normalization (>3.5 mEq/L) and 24-hour urine Cl reduction at 12 weeks.
btd_threshold_vs_soc: Sustained serum K+ >3.5 mEq/L without supplements >50% of the time vs. standard supplementation alone.

symptoms: Short stature, skeletal dysplasia, disproportionate limbs, spinal stenosis, joint problems
treatment_goal: Increase height, prevent spinal stenosis, improve mobility
first_line_soc: Vosoritide (Voxzogo, CNP analog/FGFR3 pathway inhibitor) is FDA-approved for achondroplasia ≥2 years to increase linear growth.
treatment_algorithm: Achondroplasia: vosoritide SC daily. Growth hormone (off-label for selected SGA-associated dysplasias). Limb lengthening surgery. Spinal stenosis: surgical decompression. New: FGFR3 inhibitors (infigratinib, atrasentan in trials).
us_prevalence: Achondroplasia: ~1:15,000–40,000 births; ~20,000–30,000 US patients. All osteochondrodysplasias combined: ~50,000–100,000 US patients.
patients_needing_tx: ~20,000 with achondroplasia/hypochondroplasia eligible for vosoritide or next-gen FGFR3 inhibitor.
price: $300,000–$400,000/year (vosoritide WAC ~$320,000/year; pediatric orphan pricing).
peak_sales: ~$960M at 15% share (vosoritide current trajectory ~$200M, growing rapidly).
registrational_endpoint: Annual height velocity (cm/year) and Standing Height Z-score improvement at 52 weeks vs. placebo.
btd_threshold_vs_soc: ≥1.5 cm/year improvement in height velocity vs. placebo (ACH301/MODI4 trials showed +1.57 cm/year).

symptoms: Anterior knee pain, patellar crepitus, worsening with stairs and sitting
treatment_goal: Reduce pain, restore patellar cartilage, improve function
first_line_soc: NSAIDs (ibuprofen) for analgesia; physical therapy (VMO strengthening, patellar bracing). Intra-articular corticosteroids for refractory cases.
treatment_algorithm: NSAIDs, activity modification, quadriceps strengthening. Patellar taping/bracing. Intra-articular corticosteroids or hyaluronic acid injection. Arthroscopic cartilage smoothing for Grade III–IV.
us_prevalence: ~15–25% of adolescent athletes; ~2–4 million US adolescents affected annually.
patients_needing_tx: ~1.5 million on pharmacologic management (NSAIDs, injections).
price: $1,000–$5,000/year for intra-articular cartilage regenerative therapy.
registrational_endpoint: KOOS (Knee injury and OA Outcome Score) and VAS pain reduction at 12 weeks.
btd_threshold_vs_soc: ≥15-point improvement in KOOS vs. NSAIDs alone.

symptoms: Brittle bones, recurrent fractures, blue sclerae, hearing loss, short stature
treatment_goal: Reduce fracture frequency, increase bone density, improve mobility
first_line_soc: Bisphosphonates (pamidronate or zoledronic acid IV) increase bone mineral density and reduce fracture rate; standard of care for moderate-severe OI.
treatment_algorithm: Step 1: IV bisphosphonate therapy (pamidronate 1–3 mg/kg q3–6 months or zoledronic acid). Step 2: Surgical rodding of long bones for fracture prevention. Step 3: Growth hormone (for non-lethal OI). Investigational: anti-TGFβ antibody (fresolimumab), anti-sclerostin (setrusumab - Phase 3).
us_prevalence: ~50,000 US patients with OI; ~1:15,000 live births.
patients_needing_tx: ~30,000 on bisphosphonate or investigational pharmacotherapy.
price: $50,000–$200,000/year for setrusumab or anti-TGFβ biologic; pediatric orphan pricing.
peak_sales: ~$450M at 15% share (Ultragenyx setrusumab peak sales projections ~$500M).
registrational_endpoint: Fracture rate (fractures/year) and lumbar spine BMD Z-score at 12 months vs. bisphosphonate.
btd_threshold_vs_soc: ≥40% reduction in fracture rate vs. bisphosphonate therapy.

symptoms: Joint hypermobility, skin hyperextensibility, chronic pain, joint dislocations, bruising
treatment_goal: Manage chronic pain, prevent joint injury, maintain function
first_line_soc: NSAIDs and acetaminophen for pain; physical therapy for joint stabilization. No FDA-approved disease-modifying therapy. Vitamin C supplementation for kyphoscoliotic type.
treatment_algorithm: Pain management: NSAIDs, duloxetine, pregabalin for chronic pain. Physical therapy. Cardiac surveillance for vascular EDS (TGFβR mutations): celiprolol (beta-blocker) has shown mortality benefit in vascular EDS. Joint supports. Wound care.
us_prevalence: ~200,000–600,000 US patients with EDS (all subtypes); hypermobile EDS most common (~1:5,000).
patients_needing_tx: ~100,000 on active pharmacologic management (pain, cardiovascular).
price: $5,000–$30,000/year for collagen stabilizer or TGFβ inhibitor; orphan for rare subtypes.
registrational_endpoint: Chronic pain NRS score and BISS (Beighton score improvement) at 12 months; aortic event rate for vascular EDS.
btd_threshold_vs_soc: ≥2-point pain NRS reduction vs. placebo; or ≥50% reduction in aortic events for vascular EDS.

symptoms: Anterior knee pain below kneecap, pain with jumping and squatting
treatment_goal: Reduce inflammation, restore tendon health
first_line_soc: NSAIDs (ibuprofen); eccentric quadriceps exercises; physical therapy. Corticosteroid injection if refractory.
treatment_algorithm: Activity modification, NSAIDs, eccentric loading exercises (Alfredson protocol). Corticosteroid injection for refractory pain. PRP injection increasingly used. Surgery (debridement) for chronic cases failing all conservative measures.
us_prevalence: ~1–3% of athletes; ~500,000–1,000,000 US adolescent and young adult athletes affected.
patients_needing_tx: ~300,000 on pharmacologic management (NSAIDs, injections).
price: $1,000–$5,000/year for PRP analog or tendon regeneration therapy.
registrational_endpoint: VISA-P (Victorian Institute of Sport Assessment-Patellar) score at 12 weeks.
btd_threshold_vs_soc: ≥15-point VISA-P improvement vs. physical therapy alone.

symptoms: Tall stature, aortic dilation, lens dislocation, long limbs and fingers, mitral valve prolapse
treatment_goal: Prevent aortic dissection, slow aortic root growth
first_line_soc: Beta-blockers (atenolol, propranolol) to reduce hemodynamic stress on aorta; losartan (ARB) shown to reduce aortic growth rate.
treatment_algorithm: Step 1: Beta-blockers (atenolol) lifelong. Step 2: Losartan added for aortic dilation (COMPARE trial evidence). Surgical: aortic root replacement when root >4.5–5 cm or rapid growth >3 mm/year. Annual ophthalmic exam; lensectomy if needed.
us_prevalence: ~1:5,000; ~65,000–80,000 US patients with Marfan syndrome.
patients_needing_tx: ~60,000 on pharmacotherapy (beta-blockers, ARBs).
price: $5,000–$20,000/year for TGFβ inhibitor or novel aortopathy agent; orphan pricing applicable.
registrational_endpoint: Aortic root diameter growth rate (mm/year) and aortic event rate at 3 years.
btd_threshold_vs_soc: Aortic root growth <1 mm/year vs. ~2–3 mm/year with standard beta-blocker alone.

symptoms: Yellow skin papules, cardiovascular calcification, vision loss (angioid streaks), GI bleeding
treatment_goal: Prevent cardiovascular events, protect vision, reduce calcification
first_line_soc: No FDA-approved disease-modifying therapy; anti-VEGF injections (bevacizumab/ranibizumab) for choroidal neovascularization to preserve vision.
treatment_algorithm: Anti-VEGF (bevacizumab, ranibizumab) for CNV/vision loss. Magnesium supplementation to reduce calcification (limited evidence). Antihypertensives for cardiovascular risk. Bisphosphonates (etidronate - PseudoXanthoma Elasticum trial). Avoid antiplatelets (GI bleeding risk).
patients_needing_tx: ~5,000 on active pharmacotherapy.
price: $20,000–$100,000/year for anti-calcification agent or ABCC6 gene therapy; orphan pricing.
registrational_endpoint: Groenblad-Strandberg skin severity score and BCVA change at 24 months vs. placebo.
btd_threshold_vs_soc: ≥2-line BCVA gain or prevention of CNV development vs. standard anti-VEGF alone.

symptoms: Absent or hypoplastic nails/patella, nephropathy, glaucoma, skeletal anomalies
treatment_goal: Manage nephropathy, protect renal function, treat glaucoma
first_line_soc: ACE inhibitors (enalapril) for proteinuria/nephropathy; beta-blockers or prostaglandin analogs for glaucoma.
treatment_algorithm: ACE inhibitors for nephropathy. Glaucoma: topical IOP-lowering agents. Orthopedic: surgery for elbow/patella dysplasia. Dialysis/transplant for ESRD. No FDA-approved disease-specific therapy.
us_prevalence: ~1:50,000; ~6,000–8,000 US patients with NPS.
patients_needing_tx: ~3,000 on pharmacotherapy (ACE-I for nephropathy, glaucoma drops).
price: $5,000–$20,000/year for LMX1B-targeted or nephroprotective therapy; orphan pricing.
registrational_endpoint: Urine protein-to-creatinine ratio and eGFR at 24 months.
btd_threshold_vs_soc: ≥40% reduction in proteinuria vs. ACE inhibitor alone.

symptoms: Loose, sagging skin, cardiovascular anomalies, hernias, emphysema, developmental delay
treatment_goal: Manage systemic complications, reduce cardiovascular morbidity
first_line_soc: No pharmacologic disease-modifying therapy; management of specific complications: beta-blockers for aortic dilation, dapsone has been used for elastin restoration (limited evidence).
treatment_algorithm: Symptomatic management: beta-blockers (aortic dilation), ACE-I, hernia repair, emphysema management. Dapsone (off-label) for mild forms. Copper supplementation for Menkes-like variants. No FDA-approved disease-modifying therapy.
us_prevalence: ~500–1,000 US patients with autosomal forms of cutis laxa; extremely rare.
patients_needing_tx: ~500 on pharmacotherapy for systemic manifestations.
price: $20,000–$100,000/year for gene therapy or elastin-targeted agent; orphan pricing.
registrational_endpoint: Aortic root diameter change and pulmonary function (FEV1) at 24 months.
btd_threshold_vs_soc: N/A (no active drug approved; any improvement vs. natural history qualifies).

symptoms: Chronic productive cough, recurrent pulmonary infections, pancreatic insufficiency, malnutrition
treatment_goal: Improve lung function, prevent infections, achieve near-normal life expectancy
first_line_soc: Elexacaftor/tezacaftor/ivacaftor (Trikafta/Kaftrio) for eligible patients with at least one F508del mutation (~90% of CF patients ≥2 years); CFTR modulator triple combination therapy.
treatment_algorithm: CFTR modulators: Trikafta (elexacaftor/tezacaftor/ivacaftor) for ≥90% of patients. Pancreatic enzyme replacement (PERT). Airway clearance. Inhaled tobramycin, aztreonam for Pseudomonas. DNase (dornase alfa). Hypertonic saline. Azithromycin for anti-inflammatory effects.
us_prevalence: ~40,000 US patients with CF; ~1,000 new cases/year (newborn screening).
patients_needing_tx: ~36,000 on CFTR modulators; ~40,000 on full SoC.
peak_sales: ~$8.4B at 75% penetration (Vertex Trikafta current US revenues ~$5B+, global ~$9B).
registrational_endpoint: FEV1 % predicted improvement and sweat chloride reduction at 24 weeks vs. placebo or active control.
btd_threshold_vs_soc: ≥10% absolute improvement in FEV1 pp and ≥20 mmol/L sweat chloride reduction vs. comparator (Trikafta achieved +10% FEV1 pp vs. tezacaftor/ivacaftor).

symptoms: Sunken eyes, dry mouth, decreased urine output, lethargy, tachycardia
treatment_goal: Restore fluid and electrolyte balance
first_line_soc: Oral rehydration therapy (ORT) with WHO-recommended ORS (glucose-electrolyte solution); IV normal saline or lactated Ringer for severe dehydration.
treatment_algorithm: Mild-moderate: oral ORS (75 mEq/L Na formulation, WHO standard). Severe: IV NS or LR bolus 20 mL/kg, then deficit replacement. Ondansetron for vomiting. Anti-diarrheal agents generally avoided in children.
us_prevalence: ~1.5 million pediatric ED visits/year in US for dehydration-related illness; ~200,000 hospitalizations.
patients_needing_tx: ~1.5 million pediatric dehydration episodes requiring active rehydration treatment annually.
price: $20–$100/course for ORS or IV fluids; not a novel drug market.
tam_gross: Primarily generic/commodity market; negligible novel drug TAM.
registrational_endpoint: Dehydration score normalization and time to recovery vs. IV fluids.

symptoms: Diarrhea with fluid loss; electrolyte imbalance management in children
treatment_goal: Replace fluid and electrolytes lost through diarrhea
first_line_soc: WHO oral rehydration salts (ORS): 75 mEq/L Na, 20 mEq/L K, 75 mmol/L glucose — reduces mortality from childhood diarrhea.
treatment_algorithm: ORT with standard WHO-ORS as first-line for mild-moderate dehydration. Zinc supplementation (20 mg/day × 10–14 days) recommended by WHO as adjunct for diarrhea. Probiotics (limited evidence). IV hydration reserved for failure of ORT or severe dehydration.
us_prevalence: Pediatric diarrhea affects ~25–30 million US children per year; ORT used in ~1.5 million dehydrated cases.
patients_needing_tx: ~1.5 million; primarily managed with commodity ORS — not a novel drug market.
price: $20–$50/course for ORS.
tam_gross: Commodity market; negligible novel drug TAM.
registrational_endpoint: Stool output reduction and time to rehydration vs. standard WHO-ORS.

disease: Type 1 Diabetes Mellitus in Children and Adolescents
symptoms: Polyuria, polydipsia, weight loss, DKA, hyperglycemia
treatment_goal: Achieve near-normoglycemia, prevent DKA and hypoglycemia
first_line_soc: Multiple daily insulin injections (MDI: basal + prandial insulin analogs) or continuous subcutaneous insulin infusion (CSII/insulin pump) with continuous glucose monitoring (CGM).
treatment_algorithm: MDI or pump therapy + CGM (standard of care per ADA). Teplizumab (Tzield) to delay T1D onset (FDA-approved 2022 for Stage 2 T1D). ACE-I for nephropathy. Future: beta-cell replacement, islet transplant.
us_prevalence: ~340,000 US youth (<20 years) with T1D; ~20,000–25,000 new diagnoses/year.
patients_needing_tx: ~340,000 children on insulin; ~50,000 at Stage 2 T1D eligible for teplizumab.
price: $13,900/course (teplizumab Tzield WAC 14-day course). Insulin analogs: $3,000–$10,000/year.
peak_sales: ~$350M at 50% capture of Stage 2 T1D (teplizumab). Insulin: mature market.
registrational_endpoint: Time to Stage 3 T1D (clinical diagnosis) delay; HbA1c and C-peptide retention at 2 years.
btd_threshold_vs_soc: ≥2-year delay in T1D diagnosis vs. placebo (teplizumab showed median 2-year delay in TN10 trial).

disease: Complications of Diabetes in Children and Adolescents
symptoms: DKA, hypoglycemia, chronic microvascular/macrovascular complications, nephropathy, retinopathy
treatment_goal: Prevent DKA, achieve glycemic targets, prevent long-term complications
first_line_soc: Insulin therapy (multiple daily injections or continuous subcutaneous insulin infusion) is essential for T1D; GLP-1 RAs and SGLT2 inhibitors emerging for T2D complications in pediatric population.
treatment_algorithm: T1D: intensive insulin therapy (basal-bolus or CSII with CGM). T2D complications: metformin, GLP-1 RA (liraglutide approved ≥10 years), SGLT2i (emerging). Nephropathy: ACE inhibitors. Annual screening for retinopathy, nephropathy, neuropathy.
us_prevalence: ~340,000 US youth with T1D; ~100,000 with T2D; ~150,000 with complications requiring management.
patients_needing_tx: ~150,000 with active complications on pharmacotherapy beyond insulin.
price: $5,000–$25,000/year for novel complication-preventing biologic or advanced insulin formulation.
registrational_endpoint: HbA1c reduction and DKA hospitalization rate at 26 weeks vs. standard insulin therapy.
btd_threshold_vs_soc: ≥1% absolute HbA1c reduction vs. standard insulin regimen.

symptoms: Neonatal diabetes, MODY (mild diabetes with family history), no autoimmunity, insulin secretion defect
treatment_goal: Correct underlying defect, achieve glycemic control, avoid unnecessary insulin
first_line_soc: Sulfonylureas (glibenclamide/glyburide) for KCNJ11/ABCC8 mutations (neonatal diabetes); gliclazide/glipizide for MODY 2 and 3; dietary management for MODY 2.
treatment_algorithm: K-ATP channel mutations (NDM): sulfonylurea allows insulin discontinuation in ~90% of patients. MODY 3 (HNF1A): sulfonylureas highly effective. MODY 2 (GCK): no treatment needed typically. Gene therapy in research phase.
us_prevalence: ~100,000–150,000 US cases of MODY; ~1,000–2,000 neonatal diabetes cases.
patients_needing_tx: ~100,000 potentially misdiagnosed T2D actually MODY requiring different pharmacotherapy.
price: $5,000–$30,000/year for precision oral agent or gene therapy; orphan for some subtypes.
registrational_endpoint: HbA1c normalization and insulin independence rate at 12 months in K-ATP NDM trial.
btd_threshold_vs_soc: ≥80% insulin independence rate vs. baseline (K-ATP NDM sulfonylurea already achieves ~90%; gene therapy would need non-inferiority + durability).

disease: Type 2 Diabetes Mellitus in Children and Adolescents
symptoms: Obesity, acanthosis nigricans, hyperglycemia, polyuria, often asymptomatic initially
treatment_goal: Achieve glycemic control, prevent complications, promote weight loss
first_line_soc: Metformin + lifestyle intervention as first-line; insulin for severe hyperglycemia or DKA. Liraglutide (Victoza) FDA-approved for ≥10 years with T2D.
treatment_algorithm: Metformin + lifestyle. Liraglutide for inadequate control. Insulin for severe cases. Newer agents (semaglutide trials ongoing in pediatric T2D). Weight management: intensive lifestyle + anti-obesity agents (under study).
us_prevalence: ~100,000 US youth with T2D; incidence rising (~5,000–7,000 new pediatric T2D cases/year).
patients_needing_tx: ~80,000 on pharmacotherapy beyond lifestyle.
price: $5,000–$20,000/year for GLP-1 RA or SGLT2i in pediatric T2D.
registrational_endpoint: HbA1c reduction and BMI standard deviation score at 26 weeks vs. metformin monotherapy.
btd_threshold_vs_soc: ≥1% absolute HbA1c reduction over metformin alone + ≥0.5 BMI SDS reduction.

symptoms: Early breast development/pubic hair (<8 years girls, <9 years boys), advanced bone age, growth acceleration
treatment_goal: Preserve adult height, prevent premature epiphyseal closure, address psychosocial impact
first_line_soc: GnRH agonist (leuprolide acetate, histrelin implant) for central precocious puberty: suppresses gonadotropin axis to arrest pubertal progression.
treatment_algorithm: Central PP: GnRH agonist (leuprolide monthly IM, or histrelin 12-month implant, or nafarelin nasal spray). Peripheral PP (McCune-Albright): aromatase inhibitors (letrozole, anastrozole), ketoconazole. Treat underlying cause (CAH, tumors).
us_prevalence: ~200,000 US children with CPP requiring treatment; ~10,000 new cases/year.
patients_needing_tx: ~200,000 on GnRH agonist or other pharmacotherapy.
price: $10,000–$30,000/year for GnRH agonist (histrelin implant ~$25,000–$30,000/year).
peak_sales: ~$600M at 15% share (AbbVie Lupron Depot-PED + competitors ~$800M US market).
registrational_endpoint: Bone age advancement arrest and LH suppression at 12 months vs. untreated control.
btd_threshold_vs_soc: Bone age advancement <6 months over 12-month treatment period vs. ~12–18 months without treatment.

disease: Congenital Adrenal Hyperplasia Caused by 21-Hydroxylase Deficiency
symptoms: Adrenal insufficiency (salt-wasting), virilization in females, precocious puberty, ambiguous genitalia
treatment_goal: Replace cortisol/aldosterone, suppress excess androgens, normalize growth
first_line_soc: Hydrocortisone (10–15 mg/m²/day in 3 divided doses) + fludrocortisone for salt-wasting form. Crinecerfont (Crenessity) FDA-approved 2024 as next-generation non-steroidal CRF1 receptor antagonist for adults and pediatric ≥4 years.
treatment_algorithm: Classic CAH: hydrocortisone + fludrocortisone lifelong. Crinecerfont to reduce androgen excess with lower HC doses (reduces metabolic side effects). Stress dosing education. Surgical: clitoroplasty/vaginoplasty for virilized females.
us_prevalence: ~1:14,000; ~23,000 US patients with CAH (21-OH deficiency).
patients_needing_tx: ~20,000 on pharmacotherapy.
price: $150,000–$200,000/year (crinecerfont WAC ~$158,000/year; pediatric orphan pricing).
registrational_endpoint: Androstenedione and 17-OHP normalization and glucocorticoid dose reduction at 24 weeks vs. standard HC.
btd_threshold_vs_soc: ≥50% reduction in androstenedione with simultaneous HC dose reduction ≥25% vs. standard therapy (CAHmelia trial primary endpoints).

symptoms: Short stature, slow growth velocity, delayed bone age, low IGF-1
treatment_goal: Achieve normal adult height, normalize growth velocity
first_line_soc: Recombinant human growth hormone (somatropin, daily SC injections); lonapegsomatropin (Skytrofa) weekly long-acting GH approved for pediatric GHD.
treatment_algorithm: Daily GH injections (somatropin 0.16–0.3 mg/kg/week). Long-acting alternatives: lonapegsomatropin (weekly), somapacitan (weekly). Oral GH secretagogues under development. IGF-1 monitoring. Continue until final height achieved.
us_prevalence: ~50,000–60,000 US children with GHD on GH therapy; ~10,000 new diagnoses/year.
patients_needing_tx: ~50,000 children on GH therapy.
peak_sales: ~$400M at 20% share for next-gen weekly GH or oral secretagogue.
registrational_endpoint: Height velocity (cm/year) and height SDS improvement at 52 weeks vs. daily somatropin.
btd_threshold_vs_soc: Non-inferiority in height velocity within 1.8 cm/year margin vs. daily somatropin; monthly dosing would achieve superiority designation.

symptoms: Absent pubertal signs by age 13–14, short stature, poor growth velocity, low sex hormones
treatment_goal: Induce puberty, achieve normal height and sexual maturation
first_line_soc: Low-dose sex steroid therapy: testosterone (IM depot, patch) for boys; low-dose estrogen (oral/patch) for girls. GnRH analog stimulation testing to differentiate CDGP vs. hypogonadism.
treatment_algorithm: Constitutional delay: short course sex steroids (testosterone 50 mg IM monthly × 3–6 months) to trigger spontaneous puberty. Hypogonadotropic hypogonadism: sex steroid replacement or pulsatile GnRH. Growth hormone if GHD coexists.
us_prevalence: ~2–5% of children have delayed puberty; ~500,000–1,000,000 US cases, majority constitutional delay.
patients_needing_tx: ~100,000 on pharmacologic sex steroid or hormone therapy.
price: $2,000–$10,000/year for sex steroid or kisspeptin analog therapy.
registrational_endpoint: Pubertal staging (Tanner stage advancement) and height velocity at 12 months.
btd_threshold_vs_soc: ≥2 Tanner stages advancement vs. placebo at 12 months.

symptoms: Absent or delayed puberty, small testes, low testosterone, infertility risk, gynecomastia
treatment_goal: Induce virilization, achieve normal puberty and adult testosterone levels
first_line_soc: Testosterone replacement therapy (depot testosterone IM, testosterone gel, testosterone patch) for hypogonadotropic or hypergonadotropic hypogonadism in adolescents.
treatment_algorithm: Testosterone replacement (IM testosterone enanthate/cypionate, transdermal). GnRH analog pulsatile therapy (GnRH pump) or hCG + rFSH for fertility induction in HH. Klinefelter syndrome (47,XXY): testosterone replacement in adolescence.
us_prevalence: ~1:500 males for Klinefelter; ~3,000–5,000 US adolescent boys with significant hypogonadism requiring therapy.
patients_needing_tx: ~50,000 adolescent/young adult males on testosterone replacement (including KS and other forms).
price: $2,000–$10,000/year for testosterone replacement regimen.
peak_sales: ~$37.5M at 15% share for novel long-acting testosterone or oral testosterone analog.
registrational_endpoint: Tanner stage advancement and testosterone normalization at 12 months.
btd_threshold_vs_soc: ≥2 Tanner stages advancement and testosterone >300 ng/dL at 12 months vs. no treatment.

symptoms: Tachycardia, tremor, weight loss, heat intolerance, goiter, exophthalmos
treatment_goal: Normalize thyroid function, prevent thyroid storm and complications
first_line_soc: Methimazole (thionamide) as first-line antithyroid drug in children; propylthiouracil reserved for thyroid storm or methimazole intolerance.
treatment_algorithm: Step 1: Methimazole (0.2–0.5 mg/kg/day). Step 2: Add beta-blocker (propranolol, atenolol) for symptom control. Step 3: Radioactive iodine (I-131) for persistent/relapsed Graves. Surgery: thyroidectomy for large goiters or RAI failure.
us_prevalence: ~1:5,000 children; ~20,000–30,000 US children/adolescents with hyperthyroidism (mostly Graves disease).
patients_needing_tx: ~20,000 on antithyroid pharmacotherapy.
price: $2,000–$10,000/year for novel thyroid antibody targeting or TSHR antagonist; some orphan pricing.
registrational_endpoint: Free T4 normalization and remission rate at 12 months.
btd_threshold_vs_soc: ≥60% remission rate (no relapse 12 months after stopping) vs. ~30% with methimazole.

symptoms: Lethargy, poor feeding, constipation, prolonged jaundice, intellectual disability (untreated congenital hypothyroidism)
treatment_goal: Normalize TSH and T4, prevent neurologic damage
first_line_soc: Levothyroxine (LT4, thyroxine): 10–15 mcg/kg/day in newborns; dose adjusted by age. Titratable oral formulation.
treatment_algorithm: Congenital: LT4 initiated within 2 weeks of birth (targets TSH <5 mU/L within 1 month). Acquired: LT4 titrated to age-appropriate TSH. Generic levothyroxine is standard of care; brand consistency encouraged.
us_prevalence: Congenital hypothyroidism: ~1:2,000–4,000 newborns; ~15,000–20,000 US children on LT4.
patients_needing_tx: ~20,000 children on LT4 replacement.
price: $200–$1,000/year for LT4 (generic). Novel: long-acting T4/T3 combination ~$3,000–$8,000/year.
peak_sales: ~$15M at 15% share for novel long-acting LT4/T3 combination.
registrational_endpoint: TSH normalization and neurodevelopmental outcomes (Bayley-4 MDI) at 12 months vs. standard LT4.
btd_threshold_vs_soc: ≥5-point Bayley MDI improvement vs. standard LT4 in congenital hypothyroidism treatment trial.

disease: Congenital Adrenal Hyperplasia Caused by 11Beta-Hydroxylase Deficiency
symptoms: Virilization, hypertension, hypokalemia, precocious puberty, elevated 11-deoxycortisol
treatment_goal: Suppress androgens, control blood pressure, normalize hormones
first_line_soc: Glucocorticoid replacement (hydrocortisone) to suppress ACTH and androgen excess; antihypertensives for associated hypertension.
treatment_algorithm: Hydrocortisone (HC) 10–15 mg/m²/day for lifelong ACTH suppression. Antihypertensives (spironolactone, amlodipine) for deoxycorticosterone-mediated hypertension. Monitor: androgens, BP, bone age, growth.
us_prevalence: ~1:100,000–200,000; ~300–600 US patients with 11β-hydroxylase deficiency CAH.
patients_needing_tx: ~400 on lifelong glucocorticoid + antihypertensive therapy.
price: $20,000–$100,000/year for targeted CYP11B1-modulating or ACTH receptor antagonist; pediatric orphan pricing.
registrational_endpoint: Serum 11-deoxycortisol and androstenedione normalization at 12 months.
btd_threshold_vs_soc: Androgen normalization (DHEAS/androstenedione in normal range) in ≥50% of patients vs. standard HC.

symptoms: Enlarged thyroid at birth, hypothyroidism or hyperthyroidism, airway compression
treatment_goal: Normalize thyroid function, reduce goiter size
first_line_soc: Levothyroxine for hypothyroid congenital goiter; propylthiouracil or methimazole for hyperthyroid forms (Graves-related).
treatment_algorithm: Hypothyroid goiter: levothyroxine (LT4) replacement. Hyperthyroid: methimazole/PTU (neonatal Graves). Iodine-induced goiter: iodine restriction. Surgical: thyroidectomy for airway-compromising goiters. No pharmacologic cure for structural forms.
us_prevalence: ~1:3,000–4,000 live births; ~1,000–1,500 new cases/year in US.
patients_needing_tx: ~5,000 US children with congenital goiter on thyroid pharmacotherapy.
