price: $500–$5,000/year for thyroid hormone or antithyroid agent.
registrational_endpoint: Goiter size reduction (thyroid volume on ultrasound) and TSH normalization at 12 months.
btd_threshold_vs_soc: ≥30% reduction in thyroid volume vs. LT4 alone.

symptoms: Reduced vision in one eye, poor depth perception, failure to fix and follow
treatment_goal: Improve visual acuity in amblyopic eye, restore binocular vision
first_line_soc: Patching (occlusion therapy) of the stronger eye 2–6 hours/day; atropine (1%) penalization of the fellow eye as equally effective alternative.
treatment_algorithm: Patching or atropine penalization. Correct refractive error (spectacles). Address underlying cause (strabismus surgery, cataract extraction). Dichoptic therapies (binocular video game approaches) under investigation.
us_prevalence: ~3% of children; ~2–3 million US children with amblyopia.
patients_needing_tx: ~800,000 on active pharmacologic therapy (atropine penalization).
price: $200–$1,000/year for atropine eye drops; novel neuromodulator $5,000–$20,000/year.
peak_sales: ~$192M at 15% share for novel pharmacologic approach (citicoline, levodopa, or neuromodulator).
registrational_endpoint: LogMAR visual acuity improvement in amblyopic eye at 16 weeks vs. patching alone.
btd_threshold_vs_soc: ≥3 lines LogMAR visual acuity improvement vs. patching/atropine alone.

symptoms: Eye misalignment (esotropia, exotropia), diplopia, suppression, head turn
treatment_goal: Align eyes, restore binocularity, prevent amblyopia
first_line_soc: Corrective glasses for accommodative esotropia; botulinum toxin (onabotulinumtoxinA, Botox) injection as non-surgical alignment treatment; definitive: strabismus surgery.
treatment_algorithm: Glasses correction. Botulinum toxin (Botox) for acute comitant or infantile esotropia (alternative to surgery). Prism glasses. Surgery: recession/resection of extraocular muscles. Patching for associated amblyopia.
us_prevalence: ~3–4% of children; ~2–3 million US children with strabismus.
patients_needing_tx: ~200,000 receiving botulinum toxin treatment; ~400,000 surgical patients.
price: $500–$3,000/injection for BTX-A.
peak_sales: ~$45M at 15% share for dedicated ophthalmic BTX-A formulation.
registrational_endpoint: Prism cover test (PCT) alignment within 10 prism diopters and stereoacuity at 6 months.
btd_threshold_vs_soc: ≥70% alignment success rate vs. ~50% with standard BTX-A dose.

symptoms: Photophobia, buphthalmos (enlarged eyes), tearing, corneal edema, elevated IOP
treatment_goal: Lower IOP, prevent optic nerve damage, preserve vision
first_line_soc: Surgical (angle surgery: goniotomy or trabeculotomy) is definitive first-line; topical IOP-lowering agents (timolol, dorzolamide, brimonidine) used as bridge or adjuncts.
treatment_algorithm: Primary: angle surgery (goniotomy/trabeculotomy). If failed: combined trabeculotomy-trabeculectomy or tube shunt (Ahmed). Pharmacologic: timolol + dorzolamide + brimonidine (avoid brimonidine <2 years CNS risk). Long-term monitoring.
us_prevalence: ~1:10,000–20,000 births; ~200–400 new cases/year in US.
patients_needing_tx: ~3,000–5,000 US children with infantile/childhood glaucoma on pharmacologic IOP management.
price: $2,000–$8,000/year for novel pediatric-safe IOP-lowering drug; orphan pricing.
registrational_endpoint: IOP reduction to <21 mmHg and optic nerve preservation at 12 months.
btd_threshold_vs_soc: ≥30% IOP reduction vs. standard topical timolol/dorzolamide.

symptoms: Abnormal retinal vessel growth in premature infants, risk of retinal detachment and blindness
treatment_goal: Prevent retinal detachment, preserve vision
first_line_soc: Intravitreal bevacizumab (anti-VEGF, off-label) or ranibizumab (Byooviz/Lucentis, emerging approval); laser photocoagulation for type 1 ROP.
treatment_algorithm: Type 1 ROP: intravitreal anti-VEGF (bevacizumab, ranibizumab) or laser photocoagulation. Type 2/pre-threshold: observation. Retinal detachment: surgical vitreoretinal intervention.
us_prevalence: ~14,000–16,000 premature infants/year develop ROP in US; ~1,500 require treatment.
patients_needing_tx: ~1,500 requiring intravitreal anti-VEGF or laser treatment/year.
price: $2,000–$8,000/injection; 1–2 injections typically; novel sustained-release system $5,000–$15,000/episode.
peak_sales: ~$3M at 25% share for novel ROP-specific formulation.
registrational_endpoint: Plus disease resolution and retinal attachment rate at 24 weeks vs. laser photocoagulation.
btd_threshold_vs_soc: Non-inferiority to bevacizumab (BEAT-ROP trial: 42% failure for bevacizumab vs. 60% for laser in Zone I).

symptoms: White/hazy lens, absent red reflex, nystagmus, strabismus, visual deprivation amblyopia
treatment_goal: Restore clear optical axis, prevent irreversible amblyopia
first_line_soc: Surgical cataract extraction (lens aspiration) within weeks of birth + optical rehabilitation (aphakic contact lens or IOL) + patching for amblyopia prevention.
treatment_algorithm: Early surgical removal (within 6–10 weeks for dense congenital cataract). Optical rehabilitation: contact lenses or IOL. Patching/atropine penalization for amblyopia. Pilocarpine for partial cataracts (investigational).
us_prevalence: ~1:2,500–5,000 newborns; ~700–1,500 new cases/year in US.
patients_needing_tx: ~5,000–8,000 US children with congenital cataract on ongoing pharmacologic amblyopia treatment.
price: $500–$3,000/year for pharmacologic adjunct.
registrational_endpoint: Visual acuity at 4.5 years of age (primary endpoint in IATS trial).
btd_threshold_vs_soc: ≥0.3 logMAR (3-line) improvement in BSCVA at 4.5 years vs. standard patching.

symptoms: Regurgitation, arching back (Sandifer syndrome), irritability, poor weight gain
treatment_goal: Relieve symptoms, promote normal growth
first_line_soc: Positioning, feeding modification (thickened feeds, smaller volumes) as first-line. Famotidine (H2RA) or omeprazole (PPI) for GERD with esophagitis or failure to thrive.
treatment_algorithm: Conservative: smaller frequent feeds, thickened formula, positioning. Pharmacologic: H2RA (ranitidine withdrawn; famotidine) or PPI (omeprazole, esomeprazole) for GERD with complications. Surgical: Nissen fundoplication for severe refractory GERD.
us_prevalence: ~50–67% of all infants have regurgitation; ~10–20% have symptomatic GERD requiring treatment (~1 million infants/year).
patients_needing_tx: ~400,000 infants on pharmacologic therapy (PPIs, H2RAs).
price: $500–$3,000/year for novel GERD therapy in infants.
registrational_endpoint: GERD symptom score (GERD-HRQL or I-GERQ-R) and regurgitation frequency at 4 weeks vs. placebo.
btd_threshold_vs_soc: ≥2-point I-GERQ-R reduction vs. placebo; better vs. ranitidine safety profile.

symptoms: Abdominal distension, bloody stools, pneumatosis intestinalis, sepsis in premature infants
treatment_goal: Prevent intestinal necrosis and perforation, reduce mortality
first_line_soc: IV antibiotics (ampicillin + gentamicin + metronidazole) + bowel rest + TPN; surgical: peritoneal drain or laparotomy/resection for perforation.
treatment_algorithm: Medical: NPO, NG decompression, antibiotics, TPN. Surgical: peritoneal drain or laparotomy for Bell stage III. Prevention: probiotics (Lactobacillus GG), breast milk feeding, antenatal steroids, lactoferrin.
us_prevalence: ~1–3/1,000 live births; predominantly premature infants; ~5,000–10,000 cases/year in US NICU.
patients_needing_tx: ~7,000 cases/year requiring pharmacologic management; preventive probiotics applicable to ~100,000 VLBW infants/year.
price: $5,000–$20,000/course for novel NEC prevention/treatment agent; orphan pricing for VLBW indication.
registrational_endpoint: NEC staging (Bell criteria) and surgical intervention rate at 14 days; mortality at 30 days.
btd_threshold_vs_soc: ≥40% reduction in surgical NEC or death vs. standard antibiotics + TPN.

symptoms: Prolonged jaundice, pale stools, dark urine, hepatomegaly, coagulopathy
treatment_goal: Identify treatable cause, prevent cirrhosis, support nutrition
first_line_soc: Ursodiol (ursodeoxycholic acid) for cholestasis of various etiologies; fat-soluble vitamin supplementation; treat underlying cause (Kasai for BA, thyroid/metabolic correction).
treatment_algorithm: Ursodiol 10–20 mg/kg/day. Fat-soluble vitamins (ADEK). Treat underlying cause. Kasai for biliary atresia. Medium-chain triglyceride formula. Liver transplant for end-stage.
us_prevalence: ~1:2,500 neonates; ~1,500–2,000 cases/year; ~5,000 US infants with active neonatal cholestasis.
patients_needing_tx: ~4,000 on pharmacologic management (ursodiol, vitamins).
price: $5,000–$30,000/year for novel NTCP inhibitor or FXR agonist; orphan pricing.
registrational_endpoint: GGT and serum bilirubin normalization at 12 months; transplant-free survival.
btd_threshold_vs_soc: ≥30% reduction in serum total bilirubin and ≥50% reduction in GGT vs. ursodiol alone.

symptoms: Variable: intestinal obstruction, bowel perforation, necrotizing enterocolitis, volvulus
treatment_goal: Emergency surgical correction, prevent sepsis and multi-organ failure
first_line_soc: IV antibiotics (ampicillin + gentamicin) for sepsis; surgical intervention per specific diagnosis.
treatment_algorithm: Empirical antibiotics pre-op. Surgical: bowel resection, ostomy formation, or primary repair. TPN post-op. No pharmacologic disease-specific standard.
us_prevalence: Heterogeneous group; ~5,000–10,000 neonatal surgical emergencies/year in US.
patients_needing_tx: ~10,000/year requiring perioperative pharmacotherapy.
price: $1,000–$5,000/course for antibiotic or anti-inflammatory adjunct.
registrational_endpoint: 30-day survival and bowel function recovery after neonatal surgery.

symptoms: Projectile non-bilious vomiting, olive-shaped mass, hypochloremic metabolic alkalosis
treatment_goal: Relieve gastric outlet obstruction, correct electrolyte imbalance
first_line_soc: IV fluid resuscitation and electrolyte correction followed by surgical pyloromyotomy (Ramstedt procedure) — definitive treatment.
treatment_algorithm: Pre-op: IV NS with KCl to correct hypochloremic alkalosis. Surgery: laparoscopic or open pyloromyotomy. Post-op: early oral feeding protocol. No pharmacologic alternative to surgery.
us_prevalence: ~2–5:1,000 live births; ~6,000–12,000 new cases/year in US.
patients_needing_tx: Primarily surgical; pharmacologic TAM negligible.
registrational_endpoint: Tolerance of full oral feeds and weight gain at 30 days post-pyloromyotomy.

symptoms: Intermittent colicky abdominal pain, currant jelly stool, palpable mass, bilious vomiting
treatment_goal: Reduce intussusception, prevent bowel necrosis and perforation
first_line_soc: Pneumatic or hydrostatic enema reduction (non-operative) is first-line treatment.
treatment_algorithm: Enema reduction (air or barium/saline under fluoroscopic/US guidance) — 80–90% success. Surgical reduction (open or laparoscopic) for failed enema or peritonitis. No pharmacologic treatment.
us_prevalence: ~2:1,000 children; ~2,000–4,000 cases/year in US (peak 5–10 months old).
patients_needing_tx: Primarily procedural; negligible pharmacologic market.
registrational_endpoint: Enema reduction success rate and recurrence at 48 hours.

symptoms: Intestinal obstruction in newborn, failure to pass meconium, distended abdomen, cystic fibrosis association
treatment_goal: Relieve obstruction, prevent perforation, identify and treat underlying CF
first_line_soc: Gastrografin enema (diatrizoate meglumine) for uncomplicated meconium ileus; surgical: Bishop-Koop ileostomy or primary anastomosis for complicated cases.
treatment_algorithm: Uncomplicated: Gastrografin enema under fluoroscopy. Complicated (volvulus, perforation): surgical intervention. All: test for CF (sweat chloride, CFTR genotyping). Begin CFTR modulator therapy (Trikafta) for eligible mutations.
us_prevalence: ~15–20% of CF patients (~6,000–8,000 CF births) present with meconium ileus; ~800–1,000 US cases/year.
patients_needing_tx: ~800–1,000 neonates/year; ongoing CF management thereafter.
price: $300,000+/year for Trikafta (see Cystic Fibrosis entry).
registrational_endpoint: Resolution of obstruction and meconium passage within 48 hours of treatment.

symptoms: Delayed passage of meconium, transient neonatal intestinal obstruction, abdominal distension
treatment_goal: Clear meconium plug, exclude Hirschsprung disease
first_line_soc: Gastrografin or normal saline enema for initial treatment; rectal stimulation.
treatment_algorithm: Contrast enema (Gastrografin or saline). Rectal glycerin suppository. Rectal biopsy to exclude Hirschsprung. N-acetylcysteine enema may be used. No chronic pharmacologic treatment needed in most cases.
us_prevalence: ~1:500–1,000 newborns; ~3,000–7,000 cases/year in US; usually benign and transient.
patients_needing_tx: Acute treatment only; negligible chronic pharmacologic market.
registrational_endpoint: Passage of meconium within 24 hours of treatment.

disease: Growth and Weight Faltering in Children
symptoms: Weight and/or height dropping across ≥2 percentile lines; poor appetite, developmental delay, irritability
treatment_goal: Restore adequate growth, identify and treat underlying cause
first_line_soc: High-calorie dietary supplementation; treat underlying conditions (GERD: PPI; celiac: gluten-free diet; hypothyroidism: levothyroxine). Appetite stimulants (cyproheptadine off-label) for poor appetite.
treatment_algorithm: Nutritional rehabilitation: high-calorie, high-protein diet; nasogastric tube feeds if needed. Treat etiology: GERD (PPI), celiac (GFD), CF (CFTR modulator), IBD (steroids/biologics), endocrine (hormones), psychosocial causes (behavioral feeding therapy). Appetite stimulants: cyproheptadine 0.25 mg/kg/day. GH for organic GH deficiency. Zinc supplementation in deficiency.
us_prevalence: ~5–10% of US children under 3 years; ~500,000–1 million children with significant growth faltering.
patients_needing_tx: ~400,000 with moderate-severe growth faltering requiring pharmacotherapy beyond nutrition.
price: $3,000–$10,000/year for novel growth-promoting or appetite-stimulating agent.
registrational_endpoint: Weight-for-age Z-score improvement and weight velocity (g/day) at 12 weeks.
btd_threshold_vs_soc: ≥0.5 Z-score improvement in weight-for-age vs. nutritional counseling alone at 12 weeks.

symptoms: BMI ≥95th percentile; metabolic complications, sleep apnea, orthopedic issues, poor self-esteem
treatment_goal: Achieve sustained weight loss, reduce cardiometabolic risk
first_line_soc: Intensive health behavior and lifestyle therapy (IHBLT). Pharmacotherapy: orlistat (FDA-approved ≥12 years); semaglutide 2.4 mg SC weekly (Wegovy) FDA-approved for adolescents ≥12 years with obesity (2022).
treatment_algorithm: Step 1: IHBLT (diet, physical activity, behavioral modification). Step 2: Pharmacotherapy adjunct to IHBLT: semaglutide 2.4 mg/week (approved ≥12y, 2022) or orlistat (approved ≥12y). Step 3: Bariatric surgery for severe obesity ≥13–14 years (AAP 2023 guidelines support earlier intervention). Metformin for insulin resistance/prediabetes.
us_prevalence: ~20% of US adolescents (12–19) have obesity; ~5 million US adolescents. ~6% have severe obesity (BMI ≥120% of 95th percentile).
patients_needing_tx: ~2–3 million adolescents with moderate-severe obesity warranting pharmacotherapy.
price: $12,000–$20,000/year (semaglutide/Wegovy WAC ~$16,000/year; pediatric pricing).
peak_sales: ~$4B at 10% share for novel GLP-1/GIP agent in adolescents. 2.5M × $16,000 × 10% = $4B.
registrational_endpoint: Change from baseline BMI percentile or BMI Z-score at 68 weeks vs. placebo (STEP TEENS trial design).
btd_threshold_vs_soc: ≥15% body weight reduction vs. <3% for placebo (semaglutide achieved ~16% vs. +0.6% placebo in STEP TEENS).

symptoms: Depression, anxiety, behavioral problems; impaired functioning; suicidality risk
treatment_goal: Achieve remission, restore functioning, prevent suicide
first_line_soc: Psychotherapy (CBT) ± SSRIs for depression/anxiety; antipsychotics for psychosis; mood stabilizers for bipolar. See specific disorder entries.
treatment_algorithm: Umbrella category encompassing depression, anxiety, bipolar, ADHD, conduct disorder, OCD, PTSD. See individual disease entries for specific pharmacotherapy. Overall approach: integrated behavioral health screening + evidence-based treatment.
us_prevalence: ~50% of mental health disorders begin by age 14; ~20–25% of US adolescents have a mental health condition. ~10–15 million adolescents affected.
patients_needing_tx: ~5–7 million adolescents on pharmacotherapy for mental health conditions.
price: Varies by specific condition; see individual entries.
tam_gross: Covered under specific disorder entries. Combined adolescent mental health pharmacotherapy market >$20B.
registrational_endpoint: See specific disorder entries.
btd_threshold_vs_soc: N/A (umbrella category).

symptoms: Alcohol, cannabis, opioid, stimulant, or nicotine use; impaired functioning, withdrawal, craving
treatment_goal: Achieve abstinence or harm reduction, treat withdrawal, prevent relapse
first_line_soc: Nicotine replacement therapy (patches, gum) for adolescent tobacco use disorder. Buprenorphine/naloxone for opioid use disorder ≥16 years. Naltrexone for alcohol use disorder (off-label in adolescents). No FDA-approved pharmacotherapy specifically for adolescent cannabis use disorder.
treatment_algorithm: Opioid use disorder: buprenorphine/naloxone (recommended ≥16 years; evidence in adolescents 12–17). Alcohol: naltrexone ± brief motivational intervention (BMI). Nicotine: NRT (patches) + behavioral support; varenicline limited data in adolescents. Cannabis: behavioral therapy (CBT, motivational enhancement); no proven pharmacotherapy. Stimulants: no proven pharmacotherapy. Integrated SUD treatment with mental health.
us_prevalence: ~5 million US adolescents (12–17) have a substance use disorder; alcohol (~1.7M), cannabis (~2M), illicit drugs (~1M).
patients_needing_tx: ~500,000 adolescents with moderate-severe SUD seeking pharmacotherapy.
price: $3,000–$15,000/year for novel SUD pharmacotherapy (kappa opioid antagonist, cannabidiol, novel NRT).
registrational_endpoint: Abstinence rate and time to relapse at 12 weeks; urine toxicology outcomes.
btd_threshold_vs_soc: ≥40% abstinence rate at 12 weeks vs. <20% with behavioral therapy alone.

symptoms: Risk of unintended pregnancy; need for contraceptive counseling; adolescent-specific health risks in pregnancy
treatment_goal: Prevent unintended pregnancy, ensure safe delivery if pregnant
first_line_soc: Long-acting reversible contraceptives (LARCs: IUD, implant) are most effective per AAP. Oral contraceptive pills (combined estrogen-progestin or progestin-only). Emergency contraception (levonorgestrel, ulipristal acetate) for acute use.
treatment_algorithm: Tier 1 (most effective): LARCs (hormonal/copper IUD, subdermal implant). Tier 2: Injectable progestin (Depo-Provera), oral pills. Tier 3: Barrier methods. Emergency: levonorgestrel Plan B or ulipristal acetate ella within 72–120 hours. Prenatal care for pregnant adolescents: folate, iron, prenatal vitamins.
us_prevalence: ~150,000 US adolescent pregnancies/year (2023 CDC); ~3 million US sexually active adolescent females needing effective contraception.
patients_needing_tx: ~3 million adolescents actively using or needing hormonal contraception.
price: $100–$1,000/year for OCP/injectable; $1,500–$3,000 one-time for LARC.
registrational_endpoint: Pearl Index (pregnancy rate per 100 woman-years) at 12 months.
btd_threshold_vs_soc: Pearl Index <1 vs. ~3 for typical OCP use in adolescents.

symptoms: Gender dysphoria, identity exploration; distress from incongruence between gender identity and sex assigned at birth
treatment_goal: Alleviate gender dysphoria, support wellbeing and identity
first_line_soc: GnRH agonists (leuprolide, histrelin) as puberty blockers for gender dysphoria; gender-affirming hormone therapy (testosterone for trans males; estradiol for trans females) starting mid-puberty with appropriate consent.
treatment_algorithm: Step 1: Mental health evaluation and support. Step 2: GnRH agonist (puberty blocker) at Tanner stage 2–3 to pause natal puberty (reversible). Step 3: Gender-affirming hormones (testosterone or estradiol) for adolescents. Step 4: Surgical interventions in selected adolescents ≥16–18. Mental health support throughout.
us_prevalence: ~1–2% of US adolescents identify as transgender; ~300,000–500,000 US youth. ~50,000–100,000 seeking gender-affirming medical care.
patients_needing_tx: ~50,000–100,000 US adolescents on GnRH agonists or gender-affirming hormones.
price: $15,000–$30,000/year for GnRH agonist implant; $1,000–$5,000/year for hormone therapy.
peak_sales: ~$300M at 20% share for improved GnRH analog or gender-affirming hormone formulation.
registrational_endpoint: Gender Dysphoria Severity score (Utrecht GDS) and wellbeing measures at 12 months.
btd_threshold_vs_soc: ≥30% reduction in gender dysphoria score and improved psychological wellbeing vs. untreated controls.

symptoms: Identity formation challenges, peer pressure, risk-taking behavior, emotional dysregulation
treatment_goal: Support healthy development, prevent risky behaviors and mental health disorders
first_line_soc: None (pharmacologic); primary care counseling, psychotherapy, and social support are primary. Treat emerging mental health conditions (see specific entries).
treatment_algorithm: Anticipatory guidance and developmental counseling at each well-child visit. Screen for depression (PHQ-A), substance use (CRAFFT), and anxiety. Psychotherapy (CBT, motivational interviewing) for at-risk adolescents. No pharmacologic intervention for normal psychosocial development challenges.
us_prevalence: All ~40 million US adolescents (10–19) are in psychosocial development; ~20% have clinically identifiable mental health concerns.
patients_needing_tx: No direct pharmacologic market for normal psychosocial development.
price: N/A.
registrational_endpoint: N/A.
btd_threshold_vs_soc: N/A.

disease: Health Supervision of the Well Child
symptoms: Routine health maintenance; immunizations, screening, developmental surveillance, anticipatory guidance
treatment_goal: Prevent illness, detect early health problems, optimize healthy development
first_line_soc: Childhood vaccination schedule (MMR, DTaP, IPV, Hib, PCV, Varicella, Hep A/B, influenza, HPV, etc.) per AAP/ACIP. Fluoride supplementation for dental caries prevention.
treatment_algorithm: Well-child visits: birth, 2 weeks, 1, 2, 4, 6, 9, 12, 15, 18, 24, 30 months; annual thereafter. Vaccines per ACIP schedule. Developmental screening. Vision and hearing screening. Blood pressure monitoring. Anemia and lead screening. Obesity surveillance. Fluoride varnish applications.
us_prevalence: All ~74 million US children require well-child health supervision.
patients_needing_tx: Preventive pharmacotherapy (vaccines, fluoride) covers all children; pediatric vaccine market ~$4–6B/year in US.
price: Novel vaccines: $50–$300/dose; novel preventive agents: variable.
tam_gross: 74,000,000 children × applicable vaccine/preventive cost = large market (covered by specific vaccine entries).
registrational_endpoint: N/A (preventive care category).
btd_threshold_vs_soc: N/A.

disease: Screening Tests for Infants, Children, and Adolescents
symptoms: N/A (diagnostic/preventive category); early detection of conditions before symptoms develop
treatment_goal: Identify conditions early to enable timely intervention
first_line_soc: None (pharmacologic); newborn screening (NBS) panel identifies metabolic/genetic diseases for early treatment. Developmental screening tools (ASQ, M-CHAT). Vision, hearing, anemia, lead, cholesterol, STI screenings per age.
treatment_algorithm: Newborn screening: 34–50+ conditions per state. Developmental: ASQ at 9, 18, 30 months; autism screen (M-CHAT) at 18, 24 months. Blood pressure screening annually ≥3 years. Vision screen at 3–5 years. STI screen for sexually active adolescents. Cervical cancer screen (Pap/HPV) starting age 21 (21-25 if HPV+ history).
us_prevalence: All US children receive newborn screening; ~4 million births/year.
patients_needing_tx: No direct pharmacologic market (diagnostic/screening category); treatment follows identified conditions.
price: N/A.
registrational_endpoint: N/A.
btd_threshold_vs_soc: N/A.

symptoms: Normal developmental milestone; delays, regression, or resistance to toilet training; functional incontinence
treatment_goal: Achieve independent toileting; manage training difficulties
first_line_soc: None (pharmacologic); child-oriented behavioral approach (readiness cues, positive reinforcement). Treat constipation (PEG 3350) if present.
treatment_algorithm: Non-pharmacologic: child-directed approach; parent education. If constipation contributing: PEG 3350 maintenance. If encopresis: disimpaction + maintenance PEG + behavioral training. If daytime wetting post-training: bladder training, scheduled voiding. Pharmacologic only for specific complications (see Stool Incontinence and Urinary Incontinence entries).
us_prevalence: Normal developmental milestone for all children typically 18–36 months.
patients_needing_tx: No direct pharmacologic market for toilet training itself; ~500,000/year with pathologic constipation complicating training.
price: N/A (see constipation entries).
registrational_endpoint: N/A.
btd_threshold_vs_soc: N/A.

symptoms: Recurrent fever attacks, peritonitis, pleuritis, joint pain, AA amyloidosis
treatment_goal: Prevent fever attacks and amyloidosis
first_line_soc: Colchicine (0.5–2 mg/day) is the established first-line standard of care; reduces attack frequency and prevents AA amyloidosis.
treatment_algorithm: Step 1: Colchicine lifelong (90% response). Step 2: IL-1 inhibitors (canakinumab or anakinra) for colchicine-resistant FMF. Symptomatic: NSAIDs during attacks.
us_prevalence: ~100,000–200,000 US patients of Mediterranean/Middle Eastern descent with FMF.
patients_needing_tx: ~120,000 requiring lifelong colchicine or biologic therapy.
price: $200–$2,000/year for colchicine; canakinumab $100,000+/year for refractory cases.
peak_sales: ~$93M at 15% share for novel oral IL-1/IL-18 pathway inhibitor.
registrational_endpoint: FMF attack frequency reduction at 12 months vs. placebo (in colchicine-resistant patients).
btd_threshold_vs_soc: ≥50% reduction in attack frequency vs. placebo in colchicine-resistant FMF (canakinumab CLUSTER trial: 71% attack-free at 16 months).

symptoms: Periodic fevers every 3–6 weeks, aphthous stomatitis, pharyngitis, adenitis
treatment_goal: Prevent febrile episodes, achieve remission
first_line_soc: Single-dose corticosteroid (prednisone or prednisolone 1–2 mg/kg) aborts attacks acutely; cimetidine (off-label) for prevention in some patients.
treatment_algorithm: Acute: corticosteroid (prednisone) aborts episode within hours. Prevention: cimetidine or colchicine (limited evidence). Tonsillectomy: resolves PFAPA in ~60–70%. NSAIDs and antipyretics for symptom management.
us_prevalence: ~100,000–200,000 US children with PFAPA (most common periodic fever syndrome in children).
patients_needing_tx: ~150,000 requiring pharmacologic management for attack prevention or resolution.
price: $500–$5,000/year for colchicine or novel IL-1 inhibitor.
peak_sales: ~$56M at 15% share for novel prevention agent.
registrational_endpoint: Monthly fever-free rate and attack frequency at 12 months vs. placebo.
btd_threshold_vs_soc: ≥50% reduction in fever episode frequency vs. placebo.

symptoms: Recurrent urticaria-like rash, fever, joint pain, hearing loss (NOMID), amyloidosis
treatment_goal: Suppress IL-1β-mediated inflammation, prevent organ damage
first_line_soc: Canakinumab (Ilaris, IL-1β monoclonal antibody) SC every 8 weeks; rilonacept (Arcalyst, IL-1 trap) or anakinra alternatives; FDA-approved for CAPS.
treatment_algorithm: Canakinumab (most used: 150 mg SC q8w for adults; weight-based in children). Rilonacept weekly SC. Anakinra daily SC (less preferred due to daily injections). Regular monitoring: hearing, eyes, CNS.
us_prevalence: ~1,000–2,000 US patients with CAPS (FCAS, MWS, NOMID combined); ultra-rare.
patients_needing_tx: ~1,000 requiring IL-1 inhibitor therapy.
price: $150,000–$250,000/year (canakinumab ~$200,000+/year; pediatric orphan pricing).
peak_sales: ~$40M at 20% share (Novartis Ilaris CAPS revenues ~$100M globally).
registrational_endpoint: CAPS Disease Activity Score (CDAS) reduction and biomarker (SAA, CRP) normalization at 8 weeks.
btd_threshold_vs_soc: ≥70% reduction in CDAS vs. placebo (canakinumab achieved >90% complete/near-complete response in CAPS trials).

symptoms: Prolonged fever attacks (1–3 weeks), periorbital edema, migratory skin rash, myalgia
treatment_goal: Reduce attack frequency, prevent AA amyloidosis
first_line_soc: Canakinumab (Ilaris) FDA-approved for TRAPS; etanercept (TNF inhibitor) used off-label (less effective long-term).
treatment_algorithm: Step 1: Canakinumab 150 mg q8w SC. Step 2: Anakinra daily SC for milder cases. NSAIDs/steroids for acute attacks. Monitor: SAA for amyloid prevention.
us_prevalence: ~500–1,000 US patients with TRAPS; rare autosomal dominant autoinflammatory disease.
patients_needing_tx: ~700 on pharmacologic therapy.
price: $150,000–$250,000/year (canakinumab ~$200,000/year; orphan).
registrational_endpoint: TRAPS Disease Activity Score reduction and SAA normalization at 16 weeks.
btd_threshold_vs_soc: ≥50% reduction in TRAPS activity score vs. placebo (canakinumab CLUSTER trial showed >80% response).

symptoms: Recurrent fever, lymphadenopathy, abdominal pain, elevated serum IgD, Dutch ancestry
treatment_goal: Reduce attack frequency and severity
first_line_soc: No universally effective first-line pharmacologic therapy; NSAIDs for attack management. Anakinra (IL-1 receptor antagonist) shows benefit in some patients.
treatment_algorithm: NSAIDs during attacks. Anakinra for attack prevention in refractory cases. Canakinumab or etanercept used off-label. Simvastatin (MVK pathway: reduces mevalonate) for HIDS (MVK mutations) with limited benefit.
us_prevalence: ~200–400 US cases of HIDS (MVK deficiency); ultra-rare.
patients_needing_tx: ~300 requiring pharmacologic management.
price: $50,000–$150,000/year for IL-1 inhibitor; pediatric orphan pricing.
registrational_endpoint: Attack frequency (episodes/year) and attack severity score at 12 months vs. placebo.
btd_threshold_vs_soc: ≥50% reduction in attack frequency vs. placebo.

symptoms: Pyogenic arthritis, pyoderma gangrenosum, acne, skin ulcers, joint destruction
treatment_goal: Suppress IL-1/TNF-mediated inflammation, prevent joint destruction
first_line_soc: Corticosteroids (systemic) for acute flares; anakinra (IL-1 receptor antagonist) shows benefit in PAPA syndrome.
treatment_algorithm: Corticosteroids acutely. Anakinra for joint arthritis. Canakinumab for refractory IL-1 disease. TNF inhibitors (infliximab, adalimumab) for skin manifestations. Wound care for PG ulcers.
us_prevalence: Fewer than 200 reported cases worldwide; <100 US cases. Ultra-rare.
patients_needing_tx: ~100 US patients requiring pharmacologic management.
price: $50,000–$200,000/year for IL-1 or IL-36 inhibitor; pediatric orphan pricing.
registrational_endpoint: Joint inflammation score and PG wound healing at 24 weeks vs. placebo.
btd_threshold_vs_soc: ≥2 ACR improvement criteria and ≥50% PG wound size reduction vs. placebo.

symptoms: HIV infection: immunosuppression, opportunistic infections, failure to thrive
treatment_goal: Achieve viral suppression, restore immune function, prevent AIDS-defining illness
first_line_soc: WHO-recommended pediatric ART: 2 NRTIs (abacavir + lamivudine or tenofovir + emtricitabine) + 1 INSTI (dolutegravir, preferred for children ≥4 weeks) or NNRTI.
treatment_algorithm: 2-NRTI + INSTI (dolutegravir-based regimen, now standard across all ages per WHO 2023 guidelines). Pediatric formulations: dispersible tablets (DTG 10 mg), granules. Prophylaxis: TMP-SMX for PCP. Adherence support. Switch to long-acting injectables (cabotegravir + rilpivirine for adolescents ≥12 years).
us_prevalence: ~4,600 US children <13 years with HIV; ~32,000 US adolescents 13–24 years with HIV.
patients_needing_tx: ~36,000 US children/adolescents requiring ART.
price: $15,000–$40,000/year for pediatric ART (branded regimens).
peak_sales: ~$135M at 15% share for novel pediatric long-acting formulation.
registrational_endpoint: HIV RNA <50 copies/mL at 48 weeks and CD4 count normalization.
btd_threshold_vs_soc: ≥85% viral suppression rate equivalent to or exceeding current DTG-based regimens.

disease: Human Immunodeficiency Virus (HIV) Infection in Infants and Children
symptoms: Failure to thrive, recurrent infections, lymphadenopathy, developmental delay, oral candidiasis
treatment_goal: Achieve viral suppression, normalize immune function, prevent mortality
first_line_soc: Immediate ART (dolutegravir-based 2-NRTI + INSTI regimen) for all HIV-infected infants and children, regardless of CD4 count.
treatment_algorithm: Immediate ART (WHO 2023: DTG 10 mg tablet or lopinavir/ritonavir for infants < 4 weeks). Prevention of MTCT: maternal ART + infant NVP prophylaxis × 6 weeks. Co-trimoxazole prophylaxis. Nutritional support. (See ART entry for full detail.)
us_prevalence: ~4,600 US children <13 years with HIV; ~100–200 new perinatal HIV infections/year in US.
patients_needing_tx: ~4,600 infants/children requiring ART.
peak_sales: ~$17M at 15% share for pediatric-specific long-acting injectable or broadly neutralizing antibody.
registrational_endpoint: HIV RNA undetectable (<50 c/mL) at 48 weeks in infants/children.
btd_threshold_vs_soc: ≥90% viral suppression non-inferior to DTG + 2-NRTI regimen.

symptoms: Elevated blood pressure, often asymptomatic, headache, blurred vision, secondary cause common
treatment_goal: Normalize blood pressure, identify underlying cause, prevent end-organ damage
first_line_soc: Lifestyle modification (DASH diet, weight reduction, exercise) as first-line. ACE inhibitors (lisinopril, enalapril) or ARBs for pharmacologic first-line in persistent hypertension.
treatment_algorithm: Non-pharmacologic: DASH diet, weight loss, exercise (first-line for all). Pharmacologic: ACE inhibitor or ARB first-line for most etiologies; CCB (amlodipine) if ACE-I not tolerated. Beta-blockers for renin-mediated or cardiac cause. Identify and treat secondary causes (renal, endocrine).
us_prevalence: ~4–5% of US children; ~3–4 million US children with hypertension.
patients_needing_tx: ~500,000 children on antihypertensive pharmacotherapy.
price: $1,000–$5,000/year for novel antihypertensive (pediatric-approved formulation).
registrational_endpoint: Reduction in systolic/diastolic BP (mmHg) and hypertension staging at 12 weeks.
btd_threshold_vs_soc: ≥5 mmHg greater systolic BP reduction vs. standard ACE inhibitor.

symptoms: Pharmacologic management category — covers use of antihypertensives in pediatric patients
treatment_goal: Establish safe, age-appropriate pharmacologic BP management
first_line_soc: ACE inhibitors, ARBs, CCBs, and diuretics used according to etiology; pediatric dosing per AHA/AAP 2017 guidelines.
treatment_algorithm: Class-based pharmacotherapy: ACE-I (lisinopril), ARBs (losartan), CCBs (amlodipine), thiazide diuretics (chlorthalidone), beta-blockers (atenolol). Selection based on etiology and comorbidities. All require pediatric dosing studies.
us_prevalence: ~500,000 US children requiring antihypertensive pharmacotherapy.
price: $1,000–$5,000/year for pediatric-formulated antihypertensive.
peak_sales: ~$150M at 15% share for novel or pediatric-optimized formulation.
registrational_endpoint: BP reduction to <90th percentile and safety at 12 weeks in pediatric hypertension RCT.
btd_threshold_vs_soc: ≥10 mmHg greater systolic reduction vs. placebo.

symptoms: Severely elevated BP with end-organ damage: headache, vision changes, renal failure, seizures
treatment_goal: Reduce BP rapidly but safely, prevent encephalopathy and cardiac damage
first_line_soc: IV labetalol or IV nicardipine for hypertensive emergency; oral clonidine or nifedipine for urgent non-emergency hypertension.
treatment_algorithm: IV nicardipine (preferred in pediatric PICU) or labetalol continuous infusion. Sodium nitroprusside for severe cases. Oral: clonidine, nifedipine extended-release. Transition to oral antihypertensive regimen.
us_prevalence: ~1,000–3,000 pediatric hypertensive emergency admissions/year in US.
patients_needing_tx: ~3,000 requiring IV antihypertensive therapy annually.
price: $1,000–$5,000/course for IV antihypertensive.
registrational_endpoint: BP reduction to <95th percentile within 1 hour and organ injury biomarker normalization at 24 hours.
btd_threshold_vs_soc: ≥20% more rapid BP reduction with fewer adverse events vs. IV labetalol.

symptoms: Temperature instability, apnea, poor feeding, hypotension, petechiae in neonate
treatment_goal: Eradicate bloodstream infection, prevent septic shock and death
first_line_soc: Early-onset: ampicillin + gentamicin IV; late-onset: vancomycin + gentamicin (or pip-tazo). Duration 7-14 days per pathogen.
treatment_algorithm: Early-onset sepsis: ampicillin 200 mg/kg/day + gentamicin 5 mg/kg/day IV. Late-onset: vancomycin 15 mg/kg q6-12h + gram-negative agent. Antifungal (fluconazole) for Candida. IVIG not routinely recommended. De-escalate per cultures/sensitivities. NICU monitoring with respiratory/hemodynamic support.
us_prevalence: ~75,000-80,000 cases of suspected neonatal sepsis/year in US; confirmed sepsis ~15,000-20,000/year (0.5-2% of NICU admissions).
patients_needing_tx: ~75,000/year treated empirically; ~17,500 confirmed requiring full treatment course.
price: $25,000-$60,000/course for novel anti-MDR neonatal agent or biomarker-guided therapy.
peak_sales: ~$200M at 10% penetration of empiric treatment pool. 75,000 × $40,000 × 10% × (adjunct only) = ~$100-200M.
registrational_endpoint: 28-day all-cause mortality + organ failure-free days at 28 days vs. standard antibiotics.
btd_threshold_vs_soc: ≥25% relative reduction in 28-day mortality vs. standard empiric therapy (~5-10% current mortality in confirmed cases).

symptoms: Fever or hypothermia, respiratory distress, apnea, poor feeding, bacteremia in NICU
treatment_goal: Eradicate HAI pathogens, prevent sepsis and mortality
first_line_soc: Empiric vancomycin + piperacillin-tazobactam or meropenem depending on local NICU antibiogram; de-escalate per culture results.
treatment_algorithm: Empiric broad-spectrum coverage: vancomycin (targeting MRSA/CoNS) + gram-negative coverage (gentamicin or pip-tazo or carbapenem for MDR organisms). De-escalate per sensitivities. Central line removal if CLABSI. Antifungals (fluconazole) for candida sepsis. Strict infection control protocols.
us_prevalence: ~15,000-20,000 NICU HAIs/year in US; CLABSIs, VAPs, surgical site infections. Predominantly VLBW and preterm infants.
patients_needing_tx: ~17,500/year requiring treatment for confirmed NICU HAI.
price: $20,000-$60,000/episode for novel anti-MDR organism agent or prophylactic immunoglobulin. Pediatric orphan pricing for neonatal-specific agents.
peak_sales: ~$105M at 20% penetration for novel anti-MDR neonatal agent. 17,500 × $30,000 × 20% = $105M.
registrational_endpoint: 30-day all-cause mortality + composite of serious infection-related complications vs. standard of care.
btd_threshold_vs_soc: ≥30% reduction in 30-day mortality vs. standard empiric therapy (~8-12% current mortality).

symptoms: Tachypnea, grunting, retractions, hypoxia, fever or hypothermia in neonate
treatment_goal: Eradicate causative pathogen, restore oxygenation
first_line_soc: Ampicillin + gentamicin IV for early-onset (GBS, E. coli); consider cefotaxime for meningitis co-infection; azithromycin for Chlamydia trachomatis.
treatment_algorithm: Early-onset (<72h): ampicillin + gentamicin. Late-onset (>72h): consider nosocomial pathogens - vancomycin + gram-negative coverage. Chlamydial: oral erythromycin x 14 days (or azithromycin). Viral: supportive ± palivizumab prophylaxis for RSV. Surfactant if RDS component. Mechanical ventilation as needed.
us_prevalence: ~5,000-8,000 cases/year (early- and late-onset combined); major contributor to neonatal mortality.
patients_needing_tx: ~6,500/year requiring IV antibiotics + respiratory support.
price: $15,000-$40,000/course for novel neonatal antibiotic or anti-inflammatory adjunct.
peak_sales: ~$33M at 20% penetration for novel adjunctive agent. 6,500 × $25,000 × 20% = $32.5M.
registrational_endpoint: Time to clinical stability (respiratory rate, oxygen saturation, temperature normalization) + 28-day mortality.
btd_threshold_vs_soc: ≥2-day reduction in time to clinical stability + ≥30% reduction in 28-day mortality vs. current SOC.

symptoms: Hearing loss, microcephaly, developmental delay, petechiae, jaundice
treatment_goal: Prevent hearing loss progression and neurodevelopmental sequelae
first_line_soc: Valganciclovir (16 mg/kg PO BID x 6 months) for symptomatic congenital CMV with CNS involvement; reduces hearing deterioration.
treatment_algorithm: Symptomatic neonates with CNS involvement: IV ganciclovir 6 mg/kg q12h x 6 weeks, then valganciclovir 16 mg/kg BID x 6 months total. Audiologic monitoring every 3-6 months. Developmental therapy. Asymptomatic or non-CNS: valganciclovir may be considered case-by-case.
us_prevalence: ~40,000 infants born with congenital CMV annually (0.5-1% of all US births); ~8,000 symptomatic at birth; ~32,000 asymptomatic (4,000-6,000 will develop sequelae). Pediatric population.
patients_needing_tx: ~8,000-12,000 infants/year with symptomatic or high-risk congenital CMV warranting antiviral therapy.
price: $8,000-$20,000/year (pediatric orphan pricing; currently valganciclovir ~$15,000/year brand). Note: pediatric orphan pricing applies.
peak_sales: ~$75M at 50% share of a specialized neonatal market. 10,000 × $15,000 × 50% = $75M.
registrational_endpoint: Hearing loss at 24 months (change in best-ear hearing threshold, proportion with normal hearing) vs. placebo.
btd_threshold_vs_soc: ≥15% more infants with normal hearing at 24 months vs. placebo (historical valganciclovir: ~73% vs ~57% normal hearing).

symptoms: Often asymptomatic at birth; chronic hepatitis, cirrhosis risk, hepatocellular carcinoma long-term
treatment_goal: Prevent perinatal transmission; achieve HBsAg clearance
first_line_soc: Hepatitis B immune globulin (HBIG) 0.5 mL IM + hepatitis B vaccine within 12 hours of birth for infants of HBsAg-positive mothers; complete 3-dose vaccine series.
treatment_algorithm: Prevention: HBIG + HepB vaccine within 12 hours of birth (>90% effective). TDF (tenofovir) or antiviral prophylaxis in HBeAg-positive mothers with high viral load (>200,000 IU/mL) in 3rd trimester. For established chronic HBV in children: pegIFN-alpha or entecavir per AASLD guidelines.
us_prevalence: ~1,000 perinatal HBV transmissions/year despite prevention; ~0.7 million Americans with chronic HBV. Pediatric: ~25,000 children with chronic HBV.
patients_needing_tx: ~1,000 perinatal cases/year + ~5,000 children with active chronic HBV requiring treatment.
price: $15,000-$50,000/year for novel curative HBV agent; pediatric orphan pricing may apply for pediatric indication.
peak_sales: ~$60M at 40% share for a novel pediatric curative approach. 6,000 × $25,000 × 40% = $60M.
registrational_endpoint: HBsAg loss (functional cure) at 48 weeks or HBV DNA suppression <20 IU/mL.
btd_threshold_vs_soc: HBsAg loss in >10% vs. <1% with standard NUC therapy at 48 weeks.

symptoms: Skin/eye/mouth lesions, encephalitis, disseminated disease, seizures
treatment_goal: Prevent CNS dissemination, reduce mortality and neurologic sequelae
first_line_soc: IV acyclovir 60 mg/kg/day divided q8h x 14-21 days (CNS/disseminated) or x 14 days (SEM disease); followed by oral acyclovir suppression 300 mg/m2 TID x 6 months.
treatment_algorithm: Acute: IV acyclovir 60 mg/kg/day. Duration: SEM (skin/eye/mouth) 14 days; CNS 21 days; disseminated 21 days. Suppression: oral acyclovir 300 mg/m2 TID x 6 months (reduces neurologic impairment). Serial CSF PCR monitoring. Ophthalmology follow-up.
us_prevalence: ~1,500-2,000 cases/year in US neonates; mortality 30% without treatment for disseminated; ~85% of survivors have neurologic sequelae without suppression.
patients_needing_tx: ~1,800/year requiring IV acyclovir + suppression therapy.
price: $30,000-$80,000/course for novel antiviral or immunomodulatory agent. Pediatric orphan pricing applies.
registrational_endpoint: Neurologic outcome at 12 months (Bayley Scales of Infant Development) + survival vs. historical acyclovir controls.
btd_threshold_vs_soc: ≥25% improvement in normal neurodevelopmental outcome at 12 months vs. current acyclovir (~40% normal with suppression).

symptoms: Fever, bulging fontanelle, seizures, lethargy, irritability in neonate
treatment_goal: Eradicate infection, prevent neurologic sequelae and death
first_line_soc: Ampicillin + cefotaxime (or gentamicin) IV x 14-21 days for early-onset; third-generation cephalosporins for late-onset GBS or gram-negative.
treatment_algorithm: Empiric: ampicillin 200 mg/kg/day + cefotaxime 200 mg/kg/day IV (or gentamicin if cefotaxime unavailable). GBS: ampicillin or penicillin G x 14-21 days. Gram-negative: cefotaxime x 21 days. Adjunctive dexamethasone not routinely recommended in neonates. Supportive ICU care.
us_prevalence: ~1,800-2,500 cases/year in US neonates; mortality 10-15%; neurologic sequelae in 25-50% of survivors.
patients_needing_tx: ~2,200/year all requiring IV antibiotics (100% treatment rate by definition of diagnosis).
price: $20,000-$50,000/course for a novel neuroprotective adjunct or next-generation antibiotic. Pediatric orphan pricing applies.
peak_sales: ~$20M at 30% penetration for adjunctive neuroprotective agent. 2,200 × $30,000 × 30% = $19.8M.
registrational_endpoint: Composite: 30-day mortality + moderate/severe neurologic disability at 18 months vs. standard antibiotics alone.
btd_threshold_vs_soc: ≥20% absolute reduction in composite poor outcome rate vs. standard of care (~35% current poor outcome rate).

symptoms: Intracranial calcifications, hydrocephalus, chorioretinitis, seizures, intellectual disability, hepatosplenomegaly
treatment_goal: Eradicate Toxoplasma, prevent progressive neurologic and ocular damage
first_line_soc: Pyrimethamine + sulfadiazine + leucovorin (folinic acid) × 12 months is the established 12-month treatment regimen for congenital toxoplasmosis.
treatment_algorithm: Pyrimethamine (1 mg/kg/day) + sulfadiazine (50 mg/kg/day BID) + leucovorin (10 mg 3×/week) × 12 months. Add corticosteroids (prednisone 1 mg/kg/day) for active chorioretinitis or CSF protein >1 g/dL. Monitor CBC weekly for cytopenias from pyrimethamine. Ophthalmologic follow-up every 3 months.
us_prevalence: ~400–4,000 cases/year in US (highly variable estimates); ~750 treated cases annually with full 12-month regimen.
patients_needing_tx: ~750–1,500 neonates/year requiring full pyrimethamine + sulfadiazine treatment.
price: $5,000–$20,000/year for improved toxoplasmosis therapy (atovaquone, novel antiparasitic).
registrational_endpoint: Chorioretinitis recurrence rate and neurodevelopmental outcomes at 12 months.
btd_threshold_vs_soc: Chorioretinitis recurrence <10% vs. ~30–40% with standard pyrimethamine-sulfadiazine.

symptoms: Sepsis, meningitis, respiratory distress, fever, granulomatosis infantiseptica
treatment_goal: Eradicate Listeria, prevent meningitis and death
first_line_soc: Ampicillin 200 mg/kg/day IV + gentamicin (synergy) x 14-21 days; or ampicillin alone x 3-6 weeks for meningitis.
treatment_algorithm: Ampicillin is drug of choice (Listeria naturally resistant to cephalosporins). Gentamicin added for synergy. Duration: bacteremia 14 days, meningitis 21 days. Supportive care. No maternal prophylaxis proven; dietary counseling for high-risk pregnancies.
us_prevalence: ~100-150 neonatal listeriosis cases/year in US; very rare (incidence ~0.3/100,000 live births). Pediatric orphan condition.
patients_needing_tx: ~125/year requiring IV ampicillin + gentamicin.
price: $50,000-$150,000/course for novel antibiotic with Listeria activity. Pediatric orphan pricing applies.
peak_sales: ~$2-3M (extremely small market; no commercial drug development focused solely on neonatal listeriosis).
registrational_endpoint: Survival at 30 days + sterile CSF/blood cultures at 72 hours.
btd_threshold_vs_soc: N/A (ampicillin highly effective; no novel drug development pathway commercially viable).

symptoms: Respiratory distress, hepatosplenomegaly, fever, lymphadenopathy, failure to thrive in neonate
treatment_goal: Eradicate Mycobacterium tuberculosis, prevent dissemination
first_line_soc: Isoniazid + rifampin + pyrazinamide + ethambutol x 2 months; then INH + RIF x 4 months (standard 6-month RIPE regimen adapted for neonates).
treatment_algorithm: Confirmed neonatal TB: RIPE regimen (INH 10-15 mg/kg/day + RIF 10-20 mg/kg/day + PZA 30-40 mg/kg/day + EMB 15-25 mg/kg/day) x 2 months, then INH + RIF x 4 months. If MDR-TB: linezolid, bedaquiline. BCG vaccination. Maternal TB treatment. DOTS strategy.
us_prevalence: ~100-200 perinatal TB cases/year in US (very rare; US TB incidence ~8,000/year total). Pediatric orphan condition.
patients_needing_tx: ~150/year requiring full anti-TB regimen.
price: $30,000-$100,000/course for novel pediatric-formulated TB combination or MDR-TB agent.
peak_sales: ~$2-3M (extremely small US market; global burden much larger but low-price markets).
registrational_endpoint: Culture conversion at 8 weeks + treatment success (cure + treatment completion) at 6 months.
btd_threshold_vs_soc: ≥90% culture conversion at 8 weeks with shorter regimen (4 vs. 6 months) without increased relapse.

symptoms: Rash, bone pain, hepatosplenomegaly, snuffles, jaundice, stillbirth risk
treatment_goal: Eradicate Treponema pallidum, prevent long-term sequelae
first_line_soc: Parenteral penicillin G (aqueous crystalline, 50,000 units/kg IV q12h × 10 days) is the definitive treatment for congenital syphilis.
treatment_algorithm: Penicillin G IV × 10 days (WHO/CDC guidelines). Long-acting benzathine penicillin G for lesser infections. CSF evaluation. Ophthalmologic, audiologic, and neurologic follow-up.
us_prevalence: ~4,000–5,000 cases/year in US (rising epidemic: 10-fold increase 2012–2022 per CDC).
patients_needing_tx: ~4,000–5,000 neonates/year requiring penicillin treatment.
price: $200–$500/course for penicillin G.
registrational_endpoint: RPR titer normalization at 12 months post-treatment.

symptoms: Eye discharge, redness, swelling of eyelids in newborn
treatment_goal: Eradicate infection, prevent corneal damage
first_line_soc: Erythromycin 0.5% ophthalmic ointment (prophylaxis) at birth; treatment: topical/oral antibiotics per pathogen (azithromycin for chlamydia; penicillin G for gonococcal).
treatment_algorithm: Prophylaxis: erythromycin 0.5% ointment at birth (all neonates). Treatment by etiology: Chlamydial - oral azithromycin 20 mg/kg/day x 3 days (or erythromycin x 14 days). Gonococcal - IV/IM ceftriaxone 25-50 mg/kg single dose. Chemical/silver nitrate - resolves spontaneously. Topical antibiotics for bacterial causes.
us_prevalence: ~12,000-20,000 cases/year in US; declining with universal prophylaxis. Majority chlamydial or chemical.
patients_needing_tx: ~15,000/year requiring active treatment beyond prophylaxis.
price: $50-$200/course (topical antibiotic; limited novel drug opportunity).
registrational_endpoint: Clinical cure rate (resolution of discharge and conjunctival injection) at 14 days vs. comparator.
btd_threshold_vs_soc: N/A (well-established antibiotic treatment; no unmet need for novel therapy).

symptoms: Deafness, cataracts, congenital heart disease, intellectual disability, blueberry muffin rash
treatment_goal: Prevent via maternal vaccination; manage complications
first_line_soc: No specific antiviral pharmacologic treatment; management is supportive. Prevention: MMR vaccine.
treatment_algorithm: No curative pharmacologic treatment. Manage cardiac defects (surgery), cataracts (surgery), hearing loss (hearing aids). Prevention remains the only effective strategy (MMR vaccination achieving near-elimination in US).
us_prevalence: Near-eliminated in US (<5 cases/year); historically ~20,000 cases during 1964–65 epidemic.
patients_needing_tx: < 5 new cases/year in US; pharmacologic TAM negligible.

symptoms: Multi-organ involvement: myopathy, encephalopathy, lactic acidosis, cardiomyopathy, SNHL, retinopathy
treatment_goal: Reduce oxidative stress, support mitochondrial function, prevent organ failure
first_line_soc: Mitochondrial 'cocktail': CoQ10, riboflavin (B2), thiamine, carnitine, antioxidants. No FDA-approved disease-modifying treatment. Idebenone for Leber's hereditary optic neuropathy (LHON).
treatment_algorithm: Supportive 'cocktail': CoQ10 10-30 mg/kg/day, L-carnitine, riboflavin, thiamine, B12. Idebenone (Raxone) for LHON. Avoid mitochondria-toxic drugs (metformin, valproate, statins). EPI-743 (vatiquinone - investigational). KSS/MELAS: leucovorin, L-arginine for stroke-like episodes. No curative therapy available.
us_prevalence: ~25,000-50,000 US patients with mitochondrial disease (all types). MELAS ~3,000; MERRF ~1,000; Leigh syndrome ~2,000; LHON ~6,000; CPEO ~5,000; others.
patients_needing_tx: ~30,000 patients on mitochondrial cocktail supplements or seeking pharmacologic treatment.
price: $50,000-$300,000/year for novel mitochondrial-targeted antioxidant or gene therapy. Orphan pricing.
peak_sales: ~$450M at 10% share for novel mitochondrial therapeutic. 30,000 × $150,000 × 10% = $450M.
registrational_endpoint: Newcastle Mitochondrial Disease Adult Scale (NMDAS) or pediatric equivalent change at 52 weeks + lactic acid normalization.
btd_threshold_vs_soc: ≥5-point NMDAS improvement vs. placebo + lactate reduction >20% at 52 weeks.

symptoms: Splenomegaly, hepatomegaly, bone pain, anemia, thrombocytopenia
treatment_goal: Reduce glycolipid storage, prevent bone crises and organ damage
first_line_soc: ERT: imiglucerase (Cerezyme) 60 U/kg IV q2w (or velaglucerase alfa, taliglucerase alfa). SRT: miglustat or eliglustat (oral) for Type 1 adult patients.
treatment_algorithm: Type 1 (non-neuronopathic): ERT (imiglucerase/velaglucerase 60 U/kg q2w) or SRT (eliglustat or miglustat - oral). Type 2 (acute neuronopathic): ERT may slow visceral disease, no CNS penetration; supportive care. Type 3 (chronic neuronopathic): ERT + substrate reduction; HSCT investigational.
us_prevalence: ~20,000 US patients (Type 1 most common ~18,000; Type 2/3 ~2,000). Ashkenazi Jewish enrichment.
patients_needing_tx: ~12,000 on ERT or SRT in US.
price: $200,000-$400,000/year (current ERT ~$250,000-$400,000/year; eliglustat ~$160,000/year). Rare disease pricing.
peak_sales: ~$600M at 20% share for next-gen/gene therapy (e.g., LVX101). 12,000 × $250,000 × 20% = $600M.
registrational_endpoint: Spleen volume reduction (MRI-based), hemoglobin increase ≥1.5 g/dL, platelet count normalization at 9-12 months.
btd_threshold_vs_soc: ≥30% spleen volume reduction + hemoglobin/platelet normalization in >70% vs. ~50-60% with current ERT.

symptoms: Hypoglycemia (types I/III/VI/IX), cardiomyopathy (type II), muscle weakness/cramps, hepatomegaly
treatment_goal: Prevent hypoglycemia, reverse cardiomyopathy, maintain metabolic stability
first_line_soc: GSD I: cornstarch (uncooked) + fructose/galactose-free diet. GSD II (Pompe): alglucosidase alfa (Lumizyme) ERT 20 mg/kg q2w + avalglucosidase alfa (Nexviazyme) as improved ERT.
treatment_algorithm: GSD type I: frequent feeds, cornstarch, modified Glycosade; liver transplant curative. GSD II (Pompe disease): alglucosidase alfa (Lumizyme) ERT; avalglucosidase alfa (Nexviazyme) with improved efficacy; cipaglucosidase alfa + miglustat (Pombiliti+Opfolda) as next-gen. GSD III/V/VI: dietary management. Gene therapy (GSD I, II in trials).
us_prevalence: All GSDs combined ~5,000-7,000 US patients. Pompe ~5,000-7,000; GSD I ~1,000; GSD III ~400-600; GSD V ~2,500; others rare.
patients_needing_tx: ~8,000 with GSD requiring pharmacotherapy (mostly Pompe + dietary-failure GSD I).
price: $200,000-$600,000/year for Pompe ERT/gene therapy; $50,000-$150,000 for other GSD gene therapy. Orphan pricing.
registrational_endpoint: For Pompe: 6-min walk test + forced vital capacity (FVC) change at 52 weeks vs. alglucosidase alfa.
btd_threshold_vs_soc: ≥15-point improvement in 6MWT + ≥3% improvement in FVC vs. alglucosidase alfa (as cipaglucosidase achieved).

symptoms: Intellectual disability if untreated; dietary non-compliance: cognitive decline, behavioral issues, seizures
treatment_goal: Maintain Phe levels <120 µmol/L, preserve cognitive function
first_line_soc: Sapropterin (Kuvan) 10-20 mg/kg/day orally for BH4-responsive PKU (~30% of patients). Phenylalanine-restricted diet (medical formula). Pegvaliase (Palynziq) for adults uncontrolled on diet.
treatment_algorithm: Phenylalanine-restricted diet using Phe-free medical amino acid formula. Sapropterin dihydrochloride (Kuvan) for BH4-responsive cases (PAH mutations). Pegvaliase (Palynziq) SC injection for adults with uncontrolled PKU. Gene therapy (BMN 307 mRNA/AAV - clinical trials). Large neutral amino acid supplementation.
us_prevalence: ~16,000-17,000 US patients with PKU (1:10,000-1:15,000 births). Universal newborn screening.
patients_needing_tx: ~12,000 patients requiring active pharmacotherapy (sapropterin and/or pegvaliase) beyond diet alone.
price: $100,000-$200,000/year (sapropterin ~$100,000/year; pegvaliase ~$200,000/year). Orphan pricing.
peak_sales: ~$360M at 20% share for novel gene therapy (durable) replacing chronic medication. 12,000 × $150,000 × 20% = $360M.
registrational_endpoint: Blood phenylalanine reduction to <120 µmol/L at 24 weeks + proportion with Phe normalization.
btd_threshold_vs_soc: Phe <120 µmol/L in >70% of patients vs. ~30% with sapropterin.

symptoms: Lesch-Nyhan: severe gout, self-mutilation, dystonia, intellectual disability, hyperuricemia
treatment_goal: Control hyperuricemia, manage self-injurious behavior
first_line_soc: Allopurinol 10-20 mg/kg/day to reduce uric acid levels (controls gout, nephrolithiasis, NOT neurological manifestations). Behavioral restraints for self-injury.
treatment_algorithm: Allopurinol for hyperuricemia; febuxostat if allopurinol intolerant. Neurobehavioral management for self-injury (physical restraints, protective equipment, risperidone for aggression). Baclofen for spasticity. No treatment corrects neurological deficits. Gene therapy (HPRT gene - investigational).
us_prevalence: Lesch-Nyhan syndrome: ~1,200 US patients (1:380,000 male births, X-linked). Partial HPRT deficiency (Kelley-Seegmiller): ~500 additional.
patients_needing_tx: ~1,500 patients requiring allopurinol + adjunctive neurological management.
price: $1,000,000-$3,000,000 one-time for HPRT gene therapy. Pediatric orphan pricing.
peak_sales: ~$270M at 15% for gene therapy approaching clinical trials. 1,200 × $1,500,000 × 15% = $270M.
registrational_endpoint: Serum uric acid normalization + dystonia rating scale (UDRS) improvement at 24 months.
btd_threshold_vs_soc: Uric acid <6 mg/dL + ≥30% UDRS improvement vs. allopurinol alone (which does not improve neurological manifestations).

symptoms: Jaundice, vomiting, cataracts, liver failure, sepsis in neonate; long-term cognitive and ovarian failure
treatment_goal: Prevent galactose toxicity via dietary restriction; manage long-term complications
first_line_soc: Lifelong dietary galactose elimination (lactose-free diet). No approved pharmacologic therapy for classic galactosemia.
treatment_algorithm: Classic (GALT-deficient) galactosemia: soy-based or galactose-free formula from birth. Lifelong galactose restriction. Calcium and vitamin D supplementation. Hormone replacement for POI. Speech/OT therapy. Galactose oxidase pathway enhancement (uridine supplementation - investigational). No FDA-approved drug.
us_prevalence: ~1:30,000-1:60,000 live births; ~3,000-6,000 US patients. Screened by newborn screening.
patients_needing_tx: ~5,000 patients with ongoing disease burden requiring pharmacologic innovation.
price: $200,000-$400,000/year for gene therapy or substrate reduction. Pediatric orphan pricing applies.
peak_sales: ~$150M at 10% for novel galactosemia therapy (e.g., GALT mRNA or gene therapy). 5,000 × $300,000 × 10% = $150M.
registrational_endpoint: Reduction in galactose-1-phosphate (Gal-1-P) erythrocyte levels + cognitive composite score at 52 weeks.
btd_threshold_vs_soc: Erythrocyte Gal-1-P <1 mg/dL in >60% of patients vs. <5% with dietary management alone.

symptoms: Neuropathic pain, angiokeratomas, kidney failure, cardiac hypertrophy, stroke
treatment_goal: Reduce GL-3 substrate, prevent organ failure
first_line_soc: Enzyme replacement therapy: agalsidase beta (Fabrazyme) 1 mg/kg IV q2w or agalsidase alfa (Replagal, non-US) 0.2 mg/kg q2w. Migalastat (Galafold) for amenable mutations (oral chaperone).
treatment_algorithm: ERT (agalsidase beta/alfa) q2w IV infusions. Migalastat (oral chaperone therapy) for ~35-50% of patients with amenable GLA mutations. Substrate reduction (lucerastat - trial). Symptomatic: pain (carbamazepine, gabapentin); ACE-I/ARB for proteinuria; antiplatelet/anticoagulation for stroke prevention; dialysis/transplant for ESRD.
us_prevalence: ~5,000-10,000 US patients with Fabry disease (X-linked; males more severely affected). Prevalence ~1:40,000-1:117,000.
patients_needing_tx: ~4,000 on ERT or chaperone therapy in US.
price: $300,000-$500,000/year (agalsidase beta ~$350,000/year; migalastat ~$290,000/year). Pediatric orphan + rare disease pricing.
peak_sales: ~$280M at 20% share for next-generation gene therapy or novel substrate reduction. 4,000 × $350,000 × 20% = $280M.
registrational_endpoint: Change from baseline in eGFR slope + plasma/urine GL-3 levels at 24 months vs. agalsidase.
btd_threshold_vs_soc: eGFR decline <1 mL/min/1.73m²/year vs. ~2-3 mL/min/1.73m²/year with current ERT.

symptoms: Encephalopathy, maple syrup urine odor, developmental regression, metabolic crisis
treatment_goal: Prevent neurotoxic amino acid accumulation, maintain metabolic stability
first_line_soc: Protein-restricted diet with synthetic BCAA-free medical formula; thiamine supplementation for thiamine-responsive MSUD; avoid catabolism.
treatment_algorithm: MSUD: leucine-restricted diet using BCAA-free formula. Thiamine 10-1,000 mg/day for thiamine-responsive MSUD. Emergency: high-calorie glucose/insulin IV to suppress catabolism. Liver transplantation corrects enzyme defect and allows normal diet. NA benzoate/phenylacetate for some organic acidemias (IVA, PA, MMA).
us_prevalence: MSUD: ~2,200 US patients; propionic acidemia ~1,000; methylmalonic acidemia ~1,500; isovaleric acidemia ~500. Total BCAA disorders ~5,000.
patients_needing_tx: ~5,000 requiring specialized metabolic formula and pharmacologic adjuncts.
price: $150,000-$400,000/year for mRNA therapy, gene therapy, or novel enzyme replacement. Pediatric orphan pricing applies.
peak_sales: ~$250M at 20% share for novel curative/disease-modifying therapy. 5,000 × $250,000 × 20% = $250M.
registrational_endpoint: Plasma leucine normalization + reduction in metabolic crisis hospitalizations at 12 months.
btd_threshold_vs_soc: Plasma leucine <300 µmol/L in ≥70% of patients vs. <40% with current dietary management alone.

symptoms: Vomiting, hypoglycemia after fructose ingestion, liver failure (HFI); benign (fructosuria)
treatment_goal: Prevent fructose-induced hypoglycemia and liver damage via strict dietary exclusion
first_line_soc: Strict dietary fructose, sucrose, and sorbitol elimination (hereditary fructose intolerance - HFI). No pharmacologic treatment currently approved.
treatment_algorithm: HFI: lifelong elimination of fructose, sucrose, sorbitol from diet. Emergency: IV glucose for hypoglycemia. Liver transplant for advanced liver disease. Essential fructosuria: benign, no treatment. Fructose-1,6-bisphosphatase deficiency: avoid fasting, cornstarch supplementation.
us_prevalence: HFI: ~5,000-10,000 US patients. Essential fructosuria: ~1:130,000 (benign). F-1,6-BPase deficiency: <500. Total ~10,000.
patients_needing_tx: ~5,000 with HFI requiring strict dietary management; pharmacologic unmet need is high (SRT or chaperone approaches in development).
price: $150,000-$300,000/year for mRNA/gene therapy or aldolase-B replacement. Pediatric orphan pricing applies.
registrational_endpoint: Fructose tolerance test normalization + liver function (ALT, bilirubin) at 24 weeks.
btd_threshold_vs_soc: ≥80% of patients tolerating dietary fructose without hypoglycemia vs. 0% with dietary management alone.

symptoms: Severe irritability, stiffness, developmental regression, blindness, deafness, early death
treatment_goal: Slow demyelination; curative HSCT in presymptomatic patients
first_line_soc: Hematopoietic stem cell transplantation (HSCT) in presymptomatic neonates detected by NBS; no approved pharmacologic disease-modifying therapy.
treatment_algorithm: Presymptomatic (NBS-detected): umbilical cord blood transplant (UCBT) or HSCT - best outcomes if done <30 days of life. Symptomatic: no effective disease-modifying treatment; palliative care. Gene therapy (LentiGlobin/Krabbe-specific AAV - investigational). Substrate reduction (eliglustat - off-label trial).
us_prevalence: ~200-300 US patients living (most die in infancy); ~50-100 new cases/year. Pediatric orphan disease.
patients_needing_tx: ~300 patients, predominantly pediatric, requiring HSCT or novel pharmacotherapy.
price: $2,000,000-$4,000,000 one-time for gene therapy (comparable to Zolgensma scale). Pediatric orphan pricing applies.
peak_sales: ~$270M at 30% penetration in severe orphan disease. 300 (incident) × $3,000,000 × 30% = ~$90M/year (annual incident cases ~75 × $3M = $225M × 30% share).
registrational_endpoint: Gross Motor Function Measure (GMFM) and nerve conduction velocity at 24 months vs. untreated historical controls.
btd_threshold_vs_soc: GMFM score >40 points (ambulatory) vs. historical untreated <5 points; 2-year survival >80% vs. ~10%.

symptoms: Type A/B: hepatosplenomegaly, pulmonary infiltrates, neurodegeneration. Type C: vertical supranuclear gaze palsy, ataxia, dementia, dystonia
treatment_goal: Reduce sphingomyelin/cholesterol accumulation, slow neurodegeneration
first_line_soc: NPC: miglustat (Zavesca) 200 mg TID for neurological stabilization (approved EU/US for NPC); arimoclomol (EU approval 2024). NPB: olipudase alfa (Xenpozyme) ERT approved US 2022 for NPB.
treatment_algorithm: NPC: miglustat (substrate reduction, slows neurological progression); arimoclomol (approved EU); N-acetyl cysteine; cyclodextrin (compassionate use). NPB: olipudase alfa (Xenpozyme) ERT 3 mg/kg IV q4w. NPA: no disease-modifying therapy; gene therapy/ERT trials ongoing. Supportive: anticonvulsants, physio, nutrition.
us_prevalence: NPA: ~300 US patients; NPB: ~1,200; NPC: ~1,000. Total ~2,500. Pediatric orphan disease.
patients_needing_tx: ~2,000 patients: NPB ~1,000 on olipudase alfa; NPC ~700 on miglustat.
price: $300,000-$600,000/year for ERT (olipudase alfa ~$500,000/year) or gene therapy. Pediatric orphan pricing applies.
registrational_endpoint: NPB: spleen volume reduction (MRI) + pulmonary function. NPC: NPCC-OASIS severity score change at 52 weeks.
btd_threshold_vs_soc: NPB: ≥30% spleen volume reduction vs. placebo; NPC: ≥2-point OASIS improvement vs. miglustat alone.

symptoms: Zellweger: hypotonia, seizures, liver disease, neonatal death. ALD: adrenal insufficiency, cerebral demyelination
treatment_goal: Prevent adrenal crisis; halt cerebral demyelination via HSCT/gene therapy
first_line_soc: X-ALD: Lorenzo's oil (erucic acid/oleic acid - limited evidence); hematopoietic stem cell transplantation for early cerebral ALD; elivaldogene autotemcel (Skysona) gene therapy for early cerebral ALD. Adrenal insufficiency: hydrocortisone + fludrocortisone replacement.
treatment_algorithm: X-ALD adrenomyeloneuropathy: adrenal steroid replacement; Lorenzo's oil (normalizes VLCFA, inconclusive CNS benefit). Cerebral ALD: HSCT (allogeneic) or Skysona (gene therapy, FDA 2022). Zellweger spectrum: no curative treatment; docosahexaenoic acid supplementation; bile acid replacement; supportive.
patients_needing_tx: ~5,000 X-ALD patients on active pharmacotherapy; ~200/year new cerebral ALD candidates for gene therapy.
price: $2,800,000 one-time for Skysona gene therapy; $10,000-$30,000/year for adrenal replacement. Orphan pricing.
registrational_endpoint: MRI lesion score stability + NFS (neurological function score) at 24 months vs. HSCT historical controls.
btd_threshold_vs_soc: ≥75% failure-free survival at 24 months (Skysona achieved 91% vs. ~56% for MFU-matched HSCT).

symptoms: Hypoketotic hypoglycemia, cardiomyopathy, muscle weakness, liver failure
treatment_goal: Prevent metabolic crises, hypoglycemia, and cardiomyopathy
first_line_soc: Dietary fat restriction (LCT) with MCT supplementation; avoid fasting; carnitine supplementation for LCAD/VLCAD defects; triheptanoin (Dojolvi) for long-chain FAO disorders.
treatment_algorithm: Avoid fasting (frequent feeding/glucose polymer). Fat modification: low long-chain fat, MCT supplementation. Triheptanoin (Dojolvi) FDA-approved for long-chain FAO disorders. Emergency protocol: D10W IV during illness. Carnitine if deficient. For MCAD: primarily dietary avoidance of fasting.
us_prevalence: ~2,000-4,000 US patients with all beta-oxidation disorders combined (various subtypes: VLCAD ~1,500, LCHAD ~500, MCAD ~10,000, others rare). Pediatric + adult.
patients_needing_tx: ~3,000 with long-chain FAO disorders requiring Dojolvi or MCT supplementation.
price: $100,000-$300,000/year for metabolic rescue agent (anaplerotic). Pediatric orphan pricing applies.
peak_sales: ~$120M at 20% for novel anaplerotic beyond Dojolvi. 3,000 × $200,000 × 20% = $120M.
registrational_endpoint: Reduction in major clinical events (hypoglycemia hospitalizations, rhabdomyolysis, cardiomyopathy) at 12 months vs. dietary management alone.
btd_threshold_vs_soc: ≥50% reduction in major clinical events vs. dietary management alone.

symptoms: Varies: hypoglycemia, lactic acidosis, liver disease, neurodevelopmental delay (PGM1, PGAM2, others)
treatment_goal: Prevent metabolic crises and end-organ damage
first_line_soc: Dietary management (avoid fasting, cornstarch supplementation for specific defects). No FDA-approved pharmacologic therapies for most.
treatment_algorithm: Individualized per specific enzyme defect. PGM1 deficiency: mannose supplementation. Phosphoglycerate kinase deficiency: avoid extreme exertion. PFK deficiency: dietary modification. Most lack specific approved therapy beyond supportive dietary management.
us_prevalence: Collectively ~2,000-5,000 US patients across all rare carbohydrate metabolism disorders not elsewhere classified.
patients_needing_tx: ~3,000 patients with significant pharmacologic unmet need.
price: $100,000-$400,000/year for gene therapy or substrate supplementation. Orphan pricing.
registrational_endpoint: Metabolic crisis-free days + biomarker normalization at 52 weeks.
btd_threshold_vs_soc: ≥50% reduction in metabolic crisis hospitalizations vs. dietary management alone.

symptoms: Lactic acidosis, hypotonia, encephalopathy, Leigh syndrome features (PDH deficiency); hyperammonemia (pyruvate carboxylase)
treatment_goal: Reduce lactic acidosis, support energy metabolism, prevent neurological deterioration
first_line_soc: Pyruvate dehydrogenase (PDH) deficiency: ketogenic diet; thiamine 100-1,000 mg/day for thiamine-responsive cases; dichloroacetate (DCA) - investigational. Pyruvate carboxylase deficiency: triheptanoin + biotin supplementation.
treatment_algorithm: PDH deficiency: ketogenic diet (reduces reliance on carbohydrates); thiamine for E1-alpha thiamine-responsive mutations; DCA (experimental, reduces lactate); lipoic acid, CoQ10. Pyruvate carboxylase deficiency: biotin; anaplerotic substrate (triheptanoin). No FDA-approved pharmacotherapy for pyruvate disorders.
us_prevalence: PDH deficiency: ~1,000-2,000 US patients. Pyruvate carboxylase deficiency: ~200. Other pyruvate disorders: ~300. Total ~2,500.
patients_needing_tx: ~2,000 patients with significant disease burden requiring pharmacologic management.
price: $200,000-$500,000/year for gene therapy or novel PDH activator. Pediatric orphan pricing.
registrational_endpoint: Lactate normalization + Bayley/developmental outcome improvement at 52 weeks.
btd_threshold_vs_soc: Blood lactate <2 mmol/L in >60% + improvement in gross motor function vs. dietary management alone.

symptoms: Hyperammonemia, vomiting, encephalopathy, lethargy, coma; may present as neonatal emergency
treatment_goal: Reduce ammonia to normal, prevent neurological damage
first_line_soc: Nitrogen scavengers: sodium benzoate (250 mg/kg/day) + sodium phenylacetate (Ammonul IV for acute; Ravicti/glycerol phenylbutyrate orally for maintenance). Protein restriction with essential amino acid supplementation.
treatment_algorithm: Acute: IV Ammonul (sodium benzoate + phenylacetate) + arginine/citrulline supplementation + glucose/insulin. Maintenance: Ravicti (glycerol phenylbutyrate) oral daily. Protein restriction. Citrulline or arginine supplementation per specific defect. Liver transplantation (curative). Carglumic acid (Carbaglu) for CPS1/NAGS deficiency.
us_prevalence: ~2,500-4,000 US patients with UCDs (OTC deficiency most common, ~2,000). Collectively 1:35,000 live births.
patients_needing_tx: ~3,000 patients requiring ongoing nitrogen scavenger therapy.
price: $100,000-$400,000/year (Ravicti ~$150,000-$300,000/year). Orphan pricing.
peak_sales: ~$180M at 30% for mRNA/gene therapy replacement (OTC gene therapy in phase 2). 3,000 × $200,000 × 30% = $180M.
registrational_endpoint: Time-averaged ammonia AUC + number of hyperammonemic crises at 52 weeks vs. glycerol phenylbutyrate.
btd_threshold_vs_soc: ≥50% reduction in hyperammonemic crises + ammonia <50 µmol/L in >70% vs. ~50% with Ravicti.

symptoms: Progressive motor and cognitive regression, spasticity, peripheral neuropathy, behavioral changes
treatment_goal: Halt demyelination, preserve neurological function
first_line_soc: Atidarsagene autotemcel (Libmeldy) - gene therapy approved EU 2020 for pre-/early-symptomatic late-infantile and early-juvenile MLD. Autologous HSC gene therapy.
treatment_algorithm: Gene therapy (Libmeldy/OTL-200): autologous HSCs transduced with ARSA lentiviral vector; approved in EU; investigational/compassionate use in US. HSCT (allogeneic) for pre/early symptomatic - limited benefit. No approved US pharmacotherapy. Palliative care for advanced disease.
us_prevalence: ~1,400-2,000 US patients; ~100-150 new cases/year. 1:40,000-1:160,000. Pediatric orphan disease.
patients_needing_tx: ~150 new cases/year eligible for gene therapy (presymptomatic/early symptomatic).
price: $2,500,000-$4,000,000 one-time for gene therapy (OTL-200 priced ~$3.9M in EU). Pediatric orphan pricing applies.
registrational_endpoint: Motor function scale (MFS) stability + absence of disease progression at 24 months vs. untreated natural history.
btd_threshold_vs_soc: ≥80% patients with stable or improved MFS at 24 months vs. ~5% without gene therapy.

symptoms: Homocystinuria: lens dislocation, thromboembolism, skeletal deformities, cognitive delay; CBS deficiency most common
treatment_goal: Normalize homocysteine, prevent thromboembolic events and lens dislocation
first_line_soc: Pyridoxine (B6) 200-1,000 mg/day for B6-responsive CBS deficiency (50% of cases). B6-non-responsive: methionine-restricted diet + betaine (Cystadane) 100-150 mg/kg/day.
treatment_algorithm: B6-responsive: pyridoxine; folate; B12. B6-non-responsive: methionine-restricted diet + betaine anhydrous (Cystadane) to reduce homocysteine. Antiplatelet/anticoagulation for thromboembolism prevention. Folate supplementation. Gene therapy (investigational). Betaine ~$10,000-$50,000/year.
us_prevalence: CBS-deficient homocystinuria: ~1,000-2,000 US patients. Other methionine disorders (MAT I/III, GNMT, cystathioninuria) ~500. Total ~2,500.
patients_needing_tx: ~2,000 patients requiring betaine or pyridoxine supplementation.
price: $100,000-$300,000/year for mRNA/gene therapy targeting CBS or MAT deficiency. Pediatric orphan pricing applies.
peak_sales: ~$80M at 20% share for novel curative therapy. 2,000 × $200,000 × 20% = $80M.
registrational_endpoint: Plasma total homocysteine reduction to <50 µmol/L + thromboembolic event-free survival at 52 weeks.
btd_threshold_vs_soc: tHcy <50 µmol/L in >80% vs. ~50% with betaine + diet.

symptoms: Hyperuricemia, gout, nephrolithiasis (xanthinuria); hemolytic anemia, neurological issues (adenosine deaminase deficiency)
treatment_goal: Reduce uric acid/xanthine, prevent gout and renal complications
first_line_soc: Xanthinuria: high fluid intake, dietary purine restriction; allopurinol ineffective. Adenosine deaminase deficiency (ADA-SCID): PEG-ADA (elapegademase - Revcovi) ERT weekly IM.
treatment_algorithm: Xanthinuria: hydration, low-purine diet, alkalinization. ADA-SCID: Revcovi (elapegademase-lvlr) ERT; gene therapy (Strimvelis EMA-approved; ADA-strimvelis); allogeneic HSCT. Myoadenylate deaminase deficiency: avoid extreme exertion.
us_prevalence: ADA-SCID: ~100-200 US patients (1:200,000-1:1,000,000 births); xanthinuria: ~500; other: ~300. Total ~1,000.
patients_needing_tx: ~300 patients with significant pharmacologic need (primarily ADA-SCID).
price: $1,500,000-$4,000,000 one-time for gene therapy (ADA-SCID gene therapy Strimvelis ~$665,000 EU). Pediatric orphan pricing.
registrational_endpoint: Sustained immune reconstitution (CD4+ >500/µL) at 12-24 months + ADA enzyme activity.
btd_threshold_vs_soc: Immune reconstitution in >70% without HSCT conditioning risks; Strimvelis achieved 12/12 treated patients surviving.

disease: Cholesteryl Ester Storage Disease and Wolman Disease
symptoms: Hepatomegaly, adrenal calcification (Wolman), hyperlipidemia, liver disease progression
treatment_goal: Reduce lysosomal lipid storage, prevent liver failure and adrenal insufficiency
first_line_soc: Sebelipase alfa (Kanuma) FDA-approved ERT: 1 mg/kg IV q2w for CESD; 3 mg/kg weekly (escalating to 5 mg/kg) for Wolman disease.
treatment_algorithm: Sebelipase alfa (lysosomal acid lipase replacement): 1 mg/kg q2w (CESD) or 3-5 mg/kg/week (Wolman, life-threatening). Adrenal steroid replacement for Wolman. Statins for hyperlipidemia in CESD. HSCT for Wolman (pre-ERT era). Liver transplant for end-stage liver disease.
us_prevalence: CESD: ~500 US patients; Wolman Disease: <200 (estimated). Combined ~700 US patients. Pediatric orphan disease.
patients_needing_tx: ~600 requiring sebelipase alfa or next-generation ERT.
price: $300,000-$600,000/year (current Kanuma ~$400,000/year). Pediatric orphan pricing applies.
peak_sales: ~$120M for improved next-generation LAL-ERT at 50% share. 600 × $400,000 × 50% = $120M.
registrational_endpoint: Normalization of ALT + reduction in liver fat fraction (MRI-PDFF) at 20 weeks vs. placebo.
btd_threshold_vs_soc: ALT normalization in >50% vs. <10% placebo at 20 weeks; survival benefit in Wolman (100% fatal by age 1 without treatment).

symptoms: Cherry-red spot, progressive neurodegeneration, blindness, deafness, paralysis, death in infancy/childhood
treatment_goal: Slow neurodegeneration; currently no curative therapy
first_line_soc: None (no approved pharmacologic treatment). Supportive/palliative care: anticonvulsants, nutritional support, respiratory support.
