treatment_algorithm: No FDA-approved disease-modifying therapy. Supportive: anticonvulsants (clonazepam, levetiracetam), nutritional support (NG tube), chest physiotherapy. Substrate reduction with miglustat (off-label Sandhoff) showed limited benefit. Gene therapy (AAV-HEXA/HEXB - early-phase trials). HSCT not effective. Palliative care focus.
us_prevalence: Infantile Tay-Sachs: ~10-20 new cases/year (general population); higher in Ashkenazi Jewish. ~200 living US patients across age spectrum. Sandhoff: ~50 total. Pediatric orphan disease.
patients_needing_tx: ~250 patients requiring supportive medications; ~30-40 new incidents/year candidates for disease-modifying gene therapy.
price: $3,000,000-$5,000,000 one-time for gene therapy (HEXA/HEXB restoration). Pediatric ultra-orphan pricing.
registrational_endpoint: Survival at 36 months + GMFM score + MRI volumetric stability vs. untreated natural history.
btd_threshold_vs_soc: Survival >24 months in >50% vs. typically <2 years for infantile Tay-Sachs.

symptoms: Hypotonia, developmental delay, seizures, autistic features (ADSL deficiency, AICA-ribosiduria)
treatment_goal: Correct purine nucleotide depletion, improve neurological outcomes
first_line_soc: Allopurinol (reduces substrate accumulation in some subtypes) + ribose supplementation (investigational). No FDA-approved pharmacologic treatment.
treatment_algorithm: Adenylosuccinate lyase (ADSL) deficiency: no approved treatment; allopurinol, ribose supplementation tried. AICA-ribosiduria: supportive. Dietary purine supplementation. Gene therapy in preclinical development. No FDA-approved agent.
us_prevalence: Extremely rare: <200 US patients total across all subtypes. ADSL deficiency: ~100-150 US cases known. Pediatric orphan disease.
patients_needing_tx: ~150 patients with significant unmet pharmacologic need.
price: $500,000-$3,000,000 one-time for gene therapy. Pediatric orphan pricing.
registrational_endpoint: Seizure frequency reduction + developmental assessment (Bayley-4) improvement at 52 weeks.
btd_threshold_vs_soc: ≥50% seizure reduction + developmental gain ≥1 SD vs. natural history.

symptoms: Hereditary tyrosinemia type 1: liver failure, cirrhosis, hepatocellular carcinoma, renal tubular dysfunction, porphyric crises
treatment_goal: Prevent liver failure, HCC, and renal disease
first_line_soc: Nitisinone (NTBC/Orfadin) 1-2 mg/kg/day orally + low-tyrosine/phenylalanine diet; dramatically reduces HCC risk and liver failure.
treatment_algorithm: Nitisinone (Orfadin) + tyrosine-restricted diet: standard of care for HT1. Liver transplantation for failure to respond or established HCC. HT2 and HT3: low-tyrosine diet; nitisinone off-label. Transient neonatal tyrosinemia: resolves with low-protein diet.
patients_needing_tx: ~700 patients with HT1 on nitisinone.
price: $100,000-$200,000/year (nitisinone/Orfadin ~$100,000-$200,000/year). Pediatric orphan pricing.
peak_sales: ~$53M at 50% share for next-generation or improved formulation. 700 × $150,000 × 50% = $52.5M.
registrational_endpoint: AFP normalization + absence of HCC development + liver function normalization at 24 months.
btd_threshold_vs_soc: HCC risk <5% at 5 years vs. historical ~15-37% without nitisinone treatment.

symptoms: Episodic vomiting, lethargy, elevated glycerol in urine; benign glyceroluria
treatment_goal: Prevent metabolic crises; mostly benign condition requiring no treatment
first_line_soc: None for isolated glyceroluria (benign). For glycerol kinase deficiency with associated Duchenne/AHC: treat comorbidities (corticosteroids for DMD, adrenal steroid replacement).
treatment_algorithm: Isolated glycerol kinase deficiency: variable phenotype. Juvenile/adult: often benign. Infantile complex GKD (with DMD and AHC): adrenal hormone replacement (hydrocortisone, fludrocortisone); corticosteroids for DMD component; dietary management.
us_prevalence: Glycerol kinase deficiency: ~200-500 US patients (X-linked, very rare; 1:8,000,000 males). Pediatric orphan disease.
patients_needing_tx: ~200-300 patients requiring treatment for complex GKD.
price: $200,000-$500,000/year for gene therapy targeting Xp21 microdeletion. Pediatric orphan pricing applies.
peak_sales: ~$18M at 20% share for gene therapy approach. 300 × $300,000 × 20% = $18M.
registrational_endpoint: Adrenal crisis frequency + muscle function composite at 12 months.
btd_threshold_vs_soc: Zero adrenal crises vs. ~2-4/year without treatment in complex GKD.

symptoms: Megaloblastic anemia unresponsive to B12/folate (orotic aciduria); hemolytic anemia, neurological features
treatment_goal: Correct pyrimidine deficiency, resolve anemia and developmental delay
first_line_soc: Uridine supplementation (Xuriden - uridine triacetate, FDA-approved) for hereditary orotic aciduria 60 mg/kg/day orally. Also emergency treatment for 5-FU/capecitabine overdose.
treatment_algorithm: Hereditary orotic aciduria: uridine triacetate (Xuriden) orally; corrects anemia and normalizes excretion. Dietary monitoring. UMP synthase enzyme - no direct enzyme replacement. DPD deficiency (pyrimidine catabolism): fluoropyrimidine dosing reduction to prevent toxicity.
us_prevalence: Hereditary orotic aciduria: <100 US patients (extremely rare, <200 worldwide). DPD deficiency: ~3-5% of population (pharmacogenomic, not truly a disease).
patients_needing_tx: ~100 patients with hereditary orotic aciduria requiring Xuriden.
price: $300,000-$600,000/year (current Xuriden ~$300,000/year). Pediatric orphan pricing.
peak_sales: ~$15M at 50% share of a very small market. 100 × $300,000 × 50% = $15M.
registrational_endpoint: Reduction in urinary orotic acid + normalization of hemoglobin at 52 weeks.
btd_threshold_vs_soc: Hemoglobin normalization in >80% + urine orotic acid reduction >90% vs. untreated natural history.

symptoms: Pellagra-like rash, cerebellar ataxia, psychiatric symptoms, triggered by stress/poor nutrition
treatment_goal: Prevent pellagra symptoms with nicotinamide supplementation
first_line_soc: Nicotinamide (niacin) 50-300 mg/day orally; high-protein diet to compensate for tryptophan malabsorption.
treatment_algorithm: Nicotinamide 50-300 mg/day (corrects niacin deficiency from tryptophan malabsorption). High-protein diet. Avoid triggers (sunlight, stress, low-protein diet). Prognosis excellent with treatment. Largely asymptomatic carriers do not require treatment.
us_prevalence: ~1:30,000; ~11,000 US patients identified by newborn screening (most asymptomatic). Symptomatic: <1,000.
patients_needing_tx: ~1,000 symptomatic patients on nicotinamide supplementation.
price: $500-$2,000/year (nicotinamide is OTC; no novel drug opportunity).
registrational_endpoint: Resolution of pellagra skin lesions and ataxia at 4 weeks.
btd_threshold_vs_soc: N/A (nicotinamide highly effective; no unmet need for novel therapy).

disease: Duchenne Muscular Dystrophy and Becker Muscular Dystrophy
symptoms: Progressive proximal muscle weakness, Gowers sign, calf pseudohypertrophy, cardiomyopathy
treatment_goal: Slow muscle loss, preserve ambulation, treat cardiomyopathy
first_line_soc: Corticosteroids: deflazacort (Emflaza) 0.9 mg/kg/day or prednisone 0.75 mg/kg/day to slow disease progression (standard of care for all ambulatory boys ≥2 years). Exon-skipping: eteplirsen (EXONDYS 51), golodirsen (VYONDYS 53), casimersen (AMONDYS 45), viltolarsen (VILTEPSO) per amenable mutation.
treatment_algorithm: Steroids (deflazacort/prednisone). Exon skipping (FDA-approved): eteplirsen (exon 51, ~13%), golodirsen (exon 53, ~8%), casimersen (exon 45, ~8%), viltolarsen (exon 53). Delandistrogene moxeparvovec (ELEVIDYS) gene therapy. ACE-I + beta-blocker for cardiomyopathy. Ataluren (Translarna, Europe) for nonsense mutations. Respiratory support (NIV). Physical therapy, orthoses.
us_prevalence: DMD: ~17,000-20,000 US patients; BMD: ~15,000-20,000. Total ~35,000. Pediatric predominance.
patients_needing_tx: ~15,000 DMD patients requiring pharmacotherapy (steroids + exon skipping/gene therapy as applicable).
price: $500,000-$1,000,000/year (eteplirsen ~$1M/year; ELEVIDYS ~$3.2M one-time). Orphan pricing.
peak_sales: ~$2.25B at 20% for next-generation gene therapy or exon skipping. DMD market ~$3.9B globally (2025).
registrational_endpoint: North Star Ambulatory Assessment (NSAA) change at 52 weeks; 100-meter timed test; time to loss of ambulation.
btd_threshold_vs_soc: ≥3-point NSAA improvement vs. placebo/SOC (or vs. corticosteroids alone).

symptoms: Proximal shoulder and hip-girdle weakness, slow progression, cardiomyopathy in some subtypes
treatment_goal: Slow muscle degeneration, prevent cardiac and respiratory complications
first_line_soc: None (no approved pharmacologic therapy for most subtypes). Supportive: ACE-I for cardiomyopathy; respiratory support; physical therapy.
treatment_algorithm: No FDA-approved disease-modifying pharmacotherapy. Supportive: ACE-I/beta-blocker for LGMDs with cardiomyopathy; respiratory support (NIV). Physical therapy, orthoses. Investigational: gene therapy for LGMD2E (SGT-003, Sarepta), LGMD2A (calpainopathy), others. SRP-9003 phase 3 for LGMD2E.
us_prevalence: ~30,000-50,000 US patients across all LGMD subtypes (>30 genetic subtypes; combined ~1:15,000-1:25,000).
patients_needing_tx: ~20,000 moderate-to-severe LGMD patients who would benefit from disease-modifying therapy.
price: $200,000-$500,000/year for subtype-specific gene therapy or $50,000-$150,000/year for small molecule. Orphan pricing.
peak_sales: ~$600M at 10% for a broadly applicable therapy. 20,000 × $300,000 × 10% = $600M.
registrational_endpoint: Timed 10-meter walk/run + MRC sum score change at 52 weeks vs. placebo.
btd_threshold_vs_soc: ≥20% reduction in 10-meter walk time vs. placebo; arrest of motor function decline.

symptoms: Myotonia, distal muscle weakness, cataracts, cardiac arrhythmias, excessive daytime sleepiness, cognitive impairment
treatment_goal: Reduce myotonia, prevent cardiac arrhythmias, manage hypersomnia
first_line_soc: Mexiletine for symptomatic myotonia. Pacemaker/ICD for cardiac conduction defects. Modafinil/methylphenidate for hypersomnia. No disease-modifying therapy approved.
treatment_algorithm: Mexiletine for myotonia. Cardiac: annual ECG; pacemaker for PR>200ms or bundle branch block; ICD for VT; amiodarone reserved. Hypersomnia: modafinil 200 mg/day. Respiratory: NIV for hypoventilation. Endocrine: testosterone for hypogonadism. Experimental: antisense oligonucleotides (AONs targeting DMPK expanded repeats - phase 2 trials).
us_prevalence: ~35,000-45,000 US patients (1:8,000; most common adult muscular dystrophy). DM1 (~30,000) + DM2 (~10,000-15,000).
patients_needing_tx: ~30,000 requiring active pharmacotherapy.
price: $100,000-$400,000/year for DMPK-targeting ASO or small molecule. Orphan pricing.
peak_sales: ~$600M at 10% for first disease-modifying AON therapy. 30,000 × $200,000 × 10% = $600M.
registrational_endpoint: RNA biomarker (MBNL1/CELF1 splicing ratio) + muscle function composite (GSGC) + myotonia at 52 weeks.
btd_threshold_vs_soc: ≥50% correction of DMPK-related splicing abnormalities + ≥20% reduction in myotonia rating at 52 weeks vs. placebo.

symptoms: Facial weakness, scapular winging, foot drop, progressive limb-girdle weakness, asymmetric pattern
treatment_goal: Slow DUX4-mediated muscle degeneration, preserve function
first_line_soc: None (no approved pharmacologic treatment). Physical therapy, scapular fixation surgery, ankle-foot orthoses.
treatment_algorithm: No FDA-approved pharmacotherapy. Physical therapy (core strengthening, scapular stabilization). Scapular fixation surgery for severe winged scapula. AFOs for foot drop. Lovsanlimab (anti-DUX4 - phase 3 trial). SRP-9001/losmapimod (p38 MAPK inhibitor - phase 3 investigational). Losmapimod showed signal in FSHD1.
us_prevalence: ~15,000-35,000 US patients (1:8,333-1:20,000; third most common MD). FSHD1 (~95%) and FSHD2.
patients_needing_tx: ~20,000 with moderate-severe disease who would benefit from pharmacotherapy.
price: $50,000-$150,000/year for DUX4 suppressor or p38 inhibitor. Orphan pricing.
peak_sales: ~$400M at 20% for first approved DUX4-targeted therapy. 20,000 × $100,000 × 20% = $400M.
registrational_endpoint: Reachable Workspace (RWS) + MRI fat fraction change at 52 weeks vs. placebo.
btd_threshold_vs_soc: ≥15% relative improvement in RWS + ≥2% reduction in MRI fat fraction vs. placebo.

symptoms: Neonatal hypotonia, proximal weakness, respiratory failure, skeletal deformities, feeding difficulties
treatment_goal: Improve motor function and respiratory support
first_line_soc: None (no approved pharmacologic treatment). Supportive: respiratory support (BiPAP/ventilator), physical/occupational therapy, nutritional support.
treatment_algorithm: Respiratory: NIV or invasive ventilation. Physical therapy, orthoses. Gastrostomy for feeding. No FDA-approved pharmacotherapy for most congenital myopathies (RYR1, ACTA1, MTM1, NEB, TPM mutations). Tamoxifen and NAC investigated for SELENON myopathy. Pyridostigmine off-label for some. Gene therapy (MTM1/XLMTM - investigational - FDA declined Aspiro gene therapy).
us_prevalence: ~20,000-40,000 US patients across all congenital myopathy subtypes (1:26,000 births). Pediatric + adult.
patients_needing_tx: ~10,000 with severe disease who would benefit from disease-modifying pharmacotherapy.
price: $2,000,000-$4,000,000 one-time for gene therapy (MTM1 Aspiro - compassionate) or $100,000-$200,000/year for small molecule. Orphan pricing.
tam_gross: 10,000 × $150,000/year = ~$1.5B (small molecule) or incident cases gene therapy
registrational_endpoint: Motor function measure (MFM-32) change at 52 weeks + ventilator-free time.
btd_threshold_vs_soc: ≥4-point MFM-32 improvement vs. placebo + reduced ventilator dependence.

symptoms: Early joint contractures (elbows, neck), slowly progressive humeroperoneal weakness, life-threatening cardiac arrhythmias/sudden death
treatment_goal: Prevent sudden cardiac death, slow muscle wasting
first_line_soc: Pacemaker/ICD for cardiac arrhythmias (primary SoC - device, not pharmacologic). ACE inhibitors or ARBs for cardiomyopathy prevention.
treatment_algorithm: Cardiac: pacemaker for heart block; ICD for ventricular arrhythmias; ACE-I/ARB for dilated cardiomyopathy; heart transplant for end-stage. Musculoskeletal: physical therapy for contractures; orthoses. No FDA-approved pharmacologic disease-modifying treatment. Gene therapy investigational (EMD/LMNA).
us_prevalence: ~3,000-5,000 US patients (X-linked EDMD ~1:100,000; autosomal LMNA-related ~1:70,000).
patients_needing_tx: ~4,000 patients requiring cardiac management and potential disease-modifying therapy.
price: $200,000-$500,000/year for LMNA-targeted therapy (e.g., NAC, protein aggregation inhibitor). Orphan pricing.
peak_sales: ~$120M at 10% for a novel LMNA-targeted agent. 4,000 × $300,000 × 10% = $120M.
registrational_endpoint: Composite of cardiac hospitalization, ICD firing, and muscle function decline (MRC sum score) at 36 months.
btd_threshold_vs_soc: ≥30% reduction in composite cardiac events vs. ACE-I/ICD alone.

symptoms: Episodic flaccid weakness (minutes to days), hypo- or hyperkalemia during attacks, triggering by carbohydrates/rest/cold
treatment_goal: Prevent paralytic attacks, normalize potassium balance
first_line_soc: Hypokalemic PP: dichlorphenamide (Keveyis) FDA-approved for HypoPP types 1/2 and hyperPP, 50-200 mg/day. Acetazolamide also used. Avoid triggers.
treatment_algorithm: HypoPP: acute - oral/IV potassium chloride; prevention - dichlorphenamide (Keveyis) or acetazolamide. Thyrotoxic PP: treat hyperthyroidism. HyperPP: high-carbohydrate diet; dichlorphenamide; thiazide diuretics; mexiletine for myotonia. Andersen-Tawil: dichlorphenamide + cardiac monitoring.
us_prevalence: ~1:100,000 (~3,300 US patients all subtypes combined); HypoPP most common ~2,500.
patients_needing_tx: ~2,500 patients requiring prophylactic pharmacotherapy (Keveyis or acetazolamide).
price: $150,000-$300,000/year (Keveyis ~$150,000/year). Orphan pricing.
peak_sales: ~$100M at 20% for improved/targeted sodium channel modifier. 2,500 × $200,000 × 20% = $100M.
registrational_endpoint: Attack-free days per month + attack duration at 9 weeks (as in Keveyis trials).
btd_threshold_vs_soc: ≥50% reduction in attack days/month vs. placebo (Keveyis achieved 2.7 vs. 5.4 attacks/month).

symptoms: Muscle stiffness after rest, warm-up phenomenon, grip myotonia, painless muscle hypertrophy
treatment_goal: Reduce myotonia, improve functional mobility
first_line_soc: Mexiletine (sodium channel blocker) 200 mg TID - most effective pharmacologic treatment. Carbamazepine or acetazolamide as alternatives.
treatment_algorithm: Mexiletine 150-300 mg TID (first-line): reduces myotonia; randomized trial proven. Alternatives: carbamazepine, phenytoin, acetazolamide, quinine (historical). Avoid triggers (cold, sudden movement after rest). Muscle warm-up exercises. No FDA-specifically approved drug for myotonia congenita (mexiletine is FDA-approved for arrhythmia, used off-label).
us_prevalence: ~1:23,000 (Thomsen) to ~1:50,000 (Becker); ~6,000-13,000 US patients combined.
patients_needing_tx: ~8,000 patients requiring mexiletine or alternative.
price: $20,000-$50,000/year for novel ClC-1 chloride channel modulator. Orphan pricing.
peak_sales: ~$48M at 20% share for novel ClC-1-specific agent. 8,000 × $30,000 × 20% = $48M.
registrational_endpoint: Myotonia rating scale (NIHSS or ICMM scale) + muscle relaxation time (quantitative EMG) at 12 weeks.
btd_threshold_vs_soc: ≥40% reduction in myotonia rating vs. placebo (vs. mexiletine ~60% reduction).

symptoms: Reading difficulty, poor phonological awareness, spelling errors, slow reading speed despite normal IQ
treatment_goal: Improve reading accuracy and fluency via structured literacy interventions
first_line_soc: None (no pharmacologic SoC). Evidence-based structured literacy intervention (Orton-Gillingham, Wilson Reading System) is the primary treatment.
treatment_algorithm: None pharmacologic. Structured literacy instruction (phonics-based, multisensory approaches). Assistive technology (text-to-speech). Accommodations (extra time, audio books). If co-morbid ADHD: stimulant treatment may improve attention and learning. No approved drug for core dyslexia.
us_prevalence: ~15-20% of US population; ~8-10 million school-age children with significant dyslexia.
patients_needing_tx: 0 for pharmacologic treatment (no pharmacologic SoC). ~8 million needing educational intervention.
price: $5,000-$20,000/year for a hypothetical cognitive-enhancing agent targeting phonological processing.
peak_sales: ~$800M at 1% penetration for a novel neurocognitive drug if approved. 8,000,000 × $10,000 × 1% = $800M.
registrational_endpoint: Phonological Awareness composite score (CTOPP-2) + oral reading fluency change at 26 weeks.
btd_threshold_vs_soc: ≥10-point CTOPP-2 composite improvement + ≥5 words/minute reading fluency gain vs. placebo.

symptoms: Inattention, hyperactivity, impulsivity, functional impairment in school/home
treatment_goal: Reduce ADHD symptoms, improve academic and social function
first_line_soc: Stimulants: methylphenidate (Ritalin, Concerta) 0.3-1 mg/kg/day or amphetamine salts (Adderall) 5-30 mg/day. First-line in children ≥6 years combined with behavioral therapy.
treatment_algorithm: Stimulants (MPH or amphetamine) - first-line. Behavioral therapy (behavior management training, school-based interventions) - key for preschoolers. Non-stimulants (atomoxetine, viloxazine/Qelbree, guanfacine/Intuniv, clonidine/Kapvay) - alternative or adjunctive. Combination pharmacotherapy for incomplete response. School accommodations (IEP/504 plans).
us_prevalence: ~9-11% of US children (6-17 years); ~6-7 million children with ADHD. Adult prevalence ~4-5%.
patients_needing_tx: ~4-5 million children on pharmacotherapy for ADHD in US.
price: $3,000-$10,000/year for novel non-stimulant or long-acting formulation.
peak_sales: ~$2.25B at 10% for novel mechanism (e.g., viloxazine/Qelbree ~$800M current market). 4,500,000 × $5,000 × 10% = $2.25B.
registrational_endpoint: ADHD-RS-5 total score change from baseline at 6 weeks + CGI-I response rate.
btd_threshold_vs_soc: ≥5-point ADHD-RS-5 improvement vs. placebo; CGI-I response >60% vs. ~30% placebo.

symptoms: Social communication deficits, repetitive behaviors, restricted interests, sensory sensitivities
treatment_goal: Reduce core symptoms and irritability, improve adaptive function
first_line_soc: Behavioral interventions (ABA therapy) are primary SoC. Pharmacologic: risperidone (FDA-approved for irritability in ASD ≥5 years) or aripiprazole (Abilify, ≥6 years) for irritability/aggression; not core ASD symptoms.
treatment_algorithm: ABA and behavioral therapy (core). Social skills training. Speech/OT therapy. Pharmacologic for symptoms: risperidone 0.5-3 mg/day or aripiprazole 2-15 mg/day for irritability; SSRIs for anxiety/OCD features; stimulants/atomoxetine for co-morbid ADHD; melatonin for sleep. Bumetanide investigational (EU trials). CM-AT, leucovorin, oxytocin - all negative phase 3.
us_prevalence: ~1 in 36 US children (~2.8%); ~5.4 million children and adolescents with ASD. Prevalence increasing.
patients_needing_tx: ~2 million children on pharmacotherapy for ASD-related symptoms.
price: $5,000-$20,000/year for novel core ASD therapeutic.
peak_sales: ~$2B at 10% for first core ASD disease-modifying drug. 2,000,000 × $10,000 × 10% = $2B.
registrational_endpoint: Aberrant Behavior Checklist-Social Withdrawal (ABC-SW) + SRS-2 Social Responsiveness Scale change at 12-16 weeks.
btd_threshold_vs_soc: ≥5-point ABC-SW reduction + ≥10-point SRS-2 improvement vs. placebo at 12 weeks.

symptoms: Below-average intellectual function (IQ <70), adaptive behavioral deficits, present from childhood
treatment_goal: Improve adaptive function; treat comorbid conditions (ADHD, epilepsy, ASD)
first_line_soc: None (no pharmacologic treatment for underlying ID). Symptom-directed: stimulants/atomoxetine for ADHD; antipsychotics for behavioral dysregulation; anticonvulsants for epilepsy.
treatment_algorithm: Early intervention programs (applied behavior analysis, speech therapy, OT, PT). Special education. Pharmacologic for comorbidities: anticonvulsants, antipsychotics for behavior, stimulants for ADHD. Caregiver support. No disease-modifying pharmacotherapy for ID itself.
us_prevalence: ~1-3% of US population; ~6-9 million Americans with ID. ~2-3 million children.
patients_needing_tx: ~2 million children on pharmacotherapy for ID comorbidities.
price: $10,000-$30,000/year for a syndrome-specific cognitive enhancer (e.g., for Fragile X, Down syndrome-associated ID).
tam_gross: Syndrome-specific: 200,000 × $20,000 = ~$4B (Down syndrome + Fragile X ID subsets)
registrational_endpoint: Vineland Adaptive Behavior Scales (VABS) adaptive behavior composite + cognitive battery at 52 weeks.
btd_threshold_vs_soc: ≥5-point VABS composite improvement vs. placebo at 52 weeks in relevant syndrome cohort.

symptoms: Facial dysmorphia, growth retardation, CNS abnormalities, cognitive and behavioral deficits
treatment_goal: Manage behavioral/cognitive symptoms; no curative treatment
first_line_soc: None pharmacologic for core FAS. Stimulants (methylphenidate) for co-morbid ADHD symptoms; antipsychotics for severe behavioral dysregulation.
treatment_algorithm: No disease-modifying pharmacotherapy. Behavioral interventions (ABA, parent management training). Stimulants for ADHD features. Antipsychotics (risperidone) for behavioral dysregulation. School-based supports. Early intervention programs. Social services. Choline supplementation (investigational - trials showing some benefit).
us_prevalence: FAS: ~40,000-50,000 US individuals; full FASD spectrum: ~1-5% of US school-age children (~750,000-1,000,000).
patients_needing_tx: ~200,000 on pharmacotherapy for FASD behavioral symptoms.
price: $5,000-$15,000/year for a novel neuroprotective or choline analog.
registrational_endpoint: Vineland Adaptive Behavior Scales + attention composite (NEPSY-II) at 52 weeks.
btd_threshold_vs_soc: ≥5-point VABS composite improvement + ≥10% attention improvement vs. placebo.

symptoms: Jaundice (yellow skin/sclera), elevated total serum bilirubin in first weeks of life
treatment_goal: Reduce bilirubin levels to prevent kernicterus
first_line_soc: Phototherapy (bili-lights) for TSB approaching exchange transfusion thresholds per Bhutani nomogram. IV immunoglobulin (IVIG) for hemolytic disease (Rh, ABO incompatibility).
treatment_algorithm: Phototherapy (overhead or bili-blanket) - first-line. IVIG 0.5-1 g/kg for isoimmune hemolytic disease. Exchange transfusion for extreme hyperbilirubinemia. Phenobarbital for Crigler-Najjar type II (oral induction). Tin-mesoporphyrin (investigational heme oxygenase inhibitor). Crigler-Najjar type I: liver transplantation.
us_prevalence: ~60% of term neonates develop jaundice; ~2-5% require phototherapy (~120,000-240,000/year). Pathologic causes: ~5-10% of those treated.
patients_needing_tx: ~180,000/year requiring phototherapy; ~12,000 receiving IVIG; ~500-1,000 exchange transfusions/year.
price: $5,000-$20,000/course for novel heme oxygenase inhibitor or enzyme-replacement for Crigler-Najjar.
peak_sales: ~$180M at 10% for novel pharmacologic adjunct to phototherapy. 180,000 × $10,000 × 10% = $180M.
registrational_endpoint: Rate of exchange transfusion avoidance; TSB reduction rate (mg/dL/hour) vs. phototherapy alone.
btd_threshold_vs_soc: ≥30% reduction in exchange transfusion rate vs. standard phototherapy.

symptoms: Choreoathetosis, hearing loss, upward gaze palsy, dental enamel dysplasia (sequelae of neonatal kernicterus)
treatment_goal: Prevent kernicterus; manage established neurological sequelae
first_line_soc: Prevention: aggressive phototherapy and exchange transfusion for hyperbilirubinemia in neonates. No pharmacologic treatment for established chronic encephalopathy.
treatment_algorithm: Prevention (primary): phototherapy (bili-blanket, phototherapy lights); exchange transfusion for severe cases; IVIG for hemolytic disease. Established CBE: no curative treatment. Management: hearing aids/cochlear implants; dystonia treatment (trihexyphenidyl, baclofen, DBS); PT/OT/speech therapy.
us_prevalence: ~25,000-30,000 US patients with chronic bilirubin encephalopathy/kernicterus spectrum disorders. Largely preventable.
patients_needing_tx: ~25,000 living patients with neurological sequelae; pharmacologic need for dystonia management. New cases ~500-1,000/year (preventable).
price: $20,000-$50,000/year for dystonia/motor function management. Neuroprotection: $50,000-$200,000 for novel prophylactic.
peak_sales: ~$75M at 10% for novel dystonia management agent. 25,000 × $30,000 × 10% = $75M.
registrational_endpoint: Burke-Fahn-Marsden Dystonia Rating Scale (BFMDRS) improvement + hearing outcomes at 52 weeks.
btd_threshold_vs_soc: ≥30% BFMDRS improvement vs. current management.

symptoms: Jitteriness, lethargy, apnea, cyanosis, seizures; low blood glucose (<47 mg/dL) in neonate
treatment_goal: Restore euglycemia promptly to prevent brain injury
first_line_soc: Oral feeding (breast milk/formula); buccal dextrose gel (40% dextrose, 200 mg/kg) for mild-moderate neonatal hypoglycemia; IV dextrose for severe.
treatment_algorithm: Oral feeding first. Buccal dextrose 40% gel (GlucoGel/Instaglucose) 200 mg/kg for BG <47 mg/dL. IV D10W 2 mL/kg bolus + continuous infusion for failure to respond. Diazoxide for congenital hyperinsulinism (1-15 mg/kg/day). Octreotide for refractory hyperinsulinism. Pancreatectomy for focal KATP-channel defects.
us_prevalence: ~10-15% of all neonates (~350,000-600,000/year); symptomatic or requiring intervention ~50,000-80,000/year. Congenital hyperinsulinism: ~800-1,000 cases/year.
patients_needing_tx: ~65,000/year requiring active treatment (dextrose gel + IV glucose). ~1,000 CHI patients requiring diazoxide/octreotide.
price: $3,000-$15,000/course for novel glucose-stabilizing neonatal agent; $100,000-$200,000/year for CHI (diazoxide/octreotide).
peak_sales: ~$100M at 20% for novel dextrose alternative or CHI-targeted agent. 65,000 × $5,000 × 20% + 1,000 × $150,000 × 30% = $65M + $45M = $110M.
registrational_endpoint: Blood glucose normalization (>47 mg/dL) at 30 minutes without IV requirement + recurrence rate at 6 hours.
btd_threshold_vs_soc: ≥15% more infants achieving BG normalization without IV at 30 min vs. placebo (dextrose gel achieved 73% vs. 54% IV avoidance).

symptoms: Neonatal opioid withdrawal (NAS/NOWS): irritability, poor feeding, tremors, seizures; fetal alcohol spectrum; cocaine/cannabis exposure effects
treatment_goal: Manage withdrawal symptoms, optimize neurodevelopmental outcomes
first_line_soc: Neonatal opioid withdrawal syndrome (NOWS): non-pharmacologic (swaddling, low stimulation) first-line; oral morphine or methadone (0.05-0.2 mg/kg/dose Q3-4h) for severe NOWS; clonidine as adjunct.
treatment_algorithm: NOWS: Finnegan score-guided therapy. Non-pharmacologic: skin-to-skin, rooming-in, swaddling, pacifier. Pharmacologic: oral morphine 0.02-0.1 mg/kg/dose or methadone (longer-acting); clonidine as adjunct for non-opioid symptoms. Buprenorphine (Subutex oral) for NOWS (FDA compassionate use). Phenobarbital for seizures. Maternal MAT (methadone/buprenorphine) continuation.
us_prevalence: ~30,000-50,000 neonates with NOWS/year in US (opioid epidemic). ~400,000-500,000 neonates with any prenatal drug exposure.
patients_needing_tx: ~40,000/year with NOWS requiring pharmacotherapy.
price: $3,000-$10,000/course for novel neonatal opioid withdrawal management agent.
registrational_endpoint: Peak Finnegan NAS score + length of hospital stay + duration of pharmacotherapy vs. morphine SOC.
btd_threshold_vs_soc: ≥2-day reduction in hospital LOS + ≥30% reduction in peak Finnegan score vs. standard oral morphine.

symptoms: Hyperglycemia, glucosuria, dehydration, weight loss, polyuria in VLBW preterm neonate
treatment_goal: Normalize blood glucose without hypoglycemia risk
first_line_soc: Reduce glucose infusion rate (GIR). If persistent: insulin infusion (regular insulin 0.01-0.1 U/kg/hour IV continuous) in VLBW neonates.
treatment_algorithm: Reduce dextrose concentration/rate. Continuous insulin infusion (0.01-0.1 U/kg/h) for persistent hyperglycemia >180-250 mg/dL with glucosuria. Monitor glucose Q30-60 min during insulin. Nutritional management.
us_prevalence: ~15-20% of VLBW neonates (<1,500g); ~20,000-25,000 cases/year in US NICUs.
patients_needing_tx: ~20,000/year requiring insulin infusion or glucose management.
price: $2,000-$8,000/course for neonatal-specific insulin formulation or glucose-stabilizing agent.
peak_sales: ~$20M at 20% for novel neonatal glucose management agent. 20,000 × $5,000 × 20% = $20M.
registrational_endpoint: Time to euglycemia (BG 70-180 mg/dL) + rate of hypoglycemic episodes vs. standard insulin dosing.
btd_threshold_vs_soc: ≥2-hour faster glucose normalization + ≥40% reduction in hypoglycemic episodes vs. standard insulin protocol.

symptoms: Irritability, hypotonia, poor feeding, polyuria, constipation, vomiting, lethargy in neonate
treatment_goal: Reduce serum calcium to prevent nephrocalcinosis and cardiac arrhythmias
first_line_soc: IV normal saline hydration + furosemide (calciuresis). Corticosteroids for hypervitaminosis D or sarcoid. Cinacalcet for neonatal severe hyperparathyroidism (off-label). Pamidronate for malignancy-related.
treatment_algorithm: Hydration + furosemide (first-line). Restrict calcium/Vitamin D intake. Corticosteroids (hydrocortisone or prednisolone) for Vitamin D toxicity or granulomatous disease. Cinacalcet (calcimimetic) for neonatal severe primary hyperparathyroidism (NSHPT - CaSR loss of function). Parathyroidectomy for NSHPT. Bisphosphonates (pamidronate) for hypercalcemia of malignancy.
us_prevalence: ~5,000-10,000 neonates/year with significant hypercalcemia; rare severe forms (NSHPT) ~50-100/year.
patients_needing_tx: ~7,500/year requiring pharmacologic intervention.
price: $5,000-$30,000/course for NSHPT (cinacalcet generic now); $100,000-$300,000/year for gene therapy for severe congenital forms.
peak_sales: ~$15M at 20% for novel calcimimetic or targeted agent. 7,500 × $10,000 × 20% = $15M.
registrational_endpoint: Normalization of serum calcium (<11 mg/dL) and PTH within 72 hours of treatment.
btd_threshold_vs_soc: Calcium normalization in >90% within 72 hours vs. ~60% with hydration/furosemide alone.

symptoms: Jitteriness, seizures, apnea, prolonged QTc, carpopedal spasm in neonate
treatment_goal: Normalize ionized calcium, prevent seizures and cardiac arrhythmias
first_line_soc: IV calcium gluconate 10% (200 mg/kg/dose) for symptomatic hypocalcemia; oral calcium supplementation + vitamin D for maintenance.
treatment_algorithm: Acute: IV calcium gluconate 200-400 mg/kg/day continuous or intermittent infusion. Maintenance: oral calcium salts 50-75 mg elemental Ca/kg/day + vitamin D (calcitriol 0.05-0.1 µg/kg/day for hypoparathyroidism). Magnesium replacement if concurrent hypomagnesemia. PTH-dependent: PTH (recombinant teriparatide off-label) or calcitriol.
us_prevalence: ~5-10% of neonates (early-onset); late-onset hypoCalcemia ~0.5% of neonates. ~100,000-200,000/year; symptomatic severe ~10,000-20,000/year.
patients_needing_tx: ~15,000/year with symptomatic neonatal hypocalcemia requiring IV calcium + calcitriol.
price: $2,000-$10,000/course for novel calcium homeostasis agent.
registrational_endpoint: Time to ionized calcium normalization (>1.1 mmol/L) + seizure-free rate at 72 hours.
btd_threshold_vs_soc: Calcium normalization in >95% within 4 hours + zero seizure recurrence vs. ~80% with current calcium gluconate.

symptoms: Seizures, lethargy, cerebral edema, apnea in neonate; serum Na <135 mEq/L
treatment_goal: Correct hyponatremia to prevent cerebral edema and brain injury
first_line_soc: IV 3% NaCl (3-5 mL/kg over 10-15 min) for symptomatic/severe hyponatremia; fluid restriction for dilutional hyponatremia.
treatment_algorithm: Acute symptomatic (Na <125 + seizures): hypertonic saline 3% NaCl 3-5 mL/kg IV to raise Na by 5-10 mEq/L acutely. SIADH: fluid restriction; fludrocortisone if refractory. Salt supplementation for renal losses. DDAVP for DI-related (when treating hypernatremia, not applicable here). Tolvaptan in older patients (not approved neonates).
us_prevalence: ~5-10% of sick neonates (SIADH, fluid overload common in NICU); symptomatic severe ~5,000-10,000/year.
patients_needing_tx: ~7,500/year requiring pharmacologic management.
price: Minimal - electrolyte correction. Tolvaptan analog: $5,000-$20,000/course.
registrational_endpoint: Serum sodium normalization (130-145 mEq/L) within 24 hours without seizure recurrence.
btd_threshold_vs_soc: Sodium normalization in >85% within 24 hours without neurological sequelae vs. standard hypertonic saline (~70% success).

symptoms: Fever, lethargy, irritability, poor feeding, seizures, intracranial hemorrhage risk in neonate
treatment_goal: Correct serum sodium slowly (≤10-12 mEq/L/day) to prevent cerebral edema
first_line_soc: None pharmacologic; treatment is controlled oral hydration (increased breastfeeding frequency or formula supplementation) or IV hypotonic saline for severe cases.
treatment_algorithm: Mild: increased feeding frequency (breastfeeding support, formula supplementation). Moderate/severe: controlled IV fluid replacement with D5W or 0.45% saline at rate to lower Na ≤10-12 mEq/L/day. Lactation support. If central DI: vasopressin/DDAVP. No approved pharmacologic first-line beyond hydration.
us_prevalence: ~2-5% of neonates (predominantly breastfed); ~60,000-120,000 neonates/year with any hypernatremia; severe (>160 mEq/L) ~10,000/year.
patients_needing_tx: ~10,000/year with severe hypernatremia requiring IV management.
price: Minimal pharmacologic TAM (management is hydration). DDAVP for DI: $1,000-$5,000/course.
peak_sales: ~$5M (minimal commercial opportunity for novel drug in this indication).
registrational_endpoint: Rate of sodium normalization without neurological events (IVH, seizures) at 72 hours.
btd_threshold_vs_soc: N/A (management is non-pharmacologic hydration-based).

symptoms: Dysuria, frequency, fever, flank pain, poor feeding, foul-smelling urine in infants
treatment_goal: Eradicate uropathogens, prevent pyelonephritis and renal scarring
first_line_soc: Amoxicillin-clavulanate or trimethoprim-sulfamethoxazole (TMP-SMX) PO for uncomplicated UTI 7-14 days. Ceftriaxone IV/IM for febrile UTI in infants <2 months or vomiting.
treatment_algorithm: Outpatient: TMP-SMX or amoxicillin-clavulanate 7-10 days. Febrile/complicated: cephalosporin (cefdinir, cephalexin) or ceftriaxone parenterally. Urine culture-guided de-escalation. VUR workup post-UTI. Low-dose prophylaxis (TMP-SMX or nitrofurantoin) for recurrent UTI or high-grade VUR.
us_prevalence: ~500,000-1,000,000 pediatric UTI episodes/year in US; 8% of girls and 2% of boys by age 7.
patients_needing_tx: ~750,000/year requiring antibiotic treatment.
price: $200-$1,000/course (generic antibiotics dominate); novel: $5,000-$10,000/year for biologic prophylaxis.
peak_sales: ~$50M at 10% for novel UTI prophylaxis agent. 50,000 × $3,000 × 10% + small acute premium = $50M.
registrational_endpoint: Clinical cure rate (symptom resolution + negative culture) at 14 days + recurrence rate at 6 months.
btd_threshold_vs_soc: Culture-confirmed cure ≥90% at 14 days; UTI recurrence <10% at 6 months vs. ~25-30% recurrence rate.

disease: Bacterial Meningitis in Infants Over 3 Months of Age
symptoms: Fever, severe headache, neck stiffness, photophobia, altered consciousness, bulging fontanelle (infant)
treatment_goal: Eradicate meningeal bacteria, prevent neurological sequelae
first_line_soc: Ceftriaxone 100 mg/kg/day IV (or cefotaxime) + dexamethasone 0.15 mg/kg q6h x 4 days (for H. influenzae and S. pneumoniae). Add vancomycin for cephalosporin-resistant pneumococcus.
treatment_algorithm: Empiric: ceftriaxone + vancomycin (for CRKP or PNSP). Steroids: dexamethasone IV x 4 days to reduce neurological sequelae (deafness, brain damage). Listeria coverage: add ampicillin for immunocompromised or neonates. N. meningitidis: high-dose penicillin G if sensitive. Duration 7-21 days per organism. Supportive: ICP management, anti-seizure prophylaxis if needed.
us_prevalence: ~5,000-7,000 pediatric bacterial meningitis cases/year in US (post-vaccine era); mortality ~5-10%; significant morbidity.
patients_needing_tx: ~6,000/year requiring IV antibiotics + corticosteroids.
price: $20,000-$60,000/course for novel adjunctive neuroprotective agent.
registrational_endpoint: Neurological sequelae rate (hearing loss, cognitive deficit) at 3 months post-treatment vs. dexamethasone alone.
btd_threshold_vs_soc: ≥25% absolute reduction in neurological sequelae vs. standard ceftriaxone + dexamethasone (~20-30% sequelae rate currently).

symptoms: Migratory polyarthritis, carditis, Sydenham chorea, erythema marginatum, subcutaneous nodules post-strep throat
treatment_goal: Eradicate GAS, prevent rheumatic heart disease recurrence
first_line_soc: Penicillin V (or benzathine penicillin G IM single dose) for acute GAS eradication. Aspirin for arthritis/fever. Secondary prophylaxis: benzathine penicillin G IM q3-4 weeks for 5-10 years.
treatment_algorithm: Acute: amoxicillin or penicillin V x 10 days (GAS eradication); high-dose aspirin for carditis/arthritis; corticosteroids for severe carditis. Chorea: haloperidol or valproate. Secondary prophylaxis: benzathine penicillin G IM 1.2M units q3-4 weeks (primary prevention of recurrence). Duration: 5-10 years or lifelong if significant valvular disease.
us_prevalence: ~5,000-10,000 acute RF cases/year in US (rare with antibiotic access); rheumatic heart disease ~50,000 US patients.
patients_needing_tx: ~50,000 patients on long-term benzathine penicillin prophylaxis for rheumatic heart disease.
price: $1,000-$5,000/year for improved GAS vaccine or long-acting penicillin formulation.
registrational_endpoint: GAS eradication rate (throat culture at day 14) + recurrence-free survival at 5 years.
btd_threshold_vs_soc: GAS eradication >95% at day 14; recurrence-free survival >95% at 5 years with prophylaxis.

disease: Occult Bacteremia and Fever Without Apparent Source in Infants and Young Children
symptoms: High fever (>39°C), well-appearing, no localizing signs, typically 3-36 months age
treatment_goal: Identify high-risk patients, prevent occult bacteremia complications
first_line_soc: Risk stratification (Rochester/Philadelphia criteria for neonates; AUC score for older infants). High-risk: empiric ceftriaxone 50 mg/kg IM/IV. Low-risk: observation without antibiotics.
treatment_algorithm: Age <28 days: full sepsis workup + empiric antibiotics (ampicillin + gentamicin ± cefotaxime). Age 28-90 days: risk stratification; ceftriaxone IM/IV if high risk. Age 3-36 months: reassess; WBC >15,000 with no focus → ceftriaxone empirically. Return precautions. Influenza testing; RSV/enterovirus PCR. Post-PCV13 era: occult bacteremia dramatically reduced.
us_prevalence: ~1 million febrile infants evaluated/year; occult bacteremia <1% post-vaccine (~5,000-10,000 bacteremia cases/year in children <3 years).
patients_needing_tx: ~200,000/year receive empiric antibiotics for fever without source.
price: Not applicable - management is existing antibiotics and diagnostic risk stratification.
tam_gross: ~$50M/year for novel biomarker-guided antibiotic protocols (diagnostics-driven, not drug TAM).
peak_sales: ~$10M for novel biomarker diagnostic + targeted antibiotic bundle. Primarily a diagnostic opportunity.
registrational_endpoint: N/A (no specific drug endpoint; management protocol study).
btd_threshold_vs_soc: N/A.

symptoms: Delayed speech development, poor response to sound, academic difficulties
treatment_goal: Restore hearing, optimize speech/language development
first_line_soc: None pharmacologic (hearing aids or cochlear implantation are primary interventions). Early fitting of hearing aids or cochlear implant surgery.
treatment_algorithm: Hearing amplification: hearing aids for mild-moderate loss; cochlear implant for severe-profound sensorineural loss. BAHA (bone-anchored hearing aids) for conductive loss. Early intervention/aural habilitation. Gene therapy (OTOFERLIN - Akouos/Regeneron AK-OTOF phase 1/2, DB-OTO - Decibel in trials). No approved pharmacologic treatment.
us_prevalence: ~3/1,000 newborns with significant hearing loss; ~2-3 million children with hearing impairment in US. SNHI in children ~400,000 severe/profound.
patients_needing_tx: ~400,000 with severe-profound SNHI; ~20,000 gene therapy candidates (otoferlin DFNB9, etc.).
price: $200,000-$500,000 one-time for gene therapy (AK-OTOF, DB-OTO). Pediatric orphan pricing for gene-specific subtypes.
peak_sales: ~$700M at 10% annual penetration rate for gene-based hearing restoration. 20,000 × $350,000 × 10% = $700M.
registrational_endpoint: Improvement in auditory brainstem response (ABR) thresholds ≥15 dB + speech perception scores at 12 months post-treatment.
btd_threshold_vs_soc: ABR threshold improvement ≥20 dB in >60% + open-set speech understanding >20% in previously deaf patients.

symptoms: Delayed speech, articulation errors, stuttering, language comprehension/expression deficits
treatment_goal: Improve speech intelligibility, language function, communication
first_line_soc: None pharmacologic. Speech-language therapy (SLT) is the primary intervention; AAC devices for nonverbal children.
treatment_algorithm: Speech-language pathology (SLP) intervention. Augmentative and alternative communication (AAC) for minimally verbal children. If co-morbid ADHD: stimulants. Early intervention programs. No FDA-approved pharmacotherapy for communication disorders per se.
us_prevalence: ~8-9% of children have communication disorders; ~5-6 million children in US. Includes speech sound disorders, language disorders, stuttering.
patients_needing_tx: ~5 million children needing SLP intervention; ~200,000 on pharmacotherapy for comorbidities.
price: $5,000-$15,000/year for hypothetical speech-enhancing pharmacotherapy.
peak_sales: ~$200M at 10% for a novel cognitive-speech enhancer for language disorders. 200,000 × $10,000 × 10% = $200M.
registrational_endpoint: Peabody Picture Vocabulary Test (PPVT) + Clinical Evaluation of Language Fundamentals (CELF) score change at 52 weeks.
btd_threshold_vs_soc: ≥10-point CELF core language score improvement vs. placebo at 52 weeks.

symptoms: Hoarseness, stridor, airway obstruction, recurrent laryngeal papillomas from HPV 6/11
treatment_goal: Maintain patent airway, reduce recurrence, achieve remission
first_line_soc: Surgical debridement (microlaryngoscopy with microdebrider or laser) repeated as needed. Adjuvant: bevacizumab (Avastin) intralesional injection FDA-designated Breakthrough for RRP.
treatment_algorithm: Primary: serial surgical debridement. Intralesional bevacizumab (anti-VEGF) as adjuvant significantly reduces recurrence interval. Cidofovir (intralesional, off-label) - toxicity concerns. Interferon-alpha (off-label). Pembrolizumab (anti-PD1) investigational. HPV vaccine (Gardasil) reduces incidence. Photodynamic therapy (PDT) in select centers.
us_prevalence: ~15,000-20,000 US patients with RRP (juvenile and adult onset); juvenile ~3,000-5,000 active patients requiring surgery. Incidence ~4/100,000.
patients_needing_tx: ~15,000 patients requiring adjuvant pharmacotherapy to reduce surgical frequency.
price: $20,000-$80,000/year for intralesional bevacizumab or systemic anti-HPV agent. Orphan pricing.
peak_sales: ~$180M at 30% for bevacizumab adjuvant + novel antiviral. 15,000 × $40,000 × 30% = $180M.
registrational_endpoint: Time to next surgical procedure (surgical-free interval) + Derkay severity score at 12 months vs. surgery alone.
btd_threshold_vs_soc: ≥3 months prolongation of surgical interval + ≥2-point Derkay score improvement vs. surgery alone.

symptoms: Recurrent epistaxis, nasal obstruction, nasal mass in adolescent males
treatment_goal: Achieve complete tumor control, prevent catastrophic hemorrhage
first_line_soc: Surgical resection is primary SoC (endoscopic or open). Preoperative embolization to reduce bleeding. Radiotherapy for unresectable or recurrent disease.
treatment_algorithm: Preoperative embolization (angiography-guided) 24-48h before surgery. Surgical resection (endoscopic preferred). Radiation (definitive for skull base extension). Flutamide and other anti-androgens investigated (JNA is androgen-sensitive, expressing androgen receptors). No FDA-approved pharmacologic therapy.
us_prevalence: ~1:5,000-1:60,000 adolescent males; ~1,000-3,000 new cases/year in US.
patients_needing_tx: ~2,000/year requiring surgery ± adjuvant pharmacotherapy.
price: $5,000-$20,000/course for anti-androgen or VEGFR inhibitor as neoadjuvant/adjuvant.
registrational_endpoint: Tumor volume reduction (MRI-measured) pre-operatively at 6 weeks vs. observation.
btd_threshold_vs_soc: ≥30% tumor volume reduction pre-op + ≥50% reduction in intraoperative blood loss vs. embolization alone.

symptoms: Multiple café-au-lait spots, neurofibromas, Lisch nodules, optic glioma, learning disabilities, plexiform neurofibromas
treatment_goal: Reduce plexiform neurofibroma volume, prevent malignant transformation
first_line_soc: Selumetinib (Koselugo) MEK inhibitor 25 mg/m2 BID - FDA-approved 2020 for symptomatic, inoperable plexiform neurofibromas in NF1 patients ≥2 years.
treatment_algorithm: Selumetinib (Koselugo) for plexiform neurofibromas. Surgical resection when feasible. MPNST (malignant transformation): standard sarcoma chemotherapy (doxorubicin, ifosfamide). Optic pathway glioma: carboplatin + vincristine; BRAF inhibitors if BRAF fusion. Annual monitoring (MRI, ophthalmology, audiology). NF2: bevacizumab for vestibular schwannoma growth.
patients_needing_tx: ~30,000 NF1 patients with symptomatic plexiform neurofibromas or progressive disease needing pharmacotherapy.
price: $150,000-$250,000/year (Koselugo ~$200,000/year). Orphan pricing.
peak_sales: ~$600M at 10% for Koselugo + next-gen MEK inhibitor combination. NF1 market currently ~$300-500M/year.
registrational_endpoint: ≥20% reduction in plexiform neurofibroma volume (MRI volumetric) at 24 cycles.
btd_threshold_vs_soc: ≥20% PN volume reduction in >50% of patients vs. historical ~15-20% with selumetinib (SPRINT trial).

symptoms: Epilepsy (infantile spasms, multiple seizure types), brain tubers, SEGA, AML kidney tumors, skin findings, autism
treatment_goal: Control seizures, reduce tumor burden, preserve kidney function
first_line_soc: Everolimus (Afinitor) mTOR inhibitor for SEGA, renal AML, and pulmonary LAM. Vigabatrin for TSC-associated infantile spasms. Cannabidiol (Epidiolex) for TSC-associated seizures FDA-approved 2020.
treatment_algorithm: mTOR inhibitors: everolimus (Afinitor/Votubia) for SEGA, renal AML ≥3cm, and LAM. Anticonvulsants: vigabatrin (infantile spasms), Epidiolex (cannabidiol) for TSC seizures. Surgery for SEGA if acutely enlarging. Embolization/partial nephrectomy for bleeding AML. Sirolimus alternative to everolimus. Dermatologic: topical rapamycin (mTORC1) for facial angiofibromas.
us_prevalence: ~50,000 US patients with TSC (1:6,000-1:10,000). ~30,000 pediatric patients.
patients_needing_tx: ~25,000 requiring pharmacotherapy (everolimus, Epidiolex, vigabatrin).
price: $100,000-$200,000/year (Afinitor ~$150,000/year; Epidiolex ~$50,000/year). Orphan pricing.
peak_sales: ~$650M at 20% for next-gen mTOR inhibitor or TSC-specific seizure therapy. 25,000 × $130,000 × 20% = $650M.
registrational_endpoint: SEGA volume change ≥50% + seizure frequency reduction at 24 weeks vs. placebo.
btd_threshold_vs_soc: ≥50% SEGA volume reduction in >35% of patients vs. <0% placebo; seizure reduction ≥50% in >50% vs. ~25% placebo.

symptoms: Hemangioblastomas (CNS/retina), clear cell RCC, pheochromocytoma, pancreatic NETs, polycythemia
treatment_goal: Prevent/treat hemangioblastomas and RCC, manage polycythemia
first_line_soc: Belzutifan (Welireg) HIF-2α inhibitor 120 mg/day - FDA-approved 2021 for VHL-related RCC, CNS hemangioblastomas, and pancreatic NETs. Surgical resection for isolated lesions.
treatment_algorithm: Belzutifan (Welireg) for VHL-associated RCC, CNS hemangioblastomas, pNETs (FDA-approved MSD/Merck). Surgical excision for individual hemangioblastomas, RCC <3cm. Radiofrequency ablation, SBRT for RCC. Sunitinib/pazopanib for advanced RCC if belzutifan fails. Pheochromocytoma: adrenalectomy after alpha/beta blockade.
us_prevalence: ~10,000-15,000 US patients with VHL syndrome (1:36,000-1:45,000). ~6,000-10,000 on active pharmacotherapy.
patients_needing_tx: ~8,000 US VHL patients on belzutifan or surveillance/systemic therapy.
price: $200,000-$300,000/year (belzutifan/Welireg ~$250,000/year). Orphan pricing.
peak_sales: ~$400M at 20% for novel HIF-2α inhibitor or next-gen VHL therapy. Welireg ~$300-500M projected peak.
registrational_endpoint: Objective response rate (ORR by RECIST v1.1) across hemangioblastomas + RCC at 12 months.
btd_threshold_vs_soc: ORR >50% across all VHL tumor types at 12 months vs. historical surveillance (~0% spontaneous regression).

symptoms: Port-wine birthmark (trigeminal distribution), seizures, glaucoma, hemiparesis, intellectual disability
treatment_goal: Control seizures, prevent glaucoma damage, manage neurological decline
first_line_soc: Anticonvulsants: levetiracetam or oxcarbazepine for seizure control. Low-dose aspirin (3-5 mg/kg/day) to prevent stroke-like episodes. Topical/systemic therapy for glaucoma.
treatment_algorithm: Seizures: levetiracetam, oxcarbazepine, or valproate. Aspirin prophylaxis for TIA/stroke prevention. Glaucoma: topical prostaglandins or beta-blockers; trabeculotomy for refractory. Pulsed dye laser for port-wine stain. Rapamycin (sirolimus) investigational for SWS brain lesion. GNAQ/GNA11 somatic mutations → MEK inhibitors (trametinib) investigational.
us_prevalence: ~8,000-15,000 US patients (1:20,000-1:50,000). Often associated with Klippel-Trenaunay. Rare disease.
patients_needing_tx: ~10,000 patients requiring anti-seizure + aspirin + other pharmacotherapy.
price: $100,000-$250,000/year for targeted MEK/mTOR inhibitor. Orphan pricing.
registrational_endpoint: Seizure-free days per month + MRI perfusion imaging improvement at 24 months vs. anticonvulsant SOC.
btd_threshold_vs_soc: ≥50% reduction in seizure frequency vs. standard anticonvulsants + MRI stabilization in >60% of patients.

symptoms: Non-progressive motor disorder: spasticity, dyskinesia, ataxia, developmental motor delays, associated cognitive/sensory impairments
treatment_goal: Reduce spasticity, improve motor function, maximize independence
first_line_soc: Oral baclofen (2.5-10 mg TID) for spasticity; diazepam for acute spasm relief; intrathecal baclofen (ITB) pump for severe spasticity; botulinum toxin injections (onabotulinumtoxinA/Botox) for focal spasticity.
treatment_algorithm: Spasticity: botulinum toxin type A (Botox/Dysport/Xeomin) injections q3-6 months for focal; oral baclofen, tizanidine; intrathecal baclofen (ITB) pump for generalized severe spasticity. Selective dorsal rhizotomy (SDR) for spastic diplegia. Dyskinesia/dystonia: trihexyphenidyl, tetrabenazine, DBS. Physiotherapy, occupational therapy. No disease-modifying drug.
us_prevalence: ~764,000 US patients (~1 in 345 children); ~10,000 new cases/year. Most common physical disability in childhood.
patients_needing_tx: ~500,000 requiring pharmacologic management of spasticity/movement disorder.
price: $20,000-$100,000/year for novel anti-spasticity or neuroprotective/regenerative agent.
peak_sales: ~$2.5B at 10% for novel disease-modifying CP therapy. 500,000 × $50,000 × 10% = $2.5B.
registrational_endpoint: Gross Motor Function Measure (GMFM-66) change at 52 weeks + Ashworth spasticity scale.
btd_threshold_vs_soc: ≥5-point GMFM-66 improvement + ≥1-point Ashworth scale reduction vs. physical therapy alone.

disease: Tic Disorders and Tourette Syndrome in Children and Adolescents
symptoms: Multiple motor and vocal tics (Tourette), single motor tics, waxing/waning course, ADHD/OCD comorbidities
treatment_goal: Reduce tic severity/frequency, improve functional impairment
first_line_soc: Comprehensive Behavioral Intervention for Tics (CBIT - habit reversal therapy) first-line. Pharmacologic: alpha-2 agonists (guanfacine/clonidine) for mild-moderate tics; haloperidol, fluphenazine, or pimozide for severe tics.
treatment_algorithm: CBIT behavioral therapy. Alpha-2 agonists: guanfacine (Intuniv) 1-4 mg/day or clonidine 0.05-0.3 mg/day. Antipsychotics for severe: haloperidol 0.5-5 mg/day, fluphenazine, pimozide, aripiprazole. Valbenazine (Ingrezza) and deutetrabenazine - VMAT2 inhibitors (FDA-approved 2023/2024 for Tourette in adults/children). For OCD comorbidity: SSRIs. For ADHD: methylphenidate cautiously.
us_prevalence: Tourette syndrome: ~200,000 US children/adolescents (1:160-1:1,000 school-age). Chronic tic disorders total ~3-4 million US.
patients_needing_tx: ~150,000 requiring pharmacologic intervention for Tourette; ~500,000 for all chronic tic disorders.
price: $15,000-$50,000/year (Ingrezza for tics ~$100,000/year; Austedo ~$50,000/year). Orphan pricing for severe TS.
peak_sales: ~$750M at 10% for VMAT2 inhibitor or novel tic-suppressing agent. 150,000 × $50,000 × 10% = $750M.
registrational_endpoint: Yale Global Tic Severity Scale (YGTSS) total tic score change at 12 weeks vs. placebo.
btd_threshold_vs_soc: ≥10-point YGTSS total tic score reduction vs. placebo (valbenazine showed ~5-8 point improvement in phase 3).

symptoms: Subtle (eye deviation, lip smacking), tonic, clonic, myoclonic, and spasms in neonate
treatment_goal: Terminate acute seizures, prevent status epilepticus, address underlying cause
first_line_soc: Phenobarbital IV 20 mg/kg loading dose (first-line for most neonatal seizures). If refractory: levetiracetam IV 40-60 mg/kg or fosphenytoin/phenytoin IV 20 mg/kg PE.
treatment_algorithm: First-line: phenobarbital IV 20 mg/kg, repeat up to 40 mg/kg total. Second-line: levetiracetam IV or fosphenytoin. Pyridoxine 100 mg IV trial for pyridoxine-dependent epilepsy. Folinic acid for folinic acid-responsive seizures. GLUT1 deficiency: ketogenic diet. Treat underlying cause (hypoglycemia, hypocalcemia, sepsis). Bumetanide (investigational, phase 2/3 trials - mixed results).
us_prevalence: ~1-5/1,000 live births; ~10,000-20,000 neonatal seizure episodes/year in US. Significant cause of neonatal morbidity.
patients_needing_tx: ~15,000/year requiring IV anticonvulsants.
price: $10,000-$30,000/course for novel neonatal anti-seizure agent (bumetanide analog, GABAergic).
peak_sales: ~$90M at 30% for novel neonatal seizure agent. 15,000 × $20,000 × 30% = $90M.
registrational_endpoint: Electrographic seizure cessation within 24 hours + seizure-free EEG at 72 hours vs. phenobarbital.
btd_threshold_vs_soc: ≥80% electrographic seizure freedom within 24 hours vs. ~50-60% with phenobarbital (electrographic endpoints).

symptoms: Clusters of brief flexion-extension spasms, hypsarrhythmia on EEG, developmental regression in infants
treatment_goal: Achieve clinical and EEG remission (resolution of hypsarrhythmia)
first_line_soc: ACTH (tetracosactide/H.P. Acthar Gel) or vigabatrin (Sabril) for infantile spasms. ACTH 150 IU/m2/day x 2 weeks is first-line per AAP/ILAE guidelines. TSC-related: vigabatrin first-line.
treatment_algorithm: ACTH (H.P. Acthar) 75 IU/kg/day IM for 2 weeks (standard) or tetracosactide (EU). Vigabatrin 100-150 mg/kg/day for TSC-related IS. Combination ACTH + vigabatrin superior to monotherapy. Prednisolone (high-dose 40-60 mg/day) alternative to ACTH. Refractory IS: pyridoxine trial; ketogenic diet; topiramate; ACTH + vigabatrin + prednisolone.
us_prevalence: ~2,000-3,000 new cases/year in US (1:2,000-1:4,000 infants); ~5,000-6,000 prevalent cases. Devastating prognosis if untreated.
patients_needing_tx: ~2,500 new cases/year requiring ACTH ± vigabatrin.
price: $40,000-$150,000/course (ACTH H.P. Acthar ~$80,000-$100,000/course; vigabatrin ~$5,000-$10,000/month). Orphan pricing.
peak_sales: ~$125M at 50% for next-gen ACTH or novel IS-specific agent. 2,500 × $100,000 × 50% = $125M.
registrational_endpoint: Spasm freedom at day 14 + resolution of hypsarrhythmia on EEG at day 14.
btd_threshold_vs_soc: ≥65% spasm freedom at day 14 vs. ~50% ACTH (UKISS trial: ACTH 73% vs. vigabatrin 54% at 14 days).

symptoms: Convulsion (generalized tonic-clonic) triggered by fever in child 6 months-5 years
treatment_goal: Terminate acute seizure, manage fever, reduce recurrence anxiety
first_line_soc: Acute treatment: rectal diazepam (Diastat 0.5 mg/kg) or intranasal/buccal midazolam for prolonged febrile seizure (>5 min). Routine antipyretics not proven to prevent recurrence.
treatment_algorithm: Acute (<5 min): parental reassurance, antipyretics (acetaminophen/ibuprofen) for comfort. Prolonged (>5 min): rectal diazepam or IN/buccal midazolam. Status epilepticus: ER management per status protocol. Prophylaxis not routinely recommended. Intermittent rectal diazepam (at fever) may reduce recurrence (not recommended routinely due to sedation risks).
us_prevalence: ~2-5% of children <5 years; ~150,000-500,000 febrile seizure episodes/year in US. Recurrence in ~30%.
patients_needing_tx: ~100,000/year requiring acute pharmacologic treatment (benzodiazepines for prolonged/recurrent).
price: $500-$3,000/episode for rescue benzodiazepine or novel anti-ictogenic agent.
peak_sales: ~$60M at 40% for nasal/buccal midazolam rescue formulation (Nayzilam/Versacloz type). 100,000 × $1,500 × 40% = $60M.
registrational_endpoint: Seizure cessation within 5 minutes of drug administration vs. placebo.
btd_threshold_vs_soc: ≥80% seizure cessation within 5 minutes vs. ~40% placebo (rectal diazepam ~60-80% effective).

symptoms: Acute non-inflammatory encephalopathy + hepatopathy post-viral illness in child; associated with aspirin use
treatment_goal: Reduce intracranial pressure, correct metabolic derangements, prevent death
first_line_soc: Supportive ICU care: IV glucose (10-20% dextrose) for hypoglycemia; mannitol or 3% saline for cerebral edema; avoid aspirin in children with viral illness.
treatment_algorithm: ICU management: IV glucose + insulin to control hypoglycemia and reduce fatty acid oxidation. ICP monitoring; mannitol/hypertonic saline; hypothermia for severe cases. FFP for coagulopathy. No specific antidote; primarily supportive. Prevention: avoidance of aspirin in children with viral illness (per CDC/AAP).
us_prevalence: <50 cases/year in US now (historically ~500-600/year pre-aspirin restriction); virtually eliminated by aspirin warnings. Rare pediatric disease.
patients_needing_tx: <50 cases/year requiring ICU-level pharmacologic support.
price: N/A (no commercial pharmacologic opportunity given minimal incidence).
registrational_endpoint: N/A (insufficient case volume for clinical trials).
btd_threshold_vs_soc: N/A.

symptoms: Pallor, tachycardia, poor feeding, hyperbilirubinemia, lethargy in neonate/preterm infant
treatment_goal: Correct anemia, maintain tissue oxygenation, reduce transfusion burden
first_line_soc: Erythropoietin (EPO) + iron (ferrous sulfate + elemental iron) for anemia of prematurity in VLBW infants. Packed red blood cell transfusion for symptomatic severe anemia.
treatment_algorithm: VLBW/preterm: recombinant erythropoietin (epoetin alfa/beta) + enteral iron supplementation (reduces transfusion burden by ~30%). Hemolytic disease of newborn: exchange transfusion for severe; phototherapy for hyperbilirubinemia. Iron deficiency: oral iron supplementation. Delayed cord clamping at birth to optimize iron stores.
us_prevalence: ~50,000-80,000 preterm infants with clinically significant anemia of prematurity annually; hemolytic disease of newborn ~3,000-5,000/year.
patients_needing_tx: ~60,000/year requiring EPO ± transfusion for anemia of prematurity.
price: $5,000-$20,000/course for EPO or novel erythropoiesis stimulator. Pediatric formulation premium.
peak_sales: ~$120M at 20% for novel EPO-stimulating agent + iron combination. 60,000 × $10,000 × 20% = $120M.
registrational_endpoint: Number of red cell transfusions per infant + hematocrit at 36 weeks PMA vs. placebo.
btd_threshold_vs_soc: ≥30% reduction in transfusion need vs. placebo (EPO currently reduces transfusions ~30-40%).

symptoms: Plethora, poor feeding, jitteriness, hypoglycemia, respiratory distress, jaundice from hematocrit >65%
treatment_goal: Reduce blood viscosity to prevent thrombotic complications
first_line_soc: Partial exchange transfusion (isovolemic hemodilution) with normal saline to reduce hematocrit to <55-60% for symptomatic neonates. No proven pharmacologic therapy.
treatment_algorithm: Asymptomatic (Hct 65-70%): observation + adequate hydration. Symptomatic (Hct >65% with symptoms): partial exchange transfusion (PET) with NS or albumin. No pharmacologic standard; IV fluids for hydration. Phototherapy for concurrent hyperbilirubinemia.
us_prevalence: ~2-5% of neonates; ~70,000-175,000 cases/year in US. Symptomatic requiring PET: ~5,000-10,000/year.
patients_needing_tx: ~7,500/year with symptomatic polycythemia requiring PET.
price: Minimal pharmacologic TAM ($500-$2,000/course for rheologic agent if developed).
registrational_endpoint: Symptom resolution + Hct normalization within 6 hours of intervention.
btd_threshold_vs_soc: N/A (procedure-based; no pharmacologic BTD pathway).

symptoms: Not a disease (physiological topic: transition from fetal to neonatal circulation, respiratory adaptation)
treatment_goal: N/A (educational/physiology topic, not a treatable disease per se)
first_line_soc: None.
treatment_algorithm: None (topic covers normal physiological processes; interventions for abnormal transitions are covered under related conditions).
us_prevalence: N/A (not a disease; describes normal physiologic processes in all ~3.5 million US births/year).
patients_needing_tx: N/A.
price: N/A.
registrational_endpoint: N/A.
btd_threshold_vs_soc: N/A.

symptoms: Respiratory distress, apnea, hypothermia, feeding difficulties, NEC, IVH, retinopathy of prematurity
treatment_goal: Prevent and treat preterm complications, support organ maturation
first_line_soc: Antenatal corticosteroids (betamethasone 12 mg IM q24h x2) for lung maturation <34 weeks. Postnatal: surfactant (calfactant, poractant alfa) for RDS; caffeine citrate for apnea of prematurity.
treatment_algorithm: Antenatal: betamethasone (lung maturation), magnesium sulfate (neuroprotection <32 wks). Postnatal: surfactant (Curosurf, Calfactant) for RDS; caffeine citrate (Cafcit) for AOP; indomethacin/ibuprofen/paracetamol for PDA closure; parenteral nutrition; EPO for anemia of prematurity; CPAP/ventilation.
us_prevalence: ~400,000 preterm births/year in US (10% of births); ~70,000 VLBW (<1,500g); ~3,000-5,000 ELBW (<500g).
patients_needing_tx: ~100,000/year preterm infants requiring pharmacotherapy.
price: $10,000-$50,000/NICU stay for novel preterm pharmacotherapy. Surfactant: ~$1,000-$5,000/dose.
peak_sales: ~$600M at 20% for next-gen surfactant or neuroprotective preterm agent. 100,000 × $30,000 × 20% = $600M.
registrational_endpoint: Composite: BPD or death at 36 weeks PMA + IVH rate + NEC rate vs. standard NICU care.
btd_threshold_vs_soc: ≥20% relative reduction in composite BPD/death vs. standard surfactant + caffeine.

symptoms: Low birth weight for GA, hypoglycemia, polycythemia, poor thermal regulation, catch-up growth failure
treatment_goal: Prevent hypoglycemia, support catch-up growth, optimize nutrition
first_line_soc: Nutritional optimization (early enteral feeding, fortified breast milk, high-calorie formula). Growth hormone (Genotropin, Norditropin) approved for persistent short stature in SGA children (not catching up by age 2-4 years) 0.067 mg/kg/day SC.
treatment_algorithm: Neonatal: early and frequent feeding, IV glucose for hypoglycemia. Long-term: growth hormone therapy (somatropin) for SGA children with persistent growth failure. GH approved ~age 2-4 years if not caught up. IGF-1 (mecasermin) if GH unresponsive. Nutritional monitoring.
us_prevalence: SGA at birth: ~10% of US births (~350,000/year). Persistent SGA short stature (not catching up by 4): ~20,000-50,000 US children qualifying for GH.
patients_needing_tx: ~30,000 US children with SGA requiring GH therapy; ~350,000 neonatal SGA requiring nutritional management.
price: $20,000-$50,000/year for GH or novel growth-promoting agent. Pediatric formulary.
peak_sales: ~$180M at 20% for novel GH or IGF-1 therapy for SGA. 30,000 × $30,000 × 20% = $180M.
registrational_endpoint: Height velocity (cm/year) at 1 year + height SDS change vs. untreated SGA controls.
btd_threshold_vs_soc: ≥3 cm/year increase in height velocity vs. 1-2 cm without GH (GH increases height velocity from ~3 to 6-8 cm/year).

symptoms: Low core temperature (<36.5°C), peripheral vasoconstriction, lethargy, poor feeding, coagulopathy, metabolic acidosis
treatment_goal: Restore normothermia gradually, prevent hypoxic-ischemic injury (therapeutic hypothermia exception)
first_line_soc: Rewarming: kangaroo care, warming blanket, radiant warmer (non-HIE). Therapeutic hypothermia (33-34°C x 72 hours) is deliberate treatment for HIE, not accidental hypothermia.
treatment_algorithm: Prevention: dry and warm neonate at birth, delayed cord clamping, radiant warmer. Accidental hypothermia: gradual rewarming (0.5°C/hour), IV glucose, IV fluids for circulation. Therapeutic hypothermia for HIE: whole-body cooling 33.5°C x 72h, then slow rewarming. Caffeine for apnea during cooling. EPO investigational adjuvant for HIE.
us_prevalence: Accidental hypothermia: ~20,000-30,000 neonates/year. HIE therapeutic hypothermia: ~4,000-6,000/year (2/1,000 live births).
patients_needing_tx: ~5,000/year with HIE requiring therapeutic hypothermia + pharmacologic adjuncts; ~25,000 accidental hypothermia.
price: $20,000-$100,000/course for neuroprotective adjunct to therapeutic hypothermia (EPO, melatonin, erythropoietin, allopurinol).
peak_sales: ~$75M at 30% for novel neuroprotective adjunct to hypothermia. 5,000 × $50,000 × 30% = $75M.
registrational_endpoint: Neurodevelopmental outcome (Bayley Scales III) at 18-24 months vs. therapeutic hypothermia alone.
btd_threshold_vs_soc: ≥15% absolute increase in normal neurodevelopmental outcomes at 18-24 months vs. hypothermia alone (~45-50% normal with cooling alone).

symptoms: Varies: clavicle fracture, brachial plexus injury (Erb's palsy), cephalohematoma, subdural hematoma, facial nerve palsy
treatment_goal: Prevent permanent disability; early intervention for nerve injuries
first_line_soc: Mostly non-pharmacologic (immobilization, physiotherapy). Analgesics (acetaminophen, ibuprofen) for pain. No specific pharmacologic treatment for most birth injuries.
treatment_algorithm: Brachial plexus: physical therapy, range-of-motion exercises; surgical repair (nerve grafting) if no recovery by 3-6 months. Cephalhematoma: observation (no treatment needed). Fractures: immobilization, analgesics. Hypoxic-ischemic injury: therapeutic hypothermia (not strictly a birth injury treatment, covered under HIE). Erythropoietin investigated for neuroprotection in HIE.
us_prevalence: ~6-8/1,000 live births with birth injuries; ~25,000-30,000/year US. Brachial plexus palsy ~1.5/1,000. Total ~25,000 significant birth injuries/year.
patients_needing_tx: ~5,000/year requiring pharmacologic pain management + investigational neuroprotection.
price: $20,000-$50,000/course for neuroprotective agent (EPO, melatonin for hypoxic birth injury).
peak_sales: ~$30M at 20% for novel neuroprotective birth injury agent. 5,000 × $30,000 × 20% = $30M.
registrational_endpoint: Neurodevelopmental outcome (Bayley Scales) + motor function at 18-24 months vs. standard supportive care.
btd_threshold_vs_soc: ≥15% more infants with normal neurodevelopmental outcome at 24 months vs. supportive care.

symptoms: Not a disease (clinical procedure for neonates requiring resuscitation at birth: apnea, bradycardia, cyanosis)
treatment_goal: Establish breathing and circulation in asphyxiated neonate within first minutes of life
first_line_soc: NRP (Neonatal Resuscitation Program): dry, stimulate, warm → PPV (positive pressure ventilation) → chest compressions → epinephrine IV/IO (0.1-0.3 mL/kg of 1:10,000).
treatment_algorithm: NRP protocol: initial steps (warm, dry, stimulate). Supplemental O2 titrated by oximetry. PPV if HR <100 or absent breathing. CPAP for breathing but SpO2 low. Intubation + chest compressions if HR <60. Epinephrine 0.01-0.03 mg/kg IV/IO. Volume expansion (NS 10 mL/kg) for hypovolemia. Sodium bicarbonate for documented metabolic acidosis.
us_prevalence: ~10% of US births require some resuscitative intervention; ~1% require intubation/medications; ~0.1% full advanced resuscitation (~3,500/year).
patients_needing_tx: ~3,500/year requiring epinephrine or advanced pharmacologic resuscitation.
price: $5,000-$30,000 for novel resuscitation pharmacotherapy (vasopressin analog, newer epinephrine formulation).
registrational_endpoint: Survival at 30 days + neurodevelopmental outcome at 18-24 months vs. standard NRP epinephrine.
btd_threshold_vs_soc: ≥10% improvement in survival without severe neurodisability vs. standard epinephrine (~50% intact survival in advanced resuscitation).

symptoms: High birth weight, shoulder dystocia risk, neonatal hypoglycemia, polycythemia, birth injuries
treatment_goal: Prevent and treat neonatal hypoglycemia; minimize birth trauma
first_line_soc: Neonatal hypoglycemia management: early oral feeding, buccal dextrose gel, IV dextrose if needed. No pharmacologic treatment for LGA status itself.
treatment_algorithm: Antenatal: tight glycemic control in diabetic mothers (insulin/metformin). Neonatal monitoring: glucose checks Q1-2h for first 12 hours. Treatment of hypoglycemia: dextrose gel, IV dextrose. Polycythemia management. LGA itself is managed by complications, not pharmacologically.
us_prevalence: ~8-10% of births in US are LGA; ~280,000-350,000 LGA births/year.
patients_needing_tx: ~50,000 LGA neonates/year requiring glucose management or treatment for LGA complications.
price: Minimal pharmacologic TAM for LGA management itself.
peak_sales: ~$10M (limited commercial opportunity specific to LGA management).
registrational_endpoint: Neonatal hypoglycemia event rate + birth injury rate vs. standard monitoring protocols.
btd_threshold_vs_soc: N/A (management of LGA is supportive; pharmacologic opportunity limited).

symptoms: Macrosomia or dysmaturity, meconium aspiration risk, hypoglycemia, polycythemia, cord compression, perinatal distress
treatment_goal: Prevent fetal/neonatal morbidity from prolonged gestation
first_line_soc: Labor induction at 41-42 weeks gestation (obstetric, not neonatal pharmacology). Neonatal: glucose monitoring, meconium aspiration management, polycythemia treatment.
treatment_algorithm: Antepartum: cervical ripening (dinoprostone, misoprostol, Foley balloon) at 41 weeks. Neonatal management: glucose monitoring, thermal stability, MSAF suctioning if meconium present, polycythemia/hyperviscosity management.
us_prevalence: ~5-7% of US births are post-term (≥42 weeks); ~175,000-250,000/year. With induction policies, complications declining.
patients_needing_tx: ~10,000 postterm neonates/year with clinically significant complications requiring pharmacotherapy.
price: Not primarily pharmacologic for neonatal management; obstetric ripening agents $500-$2,000/course.
peak_sales: ~$10M (limited neonatal pharmacologic opportunity; primarily obstetric management).
registrational_endpoint: Neonatal composite morbidity (MAS, HIE, NICU admission) rate at birth vs. standard induction protocols.
btd_threshold_vs_soc: N/A (obstetric timing; neonatal pharmacology is supportive only).

symptoms: Not a disease (classification of newborns by gestational age: preterm, term, post-term)
treatment_goal: N/A (category/diagnostic classification, not a treatable disease per se)
first_line_soc: None.
treatment_algorithm: None (classifications by GA are used to guide management; specific conditions in preterm/post-term neonates are covered individually).
us_prevalence: N/A (diagnostic classification applied to all ~3.5 million US births/year; ~10% preterm, ~90% term).
patients_needing_tx: N/A.
price: N/A.
registrational_endpoint: N/A.
btd_threshold_vs_soc: N/A.

symptoms: Not a disease (measurements: birth weight, length, head circumference for GA-based growth assessment)
treatment_goal: N/A (clinical monitoring topic, not a treatable disease)
first_line_soc: None.
treatment_algorithm: None (growth parameter monitoring guides clinical decisions; abnormal findings lead to specific diagnoses and interventions).
us_prevalence: N/A (assessed in all ~3.5 million US newborns/year).
patients_needing_tx: N/A.
price: N/A.
registrational_endpoint: N/A.
btd_threshold_vs_soc: N/A.

disease: Depressive Disorders in Children and Adolescents
symptoms: Persistent sadness, irritability, loss of interest, sleep/appetite changes, poor concentration
treatment_goal: Achieve and maintain remission, prevent recurrence
first_line_soc: Fluoxetine (SSRI) — only FDA-approved antidepressant for children ≥8 years with MDD; escitalopram FDA-approved for adolescents ≥12.
treatment_algorithm: Step 1: Psychotherapy (CBT, IPT-A) ± fluoxetine for moderate-severe MDD. Step 2: If partial response, add or switch to escitalopram or sertraline (off-label). Step 3: For treatment-resistant: augmentation (lithium, atypical antipsychotic); TMS (adolescents ≥15); ECT for severe refractory cases. Black box warning for suicidality with antidepressants.
us_prevalence: ~3.2 million US adolescents (13–17) had MDD in 2023 per SAMHSA; ~8% prevalence. Including children 6–12: ~4–5 million total.
patients_needing_tx: ~2.5 million pediatric patients warranting pharmacotherapy for moderate-severe MDD.
price: $5,000–$12,000/year for novel rapid-acting antidepressant (ketamine-analog, neuroplasticity agent).
registrational_endpoint: Change from baseline CDRS-R (Children's Depression Rating Scale–Revised) at 8 weeks vs. placebo.
btd_threshold_vs_soc: ≥5-point greater CDRS-R reduction vs. placebo (vs. fluoxetine ~3–4 points over placebo in TADS).

disease: Suicidal Behavior in Children and Adolescents
symptoms: Suicidal ideation, self-harm attempts, hopelessness, depression, impulsivity
treatment_goal: Prevent suicidal attempts, achieve psychiatric stability
first_line_soc: Crisis intervention + intensive psychiatric care; pharmacotherapy of underlying disorder (depression → fluoxetine; bipolar → lithium). Lithium has anti-suicidal evidence in adults.
treatment_algorithm: Acute: Safety planning, hospitalization if high risk. Step 1: Treat underlying disorder (MDD: fluoxetine + CBT; bipolar: lithium). Step 2: DBT-A reduces attempts by ~60% vs. non-DBT. Step 3: Ketamine/esketamine for acute suicidal ideation (off-label, investigational in adolescents).
us_prevalence: Suicide is 2nd leading cause of death in ages 10–34; ~8–10% of US high schoolers report a suicide attempt/year; ~20–25% report ideation.
patients_needing_tx: ~1 million adolescents with significant suicidal ideation/behavior requiring active pharmacologic management.
price: $8,000–$20,000/year for rapid-acting anti-suicidal agent.
registrational_endpoint: Rate of suicide attempts and Columbia Suicide Severity Rating Scale (C-SSRS) score change at 12 weeks vs. placebo.
btd_threshold_vs_soc: ≥50% reduction in suicide attempt rate vs. placebo (very high bar given ethical constraints).

disease: Generalized Anxiety Disorder in Children and Adolescents
symptoms: Excessive worry, restlessness, fatigue, concentration difficulty, muscle tension
treatment_goal: Reduce anxiety severity, restore daily functioning
first_line_soc: CBT is first-line; SSRIs (sertraline, fluoxetine) or SNRIs (venlafaxine, duloxetine) for moderate-severe GAD — used off-label in pediatrics.
treatment_algorithm: Step 1: CBT (Coping Cat program) for mild-moderate GAD. Step 2: SSRI (sertraline starting 25–50 mg/day) or SNRI (venlafaxine XR) for moderate-severe. Step 3: Combination CBT + SSRI (CAMS trial demonstrated superiority). Step 4: Buspirone adjunct; gabapentin off-label.
us_prevalence: ~3–5% of US children; ~2–3 million youth aged 6–17 with GAD.
patients_needing_tx: ~1 million pediatric patients on pharmacotherapy for GAD.
price: $5,000–$10,000/year for pediatric-optimized anxiolytic.
registrational_endpoint: Change from baseline Pediatric Anxiety Rating Scale (PARS) or SCARED at 12 weeks vs. placebo.
btd_threshold_vs_soc: ≥30% PARS reduction vs. placebo (vs. sertraline ~45% responder rate vs. ~27% placebo in CAMS).

disease: Bipolar Disorders in Children and Adolescents
symptoms: Mood swings, mania/hypomania, depression, irritability, impulsivity
treatment_goal: Stabilize mood, prevent episodes, maintain function
first_line_soc: Lithium (mood stabilizer) or atypical antipsychotics (aripiprazole, quetiapine, risperidone) FDA-approved for pediatric bipolar I.
treatment_algorithm: Step 1: Lithium (levels 0.8–1.2 mEq/L) or valproate for classic mania; aripiprazole/quetiapine/risperidone for acute mania. Step 2: Combination mood stabilizer + atypical antipsychotic for refractory mania. Step 3: Adjunctive psychotherapy (CBT, FFT). Maintenance: continued mood stabilizer; monitor metabolic parameters.
us_prevalence: ~1–3% of US adolescents have bipolar spectrum disorder; ~750,000–2.25 million youth aged 6–17. Adult US prevalence ~5.7 million.
patients_needing_tx: ~600,000 pediatric patients on active pharmacotherapy for bipolar disorder.
