price: $8,000–$15,000/year for novel bipolar agent with better tolerability profile.
registrational_endpoint: Change from baseline YMRS (Young Mania Rating Scale) at 3 weeks in pediatric acute mania trial.
btd_threshold_vs_soc: ≥4-point greater YMRS reduction vs. placebo (vs. aripiprazole ~8 vs. ~3 placebo, difference ~5 points).

disease: Social Anxiety Disorder in Children and Adolescents
symptoms: Intense fear of social situations, embarrassment, avoidance, somatic symptoms
treatment_goal: Reduce social fear, increase social participation
first_line_soc: CBT plus SSRIs (sertraline or fluoxetine, off-label); combined treatment most effective.
treatment_algorithm: Step 1: CBT with exposure-based therapy (social skills, graduated exposure). Step 2: SSRI (sertraline preferred). Step 3: SNRI (venlafaxine XR). Step 4: Augmentation with D-cycloserine to enhance exposure therapy (investigational).
us_prevalence: ~5–7% of US adolescents; ~1.5–2.5 million youth.
patients_needing_tx: ~700,000 on pharmacotherapy for social anxiety.
registrational_endpoint: Change from baseline LSAS-CA (Liebowitz Social Anxiety Scale for Children and Adolescents) at 12 weeks.
btd_threshold_vs_soc: ≥15-point LSAS-CA reduction vs. placebo.

disease: Acute and Posttraumatic Stress Disorders (ASD and PTSD) in Children and Adolescents
symptoms: Intrusive memories, hypervigilance, nightmares, avoidance, emotional dysregulation post-trauma
treatment_goal: Reduce PTSD symptoms, restore adaptive functioning
first_line_soc: Trauma-Focused Cognitive Behavioral Therapy (TF-CBT) is gold standard. Pharmacologic: sertraline (Zoloft) off-label (FDA-approved for OCD in children, evidence in pediatric PTSD).
treatment_algorithm: TF-CBT (12-25 sessions). EMDR (Eye Movement Desensitization and Reprocessing). Pharmacologic: SSRIs (sertraline, fluoxetine) off-label; prazosin for nightmares/sleep disturbances; guanfacine for hyperarousal (off-label). No FDA-approved drug specifically for pediatric PTSD. D-cycloserine as augmentation.
us_prevalence: Pediatric PTSD: ~1-6% lifetime; ~1-2 million US children and adolescents with PTSD. ASD (acute stress): ~200,000/year post-trauma.
patients_needing_tx: ~500,000 children with PTSD requiring pharmacotherapy as adjunct to therapy.
price: $5,000-$20,000/year for novel PTSD pharmacotherapy (MDMA-assisted, stellate ganglion block, neuropeptide Y).
peak_sales: ~$500M at 10% for novel pediatric PTSD pharmacotherapy. 500,000 × $10,000 × 10% = $500M.
registrational_endpoint: UCLA PTSD Reaction Index (UCLA-PTSD-RI) change at 12 weeks vs. sertraline/placebo.
btd_threshold_vs_soc: ≥15-point UCLA-PTSD-RI reduction vs. placebo (sertraline ~10-12 points) + remission rate >50%.

symptoms: Excessive fear of separation from caregivers, school refusal, nightmares, somatic complaints
treatment_goal: Reduce separation distress, restore school attendance
first_line_soc: CBT (with family involvement) is first-line; SSRIs (sertraline, fluoxetine) for moderate-severe or CBT-refractory cases.
treatment_algorithm: Step 1: CBT (Coping Cat, BRAVE program) with parent involvement. Step 2: SSRI (sertraline 25–200 mg/day or fluoxetine). Step 3: Combination CBT + SSRI for severe cases (CAMS trial data). Step 4: School re-entry protocol; family therapy.
us_prevalence: Most common anxiety disorder in children <12; ~4–5% of youth; ~1.5–2 million children.
patients_needing_tx: ~600,000 children on pharmacotherapy (SSRI) for SAD.
registrational_endpoint: Change from baseline SAAS (Separation Anxiety Assessment Scale) or PARS at 12 weeks.
btd_threshold_vs_soc: ≥30% PARS reduction vs. placebo.

symptoms: Aggression, rule-breaking, destruction, deceitfulness, serious violations of norms
treatment_goal: Reduce aggression, improve prosocial behavior
first_line_soc: None (pharmacologic); behavioral therapy (parent management training, MST) is primary. Risperidone is used off-label for severe aggression.
treatment_algorithm: Psychosocial: Parent Management Training (PMT), Multisystemic Therapy (MST), Functional Family Therapy. Pharmacologic (adjunctive, off-label): risperidone or aripiprazole for severe aggression; stimulants if comorbid ADHD; mood stabilizers (lithium, valproate) for explosive aggression.
us_prevalence: ~2–10% of US children; ~1.5–5 million youth aged 6–17.
patients_needing_tx: ~500,000 with severe conduct disorder on pharmacotherapy for aggression.
price: $5,000–$10,000/year for novel anti-aggression agent.
registrational_endpoint: Change from baseline Modified Overt Aggression Scale (MOAS) at 8 weeks vs. placebo in pediatric patients with conduct disorder.
btd_threshold_vs_soc: ≥30% reduction in MOAS vs. placebo (vs. risperidone ~40% vs. ~15% for placebo).

disease: Obsessive-Compulsive Disorder and Related Disorders in Children and Adolescents
symptoms: Intrusive obsessions, compulsive rituals, significant time loss, impaired function
treatment_goal: Reduce OCD severity, restore daily functioning
first_line_soc: Sertraline or fluvoxamine (FDA-approved for pediatric OCD) + ERP (Exposure and Response Prevention therapy).
treatment_algorithm: Step 1: ERP therapy ± SSRI (sertraline, fluoxetine, or fluvoxamine — all FDA-approved for pediatric OCD). Step 2: Higher-dose SSRI if partial response. Step 3: Augmentation with atypical antipsychotic (aripiprazole, risperidone) for refractory OCD. Step 4: Clomipramine (TCA) for severe refractory; IV clomipramine investigational.
us_prevalence: ~500,000–1 million US children (2–4% of children; prevalence ~1–3% of youth population).
patients_needing_tx: ~400,000 pediatric patients on pharmacotherapy for OCD.
price: $6,000–$12,000/year for novel glutamatergic or immune-modulating OCD agent.
registrational_endpoint: Change from baseline CY-BOCS (Children's Yale-Brown Obsessive Compulsive Scale) at 12 weeks vs. placebo.
btd_threshold_vs_soc: ≥5-point CY-BOCS reduction vs. placebo (vs. fluvoxamine ~6 vs. ~3 for placebo in pediatric trials).

disease: Nonsuicidal Self-Injury (NSSI) in Children and Adolescents
symptoms: Deliberate self-cutting, burning, hitting without suicidal intent; emotional dysregulation
treatment_goal: Stop self-injurious behavior, develop coping skills
first_line_soc: None (pharmacologic); DBT-A (Dialectical Behavior Therapy for Adolescents) is evidence-based first-line.
treatment_algorithm: Psychotherapy: DBT-A (primary); CBT; emotion regulation therapy. Pharmacologic (off-label, adjunctive): SSRIs for comorbid depression/anxiety; NAC (N-acetylcysteine) investigational; naltrexone for compulsive NSSI (very limited evidence). Treat comorbidities.
us_prevalence: ~17–18% of US adolescents report lifetime NSSI; clinically significant repetitive NSSI ~5–7% or ~1.5–2 million youth.
patients_needing_tx: ~500,000 with repetitive NSSI seeking or receiving pharmacotherapy for comorbid conditions.
price: $4,000–$8,000/year for novel emotion dysregulation pharmacotherapy.
registrational_endpoint: Frequency of NSSI episodes per week and Difficulties in Emotion Regulation Scale (DERS) at 16 weeks vs. control.
btd_threshold_vs_soc: ≥50% reduction in NSSI frequency vs. control arm.

disease: Panic Disorder in Children and Adolescents
symptoms: Recurrent unexpected panic attacks, palpitations, chest pain, intense fear, avoidance
treatment_goal: Reduce panic frequency, eliminate anticipatory anxiety
first_line_soc: SSRIs (sertraline, fluoxetine — off-label) combined with CBT (panic-focused); SSRIs are first-line pharmacotherapy.
treatment_algorithm: Step 1: CBT (panic-focused, interoceptive exposure). Step 2: SSRI (sertraline or fluoxetine). Step 3: SNRI (venlafaxine XR). Step 4: Low-dose benzodiazepines (clonazepam, off-label) for acute severe episodes; avoid chronic use. Combination CBT + SSRI for severe cases.
us_prevalence: ~2–3% of US adolescents; ~600,000–1 million youth with panic disorder.
patients_needing_tx: ~400,000 on pharmacotherapy for panic disorder.
price: $5,000–$10,000/year for novel rapid-acting anxiolytic.
registrational_endpoint: Panic Attack Frequency (PAF) and Panic Disorder Severity Scale for Children (PDSS-C) at 12 weeks.
btd_threshold_vs_soc: ≥50% reduction in panic attack frequency vs. placebo.

disease: Somatic Symptom and Related Disorders in Children
symptoms: Physical symptoms (pain, fatigue, nausea) without medical explanation; excessive health worry
treatment_goal: Reduce symptom burden, improve daily functioning
first_line_soc: None (pharmacologic); CBT and family-based therapy are primary. Treat underlying anxiety/depression with SSRIs if present.
treatment_algorithm: Biopsychosocial approach: CBT, mindfulness, graded exercise. Pharmacologic: SSRIs for comorbid anxiety/depression; low-dose amitriptyline for functional pain. Avoid unnecessary testing. Physical therapy for functional neurological symptoms.
us_prevalence: ~5–10% of pediatric outpatients; functional somatic symptoms affect ~2–4 million US children.
patients_needing_tx: ~500,000 with significant somatic disorder seeking pharmacotherapy.
registrational_endpoint: Change from baseline Children's Somatic Symptoms Inventory (CSSI-24) and functional impairment scale at 12 weeks.
btd_threshold_vs_soc: ≥30% CSSI-24 reduction vs. control.

symptoms: Intense fear of open or crowded spaces, avoidance, often co-occurring with panic disorder, school refusal
treatment_goal: Reduce avoidance, restore normal daily activities including school attendance
first_line_soc: CBT with gradual exposure is gold standard. SSRIs (sertraline, fluoxetine) as pharmacotherapy adjunct for moderate-severe cases.
treatment_algorithm: Step 1: CBT with interoceptive and situational exposure (panic-focused for co-occurring PD). Step 2: SSRI (sertraline starting 25 mg/day escalating to 50–200 mg). Step 3: SNRI (venlafaxine XR) if SSRI-refractory. Step 4: Short-term low-dose benzodiazepine for acute severe situations (not for chronic use). School re-entry program.
us_prevalence: ~2–3% of US adolescents; ~600,000–1 million youth with clinically significant agoraphobia (often comorbid with panic disorder).
patients_needing_tx: ~300,000 adolescents on pharmacotherapy for agoraphobia (typically SSRI).
price: $5,000–$10,000/year for novel anxiolytic.
registrational_endpoint: Mobility Inventory for Agoraphobia (MIA) change and panic attack frequency at 12 weeks vs. placebo.
btd_threshold_vs_soc: ≥40% reduction in agoraphobic avoidance on MIA vs. placebo.

symptoms: Angry/irritable mood, argumentative, defiant, vindictive behavior toward authority
treatment_goal: Reduce oppositional behavior, improve family/school function
first_line_soc: None (pharmacologic); behavioral interventions (Parent Management Training) are first-line. Pharmacotherapy targets comorbidities (ADHD, anxiety).
treatment_algorithm: Psychosocial: Parent Management Training (PMT), Collaborative Problem Solving (CPS). Pharmacologic: treat comorbid ADHD (stimulants), anxiety (SSRIs), or mood instability (low-dose risperidone off-label for severe aggression). No FDA-approved drug specifically for ODD.
us_prevalence: ~3–5% of US children; ~1.5–2.5 million youth aged 4–16.
patients_needing_tx: ~300,000 on pharmacotherapy primarily for comorbid ADHD or aggression.
price: $4,000–$8,000/year for novel irritability/aggression-targeting agent.
registrational_endpoint: Change from baseline Oppositional Subscale of SNAP-IV or Affective Reactivity Index (ARI) at 8 weeks.
btd_threshold_vs_soc: ≥30% ARI reduction vs. placebo.

symptoms: Hallucinations, delusions, disorganized thinking, flat affect, social withdrawal
treatment_goal: Reduce psychosis, prevent relapse, maintain function
first_line_soc: Atypical antipsychotics: aripiprazole (FDA-approved ≥13 years), risperidone (FDA-approved ≥13 years), quetiapine, olanzapine for early-onset schizophrenia.
treatment_algorithm: Acute: low-dose atypical antipsychotic (aripiprazole or risperidone). Maintenance: continued antipsychotic at lowest effective dose. Adjunctive: psychoeducation, CBTp, family therapy. Clozapine for treatment-resistant EOS. Avoid typicals (high EPS risk in youth).
us_prevalence: Early-onset schizophrenia (EOS, onset <18) affects ~0.1–0.5% of youth; ~100,000–200,000 US adolescents.
patients_needing_tx: ~100,000 pediatric patients on antipsychotic therapy for schizophrenia.
price: $10,000–$20,000/year for novel antipsychotic with better tolerability (minimal metabolic effects).
registrational_endpoint: Change from baseline PANSS total score at 6 weeks vs. placebo in adolescents with acute schizophrenia.
btd_threshold_vs_soc: ≥10-point greater PANSS reduction vs. placebo (vs. aripiprazole ~10–13 vs. ~6 placebo in adolescent trial).

disease: Wheezing and Asthma in Infants and Young Children
symptoms: Recurrent wheezing, cough, breathlessness, chest tightness in children <5
treatment_goal: Prevent wheeze episodes, reduce ER visits and hospitalizations
first_line_soc: Inhaled albuterol (SABA) for acute episodes; low-dose ICS (fluticasone or budesonide nebulized) for persistent asthma. Montelukast (leukotriene receptor antagonist) as alternative/adjunct.
treatment_algorithm: Intermittent wheeze: SABA prn ± short ICS burst during episodes. Persistent asthma ≥4 weeks: daily low-dose ICS (fluticasone 88 mcg/day or budesonide 0.25 mg nebulized BID). Step up: medium-dose ICS or add LABA (not <5 years), montelukast. Dupilumab FDA-approved for children ≥6 with uncontrolled moderate-severe asthma.
us_prevalence: ~5.5 million US children <18 have asthma; in infants/toddlers <5 years: ~1.5–2 million with recurrent wheeze.
patients_needing_tx: ~3 million children on daily controller therapy.
price: $3,000–$20,000/year depending on biologic vs. generic ICS.
peak_sales: ~$1.5B at 10% share for novel biologic in pediatric asthma.
registrational_endpoint: Annualized rate of severe asthma exacerbations over 52 weeks vs. placebo/SoC.
btd_threshold_vs_soc: ≥50% reduction in annualized exacerbation rate vs. placebo (dupilumab achieved ~68% reduction in VOYAGE trial).

disease: Respiratory Syncytial Virus (RSV) and Human Metapneumovirus Infections
symptoms: Rhinorrhea, cough, wheeze, fever, respiratory distress, hypoxia in infants/elderly
treatment_goal: Prevent severe disease, reduce hospitalizations
first_line_soc: Nirsevimab (Beyfortus) — FDA-approved monoclonal antibody for RSV prevention in all infants <8 months and high-risk infants up to 24 months (2023). Palivizumab: monthly IM prophylaxis for high-risk infants.
treatment_algorithm: Prevention: nirsevimab single IM dose before RSV season (replaced palivizumab for most infants). Treatment: supportive (O2, hydration); ribavirin for severe/immunocompromised (limited). Maternal RSVpreF vaccine (Abrysvo, Pfizer; Mresvia, Moderna mRNA). No approved therapy for hMPV.
us_prevalence: RSV causes ~58,000–80,000 hospitalizations in children <5/year; ~177,000 hospitalizations in adults ≥65/year in US.
patients_needing_tx: ~3.5 million US infants/year eligible for RSV prophylaxis; ~80,000 children hospitalized. Nirsevimab WAC ~$495/dose.
price: $500–$1,500/dose for next-gen extended half-life mAb or oral antiviral.
peak_sales: ~$1.75B at 50% share (Beyfortus already at ~$800M–1B revenue; next-gen could displace). Math: 3.5M × $1,000 × 50% = $1.75B.
registrational_endpoint: RSV-mediated LRTI hospitalization rate from birth through 150 days vs. placebo.
btd_threshold_vs_soc: <2% hospitalization rate vs. ~4–5% in unprotected infants (≥70% reduction).

symptoms: Wheezing, tachypnea, chest retractions, nasal congestion, fever in infants
treatment_goal: Support oxygenation, prevent respiratory failure
first_line_soc: Supportive care only (hydration, supplemental O2); no proven pharmacologic benefit from bronchodilators, steroids, or antibiotics per AAP guidelines.
treatment_algorithm: Supportive: nasal suctioning, O2 supplementation, IV/NG hydration. High-flow nasal cannula (HFNC) for moderate-severe. CPAP/ventilation for respiratory failure. Palivizumab prophylaxis for high-risk infants (RSV prevention). No approved treatment for acute bronchiolitis.
us_prevalence: ~100,000 hospitalizations/year in US infants; leading cause of infant hospitalization in first year of life.
patients_needing_tx: ~100,000 hospitalized/year; pharmacologic treatment pool very small (prophylaxis market larger).
price: $1,000–$3,000/course for inhaled antiviral or anti-inflammatory agent.
peak_sales: ~$60M at 30% share for a proven acute treatment.
registrational_endpoint: Time to medical readiness for discharge (modified Respiratory Assessment Change Score) vs. placebo.
btd_threshold_vs_soc: ≥12 hours reduction in hospital LOS vs. standard supportive care.

symptoms: Barking cough, inspiratory stridor, hoarseness, low-grade fever in toddlers
treatment_goal: Relieve airway obstruction, reduce hospitalizations
first_line_soc: Oral dexamethasone (single dose 0.15–0.6 mg/kg) for moderate-severe croup; nebulized epinephrine for severe/impending respiratory failure.
treatment_algorithm: Mild croup: humidified air, reassurance. Moderate: oral dexamethasone 0.15–0.6 mg/kg single dose (budesonide or IM dexamethasone if vomiting). Severe: nebulized racemic epinephrine (0.5 mL of 2.25% in NS) + dexamethasone; hospital admission; intubation for impending failure.
us_prevalence: ~3–6% of children annually; ~150,000–300,000 children/year with significant croup; ~30,000–40,000 hospitalizations/year.
patients_needing_tx: ~150,000/year requiring pharmacotherapy (steroid ± epinephrine).
price: $200–$1,000/course for improved steroid formulation or novel anti-inflammatory.
registrational_endpoint: Westley Croup Score improvement at 2 hours and reduction in hospitalization rate vs. placebo.
btd_threshold_vs_soc: ≥1.5-point Westley score reduction vs. placebo at 2 hours (dexamethasone achieves ~2 vs. ~0.5 for placebo).

symptoms: Severe inspiratory stridor, high fever, toxic appearance, respiratory distress, croup-like bark
treatment_goal: Secure airway, eradicate bacterial infection
first_line_soc: Airway management (intubation often required) + IV antibiotics: ampicillin-sulbactam or cefuroxime; add antistaphylococcal coverage (vancomycin if MRSA suspected).
treatment_algorithm: ICU admission; endotracheal intubation in most. IV antibiotics: ampicillin-sulbactam or ceftriaxone + vancomycin for MRSA coverage. Nebulized racemic epinephrine as temporizing measure. Airway suctioning. Duration 7–14 days IV antibiotics then oral step-down.
us_prevalence: Rare; ~3,000–5,000 cases/year in US children (primarily <6 years).
patients_needing_tx: ~3,000–5,000 children/year requiring IV antibiotics (short-course acute treatment).
price: $500–$2,000/course for novel anti-MRSA topical tracheal antibiotic.
registrational_endpoint: Time to extubation and bacterial eradication at 48–72 hours.
btd_threshold_vs_soc: Time to extubation <24 hours vs. >36 hours with standard IV antibiotics.

symptoms: Chronic oxygen dependence, recurrent wheeze, poor growth, pulmonary hypertension in ex-premature infants
treatment_goal: Wean from oxygen/respiratory support, prevent pulmonary hypertension
first_line_soc: Diuretics (furosemide, chlorothiazide + spironolactone) for pulmonary edema; postnatal corticosteroids (dexamethasone) for ventilator-dependent BPD; caffeine; inhaled bronchodilators.
treatment_algorithm: Prevention: antenatal steroids; surfactant; caffeine; vitamin A. Treatment: diuretics (furosemide 1 mg/kg/day); inhaled corticosteroids (budesonide); systemic steroids for ventilator-dependent BPD (low-dose dexamethasone); sildenafil or bosentan for BPD-associated PAH. Nutritional support; RSV prophylaxis.
us_prevalence: ~10,000–12,000 new BPD cases/year in US (VLBW infants); ~30,000–40,000 children living with BPD-related morbidity.
patients_needing_tx: ~10,000–12,000 new cases/year on active pharmacotherapy; ~30,000 with chronic BPD.
price: $5,000–$20,000/year for novel BPD therapeutic (stem cell, anti-inflammatory, anti-VEGF modulator).
peak_sales: ~$90M at 30% share. Could qualify for pediatric orphan pricing.
registrational_endpoint: BPD severity classification (Grade I/II/III) at 36 weeks PMA and oxygen/respiratory support requirement at 28 days.
btd_threshold_vs_soc: ≥30% reduction in severe BPD rate (Grade III) vs. standard care.

disease: Persistent Pulmonary Hypertension of the Newborn (PPHN)
symptoms: Severe hypoxemia after birth, cyanosis, respiratory distress, high PVR, right-to-left shunting
treatment_goal: Reduce pulmonary vascular resistance, achieve adequate oxygenation
first_line_soc: Inhaled nitric oxide (iNO, INOmax) — FDA-approved for term/near-term neonates with PPHN; vasodilates pulmonary vasculature selectively.
treatment_algorithm: Step 1: Optimize ventilation, correct acidosis, maintain normothermia. Step 2: iNO (20 ppm). Step 3: Sildenafil (PO/IV, off-label in neonates) for iNO-refractory PPHN. Step 4: Prostacyclins (iloprost, epoprostenol) for refractory. Step 5: ECMO for life-threatening refractory PPHN.
us_prevalence: ~1–2/1,000 live births; ~4,000–8,000 cases/year in US.
patients_needing_tx: ~4,000–8,000 neonates/year requiring iNO and/or additional PAH agents.
price: $10,000–$30,000/course for novel rapid-onset pulmonary vasodilator.
registrational_endpoint: Oxygenation index (OI) reduction at 4 hours post-treatment and ECMO rate reduction vs. placebo.
btd_threshold_vs_soc: OI reduction ≥50% at 4 hours vs. placebo; ECMO rate <15% vs. ~40% in iNO trials.

symptoms: Grunting, nasal flaring, tachypnea, retractions, cyanosis in premature infants; surfactant deficiency
treatment_goal: Restore surfactant, achieve adequate oxygenation and ventilation
first_line_soc: Surfactant replacement therapy (beractant/Survanta, calfactant/Infasurf, poractant alfa/Curosurf) — gold standard for premature infants <30 weeks with RDS.
treatment_algorithm: Prevention: antenatal corticosteroids (betamethasone) for at-risk preterm deliveries. Respiratory support: CPAP for mild; intubation for moderate-severe. Surfactant: early rescue (<2 hours) or prophylactic. INSURE technique (Intubate-Surfactant-Extubate to CPAP). Caffeine for AOP. Gentle ventilation strategies.
us_prevalence: ~30,000–40,000 cases/year in US (primarily <34 weeks GA); affects ~60% of infants <28 weeks.
patients_needing_tx: ~30,000–35,000 neonates/year receiving surfactant therapy.
price: $500–$3,000/dose for next-generation synthetic surfactant.
peak_sales: ~$32M at 50% share for improved synthetic surfactant (CHF 5633, lucinactant).
registrational_endpoint: FiO2 requirement at 72 hours and BPD rate at 36 weeks PMA vs. comparator surfactant.
btd_threshold_vs_soc: ≥25% reduction in BPD rate vs. standard poractant alfa.

symptoms: Respiratory distress at birth, meconium-stained amniotic fluid, hypoxia, tachypnea, air-trapping
treatment_goal: Support oxygenation, prevent PPHN and respiratory failure
first_line_soc: Supportive care: supplemental O2, CPAP, mechanical ventilation; surfactant therapy for severe MAS; inhaled nitric oxide (iNO) for PPHN.
treatment_algorithm: Mild: O2 via hood. Moderate-severe: CPAP/mechanical ventilation. Surfactant (Survanta, Calfactant) for surfactant inactivation by meconium. iNO for PPHN (starting 20 ppm). HFOV for refractory cases. ECMO for life-threatening hypoxemia. Antibiotics (ampicillin + gentamicin) if infection suspected.
us_prevalence: ~25,000–30,000 cases/year in US (5–10% of meconium-stained deliveries develop MAS).
patients_needing_tx: ~10,000–15,000 severe MAS cases/year requiring active pharmacotherapy (surfactant, iNO).
price: $2,000–$10,000/course for improved surfactant or anti-inflammatory.
registrational_endpoint: Duration of mechanical ventilation and oxygenation index improvement at 24–48 hours.
btd_threshold_vs_soc: ≥25% reduction in duration of mechanical ventilation vs. standard surfactant.

symptoms: Cessation of breathing >20 seconds, bradycardia, oxygen desaturation in premature infants
treatment_goal: Prevent apnea episodes, enable safe discharge
first_line_soc: Caffeine citrate (methylxanthine) — FDA-approved, standard of care for AOP in premature infants; reduces apnea episodes and improves neurodevelopment.
treatment_algorithm: First-line: caffeine citrate IV/PO (loading 20 mg/kg, maintenance 5–10 mg/kg/day). Respiratory support: CPAP/HFNC adjunct. If caffeine fails: IPPV. Discharge criteria: ≥5–7 apnea-free days. Doxapram (off-label in US) for caffeine-refractory cases.
us_prevalence: ~30,000–40,000 premature infants/year in US with clinically significant AOP; essentially all infants <28 weeks GA have AOP.
patients_needing_tx: ~30,000–40,000 premature infants/year requiring caffeine treatment.
price: $500–$2,000/course for novel respiratory stimulant or optimized caffeine formulation.
peak_sales: ~$25M at 50% share (small neonatal market; caffeine generic).
registrational_endpoint: Rate of apnea episodes per day at day 7 and rate of extubation success vs. placebo.
btd_threshold_vs_soc: ≥30% reduction in apnea episodes vs. caffeine standard dosing.

symptoms: Pneumothorax, pneumomediastinum; sudden respiratory deterioration, decreased breath sounds, hypoxia in neonates
treatment_goal: Evacuate air, re-expand lung, prevent recurrence
first_line_soc: None (pharmacologic); treatment is procedural (needle aspiration, chest tube) for tension pneumothorax. Supplemental O2 for small pneumothorax (nitrogen washout accelerates reabsorption).
treatment_algorithm: Small/asymptomatic: 100% O2 therapy (accelerates reabsorption). Symptomatic: needle thoracostomy → chest tube drainage. Mechanical ventilation optimization to minimize barotrauma. No pharmacologic standard of care.
us_prevalence: ~2,000–5,000 neonatal pneumothorax cases/year in US (1–2% of all NICU admissions).
patients_needing_tx: Primarily procedural; ~1,000–2,000 may benefit from pharmacologic adjuncts.
price: N/A (surgical/procedural management dominant).
registrational_endpoint: Time to air-leak resolution and rate of re-accumulation at 24–48 hours.
btd_threshold_vs_soc: N/A.

disease: Respiratory Support in Neonates and Infants
symptoms: Various respiratory failure causes requiring O2 supplementation, CPAP, or mechanical ventilation
treatment_goal: Maintain adequate gas exchange, wean from respiratory support
first_line_soc: Not a disease per se; supportive/procedural. Pharmacologic adjuncts: caffeine for AOP, surfactant for RDS, iNO for PPHN, steroids for BPD, diuretics.
treatment_algorithm: Management platform: supplemental O2, HFNC, CPAP, HFOV, ECMO. Pharmacologic adjuncts individualized per underlying condition. See specific diagnoses (RDS, BPD, PPHN, AOP).
us_prevalence: ~80,000–100,000 neonates/infants/year requiring respiratory support in US NICUs.
patients_needing_tx: Multiple pharmacologic agents per the underlying condition; see individual disease entries.
price: Variable by agent ($500–$30,000/course).
registrational_endpoint: Days on respiratory support and rate of successful extubation.
btd_threshold_vs_soc: N/A (umbrella supportive care category).

symptoms: Rapid breathing, mild respiratory distress at birth, self-resolving within 24–72 hours
treatment_goal: Supportive oxygenation; prevent progression to respiratory failure
first_line_soc: Supportive care: supplemental O2, CPAP if needed; spontaneously resolves within 24–72 hours. No pharmacologic first-line treatment.
treatment_algorithm: Supportive: O2 supplementation, CPAP for hypoxemia; IV fluids if unable to feed; antibiotics if sepsis cannot be excluded. Furosemide studied but not routinely recommended (Cochrane 2020: insufficient evidence).
us_prevalence: ~60,000–80,000 cases/year in US (1–2% of all births; more common after C-section).
patients_needing_tx: ~5,000–10,000 requiring pharmacologic intervention beyond O2.
price: N/A (self-limiting; no pharmacologic market).
registrational_endpoint: Duration of oxygen supplementation and hospitalization length at 48 hours.
btd_threshold_vs_soc: N/A.

symptoms: Joint swelling, stiffness, pain, limited motion, morning stiffness, uveitis risk
treatment_goal: Achieve clinical remission, prevent joint damage and blindness
first_line_soc: NSAIDs (naproxen, ibuprofen) for mild; methotrexate (10–15 mg/m²/week SC/PO) for oligoarticular/polyarticular JIA; intraarticular corticosteroids.
treatment_algorithm: Step 1: NSAIDs + physical therapy. Step 2: Intraarticular triamcinolone. Step 3: Methotrexate for polyarticular JIA; systemic steroids for severe systemic JIA. Step 4: Biologics — etanercept (FDA-approved ≥2y polyarticular), adalimumab, abatacept, tocilizumab (systemic JIA), canakinumab (systemic JIA). Step 5: JAK inhibitors (baricitinib FDA-approved for polyarticular JIA ≥2y, 2023).
us_prevalence: ~294,000 children with JIA in US; ~50,000 new diagnoses/year.
patients_needing_tx: ~150,000 on biologic/DMARD therapy; ~50,000 on systemic therapy.
price: $20,000–$50,000/year for biologic/JAK inhibitor; pediatric dosing formulations.
registrational_endpoint: JIA ACR Pedi 30 response (≥30% improvement) at 16–24 weeks vs. placebo.
btd_threshold_vs_soc: ≥30% higher ACR Pedi 70 response rate vs. placebo (abatacept achieved ~33% difference).

symptoms: High unremitting fever, cytopenias, hyperferritinemia, splenomegaly, coagulopathy, CNS involvement
treatment_goal: Suppress cytokine storm, prevent multi-organ failure
first_line_soc: High-dose corticosteroids (methylprednisolone 30 mg/kg/day × 3 days pulse); cyclosporine for refractory. Anakinra (IL-1 blocker) increasingly used as first-line for MAS/sJIA.
treatment_algorithm: Step 1: High-dose methylprednisolone pulses. Step 2: Cyclosporine 2–7 mg/kg/day for corticosteroid-refractory. Step 3: Anakinra (IL-1 inhibitor) — highly effective in MAS-sJIA. Step 4: Emapalumab (anti-IFN-γ, FDA-approved for primary HLH) considered for fulminant MAS. IVIG adjunctive. HLH-94 protocol for fulminant.
us_prevalence: ~2,000–3,000 MAS episodes/year (mostly complicating sJIA); ~10–20% of sJIA patients develop MAS.
patients_needing_tx: ~2,000–3,000 acute episodes/year requiring aggressive pharmacotherapy.
price: $50,000–$200,000/course for targeted cytokine therapy (emapalumab ~$150,000/course).
registrational_endpoint: MAS response rate (ferritin normalization, cytopenias resolution) at 8 weeks vs. corticosteroids alone.
btd_threshold_vs_soc: ≥50% complete response rate at 8 weeks vs. ~25% with corticosteroids alone.

symptoms: Prolonged fever, rash, conjunctivitis, red lips, swollen lymph node
treatment_goal: Prevent coronary artery aneurysms (CAA), reduce inflammation
first_line_soc: IVIG (2 g/kg IV single infusion) + high-dose aspirin (80–100 mg/kg/day) within first 10 days — reduces CAA from ~25% to ~4%.
treatment_algorithm: Step 1: IVIG 2 g/kg + aspirin. If fever persists >36 hours: second IVIG dose or infliximab (5 mg/kg). Step 2: Corticosteroids (prednisolone) adjunctive for high-risk CAA. Cyclosporine for IVIG-resistant. Step 3: Anticoagulation (heparin → warfarin or LMWH) for large aneurysms. Anakinra for MAS/refractory KD.
us_prevalence: ~5,000–6,000 US cases/year; leading cause of acquired heart disease in US children. Estimated 19–25 per 100,000 children <5 years.
patients_needing_tx: ~5,000–6,000 children/year requiring IVIG + aspirin (acute treatment).
price: $15,000–$30,000/course for next-gen anti-IL-1 or anti-IL-6 targeted therapy.
peak_sales: ~$55M at 50% share for IVIG-refractory patients (biologic adjunct).
registrational_endpoint: Coronary artery Z-score normalization at 6–8 weeks and rate of CAA formation vs. IVIG alone.
btd_threshold_vs_soc: CAA rate <2% vs. ~8% with IVIG monotherapy in high-risk patients.

symptoms: Daytime wetting, urgency, nocturia, bed-wetting; impaired bladder control
treatment_goal: Achieve continence, prevent secondary psychological harm
first_line_soc: Behavioral interventions (bladder training, scheduled voiding) first. Desmopressin (DDAVP) FDA-approved for nocturnal enuresis ≥6 years; oxybutynin for overactive bladder.
treatment_algorithm: Monosymptomatic nocturnal enuresis: enuresis alarm (behavioral) + desmopressin (0.2–0.4 mg PO or 120 mcg sublingual). Overactive bladder: oxybutynin (FDA-approved ≥5y), solifenacin (FDA-approved ≥6y, 2018). Dysfunctional voiding: biofeedback + anticholinergics. Bladder training for all.
us_prevalence: ~5–15% of children 5–10 years; ~2–3 million US children with significant urinary incontinence.
patients_needing_tx: ~1.5 million on pharmacotherapy (desmopressin + anticholinergics).
price: $2,000–$8,000/year for novel beta-3 agonist or selective anticholinergic.
registrational_endpoint: Number of wet nights per week (primary) and proportion achieving ≥50% reduction in wet nights at 12 weeks.
btd_threshold_vs_soc: ≥80% dry nights vs. ~60% with desmopressin alone.

symptoms: Involuntary fecal soiling (encopresis), constipation, overflow incontinence, social embarrassment
treatment_goal: Achieve regular bowel control, prevent constipation, improve quality of life
first_line_soc: Osmotic laxatives (PEG 3350, MiraLax) for constipation/fecal impaction; dietary fiber; behavioral bowel training.
treatment_algorithm: Step 1: Disimpaction (PEG 3350 high-dose or enemas). Step 2: Maintenance PEG 3350 (0.4–0.8 g/kg/day). Step 3: Behavioral training (scheduled sitting after meals). Step 4: Biofeedback for functional fecal incontinence without constipation. No FDA-approved drug specifically for pediatric encopresis.
us_prevalence: ~3–4% of school-age children have encopresis; ~1.5–2 million US children 4–12.
patients_needing_tx: ~1 million children on active laxative therapy.
price: $500–$2,000/year for novel motility/microbiome agent.
registrational_endpoint: Days per week with clean underwear (soiling frequency) at 8 weeks vs. PEG placebo.
btd_threshold_vs_soc: ≥50% reduction in soiling episodes/week vs. PEG alone.

symptoms: Brief unexpected episode of color change, tone change, unresponsiveness, or abnormal breathing in infant
treatment_goal: Identify serious underlying cause, reassure and educate caregivers
first_line_soc: None (pharmacologic); BRUE is a diagnosis of exclusion requiring investigation. Low-risk BRUE: reassurance and education per AAP. Higher-risk: hospitalization for monitoring.
treatment_algorithm: No pharmacologic treatment for BRUE itself. Evaluate for and treat: GERD (if suspected cause), arrhythmia, seizure, infection. Low-risk BRUE: pulse oximetry monitoring, parent education, no workup per 2016 AAP guideline. Higher-risk: full workup and monitoring.
us_prevalence: ~12 per 10,000 infants in first year of life; ~40,000–50,000 events/year in US.
patients_needing_tx: No direct pharmacologic market (management of underlying cause only).
price: N/A.
registrational_endpoint: N/A.
btd_threshold_vs_soc: N/A.

disease: Sudden Unexpected Infant Death (SUID) and Sudden Infant Death Syndrome (SIDS)
symptoms: Sudden unexplained death in infant <1 year during sleep; no identifiable cause at autopsy
treatment_goal: Identify at-risk infants, implement safe sleep to prevent SIDS
first_line_soc: None (pharmacologic); prevention through safe sleep practices (back sleeping, firm mattress, no co-sleeping per AAP). No pharmacologic intervention proven to prevent SIDS.
treatment_algorithm: Prevention: Safe Sleep ABCs (Alone, on Back, in Crib). No pharmacologic prevention proven. Investigation: cardiac monitoring in high-risk (apnea history); caffeine for AOP in premature. Serotonin pathway and cardiac channelopathy research ongoing for biological targets.
us_prevalence: ~3,400 SUID deaths/year in US (SIDS ~1,400; ill-defined ~1,000; accidental suffocation ~1,000).
patients_needing_tx: No pharmacologic market currently; research ongoing.
price: No current pharmacologic market.
registrational_endpoint: N/A (no pharmacologic trial endpoint established).
btd_threshold_vs_soc: N/A.

disease: Rash in Infants and Young Children
symptoms: Skin eruption; varied morphology (macular, papular, vesicular); may signal viral, bacterial, allergic, or inflammatory cause
treatment_goal: Identify cause, relieve itch/discomfort, treat infection if present
first_line_soc: Treat underlying cause: topical corticosteroids (hydrocortisone 1%) for eczema/contact dermatitis; antihistamines (diphenhydramine, cetirizine) for urticaria; antibiotics for bacterial. Emollients for dry skin.
treatment_algorithm: Eczema/atopic dermatitis: emollients + TCS → tacrolimus/pimecrolimus → dupilumab (FDA-approved ≥6 months, 2023) for moderate-severe. Viral exanthem: supportive care. Bacterial (impetigo): topical mupirocin or retapamulin; oral cephalexin for widespread. Urticaria: antihistamines (cetirizine, loratadine). Meningococcal/purpuric: emergency IV antibiotics.
us_prevalence: Symptom affecting ~30% of infants in first year; atopic dermatitis alone ~13% of US children (~7 million).
patients_needing_tx: ~4 million pediatric patients/year with significant rash requiring pharmacotherapy.
price: $5,000–$30,000/year for biologic (dupilumab, tralokinumab) for atopic dermatitis.
tam_gross: 3,000,000 with moderate-severe eczema × $15,000 = ~$45B (global eczema market; US portion ~$15B)
peak_sales: ~$2B at 15% share for novel atopic dermatitis biologic in pediatric population.
registrational_endpoint: IGA score ≤1 and ≥2-point improvement from baseline at 16 weeks (for atopic dermatitis indication).
btd_threshold_vs_soc: ≥40% IGA 0/1 response vs. <15% placebo rate (dupilumab in LIBERTY AD PEDS: ~52% IGA 0/1 at 16 weeks).

symptoms: Infrequent hard stools, straining, abdominal pain, withholding behavior, fecal soiling
treatment_goal: Restore regular soft bowel movements, prevent recurrence
first_line_soc: PEG 3350 (polyethylene glycol, MiraLax) — osmotic laxative; first-line per NASPGHAN guidelines. Dose: 0.4–0.8 g/kg/day. Generic and OTC; highly effective.
treatment_algorithm: Step 1: Disimpaction (PEG high-dose or rectal enema). Step 2: Maintenance PEG 3350. Step 3: Add lactulose or senna if PEG alone insufficient. Step 4: Lubiprostone (FDA-approved for functional constipation in children ≥6 years, 2013). Linaclotide (FDA-approved for IBS-C ≥6 years, 2021). Step 5: Biofeedback and behavioral training.
us_prevalence: ~3–5% of all pediatric visits; ~1–2 million US children with chronic functional constipation.
patients_needing_tx: ~1 million children on regular laxative therapy.
price: $3,000–$8,000/year for novel secretagogue or prokinetic.
registrational_endpoint: Spontaneous complete bowel movements per week (SCBM/week) increase at 12 weeks vs. PEG.
btd_threshold_vs_soc: ≥1 additional SCBM/week vs. PEG alone.

symptoms: Acute or chronic cough; productive or dry; disrupts sleep and daily activities
treatment_goal: Identify and treat underlying cause, relieve cough
first_line_soc: Treat underlying cause: antibiotics for bacterial infection; bronchodilators/ICS for asthma; nasal corticosteroids for upper airway cough. Honey (≥1 year) for acute cough has evidence. Codeine/DXM OTC cough suppressants not recommended in children.
treatment_algorithm: Diagnosis-driven: asthma → ICS/SABA; protracted bacterial bronchitis → amoxicillin-clavulanate × 2–4 weeks; GERD → PPI; allergic → nasal steroids + antihistamines. Chronic cough: chest CT, bronchoscopy if indicated. No cough suppressant approved/recommended for <12 years.
us_prevalence: Cough is the most common reason for pediatric outpatient visit; ~30 million visits/year in US.
patients_needing_tx: Mostly treated with generic agents; novel drug market exists for chronic refractory cough (P2X3 antagonist).
price: $5,000–$15,000/year for novel cough suppressant (gefapixant-like P2X3 antagonist for pediatric chronic cough).
registrational_endpoint: 24-hour cough frequency (acoustic monitoring) reduction at 12 weeks vs. placebo.
btd_threshold_vs_soc: ≥30% reduction in 24-hour cough count vs. placebo (gefapixant achieved ~18–18% in adult RCC trials).

symptoms: Elevated body temperature ≥38°C (100.4°F), irritability, poor feeding, flushing, febrile seizure risk
treatment_goal: Provide comfort, identify serious bacterial infection, prevent febrile seizure recurrence
first_line_soc: Acetaminophen (10–15 mg/kg q4–6h) or ibuprofen (≥6 months; 5–10 mg/kg q6–8h) for fever ≥38.5–39°C causing discomfort.
treatment_algorithm: Fever management: alternating/combination acetaminophen + ibuprofen if needed (debated). Antibiotics only for proven/high-risk bacterial infection (not routine for viral fever). For infants <29 days: hospitalization + empiric ampicillin + gentamicin (high risk of serious bacterial infection). For infants 29–60 days: risk stratification (Rochester criteria); lumbar puncture if high risk.
us_prevalence: ~30 million pediatric febrile episodes/year in US; #1 reason for pediatric ER visit.
patients_needing_tx: ~20 million US children/year receiving antipyretics; generic market. Novel drug market limited.
price: $100–$500/course for novel antipyretic with better profile.
peak_sales: ~$200M for novel prescription antipyretic at 2.5% share.
registrational_endpoint: Time to fever resolution (<38°C) and patient comfort score at 2–4 hours vs. acetaminophen/ibuprofen.
btd_threshold_vs_soc: ≥30-minute faster fever resolution vs. ibuprofen AND superior comfort score.

disease: Nausea and Vomiting in Infants and Children
symptoms: Vomiting, nausea, poor feeding, dehydration risk; multiple etiologies
treatment_goal: Control vomiting, prevent dehydration, identify serious cause
first_line_soc: Ondansetron (0.15 mg/kg PO/IV) — most effective antiemetic in children ≥1 month for AGE; reduces hospitalization rates. ORS co-administration.
treatment_algorithm: Step 1: ORS with small frequent volumes + ondansetron single dose (AAP-supported for moderate-severe vomiting with dehydration). Step 2: IV fluid if oral failure. Step 3: Treat cause: metoclopramide for gastroparesis; erythromycin for prokinesis; domperidone (not FDA-approved in US). Cyclic vomiting syndrome: cyproheptadine or amitriptyline for prevention; sumatriptan for acute episodes.
us_prevalence: ~3 million pediatric AGE episodes/year requiring medical attention; cyclic vomiting syndrome ~2% of children.
patients_needing_tx: ~2 million children/year receiving antiemetics; 500,000 with CVS.
price: $500–$3,000/course for novel antiemetic or CVS preventive.
registrational_endpoint: Number of vomiting episodes in first 24 hours and rate of IV fluid requirement vs. placebo.
btd_threshold_vs_soc: ≥50% reduction in IV hydration rate vs. placebo (ondansetron: 14% vs. 31% IV in RCTs).

symptoms: Loose/watery frequent stools, dehydration, fever, abdominal cramps, nausea
treatment_goal: Prevent dehydration, resolve infection, restore normal bowel function
first_line_soc: Oral rehydration therapy (ORS) — WHO/UNICEF gold standard for dehydration prevention/treatment. Zinc supplementation reduces diarrhea severity in children <5 in developing countries.
treatment_algorithm: Step 1: ORS for mild-moderate dehydration; IV fluids for severe. Step 2: Continue feeding (BRAT diet not recommended). Step 3: Zinc (20 mg/day × 10–14 days) for children <5 in resource-limited settings. Step 4: Antibiotics for specific pathogens (azithromycin for Campylobacter, metronidazole for C. diff, TMP-SMX for Shigella). Racecadotril (enkephalinase inhibitor, not FDA-approved in US). Probiotics (L. rhamnosus GG) modest evidence.
us_prevalence: ~500 million episodes globally/year; in US: ~1.5 million pediatric office visits + ~220,000 hospitalizations/year for diarrheal illness.
patients_needing_tx: ~1.5 million US children/year with acute gastroenteritis warranting pharmacologic attention.
price: $500–$2,000/course for novel antidiarrheal or microbiome restoration agent.
registrational_endpoint: Time to resolution of diarrhea (≤3 stools/day) and stool output reduction at 48–72 hours vs. ORS + placebo.
btd_threshold_vs_soc: ≥24-hour faster resolution vs. ORS alone.

symptoms: Inconsolable crying ≥3 hours/day ≥3 days/week in healthy infant <3 months
treatment_goal: Reduce crying episodes, support caregiver
first_line_soc: None proven; soothing techniques (swaddling, white noise). Simethicone (gas drops) widely used but evidence weak. Probiotics (Lactobacillus reuteri DSM17938) modestly effective in breastfed infants.
treatment_algorithm: Non-pharmacologic: soothing (swaddling, swinging, white noise); car-ride simulation. Pharmacologic: L. reuteri DSM17938 probiotic (modest evidence, reduced crying ~50 min/day in breastfed infants). Simethicone: no proven benefit (Cochrane). Dicyclomine contraindicated <6 months. Trial elimination diet for breastfeeding mothers (cow's milk protein).
us_prevalence: ~10–26% of infants; ~400,000–1 million US infants with colic annually.
patients_needing_tx: ~600,000 infants/year with colic where parents seek pharmacologic/probiotic treatment.
price: $200–$1,000/course for novel gut microbiome or motility agent.
registrational_endpoint: Daily crying/fussing duration (hours/day) at 4 weeks vs. placebo.
btd_threshold_vs_soc: ≥60-minute/day reduction in crying vs. placebo (L. reuteri achieves ~50 min/day).

symptoms: Excessive or prolonged crying beyond normal developmental patterns; symptom of underlying distress
treatment_goal: Identify cause, address underlying condition, support family
first_line_soc: None (pharmacologic); identify and treat underlying cause (pain, hunger, colic, illness). Behavioral support for caregivers.
treatment_algorithm: Evaluation: rule out pain (otitis media, reflux, hair tourniquet, intussusception). Treat underlying condition. Support caregiver: parental education; prevention of shaken baby syndrome. No standalone pharmacologic therapy for crying.
us_prevalence: Symptom, not disease; hundreds of thousands of pediatric ER visits/year for excessive crying.
patients_needing_tx: No direct pharmacologic market; treatment targets underlying condition.
price: N/A.
registrational_endpoint: N/A.
btd_threshold_vs_soc: N/A.

symptoms: Normal developmental distress with parent separation or unfamiliar people (peak 6–24 months)
treatment_goal: Support normal developmental progression; differentiate from pathologic SAD
first_line_soc: None (pharmacologic); developmentally normal behavior managed with parental reassurance, consistent routines, and gradual separation.
treatment_algorithm: Non-pharmacologic: consistent caregiver response; predictable routines; graduated exposure. If persistence beyond 3 years suggests pathologic separation anxiety disorder (see SAD entry). Pharmacologic treatment only if meets clinical criteria for anxiety disorder (SSRIs as per SAD).
us_prevalence: ~100% of infants experience transient stranger/separation anxiety; pathologic SAD (see above) affects ~4% of children.
patients_needing_tx: No pharmacologic market for normal developmental anxiety.
price: N/A (normal developmental phenomenon).
registrational_endpoint: N/A.
btd_threshold_vs_soc: N/A.

disease: Agitation, Confusion, and Neuromuscular Blockade in Critically Ill Patients
symptoms: Restlessness, delirium, combativeness, confusion, inability to tolerate ventilator
treatment_goal: Achieve analgesia-first sedation; prevent delirium; facilitate ventilator tolerance
first_line_soc: Propofol (short-term light sedation) or dexmedetomidine (Precedex) as preferred sedative agents per PADIS guidelines; analgesic-first approach (fentanyl, morphine for pain). Haloperidol or quetiapine for delirium treatment (not prevention per MIND trial).
treatment_algorithm: PADIS (Pain, Agitation/Sedation, Delirium, Immobility, Sleep) guidelines: Analgesia-first: opioids (fentanyl IV for procedure pain; morphine/hydromorphone). Sedation: minimize depth — use RASS target 0 to -2. Propofol IV (short-term intubation, ICU sedation). Dexmedetomidine (alpha-2 agonist): reduces delirium, allows extubation while sedated, avoids respiratory depression. Midazolam (benzodiazepine): avoid as preferred sedative (↑delirium); used for refractory agitation. Delirium: haloperidol, quetiapine (evidence mixed but widely used). NMB: cisatracurium continuous infusion for severe ARDS or ventilator dyssynchrony. Novel: brexanolone, remimazolam (ultrashort benzo with reversal). Dexmedetomidine for alcohol withdrawal delirium (DAWS).
us_prevalence: ~750,000–1M ICU patients/year requiring sedation; delirium in ICU: ~50–80% of mechanically ventilated patients (~400,000–600,000/year).
patients_needing_tx: ~700,000/year require pharmacologic sedation/analgesia management; ~500,000/year with ICU delirium requiring treatment.
price: $3,000–$8,000 per ICU stay for novel sedation/delirium agent (short inpatient use; priced vs. dexmedetomidine generic ~$500–$1,500/day).
registrational_endpoint: Delirium-free days (CAM-ICU negative days) over 14 days from randomization; proportion without ICU delirium at 7 days; Richmond Agitation-Sedation Scale (RASS) within target range.
btd_threshold_vs_soc: ≥3 additional delirium-free and coma-free days vs. dexmedetomidine at 14 days; ≥30% reduction in delirium incidence vs. haloperidol in mechanically ventilated patients.

symptoms: Severe shortness of breath, low oxygen levels, bilateral lung infiltrates, rapid deterioration
treatment_goal: Restore adequate oxygenation; prevent VILI; reduce mortality
first_line_soc: Lung-protective mechanical ventilation (low tidal volume 6 mL/kg IBW, PEEP titration, plateau pressure ≤30 cmH₂O — ARDSNet protocol) is the cornerstone of care. Prone positioning for moderate-severe ARDS (PaO₂/FiO₂ <150, ≥16h/day — PROSEVA trial: 28-day mortality reduced 16% absolute).
treatment_algorithm: Ventilator: Low VT (6 mL/kg IBW), PEEP per ARDSNet table, FiO₂ titration to SpO₂ 92–96%. Prone positioning (≥16h/day): strong mortality benefit for P:F <150. Neuromuscular blockade: cisatracurium for moderate-severe ARDS (reduces ventilator dyssynchrony). Conservative fluid strategy (FACTT trial). Corticosteroids: methylprednisolone (IV 0.5–2 mg/kg/day) for early moderate-severe ARDS and late fiboproliferative phase — moderate benefit. IL-6 inhibition (tocilizumab): benefit in COVID-ARDS. No approved pharmacologic SoC beyond steroids and supportive care. Novel: Imatinib (TIE2 pathway stabilizer) — Phase 2/3 trials ongoing for ARDS. Aviptadil (VIP analog) — trials. Interferon-β (Phase 2). ECMO for refractory severe ARDS. Surfactant (not proven in adult ARDS).
us_prevalence: ~591,000 new ARDS cases/year in US (DelveInsight 2023 estimate); ~177K mild, ~276K moderate, ~138K severe. Prevalent ICU patients with ARDS at any given time: ~25,000–35,000.
patients_needing_tx: ~400,000–500,000/year with moderate-severe ARDS requiring pharmacologic adjuncts (steroids, NMB, experimental agents). Novel pharmacologic addressable: ~250,000/year (moderate-severe).
price: $15,000–$30,000 per ARDS episode for acute-use novel agent (short inpatient course; valued against ICU cost and mortality reduction).
registrational_endpoint: 28-day all-cause mortality (primary) in moderate-severe ARDS (PaO₂/FiO₂ ≤200 on PEEP ≥5); ventilator-free days at 28 days as key secondary.
btd_threshold_vs_soc: ≥6 absolute percentage point reduction in 28-day mortality (from ~35–40% baseline) vs. lung-protective ventilation + placebo; ≥2 additional ventilator-free days at 28 days.

symptoms: Inability to maintain adequate O₂ or CO₂ levels; dyspnea, cyanosis, altered mental status
treatment_goal: Restore gas exchange; treat underlying etiology; avoid ventilator-induced injury
first_line_soc: Supplemental oxygen and/or non-invasive ventilation (NIV/BiPAP) as initial intervention. Invasive mechanical ventilation for refractory cases. Treat underlying cause (bronchodilators, diuretics, antibiotics, anticoagulation as appropriate).
treatment_algorithm: Oxygen escalation: NC → Venturi mask → HFNC (humidified high-flow O₂) → NIV (CPAP/BiPAP) → invasive MV. For ARDS: lung-protective ventilation + prone positioning (see ARDS entry). Hypercapnic (COPD): NIV + bronchodilators + corticosteroids. Cardiogenic: diuretics + vasodilators + inotropes. Pharmacologic adjuncts: corticosteroids (methylprednisolone for ARDS, severe pneumonia); iNO (inhaled nitric oxide) for refractory hypoxemia; epoprostenol inhaled; neuromuscular blockade for ventilator dyssynchrony. ECMO for refractory (VV-ECMO for respiratory, VA-ECMO for cardiogenic). Weaning: SAT (spontaneous awakening trial) + SBT (spontaneous breathing trial) + early mobilization.
us_prevalence: ~1.5–2 million hospitalized patients/year with acute respiratory failure requiring some form of respiratory support. ~700,000–900,000 require mechanical ventilation/year.
patients_needing_tx: ~800,000–1M/year requiring pharmacologic adjuncts; novel agents (iNO, novel biologics) addressable: ~300,000.
price: $10,000–$25,000 per hospitalization for novel inhaled or IV adjunct (iNO $15–25K/episode; novel biologics similar range).
registrational_endpoint: 28-day all-cause mortality; ventilator-free days at 28 days; time to successful extubation; change in PaO₂/FiO₂ ratio at 72 hours.
btd_threshold_vs_soc: ≥6 absolute percentage point reduction in 28-day mortality vs. standard ventilatory management; ≥3 additional ventilator-free days at 28 days.

symptoms: Low oxygen saturation, confusion, cyanosis, tachycardia, shortness of breath
treatment_goal: Identify and correct underlying cause; restore adequate oxygenation (SpO₂ ≥92%)
first_line_soc: Supplemental oxygen (nasal cannula, Venturi mask, HFNC, or NIV) is first-line supportive care. Treat underlying cause: bronchodilators for bronchospasm, diuretics for pulmonary edema, antibiotics for pneumonia.
treatment_algorithm: Oxygen delivery: escalating oxygen (NC → simple mask → Venturi mask → HFNC → NIV/CPAP → mechanical ventilation). Treat etiology: bronchospasm (albuterol, ipratropium nebulized), pulmonary edema (furosemide, nitroglycerin), pneumonia (antibiotics), PE (anticoagulation, thrombolytics), ARDS (see ARDS entry), hypoventilation (reversal agents: naloxone, flumazenil; ventilatory support). Systemic hypoxia (anemia): packed RBCs, EPO for chronic anemia. Methemoglobinemia: methylene blue IV. Cyanide poisoning: hydroxocobalamin. No single pharmacologic SoC — treatment is etiology-specific.
us_prevalence: ~2–4 million hospitalized patients per year experience significant hypoxia requiring intervention; SpO₂ <90% in ~15–25% of ICU patients at some point.
patients_needing_tx: ~2–3M episodes/year requiring pharmacologic treatment (bronchodilators, diuretics, antibiotics, vasopressors as relevant). Novel pulmonary vasodilator/gas exchange enhancer: ~300,000 (ARDS/severe lung disease).
price: $10,000–$20,000 per hospitalization for novel gas-exchange modulator/iNO equivalent (iNO: ~$10K–$20K/hospitalization).
registrational_endpoint: Change in PaO₂/FiO₂ ratio from baseline at 72 hours; proportion achieving SpO₂ ≥92% without mechanical ventilation; ventilator-free days at 28 days.
btd_threshold_vs_soc: ≥30 mmHg improvement in PaO₂/FiO₂ ratio + ≥30% reduction in intubation rate vs. standard supplemental O₂ at 48 hours.

symptoms: Inability to maintain ventilation/oxygenation; varied by type (pump failure, V/Q mismatch, shunt)
treatment_goal: Correct underlying cause; provide ventilatory support; prevent end-organ damage
first_line_soc: Supportive: NIV (BiPAP) for type II (hypercapnic) respiratory failure from COPD exacerbation; invasive mechanical ventilation for refractory cases. Treat underlying etiology (bronchodilators, diuretics, antibiotics as appropriate).
treatment_algorithm: Type I (hypoxemic non-ARDS): supplemental O₂ → HFNC → NIV → mechanical ventilation; treat etiology (PE: anticoagulation/thrombolytics; pulmonary edema: diuretics; pneumonia: antibiotics). Type II (hypercapnic): NIV (BiPAP/CPAP) first-line for COPD exacerbation (reduces intubation by 40%). Inhaled bronchodilators (salbutamol, ipratropium) + IV/PO corticosteroids for COPD. Type IV (circulatory/mixed): vasopressors (norepinephrine), inotropes (dobutamine), fluids. Neuromuscular causes (Guillain-Barré, MG): IVIG, plasmapheresis. Novel: heliox (helium-oxygen) for severe airway obstruction. Extubation readiness: methylprednisolone pre-extubation for post-extubation stridor.
us_prevalence: ~800,000–1M episodes of non-ARDS respiratory failure/year in US requiring ICU intervention. ~350,000 COPD-related respiratory failure episodes/year.
patients_needing_tx: ~800,000/year require pharmacologic management; novel targeted agent addressable: ~200,000–300,000 (refractory cases).
price: $8,000–$18,000 per episode for novel adjunct to ventilatory support.
registrational_endpoint: Ventilator-free days at 28 days; rate of intubation avoidance (NIV success) at 48–72 hours; 28-day all-cause mortality.
btd_threshold_vs_soc: ≥4 additional ventilator-free days vs. standard care + NIV at 28 days; ≥20% absolute reduction in intubation rate vs. standard bronchodilator/steroid care.

symptoms: Rising CO₂ (hypercapnia), respiratory acidosis, drowsiness, inability to breathe adequately
treatment_goal: Restore adequate ventilation; correct respiratory acidosis; treat underlying cause
first_line_soc: Non-invasive ventilation (NIV/BiPAP) is first-line for hypercapnic ventilatory failure (COPD exacerbation, OHS, NMD) — reduces intubation and mortality. Invasive mechanical ventilation for NIV failure or contraindication.
treatment_algorithm: NIV (BiPAP): first-line for acute-on-chronic hypercapnic respiratory failure (COPD: reduces intubation by ~40%–65%, mortality by ~10%), OHS, NMD. Pharmacologic: treat underlying cause — bronchodilators (salbutamol, ipratropium) + systemic corticosteroids for COPD exacerbation; IVIG/plasmapheresis for Guillain-Barré; pyridostigmine for myasthenic crisis; NPPV for OHS. Respiratory stimulants: progesterone, doxapram IV (short-term); acetazolamide for metabolic alkalosis-driven hypoventilation. Invasive MV: volume-controlled or pressure-controlled ventilation; target permissive hypercapnia. Weaning: SBT, PAV (proportional assist ventilation). Novel: KL4 surfactant, phrenic nerve stimulators (Avery), diaphragm pacing.
us_prevalence: ~500,000–700,000 episodes/year of ventilatory failure requiring mechanical ventilation (NIV or invasive). COPD exacerbation alone: ~500,000 hospitalizations/year with ~100,000 requiring ventilatory support.
patients_needing_tx: ~400,000–600,000/year requiring pharmacologic adjunct to ventilatory support. Novel respiratory stimulant/NMD drug addressable: ~100,000–150,000.
price: $8,000–$20,000 per episode for novel respiratory stimulant or NMD-specific adjunct (doxapram IV ~$500/day hospital; novel agent premium).
registrational_endpoint: PaCO₂ reduction ≥10 mmHg from baseline at 4 hours; NIV success rate (avoidance of intubation) at 24 hours; 30-day re-intubation/re-hospitalization rate.
btd_threshold_vs_soc: ≥15 mmHg PaCO₂ reduction + ≥30% absolute reduction in intubation rate vs. standard NIV + standard bronchodilators at 24 hours.

symptoms: Complete cessation of breathing; often preceded by severe respiratory distress; leads to cardiac arrest if untreated
treatment_goal: Immediate restoration of ventilation; prevent hypoxic brain injury and cardiac arrest
first_line_soc: Immediate bag-valve-mask (BVM) ventilation and emergency intubation/airway establishment. Reversal agents if drug-induced: naloxone (opioid overdose, 0.4–2 mg IV/IM/IN) or flumazenil (benzodiazepine reversal — use cautiously).
treatment_algorithm: Immediate: BVM ventilation → emergency intubation (RSI) with succinylcholine or rocuronium for airway. Reversal: naloxone for opioid-induced respiratory arrest (standard: 0.4–2 mg IV; high-dose: 4–8 mg for fentanyl/synthetic opioids). Flumazenil for benzodiazepine overdose (rarely used due to seizure risk). Post-intubation management: mechanical ventilation. Treat underlying cause (opioid overdose, metabolic derangement, neuromuscular disease). ECMO for refractory cases. Novel: respiratory stimulants (doxapram IV for post-anesthetic respiratory depression; almitrine — limited evidence). KOR antagonists for opioid overdose with superior duration.
us_prevalence: ~350,000 OHCA events/year (many preceded by respiratory arrest); ~80,000–100,000 drug-induced respiratory arrest events/year (primarily opioid overdose). Total acute respiratory arrest: ~150,000–200,000/year requiring pharmacologic intervention.
patients_needing_tx: ~150,000–200,000/year acute respiratory arrest episodes; drug-induced: ~100,000/year (naloxone territory).
price: $500–$5,000 per episode for novel reversal/resuscitation agent (naloxone generic: $20–$150; next-gen opioid reversal: $500–$2,000).
tam_gross: 100K opioid-related episodes × $1,500 = $150M; broader ICU respiratory arrest pharmacology: 50K × $5K = $250M. Total ~$400M/year
peak_sales: 100K opioid reversal episodes × $1,500 × 40% share = $60M; ICU: 50K × $5K × 25% = $63M. Total ~$125M/year
registrational_endpoint: Time to resumption of spontaneous adequate ventilation (RR ≥8 breaths/min, SpO₂ ≥90%); intubation avoidance at 60 minutes; neurological outcome at 30 days.
btd_threshold_vs_soc: ≥80% restoration of adequate spontaneous ventilation within 5 minutes vs. standard naloxone; superiority in duration of action (≥90 min vs. naloxone ~30–60 min) + lower re-sedation rate.

symptoms: Sudden inability to speak/breathe, clutching throat, cyanosis; foreign body airway obstruction
treatment_goal: Immediate clearance of airway obstruction; restore ventilation
first_line_soc: Heimlich maneuver (abdominal thrusts) for conscious adult with complete airway obstruction. Back blows + chest thrusts for infants. Emergency cricothyrotomy for failed clearance. No pharmacologic SoC.
treatment_algorithm: Mild obstruction: encourage coughing. Severe/complete: Heimlich maneuver (5 abdominal thrusts, alternate with 5 back blows per updated 2023 guidance). Unconscious: CPR + attempt visualization/removal with Magill forceps during compressions. Emergency surgical airway (needle/surgical cricothyrotomy) if maneuvers fail. LifeVac/LifeWand (FDA-cleared suction devices) as adjunct. No pharmacologic agents to relieve choking. Post-event: bronchoscopy for residual foreign body. Prevention: safe eating practices.
us_prevalence: ~5,000 choking deaths/year in US; ~100,000+ ER visits/year for airway foreign body. FBAO is 4th leading cause of unintentional injury death.
patients_needing_tx: No pharmacologic treatment. Mechanical/device market only. Hypothetical novel pharmacologic: negligible.
price: N/A — no pharmacologic indication. Hypothetical mucolytic/bronchodilator adjunct: $100–$500 per episode.
peak_sales: ~$50M (topical/inhaled bronchodilator adjunct for post-obstruction bronchospasm in hospital setting)
registrational_endpoint: N/A for pharmacologic endpoint. Device registration: successful airway clearance rate ≥90% within 60 seconds vs. standard Heimlich maneuver.

symptoms: Fever or hypothermia, rapid heart rate, low blood pressure, organ dysfunction, confusion
treatment_goal: Control infection; restore hemodynamics; prevent multi-organ failure and death
first_line_soc: Early broad-spectrum IV antibiotics (within 1 hour of recognition) + IV crystalloid resuscitation (30 mL/kg in first 3 hours) per Surviving Sepsis Campaign Hour-1 Bundle. Norepinephrine as first-line vasopressor for septic shock.
treatment_algorithm: Surviving Sepsis Campaign 2024 Bundle: Blood cultures → antibiotics (within 1h), lactate measurement, IV fluids (30 mL/kg if lactate ≥4 or hypotensive). Vasopressors: norepinephrine first-line (MAP ≥65); add vasopressin, phenylephrine, or epinephrine if needed. Corticosteroids: IV hydrocortisone 200 mg/day for refractory septic shock (ADRENAL, APROCCHSS trials). Source control (drainage, debridement). Tight glycemic control (140–180 mg/dL). Stress ulcer prophylaxis (PPIs), DVT prophylaxis. Novel: Angiotensin II (GIAPREZA/selepressin): FDA-approved 2017 for vasodilatory shock. Nangibotide (RESOLUTE trial, anti-TREM-1 antibody) — Phase 3. Eritoran (TLR4 antagonist) — failed Phase 3. IL-6 inhibitors (tocilizumab) in cytokine storm/sepsis superimposed on COVID. Activated protein C withdrawn. Thymosin α1: not approved in US. 2025: CytoSorb hemoadsorption device; CAR-T cell immunosuppression for HLH-like sepsis presentations.
us_prevalence: ~1.7 million sepsis cases/year in US; ~270,000–400,000 deaths/year. Septic shock: ~400,000–600,000 cases/year. Hospital prevalence: ~6–10% of hospitalized patients.
patients_needing_tx: ~1.7M/year acute pharmacologic treatment (antibiotics, vasopressors, corticosteroids). Novel adjunct agent addressable: ~500,000–700,000/year (moderate-severe sepsis).
price: $8,000–$20,000 per sepsis episode for novel immunomodulator/adjunct (short inpatient course; angiotensin II ~$15K/day × 3–5 days average).
registrational_endpoint: 28-day all-cause mortality (primary) for septic shock trials; 90-day mortality; number of organ failure-free days (SOFA score normalization); ICU/hospital LOS.
btd_threshold_vs_soc: ≥8 absolute percentage point reduction in 28-day mortality vs. standard-of-care resuscitation + antibiotics; ≥3 additional organ failure-free days in septic shock patients.

symptoms: Sudden loss of consciousness, absent pulse, absent respirations; without CPR universally fatal
treatment_goal: Restore spontaneous circulation; preserve neurological function; prevent recurrence
first_line_soc: CPR + defibrillation (AED) is the only life-saving intervention. Epinephrine 1 mg IV q3–5 min (ACLS standard) during resuscitation to restore circulation.
treatment_algorithm: ACLS: High-quality CPR + early defibrillation (for shockable rhythms VF/pVT). Vasopressors: epinephrine 1 mg IV q3–5 min. Antiarrhythmics for refractory VF/VT: amiodarone 300 mg IV (then 150 mg) or lidocaine. Treat reversible causes (5H/5T): hypovolemia, hypoxia, tension pneumothorax, tamponade, toxins, PE, MI. Post-ROSC: targeted temperature management (TTM at 33–36°C — TTM2 trial), PCI for STEMI. Vasopressors post-resuscitation (norepinephrine for hemodynamic support). Novel: vasopressin + methylprednisolone + epinephrine (VAM) bundle showed improved ROSC in Greek trials. Double sequential defibrillation for refractory VF. ECPR (extracorporeal CPR/ECMO) for refractory OHCA.
us_prevalence: ~350,000 OHCA/year; ~250,000 in-hospital cardiac arrests (IHCA). Combined: ~600,000 cardiac arrests/year in US (AHA/SCA Foundation 2024–2025). ROSC achieved: ~25–30% OHCA; ~45–50% IHCA.
patients_needing_tx: ~600,000 acute pharmacologic resuscitation episodes/year. Post-ROSC survivors needing ongoing pharmacologic management: ~60,000–80,000/year.
price: $5,000–$15,000 per episode for novel resuscitation agent (inpatient acute use; epinephrine generic is cents — novel mechanism needed).
registrational_endpoint: Survival to hospital discharge with favorable neurological outcome (CPC 1–2 or mRS 0–3) at 30 days; return of spontaneous circulation (ROSC) rate as key secondary.
btd_threshold_vs_soc: ≥8 absolute percentage point improvement in neurologically intact survival vs. epinephrine at 30 days; ROSC improvement ≥10% absolute.

symptoms: Low blood pressure, cold/clammy skin, rapid pulse, confusion, reduced urine output
treatment_goal: Restore tissue perfusion and organ function; treat underlying cause
first_line_soc: IV crystalloid resuscitation (30 mL/kg balanced crystalloids) and vasopressors (norepinephrine first-line for vasodilatory/septic shock; epinephrine for anaphylactic shock) per shock type.
treatment_algorithm: Distributive/septic: norepinephrine → vasopressin → epinephrine → angiotensin II (GIAPREZA). Corticosteroids for refractory septic shock (hydrocortisone). Hypovolemic: IV crystalloids, packed RBCs, FFP (traumatic hemorrhagic shock — 1:1:1 blood product ratio, TXA within 3 hours). Cardiogenic: dobutamine/dopamine, milrinone (inotropes), IABP, Impella, VA-ECMO. Obstructive: treat cause (PE: anticoagulation, embolectomy; tension PTX: needle decompression). Anaphylactic: epinephrine IM (first-line), corticosteroids, antihistamines, fluids. Novel: vasopressin analogs, angiotensin II, selepressin (Phase 3 trials), novel vasoconstrictors.
us_prevalence: ~500,000–700,000 shock episodes/year in US requiring ICU care (all types combined: septic, cardiogenic, hypovolemic, obstructive, distributive). Septic shock alone: 400,000–600,000/year.
patients_needing_tx: ~500,000–600,000/year requiring pharmacologic vasopressor/resuscitation therapy. Novel adjunct addressable: ~300,000.
price: $8,000–$20,000 per shock episode for novel vasopressor/resuscitation agent.
registrational_endpoint: Time to hemodynamic stability (MAP ≥65 mmHg, vasopressor weaning) and 28-day mortality; organ failure-free days at 28 days; lactate normalization at 6 hours.
btd_threshold_vs_soc: ≥8% absolute reduction in 28-day mortality vs. norepinephrine standard + faster vasopressor weaning (≥24 hours earlier); lactate normalization in ≥60% at 6 hours vs. ≤40% for norepinephrine.

symptoms: Reduced urine output (<0.5 mL/kg/h), fluid retention, rising creatinine, edema
treatment_goal: Identify cause; restore renal perfusion; prevent progression to AKI/renal failure
first_line_soc: IV fluid resuscitation (0.9% NaCl or balanced crystalloids) for prerenal oliguria/hypovolemia. Furosemide for fluid overload oliguria. Correct underlying hemodynamic cause (vasopressors for shock).
treatment_algorithm: Prerenal: IV fluids (balanced crystalloids preferred: Plasmalyte, LR). Vasodilatory/septic shock: norepinephrine (vasopressor). Cardiorenal oliguria: loop diuretics (furosemide), consider renal replacement therapy (RRT). Obstructive (postrenal): Foley catheter, urologic decompression. Intrinsic renal (ATN): supportive care; avoid nephrotoxins; RRT for severe AKI (KDIGO criteria). Dopamine at 'renal dose' not effective (abandoned). Novel: AKI prevention — sachituzumab govitecan (investigational); fenoldopam (selective DA1 agonist) — limited evidence. Alkaline phosphatase (recAP) for sepsis-associated AKI — Phase 2/3 trials. No approved drug reverses established oliguria.
us_prevalence: ~2–3 million AKI episodes/year in US; ~1.3 million requiring ICU level management; oliguria as presenting sign in ~50–60% of ICU AKI cases (~650,000–780,000/year).
patients_needing_tx: ~600,000–700,000 ICU oliguria episodes/year requiring pharmacologic intervention. Novel AKI-prevention drug addressable: ~200,000–300,000 (high-risk patients).
price: $5,000–$15,000 per episode for novel AKI-preventing/treating agent (inpatient acute use; recAP trials suggest ~$10K range).
registrational_endpoint: Major adverse kidney events at 90 days (MAKE90: dialysis dependence, ≥25% creatinine increase, or death); change in serum creatinine from baseline at 72 hours.
btd_threshold_vs_soc: ≥30% relative reduction in MAKE90 vs. standard of care (fluids/vasopressors) at 90 days; ≥0.5 mg/dL creatinine attenuation at peak AKI vs. placebo.

symptoms: Progressive dyspnea, dry cough, bilateral basal crackles, honeycombing on CT
treatment_goal: Slow FVC decline; reduce acute exacerbations; preserve function
first_line_soc: Nintedanib (Ofev — FDA 2014) and pirfenidone (Esbriet — FDA 2014) are both first-line FDA-approved anti-fibrotic agents.
treatment_algorithm: FDA-approved: nintedanib (150 mg BID) or pirfenidone (801 mg TID). Both slow FVC decline ~50% vs. placebo. Combination therapy under investigation. Pulmonary rehab. Supplemental O2. Lung transplant for progressive disease. 2024–2025 pipeline: inhaled treprostinil (Tyvaso) approved for ILD/PH-ILD 2021; pamrevlumab (anti-CTGF) Phase 3; inhaled nintedanib; TGFB inhibitors; HIF-2α inhibitors; senolytic therapy (dasatinib + quercetin). Phase 3 ongoing: bexotegrast (PLN74809), aldeyra TP-044.
us_prevalence: ~120,000–200,000 US patients (incidence 10 per 100,000/yr; Chest 2023).
patients_needing_tx: ~100,000 patients on anti-fibrotic pharmacotherapy.
price: $80,000–$130,000/year; nintedanib WAC ~$110,000/yr, pirfenidone ~$100,000/yr.
peak_sales: Competitive but large unmet need. Novel add-on or replacement: 20–30% share. Peak sales ~$1.0–3.0B.
registrational_endpoint: FVC decline rate (mL/year) at 52 weeks; time to acute exacerbation; DLCO.
btd_threshold_vs_soc: ≥50 mL/year FVC decline reduction vs. nintedanib or pirfenidone; reduction in acute exacerbation rate.

symptoms: Wheezing, brown mucus plugs, recurrent lung infiltrates in asthmatics
treatment_goal: Suppress allergic response to Aspergillus; prevent lung damage
first_line_soc: Oral corticosteroids (prednisolone) are first-line for acute exacerbations. Itraconazole (antifungal) added as steroid-sparing agent.
treatment_algorithm: Acute exacerbation: prednisolone 0.5 mg/kg/day × 2 weeks, then taper. Antifungal: itraconazole or voriconazole (steroid-sparing, reduces Aspergillus burden). Biologics: omalizumab (anti-IgE) and mepolizumab/benralizumab (anti-IL-5) increasingly used off-label as steroid-sparing; omalizumab FDA-approved for asthma (used off-label for ABPA component). Inhaled corticosteroids for underlying asthma.
us_prevalence: ~2% of asthmatic patients; ~500,000 US cases (26 million asthmatics × 2%).
patients_needing_tx: ~250,000 patients needing active pharmacotherapy.
price: $20,000–$40,000/year; biologic pricing benchmark.
peak_sales: Biologics entering this space. Novel dedicated drug: 15–20% share. Peak sales ~$400–700M.
registrational_endpoint: IgE level reduction ≥50%; exacerbation rate reduction; FEV1 improvement at 6 months.
btd_threshold_vs_soc: ≥50% IgE reduction AND ≥30% exacerbation reduction vs. itraconazole + prednisolone.

symptoms: Cough, dyspnea, fever after antigen exposure; diffuse lung infiltrates
treatment_goal: Remove antigen; reduce inflammation; prevent fibrotic progression
first_line_soc: Remove causative antigen (mandatory). Systemic corticosteroids (prednisone) for significant acute/chronic cases.
treatment_algorithm: Antigen avoidance is primary and most effective. Corticosteroids: prednisone 40–60 mg/day for 2 weeks, then taper. Steroid-sparing: azathioprine, mycophenolate, hydroxychloroquine. Fibrotic HP (f-HP): nintedanib (FDA-approved for f-HP as part of progressive fibrosing ILD — Ofev indication expanded 2020). Lung transplant for advanced f-HP.
us_prevalence: ~90,000–200,000 US patients; incidence 1.7–2.7 per 100,000 (ERJ 2020).
patients_needing_tx: ~100,000 patients needing active pharmacotherapy.
price: $50,000–$100,000/year for novel anti-fibrotic/biologic.
peak_sales: Nintedanib approved for f-HP; novel biologic for anti-inflammatory phase: 15–20% share. Peak sales ~$400–800M.
registrational_endpoint: FVC decline rate over 52 weeks; DLCO; acute exacerbation rate.
btd_threshold_vs_soc: ≥35% reduction in FVC decline vs. placebo; superiority in progression-free survival vs. nintedanib.

symptoms: Fatigue, cough, skin lesions, lymphadenopathy, multiorgan granulomas; often self-limited
treatment_goal: Suppress granuloma formation; preserve organ function; reduce fatigue
first_line_soc: Oral corticosteroids (prednisone 20–40 mg/day) for symptomatic or progressive pulmonary, cardiac, or neurologic sarcoidosis.
treatment_algorithm: Asymptomatic: observation. Stage II–IV with symptoms: prednisone 20–40 mg/day × 6–12 months, taper. Steroid-sparing: methotrexate (most commonly used), azathioprine, hydroxychloroquine (for skin/hypercalcemia). Refractory: infliximab or adalimumab (anti-TNF — off-label but evidence-based; 2024 guidelines). Neurosarcoidosis: high-dose steroids + infliximab. Cardiac sarcoidosis: steroids + ICD. Renal transplant for end-stage. No FDA-approved drug specifically for sarcoidosis (as of 2025).
us_prevalence: ~200,000 US patients; Black Americans disproportionately affected (~40/100,000 vs. 5–10/100,000 in whites). FSR registry 2024.
patients_needing_tx: ~100,000–120,000 patients requiring systemic therapy (steroids or steroid-sparing immunosuppressants).
price: $25,000–$80,000/year for a novel targeted biologic (anti-cytokine or JAK inhibitor).
peak_sales: ~15–20% market share; ~$800M–1.1B peak sales for a novel approved anti-sarcoidosis drug.
registrational_endpoint: FVC change at 24 weeks; King's Sarcoidosis Questionnaire (KSQ); composite organ improvement score.
btd_threshold_vs_soc: FVC improvement ≥5%; KSQ improvement ≥5 points; or 50% reduction in corticosteroid dose vs. placebo at 24 weeks.

symptoms: Progressive breathlessness, dry cough, bibasilar ground-glass opacities on CT
treatment_goal: Halt fibrotic progression; preserve lung function
first_line_soc: Immunosuppression: corticosteroids + azathioprine or mycophenolate mofetil; treat underlying CTD (connective tissue disease) if present.
treatment_algorithm: Fibrotic NSIP: prednisone + azathioprine or mycophenolate mofetil (MMF). Cellular NSIP: corticosteroids alone often effective. CTD-associated NSIP (most common): treat underlying disease (rituximab for RA/SSc-ILD; mycophenolate for SSc-ILD). Anti-fibrotics (nintedanib) approved for ILD associated with SSc and progressive pulmonary fibrosis — applicable for progressive fibrotic NSIP. Lung transplant for end-stage. Nintedanib (Ofev) FDA-approved for progressive fibrosing ILDs including NSIP in 2020.
us_prevalence: ~10,000–20,000 US patients; second most common idiopathic IIP after IPF.
patients_needing_tx: ~12,000–15,000 patients requiring systemic immunosuppression or anti-fibrotic therapy.
price: $80,000–$150,000/year; comparable to nintedanib (~$100,000/year).
peak_sales: ~25% market share; ~$350M peak sales for a novel targeted therapy superior to current SoC.
registrational_endpoint: Change in FVC % predicted at 52 weeks; time to acute exacerbation or death.
btd_threshold_vs_soc: FVC decline ≤5% vs. >10% with placebo; or ≥100 mL absolute FVC preservation vs. comparator.

symptoms: Progressive dyspnea, productive cough with gelatinous sputum; alveoli filled with surfactant-like material
treatment_goal: Remove proteinaceous material; restore alveolar gas exchange
first_line_soc: Whole lung lavage (WLL) is the gold-standard treatment. For autoimmune PAP: inhaled or subcutaneous GM-CSF (molgramostim; FDA Breakthrough designation).
treatment_algorithm: Whole lung lavage (WLL): sequential bilateral lavage under general anesthesia; provides 6–12+ months of benefit. Autoimmune PAP (90% of cases): GM-CSF therapy — inhaled molgramostim (Savara, Phase 3 IMPALA-2) or subcutaneous sargramostim; rituximab for refractory cases. Secondary PAP: treat underlying cause (infections, dust, hematologic malignancy). Congenital PAP: exogenous surfactant, lung transplant.
us_prevalence: ~6,000–10,000 US patients. Orphan disease. NORD/PAP registry estimates.
patients_needing_tx: ~4,000–6,000 patients with autoimmune PAP requiring systemic or inhaled GM-CSF therapy.
price: $100,000–$200,000/year; orphan pricing.
peak_sales: ~$150–300M peak sales; molgramostim (Savara) targeting this market.
registrational_endpoint: Change in alveolar-arterial oxygen gradient; DLCO % predicted at 24 weeks; responder rate (≥10 mm Hg improvement in A-a gradient).
btd_threshold_vs_soc: ≥40% responder rate vs. ≤10% for WLL alone at 24 weeks; or non-inferiority to WLL with better safety.

symptoms: Progressive cough, dyspnea, bilateral peripheral infiltrates, blood eosinophilia
treatment_goal: Resolve eosinophilic infiltrates; prevent relapse; minimize steroid use
first_line_soc: Oral corticosteroids (prednisone 40–60 mg/day) are highly effective first-line treatment.
treatment_algorithm: Corticosteroids first-line: prednisone 0.5–1 mg/kg/day, taper slowly over 6–12 months (high relapse rate with rapid taper). ICS may help maintain remission. Anti-IL-5 biologics (mepolizumab, benralizumab) as steroid-sparing in relapsing/refractory cases (off-label; case series evidence).
us_prevalence: ~10,000–30,000 US cases (rare chronic condition).
patients_needing_tx: ~20,000 patients receiving pharmacotherapy.
price: $25,000–$50,000/year for a biologic steroid-sparing agent.
peak_sales: Small market. Anti-IL-5 off-label use growing. Novel dedicated drug: 20–30% share. Peak sales ~$100–200M.
