registrational_endpoint: CT clearing rate; eosinophil count normalization; steroid dose reduction ≥50% at 24 weeks.
btd_threshold_vs_soc: ≥50% corticosteroid dose reduction; relapse-free survival at 12 months superior to corticosteroids alone.

symptoms: Flu-like illness, bilateral infiltrates, non-resolving pneumonia, steroid-responsive
treatment_goal: Resolve organizing pneumonia; prevent relapse
first_line_soc: Oral corticosteroids (prednisone 0.75–1 mg/kg/day) are highly effective first-line treatment.
treatment_algorithm: Prednisone: start 0.75–1 mg/kg/day for 4–8 weeks, taper slowly over 6–12 months (high relapse rate). Macrolide antibiotics (azithromycin) for mild/early cases. Second-line for refractory/relapsing: azathioprine, cyclophosphamide, rituximab. Good prognosis overall (>80% recover with steroids).
us_prevalence: ~3–6 cases/100,000/year; ~10,000–20,000 active US cases.
patients_needing_tx: ~15,000 patients receiving pharmacotherapy.
price: $20,000–$50,000/year for a steroid-sparing biologic.
peak_sales: Very small market. Steroid-responsive — low need for novel drug. Peak sales ~$30–70M.
registrational_endpoint: CT clearing rate; corticosteroid dose reduction ≥50% at 6 months; relapse-free survival.
btd_threshold_vs_soc: ≥40% reduction in relapse rate vs. corticosteroid monotherapy.

symptoms: Progressive breathlessness, recurrent pneumothorax, chylous effusions in women
treatment_goal: Slow lung function decline; prevent pneumothorax recurrence
first_line_soc: Sirolimus (rapamycin) — mTOR inhibitor — FDA-approved 2015 for LAM; 2 mg/day orally.
treatment_algorithm: Sirolimus (rapamycin) 2 mg/day — first and only FDA-approved therapy; slows FEV1 decline, reduces chylous effusions and lymphangioleiomyomas. Everolimus as alternative mTOR inhibitor. Bronchodilators for airflow obstruction. Pleurodesis or surgical repair for pneumothorax. Avoid estrogen-containing contraceptives. Lung transplantation for end-stage disease. Progesterone historically used — no longer recommended.
us_prevalence: ~3,500–6,000 US patients; almost exclusively women of childbearing age. LAM Foundation registry data.
patients_needing_tx: ~2,500–4,000 patients with progressive disease warranting sirolimus therapy. Most have moderate-severe lung function impairment.
price: $80,000–$150,000/year; orphan ultra-rare disease with existing high-priced mTOR therapy (sirolimus ~$40K/year branded).
peak_sales: ~20–30% market share; ~$60–90M peak sales. Small orphan market.
registrational_endpoint: Change in FEV1 slope (mL/year) over 12 months; 6MWT distance at 12 months.
btd_threshold_vs_soc: ≥30% reduction in FEV1 decline rate vs. sirolimus (e.g., <30 mL/year loss) or superiority in 6MWT.

symptoms: Cough, dyspnea, ground-glass opacities on CT; strongly linked to cigarette smoking
treatment_goal: Smoking cessation; reduce inflammation; improve lung function
first_line_soc: Smoking cessation is the cornerstone treatment. Corticosteroids (prednisone) for symptomatic or progressive cases.
treatment_algorithm: Smoking cessation: mandatory and often sufficient for clinical improvement. Corticosteroids (prednisone 40–60 mg/day) for patients who remain symptomatic after smoking cessation. Pulmonary rehabilitation. Monitor with serial HRCT and PFTs. Anti-fibrotics (nintedanib) for progressive fibrotic phenotype. No FDA-approved specific drug. Lung transplant for advanced disease.
us_prevalence: ~3,000–8,000 US patients; almost exclusively smokers or former smokers.
patients_needing_tx: ~3,000–5,000 patients requiring corticosteroid therapy beyond smoking cessation.
peak_sales: ~$50–80M peak sales; niche smoking-related ILD market.
registrational_endpoint: Change in FVC % predicted at 12 months; DLCO improvement; CT ground-glass scoring reduction.
btd_threshold_vs_soc: FVC improvement ≥5% or DLCO improvement ≥10% vs. observation at 12 months.

symptoms: Dyspnea, cough, recurrent pneumothorax; cystic and nodular lung lesions; smoking-associated
treatment_goal: Smoking cessation (primary); slow cystic progression; prevent pneumothorax
first_line_soc: Smoking cessation is the most critical intervention. BRAF-targeted therapy (vemurafenib, dabrafenib-trametinib) for BRAF V600E-mutant PLCH is emerging first-line for progressive disease.
treatment_algorithm: Smoking cessation halts progression in ~60% of cases. BRAF V600E-mutant PLCH: vemurafenib or dabrafenib + trametinib (BRAF/MEK inhibitors) — significant clinical trial data; cladribine for BRAF wild-type. Corticosteroids have limited evidence. Cladribine (2-CdA) for refractory multisystem LCH. Lung transplant for advanced disease. MAPK pathway inhibitors are increasingly used per HISTIO guidelines.
us_prevalence: ~3,000–6,000 US patients; ~25% of all LCH patients have pulmonary involvement; rare.
patients_needing_tx: ~2,000–3,000 patients requiring BRAF/MEK inhibitor or other systemic therapy.
price: $80,000–$150,000/year; BRAF/MEK inhibitors (vemurafenib ~$100,000/year).
peak_sales: ~$50–100M peak sales for a novel targeted therapy.
registrational_endpoint: DLCO change at 12 months; radiographic CT scoring (cyst score); time to pneumothorax.
btd_threshold_vs_soc: ≥20% DLCO improvement; ≥50% radiographic response at 12 months vs. observation.

symptoms: Progressive cough, dyspnea, bilateral lung infiltrates; associated with autoimmune disease or HIV
treatment_goal: Reduce lymphocytic infiltration; improve oxygenation
first_line_soc: Corticosteroids (prednisone 1 mg/kg/day) are first-line; treat underlying disease (e.g., antiretrovirals for HIV-associated LIP).
treatment_algorithm: Prednisone 1 mg/kg/day tapered over months. Treat underlying cause: antiretrovirals (ART) for HIV-LIP; rituximab or hydroxychloroquine for Sjögren syndrome-associated LIP. Azathioprine or mycophenolate for steroid-sparing. No FDA-approved specific drug. Monitor HRCT and DLCO. Lung transplant for end-stage.
us_prevalence: ~2,000–5,000 cases; rare; often secondary to Sjögren syndrome (~20–25% of LIP cases) or HIV.
patients_needing_tx: ~2,000–3,000 patients requiring pharmacologic therapy beyond treating underlying cause.
price: $50,000–$100,000/year for a targeted biologic.
peak_sales: ~$20–40M peak sales; ultra-rare with modest commercial interest.
registrational_endpoint: Change in DLCO % predicted at 12 months; CT scoring of infiltrate reduction.
btd_threshold_vs_soc: ≥20% improvement in DLCO vs. corticosteroids alone at 12 months.

symptoms: Dyspnea, cough in smokers; ground-glass opacities on HRCT
treatment_goal: Halt progression; improve lung function; stop smoking
first_line_soc: Smoking cessation is the most effective intervention. Corticosteroids (prednisone) for moderate-severe disease.
treatment_algorithm: Smoking cessation (mandatory). Corticosteroids: prednisone 20–40 mg/day for symptomatic disease. Steroid-sparing agents (azathioprine, hydroxychloroquine) for chronic cases. Anti-fibrotics (nintedanib, pirfenidone) used off-label in progressive fibrotic DIP. Lung transplant for refractory cases.
us_prevalence: Rare; ~2,000–5,000 US cases.
patients_needing_tx: ~3,000 patients receiving pharmacotherapy.
peak_sales: Very rare. Managed off-label. Peak sales ~$15–30M.
registrational_endpoint: FVC % predicted improvement; DLCO improvement; HRCT improvement at 24 weeks.
btd_threshold_vs_soc: ≥15% FVC improvement; CT improvement vs. corticosteroid monotherapy.

symptoms: Acute respiratory failure, cough, fever, bilateral infiltrates, marked eosinophilia
treatment_goal: Resolve eosinophilic inflammation; restore lung function
first_line_soc: High-dose systemic corticosteroids (prednisone 40–60 mg/day or IV methylprednisolone) are first-line and highly effective.
treatment_algorithm: Corticosteroids: IV methylprednisolone in ICU, oral prednisone for milder cases. Remove inciting agent (drug, smoking). Rapid response expected within 24–48 hours. Taper over 2–4 weeks. Anti-IL-5 agents (mepolizumab, benralizumab) not routinely used but may be considered for refractory cases.
us_prevalence: Rare; ~1,000–3,000 cases/year (incidence-based; largely acute).
patients_needing_tx: ~2,500 patients/year needing pharmacotherapy.
price: $25,000–$50,000/course; anti-eosinophil biologics benchmark.
peak_sales: Very rare acute disease. Managed with corticosteroids. Novel drug minimal market. Peak sales ~$10–30M.
registrational_endpoint: PaO2/FiO2 improvement ≥100 mmHg; BAL eosinophil count reduction ≥80% at day 7.
btd_threshold_vs_soc: ≥50% faster resolution vs. corticosteroids alone; no ICU escalation.

symptoms: Progressive upper lobe fibrosis, flattened chest, restrictive lung disease
treatment_goal: Slow progression; manage respiratory failure
first_line_soc: No proven pharmacotherapy. Corticosteroids tried with limited benefit. Anti-fibrotics (nintedanib, pirfenidone) used off-label.
treatment_algorithm: No approved therapy. Case series suggest nintedanib or pirfenidone may stabilize. Lung transplant only potentially curative option. Supportive oxygen, pulmonary rehab. This is an ultra-rare ILD subtype.
us_prevalence: Very rare; ~500–2,000 US cases (ILD registries).
patients_needing_tx: ~1,000 patients receiving off-label anti-fibrotics.
peak_sales: Ultra-rare. Orphan designation likely. Peak sales ~$20–50M.
registrational_endpoint: FVC decline rate; DLCO; CT fibrosis progression at 52 weeks.
btd_threshold_vs_soc: ≥30% FVC decline reduction vs. placebo; CT stabilization vs. active comparator.

symptoms: Recurrent hemoptysis, anemia, diffuse alveolar infiltrates in children/young adults
treatment_goal: Control alveolar hemorrhage; prevent pulmonary fibrosis
first_line_soc: Systemic corticosteroids (prednisone) are first-line. Hydroxychloroquine is commonly used as steroid-sparing maintenance.
treatment_algorithm: Corticosteroids: prednisone 1–2 mg/kg/day for acute episodes; long-term low-dose maintenance. Hydroxychloroquine as steroid-sparing (most widely used maintenance agent). Azathioprine, cyclophosphamide for refractory cases. Chelation therapy for iron overload. Lung transplant for end-stage.
us_prevalence: Ultra-rare; ~500–1,000 US cases (mostly pediatric).
patients_needing_tx: ~750 patients receiving pharmacotherapy.
registrational_endpoint: Pulmonary hemorrhage events per year; hemoglobin level; DLCO improvement at 6 months.
btd_threshold_vs_soc: ≥60% reduction in hemorrhage episodes; improved DLCO vs. corticosteroids alone.

symptoms: Rapidly progressive respiratory failure; bilateral lung infiltrates; no identifiable cause
treatment_goal: Support ventilation; reduce lung inflammation; improve survival
first_line_soc: High-dose systemic corticosteroids (methylprednisolone pulse therapy) plus mechanical ventilation support. No proven pharmacotherapy; empiric corticosteroids standard.
treatment_algorithm: Supportive: mechanical ventilation (lung-protective strategy), ECMO for severe cases. Corticosteroids (empiric — limited evidence): IV methylprednisolone 1 g/day × 3 days, then taper. Cyclophosphamide in some protocols. Anticoagulation for DAD. Mortality 50–60%. No approved drug.
us_prevalence: Very rare; ~500–1,000 cases/year.
patients_needing_tx: ~750 patients receiving pharmacotherapy.
peak_sales: Extremely rare acute disease. Novel anti-fibrotic/anti-inflammatory: minimal commercial market. Peak sales ~$10–20M.
registrational_endpoint: 28-day and 90-day all-cause mortality; ventilator-free days.
btd_threshold_vs_soc: ≥20% absolute reduction in 90-day mortality vs. supportive care.

symptoms: Transient migratory pulmonary infiltrates, blood eosinophilia, mild cough, fever
treatment_goal: Eliminate parasitic trigger; resolve eosinophilic infiltrates
first_line_soc: Albendazole or ivermectin for helminth-associated Löffler syndrome; corticosteroids for idiopathic or severe cases.
treatment_algorithm: Identify and treat underlying parasitic infection: albendazole 400 mg BID × 3 days for Ascaris, hookworm; ivermectin 200 mcg/kg × 1–2 doses for Strongyloides. Corticosteroids (prednisone 20–40 mg/day) for symptomatic or idiopathic cases. Antiparasitics resolve infiltrates in 2–4 weeks. No chronic therapy usually needed.
us_prevalence: ~5,000–15,000 cases/year in the US; many are immigrants or travelers with helminth exposure.
patients_needing_tx: ~10,000 patients/year needing antiparasitic treatment.
price: $500–$5,000/course (antiparasitic or novel eosinophil-targeting agent).
peak_sales: ~$5–15M; generic antiparasitics dominate; minimal novel drug opportunity.
registrational_endpoint: Eosinophil count normalization; radiographic resolution of infiltrates at day 28.
btd_threshold_vs_soc: ≥80% radiographic resolution and eosinophil normalization at day 14 vs. placebo/standard.

symptoms: Persistent breathlessness, chronic cough, sputum, progressive airflow limitation
treatment_goal: Reduce symptoms; prevent exacerbations; slow decline; improve exercise tolerance
first_line_soc: Long-acting bronchodilators (LABAs and/or LAMAs) are first-line maintenance therapy. LAMA (tiotropium/Spiriva) is standard for moderate-severe COPD.
treatment_algorithm: Step 1: Short-acting bronchodilators (SABA, SAMA) as needed. Step 2: LAMA (tiotropium, umeclidinium, aclidinium) or LABA (salmeterol, formoterol, vilanterol, indacaterol). Step 3: LABA+LAMA (Anoro Ellipta, Stiolto Respimat, Duaklir). Step 4: LABA+ICS+LAMA triple therapy (Trelegy Ellipta, Breztri Aerosphere) for eosinophilic/frequent exacerbators. Step 5: Roflumilast (PDE4 inhibitor, Daliresp) for chronic bronchitis phenotype. Acute exacerbations: SABAs, SAMAs, systemic corticosteroids, antibiotics. 2024: dupilumab (Dupixent) FDA-approved 2023 for COPD with eosinophils ≥300; itepekimab (anti-IL-33, Sanofi/Regen) Phase 3.
us_prevalence: ~16 million diagnosed US adults (~6.4% prevalence; CDC 2024); ~6 million undiagnosed.
patients_needing_tx: ~12 million patients needing pharmacotherapy.
price: $20,000–$45,000/year for biologics; LABA/LAMA generic ~$3,000/yr.
peak_sales: Dupilumab COPD: $2–3B projected peak. New biologic: 10–15% biologic-eligible share. Peak sales ~$1.5–3.5B.
registrational_endpoint: Moderate/severe exacerbation rate reduction at 52 weeks; trough FEV1 improvement; E-RS:COPD score.
btd_threshold_vs_soc: ≥30% exacerbation rate reduction vs. triple ICS/LABA/LAMA; FEV1 ≥100 mL improvement; unselected population.

symptoms: Episodic wheezing, shortness of breath, chest tightness, cough
treatment_goal: Achieve asthma control; minimize exacerbations; normalize lung function
first_line_soc: Inhaled corticosteroids (ICS) are first-line controller therapy; short-acting beta-agonists (SABAs, albuterol) for rescue. ICS+LABA for moderate-severe asthma.
treatment_algorithm: Step 1: SABA (albuterol) as needed. Step 2: Low-dose ICS (fluticasone, budesonide). Step 3: ICS + LABA (Advair, Symbicort, Dulera, Breo Ellipta). Step 4: ICS+LABA+LAMA (Trelegy). Step 5: Biologics for eosinophilic/allergic severe asthma: omalizumab (Xolair), mepolizumab (Nucala), benralizumab (Fasenra), dupilumab (Dupixent — FDA 2018), tezepelumab (Tezspire — FDA 2021), itepekimab (Regn-anti-IL-33). Oral corticosteroids for exacerbations. Asthma biologics growing rapidly 2024–2025.
us_prevalence: ~26–27 million US adults and children (~8% prevalence; CDC 2024).
patients_needing_tx: ~20 million patients needing controller therapy; ~2.5 million severe asthma patients eligible for biologics.
price: $30,000–$50,000/year for biologics; ICS/LABA generics ~$2,000/yr.
peak_sales: Tezepelumab (Tezspire): ~$500M-1.0B peak. New biologic entrant: 10–20% severe share. Peak sales ~$1.5–4.0B.
registrational_endpoint: Annualized exacerbation rate reduction ≥50%; FEV1 improvement; ACQ-6 score at 52 weeks.
btd_threshold_vs_soc: Exacerbation rate reduction ≥30% vs. active biologic comparator (mepolizumab/dupilumab); unselected population response.

symptoms: Chronic productive cough, recurrent lung infections, airway dilation
treatment_goal: Reduce exacerbations; manage secretions; prevent progression
first_line_soc: Airway clearance techniques (physiotherapy, oscillating devices) plus antibiotics for exacerbations (amoxicillin-clavulanate, ciprofloxacin) are standard. Inhaled azithromycin or tobramycin for chronic Pseudomonas suppression.
treatment_algorithm: Airway clearance: hypertonic saline, oscillating positive expiratory pressure. Antibiotics: acute exacerbation — ciprofloxacin or amoxicillin-clavulanate; chronic Pseudomonas suppression — inhaled tobramycin/azithromycin 3×/week. Bronchodilators (ICS+LABA for obstructive component). Dornase alfa contraindicated in non-CF bronchiectasis. 2024–2025: brensocatib (dipeptidyl peptidase-1 inhibitor, Insmed) — FDA-approved 2024 for reducing exacerbations in non-CF bronchiectasis.
us_prevalence: ~500,000 US adults with symptomatic bronchiectasis (Chest Journal/ERS data).
patients_needing_tx: ~350,000 patients needing active pharmacotherapy.
price: $25,000–$50,000/year; brensocatib WAC ~$30,000/yr (recently approved).
peak_sales: Brensocatib (Insmed): projected $1.5–2.5B peak. Competing novel drug: 15–25% share. Peak sales ~$800M–2.0B.
registrational_endpoint: Annualized exacerbation rate reduction at 52 weeks; time to first exacerbation.
btd_threshold_vs_soc: ≥25% exacerbation rate reduction vs. brensocatib or active antibiotic comparator.

symptoms: Early-onset emphysema, chronic cough, dyspnea, sometimes liver disease
treatment_goal: Slow lung destruction; augment deficient protein level
first_line_soc: Alpha-1 proteinase inhibitor (A1PI) augmentation therapy (Prolastin-C, Aralast NP, Zemaira — IV infusions weekly) is the only FDA-approved disease-modifying therapy for lung disease in AATD.
treatment_algorithm: A1PI augmentation therapy: weekly IV infusions targeting trough serum AAT >11 µM. Supplemental oxygen for hypoxemia. Standard COPD management: SABAs, LABAs, ICSs, LAMAs. Lung volume reduction; lung transplant for advanced disease. 2024 pipeline: RNA interference (fazirsiran — subcutaneous, Takeda/Arrowhead, Phase 3 — reduces mutant Z-AAT in liver); gene therapy trials ongoing.
us_prevalence: ~100,000 diagnosed US patients; ~200,000 undiagnosed (AAT Foundation).
patients_needing_tx: ~50,000 patients on augmentation therapy; growing with new SQ approaches.
price: $100,000–$300,000/year; current IV augmentation ~$120,000–$200,000/yr WAC.
peak_sales: Fazirsiran (SQ RNAi) could replace IV augmentation. 40–60% uptake. Peak sales ~$1.0–2.5B.
registrational_endpoint: CT-measured lung density (PD15 or TLC950) at 24 months; serum AAT trough level.
btd_threshold_vs_soc: Significant PD15 preservation vs. IV augmentation therapy; ≥80% reduction in liver Z-AAT; fewer infusions.

symptoms: Asthma symptoms (wheeze, cough, dyspnea) triggered by or worsened by workplace exposures
treatment_goal: Remove from occupational trigger; control inflammation; prevent lung function loss
first_line_soc: Remove from causative workplace exposure. ICS-LABA (same as occupational asthma SoC: budesonide-formoterol or fluticasone-salmeterol). Short-acting SABA rescue.
treatment_algorithm: Work removal is the most effective intervention — ~50% achieve remission. Pharmacologic same as asthma: SABA rescue + ICS controller; ICS-LABA for moderate-severe. LTRA (montelukast) as add-on. For refractory occupational asthma with eosinophilic phenotype: dupilumab (Dupixent) or mepolizumab (Nucala) anti-IL-5. Personal protective equipment if removal not possible. Medical-legal documentation. Monitor serial spirometry.
us_prevalence: ~15–20% of adult asthma cases (~4–5 million) are work-related or work-exacerbated. NIOSH data.
patients_needing_tx: ~2–3 million workers with active work-related asthma requiring pharmacologic management.
price: $20,000–$50,000/year for targeted biologic; $2,000–$5,000 for ICS-LABA.
tam_gross: 2.5M × $30,000 avg = $75B (included in asthma market); WRA-specific biologic TAM: ~$5–10B
peak_sales: Subsumed under asthma biologics market. WRA-specific indication add-on: ~$200–400M incremental peak sales.
registrational_endpoint: FEV1 change; asthma control questionnaire (ACQ) score; exacerbation rate per year; SABA use/week.
btd_threshold_vs_soc: ACQ improvement ≥0.5 vs. ICS-LABA; exacerbation reduction ≥50%; FEV1 improvement ≥200 mL vs. ICS-LABA alone.

symptoms: Exacerbated asthma, COPD, reduced lung function, cardiovascular stress
treatment_goal: Control exacerbations; improve lung protection; reduce systemic inflammation
first_line_soc: No specific pharmacotherapy for air pollution-related illness per se. Standard treatment of triggered conditions (bronchodilators for asthma/COPD exacerbations). Avoidance is primary prevention.
treatment_algorithm: Treat underlying conditions: SABAs/ICSs for asthma exacerbations; SABA + SAMA for COPD exacerbations. Anti-inflammatory: systemic corticosteroids for acute exacerbations. N-acetylcysteine (mucolytic, antioxidant) studied. No approved drug specifically for air pollution exposure. Antioxidant supplementation (vitamin C/E) under study.
us_prevalence: ~150+ million Americans exposed to unhealthy air quality days (ALA State of Air 2024); millions with pollution-attributable disease.
patients_needing_tx: ~5 million patients with clinically significant pollution-exacerbated respiratory disease.
price: $5,000–$15,000/year for a protective/anti-inflammatory agent.
peak_sales: No defined therapeutic category; managed within asthma/COPD. Novel protective agent: 3–5% share. Peak sales ~$300–600M.
registrational_endpoint: FEV1 % predicted improvement; exacerbation rate reduction at 12 months in high-pollution exposed cohort.
btd_threshold_vs_soc: ≥20% reduction in exacerbation rate vs. standard bronchodilator in PM2.5-exposed patients.

symptoms: Progressive dyspnea, bilateral basal crackles, restrictive lung disease from asbestos fibers
treatment_goal: Slow progression; reduce symptoms; prevent complications
first_line_soc: No disease-modifying pharmacotherapy proven for asbestosis. Symptomatic management, pulmonary rehabilitation, oxygen for hypoxemia. Anti-fibrotics (nintedanib, pirfenidone) used off-label by analogy with IPF.
treatment_algorithm: No approved specific drug. Supportive: supplemental O2, pulmonary rehab. Anti-fibrotics off-label: nintedanib and pirfenidone may slow fibrosis progression (evidence extrapolated from IPF). Avoidance of further asbestos exposure mandatory. Lung transplant for advanced disease. Annual surveillance for mesothelioma and lung cancer.
us_prevalence: ~200,000 US cases (NIOSH/ATSDR); incidence declining but prior exposures persist.
patients_needing_tx: ~100,000 patients with progressive asbestosis needing anti-fibrotic treatment.
price: $50,000–$80,000/year; IPF anti-fibrotic pricing benchmark (nintedanib ~$100,000/yr).
peak_sales: Managed off-label with IPF drugs. Dedicated approval could capture 20–30% share. Peak sales ~$400–700M.
registrational_endpoint: FVC % predicted decline rate at 52 weeks; DLCO change.
btd_threshold_vs_soc: ≥30% reduction in FVC decline rate vs. placebo; superiority vs. nintedanib in a head-to-head study.

symptoms: Occupational dust-induced progressive lung fibrosis; cough, dyspnea
treatment_goal: Slow progression; relieve symptoms; prevent complications
first_line_soc: No disease-modifying pharmacotherapy approved. Remove from coal dust exposure. Symptomatic: bronchodilators for obstruction; oxygen for hypoxemia.
treatment_algorithm: Remove from exposure. Treat COPD component with bronchodilators (LABAs, LAMAs, ICS). Supplemental oxygen. N-acetylcysteine (antioxidant — limited evidence). Anti-fibrotics (nintedanib, pirfenidone) considered off-label for progressive fibrotic component. Pulmonary rehabilitation. Lung transplant end-stage.
us_prevalence: ~500,000 miners with CWP nationally (NIOSH); progressive massive fibrosis in ~10,000 active severe cases.
patients_needing_tx: ~100,000 patients needing bronchodilator pharmacotherapy.
peak_sales: Managed off-label with COPD/IPF drugs. Dedicated approval: 15–20% share. Peak sales ~$200–400M.
registrational_endpoint: FVC decline rate at 52 weeks; DLCO change; CT fibrosis score.
btd_threshold_vs_soc: ≥30% reduction in FVC decline rate vs. placebo; superior to nintedanib in progressive fibrosis subtype.

symptoms: Respiratory/mucous membrane symptoms, fatigue tied to specific building exposure
treatment_goal: Remove patient from exposure; treat specific triggered conditions
first_line_soc: Identification and remediation of building exposure is primary. Treat underlying triggered conditions (asthma, hypersensitivity pneumonitis) per standard protocols.
treatment_algorithm: Remove from exposure source. Treat specific diagnoses: ICS/LABA for triggered asthma; corticosteroids for HP; antihistamines for allergic rhinitis. Immunotherapy for identified allergens. No specific drug for building-related illness syndrome. Indoor air quality remediation essential.
us_prevalence: ~30% of workers in affected buildings report symptoms; ~5–10 million with clinically significant building-related illness.
patients_needing_tx: ~500,000 patients with clinically significant respiratory building-related illness needing pharmacotherapy.
peak_sales: No dedicated pharmacotherapy market. Managed within existing classes. Peak sales ~$50–100M.
registrational_endpoint: Symptom score reduction; FEV1 improvement after building removal at 12 weeks.
btd_threshold_vs_soc: ≥40% symptom score reduction vs. placebo in building-associated respiratory disease cohort.

symptoms: Pleural plaques, thickening, effusion; breathlessness; no malignancy
treatment_goal: Symptomatic relief; monitor for progression to mesothelioma
first_line_soc: No specific pharmacotherapy. Symptomatic management (analgesics for pain). Regular surveillance imaging. Smoking cessation.
treatment_algorithm: No disease-modifying pharmacotherapy. Pleurodesis for recurrent effusion. Symptomatic: NSAIDs or acetaminophen for chest pain. Monitor with annual CT for mesothelioma. Pulmonary rehabilitation. Refer to occupational medicine. Smoking cessation reduces combined lung cancer risk.
us_prevalence: ~800,000–1.5 million US workers with asbestos-related pleural disease (NIOSH estimates).
patients_needing_tx: ~200,000 patients with symptomatic disease needing pharmacotherapy.
peak_sales: No pharmacologic market for asbestos-related benign pleural disease. Peak sales ~$20–50M (if anti-fibrotic agent proven).
registrational_endpoint: Pleural thickness progression on CT at 24 months; dyspnea score improvement.
btd_threshold_vs_soc: ≥30% reduction in pleural thickness progression; clinically significant dyspnea improvement vs. placebo.

symptoms: Progressive dyspnea, chronic cough, nodular lung opacities; occupational silica dust exposure
treatment_goal: Slow progression; prevent complications (TB, lung cancer, respiratory failure)
first_line_soc: No pharmacologic disease-modifying therapy. Silica exposure cessation mandatory. Supportive: bronchodilators, supplemental oxygen, TB prophylaxis (silicosis triples TB risk).
treatment_algorithm: No FDA-approved therapy. Remove from silica exposure immediately. Anti-TB prophylaxis (isoniazid or rifampin) given high TB risk. Whole lung lavage (WLL) for accelerated silicosis — symptomatic relief only. Bronchodilators for airflow obstruction. Corticosteroids (limited evidence). Lung transplant for advanced disease. INOS (inhaled nitric oxide) — investigational. Tetrandrine — studied in China, not FDA-approved.
us_prevalence: ~3,000–5,000 prevalent US cases; resurgence of accelerated silicosis in engineered stone countertop workers (2022–2025). NIOSH data.
patients_needing_tx: ~3,000 patients requiring pharmacologic support (bronchodilators, TB prophylaxis, O2); ~500 with accelerated silicosis needing WLL.
price: $30,000–$80,000/year for a novel disease-modifying agent.
peak_sales: ~$30–60M peak sales; rare occupational lung disease.
registrational_endpoint: Change in FVC % predicted at 52 weeks; CT radiographic progression score; time to acute silicosis exacerbation.
btd_threshold_vs_soc: FVC decline ≤3% vs. ≥8% natural history; or ≥30% reduction in CT progression score at 52 weeks.

symptoms: Acute cough, bronchospasm, mucosal irritation; potentially ARDS
treatment_goal: Support airway; reduce inflammation; treat bronchospasm
first_line_soc: Remove from exposure. Bronchodilators (albuterol nebulized) for bronchospasm. Systemic corticosteroids for severe cases.
treatment_algorithm: Decontamination. Bronchodilators: nebulized albuterol, ipratropium. Systemic corticosteroids (methylprednisolone) for reactive airways dysfunction syndrome (RADS). Nebulized sodium bicarbonate for certain acidic gas injuries. Mechanical ventilation for ARDS. No specific approved drug.
us_prevalence: ~10,000–20,000 significant occupational/accidental cases/year.
patients_needing_tx: ~15,000 patients needing acute pharmacotherapy.
peak_sales: Acute market; no chronic drug opportunity. Peak sales ~$10–25M.
registrational_endpoint: FEV1 normalization; airway hyperresponsiveness reduction at 4 weeks.
btd_threshold_vs_soc: ≥30% faster FEV1 recovery vs. bronchodilator alone.

symptoms: Occupational granulomatous lung disease mimicking sarcoidosis; beryllium sensitization
treatment_goal: Reduce granulomatous inflammation; preserve lung function
first_line_soc: Corticosteroids (prednisone 40–60 mg/day) when symptomatic. Remove from beryllium exposure.
treatment_algorithm: Remove from beryllium exposure (mandatory). Symptomatic disease: prednisone 40 mg/day, taper over months. Methotrexate or azathioprine for steroid-sparing in chronic disease. Supplemental oxygen. Lung transplant for end-stage. No approved specific drug.
us_prevalence: ~1,000–2,000 active cases (NIOSH); ~10,000 beryllium-sensitized workers at risk.
patients_needing_tx: ~1,000 symptomatic patients needing pharmacotherapy.
peak_sales: Rare occupational lung disease. Peak sales ~$10–20M.
registrational_endpoint: DLCO improvement; BAL lymphocyte proliferation test; dyspnea scale at 6 months.
btd_threshold_vs_soc: ≥25% DLCO improvement vs. corticosteroids alone; stable or improved CT findings.

symptoms: Chest tightness and Monday breathing difficulty in textile workers (cotton dust)
treatment_goal: Relieve airflow obstruction; prevent progression to chronic lung disease
first_line_soc: Remove from cotton dust exposure. Bronchodilators (SABAs, LABAs) and ICS for symptomatic obstruction.
treatment_algorithm: Primary: remove from exposure, use dust control. Symptomatic: bronchodilators (salbutamol/albuterol, tiotropium); ICS for inflammation; oral prednisolone for acute exacerbations. Pulmonary rehabilitation for chronic disease. No drug specific to byssinosis approved.
us_prevalence: ~1,000–5,000 active US cases (declining with dust regulations; NIOSH/OSHA data).
patients_needing_tx: ~3,000 patients receiving bronchodilator pharmacotherapy.
peak_sales: Very small market. Managed with existing COPD/asthma drugs. Peak sales ~$3–5M.
registrational_endpoint: FEV1 improvement; change in Monday morning FEV1 dip at 6 months.
btd_threshold_vs_soc: ≥20% reduction in Monday FEV1 decline vs. placebo in cotton textile workers.

symptoms: Usually asymptomatic; incidental finding on CT; may represent malignancy or benign granuloma
treatment_goal: Risk-stratify; diagnose malignancy; guide watchful waiting vs. biopsy/resection
first_line_soc: No pharmacologic SoC; management is diagnostic (CT surveillance, PET, biopsy) per Fleischner Society guidelines.
treatment_algorithm: Risk stratification by size, morphology, patient risk factors (Fleischner Society, Lung-RADS). <6 mm, low risk: no follow-up. 6–8 mm: CT at 6–12 months. >8 mm: PET-CT ± biopsy. Malignant: VATS resection for early-stage NSCLC. Benign granuloma: no treatment. Novel: artificial intelligence-assisted CT analysis tools (not pharmacologic). No FDA-approved pharmacologic therapy for the nodule itself.
us_prevalence: ~1.5 million new incidental pulmonary nodules detected/year; >8 million prevalent. Low-dose CT screening programs increasing detection.
patients_needing_tx: ~150,000 malignant nodules (early NSCLC) requiring surgical or pharmacologic treatment; the vast majority are benign.
price: $150,000–$300,000/year for targeted NSCLC therapy after diagnosis.
tam_gross: 150,000 malignant × $200,000 = $30B TAM (NSCLC market); SPN-specific drug market is N/A
peak_sales: No pharmacologic market for SPN per se. Early-stage NSCLC adjuvant therapies (osimertinib, atezolizumab): multi-billion markets.
registrational_endpoint: N/A for SPN-specific drug; early NSCLC: disease-free survival (DFS) in adjuvant setting.
btd_threshold_vs_soc: N/A for benign SPN; for malignant SPN (early NSCLC): DFS HR <0.70 vs. observation/chemotherapy.

symptoms: Episodic stridor, throat tightness, dyspnea during exercise or stress; normal spirometry at rest
treatment_goal: Abort acute episodes; prevent recurrences through behavioral therapy
first_line_soc: Speech therapy (breathing retraining) is first-line. No pharmacologic SoC; heliox for acute episodes.
treatment_algorithm: Speech therapy with respiratory retraining is the cornerstone treatment. Psychological evaluation and CBT for anxiety-triggered VFD. Heliox (helium-oxygen) for acute severe episodes in ER. Botulinum toxin injection (onabotulinumtoxinA) for treatment-refractory cases. Proton pump inhibitors if LPR (laryngopharyngeal reflux) is a trigger. Anticholinergics (ipratropium) have limited benefit. Biofeedback therapy. No approved pharmacologic therapy specifically for VFD.
us_prevalence: ~300,000–500,000 US patients; predominantly females; frequently misdiagnosed as asthma.
patients_needing_tx: ~200,000–300,000 patients with recurrent VFD requiring pharmacologic adjuncts.
price: $5,000–$20,000/year for a targeted pharmacotherapy.
peak_sales: Primarily behavioral disorder; pharmacologic market limited. Peak sales ~$50–100M for novel neurolaryngology agent.
registrational_endpoint: VFD episode frequency per month; Dyspnea Index (DI) score; exercise laryngoscopy normalization.
btd_threshold_vs_soc: ≥50% reduction in VFD episode frequency; DI score improvement ≥10 points vs. sham at 12 weeks.

symptoms: Cough, dyspnea, infiltrates temporally linked to drug exposure
treatment_goal: Stop offending drug; manage pulmonary inflammation
first_line_soc: Discontinue offending drug (most important intervention). Systemic corticosteroids (prednisone) for significant inflammation/fibrosis.
treatment_algorithm: Stop causative drug immediately. Corticosteroids for inflammatory/organizing pneumonia patterns: prednisone 0.5–1 mg/kg/day. Anti-fibrotics for fibrotic patterns (nintedanib — approved for progressive ILDs). Supportive oxygen. Bleomycin, methotrexate, amiodarone most common offenders; immune checkpoint inhibitors increasingly recognized.
us_prevalence: ~50,000–150,000 cases/year (checkpoint inhibitor pneumonitis alone ~3–5% of ICI users).
patients_needing_tx: ~75,000 patients needing corticosteroid/immunosuppressive pharmacotherapy.
peak_sales: Managed with corticosteroids. Anti-inflammatory novel agent: 10–15% share. Peak sales ~$100–200M.
registrational_endpoint: CT resolution rate; FVC improvement; prednisone dose reduction ≥50% at 12 weeks.
btd_threshold_vs_soc: ≥30% faster CT resolution; ≥50% steroid sparing vs. high-dose corticosteroid alone.

symptoms: Chest pain, dyspnea, superior vena cava syndrome, incidental finding on imaging
treatment_goal: Diagnose and treat underlying etiology (lymphoma, thymoma, germ cell tumor)
first_line_soc: Treatment depends on etiology: chemotherapy (e.g., ABVD for lymphoma), surgical resection for thymoma, cisplatin-based regimens for germ cell tumors.
treatment_algorithm: Diagnosis first (CT/PET/biopsy). Lymphoma: ABVD (doxorubicin, bleomycin, vinblastine, dacarbazine) or CHOP ± rituximab. Thymoma: surgical resection ± adjuvant radiation/chemotherapy. Germ cell tumors: BEP (bleomycin, etoposide, cisplatin). Thyroid masses: surgery ± radioiodine. Benign cysts: surgical excision. No single pharmacologic SoC applicable across all.
us_prevalence: ~25,000–40,000 new cases/year (all mediastinal masses combined); majority benign.
patients_needing_tx: ~15,000–20,000 patients requiring pharmacologic treatment for malignant etiologies.
price: $50,000–$200,000/year depending on cancer type.
peak_sales: Fragmented by etiology; market size varies widely. For a thymoma-specific drug: ~$200–400M peak sales.
registrational_endpoint: Objective response rate (ORR) per RECIST; progression-free survival (PFS).
btd_threshold_vs_soc: ORR ≥30% in thymoma (vs. ~10–15% historical); PFS improvement ≥2 months.

symptoms: Pleural effusion, chest pain, dyspnea; linked to asbestos; poor prognosis
treatment_goal: Extend survival; control effusions; palliate symptoms
first_line_soc: Nivolumab + ipilimumab (FDA-approved 2020, CheckMate 743) for unresectable malignant pleural mesothelioma (MPM).
treatment_algorithm: Unresectable MPM: nivolumab + ipilimumab (IO doublet, FDA-approved 2020). Chemotherapy: pemetrexed + cisplatin/carboplatin (first established regimen). Bevacizumab added in some regimens. Surgical: pleurectomy/decortication or EPP in select patients. Radiation therapy palliative. Ramucirumab + gemcitabine second-line. Novel targets: BAP1, NF2, CDKN2A in trials. Talc pleurodesis for effusion control.
us_prevalence: ~3,000 new cases/year; ~10,000 prevalent patients. ACS Cancer Statistics 2025.
patients_needing_tx: ~8,000 patients actively on systemic therapy; majority unresectable at diagnosis.
price: $150,000–$250,000/year; comparable to other thoracic oncology agents.
peak_sales: ~15–20% market share in a competitive IO space; ~$240–320M peak sales for a novel agent.
registrational_endpoint: Overall survival (OS); progression-free survival (PFS) per modified RECIST for MPM.
btd_threshold_vs_soc: OS improvement ≥3 months vs. pemetrexed + platinum; HR <0.75 for OS.

symptoms: Lung collapse, reduced breath sounds, hypoxemia, post-operative complication
treatment_goal: Re-expand collapsed lung; restore oxygenation
first_line_soc: Pulmonary toilet (physiotherapy, incentive spirometry) is first-line. Bronchoscopy for mucus plugging. No specific pharmacotherapy for atelectasis itself.
treatment_algorithm: Mechanical: chest physiotherapy, incentive spirometry, deep breathing, CPAP/PEEP for post-surgical. Bronchoscopy for lobar atelectasis from mucus plug. Nebulized DNase (dornase alfa) for mucus-related atelectasis (e.g., CF). Avoid sedation/opioids where possible. No specific approved drug.
us_prevalence: ~500,000 post-operative/hospital cases/year; highly prevalent in ICU.
patients_needing_tx: ~200,000 patients requiring active pharmacologic intervention (DNase, mucolytics).
peak_sales: Mostly managed with physiotherapy. Pharmacologic market minimal. Peak sales ~$50–100M.
registrational_endpoint: Chest X-ray resolution rate; oxygenation (SpO2) improvement at 48 hours.
btd_threshold_vs_soc: ≥30% faster resolution vs. standard physiotherapy; SpO2 ≥94% achieved ≥24 hours earlier.

symptoms: Severe chest pain, fever, dysphagia; post-surgical or descending infection from neck
treatment_goal: Control infection; drain mediastinum; prevent septic death
first_line_soc: Broad-spectrum IV antibiotics (vancomycin + piperacillin-tazobactam or carbapenem) plus urgent surgical drainage.
treatment_algorithm: Immediate IV antibiotics: vancomycin for MRSA coverage + piperacillin-tazobactam or imipenem for gram-negative and anaerobic coverage. Surgical debridement and drainage; continuous mediastinal irrigation. Fibrinolytic agents (intra-pleural tPA/DNase) for loculated collections. Hyperbaric oxygen as adjunct. Antifungals (fluconazole, caspofungin) if fungal etiology. Chronic fibrosing mediastinitis (histoplasma): itraconazole.
us_prevalence: ~2,000–4,000 cases/year of post-sternotomy mediastinitis; descending necrotizing mediastinitis ~500–1,000 cases/year.
patients_needing_tx: ~3,000–4,500 patients/year requiring pharmacologic + surgical management.
peak_sales: Acute infection; limited chronic drug opportunity. Peak sales ~$10–20M for novel antibiotic.
registrational_endpoint: 28-day all-cause mortality; clinical cure at day 14.
btd_threshold_vs_soc: Mortality reduction ≥25% vs. standard antibiotics + surgery.

symptoms: Sudden chest pain, subcutaneous neck crepitus, dyspnea; air in mediastinum
treatment_goal: Resolve air; treat underlying cause; prevent tension pneumomediastinum
first_line_soc: Supportive care: bed rest, supplemental oxygen (accelerates air reabsorption), analgesics. No pharmacologic SoC.
treatment_algorithm: Spontaneous pneumomediastinum: observation, bed rest, high-flow O2 (to accelerate nitrogen absorption), analgesics. Secondary: treat underlying cause (esophageal perforation: antibiotics + surgical repair; asthma exacerbation: bronchodilators + steroids). NPO for suspected esophageal leak. Surgical decompression rarely needed. Antibiotics if infection risk (iatrogenic perforation).
us_prevalence: ~5,000–10,000 cases/year; mostly young adults with spontaneous form; iatrogenic ~50% of cases.
patients_needing_tx: ~8,000 patients/year requiring pharmacologic management.
price: $1,000–$5,000/acute course.
peak_sales: Largely self-resolving; minimal drug opportunity. Peak sales ~$5–10M.
registrational_endpoint: Time to radiographic resolution; pain score improvement at 48 hours.
btd_threshold_vs_soc: ≥30% faster radiographic resolution vs. supportive care alone.

symptoms: Dyspnea, pleuritic chest pain, dullness to percussion, reduced breath sounds
treatment_goal: Drain effusion; treat underlying cause; prevent recurrence
first_line_soc: Thoracentesis for diagnosis and symptom relief. Treat underlying cause: diuretics (furosemide + spironolactone) for heart failure; antibiotics for parapneumonic; chemotherapy for malignant effusion.
treatment_algorithm: Transudative (heart failure): diuretics + ACE inhibitors/ARBs. Exudative parapneumonic: antibiotics; intrapleural tPA + DNase for complicated parapneumonic. Malignant: pleurodesis (talc), tunneled pleural catheter, or systemic chemotherapy/targeted therapy. Hepatic hydrothorax: treat portal hypertension (diuretics, TIPS). Chylothorax: octreotide, dietary modification. TB effusion: antituberculosis regimens.
us_prevalence: ~1.5 million cases/year; heart failure (~500,000), parapneumonic (~500,000), malignant (~150,000). Chest 2022 data.
patients_needing_tx: ~500,000–700,000 patients needing pharmacologic therapy beyond drainage.
price: $5,000–$50,000/course depending on etiology.
tam_gross: Highly fragmented by cause; no single novel drug market. Malignant PE: ~$100,000/year × 150,000 = $15B TAM
peak_sales: Focus on malignant effusion prevention: ~$200–400M peak sales for a novel anti-effusion biologic.
registrational_endpoint: Time to effusion recurrence after pleurodesis; dyspnea score (Borg scale); pleural fluid-free survival at 90 days.
btd_threshold_vs_soc: ≥30% reduction in effusion recurrence rate at 90 days vs. talc pleurodesis.

symptoms: Progressive dyspnea, restrictive lung defect, thickened pleura on imaging; often asbestos-linked
treatment_goal: Manage symptoms; prevent progression; treat underlying cause
first_line_soc: No pharmacologic therapy approved. Treat underlying cause (remove asbestos exposure). Supportive care: pulmonary rehabilitation, supplemental oxygen.
treatment_algorithm: No effective pharmacologic therapy exists. Avoid further asbestos/pleural irritant exposure. Pulmonary rehabilitation. Supplemental O2 for hypoxia. Corticosteroids occasionally used for inflammatory fibrothorax (limited evidence). Decortication (surgical) for restrictive fibrothorax causing severe ventilatory impairment. Anti-fibrotic agents (nintedanib) under investigation for pleural fibrosis.
us_prevalence: ~50,000–100,000 US patients with asbestos-related pleural fibrosis; broader pleural fibrosis ~200,000.
patients_needing_tx: ~20,000–30,000 with symptomatic restrictive disease warranting systemic treatment.
price: $50,000–$100,000/year for an anti-fibrotic targeting pleural fibrosis.
registrational_endpoint: Change in FVC % predicted at 52 weeks; pleural thickness by CT at 52 weeks.
btd_threshold_vs_soc: ≥100 mL FVC preservation vs. placebo at 52 weeks; or ≥20% reduction in pleural thickness.

symptoms: Sharp pleuritic chest pain worsening with breathing; friction rub; mild fever; self-limiting
treatment_goal: Pain relief; anti-inflammatory therapy; reassurance
first_line_soc: NSAIDs (ibuprofen 400–600 mg TID) are first-line for pain and inflammation; colchicine added for recurrent cases.
treatment_algorithm: NSAIDs (ibuprofen or indomethacin) for 7–14 days. Colchicine 0.5 mg BID × 3 months for recurrent pleuritis (analogy to pericarditis). Aspirin alternative. Corticosteroids for refractory or immunological etiology (avoid in infectious). Rest and monitoring. Antiviral therapy (e.g., acyclovir) only if herpes-related. Most cases resolve within 2–4 weeks.
us_prevalence: ~50,000–100,000 cases/year; often under-diagnosed as 'chest wall pain.'
patients_needing_tx: ~70,000 patients/year requiring pharmacologic treatment.
peak_sales: Treated with generics; minimal novel drug opportunity. Peak sales ~$10–20M.
registrational_endpoint: Pleuritic pain VAS score at 72 hours; recurrence rate at 6 months.
btd_threshold_vs_soc: Pain reduction ≥50% at 72 hours vs. NSAID; recurrence rate ≤10% at 6 months vs. ~30% without colchicine.

symptoms: Sudden pleuritic chest pain, dyspnea, absent breath sounds on one side
treatment_goal: Re-expand lung; prevent recurrence; remove air from pleural space
first_line_soc: Small primary spontaneous pneumothorax: observation or needle aspiration. Large/secondary: chest tube drainage. Recurrent: video-assisted thoracoscopic surgery (VATS) pleurodesis.
treatment_algorithm: Conservative management for small primary spontaneous pneumothorax (<2 cm on X-ray): observation + supplemental O2. Aspiration (manual or catheter) for moderate. Chest tube for large, secondary, or tension pneumothorax. Chemical pleurodesis: talc or doxycycline instillation for recurrent cases. VATS bullectomy + pleurodesis for recurrence prevention. Tension pneumothorax: emergency needle decompression. Antibiotics only if empyema co-exists.
us_prevalence: ~20,000 hospitalizations/year; primary spontaneous in young males; secondary in COPD, CF, LAM.
patients_needing_tx: ~15,000 patients/year requiring chest tube + pharmacologic pleurodesis.
peak_sales: Self-limiting; no chronic drug market. Novel pleural sealant/pleurodesis agent: ~$20–50M peak sales.
registrational_endpoint: Recurrence-free survival at 12 months; time to lung re-expansion.
btd_threshold_vs_soc: Recurrence rate ≤10% at 12 months vs. ~30% without intervention.

symptoms: Fever, productive cough, pleuritic chest pain, lobar infiltrate
treatment_goal: Eradicate causative pathogen; resolve inflammation; prevent complications
first_line_soc: For outpatient CAP: amoxicillin (first-line) or doxycycline; azithromycin for atypical pathogens. For hospitalized non-ICU: beta-lactam + macrolide or respiratory fluoroquinolone.
treatment_algorithm: Outpatient: amoxicillin 1g TID; doxycycline; azithromycin (for atypicals). Hospitalized non-ICU: beta-lactam (ceftriaxone, ampicillin-sulbactam) + azithromycin OR respiratory fluoroquinolone (levofloxacin, moxifloxacin). ICU: beta-lactam + azithromycin + anti-pseudomonal coverage. MRSA: vancomycin or linezolid. Viral CAP (COVID-19/influenza): antivirals. IDSA/ATS guidelines 2023.
us_prevalence: ~4–5 million outpatient episodes and 600,000 hospitalizations annually (CDC).
patients_needing_tx: ~4.5 million patients/year needing antibiotic pharmacotherapy.
price: $1,000–$5,000/course for novel antibiotics.
peak_sales: Generic antibiotics dominate. Novel antibiotic: 5–10% hospital-segment share. Peak sales ~$300–700M.
registrational_endpoint: Clinical stability (temp <38°C, RR <24, HR <100, SBP ≥90, SpO2 ≥90%) at day 5; clinical cure at day 10.
btd_threshold_vs_soc: Non-inferiority vs. standard antibiotic (≤10% cure rate margin); ≥15% superiority in MDR-CAP patients.

symptoms: New lung infiltrate ≥48 hours after hospital admission; fever, purulent sputum, leukocytosis
treatment_goal: Eradicate causative bacteria; reduce ICU stay and mortality
first_line_soc: Empiric broad-spectrum antibiotics covering MDR organisms: piperacillin-tazobactam or carbapenems ± vancomycin/linezolid for MRSA coverage.
treatment_algorithm: Non-severe HAP: piperacillin-tazobactam or cefepime; add MRSA coverage (vancomycin/linezolid) if risk factors. Severe/VAP: carbapenem (meropenem) + vancomycin/linezolid. De-escalate based on culture. Novel antibiotics for MDR/XDR Gram-negatives: ceftazidime-avibactam, ceftolozane-tazobactam, meropenem-vaborbactam, imipenem-cilastatin-relebactam, cefiderocol. Duration 7 days (IDSA 2016).
us_prevalence: ~500,000 cases/year; ~250,000 ventilator-associated pneumonia episodes.
patients_needing_tx: ~500,000 patients/year requiring antibiotic pharmacotherapy.
price: $3,000–$15,000/course for novel antibiotics.
peak_sales: Key unmet need for MDR organisms. Novel antibiotic: 10–15% critical care share. Peak sales ~$400–800M.
registrational_endpoint: Clinical cure at day 14; 28-day all-cause mortality; microbiologic eradication rate.
btd_threshold_vs_soc: Non-inferiority vs. meropenem (≤10% margin); superiority in carbapenem-resistant organisms (CRO) subset.

symptoms: Fever, productive cough, dyspnea in patients with HIV, transplant, or chemotherapy
treatment_goal: Broad pathogen coverage; restore immune function; prevent respiratory failure
first_line_soc: Empiric broad-spectrum antibiotics: piperacillin-tazobactam or carbapenem + vancomycin ± antifungal (voriconazole) based on risk profile.
treatment_algorithm: Empiric: piperacillin-tazobactam or imipenem/meropenem + vancomycin (MRSA coverage). Anti-PCP: TMP-SMX if PCP risk. Antifungal: voriconazole or isavuconazole for invasive aspergillosis; caspofungin for Candida. CMV pneumonitis: ganciclovir/valganciclovir. Respiratory syncytial virus: inhaled ribavirin. IVIG for hypogammaglobulinemia. G-CSF to restore neutrophil count. Adjust based on culture/BAL results.
us_prevalence: ~100,000–200,000 hospitalizations/year in immunocompromised patients in the US.
patients_needing_tx: ~150,000 patients/year requiring specialized antimicrobial therapy.
price: $10,000–$50,000/course for a novel broad-spectrum antimicrobial or immunomodulator.
peak_sales: ~$200–500M peak sales for a novel agent addressing drug-resistant pathogens in this setting.
registrational_endpoint: Clinical success (survival + radiographic improvement) at day 28; 30-day all-cause mortality.
btd_threshold_vs_soc: 28-day mortality reduction ≥20% or clinical success ≥70% in mold/resistant bacterial pneumonia vs. standard of care.

symptoms: New pulmonary infiltrate + fever/leukocytosis/purulent secretions >48h after mechanical ventilation
treatment_goal: Eradicate pathogens; wean from ventilator; minimize antibiotic resistance
first_line_soc: Empiric antibiotics based on local antibiogram: piperacillin-tazobactam or cefepime + vancomycin (for MRSA risk) ± aminoglycoside or polymyxin for MDR gram-negatives.
treatment_algorithm: Empiric: piperacillin-tazobactam or cefepime; vancomycin or linezolid for MRSA; carbapenem for extended-spectrum β-lactamase (ESBL) producers. MDR Acinetobacter/Pseudomonas: colistin or polymyxin B + carbapenem or cefiderocol. Ceftazidime-avibactam (Avycaz) for KPC-producing Klebsiella. Inhaled tobramycin or colistin as adjunct. Duration: 7–8 days for non-MDR. De-escalate based on culture. Novel: cefiderocol, imipenem-cilastatin-relebactam approved for VAP (2019–2020).
us_prevalence: ~150,000–200,000 VAP episodes/year in ICUs; ~10–15% mortality. CDC NHSN data.
patients_needing_tx: ~180,000 patients/year requiring targeted antibiotic therapy.
price: $10,000–$40,000/treatment course for novel antibiotics.
peak_sales: ~$200–500M peak sales for a novel antibiotic with activity vs. MDR gram-negatives in VAP.
registrational_endpoint: 28-day all-cause mortality; clinical cure at day 14; microbiological eradication.
btd_threshold_vs_soc: 28-day mortality ≤15% vs. ~20–25% with standard therapy; clinical cure rate ≥65%.

symptoms: Cough with/without sputum, low-grade fever, chest discomfort
treatment_goal: Symptom relief; avoid unnecessary antibiotics
first_line_soc: Supportive care (rest, fluids, analgesics) is first-line. Cough suppressants (dextromethorphan) and bronchodilators (albuterol) for significant bronchospasm. Antibiotics not indicated for viral etiology.
treatment_algorithm: Viral etiology (~90%): no antibiotics. Symptomatic: antipyretics (acetaminophen/ibuprofen); dextromethorphan or guaifenesin for cough; short-acting bronchodilator (albuterol) if wheezing. Honey (proven cough suppressant). Antibiotics only if bacterial superinfection (purulent sputum + fever >3 days). Pertussis: azithromycin.
us_prevalence: ~10 million cases/year (short-duration acute illness; incidence-based).
patients_needing_tx: ~2 million patients with acute bronchitis receiving pharmacotherapy beyond OTC care.
price: $500–$2,000/course; acute short-term treatment; OTC competition limits price.
peak_sales: Very low-price acute market. Novel drug (e.g., anti-inflammatory): 5–10% share. Peak sales ~$50–150M.
registrational_endpoint: Cough resolution rate and days-to-cough cessation vs. placebo at day 7.
btd_threshold_vs_soc: ≥2-day faster cough resolution vs. standard care; ≥30% reduction in symptom severity score.

symptoms: Cough, fever, hypoxia after aspiration; segmental consolidation on imaging
treatment_goal: Treat/prevent infection; reduce lung inflammation
first_line_soc: For aspiration pneumonia: antibiotics covering anaerobes and gram-negatives (ampicillin-sulbactam, or clindamycin + cephalosporin). For chemical pneumonitis: supportive care, no antibiotics initially.
treatment_algorithm: Pneumonitis (chemical): supportive care, oxygen, avoid antibiotics unless secondary infection develops. Pneumonia (bacterial): ampicillin-sulbactam, moxifloxacin, or clindamycin. Hospital-acquired: add antipseudomonal coverage. Proton pump inhibitors for GERD prevention. Dysphagia rehabilitation. Corticosteroids not routinely recommended.
us_prevalence: ~600,000 aspiration events requiring hospitalization/year (CDC/HCUP).
patients_needing_tx: ~400,000 patients/year needing antibiotic pharmacotherapy.
peak_sales: Managed with existing antibiotics. Novel drug unlikely major market. Peak sales ~$50–150M.
registrational_endpoint: Clinical cure rate at day 7; 30-day pneumonia-related hospitalization rate.
btd_threshold_vs_soc: ≥15% absolute improvement in clinical cure rate vs. ampicillin-sulbactam.

symptoms: Subacute dyspnea, dry cough, fever, hypoxia in immunocompromised patients
treatment_goal: Eradicate Pneumocystis; restore oxygenation; prevent recurrence
first_line_soc: Trimethoprim-sulfamethoxazole (TMP-SMX) 15–20 mg/kg/day (trimethoprim component) for 21 days is first-line treatment.
treatment_algorithm: Treatment: TMP-SMX (Bactrim) 15–20 mg/kg/day × 21 days. Adjunct corticosteroids (prednisone 40 mg BID × 5 days → taper) for PaO2 <70 mmHg — improves mortality. Alternatives: pentamidine IV, atovaquone, clindamycin + primaquine, dapsone + trimethoprim. Prophylaxis: TMP-SMX 1 DS tablet 3×/week; alternatives: atovaquone, dapsone, aerosolized pentamidine. Treat underlying immunodeficiency (ART for HIV).
us_prevalence: ~10,000–15,000 cases/year in immunocompromised patients (HIV, organ transplant, cancer chemotherapy). CDC surveillance.
patients_needing_tx: ~12,000 patients/year requiring treatment; ~100,000+ on chronic prophylaxis.
price: $5,000–$20,000/treatment course for a novel agent; prophylaxis ~$1,000–3,000/year.
peak_sales: Generic TMP-SMX dominates; novel agent for TMP-SMX intolerant or resistant: ~$50–100M peak sales.
registrational_endpoint: Clinical success (survival + treatment completion) at day 21; PaO2 improvement at day 8.
btd_threshold_vs_soc: Clinical success rate ≥85% in TMP-SMX-refractory patients; non-inferiority margin ≤10% vs. TMP-SMX.

symptoms: Cough with putrid sputum, fever, weight loss, cavitary lung lesion
treatment_goal: Eradicate infection; drain abscess; restore lung parenchyma
first_line_soc: Prolonged antibiotics (6–8 weeks): amoxicillin-clavulanate or clindamycin + metronidazole for anaerobic coverage.
treatment_algorithm: Antibiotics: amoxicillin-clavulanate first choice; clindamycin + metronidazole alternative; penicillin-allergic: moxifloxacin or carbapenem. Duration 6–8 weeks. Chest physiotherapy for drainage. Bronchoscopic drainage for accessible lesions. Surgical resection for chronic/non-responding abscess.
us_prevalence: ~2,000–5,000 hospitalizations/year.
patients_needing_tx: ~4,000 patients/year requiring prolonged antibiotic therapy.
peak_sales: Managed with generic antibiotics. Novel antibiotics for MDR organisms. Peak sales ~$10–20M.
registrational_endpoint: Clinical cure and radiographic resolution at day 42; fever resolution at day 7.
btd_threshold_vs_soc: Cure rate ≥85%; radiographic resolution ≥70% at 6 weeks vs. standard antibiotics.

symptoms: Persistent cough (>8 weeks); multiple potential etiologies
treatment_goal: Identify and treat underlying cause; reduce cough severity and frequency
first_line_soc: Treat underlying cause first (ICS for asthma, PPI for GERD, ACEI switch for drug-induced). Gefapixant (P2X3 antagonist) FDA-approved 2023 for refractory/unexplained chronic cough (first in class).
treatment_algorithm: Stepwise: treat UACS (nasal spray), GERD (PPI), asthma (ICS). For refractory chronic cough (RCC): gefapixant (Lyfnua 45 mg BID — FDA-approved 2023); opioids (codeine, morphine) second-line; gabapentin/pregabalin off-label; speech therapy (cough suppression). 2024: camlipixant (BAY 1817080) in Phase 3 trials; BLU-5937; oral Nav channel inhibitors.
us_prevalence: ~26 million adults with chronic cough (~10% prevalence; ATS/ACCP); refractory/unexplained ~3 million.
patients_needing_tx: ~3 million patients with RCC needing pharmacotherapy.
price: $15,000–$25,000/year; gefapixant WAC ~$19,000/yr.
peak_sales: Gefapixant: ~$500M–1.0B projected. Camlipixant/next-gen P2X3: 20–30% share. Peak sales ~$1.0–2.5B.
registrational_endpoint: Objective cough frequency (Leicester Cough Monitor); VAS cough severity ≥30% reduction at 12 weeks.
btd_threshold_vs_soc: ≥30% objective cough frequency reduction vs. gefapixant; fewer taste adverse events; improved tolerability.

symptoms: High-pitched expiratory sound; bronchospasm; associated with asthma, COPD, cardiac wheeze
treatment_goal: Relieve bronchospasm; identify and treat underlying cause
first_line_soc: Short-acting β2 agonist (SABA; albuterol/salbutamol) inhaler is first-line for acute wheeze.
treatment_algorithm: Acute wheeze: albuterol (Proventil, Ventolin) 2–4 puffs q4h PRN via MDI or nebulizer. Add ipratropium (Atrovent) for COPD-associated wheeze. Oral or IV corticosteroids for acute severe exacerbations. Identify cause: asthma → ICS-LABA; COPD → LAMA + LABA; cardiac wheeze → diuretics; anaphylaxis → epinephrine. Chronic wheeze: treat underlying condition (see Asthma, COPD).
us_prevalence: ~50 million US adults report wheezing in a given year; most are managed under asthma or COPD diagnoses.
patients_needing_tx: ~30 million patients using bronchodilators for recurrent wheeze.
price: $500–$3,000/year for inhaled SABA (generics); novel biologic for refractory wheeze $20,000–$50,000/year.
tam_gross: 30M × $1,500 avg SABA cost = $45B (SABA market inclusive); novel wheeze-specific biologic: ~$2–5B TAM
peak_sales: Symptom addressed by asthma/COPD drugs. Novel fast-acting bronchodilator: ~$200–500M peak sales.
registrational_endpoint: Wheeze severity score; bronchodilator reversibility (FEV1 change); rescue albuterol use/week.
btd_threshold_vs_soc: ≥15% FEV1 improvement vs. albuterol; or ≥50% reduction in rescue SABA use/week at 12 weeks.

symptoms: Subjective breathlessness; distressing sensation of difficulty breathing
treatment_goal: Reduce dyspnea symptom burden; improve exercise tolerance and quality of life
first_line_soc: Treat underlying cause (COPD, HF, asthma). Low-dose oral/parenteral opioids (morphine, hydromorphone) most evidence-based for refractory dyspnea in palliative/advanced disease.
treatment_algorithm: Etiology-directed: bronchodilators (COPD), diuretics (HF), ICS (asthma). For refractory dyspnea: low-dose sustained-release morphine 10–30 mg/day — best evidence for palliative dyspnea (Cochrane 2016); anxiolytics (benzodiazepines) adjunctive; supplemental oxygen for hypoxemic only; non-pharmacologic: fan, breathing training, pulmonary rehab.
us_prevalence: ~25 million US adults report dyspnea; refractory/unexplained dyspnea ~3–4 million.
patients_needing_tx: ~2 million patients needing pharmacotherapy for refractory/palliative dyspnea.
peak_sales: Novel non-opioid dyspnea drug: 10–15% share. Peak sales ~$500M–1.0B.
registrational_endpoint: Modified Borg dyspnea scale ≥2-unit improvement; MMRC dyspnea scale; 6-MWT distance at 8 weeks.
btd_threshold_vs_soc: ≥2-unit Borg scale reduction vs. placebo; non-inferior to low-dose morphine with better safety profile.

symptoms: Dizziness, tingling, chest pain from over-breathing; anxiety-driven
treatment_goal: Normalize breathing pattern; treat underlying anxiety
first_line_soc: Breathing retraining (physiotherapy/CBT-based breathing exercises) is first-line. Treat comorbid anxiety with SSRIs if present.
treatment_algorithm: Breathing retraining: diaphragmatic breathing, CO2 rebreathing exercises. CBT for anxiety. Pharmacologic (for comorbid anxiety): SSRIs/SNRIs; benzodiazepines short-term for acute episodes. No specific drug for hyperventilation itself.
us_prevalence: ~10% of patients with breathing complaints; ~1–2 million clinically significant cases.
patients_needing_tx: ~500,000 patients receiving pharmacotherapy for comorbid anxiety.
peak_sales: Managed within anxiety disorder market. Dedicated drug minimal. Peak sales ~$50–100M.
registrational_endpoint: Nijmegen Questionnaire score ≥10-point reduction; end-tidal CO2 normalization at 8 weeks.
btd_threshold_vs_soc: ≥40% Nijmegen score reduction vs. breathing retraining alone.

symptoms: High-pitched inspiratory noise from upper airway obstruction; may signal life-threatening cause
treatment_goal: Identify and relieve obstruction; ensure airway patency
first_line_soc: Emergent: nebulized racemic epinephrine + systemic corticosteroids (dexamethasone) for croup/post-extubation stridor. Treat underlying cause.
treatment_algorithm: Acute croup: dexamethasone 0.6 mg/kg PO/IM single dose; nebulized racemic epinephrine 0.5 mL in 2.5 mL NS. Anaphylaxis: epinephrine 0.3 mg IM. Epiglottitis: IV antibiotics (ceftriaxone) + airway management. Angioedema-induced stridor: icatibant (Firazyr) for hereditary angioedema; epinephrine for allergic. Post-extubation: helium-oxygen (heliox) mixture. Vocal cord dysfunction: speech therapy, anxiolytics. Subglottic stenosis: dilation, surgery, or corticosteroids.
us_prevalence: ~500,000 cases of stridor requiring medical attention/year across all etiologies.
patients_needing_tx: ~400,000 patients/year requiring acute pharmacologic treatment.
price: $500–$10,000/acute episode.
peak_sales: Highly fragmented; no single drug. Specific indications (hereditary angioedema): kallikrein/bradykinin inhibitors ~$300–500M market.
registrational_endpoint: Time to stridor resolution; stridor severity score at 2 hours; intubation rate.
btd_threshold_vs_soc: ≥50% faster stridor resolution; intubation rate ≤2% vs. ~5–10% historical for severe croup.

symptoms: Coughing up blood; ranges from blood-streaked sputum to massive hemorrhage
treatment_goal: Stop bleeding; identify and treat cause; prevent recurrence
first_line_soc: Bronchoscopy (diagnostic and therapeutic) for massive hemoptysis. Treat underlying cause (TB: rifampin-based; CF: inhaled tobramycin; bronchiectasis: antibiotics). Tranexamic acid (antifibrinolytic) for non-massive hemoptysis.
treatment_algorithm: Massive hemoptysis: airway protection, bronchoscopy, bronchial artery embolization. Treat etiology: antibiotics for infection; antifungals for Aspergillus; anti-TB drugs; anti-fibrotics. Tranexamic acid (PO/IV/nebulized) for non-massive hemoptysis. Vasopressin IV historically. No specific approved drug for hemoptysis per se.
us_prevalence: ~100,000–200,000 episodes of clinically significant hemoptysis/year.
patients_needing_tx: ~150,000 patients needing pharmacotherapy.
peak_sales: Managed symptomatically. Novel hemostatic agent: 5–10% uptake. Peak sales ~$30–70M.
registrational_endpoint: Time to cessation of hemoptysis; reduction in blood volume (quantified); recurrence rate at 30 days.
btd_threshold_vs_soc: ≥40% faster hemoptysis cessation vs. tranexamic acid; lower recurrence rate at 30 days.

symptoms: Progressive dyspnea on exertion, fatigue, syncope, right heart failure
treatment_goal: Lower pulmonary vascular resistance; improve exercise capacity; prevent RV failure
first_line_soc: For PAH (Group 1): combination oral therapy — endothelin receptor antagonist (ambrisentan or macitentan) + PDE5 inhibitor (tadalafil or sildenafil). AMBITION trial established this as standard.
treatment_algorithm: Group 1 PAH: combination ERA + PDE5i; escalate to triple therapy with IP receptor agonist (selexipag/Uptravi) or prostacyclin (treprostinil subcutaneous/inhaled, IV epoprostenol). sGC stimulator riociguat (Adempas). Sotatercept (Winrevair, FDA approved 2024) — ActRIIA fusion protein — major 2024 breakthrough; added to combination regimens. Group 2 (LV disease): treat HF. Group 3 (lung disease): treat underlying lung disease. Group 4 (CTEPH): riociguat + pulmonary endarterectomy. Group 5: treat underlying cause.
us_prevalence: ~500,000 US patients with all-cause PH; ~100,000 with PAH (Group 1). PHA registry data 2024.
patients_needing_tx: ~80,000–100,000 PAH patients requiring advanced drug therapy.
peak_sales: PAH is highly competitive. Novel agent (sotatercept-class): ~$2–4B peak sales at 20–30% market share.
registrational_endpoint: 6-minute walk distance (6MWT) at 24 weeks; composite clinical worsening event-free survival; PVR reduction.
btd_threshold_vs_soc: ≥30 m improvement in 6MWT vs. background therapy; or ≥20% PVR reduction; or significant event-free survival improvement.

symptoms: Acute dyspnea, pleuritic chest pain, hemoptysis, tachycardia, hypoxia
treatment_goal: Anticoagulate; prevent clot extension; reduce PE-related mortality
first_line_soc: Anticoagulation: DOACs (rivaroxaban or apixaban) are first-line for most patients; unfractionated or LMWH heparin for high-risk/massive PE followed by DOAC.
treatment_algorithm: Anticoagulation: apixaban (Eliquis) 10 mg BID × 7 days then 5 mg BID; rivaroxaban (Xarelto) 15 mg BID × 21 days then 20 mg QD. Alternative: LMWH → warfarin. Massive/submassive PE: systemic thrombolysis (alteplase tPA 100 mg over 2 hours); catheter-directed thrombolysis for high risk. Surgical embolectomy. Inferior vena cava (IVC) filter in anticoagulation contraindication. Extended anticoagulation for unprovoked PE. Tenecteplase single-dose thrombolysis. Betrixaban, edoxaban alternatives.
us_prevalence: ~600,000–900,000 PE episodes/year; ~100,000–180,000 deaths/year. CDC VTE data 2024.
patients_needing_tx: ~700,000 patients/year requiring anticoagulation; ~50,000–80,000 with high-risk PE requiring thrombolysis or catheter intervention.
price: $8,000–$20,000/year for chronic anticoagulation (DOACs); $50,000–$100,000 for novel single-dose thrombolytic.
peak_sales: DOAC market heavily generic/branded. Novel once-monthly or factor XI inhibitor for PE: ~$1–2B peak sales.
registrational_endpoint: Composite of PE recurrence, major bleeding, and all-cause death at 6 months; 30-day mortality for massive PE.
btd_threshold_vs_soc: Composite endpoint rate ≤5% vs. ~8–10% for DOAC SoC; or bleeding reduction ≥30% with non-inferior efficacy.

symptoms: Hypoxemia due to intrapulmonary vascular dilation in liver disease; platypnea-orthodeoxia
treatment_goal: Improve oxygenation; definitive cure with liver transplant
first_line_soc: No effective pharmacotherapy proven. Liver transplant is the only definitive treatment. Supplemental oxygen for hypoxemia.
treatment_algorithm: Liver transplant — only cure. Supplemental O2. No pharmacotherapy proven effective: methylene blue, garlic extract, pentoxifylline studied but no consistent evidence. Pilot data for sorafenib. Norfloxacin (antibiotic) — small pilot showing reduction in HPS. Somatostatin analogues (octreotide) investigational.
us_prevalence: ~5–12% of cirrhotic patients; ~100,000–200,000 US patients.
patients_needing_tx: ~50,000 patients with moderate-severe HPS needing pharmacotherapy.
peak_sales: No proven drug; high unmet need. Novel hepatopulmonary vasodilator: 20–30% uptake. Peak sales ~$150–300M.
registrational_endpoint: PaO2 improvement ≥10 mmHg; alveolar-arterial gradient reduction at 24 weeks.
btd_threshold_vs_soc: ≥15 mmHg PaO2 improvement; significant A-a gradient reduction vs. oxygen alone.

symptoms: Alveolar hemorrhage plus rapidly progressive glomerulonephritis; hemoptysis, hematuria, dyspnea
treatment_goal: Suppress autoimmune process; preserve renal and pulmonary function; prevent death
first_line_soc: High-dose IV methylprednisolone + cyclophosphamide (or rituximab for ANCA-associated) + plasmapheresis for anti-GBM disease.
treatment_algorithm: Anti-GBM (Goodpasture): plasmapheresis daily × 14 days; high-dose IV methylprednisolone; cyclophosphamide. ANCA-associated PRS: rituximab + methylprednisolone (RAVE trial); cyclophosphamide alternative. Maintenance: rituximab or azathioprine. Avacopan (Tavneos, approved 2021) for ANCA-AAV replaces high-dose steroids. Plasmapheresis for refractory cases. Dialysis/transplant for renal failure.
us_prevalence: ~3,000–5,000 cases/year; ANCA-associated vasculitis most common cause (~70%), anti-GBM ~10–20%.
patients_needing_tx: ~3,500 patients/year requiring aggressive immunosuppression.
price: $80,000–$200,000/year for a biologic or complement inhibitor.
peak_sales: ~$80–150M peak sales; overlapping with ANCA vasculitis market.
registrational_endpoint: eGFR preservation at 6 months; dialysis-free survival; alveolar hemorrhage resolution by day 14.
btd_threshold_vs_soc: Dialysis-free rate ≥50% at 6 months vs. ~30–40% historical; eGFR improvement ≥15 mL/min vs. standard therapy.

symptoms: Hemoptysis, anemia, diffuse pulmonary infiltrates, respiratory failure
treatment_goal: Stop bleeding; treat underlying cause; preserve lung function
first_line_soc: High-dose IV corticosteroids (methylprednisolone 1 g/day × 3 days) plus immunosuppression for immune-mediated causes. Plasma exchange for anti-GBM or ANCA vasculitis.
treatment_algorithm: Identify etiology: ANCA vasculitis, anti-GBM, SLE, drug-induced. High-dose IV methylprednisolone. Cyclophosphamide or rituximab for AAV. Plasma exchange for anti-GBM/ANCA with rapidly progressive GN. Aminocaproic acid for non-inflammatory causes. Mechanical ventilation support.
us_prevalence: ~3,000–5,000 hospitalizations/year (incidence-based).
patients_needing_tx: ~4,000 patients/year requiring active pharmacotherapy.
peak_sales: Rare; managed within AAV/anti-GBM treatment paradigm. Peak sales ~$30–60M.
registrational_endpoint: Time to cessation of active bleeding; 30-day mortality; oxygenation (PaO2/FiO2) at day 7.
btd_threshold_vs_soc: ≥30% absolute improvement in 30-day survival vs. standard immunosuppressive therapy.

symptoms: Progressive dyspnea, fatigue, right heart failure in cirrhosis/portal hypertension patients
treatment_goal: Lower pulmonary artery pressure; enable liver transplant candidacy
first_line_soc: Pulmonary arterial hypertension (PAH)-specific therapy: endothelin receptor antagonists (macitentan, ambrisentan), PDE5 inhibitors (sildenafil, tadalafil), or prostacyclin analogs (IV epoprostenol).
treatment_algorithm: Same agents as PAH: endothelin receptor antagonists (macitentan/Opsumit, ambrisentan/Letairis), PDE5 inhibitors (sildenafil/Revatio, tadalafil/Adcirca), sGC stimulators (riociguat/Adempas), IP receptor agonists (selexipag/Uptravi), IV epoprostenol (Flolan) for severe cases. Goal: reduce mPAP <35 mmHg to qualify for liver transplant. Beta-blockers contraindicated. Liver transplant can cure in select patients.
us_prevalence: ~1,000–2,000 US patients; ~2–6% of portal hypertension patients. Rare condition.
patients_needing_tx: ~1,500 patients on active PAH pharmacotherapy; most require combination therapy.
price: $80,000–$200,000/year; comparable to other PAH drugs.
peak_sales: ~$30–60M peak sales for a novel agent specifically addressing portopulmonary PH.
registrational_endpoint: Change in 6MWT at 24 weeks; PVR reduction by right heart catheterization; mPAP <35 mmHg response rate.
btd_threshold_vs_soc: ≥30 m improvement in 6MWT or ≥20% PVR reduction vs. placebo at 24 weeks.

symptoms: Acute dyspnea, hypoxia, chest pain following fat/air/tumor/amniotic fluid emboli
treatment_goal: Supportive care; remove or neutralize embolic material; prevent recurrence
first_line_soc: Supportive: supplemental oxygen, vasopressors for hemodynamic instability. Fat embolism: corticosteroids (controversial). Air embolism: Trendelenburg positioning, 100% O2, needle aspiration. No FDA-approved specific pharmacologic therapy.
treatment_algorithm: Depends on embolism type. Fat embolism syndrome: supportive ICU care, high-flow O2; corticosteroids for prevention in at-risk fracture patients (methylprednisolone 500 mg IV). Air embolism: hyperbaric oxygen. Tumor microembolism: treat primary malignancy. Amniotic fluid embolism: cardiopulmonary resuscitation, ECMO. Septic embolism: broad-spectrum antibiotics, treat endocarditis source. No approved specific pharmacotherapy.
us_prevalence: ~5,000–15,000 cases/year across all subtypes; fat embolism most common (~1–3% of long bone fractures).
patients_needing_tx: ~10,000 patients/year requiring acute pharmacologic support.
price: $5,000–$30,000/acute course.
peak_sales: Acute, episodic condition. Novel agent peak sales ~$30–60M.
registrational_endpoint: 28-day all-cause mortality; time to hemodynamic stabilization; PaO2/FiO2 ratio at 72 hours.
btd_threshold_vs_soc: Mortality reduction ≥30% or PaO2/FiO2 normalization ≥50% faster vs. supportive care alone.

symptoms: Rapidly progressive kidney failure, pulmonary hemorrhage, hemoptysis
treatment_goal: Stop autoimmune attack; preserve kidney function and lung
first_line_soc: Plasma exchange (plasmapheresis) + cyclophosphamide + high-dose corticosteroids is the standard regimen.
treatment_algorithm: Plasma exchange (daily × 2 weeks): removes anti-GBM antibodies. Oral/IV cyclophosphamide (immunosuppression). IV methylprednisolone then oral prednisolone taper. Avacopan (C5a receptor antagonist) shows adjunctive benefit in some AAV studies; explored in anti-GBM. Dialysis if oliguric. Early treatment crucial for renal recovery.
us_prevalence: Very rare; ~600 new cases/year (~1–2 per million).
patients_needing_tx: ~600 patients/year needing active immunosuppressive therapy.
price: $80,000–$200,000/year for a novel biologic or complement inhibitor.
peak_sales: Very rare; orphan disease opportunity. 30–40% uptake of novel targeted agent. Peak sales ~$30–50M.
registrational_endpoint: Anti-GBM antibody titer reduction; eGFR preservation at 6 months; dialysis-free survival.
btd_threshold_vs_soc: ≥50% faster anti-GBM antibody clearance; ≥30% absolute improvement in dialysis-free survival.

symptoms: Loud snoring, witnessed apneas, daytime sleepiness, morning headaches
treatment_goal: Eliminate apneas; improve sleep quality; reduce cardiovascular risk
first_line_soc: CPAP (continuous positive airway pressure) is first-line device therapy. For pharmacotherapy: tirzepatide/GLP-1 agonists showed significant AHI reduction (2024 SURMOUNT-OSA trial); solriamfetol (Sunosi) and armodafinil for residual daytime sleepiness.
treatment_algorithm: CPAP: most effective for AHI reduction. Oral appliances (mandibular advancement devices) for mild-moderate OSA. Weight loss (GLP-1 agonists: semaglutide, tirzepatide — FDA approval for OSA submitted 2024 based on SURMOUNT-OSA). Hypoglossal nerve stimulator (Inspire) for CPAP-intolerant. Positional therapy. Pharmacotherapy for residual EDS: solriamfetol (Sunosi), armodafinil (Nuvigil). Tirzepatide is the first drug with strong OSA AHI-reduction data.
us_prevalence: ~30–40 million US adults with OSA; ~15 million diagnosed and treated. AASM 2025 estimates.
patients_needing_tx: ~10–15 million patients seeking pharmacologic adjuncts; 5–8 million with obesity-driven OSA eligible for GLP-1 therapy.
price: $15,000–$25,000/year for GLP-1 class; ~$3,000–5,000/year for wakefulness agents.
tam_gross: 12M patients × $18,000 average = $216B TAM (GLP-1 dominant); pharmacologic OSA-specific: ~$8B TAM
peak_sales: Tirzepatide OSA indication could contribute ~$2–4B to peak sales within broader obesity franchise. Pure OSA drug: ~$500M–1B.
registrational_endpoint: Change in AHI (apnea-hypopnea index) from baseline at 52 weeks; Epworth Sleepiness Scale score.
btd_threshold_vs_soc: AHI reduction ≥50% from baseline; or AHI <15 events/hour at 52 weeks vs. placebo.

symptoms: Breathing pauses during sleep without airway obstruction; fatigue, insomnia
treatment_goal: Normalize breathing pattern; improve sleep quality and daytime function
first_line_soc: Adaptive servo-ventilation (ASV) or positive airway pressure therapy for most forms. Acetazolamide (carbonic anhydrase inhibitor) used for high-altitude CSA and some idiopathic forms.
treatment_algorithm: Treat underlying cause (HF, opioid use, altitude). Positive pressure therapy: BiPAP, ASV (for HF-related Cheyne-Stokes). Acetazolamide for high-altitude and idiopathic CSA. Supplemental oxygen for HF-related. Reduce/discontinue opioids if opioid-induced. 2024: solriamfetol (Sunosi) studied for residual sleepiness.
us_prevalence: ~5–7% of sleep apnea population is CSA; ~0.9% general population; ~2–3 million US cases.
patients_needing_tx: ~500,000 patients with CSA receiving pharmacotherapy.
peak_sales: Mostly device-managed. Novel drug: 5–10% pharmacotherapy share. Peak sales ~$150–350M.
registrational_endpoint: AHI (apnea-hypopnea index) ≥50% reduction; central apnea index reduction at 3 months.
btd_threshold_vs_soc: ≥50% AHI reduction; AHI <5/hour in ≥30% of patients vs. acetazolamide comparator.

symptoms: Snoring, mouth breathing, bedwetting, behavioral problems, poor growth
treatment_goal: Normalize breathing; improve neurobehavioral outcomes; promote growth
first_line_soc: Adenotonsillectomy (surgical) is first-line. Montelukast (leukotriene receptor antagonist) + intranasal corticosteroids for mild pediatric OSA as pharmacologic option.
treatment_algorithm: Adenotonsillectomy: first-line for most children (60–80% cure rate). Intranasal fluticasone or budesonide: reduces lymphoid tissue, improves AHI. Montelukast (Singulair): leukotriene receptor antagonist reduces adenoid tissue; used for mild OSA pre- or post-surgery. CPAP for non-surgical candidates. Orthodontic expansion for craniofacial OSA. Weight management for obese children.
us_prevalence: ~2–3% of children (1.5–2 million US children) have OSA; peak at ages 2–8.
patients_needing_tx: ~500,000–800,000 children requiring pharmacologic intervention (intranasal steroids, montelukast) as adjunct or primary therapy.
price: $2,000–$8,000/year for pediatric-approved pharmacotherapy.
peak_sales: ~10–15% market share with novel drug; ~$300–450M peak sales.
registrational_endpoint: Change in AHI at 12 weeks; Pediatric Sleep Questionnaire (PSQ) score; oxygen desaturation index.
