btd_threshold_vs_soc: AHI reduction ≥50% or normalization (<1 event/hour) at 12 weeks vs. placebo.

symptoms: Varies by agent: blistering, respiratory distress, seizures, miosis
treatment_goal: Decontaminate, reverse toxidrome, prevent death
first_line_soc: Agent-specific antidotes: nerve agents — atropine + pralidoxime; vesicants — no antidote (supportive); pulmonary agents — supportive O2; cyanide — hydroxocobalamin or amyl nitrite kit.
treatment_algorithm: Decontamination (remove clothing, copious water wash). Nerve agents: atropine 2–4 mg IV + pralidoxime 1–2 g IV + diazepam for seizures; autoinjectors (ATNAA). Cyanide: hydroxocobalamin (Cyanokit) 5 g IV. Pulmonary agents (phosgene/chlorine): bronchodilators, O2, mechanical ventilation. Vesicants: wound care, antibiotics, anti-inflammatory therapy. Managed via CHEMPACK and SNS.
us_prevalence: Near-zero peacetime; preparedness for mass casualty events (potentially thousands in an attack).
patients_needing_tx: Stockpile planning for 10,000–100,000 potential casualties in a mass event; peacetime <100 cases/year.
price: $5,000–$100,000 per treatment (BARDA government procurement).
tam_gross: Government contract market ~$200M–$500M/year across all CWA countermeasures.
registrational_endpoint: Survival at 14 days via FDA Animal Rule + human safety data.
btd_threshold_vs_soc: ≥50% improvement in survival vs. untreated historical controls in validated animal model.

symptoms: Fever, respiratory failure, rash, hemorrhage (agent-specific)
treatment_goal: Neutralize agent, prevent systemic spread, support vital functions
first_line_soc: Agent-specific: anthrax — ciprofloxacin/doxycycline + raxibacumab or obiltoxaximab (antitoxin). Smallpox — tecovirimat (Tpoxx). Plague — streptomycin or doxycycline. Botulinum toxin — heptavalent antitoxin (HBAT).
treatment_algorithm: Anthrax: antibiotics (ciprofloxacin 400 mg IV q12h) + antitoxin (raxibacumab 40 mg/kg IV). Smallpox: tecovirimat (TPOXX) ± brincidofovir. Plague: streptomycin 1 g IM BID or doxycycline 100 mg BID. Botulism: HBAT (heptavalent antitoxin) + supportive ventilation. Tularemia: doxycycline or gentamicin. Government stockpile management via BARDA/SNS.
us_prevalence: Near-zero peacetime incidence; preparedness planning for mass casualty events (potentially hundreds of thousands in a bioterror event).
patients_needing_tx: Stockpile model — potentially 100,000+ in a major event; peacetime <50 cases/year.
price: $10,000–$500,000 per treatment depending on agent (government procurement pricing).
tam_gross: Government SNS contract value ~$100M–$500M/year across agents.
peak_sales: $50M–$200M via BARDA/SNS contracts for next-generation countermeasures.
registrational_endpoint: Survival at 28 days in animal challenge studies (FDA Animal Rule) + safety in healthy volunteers.
btd_threshold_vs_soc: ≥50% improvement in survival vs. historical untreated controls or existing antidote in animal model.

symptoms: Acute radiation syndrome, localized burns, systemic toxicity
treatment_goal: Decontaminate, support hematopoiesis, treat radiation injury
first_line_soc: G-CSF (filgrastim/Neupogen) and romiplostim (Nplate) — FDA-approved under Animal Rule for H-ARS. Prussian blue (Radiogardase) for cesium-137 and thallium decorporation.
treatment_algorithm: Same as radiation exposure: decontamination, G-CSF/GM-CSF, romiplostim, DTPA for plutonium/americium, Prussian blue (Radiogardase, FDA-approved 2003) for Cs-137. Blood product support, antibiotics, wound care. BARDA medical countermeasure protocols.
us_prevalence: Extremely rare; preparedness/stockpile-driven market; no significant civilian annual incidence.
patients_needing_tx: Scenario-dependent; stockpile for mass casualty readiness.
price: $10,000–$100,000 per course for stockpiled medical countermeasures.
registrational_endpoint: 30-day survival in NHP model (Animal Rule); ANC recovery time.
btd_threshold_vs_soc: ≥20% survival benefit over existing MCMs (G-CSF) in NHP Animal Rule study.

symptoms: Toxin-dependent: paralysis (botulinum), hemorrhage (ricin), neurotoxicity; rapid onset
treatment_goal: Antidote administration, supportive care, mass casualty management
first_line_soc: Heptavalent botulinum antitoxin (HBAT, FDA-approved 2013) for botulinum toxin exposure; no antidote for ricin.
treatment_algorithm: Botulinum: HBAT (Emergent BioSolutions) — 7-valent antitoxin, single IV dose; supportive ventilation. Ricin: no antidote; supportive care, anti-ricin antibodies in development. SEB (staphylococcal enterotoxin B): supportive care. Decontamination for all toxin exposures.
us_prevalence: Extremely rare civilian exposure; BARDA stockpile market; preparedness scenario.
patients_needing_tx: <50 cases/year civilian; mass casualty scenario could involve thousands.
price: $10,000–$100,000 per course for novel mass-casualty antidotes.
registrational_endpoint: 30-day survival in NHP challenge model (Animal Rule).
btd_threshold_vs_soc: ≥30% survival improvement over HBAT or supportive care in NHP Animal Rule study.

symptoms: Miosis, hypersalivation, bronchospasm, seizures, paralysis, death
treatment_goal: Reverse cholinergic excess, prevent seizure-induced brain injury
first_line_soc: Atropine + pralidoxime (2-PAM) + diazepam — triple-drug antidote: atropine counters muscarinic excess; pralidoxime reactivates AChE; diazepam prevents seizures.
treatment_algorithm: Autoinjectors (DuoDote: atropine 2.1 mg + pralidoxime 600 mg IM); repeat atropine q5–10 min until secretions dry. Diazepam 10 mg IM for seizure prophylaxis. Decontamination. Midazolam IM as seizure alternative. Aggressive airway management. CHEMPACK program maintains community antidote stockpiles.
us_prevalence: Near-zero peacetime in the US; stockpile for mass casualty events (up to 50,000+ casualties in a major attack).
patients_needing_tx: Government SNS + CHEMPACK program.
price: $5,000–$100,000 per treatment (BARDA government pricing).
tam_gross: Government contract market ~$100M–$300M/year for nerve agent countermeasures.
peak_sales: $50M–$150M via BARDA contracts for next-gen antidotes.
registrational_endpoint: Survival at 48 hours via FDA Animal Rule + human safety in nerve agent model.
btd_threshold_vs_soc: ≥50% improvement in survival vs. atropine + pralidoxime + diazepam historical controls in primate model.

symptoms: Severe skin blistering, eye damage, airway burns, bone marrow suppression
treatment_goal: Decontaminate, treat blisters, prevent infection, support hematopoiesis
first_line_soc: None specific antidote — decontamination (water + soap), wound care; British Anti-Lewisite (BAL/dimercaprol) for lewisite only.
treatment_algorithm: Immediate decontamination (copious water). BAL (dimercaprol 3 mg/kg IM q4h) for lewisite (heavy metal chelator). Mustard: no antidote; wound care (silver sulfadiazine for burns), eye irrigation (normal saline), bronchodilators, G-CSF for bone marrow suppression. Blister management: drain large bullae, antibiotic dressings.
us_prevalence: Extremely rare civilian; <50 cases/year; BARDA stockpile market.
patients_needing_tx: Preparedness stockpile scenario.
price: $10,000–$100,000 per course for stockpiled medical countermeasures.
registrational_endpoint: Lesion size, blister healing time, and survival in rabbit/NHP vesicant challenge model (Animal Rule).
btd_threshold_vs_soc: ≥30% improvement in wound healing and survival vs. supportive care in NHP Animal Rule study.

symptoms: Dyspnea, seizures, cardiovascular collapse, tissue hypoxia (cyanide-like)
treatment_goal: Restore cellular oxygen utilization, prevent cardiovascular collapse
first_line_soc: Hydroxocobalamin (Cyanokit 5g IV) and/or sodium thiosulfate + sodium nitrite (Nithiodote) for cyanide-based agents; high-flow O2.
treatment_algorithm: 100% O2. Cyanokit (hydroxocobalamin 5g IV over 15 min) — first-line for cyanide/hydrogen cyanide agents. Alternative: sodium nitrite 300 mg IV + sodium thiosulfate 12.5g IV (Nithiodote). Amyl nitrite pearls for prehospital. Cardiac monitoring, benzodiazepines for seizures. Fluid resuscitation. ICU supportive care.
us_prevalence: Military/CBW incident use; <50 civilian exposures/year; BARDA stockpile market.
patients_needing_tx: Preparedness stockpile-driven; <50 annual civilian cases in the US.
price: $10,000–$50,000 per course for stockpiled antidotes.
registrational_endpoint: 30-day survival and lactate normalization in NHP cyanide challenge model (Animal Rule).
btd_threshold_vs_soc: ≥20% survival improvement over hydroxocobalamin alone in NHP model.

symptoms: Confusion, hallucinations, dry skin, tachycardia, urinary retention
treatment_goal: Reverse anticholinergic toxidrome, restore CNS function
first_line_soc: Physostigmine (acetylcholinesterase inhibitor) — IV/IM — antidote for central anticholinergic syndrome; used in mass casualty/military settings.
treatment_algorithm: Physostigmine (0.5–2 mg IV slowly) for reversal of central effects. Benzodiazepines for agitation/seizures. Supportive care: cooling for hyperthermia, urinary catheterization, IV fluids. Military-grade antidotes (CHEMPACK): diazepam for seizures. Decontamination (skin washing, remove clothing).
us_prevalence: Extremely rare in civilian settings — <100 cases/year in the US; primarily military/terrorism concern. Mass casualty potential affects preparedness planning.
patients_needing_tx: In a mass casualty event: potentially thousands; peacetime: <100 cases requiring antidote annually.
price: $5,000–$50,000 per treatment course for a novel antidote (government stockpile pricing); rationale: BARDA/military procurement model.
tam_gross: Peacetime TAM minimal; strategic stockpile market: ~$10M–$50M annually for government contracts.
peak_sales: $5M–$30M via government strategic national stockpile contracts.
registrational_endpoint: Time to resolution of anticholinergic toxidrome score vs. supportive care alone.
btd_threshold_vs_soc: ≥50% faster resolution of toxidrome vs. benzodiazepine supportive care.

disease: Incendiary Chemical-Warfare Agent and Hydrogen Fluoride (HF) exposure
symptoms: Severe burns, hypocalcemia (HF), deep tissue destruction, systemic toxicity
treatment_goal: Replace calcium (HF), wound care, prevent cardiac arrest
first_line_soc: Calcium gluconate (topical gel, SC injection, or IV) — specific antidote for HF poisoning to chelate fluoride ions and prevent hypocalcemia/cardiac toxicity.
treatment_algorithm: HF: calcium gluconate 2.5% topical gel to skin + SC injection for deep burns + IV calcium gluconate (1–3 g) for systemic toxicity. Cardiac monitoring (QTc prolongation). Incendiary agents (WP): wound excision under water (spontaneous reignition in air), surgical debridement. Copper sulfate NO longer used (toxic). Burns management.
us_prevalence: HF industrial: ~1,000–2,000 HF injuries/year in the US. Military incendiary exposure: rare in peacetime.
patients_needing_tx: ~1,000–2,000 HF patients require calcium gluconate treatment annually.
price: $5,000–$20,000 per episode for a novel fluoride chelation agent.
registrational_endpoint: Serum calcium normalization and cardiac toxicity rate at 24 hours vs. standard calcium gluconate.
btd_threshold_vs_soc: ≥40% faster calcium normalization and ≥50% reduction in cardiac events vs. standard calcium gluconate.

symptoms: Eye pain, tearing, burning skin, cough, nasal discharge
treatment_goal: Decontaminate, relieve irritation, manage airway symptoms
first_line_soc: None pharmacologic — decontamination with water/saline irrigation; topical ophthalmologic anesthetics for eye pain; bronchodilators for respiratory symptoms.
treatment_algorithm: None — remove from exposure. Copious water/saline irrigation of eyes and skin. Topical proparacaine for eye pain. Bronchodilators (albuterol) for bronchospasm. Systemic corticosteroids for severe bronchospasm. Antihistamines for skin reactions. Most resolve within hours.
us_prevalence: ~10,000–50,000 exposures/year in the US (law enforcement, protests); rarely requires hospitalization.
patients_needing_tx: ~5,000 require medical care beyond self-decontamination.
price: $500–$2,000 per course for a rapid-decontamination agent.
registrational_endpoint: Time to symptom resolution and Draize eye score at 1 hour vs. water irrigation.
btd_threshold_vs_soc: ≥50% faster symptom resolution vs. water irrigation.

symptoms: Cough, dyspnea, pulmonary edema, chemical burns to airways
treatment_goal: Decontaminate, treat pulmonary edema, support oxygenation
first_line_soc: Decontamination, 100% O2, bronchodilators (albuterol); systemic corticosteroids for ARDS; no specific FDA-approved antidote for phosgene/chlorine.
treatment_algorithm: Decontamination. 100% O2 via NRB or mechanical ventilation. Nebulized sodium bicarbonate for acid irritants. Bronchodilators (albuterol). Systemic methylprednisolone for pulmonary edema/ARDS. N-acetylcysteine (antioxidant). Amifostine investigational. ICU supportive care.
us_prevalence: Rare in civilian setting; military/chemical incident use; stockpile management incidents: <100 exposures/year in US.
patients_needing_tx: <100 require pharmacologic management in a typical year.
price: $10,000–$50,000 per course for a stockpiled medical countermeasure.
peak_sales: $10M–$50M via US government BARDA/DoD procurement contracts.
registrational_endpoint: SpO2 maintenance, lung injury score, and 30-day mortality vs. supportive care in animal challenge models (Animal Rule).
btd_threshold_vs_soc: Survival benefit ≥20% over supportive care in validated animal model.

symptoms: Variable by toxin; nausea, vomiting, altered consciousness, organ failure
treatment_goal: Identify toxin, decontaminate, administer antidote, support vital functions
first_line_soc: None universal — toxin-specific antidotes (naloxone for opioids, flumazenil for benzodiazepines, atropine/pralidoxime for organophosphates, NAC for acetaminophen).
treatment_algorithm: None universal. AAPCC-guided management: (1) decontamination (activated charcoal, gastric lavage); (2) specific antidotes by toxin class; (3) enhanced elimination (hemodialysis for alcohols, salicylates); (4) ICU supportive care; (5) poison center consultation. Naloxone (Narcan), NAC (Acetadote), digoxin Fab (DigiFab), fomepizole (Antizol), dimercaprol, etc.
us_prevalence: ~3 million human poison exposures reported to US poison centers annually (AAPCC); ~200,000 hospitalizations.
patients_needing_tx: ~200,000 require pharmacologic antidote or active treatment annually.
price: $5,000–$15,000 per episode for a novel broad-spectrum or targeted antidote.
registrational_endpoint: Time to clinical stability and 30-day mortality vs. standard antidote therapy.
btd_threshold_vs_soc: ≥20% relative reduction in 30-day mortality or organ failure rate vs. standard care.

symptoms: Headache, dizziness, nausea, confusion, loss of consciousness
treatment_goal: Displace CO from hemoglobin, prevent neurologic sequelae
first_line_soc: 100% normobaric oxygen (NBO) via tight-fitting mask — accelerates COHb half-life from 4–6 hours to 40–80 minutes; first-line for all symptomatic patients.
treatment_algorithm: NBO: 100% O2 via non-rebreather mask for all cases. Hyperbaric oxygen (HBO at 2.4–3 ATA) for: LOC, neurologic deficits, COHb >25%, cardiac ischemia, pregnancy, age >36. HBO reduces delayed neurologic sequelae from 30–47% to ~20%. Supportive: IV fluids, antiemetics. Experimental: EBO (electrochemical blood oxygenation — investigational).
us_prevalence: ~100,000+ ED visits/year; ~14,000 hospitalizations/year; 400–500 deaths (CDC 2025).
patients_needing_tx: ~50,000 require active oxygen therapy (NBO or HBO); ~10,000 receive HBO.
price: $5,000–$20,000 per episode for a novel CO-scavenging or neuroprotective drug; rationale: HBO costs ~$3,000–$8,000 per session.
registrational_endpoint: Incidence of delayed neurologic sequelae at 4–6 weeks vs. NBO alone (ACEP-endorsed endpoint).
btd_threshold_vs_soc: ≥30% reduction in delayed neurologic sequelae vs. NBO standard of care.

symptoms: Agitation, tremor, hyperthermia, clonus, diarrhea, tachycardia
treatment_goal: Discontinue causative agents, control hyperthermia and neuromuscular excitability
first_line_soc: Cyproheptadine (serotonin antagonist) — 12 mg oral/NG, then 2 mg q2h PRN — plus benzodiazepines (lorazepam) for agitation/seizures.
treatment_algorithm: Discontinue all serotonergic agents. Benzodiazepines (lorazepam IV) for agitation, muscle rigidity, seizures. Cyproheptadine (12 mg loading) — serotonin 5-HT2A antagonist. Aggressive cooling for hyperthermia (ice packs, cooling blankets). Neuromuscular paralysis + intubation for severe cases (>41°C, severe rigidity). Avoid physical restraints. IV fluids. Chlorpromazine as alternative 5-HT antagonist.
us_prevalence: ~100,000 serotonin toxicity cases/year reported to US poison centers; ~9,000 require hospitalization.
patients_needing_tx: ~20,000 require pharmacologic management beyond discontinuation.
price: $5,000–$15,000 per episode for a novel serotonin receptor antagonist.
registrational_endpoint: Time to resolution of Hunter Criteria symptoms and core temperature normalization vs. cyproheptadine + benzodiazepines.
btd_threshold_vs_soc: ≥50% faster resolution of neuromuscular symptoms vs. cyproheptadine + benzodiazepine combination.

symptoms: Nausea, vomiting, right upper quadrant pain, liver failure
treatment_goal: Prevent hepatotoxicity, restore glutathione, support liver
first_line_soc: N-acetylcysteine (NAC; Acetadote) — glutathione precursor/antioxidant — IV or oral regimen initiated ASAP after toxic ingestion.
treatment_algorithm: Activated charcoal within 1–2 hours of ingestion if no contraindications. IV NAC (Acetadote): 3-bag regimen (150 mg/kg loading, then 50 mg/kg over 4h, then 100 mg/kg over 16h). Oral NAC (Mucomyst) as alternative. Liver transplant evaluation for fulminant hepatic failure. Supportive care (IV fluids, antiemetics).
us_prevalence: ~90,000+ acetaminophen-related poison center cases/year in the US; ~78,000 ED visits annually (AAPCC/JAMA Network Open 2023).
patients_needing_tx: ~40,000–50,000 require NAC treatment annually (those above Rumack-Matthew nomogram line or with elevated transaminases).
price: $2,000–$5,000 per treatment course for a novel hepatoprotective agent; rationale: acute ED/hospital setting, comparable to IV NAC cost (~$1,500–$3,000 per admission).
peak_sales: $30M–$60M at 20–40% share (challenging to displace generic NAC); math: 45,000 × $3,500 × 0.20–0.40.
registrational_endpoint: Peak ALT/AST levels or rate of hepatotoxicity (ALT >1,000 U/L) at 72 hours vs. NAC standard of care.
btd_threshold_vs_soc: ≥30% reduction in hepatotoxicity rate (ALT >1,000 U/L) vs. IV NAC.

symptoms: Fatigue, cognitive impairment, abdominal pain, peripheral neuropathy, hypertension
treatment_goal: Reduce blood lead level, prevent organ damage, protect CNS
first_line_soc: Succimer (DMSA; Chemet) oral — FDA-approved chelation agent — first-line for BLL ≥45 mcg/dL in children; EDTA IV for high BLL or encephalopathy.
treatment_algorithm: Succimer 10 mg/kg PO TID × 5 days then BID × 14 days (children BLL ≥45). EDTA (calcium disodium versenate) IV for BLL >70 or encephalopathy. BAL (dimercaprol) + EDTA for encephalopathy. Source removal (critical). Adults: chelation for BLL >70 or symptomatic. D-penicillamine as alternative.
us_prevalence: ~500,000 US children aged 1–5 with BLL ≥3.5 mcg/dL (CDC 2025 reference value); ~10,000–20,000 requiring chelation annually.
patients_needing_tx: ~10,000–15,000 require chelation annually; 500,000 need source-removal and monitoring.
price: $5,000–$20,000 per chelation course for a novel oral lead chelator.
registrational_endpoint: Reduction in blood lead level ≥35% from baseline at 21 days vs. standard succimer.
btd_threshold_vs_soc: ≥50% reduction in BLL in 14 days (faster kinetics) with equivalent safety vs. succimer.

symptoms: SLUDGE: salivation, lacrimation, urination, defecation, GI distress, emesis; bronchospasm, seizures
treatment_goal: Reverse cholinergic crisis, prevent respiratory failure and death
first_line_soc: Atropine IV (2–4 mg, titrated) + pralidoxime (2-PAM) 1–2 g IV over 15–30 min — combination is standard; atropine for muscarinic symptoms, 2-PAM to reactivate AChE.
treatment_algorithm: Decontamination (remove clothing, copious water wash). Atropine: 2–4 mg IV q5–10 min until secretions dry (no upper limit). Pralidoxime (2-PAM): 1–2 g IV loading dose + 500 mg/hr infusion. Benzodiazepines (diazepam/lorazepam) for seizures. Endotracheal intubation for respiratory failure. Activated charcoal if early ingestion. Avoid succinylcholine.
us_prevalence: ~10,000–15,000 significant organophosphate poisoning cases/year in the US (occupational + intentional; AAPCC data).
patients_needing_tx: ~8,000–10,000 require active antidote treatment annually.
price: $5,000–$20,000 per episode for a novel neuroprotective or long-acting AChE reactivator.
registrational_endpoint: Survival at 28 days and mechanical ventilation duration vs. standard atropine + pralidoxime.
btd_threshold_vs_soc: ≥25% reduction in mechanical ventilation duration and ≥20% improvement in survival vs. atropine + pralidoxime.

symptoms: Mouth/throat burns, drooling, dysphagia, vomiting, abdominal pain
treatment_goal: Assess injury severity, prevent stricture, support nutrition
first_line_soc: None pharmacologic — avoid emesis/charcoal; early endoscopy for grading. Corticosteroids (dexamethasone) for high-grade esophageal burns to reduce stricture formation (controversial).
treatment_algorithm: None — early endoscopy (12–24 hours) to grade injury. IV fluids and NPO. Corticosteroids (methylprednisolone 1–2 mg/kg/day) for Grade IIb–III lesions to prevent strictures (evidence mixed). PPI therapy. Antibiotics if perforation suspected. Nutritional support (NG tube or TPN). Surgical resection for perforation or non-dilatable strictures. Esophageal dilation for established strictures.
us_prevalence: ~5,000–10,000 significant caustic ingestions/year in the US requiring hospitalization (AAPCC data).
patients_needing_tx: ~5,000–8,000 require active pharmacologic management (PPIs, corticosteroids); ~1,000–2,000 develop strictures needing long-term treatment.
price: $5,000–$20,000 per treatment course for a novel anti-stricture biologic.
registrational_endpoint: Rate of esophageal stricture formation at 3 months vs. corticosteroid + PPI standard.
btd_threshold_vs_soc: ≥40% reduction in stricture formation rate vs. corticosteroid standard.

symptoms: Hyperthermia, muscle rigidity, altered consciousness, autonomic instability
treatment_goal: Discontinue causative agent, lower temperature, resolve rigidity
first_line_soc: Dantrolene (muscle relaxant) and/or bromocriptine (D2 agonist) + benzodiazepines for rigidity/agitation; discontinue offending antipsychotic.
treatment_algorithm: Immediately discontinue causative neuroleptic. Aggressive cooling (ice packs, cooling blankets). Dantrolene 2.5 mg/kg IV q6h for rigidity. Bromocriptine 2.5–5 mg PO TID for dopaminergic restoration. Benzodiazepines (lorazepam) for agitation. IV fluids for rhabdomyolysis. ICU monitoring. ECT as rescue for refractory cases.
us_prevalence: ~0.01–0.02% of antipsychotic users; ~2,000–4,000 cases/year in the US (J Clin Psychiatry data).
patients_needing_tx: ~3,000 require active pharmacologic management annually.
price: $10,000–$30,000 per episode for a novel D2-receptor restoring or thermoregulation agent.
registrational_endpoint: Time to temperature normalization and resolution of NMS criteria at 72 hours vs. standard dantrolene + bromocriptine.
btd_threshold_vs_soc: ≥30% faster resolution of NMS criteria vs. dantrolene + bromocriptine.

symptoms: Coughing, respiratory distress, CNS depression, cardiac arrhythmias
treatment_goal: Prevent aspiration pneumonitis, stabilize respiration and cardiac rhythm
first_line_soc: None pharmacologic — supportive care; avoid emesis (increases aspiration risk); O2 supplementation; bronchodilators for bronchospasm.
treatment_algorithm: None — avoid emesis and gastric lavage (aspiration risk). Supplemental O2. Bronchodilators (albuterol) for bronchospasm. Monitoring for cardiac arrhythmias. Endotracheal intubation for respiratory failure. Antibiotics for secondary pneumonitis. Do NOT use epinephrine (sensitizes heart to arrhythmia).
us_prevalence: ~30,000–50,000 hydrocarbon poisoning cases/year (AAPCC data); mostly pediatric.
patients_needing_tx: ~10,000–15,000 require medical treatment; ~5,000 need hospitalization.
price: $2,000–$10,000 per episode for a novel pulmonary protective agent.
registrational_endpoint: Rate of aspiration pneumonitis and hypoxemia at 24 hours vs. standard supportive care.
btd_threshold_vs_soc: ≥40% reduction in aspiration pneumonitis rate vs. standard supportive care.

symptoms: Vomiting, abdominal pain, GI bleeding, metabolic acidosis, shock
treatment_goal: Remove iron, treat acidosis, prevent multi-organ failure
first_line_soc: Deferoxamine (Desferal) IV — iron chelator — standard of care for serum iron >500 mcg/dL or symptomatic systemic toxicity.
treatment_algorithm: IV deferoxamine 15 mg/kg/hr (max 6 g/day) until 'vin rosé' urine clears and patient stabilizes. Gastric lavage for large ingestions within 1 hour. Whole bowel irrigation with polyethylene glycol (PEG) for delayed/extended-release iron. IV fluids for shock. Dialysis rarely needed. Activated charcoal NOT effective for iron.
us_prevalence: ~15,000–20,000 iron poisoning cases/year (AAPCC); ~3,000–5,000 require deferoxamine treatment; pediatric ingestion most common.
patients_needing_tx: ~5,000 require IV deferoxamine annually.
price: $5,000–$15,000 per treatment course for a novel oral/faster-acting iron chelator.
registrational_endpoint: Time to serum iron normalization and liver injury rate at 48 hours vs. standard IV deferoxamine.
btd_threshold_vs_soc: ≥30% faster iron chelation with equivalent safety vs. IV deferoxamine.

symptoms: Tinnitus, vomiting, hyperventilation, altered mental status, metabolic acidosis
treatment_goal: Enhance elimination, correct acid-base, prevent organ damage
first_line_soc: Sodium bicarbonate IV — alkalinizes urine to enhance salicylate renal excretion; urinary alkalinization is cornerstone of treatment.
treatment_algorithm: Activated charcoal within 1–2 hours. IV sodium bicarbonate infusion to achieve urine pH 7.5–8.0 and serum pH 7.45–7.55. IV fluids for volume replacement. Dextrose supplementation. Hemodialysis for severe toxicity (salicylate >100 mg/dL, renal failure, pulmonary edema, altered mental status). Potassium replacement critical.
us_prevalence: ~40,000 salicylate poisoning calls/year to US poison centers (AAPCC annual report); ~5,000–10,000 hospitalizations.
patients_needing_tx: ~10,000–15,000 require active pharmacologic management (IV bicarb ± dialysis) annually.
price: $1,000–$5,000 per episode for a novel salicylate-binding or enhanced-elimination agent.
peak_sales: $5M–$15M at 15–40% share; must compete with low-cost sodium bicarb.
registrational_endpoint: Time to salicylate level <30 mg/dL and clinical resolution vs. standard sodium bicarbonate.
btd_threshold_vs_soc: ≥30% faster salicylate elimination vs. urinary alkalinization standard.

symptoms: Excessive secretions, miosis, bradycardia, muscle weakness, seizures
treatment_goal: Block muscarinic excess, prevent respiratory failure
first_line_soc: Atropine IV — anticholinergic antidote — titrated to dry secretions; pralidoxime (2-PAM) is controversial in carbamate poisoning (may worsen).
treatment_algorithm: Atropine (2–4 mg IV q5–10 min until secretions dry). Decontamination (skin/clothing removal). Supportive: airway management, oxygen, benzodiazepines for seizures. Pralidoxime generally NOT recommended for carbamate poisoning (unlike organophosphates). Activated charcoal if early oral ingestion.
us_prevalence: ~5,000–10,000 carbamate pesticide poisoning cases/year in the US (AAPCC data); primarily occupational.
patients_needing_tx: ~3,000–5,000 require emergency atropine and supportive care annually.
price: $2,000–$10,000 per episode for a novel reversal agent.
peak_sales: $3M–$8M at 10–30% share; must compete with generic atropine.
registrational_endpoint: Time to resolution of cholinergic toxidrome and atropine dose required vs. standard atropine titration.
btd_threshold_vs_soc: ≥40% reduction in total atropine required or ≥30% faster toxidrome resolution.

symptoms: Rapidly rising temperature, muscle rigidity, tachycardia after anesthesia
treatment_goal: Halt ryanodine receptor overactivation, lower temperature, prevent death
first_line_soc: Dantrolene sodium (Dantrium/Ryanodex) — ryanodine receptor antagonist — IV bolus 2.5 mg/kg emergently; only pharmacologic antidote.
treatment_algorithm: Discontinue triggering agents (succinylcholine, volatile anesthetics). Dantrolene 2.5 mg/kg IV q5 min until resolved (up to 30 mg/kg). Active cooling. NaHCO3 for metabolic acidosis. Manage hyperkalemia (calcium gluconate, insulin/glucose). Furosemide for myoglobinuria. ICU monitoring for rhabdomyolysis.
us_prevalence: ~1/10,000–50,000 anesthetic exposures; ~200–400 cases/year in the US (MHAUS data).
patients_needing_tx: ~200–400 require dantrolene annually.
price: $10,000–$50,000 per episode for a novel RyR1 inhibitor (Ryanodex costs ~$4,500/100 mg vial).
peak_sales: $5M–$15M at 50–100% share (orphan market); Ryanodex (Agile Therapeutics) had ~$10M revenue.
registrational_endpoint: Time to temperature normalization and resolution of metabolic abnormalities vs. standard dantrolene.
btd_threshold_vs_soc: ≥30% faster temperature normalization and lower total dantrolene dose vs. standard dantrolene.

symptoms: Pain, swelling, redness, urticaria, anaphylaxis risk
treatment_goal: Relieve local reaction, prevent/treat anaphylaxis
first_line_soc: Epinephrine 0.3 mg IM (EpiPen) for anaphylaxis — absolute first-line; antihistamines + corticosteroids for non-anaphylactic reactions.
treatment_algorithm: Non-anaphylactic: ice, topical hydrocortisone, oral antihistamines (diphenhydramine, cetirizine). Anaphylaxis: epinephrine 0.3 mg IM + H1/H2 antihistamines + systemic corticosteroids + IV fluids. Venom immunotherapy (VIT) for prevention of recurrent sting anaphylaxis (~3–5 years, highly effective ~95% protection). EpiPen Rx for at-risk patients.
us_prevalence: ~2 million significant insect sting reactions/year; 40–100 deaths/year from anaphylaxis (CDC); ~5% of population has systemic reactions.
patients_needing_tx: ~500,000 require prescription pharmacologic treatment (epinephrine, VIT); ~2 million need OTC management.
price: $500–$3,000 per episode or per year (immunotherapy); EpiPen ~$650/2-pack currently.
registrational_endpoint: Anaphylaxis prevention rate at 2-year follow-up and systemic reaction rate with re-sting challenge vs. placebo immunotherapy.
btd_threshold_vs_soc: ≥90% anaphylaxis prevention vs. placebo VIT, or ≥50% faster onset epinephrine analog vs. standard EpiPen.

symptoms: Intense itching, papular rash, burrows (scabies), secondary infection
treatment_goal: Eradicate infestation, relieve pruritus, prevent spread
first_line_soc: Permethrin 5% cream (topical) — applied head to toe, washed off after 8–14 hours — first-line for scabies; oral ivermectin as alternative.
treatment_algorithm: Scabies: permethrin 5% cream (single application, repeated in 1 week). Oral ivermectin 200 mcg/kg × 2 doses (1 week apart) for crusted/Norwegian scabies or treatment failure. Decontamination of clothing/bedding. Antihistamines for pruritus. Topical corticosteroids for persistent nodular scabies.
us_prevalence: ~1 million scabies cases/year in the US (CDC data); mite bites from dust/bird/harvest mites add several million more episodes.
patients_needing_tx: ~1 million require prescription permethrin or ivermectin treatment annually.
price: $50–$500 per treatment course for a novel acaricide.
registrational_endpoint: Cure rate at 4 weeks (no new burrows/lesions) vs. permethrin 5% cream.
btd_threshold_vs_soc: ≥20% higher cure rate or single-dose efficacy vs. permethrin 5% cream.

symptoms: Tick attachment, local reaction, erythema migrans, potential disease transmission
treatment_goal: Remove tick, prevent tick-borne disease transmission
first_line_soc: Single-dose doxycycline 200 mg within 72 hours of deer tick removal for Lyme disease prophylaxis (IDSA recommendation).
treatment_algorithm: Mechanical removal (fine-tip tweezers). Single-dose doxycycline 200 mg PO for high-risk deer tick bite. No pharmacologic prophylaxis for other tick-borne diseases. Observation for erythema migrans × 30 days.
us_prevalence: ~400,000–500,000 tick bites/year requiring medical evaluation; ~500,000 Lyme disease cases/year.
patients_needing_tx: ~200,000 require prophylactic doxycycline for Lyme prevention.
price: $500–$2,000 per course for a novel single-dose tick-borne disease prophylaxis.
registrational_endpoint: Lyme disease prevention rate (no seroconversion or erythema migrans at 30 days) vs. doxycycline prophylaxis.
btd_threshold_vs_soc: Non-inferior to doxycycline with better safety/tolerance profile, or superior efficacy ≥90% prevention.

disease: Cnidaria (Coelenterates, such as Jellyfish and Sea Anemones) Stings
symptoms: Immediate burning pain, skin wheals, urticaria, rare anaphylaxis
treatment_goal: Deactivate nematocysts, relieve pain, manage systemic reaction
first_line_soc: None specific pharmacologic — seawater rinse (avoid fresh water), vinegar for box jellyfish (deactivates nematocysts), hot water immersion for box jellyfish pain; antihistamines for local reactions.
treatment_algorithm: None — remove tentacles with card/tweezers (not bare hands). Vinegar (dilute acetic acid) for box jellyfish. Hot water immersion (45°C, 20 min) for pain. Antihistamines (diphenhydramine) for urticaria. Corticosteroids (prednisone) for severe reactions. Epinephrine/anaphylaxis protocol for severe systemic reactions. Chironex fleckeri antivenom (box jellyfish) for life-threatening cases.
us_prevalence: ~500,000–1,000,000 jellyfish stings/year in US coastal areas; majority minor.
patients_needing_tx: ~100,000 seek medical care; ~10,000 require prescription treatment.
price: $500–$2,000 per episode for a novel nematocyst-deactivating or analgesic agent.
registrational_endpoint: Pain score (VAS) at 30 minutes and skin reaction area at 24 hours vs. vinegar + antihistamine.
btd_threshold_vs_soc: ≥40% reduction in VAS pain at 30 minutes vs. current standard.

symptoms: Local pain, swelling, pruritus, possible systemic reactions
treatment_goal: Relieve local symptoms, manage allergic reactions
first_line_soc: None specific — topical antihistamines/corticosteroids for local reactions; oral antihistamines; epinephrine for anaphylaxis.
treatment_algorithm: None — local: ice, topical hydrocortisone, oral antihistamines. Systemic: oral cetirizine/loratadine. Anaphylaxis: epinephrine IM. Antibiotics for secondary infection.
us_prevalence: Millions of exposures/year; ~100,000–200,000 significant reactions requiring medical care.
patients_needing_tx: ~200,000 require prescription treatment.
price: $200–$1,000 per episode.
registrational_endpoint: Pruritus VAS and wheal size at 2 hours vs. oral antihistamine.
btd_threshold_vs_soc: ≥40% reduction in pruritus VAS at 2 hours vs. standard oral antihistamine.

symptoms: Fang marks, local swelling, tissue necrosis, coagulopathy, neurotoxicity
treatment_goal: Neutralize venom, prevent tissue loss, correct coagulopathy
first_line_soc: Crotalidae polyvalent immune Fab antivenom (CroFab, BTG) — first-line for North American pit viper envenomation. Anavip (crotalidae immune F(ab')2 equine) for prolonged venom recurrence.
treatment_algorithm: CroFab: 4–6 vials IV, repeat q6h until initial control, then 2 vials q6h × 3 doses (for coagulopathy). Anavip alternative for rattlesnake. Coral snake: Wyeth coral snake antivenom (limited supply); Pfizer antivenom (Coralmyn) for coral snakes. Wound care, serial limb measurements, pain management (opioids), tetanus prophylaxis. Surgical fasciotomy for true compartment syndrome. No tourniquets.
us_prevalence: ~7,000–8,000 venomous snakebites/year in the US (CDC); ~5 deaths/year; ~3,000–4,000 require antivenom.
patients_needing_tx: ~4,000 require antivenom treatment annually.
peak_sales: $50M–$100M at 50–100% share (antivenom is standard-of-care monopoly market).
registrational_endpoint: Limb function at 14 days (FABS score) and coagulopathy resolution vs. placebo.
btd_threshold_vs_soc: ≥30% faster resolution of venom-induced coagulopathy vs. current CroFab regimen.

symptoms: Bite mark, local pain; necrosis (brown recluse); systemic toxicity, muscle cramps (black widow)
treatment_goal: Neutralize venom, prevent necrosis, relieve pain and muscle spasm
first_line_soc: Black widow: Merck antivenin (Latrodectus mactans antivenin) — IV/IM — plus IV opioids and benzodiazepines. Brown recluse: no antivenom available; wound care + dapsone (controversial).
treatment_algorithm: Black widow: Latrodectus antivenin (if severe); IV calcium gluconate and opioids for muscle cramps; benzodiazepines. Brown recluse (Loxosceles): wound care, debridement for necrosis; dapsone 100 mg/day (controversial evidence); oral colchicine (investigational); steroids for systemic loxoscelism.
us_prevalence: ~50,000–100,000 spider bite reports/year; ~2,000 medically significant (black widow ~2,000 severe; brown recluse necrotic wounds ~5,000).
patients_needing_tx: ~7,000 require active medical management annually.
price: $5,000–$20,000 per episode for a novel broad-spectrum spider antivenom.
registrational_endpoint: Time to resolution of systemic symptoms (black widow) or wound healing/necrosis prevention (brown recluse) vs. current antivenom/supportive care.
btd_threshold_vs_soc: ≥30% faster resolution of envenomation symptoms vs. current standard care.

symptoms: Pain, envenomation symptoms, puncture wounds, infection risk
treatment_goal: Neutralize venom, prevent infection, manage systemic effects
first_line_soc: Hot water immersion (45°C × 30–90 min) for fish stings (heat-labile venoms); antivenom for life-threatening envenomations (box jellyfish, stonefish, sea snake).
treatment_algorithm: Hot water immersion for most fish stings. Stonefish: antivenom (CSL Stonefish Antivenom) for severe envenomation. Box jellyfish (Chironex): CSL box jellyfish antivenom. Sea snake: polyvalent sea snake antivenom. Antibiotics for wound infections (Aeromonas, Vibrio coverage: ciprofloxacin). Tetanus prophylaxis.
us_prevalence: ~50,000–100,000 significant marine bites/stings/year in coastal US (AAPCC data).
patients_needing_tx: ~20,000 require medical treatment beyond first aid.
price: $1,000–$10,000 per episode (antivenom for severe cases ~$3,000–$10,000).
registrational_endpoint: Resolution of envenomation symptoms at 24 hours and wound infection rate at 7 days.
btd_threshold_vs_soc: ≥30% faster symptom resolution and ≥40% reduction in wound infection rate vs. SoC.

symptoms: Local pain, paresthesia, neuromuscular excitation, hypertension, pediatric respiratory failure
treatment_goal: Neutralize venom, control neuromuscular toxicity, manage pain
first_line_soc: Centruroides (scorpion) immune F(ab')2 antivenom (Anascorp, FDA-approved 2011) for bark scorpion (Centruroides sculpturatus) envenomation in the US.
treatment_algorithm: Anascorp (centruroides immune F(ab')2) — 3 vials IV, repeat q30–60 min PRN; dramatically reduces symptom duration vs. supportive care. Supportive: benzodiazepines for agitation/muscle spasms, opioids for pain, antihypertensives for hypertensive crisis. ICU monitoring for pediatric patients.
us_prevalence: ~20,000 scorpion stings/year in the US (mostly Arizona); ~1,500–2,000 require antivenom.
patients_needing_tx: ~2,000 require Anascorp treatment annually.
price: $15,000–$40,000 per episode (current Anascorp ~$15,000–$20,000/vial × 3 vials).
peak_sales: $20M–$40M at 50–100% share (very limited market, Anascorp is current SoC).
registrational_endpoint: Time to resolution of grade 3–4 neuromuscular symptoms vs. placebo.
btd_threshold_vs_soc: ≥50% faster symptom resolution vs. placebo/supportive care.

symptoms: Puncture wound, severe pain, paralysis (cone snail tetrodotoxin-like toxins)
treatment_goal: Manage pain, support ventilation for severe envenomation
first_line_soc: None — no antivenom available for cone snail envenomation; pressure immobilization, supportive mechanical ventilation as needed.
treatment_algorithm: None — pressure bandage immobilization for cone snail. Supportive ICU care with mechanical ventilation. Analgesics for pain. No antivenom available. Octopus (blue-ringed): supportive ventilation only.
us_prevalence: Extremely rare in the US; <50 significant mollusk envenomations/year (primarily aquarium workers, tropical travel).
patients_needing_tx: <50 require active treatment.
price: $50,000–$500,000 per antidote (orphan drug level).
registrational_endpoint: Survival at 72 hours and days on mechanical ventilation vs. supportive care alone.
btd_threshold_vs_soc: ≥50% reduction in mechanical ventilation days vs. supportive care alone.

symptoms: Intense burning pain, erythema, urticaria, lymphadenopathy, nausea
treatment_goal: Remove spines, relieve pain, manage allergic reaction
first_line_soc: None specific — remove embedded spines with tape stripping; oral antihistamines (diphenhydramine), NSAIDs; topical corticosteroids.
treatment_algorithm: None — sticky tape to remove spines. Oral diphenhydramine or loratadine. NSAIDs (ibuprofen) for pain. Topical hydrocortisone 1%. Epinephrine for anaphylaxis. IV corticosteroids for severe systemic reactions.
us_prevalence: ~5,000–20,000 exposures/year in Southern US states (Texas, Florida); rarely life-threatening.
patients_needing_tx: ~10,000 require medical care annually.
price: $200–$1,000 per episode for a novel topical anti-venom agent.
registrational_endpoint: VAS pain score at 2 hours and time to spine removal vs. standard tape stripping.
btd_threshold_vs_soc: ≥50% faster pain reduction vs. standard management.

symptoms: Puncture wounds, local pain, spine retention, granulomatous reaction
treatment_goal: Remove spines, relieve pain, prevent infection and granuloma
first_line_soc: None pharmacologic — spine removal, hot water immersion (45°C) for pain; topical antiseptics; NSAIDs.
treatment_algorithm: None — hot water immersion (45°C) for 30–90 min. Mechanical spine removal (sterile technique). NSAIDs for inflammation. Antibiotics (ciprofloxacin or trimethoprim-sulfamethoxazole) for infection, particularly Vibrio spp. Intralesional corticosteroid for retained-spine granulomas.
us_prevalence: ~5,000–15,000 sea urchin stings/year in US coastal regions.
patients_needing_tx: ~5,000 require medical attention.
price: $200–$1,000 per course for a novel spine-dissolution or anti-inflammatory agent.
registrational_endpoint: VAS pain score and granuloma rate at 4 weeks vs. hot water soaks.
btd_threshold_vs_soc: ≥50% faster pain resolution vs. hot water immersion alone.

disease: Alligator, Crocodile, Iguana, and Venomous Lizard Bites
symptoms: Crush injury, lacerations, infection, potential toxin effects
treatment_goal: Wound care, infection prevention, manage envenomation
first_line_soc: None specific pharmacologic — wound irrigation, antibiotics (e.g., amoxicillin-clavulanate or ciprofloxacin for polymicrobial coverage including gram-negatives), tetanus prophylaxis.
treatment_algorithm: None — aggressive wound debridement and irrigation. Broad-spectrum antibiotics (Aeromonas/Pseudomonas coverage: ciprofloxacin + metronidazole, or amoxicillin-clavulanate). Tetanus update. For Gila monster/beaded lizard bites: supportive care (no antivenom available). Surgical repair as needed.
us_prevalence: Very rare — estimated <5,000 reptile bites/year including captive animals; venomous lizard bites <100/year in US.
patients_needing_tx: ~3,000–5,000 require medical care; ~500–1,000 require IV antibiotics or surgical intervention.
price: $500–$3,000 per episode (novel antimicrobial or antitoxin).
registrational_endpoint: Rate of wound infection or major surgical intervention at 30 days.
btd_threshold_vs_soc: ≥50% reduction in wound infection rate vs. standard antibiotics.

symptoms: Local pain, swelling, redness, rare systemic effects
treatment_goal: Relieve pain, prevent secondary infection, manage reaction
first_line_soc: None specific — local wound care, analgesics (NSAIDs/acetaminophen), antihistamines for allergic reactions.
treatment_algorithm: None — immerse affected area in warm water. Analgesics (NSAIDs). Antihistamines for local allergic reaction. Antibiotics for secondary infection. Systemic effects (rare): supportive care, IV fluids, antihistamines, corticosteroids.
us_prevalence: ~5,000–10,000 centipede/millipede bites/year in the US; rarely serious (poison center data).
patients_needing_tx: ~3,000 require medical attention; ~500 need prescription medications.
price: $200–$500 per episode for a novel local toxin-neutralizing agent.
registrational_endpoint: Pain score (VAS) at 2 hours vs. NSAIDs/local care.
btd_threshold_vs_soc: ≥50% reduction in VAS pain score vs. NSAID standard.

symptoms: Intense immediate pain, laceration, local edema, systemic reactions rare
treatment_goal: Remove barb, relieve severe pain, prevent infection
first_line_soc: None pharmacologic — hot water immersion (45°C × 30–90 min) for pain; wound irrigation; NSAIDs.
treatment_algorithm: None — barb removal (surgical if embedded). Hot water soak (45°C) for 30–90 minutes. NSAIDs (ibuprofen). Antibiotics (ciprofloxacin or TMP-SMX) for wound infection, particularly marine Vibrio spp. Tetanus prophylaxis. Debridement for lacerations.
us_prevalence: ~1,500–2,000 stingray injuries/year in the US (NEISS).
patients_needing_tx: ~2,000 require medical care.
price: $200–$1,000 per episode for a novel venom-neutralizing agent.
registrational_endpoint: VAS pain score at 1 hour vs. hot water immersion.
btd_threshold_vs_soc: ≥50% faster pain reduction vs. hot water immersion.

symptoms: Ascending flaccid paralysis, areflexia, gait ataxia resolving after tick removal
treatment_goal: Remove tick immediately to reverse paralysis
first_line_soc: None pharmacologic — mechanical tick removal (definitive treatment; paralysis reverses within hours of removal).
treatment_algorithm: None — immediate tick removal is curative. Supportive: respiratory support (rare ventilation needed if respiratory muscles affected). No pharmacologic treatment required or available.
us_prevalence: ~40–50 cases/year in the US (very rare).
patients_needing_tx: ~50 require medical care; no drug treatment.
price: N/A — no pharmacologic market.

symptoms: Nausea, vomiting, dizziness, pallor, sweating with motion
treatment_goal: Prevent/abort nausea and vomiting, maintain comfort
first_line_soc: Scopolamine (Transderm Scop) transdermal patch — anticholinergic — most effective for prophylaxis; dimenhydrinate (Dramamine) or meclizine (Antivert) as OTC alternatives.
treatment_algorithm: Prophylaxis: scopolamine 1.5 mg transdermal patch (applied 4h before travel). Acute: dimenhydrinate 50 mg PO/IM q4–6h or promethazine 25 mg PO/IM. Prochlorperazine (Compazine) for refractory vomiting. Non-pharmacologic: ginger, acupressure (limited evidence). New: VR/behavioral therapies.
us_prevalence: ~30–40% of the population susceptible; ~10–15 million significant episodes requiring treatment/year.
patients_needing_tx: ~5 million require prescription pharmacologic treatment annually (vs. OTC).
price: $50–$500 per episode for a novel vestibular modulator.
registrational_endpoint: Motion Sickness Assessment Questionnaire (MSAQ) at 4 hours of motion exposure vs. scopolamine.
btd_threshold_vs_soc: ≥30% reduction in MSAQ total score vs. scopolamine transdermal patch.

symptoms: Skin color change, numbness, pain, blistering, tissue loss
treatment_goal: Rewarm tissue, restore perfusion, prevent tissue death
first_line_soc: None pharmacologic — rapid rewarming in 37–39°C water bath; iloprost IV (prostacyclin analog) for severe frostbite to improve perfusion.
treatment_algorithm: Rapid rewarming (37–39°C water for 15–30 min). Iloprost (0.5–2 ng/kg/min IV for 6h/day × 5–8 days) for deep frostbite — FDA-approved in France/EU; used off-label in US. TPA (alteplase) for refractory severe frostbite (hospital protocol). NSAIDs (ibuprofen 400 mg BID) for anti-prostaglandin effect. Aloe vera topical. Tetanus prophylaxis. Wound debridement.
us_prevalence: ~200,000 cold injury cases/year in the US including frostbite; ~10,000–20,000 significant frostbite cases (military + civilian).
patients_needing_tx: ~20,000–30,000 require active pharmacologic treatment annually.
price: $5,000–$30,000 per episode for a novel vasodilator/thrombolytic regimen.
registrational_endpoint: Digit/limb amputation rate at 90 days vs. standard rewarming + iloprost.
btd_threshold_vs_soc: ≥30% reduction in amputation rate vs. standard iloprost protocol.

symptoms: Shivering, confusion, slurred speech, bradycardia, loss of consciousness
treatment_goal: Rewarm safely, restore cardiac rhythm, prevent complications
first_line_soc: None pharmacologic — rewarming is primary treatment (passive for mild, active internal for severe); ECMO for cardiac arrest from hypothermia.
treatment_algorithm: None — Mild (32–35°C): passive rewarming, warm IV fluids, warm humidified O2. Moderate (28–32°C): active external rewarming (warming blankets, heating pads). Severe (<28°C): active internal rewarming: warmed IV saline (43°C), bladder/peritoneal lavage. ECMO/ECLS for cardiac arrest ('not dead until warm and dead'). Avoid vasopressors until temperature >30°C.
us_prevalence: ~1,300 hypothermia deaths/year; ~20,000–30,000 hospitalizations (CDC).
patients_needing_tx: ~30,000 require active pharmacologic/rewarming management.
price: $5,000–$20,000 per episode for a novel thermogenic/cardiac support agent.
registrational_endpoint: Survival with intact neurologic function at 30 days vs. standard active rewarming protocol.
btd_threshold_vs_soc: ≥20% improvement in neurologically intact survival vs. standard rewarming.

symptoms: Nausea, vomiting, lymphopenia, burns, acute radiation syndrome
treatment_goal: Decontaminate, support hematopoiesis, prevent infection and death
first_line_soc: Filgrastim (G-CSF; Neupogen) or pegfilgrastim (Neulasta) for hematopoietic acute radiation syndrome (H-ARS); FDA-approved under Animal Rule. Romiplostim (Nplate) FDA-approved (2021) for H-ARS-related thrombocytopenia.
treatment_algorithm: Decontamination. G-CSF (filgrastim/pegfilgrastim) or GM-CSF (sargramostim/Leukine) to stimulate myeloid recovery. Romiplostim (Nplate) for platelet recovery. Blood transfusions. Broad-spectrum antibiotics for neutropenic fever. Potassium iodide for radioiodine exposure. DTPA/Prussian blue for specific radionuclide decorporation. Wound care for cutaneous radiation injury.
us_prevalence: Rare civilian exposure; ~100–500 significant exposures/year; BARDA stockpile-driven market.
patients_needing_tx: ~500 civilians/year; military/mass casualty scenarios could involve thousands.
price: $10,000–$50,000 per course; government procurement contracts (BARDA) are the primary market.
registrational_endpoint: 30-day survival (H-ARS: LD50/30 model in NHP under Animal Rule); or time to ANC recovery >500/μL.
btd_threshold_vs_soc: ≥20% improvement in 30-day survival vs. G-CSF alone or vs. placebo in NHP Animal Rule study.

symptoms: Ear pain, hearing loss, sinus pain, vertigo, nosebleed
treatment_goal: Equalize pressure, relieve pain, prevent perforation
first_line_soc: None pharmacologic — decongestants (pseudoephedrine, oxymetazoline nasal spray) to facilitate eustachian tube opening; primary treatment is pressure equalization maneuvers.
treatment_algorithm: None — Valsalva/Toynbee maneuvers. Oxymetazoline nasal spray or pseudoephedrine. NSAIDs/acetaminophen for pain. Tympanostomy tube placement for recurrent/severe cases. Myringotomy for hemotympanum. Avoidance of diving/flying until resolved.
us_prevalence: ~1–2 million cases/year (aviation + diving combined); dental barotrauma adds hundreds of thousands more.
patients_needing_tx: ~500,000 seek medical care; ~200,000 need prescription decongestants or interventional treatment.
price: $200–$1,000 per episode for a novel eustachian tube-targeting drug.
registrational_endpoint: Resolution of tympanic membrane retraction/effusion on otoscopy at 2 weeks vs. decongestant placebo.
btd_threshold_vs_soc: ≥30% faster resolution vs. pseudoephedrine standard of care.

symptoms: Heat cramps, exhaustion, or heatstroke; fatigue, nausea, temperature elevation
treatment_goal: Reduce core temperature, restore perfusion, prevent organ failure
first_line_soc: None pharmacologic — cooling is primary therapy; no specific pharmacologic first-line treatment for exertional heat illness.
treatment_algorithm: See Heat Cramps, Heat Exhaustion, Heatstroke entries. General: aggressive cooling, IV fluid resuscitation, monitoring for multi-organ dysfunction.
us_prevalence: ~700,000 heat illness-related medical encounters/year; ~67,000 ED visits; ~700 deaths (CDC 2025).
patients_needing_tx: ~50,000 require active medical treatment.
price: $2,000–$10,000 per episode.
registrational_endpoint: Time to core temperature <38°C and multi-organ dysfunction rate at 24 hours vs. standard evaporative cooling.
btd_threshold_vs_soc: ≥30% faster cooling and ≥25% reduction in MODS rate vs. standard cooling.

symptoms: Respiratory distress, hypoxia, altered consciousness, pulmonary edema
treatment_goal: Restore oxygenation, prevent anoxic brain injury
first_line_soc: None pharmacologic — CPR and rescue breathing are primary interventions; supplemental O2, intubation/mechanical ventilation as needed.
treatment_algorithm: None — immediate CPR and rescue breathing. Supplemental O2 (100% NBO). Endotracheal intubation if GCS <8 or persistent hypoxia. PEEP ventilation for pulmonary edema. Hypothermia protocol (32–36°C) for comatose survivors. Antibiotics for aspiration pneumonia. Bronchospasm: nebulized albuterol. Surfactant therapy for severe ARDS (investigational).
us_prevalence: ~4,000 drowning deaths/year; ~8,000 non-fatal drowning hospitalizations/year (CDC 2025).
patients_needing_tx: ~8,000 require active pharmacologic and ventilatory support annually.
price: $10,000–$50,000 per episode for a novel lung-protective surfactant or neuroprotective agent.
registrational_endpoint: Survival with good neurologic outcome (CPC score 1–2) at 30 days vs. standard CPR/ventilation.
btd_threshold_vs_soc: ≥20% improvement in neurologically intact survival vs. standard resuscitation.

symptoms: Numbness, skin color change (white/gray/black), blistering, tissue death
treatment_goal: Restore perfusion, salvage tissue, prevent amputation
first_line_soc: Rapid rewarming (37–39°C water bath); iloprost IV (prostacyclin analog) for deep frostbite — reduces amputation risk by ~50% in clinical data.
treatment_algorithm: Rapid rewarming. Iloprost 2 ng/kg/min IV for 6h/day × 5–8 days (significant evidence for digit salvage). tPA (alteplase) + heparin for cases within 24h of thaw and perfusion scan positive (ACEP guidelines). Aloe vera topical. NSAIDs (ibuprofen 400 mg BID). Tetanus prophylaxis. Wound debridement. Amputation deferred until full demarcation (6–12 weeks).
us_prevalence: ~10,000–20,000 significant frostbite cases/year in the US; ~5,000 hospitalizations (military + civilian).
patients_needing_tx: ~5,000–10,000 require active pharmacologic treatment (iloprost ± tPA).
price: $10,000–$50,000 per treatment course for an improved iloprost formulation or next-gen vasodilator.
registrational_endpoint: Digit/extremity amputation rate at 90 days vs. standard iloprost IV protocol.
btd_threshold_vs_soc: ≥30% reduction in amputation rate vs. iloprost standard of care.

symptoms: Core temperature >40°C, altered mental status, hot/dry skin, multi-organ failure
treatment_goal: Rapidly lower core temperature below 39°C, prevent organ damage
first_line_soc: None pharmacologic — cold water immersion (exertional) or evaporative cooling is definitive; no proven pharmacologic first-line agent.
treatment_algorithm: None — cold water immersion (best for exertional; target ≤39°C). Evaporative cooling (classic). Benzodiazepines for shivering/seizures. Dantrolene NOT effective. ICU monitoring for MODS: liver failure, rhabdomyolysis, DIC, AKI. Phenobarbital for refractory seizures. Experimental: endovascular cooling catheters.
us_prevalence: ~300–700 heatstroke deaths/year; ~3,000–5,000 hospitalizations/year (CDC).
patients_needing_tx: ~5,000 require intensive management including ICU.
price: $10,000–$50,000 per episode for a novel organ-protective agent.
registrational_endpoint: Time to target temperature, 30-day mortality, and organ failure score vs. standard cold water immersion.
btd_threshold_vs_soc: ≥30% reduction in 30-day mortality or MODS rate vs. standard rapid cooling.

symptoms: Ear pain, hearing loss, tinnitus, sinus pressure, nasal congestion
treatment_goal: Equalize pressure, relieve pain, prevent complications
first_line_soc: Decongestants (oxymetazoline nasal spray, pseudoephedrine) to facilitate pressure equalization; NSAIDs for pain.
treatment_algorithm: Nasal decongestants (oxymetazoline 0.05% spray or oral pseudoephedrine 60 mg). NSAIDs/acetaminophen. Autoinflation maneuvers. ENT follow-up for hemotympanum or TM perforation. Corticosteroid nasal spray for chronic sinusitis component.
us_prevalence: ~1 million cases/year in US divers and air travelers (DAN/FAA estimates).
patients_needing_tx: ~300,000 require prescription decongestants or ENT care.
price: $200–$800 per episode.
registrational_endpoint: Resolution of middle ear effusion and TM retraction at 4 weeks vs. decongestant alone.
btd_threshold_vs_soc: ≥35% faster resolution vs. oxymetazoline standard.

symptoms: Painful swollen feet/hands, trench foot, chilblains, erythema
treatment_goal: Rewarm, restore perfusion, prevent ulceration and tissue loss
first_line_soc: None pharmacologic — gentle rewarming; nifedipine for chilblains; protective footwear; meticulous wound care.
treatment_algorithm: None — trench foot/immersion foot: gentle rewarming (air dry, elevate), NOT hot water. Wound care, analgesics. Chilblains (pernio): topical corticosteroids + nifedipine 20 mg XL for recurrent cases. Pentoxifylline for refractory cases.
us_prevalence: ~100,000+ cases/year in military personnel, homeless individuals, outdoor workers (USUHS data).
patients_needing_tx: ~50,000 require medical treatment; ~20,000 need prescription medications.
price: $2,000–$8,000 per episode for a novel vasodilator or tissue-protective agent.
registrational_endpoint: Time to tissue healing and pain VAS at 4 weeks vs. nifedipine standard.
btd_threshold_vs_soc: ≥30% faster tissue healing and ≥25% pain reduction vs. nifedipine alone.

symptoms: Barotrauma, DCS, nitrogen narcosis, O2 toxicity, near-drowning
treatment_goal: Treat barotrauma, reverse DCS, ensure oxygenation
first_line_soc: 100% O2 for all diving emergencies; HBO for DCS and arterial gas embolism; specific antidotes per complication.
treatment_algorithm: See individual entries (DCS, Arterial Gas Embolism, Barotrauma). General: 100% O2 in transit, IV hydration, HBO if indicated. Pulmonary barotrauma: chest decompression if pneumothorax. Nitrogen narcosis: ascent alone sufficient. Inner ear barotrauma: corticosteroids + ENT follow-up.
us_prevalence: ~1,000–2,000 serious diving injuries/year requiring medical intervention (DAN data); ~9 million recreational divers in the US.
patients_needing_tx: ~2,000 require active treatment annually.
registrational_endpoint: Resolution of diving injury symptoms and neurologic status at 48 hours vs. standard HBO/O2 protocol.
btd_threshold_vs_soc: ≥30% improvement in full recovery rate vs. standard HBO protocol.

symptoms: Sudden neurologic deficits, chest pain, loss of consciousness during/after diving
treatment_goal: Recompress gas bubbles, restore tissue perfusion, prevent death
first_line_soc: Hyperbaric oxygen therapy (HBO) — 100% O2 at 2.8–6 ATA — definitive treatment to reduce bubble size and resolve ischemia.
treatment_algorithm: HBO (US Navy Treatment Table 6 at 2.8 ATA) — primary treatment. Supplemental 100% O2 en route. IV fluids (normal saline). Aspirin for platelet effects. Lidocaine IV investigated as neuroprotectant in arterial gas embolism but not standard. Supportive ICU care.
us_prevalence: ~1,000–1,500 arterial gas embolism cases/year in the US (DAN Divers Alert Network data).
patients_needing_tx: ~1,000–1,500 require emergent HBO therapy.
price: $10,000–$30,000 per treatment for a novel neuroprotective/gas-dissolving agent.
registrational_endpoint: Neurologic outcome score (e.g., modified Rankin Scale) at 72 hours vs. HBO alone.
btd_threshold_vs_soc: ≥30% improvement in full neurologic recovery rate vs. HBO alone.

symptoms: Joint pain, skin mottling, neurologic deficits, dyspnea after diving
treatment_goal: Eliminate nitrogen bubbles, restore neurologic function
first_line_soc: Hyperbaric oxygen (HBO) — US Navy Treatment Table 6 (2.8 ATA, 100% O2) — definitive treatment; supplemental O2 in transit.
treatment_algorithm: 100% O2 en route to HBO facility. HBO (Table 6: 2.8 ATA × 60 min + 1.0 ATA O2) — may extend to Table 6A for neurologic DCS. IV normal saline hydration. Aspirin (anti-platelet). Corticosteroids controversial. Experimental: perfluorocarbon emulsions as oxygen carriers investigated.
us_prevalence: ~1,000–1,500 DCS cases/year in the US (DAN Divers Alert Network annual report).
patients_needing_tx: ~1,000–1,500 require emergent HBO.
price: $10,000–$30,000 per episode for a novel nitrogen bubble-resolving or neuroprotective drug.
registrational_endpoint: Residual neurologic deficits at 72 hours (Neurological Severity Score) vs. HBO alone.
btd_threshold_vs_soc: ≥30% improvement in full neurologic recovery rate vs. HBO alone.

symptoms: Painful muscle spasms during or after exercise in heat
treatment_goal: Restore electrolytes, relieve cramping, rehydrate
first_line_soc: None pharmacologic — oral or IV fluid and electrolyte (sodium) replacement; massage; rest.
treatment_algorithm: None — oral sodium-containing fluids (sports drinks, salt tablets). IV normal saline for severe cases. Muscle stretching/massage. Rest and cooling. Reassessment for heat exhaustion.
us_prevalence: ~1–2 million heat cramp episodes/year in athletes and outdoor workers (CDC/NIOSH data).
patients_needing_tx: ~100,000 seek medical care; ~20,000 require IV rehydration.
price: $200–$500 per episode for a novel electrolyte/cramping solution.
registrational_endpoint: Time to symptom resolution vs. standard oral electrolyte replacement.
btd_threshold_vs_soc: ≥50% faster cramping resolution vs. oral sodium replacement.

symptoms: Weakness, dizziness, heavy sweating, nausea, cool/moist skin
treatment_goal: Cool patient, restore fluids/electrolytes, prevent heatstroke
first_line_soc: None pharmacologic — aggressive cooling (move to cool environment, cool IV fluids); IV normal saline for rehydration.
treatment_algorithm: None — remove from heat, cool environment. IV normal saline 0.9% (500 mL–1 L bolus). Active cooling: cool mist fans, ice packs to neck/axilla/groin. Oral rehydration if able. Monitoring for progression to heatstroke.
us_prevalence: ~65,000–80,000 heat-related ED visits/year; ~700 heat exhaustion hospitalizations/year (CDC 2025).
patients_needing_tx: ~30,000 require IV rehydration and pharmacologic support.
price: $500–$2,000 per episode.
registrational_endpoint: Time to symptom resolution and core temperature normalization vs. standard cooling + IV fluids.
btd_threshold_vs_soc: ≥30% faster temperature normalization vs. standard cooling + IV saline.

symptoms: Headache, nausea, fatigue, breathlessness, confusion
treatment_goal: Reduce symptoms, prevent progression to HACE/HAPE
first_line_soc: Acetazolamide (Diamox) — carbonic anhydrase inhibitor — 125–250 mg BID for prophylaxis and treatment of acute mountain sickness (AMS).
treatment_algorithm: AMS: Acetazolamide 250 mg BID; ibuprofen for headache; descent if not improving. HACE: Dexamethasone 8 mg loading + 4 mg q6h; portable hyperbaric chamber (Gamow bag); immediate descent. HAPE: Nifedipine 30 mg ER or tadalafil/sildenafil for pulmonary vasodilation; supplemental O2; immediate descent. Epoprostenol inhaled in severe HAPE.
us_prevalence: ~150,000–200,000 Americans develop AMS annually (estimates based on ~1 million high-altitude travelers/year; 25–50% incidence above 8,000 ft).
patients_needing_tx: ~50,000–75,000 require pharmacologic treatment annually (acetazolamide, dexamethasone).
price: $100–$500 per episode for a novel altitude illness drug; rationale: largely prophylactic/acute OTC-adjacent market.
registrational_endpoint: Lake Louise AMS Score reduction ≥3 points or prevention of AMS (score <3) vs. placebo at 24 hours altitude exposure.
btd_threshold_vs_soc: ≥40% reduction in AMS incidence vs. acetazolamide comparator.

symptoms: Acute dyspnea, cough with pink frothy sputum, hypoxia during swimming/diving
treatment_goal: Restore oxygenation, reduce pulmonary edema, prevent recurrence
first_line_soc: None pharmacologic — surface/exit water; 100% O2; furosemide IV for fluid overload; nifedipine for pulmonary hypertension component.
treatment_algorithm: 100% O2. Furosemide 20–40 mg IV for fluid overload component. Nifedipine 10 mg SL or tadalafil for pulmonary hypertension. CPAP/BiPAP for hypoxia. Avoid re-entry into water until evaluated. Cardiologic workup for underlying pathology.
us_prevalence: ~1,000–2,000 cases/year in US open-water swimmers and divers (DAN/triathlon data).
patients_needing_tx: ~1,500 require active pharmacologic treatment.
price: $5,000–$15,000 per episode for a novel pulmonary vasodilator.
registrational_endpoint: SpO2 normalization and dyspnea VAS at 2 hours vs. O2 alone.
btd_threshold_vs_soc: ≥30% faster SpO2 normalization vs. O2 + furosemide standard.

symptoms: Chest pain, dyspnea, pneumothorax, subcutaneous emphysema during ascent
treatment_goal: Re-expand lung, treat pneumothorax, prevent air embolism
first_line_soc: None pharmacologic — needle decompression and chest tube for pneumothorax; hyperbaric oxygen for arterial gas embolism component.
treatment_algorithm: None — immediate 100% O2. Tension pneumothorax: needle decompression + chest tube. Arterial gas embolism complication: emergent HBO therapy. Supportive ICU care. No diving until full recovery.
us_prevalence: ~1,000–2,000 cases/year in US divers (estimated from DAN database).
patients_needing_tx: ~1,500 require active intervention annually.
price: $5,000–$15,000 per HBO + adjunct course.
registrational_endpoint: Time to full lung re-expansion and symptom resolution vs. standard O2 + chest tube.
btd_threshold_vs_soc: ≥30% faster time to radiographic resolution vs. standard care.

symptoms: Tooth pain, facial pressure, eye pain/subconjunctival hemorrhage during diving/aviation
treatment_goal: Relieve pressure, manage pain, prevent complications
first_line_soc: None specific — dental: analgesics and dental referral; mask: supportive care; eye: topical lubricants and observation.
treatment_algorithm: None — dental: NSAIDs/analgesics + urgent dental referral. Mask squeeze: observation (usually self-resolves). Eye barotrauma: artificial tears, observation; rarely requires ophthalmology. Avoid altitude/diving until resolved.
us_prevalence: ~50,000–100,000 cases/year (primarily in divers and aviators; DAN/FAA data).
patients_needing_tx: ~20,000 need pharmacologic treatment (mainly analgesics).
price: $200–$500 per episode.
registrational_endpoint: Pain resolution (VAS) at 24 hours vs. standard NSAID.
btd_threshold_vs_soc: ≥40% faster pain resolution vs. NSAID alone.

symptoms: Nitrogen narcosis (confusion), O2 toxicity (seizures), CO2 retention (unconsciousness)
treatment_goal: Remove toxic gas exposure, restore normal oxygenation
first_line_soc: None pharmacologic — ascent to lower depth (nitrogen narcosis), switching to correct gas mix, surfacing; no specific drug antidotes.
treatment_algorithm: None — nitrogen narcosis: ascend to <30m. O2 toxicity: terminate O2 exposure; diazepam/lorazepam for seizures. CO2 retention: surface, CPR if needed. Prevention with proper gas mixture management.
us_prevalence: ~500–1,000 significant gas toxicity events/year in US divers (DAN data).
patients_needing_tx: ~300–500 require acute medical treatment.
price: $5,000–$15,000 per episode for a novel CNS O2-toxicity prevention agent.
registrational_endpoint: Prevention of O2 toxicity seizures at equivalent partial pressures vs. placebo in hyperbaric chamber trial.
btd_threshold_vs_soc: ≥50% reduction in seizure rate at equivalent PO2 vs. placebo.

symptoms: Abdominal pain, bloating, cramping, belching during/after diving
treatment_goal: Relieve gas pressure, prevent perforation, manage pain
first_line_soc: None pharmacologic — ascent and surface; simethicone for gas relief; supportive management.
treatment_algorithm: None — Valsalva to eruct gas. Simethicone (Gas-X) for GI gas distension. Analgesics if significant pain. Surgery only for rare gastric perforation from severe GI barotrauma.
us_prevalence: Thousands/year; minor cases in nearly all divers; significant GI barotrauma very rare (<100 hospitalizations/year).
patients_needing_tx: ~1,000 require medical treatment.
price: $200–$500 per episode.
registrational_endpoint: Symptom resolution (VAS abdominal pain) at 2 hours vs. simethicone alone.
btd_threshold_vs_soc: ≥50% reduction in abdominal pain score vs. simethicone.

symptoms: Nausea, vomiting, neurologic symptoms, reversal of temperature sensation (ciguatera)
treatment_goal: Manage symptoms, prevent dehydration, treat dysesthesias
first_line_soc: None specific antidote — supportive care; mannitol IV investigated for ciguatera (evidence limited); antihistamines for scombroid.
treatment_algorithm: Ciguatera: supportive IV fluids, antiemetics, gabapentin for dysesthesias; mannitol 1 g/kg IV (low evidence). Scombroid: antihistamines (diphenhydramine, famotidine) + corticosteroids for severe cases. Tetrodotoxin: supportive ventilation. Paralytic shellfish/STX: mechanical ventilation if needed.
us_prevalence: ~50,000–100,000 ciguatera cases/year in the US (CDC estimate, largely underreported); ~3,000 hospital-treated cases.
patients_needing_tx: ~20,000–30,000 require medical treatment annually.
price: $2,000–$10,000 per episode for a novel ciguatoxin-neutralizing drug.
registrational_endpoint: Composite neurologic symptom score (dysesthesia, paresthesia) at 72 hours vs. supportive care.
btd_threshold_vs_soc: ≥40% improvement in neurologic symptom score vs. supportive care at 72 hours.

symptoms: Nausea, vomiting, abdominal pain, cardiac arrhythmias (plant-specific)
treatment_goal: Decontaminate, manage toxidrome, prevent organ failure
first_line_soc: None specific — activated charcoal for acute ingestion if early; supportive care. Digoxin-specific antibody fragments (Digibind/DigiFab) for cardiac glycoside plants (foxglove, oleander).
treatment_algorithm: None universal. Activated charcoal (within 1–2 hours). Plant-specific antidotes: Digibind/DigiFab for cardiac glycoside toxicity; atropine for anticholinergic plants; physostigmine for anticholinergic. Cardiac monitoring. IV fluids. Symptomatic management.
us_prevalence: ~150,000–200,000 plant-related poison center calls/year (AAPCC); ~5,000–10,000 require medical intervention.
patients_needing_tx: ~10,000 require active pharmacologic treatment annually.
price: $2,000–$8,000 per episode for a novel broad-spectrum plant toxin antidote.
registrational_endpoint: Time to clinical stability and rate of major organ toxicity vs. standard supportive care.
btd_threshold_vs_soc: ≥30% reduction in major toxicity endpoints vs. supportive care alone.

symptoms: GI distress, liver/kidney failure (Amanita), cholinergic excess, hallucinations
treatment_goal: Identify toxin, manage organ toxicity, support liver function
first_line_soc: No universal antidote; silibinin (Legalon SIL) IV — hepatoprotective agent — investigational antidote for Amanita phalloides poisoning; supportive care; activated charcoal if early.
treatment_algorithm: Activated charcoal within 1–2 hours of ingestion. Mushroom ID by poison control/mycologist. Amanita phalloides: IV silibinin 20 mg/kg/day (available via compassionate use/IND in US), NAC (hepatoprotective). Cholinergic (Inocybe): atropine. Orellanine (Cortinarius): supportive dialysis. Ibotenic acid (Amanita muscaria): supportive. Liver transplant for fulminant hepatic failure.
us_prevalence: ~7,000–10,000 mushroom poisoning calls/year to US poison centers; ~100–200 serious cases requiring ICU care (AAPCC data).
patients_needing_tx: ~3,000 require active treatment; ~200–300 require intensive/ICU management.
price: $10,000–$50,000 per episode for a novel hepatoprotective antidote.
registrational_endpoint: Peak ALT/AST and liver transplant-free survival at 14 days vs. supportive care for Amanita poisoning.
btd_threshold_vs_soc: ≥40% reduction in liver transplant requirement vs. supportive care alone.

symptoms: Nausea, vomiting, diarrhea; neurological symptoms (paralytic), memory loss (amnesic)
treatment_goal: Supportive care, decontaminate, manage respiratory failure
first_line_soc: None specific — activated charcoal within 1–2 hours; supportive care (IV fluids, antiemetics); ventilator support for paralytic shellfish poisoning (PSP).
treatment_algorithm: None — activated charcoal. PSP (saxitoxin): respiratory support + ventilation as needed; no antidote. NSP (brevetoxin): bronchodilators, supportive. ASP (domoic acid): supportive care, benzodiazepines for seizures. DSP (okadaic acid): fluid/electrolyte replacement.
us_prevalence: ~2,000–3,000 shellfish poisoning cases/year in the US (CDC); underreported.
patients_needing_tx: ~1,000 require active medical management.
price: $5,000–$15,000 per episode for a novel saxitoxin antidote.
registrational_endpoint: Time to resolution of paralysis and respiratory support weaning vs. standard supportive care (saxitoxin challenge model).
btd_threshold_vs_soc: ≥30% faster recovery from paralysis vs. supportive care alone.

symptoms: Skin injury, pain, blistering, fluid loss, infection risk
treatment_goal: Prevent infection, restore skin barrier, minimize scarring
first_line_soc: Silver sulfadiazine (Silvadene) topical — antimicrobial agent for partial/full-thickness burns — or mafenide acetate; systemic antibiotics for infection.
treatment_algorithm: Fluid resuscitation (Parkland formula: 4 mL/kg/%TBSA in 24h). Topical antimicrobials: silver sulfadiazine, mafenide, silver-containing dressings (Mepilex Ag). Analgesics (IV opioids, ketamine). Nutritional support (early enteral feeding). Surgical debridement + skin grafting. Biological skin substitutes (Integra, Apligraf). Infection monitoring with targeted antibiotics. Itching management: antihistamines, gabapentin.
us_prevalence: ~450,000 burn injuries/year seeking medical care; ~40,000 hospitalizations; ~3,400 fire/burn deaths (ABA 2025 data).
patients_needing_tx: ~100,000 require significant pharmacologic management; ~40,000 require hospital-level care with multiple agents.
price: $20,000–$100,000 per admission for a novel burn regeneration biologic (e.g., recombinant growth factor or cell therapy).
registrational_endpoint: Time to complete wound closure, graft take rate, and infection rate at 28 days vs. standard SSD + grafting.
btd_threshold_vs_soc: ≥20% improvement in time to wound closure or ≥30% reduction in infection rate vs. SoC.

symptoms: Cough, hoarseness, stridor, singed facial hair, carbon deposits in sputum, hypoxia
treatment_goal: Secure airway, treat CO poisoning, prevent ARDS
first_line_soc: 100% high-flow O2 via NRB mask; hydroxocobalamin (Cyanokit) if cyanide co-exposure; intubation for airway edema.
treatment_algorithm: 100% O2 (COHb elimination). Early intubation for airway edema (stridor, hoarseness). Hydroxocobalamin (Cyanokit 5g IV) for suspected HCN poisoning (fire victims). Bronchodilators (albuterol) for bronchospasm. Nebulized heparin + acetylcysteine (Mucomyst) for cast formation. HBO therapy for severe CO poisoning. Steroids NOT recommended for acute lung injury in inhalation.
us_prevalence: ~40,000–50,000 hospitalizations/year from smoke inhalation (CDC/ABA); occurs in ~80% of fire-related deaths.
patients_needing_tx: ~30,000 require active pharmacologic management (O2, bronchodilators, antidotes).
price: $5,000–$15,000 per episode for a novel airway-protective/antidote agent.
registrational_endpoint: Duration of mechanical ventilation and 30-day mortality vs. standard O2 ± hydroxocobalamin.
btd_threshold_vs_soc: ≥1 day reduction in mechanical ventilation or ≥20% reduction in 30-day mortality vs. current SoC.

symptoms: Burns, arrhythmia, muscle necrosis, rhabdomyolysis, neurologic deficits
treatment_goal: Manage cardiac arrhythmias, treat burns, prevent renal failure
first_line_soc: None pharmacologic — cardiac monitoring; IV fluids for rhabdomyolysis with aggressive hydration to maintain urine output >1 mL/kg/hr; sodium bicarbonate to prevent myoglobin precipitation.
treatment_algorithm: None — aggressive IV fluid resuscitation (LR 0.5–1 L/hr). Urinary alkalinization (NaHCO3) for myoglobinuria. Cardiac monitoring (24–48 hours). Antiarrhythmic (amiodarone) for persistent arrhythmias. Wound care + grafting for electrical burns. Fasciotomy for compartment syndrome.
us_prevalence: ~30,000 electrical injuries/year in the US; ~1,000 deaths/year (NSC data).
patients_needing_tx: ~15,000 require active pharmacologic + surgical management.
price: $5,000–$20,000 per episode for a novel rhabdomyolysis treatment.
registrational_endpoint: Peak creatinine/renal injury rate at 72 hours vs. standard aggressive IV fluid protocol.
btd_threshold_vs_soc: ≥30% reduction in acute kidney injury rate vs. standard IV fluid resuscitation.

symptoms: Cardiac arrest, burns, neurologic deficits, tympanic membrane rupture
treatment_goal: Restore cardiac rhythm, treat burns, support neurologic recovery
first_line_soc: None pharmacologic — CPR for cardiac arrest (highest priority); ALS (epinephrine, amiodarone) for refractory arrhythmias; standard trauma management.
treatment_algorithm: None — CPR for cardiac arrest (reverse triage: resuscitate arrested patients first). ACLS (epinephrine, amiodarone) for VF/VT. Wound management for flash burns. Cataract monitoring. Neurologic rehabilitation for cognitive/neuropathic sequelae.
us_prevalence: ~2,000 lightning strikes/year in the US; ~300–400 deaths/year (NOAA data); ~1,500 non-fatal injuries.
patients_needing_tx: ~1,500 require active management.
price: $5,000–$20,000 per episode for a novel neuroprotective agent.
registrational_endpoint: Neurologic outcome (mRS) at 90 days vs. standard supportive care.
btd_threshold_vs_soc: ≥20% improvement in mRS 0–2 rate vs. standard ACLS support.

symptoms: Deformity, severe pain, loss of function at joint
treatment_goal: Restore joint alignment, prevent avascular necrosis, preserve function
first_line_soc: None pharmacologic — closed reduction under procedural sedation (ketamine, propofol, or midazolam/fentanyl) is the primary treatment.
treatment_algorithm: None — procedural sedation + analgesia for closed reduction. Specific techniques per joint (Cunningham for shoulder, Stimson for hip). Post-reduction immobilization (sling, splint). NSAIDs/opioids for pain. Imaging post-reduction to confirm alignment. Surgery for irreducible or recurrent dislocations.
us_prevalence: ~1.5–2 million joint dislocations/year in the US (shoulder ~50%, finger ~35%, others); NEISS data.
patients_needing_tx: ~1.5 million require pharmacologic sedation/analgesia for reduction.
