price: $500–$2,000 per episode for a novel procedural sedation agent.
registrational_endpoint: Reduction success rate, time to reduction, and Operator/Patient Satisfaction Score vs. standard ketamine sedation.
btd_threshold_vs_soc: ≥95% reduction success with faster onset and improved safety profile vs. standard ketamine sedation.

symptoms: Knee deformity, severe pain, visible patella displaced laterally, inability to walk
treatment_goal: Reduce patella, stabilize, prevent recurrent dislocation
first_line_soc: None pharmacologic — closed reduction (knee extension); MPFL reconstruction for recurrent dislocation; NSAIDs for pain.
treatment_algorithm: None — closed reduction under analgesia. Knee immobilizer × 3–4 weeks. PT for VMO strengthening. MPFL (medial patellofemoral ligament) reconstruction for recurrent dislocators or trochlear dysplasia. Tibial tubercle osteotomy for TT-TG >20 mm.
us_prevalence: ~100,000–150,000 patellar dislocations/year in the US; higher incidence in adolescent females.
patients_needing_tx: ~100,000 require active management; ~30,000 undergo MPFL reconstruction.
price: $5,000–$15,000 per biologic stabilization agent.
registrational_endpoint: Recurrent dislocation rate and Kujala score at 24 months vs. standard MPFL reconstruction.
btd_threshold_vs_soc: ≥25% reduction in recurrent dislocation rate vs. standard surgical care at 24 months.

symptoms: Severe shoulder pain, deformity, arm held in fixed position, inability to move
treatment_goal: Reduce dislocation, relieve pain, prevent recurrence
first_line_soc: None pharmacologic — closed reduction (Cunningham, FARES, Stimson techniques) with procedural sedation; sling immobilization; NSAIDs.
treatment_algorithm: None — procedural sedation (ketamine or propofol) for closed reduction. Post-reduction sling × 3–6 weeks. PT for rotator cuff and stabilizer strengthening. Arthroscopic Bankart repair for recurrent dislocators (>2 events or high-demand athletes). Latarjet procedure for bone loss >20%.
us_prevalence: ~200,000 shoulder dislocations/year in the US; highest rate in young athletic males.
patients_needing_tx: ~200,000 require reduction; ~30,000–50,000 undergo surgical stabilization.
price: $3,000–$8,000 per course for a novel capsulolabral healing biologic.
registrational_endpoint: Recurrent dislocation rate and WOSI (Western Ontario Shoulder Instability) score at 24 months vs. standard Bankart repair.
btd_threshold_vs_soc: ≥25% reduction in recurrent dislocation vs. standard Bankart repair at 2 years.

symptoms: Severe hip pain, leg internally/externally rotated, inability to walk
treatment_goal: Restore hip anatomy, prevent AVN, preserve function
first_line_soc: None pharmacologic — emergent closed reduction under deep sedation; early reduction (<6 hours) critical to prevent AVN.
treatment_algorithm: None — emergent closed reduction under IV sedation (propofol or ketamine). CT post-reduction to assess acetabular fractures. Protected weight bearing × 4–6 weeks. NSAIDs/opioids. Surgery for irreducible dislocations or femoral head fractures.
us_prevalence: ~25,000–35,000 hip dislocations/year in the US (MVAs most common cause); AAOS data.
patients_needing_tx: ~30,000 require emergent management.
price: $5,000–$15,000 per episode.
registrational_endpoint: Avascular necrosis rate at 2 years and HOOS score at 12 months vs. standard closed reduction.
btd_threshold_vs_soc: ≥30% reduction in AVN rate vs. standard reduction protocol.

symptoms: Elbow pain, deformity, inability to bend arm, swelling
treatment_goal: Restore joint alignment, preserve stability and function
first_line_soc: None pharmacologic — closed reduction under procedural sedation; NSAIDs post-reduction.
treatment_algorithm: None — procedural sedation (ketamine or propofol) for closed reduction. Post-reduction splinting for 1–2 weeks. NSAIDs. PT for rehabilitation. Surgery for irreducible dislocations or associated fractures (terrible triad: radial head + coronoid + LCL).
us_prevalence: ~100,000–150,000 elbow dislocations/year in the US; second most common large joint dislocation.
patients_needing_tx: ~100,000 require procedural management + pharmacologic pain control.
price: $1,000–$3,000 per episode.
registrational_endpoint: ROM and MEPS (Mayo Elbow Performance Score) at 3 months vs. standard closed reduction.
btd_threshold_vs_soc: ≥15-point MEPS improvement vs. standard closed reduction.

symptoms: Severe knee pain, gross deformity, vascular compromise, multi-ligament injury
treatment_goal: Restore vascular perfusion, reduce dislocation, repair ligaments
first_line_soc: None pharmacologic — emergent closed reduction; vascular surgery for popliteal artery injury (~30–40% cases); surgical multi-ligament reconstruction.
treatment_algorithm: None — emergent closed reduction under sedation. Vascular assessment (ABI, CT angio if ABI <0.9). Popliteal artery repair if injured. Multi-ligament reconstruction: staged (acute + 2 weeks) or chronic. DVT prophylaxis. NSAIDs/opioids.
us_prevalence: ~5,000–10,000 knee dislocations/year in the US (high-energy MVA and sports).
patients_needing_tx: ~8,000 require emergent + surgical management.
price: $10,000–$30,000 per episode for a novel ligament/vascular repair biologic.
registrational_endpoint: IKDC score and amputation rate at 12 months vs. standard reduction + vascular/ligament repair.
btd_threshold_vs_soc: ≥20% improvement in IKDC score and ≥30% reduction in vascular complications vs. SoC.

symptoms: Finger deformity, pain, swelling, inability to bend digit
treatment_goal: Restore alignment, protect ligaments, preserve function
first_line_soc: None pharmacologic — digital block anesthesia (lidocaine) + closed reduction; buddy taping; NSAIDs for pain.
treatment_algorithm: None — digital nerve block with 1–2% lidocaine. Closed reduction. Buddy taping + splinting for 2–3 weeks. NSAIDs. Surgery for irreducible or complex fracture-dislocations.
us_prevalence: ~1 million finger dislocations/year in the US (NEISS; most common dislocation).
patients_needing_tx: ~500,000 seek medical care; ~300,000 require pharmacologic management.
price: $200–$500 per episode.
registrational_endpoint: DASH score and ROM at 4 weeks vs. standard digital block + reduction.
btd_threshold_vs_soc: ≥10-point improvement in DASH score or ≥20% faster return to full ROM.

symptoms: Wrist pain, swelling, numbness, loss of grip, visible deformity
treatment_goal: Restore carpal alignment, prevent avascular necrosis
first_line_soc: None pharmacologic — emergent closed/open reduction plus surgical ligament repair (ORIF).
treatment_algorithm: None — emergent reduction (closed under conscious sedation or open). ORIF with K-wires and ligament repair. Cast immobilization 8–12 weeks. PT for wrist ROM. Long-term monitoring for avascular necrosis of lunate.
us_prevalence: ~5,000–7,000 perilunate/lunate dislocations/year in the US (rare, ~1% of all wrist injuries).
patients_needing_tx: ~6,000 require surgical intervention annually.
price: $3,000–$8,000 per course for a novel avascular necrosis–preventive biologic.
registrational_endpoint: Rate of avascular necrosis at 24 months and wrist DASH score vs. standard ORIF.
btd_threshold_vs_soc: ≥40% reduction in avascular necrosis incidence vs. standard care.

symptoms: Elbow pain in toddlers, arm held semiflexed, refused use of arm
treatment_goal: Reduce subluxation, restore full arm use immediately
first_line_soc: None pharmacologic — closed reduction (hyperpronation or supination-flexion technique) by clinician.
treatment_algorithm: None — closed reduction (hyperpronation technique preferred): immediate resolution in >95% of cases. No immobilization usually needed. Parental education on prevention.
us_prevalence: ~250,000 'nursemaid's elbow' cases/year in the US in children age 1–4 years.
patients_needing_tx: ~250,000 require ED evaluation and reduction annually.
price: N/A — no drug role; minimal commercial opportunity.
peak_sales: N/A — procedural management, no pharmacologic market.

symptoms: Pain, vision loss, hemorrhage, diplopia, foreign body sensation
treatment_goal: Preserve vision, repair injury, prevent infection
first_line_soc: None pharmacologic — emergent ophthalmologic evaluation; antibiotics for open globe injuries; cycloplegics for traumatic iritis.
treatment_algorithm: None — hyphema: topical cycloplegics (atropine 1%), corticosteroids (prednisolone acetate), aminocaproic acid to prevent rebleed. Open globe: emergent surgical repair + IV antibiotics (cefazolin + gentamicin). Retinal detachment: surgical. Chemical burns: immediate copious irrigation then ophthalmology.
us_prevalence: ~2.5 million eye injuries/year in the US; ~800,000 ED visits (NEI/NEISS data).
patients_needing_tx: ~500,000 require prescription medications; ~50,000 require surgical intervention.
price: $500–$5,000 per episode for a novel neuroprotective or anti-fibrotic ocular agent.
registrational_endpoint: Best-corrected visual acuity (BCVA) at 90 days vs. standard treatment for specific injury type.
btd_threshold_vs_soc: ≥2 line improvement in BCVA vs. standard treatment at 90 days.

symptoms: Severe eye pain, vision loss, hypotony, hemorrhage, visible wound
treatment_goal: Emergent surgical repair, prevent endophthalmitis, preserve vision
first_line_soc: None pharmacologic — emergent surgical repair; IV antibiotics (cefazolin + gentamicin or fluoroquinolone) for prophylaxis against endophthalmitis.
treatment_algorithm: None — emergent primary surgical repair. Prophylactic antibiotics (IV fluoroquinolone or cefazolin + gentamicin). Eye shield (no pressure). Tetanus prophylaxis. Post-op corticosteroids for inflammation. VEGF inhibitors for vitreous hemorrhage/traction retinal detachment (adjunct).
us_prevalence: ~50,000–100,000 open globe injuries/year in the US (NEISS/NEI data); leading cause of monocular blindness.
patients_needing_tx: ~75,000 require surgical + pharmacologic management.
price: $5,000–$20,000 per episode for a novel neuroprotective ocular agent.
registrational_endpoint: BCVA at 12 months and endophthalmitis rate at 30 days vs. standard surgical repair + antibiotics.
btd_threshold_vs_soc: ≥50% reduction in endophthalmitis rate and ≥2-line BCVA improvement vs. SoC.

symptoms: Severe eye pain, conjunctival blanching, corneal opacity, vision loss
treatment_goal: Immediate irrigation, restore ocular surface, prevent blindness
first_line_soc: Immediate copious eye irrigation (water/saline/Morgan lens) — most critical first step; topical steroids (dexamethasone) + antibiotics + ascorbic acid for chemical burns.
treatment_algorithm: Immediate irrigation ≥20–30 min to pH 7.0. Topical corticosteroids (dexamethasone 0.1% q1–2h initially). Topical ascorbic acid (10% q2h for alkali burns). Topical antibiotics. Cycloplegics for pain. Amniotic membrane transplant for severe Roper-Hall Grade III–IV. VEGF inhibitors, mitomycin-C for corneal vascularization. Keratoplasty for end-stage corneal damage.
us_prevalence: ~50,000–70,000 significant ocular burns/year in the US (chemical + thermal) — NEI data.
patients_needing_tx: ~50,000 require prescription pharmacologic treatment; ~5,000 require surgical intervention.
price: $2,000–$20,000 per episode for a novel corneal regenerative agent.
registrational_endpoint: BCVA and corneal re-epithelialization rate at 28 days vs. standard irrigation + corticosteroid + antibiotic.
btd_threshold_vs_soc: ≥30% higher rate of complete corneal re-epithelialization at 14 days vs. standard treatment.

symptoms: Eye pain, photophobia, blurred vision, red eye after trauma
treatment_goal: Reduce intraocular inflammation, prevent synechiae, protect vision
first_line_soc: Topical corticosteroids (prednisolone acetate 1%) and cycloplegic agents (cyclopentolate, atropine) for ciliary spasm.
treatment_algorithm: Step 1: Topical prednisolone acetate 1% q1h initially, tapering over weeks. Cycloplegic (cyclopentolate 1% or atropine 1% for severe cases). Step 2: Topical NSAIDs (ketorolac) adjunctively. Step 3: Periocular/systemic corticosteroids for severe cases. Monitor for secondary glaucoma (IOP checks).
us_prevalence: ~200,000–300,000 cases of traumatic iridocyclitis annually, associated with blunt or penetrating eye trauma.
patients_needing_tx: ~200,000 require topical steroid + cycloplegic treatment.
price: $500–$2,000 per course for a novel topical anti-inflammatory.
registrational_endpoint: Anterior chamber cell grade reduction at 2 weeks vs. prednisolone acetate.
btd_threshold_vs_soc: ≥1 step improvement in anterior chamber cell grade superior to prednisolone acetate 1% at 2 weeks.

disease: Corneal Abrasions and Corneal Foreign Bodies
symptoms: Eye pain, tearing, photophobia, foreign body sensation, blurred vision
treatment_goal: Remove foreign body, promote healing, prevent infection
first_line_soc: None pharmacologic — topical anesthetics for initial exam (proparacaine), antibiotic eye drops (fluoroquinolone or trimethoprim/polymyxin B) to prevent secondary infection.
treatment_algorithm: None — removal of foreign body (irrigation/slit-lamp). Topical antibiotics (erythromycin ointment, trimethoprim/polymyxin B, or fluoroquinolone drops). NSAIDs topical (ketorolac 0.5%) for pain. Cycloplegics for significant iridospasm. Avoid patching (delays healing). Follow-up in 24 hours.
us_prevalence: ~1–2 million corneal abrasion ED visits/year in the US (NEISS data); one of the most common eye emergencies.
patients_needing_tx: ~1 million require prescription topical antibiotics or NSAIDs.
price: $50–$300 for a novel accelerated corneal healing drop.
registrational_endpoint: Time to corneal re-epithelialization at 24–48 hours vs. topical antibiotic alone.
btd_threshold_vs_soc: ≥30% faster re-epithelialization vs. standard antibiotic drops.

symptoms: Eyelid swelling, bruising, bleeding, ptosis, laceration
treatment_goal: Repair integrity, restore function, prevent scarring
first_line_soc: None pharmacologic — wound closure (sutures); antibiotics (erythromycin ointment or oral amoxicillin-clavulanate) for contaminated wounds.
treatment_algorithm: None — contusions: ice, observation. Lacerations: careful repair preserving lid margin integrity; ophthalmology consultation for canalicular/canthal involvement. Topical antibiotic ointment post-repair.
us_prevalence: ~500,000 eyelid injuries/year in the US (NEISS data).
patients_needing_tx: ~200,000 require prescription topical antibiotics or surgical repair.
price: $200–$500 per episode.
registrational_endpoint: Wound healing at 2 weeks and scar grading at 3 months vs. standard closure + topical antibiotic.
btd_threshold_vs_soc: ≥30% improvement in cosmetic scar score at 3 months.

symptoms: Facial pain, swelling, deformity, altered vision, airway compromise
treatment_goal: Maintain airway, restore facial anatomy, prevent infection
first_line_soc: None pharmacologic — definitive management is surgical (ORIF); NSAIDs/opioids for pain; prophylactic antibiotics (amoxicillin-clavulanate) for open fractures.
treatment_algorithm: None — airway management (intubation/cric if needed). ORIF for displaced facial fractures (zygoma, mandible, orbit, NOE). Prophylactic antibiotics. Analgesics. Ophthalmology for orbital injuries. Cosmetic reconstruction as secondary procedure.
us_prevalence: ~3–4 million facial trauma events/year; ~500,000 require hospitalization (NTDB).
patients_needing_tx: ~1 million require prescription treatment (analgesics, antibiotics, steroids).
price: $5,000–$20,000 for a novel bone-healing or soft-tissue repair adjunct.
registrational_endpoint: Facial symmetry score (FACE-Q) and time to union at 12 weeks vs. standard ORIF.
btd_threshold_vs_soc: ≥15% improvement in FACE-Q score and ≥25% faster union vs. SoC.

disease: Fractures of the Mandible and Midface
symptoms: Facial pain, malocclusion, numbness, swelling, trismus
treatment_goal: Restore occlusion, repair fractures, prevent infection
first_line_soc: None pharmacologic — ORIF with titanium plates/screws for displaced fractures; amoxicillin-clavulanate prophylaxis for open fractures.
treatment_algorithm: None — prophylactic antibiotics (penicillin or amoxicillin-clavulanate for contaminated/open fractures). ORIF under general anesthesia. Liquid diet during healing. NSAIDs/opioids for pain. Maxillomandibular fixation (MMF) for condylar fractures in some cases.
us_prevalence: ~200,000–300,000 mandible/midface fractures/year in the US (MVAs, assaults, falls).
patients_needing_tx: ~250,000 require pharmacologic management (antibiotics, analgesics).
price: $5,000–$15,000 for a bone regeneration adjunct.
registrational_endpoint: Occlusion score and FACE-Q at 6 weeks vs. standard ORIF.
btd_threshold_vs_soc: ≥15% improvement in occlusion/FACE-Q vs. standard ORIF.

symptoms: Nasal pain, swelling, deformity, epistaxis, nasal obstruction
treatment_goal: Restore nasal alignment, control bleeding, prevent cosmetic deformity
first_line_soc: None pharmacologic — closed reduction under local/sedation for displaced fractures; NSAIDs for pain; epistaxis management.
treatment_algorithm: None — ice and NSAIDs for 3–5 days pre-reduction. Closed reduction at 5–10 days post-injury (after swelling subsides). Formal rhinoplasty 6 months post if persistent deformity. Septal hematoma: incision and drainage to prevent septal abscess.
us_prevalence: ~500,000–800,000 nasal fractures/year in the US (most common facial fracture; AAOS data).
patients_needing_tx: ~400,000 require pharmacologic management + reduction.
price: $1,000–$5,000 per episode for a novel anti-swelling/healing agent.
registrational_endpoint: Cosmetic outcome (FACE-Q nasal score) and airway patency at 6 weeks vs. standard reduction.
btd_threshold_vs_soc: ≥20% improvement in FACE-Q nasal score vs. standard closed reduction.

symptoms: Ear pain, swelling, hematoma (cauliflower ear), lacerations
treatment_goal: Drain hematoma, repair lacerations, prevent deformity
first_line_soc: None pharmacologic — needle aspiration or incision/drainage of auricular hematoma; antibiotics (amoxicillin-clavulanate) for infected wounds.
treatment_algorithm: None — hematoma: aspiration + pressure dressing within 48–72 hours. Lacerations: irrigation + closure. Antibiotics for infected injuries (amoxicillin-clavulanate). Analgesics (NSAIDs). ENT follow-up for complex injuries.
us_prevalence: ~200,000–400,000 external ear injuries/year in the US (sports + trauma).
patients_needing_tx: ~100,000 require pharmacologic treatment (antibiotics ± analgesics).
price: $500–$2,000 per episode.
registrational_endpoint: Hematoma recurrence rate and cosmetic outcome at 4 weeks vs. standard aspiration + compression.
btd_threshold_vs_soc: ≥40% reduction in hematoma recurrence vs. standard treatment.

symptoms: Hearing loss, hemotympanum, facial nerve palsy, Battle's sign, CSF otorrhea
treatment_goal: Preserve hearing, treat facial nerve injury, prevent meningitis
first_line_soc: None pharmacologic — surgical decompression for facial nerve palsy; observation for CSF leak; prophylactic antibiotics controversial.
treatment_algorithm: None — serial audiology. Longitudinal fractures: observation; ORIF for ossicular chain disruption. Transverse: immediate facial nerve exploration if complete palsy. CSF otorrhea: bed rest (most seal spontaneously). Prophylactic antibiotics for meningitis prevention controversial (not universally recommended). Steroids for incomplete facial palsy.
us_prevalence: ~10,000–20,000 temporal bone fractures/year in the US (from head trauma).
patients_needing_tx: ~5,000 require active neurological/surgical management.
price: $5,000–$15,000 per course for a novel neuroprotective agent for facial nerve recovery.
registrational_endpoint: House-Brackmann facial nerve grade recovery at 12 months vs. standard observation/decompression.
btd_threshold_vs_soc: ≥30% improvement in House-Brackmann grade vs. standard decompression alone.

symptoms: Pain, swelling, deformity, inability to use affected limb
treatment_goal: Restore anatomy, achieve union, restore function
first_line_soc: None pharmacologic — depends on fracture type (see specific fracture entries); NSAIDs/analgesics for pain; teriparatide or bisphosphonates for osteoporotic fracture prevention.
treatment_algorithm: None — broad category. Management by fracture site, displacement, patient age. Pain: NSAIDs, acetaminophen, opioids. Osteoporotic fractures: bisphosphonates (alendronate), teriparatide, denosumab for secondary prevention. DVT prophylaxis for major fractures. Surgical vs. conservative based on stability.
us_prevalence: ~6–7 million fractures/year in the US (all types combined; AAOS 2025 data).
patients_needing_tx: ~5 million require pharmacologic management; ~2 million undergo surgical fixation.
price: $5,000–$30,000 per treatment for a novel bone-healing biologic (across all fracture types).
registrational_endpoint: Time to radiographic union and functional outcome score at 12 weeks vs. standard surgical/conservative treatment.
btd_threshold_vs_soc: ≥25% faster time to union vs. current SoC in target fracture type.

symptoms: Acute back pain, height loss, kyphotic deformity, limited mobility
treatment_goal: Control pain, restore vertebral height, prevent further collapse
first_line_soc: Analgesics (acetaminophen + NSAIDs); calcitonin (salmon calcitonin nasal spray, Miacalcin) for acute pain. Vertebroplasty/kyphoplasty for refractory cases.
treatment_algorithm: Step 1: Bed rest, analgesics (acetaminophen, NSAIDs, short-term opioids). Salmon calcitonin (Miacalcin 200 IU nasal spray) for acute pain (analgesic effect). Step 2: Bracing. Step 3: Vertebroplasty or balloon kyphoplasty (cement augmentation) for persistent pain. Step 4: Teriparatide (Forteo) or romosozumab (Evenity) for underlying osteoporosis prevention. Bisphosphonates for long-term prevention.
us_prevalence: ~700,000–1 million vertebral compression fractures/year in the US; primarily osteoporotic in elderly women.
patients_needing_tx: ~500,000 require prescription analgesic/hormonal management; ~150,000 undergo vertebroplasty/kyphoplasty.
price: $5,000–$20,000 per course for a novel bone anabolic agent with acute pain relief.
registrational_endpoint: VAS back pain at 1 month and vertebral height restoration (imaging) vs. analgesics ± kyphoplasty.
btd_threshold_vs_soc: ≥30% reduction in VAS pain and ≥2 mm vertebral height restoration vs. standard analgesics at 1 month.

symptoms: Sharp chest pain worsened by breathing, tenderness, splinting
treatment_goal: Adequate pain control, prevent pneumonia, restore breathing
first_line_soc: Multimodal analgesia: oral NSAIDs + acetaminophen; intercostal nerve blocks (bupivacaine); epidural analgesia for multiple rib fractures.
treatment_algorithm: Step 1: NSAIDs + acetaminophen. Step 2: Intercostal nerve block or PECS/serratus plane blocks. Step 3: Epidural or paravertebral block for multiple (≥4) fractures. Step 4: Rib fixation surgery (ORIF) for flail chest or severely displaced fractures. Incentive spirometry. Low-dose naltrexone investigated. IV lidocaine infusion for hospitalized patients.
us_prevalence: ~300,000–400,000 rib fractures/year in the US; majority from falls and MVAs.
patients_needing_tx: ~300,000 require analgesic management; ~50,000 require regional anesthesia or hospitalization.
price: $1,000–$5,000 per course for a novel analgesic/healing agent.
registrational_endpoint: VAS pain score at rest and with movement, and pulmonary function (FVC) at 72 hours vs. standard multimodal analgesia.
btd_threshold_vs_soc: ≥30% reduction in VAS pain score or ≥15% improvement in FVC vs. standard analgesia.

symptoms: Pelvic pain, instability, inability to bear weight, hemorrhage
treatment_goal: Control hemorrhage, stabilize pelvis, prevent mortality
first_line_soc: None pharmacologic — emergent pelvic binder/external fixation for hemorrhage control; angioembolization; ORIF for definitive fixation.
treatment_algorithm: None — ATLS protocol. Pelvic binder for ring disruption. Angiographic embolization for arterial bleeding. Packing for venous hemorrhage. ORIF with iliosacral screws/plating after stabilization. TXA for hemorrhage. ICU supportive care.
us_prevalence: ~100,000 pelvic fractures/year; high-energy fractures account for ~15,000–20,000 hospitalizations with significant mortality risk.
patients_needing_tx: ~20,000 high-energy pelvic fractures require aggressive hemostatic and surgical management.
price: $10,000–$25,000 per episode for a novel hemostatic/anti-inflammatory agent.
registrational_endpoint: 28-day mortality and need for blood transfusion (units pRBC) vs. standard resuscitation.
btd_threshold_vs_soc: ≥25% relative reduction in 28-day mortality vs. standard care.

symptoms: Hip/groin pain, inability to walk, shortened/externally rotated leg
treatment_goal: Surgical fixation within 24–48 hours, restore mobility, prevent death
first_line_soc: None pharmacologic — surgical fixation (hemiarthroplasty for femoral neck; ORIF for intertrochanteric) is definitive; bisphosphonates/denosumab for secondary osteoporosis prevention.
treatment_algorithm: None — ORIF (DHS or cephalomedullary nail) for intertrochanteric fractures. Hemiarthroplasty or THA for displaced femoral neck fractures. Pre-op: nerve block (FICB/PENG) for pain. DVT prophylaxis (enoxaparin). Post-op: calcium + vitamin D, bisphosphonates (alendronate), physical therapy. Teriparatide for refractory osteoporosis.
us_prevalence: ~300,000 hip fractures/year in the US; 1-year mortality ~25% (AAOS 2025).
patients_needing_tx: ~300,000 require surgical + pharmacologic management; ~200,000 started on anti-osteoporotic therapy post-fracture.
price: $15,000–$50,000 per episode for a novel fracture repair or bone-regenerative biologic.
registrational_endpoint: 1-year mortality, mobility restoration (Parker Mobility Score), and TUG test at 6 months vs. standard ORIF/hemiarthroplasty.
btd_threshold_vs_soc: ≥20% reduction in 1-year mortality and ≥15% improvement in mobility vs. SoC.

symptoms: Pain, swelling, bruising, inability to bear weight
treatment_goal: Restore alignment, achieve union, regain function
first_line_soc: None pharmacologic — management is operative ORIF for unstable fractures or cast immobilization for stable fractures; NSAIDs/acetaminophen for pain.
treatment_algorithm: None — stable fractures: walking boot or short-leg cast 4–6 weeks. Unstable/bimalleolar/trimalleolar: ORIF with plates/screws. Anticoagulation (LMWH) for DVT prophylaxis post-op. NSAIDs/opioids for acute pain. PT for rehabilitation.
us_prevalence: ~1.25 million ankle fractures/year in the US (incidence ~180/100,000; J Orthop Trauma estimates).
patients_needing_tx: ~600,000 require operative or significant pharmacologic management; ~400,000 undergo ORIF.
price: $3,000–$8,000 per episode for a bone-healing biologic or pharmacologic accelerant.
registrational_endpoint: Time to radiographic union and full weight bearing at 12 weeks vs. standard care.
btd_threshold_vs_soc: ≥25% faster time to union vs. standard immobilization.

symptoms: Thigh pain, deformity, inability to walk, swelling, shortening
treatment_goal: Achieve stable fixation, restore length and alignment, allow early mobilization
first_line_soc: None pharmacologic — intramedullary nailing (IMN) is gold standard; NSAIDs/opioids for pain; DVT prophylaxis with LMWH.
treatment_algorithm: None — antegrade IMN (titanium or reamed intramedullary nail). Temporary traction splint pre-op. DVT prophylaxis: enoxaparin 40 mg SC daily. NSAIDs/opioids. Blood transfusion if needed (femur fracture = 2–3 L blood loss potential). PT for rehabilitation.
us_prevalence: ~200,000 femoral shaft fractures/year in the US (AAOS data).
patients_needing_tx: ~200,000 require surgical + pharmacologic management.
price: $5,000–$15,000 per bone-healing adjunct.
registrational_endpoint: Time to full weight bearing and union rate at 16 weeks vs. standard IMN.
btd_threshold_vs_soc: ≥25% faster union with full weight bearing vs. IMN standard.

symptoms: Point tenderness, pain with weight-bearing activity, subtle on X-ray
treatment_goal: Allow healing, prevent complete fracture, return to activity safely
first_line_soc: Rest, activity modification, and protective weight-bearing; NSAIDs used cautiously (may impair healing).
treatment_algorithm: Activity modification/relative rest. Protective boot/brace. Calcium/vitamin D supplementation. Bisphosphonates for atypical femoral stress fractures or in high-risk populations. Teriparatide (Forteo) anabolic therapy investigational for accelerating healing. ORIF for high-risk anatomic sites (femoral neck, navicular, 5th metatarsal).
us_prevalence: ~500,000–1 million stress fractures/year in the US (athletes, military); females have 2× male incidence.
patients_needing_tx: ~500,000 require active management; ~50,000 require surgery or bisphosphonate therapy.
price: $1,000–$5,000 per course for a novel anabolic healing agent.
registrational_endpoint: Time to CT healing and return-to-activity at 12 weeks vs. standard rest.
btd_threshold_vs_soc: ≥30% faster CT-confirmed healing vs. standard activity modification.

symptoms: Foot pain at base of 5th toe, swelling, difficulty walking
treatment_goal: Achieve bony union, restore pain-free ambulation
first_line_soc: None pharmacologic — Zone 1 (avulsion): walking boot + NSAIDs; Zone 2 (Jones fracture): non-weight-bearing cast or ORIF; NSAIDs for pain.
treatment_algorithm: None — Zone 1: walking boot 4–6 weeks. Zone 2 (Jones): NWB short-leg cast or ORIF with intramedullary screw for athletes. Zone 3 (stress): similar to Jones + bone stimulation for delayed union. NSAIDs.
us_prevalence: ~100,000–150,000 fifth metatarsal fractures/year in the US.
patients_needing_tx: ~100,000 require active management; ~30,000 undergo ORIF.
price: $3,000–$8,000 per episode.
registrational_endpoint: Time to union on CT and AOFAS score at 12 weeks vs. standard cast/ORIF.
btd_threshold_vs_soc: ≥25% faster CT-confirmed union vs. standard treatment.

symptoms: Heel pain, swelling, inability to bear weight, bruising
treatment_goal: Restore heel anatomy, achieve union, return to walking
first_line_soc: None pharmacologic — displaced intra-articular fractures: ORIF or primary arthrodesis; undisplaced: cast immobilization + NSAIDs.
treatment_algorithm: None — non-displaced: cast/boot for 6–8 weeks + NSAIDs/acetaminophen. Displaced intra-articular: ORIF with plates and screws. Primary subtalar arthrodesis in severely comminuted cases. DVT prophylaxis (LMWH) post-op. Physical therapy.
us_prevalence: ~40,000–60,000 calcaneal fractures/year in the US (incidence ~0.3–0.5% of all fractures).
patients_needing_tx: ~50,000 require pharmacologic pain management and/or surgical bone-healing adjuncts.
price: $5,000–$15,000 for a novel bone healing/regeneration adjunct.
registrational_endpoint: Maryland Foot Score or AOFAS hindfoot score at 12 months vs. standard ORIF.
btd_threshold_vs_soc: ≥15-point improvement in AOFAS score vs. SoC at 12 months.

symptoms: Midfoot pain, swelling, bruising, inability to bear weight
treatment_goal: Restore midfoot alignment, achieve union, prevent arthritis
first_line_soc: None pharmacologic — stable injuries: cast; unstable/displaced: ORIF or primary arthrodesis; NSAIDs for pain.
treatment_algorithm: None — non-displaced (stable): non-weight-bearing cast 6–8 weeks + NSAIDs. Displaced: ORIF with screws/plates or primary arthrodesis (preferred for purely ligamentous injuries). DVT prophylaxis. PT for rehabilitation.
us_prevalence: ~30,000–40,000 Lisfranc injuries/year; diagnosis frequently missed (J Orthop Trauma data).
patients_needing_tx: ~30,000 require surgical or active management.
price: $5,000–$15,000 per episode.
registrational_endpoint: American Orthopaedic Foot and Ankle Society (AOFAS) score at 12 months vs. standard ORIF.
btd_threshold_vs_soc: ≥15-point AOFAS improvement vs. standard ORIF/arthrodesis.

symptoms: Toe pain, swelling, bruising, difficulty walking, deformity
treatment_goal: Realign fracture, prevent deformity, restore comfortable ambulation
first_line_soc: None pharmacologic — buddy taping and stiff-soled shoe; ORIF only for great toe displaced/intra-articular fractures.
treatment_algorithm: None — buddy taping for 3–6 weeks. Hard-soled shoe. NSAIDs for pain. Post-reduction X-ray. ORIF for great toe displaced fractures affecting articular surface or causing >2 mm step-off.
us_prevalence: ~500,000 toe fractures/year in the US.
patients_needing_tx: ~500,000 require management; only ~10,000 require prescription care.
price: N/A — no meaningful pharmacologic market.

symptoms: Wrist pain, swelling, deformity (dinner fork), limited function
treatment_goal: Restore wrist alignment, achieve union, regain grip
first_line_soc: None pharmacologic — stable fractures: cast immobilization; unstable/displaced: ORIF with volar locking plate; NSAIDs for pain.
treatment_algorithm: None — non-displaced: cast immobilization 4–6 weeks + NSAIDs. Displaced: ORIF volar locking plate (Synthes, Acumed). Dorsal approach for dorsal die-punch. Calcium + vitamin D supplementation for osteoporotic fractures. PT post-immobilization.
us_prevalence: ~640,000 distal radius fractures/year in the US — most common forearm fracture (AAOS/JBJS data).
patients_needing_tx: ~500,000 require pharmacologic management; ~200,000 undergo ORIF.
price: $3,000–$8,000 for bone-healing accelerator per episode.
registrational_endpoint: DASH score and time to radiographic union at 12 weeks vs. standard ORIF + cast.
btd_threshold_vs_soc: ≥25% faster union and ≥10-point DASH improvement vs. SoC.

symptoms: Shoulder pain, swelling, visible deformity, limited arm movement
treatment_goal: Achieve bony union, restore shoulder function
first_line_soc: None pharmacologic — midshaft clavicle fractures: conservative (sling for 4–6 weeks) or surgical (ORIF/intramedullary fixation); NSAIDs/opioids for pain.
treatment_algorithm: None — most managed conservatively: sling + NSAIDs/acetaminophen. Surgical (ORIF with plate/screw): displaced midshaft fractures in active patients. Distal clavicle fractures: conservative or hook plate fixation. PT after immobilization.
us_prevalence: ~450,000–600,000 clavicle fractures/year in the US (5–10% of all fractures; AAOS data).
patients_needing_tx: ~400,000 need pharmacologic pain management; ~150,000 require ORIF.
price: $3,000–$8,000 for a novel bone-healing adjunct per episode.
registrational_endpoint: Time to radiographic union and DASH score at 12 weeks vs. standard conservative/surgical treatment.
btd_threshold_vs_soc: ≥25% faster union rate and ≥10-point DASH improvement vs. SoC.

symptoms: Knuckle pain, swelling, deformity (boxer's fracture of 4th/5th MCN)
treatment_goal: Achieve union, restore grip and finger alignment
first_line_soc: None pharmacologic — conservative: ulnar gutter splint for 3–4 weeks (5th MCN up to 40° angulation acceptable); NSAIDs; ORIF for significant angulation or malrotation.
treatment_algorithm: None — conservative splinting for most 5th MCN fractures. ORIF (K-wires or plate) for unacceptable angulation (>20° 2nd/3rd MCN, >30–40° 4th/5th). NSAIDs for pain. Early protected ROM in 3–4 weeks.
us_prevalence: ~500,000–700,000 metacarpal fractures/year (most common hand fracture); 5th MCN ~35–40% of all (AAOS).
patients_needing_tx: ~400,000 require pharmacologic management.
price: $2,000–$5,000 per episode.
registrational_endpoint: Grip strength and DASH score at 6 weeks vs. standard ulnar gutter splinting.
btd_threshold_vs_soc: ≥15% improvement in grip strength vs. standard conservative management.

symptoms: Pain, swelling, tenderness at growth plate, limited motion
treatment_goal: Restore alignment, protect growth plate, prevent deformity
first_line_soc: None pharmacologic — reduction (closed or open) plus cast immobilization based on Salter-Harris classification.
treatment_algorithm: None — SH type I/II: closed reduction + casting; SH type III/IV: ORIF to restore articular surface; SH type V: cast immobilization. NSAIDs for pain. Growth monitoring to detect leg-length discrepancy.

patients_needing_tx: ~300,000 annually require active orthopedic management.
price: $2,000–$5,000 per course for a novel growth-plate protective biologic.
registrational_endpoint: Rate of growth arrest (physeal bar formation) and angular deformity at 2 years vs. standard management.
