symptoms: Shoulder pain, bruising, inability to raise arm, swelling
treatment_goal: Restore shoulder function, prevent avascular necrosis
first_line_soc: None pharmacologic — most managed nonoperatively (sling immobilization); ORIF or arthroplasty for displaced/complex fractures.
treatment_algorithm: None — nondisplaced/minimally displaced (majority): sling × 3–6 weeks + PT. Displaced/complex: ORIF with locking plate or intramedullary nail; hemiarthroplasty or reverse shoulder arthroplasty for elderly with comminuted head-splitting fractures. Bisphosphonates for osteoporosis prevention.
us_prevalence: ~100,000–120,000 proximal humeral fractures/year in the US; 3rd most common fracture in elderly.
patients_needing_tx: ~100,000 require active management; ~25,000–30,000 undergo surgical intervention.
price: $5,000–$15,000 per course for a novel anabolic bone agent.
registrational_endpoint: Constant-Murley shoulder score and fracture healing rate at 12 months vs. standard care.
btd_threshold_vs_soc: ≥10-point improvement in Constant-Murley score vs. standard management at 12 months.

symptoms: Elbow pain, tenderness, limited forearm rotation after fall
treatment_goal: Restore elbow function, prevent stiffness, maintain rotation
first_line_soc: None pharmacologic — type I: sling + early motion; type II/III: ORIF or radial head arthroplasty.
treatment_algorithm: None — Mason type I: sling × 1–2 weeks + early PT. Type II: aspiration of hemarthrosis + sling or ORIF. Type III: ORIF or radial head replacement (Evolent). Essex-Lopresti: stabilization of distal radioulnar joint. NSAIDs for pain.
us_prevalence: ~150,000–200,000 radial head fractures/year in the US; most common elbow fracture in adults.
patients_needing_tx: ~150,000 require active management; ~30,000 undergo ORIF or arthroplasty.
price: $3,000–$8,000 per biologic healing agent.
registrational_endpoint: Elbow ROM (pronation/supination arc) and Mayo Elbow Performance Score at 12 months vs. standard care.
btd_threshold_vs_soc: ≥15° improvement in total forearm rotation arc vs. standard management.

symptoms: Snuffbox tenderness, wrist pain with grip, delayed diagnosis common
treatment_goal: Achieve bony union, prevent avascular necrosis, restore wrist function
first_line_soc: None pharmacologic — nondisplaced: thumb spica cast 8–12 weeks; displaced/proximal pole: ORIF (Herbert screw).
treatment_algorithm: None — nondisplaced stable: thumb spica cast or ORIF (early return to activity). Proximal pole/displaced: ORIF with headless compression screw (Acutrak, Herbert). Delayed union/nonunion: bone graft + fixation. Avascular necrosis: vascularized bone graft. Bone stimulator (electromagnetic) for delayed union.
us_prevalence: ~100,000 scaphoid fractures/year in the US; most common carpal fracture.
patients_needing_tx: ~100,000 require active management; ~30,000–40,000 undergo ORIF.
price: $3,000–$8,000 per course for a novel bone healing agent.
registrational_endpoint: Time to union on CT at 12 weeks and wrist DASH score at 6 months vs. standard casting/ORIF.
btd_threshold_vs_soc: ≥25% faster time to union on CT vs. standard management.

symptoms: Elbow pain, swelling, deformity, limited range of motion
treatment_goal: Restore articular anatomy, preserve elbow function
first_line_soc: None pharmacologic — ORIF for displaced fractures; NSAIDs/opioids for pain. Total elbow arthroplasty for comminuted fractures in elderly.
treatment_algorithm: None — non-displaced: posterior splint + NSAIDs. Displaced: ORIF with plates (parallel plating preferred) or TEA in elderly. DVT prophylaxis. PT starting 2–3 weeks post-op.
us_prevalence: ~30,000–50,000 distal humeral fractures/year; bimodal (young males high-energy; elderly osteoporotic) — AAOS data.
patients_needing_tx: ~40,000 require surgical + pharmacologic management.
price: $5,000–$15,000 for bone healing adjunct.
registrational_endpoint: Mayo Elbow Performance Score and ROM at 12 months vs. standard ORIF.
btd_threshold_vs_soc: ≥15% improvement in elbow function score vs. ORIF alone.

symptoms: Elbow pain at tip, swelling, inability to extend elbow, palpable gap
treatment_goal: Restore extensor mechanism, achieve union, regain elbow extension
first_line_soc: None pharmacologic — non-displaced: cast immobilization; displaced: ORIF (tension band wiring or plate); NSAIDs for pain.
treatment_algorithm: None — non-displaced: long-arm splint 4–6 weeks + NSAIDs. Displaced: ORIF with TBW or precontoured plate. PT for ROM restoration. Hardware removal sometimes needed for symptomatic hardware.
us_prevalence: ~50,000–80,000 olecranon fractures/year in the US.
patients_needing_tx: ~60,000 require surgical or significant medical management.
price: $3,000–$8,000 for bone healing adjunct.
registrational_endpoint: MEPS (Mayo Elbow Performance Score) and elbow ROM at 6 months vs. standard ORIF.
btd_threshold_vs_soc: ≥15-point MEPS improvement vs. ORIF alone.

symptoms: Forearm pain, swelling, deformity, inability to rotate forearm
treatment_goal: Restore forearm alignment, rotation, and hand function
first_line_soc: None pharmacologic — isolated ulnar shaft (nightstick): long arm cast; both-bone fractures: ORIF with compression plate.
treatment_algorithm: None — isolated ulnar: long arm cast if <50% displaced; ORIF with compression plate if >50% displacement. Both-bone forearm fractures: ORIF with 3.5mm compression plates bilaterally. Pediatric: cast (remodeling expected). NSAIDs for pain.
us_prevalence: ~100,000 forearm shaft fractures/year in the US.
patients_needing_tx: ~50,000–60,000 require active management; ~40,000 undergo ORIF.
price: $3,000–$8,000 per course for a novel bone healing agent.
registrational_endpoint: Time to radiographic union and DASH score at 6 months vs. standard ORIF.
btd_threshold_vs_soc: ≥4-week faster time to union on X-ray vs. standard ORIF.

symptoms: Fingertip pain, swelling, subungual hematoma, nail bed injury
treatment_goal: Protect injury, relieve hematoma pressure, prevent deformity
first_line_soc: None pharmacologic — trephination for subungual hematoma; splinting; NSAIDs for pain.
treatment_algorithm: None — nail trephination (18-gauge needle or cautery) for subungual hematoma. Protective splinting for 2–4 weeks. NSAIDs/acetaminophen. Nail bed repair under digital block if laceration. Antibiotic prophylaxis for open injuries.
us_prevalence: ~500,000–1,000,000 fingertip injuries/year (one of the most common hand injuries; AAOS data).
patients_needing_tx: ~300,000 require pharmacologic management (analgesics, antibiotics).
price: $200–$500 per episode.
registrational_endpoint: Nail bed healing and range of motion at 6 weeks vs. standard splinting.
btd_threshold_vs_soc: ≥30% faster nail bed healing vs. standard care.

symptoms: Tissue disruption, bleeding, infection risk, variable depth and contamination
treatment_goal: Achieve wound closure, prevent infection, promote healing
first_line_soc: None pharmacologic — wound irrigation, debridement, primary/secondary closure; topical antiseptics; systemic antibiotics for infected wounds.
treatment_algorithm: None — irrigation (NS or tap water). Debridement. Primary closure (sutures/staples/glue) for clean wounds. Secondary intention for infected or contaminated wounds. Topical antibiotics (mupirocin, silver sulfadiazine for burns). Systemic antibiotics (cephalexin for infected wounds, amoxicillin-clavulanate for bites). NPWT (negative pressure wound therapy) for complex wounds. Biologics (becaplermin gel for diabetic wounds).
us_prevalence: ~30 million acute wound cases/year in the US (all causes); ~8 million require ED management.
patients_needing_tx: ~2 million require prescription antibiotics or specialty wound care.
price: $500–$3,000 per course for a novel wound healing agent.
registrational_endpoint: Complete wound closure rate at 28 days and infection rate vs. standard wound care.
btd_threshold_vs_soc: ≥20% higher complete closure rate and ≥30% lower infection rate vs. standard wound care at 28 days.

symptoms: Skin wound, bleeding, pain, infection risk
treatment_goal: Achieve hemostasis, close wound, prevent infection
first_line_soc: None pharmacologic — wound irrigation, closure (sutures/staples/glue); topical antibiotics (mupirocin or bacitracin); local anesthesia (lidocaine).
treatment_algorithm: None — wound irrigation (saline). Closure: primary (sutures, staples, Dermabond). Topical antibiotic ointment. Tetanus prophylaxis. Oral antibiotics for contaminated wounds (amoxicillin-clavulanate). Delayed primary closure for contaminated/infected wounds.
us_prevalence: ~8–12 million lacerations requiring medical care/year in the US (NEISS).
patients_needing_tx: ~3–4 million require prescription antibiotics or prescription local anesthetic.
price: $100–$500 per episode for a novel wound-closure/antimicrobial agent.
registrational_endpoint: Wound infection rate and cosmetic outcome at 2 weeks vs. standard closure + topical antibiotic.
btd_threshold_vs_soc: ≥30% reduction in wound infection rate and ≥20% improvement in cosmetic score vs. SoC.

symptoms: Wound, pain, infection risk (Pasteurella, Eikenella, polymicrobial)
treatment_goal: Prevent infection, repair wound, assess for tendon/joint injury
first_line_soc: Amoxicillin-clavulanate (Augmentin) 875/125 mg PO BID × 3–5 days — first-line prophylaxis/treatment for both human and animal bites covering polymicrobial flora.
treatment_algorithm: Wound irrigation + debridement. Amoxicillin-clavulanate 875/125 mg BID. For penicillin allergy: doxycycline or metronidazole + TMP-SMX. Rabies PEP for wild animal bites. Tetanus prophylaxis. Hand surgery for fight bites with joint penetration. Dog bites: close wound primarily; cat bites: leave open. Hospitalization + IV antibiotics for established infection.
us_prevalence: ~4.5–5 million dog bites/year; ~300,000 ED visits; ~400,000 human bites/year (CDC data).
patients_needing_tx: ~1 million require antibiotic treatment annually.
price: $500–$2,000 per course for a novel antimicrobial or wound-healing agent.
registrational_endpoint: Wound infection rate at 7 days vs. amoxicillin-clavulanate standard.
btd_threshold_vs_soc: ≥40% reduction in wound infection rate vs. amoxicillin-clavulanate.

symptoms: Severe limb pain, tense swelling, pain with passive stretch, paresthesias
treatment_goal: Relieve compartment pressure, restore perfusion, prevent necrosis
first_line_soc: None pharmacologic — emergent fasciotomy is the only definitive treatment; IV fluids and pain management as bridge.
treatment_algorithm: None — emergent surgical fasciotomy (all 4 compartments in leg). IV fluid resuscitation. Analgesics (IV opioids, ketamine). Monitor compartment pressures (>30 mmHg or within 30 mmHg of diastolic BP is indication for fasciotomy). Wound care post-fasciotomy; split-thickness skin grafting often required.
us_prevalence: ~40,000–50,000 acute compartment syndrome cases/year in the US (post-fracture, crush injury, burns).
patients_needing_tx: ~40,000 require emergent fasciotomy; all require pharmacologic supportive care.
price: $5,000–$20,000 per episode for a novel pressure-reducing or fasciotomy-sparing agent.
registrational_endpoint: Rate of fasciotomy avoidance and limb salvage at 30 days vs. standard pressure monitoring.
btd_threshold_vs_soc: ≥30% reduction in fasciotomy requirement without increased complications vs. SoC.

symptoms: Cut with bleeding, wound edges separated, pain, infection risk
treatment_goal: Close wound, prevent infection, minimize scarring
first_line_soc: None pharmacologic — primary closure with sutures, staples, or tissue adhesive (Dermabond); tetanus prophylaxis.
treatment_algorithm: None — wound irrigation with NS. Primary closure (sutures/staples/glue). Tissue adhesive (cyanoacrylate) for simple facial lacerations. Antibiotic ointment (bacitracin). Tetanus immunization update. Antibiotics (cephalexin) for contaminated wounds. Wound check at 48–72 hours.
us_prevalence: ~8–12 million laceration-related ED visits/year in the US (NEISS data).
patients_needing_tx: ~6 million require active wound closure; ~500,000 require antibiotics.
price: $50–$500 per course for a novel wound-closure biologic or antimicrobial.
registrational_endpoint: Wound closure rate, scar assessment (POSAS score), and infection rate at 30 days vs. standard sutures.
btd_threshold_vs_soc: ≥30% better cosmetic outcome (POSAS) and ≥50% lower infection rate vs. sutures.

symptoms: Skin scraping, pain, bleeding, wound contamination
treatment_goal: Clean wound, prevent infection, promote healing
first_line_soc: None — treatment is mechanical cleansing, topical antiseptics (e.g., povidone-iodine), and non-adherent dressings.
treatment_algorithm: None — wound irrigation with saline/water, removal of debris, topical antibiotic ointment (bacitracin/neomycin), moist wound dressings. Tetanus prophylaxis if indicated. Systemic antibiotics only for infected wounds.
us_prevalence: ~10 million+ abrasion-related ED visits and outpatient encounters annually (NEISS data).
patients_needing_tx: ~500,000 require prescription topical agents or antibiotics for infected abrasions; the vast majority are self-managed.
price: $50–$200 per course for a novel advanced wound-healing topical; rationale: OTC/Rx topical market comparables.
registrational_endpoint: Time to complete wound closure vs. standard moist dressing (RCT).
btd_threshold_vs_soc: ≥30% faster time to complete closure vs. comparator.

symptoms: Blast lung, TBI, burns, fragmentation wounds, barotrauma
treatment_goal: Control hemorrhage, restore oxygenation, manage polytrauma
first_line_soc: None pharmacologic — damage control resuscitation: 1:1:1 ratio blood product transfusion; TXA (tranexamic acid) within 3 hours of injury.
treatment_algorithm: None — TXA 1 g IV over 10 min within 3 hours (CRASH-2/MATTERs data). Damage control surgery. Hemostatic resuscitation (blood products 1:1:1). Airway management + mechanical ventilation. HBO for blast lung in select cases. Neurologic monitoring for blast TBI. Antibiotics for contaminated wounds.
us_prevalence: ~500–1,000 significant blast injuries/year in the US (military + civilian combined; FBI/DoD data).
patients_needing_tx: ~500–1,000 require active pharmacologic management.
price: $10,000–$50,000 per episode for novel blast neuroprotective/hemostatic agent.
registrational_endpoint: 28-day all-cause mortality and morbidity vs. standard damage-control resuscitation.
btd_threshold_vs_soc: ≥20% reduction in 28-day mortality vs. standard TXA + damage-control resuscitation.

symptoms: Hematuria, pelvic pain, urinary difficulty, visible injury
treatment_goal: Preserve renal/urinary function, control hemorrhage, prevent infection
first_line_soc: None pharmacologic — most managed conservatively (catheterization, observation) or surgically; antibiotics for contaminated injuries.
treatment_algorithm: None — renal trauma: observation for Grade I–III; embolization or nephrectomy for Grade IV–V. Bladder/urethral: see specific entries. Broad-spectrum antibiotics for open injuries. NSAIDs/opioids for pain.
us_prevalence: ~100,000 GU tract trauma cases/year in the US (NTDB; includes renal, bladder, urethral).
patients_needing_tx: ~80,000 require pharmacologic management.
price: $3,000–$10,000 per episode.
registrational_endpoint: GFR preservation and complication rate at 3 months vs. standard conservative/surgical care.
btd_threshold_vs_soc: ≥20% improvement in GFR preservation and ≥25% reduction in complications vs. SoC.

symptoms: Flank pain, hematuria, bruising, hypotension in severe injury
treatment_goal: Preserve renal function, control hemorrhage, prevent infection
first_line_soc: None pharmacologic — grade I–III: conservative management; grade IV–V: interventional radiology (angioembolization) or surgery.
treatment_algorithm: None — AAST grade I–III: bed rest, IV fluids, serial imaging. Grade IV–V: angiographic embolization preferred; nephrectomy for uncontrolled hemorrhage. Antibiotics for urinoma/infection. BP management. Follow-up imaging for renal salvage rate.
us_prevalence: ~250,000 renal injuries/year (most minor); ~10% are severe (grade IV–V).
patients_needing_tx: ~25,000 require active intervention beyond observation.
price: $5,000–$15,000 per course for a novel renal-protective agent.
registrational_endpoint: Renal salvage rate and GFR preservation at 6 months vs. standard care.
btd_threshold_vs_soc: ≥20% improvement in renal salvage rate at 6 months vs. standard management.

symptoms: Pelvic pain, blood in urine, inability to urinate, lower abdominal bruising
treatment_goal: Drain urine, repair perforation, prevent infection/peritonitis
first_line_soc: None pharmacologic — intraperitoneal ruptures require surgical repair; extraperitoneal managed with catheter drainage ± antibiotics.
treatment_algorithm: None — extraperitoneal rupture: Foley catheter drainage for 10–14 days + prophylactic antibiotics. Intraperitoneal: emergency laparotomy and two-layer bladder repair. Broad-spectrum antibiotics (ceftriaxone). Suprapubic catheter if urethral injury coexists.
us_prevalence: ~30,000–40,000 bladder injuries/year in the US, primarily from motor vehicle accidents and pelvic fractures (NTDB data).
patients_needing_tx: ~30,000 require catheter + antibiotics; ~10,000 require surgical repair.
price: $2,000–$10,000 for a novel bladder repair/regenerative therapy.
registrational_endpoint: Bladder integrity at 6 weeks (cystogram) and rate of urinary complications vs. standard catheter drainage.
btd_threshold_vs_soc: ≥30% reduction in urinary complications vs. standard of care.

symptoms: Genital pain, swelling, bruising, urinary difficulty, bleeding
treatment_goal: Control hemorrhage, repair injury, preserve function
first_line_soc: None pharmacologic — surgical exploration/repair as needed; antibiotics for contaminated wounds; NSAIDs for pain.
treatment_algorithm: None — hematoma: conservative management vs. surgical drainage. Lacerations: irrigation + surgical repair. Penile fracture: urgent surgical repair. Scrotal/testicular trauma: exploration if uncertain viability. Antibiotics for contaminated wounds (broad-spectrum).
us_prevalence: ~50,000–100,000 genital trauma events/year in the US requiring medical care.
patients_needing_tx: ~30,000 require pharmacologic treatment (analgesics, antibiotics).
price: $2,000–$10,000 per episode.
registrational_endpoint: Complication rate (infection, bleeding, erectile dysfunction) at 90 days vs. standard surgical care.
btd_threshold_vs_soc: ≥30% reduction in complication rate vs. standard care.

symptoms: Blood at urethral meatus, inability to void, perineal hematoma, urinary retention
treatment_goal: Divert urine, preserve urethral continuity, prevent stricture
first_line_soc: None pharmacologic — suprapubic catheterization for urinary diversion; primary repair or delayed urethroplasty for posterior urethral disruption.
treatment_algorithm: None — anterior urethral: primary repair if clean laceration; suprapubic catheter for diversion. Posterior urethral (pelvic fracture): immediate suprapubic catheter; delayed urethroplasty (3–6 months). Urethral dilation for stricture. Mitomycin-C injection investigational for stricture prevention.
us_prevalence: ~5,000–8,000 urethral injuries/year in the US; majority associated with pelvic fractures.
patients_needing_tx: ~5,000 require active management.
price: $5,000–$15,000 per course for a stricture-prevention agent.
registrational_endpoint: Stricture rate at 12 months and maximum urinary flow rate vs. standard urethroplasty.
btd_threshold_vs_soc: ≥25% reduction in stricture rate vs. standard urethroplasty.

symptoms: Flank pain, hematuria, urinary leak, urinoma formation
treatment_goal: Repair ureter, prevent urinoma, preserve renal function
first_line_soc: None pharmacologic — ureteral stenting or percutaneous nephrostomy for decompression; surgical repair (ureterorrhaphy, ureteroneocystostomy) for complete transection.
treatment_algorithm: None — partial injury: ureteral stent (J-J) × 4–6 weeks. Complete transection: primary repair or reimplantation (ureteroneocystostomy, Boari flap, psoas hitch). Antibiotics for infection. Nephrectomy as last resort.
us_prevalence: ~8,000–12,000 ureteral injuries/year (iatrogenic > trauma); ~2,000 traumatic.
patients_needing_tx: ~2,000 require active surgical/urologic management.
price: $5,000–$15,000 per course for a novel wound-healing agent.
registrational_endpoint: Ureteral patency on IVP/CT urogram at 3 months vs. standard surgical repair.
btd_threshold_vs_soc: ≥20% improvement in ureteral patency rate vs. standard repair.

symptoms: Headache, confusion, memory loss, seizures, coma (severe)
treatment_goal: Reduce intracranial pressure, prevent secondary injury, preserve function
first_line_soc: No FDA-approved neuroprotective drug; ICP management: mannitol (20% solution) or hypertonic saline (23.4%); surgical decompressive craniectomy for severe ICP elevation.
treatment_algorithm: Mild TBI: rest, acetaminophen, cognitive therapy. Moderate-severe: ICU ICP monitoring; mannitol or hypertonic saline for ICP; propofol for sedation; anti-epileptics (levetiracetam, phenytoin) for seizure prophylaxis; surgical evacuation of hematoma; decompressive craniectomy. Progesterone (failed Phase III). Amantadine for disorders of consciousness. TXA (CRASH-3 trial data). Rehabilitation.
us_prevalence: ~2.5–3.8 million TBI-related ED visits/year; ~214,000 hospitalizations; ~69,000 deaths (CDC 2025).
patients_needing_tx: ~250,000 require active pharmacologic management; ~50,000 need ICU-level neurointensive care.
price: $20,000–$100,000 per course for a novel neuroprotective or neurorestorative agent.
registrational_endpoint: Glasgow Outcome Scale-Extended (GOS-E) at 6 months, or disability rating scale, vs. standard care in moderate-severe TBI.
btd_threshold_vs_soc: ≥20% improvement in favorable neurological outcome (GOS-E ≥5) at 6 months vs. standard care.

symptoms: Back/neck pain, neurological deficits, paralysis, instability
treatment_goal: Decompress spinal cord, stabilize spine, preserve neurological function
first_line_soc: None pharmacologic — surgical stabilization (ACDF, ORIF, laminectomy) for unstable fractures or cord compression; high-dose methylprednisolone remains controversial.
treatment_algorithm: ATLS spine precautions. Surgical decompression and fixation for unstable injuries. High-dose methylprednisolone (30 mg/kg + 5.4 mg/kg/hr × 24h) — controversial; not universally recommended (AO Spine 2022 guidelines). ICU hemodynamic management (MAP >85). Bladder/bowel care. PT/OT rehabilitation. Spasticity management (baclofen).
us_prevalence: ~500,000 spinal injuries/year; ~18,000 new spinal cord injuries/year (NSCISC); ~300,000 Americans living with SCI.
patients_needing_tx: ~18,000 new acute SCI require active management; 300,000 living with SCI need ongoing pharmacologic management.
price: $50,000–$200,000 per course for acute neuroprotective biologic.
registrational_endpoint: AIS grade conversion and ISNCSCI motor score at 6 and 12 months vs. standard management.
btd_threshold_vs_soc: ≥20% AIS grade conversion rate improvement vs. standard care at 12 months.

symptoms: Headache, dizziness, confusion, memory problems, light sensitivity
treatment_goal: Reduce symptoms, prevent second impact, facilitate safe return to play
first_line_soc: None pharmacologic — cognitive and physical rest; graduated return-to-play (RTP) protocol.
treatment_algorithm: None — symptomatic: acetaminophen for headache (avoid NSAIDs initially). Vestibular PT for dizziness. Cognitive behavioral therapy for prolonged symptoms. Amantadine (investigational for PCS). Melatonin for sleep. Omega-3 fatty acids (investigational). No FDA-approved pharmacologic treatment. Avoid alcohol and contact sports until symptom-free.
us_prevalence: ~1.6–3.8 million sports-related concussions/year in the US; ~500,000 require medical evaluation.
patients_needing_tx: ~500,000 require active symptom management; ~100,000 have prolonged post-concussion syndrome (PCS).
price: $5,000–$20,000 per course for a novel neuroprotective drug for PCS.
registrational_endpoint: Sport Concussion Assessment Tool (SCAT5) score, symptom duration, and time to RTP vs. rest alone.
btd_threshold_vs_soc: ≥30% faster return-to-play and ≥30% reduction in symptom duration vs. standard graduated RTP protocol.

symptoms: Paralysis, sensory loss, bladder/bowel dysfunction, respiratory failure
treatment_goal: Preserve remaining function, prevent complications, promote recovery
first_line_soc: High-dose methylprednisolone (controversial; NASCIS II/III regimen) within 8 hours — 30 mg/kg bolus then 5.4 mg/kg/hr × 24 h. Surgical decompression for instability.
treatment_algorithm: Surgical stabilization for unstable fractures. High-dose methylprednisolone (use debated — not universally recommended for pediatrics). ICU management (respiratory, hemodynamic). Bladder/bowel program. PT/OT rehabilitation. Baclofen (oral or intrathecal) for spasticity. Emerging: riluzole, minocycline (investigational neuroprotection).
us_prevalence: ~1,500 pediatric spinal cord injuries/year in the US; ~25% are complete injuries.
patients_needing_tx: ~1,500 require acute neurologic management; ~750 need long-term pharmacologic management (spasticity, pain).
price: $50,000–$200,000 per course for a novel neuroprotective/regenerative biologic.
registrational_endpoint: AIS (ASIA Impairment Scale) grade conversion at 12 months vs. standard management.
btd_threshold_vs_soc: ≥20% rate of AIS grade improvement vs. standard of care at 12 months.

symptoms: Pain around knee, stiffness, swelling, limited function
treatment_goal: Reduce pain, restore function, identify treatable cause
first_line_soc: NSAIDs (ibuprofen, naproxen) — first-line pharmacologic treatment for most non-traumatic knee pain conditions.
treatment_algorithm: NSAIDs/acetaminophen first-line. Corticosteroid injection for inflammatory arthritis/bursitis. Hyaluronic acid injection for OA. PT and activity modification. Topical diclofenac (Voltaren). Surgery for structural injuries (meniscal tear, ACL, arthroplasty for OA).
us_prevalence: ~20–25 million Americans report chronic knee pain; ~10 million knee-related ED/outpatient visits/year.
patients_needing_tx: ~10 million require pharmacologic treatment annually.
price: $5,000–$20,000/year for a novel knee OA disease-modifying agent; acute episodes: $500–$2,000.
registrational_endpoint: KOOS pain subscale at 6 months vs. NSAIDs/placebo for specific etiology.
btd_threshold_vs_soc: ≥20-point improvement in KOOS pain subscale vs. NSAID comparator.

symptoms: Knee pain, swelling, locking/clicking, joint line tenderness
treatment_goal: Reduce pain, restore function, preserve meniscal tissue
first_line_soc: NSAIDs — first-line pharmacologic; PT for conservative management; arthroscopic meniscectomy or repair depending on tear pattern.
treatment_algorithm: NSAIDs + PT for partial/degenerative tears (comparable to surgery in middle-aged: MOON/METEOR trials). Arthroscopic meniscectomy for mechanical symptoms. Meniscal repair for peripheral tears in young patients. Meniscal allograft transplant for post-meniscectomy syndrome.
us_prevalence: ~1 million meniscal surgeries/year in the US; ~3–4 million new meniscal injuries/year (AAOS data).
patients_needing_tx: ~2 million require pharmacologic management; ~1 million undergo surgery.
price: $5,000–$20,000 per meniscal repair biologic.
registrational_endpoint: KOOS Symptoms subscale and return to sport at 12 months vs. arthroscopic meniscectomy.
btd_threshold_vs_soc: ≥15-point improvement in KOOS Symptoms vs. meniscectomy with ≥50% reduction in reoperation rate.

symptoms: Knee pain, instability, swelling, limited weight bearing
treatment_goal: Protect ligament, reduce pain, restore stability
first_line_soc: None pharmacologic — RICE + NSAIDs; surgical ACL/MCL reconstruction for complete tears in active patients.
treatment_algorithm: None — MCL: conservative RICE + brace + PT. ACL: surgical reconstruction (BTB or hamstring autograft) for athletes; conservative for low-demand patients. LCL/PCL complex: surgical reconstruction. NSAIDs. PT for all.
us_prevalence: ~1.5–2 million knee ligament sprains/year in the US; ~250,000 ACL tears/year (AAOS).
patients_needing_tx: ~1 million require prescription treatment; ~250,000 undergo ACL reconstruction.
price: $5,000–$20,000 for a novel ligament healing biologic.
registrational_endpoint: IKDC score and re-injury rate at 24 months vs. standard autograft ACL reconstruction.
btd_threshold_vs_soc: ≥15% improvement in IKDC score and ≥30% reduction in re-injury rate vs. standard ACL reconstruction.

symptoms: Lateral elbow pain, weak grip, pain with wrist extension
treatment_goal: Reduce pain, restore grip strength, promote tendon healing
first_line_soc: NSAIDs (naproxen, ibuprofen) or topical diclofenac — first-line pharmacologic for pain relief; PT and activity modification equally important.
treatment_algorithm: NSAIDs/topical diclofenac. PT (eccentric strengthening). Counterforce bracing. Corticosteroid injection (short-term benefit only; worse long-term outcomes). PRP injection (growing evidence for structural healing). Surgery (lateral release) for refractory cases (>12 months).
us_prevalence: ~3–5 million Americans affected; ~200,000 new cases/year seeking medical care (AAOS data).
patients_needing_tx: ~2 million require pharmacologic treatment; ~500,000 receive injections annually.
price: $500–$5,000 per course for a novel regenerative injection or sustained-release anti-inflammatory.
registrational_endpoint: VAS pain and DASH score at 12 weeks vs. corticosteroid injection (standard).
btd_threshold_vs_soc: ≥30% improvement in VAS/DASH at 12 weeks with durable benefit at 1 year vs. corticosteroid.

symptoms: Shoulder pain, weakness raising arm, night pain, catching sensation
treatment_goal: Relieve pain, restore shoulder strength and function
first_line_soc: NSAIDs (ibuprofen, naproxen) and physical therapy; subacromial corticosteroid injection for acute pain.
treatment_algorithm: Step 1: NSAIDs × 4–6 weeks + PT (rotator cuff strengthening). Step 2: Subacromial corticosteroid injection (triamcinolone/methylprednisolone). Step 3: Surgical repair (arthroscopic) for full-thickness tears failing conservative management. PRP injection (investigational). Biologic scaffolds (GraftJacket) for augmentation.
us_prevalence: ~2 million new rotator cuff injuries/year in the US; prevalence rises with age (>50% of adults >70 have some tear).
patients_needing_tx: ~1 million require prescription treatment; ~500,000 undergo subacromial injection; ~250,000 undergo surgical repair annually.
price: $2,000–$8,000 per course for a novel tendon-healing biologic (e.g., recombinant growth factor).
registrational_endpoint: ASES (American Shoulder and Elbow Surgeons) score and MRI confirmation of tendon integrity at 12 months vs. PT + injection.
btd_threshold_vs_soc: ≥15-point improvement in ASES score vs. standard PT + corticosteroid injection at 12 months.

symptoms: Posterior thigh pain, spasm, bruising, limited hip flexion
treatment_goal: Reduce pain, restore strength, prevent re-injury
first_line_soc: None pharmacologic — RICE + NSAIDs; PRP injection for Grade II–III tears (increasing use); structured PT.
treatment_algorithm: None — RICE + NSAIDs/acetaminophen. PT (progressive loading protocol). PRP injection for Grade II–III (growing evidence). Surgery for complete proximal avulsions in athletes. Return to sport: 6–12 weeks.
us_prevalence: ~500,000–1,000,000 hamstring strains/year in the US; most common sports injury (NSCA data).
patients_needing_tx: ~500,000 seek medical care; ~200,000 require prescription pharmacologic treatment.
price: $2,000–$10,000 for a novel regenerative biologic or anti-fibrotic.
registrational_endpoint: Time to return to sport and re-injury rate at 6 months vs. PT alone.
btd_threshold_vs_soc: ≥25% faster return to sport and ≥30% reduction in re-injury rate vs. PT alone.

symptoms: Shoulder pain, instability, clicking, weakness, decreased ROM
treatment_goal: Restore shoulder stability, reduce pain, prevent recurrent dislocation
first_line_soc: None pharmacologic — PT (rotator cuff strengthening) for SLAP tears in older patients; arthroscopic labral repair (Bankart repair) for anterior tears in young athletes.
treatment_algorithm: None — conservative: PT for 3–6 months for Type I/II SLAP in older patients. Surgical: arthroscopic Bankart/SLAP repair for young athletes, contact sports. NSAIDs + corticosteroid injection for symptom management.
us_prevalence: ~200,000–400,000 labral tears/year in the US (AAOS; high incidence in overhead athletes).
patients_needing_tx: ~200,000 require surgical or significant pharmacologic management.
price: $5,000–$20,000 for a novel regenerative biologic.
registrational_endpoint: Western Ontario Shoulder Instability Index (WOSI) score at 12 months vs. standard arthroscopic repair.
btd_threshold_vs_soc: ≥15% improvement in WOSI score and recurrence rate vs. standard arthroscopic repair.

symptoms: Pain, swelling, limited movement, bruising in various anatomic regions
treatment_goal: Reduce pain and inflammation, restore function, prevent re-injury
first_line_soc: NSAIDs (ibuprofen, naproxen) for acute pain/inflammation; PRICE principle (protection, rest, ice, compression, elevation).
treatment_algorithm: PRICE. NSAIDs oral/topical (diclofenac gel). PT for rehabilitation. Corticosteroid injections for persistent bursitis/tendinopathy. PRP injections (investigational). Surgical intervention for significant structural injuries.
us_prevalence: ~8.6 million sports-related injuries/year in the US (CDC); ~3.5 million require medical attention.
patients_needing_tx: ~2 million require prescription NSAIDs or PT; ~500,000 require injections or surgery.
price: $500–$3,000 per course for a novel anti-inflammatory or tissue-healing biologic.
registrational_endpoint: VAS pain score and return-to-sport time at 6 weeks vs. standard NSAIDs + PT.
btd_threshold_vs_soc: ≥30% faster return to sport and ≥30% lower pain VAS vs. NSAIDs + PT at 6 weeks.

symptoms: Joint pain, swelling, bruising, instability after ligament stretch/tear
treatment_goal: Reduce pain and swelling, restore stability and function
first_line_soc: PRICE (protection, rest, ice, compression, elevation) + NSAIDs (ibuprofen, naproxen).
treatment_algorithm: PRICE. Oral NSAIDs × 5–7 days. PT for proprioception and strength. Functional bracing/taping. Severe sprains/grade III: surgical repair (ATFL for ankle, ACL for knee). Hyaluronic acid or PRP injections investigational for chronic sprains.
us_prevalence: ~28 million sprains/year in the US (ankle, wrist, knee); ~2 million require medical care.
patients_needing_tx: ~2 million require prescription NSAIDs or PT referral.
price: $500–$2,000 per course for a novel ligament-healing biologic.
registrational_endpoint: VAS pain score, ankle joint stability, and return-to-activity time at 6 weeks vs. PRICE + NSAIDs.
btd_threshold_vs_soc: ≥30% faster recovery and ≥25% improvement in functional outcomes vs. PRICE + NSAIDs.

symptoms: Inner elbow pain, weak grip, pain with wrist flexion/forearm pronation
treatment_goal: Reduce pain, promote tendon healing, restore function
first_line_soc: NSAIDs (ibuprofen, naproxen) or topical diclofenac — first-line; PT for eccentric strengthening.
treatment_algorithm: NSAIDs/topical anti-inflammatory. PT (eccentric loading). Activity modification. Counterforce brace. Corticosteroid injection (short-term benefit). PRP injection (growing evidence). Surgery for refractory cases (>12 months).
us_prevalence: ~1–2 million Americans affected (less common than lateral epicondylitis); ~100,000 new cases/year requiring medical care.
patients_needing_tx: ~700,000 require pharmacologic treatment.
price: $500–$3,000 per course.
registrational_endpoint: VAS pain and DASH score at 12 weeks vs. corticosteroid injection.
btd_threshold_vs_soc: ≥30% improvement in VAS at 12 weeks with sustained benefit at 1 year vs. corticosteroid.

symptoms: Sudden heel pain, inability to push off, palpable gap
treatment_goal: Restore tendon continuity, regain functional strength
first_line_soc: None pharmacologic — management is surgical repair or conservative cast/boot immobilization followed by structured rehabilitation.
treatment_algorithm: None — surgical repair (open/mini-open tenorrhaphy) preferred in active patients; conservative management (functional bracing in equinus) for lower-demand patients. NSAIDs for pain. PRP injections investigated but not standard. Physical therapy is cornerstone of recovery.
us_prevalence: ~100,000 Achilles tendon ruptures/year in the US (incidence ~30–40/100,000; AAOS data).
patients_needing_tx: ~100,000 require surgical or structured conservative management; ~50,000 undergo operative repair.
price: $5,000–$20,000 per treatment for a novel tendon regenerative biologic; rationale: orthopedic biologic niche, similar to PRP/growth factor injectables.
registrational_endpoint: Achilles Tendon Total Rupture Score (ATRS) at 12 months vs. standard surgical repair.
btd_threshold_vs_soc: ≥10-point improvement in ATRS vs. SoC at 12 months.

symptoms: Pain, swelling, bruising, instability, limited motion
treatment_goal: Reduce pain/swelling, restore stability and function
first_line_soc: None pharmacologic — RICE (rest, ice, compression, elevation); NSAIDs (ibuprofen) for pain and inflammation.
treatment_algorithm: None — RICE + functional rehabilitation. NSAIDs/acetaminophen for pain. Functional bracing/taping. PT for proprioceptive training. Corticosteroid injection not recommended acutely. Surgery for chronic instability (Broström procedure).
us_prevalence: ~2 million ankle sprains/year presenting to medical care; estimated 25,000/day in the US (Foot Ankle Int data).
patients_needing_tx: ~1 million seek prescription treatment; ~500,000 need significant pharmacologic management.
price: $500–$2,000 for a novel intra-articular or topical ligament-healing agent.
registrational_endpoint: Functional Ankle Disability Index (FADI) or time to return to activity vs. RICE + NSAIDs.
btd_threshold_vs_soc: ≥20% faster return to full activity vs. RICE + NSAID comparator.

symptoms: Deep buttock pain, sciatica, worsened by sitting, hip rotation
treatment_goal: Reduce nerve compression, relieve pain, restore function
first_line_soc: NSAIDs (ibuprofen, naproxen) plus physical therapy; muscle relaxants (cyclobenzaprine) for spasm.
treatment_algorithm: Step 1: NSAIDs + PT (piriformis stretching, hip strengthening). Step 2: Corticosteroid/local anesthetic injection into piriformis muscle. Step 3: Botulinum toxin A injection (Botox — off-label) for refractory spasm. Surgery (piriformis release) rarely needed.
us_prevalence: ~2.4 million Americans estimated with piriformis syndrome; diagnosis often underrecognized among low back pain patients.
patients_needing_tx: ~500,000 require prescription treatment beyond OTC NSAIDs.
price: $1,000–$3,000 per course for a targeted nerve-decompression biologic.
registrational_endpoint: VAS pain score and functional assessment (FAIR test) at 12 weeks vs. PT + NSAIDs.
btd_threshold_vs_soc: ≥50% reduction in VAS pain vs. PT + NSAID standard at 12 weeks.

symptoms: Sudden arm weakness, pain, bulging muscle (Popeye sign)
treatment_goal: Restore arm strength and function, repair tendon
first_line_soc: None pharmacologic — distal biceps tears: surgical reattachment preferred for active patients. Proximal: often managed conservatively with NSAIDs and PT.
treatment_algorithm: None — distal biceps rupture: single-incision or two-incision surgical repair within 2–3 weeks. Proximal long head: often conservative (sling, NSAIDs, PT). PRP injection under investigation. PT for rehabilitation.
us_prevalence: ~100,000 biceps tendon tears/year; distal ~10,000/year; proximal ~90,000/year (J Shoulder Elbow Surg data).
patients_needing_tx: ~50,000 require surgical intervention; ~100,000 need pharmacologic pain management.
price: $5,000–$15,000 per biologic tendon repair agent.
registrational_endpoint: ASES shoulder score or DASH score at 6 months vs. standard surgical/conservative repair.
btd_threshold_vs_soc: ≥15% improvement in functional score vs. SoC at 6 months.

symptoms: Tibial pain during exercise, tenderness along shin, swelling
treatment_goal: Reduce pain, allow training modification, prevent stress fracture
first_line_soc: Rest, ice, NSAIDs (ibuprofen), activity modification, and orthotics.
treatment_algorithm: Step 1: Activity modification + ice + NSAIDs. Step 2: Orthotic insoles for biomechanical correction. Step 3: PT (gait retraining, calf strengthening). Step 4: Return to running protocol after 2–4 weeks. Rule out stress fracture with MRI. No surgical option unless compartment syndrome develops.
us_prevalence: ~2–3 million cases/year in US athletes and military recruits.
patients_needing_tx: ~500,000 require prescription NSAIDs or PT referral.
price: $500–$2,000 per course for a targeted tibial healing agent.
registrational_endpoint: VAS pain score and return-to-activity time at 6 weeks vs. rest + NSAIDs.
btd_threshold_vs_soc: ≥30% faster return to pain-free activity vs. standard rest + NSAIDs.

symptoms: Shoulder pain, pain with overhead activities, night pain, limited ROM
treatment_goal: Reduce bursitis inflammation, restore pain-free shoulder motion
first_line_soc: NSAIDs (ibuprofen, naproxen) and physical therapy; subacromial corticosteroid injection (methylprednisolone or triamcinolone).
treatment_algorithm: Step 1: NSAIDs + PT (shoulder impingement exercises). Step 2: Subacromial injection (corticosteroid ± local anesthetic, ultrasound-guided). Step 3: Arthroscopic subacromial decompression for refractory cases. PRP injection investigational.
us_prevalence: ~1 million new subacromial bursitis diagnoses/year; included in ~2 million shoulder pain presentations.
patients_needing_tx: ~500,000 require prescription NSAIDs or injection.
price: $500–$2,000 per course for a novel sustained-release anti-inflammatory.
registrational_endpoint: VAS pain and ASES shoulder score at 6 weeks vs. corticosteroid injection.
btd_threshold_vs_soc: ≥15-point improvement in ASES score vs. subacromial corticosteroid injection at 6 weeks.

symptoms: Inability to extend knee, quadriceps/patellar tendon defect, pain, swelling
treatment_goal: Repair extensor mechanism, restore active knee extension
first_line_soc: None pharmacologic — surgical repair of torn quadriceps or patellar tendon; NSAIDs for pain.
treatment_algorithm: None — primary surgical repair (end-to-end) with augmentation sutures. Post-op: knee immobilizer → progressive ROM at 4–6 weeks → PT. NSAIDs. DVT prophylaxis.
us_prevalence: ~50,000–80,000 quadriceps and patellar tendon injuries/year in the US.
patients_needing_tx: ~60,000 require surgical intervention and pharmacologic support.
price: $5,000–$15,000 per biologic tendon repair agent.
registrational_endpoint: Lysholm Knee Score at 6 months and quad strength at 3 months vs. standard surgical repair.
btd_threshold_vs_soc: ≥15-point Lysholm improvement vs. standard tendon repair.

symptoms: Shoulder pain, swelling, deformity at collarbone, limited motion
treatment_goal: Reduce pain, restore shoulder function and stability
first_line_soc: None pharmacologic — NSAIDs/analgesics for pain; primary treatment is rest, ice, sling immobilization, and physical therapy.
treatment_algorithm: None — Grade I–II: conservative (sling, NSAIDs, PT). Grade III: controversial — conservative vs. surgical reconstruction. Grade IV–VI: surgical repair/reconstruction (Weaver-Dunn or ligament transfer procedures). Corticosteroid injection for persistent pain.
us_prevalence: ~200,000 AC joint injuries/year in the US; 9% of all shoulder injuries (AAOS estimates).
patients_needing_tx: ~150,000 need pharmacologic pain management; ~30,000 require surgical intervention.
price: $1,000–$5,000 per treatment course (novel biologic or corticosteroid formulation).
registrational_endpoint: Constant-Murley Shoulder Score at 12 weeks vs. standard NSAID/PT.
btd_threshold_vs_soc: ≥15-point improvement in Constant-Murley Score vs. SoC.

symptoms: Drooping fingertip, inability to extend DIP joint, pain at DIP
treatment_goal: Splint in extension, restore DIP extension, prevent swan neck
first_line_soc: None pharmacologic — continuous DIP extension splinting for 6–8 weeks; NSAIDs for pain.
treatment_algorithm: None — DIP extension splint (Stack/thermoplastic) worn continuously for 6–8 weeks. NSAIDs for pain. Surgery for fracture >30% articular surface or joint subluxation.
us_prevalence: ~100,000–200,000 mallet fingers/year in the US (common sports injury; AAOS data).
patients_needing_tx: ~150,000 require prescription splint and NSAIDs.
price: $500–$2,000 per episode for a novel tendon healing adjunct.
registrational_endpoint: Extensor lag at DIP joint at 8 weeks vs. standard extension splinting.
btd_threshold_vs_soc: ≥5° improvement in DIP extensor lag vs. splinting alone.

symptoms: Inability to flex fingertip, pain, swelling (ring finger most common)
treatment_goal: Reattach tendon, restore finger flexion
first_line_soc: None pharmacologic — surgical reattachment of avulsed tendon within 7–10 days; NSAIDs for pain.
treatment_algorithm: None — urgent surgical repair (direct suture or bone anchor fixation) for complete FDP avulsion. NSAIDs. Postoperative splinting + structured PT (Kleinert or Duran protocol).
us_prevalence: ~10,000–20,000 FDP avulsions/year in the US (primarily athletes, AAOS data).
patients_needing_tx: ~15,000 require surgical + pharmacologic management.
price: $5,000–$10,000 for a novel tendon repair biologic.
registrational_endpoint: Grip strength and ROM at 3 months vs. standard surgical repair.
btd_threshold_vs_soc: ≥15% improvement in grip strength vs. standard tendon repair.

symptoms: Medial elbow pain, ulnar nerve symptoms, instability with valgus stress
treatment_goal: Restore medial elbow stability, relieve pain, return to sport
first_line_soc: None pharmacologic — rest, ice, NSAIDs, and PT for low-grade sprains; UCL reconstruction (Tommy John) for complete tears in overhead athletes.
treatment_algorithm: None — grade I/II: NSAIDs + PT (flexor-pronator strengthening) × 6–12 weeks. Grade III/complete tears in athletes: UCL reconstruction (Tommy John surgery — palmaris longus or gracilis autograft). PRP injection for partial tears (investigational). PT for 12–18 months post-reconstruction.
us_prevalence: ~25,000–30,000 UCL injuries/year in the US; ~1,500–2,000 UCL reconstructions annually in baseball.
patients_needing_tx: ~15,000 require prescription care; ~2,000 undergo UCL reconstruction.
price: $3,000–$8,000 per course for a novel ligament healing biologic.
registrational_endpoint: VAS pain, elbow stability (stress X-ray), and return-to-sport rate at 12 months vs. standard PRP or PT.
btd_threshold_vs_soc: ≥30% improvement in ligament integrity on MRI or stress X-ray vs. PT alone at 12 months.

symptoms: Big toe pain, swelling, bruising, limited push-off, 'turf toe'
treatment_goal: Protect joint, reduce pain, restore toe function
first_line_soc: None pharmacologic — RICE, NSAIDs, stiff-soled shoe or taping; Grade III: surgical ligament repair.
treatment_algorithm: None — Grade I–II: RICE, NSAIDs, protective footwear, buddy tape. Grade III: surgical ligament repair if unstable. PT for proprioceptive training. Return to activity 3–6 weeks.
us_prevalence: ~100,000 significant turf toe injuries/year in the US; common in field sports (JBJS data).
patients_needing_tx: ~50,000 require prescription treatment (NSAIDs, orthotics).
price: $500–$2,000 per course.
registrational_endpoint: AOFAS metatarsophalangeal score at 6 weeks vs. standard conservative care.
btd_threshold_vs_soc: ≥15-point AOFAS improvement vs. RICE + NSAID standard.

symptoms: Pain, bruising, internal bleeding, organ damage
treatment_goal: Control hemorrhage, repair organ injury, prevent sepsis
first_line_soc: None — management is surgical (laparotomy/laparoscopy) and supportive (IV fluids, blood products). No pharmacologic first-line SoC.
treatment_algorithm: None — primary management is damage-control surgery, hemostatic resuscitation (1:1:1 PRBCs:FFP:platelets), and ICU support. Antibiotics (e.g., cefazolin) are given perioperatively to prevent infection. TXA (tranexamic acid) may be used for hemorrhage control.
us_prevalence: ~1.5 million emergency visits/year involve abdominal trauma; ~250,000 require hospitalization (CDC/NTDB).
patients_needing_tx: ~250,000 require surgical or intensive intervention annually; the majority are managed surgically, not pharmacologically.
price: $5,000–$15,000 per episode for a novel hemostatic or organ-protective agent; rationale: acute hospital-use drug, comparable to TXA adjuncts.
peak_sales: $125M–$250M at 5–10% share (acute care adoption is slow); math: 250,000 × $10,000 × 0.05–0.10.
registrational_endpoint: 28-day all-cause mortality or need for surgical intervention (superiority vs. placebo in randomized controlled trial).
btd_threshold_vs_soc: ≥20% relative reduction in 28-day mortality or major surgical intervention rate vs. SoC.

symptoms: Chest pain, decreased breath sounds, shock, bloody chest drain output
treatment_goal: Drain blood from pleural space, control bleeding, restore lung expansion
first_line_soc: None pharmacologic — tube thoracostomy (32–36 Fr chest tube) is primary treatment; blood products for hemorrhagic shock.
treatment_algorithm: None — chest tube drainage. Blood product resuscitation (PRBCs, FFP). Surgery (VATS or thoracotomy) for clotted hemothorax or persistent bleeding (>200 mL/hr). tPA (alteplase) fibrinolytics for retained hemothorax (clinical guideline).
us_prevalence: ~200,000–300,000 traumatic hemothorax cases/year (NTDB; ~30% of penetrating chest trauma).
patients_needing_tx: ~200,000 require tube thoracostomy and pharmacologic support.
price: $5,000–$20,000 per episode for a novel fibrinolytic/hemostatic agent.
registrational_endpoint: Rate of retained hemothorax requiring additional intervention at 30 days vs. standard chest tube alone.
btd_threshold_vs_soc: ≥40% reduction in retained hemothorax rate vs. standard tube thoracostomy.

symptoms: Chest pain, dyspnea, hemothorax, rib fractures, pneumothorax
treatment_goal: Control hemorrhage, restore ventilation, treat specific injuries
first_line_soc: None pharmacologic — chest tube for pneumothorax/hemothorax; surgical management; analgesia.
treatment_algorithm: None — chest tube (32–40F) for hemopneumothorax. Multimodal analgesia (epidural, intercostal blocks, NSAIDs). Rib fixation for flail chest. Thoracotomy for massive hemothorax. TXA for hemorrhage. Mechanical ventilation for respiratory failure.
us_prevalence: ~300,000 significant thoracic trauma cases/year; leading cause of traumatic death after TBI.
patients_needing_tx: ~100,000 require pharmacologic analgesic or hemostatic management.
price: $5,000–$15,000 per episode for a novel analgesic or hemostatic agent.
registrational_endpoint: VAS pain score at 48 hours and duration of mechanical ventilation vs. standard multimodal analgesia.
btd_threshold_vs_soc: ≥30% reduction in VAS pain or ≥1 day less mechanical ventilation vs. standard care.

symptoms: Right upper quadrant pain, abdominal tenderness, hemodynamic instability
treatment_goal: Control hemorrhage, preserve liver function, prevent complications
first_line_soc: None pharmacologic — non-operative management (NOM) for Grade I–III; angioembolization or surgery for higher grades; IV fluids and blood products for resuscitation.
treatment_algorithm: None — NOM for hemodynamically stable Grade I–III (85–90% of cases): IV fluids, blood products, serial monitoring. Hepatic angioembolization for Grade III–IV with active bleeding. Damage-control surgery for Grade IV–V unstable. Antibiotics for bile leak/biloma.
us_prevalence: ~30,000–40,000 hepatic injuries/year in the US (NTDB; most common solid organ injury in abdominal trauma).
patients_needing_tx: ~30,000 require active management.
price: $10,000–$30,000 per episode for novel hemostatic/liver protective agent.
registrational_endpoint: Failure of NOM rate and 30-day mortality vs. standard observation protocol.
btd_threshold_vs_soc: ≥25% reduction in NOM failure rate vs. standard conservative management.

symptoms: Chest pain, cough, hemoptysis, hypoxia after blunt chest trauma
treatment_goal: Maintain oxygenation, prevent ARDS, avoid fluid overload
first_line_soc: None pharmacologic — supportive: supplemental O2, fluid restriction, early mechanical ventilation if needed.
treatment_algorithm: None — careful fluid management (avoid overload). Supplemental O2 or mechanical ventilation (low tidal volume for ARDS). Epidural analgesia for pain control to enable deep breathing. Incentive spirometry. Diuretics for pulmonary edema if needed. Antibiotics for pneumonia complication.
us_prevalence: ~150,000–200,000 cases/year associated with significant chest trauma (MVA, falls); ~17,000 are ventilator-dependent.
patients_needing_tx: ~50,000 require ICU-level respiratory management.
price: $5,000–$15,000 per course for a novel pulmonary protection biologic.
registrational_endpoint: PaO2/FiO2 ratio at 48–72 hours and duration of mechanical ventilation vs. standard supportive care.
btd_threshold_vs_soc: ≥20 point improvement in PaO2/FiO2 ratio or ≥1 day reduction in mechanical ventilation vs. standard care.

symptoms: Paradoxical chest wall movement, severe pain, respiratory failure
treatment_goal: Stabilize chest wall, ensure adequate ventilation, control pain
first_line_soc: None pharmacologic — epidural analgesia (bupivacaine) or IV/regional analgesia as cornerstone; mechanical ventilation for respiratory failure; surgical rib fixation for severe cases.
treatment_algorithm: None — multimodal analgesia: thoracic epidural (bupivacaine), serratus/paravertebral nerve blocks, IV opioids + NSAIDs, ketamine. Oxygen supplementation. Mechanical ventilation if failing. Surgical rib fixation (SSRF) for severe flail chest to reduce intubation duration. ICU monitoring.
us_prevalence: ~30,000–50,000 flail chest injuries/year in the US from MVAs and falls (NTDB data).
patients_needing_tx: ~30,000 require intensive analgesic and ventilatory management.
price: $5,000–$20,000 per episode for a novel analgesic/chest wall stabilizing agent.
registrational_endpoint: Days on mechanical ventilation and pulmonary complication rate at 30 days vs. standard epidural analgesia.
btd_threshold_vs_soc: ≥25% reduction in mechanical ventilation days vs. epidural standard of care.

symptoms: Beck's triad: hypotension, muffled heart sounds, distended neck veins
treatment_goal: Relieve pericardial pressure, restore cardiac output
first_line_soc: None pharmacologic — pericardiocentesis (needle drainage) is definitive emergency treatment; IV fluids as temporizing measure.
treatment_algorithm: None — IV fluid bolus as bridge to drainage. Pericardiocentesis (ultrasound-guided preferred). Surgical pericardial window for traumatic/recurrent tamponade. Avoid vasodilators/diuretics. Treat underlying cause (malignancy: systemic therapy; bacterial: antibiotics; uremic: dialysis).
us_prevalence: ~10,000–15,000 cardiac tamponade events/year; ~2,000 traumatic (NTDB); remainder from medical causes (malignancy, pericarditis).
patients_needing_tx: ~10,000 require emergency drainage; ~15,000 need pharmacologic treatment of underlying cause.
price: $10,000–$30,000 per episode for a novel fibrinolytic/sclerosant pericardial agent.
registrational_endpoint: Recurrence rate at 6 months and hemodynamic stabilization time vs. pericardiocentesis alone.
btd_threshold_vs_soc: ≥40% reduction in pericardial effusion recurrence at 6 months.

symptoms: Left upper quadrant pain, tenderness, hypotension, shoulder pain (Kehr's sign)
treatment_goal: Control hemorrhage, preserve spleen, prevent mortality
first_line_soc: None pharmacologic — grade I–III: nonoperative management with observation; grade IV–V: angioembolization or splenectomy.
treatment_algorithm: None — AAST grade I–III: hemodynamically stable → NOM (bed rest, serial imaging). Grade III–IV: angioembolization. Grade IV–V unstable: splenectomy. Post-splenectomy vaccines (pneumococcal, meningococcal, Hib). TXA for hemorrhage. ICU supportive care.
us_prevalence: ~300,000 splenic injuries/year (most minor); ~60,000 require hospitalization.
patients_needing_tx: ~20,000 require angioembolization or surgery.
price: $5,000–$15,000 per episode for a novel hemostatic agent.
registrational_endpoint: Spleen salvage rate and 30-day need for surgical intervention vs. standard NOM.
btd_threshold_vs_soc: ≥20% improvement in spleen salvage rate vs. standard NOM + embolization.

symptoms: Sucking chest wound, dyspnea, reduced breath sounds, tracheal deviation
treatment_goal: Seal chest defect, restore lung expansion, prevent tension pneumothorax
first_line_soc: None pharmacologic — chest seal dressing (vented, e.g., Hyfin or Russell) as immediate treatment; tube thoracostomy for definitive management.
treatment_algorithm: None — immediate chest seal (3-sided occlusive or vented seal). Tube thoracostomy (32–36 Fr) placed away from wound. Surgical closure of chest wall defect. Antibiotics for contaminated wounds. Pain management (NSAIDs/opioids/nerve blocks).
us_prevalence: ~10,000–20,000 penetrating chest wounds/year in the US (civilian + military) causing open pneumothorax.
patients_needing_tx: ~15,000 require active management.
price: $5,000–$15,000 per episode for a novel pleural sealing agent.
registrational_endpoint: Lung re-expansion rate and complication rate at 72 hours vs. standard chest seal + tube thoracostomy.
btd_threshold_vs_soc: ≥30% reduction in complications vs. standard chest seal approach.

symptoms: Chest pain, palpitations, arrhythmia, hypotension after chest trauma
treatment_goal: Detect arrhythmia, support cardiac function, prevent sudden death
first_line_soc: None pharmacologic — cardiac monitoring and supportive care; antiarrhythmics (e.g., amiodarone) for significant arrhythmias.
treatment_algorithm: None — ECG monitoring (24–48 hours if abnormal ECG). Antiarrhythmic therapy (amiodarone for VT/VF; metoprolol for AF). Inotropes/vasopressors for cardiogenic shock. Pericardiocentric for traumatic tamponade. Surgical repair for cardiac rupture.
us_prevalence: ~20,000–30,000 clinically significant blunt cardiac injuries/year (NTDB; ~15–20% of major thoracic trauma patients).
patients_needing_tx: ~15,000 require active cardiac monitoring + pharmacologic treatment.
price: $3,000–$10,000 per episode for a cardioprotective agent.
registrational_endpoint: Rate of significant arrhythmia or cardiac mortality within 7 days vs. standard monitoring.
btd_threshold_vs_soc: ≥30% reduction in major cardiac events vs. supportive care.

symptoms: Severe chest/back pain, hypotension, widened mediastinum
treatment_goal: Prevent aortic rupture, achieve hemostasis, repair defect
first_line_soc: None pharmacologic — definitive management is endovascular aortic repair (TEVAR) or open surgical repair; permissive hypotension and heart rate control (e.g., esmolol/labetalol) to reduce aortic wall stress pre-operatively.
treatment_algorithm: None — anti-impulse therapy: IV esmolol or labetalol to maintain SBP <120 mmHg and HR <60 pre-op. TEVAR (thoracic endovascular aortic repair) is preferred for most patients. Open repair (left heart bypass) for cases not suitable for TEVAR. ICU monitoring.
us_prevalence: ~7,500–10,000 traumatic aortic injuries/year in the US; ~2,000 reach hospital alive (AAST NTDB data).
patients_needing_tx: ~2,000 patients/year require operative intervention; ~1,500 receive TEVAR.
price: $15,000–$50,000 per episode for a novel aortic sealant/hemostatic agent adjunct.
registrational_endpoint: 30-day mortality and procedure success rate vs. standard TEVAR alone.
btd_threshold_vs_soc: ≥30% reduction in 30-day mortality vs. TEVAR alone.

symptoms: Severe dyspnea, hypotension, absent breath sounds, tracheal deviation, cardiovascular collapse
treatment_goal: Immediate decompression, restore ventilation and hemodynamics
first_line_soc: None pharmacologic — immediate needle decompression (14g angiocath, 2nd ICS mid-clavicular line or 4th ICS anterior axillary line); followed by chest tube thoracostomy.
treatment_algorithm: None — emergency needle decompression (can be lifesaving within minutes). Definitive chest tube (28–32F). In-hospital: video-thoracoscopy for recurrent cases. No pharmacologic treatment exists. Supportive care.

patients_needing_tx: ~7,000 require emergent decompression.
price: N/A — procedural emergency; no pharmacologic opportunity.

symptoms: Chest pain, dyspnea, absent breath sounds, reduced chest movement
treatment_goal: Re-expand lung, relieve respiratory compromise
first_line_soc: None pharmacologic — chest tube thoracostomy (28–36F) for significant pneumothorax; needle aspiration for small occult pneumothorax.
treatment_algorithm: None — simple: aspiration or observation. Moderate-large: chest tube (28–36F) water-seal drainage. Tension component: immediate needle decompression. VATS for persistent air leak. No pharmacologic treatment. Pleurodesis for recurrent traumatic pneumothorax (doxycycline or talc).
us_prevalence: ~50,000–75,000 traumatic pneumothorax cases/year in the US.
patients_needing_tx: ~50,000 require active pleural intervention.
price: N/A — procedural management; limited pharmacologic opportunity.

symptoms: Chronic pelvic pain, bladder pressure, urinary urgency, frequency, nocturia
treatment_goal: Reduce bladder pain, decrease urinary frequency, improve quality of life
first_line_soc: Pentosan polysulfate sodium (Elmiron; glycosaminoglycan layer replenisher; FDA-approved) 100mg TID. Tricyclic antidepressants (amitriptyline 25–75mg; neuromodulator) as first-line pain agents.
treatment_algorithm: Step 1: Dietary modification (eliminate bladder irritants: coffee, citrus, spicy foods). Physical therapy (pelvic floor). Step 2: Oral agents—pentosan polysulfate (Elmiron) 100mg TID; amitriptyline 10–75mg nightly; hydroxyzine (antihistamine) 25–50mg nightly. Step 3: Bladder instillations—DMSO (dimethyl sulfoxide; FDA-approved); heparin + lidocaine cocktail. Step 4: Cyclosporine A for refractory cases. Intravesical Botox (onabotulinumtoxinA; FDA-approved for OAB but used off-label for IC/BPS). Sacral nerve stimulation. Oral cyclosporine vs. Elmiron: superior but toxic. 2024: Suzetrigine (Nav1.8 inhibitor) for pain component investigated.
us_prevalence: ~3–8 million US women and ~1–4 million US men have IC/BPS; ~12 million total. Significantly underdiagnosed.
patients_needing_tx: ~5–7 million with clinically significant IC/BPS requiring pharmacologic treatment.
price: $10,000–$30,000/year (novel neuromodulator or GAG layer replenisher).
registrational_endpoint: Global Response Assessment (GRA) ≥+2 at 12 weeks (at least 'moderately improved'); ICSI (Interstitial Cystitis Symptom Index) total score reduction ≥30%.
btd_threshold_vs_soc: GRA responder rate ≥50% at 12 weeks vs. ~25–30% with pentosan polysulfate or amitriptyline.

symptoms: Involuntary urine leakage on exertion or urgency, pad use, reduced QoL
treatment_goal: Reduce leakage episodes, restore continence, improve quality of life
first_line_soc: Stress UI (SUI): Pelvic floor exercises (PFMT); duloxetine (SNRI; off-label in US, approved in Europe as Yentreve) for pharmacologic SoC. Urgency UI (UUI): Anticholinergics (oxybutynin) or mirabegron (beta-3 agonist).
treatment_algorithm: SUI: PFMT (most effective first-line); duloxetine 40mg BID (SNRI; increases urethral sphincter tone via pudendal nerve)—approved in EU, off-label in US; surgical mid-urethral sling. UUI: Anticholinergics (oxybutynin, tolterodine, solifenacin, fesoterodine, darifenacin, trospium); beta-3 agonist (mirabegron, vibegron). Mixed: Combined therapies. Refractory UUI: Intravesical Botox 100U (FDA-approved); sacral neuromodulation (InterStim). Overflow incontinence: CIC + alpha-blockers; bethanechol. Topical estrogen for postmenopausal atrophic vaginitis-related incontinence.
us_prevalence: ~37 million US adults have some urinary incontinence; ~25% of women >65 years. Stress UI most common in women; UUI increases with age. Men: ~5–15% post-prostatectomy.
patients_needing_tx: ~20–25 million require some form of treatment; ~10–12 million use pharmacologic agents.
registrational_endpoint: Mean weekly UI episodes reduction from baseline at 12 weeks (UUI); proportion achieving continence (0 UI episodes/day); Incontinence Quality of Life (I-QOL) score improvement.
btd_threshold_vs_soc: ≥70% UI episode responder rate (≥50% reduction) vs. ~45–55% with oxybutynin or mirabegron.

symptoms: Urinary incontinence, retention, frequency, UTIs, inability to void voluntarily
treatment_goal: Maintain safe bladder pressures, prevent UTIs, achieve continence
first_line_soc: Anticholinergics (oxybutynin, tolterodine; muscarinic receptor antagonists) for overactive neurogenic bladder; alpha-1 blockers (tamsulosin) for detrusor-sphincter dyssynergia; clean intermittent catheterization (CIC).
treatment_algorithm: Overactive/spastic neurogenic bladder: Anticholinergics—oxybutynin (Ditropan) 5mg TID or ER 10–30mg/day, tolterodine (Detrol) 2mg BID, solifenacin (VESIcare) 5–10mg/day, trospium. Beta-3 agonists: Mirabegron (Myrbetriq) 25–50mg/day—FDA-approved. Onabotulinumtoxin A (Botox) intravesical 200U—FDA-approved for neurogenic detrusor overactivity. Underactive/retention: CIC + alpha-blockers (tamsulosin); bethanechol (cholinergic agonist). Detrusor-sphincter dyssynergia: CIC + alpha-blockers; baclofen (muscle relaxant) IT for spasticity. Antibiotic prophylaxis for recurrent UTIs.
us_prevalence: ~4–6 million Americans have neurogenic bladder (spinal cord injury ~300,000; MS ~1 million; Parkinson's ~1 million; spina bifida ~200,000; other neurologic conditions).
patients_needing_tx: ~3–4 million require active pharmacologic management.
registrational_endpoint: Mean daily urinary incontinence episodes reduction from baseline at 12 weeks; urodynamic improvement (maximum cystometric capacity increase); UTI-free rate.
btd_threshold_vs_soc: ≥50% reduction in UI episodes/day + ≥30% improvement in max cystometric capacity vs. oxybutynin alone.

symptoms: Persistent inability to achieve or maintain erection for satisfactory sex
treatment_goal: Restore erectile function, improve sexual satisfaction and quality of life
first_line_soc: PDE5 inhibitors: sildenafil (Viagra; 50–100mg PRN) or tadalafil (Cialis; 10–20mg PRN or 5mg daily). Most effective oral first-line agents for most etiologies of ED.
treatment_algorithm: Step 1: PDE5 inhibitors—sildenafil (Viagra), vardenafil (Levitra), tadalafil (Cialis), avanafil (Stendra). Tadalafil 5mg daily for continuous use. Step 2: Testosterone replacement for hypogonadal men (testosterone gel, injections, pellets). Step 3: ICI (intracavernosal injection therapy)—alprostadil (Caverject, MUSE; prostaglandin E1). Combination: papaverine + phentolamine ± alprostadil (Trimix). Step 4: Vacuum erection device. Step 5: Penile prosthesis surgery. 2024 novel: Low-intensity shockwave therapy (not pharmacologic); topical alprostadil cream (Vitaros—approved in Europe).
us_prevalence: ~30 million US men have ED; ~50% of men aged 40–70 report some degree of ED (Massachusetts Male Aging Study).
patients_needing_tx: ~20–25 million seek treatment; ~15 million use PDE5 inhibitors; ~5 million refractory to oral therapy.
price: $2,000–$5,000/year (oral agent; generics have dramatically reduced costs; PDE5i generics ~$300–800/year).
registrational_endpoint: IIEF-EF (International Index of Erectile Function—Erectile Function domain) score improvement ≥4 points from baseline; SEP Q2/Q3 diary responder rate.
btd_threshold_vs_soc: IIEF-EF score ≥26 (normal) in ≥50% at 12 weeks vs. ~30–40% with sildenafil 100mg; or efficacy in PDE5i non-responders.

symptoms: Severe colicky flank pain, hematuria, nausea, vomiting
treatment_goal: Relieve acute pain, facilitate stone passage, prevent recurrence
first_line_soc: NSAIDs (ketorolac 15–30 mg IV/IM or diclofenac) are first-line analgesics for renal colic — superior to opioids for pain control. Alpha-1 blockers (tamsulosin 0.4 mg/day) as medical expulsive therapy (MET) for ureteral stones 5–10 mm.
treatment_algorithm: Acute pain management: NSAIDs (ketorolac, diclofenac) first-line; opioids (morphine IV) for breakthrough; IV fluids for hydration. Medical expulsive therapy (MET): tamsulosin 0.4 mg or nifedipine 30 mg for distal ureteral stones ≤10 mm — increases passage rate by ~30%. Metabolic stone prevention: (1) Calcium oxalate stones: high fluid intake, thiazide diuretics (HCTZ) for hypercalciuria, potassium citrate for hypocitraturia; (2) Uric acid stones: allopurinol, potassium citrate (alkalinize urine); (3) Cystine stones: D-penicillamine or tiopronin, high fluid, alkalinization; (4) Struvite: treat UTI (Proteus); acetohydroxamic acid (AHA) — urease inhibitor. Procedural: SWL for ≤2 cm kidney stones; ureteroscopy + laser lithotripsy; PCNL for >2 cm.
us_prevalence: Lifetime prevalence 12.85% US (Nature 2025); ~1 million hospitalizations/year; recurrence rate ~50% in 5–10 years without prevention. ~3–4 million active stone disease cases at any time in the US.
patients_needing_tx: ~1 million acute stone episodes/year requiring analgesia/MET; ~3 million with recurrent stones requiring chronic prevention therapy. MET-treatable pool: ~500,000/year.
price: $5,000–$15,000/year for a novel stone prevention biologic or targeted small molecule (e.g., oxalate degrading enzyme like ALLN-346, under development). Generic prevention drugs ~$100–$500/year.
tam_gross: ~3 million chronic prevention patients × $8,000/year = ~$24 billion TAM. (3,000,000 × $8,000 = $24,000,000,000)
peak_sales: ~$500 million–$1 billion for a novel superior stone prevention agent. (3,000,000 × 3% × $8,000 = $720M). ALLN-346 (Allena Pharmaceuticals) is an example targeting recurrent calcium oxalate stones.
registrational_endpoint: Stone recurrence rate (imaging-confirmed new stone) at 24 months; 24-hour urine oxalate excretion reduction from baseline for oxalate-targeting agents.
btd_threshold_vs_soc: ≥30% relative reduction in 24-month stone recurrence rate vs. standard care; ≥30% reduction in 24-hour urinary oxalate vs. placebo at 12 months for hyperoxaluric patients.

symptoms: Severe flank pain, hematuria, nausea, vomiting, dysuria, urinary urgency
treatment_goal: Pass or remove stone, relieve pain, prevent recurrence
first_line_soc: Medical expulsive therapy (MET): tamsulosin 0.4mg daily (alpha-1A blocker) to facilitate passage of ureteral stones ≤10mm. NSAIDs (ketorolac, diclofenac; superior to opioids for acute renal colic pain). Opioids for rescue analgesia.
treatment_algorithm: Acute management: NSAIDs (ketorolac 30mg IV, diclofenac 75mg IM) + antiemetics + hydration. MET: Tamsulosin 0.4mg × 4 weeks for stones 5–10mm. Interventional: ESWL (shock wave lithotripsy) for stones >10mm; ureteroscopy with laser lithotripsy for impacted stones. Prevention: Hydration + stone-specific prevention (thiazide diuretics [HCTZ] for hypercalciuric; allopurinol for uric acid; alkalinization [sodium bicarbonate/potassium citrate] for uric acid/cystine; D-penicillamine/tiopronin for cystine; low-oxalate diet for calcium oxalate). Novel: Reloxaliase (enzyme for primary oxaluria) and ALLN-177 in trials.
us_prevalence: ~11% of US adults have kidney stones; ~600,000 ED visits/year (most common urologic emergency); prevalence rising (~3.8 million symptomatic in 2024).
patients_needing_tx: ~1.5–2 million stone episodes/year requiring pharmacologic management; ~3 million recurrent stone patients needing preventive therapy.
price: $3,000–$10,000/year (preventive agent).
registrational_endpoint: Stone passage rate within 4 weeks (for acute MET); stone recurrence rate at 2 years (for preventive agents); 24-hour urine lithogenic risk factor normalization.
btd_threshold_vs_soc: Stone passage rate ≥80% at 4 weeks vs. ~55–65% with tamsulosin alone; or ≥50% reduction in 2-year stone recurrence vs. hydration alone.

symptoms: Flank pain, decreased urine output, hydronephrosis, recurrent UTIs, renal impairment
treatment_goal: Relieve obstruction, preserve renal function, prevent infection
first_line_soc: Depends on cause: Ureteral stones—alpha-1 blockers (tamsulosin 0.4mg; medical expulsive therapy) for stones ≤10mm. Obstruction relief (stent, nephrostomy) for upper tract obstruction. Antibiotics for concurrent infection.
treatment_algorithm: Ureteral stone: Alpha-1 blockers (tamsulosin)—increases stone passage rate by ~25% for stones ≤10mm. Nonsteroidal anti-inflammatories for pain. BPH: Alpha-blockers + 5-ARIs (see BPH entry). Posterior urethral valves/stricture: Surgical dilation/incision. Retroperitoneal fibrosis: Corticosteroids (prednisone) + ureteral stenting; tamoxifen for hormone-responsive fibrosis. Prostate cancer obstruction: ADT + alpha-blockers. Ureteral stenting or percutaneous nephrostomy for acute decompression.
us_prevalence: ~2–3 million US patients with obstructive uropathy annually (BPH-related most common ~14 million; stones ~500,000 hospitalizations/year).
patients_needing_tx: ~1.5–2 million requiring active pharmacologic treatment.
registrational_endpoint: Stone passage rate within 4 weeks (for ureteral obstruction); time to renal function normalization; IPSS improvement at 12 weeks.
btd_threshold_vs_soc: Stone passage rate ≥80% at 4 weeks vs. ~55–65% with tamsulosin MET; or IPSS reduction ≥8 in BPH-related obstruction.

symptoms: Weak urine stream, frequency, urgency, nocturia, incomplete bladder emptying
treatment_goal: Relieve LUTS, improve urinary flow, prevent urinary retention
first_line_soc: Alpha-1 blockers: tamsulosin (Flomax; alpha-1A adrenergic antagonist) 0.4mg daily or silodosin (Rapaflo)—relax prostate smooth muscle, fastest symptom relief. 5-alpha reductase inhibitors (5-ARIs): finasteride (Proscar) or dutasteride (Avodart) for large prostates (>30–40mL).
treatment_algorithm: Step 1: Alpha-1 blockers—tamsulosin 0.4mg/day (Flomax), alfuzosin (Uroxatral), doxazosin (Cardura), silodosin (Rapaflo). Step 2: 5-ARIs—finasteride 5mg/day (Proscar) or dutasteride 0.5mg/day (Avodart) for larger prostates; reduce prostate size by ~20–30% over 6 months. Combination: Alpha-1 blocker + 5-ARI (Jalyn = dutasteride + tamsulosin) for high-risk progression. Step 3: PDE5 inhibitors—tadalafil 5mg/day (Cialis; FDA-approved for BPH + ED). Anticholinergics/beta-3 agonists (mirabegron, solifenacin) for overactive bladder component. Surgical: TURP, HoLEP, Urolift for refractory cases. 2024: Noveome/platelet-rich plasma studies ongoing.
us_prevalence: ~14 million American men with symptomatic BPH requiring treatment; histologic prevalence >80% of men over 70. LUTS affects ~50% of men >60.
patients_needing_tx: ~10–14 million men require pharmacologic management; ~1 million/year surgical interventions.
price: $3,000–$8,000/year (novel disease-modifying or combination agent).
registrational_endpoint: IPSS (International Prostate Symptom Score) total score reduction from baseline at 12 weeks; maximum urinary flow rate (Qmax) improvement.
btd_threshold_vs_soc: Mean IPSS reduction ≥6 points vs. ~5 points with tamsulosin alone; or Qmax improvement ≥3 mL/s + IPSS ≥6 at 12 weeks.

disease: Prostatitis (Etiology, Classification, Symptoms and Signs, Diagnosis, Treatment)
symptoms: Pelvic pain, dysuria, urinary frequency, perineal discomfort
treatment_goal: Eradicate infection (bacterial); reduce chronic pelvic pain (CPPS)
first_line_soc: Category I/II (Acute/Chronic Bacterial Prostatitis): fluoroquinolones (levofloxacin 500 mg/day × 4–6 weeks or ciprofloxacin 500 mg BID × 4–6 weeks) are first-line — excellent prostate tissue penetration. Category III (CPPS/CNBP): alpha-blockers (tamsulosin) + NSAIDs as first-line empirical therapy.
treatment_algorithm: NIH Classification: (1) Category I (ABP): IV fluoroquinolone or cephalosporin × 2–4 weeks then oral × 4–6 weeks total; Foley catheter for retention; hospitalization for sepsis; (2) Category II (CBP): oral levofloxacin or ciprofloxacin × 4–6 weeks (minimum; PubMed 2025); alternative if resistant: fosfomycin or TMP-SMX; (3) Category III (CPPS): alpha-blockers (tamsulosin, silodosin); NSAIDs; tadalafil 5 mg/day (improved blood flow, Harvard Health 2025); quercetin (anti-inflammatory supplement, modest evidence); pollen extract (Cernilton); multimodal: physiotherapy (pelvic floor), acupuncture, CBT; (4) Category IV (Asymptomatic): no treatment. Antibiotics not effective for CPPS (non-bacterial).
us_prevalence: Prostatitis accounts for ~8% of urologist visits; ~2 million outpatient visits/year (CDC/AUA). Lifetime prevalence in men ~10–16%. CPPS (Cat III) is most common (~95% of prostatitis). CBP (Cat II) ~8% of prostatitis cases.
patients_needing_tx: ~2 million prostatitis-related treatment episodes/year; ~1.5 million with CPPS needing ongoing management. Pharmacotherapy-seeking: ~1.5–2 million.
price: $10,000–$25,000/year for a novel CPPS-targeted agent (e.g., cannabinoid receptor modulator, mast cell stabilizer, interleukin inhibitor). Antibiotics generic; alpha-blockers generic.
peak_sales: ~$600 million–$1.2 billion for a novel CPPS-targeted agent at 4–6% share. (1,500,000 × 5% × $15,000 = $1.125B; realistic ~$600M–$1B). High unmet need in CPPS.
registrational_endpoint: National Institutes of Health Chronic Prostatitis Symptom Index (NIH-CPSI) total score reduction from baseline at 12 weeks; ≥6-point improvement as responder threshold.
btd_threshold_vs_soc: ≥6-point NIH-CPSI total score reduction in ≥40% of patients vs. ≤20% placebo at 12 weeks; sustained response at 24 weeks in ≥30% vs. ≤15% placebo.

symptoms: Pelvic pain, dysuria, urinary frequency, urgency, perineal discomfort
treatment_goal: Eradicate infection (if bacterial), relieve pelvic pain, improve urinary symptoms
first_line_soc: Acute bacterial: IV ciprofloxacin or fluoroquinolone × 2–4 weeks (culture-directed); IV broad-spectrum antibiotics for severe/hospitalized. Chronic bacterial: Oral ciprofloxacin 500mg BID × 4–6 weeks or levofloxacin 500mg × 4–6 weeks.
treatment_algorithm: Acute bacterial: IV ciprofloxacin or ampicillin + gentamicin; oral fluoroquinolone after clinical improvement. Chronic bacterial: Fluoroquinolone × 4–6 weeks (ciprofloxacin or levofloxacin). Chronic pelvic pain syndrome (CP/CPPS—most common category): Multi-modal: alpha-blockers (tamsulosin; reduces urethral/bladder neck spasm), 5-ARIs for large prostate, NSAID (diclofenac), quercetin (antioxidant), antibiotics trial × 4 weeks, muscle relaxants, neuromodulators (amitriptyline, pregabalin), pelvic PT.
us_prevalence: ~2 million physician visits/year for prostatitis in the US; ~35–50% of men experience prostatitis symptoms at some point. Chronic prostatitis/CPPS: ~10–15% of adult males.
patients_needing_tx: ~2 million require pharmacologic management annually.
price: $5,000–$15,000/year (novel anti-inflammatory or neuromodulator).
registrational_endpoint: NIH-CPSI (Chronic Prostatitis Symptom Index) total score reduction ≥6 points from baseline at 12 weeks; symptom responder rate.
btd_threshold_vs_soc: NIH-CPSI responder rate (≥6 point improvement) ≥50% at 12 weeks vs. ~30% with tamsulosin + NSAID combination.

symptoms: Severe pelvic pain, fever, urinary retention, perineal swelling, sepsis signs
treatment_goal: Drain abscess, eradicate infection, prevent septicemia and recurrence
