first_line_soc: IV broad-spectrum antibiotics (3rd-generation cephalosporin + metronidazole, or fluoroquinolone-based for uncomplicated cases) plus urgent surgical drainage (transrectal ultrasound-guided aspiration or transurethral drainage).
treatment_algorithm: Combined medical-surgical approach: (1) Hospitalization + IV antibiotics: ceftriaxone 1–2 g IV BID ± metronidazole 500 mg IV TID; or piperacillin-tazobactam for polymicrobial/resistant risk; (2) Drainage: TRUS-guided aspiration (preferred, minimally invasive) or transurethral unroofing; (3) Step-down to oral fluoroquinolone (ciprofloxacin 500 mg BID or levofloxacin 500 mg/day) × 4–6 weeks post-drainage; (4) Urinary catheter for acute retention; (5) Blood and urine cultures to guide antibiotic therapy; (6) Investigation for underlying immunosuppression (diabetes, HIV). Small abscesses (<1–2 cm) may respond to antibiotics alone.
us_prevalence: Prostate abscess is uncommon; estimated ~2,000–5,000 cases/year in the US, mainly in men with acute bacterial prostatitis, diabetes, or immunosuppression. True prevalence difficult to establish — often a complication of ABP.
patients_needing_tx: ~2,000–5,000 requiring acute inpatient antibiotic + drainage treatment annually. Entirely generic antibiotic market; no novel drug opportunity specific to this rare indication.
price: $5,000–$15,000/course for novel antibiotics active against MDR organisms (biofilm-penetrating). Market opportunity is as part of broader complicated UTI/prostatitis labeling.
peak_sales: ~$5–10 million specific to prostate abscess. Broader complicated prostatitis/UTI indication: $200–$500 million.
registrational_endpoint: Abscess resolution on imaging (TRUS) at Day 14; clinical cure (temperature <37.8°C, pain resolution) at Day 14.
btd_threshold_vs_soc: ≥80% complete abscess resolution on imaging at Day 14 with novel antibiotic ± drainage vs. ≤60% with standard ceftriaxone-based regimen, in MDR Gram-negative prostate abscess.

symptoms: Scrotal or testicular pain, variable onset, may radiate to groin or abdomen
treatment_goal: Identify emergency (torsion, abscess), relieve pain, treat underlying cause
first_line_soc: Analgesics (NSAIDs, acetaminophen) for pain relief. Antibiotics for infectious causes (epididymitis, orchitis). Emergency surgical exploration for testicular torsion.
treatment_algorithm: Acute scrotal pain: Emergency workup (Doppler ultrasound) to rule out torsion. NSAIDs (ibuprofen, ketorolac IV) for analgesia. Antibiotics for epididymo-orchitis (see epididymitis). Chronic scrotal pain: Multi-modal—NSAIDs, tricyclics (amitriptyline), spermatic cord block with bupivacaine, pregabalin/gabapentin, microvascular surgery for varicocele, epididymectomy for chronic epididymitis.
us_prevalence: ~300,000–500,000 men/year present with acute scrotal pain requiring evaluation; chronic scrotal pain: ~100,000–200,000.
patients_needing_tx: ~400,000/year requiring pharmacologic management.
price: $1,000–$5,000 per course or $5,000–$15,000/year for chronic.
registrational_endpoint: NRS pain score reduction ≥50% at 4 weeks for chronic scrotal pain; proportion achieving pain-free status.
btd_threshold_vs_soc: ≥50% pain responder rate at 4 weeks vs. ~25% with NSAIDs alone.

symptoms: Scrotal pain, swelling, tenderness behind testicle, dysuria, fever
treatment_goal: Eradicate causative infection, relieve pain, prevent chronic complications
first_line_soc: <35 years (STI): Ceftriaxone 500mg IM single dose + doxycycline 100mg BID × 10 days. >35 years (enteric organisms): Ofloxacin 300mg BID × 10 days or levofloxacin 500mg × 10 days.
treatment_algorithm: STI-associated (gonorrhea/chlamydia): Ceftriaxone 500mg IM + doxycycline 100mg BID × 10 days (CDC 2021 guidelines). Enteric organism-associated (older men, recent urologic procedures): Levofloxacin 500mg × 10 days or ofloxacin 300mg BID × 10 days. Adjuncts: NSAIDs (ibuprofen) for scrotal elevation + pain. Abscess: Surgical drainage. Chronic epididymitis: NSAIDs, doxycycline, nerve block, spermatic cord block.
us_prevalence: ~600,000 cases/year in the US; predominantly in men aged 19–35 (CDC).
patients_needing_tx: ~600,000/year requiring antibiotic treatment.
price: $500–$3,000 per course.
registrational_endpoint: Clinical cure (scrotal pain resolution + normalization of exam) at day 14; STI eradication rate.
btd_threshold_vs_soc: Clinical cure ≥95% at day 14 vs. ~85% with ceftriaxone + doxycycline standard course.

symptoms: Painless scrotal swelling or mass, hydrocele, spermatocele, varicocele, tumor
treatment_goal: Diagnose cause (benign vs. malignant), treat if symptomatic or malignant
first_line_soc: Workup (ultrasound, tumor markers): Testicular cancer—radical orchiectomy + cisplatin-based chemotherapy (BEP: bleomycin, etoposide, cisplatin) for malignant tumors. Benign (hydrocele, spermatocele): Observation or surgical aspiration.
treatment_algorithm: Testicular cancer (GCT—seminoma/NSGCT): Post-orchiectomy—BEP (bleomycin + etoposide + cisplatin) chemotherapy for Stage II/III. Surveillance for Stage I. TIP (paclitaxel + ifosfamide + cisplatin) for relapsed GCT. Retroperitoneal lymph node dissection (RPLND). Hydrocele: Observation; aspiration + sclerotherapy; surgical repair. Varicocele: Surgical ligation or embolization. Epididymal cysts/spermatocele: Observation.
us_prevalence: ~10,000 new testicular cancer cases/year in the US (SEER data 2024); ~1 million men with hydrocele; ~15% of male infertility related to varicocele.
patients_needing_tx: Testicular cancer: ~10,000/year needing chemotherapy or surgery. Benign scrotal masses: ~500,000/year requiring evaluation.
price: $50,000–$100,000 per chemotherapy course; rare indication premium for refractory GCT.
registrational_endpoint: Complete response rate at cycle 4 (BEP) for Stage II/III GCT; 5-year relapse-free survival.
btd_threshold_vs_soc: Complete response ≥90% in Stage II seminoma vs. ~85% with BEP × 4 cycles; or superior outcomes in cisplatin-refractory GCT.

symptoms: Testicular pain, swelling, redness, fever, nausea
treatment_goal: Eradicate infection, reduce inflammation, preserve fertility
first_line_soc: Bacterial orchitis: Antibiotics based on age/etiology—same as epididymitis (see above). Viral mumps orchitis: Supportive only (NSAIDs, scrotal support); no antiviral proven effective.
treatment_algorithm: Bacterial (usually orchioepididymitis): STI-related (<35): Ceftriaxone IM + doxycycline × 10 days. Enteric (>35): Levofloxacin 500mg × 10 days. Viral (mumps): NSAIDs + scrotal support; interferon alpha-2b studied but not standard. Autoimmune: Corticosteroids. Adjuncts: NSAIDs (ibuprofen 400–600mg TID), analgesics, scrotal elevation.
us_prevalence: ~20,000–30,000 cases/year in the US (bacterial); mumps orchitis rare post-vaccination era (~1,000–2,000 breakthrough cases/year).
patients_needing_tx: ~25,000/year requiring antibiotic or supportive pharmacologic treatment.
price: $500–$2,000 per course.
registrational_endpoint: Clinical cure (pain resolution + scrotal swelling regression) at day 14; testicular function preservation at 6 months.
btd_threshold_vs_soc: Clinical cure ≥90% at day 14 vs. ~80% with standard antibiotic regimen.

symptoms: Sudden severe scrotal pain, high-riding testicle, nausea, vomiting, absent cremasteric reflex
treatment_goal: Emergent manual or surgical detorsion within 6 hours to preserve testicle
first_line_soc: Emergent surgical exploration and detorsion + bilateral orchiopexy (within 6 hours for >90% salvage rate). Manual detorsion (outward rotation) may serve as bridge to surgery. IV opioid analgesics for pain.
treatment_algorithm: IV opioid analgesics (morphine, hydromorphone) for pain management as bridge to surgery. Emergent surgical detorsion and bilateral orchiopexy. Pre-operative: IV fluids, NPO. Post-operative: NSAIDs (ibuprofen), antibiotics if any contamination. No pharmacologic agent can replace surgical exploration. Time is testicle.
us_prevalence: ~1 per 4,000 males <25 years; ~4,500–6,000 cases/year in the US. Surgical emergency.
patients_needing_tx: ~5,000 cases/year requiring emergent surgical management + analgesics.
price: $500–$2,000 per episode (ischemia-protecting agent as adjunct to surgery).
registrational_endpoint: Testicular salvage rate (viable testis on Doppler at 6 months); testicular atrophy rate at 12 months.
btd_threshold_vs_soc: Salvage rate ≥95% when drug administered within 6 hours vs. ~80–90% with surgery alone at 6 hours.

symptoms: Penile plaque, painful erections, penile curvature, erectile dysfunction
treatment_goal: Reduce curvature, relieve pain, restore erectile function and sexual activity
first_line_soc: Collagenase clostridium histolyticum (Xiaflex; intralesional collagenase; FDA-approved 2013) for men with Peyronie's disease with curvature ≥30°. Combined with manual modeling.
treatment_algorithm: Acute phase (pain): NSAIDs; vitamin E (antioxidant; limited evidence); oral potassium para-aminobenzoate (Potaba) — not commercially available in US; colchicine (anti-inflammatory). Chronic phase (curvature): Intralesional collagenase clostridium histolyticum (Xiaflex; FDA-approved): 4 treatment cycles × 3 injections + modeling. Verapamil intralesional injection (calcium channel blocker). Extracorporeal shockwave therapy (ESWT). Surgical: penile plication, plaque incision/grafting, prosthesis for severe curvature + ED. 2024: Penile traction therapy (PTT) + collagenase combination studied.
us_prevalence: ~700,000–1 million US men with Peyronie's disease (~3% of men >40); many underreport.
patients_needing_tx: ~400,000–500,000 men with clinically significant curvature requiring treatment.
price: $20,000–$40,000 per treatment course (collagenase injections ~$30,000 for full course currently).
registrational_endpoint: Penile curvature reduction (degrees) from baseline at week 24; Peyronie's Disease Questionnaire (PDQ) bother score improvement.
btd_threshold_vs_soc: Mean curvature reduction ≥35% from baseline at week 24 vs. ~20–34% with collagenase injection alone; or ≥50% curvature reduction in 40% of patients.

symptoms: Weak urinary stream, straining, frequency, incomplete emptying, recurrent UTIs
treatment_goal: Restore urethral patency, improve voiding function, prevent recurrence
first_line_soc: Endoscopic direct vision internal urethrotomy (DVIU) or urethral dilation—first-line procedural SoC. Alpha-blockers (tamsulosin) for symptomatic relief. No pharmacologic agent dissolves strictures.
treatment_algorithm: Endoscopic: DVIU (incision under direct vision) or bougie dilation—~50% recurrence at 1 year. Optical dilation with catheter. Pharmacologic adjunct post-DVIU: Intraurethral mitomycin C (chemotherapy agent to reduce scar recurrence) has shown benefit in trials. Urethroplasty (surgical) for recurrent strictures—superior long-term outcomes. Alpha-blockers for symptomatic relief. Clean intermittent self-catheterization (CISC) for maintenance.
us_prevalence: ~600,000–800,000 US males with urethral strictures; ~100,000–150,000 DVIU or dilations/year.
patients_needing_tx: ~600,000 active cases; ~150,000/year requiring procedural + pharmacologic management.
price: $5,000–$15,000 per treatment course (anti-fibrotic injection or novel post-DVIU drug).
registrational_endpoint: Stricture recurrence rate at 1 year post-DVIU (urethrography-confirmed); maximum flow rate (Qmax) maintenance.
btd_threshold_vs_soc: Recurrence-free rate ≥70% at 1 year vs. ~50% with DVIU alone; or Qmax ≥15 mL/s maintained in ≥75% at 1 year.

symptoms: Penile skin changes: plaques, papules, ulcers, pigmentation, rash
treatment_goal: Diagnose and treat underlying cause (infection, inflammatory, premalignant)
first_line_soc: Depends on etiology: STI lesions—pathogen-specific antibiotics/antivirals; lichen sclerosus—clobetasol 0.05% topical (potent corticosteroid); genital warts—topical imiquimod (immune modifier) or podophyllotoxin; premalignant (PeIN)—topical 5-fluorouracil or imiquimod.
treatment_algorithm: Lichen sclerosus: Topical clobetasol 0.05% BID × 3 months → maintenance. Genital warts (HPV): Topical imiquimod 5% (Aldara; immune modifier) TID × up to 16 weeks; or topical podophyllotoxin 0.5% BID × 3 days/week × 4 weeks; sinecatechins (Veregen; green tea extract) 15% ointment TID—FDA approved for genital warts. PeIN (premalignant): Topical 5-FU or imiquimod; photodynamic therapy; surgical excision. Psoriasis: Mild-moderate topical steroids. Contact dermatitis: Avoidance + topical steroids.
us_prevalence: ~1–3 million US males with clinically significant cutaneous penile lesions annually (broad category); genital warts: ~1 million new cases/year; lichen sclerosus: ~300,000.
patients_needing_tx: ~1.5–2 million cases/year requiring pharmacologic management.
registrational_endpoint: Complete clearance of lesions at 16 weeks (for genital warts); clinical remission (Physician Global Assessment ≤1) at 12 weeks.
btd_threshold_vs_soc: Complete clearance ≥65% at 16 weeks vs. ~50% with imiquimod for genital warts; or ≥80% for lichen sclerosus vs. ~60% with clobetasol.

symptoms: Penile glans redness, swelling, discharge, itching, pain
treatment_goal: Eradicate infection, relieve inflammation, prevent recurrence
first_line_soc: Topical antifungals (clotrimazole 1% cream; azole antifungal) for candidal balanitis. Topical antibiotics (fusidic acid or mupirocin) or oral antibiotics (amoxicillin-clavulanate) for bacterial balanitis.
treatment_algorithm: Candidal: Topical clotrimazole 1% BID × 7 days; severe/recurrent: fluconazole 150mg PO single dose. Bacterial: topical fusidic acid or oral penicillin/amoxicillin-clavulanate. Anaerobes (Zoon's balanitis, plasma cell): topical mild steroids (hydrocortisone 1%) + gentle hygiene. Contact dermatitis: topical hydrocortisone, eliminate irritant. Lichen sclerosus (BXO): Topical potent steroids (clobetasol 0.05%). Circinate balanitis (reactive arthritis): topical steroids. Recurrent candidal balanitis: circumcision reduces risk.
us_prevalence: ~1–3% of uncircumcised males; ~3,000,000–6,000,000 US males affected. Annual incidence of episodes: ~2–3 million.
patients_needing_tx: ~2–3 million episodes/year requiring pharmacologic treatment.
price: $200–$1,000 per course (novel topical agent).
registrational_endpoint: Clinical cure (resolution of erythema, discharge, symptoms) at day 14 vs. clotrimazole for candidal balanitis.
btd_threshold_vs_soc: Clinical cure in ≥95% at day 14 vs. ~85% with clotrimazole 7-day course.

symptoms: Penile glans/foreskin inflammation, pain, discharge, redness
treatment_goal: Resolve infection/inflammation, prevent recurrence and complications
first_line_soc: Topical antifungals (clotrimazole 1% cream) for Candida balanitis (most common cause); topical antibiotics (fusidic acid or mupirocin) or oral antibiotics for bacterial causes. Topical mild corticosteroids for inflammatory (non-infectious) balanitis.
treatment_algorithm: Determine etiology: (1) Candida (most common, especially in diabetics/uncircumcised): clotrimazole 1% or miconazole cream BID × 1–2 weeks; if recurrent, oral fluconazole 150 mg single dose; (2) Bacterial: topical fusidic acid or mupirocin; oral amoxicillin-clavulanate or metronidazole for anaerobic mix; (3) STI-related (gonococcal, trichomonal): treat per STI guidelines; (4) Inflammatory/contact dermatitis: topical hydrocortisone 1% ± emollients; (5) Lichen sclerosus/Zoon's: topical clobetasol 0.05% (potent corticosteroid); (6) Hygiene measures: gentle cleaning, avoid irritants; circumcision for recurrent cases.
us_prevalence: Balanitis affects ~11% of urology clinic attendees; estimated 1–2 million cases/year in uncircumcised US males. Exact US prevalence 2025 data limited; global prevalence ~3% of uncircumcised males.
patients_needing_tx: ~1–1.5 million cases/year requiring pharmacologic treatment (topical antifungal/antibiotic). Most treated with OTC or low-cost generic topicals; <100,000 require systemic therapy.
price: $500–$2,000/course for a novel topical antifungal/anti-inflammatory combination. Market is generic-dominated.
peak_sales: ~$50–100 million for a novel superior combination topical. (1,000,000 × 5% × $1,000 = $50M).
registrational_endpoint: Clinical cure rate (resolution of signs/symptoms) at Day 14; mycological cure for Candida balanitis (negative culture).
btd_threshold_vs_soc: ≥15% improvement in clinical cure rate vs. clotrimazole 1% at Day 14; ≥80% mycological cure at Day 14 vs. ≤65% with comparator.

symptoms: Inflammation of glans and foreskin, pain, discharge, phimosis risk
treatment_goal: Eradicate dual glans/foreskin infection, prevent phimosis progression
first_line_soc: Topical antifungal (clotrimazole) + topical antibiotic (fusidic acid) combination; oral antibiotics for severe bacterial cases.
treatment_algorithm: Same approach as balanitis but including foreskin. Mild: Topical clotrimazole (antifungal) + topical fusidic acid (antibacterial). Moderate-severe bacterial: Oral amoxicillin-clavulanate or cephalexin × 7 days. If candidal: Fluconazole 150mg PO single dose. Recurrent: Circumcision to prevent ongoing infections. Associated phimosis: Topical clobetasol 0.05% BID × 4–6 weeks (prepuce stretching).
us_prevalence: ~2–5% of uncircumcised males; ~1–2 million cases/year requiring treatment. Significant in diabetic patients.
patients_needing_tx: ~1–2 million cases/year requiring pharmacologic management.
price: $200–$1,000 per course.
registrational_endpoint: Clinical cure (resolution of inflammation, discharge) at day 14 vs. combination topical SoC.
btd_threshold_vs_soc: Clinical cure in ≥95% at day 14 vs. ~80–85% with combination topical therapy.

symptoms: Inability to retract foreskin (phimosis); painful trapped foreskin (paraphimosis emergency)
treatment_goal: Restore foreskin mobility; emergency reduction of paraphimosis
first_line_soc: Phimosis: topical high-potency corticosteroid (betamethasone 0.05% or clobetasol 0.05% cream BID × 4–8 weeks) combined with gentle stretching — first-line non-surgical. Paraphimosis: manual reduction ± osmotic agents (granulated sugar, ice) in ER.
treatment_algorithm: Phimosis (non-emergency): (1) Topical betamethasone 0.05% or clobetasol 0.05% BID × 6–8 weeks with gentle stretching — 70–90% success; (2) Preputioplasty or dorsal slit if steroids fail; (3) Circumcision for definitive cure or recurrent/complicated phimosis. Pathologic phimosis (lichen sclerosus): topical clobetasol 0.05%; consider circumcision. Paraphimosis (urologic emergency): (1) Lubrication and manual compression/reduction; (2) Topical anesthetic (EMLA cream) for comfort; (3) Osmotic therapy: granulated sugar applied 20–30 min to reduce edema; (4) Hyaluronidase injection into edematous tissue; (5) Dorsal slit under local anesthesia if manual reduction fails; (6) Elective circumcision after resolution.
us_prevalence: Phimosis affects ~1% of adult men and up to 8% of prepubertal boys; ~1–2 million US males with clinically significant phimosis. Paraphimosis ~100,000 emergency presentations/year. US circumcision rate declining, increasing future burden.
patients_needing_tx: ~500,000–800,000 phimosis patients annually requiring pharmacologic (topical steroid) or surgical treatment; ~100,000 paraphimosis cases requiring emergency management.
price: $500–$2,000/course for a novel superior topical agent. Market dominated by generic topical steroids.
peak_sales: ~$30–60 million for a novel improved topical. (600,000 × 5% × $1,000 = $30M). Very small commercial market.
registrational_endpoint: Proportion with full foreskin retractability at 8 weeks; reduction in phimosis severity grade (0–3 validated scale).
btd_threshold_vs_soc: ≥70% complete retractability at Week 8 vs. ≤50% with betamethasone 0.05% standard; ≥30% reduction in recurrence rate at 12-month follow-up.

symptoms: Inability to retract foreskin, pain with erection, difficulty urinating, recurrent balanitis
treatment_goal: Restore foreskin retractability, relieve symptoms, prevent complications
first_line_soc: Topical potent corticosteroid (betamethasone 0.05% or clobetasol 0.05% cream; BID × 4–8 weeks) applied to tight prepuce with gentle stretching exercises—~75–85% success rate.
treatment_algorithm: Step 1: Topical betamethasone or clobetasol 0.05% BID × 4–8 weeks + manual stretching—highly effective for pathologic phimosis. Step 2: Surgical—preputioplasty or circumcision for cases failing steroid therapy or for recurrence. BXO (balanitis xerotica obliterans/lichen sclerosus): More aggressive topical steroids + circumcision often required.
us_prevalence: Pathologic phimosis (BXO): ~2–3% of uncircumcised males; ~500,000–1 million US males. Physiologic phimosis resolves with age in children.
patients_needing_tx: ~300,000–500,000 adults with pathologic phimosis requiring topical steroid therapy or surgery.
price: $500–$2,000 per course (topical fibrotic remodeling agent).
registrational_endpoint: Complete foreskin retractability at week 8 (clinical examination grading by Kikiros/Beaugé scale).
btd_threshold_vs_soc: Complete retractability in ≥90% at 8 weeks vs. ~75–85% with clobetasol.

symptoms: Foreskin inflammation, redness, swelling, discharge, pain
treatment_goal: Eradicate infection, reduce inflammation, prevent recurrence
first_line_soc: Same approach as balanoposthitis (foreskin-only inflammation): topical antifungals (clotrimazole) for candidal; topical/oral antibiotics for bacterial. Hygiene measures paramount.
treatment_algorithm: Candidal posthitis: Clotrimazole 1% cream BID × 7 days; fluconazole 150mg PO × 1 for severe. Bacterial: Topical fusidic acid or oral amoxicillin-clavulanate. Atopic/contact dermatitis: Mild topical corticosteroid + emollient. Recurrent: Circumcision most effective preventive measure.
us_prevalence: ~500,000–1 million episodes/year in uncircumcised males in the US.
patients_needing_tx: ~700,000/year requiring pharmacologic treatment.
price: $200–$800 per course.
registrational_endpoint: Clinical cure (resolution of erythema and discharge) at day 14 vs. standard topical antifungal/antibacterial.
btd_threshold_vs_soc: Clinical cure ≥95% at day 14 vs. ~85% with clotrimazole or fusidic acid.

symptoms: Prolonged painful erection (>4 hours) unrelated to sexual stimulation
treatment_goal: Detumescence within 4–6 hours to prevent ischemic injury and ED
first_line_soc: Intracavernosal injection of sympathomimetic: phenylephrine (100–200mcg every 3–5 min; alpha-1 agonist) is first-line pharmacologic treatment per AUA guidelines.
treatment_algorithm: Ischemic (low-flow) priapism: Step 1—Oral pseudoephedrine or terbutaline for early/mild cases. Step 2—Intracavernosal phenylephrine 100–500mcg (AUA first-line; alpha-1 agonist). Step 3—Aspiration + saline irrigation of cavernosa. Step 4—Surgical shunt for refractory. Sickle cell-related: Opioid analgesics + IV fluids + oxygen; exchange transfusion; etilefrine. Stuttering priapism: Oral PDE5 inhibitors (counterintuitive but reduce NO-triggered recurrences), antiandrogens (ketoconazole, flutamide), digoxin, baclofen. Drug-induced (cocaine, antipsychotics): Discontinue + phenylephrine.
us_prevalence: ~1.5 cases/100,000 person-years; ~5,000–10,000 cases/year in the US requiring ED treatment. Higher in sickle cell disease patients.
patients_needing_tx: ~8,000 episodes/year requiring urgent pharmacologic management.
price: $1,000–$5,000 per episode.
registrational_endpoint: Detumescence rate within 60 minutes of intracavernosal injection; time to detumescence.
btd_threshold_vs_soc: Detumescence in ≥90% within 60 min vs. ~75% with phenylephrine alone; or preserved erectile function at 6 months ≥80% vs. ~60% with current SoC.

symptoms: Foreskin retracted and trapped behind glans, painful swelling, emergency
treatment_goal: Reduce paraphimosis urgently, restore foreskin position, prevent ischemia
first_line_soc: Manual reduction (osmotic compression: ice, sugar, hypertonic solution) + topical lidocaine for pain. Dorsal slit or emergency circumcision if manual reduction fails.
treatment_algorithm: Immediate: Topical lidocaine gel (anesthetic) + ice pack to reduce edema. Osmotic dehydration (granulated sugar or 50% dextrose applied to foreskin). Manual reduction by squeezing glans distally. Hyaluronidase injection to reduce edema (Wydase; enzyme). If reduction fails: Dorsal slit procedure (surgical emergency) under local anesthetic. Definitive: Elective circumcision to prevent recurrence.
us_prevalence: ~5,000–10,000 cases/year requiring ED management in the US.
patients_needing_tx: ~8,000/year requiring pharmacologic + procedural management.
price: $100–$500 per episode (novel osmotic or enzymatic agent).
registrational_endpoint: Successful manual reduction rate without surgical intervention within 30 minutes of drug application.
btd_threshold_vs_soc: Successful manual reduction in ≥90% vs. ~70% with sugar/ice alone.

symptoms: Excessive daytime urination (>8×/day), urgency, disrupted sleep (nocturia)
treatment_goal: Reduce voiding frequency, relieve urgency, improve quality of sleep
first_line_soc: Anticholinergics (oxybutynin; tolterodine; muscarinic receptor antagonists) or beta-3 agonists (mirabegron [Myrbetriq]; FDA-approved) as first-line for overactive bladder-related frequency.
treatment_algorithm: Behavioral therapy: Bladder training, fluid restriction, evening fluid restriction for nocturia. Pharmacologic: Antimuscarinic agents—oxybutynin 5–10mg TID (or ER), tolterodine (Detrol LA) 4mg, solifenacin (VESIcare) 5–10mg, fesoterodine (Toviaz) 4–8mg, trospium, darifenacin. Beta-3 agonist: Mirabegron (Myrbetriq) 25–50mg daily. Combination: Mirabegron + solifenacin (Vibegron/Veloxis—vibegron approved 2020). Nocturia-specific: Desmopressin (Noctiva—low-dose nasal spray; FDA-approved 2017 for nocturia in adults). Refractory OAB: Intravesical Botox 100U; sacral neuromodulation.
us_prevalence: ~33 million US adults with overactive bladder (OAB) causing urinary frequency; ~12–13% of US adults.
patients_needing_tx: ~15–20 million seeking pharmacologic treatment; ~8–10 million on prescription agents.
registrational_endpoint: Mean number of micturitions/24h reduction from baseline at 12 weeks; responder rate (≥3 fewer voids/24h); urge urinary incontinence episodes/day.
btd_threshold_vs_soc: ≥3 fewer voids/24h in ≥60% vs. ~40–45% with tolterodine alone; or superior in dry-mouth-intolerant patients.

symptoms: Painful or burning urination, urinary frequency, urgency, cloudy urine
treatment_goal: Identify and treat underlying cause; relieve pain promptly
first_line_soc: Uncomplicated UTI (most common cause): Nitrofurantoin (100mg BID × 5 days) or TMP-SMX DS (Bactrim; × 3 days) or fosfomycin (3g × 1 dose)—all FDA-approved first-line antibiotics for uncomplicated cystitis. Phenazopyridine (analgesic) for immediate pain relief.
treatment_algorithm: UTI (uncomplicated cystitis in women): Nitrofurantoin, TMP-SMX, or fosfomycin per IDSA guidelines. Phenazopyridine (Pyridium; urinary analgesic) for immediate pain relief (3 days max). Complicated UTI/pyelonephritis: Fluoroquinolones (ciprofloxacin), cephalosporins, or carbapenems for MDR organisms. STI-related: see cervicitis/urethritis. Non-infectious (IC/BPS, atrophic vaginitis): Specific agents per diagnosis.
us_prevalence: ~8–10 million UTI cases/year causing dysuria in women alone; ~250 million UTIs globally. Dysuria as a symptom: ~25–30 million US adults annually.
patients_needing_tx: ~8–10 million UTI-related cases requiring antibiotics; broader dysuria population ~15–20 million.
price: $500–$3,000 per course (novel antibiotic or analgesic combination).
registrational_endpoint: Clinical cure (symptom resolution) + microbiological cure (negative urine culture) at day 7 vs. SoC antibiotic.
btd_threshold_vs_soc: Clinical + microbiological cure ≥90% at day 7 vs. ~80–85% with nitrofurantoin or TMP-SMX.

symptoms: Inability to void, suprapubic pain/distension, large post-void residual
treatment_goal: Relieve acute retention, improve bladder emptying, identify and treat cause
first_line_soc: Acute urinary retention: Urethral catheterization for immediate relief. Alpha-1 blockers (tamsulosin 0.4mg; BPH-related) to facilitate TWOC (trial without catheter). Bethanechol (cholinergic agonist) for underactive bladder.
treatment_algorithm: Acute AUR: Immediate urethral catheterization. Alpha-1 blocker (tamsulosin 0.4mg) started immediately—improves successful TWOC from ~40% to ~60%. BPH-related: Long-term alpha-1 blocker ± 5-ARI. Drug-induced (anticholinergics, opioids): Discontinue offending agent. Underactive detrusor: Bethanechol 25–50mg QID (cholinergic agonist; limited evidence). CIC for chronic retention. Surgical: TURP for BPH. Neurologic cause: see neurogenic bladder.
us_prevalence: ~50,000 hospitalizations/year for acute urinary retention in men; BPH-related AUR affects ~3 per 1,000 men over 45/year (NHS/US data). Chronic retention: ~500,000–1 million.
patients_needing_tx: ~500,000 acute episodes and ~500,000 chronic cases requiring pharmacologic management annually.
registrational_endpoint: Successful TWOC rate at day 3 post-catheter removal (for AUR); mean post-void residual (PVR) at 12 weeks (chronic retention).
btd_threshold_vs_soc: Successful TWOC ≥75% vs. ~55–60% with tamsulosin alone; or PVR <150mL in ≥70% at 12 weeks vs. ~50% with alpha-blocker alone.

symptoms: Blood in ejaculate; typically painless, often benign and self-limiting
treatment_goal: Rule out serious pathology; treat underlying cause if identified
first_line_soc: In most cases (<40 years, no risk factors), no pharmacologic treatment required — expectant management. If infection identified: appropriate antibiotics (doxycycline or ciprofloxacin for prostatitis/urethritis).
treatment_algorithm: Evaluate for underlying causes: (1) Infection (most common): prostatitis, epididymitis, STI — treat with targeted antibiotics; (2) Benign causes (seminal vesicle cysts, calculi): observation, resolves spontaneously in weeks–months; (3) Vascular malformation: transurethral resection or embolization if persistent; (4) Neoplasm (rare, >40 years): biopsy/staging workup; (5) Post-biopsy (prostate): reassurance. Empiric doxycycline 100 mg BID × 2–4 weeks if prostatitis suspected. No specific novel pharmacotherapy; treatment is entirely etiology-directed.
us_prevalence: Affects ~5–10% of men at some point; annual incidence ~100,000–250,000 clinical presentations; most self-resolve within 1–2 months. Majority are benign.
patients_needing_tx: ~100,000–200,000 cases/year requiring evaluation and potentially antibiotic therapy; persistent cases (~20,000–50,000) may need further urological workup.
price: N/A for dedicated novel drug — treated as part of prostatitis/infection treatment ($500–$2,000 antibiotic course). No commercial opportunity for hematospermia-specific drug.
peak_sales: ~$5–15 million. No dedicated drug opportunity; any novel agent would be labeled for prostatitis or prostate infection.
registrational_endpoint: Resolution of hematospermia at 4–8 weeks; negative microbiological culture at end of antibiotic treatment.
btd_threshold_vs_soc: N/A. No unmet need requiring breakthrough qualification; condition largely self-limiting.

symptoms: Irregular, heavy, or prolonged menstrual bleeding, intermenstrual bleeding
treatment_goal: Control bleeding, correct anemia, treat underlying cause
first_line_soc: Levonorgestrel IUS (Mirena; intrauterine progestin system) or combined oral contraceptives (COCs; estrogen-progestin) for hormonal control. Tranexamic acid (Lysteda; antifibrinolytic) for acute heavy menstrual bleeding.
treatment_algorithm: Acute HMB: Tranexamic acid 1.3g TID × 5 days (FDA-approved for HMB); high-dose estrogen IV (CEE 25mg) for acute hemorrhage. Structural causes (PALM): Hysteroscopic polypectomy, myomectomy. Chronic HMB—hormonal: LNG-IUS (Mirena/Liletta), COCs, progestin-only (norethisterone 5mg TID × 21 days/month); depot medroxyprogesterone acetate (DMPA). Non-hormonal: Tranexamic acid, NSAIDs (mefenamic acid). 2022+: Relugolix-estradiol-norethindrone (Myfembree; GnRH antagonist + add-back) or elagolix (Orilissa) for fibroid/endometriosis-associated HMB. Endometrial ablation for surgical candidates.
us_prevalence: ~1 in 5 women of reproductive age (20%); ~10–14 million US women with AUB requiring medical attention.
patients_needing_tx: ~8–10 million require pharmacologic treatment.
price: $8,000–$20,000/year (novel oral GnRH antagonist or biologic).
registrational_endpoint: Menstrual blood loss reduction ≥50% from baseline (alkaline hematin method) at 6 months; proportion achieving <80 mL per cycle.
btd_threshold_vs_soc: ≥70% of patients achieve <80 mL/cycle at 6 months vs. ~50% with LNG-IUS or tranexamic acid.

symptoms: Irregular periods, acne, excess hair growth, weight gain, ovarian cysts, infertility
treatment_goal: Regulate menstrual cycles, reduce hyperandrogenism, restore fertility if desired
first_line_soc: Combined oral contraceptives (COCs; estrogen-progestin) for menstrual regulation and androgen suppression in non-fertility-seeking women. Metformin (biguanide; insulin sensitizer) for insulin resistance component and metabolic improvement.
treatment_algorithm: Menstrual irregularity + hyperandrogenism: COCs (e.g., Yaz with drospirenone for anti-androgen activity); spironolactone 50–200mg/day (aldosterone antagonist; anti-androgen) for hirsutism/acne. Insulin resistance/metabolic: Metformin 500–2000mg/day; inositol (d-chiro-inositol + myo-inositol). Infertility/ovulation induction: Letrozole (Femara; aromatase inhibitor; now preferred over clomiphene per PCOSMIC trial) or clomiphene citrate (SERM); gonadotropins (FSH/LH); IVF. Weight management: GLP-1 agonists (liraglutide/semaglutide—ozempic off-label widely used 2023–2025; FDA-approved for obesity). 2024: Myo-inositol + metformin combination data expanding.
us_prevalence: ~5–10 million US women of reproductive age with PCOS (~6–10% prevalence; Endocrine Society). One of the most common endocrine disorders in reproductive-age women.
patients_needing_tx: ~6–8 million US women with PCOS require pharmacologic management.
price: $5,000–$20,000/year (novel GLP-1 agonist or androgen-targeted therapy).
registrational_endpoint: Proportion with regular menstrual cycles (cycle length 21–35 days) at 6 months; free androgen index normalization; OGTT/insulin improvement.
btd_threshold_vs_soc: ≥70% achieving regular cycles at 6 months vs. ~50% with COC + metformin; or superior improvement in metabolic parameters (fasting insulin, AMH) vs. current SoC.

symptoms: Painful menstrual cramps, lower abdominal pain, back pain, nausea during menses
treatment_goal: Relieve menstrual pain, reduce prostaglandin-driven cramping
first_line_soc: NSAIDs: ibuprofen 400–600mg TID or naproxen sodium 550mg BID starting 1–2 days before menses (prostaglandin synthesis inhibitors; most effective first-line). Combined oral contraceptives (COCs) for ongoing hormonal suppression.
treatment_algorithm: Step 1: NSAIDs—ibuprofen, naproxen, mefenamic acid, celecoxib (COX-2 inhibitor); start 1–2 days before menstruation. Step 2: COCs (suppress ovulation + reduce prostaglandins) or LNG-IUS (Mirena). Step 3: Progestins—DMPA injection, etonogestrel implant. For secondary dysmenorrhea (endometriosis): See endometriosis entry. Heat therapy adjunct. Step 4 (refractory/severe): GnRH agonists (leuprolide) or antagonists (elagolix) for endometriosis-associated.
us_prevalence: ~50–90% of women of reproductive age experience dysmenorrhea; ~50 million US women affected. Severe requiring medical intervention: ~20–30 million.
patients_needing_tx: ~20 million with moderate-severe dysmenorrhea requiring pharmacologic treatment.
price: $1,000–$5,000/year (novel prostaglandin inhibitor or selective COX-2 + other target).
registrational_endpoint: VAS/NRS pain score reduction at 24 hours of treatment; proportion of cycles achieving ≥50% pain reduction (responder rate) over 3 cycles.
btd_threshold_vs_soc: ≥70% responder rate at 24h vs. ~55–65% with ibuprofen 400–600mg.

symptoms: Mood swings, irritability, bloating, breast tenderness, fatigue before menses
treatment_goal: Relieve premenstrual symptoms, improve daily functioning
first_line_soc: Selective serotonin reuptake inhibitors (SSRIs): fluoxetine (Sarafem; FDA-approved for PMDD) 20mg daily or luteal-phase dosing. Continuous or intermittent dosing. COCs for hormonal suppression.
treatment_algorithm: Mild-moderate PMS: Calcium 1200mg/day + exercise. SSRIs: Fluoxetine (Sarafem 20mg/day), sertraline (50–150mg), paroxetine CR, citalopram; continuous or luteal-phase dosing—FDA-approved for PMDD. SNRIs: Venlafaxine. Physical symptoms: Diuretics (spironolactone 25–100mg) for bloating; NSAIDs for pain; COCs with drospirenone (Yaz—FDA-approved for PMDD). Severe PMDD refractory: GnRH agonist (leuprolide) + add-back HRT. 2024: Zuranolone (Zurzuvae; neuroactive steroid/GABA-A modulator) studied for PMDD.
us_prevalence: ~75% of women experience some PMS; severe PMS/PMDD: ~3–8% of reproductive-age women (~5–8 million US women).
patients_needing_tx: ~5 million with moderate-severe PMDD/PMS requiring pharmacologic therapy.
price: $3,000–$10,000/year (novel neuroactive steroid or targeted hormonal agent).
registrational_endpoint: Daily Record of Severity of Problems (DRSP) total score reduction ≥50% from baseline vs. placebo over 3 cycles; proportion achieving remission.
btd_threshold_vs_soc: Remission rate ≥60% at cycle 3 vs. ~40–50% with fluoxetine/sertraline.

symptoms: Absent menstrual periods for 3+ months; may have hot flashes, galactorrhea
treatment_goal: Restore normal menstruation, treat underlying cause, preserve fertility/bone health
first_line_soc: Depends on cause: Hypothalamic amenorrhea—nutrition/weight restoration + pulsatile GnRH or clomiphene (ovulation induction). Prolactinoma—cabergoline (dopamine agonist; first-line for hyperprolactinemia). Primary ovarian insufficiency—hormone replacement therapy (HRT: estrogen + progesterone).
treatment_algorithm: Hypothalamic: Nutritional rehabilitation; clomiphene citrate 50–150mg (SERM; ovulation induction) for fertility; kisspeptin analogs under investigation. Hyperprolactinemia: Cabergoline (Dostinex; D2 agonist) 0.5–1mg 2×/week or bromocriptine (Parlodel) 2.5–7.5mg/day—cabergoline preferred. Thyroid: Levothyroxine. Hyperandrogenism (PCOS): COCs + metformin. POI: HRT (estradiol + progestogen). Uterine synechiae (Asherman's): Hysteroscopic adhesiolysis + high-dose estrogen.
us_prevalence: ~2–5% of women of reproductive age; ~2–4 million US women have secondary amenorrhea at any point.
patients_needing_tx: ~2 million requiring pharmacologic management.
price: $5,000–$20,000/year (novel GnRH agonist pulsatile delivery or kisspeptin analog).
registrational_endpoint: Return of menstruation (ovulatory cycles) by 6 months; LH/FSH normalization; bone density maintenance (for POI).
btd_threshold_vs_soc: Ovulatory cycles restored in ≥70% at 6 months vs. ~50% with clomiphene; or live birth rate ≥25% at 12 months vs. ~15% with current ovulation induction.

symptoms: Chronic pelvic pain, pelvic heaviness, dyspareunia, dilated pelvic veins
treatment_goal: Reduce venous congestion, relieve pelvic pain
first_line_soc: Medroxyprogesterone acetate (MPA; progestin; suppresses ovarian hormones and reduces venous engorgement) 30–50mg/day or depot MPA (Depo-Provera). Endovascular embolization for pelvic vein reflux.
treatment_algorithm: Hormonal: MPA 30mg/day × 6 months; GnRH agonists (leuprolide) for severe cases (temporary). Analgesia: NSAIDs + acetaminophen. Interventional: Endovascular ovarian vein embolization—most definitive non-surgical option. Surgical: Ovarian vein ligation/hysterectomy + BSO for refractory cases. 2024: Gonadotropin pathway modulation increasingly used.
us_prevalence: ~13–40% of women with chronic pelvic pain have pelvic venous insufficiency; ~5–15 million US women potentially affected.
patients_needing_tx: ~2–4 million with clinically significant pelvic venous disorder requiring pharmacologic management.
registrational_endpoint: Chronic pelvic pain VAS score reduction ≥50% at 6 months; pelvic pain frequency and dyspareunia improvement.
btd_threshold_vs_soc: ≥60% pain responder rate at 6 months vs. ~30–40% with MPA 30mg alone.

symptoms: Amenorrhea <40 years, hot flashes, infertility, low estrogen, elevated FSH
treatment_goal: Replace hormones, preserve bone density, manage symptoms, support fertility goals
first_line_soc: Hormone replacement therapy (HRT): Estradiol 100mcg/day transdermal or equivalent oral estrogen + cyclic progestogen (medroxyprogesterone acetate 5–10mg/day × 12 days/month).
treatment_algorithm: HRT: Transdermal estradiol (higher doses than menopause HRT, e.g., 100mcg patch) + progestogen (to protect uterus). Contraception counseling (spontaneous ovulation possible). Bone health: Calcium 1200mg/day + vitamin D 800–2000 IU/day; bisphosphonates if osteoporosis confirmed. Fertility: IVF with donor eggs; spontaneous pregnancy in ~5%. Psychological support. Androgens (DHEA, testosterone) for libido. 2024: Kisspeptin analogs and pulsatile GnRH under investigation to restore ovarian function.
us_prevalence: ~1% of women <40 years; ~500,000–600,000 US women with POI (National Institutes of Health data).
patients_needing_tx: ~500,000 US women require lifelong HRT and monitoring.
price: $5,000–$20,000/year (novel ovarian-function-restoring agent or improved HRT formulation).
registrational_endpoint: Estradiol serum normalization (>50 pg/mL) and FSH reduction (<25 mIU/mL) at 6 months; BMID maintenance; vasomotor symptom score improvement.
btd_threshold_vs_soc: Restoration of ovulatory cycles in ≥20% vs. ~5% spontaneous recovery; or superior BMD maintenance vs. standard HRT at 2 years.

symptoms: Palpable breast lump, firmness, skin changes, nipple discharge, axillary nodes
treatment_goal: Diagnose benign vs. malignant; treat accordingly; reassure if benign
first_line_soc: Malignant breast mass: Surgery (lumpectomy/mastectomy) + systemic therapy based on tumor receptor status. Benign (fibroadenoma): Observation or excision. No pharmacologic SoC for benign masses themselves.
treatment_algorithm: Evaluation: Triple assessment (clinical + imaging + biopsy). Malignant: ER+/HER2- early breast cancer: Endocrine therapy (tamoxifen, aromatase inhibitors [anastrozole, letrozole, exemestane]) ± CDK4/6 inhibitors (palbociclib, ribociclib, abemaciclib). HER2+: Trastuzumab + pertuzumab ± chemotherapy. TNBC: Chemotherapy ± immunotherapy (pembrolizumab, olaparib for BRCA). Neoadjuvant chemotherapy for locally advanced. See cancer management guidelines. Benign cysts: Aspiration. Fibroadenoma: Observation; Adiana (not pharmacologic).
us_prevalence: ~300,000 new breast cancer diagnoses/year (ACS 2024); ~1–2 million benign breast biopsies/year.
patients_needing_tx: ~300,000 malignant cases needing systemic treatment; ~500,000 high-risk benign cases needing chemoprevention (tamoxifen, raloxifene).
price: $80,000–$200,000/year (novel antibody-drug conjugate or targeted breast cancer therapy).
registrational_endpoint: pCR (pathologic complete response) at surgery after neoadjuvant therapy; event-free survival (EFS) at 5 years; overall survival.
btd_threshold_vs_soc: pCR ≥60% in TNBC vs. ~50% with current chemo + immunotherapy; or ≥5% absolute EFS improvement vs. SoC at 5 years.

symptoms: Cyclic or non-cyclic breast pain, tenderness, fullness, sensitivity
treatment_goal: Relieve breast pain, identify any underlying pathology
first_line_soc: Evening primrose oil (gamma-linolenic acid; well-tolerated OTC; limited evidence) or non-pharmacologic (well-fitted bra, lifestyle). Danazol (androgen; attenuates hormonal fluctuations)—most effective pharmacologic agent, but adverse effects limit use.
treatment_algorithm: Mild-moderate cyclic: Evening primrose oil 2–3g/day; correct ill-fitting bra; reduce caffeine. Moderate-severe: Danazol 100–200mg/day (androgenic steroid; reduces estrogen/progesterone fluctuations; FDA-approved for mastalgia in some indications) × 6 months; or bromocriptine 2.5mg BID (dopamine agonist; reduces prolactin). Tamoxifen 10–20mg/day (SERM) for 3–6 months—effective but off-label. Topical diclofenac gel for localized non-cyclic pain. Cabergoline for hyperprolactinemia-associated mastalgia.
us_prevalence: ~70% of women experience mastalgia at some point; ~20–30 million US women at any given time. Clinically significant requiring treatment: ~5–8 million.
patients_needing_tx: ~3–5 million seeking pharmacologic management for significant mastalgia.
registrational_endpoint: Cardiff Breast Pain Score reduction ≥50% from baseline at 12 weeks; proportion pain-free at 3 months.
btd_threshold_vs_soc: ≥60% responder rate (≥50% pain reduction) at 12 weeks vs. ~35–40% with danazol or tamoxifen.

symptoms: Unilateral or bilateral nipple discharge, may be clear, milky, bloody, or colored
treatment_goal: Identify cause (galactorrhea vs. ductal pathology), treat accordingly
first_line_soc: Galactorrhea (hyperprolactinemia): Cabergoline 0.5–2mg 2×/week (dopamine agonist; reduces prolactin). Ductal pathology (pathologic discharge): Surgical duct excision (microdochectomy) is definitive.
treatment_algorithm: Galactorrhea: Stop offending medication (antipsychotics, metoclopramide, estrogens). If hyperprolactinemia: Cabergoline (Dostinex; preferred) or bromocriptine (Parlodel). Pituitary adenoma: Cabergoline ± neurosurgery. Physiologic/bilateral milky: Reassurance, wean from stimulation. Pathologic (bloody/unilateral): Biopsy + microdochectomy; if malignant—oncology management.
us_prevalence: ~3–10% of women experience nipple discharge during their lifetime; galactorrhea: ~2–5% of women (~2–3 million US women).
patients_needing_tx: ~2–3 million with galactorrhea requiring pharmacologic management; ~100,000–200,000 with pathologic discharge requiring surgery.
price: $3,000–$10,000/year (novel dopamine agonist or prolactin receptor antagonist).
registrational_endpoint: Serum prolactin normalization and cessation of nipple discharge at 3 months; proportion achieving galactorrhea remission.
btd_threshold_vs_soc: Galactorrhea remission in ≥90% at 3 months vs. ~85% with cabergoline; or superior tolerability with equivalent efficacy.

symptoms: Painful periods, pelvic pain, dyspareunia, infertility, painful bowel movements
treatment_goal: Eliminate pain, preserve fertility, suppress lesion growth, improve quality of life
first_line_soc: Combined oral contraceptives (COCs; suppress ovulation and estrogen) or progestins (dienogest, norethindrone; suppress endometrial growth). NSAIDs for pain.
treatment_algorithm: First-line: NSAIDs (ibuprofen, naproxen) + COCs for pain. Hormonal suppression: (1) Progestins—dienogest (Visanne; most studied; approved in EU/Asia, off-label in US) 2mg/day; norethindrone acetate 5mg/day; DMPA; LNG-IUS (Mirena). (2) GnRH agonists—leuprolide (Lupron; induces medical menopause) 3.75mg IM monthly + add-back HRT (norethindrone/estradiol) to limit bone loss. (3) GnRH antagonists—elagolix (Orilissa; 150mg/day or 200mg BID; FDA-approved 2018) + elagolix-estradiol-norethindrone (Oriahnn; FDA-approved 2020 for uterine fibroids); relugolix + add-back (Myfembree; FDA-approved 2022 for endometriosis-associated pain). (4) Aromatase inhibitors (letrozole, anastrozole; off-label) for refractory. Surgical: Laparoscopic excision/ablation for diagnosis + treatment; optimal for fertility. 2024: Linzagolix (GnRH antagonist) in trials; OPK-88003 anti-IL-33 studied.
us_prevalence: ~5–10 million US women with endometriosis; ~1 in 10 reproductive-age women. Median diagnostic delay: 7–10 years. Rising awareness driving earlier diagnosis.
patients_needing_tx: ~5–7 million require pharmacologic management; ~1–2 million on GnRH agonists/antagonists; ~3 million on COCs/progestins.
price: $15,000–$40,000/year (GnRH antagonist with add-back; relugolix ~$15,000/year; elagolix ~$15,000/year).
registrational_endpoint: Dysmenorrhea NRS score and NMPP (non-menstrual pelvic pain) NRS score reduction from baseline at 24 weeks vs. placebo (FDA composite endpoint).
btd_threshold_vs_soc: ≥50% reduction in dysmenorrhea NRS + ≥50% NMPP reduction in ≥40% of patients at 24 weeks vs. ~25% placebo response; or superiority over elagolix 200mg BID with better safety profile.

symptoms: Painful vulvar nodule or cyst, cyclic pain, swelling with menses
treatment_goal: Excise or treat vulvar endometriotic lesion, prevent recurrence
first_line_soc: Surgical excision is the definitive treatment. Medical suppression (GnRH agonists, progestins) to reduce lesion size and symptoms pre- or post-operatively.
treatment_algorithm: Surgical: Complete excision of endometrioma; incomplete excision leads to recurrence. Medical (adjuvant): GnRH agonist (leuprolide) pre-operatively × 3 months to reduce lesion size; hormonal suppression post-op (COCs, dienogest, GnRH antagonists—elagolix, relugolix) to prevent recurrence. NSAIDs for cyclic pain. Intralesional steroid injection not well-studied.
us_prevalence: Rare subtype of endometriosis; ~5,000–15,000 US women with vulvar endometriomas. Mostly women with known pelvic endometriosis.
patients_needing_tx: ~10,000 US patients requiring treatment.
price: $10,000–$30,000/year (GnRH antagonist or local anti-endometriotic agent).
registrational_endpoint: Lesion size reduction on ultrasound at 3 months; recurrence-free rate at 1 year post-excision.
btd_threshold_vs_soc: Recurrence-free rate ≥90% at 1 year vs. ~60–70% with surgery alone + no hormonal suppression.

symptoms: Not a disease; method for preventing pregnancy with potential side effects (nausea, mood changes, VTE)
treatment_goal: Prevent pregnancy; may also treat acne, dysmenorrhea, PCOS, endometriosis
first_line_soc: Combined oral contraceptives (COCs; estrogen + progestin): ethinyl estradiol 20–35mcg + various progestins (levonorgestrel, norgestimate, desogestrel, drospirenone). Progestin-only pill (POP; norethindrone 0.35mg—'minipill').
treatment_algorithm: COCs: Monophasic, biphasic, triphasic formulations. Low-dose EE (20mcg) + progestins for VTE risk reduction. Drospirenone-containing (Yaz, Yasmin, Loryna)—anti-androgenic; FDA-approved for PMDD and acne. Extended-cycle pills (Seasonique, Lybrel) for amenorrhea. Progestin-only: Norethindrone 0.35mg (minipill); slynd (drospirenone 4mg; newer POP with 24h missed-pill window—FDA-approved 2019). 2023–2024: First OTC progestin-only pill (Opill; norgestrel 0.075mg; FDA-approved 2023—first OTC daily oral contraceptive in US).
us_prevalence: ~12–16 million US women use oral contraceptives (~16–20% of all contraceptive users). Declining slightly with IUD/implant growth.
patients_needing_tx: ~14 million users; OTC Opill expanding access to ~3–5 million new users.
price: $200–$1,500/year (branded COC; generics ~$50–200/year).
registrational_endpoint: Pearl Index ≤1.0/100 woman-years; unintended pregnancy rate at 13 cycles.
btd_threshold_vs_soc: Pearl Index <0.5 + superior tolerability (nausea, mood, libido) vs. standard COC.

symptoms: Not a disease; refers to contraceptive methods requiring pericoital use (condoms, diaphragm, spermicide)
treatment_goal: Prevent unintended pregnancy with coitally-dependent method
first_line_soc: Male condoms (most widely used barrier; also STI-protective). Female condoms, diaphragm with spermicide (nonoxynol-9), cervical cap. Phexxi (lactic acid-citric acid-potassium bitartrate gel; FDA-approved 2020) for on-demand vaginal pH modification.
treatment_algorithm: Male condom: 87–98% effective. Female condom: 79–95%. Diaphragm + spermicide: 88–94%. Cervical cap + spermicide. Nonoxynol-9 spermicide (cream, gel, foam). Phexxi (Evofem; vaginal gel maintaining pH <3.5; FDA-approved 2020)—novel non-hormonal option. Contraceptive sponge (Today Sponge). Emergency option if barrier fails: see Emergency Contraception entry.
us_prevalence: ~70 million US women of reproductive age; ~9% use male condoms; ~1% diaphragm/cervical cap. Market growing for non-hormonal options.
patients_needing_tx: ~6–7 million US women using barrier contraceptives regularly.
registrational_endpoint: Cumulative pregnancy rate at 7 cycles (Pearl Index) vs. placebo or active comparator.
btd_threshold_vs_soc: Pearl Index ≤3 per 100 woman-years vs. ~7–10 for spermicide alone or 12–18 unprotected; or non-inferiority to diaphragm + spermicide.

symptoms: Not a disease; long-acting reversible contraceptive with potential side effects (spotting, cramping)
treatment_goal: Provide highly effective long-term reversible contraception
first_line_soc: Levonorgestrel-releasing IUS (Mirena 52mg/5yr; Liletta; Kyleena; Skyla)—most widely used hormonal IUD. Copper IUD (Paragard)—non-hormonal, most effective EC.
treatment_algorithm: Hormonal IUDs: Mirena 52mg LNG (5–8 years); Liletta (8 years); Kyleena 19.5mg LNG (5 years); Skyla 13.5mg LNG (3 years)—progressively smaller for nulliparous. Copper IUD (Paragard): 10–12 years; non-hormonal; most effective EC within 5 days. NSAIDs (ibuprofen) for insertion pain and first-month cramping. Local anesthetics (lidocaine) for paracervical block pre-insertion. Misoprostol 400mcg for cervical softening pre-insertion (esp. nulliparous).
us_prevalence: ~6–8 million US women use IUDs (~8–10% of all contraceptive users). Market growing post-Dobbs ruling.
patients_needing_tx: ~6–8 million users; ~1.5 million new insertions/year. Market for insertion aids and side-effect management.
price: $800–$1,200 per device (5-year); $150–$300/year equivalent.
registrational_endpoint: Cumulative pregnancy rate at 1 year (Pearl Index ≤1.0/100 woman-years) vs. comparator IUD.
btd_threshold_vs_soc: Pearl Index ≤0.5 at 1 year + improved tolerability (reduced spotting/pain) vs. Mirena or Liletta.

disease: Transdermal and Vaginal Ring Hormonal Contraceptives
symptoms: Not a disease; hormonal delivery via patch or ring; side effects include breakthrough bleeding, skin irritation, ring discomfort
treatment_goal: Prevent pregnancy via non-oral hormonal delivery route
first_line_soc: Transdermal patch: Xulane (norelgestromin 150mcg/day + EE 35mcg/day; weekly × 3/4 weeks). Vaginal ring: NuvaRing (etonogestrel 120mcg/day + EE 15mcg/day; monthly) or Annovera (segesterone + EE; annual; FDA-approved 2018).
treatment_algorithm: Patch (Xulane, Twirla): Weekly patch × 3 weeks on/off. Vaginal ring: NuvaRing (monthly ring; 3 weeks in/1 week out); Annovera (annual vaginal ring; 90 days effective). All ~91–99% effective. Manage breakthrough bleeding with NSAIDs. Same VTE risk considerations as COCs (EE component). Annovera advantage: one ring per year, less compliance burden.
us_prevalence: ~2 million US women use patch or ring (~3% of contraceptive users). Ring growing; patch declining.
patients_needing_tx: ~2 million users.
registrational_endpoint: Pearl Index ≤1/100 woman-years over 13 cycles; pregnancy rate at cycle 13.
btd_threshold_vs_soc: Pearl Index <0.5 + superior cycle control (fewer spotting days) vs. NuvaRing.

symptoms: Not a disease; used after unprotected intercourse or contraceptive failure
treatment_goal: Prevent unintended pregnancy within 72–120 hours of unprotected intercourse
first_line_soc: Levonorgestrel 1.5mg (Plan B One-Step; progestin; most effective within 72h). Ulipristal acetate 30mg (ella; selective progesterone receptor modulator; effective up to 120h, superior efficacy especially in obese women; FDA-approved 2010).
treatment_algorithm: Within 72h: Levonorgestrel (Plan B One-Step; Aftera; Take Action; OTC)—75–89% effective. Up to 120h: Ulipristal acetate (ella; Rx in US)—superior efficacy across all 5 days (~85%), especially in BMI>26kg/m². Most effective: Copper IUD (>99% effective, up to 5 days). Yuzpe regimen (combined OC high-dose)—least effective, obsolete. 2024: Generic ulipristal increasing access.
us_prevalence: ~11 million EC uses/year in the US (Guttmacher Institute 2024).
patients_needing_tx: ~11 million annual uses requiring pharmacologic EC.
price: $50–$150 per course (OTC/Rx single dose).
registrational_endpoint: Pregnancy rate at 1 month (expected vs. observed via Pearl Index); superiority vs. levonorgestrel for BMI >26.
btd_threshold_vs_soc: Pregnancy prevention rate ≥97% at 120h vs. ~85% for ulipristal; or equivalent ulipristal efficacy across all BMI categories.

symptoms: Not a disease; subdermal etonogestrel implant with side effects (irregular bleeding, arm discomfort)
treatment_goal: Provide most effective long-acting reversible contraception (3+ years)
first_line_soc: Etonogestrel implant (Nexplanon; 68mg; subdermal single rod; progestin implant)—99.9% effective × 3 years (possibly up to 5 years, per recent studies). Most effective form of reversible contraception.
treatment_algorithm: Nexplanon: 68mg ENG rod in upper arm; effective ~3 years (new data support 5 years). Mechanism: Suppresses ovulation, thickens cervical mucus. Irregular bleeding most common side effect; managed with NSAIDs (mefenamic acid) or short COC course. No other pharmacologic agents needed for the device itself. Insertion/removal: minor office procedure with local anesthetic.
us_prevalence: ~2.5–3 million US women use implants (~3–4% of contraceptive users). Fastest-growing LARC method.
patients_needing_tx: ~2.5 million users; ~700,000 new insertions/year.
price: $800–$1,200 per device (3-year; ~$300/year equivalent).
registrational_endpoint: Pearl Index over 3 years (expected ≤0.1/100 woman-years); discontinuation due to bleeding at 12 months.
btd_threshold_vs_soc: Pearl Index ≤0.1 for 5 years (extended duration) + ≥20% reduction in irregular bleeding episodes vs. Nexplanon 3-year label.

symptoms: Not a disease; injectable progestin contraception with side effects (irregular bleeding, weight gain, bone density loss)
treatment_goal: Provide highly effective medium-term contraception with 3-month dosing interval
first_line_soc: Depot medroxyprogesterone acetate (DMPA; Depo-Provera 150mg IM or Depo-SubQ Provera 104 SC) every 12–13 weeks. Efficacy ~94–99%.
treatment_algorithm: DMPA 150mg IM (Depo-Provera) or 104mg SC (Depo-SubQ) every 12–13 weeks. Amenorrhea in ~50% after 1 year. Bone density loss with prolonged use (returns after discontinuation). Counseling for irregular bleeding (NSAIDs, low-dose COC short-term for breakthrough bleeding). WHO recommends use not beyond 2 years in adolescents due to bone concerns.
us_prevalence: ~3 million US women use injectable contraception (~4% of all users). Prevalent in low-income and adolescent populations.
patients_needing_tx: ~3 million annual users; ~1 million new starts/year.
price: $100–$400/year (generic DMPA widely available).
registrational_endpoint: Pearl Index ≤1/100 woman-years at 2 years; bone mineral density change at 2 years vs. comparator.
btd_threshold_vs_soc: Pearl Index ≤0.5 + no significant BMD loss at 2 years vs. DMPA's ~3–5% BMD reduction.

symptoms: Not a disease; intentional termination of pregnancy up to gestational age limit
treatment_goal: Safe and effective termination of pregnancy
first_line_soc: Medical abortion (≤10–12 weeks): Mifepristone 200mg PO (anti-progestogen; FDA-approved) + misoprostol 800mcg buccal/vaginal 24–48h later (prostaglandin E1; uterotonic). Efficacy >95–98%.
treatment_algorithm: First trimester (≤10 weeks): Mifepristone 200mg + misoprostol 800mcg buccal/vaginal (48h later)—FDA-approved regimen (2000, expanded label 2024 via mail order under REMS). Efficacy ~95–98% complete abortion. Second trimester: Mifepristone + misoprostol; or misoprostol alone. Surgical D&E at ≥14 weeks (most effective). Dilation + evacuation with osmotic dilators (laminaria, Dilapan-S). Intraoperative oxytocin, methergine (ergot) for hemostasis. Doxycycline prophylaxis pre-surgical.
us_prevalence: ~900,000–1 million abortions/year in the US (Guttmacher 2023 data); ~63% are medical abortions. Rising with telehealth expansion.
patients_needing_tx: ~900,000/year requiring pharmacologic or surgical management.
price: $150–$600 per medical abortion course.
registrational_endpoint: Complete abortion rate (no surgical intervention) at day 8 ultrasound; ongoing pregnancy rate (treatment failure) at day 8.
btd_threshold_vs_soc: Complete abortion ≥99% at day 8 vs. ~95–97% with mifepristone + misoprostol standard regimen.

symptoms: Not a disease; method of tracking fertile window to avoid unprotected intercourse
treatment_goal: Prevent pregnancy without pharmacologic agents by identifying and avoiding fertile days
first_line_soc: None pharmacologic. Fertility awareness methods (FAM): calendar/rhythm, cervical mucus (Billings), basal body temperature (BBT), symptothermal method. Digital apps (Clue, Natural Cycles—FDA-cleared 2018).
treatment_algorithm: Calendar/rhythm method: Low efficacy (~76–88%). Symptothermal: More effective ~98% with perfect use. Natural Cycles app (FDA-cleared digital contraceptive; algorithm-based): 93–98% effective with typical use. No pharmacologic SoC. OTC hormone monitoring devices (Daysy, Clearblue): detect LH surge for fertility window. Education counseling essential.
us_prevalence: ~2–3% of women using FAM as primary contraception; ~2–3 million US women.
patients_needing_tx: Not pharmacologically treated; digital health market. Novel pharmacologic: None applicable.
price: N/A (non-pharmacologic method).

symptoms: Not a disease; irreversible sterilization procedure
treatment_goal: Permanent prevention of pregnancy (bilateral tubal ligation or vasectomy)
first_line_soc: None pharmacologic. Surgical: Bilateral tubal ligation (BTL) or salpingectomy (preferred; reduces ovarian cancer risk) for women; vasectomy for men.
treatment_algorithm: Female sterilization: Laparoscopic BTL (Pomeroy, Parkland, Irving); bilateral salpingectomy (preferred 2024 per ACOG—also reduces ovarian/fallopian tube cancer risk). Hysteroscopic (Essure) withdrawn from US market (2018). Male: Vasectomy. Pharmacologic: None for the procedure itself. Analgesics/NSAIDs for post-operative pain. No pharmacologic permanent contraception exists.
us_prevalence: ~700,000 female sterilizations/year; ~500,000 vasectomies/year in the US.
patients_needing_tx: N/A for pharmacologic treatment; ~1.2 million procedures/year with peri-procedural analgesics.

symptoms: Not a single disease; encompasses hormonal imbalances affecting menstrual cycle, fertility, and sexual development
treatment_goal: Correct hormonal imbalances, restore normal reproductive endocrine function
first_line_soc: Depends on specific condition: hypothyroidism—levothyroxine; hyperprolactinemia—cabergoline; hypogonadism—estrogen + progestogen HRT; ovulatory dysfunction—see PCOS or ovulatory dysfunction entries.
treatment_algorithm: Hypothalamic: Pulsatile GnRH (kisspeptin analogs in trials); clomiphene/letrozole for ovulation induction. Pituitary: Cabergoline for prolactinoma; dopamine agonists. Ovarian: HRT for POI; antiandrogens for hyperandrogenism (spironolactone, COCs). Adrenal: Hydrocortisone for CAH; fludrocortisone for mineralocorticoid insufficiency. Thyroid: Levothyroxine. Multi-system approach: Endocrinology-gynecology collaborative care.
us_prevalence: As a field/category, encompasses millions of US women. Specific conditions: hypothyroidism ~10 million; hyperprolactinemia ~1 million; hypogonadotropic hypogonadism ~100,000.
patients_needing_tx: ~10–15 million US women with some form of reproductive endocrine disorder requiring pharmacologic treatment.
price: $5,000–$30,000/year depending on specific agent.
registrational_endpoint: Specific to underlying condition: ovulatory rate, hormonal normalization, symptom scores at 6 months.
btd_threshold_vs_soc: Condition-specific; generally ≥30% improvement over current SoC for dominant presenting disorder.

symptoms: Absent or significantly delayed orgasm despite adequate stimulation, distress
treatment_goal: Improve orgasmic function, enhance sexual satisfaction, reduce distress
first_line_soc: Sex therapy and cognitive behavioral therapy (CBT/directed masturbation) is first-line. No FDA-approved pharmacologic agent specifically for female orgasmic disorder.
treatment_algorithm: Behavioral/psychological: Directed masturbation training, CBT, sensate focus, sex therapy—most effective. Pharmacologic (off-label/investigational): Bupropion 150–300mg (NDRI; activates dopamine/norepinephrine; modest benefit for orgasm in some series); buspirone; off-label testosterone (improves orgasm in hypogonadal women). Phosphodiesterase-5 inhibitors (sildenafil; studied in women—mixed results). Melanocortin agonists (bremelanotide/Vyleesi—FDA-approved for HSDD, not specifically FOD). 2024: Melanocortin/oxytocin pathway modulation in trials.
us_prevalence: ~10–15% of US women; ~15–20 million women report orgasmic dysfunction causing distress.
patients_needing_tx: ~5–8 million with clinically distressing FOD seeking treatment.
price: $5,000–$20,000/year (novel CNS-targeting sexual pharmacologic agent).
registrational_endpoint: Female Sexual Function Index (FSFI)—orgasm domain score improvement ≥1.2 points from baseline at 12 weeks; proportion achieving orgasm in ≥50% of encounters.
btd_threshold_vs_soc: FSFI orgasm domain improvement ≥1.5 points + ≥50% encounter responder rate in ≥40% of patients vs. ~10–15% placebo.

symptoms: Persistent pain during intercourse, fear of penetration, vaginal muscle spasm, avoidance
treatment_goal: Eliminate pelvic pain, enable comfortable penetration, reduce fear and avoidance
first_line_soc: Pelvic floor physical therapy (PFPT) as first-line non-pharmacologic. Topical lidocaine 4% gel (local anesthetic) for vulvar vestibulitis-associated pain. Low-dose vaginal diazepam (muscle relaxant) for vaginismus component.
treatment_algorithm: Step 1: PFPT + CBT/sex therapy (most evidence). Step 2: Topical lidocaine 4% gel or EMLA (lidocaine + prilocaine) applied pre-intercourse. Step 3: Low-dose TCAs (amitriptyline 10–25mg nightly) for vulvodynia neuropathic pain. Step 4: Vestibulectomy for provoked vestibulodynia (hormonal causes). Topical estrogen + testosterone for atrophic component. Vaginal dilators + relaxation training. Botulinum toxin A injection (100U) into levator ani/vestibule—increasing evidence for vaginismus. 2024: Intravaginal lidocaine suppositories (Osphena adjunct) in development.
us_prevalence: ~7–15% of reproductive-age women; ~10–20 million US women affected by GPPPD/vulvodynia/vaginismus.
patients_needing_tx: ~5–8 million with clinically significant GPPD requiring treatment.
price: $5,000–$20,000/year (novel neuropathic pain agent or local biologic).
registrational_endpoint: FSFI pain domain score improvement ≥1.2 at 12 weeks; proportion of successful pain-free intercourse attempts (≥1× in past 4 weeks).
btd_threshold_vs_soc: FSFI pain domain ≥26 (no dysfunction) in ≥30% at 12 weeks vs. ~10% with topical lidocaine + PFPT.

symptoms: Absent or reduced sexual interest/arousal, lack of desire, no erotic thoughts, distress
treatment_goal: Restore sexual interest and arousal, reduce distress, improve sexual satisfaction
first_line_soc: Flibanserin (Addyi; 5-HT1A agonist/5-HT2A antagonist; FDA-approved 2015) 100mg nightly for premenopausal women with HSDD. Bremelanotide (Vyleesi; MC4R agonist; SC injection; FDA-approved 2019) for premenopausal HSDD.
treatment_algorithm: Premenopausal: Flibanserin (Addyi) 100mg nightly—modest effect (0.5 additional satisfying sexual events/month, $800/month). Bremelanotide (Vyleesi) 1.75mg SC injection PRN (45min before sex). Off-label: Testosterone cream (compounded; 300mcg/day transdermal); bupropion 150–300mg. Postmenopausal: Testosterone patch/gel (off-label in US; FDA-rejected but EMA-approved Intrinsa historically). Ospemifene for dyspareunia contributing to HSDD. Sex therapy + CBT + couples therapy. 2024: Kisspeptin analogs, oxytocin intranasal studies.
us_prevalence: ~10% of US women (all ages) have HSDD/SIAD; ~7–10 million US women with clinically distressing low desire.
patients_needing_tx: ~5–7 million with distressing HSDD/SIAD seeking pharmacologic treatment.
registrational_endpoint: FSFI desire domain score improvement ≥1.2 + increase in satisfying sexual events (SSEs) from baseline at 12 weeks; FSDS-R total score reduction.
btd_threshold_vs_soc: ≥1 additional SSE/week in ≥30% of patients vs. ~10–15% with flibanserin; or FSFI desire normalization (≥4.28) in ≥40%.

symptoms: Spontaneous unwanted persistent genital arousal unrelated to sexual desire, unresolved by orgasm
treatment_goal: Reduce spontaneous genital arousal, relieve distress
first_line_soc: No FDA-approved pharmacologic treatment. Topical anesthetics (lidocaine gel) for symptom relief. SSRIs or SNRIs for associated anxiety/depression components. Tramadol (opioid; anecdotal evidence); gabapentin or pregabalin for neuropathic component.
treatment_algorithm: Neurological workup first (exclude sacral nerve cysts/Tarlov cysts, spinal cord lesions). Topical lidocaine gel for temporary relief. Gabapentin/pregabalin (calcium channel alpha-2-delta ligands; neuropathic pain) for chronic management. SSRIs (fluoxetine, sertraline) for anxiety and serotonergic modulation. Tramadol. Cognitive behavioral therapy + mindfulness. Pelvic floor physical therapy. 2024: Case reports of pudendal nerve block, sacral neuromodulation benefit.
us_prevalence: Rare; estimated prevalence ~1–3% of women (~1–3 million US women); many underreport. Rising recognition post-2021 DSM inclusion.
patients_needing_tx: ~500,000–1 million US women with clinically significant PGAD seeking treatment.
registrational_endpoint: PGAD-QoL questionnaire score improvement; daily symptom diary—proportion of days with ≤1 arousal episode at 12 weeks.
btd_threshold_vs_soc: ≥50% reduction in PGAD episodes/week + QoL normalization in ≥40% vs. ~10–15% placebo.

symptoms: Decreased libido, painful intercourse, vaginal dryness, and reduced arousal postpartum
treatment_goal: Restore sexual function, treat hypoestrogenia, improve libido and comfort
first_line_soc: Topical vaginal estrogen (Vagifem, Premarin cream; low-dose estradiol) for vaginal dryness/atrophy. Vaginal moisturizers (Replens) and lubricants for dyspareunia. Pelvic floor PT for postpartum muscular changes.
treatment_algorithm: Vaginal atrophy/dryness: Topical estradiol (Vagifem 10mcg vaginal tablets, Imvexxy rings, Premarin cream) or prasterone (Intrarosa; dehydroepiandrosterone/DHEA intravaginal; FDA-approved 2016)—not transferred to breastfed infant. Ospemifene (Osphena; SERM; oral; FDA-approved) for dyspareunia when topical not preferred. Libido: Low-dose testosterone (off-label); bremelanotide (Vyleesi; MC4R agonist; FDA-approved for HSDD, studied postpartum). Address postpartum depression (brexanolone/Zulresso or SSRIs).
us_prevalence: ~80% of women report sexual dysfunction 3 months postpartum; ~40% at 12 months. ~1.5–2 million women/year affected (based on ~4M births/year).
patients_needing_tx: ~1–2 million postpartum women/year seeking pharmacologic management.
registrational_endpoint: FSFI total score ≥26.55 (no dysfunction) at 12 weeks postpartum; proportion returning to baseline sexual function by 6 months.
btd_threshold_vs_soc: FSFI total score normalization in ≥50% at 12 weeks vs. ~25–30% with topical estrogen alone.

symptoms: Abnormal semen analysis: low sperm count, motility, morphology; male infertility
treatment_goal: Improve sperm parameters, achieve fertilization via natural or assisted means
first_line_soc: Treat reversible causes: Clomiphene (SERM; off-label) or letrozole to increase gonadotropin stimulation of spermatogenesis. Gonadotropin therapy (FSH + hCG) for hypogonadotropic hypogonadism.
treatment_algorithm: Hypogonadotropic hypogonadism: hCG 1,500–2,000IU IM 3×/week + rFSH 150IU 3×/week × 6–12 months—induces spermatogenesis. Idiopathic oligospermia: Clomiphene 25mg every other day × 3–6 months (off-label); anastrozole 1mg/day (aromatase inhibitor; off-label—reduces estrogen, raises testosterone/FSH). Antioxidants: Vitamin E, C, selenium, CoQ10 (supplementation; limited evidence). Varicocele: Surgical repair (varicocelectomy). Obstruction: Surgical reconstruction or sperm retrieval + ICSI. Severe azoospermia: Micro-TESE + ICSI.
us_prevalence: ~7–8% of men have sperm disorders; ~3–5 million US men with male factor infertility. ~40–50% of infertility involves male factor.
patients_needing_tx: ~1–2 million US men seeking treatment for sperm disorders.
price: $3,000–$10,000 per treatment cycle.
registrational_endpoint: Sperm concentration improvement (×10⁶/mL) + total motility (%) at 6 months; clinical pregnancy rate per IUI cycle.
btd_threshold_vs_soc: ≥2-fold improvement in sperm concentration and ≥50% improvement in total motility at 6 months vs. ~20–30% improvement with clomiphene; or clinical pregnancy rate ≥25%.

symptoms: 2+ consecutive pregnancy losses; anxiety, grief, desire for fertility preservation
treatment_goal: Identify and treat underlying cause, prevent future pregnancy loss
first_line_soc: Progesterone supplementation (vaginal progesterone 200–400mg/day; PRISM trial 2019—modest benefit in select patients). Low-dose aspirin (81mg/day) + LMWH (enoxaparin; 40mg SC/day) for antiphospholipid antibody syndrome (APS)—highly effective for APS-related RPL.
treatment_algorithm: APS: LDA 81mg + LMWH (enoxaparin 40mg SC/day) throughout pregnancy—reduces RPL by ~50% in APS. Thyroid abnormalities: Levothyroxine (maintain TSH <2.5 mIU/L). Progesterone: Vaginal progesterone 400mg BID for threatened/early pregnancy (Endometrin, Prometrium). IVIG for refractory immune-mediated RPL (limited evidence). Paternal lymphocyte immunization (experimental). Hysteroscopic septum resection for uterine septum. Genetic counseling + PGT-A for chromosomal RPL. Heparin monotherapy for non-APS thrombophilia: controversial (ALIFE2 2022—no benefit).
us_prevalence: ~1–2% of couples experience RPL (≥2 consecutive losses); ~500,000–1 million US women affected.
patients_needing_tx: ~400,000–500,000 US women with RPL requiring pharmacologic investigation and management.
price: $5,000–$20,000/year (novel immune-modulatory or progesterone receptor agent).
registrational_endpoint: Live birth rate in subsequent pregnancy at ≥24 weeks; pregnancy loss rate within 12 weeks; cumulative live birth rate at 2 years.
btd_threshold_vs_soc: Live birth rate ≥70% vs. ~50–60% with progesterone + aspirin/LMWH; or ≥30% relative reduction in idiopathic RPL vs. placebo.

symptoms: Irregular or absent ovulation, irregular periods, infertility, elevated androgens
treatment_goal: Induce ovulation, achieve pregnancy, correct hormonal imbalance
first_line_soc: Letrozole (Femara; aromatase inhibitor; 2.5–7.5mg/day cycle days 3–7) is first-line for ovulation induction in PCOS (PCOSMIC trial; superior to clomiphene). Clomiphene citrate (Clomid; SERM) as alternative.
treatment_algorithm: Step 1: Letrozole 2.5–7.5mg/day × 5 days (Cycle Day 3–7)—now preferred over clomiphene per ACOG/ESHRE 2023. Step 2: Clomiphene citrate 50–150mg/day × 5 days. Step 3: Gonadotropins (FSH ± LH) for clomiphene/letrozole-resistant anovulation. Step 4: Laparoscopic ovarian drilling (LOD; surgical; for PCOS). Step 5: IVF if all else fails. Metformin as adjunct for PCOS. hCG trigger shot (Ovidrel) when follicle ≥18mm.
us_prevalence: ~40% of female infertility is due to ovulatory dysfunction; ~2–3 million US women at any given time. PCOS accounts for ~75% of ovulatory dysfunction.
patients_needing_tx: ~1–2 million women/year require ovulation induction therapy.
price: $1,000–$5,000 per treatment cycle.
registrational_endpoint: Live birth rate per cycle; ovulation rate at cycle 1; cumulative live birth rate at 6 cycles.
btd_threshold_vs_soc: Cumulative live birth rate ≥40% at 6 cycles vs. ~30–35% with letrozole + standard IUI; or higher single-cycle live birth rate with fewer multiple gestations.

symptoms: Tubal obstruction, hydrosalpinx, pelvic adhesions; infertility, pelvic pain
treatment_goal: Restore tubal patency or bypass obstruction; achieve pregnancy
first_line_soc: Surgery (laparoscopic salpingostomy or salpingectomy for hydrosalpinx + IVF) is first-line. Antibiotic treatment for active PID to prevent tubal damage (see PID entry).
treatment_algorithm: Tubal occlusion: Laparoscopic or hysteroscopic tubal cannulation for proximal occlusion. Hydrosalpinx: Salpingectomy (removal) before IVF—improves IVF success rates by ~40%. IVF: Preferred over surgical repair for bilateral tubal disease, age >35. Pelvic adhesions: Laparoscopic adhesiolysis. Pharmacologic adjuncts: Corticosteroids (dexamethasone) post-adhesiolysis—limited evidence. Anti-adhesion barriers (Interceed, Seprafilm) in surgery. No pharmacologic SoC for tubal disease itself.
us_prevalence: ~12–30% of female infertility due to tubal/peritoneal factors; ~400,000–600,000 US women.
patients_needing_tx: ~400,000 US women requiring surgical evaluation + infertility management annually.
price: $5,000–$15,000 per cycle (IVF drugs + anti-adhesion agent).
registrational_endpoint: Clinical pregnancy rate per IVF transfer; live birth rate per retrieval; tubal patency rate at 6 months post-surgery.
btd_threshold_vs_soc: Live birth rate ≥45% per transfer in tubal-factor IVF vs. ~35–40% with standard IVF; or ≥50% reduction in re-adhesion rate post-surgery vs. Seprafilm alone.

symptoms: Inability to conceive despite normal workup; no identifiable structural or hormonal cause
treatment_goal: Achieve pregnancy via empiric stimulation or IVF without specific correction
first_line_soc: Empiric superovulation with IUI: Letrozole or clomiphene + timed IUI × 3–6 cycles. IVF for persistent unexplained infertility.
treatment_algorithm: Step 1: Expectant management (natural cycle) × 6–12 months if age <35. Step 2: Empiric oral ovulation induction—letrozole 2.5–5mg/day (preferred) or clomiphene 50–100mg + timed IUI × 3–6 cycles. Step 3: Gonadotropins (low-dose FSH) + IUI. Step 4: IVF ± ICSI. Adjuncts: Endometrial receptivity array (ERA) for implantation optimization; pre-implantation genetic testing (PGT-A). Empiric LMWH for possible implantation failure. Prednisolone for NK cell-related implantation failure (controversial).
us_prevalence: ~25–30% of infertile couples have unexplained infertility; ~500,000–800,000 US couples.
patients_needing_tx: ~600,000 couples/year requiring empiric pharmacologic treatment.
registrational_endpoint: Cumulative live birth rate at 6 cycles; time to pregnancy.
btd_threshold_vs_soc: Cumulative live birth rate ≥35% at 6 cycles vs. ~20–25% with letrozole + IUI standard protocol.

symptoms: Low AFC, elevated FSH, low AMH, poor ovarian response to stimulation
treatment_goal: Maximize egg retrieval with COS, preserve fertility options
first_line_soc: High-dose gonadotropin stimulation (Gonal-f or Follistim 300–450IU/day SC)—maximal stimulation. GnRH antagonist protocol. No pharmacologic intervention restores ovarian reserve.
treatment_algorithm: Poor responder protocols: Maximum FSH stimulation (450IU/day) ± LH supplementation (Pergonal, Menopur). Minimal stimulation/natural IVF cycle protocols. DHEA 75mg/day × 3 months (adrenal androgen precursor; improves response in some studies—not FDA-approved). CoQ10 600mg/day (antioxidant; mitochondrial support—controversial). Platelet-rich plasma (PRP) ovarian injection—experimental. Progestin-primed ovarian stimulation (PPOS). Egg donation as alternative.
us_prevalence: ~10–30% of infertile women have DOR; ~300,000–500,000 US women with significant DOR.
patients_needing_tx: ~200,000–300,000 requiring advanced stimulation protocols annually.
price: $5,000–$15,000 per cycle (premium stimulation protocol drugs).
registrational_endpoint: Number of oocytes retrieved per stimulation cycle; clinical pregnancy rate per transfer; POSEIDON group-specific live birth rate.
