btd_threshold_vs_soc: ≥4 mature oocytes retrieved in ≥50% of DOR patients vs. ~30% with standard high-dose rFSH alone; or live birth rate ≥25% in POSEIDON group 1/2.

symptoms: Not a disease; describes IVF, ICSI, embryo transfer techniques for infertile couples
treatment_goal: Achieve successful pregnancy through assisted conception techniques
first_line_soc: Controlled ovarian stimulation (COS): Gonadotropins (FSH [Gonal-f, Follistim]; LH; or combined hMG) + GnRH antagonist (ganirelix, cetrorelix) to prevent premature LH surge. Trigger: hCG (Pregnyl/Ovidrel) or leuprolide (GnRH agonist trigger). Luteal support: Progesterone (Endometrin, Crinone, Prometrium IM).
treatment_algorithm: COS: Recombinant FSH (Gonal-f, Follistim, Bemfola)—75–450IU/day SC for 8–14 days. GnRH antagonist (cetrorelix, ganirelix)—flexible protocol. Or GnRH agonist downregulation (leuprolide long protocol). Trigger: hCG 10,000U IM or recombinant hCG (Ovidrel 250mcg SC); GnRH agonist trigger for OHSS risk reduction. Luteal support: Progesterone 400mg vaginal BID (Endometrin/Crinone) or 50mg IM daily. PGT-A (genetic testing of embryos). Novel: Kisspeptin trigger (Phase 3 ongoing 2024); long-acting FSH (corifollitropin alfa; Elonva—EMA-approved).
us_prevalence: ~300,000 ART cycles/year in the US (SART 2024); ~2.5% of all US births from ART. ART use growing ~5–7%/year.
patients_needing_tx: ~300,000 cycles/year; ~200,000 couples undergoing ART.
price: $3,000–$10,000 per cycle (for stimulation drugs alone; total IVF cycle cost $15,000–$25,000).
registrational_endpoint: Live birth rate per retrieval cycle; clinical pregnancy rate; OHSS incidence (safety endpoint).
btd_threshold_vs_soc: Live birth rate ≥50% per retrieval in women <38 years vs. ~40% with standard rFSH + antagonist protocol; or equivalent efficacy with ≥30% OHSS reduction.

symptoms: Cervical mucus hostile to sperm; infertility, failed sperm penetration testing
treatment_goal: Improve cervical mucus quality or bypass cervix to enable fertilization
first_line_soc: Intrauterine insemination (IUI; bypasses cervical mucus) is most effective. Low-dose estrogen (ethinyl estradiol 0.02–0.05mg/day) during follicular phase to improve mucus quality.
treatment_algorithm: Empiric: Low-dose estrogen supplementation (EE 10–20mcg/day) during follicular phase to improve mucus quantity and quality. Treat underlying infection (Chlamydia, mycoplasma): Doxycycline. Anti-sperm antibody-related: Prednisone trials (limited evidence). IUI (most effective bypass strategy). IVF for cases not responding to IUI. Cervical dilation/cerclage for structural cervical causes.
us_prevalence: Cervical factor contributes to ~5% of infertility cases; ~300,000–500,000 US infertility couples with cervical factor.
patients_needing_tx: ~300,000–400,000 US couples annually.
registrational_endpoint: Sperm penetration test normalization; clinical pregnancy rate per cycle after IUI treatment.
btd_threshold_vs_soc: Clinical pregnancy rate ≥20% per cycle vs. ~10–15% with standard estrogen + IUI.

symptoms: Hot flashes, night sweats, vaginal dryness, mood changes, sleep disturbances, bone loss
treatment_goal: Relieve vasomotor symptoms, prevent osteoporosis, improve quality of life
first_line_soc: Hormone replacement therapy (HRT): Systemic estrogen ± progestogen (conjugated equine estrogens/CEE, estradiol) for moderate-to-severe vasomotor symptoms. Low-dose vaginal estrogen for genitourinary syndrome of menopause (GSM).
treatment_algorithm: HRT: Estradiol patches (0.025–0.1mg/24h), gels, oral CEE (Premarin 0.3–1.25mg/day) ± micronized progesterone (Prometrium 200mg/day × 12 days/month; or continuous 100mg/day). Systemic non-hormonal: Fezolinetant (Veozah; NK3R antagonist; FDA-approved 2023)—first non-hormonal approved for vasomotor symptoms; 45mg/day; SKYLIGHT trials. Elinzanetant (Phase 3 ongoing 2024). SSRIs/SNRIs: Escitalopram 10–20mg, venlafaxine 75mg (off-label VMS). Paroxetine (Brisdelle 7.5mg—FDA-approved for VMS 2013). Gabapentin 300mg TID (off-label). GSM: Topical vaginal estrogen (Vagifem, Estring, Premarin cream); prasterone (Intrarosa; DHEA intravaginal—FDA-approved 2016); ospemifene (Osphena 60mg PO—FDA-approved 2013 for dyspareunia). Bone: Bisphosphonates if osteoporosis.
us_prevalence: ~55 million US women are menopausal or postmenopausal (~50 million perimenopausal/menopausal); ~75–80% experience vasomotor symptoms, ~30–50% severely.
patients_needing_tx: ~20–25 million with moderate-severe VMS seeking treatment; ~8–10 million on HRT; ~2–5 million on non-hormonal agents.
price: $5,000–$15,000/year (branded NK3R antagonist like fezolinetant).
registrational_endpoint: Mean number of moderate-to-severe hot flash frequency reduction/week from baseline at week 12; proportion ≥50% hot flash frequency reduction (responder rate).
btd_threshold_vs_soc: ≥75% responder rate (≥50% VMS frequency reduction) at 12 weeks vs. ~50–60% with fezolinetant 45mg; or equivalent efficacy to HRT with superior safety for breast cancer survivors.

symptoms: Heavy painful periods, enlarged uterus, pelvic pressure, dyspareunia
treatment_goal: Relieve pain, reduce bleeding, preserve or sacrifice uterus based on fertility desire
first_line_soc: LNG-IUS (Mirena; levonorgestrel 52mg; intrauterine device)—most effective non-surgical option, reduces uterine bleeding and pain. NSAIDs for pain. COCs for younger patients.
treatment_algorithm: Step 1: NSAIDs + LNG-IUS (Mirena)—superior to oral progestins; ~50% amenorrhea at 12 months. Step 2: GnRH agonists (leuprolide) for 3–6 months (medical menopause; temporary). Step 3: GnRH antagonists with add-back: elagolix (Orilissa) or relugolix + add-back (Myfembree; FDA-approved May 2022 for adenomyosis-associated AUB)—first GnRH antagonist approved for adenomyosis. Oral progestins (dienogest 2mg/day; well-studied in EU; off-label in US). Surgical: Uterine artery embolization (UAE); MRI-guided focused ultrasound (HIFU); hysterectomy (definitive). 2024: Linzagolix in Phase 3.
us_prevalence: ~20–35% of women have adenomyosis at hysterectomy; ~3–5 million symptomatic US women.
patients_needing_tx: ~2–3 million symptomatic patients requiring pharmacologic therapy.
price: $8,000–$20,000/year (GnRH antagonist + add-back therapy).
registrational_endpoint: Dysmenorrhea NRS score reduction ≥50% from baseline at 24 weeks; menstrual blood loss normalization (<80 mL/cycle); uterine volume reduction on MRI.
btd_threshold_vs_soc: ≥60% dysmenorrhea responder rate at 24 weeks vs. ~40–45% with LNG-IUS or elagolix; or uterine volume reduction ≥30% at 6 months.

symptoms: Pelvic fullness or pain, incidental ovarian cyst, bloating, irregular periods
treatment_goal: Distinguish benign from malignant, manage symptoms, prevent complications
first_line_soc: Observation (watchful waiting) with serial ultrasound for most simple cysts <5–7cm. Oral contraceptives for functional cysts (suppress ovulation, prevent new cysts). Surgical resection for complex, symptomatic, or large cysts.
treatment_algorithm: Functional cysts: COCs (suppress FSH/LH; prevent recurrence). Endometriomas: GnRH agonist (leuprolide) + add-back or COC for suppression; laparoscopic cystectomy for large/symptomatic. Dermoid cysts: Laparoscopic cystectomy. PCOS-related polycystic ovaries: COC + metformin. Persistent simple cysts >7cm: Laparoscopic cystectomy. Pre-operative ERAS protocol: analgesics, dexamethasone for anti-nausea.
us_prevalence: ~10–15% of women of reproductive age have benign adnexal masses; ~5–8 million US women at any time. ~200,000 surgeries/year for ovarian cysts.
patients_needing_tx: ~3–4 million requiring hormonal management or surgery.
price: $5,000–$15,000/year (novel cyst-suppressing agent).
registrational_endpoint: Cyst regression (>50% volume reduction) at 3 months; prevention of new cysts over 6 months; surgical intervention-free rate at 1 year.
btd_threshold_vs_soc: Cyst regression ≥70% at 3 months vs. ~40% with COC alone; or new cyst prevention in ≥85% vs. ~60% with COC.

symptoms: Postcoital bleeding, intermenstrual spotting, mucus discharge; often asymptomatic
treatment_goal: Remove symptomatic polyp, confirm benign pathology, prevent recurrence
first_line_soc: Office polypectomy (avulsion/twisting off) is definitive for most. No pharmacologic SoC for polyp itself.
treatment_algorithm: Simple polypectomy (office twisting or forceps removal with endocervical curettage). Histopathologic assessment to exclude adenocarcinoma. Recurrence: Hysteroscopic polypectomy for persistent or large polyps. Pharmacologic: COCs or progestins (norethindrone) may reduce recurrence in estrogen-dominant women. No approved drug for polyp regression.
us_prevalence: ~4–5% of gynecology examinations; ~3–5 million US women with cervical polyps. Annual detection: ~500,000–800,000.
patients_needing_tx: ~600,000/year requiring polypectomy; pharmacologic (hormonal) prevention addressable: ~200,000 recurrent cases.
price: $1,000–$3,000/year (novel hormonal polyp-prevention agent).
registrational_endpoint: Recurrence-free rate at 1 year post-polypectomy; polyp regression rate at 3 months with hormonal treatment.
btd_threshold_vs_soc: Recurrence-free rate ≥85% at 1 year vs. ~70% with polypectomy alone.

disease: Bartholin Gland Cyst and Bartholin Gland Abscess
symptoms: Painless (cyst) or painful vulvar swelling at 4 or 8 o'clock position, pain with walking/intercourse
treatment_goal: Drain abscess, relieve symptoms, prevent recurrence
first_line_soc: Abscess: Incision and drainage (I&D) or Word catheter placement (most effective). Antibiotics (amoxicillin-clavulanate or doxycycline if STI suspected) as adjunct for cellulitis.
treatment_algorithm: Cyst (small, asymptomatic): Observation + sitz baths. Abscess: Word catheter insertion (I&D + balloon catheter left in place × 4–6 weeks to form fistula). Marsupialization for recurrent cases. Antibiotics: Amoxicillin-clavulanate for surrounding cellulitis; doxycycline + ceftriaxone if gonorrhea/chlamydia suspected. Silver nitrate or CO2 laser ablation alternatives for recurrent cysts. Excision (Bartholin gland removal) for postmenopausal women (cancer exclusion).
us_prevalence: ~2% of women develop Bartholin gland cysts/abscesses during lifetime; ~200,000–300,000 cases/year requiring treatment in the US.
patients_needing_tx: ~250,000/year requiring procedural + possible pharmacologic management.
price: $500–$2,000 per episode.
registrational_endpoint: Abscess resolution rate at 2 weeks; recurrence-free rate at 6 months.
btd_threshold_vs_soc: Recurrence-free rate ≥90% at 6 months vs. ~70–80% with Word catheter placement.

symptoms: Amenorrhea or hematometra (blood pooling), painful periods, difficulty with procedures, infertility
treatment_goal: Dilate cervix to restore drainage and menstruation; treat underlying cause
first_line_soc: Cervical dilation (Hegar or Pratt dilators) under local anesthesia or sedation. Misoprostol 400mcg vaginally 12–24h before dilation (cervical ripening agent) to soften cervix.
treatment_algorithm: Misoprostol 400mcg vaginally (or 200mcg sublingual) 12–24h before dilation—significantly facilitates cervical dilation. Estrogen (oral or vaginal estradiol × 4–6 weeks) to improve tissue elasticity for acquired stenosis. Cervical dilation under US guidance. Hysteroscopic balloon dilation for post-surgical cases. Stenting (occasional). No pharmacologic agent corrects the stenosis permanently.
us_prevalence: ~100,000–200,000 US women with symptomatic cervical stenosis. Most post-LEEP, cone biopsy, or endometrial ablation.
patients_needing_tx: ~150,000 requiring dilation + pharmacologic preparation.
price: $500–$3,000 per course.
registrational_endpoint: Successful dilation to ≥3mm in a single procedure (using misoprostol vs. placebo); recurrence of stenosis at 6 months.
btd_threshold_vs_soc: Successful dilation ≥90% at single attempt vs. ~70% with misoprostol alone.

symptoms: Painless vulvar lump, local pressure or discomfort, occasional discharge if infected
treatment_goal: Excise symptomatic cyst; treat infection if present
first_line_soc: Surgical excision for symptomatic cysts. Antibiotics (amoxicillin-clavulanate) if infected. No pharmacologic SoC.
treatment_algorithm: Incision and drainage for infected cysts (abscess). Complete excision for recurrent or persistent cysts. Antibiotics for surrounding cellulitis: amoxicillin-clavulanate or cephalexin orally. Topical antibiotics (fusidic acid) for minor superficial infection. No pharmacologic agent dissolves epidermal cysts.
us_prevalence: Common; ~500,000–1 million vulvar cyst cases evaluated/year in the US.
patients_needing_tx: ~300,000/year requiring medical/surgical management.
price: $200–$1,000 per episode.
registrational_endpoint: Cyst resolution at 4 weeks; infection clearance at 2 weeks; recurrence-free rate at 6 months.
btd_threshold_vs_soc: Cyst resolution without surgery in ≥70% vs. ~0% with antibiotics alone (most require excision).

symptoms: Sudden severe unilateral pelvic pain, nausea, vomiting, adnexal mass
treatment_goal: Urgent surgical detorsion to preserve ovarian function; prevent ischemic necrosis
first_line_soc: Emergency laparoscopic detorsion is the definitive management. No pharmacologic first-line SoC. IV opioid analgesics (morphine, hydromorphone) for pain control pre-operatively.
treatment_algorithm: IV opioid analgesics for pain management as bridge to surgery. Emergency laparoscopic detorsion (untwisting) ± cystectomy. Oophorectomy only if irreversible ischemic necrosis confirmed. Post-operatively: Analgesics (NSAIDs + acetaminophen). Oral contraceptives to suppress ovulation and prevent recurrent cyst formation. GnRH agonists for underlying endometriosis-related adnexal mass.
us_prevalence: ~1 per 4,000–9,000 gynecological emergency visits; ~30,000–80,000 cases/year in the US. Peak age 20–30 years.
patients_needing_tx: ~50,000 cases/year requiring emergency surgery + analgesics.
price: $1,000–$3,000 per episode (novel ischemia-protective agent as surgical adjunct).
registrational_endpoint: Ovarian salvage rate (viable ovary at 3-month follow-up ultrasound); recurrence-free rate at 1 year.
btd_threshold_vs_soc: Ovarian salvage ≥95% when ischemia-protective agent administered before detorsion vs. ~80–85% with immediate detorsion alone.

symptoms: Periurethral swelling, dysuria, pain with intercourse, urinary difficulty
treatment_goal: Drain or excise cyst to relieve symptoms
first_line_soc: Surgical excision or marsupialization is definitive. No pharmacologic SoC.
treatment_algorithm: Small asymptomatic: Observation. Symptomatic: Surgical marsupialization or excision under local anesthesia. Antibiotics (amoxicillin-clavulanate) for associated infection/abscess. No pharmacologic agent dissolves Skene gland cysts.
us_prevalence: Rare; ~5,000–15,000 cases requiring treatment annually in the US.
patients_needing_tx: ~10,000/year requiring surgical + possible antibiotic management.
price: $500–$2,000 per episode.
registrational_endpoint: Cyst resolution (ultrasound-confirmed) at 6 weeks post-treatment; recurrence-free rate at 1 year.
btd_threshold_vs_soc: Cyst resolution in ≥90% at 6 weeks vs. ~75% with surgical marsupialization alone.

disease: Anterior and Posterior Vaginal Wall Prolapse
symptoms: Vaginal bulge, pelvic pressure, urinary leakage or retention, constipation, dyspareunia
treatment_goal: Restore pelvic organ support, relieve prolapse symptoms, improve quality of life
first_line_soc: Pelvic floor physical therapy (PFPT). Pessary (vaginal support device; first-line nonsurgical). Topical vaginal estrogen for tissue quality in postmenopausal women.
treatment_algorithm: Conservative: PFPT + vaginal pessary (ring, Gellhorn, or donut pessary). Topical vaginal estrogen (low-dose estradiol; Vagifem 10mcg or Premarin cream) for tissue optimization pre-surgery or for pessary users. Surgical: Anterior colporrhaphy (cystocele), posterior colporrhaphy (rectocele); laparoscopic paravaginal repair; mesh-augmented repair (caution: FDA mesh warnings 2019). No pharmacologic agent repairs prolapse.
us_prevalence: ~3–6% of US women; ~3.5–7 million affected. Pelvic organ prolapse surgery: ~300,000–400,000/year.
patients_needing_tx: ~1–2 million symptomatic patients seeking pharmacologic + conservative management.
price: $5,000–$15,000/year (novel pelvic floor remodeling agent or topical estrogen combo).
registrational_endpoint: POP-Q stage improvement at 12 months; PFDI-20 (Pelvic Floor Distress Inventory) score reduction; recurrence rate at 2 years post-surgery.
btd_threshold_vs_soc: ≥2 POP-Q stage improvement in ≥50% at 12 months vs. ~20–30% with PFPT alone; or 2-year recurrence rate ≤10% vs. ~20–30% with surgical repair alone.

symptoms: Uterine descent into or through vagina, pelvic heaviness, urinary symptoms, difficulty defecating
treatment_goal: Restore uterine/apical support, relieve symptoms, maintain sexual and bladder function
first_line_soc: Vaginal pessary (ring, Gellhorn) + topical vaginal estrogen for tissue conditioning. Surgical repair (sacrospinous fixation, sacrohysteropexy, hysterectomy + vault suspension) for definitive treatment.
treatment_algorithm: Same as anterior/posterior prolapse management for conservative: pessary + topical estrogen. Surgical: Vaginal—sacrospinous ligament fixation (SSLF); robotic/laparoscopic sacrohysteropexy (uterus-sparing); total vaginal hysterectomy + McCall culdoplasty for completed prolapse. Obliterative (colpocleisis): For elderly non-sexually active. Pharmacologic adjuncts: Vaginal estrogen pre/post-operatively to optimize tissue. No pharmacologic cure for uterine prolapse.
us_prevalence: ~1–3 million US women with symptomatic uterine prolapse; ~150,000 hysterectomies for prolapse/year.
patients_needing_tx: ~1 million requiring pessary + topical estrogen; ~150,000/year surgical.
registrational_endpoint: POP-Q point C (cervix/vault) at or above -1 at 12 months; prolapse symptom score (PFDI-20 prolapse subscale) improvement.
btd_threshold_vs_soc: POP-Q anatomical success (C ≥ -1) in ≥75% at 2 years vs. ~60% with sacrospinous fixation alone.

symptoms: Heavy menstrual bleeding, pelvic pressure, frequent urination, dyspareunia, infertility
treatment_goal: Control heavy bleeding, reduce fibroid bulk, preserve uterus/fertility when possible
first_line_soc: Oral GnRH antagonist + hormonal add-back therapy: relugolix/estradiol/norethindrone acetate (Myfembree; FDA-approved May 2021) or elagolix/estradiol/norethindrone (Oriahnn; FDA-approved 2020)—first FDA-approved oral medical treatments for uterine fibroid HMB.
treatment_algorithm: Non-surgical medical options: (1) GnRH antagonist combinations—Myfembree (relugolix 40mg + E2 1mg + NETA 0.5mg/day; FDA-approved), Oriahnn (elagolix 300mg BID + E2/NETA; FDA-approved). (2) GnRH agonists (leuprolide, goserelin, nafarelin) + add-back HRT—pre-surgical downsizing. (3) Tranexamic acid (Lysteda) for acute HMB. (4) LNG-IUS (Mirena) for HMB control without bulk symptoms. (5) Oral progestins (norethisterone) for temporary HMB control. Procedural: Uterine artery embolization (UAE), MRgFUS, endometrial ablation. Surgical: Myomectomy (fertility-sparing), hysterectomy (definitive). 2024: Linzagolix (GnRH antagonist; once-daily; Phase 3 completed) under FDA review.
us_prevalence: ~26 million US women have uterine fibroids (~26% of reproductive-age women); symptomatic requiring treatment: ~6–7 million (ACOG/NICHD 2024).
patients_needing_tx: ~6–7 million US women with symptomatic fibroids; ~1.5–2 million needing prescription medical therapy; ~400,000 hysterectomies/year largely fibroid-driven.
price: $15,000–$35,000/year (oral GnRH antagonist combination; Myfembree WAC ~$20,000/year).
registrational_endpoint: Proportion achieving menstrual blood loss <80 mL/cycle at month 6 (alkaline hematin method); proportion achieving ≥50% reduction in HMB from baseline.
btd_threshold_vs_soc: MBL <80 mL in ≥75% at month 6 vs. ~55–68% with Myfembree or Oriahnn; or superior fibroid volume reduction.

symptoms: Pelvic pressure, dyspareunia, heavy bleeding, obstructed labor, urinary symptoms
treatment_goal: Relieve obstruction, control bleeding, remove if symptomatic or obstructing labor
first_line_soc: GnRH agonists (leuprolide acetate [Lupron Depot; 3.75mg IM monthly] + add-back HRT) to shrink fibroid before surgery. Surgical myomectomy or hysterectomy for definitive treatment.
treatment_algorithm: Medical preoperative: GnRH agonists (leuprolide, goserelin)—shrink fibroids ~30–50% over 3–6 months, reduce intraoperative bleeding. GnRH antagonists: Elagolix + estradiol/norethindrone (Oriahnn; FDA-approved 2020 for fibroid HMB) or relugolix + E2/NET (Myfembree; FDA-approved 2021)—oral, rapidly reversible. Tranexamic acid for acute heavy bleeding. Surgical: Cervical myomectomy (complex; fertility preservation); hysterectomy if severe. No pharmacologic cure—surgery or procedures (UAE, MRgFUS) definitive.
us_prevalence: ~1–3% of all fibroids are cervical; ~200,000–600,000 US women with cervical leiomyomas. (Uterine fibroids overall: ~26 million US women.)
patients_needing_tx: ~150,000–400,000 with symptomatic cervical fibroids requiring pharmacologic or surgical management.
price: $15,000–$30,000/year (oral GnRH antagonist + add-back).
registrational_endpoint: Fibroid volume reduction ≥25% at 6 months; heavy menstrual bleeding responder rate (MBL <80mL/cycle) at 6 months.
btd_threshold_vs_soc: Fibroid volume reduction ≥40% and MBL <80mL/cycle in ≥70% at 6 months vs. ~50% with leuprolide.

symptoms: Pelvic pain, preterm contractions, placental abruption risk, obstructed labor, increased cesarean rate
treatment_goal: Manage fibroid-related complications in pregnancy, maintain fetal well-being
first_line_soc: Analgesics (acetaminophen; NSAIDs avoided in pregnancy ≥20 weeks) for pain from fibroid degeneration. Tocolytics (nifedipine) for preterm labor associated with fibroids.
treatment_algorithm: Red degeneration pain: Acetaminophen, bed rest, hydration; NSAIDs only <20 weeks if necessary. Tocolysis for preterm contractions: Nifedipine 20–30mg (CCB). Antepartum hemorrhage: Resuscitation. Intraoperative at cesarean: Oxytocin for uterine atony post-delivery; myomectomy at cesarean controversial (increased bleeding). Post-partum: Uterotonic agents (oxytocin, carboprost) for hemorrhage risk. GnRH agonists/antagonists deferred until after delivery and breastfeeding complete.
us_prevalence: ~10–40% of pregnant women have fibroids; ~400,000–1.6 million pregnant women with fibroids/year in the US. Symptomatic complications: ~5–10% of those.
patients_needing_tx: ~50,000–160,000 pregnant women per year with symptomatic fibroid complications requiring pharmacologic management.
price: $2,000–$8,000 per pregnancy.
registrational_endpoint: Preterm birth rate <37 weeks; composite maternal-fetal morbidity (abruption, preterm birth, blood transfusion); pain NRS score reduction.
btd_threshold_vs_soc: ≥30% reduction in preterm birth rate or composite morbidity vs. supportive care alone.

symptoms: Purulent vaginal discharge, burning, dyspareunia, erythema, loss of lactobacilli
treatment_goal: Reduce inflammation, restore normal vaginal flora, relieve symptoms
first_line_soc: Desquamative inflammatory vaginitis (DIV): Intravaginal clindamycin 2% cream (antibiotic + anti-inflammatory) QHS × 4–6 weeks—most widely used. Intravaginal hydrocortisone 25mg suppositories for inflammatory component.
treatment_algorithm: DIV: Vaginal clindamycin 2% cream × 4–6 weeks; or vaginal hydrocortisone 25mg suppositories; or combination. If treatment fails: Estrogen therapy (vaginal estrogen cream) for post-menopausal/hypoestrogenic causes. Cytolytic vaginosis: Baking soda douches (alkalinization). Atrophic vaginitis: Vaginal estrogen (estriol cream, Premarin cream, Vagifem tablets, Imvexxy, Yuvafem). Ospemifene (Osphena; SERM) oral for dyspareunia from atrophic vaginitis. Prasterone (Intrarosa; DHEA vaginal insert; FDA-approved 2016) for moderate-severe dyspareunia.
us_prevalence: ~1–5% of women with vaginal complaints have DIV (uncommon). Atrophic vaginitis (genitourinary syndrome of menopause): ~50% of post-menopausal women (~15–20 million US women).
patients_needing_tx: ~10–15 million with atrophic vaginitis + inflammatory vaginitis requiring pharmacologic treatment.
price: $3,000–$10,000/year (novel vaginal microbiome-restoring agent or improved vaginal estrogen).
registrational_endpoint: Vaginal pH normalization (<4.5); Most Bothersome Symptom (MBS) score improvement (dryness or dyspareunia VAS) at 12 weeks; vaginal maturation index improvement.
btd_threshold_vs_soc: MBS responder rate ≥70% at 12 weeks vs. ~55% with vaginal estrogen cream; or DIV remission ≥85% at 6 weeks vs. ~60% with vaginal clindamycin.

symptoms: Thick white 'cottage cheese' discharge, intense vaginal itching, burning, erythema
treatment_goal: Eradicate Candida, relieve symptoms, prevent recurrence
first_line_soc: Azole antifungals: intravaginal miconazole (Monistat; OTC) or clotrimazole 1–7 day courses. Oral fluconazole (Diflucan 150mg single dose; azole antifungal) for uncomplicated VVC.
treatment_algorithm: Uncomplicated VVC: OTC topical azoles (miconazole, clotrimazole, tioconazole 1–7 day courses) or oral fluconazole 150mg × 1 dose—equivalent efficacy. Complicated (severe, recurrent ≥4×/year, non-albicans): Fluconazole 150mg q72h × 3 doses (induction) then weekly × 6 months (maintenance). Non-albicans Candida: Boric acid 600mg vaginal suppositories QHS × 14 days; flucytosine 17% cream; nystatin. 2021: Ibrexafungerp (Brexafemme; triterpenoid; first-in-class; FDA-approved 2021 for VVC) 300mg BID × 1 day; also approved for recurrent VVC. Oteseconazole (Vivjoa; oral azole with high selectivity; FDA-approved 2022 for recurrent VVC).
us_prevalence: ~75% of women have ≥1 VVC episode in their lifetime; ~1.4 billion annual episodes globally; ~8–15 million cases/year in the US. Recurrent VVC: ~8% of women (~6 million US women).
patients_needing_tx: ~10–12 million VVC episodes/year treated in the US; ~6 million with recurrent VVC needing chronic suppressive therapy.
price: $500–$3,000 per course; $5,000–$15,000/year for recurrent VVC (Oteseconazole WAC ~$11,000/year).
registrational_endpoint: Therapeutic cure (clinical + mycological cure) at test-of-cure visit (day 25–28); for recurrent VVC—proportion with ≤1 recurrence at 52 weeks.
btd_threshold_vs_soc: Therapeutic cure ≥85% at day 25 vs. ~73–80% with fluconazole single-dose; or recurrence-free rate ≥75% at 52 weeks vs. ~55–60% with weekly fluconazole maintenance.

symptoms: Thin gray-white vaginal discharge, fishy odor (especially post-coitus), mild itching, pH >4.5
treatment_goal: Eradicate BV, relieve symptoms, prevent recurrence and complications (PID, PTB)
first_line_soc: Metronidazole (Flagyl; nitroimidazole antibiotic) 500mg PO BID × 7 days OR metronidazole 0.75% vaginal gel 5g daily × 5 days. Clindamycin (lincosamide antibiotic) 2% vaginal cream 5g QHS × 7 days as alternative.
treatment_algorithm: First-line: Metronidazole 500mg PO BID × 7 days (oral preferred for non-pregnant); or metronidazole vaginal gel 0.75% × 5 days; clindamycin cream 2% × 7 days; tinidazole 2g PO × 2 days. Pregnancy: Metronidazole 500mg BID × 7 days (preferred oral or vaginal). Recurrent BV (≥3 episodes/year): Suppressive metronidazole 0.75% gel 2×/week × 16 weeks post-treatment; monthly boric acid vaginal suppositories; Lactin-V (L. crispatus vaginal probiotic; FDA-approved 2023 for recurrent BV). 2024–2025: LACTIN-V (Lactin-V; Phase 3) + Otexa (secnidazole combo) + new nitroimidazoles for biofilm.
us_prevalence: ~21 million US women aged 14–49 have BV annually (~29% prevalence; CDC 2024); ~1 in 3 women with vaginal symptoms. Most common vaginal condition.
patients_needing_tx: ~10–15 million symptomatic BV cases/year requiring pharmacologic treatment; ~2–3 million with recurrent BV.
price: $500–$3,000 per course or $5,000–$12,000/year for recurrent BV management.
registrational_endpoint: Clinical cure (Amsel criteria absence OR Nugent score ≤3) at test-of-cure visit (day 21–30); recurrence-free rate at 12 weeks for recurrent BV.
btd_threshold_vs_soc: Clinical cure ≥90% at day 21 vs. ~70–80% with metronidazole 7-day course; or recurrence-free rate ≥70% at 12 weeks vs. ~50% with standard metronidazole.

symptoms: Mucopurulent cervical discharge, cervical friability/erythema, dyspareunia, dysuria
treatment_goal: Eradicate STI pathogens, prevent ascending infection (PID), treat partners
first_line_soc: Presumptive treatment per CDC 2021 STI guidelines: Ceftriaxone 500mg IM (gonorrhea coverage) + doxycycline 100mg PO BID × 7 days (chlamydia coverage)—covers both most common bacterial causes simultaneously.
treatment_algorithm: Gonorrhea (GC): Ceftriaxone 500mg IM × 1 (>150kg: 1g IM); no oral cephalosporins for uncomplicated GC (resistance). Chlamydia: Doxycycline 100mg BID × 7 days (preferred) or azithromycin 1g × 1 (inferior cure rates, used if adherence concern). Test of cure for GC 1–2 weeks post-treatment (NAAT). Partner treatment (EPT). M. genitalium: Moxifloxacin 400mg × 10 days (last resort) or azithromycin 1g (1st line if susceptible). Herpes: Acyclovir/valacyclovir for HSV cervicitis. Screen for other STIs (HIV, syphilis, trichomoniasis). Recurrent: Reinfection vs. treatment failure—reculture.
us_prevalence: ~1.6 million gonorrhea cases/year + ~4 million chlamydia cases/year in the US (CDC STI Surveillance 2024); many presenting as cervicitis. Total symptomatic cervicitis: ~1–2 million presentations/year.
patients_needing_tx: ~2–3 million STI-related cervicitis cases/year requiring antibiotic treatment.
price: $500–$3,000 per course (novel gonorrhea or chlamydia antibiotic).
registrational_endpoint: Microbiological cure (NAAT-negative at 4 weeks); clinical cure (discharge resolution, cervical friability resolution) vs. SoC.
btd_threshold_vs_soc: Microbiological cure ≥95% at 4 weeks vs. ~90% with ceftriaxone + doxycycline; or effective single-dose oral regimen with equivalent cure rates.

symptoms: Lower abdominal pain, cervical motion tenderness, adnexal tenderness, fever, vaginal discharge
treatment_goal: Eradicate polymicrobial infection, prevent tubo-ovarian abscess, preserve fertility
first_line_soc: Outpatient (mild-moderate): Ceftriaxone 500mg IM × 1 + doxycycline 100mg BID × 14 days + metronidazole 500mg BID × 14 days (CDC 2021 guidelines). Inpatient (severe/TOA): IV cefoxitin 2g q6h + doxycycline 100mg q12h OR IV clindamycin 900mg q8h + gentamicin.
treatment_algorithm: Outpatient: Ceftriaxone 500mg IM + doxycycline 100mg BID + metronidazole 500mg BID × 14 days. Cover GC, Chlamydia, anaerobes. Inpatient: IV cefotetan or cefoxitin + doxycycline IV/PO; or IV clindamycin + gentamicin; transition to oral after clinical improvement × 14 days total. TOA (tubo-ovarian abscess): IV antibiotics + CT-guided drainage if ≥8cm or failing antibiotics. Partner treatment essential (EPT). Intrauterine device: IUCD can be retained in most mild-moderate PID cases. Screen for HIV and other STIs. 2024 update: Increased ceftriaxone dose (500mg vs. prior 250mg) for GC resistance.
us_prevalence: ~800,000–1 million PID cases/year in the US (CDC); ~100,000–200,000 hospitalizations/year. Second most common STI complication after cervicitis.
patients_needing_tx: ~800,000–1 million cases/year all requiring antibiotic treatment.
price: $1,000–$5,000 per course (novel antibiotic combination targeting biofilm-forming polymicrobial PID).
registrational_endpoint: Clinical cure (defervescence, pain resolution, cervical motion tenderness absent) at day 3; 30-day TOA formation rate; fertility outcomes at 12 months.
btd_threshold_vs_soc: Clinical cure ≥95% at day 3 vs. ~85% with ceftriaxone + doxycycline + metronidazole; or ≥30% reduction in TOA formation rate.

symptoms: Not a disease; risk factors include prior preterm birth, hypertension, diabetes, multiple gestation
treatment_goal: Mitigate identified risks, optimize maternal and fetal outcomes
first_line_soc: Low-dose aspirin (81mg/day; antiplatelet) for preeclampsia prevention in high-risk patients. Folic acid 0.4–5mg/day for NTD prevention. LMWH for thrombophilia-associated risk.
treatment_algorithm: Preeclampsia risk: LDA 81mg/day × 12–36 weeks + calcium supplementation 1.5g/day. Neural tube defects: Folic acid 0.4–5mg/day preconceptionally. GDM prevention: Diet, exercise, metformin. Thrombophilia/prior VTE: LMWH (enoxaparin) prophylaxis throughout pregnancy. Prior preterm birth: Vaginal progesterone + cervical length monitoring. Thyroid: Levothyroxine for hypothyroidism. Rubella/varicella/influenza: Vaccination preconception.
us_prevalence: ~4 million pregnancies/year in the US; ~30–40% have at least one identified risk factor requiring pharmacologic intervention.
patients_needing_tx: ~1.5–2 million high-risk pregnancies/year requiring preventive pharmacotherapy.
price: $3,000–$15,000 per pregnancy course.
registrational_endpoint: Rate of specified complication (preeclampsia, PTB, GDM, VTE) at term; composite adverse outcome rate per 1000 high-risk pregnancies.
btd_threshold_vs_soc: ≥30% relative reduction in target complication vs. current standard preventive protocol.

symptoms: Hypertension ≥140/90, proteinuria, headache, visual changes, swelling after 20 weeks
treatment_goal: Prevent maternal seizures, deliver safely, prevent maternal/fetal complications
first_line_soc: Antihypertensives (labetalol IV, nifedipine oral, hydralazine IV) for severe hypertension (≥160/110). Magnesium sulfate IV (anticonvulsant; seizure prophylaxis for preeclampsia/eclampsia). Low-dose aspirin 81–162mg from 12–28 weeks for primary prevention in high-risk women.
treatment_algorithm: Severe HTN: IV labetalol (alpha+beta blocker) 20mg IV q10min, or IV hydralazine, or nifedipine IR 10mg oral. Seizure prevention: MgSO4 4–6g IV loading → 1–2g/h maintenance (Zuspan protocol)—gold standard. Prevention: LDA 81mg/day from 12–28 weeks for high-risk (ACOG/USPSTF 2021). Antenatal care: Antihypertensive maintenance (labetalol, nifedipine ER, methyldopa). Delivery: Definitive treatment for severe preeclampsia at ≥34 weeks; antenatal steroids at <34 weeks. Anti-sFlt-1 strategies (LO1/placental factor: RNA oligonucleotide approaches in trials, 2024). Novel: Pravastatin, L-arginine in trials for prevention.
us_prevalence: ~5–8% of all pregnancies (250,000–320,000 cases/year in the US per ACOG 2024); eclampsia (seizures): ~0.05%.
patients_needing_tx: ~300,000/year with preeclampsia requiring antihypertensives + MgSO4; ~200,000 high-risk women requiring LDA prevention.
price: $5,000–$30,000 per pregnancy (novel preventive biologic or RNA therapeutic).
registrational_endpoint: Prevention: Preeclampsia incidence reduction at ≥34 weeks vs. placebo. Treatment: Composite of severe maternal morbidity + preterm birth <34 weeks.
btd_threshold_vs_soc: ≥50% relative reduction in preeclampsia incidence vs. LDA alone; or ≥30% reduction in composite maternal morbidity.

symptoms: Regular contractions <37 weeks with cervical change, lower abdominal tightening
treatment_goal: Delay delivery to allow corticosteroids and fetal maturity; prevent preterm birth
first_line_soc: Tocolysis with nifedipine (Procardia; CCB; first-line oral tocolytic per ACOG) or indomethacin (COX inhibitor; <32 weeks). Betamethasone 12mg IM × 2 doses (corticosteroid for fetal lung maturity).
treatment_algorithm: Tocolysis (for 48h steroids window): Step 1—Nifedipine 20–30mg IR then 10–20mg q4–6h (CCB; first-line per ACOG 2024). Step 2—Indomethacin 50–100mg PR/PO load then 25mg q6h × 48h (<32 weeks). Step 3—MgSO4 for tocolysis or neuroprotection (<32 weeks; second-line tocolytic). Step 4—Atosiban (Tractocile; oxytocin antagonist; approved in Europe but not US). 17-OHPC (Makena; 250mg IM/weekly) withdrawn from market in 2023 (FDA). Progesterone (vaginal; Prometrium) for short cervix. Azithromycin for latency. Cerclage for cervical insufficiency.
us_prevalence: ~10.5% of births in the US are preterm (~400,000/year, CDC 2024); active preterm labor episodes: ~200,000–300,000/year.
patients_needing_tx: ~200,000 preterm labor episodes/year requiring tocolysis + corticosteroids; ~400,000 women with risk factors for preventive progesterone.
price: $3,000–$15,000 per pregnancy course.
registrational_endpoint: Proportion delivering ≥48h after treatment initiation; proportion delivering ≥34 weeks; neonatal composite morbidity rate.
btd_threshold_vs_soc: ≥75% deliver ≥48h after tocolysis vs. ~60–70% with nifedipine; or ≥20% absolute reduction in preterm birth <34 weeks vs. nifedipine.

symptoms: Severe nausea and vomiting in pregnancy, weight loss >5%, dehydration, electrolyte abnormalities
treatment_goal: Control vomiting, restore hydration, maintain nutrition, prevent complications
first_line_soc: Doxylamine-pyridoxine (Bonjesta/Diclegis; delayed-release; FDA-approved 2013/2016) as first-line pharmacologic. IV ondansetron (5-HT3 antagonist) + IV fluid resuscitation for hospitalized cases.
treatment_algorithm: Mild: Doxylamine 10mg + pyridoxine 10mg (Bonjesta ER 2 tabs; FDA category A equivalent); ginger; dietary modification. Moderate: Promethazine 25mg IV/PO; metoclopramide 10mg IV/PO; pyridoxine IV. Severe (HG): IV rehydration + ondansetron 4–8mg IV q6–8h; methylprednisolone IV for refractory (with caution re: oral cleft if <10 weeks); thiamine supplementation (Wernicke's prevention). Droperidol + diphenhydramine combination IV. Nasogastric/TPN nutrition for refractory cases. 2024: Ondansetron controversies (QTc) re-examined; metoclopramide remains standard.
us_prevalence: ~1–3% of pregnancies experience HG; ~4 million births/year × 2% = ~80,000 HG cases/year in the US. Routine nausea/vomiting (morning sickness): ~70–80% of pregnancies.
patients_needing_tx: ~80,000 HG cases + ~2 million moderate nausea/vomiting cases requiring pharmacologic management.
price: $2,000–$8,000 per pregnancy course.
registrational_endpoint: PUQE-24 (Pregnancy-Unique Quantification of Emesis and Nausea) score improvement; hospitalization rate reduction; weight gain/loss from baseline at 4 weeks.
btd_threshold_vs_soc: ≥50% of HG patients maintain weight and avoid hospitalization vs. ~35% with doxylamine-pyridoxine alone.

symptoms: Not a disease; represents outcomes. Risk factors: hemorrhage, preeclampsia, sepsis, thromboembolism.
treatment_goal: Prevent maternal deaths; reduce perinatal mortality through optimized antenatal care
first_line_soc: Aspirin 81–162mg/day (antiplatelet; preeclampsia prevention in high-risk women); magnesium sulfate IV for seizure prophylaxis in preeclampsia; oxytocin (uterotonic) for hemorrhage prevention at delivery.
treatment_algorithm: Preeclampsia prevention: Low-dose aspirin (LDA) 81–162mg/day from 12 weeks for high-risk patients (AHA/ACOG 2023). Hemorrhage prevention: Active management of third stage—oxytocin 10U IM (most effective); carbetocin (long-acting oxytocin analog; WHO-approved). Sepsis: IV antibiotics. Thromboembolism: LMWH prophylaxis. Aspirin + calcium supplementation (WHO) for high-risk PE. VTE prophylaxis for high-risk (LMWH—enoxaparin). 2024: Nirsevimab for RSV prevention in neonates reduces perinatal mortality.
us_prevalence: US maternal mortality rate: ~22–24/100,000 live births (CDC 2024); ~800–1,000 maternal deaths/year in the US. Perinatal mortality: ~6/1,000 births.
patients_needing_tx: ~4 million pregnancies/year with targeted preventive pharmacology (aspirin, oxytocin, LMWH for high-risk subsets); ~400,000 high-risk pregnancies requiring LDA.
price: $1,000–$5,000 per pregnancy (preventive pharmacologic package).
registrational_endpoint: Maternal mortality rate reduction; severe maternal morbidity composite (ICU admission, blood transfusion, hysterectomy, organ failure) per 10,000 deliveries.
btd_threshold_vs_soc: ≥30% relative reduction in severe maternal morbidity vs. current LDA + oxytocin protocols.

symptoms: Sudden gush or leaking of amniotic fluid, confirmation with pooling/Fern/PAMG-1 test
treatment_goal: Prevent chorioamnionitis, achieve fetal maturity, time delivery appropriately
first_line_soc: Antibiotics (ampicillin IV + erythromycin PO, then amoxicillin × 7 days; latency antibiotics per ACOG) to prolong latency and prevent infection. Corticosteroids (betamethasone) for fetal lung maturity at <34 weeks.
treatment_algorithm: Latency antibiotics: IV ampicillin 2g q6h + IV erythromycin 250mg q6h × 48h, then oral amoxicillin 250mg q8h + erythromycin base 333mg q8h × 5 days (ACOG PPROM management). Group B Strep prophylaxis: IV penicillin G or ampicillin during labor. Corticosteroids: Betamethasone 12mg IM × 2 doses for <34 weeks. MgSO4 neuroprotection at <32 weeks. Tocolysis: Controversial in PPROM; used for 48h to allow steroids. Deliver at 34 weeks for PPROM.
us_prevalence: PROM at term: ~8% of pregnancies; PPROM (<37 weeks): ~3%; ~120,000–240,000 cases/year in the US.
patients_needing_tx: ~120,000 PPROM cases/year requiring antibiotics + steroids management.
price: $3,000–$10,000 per pregnancy.
registrational_endpoint: Latency period (days to delivery) from PPROM; neonatal composite morbidity (RDS, sepsis, NEC, IVH, death) at 28 days.
btd_threshold_vs_soc: ≥48h additional latency over current antibiotic SoC; ≥20% reduction in neonatal composite morbidity.

disease: Hemolytic Disease of the Fetus and Neonate
symptoms: Fetal anemia, hydrops fetalis, neonatal jaundice, hemolytic anemia in neonate
treatment_goal: Prevent maternal sensitization, treat fetal anemia, prevent kernicterus
first_line_soc: Prevention: Rh(D) immune globulin (RhoGAM; 300mcg IM at 28 weeks + within 72h of delivery) for Rh-negative women carrying Rh-positive fetus—prevents maternal sensitization.
treatment_algorithm: Prevention: RhoGAM (anti-D IgG) 300mcg at 28 weeks + postpartum + after any sensitizing event. Monitoring: MCA Doppler ultrasound for fetal anemia. Treatment (sensitized fetus): Intrauterine transfusion (IUT) with O-negative packed RBCs. Neonatal: Phototherapy for jaundice; IV immunoglobulin (IVIG); exchange transfusion for severe neonatal hemolysis. 2024: Anti-FcRn (nipocalimab—Phase 3 for HDFN; mAb reducing maternal IgG antibodies) shown promising in Phase 3 trials.
us_prevalence: ~6,000–8,000 cases/year of HDFN in the US (with widespread RhoGAM use); without prevention, would be much higher. IUT needed: ~500–1,000 cases/year.
patients_needing_tx: ~3–4 million Rh-negative pregnancies/year receive RhoGAM prophylaxis; ~5,000–8,000 active HDFN cases needing treatment.
price: $50,000–$200,000 per pregnancy (for novel anti-FcRn mAb like nipocalimab).
registrational_endpoint: Proportion of fetuses requiring IUT; incidence of severe fetal anemia (MCA peak systolic velocity >1.5 MoM) requiring intervention; livebirth rate.
btd_threshold_vs_soc: ≥50% relative reduction in fetal transfusions required vs. standard monitoring + IUT in sensitized pregnancies.

symptoms: Reduced amniotic fluid, decreased fetal movement, small fundal height, fetal distress
treatment_goal: Restore amniotic fluid, improve fetal outcomes, prevent cord compression
first_line_soc: Maternal hydration (IV or oral hypotonic fluid) as temporizing measure. Amnioinfusion (transcervical infusion of saline) for intrapartum cord decompression. Delivery if at term.
treatment_algorithm: IV maternal hydration (2–3L water or 1L IV NS) may temporarily increase AFI. Amnioinfusion during labor for cord compression/variable decelerations. NSAID reversal: Stop indomethacin if cause (reduces fetal urine output). Treat renal anomalies causing fetal auria. Serial amnioinfusion for renal agenesis (investigational). Delivery if ≥36–37 weeks.
us_prevalence: ~3–5% of pregnancies; ~120,000–200,000 cases/year in the US.
patients_needing_tx: ~100,000–150,000 cases/year requiring monitoring and management.
price: $2,000–$8,000 per pregnancy course.
registrational_endpoint: Amniotic fluid index (AFI) normalization (>5cm) at 1 week; neonatal outcomes (5-minute Apgar, perinatal asphyxia rate).
btd_threshold_vs_soc: AFI normalization in ≥60% at 1 week vs. ~30% with IV hydration alone; or ≥20% reduction in emergency cesarean rate.

symptoms: Painful vaginal bleeding, uterine rigidity, fetal distress, abdominal pain
treatment_goal: Stabilize mother, assess fetal status, expedite delivery if severe
first_line_soc: IV fluid resuscitation + blood transfusion for hemorrhage. Magnesium sulfate for neuroprotection if preterm (<32 weeks). Corticosteroids (betamethasone) for fetal lung maturity if <34 weeks. Emergent delivery (cesarean) for severe cases.
treatment_algorithm: Hemodynamic stabilization: IV crystalloids, packed RBCs, FFP for coagulopathy (DIC risk). Fetal neuroprotection: MgSO4 IV 6g bolus → 2g/h for <32 weeks. Fetal lung maturity: Betamethasone 12mg IM × 2 if <34 weeks. Mild abruption with preterm fetus: Tocolysis (nifedipine) controversial but sometimes used. Severe/major abruption: Emergent cesarean. Monitoring for DIC: FFP, cryoprecipitate, platelets.
us_prevalence: ~0.5–1% of pregnancies; ~20,000–40,000 cases/year in the US.
patients_needing_tx: ~30,000/year requiring active pharmacologic and/or surgical management.
price: $5,000–$20,000 per episode.
registrational_endpoint: Composite of maternal mortality, DIC, hysterectomy rate; neonatal intact survival to 28 days.
btd_threshold_vs_soc: ≥30% reduction in composite severe maternal morbidity (DIC, hysterectomy, death) vs. standard resuscitation + delivery.

symptoms: Painless cervical dilation/effacement in second trimester, recurrent pregnancy loss
treatment_goal: Prevent preterm birth, maintain pregnancy to viable gestational age
first_line_soc: Cervical cerclage (surgical; stitch to reinforce cervix). Vaginal progesterone (progesterone 200mg vaginal suppositories daily or 8% gel) for short cervix (<25mm) on ultrasound.
treatment_algorithm: Cerclage: History-indicated (prophylactic, at 12–14 weeks), ultrasound-indicated (short cervix), or emergency rescue cerclage. Progesterone: Vaginal progesterone 200mg/night or 17-hydroxyprogesterone caproate (17-OHPC; IM 250mg weekly) for women with prior preterm birth + short cervix. Arabin pessary (mechanical). Bed rest—limited evidence. Indomethacin for tocolysis in rescue situations. Antibiotics for subclinical infection.
us_prevalence: ~1% of pregnancies are affected by cervical insufficiency; ~30,000–40,000 cases/year in the US.
patients_needing_tx: ~35,000/year requiring cerclage and/or progesterone supplementation.
price: $3,000–$10,000 per pregnancy course.
registrational_endpoint: Rate of delivery <34 weeks' gestation vs. no cerclage; preterm birth prevention rate.
btd_threshold_vs_soc: Delivery <34 weeks in ≤20% vs. ~35% with cerclage alone; or ≥50% relative reduction in PTB <34 weeks.

symptoms: Excessive amniotic fluid, large-for-dates fundal height, discomfort, dyspnea
treatment_goal: Reduce fluid to relieve symptoms, identify fetal anomaly, prevent preterm labor
first_line_soc: Indomethacin (NSAID; reduces fetal urine output by 30–50% via cyclooxygenase inhibition) 25mg q6–8h for severe symptomatic polyhydramnios, especially <32 weeks.
treatment_algorithm: Mild-moderate: Observation with serial AFI measurement; treat underlying cause (gestational diabetes → insulin/metformin). Severe/symptomatic: Indomethacin 25mg PO q6h (COX-1/2 inhibitor; reduces fetal urine production; limited to <32 weeks due to ductus arteriosus risk). Serial amnioreduction (amniocentesis for fluid drainage). Sulindac (alternative NSAID). Monitor for ductus closure.
us_prevalence: ~1–3% of pregnancies; ~40,000–120,000 cases/year in the US. Most are mild and idiopathic.
patients_needing_tx: ~30,000–50,000 with symptomatic polyhydramnios requiring pharmacologic treatment.
price: $2,000–$8,000 per pregnancy course.
registrational_endpoint: AFI reduction to normal range (<24cm deepest pocket <8cm) at 1 week; symptom relief score; preterm labor rate.
btd_threshold_vs_soc: AFI normalization in ≥70% at 1 week vs. ~50% with indomethacin 25mg q6h.

symptoms: Painless bright red vaginal bleeding in third trimester, soft non-tender uterus
treatment_goal: Prevent hemorrhage, achieve fetal lung maturity before delivery, plan cesarean
first_line_soc: Hospitalization, pelvic rest, and antenatal corticosteroids (betamethasone 12mg IM × 2 doses for lung maturity if <34 weeks) for preterm. Planned cesarean delivery at 36–37 weeks. Tocolytics for concurrent preterm labor.
treatment_algorithm: Expectant management: Pelvic rest, no intercourse, hospitalization for significant bleeding. Corticosteroids: Betamethasone (Celestone) 12mg IM q24h × 2 doses (or dexamethasone) for fetal lung maturity at 24–34 weeks. Tocolysis (if preterm contractions): Nifedipine (CCB; first-line), indomethacin (<32 weeks), or magnesium sulfate. Anemia management: IV iron or blood transfusion. Placenta accreta spectrum (often coexists): plan for complex delivery.
us_prevalence: ~0.5% of pregnancies; ~20,000–25,000 cases/year in the US. Incidence rising with increasing C-section rates.
patients_needing_tx: ~20,000/year requiring pharmacologic management (steroids, tocolytics).
price: $2,000–$8,000 per pregnancy.
registrational_endpoint: Gestational age at delivery; composite neonatal outcome (RDS, IVH, NEC, sepsis at 28 days); maternal blood transfusion requirement.
btd_threshold_vs_soc: Delivery ≥36 weeks in ≥75% of managed patients vs. ~60% with current expectant management; or ≥30% reduction in blood transfusion rate.

symptoms: Fetal death ≥20 weeks gestation; absent fetal movement, loss of fetal heart tones
treatment_goal: Identify cause, safely manage delivery, prevent recurrence in future pregnancies
first_line_soc: Labor induction with misoprostol (prostaglandin E1 analog; cervical ripening + uterotonic) or mifepristone + misoprostol combination for cervical preparation and delivery induction.
treatment_algorithm: Delivery induction: Mifepristone 200mg PO × 24–48h pre-treatment (anti-progestogen; sensitizes uterus) + misoprostol 400mcg vaginal/sublingual q3–6h (prostaglandin E1; most effective combination). Or misoprostol alone: higher doses for second trimester. Analgesia: Epidural. Grief support + psychological support. Recurrence prevention: Next pregnancy—LDA for previous preeclampsia-related; low-dose heparin for thrombophilia; progesterone for cervical insufficiency; optimize comorbidities (diabetes, thyroid).
us_prevalence: ~1 stillbirth per 175 births in the US; ~24,000 stillbirths/year (CDC NCHS 2024).
patients_needing_tx: ~24,000 cases/year requiring delivery induction + post-delivery care. Prevention addressable: ~100,000 high-risk pregnancies.
price: $1,000–$5,000 per delivery management.
registrational_endpoint: Successful induction-to-delivery within 24 hours; maternal complications (uterine rupture, retained products, sepsis) at 48 hours.
btd_threshold_vs_soc: Delivery within 24h in ≥90% vs. ~75–85% with misoprostol alone; or ≥30% reduction in maternal complications.

symptoms: Intensely itchy urticarial papules and plaques in striae, last trimester, primigravida
treatment_goal: Relieve pruritus, control rash, reassure (resolves post-partum)
first_line_soc: Topical corticosteroids (clobetasol 0.05% or triamcinolone; potent anti-inflammatory) + oral antihistamines (chlorpheniramine, loratadine) for severe itch.
treatment_algorithm: Mild: Emollients + topical low-potency steroids (hydrocortisone 1%). Moderate: Topical potent steroids (clobetasol 0.05%) BID. Severe: Oral prednisolone 20–40mg/day tapering; systemic antihistamines (chlorpheniramine, promethazine). Self-limiting—resolves within days-weeks post-partum. No systemic immunosuppressants typically needed.
us_prevalence: ~1 in 160–300 pregnancies; ~15,000–25,000 cases/year in the US. Most common pregnancy dermatosis.
patients_needing_tx: ~20,000/year requiring pharmacologic symptom management.
price: $500–$3,000 per pregnancy course (novel topical antipruritic).
registrational_endpoint: Pruritus VAS score reduction ≥50% at 7 days; rash extent/severity score (EASI-equivalent) at 7 days.
btd_threshold_vs_soc: ≥70% pruritus responder rate at 7 days vs. ~50% with topical clobetasol alone.

symptoms: Painless vaginal bleeding at membrane rupture; fetal heart rate decelerations, fetal distress
treatment_goal: Diagnose prenatally, allow planned cesarean before rupture, prevent fetal death
first_line_soc: Hospitalization at 30–32 weeks + planned cesarean at 34–35 weeks (ACOG). Antenatal corticosteroids (betamethasone) for fetal lung maturity at <34 weeks.
treatment_algorithm: Prenatal diagnosis (transvaginal Doppler ultrasound): Hospitalize at 30–32 weeks for continuous fetal monitoring. Antenatal corticosteroids: Betamethasone 12mg IM × 2. Planned cesarean at 34–35 weeks (before labor begins). No specific pharmacologic agent to treat the vascular anomaly. Emergency cesarean for any bleeding or labor onset.
us_prevalence: ~1 in 2,000–5,000 pregnancies; ~800–2,000 cases/year in the US. High fetal mortality (>95%) if undiagnosed, <3% if diagnosed prenatally.
patients_needing_tx: ~1,500 cases/year requiring antenatal corticosteroids + planned cesarean.
price: $2,000–$8,000 per pregnancy.
registrational_endpoint: Intact fetal survival at delivery; gestational age at delivery; fetal hemoglobin/blood count at birth.
btd_threshold_vs_soc: Intact fetal survival ≥98% vs. ~95% with current planned cesarean + monitoring protocol.

symptoms: Intensely pruritic blistering urticarial rash in pregnancy, periumbilical onset
treatment_goal: Control pruritus and blister formation, prevent maternal-fetal complications
first_line_soc: Systemic corticosteroids: prednisolone 0.5mg/kg/day (initially 40–60mg/day) tapering slowly. Topical potent corticosteroids for mild cases.
treatment_algorithm: Mild-moderate: Topical clobetasol 0.05% + oral antihistamines (chlorpheniramine, loratadine). Moderate-severe: Oral prednisolone (40–60mg/day → taper with disease activity). Severe/refractory: IVIG (IV immunoglobulin) 2g/kg/cycle—used in pregnancy; rituximab (anti-CD20) and plasmapheresis in very severe cases. Post-partum: Dapsone or colchicine for maintenance. Anti-BP180 antibody monitoring.
us_prevalence: ~1 in 50,000–60,000 pregnancies; ~60–80 cases/year in the US. Extremely rare autoimmune blistering dermatosis.
patients_needing_tx: ~60–80 cases/year requiring systemic corticosteroid management.
price: $20,000–$80,000 per pregnancy course (novel anti-BP180 autoantibody-targeted biologic).
registrational_endpoint: Complete blister clearance (blister count = 0) at week 4; pruritus VAS normalization; anti-BP180 IgG titer reduction.
btd_threshold_vs_soc: Complete blister clearance in ≥80% at week 4 vs. ~60% with prednisolone monotherapy.

disease: Social and Illicit Drugs During Pregnancy
symptoms: Fetal growth restriction, preterm birth, NAS, teratogenicity from substance exposure
treatment_goal: Achieve sobriety, protect fetal development, prevent neonatal abstinence syndrome
first_line_soc: Opioid use disorder in pregnancy: Methadone (opioid agonist; OBOT/OTP) or buprenorphine (partial mu-opioid agonist; Subutex—preferred in pregnancy; FDA-approved) as medication-assisted treatment (MAT).
treatment_algorithm: Opioid Use Disorder: Buprenorphine (Subutex 4–24mg/day; preferred over methadone in pregnancy; reduces NAS severity and duration) or methadone (OBOT; 40–100+ mg/day). Alcohol: Thiamine, folic acid; no pharmacotherapy specifically for alcohol cessation in pregnancy (disulfiram, naltrexone avoided; benzodiazepines for severe withdrawal only). Cocaine/amphetamine: Behavioral only; no approved pharmacotherapy. Nicotine: NRT (nicotine patch acceptable); bupropion controversial; varenicline avoided. Cannabis: Cessation counseling only.
us_prevalence: ~5–10% of pregnancies involve substance use; OUD in pregnancy: ~7–8/1,000 deliveries (~28,000–32,000/year). Nicotine use in pregnancy: ~7% (~280,000/year, CDC).
patients_needing_tx: ~100,000–150,000 pregnant women per year require pharmacologic treatment for substance use.
price: $5,000–$15,000 per pregnancy course (improved buprenorphine formulation or novel NAS-reducing agent).
registrational_endpoint: NAS incidence and severity (Finnegan score); neonatal outcomes (birthweight, gestational age at delivery); maternal retention in MAT at 6 months.
btd_threshold_vs_soc: ≥40% reduction in NAS incidence or severity vs. methadone; or superior maternal retention in treatment at 6 months vs. standard buprenorphine formulation.

symptoms: Not a disease; represents risk of fetal teratogenicity, toxicity from maternal drug use
treatment_goal: Use safest effective medications; balance maternal disease control with fetal safety
first_line_soc: No pharmacologic SoC; refers to prescribing principles. FDA Pregnancy and Lactation Labeling Rule (PLLR) guides risk-benefit discussion for each drug.
treatment_algorithm: Framework: Avoid Pregnancy Category X drugs (isotretinoin, warfarin, thalidomide, methotrexate). Safe drugs: Penicillins, cephalosporins, acetaminophen, proton pump inhibitors (Category B), albuterol, insulin, low-dose aspirin. Avoid: NSAIDs (≥20 weeks), tetracyclines, fluoroquinolones, ACE inhibitors. Folic acid supplementation universal. Risk-benefit for essential medications (SSRIs, antiepileptics, biologics for IBD/RA). Registries: OTIS, MotherToBaby for data collection.
us_prevalence: ~90% of pregnant women take at least one medication during pregnancy; ~4 million pregnancies/year involve drug safety counseling.
patients_needing_tx: ~3–4 million pregnant women requiring drug safety guidance; ~500,000 on medications requiring teratogenicity monitoring.
price: N/A (counseling/risk assessment; teratology information service).

symptoms: Vaginal bleeding, cramping, passage of tissue before 20 weeks, hCG decline
treatment_goal: Complete uterine evacuation, prevent infection, support emotionally
first_line_soc: Expectant management for incomplete/inevitable abortion. Misoprostol (prostaglandin E1 analog; 800mcg vaginally) for medical management of missed or incomplete abortion—highly effective.
treatment_algorithm: Expectant: Complete spontaneous resolution in ~50–80% within 2 weeks. Medical: Misoprostol 800mcg vaginally (or sublingual/buccal; FDA-approved for gastric ulcers; off-label for SAB management but well-established) ± mifepristone 200mg PO 24–48h pre-treatment (improves success from ~67% to ~88%). Surgical: Suction aspiration (MVA) or D&C for failed medical management or patient preference. RhoGAM for Rh-negative. Antibiotics (doxycycline 100mg BID) post-surgical evacuation for prophylaxis.
us_prevalence: ~700,000–900,000 spontaneous abortions/year in the US (~15–20% of recognized pregnancies). Most before 12 weeks.
patients_needing_tx: ~500,000–700,000/year requiring medical or surgical management.
price: $500–$3,000 per course.
registrational_endpoint: Complete evacuation rate (no tissue on ultrasound) at day 8; surgical intervention rate within 30 days.
btd_threshold_vs_soc: Complete evacuation in ≥92% at day 8 vs. ~84–88% with mifepristone + misoprostol.

symptoms: Lower abdominal pain, vaginal bleeding, missed period, shoulder tip pain if ruptured
treatment_goal: Eliminate ectopic pregnancy, preserve fertility, prevent rupture and death
first_line_soc: Methotrexate (MTX; folate antagonist/anti-metabolite; IM single dose 50mg/m²) for unruptured ectopic pregnancy with hCG <5,000 mIU/mL and no cardiac activity. Surgical salpingostomy or salpingectomy for ruptured or unsuitable cases.
treatment_algorithm: Medical: MTX 50mg/m² IM single dose (success rate ~75–80% if hCG <5,000); or two-dose regimen for intermediate hCG. Monitor hCG decline. Surgical: Laparoscopic salpingectomy (preferred for rupture or failed MTX) or salpingostomy (fertility preservation). Expectant management for declining hCG <1,000. RhoGAM (anti-D IgG) for Rh-negative patients. 2024: Oral methotrexate studies ongoing; gefitinib (EGFR inhibitor) studied in preclinical models for non-tubal ectopics.
us_prevalence: ~100,000–120,000 ectopic pregnancies/year in the US; ~2% of all pregnancies. Leading cause of first-trimester maternal death (3–4% of pregnancy-related deaths).
patients_needing_tx: ~100,000/year requiring either MTX or surgical treatment.
price: $2,000–$8,000 per treatment course.
registrational_endpoint: Treatment success without surgical intervention at 4 weeks (hCG <5 mIU/mL); need for additional intervention rate.
btd_threshold_vs_soc: Treatment success in ≥90% without surgery vs. ~75–80% with single-dose MTX; or equivalent efficacy for hCG 5,000–15,000 vs. current MTX failure range.

symptoms: Fever, uterine tenderness, purulent discharge after abortion, sepsis signs
treatment_goal: Eradicate uterine infection, prevent septic shock, remove infected tissue
first_line_soc: Broad-spectrum IV antibiotics: gentamicin + clindamycin + ampicillin (triple regimen). Immediate uterine evacuation (suction curettage) under antibiotic cover.
treatment_algorithm: Antibiotics: IV gentamicin 5mg/kg/day + clindamycin 900mg IV q8h + ampicillin 2g IV q6h (ACOG triple therapy). Or cefoxitin + doxycycline. IV fluids, vasopressors for septic shock. Uterine evacuation (suction curettage) once antibiotics initiated. Intensive care for severe cases. Mifepristone + misoprostol for incomplete septic abortion management (cautious use). Blood cultures for resistant organisms. RhoGAM for Rh-negative patients.
us_prevalence: ~500–2,000 cases/year of septic abortion in the US. Relatively rare with legal abortion access; higher in restrictive settings globally.
patients_needing_tx: ~1,000–2,000/year requiring IV antibiotics + uterine evacuation.
price: $5,000–$15,000 per episode.
registrational_endpoint: Clinical cure (afebrile + negative blood culture + uterine tenderness resolution) at 72 hours; ICU admission rate; 30-day survival.
btd_threshold_vs_soc: Clinical cure ≥90% at 72h vs. ~75–80% with standard triple antibiotic + evacuation.

symptoms: Blood loss >500mL (vaginal) or >1,000mL (cesarean) within 24h of delivery; hypotension, tachycardia
treatment_goal: Achieve hemostasis, restore circulating volume, prevent maternal death
first_line_soc: Uterotonic agents: Oxytocin (Pitocin; 10–40 units IV/IM) first-line; carboprost (Hemabate; 15-methyl-PGF2α), misoprostol 600–1000mcg, methylergonovine (Methergine). Tranexamic acid (TXA; 1g IV within 3h of birth; WHO-recommended 2017).
treatment_algorithm: Step 1: Uterine massage + oxytocin 20–40 units IV infusion. Step 2: Additional uterotonics—carboprost (Hemabate) 0.25mg IM q15min × 8 doses (contraindicated in asthma); methylergonovine (Methergine) 0.2mg IM q2–4h; misoprostol 600–1000mcg rectal/sublingual. Step 3: Tranexamic acid (TXA) 1g IV (antifibrinolytic; WOMAN Trial 2017—reduces death from PPH when given within 3h). Step 4: Intrauterine balloon (Bakri balloon); uterine compression sutures (B-Lynch). Step 5: Radiologic embolization. Step 6: Hysterectomy. Recombinant factor VIIa for refractory coagulopathy.
us_prevalence: ~3–5% of all deliveries; ~120,000–200,000 cases/year in the US (including PPH as leading maternal morbidity cause—~35% of maternal deaths globally).
patients_needing_tx: ~160,000 PPH cases/year; novel uterotonic or hemostatic drug addressable: ~80,000 moderate-severe cases.
price: $2,000–$10,000 per episode.
registrational_endpoint: Blood loss reduction (mL) at 2h post-delivery; proportion requiring blood transfusion; proportion requiring additional uterotonic/surgical intervention.
btd_threshold_vs_soc: ≥30% relative reduction in blood transfusion requirement vs. oxytocin + TXA standard protocol.

symptoms: Maternal fever during labor, uterine tenderness, maternal/fetal tachycardia, foul-smelling amniotic fluid
treatment_goal: Eradicate infection, expedite delivery, prevent neonatal sepsis
first_line_soc: IV ampicillin 2g q6h + IV gentamicin 1.5mg/kg q8h (classic Gibbs regimen) during labor, followed by delivery. Clindamycin 900mg IV q8h added for cesarean delivery to cover anaerobes.
treatment_algorithm: Intrapartum: IV ampicillin + gentamicin (Gibbs regimen)—start immediately upon diagnosis; expedite delivery. For cesarean: Add clindamycin 900mg IV q8h. Broad-spectrum alternative: Cefoxitin 2g IV q8h or piperacillin-tazobactam. Neonatal sepsis workup + empiric antibiotics for neonate. Antipyretics (acetaminophen) for maternal fever. Post-delivery: Continue antibiotics × 24–48h.
us_prevalence: ~1–4% of all deliveries; ~40,000–160,000 cases/year in the US.
patients_needing_tx: ~100,000 cases/year requiring IV antibiotic management.
price: $3,000–$10,000 per episode.
registrational_endpoint: Maternal clinical cure rate (afebrile + resolution of uterine tenderness) at 48h postpartum; neonatal sepsis rate at 72h of life.
btd_threshold_vs_soc: Maternal cure ≥95% at 48h + neonatal sepsis rate <2% vs. ~90% cure and ~5% neonatal sepsis with standard ampicillin-gentamicin.

disease: Fetal Presentation, Position, and Lie (Including Breech Presentation)
symptoms: Breech or transverse fetal lie at term; increases cesarean risk
treatment_goal: Achieve cephalic presentation for vaginal delivery; external version or plan cesarean
first_line_soc: External cephalic version (ECV) at ≥36–37 weeks + tocolysis: terbutaline 0.25mg SC (beta-2 agonist; relaxes uterine muscle) or nifedipine (CCB) to facilitate fetal rotation.
treatment_algorithm: Tocolysis for ECV: Terbutaline 0.25mg SC (preferred in US) × 1 dose or nifedipine 10mg PO 30min pre-ECV; ritodrine (less used). ECV success rate: ~40–60% (decreases VTE risk of cesarean). Failed ECV: Planned cesarean at 39 weeks. Moxibustion at BL67 acupoint—complementary (limited evidence). Regional anesthesia (spinal/epidural) + ECV: higher success rates in RCTs.
us_prevalence: ~3–4% of singleton pregnancies have breech presentation at term; ~120,000–160,000 cases/year in the US.
patients_needing_tx: ~130,000 cases/year requiring tocolysis for ECV attempt.
price: $500–$2,000 per procedure (tocolytic + analgesic).
registrational_endpoint: ECV success rate (cephalic presentation confirmed on ultrasound ≥24h post-ECV); vaginal delivery rate.
btd_threshold_vs_soc: ECV success ≥70% vs. ~50% with terbutaline alone; or vaginal delivery rate ≥60% in ECV-attempted group.

symptoms: Placenta fails to separate at delivery; massive postpartum hemorrhage, uterine inversion risk
treatment_goal: Prevent life-threatening hemorrhage; planned surgical management
first_line_soc: Planned cesarean hysterectomy (with placenta in situ) in a tertiary center is the definitive management. Pre-operative: uterine artery embolization or balloon catheters for hemorrhage control. Uterotonic agents (oxytocin, carboprost) during surgery.
treatment_algorithm: Pre-operative: Bilateral uterine/internal iliac balloon catheters or IR embolization setup. Intraoperative uterotonics: Oxytocin 20–40 units in 1L NS after delivery + carboprost (Hemabate; 15-methyl-PGF2α) 0.25mg IM q15-90min ± methylergonovine (Methergine) for uterine atony. Tranexamic acid 1g IV (antifibrinolytic) at start of surgery. Massive transfusion protocol (1:1:1 PRBCs:FFP:Plt). Conservative options: Uterine-preserving surgery + methotrexate (for retained placenta accreta)—limited data. Mifepristone + MTX post-surgery for residual trophoblastic tissue.
us_prevalence: ~3/1,000 deliveries (rising with cesarean rates); ~12,000–15,000 cases/year in the US.
patients_needing_tx: ~13,000/year requiring pharmacologic + surgical management.
price: $5,000–$20,000 per episode.
registrational_endpoint: Estimated blood loss and transfusion requirement at surgery; hysterectomy rate; maternal ICU admission rate.
btd_threshold_vs_soc: ≥30% reduction in transfusion requirements + ≥20% reduction in hysterectomy rate vs. standard uterotonic protocol.

symptoms: Fetal shoulders impacted after head delivery; failure of normal delivery maneuvers
treatment_goal: Deliver fetus safely, prevent birth asphyxia and brachial plexus injury
first_line_soc: Obstetric maneuvers (McRoberts, suprapubic pressure) are primary management. No pharmacologic first-line SoC.
treatment_algorithm: Primary: McRoberts maneuver (hyperflexion of maternal thighs), suprapubic pressure. Advanced: Rubin II, Woods screw, Gaskin all-fours maneuver; posterior arm delivery; Zavanelli maneuver (head replacement) → emergent cesarean. Pharmacologic adjuncts: Uterine relaxants (nitroglycerin IV 100–200mcg or sublingual; tocolytic to allow fetal manipulation). No specific pharmacologic agent for shoulder dystocia itself. Neonatal resuscitation: epinephrine, O2 as needed.
us_prevalence: ~0.5–2% of vaginal deliveries; ~30,000–80,000 cases/year in the US.
patients_needing_tx: ~50,000 cases/year requiring obstetric maneuvers ± pharmacologic uterine relaxation.
price: $500–$2,000 per episode (uterine relaxant + neonatal resuscitation drugs).
registrational_endpoint: Head-to-body delivery interval; permanent brachial plexus injury rate; neonatal asphyxia rate (5-min Apgar <7).
btd_threshold_vs_soc: Head-to-body delivery ≤3 min in ≥90% vs. ~75% with standard maneuvers; or brachial plexus injury rate <1% vs. ~4–15% with current protocols.

symptoms: Sudden severe abdominal pain, fetal heart decelerations, loss of uterine contour, maternal shock
treatment_goal: Emergency delivery, repair uterine defect, prevent maternal and fetal death
first_line_soc: Emergency cesarean + uterine repair or hysterectomy. Pre-operatively: IV fluids, vasopressors (norepinephrine), blood transfusion. No pharmacologic agent prevents rupture once diagnosed.
treatment_algorithm: Emergency: IV fluid bolus + blood transfusion; vasopressors (norepinephrine, dopamine) for shock. Immediate crash cesarean. Surgical: Primary repair if feasible; hysterectomy for extensive disruption. Post-op: broad-spectrum antibiotics (cefazolin or piperacillin-tazobactam); oxytocin for uterine tone post-repair. Prevention (for TOLAC): Prostaglandin avoidance for cervical ripening in prior cesarean; misoprostol contraindicated in prior classical scar.
us_prevalence: ~1–7 per 10,000 deliveries; ~400–2,800 cases/year in the US. Higher in TOLAC patients (~0.5–0.9% of VBAC attempts).
patients_needing_tx: ~1,500 cases/year requiring emergency resuscitation + surgical management.
price: $5,000–$20,000 per episode.
registrational_endpoint: Maternal survival to discharge; neonatal intact neurological survival; blood transfusion requirement.
btd_threshold_vs_soc: Maternal survival ≥98% + neonatal intact survival ≥80% vs. current ~95% maternal survival with emergency cesarean.

symptoms: Cord visible or palpable through cervix after membrane rupture; fetal heart rate decelerations
treatment_goal: Relieve cord compression urgently, deliver fetus within minutes
first_line_soc: Immediate digital elevation of fetal presenting part off cord. Emergent cesarean delivery. Tocolysis with terbutaline 0.25mg SC (beta-2 agonist; relaxes uterine contractions) to reduce cord compression during preparation for surgery.
treatment_algorithm: Emergency: Manual digital elevation of presenting part (nurse holds fetal head off cord). Trendelenburg or knee-chest position. Bladder filling with 500mL NS to elevate fetus. Tocolysis: Terbutaline 0.25mg SC OR ritodrine IV (if available) to relax uterus. Emergency cesarean within 30 minutes ('crash section'). O2 by face mask for mother. No specific pharmacologic agent resolves prolapse.
us_prevalence: ~1–6 per 1,000 deliveries; ~4,000–24,000 cases/year in the US.
patients_needing_tx: ~10,000 cases/year requiring emergency tocolysis + delivery.
price: $500–$2,000 per episode.
registrational_endpoint: Decision-to-delivery interval; neonatal umbilical artery pH; 5-min Apgar score ≥7 rate.
btd_threshold_vs_soc: Decision-to-delivery ≤20 min + neonatal pH ≥7.2 in ≥90% vs. ~75% with standard emergency protocol.

symptoms: Uterus prolapses through cervix at/after delivery; severe pain, hemorrhage, shock
treatment_goal: Manually replace uterus, prevent hemorrhagic shock, prevent recurrence
first_line_soc: IV tocolysis (terbutaline 0.25mg SC or nitroglycerin 50–100mcg IV) to relax uterine muscle for manual replacement. Oxytocin 20–40U IV after replacement to maintain uterine tone.
treatment_algorithm: Uterine relaxation: IV terbutaline, MgSO4 4g IV, nitroglycerin 50–100mcg IV, or halogenated anesthetic (in OR). Manual Johnson maneuver (replacement). After replacement: Oxytocin + misoprostol + methylergonovine for uterine contraction. IV fluid resuscitation + blood products for hemorrhagic shock. Surgical replacement (Haultain procedure) if manual fails. Tranexamic acid 1g IV for associated hemorrhage.
us_prevalence: ~1 per 2,000–20,000 deliveries; ~200–2,000 cases/year in the US. Very rare.
patients_needing_tx: ~1,000 cases/year requiring pharmacologic management.
price: $1,000–$5,000 per episode.
registrational_endpoint: Successful manual replacement rate; time to replacement; hemodynamic stabilization at 1 hour.
btd_threshold_vs_soc: Successful replacement in ≥95% with novel rapid-acting uterine relaxant vs. ~85% with terbutaline/MgSO4.

symptoms: Sudden cardiovascular collapse, respiratory failure, DIC during labor or immediately postpartum
treatment_goal: Restore hemodynamics, prevent maternal death, treat coagulopathy
first_line_soc: Immediate resuscitation: IV fluids + vasopressors (norepinephrine; phenylephrine); mechanical ventilation. FFP, cryoprecipitate, platelets for DIC. Cardiopulmonary resuscitation if cardiac arrest.
treatment_algorithm: Cardiopulmonary resuscitation. Vasopressors: Norepinephrine IV for hypotension. Inotropes: Dobutamine/epinephrine for cardiogenic shock. Oxygenation: Endotracheal intubation + ARDS net ventilation. DIC management: FFP 4 units, cryoprecipitate 10 units, packed RBCs, platelets. Tranexamic acid (1g IV; antifibrinolytic) for hemorrhage. Atropine for bradycardia. ECMO in refractory cardiac arrest. No specific pharmacologic antidote for AFE. Ondansetron + epinephrine combination studied (animal models). Very high mortality (~20–40%).
us_prevalence: ~1–2 per 100,000 deliveries; ~40–80 cases/year in the US. Rare but catastrophic.
patients_needing_tx: ~60 cases/year requiring aggressive resuscitation.
price: $10,000–$50,000 per episode (novel antidote or complement inhibitor).
registrational_endpoint: Maternal survival to hospital discharge; organ failure-free days at 28 days; DIC resolution rate at 24 hours.
btd_threshold_vs_soc: Maternal survival ≥80% vs. ~60–70% with current supportive care; or DIC resolution in ≥90% at 24h.

disease: Analgesia and Anesthesia for Labor and Delivery
symptoms: Labor pain; inadequate analgesia affects maternal experience and outcomes
treatment_goal: Provide safe and effective pain relief during labor without fetal compromise
first_line_soc: Neuraxial analgesia (epidural; bupivacaine + fentanyl infusion) is the gold standard for labor pain. Spinal anesthesia (bupivacaine + opioid) for cesarean delivery.
treatment_algorithm: Epidural: Bupivacaine 0.0625–0.125% + fentanyl 2–3mcg/mL PCEA (patient-controlled epidural analgesia). Combined spinal-epidural (CSE): Intrathecal bupivacaine 2.5mg + fentanyl 15–25mcg initial bolus. For cesarean: Hyperbaric bupivacaine 0.75% 10–15mg IT + fentanyl/morphine. Systemic: IV fentanyl PCA; IV/IM remifentanil (short-acting opioid; PCIA); nitrous oxide (Entonox; 50% N₂O:O₂) inhaled. Epidural analgesia conversion to spinal for operative delivery. Post-cesarean: IT morphine 0.1–0.3mg + scheduled NSAIDs + acetaminophen.
us_prevalence: ~4 million deliveries/year in the US; ~73% receive neuraxial analgesia (NCHS data). ~1 million cesarean deliveries/year.
patients_needing_tx: ~4 million deliveries/year requiring pharmacologic analgesia.
price: $500–$3,000 per delivery (neuraxial drug costs; exclusive of procedure fees).
registrational_endpoint: Labor pain VAS score at 30 min post-epidural placement; patient satisfaction with pain control (COMFORTB/VAS ≥75%); motor block score (Bromage scale).
btd_threshold_vs_soc: Pain VAS ≤20/100 in ≥90% at 30 min with novel non-opioid neuraxial agent vs. ~85% with bupivacaine + fentanyl.

symptoms: Not a disease; management of spontaneous vaginal birth and immediate postpartum
treatment_goal: Facilitate safe delivery, prevent PPH, manage third stage, ensure neonatal wellbeing
first_line_soc: Active management of third stage of labor (AMTSL): oxytocin 10U IM immediately after delivery of anterior shoulder (uterotonic; reduces PPH by ~60%). Controlled cord traction. Uterine massage.
treatment_algorithm: AMTSL: Oxytocin 10U IM (most effective component). Misoprostol 600mcg sublingual (alternative where oxytocin unavailable—WHO). Controlled cord traction for placental delivery. Perineal repair: Local anesthetic (lidocaine 1% infiltration) for episiotomy/laceration repair. Routine episiotomy no longer recommended. Prophylactic antibiotics (ampicillin/cefazolin) for AMTSL in some protocols. Carbetocin (long-acting oxytocin analog; WHO-approved 2018, not FDA-approved) shows superiority in low-resource settings.
us_prevalence: ~2.7 million spontaneous vaginal deliveries/year in the US.
patients_needing_tx: ~2.7 million requiring AMTSL with oxytocin and analgesia.
price: $500–$2,000 per delivery (novel uterotonic).
registrational_endpoint: PPH rate (>500mL blood loss); need for additional uterotonic intervention; perineal healing at 6 weeks.
btd_threshold_vs_soc: PPH rate ≤2% vs. ~5% with oxytocin 10U IM alone; or ≥30% relative reduction in need for additional uterotonics.

symptoms: Not a disease; surgical delivery by uterine incision; associated with post-op pain, infection risk
treatment_goal: Safe delivery with optimal maternal and neonatal outcomes; minimize complications
first_line_soc: Spinal anesthesia with hyperbaric bupivacaine + intrathecal morphine. Pre-operative antibiotics (cefazolin 2g IV; prophylaxis). Post-op: multimodal analgesia (IT morphine + NSAIDs + acetaminophen + TAP block).
treatment_algorithm: Prophylactic antibiotics: Cefazolin 2g IV (or 3g if BMI >30) ≤60 min before incision + azithromycin 500mg IV for non-elective CD (AMOR trial 2016—reduces maternal infection). Anesthesia: Spinal or epidural. Uterotonics: Oxytocin infusion post-delivery. Post-op pain: IT morphine 0.1–0.15mg + scheduled ibuprofen 400–600mg q6h + acetaminophen 1g q6h + TAP block (bupivacaine 0.25%) or quadratus lumborum block. Enhanced Recovery After Cesarean (ERAC) protocol: carbohydrate loading pre-op, early ambulation, NSAID-dominant analgesia.
us_prevalence: ~32% of US births by cesarean; ~1.28 million cesarean deliveries/year.
patients_needing_tx: ~1.28 million/year requiring pre-op antibiotics, intraoperative uterotonics, and post-operative analgesia.
price: $1,000–$5,000 per delivery (antibiotic prophylaxis + enhanced recovery pharmacology package).
registrational_endpoint: Surgical site infection rate at 30 days; post-operative opioid consumption (MME) in 48h; PROMIS pain score at 24h.
btd_threshold_vs_soc: SSI rate ≤1% vs. ~3–5% with cefazolin alone; or opioid-free recovery in ≥80% at 48h vs. ~30% with standard ERAC.

symptoms: Not a disease; refers to supportive management of physiologic labor progression
treatment_goal: Support physiologic labor, provide safe pain management, prevent complications
first_line_soc: Neuraxial analgesia (epidural) for pain; oxytocin augmentation when labor dystocia identified (active management of labor). IV fluid (LR) maintenance.
treatment_algorithm: Hydration: IV LR 125 mL/h to prevent ketosis and support labor. Analgesia: Epidural (bupivacaine + fentanyl); IV/IM opioids for women declining epidural. Oxytocin augmentation (Pitocin 2–40 mIU/min): For arrest of active phase or protraction disorder. GBS prophylaxis: IV penicillin G 5M units q4h (or ampicillin, cefazolin, clindamycin) for GBS-positive patients. Antiemetics: Ondansetron for nausea in labor.
