Antacids (sodium citrate + ranitidine/omeprazole) as aspiration prophylaxis for operative deliveries.
us_prevalence: ~4 million deliveries/year in the US.
patients_needing_tx: ~4 million deliveries requiring pharmacologic support.
price: $200–$1,000 per delivery (labor support pharmacology).
registrational_endpoint: Duration of active phase; cesarean delivery rate; patient satisfaction with labor experience (NPS/VAS).
btd_threshold_vs_soc: ≥20% reduction in cesarean rate for labor dystocia vs. standard oxytocin augmentation.

symptoms: Contractions initiated artificially; indications include post-dates, PROM, preeclampsia, IUGR
treatment_goal: Safely achieve vaginal delivery by initiating effective uterine contractions
first_line_soc: Cervical ripening: Misoprostol 25mcg intravaginally q3–6h (prostaglandin E1; most effective for ripening + induction) or dinoprostone 0.5mg intracervical (Prepidil; PGE2) or dinoprostone vaginal insert (Cervidil 10mg). Labor induction: Oxytocin IV 1–6 mIU/min titrated.
treatment_algorithm: Unfavorable cervix (Bishop <6): Mechanical methods (balloon catheter/Foley; osmotic dilators) + pharmacologic ripening—misoprostol 25–50mcg intravaginal q3–6h (Cytotec; off-label but standard of care) or Mifepristone 200mg PO 24–48h before misoprostol (improving evidence 2024 for IOL; FDA-approved for mifepristone + misoprostol IOL in several countries). Cervidil (dinoprostone insert)—removable, safer. Oxytocin (Pitocin): Standard for labor augmentation after ripening; low-dose 0.5–2 mIU/min vs. high-dose 6 mIU/min protocols.
us_prevalence: ~25–30% of US births involve labor induction; ~1 million inductions/year.
patients_needing_tx: ~1 million inductions/year requiring cervical ripening + labor induction agents.
price: $300–$2,000 per induction course.
registrational_endpoint: Vaginal delivery rate within 24h of induction start; decision-to-delivery interval; uterine hyperstimulation rate.
btd_threshold_vs_soc: Vaginal delivery ≥75% within 24h vs. ~60% with standard misoprostol alone; or ≥30% reduction in cesarean rate with equivalent safety.

symptoms: Slow cervical dilation, failure to progress in active labor, prolonged pushing phase
treatment_goal: Accelerate labor progression, prevent maternal exhaustion, avoid unnecessary cesarean
first_line_soc: Oxytocin augmentation (IV Pitocin 2–6 mIU/min, titrating up to 40 mIU/min per protocol) is the primary pharmacologic intervention. Adequate epidural analgesia to reduce maternal distress.
treatment_algorithm: Oxytocin (Pitocin): Low-dose (1–2 mIU/min q15–40min) vs. high-dose (6 mIU/min q15–40min) protocols—high-dose reduces cesarean rate but increases tachysystole. Amniotomy (artificial rupture of membranes)—combined with oxytocin. Epidural analgesia: Reduces maternal distress and allows continued labor. Prostaglandins (misoprostol) for augmentation if cervix unfavorable (off-label). Hyoscine (Buscopan; antispasmodic)—used in some centers to accelerate cervical dilation (evidence limited).
us_prevalence: ~20–30% of labors require augmentation for slow progress; ~800,000–1.2 million cases/year in the US.
patients_needing_tx: ~1 million labors/year requiring pharmacologic augmentation.
price: $300–$2,000 per delivery.
registrational_endpoint: Rate of cervical dilation (cm/h) under augmentation; proportion achieving vaginal delivery within 6h of augmentation start; cesarean delivery rate.
btd_threshold_vs_soc: Cesarean rate ≤10% for labor arrest vs. ~25% with standard oxytocin alone; or ≥0.5 cm/h faster dilation.

disease: Fetal Monitoring During Labor and Delivery
symptoms: Not a disease; addresses fetal heart rate interpretation for detecting intrapartum distress
treatment_goal: Detect fetal distress early, intervene to prevent hypoxia and birth injury
first_line_soc: Continuous electronic fetal monitoring (EFM) is standard. Intrauterine resuscitation with IV fluid bolus + lateral decubitus positioning + oxygen. Terbutaline 0.25mg SC for Category III FHR patterns (uterine tachysystole from oxytocin).
treatment_algorithm: EFM interpretation per NICHD criteria. Non-reassuring FHR: Lateral decubitus position, IV fluid bolus (500mL NS), supplemental O₂. Uterine tachysystole: Stop oxytocin + terbutaline 0.25mg SC OR nitroglycerin 100–200mcg IV for emergent tocolysis. Prolonged Category III: Emergency delivery. Fetal scalp stimulation/pH testing. No novel pharmacologic agent widely validated for intrapartum fetal neuroprotection except MgSO4 for <32 weeks.
us_prevalence: ~4 million deliveries/year with EFM; universal in hospital births.
patients_needing_tx: ~100,000–200,000 deliveries/year require pharmacologic intervention for non-reassuring FHR (terbutaline, oxytocin cessation).
price: $1,000–$5,000 per episode (novel fetal neuroprotective agent).
registrational_endpoint: Neonatal umbilical artery pH ≥7.2; Apgar score ≥7 at 5 min; neonatal HIE rate (hypoxic-ischemic encephalopathy).
btd_threshold_vs_soc: HIE incidence ≤0.5/1,000 term births vs. ~1.5–3/1,000 with current monitoring + terbutaline protocols.

symptoms: Prolonged second stage or fetal distress requiring forceps or vacuum-assisted delivery
treatment_goal: Expedite delivery safely, prevent fetal asphyxia, avoid unnecessary cesarean
first_line_soc: Adequate neuraxial analgesia/anesthesia (epidural or pudendal block with lidocaine). No specific pharmacologic first-line—intervention is primarily mechanical (forceps or vacuum extractor).
treatment_algorithm: Analgesia: Adequate epidural or spinal anesthesia. Pudendal nerve block (lidocaine 1% 10mL each side) if epidural not placed. Uterotonic: Oxytocin infusion continued for adequate contractions during attempt. Tocolysis if needed for repositioning. Episiotomy: Lidocaine local infiltration. Neonatal resuscitation drugs on standby (epinephrine, naloxone, oxygen).
us_prevalence: ~3% of US deliveries use operative vaginal delivery (declining from ~10% two decades ago); ~90,000–120,000 cases/year.
patients_needing_tx: ~100,000/year requiring analgesia + oxytocin for operative vaginal delivery.
price: $500–$2,000 per delivery.
registrational_endpoint: Successful vaginal delivery rate without escalation to cesarean; neonatal scalp injury rate; maternal perineal trauma rate.
btd_threshold_vs_soc: Successful operative delivery ≥85% vs. ~80% with current instruments; or neonatal cephalohematoma rate ≤5% vs. ~12% with vacuum.

symptoms: Not a disease; encompasses physical and psychological recovery after delivery
treatment_goal: Ensure maternal recovery, support breastfeeding, screen for postpartum complications
first_line_soc: NSAIDs (ibuprofen) for perineal/uterine pain. Oxytocin or methylergonovine for uterine involution. Iron supplementation for postpartum anemia. Contraception counseling at 6 weeks.
treatment_algorithm: Pain management: NSAIDs (ibuprofen 400–600mg q6–8h preferred over opioids), acetaminophen 1g q6h, witch hazel pads for perineal pain. Uterine involution: Methylergonovine (Methergine) 0.2mg q6–8h × 24–48h for subinvolution/atony. Postpartum anemia: IV iron (ferric carboxymaltose; Ferinject—IV iron preferred if Hb <8g/dL) or oral ferrous sulfate. Constipation: Docusate sodium + senna. VTE prophylaxis: LMWH for high-risk post-cesarean. Breastfeeding: Domperidone or metoclopramide (galactagogue; limited evidence). Depression screening + SSRIs for PPD (see below).
us_prevalence: ~4 million deliveries/year in the US; all require postpartum care.
patients_needing_tx: ~4 million postpartum women require pharmacologic management at some level.
price: $500–$3,000 per postpartum course.
registrational_endpoint: Postpartum hemoglobin normalization at 6 weeks; perineal pain VAS at 7 days; breastfeeding rates at 6 weeks.
btd_threshold_vs_soc: Hemoglobin normalization ≥80% at 6 weeks vs. ~60–70% with oral iron; or postpartum opioid-free in ≥90% vs. ~60–70% currently.

symptoms: Breast pain, warmth, redness, fever during breastfeeding; flu-like symptoms
treatment_goal: Eradicate infection, preserve breastfeeding, prevent abscess formation
first_line_soc: Dicloxacillin 500mg QID × 10–14 days (anti-staphylococcal penicillin; covers MSSA); or cephalexin 500mg QID. Continue breastfeeding. Warm compresses + massage.
treatment_algorithm: Mild-moderate: Dicloxacillin 500mg QID × 10–14 days or cephalexin 500mg QID. MRSA risk factors: TMP-SMX DS BID or clindamycin 300mg QID × 10–14 days. Penicillin allergy: Clindamycin or azithromycin (limited S. aureus coverage). Continue breastfeeding + regular emptying. Abscess formation: Ultrasound-guided aspiration + continued antibiotics. Antifungal (fluconazole) for concurrent Candida nipple infection. Analgesics: Ibuprofen + acetaminophen.
us_prevalence: ~10–20% of breastfeeding women develop mastitis; ~400,000–800,000 cases/year in the US.
patients_needing_tx: ~600,000/year requiring antibiotic + supportive treatment.
price: $500–$2,000 per course.
registrational_endpoint: Clinical cure (afebrile + no breast erythema/tenderness) at day 10; abscess formation rate; breastfeeding continuation at 4 weeks.
btd_threshold_vs_soc: Clinical cure ≥95% at day 10 with preserved breastfeeding in ≥80% vs. ~85% cure + ~60–70% breastfeeding continuation with dicloxacillin.

symptoms: Depressed mood, anxiety, anhedonia, fatigue, difficulty bonding with infant after delivery
treatment_goal: Achieve remission, restore maternal functioning, support infant bonding
first_line_soc: SSRIs (sertraline [Zoloft] 50–200mg; paroxetine; escitalopram)—first-line pharmacologic for PPD. Therapy (CBT, IPT) for mild-moderate. Brexanolone (Zulresso; IV neurosteroid/GABA-A modulator; FDA-approved 2019) for severe/rapid-response need.
treatment_algorithm: Mild-moderate: Psychotherapy (CBT, IPT) ± SSRI (sertraline 50–200mg/day—safest in breastfeeding; escitalopram, paroxetine). SNRIs: venlafaxine, duloxetine. Severe: Brexanolone (Zulresso) 60-hour IV infusion (FDA-approved 2019; ~70% response rate; administered in certified healthcare setting; ~$34,000/course). Zuranolone (Zurzuvae; oral neuroactive steroid/GABA-A PAM; FDA-approved August 2023 for PPD—first oral treatment)—50mg/day × 14 days; breakthrough therapy designation. ECT for severe/refractory. 2024: Zuranolone widely adopted as preferred oral agent for PPD.
us_prevalence: ~10–15% of postpartum women; ~400,000–600,000 cases/year in the US (CDC 2024). Underdiagnosed: ~50% not identified.
patients_needing_tx: ~400,000–500,000 cases/year requiring pharmacologic and/or psychotherapy intervention.
price: $500–$2,000 per 14-day course (zuranolone); $34,000 for brexanolone infusion.
registrational_endpoint: HAM-D-17 score change from baseline at Day 15 (end of treatment); response (≥50% HAM-D reduction) and remission (HAM-D ≤7) rates.
btd_threshold_vs_soc: Remission rate ≥45% at Day 15 vs. ~30% with SSRI; or ≥50% remission rate vs. ~40% with brexanolone at 30-day follow-up.

symptoms: Fever, uterine tenderness, purulent lochia, malaise within days of delivery
treatment_goal: Eradicate polymicrobial uterine infection, prevent sepsis and complications
first_line_soc: IV clindamycin 900mg q8h + gentamicin 5mg/kg/day (Gibbs regimen; gold standard) until afebrile × 48h. Then no oral antibiotics needed for most patients.
treatment_algorithm: IV clindamycin + gentamicin (standard Gibbs regimen). Alternative: Ampicillin-sulbactam (Unasyn) 3g IV q6h; or piperacillin-tazobactam. Add ampicillin for enterococcal coverage in refractory cases. Step down to oral after 48h afebrile (amoxicillin-clavulanate). Anaerobic coverage critical (above regimens sufficient). Evaluate for retained products if no improvement; manual/suction extraction. Blood cultures for severe sepsis.
us_prevalence: ~1–3% vaginal deliveries; ~5–10% cesarean deliveries (despite prophylaxis); ~100,000–200,000 cases/year in the US.
patients_needing_tx: ~150,000/year requiring IV antibiotic management.
price: $2,000–$8,000 per episode.
registrational_endpoint: Clinical cure (afebrile + uterine tenderness resolution + normal WBC) at 72h; 30-day re-hospitalization rate.
btd_threshold_vs_soc: Clinical cure ≥95% at 72h vs. ~85–90% with clindamycin + gentamicin standard regimen.

symptoms: Flank pain, fever, costovertebral angle tenderness, dysuria in postpartum period
treatment_goal: Eradicate upper urinary tract infection, prevent urosepsis
first_line_soc: IV ceftriaxone 1–2g/day (third-generation cephalosporin) or IV ampicillin + gentamicin for culture-directed therapy. Oral step-down after 24–48h afebrile.
treatment_algorithm: IV ceftriaxone 1g q24h (first-line for non-resistant organisms); add ampicillin for enterococcal coverage or gentamicin for severe cases. Fluoroquinolones (ciprofloxacin) avoided in breastfeeding unless culture-directed. Oral step-down: Cephalexin 500mg QID × 14 days or amoxicillin-clavulanate. Hydration (IV + oral). Analgesics: NSAIDs or acetaminophen. Postpartum vesico-ureteral reflux (if recurrent): Prophylactic nitrofurantoin or TMP-SMX.
us_prevalence: ~1–2% of deliveries; ~40,000–80,000 cases/year in the US.
patients_needing_tx: ~60,000/year requiring IV + oral antibiotic management.
registrational_endpoint: Microbiological cure (negative urine culture at day 7); clinical cure (afebrile + symptom resolution) at day 5.
btd_threshold_vs_soc: Microbiological cure ≥95% at day 7 vs. ~85–90% with ceftriaxone; or shorter IV course duration with equivalent outcomes.

symptoms: Wheezing, dyspnea, chest tightness, cough; poorly controlled asthma worsens with pregnancy
treatment_goal: Maintain lung function, prevent hypoxia, control asthma to optimize fetal outcomes
first_line_soc: Inhaled short-acting beta-2 agonist (SABA: albuterol MDI; safe in pregnancy) for rescue. Inhaled corticosteroid (ICS: budesonide [Pulmicort]; FDA Pregnancy Category B; preferred ICS in pregnancy) for persistent asthma.
treatment_algorithm: Step-wise NAEPP approach: Step 1 (mild intermittent): Albuterol PRN. Step 2 (mild persistent): Low-dose budesonide + albuterol PRN. Step 3 (moderate persistent): Medium-dose budesonide + LABA (salmeterol; formoterol—generally safe in pregnancy). Step 4 (severe): High-dose ICS + LABA ± oral corticosteroids. Biologics: Omalizumab (anti-IgE; Xolair) continued if started pre-pregnancy; dupilumab under study. Avoid NSAIDs in aspirin-sensitive asthmatics. Oral prednisone for exacerbations. Safety: Budesonide most studied; all SABA safe.
us_prevalence: ~8–13% of US pregnancies (~320,000–520,000/year) complicated by asthma (ACOG/NIH data).
patients_needing_tx: ~400,000 pregnant asthmatics/year requiring pharmacologic management.
price: $5,000–$20,000/year (novel biologic with pregnancy safety data).
registrational_endpoint: Asthma control questionnaire (ACQ) score at 12 weeks; exacerbation rate during pregnancy; FEV1 stability; preterm birth rate.
btd_threshold_vs_soc: Well-controlled asthma (ACQ ≤0.75) in ≥70% at 12 weeks vs. ~55% with ICS alone; or ≥30% reduction in exacerbation rate vs. standard step-up therapy.

symptoms: Flare of underlying autoimmune disease during pregnancy; fetal complications vary by condition
treatment_goal: Control autoimmune disease safely without teratogenic agents; prevent maternal and fetal complications
first_line_soc: Depends on specific condition. SLE: Hydroxychloroquine (Plaquenil; antimalarial; safe in pregnancy—reduces SLE flares) continued throughout. RA: TNF inhibitors (certolizumab pegol [Cimzia]—no Fc region, minimal placental transfer; preferred in pregnancy) or hydroxychloroquine.
treatment_algorithm: SLE: Hydroxychloroquine 200–400mg/day (continued; reduces flares + neonatal lupus NL-CHB risk). Prednisone for flares (use minimized). Azathioprine safe if immunosuppression needed. No mycophenolate (teratogenic). RA: Certolizumab pegol (Cimzia; anti-TNF; no placental transfer in 3rd trimester—most evidence); hydroxychloroquine. Avoid methotrexate (teratogenic). ITP: IVIG + prednisone. IBD: Biologics safe (vedolizumab; certolizumab). Neonatal monitoring for maternal anti-SSA/SSB (Sjögren's/SLE) antibody-mediated congenital heart block (CHB): prophylactic dexamethasone controversial.
us_prevalence: ~1–3% of pregnancies complicated by autoimmune disease; ~40,000–120,000 cases/year (SLE ~50,000; RA ~30,000; IBD ~40,000 during pregnancy).
patients_needing_tx: ~100,000 pregnancies/year requiring disease-specific pharmacologic management.
price: $20,000–$60,000/year (novel biologic with pregnancy safety registry).
registrational_endpoint: Disease activity score (SLE DAI, DAS28) stability during pregnancy; flare rate; neonatal CHD rate for SLE pregnancies.
btd_threshold_vs_soc: Disease flare rate ≤20% during pregnancy vs. ~35–45% without hydroxychloroquine; or neonatal CHB rate ≤1% in anti-SSA+ mothers.

symptoms: Elevated BP ≥140/90 in pregnancy; may be chronic HTN, gestational HTN, or preeclampsia
treatment_goal: Prevent stroke/organ damage, maintain fetal perfusion, prevent preeclampsia progression
first_line_soc: Oral antihypertensives: Labetalol (alpha-beta blocker) 100–400mg TID, or nifedipine ER 30–90mg/day (CCB), or methyldopa 250mg TID (central alpha-2 agonist). For severe BP ≥160/110: IV labetalol, oral nifedipine IR, or IV hydralazine.
treatment_algorithm: Mild-moderate (140–159/90–109): Oral labetalol, nifedipine ER, or methyldopa (methyldopa preferred for chronic HTN in 1st trimester). Severe (≥160/110): IV labetalol 20–80mg q20min; oral nifedipine IR 10–20mg; IV hydralazine 5–10mg q20min. Avoid: ACEi, ARBs, direct renin inhibitors (teratogenic). LDA prophylaxis for preeclampsia prevention (high-risk patients). MgSO4 for seizure prevention if severe features. 2023–2024: Lower treatment thresholds endorsed by ACOG (treat ≥140/90 consistently to reduce adverse outcomes).
us_prevalence: ~10–15% of pregnancies complicated by hypertension (chronic + gestational + preeclampsia); ~400,000–600,000/year in the US.
patients_needing_tx: ~500,000 hypertensive pregnancies/year requiring antihypertensive pharmacologic management.
price: $3,000–$12,000 per pregnancy.
registrational_endpoint: Target BP achieved (systolic <140, diastolic <90) for ≥75% of readings during pregnancy; stroke/severe maternal morbidity rate; fetal growth restriction rate.
btd_threshold_vs_soc: BP control ≥90% of readings at target vs. ~65% with current oral antihypertensives; or ≥30% reduction in severe maternal hypertensive events.

symptoms: Dyspnea, palpitations, chest pain, reduced exercise tolerance, edema in pregnancy
treatment_goal: Optimize cardiac function, prevent decompensation, ensure safe delivery
first_line_soc: Depends on cardiac condition. Peripartum cardiomyopathy (PPCM): Bromocriptine (dopamine agonist; reduces prolactin-mediated cardiotoxicity; European guidelines 2022) + standard heart failure therapy (loop diuretics; beta-blockers; ACE after delivery). Congenital/valvular heart disease: disease-specific management.
treatment_algorithm: PPCM: Bromocriptine 2.5mg BID × 2–8 weeks (reduces prolactin → less 16kDa prolactin fragment toxicity; ESC recommendation); diuretics (furosemide—safe in pregnancy); beta-blockers (carvedilol, metoprolol after delivery); ACEi/ARB post-delivery; LMWH anticoagulation. Arrhythmias: Beta-blockers (metoprolol, atenolol limited), adenosine (SVT), digoxin (AFIB with RVR). Anticoagulation: LMWH for mechanical valves (warfarin teratogenic in 1st trimester). Preterm: Betamethasone for fetal lungs. Pulmonary arterial hypertension: Sildenafil, ambrisentan (prostanoid analogs)—continued if started pre-pregnancy (avoid bosentan—teratogenic).
us_prevalence: ~4% of pregnancies complicated by cardiac disease; ~160,000/year. PPCM: ~1,000–4,000 cases/year. Congenital HD increasing as adult survivors reproduce.
patients_needing_tx: ~160,000 cardiac-complicated pregnancies/year requiring pharmacologic management.
registrational_endpoint: EF normalization (LVEF ≥50%) at 6 months postpartum for PPCM; composite MACE (maternal death, HF hospitalization, arrhythmia) during pregnancy and 6 months postpartum.
btd_threshold_vs_soc: EF normalization ≥70% at 6 months vs. ~50% with standard HF therapy alone for PPCM; or ≥30% reduction in MACE with novel cardioprotective agent.

symptoms: Elevated blood glucose in pregnancy (GDM) or pre-existing DM; macrosomia, polyhydramnios, fetal complications
treatment_goal: Maintain euglycemia, prevent macrosomia, reduce perinatal complications
first_line_soc: Gestational diabetes: Medical nutrition therapy (MNT) + insulin (NPH + regular or detemir + insulin aspart; most evidence for safety). Metformin oral as alternative first-line per some guidelines.
treatment_algorithm: GDM: Step 1: MNT + exercise × 2 weeks. Step 2: Insulin therapy if targets not met—insulin NPH 0.1–0.2 U/kg at bedtime + regular insulin pre-meal (or insulin detemir + aspart—newer safer analogs). Metformin: ACOG 2018 allows as alternative to insulin for GDM; crosses placenta (long-term neonatal safety reassuring but studied). Glyburide: No longer recommended (excess macrosomia/hypoglycemia per JAMA 2015). Pre-gestational DM: Tight glucose control pre-conception + insulin optimization. Complications: NSAIDs avoided ≥20 weeks; manage HTN (labetalol, nifedipine). 2024: Continuous glucose monitoring (CGM) increasingly used in T1DM pregnancy.
us_prevalence: ~6–9% of all US pregnancies have GDM (~240,000–360,000/year); pre-gestational DM: ~1–2% (~40,000–80,000/year).
patients_needing_tx: ~300,000 GDM patients requiring pharmacologic insulin or metformin; ~60,000 pre-gestational DM.
price: $3,000–$15,000 per pregnancy (insulin analogues + monitoring).
registrational_endpoint: Mean fasting and post-prandial glucose within targets (fasting <95, 1h <140, 2h <120 mg/dL) at 34 weeks; macrosomia rate (BW >4000g); neonatal hypoglycemia rate.
btd_threshold_vs_soc: Macrosomia rate ≤8% vs. ~15–20% with standard insulin/MNT; or equivalent glucose control with superior maternal/fetal safety vs. insulin in GDM.

symptoms: Elevated creatinine, proteinuria, edema, hypertension; increased preterm birth and fetal growth restriction
treatment_goal: Preserve renal function, control BP, prevent preterm birth, optimize fetal outcomes
first_line_soc: BP control: Labetalol, nifedipine ER, or methyldopa (same as pregnancy HTN). Avoid ACEi/ARBs (nephroprotective but teratogenic). Folic acid supplementation. LMWH for heavy proteinuria/nephrotic syndrome.
treatment_algorithm: Antihypertensives: Labetalol, nifedipine ER, methyldopa. Proteinuria management: Avoid ACEi/ARBs (switch pre-pregnancy). Immunosuppression for autoimmune nephritis: Prednisolone, hydroxychloroquine, azathioprine (safe). Avoid mycophenolate (teratogenic). Folic acid 5mg/day (renal patients). Erythropoietin (EPO; darbepoetin alfa) for anemia of CKD in pregnancy. VTE prophylaxis: LMWH for nephrotic-range proteinuria. Dialysis: Intensified (36–40h/week) to reduce BUN and improve fetal outcomes.
us_prevalence: ~3% of pregnancies complicated by some degree of renal disease; ~120,000/year. CKD in pregnancy rising with advancing maternal age.
patients_needing_tx: ~100,000/year requiring pharmacologic BP control + supportive nephrology management.
registrational_endpoint: Serum creatinine change from prepregnancy baseline at 3 months postpartum; preterm birth rate; fetal growth restriction rate.
btd_threshold_vs_soc: Preterm birth rate ≤20% vs. ~30–50% in moderate-severe CKD; or renal function preservation (creatinine increase <25% from baseline) in ≥80% with novel nephroprotective agent.

symptoms: Fever, cough, dyspnea, hypoxia with higher severity risk; preterm birth, fetal distress in severe cases
treatment_goal: Treat maternal COVID-19 safely, prevent preterm birth and fetal hypoxia
first_line_soc: Mild-moderate: No antiviral treatment needed + monitoring. Moderate-severe (hospitalized, hypoxic): Remdesivir (IV antiviral; Veklury; generally used if O2 required) + dexamethasone 6mg/day × 10 days for hypoxic hospitalized COVID-19.
treatment_algorithm: Prevention: mRNA COVID vaccines (Pfizer/Moderna—safe and recommended in pregnancy; reduces severe disease + preterm birth). Mild outpatient COVID: Nirmatrelvir/ritonavir (Paxlovid)—FDA EUA; ritonavir interaction concerns in pregnancy but generally used when benefit outweighs risk. Moderate-severe: Remdesivir IV (Veklury) + dexamethasone 6mg/day. Convalescent plasma in select cases. Tocilizumab (anti-IL-6) for cytokine storm (with caution). Antithrombotic: LDA + prophylactic LMWH for COVID-associated coagulopathy in pregnancy.
us_prevalence: COVID-19 remains endemic; ~5–10% of pregnant women may test positive at some point in pregnancy. Severe disease: ~2–5% of positive cases in pregnancy.
patients_needing_tx: ~200,000–400,000 COVID-19 cases in pregnancy/year (estimated); ~10,000–20,000 requiring pharmacologic treatment beyond supportive care.
price: $500–$5,000 per course (antiviral).
registrational_endpoint: Progression to severe COVID-19 (hospitalization or O2 requirement) at day 28; preterm birth rate; neonatal outcomes.
btd_threshold_vs_soc: ≥85% reduction in progression to severe disease vs. ~70% with Paxlovid in non-pregnant adults; or demonstrated superior placental/fetal safety vs. current EUA agents.

symptoms: Pruritus, jaundice, elevated liver enzymes, nausea; varies by specific hepatic disorder
treatment_goal: Manage liver dysfunction safely, prevent preterm delivery and fetal demise
first_line_soc: Intrahepatic cholestasis of pregnancy (ICP): Ursodeoxycholic acid (UDCA; Actigall; bile acid; FDA Category B) 10–15mg/kg/day—reduces pruritus + improves fetal outcomes. AFLP (acute fatty liver of pregnancy): Delivery is definitive.
treatment_algorithm: ICP: UDCA 500–1000mg/day (reduces maternal pruritus ~55–60% better than placebo; bile acid-modulating effect); cholestyramine for itch if UDCA insufficient. Delivery at 36–37 weeks recommended. AFLP: Immediate delivery + intensive supportive care (fresh frozen plasma, glucose IVF). HELLP syndrome: Delivery + IV MgSO4 + antihypertensives. HBV in pregnancy: Tenofovir disoproxil fumarate (TDF) in 3rd trimester for high viral load (HBV DNA >200,000 IU/mL) to prevent vertical transmission + HBIG + HBV vaccine to neonate. HCV: DAA therapy delayed until postpartum.
us_prevalence: ICP: ~0.5–2% of pregnancies (~20,000–80,000/year); AFLP: ~1 per 10,000 (~400/year); HBV in pregnancy: ~25,000–30,000 HBsAg+ deliveries/year.
patients_needing_tx: ~80,000 hepatic-disorder-in-pregnancy cases/year requiring pharmacologic management.
price: $3,000–$15,000 per pregnancy.
registrational_endpoint: Pruritus VAS score reduction at 2 weeks (ICP); bile acid normalization; stillbirth rate (ICP); HBV neonatal transmission rate.
btd_threshold_vs_soc: Pruritus reduction ≥75% at 2 weeks vs. ~55% with UDCA; or HBV transmission rate ≤0.5% vs. ~5–10% without TDF + immunoprophylaxis.

symptoms: Hypothyroidism: fatigue, constipation, weight gain; Hyperthyroidism: palpitations, weight loss, anxiety
treatment_goal: Maintain euthyroid status; prevent adverse fetal effects from thyroid dysfunction
first_line_soc: Hypothyroidism: Levothyroxine (L-T4; thyroid hormone replacement)—dose increased by ~25–50% in pregnancy; titrate to TSH 0.1–2.5 mIU/L. Hyperthyroidism/Graves': Propylthiouracil (PTU; thionamide; preferred in 1st trimester) then methimazole (2nd/3rd trimesters).
treatment_algorithm: Hypothyroidism: Levothyroxine dose escalation (~30–50% increase early in pregnancy); monitor TSH q4–8 weeks; goal TSH 0.1–2.5 mIU/L (1st trimester) → <3 mIU/L (2nd/3rd). Hyperthyroidism: PTU 50–300mg/day (1st trimester; methimazole teratogenic in 1st trimester—PTU-hepatotoxicity monitored). Methimazole 5–30mg/day (2nd/3rd trimester switch after organogenesis). Propranolol for symptom control in first trimester. Radioactive iodine (RAI) absolutely contraindicated in pregnancy. Thyroid storm: Iodine (Lugol's) + PTU + propranolol + hydrocortisone. Fetal/neonatal thyroid monitoring.
us_prevalence: ~2–5% of pregnancies complicated by thyroid disease; ~80,000–200,000/year. Hypothyroidism: ~2–3%; hyperthyroidism: ~0.1–0.4%.
patients_needing_tx: ~150,000 pregnancies/year requiring thyroid pharmacologic management.
registrational_endpoint: Proportion with TSH within trimester-specific target range at each trimester; fetal/neonatal thyroid function normality; preterm birth rate.
btd_threshold_vs_soc: TSH within target in ≥90% throughout pregnancy vs. ~70–75% with standard levothyroxine titration.

symptoms: Acute appendicitis, cholecystitis, ovarian torsion, etc. occurring during pregnancy
treatment_goal: Safely perform surgery to treat acute condition without adverse fetal effects
first_line_soc: Antibiotic prophylaxis (cefazolin 2g IV; safe in pregnancy). Regional or general anesthesia (propofol, sevoflurane—safe; N₂O avoided >2nd trimester). Fetal monitoring pre/post-operatively. Laparoscopy preferred over open when feasible (SAGES/ACOG).
treatment_algorithm: Appendicitis: Non-operative antibiotics (amoxicillin-clavulanate) attempted in some uncomplicated cases; surgical appendectomy standard. Cholecystitis: Laparoscopic cholecystectomy (2nd trimester preferred). Ovarian torsion: Laparoscopic detorsion. General: Ondansetron for nausea; MgSO4 tocolysis peri-operatively for preterm risk. Analgesia: Acetaminophen 1st line; opioids short-term; NSAIDs avoided ≥20 weeks. Antenatal steroids if preterm delivery risk from surgery.
us_prevalence: ~0.5–2% of pregnancies require non-obstetric surgery; ~20,000–80,000 cases/year in the US.
patients_needing_tx: ~50,000 cases/year requiring perioperative pharmacologic management.
price: $2,000–$8,000 per surgical episode.
registrational_endpoint: Surgical site infection rate; preterm labor rate within 7 days of surgery; fetal loss rate; neonatal outcomes.
btd_threshold_vs_soc: Preterm labor rate ≤5% post-surgery vs. ~10–15% with standard peri-operative management.

symptoms: Depends on cancer type; pregnancy may delay diagnosis; treatment requires safety modifications
treatment_goal: Treat malignancy safely to optimize maternal survival while minimizing fetal harm
first_line_soc: Cervical cancer (most common GYN cancer in pregnancy): Surgery (radical hysterectomy or trachelectomy) in first trimester. Breast cancer: Surgery + chemotherapy (AC regimen after 1st trimester; doxorubicin + cyclophosphamide safe after 14 weeks). Avoid radiation/targeted therapies (trastuzumab) until postpartum.
treatment_algorithm: Principles: No systemic chemotherapy in first trimester (organogenesis). After 14 weeks: Most chemotherapy regimens tolerated. Breast cancer: AC (doxorubicin/cyclophosphamide) safe in 2nd/3rd trimester; taxanes studied. Cervical cancer: Neoadjuvant cisplatin chemotherapy to delay definitive surgery. Hematologic malignancies (leukemia, lymphoma): CHOP (cyclophosphamide, doxorubicin, vincristine, prednisone) or ABVD safe after 1st trimester. Avoid: Methotrexate, thalidomide, targeted agents (HER2 blockers, tyrosine kinase inhibitors), immunotherapy. Delivery timing optimization.
us_prevalence: ~1 per 1,000–2,000 pregnancies complicated by cancer; ~2,000–4,000 cancer cases in pregnancy/year in the US. Breast cancer most common (~40%).
patients_needing_tx: ~3,000 cases/year requiring systemic treatment during pregnancy.
price: $30,000–$100,000 per course (chemotherapy regimen).
registrational_endpoint: Maternal 2-year PFS/OS; neonatal congenital malformation rate; neurocognitive outcomes of children at 18 months.
btd_threshold_vs_soc: Equivalent maternal PFS vs. non-pregnant historical controls with ≤5% increase in adverse neonatal outcomes.

symptoms: Seizures, altered consciousness, anticonvulsant teratogenicity risk during pregnancy
treatment_goal: Maintain seizure control, minimize fetal exposure to teratogenic anticonvulsants
first_line_soc: Lamotrigine (Lamictal; sodium channel blocker; safest AED in pregnancy—lowest teratogenicity) + folic acid 5mg/day supplementation. Or levetiracetam (Keppra; SV2A binding; also low teratogenic risk).
treatment_algorithm: Safest AEDs in pregnancy: Lamotrigine (monitor levels—clearance increases significantly in pregnancy; dose escalation required); levetiracetam; oxcarbazepine. Avoid: Valproate (highest teratogenicity: NTDs 5–9%, cognitive impairment), phenytoin (fetal hydantoin syndrome), carbamazepine (NTDs). Folic acid 5mg/day (high-dose for women on AEDs). For status epilepticus in pregnancy: IV lorazepam (benzodiazepine) + IV levetiracetam or fosphenytoin. Neonatal vitamin K prophylaxis for enzyme-inducing AED exposure. Breastfeeding: Lamotrigine and levetiracetam compatible.
us_prevalence: ~0.3–0.5% of pregnancies complicated by epilepsy; ~12,000–20,000 pregnancies/year in the US.
patients_needing_tx: ~15,000 epileptic pregnancies/year requiring AED management.
price: $5,000–$20,000/year (novel AED with confirmed low teratogenicity).
registrational_endpoint: Seizure-free rate during pregnancy; major congenital malformation rate; lamotrigine serum levels within therapeutic range at each trimester.
btd_threshold_vs_soc: Seizure-free rate ≥85% throughout pregnancy vs. ~70–75% with lamotrigine alone; or MCM rate <1% vs. ~2–3% with lamotrigine.

symptoms: Not a disease; cell-free DNA (cfDNA) screening for chromosomal abnormalities
treatment_goal: Identify pregnancies at high risk for chromosomal conditions; guide invasive testing decisions
first_line_soc: Cell-free DNA (cfDNA/NIPT): Analyzes fetal DNA fragments in maternal blood (Panorama, MaterniT21, Harmony)—99% sensitivity for T21, T18, T13. Combined first-trimester screen (NT + serum markers).
treatment_algorithm: cfDNA NIPT: Positive predictive value varies by prevalence; gold standard for chromosome 21, 18, 13, sex chromosome aneuploidies. First-trimester screen: NT + PAPP-A + free β-hCG at 11–13 weeks. Second-trimester quad screen: AFP, hCG, estriol, inhibin A. Diagnostic follow-up: Amniocentesis or CVS for NIPT positives. No pharmacologic intervention—all diagnostic/counseling.
us_prevalence: ~4 million pregnancies/year; ~50–60% use NIPT. ~7,000 Down syndrome pregnancies/year identified.
patients_needing_tx: N/A for pharmacologic treatment.

disease: Preconception or Prenatal Carrier Testing of Parents
symptoms: Not a disease; genetic testing to identify carrier status for recessive/X-linked disorders
treatment_goal: Identify at-risk couples before or during pregnancy; enable informed reproductive decisions
first_line_soc: Expanded carrier screening (ECS): Single-gene (ACOG Tier 1: CF, SMA, fragile X) or expanded panel (Natera Horizon, Invitae—240+ conditions). No pharmacologic intervention.
treatment_algorithm: Pre-conception or 1st trimester: ECS panel (ACOG recommends pan-ethnic screen for CF, SMA, hemoglobinopathies). If both parents carriers: Pre-implantation genetic testing (PGT-M) or prenatal diagnosis (CVS/amnio). SMA carriers (both): Consider PGT-M or diagnostic. For at-risk pregnancies: Fetal therapy in select conditions (e.g., dexamethasone for CAH). No pharmacologic SoC for carrier testing process.
us_prevalence: ~4 million pregnancies/year; ~25% use ECS preconception; ~40% during pregnancy.
patients_needing_tx: N/A for pharmacologic intervention.

symptoms: Not a disease; counseling service for chromosomal/genetic risk assessment
treatment_goal: Provide risk-based education; support informed decision-making for at-risk families
first_line_soc: None pharmacologic. Genetics counselor/specialist consultation.
treatment_algorithm: Non-pharmacologic: Board-certified genetic counselor consultation. Risk assessment: Family history, advanced maternal age, prior affected child, carrier status, teratogen exposure, abnormal cfDNA/prenatal screen. Pharmacologic context: Folic acid 5mg/day for high-risk NTD family history; dexamethasone 0.2mg/kg/day (off-label) for CAH-risk pregnancies (prevents virilization in 46,XX fetus).
us_prevalence: ~1 million women/year receive formal genetic counseling in pregnancy.
patients_needing_tx: N/A for pharmacologic SoC.

symptoms: Not a disease; procedures (amniocentesis, CVS) to obtain fetal genetic material
treatment_goal: Obtain definitive fetal chromosomal/genetic diagnosis to guide pregnancy management
first_line_soc: Amniocentesis (≥15 weeks) or chorionic villus sampling (CVS; 10–13 weeks) for karyotyping/chromosomal microarray. Rh(D) immune globulin (RhoGAM) for Rh-negative women post-procedure.
treatment_algorithm: CVS (10–13 weeks): Chorionic villus tissue sampling → karyotype, CMA, FISH, PCR. Amniocentesis (≥15 weeks): Amniotic fluid → same testing. Post-procedure: RhoGAM 300mcg IM for Rh-negative patients. Sedation: Topical lidocaine gel for skin anesthesia; IV lorazepam for anxious patients (rarely needed). Antibiotics not routinely indicated.
us_prevalence: ~200,000 invasive prenatal procedures/year in the US (declining with cfDNA uptake).
patients_needing_tx: ~200,000/year requiring procedural support (RhoGAM, local anesthetics).
price: N/A (procedural pharmacologic support ~$100–500 per procedure).

disease: Nausea and Vomiting During Early Pregnancy
symptoms: Morning sickness, nausea, vomiting in first trimester; affects 70–80% of pregnant women
treatment_goal: Relieve nausea and vomiting, maintain hydration and nutrition
first_line_soc: Doxylamine + pyridoxine (Bonjesta/Diclegis; FDA Category A equivalent; first-line prescription option). Vitamin B6 (pyridoxine 10–25mg TID) alone as safest first-line. Ginger supplementation.
treatment_algorithm: Mild: Ginger (250mg QID); vitamin B6 (pyridoxine) 10–25mg TID; dietary modification. Moderate: Doxylamine 10mg + pyridoxine 10mg (Bonjesta ER 2 tabs QHS or BID, FDA-approved). Promethazine 25mg q4–6h; metoclopramide 10mg TID; trimethobenzamide 300mg TID. Severe (Hyperemesis Gravidarum): IV ondansetron + hydration (see HG entry). All FDA-approved antiemetics for nausea of pregnancy: doxylamine-pyridoxine, promethazine, metoclopramide, ondansetron (off-label).
us_prevalence: ~70–80% of pregnancies; ~3 million cases/year requiring some management. Severe HG: ~1–3% (see separate entry).
patients_needing_tx: ~2 million cases/year requiring pharmacologic antiemetic therapy.
price: $500–$3,000 per pregnancy course.
registrational_endpoint: PUQE score reduction ≥30% from baseline at week 2 of treatment; proportion requiring additional antiemetic intervention.
btd_threshold_vs_soc: PUQE remission (score ≤6) in ≥70% at week 2 vs. ~50% with doxylamine-pyridoxine alone.

symptoms: Spotting or bleeding before 20 weeks; may indicate threatened abortion, ectopic, or implantation
treatment_goal: Identify cause, support ongoing pregnancy if viable, manage complications
first_line_soc: Vaginal progesterone (Endometrin 200mg BID or Prometrium 200mg/day) for threatened abortion with subchorionic hematoma in women with prior pregnancy loss—some evidence (PRISM trial).
treatment_algorithm: Threatened abortion: Bed rest (limited evidence) + vaginal progesterone 200–400mg/day (RCT benefit in prior miscarriage subgroup; PRISM trial 2019). Reassurance + serial βhCG and US. Ectopic: MTX or surgery (see ectopic entry). Subchorionic hematoma: Progesterone, pelvic rest. Mifepristone + misoprostol if missed abortion confirmed. RhoGAM for Rh-negative patients. Dydrogesterone (oral progestogen)—widely used in Europe (PRISM/PROMISE evidence); not FDA-approved for this indication in US.
us_prevalence: ~15–25% of recognized pregnancies bleed in 1st trimester; ~600,000–1 million cases/year.
patients_needing_tx: ~500,000 cases/year receiving pharmacologic management (progesterone, RhoGAM).
price: $1,000–$5,000 per pregnancy.
registrational_endpoint: Ongoing pregnancy rate at 12 weeks; miscarriage rate at 20 weeks vs. placebo.
btd_threshold_vs_soc: Ongoing pregnancy rate ≥75% at 12 weeks vs. ~65% with vaginal progesterone alone in threatened abortion with prior miscarriage.

symptoms: Lower abdominal or pelvic pain in first trimester; requires exclusion of ectopic or miscarriage
treatment_goal: Identify cause, reassure if benign, treat ectopic/miscarriage as needed
first_line_soc: Acetaminophen (Category B; safest analgesic in pregnancy) for symptomatic relief. Avoid NSAIDs in 1st trimester. If ectopic: MTX or surgery (see ectopic entry). If round ligament pain: Acetaminophen + positional modification.
treatment_algorithm: Round ligament pain (most common): Acetaminophen 500–1000mg PRN; supportive maternity belt; modified activity. Symphysis pubis dysfunction: Pelvic PT + analgesics. Threatened abortion: Vaginal progesterone (see SAB entry). Ovarian cyst: Observation + acetaminophen; surgery if torsion. UTI-related: Antibiotics (nitrofurantoin, cephalexin).
us_prevalence: ~30–50% of women have some pelvic discomfort in early pregnancy; clinically significant requiring evaluation: ~1–2 million/year.
patients_needing_tx: ~1 million/year requiring pharmacologic pain management.
price: $500–$2,000 per pregnancy course.
registrational_endpoint: Pain NRS score reduction ≥50% at 48 hours; proportion returning to normal activity at 1 week.
btd_threshold_vs_soc: Pain NRS ≤3 in ≥70% at 48h vs. ~55% with acetaminophen alone.

symptoms: Vaginal bleeding after 20 weeks; painless (previa) or painful (abruption), fetal distress
treatment_goal: Identify cause, stabilize mother and fetus, plan safe delivery timing
first_line_soc: IV fluid resuscitation + blood transfusion for hemorrhage. Antenatal corticosteroids (betamethasone 12mg IM × 2) for fetal lung maturity if <34 weeks. Management depends on etiology (placenta previa vs. abruption vs. vasa previa).
treatment_algorithm: Placenta previa: Betamethasone (lung maturity) + pelvic rest + planned cesarean at 36–37 weeks. Placental abruption: IV resuscitation, MgSO4 neuroprotection (<32 weeks), emergent delivery if severe. Vasa previa: Hospitalization + planned cesarean at 34–35 weeks. Bloody show/cervical change (normal): Expectant + monitoring. Coagulopathy (abruption-DIC): FFP, cryoprecipitate, platelets, tranexamic acid (Cyklokapron/TXA).
us_prevalence: ~1–5% of pregnancies have significant antepartum hemorrhage; ~40,000–200,000 cases/year in the US requiring intervention.
patients_needing_tx: ~100,000 cases/year requiring active pharmacologic + obstetric management.
price: $5,000–$15,000 per pregnancy episode.
registrational_endpoint: Maternal composite morbidity (transfusion, DIC, hysterectomy); neonatal intact survival to 28 days; gestational age at delivery.
btd_threshold_vs_soc: ≥30% reduction in composite maternal morbidity vs. current SoC resuscitation + delivery protocols.

symptoms: Bilateral leg and ankle swelling, pitting edema, discomfort during third trimester
treatment_goal: Relieve discomfort, distinguish from pathological edema (preeclampsia, DVT)
first_line_soc: Conservative measures (leg elevation, compression stockings). No pharmacologic first-line SoC for physiological edema. Diuretics (furosemide) only if cardiac or renal pathology.
treatment_algorithm: Physiological edema: Leg elevation, compression stockings (20–30 mmHg), reduced sodium intake, walking. Pathological (preeclampsia): See preeclampsia entry. DVT: LMWH (enoxaparin). Diuretics avoid in physiological edema (reduce placental perfusion). Magnesium supplementation may reduce cramping.
us_prevalence: ~50–80% of pregnant women experience lower extremity edema; ~3 million cases/year.
patients_needing_tx: Most managed conservatively; ~200,000 with concerning edema requiring pharmacologic workup/treatment.
price: $1,000–$3,000/year (novel safe anti-edema agent in pregnancy).
registrational_endpoint: Edema volumetric reduction (plethysmography) at 2 weeks; symptom score improvement; safety (no placental blood flow compromise).
btd_threshold_vs_soc: ≥50% edema volume reduction at 2 weeks vs. ~25% with compression alone.
